Original_Search_Title: MP Spends ¢398 Million On Projects
Headline: Ghana: MP Spends Â¢398 Million On Projects
Body_Text:
The Member of Parliament (MP) for the Kpone-Katamanso Constituency, Hon. Nii Laryea Afotey Agbo, a.k.a. Lion has undertaken a number of development projects throughout the constituency with his share of the HIPC and GETFund funds.
According to him, he has spent about ¢398 million on the various projects he has undertaken in his constituency between January and December, this year.
Speaking in an interview with the paper last Saturday, the Kpone Katamanso legislator said he has purchased three (3) corn-mills and three (3) cassava grinding machines at a cost of ¢33 million out of his share of the HIPC Funds for some women at three villages in the constituency namely; Gonteng, Old Saasabi and Mensah Bar, for income-generating activities.
He continued that about a month ago, he gave out ¢60 million out of his share of the HIPC Funds to be used to purchase electricity poles, to help provide electricity to the people of Zeenu, also in his constituency.
Lion, further explained that he gave out also ¢30 million, again from his share of the HIPC Funds for work to be carried out on the Ashaiman Jerusalem Community and Zeenu Dam site project.
"I have also bought light poles worth ¢20 million to be used to help extend electricity to residents of Kubekro Numbers 1 & 2, all villages in my constituency, from my share of the GETFund.
"Out of my GETFund resources, I have also given out ¢100 million for the painting and provision of electric ceiling fans for the Primary and Junior Secondary Schools (JSS) buildings at the Michel Camp, also in my constituency", the Hon. MP stated.
According to him, he has purchased a number of light poles to be erected in areas such as Kpone, Zeenu, Kpone-Kokompe and Gbezile all in his constituency and bulbs at a cost of ¢155 million also from the GETFund adding "In fact the workers have already started erecting the light poles in some of the areas".

Original_Search_Title: Why Nigeria Must Be Africa's Financial Hub-Soludo
Headline: Africa: Why Nigeria Must Be Africa's Financial Hub-Soludo
Body_Text:
Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) Governor, Professor Charles Soludo, has said that the goal of the Financial System Strategy 2020, which is to make Nigeria Africa's financial hub, is important to sustaining the nation's economy.
Speaking, last week, on "Financial System Strategy 2020: The Next Development Frontier", at the second edition of NTA annual public lecture series, Soludo observed that, "as Africa's potential largest economy, it needs to be the dominant financial services market for sustainable economy."
He explained that, "Usually, a financial hub has much larger financial services than the requirement of the local economy. Examples of such hub are those of New York, London, Hong Kong, Singapore, Tokyo, Dubai, Zurich and Seoul. The closest hubs to Nigeria are Dubai, Zurich and London."
He noted that South Africa is a mini-hub for Southern Africa while Nigeria is a mini-hub for Western and Central Africa. But, no major hub exists in Africa. "The Gulf of Guinea oil exploration and its collateral fallouts, along with the positive outcome of reforms across Africa, places Nigeria at the vortex of an emerging international financial activity. If Nigeria does not act to take advantage of this emerging opportunity, another nation will move to fill the emerging need," he averred.
Soludo observed that, "Several countries have used the concept of the hub to orchestrate economic growth namely Dubai, Singapore, Hong Kong. Some hubs grew organically out of natural need and historical developments namely Londan, Zurich and New York. Some grew out of circumstance and accident of history namely Hong Kong, while others grew out of deliberate decisions to create them and use them to attract and retain developments namely Dubai, Singapore and Panama."
Nigeria, he noted, has the advantage of location, large economy, language, population size and potential skill, adding that the country, however, does not have the luxury of waiting to grow organically, hence, it is, therefore, proposed that it adopts, deliberately, the engineering approach which is through deliberate policies and actions.
Soludo observed further that the country has the needed economic potentials to support its emergence as Africa's financial hub. He said, for example, "Nigeria comprise 79 per cent of the gross domestic product (GDP) of ECOWAS sub-region. One in every six blacks in the world is a Nigerian; The country is the world's sixth in gas reserves; Nigeria's foreign direct investment (FDI) is one of the highest in Africa. It has one of the highest returns on investment in real dollar terms.
Furthermore, he noted, "Nigeria has sustained macroeconomic stability, including stable exchange and appreciating local currency, backed by the highest level of reserves, in Africa, of about $42 billion. Nigeria has a rapidly growing non-oil sector, which is recording phenomenal growth in some sectors, with telecoms growing at 30 per cent per annum. In cassava, it is now world's highest producer, solid minerals, services and even manufacturing are growing and Nigeria's banking sector is the fastest growing in Africa while the growth of the nation's capital market is about the fastest in the world." He said, in addition to these, "Nigeria's base GDP (2005 year end) was $113 billion and the average growth rate for 2003 to 2004 has been 7.4 per cent while Non-oil grew at about 8.8 per cent.
Also, more than half of Nigeria's population is made up of teenagers or youths, which implies a vibrant population for the future which is necessary to unleash and take advantage of economic growth".

Original_Search_Title: MTN Paints Scribes Yellow
Headline: Uganda: MTN Paints Scribes Yellow
Body_Text:
It has become a norm for MTN Uganda to host the media at a "yellow party" every year. Scribes who turned up for this year's party last week were convinced this was the highlight of their nightlife on their social calendar.
Time keeping was good and by 9 p.m. Ange Mystique was filled with all categories of journalists; editors, newsreaders, reporters, salesmen and needless to say, a few flukers.
Snacks ranged from yellow cassava to chicken and drinks flowed.
Many drank until they started staggering, though some were overheard complaining that it was not a good idea for MTN to stage the party on a Wednesday with two more working days ahead.
The night wasn't lacking on live performances from some of Uganda and East Africa's finest artistes. When Obsessions took to the stage, there was no room for who-does-what in their respective media houses but rather who-is-who on the dance floor. They sang some of their latest ballads Jukira and Jangu.
Nubian Li and 2006 Pam artiste of the year, Bobi Wine also moved the crowds with songs like Bada, Adam ne Kawa and Ghetto but the night went to Kenyan star Nameless who was imported by MTN specifically for the media party.
When he sampled Ninanoki (featuring Amani), the song that introduced him to the Ugandan audience in 2002, the audience went crazy and it was a non-stop thrill as he sang other songs like Juju and his latest hit Sinzia, which was the epitome of the show.
When performances ended like close to midnight, it was back to business as usual and the pressmen had to decide whether to stay and shake to the favourite music or retire for the next day's work.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Donates N35m to Six Communities
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Donates N35m to Six Communities
Body_Text:
Warri — SHELL Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) has donated over N35million to six rural and riverine communities in Warri South, South- West, Isoko North and Ekeremo local government councils of Delta and Bayelsa States.
The amount covers four 16 seater Hiace buses donated to Kokodiagbene and Angalaweigbene communities; Micro-credit loan to Kantu, Omadino and Kokodiagbene communities; a rice processing mill to Ayamassa community and a Cassava mill valued at N2,437,940 to the Ovrode community respectively.
Making the presentation on behalf of the SPDC, Mr.Yemi Awolola the Interface Cordinator, land Areas, SPDC-West and the Asset Manager, Northern Swamp, Mr. M Ikhureigbe said the donation is aimed at empowering host communities, stressing that these empowerment tools would serve as a good example of their sustainable approach to community development with emphasis on the full participation of the benefiting communities in project identification, planning, execution, operation, maintenance and monitoring.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Donates N35m to Six Rural Communities
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Donates N35m to Six Rural Communities
Body_Text:
Warri — IN demonstration of care and good neighbourliness, the Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) has donated over N35million to six rural and riverine communities in Warri South, South- West, Isoko North and Ekeremo local government councils of Delta and Bayelsa States.
The amount covers four 16- seater Hiace buses donated to Kokodiagbene and Angalaweigbene communities; Micro-credit loan to Kantu, Omadino and Kokodiagbene communities; a rice processing mill to Ayamassa community and a cassava mill valued at N2,437,940 to the Ovrode community respectively.
Making the presentation on behalf of the SPDC, Mr.Yemi Awolola the Interface Cordinator, land Areas, SPDC-West and the Asset Manager, Northern Swamp, Mr. M Ikhureigbe said the donation is aimed at empowering host communities, stressing that these empowerment tools would serve as a good example of their sustainable approach to community development with emphasis on the full participation of the benefiting communities in project identification, planning, execution, operation, maintenance and monitoring.
Congratulating the benefiting communities and women co-operative groups, the SPDC challenged them to make a success out of the various schemes haven been trained in cooperative development, entrepreneurial development, small business marketing ,banking and financial management, monitoring and project supervision adding that it is their hope that the micro- credit schemes and the buses would be put into maximum use for their benefit.
They called on the communities to always provide a good working environment for the company's operation to enable them achieve the highest standards and integrity in sustainable community development approaches.

Original_Search_Title: Have the Reforms Reformed Us?
Headline: Nigeria: Have the Reforms Reformed Us?
Body_Text:
Lagos — "Have all these reform measures translated to the alleviation or eradication of poverty among poor Nigerians? The definitive answer is NO! Poor Nigerians have had it hard on them because we have a government that care for the rich and do not consider the plight of the common man. For an administration that has increased the price of petroleum products five times in six years without due consideration of involving the masses in a dialogue is most shameful and sad. With all the reforms going on, privatisation of government companies, where government officials use 'fronts' in buying over government companies, and one man wants to own all syndrome which we are told is quietly causing the feud between the two top men in Abuja, is annoying. Much for a President who does know that price of 'garri', the staple food of the common man in Nigeria, is most disturbing. We are trapped firmly in the period of big man politics, a politics founded totally on individuality.Yes I do agree that 'Nigerians have not significantly benefited from these reforms not because the reforms are fundamentally flawed but simply because of the socio-political and institutional weaknesses of the Nigerian state.
The gains and dividends of reforms are concentrated in the hands of the ruling class, their cronies and some sections of the Nigerian elite. Take, for instance, the setting up of the corporation called TRANSCORP. To me, it is laudable but I do not see that company making it in time to come, for we know that Nigerians are good at starting and setting up companies and schemes. Like the Onwuka High Tech Company that today is moribund, sustainability will be the bane of that corporation - mark it! Though I am told that the major founders of the corporation belong to the clique called AGIP (Any Government In Power).
Did I hear you say "the telecommunications sector which used to be comatose has been completely revolutionized? The refineries are back in operation and fuel queues have virtually disappeared following the deregulation of the petroleum and gas sectors of the economy"? Well that may be true but do you know that Nigerians pay the highest tariff for airtime in the world? Yes it is true. So what kind of reform is that, that chokes the people and milks them? Can't the government intervene? Surely they can, but they will not, because the big guys in Apo Villa are given free airtime by these selfish profit-driven major IPOs who claim they are doing us well. Most sarcastically they come to splash us with their "bonanza" and yearly elaborate anniversaries, while they rake in billions. What about the refineries? Did you say they are working? For your information, Kaduna Refinery is closed, Port Harcourt Refinery's capacity utilisation is below 50 per cent, etc.
Whatever petroleum product you find in the country today is imported, forget about all the talk of our refineries working; it is all misinformation. Poverty is still prevalent in the country despite the government's effort of creating over 1,000,000 jobs as claimed since inception. Where are the jobs.? Have you seen one? It is all misinformation and propaganda. The present government's disappointment is more than enough for us to bear. Take the "Heartbeat" State (Edo) for example. It is classed amongst the most undeveloped states at present in the country. There has been no project undertaken by the government here that has ever been completed. The fruit juice factory was commissioned three years ago by the President but believe me till date, not a single fruit juice has been produced.
Today, the factory is over-grown with weeds with no single equipment installed. Take the cement factory, road construction, cassava processing factories, to mention but a few. Some projects have commenced but till date none has been commissioned even after seven years in power. The roads in the state are so bad that the slightly motorable roads are the ones done by the Federal Government or its parastatals. During the raining season, don't come out when it rains, for you will be overwhelmed by flood. Houses are destroyed each year, people die from flood disaster in the city, and each quarter the state is given ecological funds, which goes into the hands of a select few. The state is laden with corrupt politicians and civil servants who sing the praise of the helms men, nobody opposes the men-on-top. All are held bound by the gangsterism established in the state. Come to my state and see 'official corruption' personified.
Even the outgoing Nigeria Labour Congress President summarised my state's plight, as "... one of the states that have suffered poor governance for so long and even till now".
•Mr. Uwagbae, a social critic, writes from Edo State.

Original_Search_Title: Help for Child-Headed Homes
Headline: Zambia: Help for Child-Headed Homes
Body_Text:
Zambia is grappling with the growing problem of thousands of child-headed homes, created by one of the highest HIV infection rates in the world.
Kapiri Mposhi, a commercial hub in central Zambia, about 200km north of the capital, Lusaka, has one of the highest levels of HIV prevalence in the country and a significant number of child-headed homes. UNAIDS estimates the national HIV rate at 17 percent.
The town is situated halfway between the industrial Copperbelt province and Lusaka on the Great North road, the only highway linking Zambia to Tanzania in the northeast, and is a mandatory stop for truckers. It is also a significant stop on the railway line connecting Zambia and Tanzania.
"Because it is a transport hub, frequented by truckers, the town has become a commercial sex centre with a high HIV/AIDS prevalence rate," said James Zulu, a spokesman for the Zambia Red Cross Society, which runs programmes supporting child-headed households.
The UNAIDS '2006 Report on the Global AIDS Epidemic' estimated that Zambia had about 710,000 AIDS orphans, of which over six percent were homeless and less than one percent were accommodated in orphanages. The rest either fend for themselves in their parents' homes or are looked after by elderly grandparents.
According to Charles Mwinuna, district director of health in Kapiri Mposhi, there are over 850 officially registered orphans and vulnerable children (OVC) in a population of less than 300,000 but the unofficial number could be higher. "We have not been able to establish as yet how many of these vulnerable children are currently in child-headed homes, but we know that they should be in big numbers because of the locality and socioeconomic nature of this town, which has put people at greater risk of contracting HIV/AIDS."
Many orphaned children end up as sex workers along the highway, but there are exceptions. Memory Kawaya, 16, has looked after her two younger siblings for the past seven years. The family of three lives in a grass-thatched hut on the outskirts of town. She attends schools and sells mushrooms to help support the family, and she and her siblings have twice tested negative for HIV. Kawaya is determined to maintain her negative status by abstaining from sex.
"Many people have been approaching me for sex and [they] promise to give me money, but I refuse to give in because of what I have heard and seen about HIV/AIDS. I want to become a teacher after completing school, so that I can look after my brother and sister - that's why I don't even think about any sexual relationship," she said.
Kawaya was nine when she lost her mother, and her two siblings were aged seven and five. "We do not have any relative to look after us ... we have been living like this and we often eat only once per day - we can't afford three meals. I am only happy that we are all in school, although I am usually frustrated by my headmaster, who is always harsh with me for not looking as smart as other pupils," she said. They grow cassava, beans and maize in an adjoining plot to help feed themselves.
The Red Cross Society has started initiatives working with vulnerable children. "We have identified 734 orphans and vulnerable children in the town, whom we support with groceries, clothing and school fees," said Zulu. The Red Cross caregivers also visit child-headed households in Kapiri Mposhi to ensure they are coping.
Zulu said the society was also encouraging older children to run small businesses, mostly vending, to support their families. "We do not want to encourage child labour, but we provide them with small amounts of money to buy groceries or other foodstuffs to sell."
[Produced in partnership with the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies: www.ifrc.org]

Original_Search_Title: A Pig for a Name
Headline: Uganda: A Pig for a Name
Body_Text:
Kampala — MONDAY
The Marathon is finally over and though I was not the winner, there were many others behind me. I used my experience from my boxing days to complete the race. The thing is to look around and find motivation for success. I looked around and there were old people, fat people, short people and women! I decided that none of them would win me!
TUESDAY
My next challenge is to see how to cash in on the ongoing name-for-pig project. Who cares if some mzungu believes I can be called Hornsleth or whomever, as long as he parts with a domestic animal. Such a name for me, is better than names like Zirabamuzaale, Nakuzambwa, Munakukama or Bakalikwila!
WEDNESDAY
There is a problem with rearing pigs and goats: Laura has refused to be called Mrs. Hornysleth; the doctor has given me a long list of things I am not to eat or die. These include all dairy products. I am to eat only cassava, potatoes and katogo!
THURSDAY
The rains have heard our cries for the re-filling of Lake Victoria. Next week a few friends and I will visit the lake to make an environmental impact assessment of this month's rains on the lake. But the people in Bwaise and Kalerwe are cursing the day they agreed to settle in those swamps!
FRIDAY
It is Friday when I had promised Laura an outing but the good Lord might yet be with me and more rains come down. As much as she loves an outing, the girl hates cold and muddy nights in Ssebbagala's pot holed City. If it rains the outing is off and some money is saved and I remain loaded!
SATURDAY
Now that they have kidnapped an artiste just for making a film and showing it, I have to be very careful since I am an upcoming film writer and producer! My head is full of film projects, I am afraid if they see what is inside my medulla oblongata they may kidnap me! I need to go to Police for protection?
SUNDAY
Since God is everywhere and today Umeme has had mercy on us, I will stay and attend church via television. I will either tune in to God via California or our own miracle Centre here in Rubaga.

Original_Search_Title: Stakeholders' Forum or Good Governance?
Headline: Nigeria: Stakeholders' Forum or Good Governance?
Body_Text:
Yours sincerely was a participant at the last controversial Presidential Stakeholders' Forum on Education at Abuja.
I therefore bear testimony that feverish mass assemblage of people officially talked at (not necessarily talked with!) on policy issues always after discovery of some huge rot under the advertised banner of a stakeholders' forum cannot and should not be substitute for policy formulation and policy implementation by those elected and appointed to govern. Simply put, stakeholders' forum is no substitute for good governance. Stakeholders' forums are assuming notoriety as official platforms for governmental policy grandstanding increasingly aimed at concealing failed governance when the actual expectations of the electorate of those elected and appointed to govern is service delivery, service delivery and service delivery ad infinitum. The concept of stakeholders has been promoted by varying donor agencies and multilateral institutions such as International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank. This is after much considerable global criticism of the top-down approach of these "development partners" to development issues in developing countries. It was not uncommon for instance for poverty alleviation policies conceived in Washington, D.C. to be rammed down the throats of some poor consumers in Malawi. Stakeholders' forum is a smart but nonetheless top-down device to either legitimise or rationalise policy preferences through pretence at some involvement of the people about their plight. Even at that, it is doubtful if the multilateral institutions which invented this cleaver legitimisation device ever imagined that routine collection and summoning of potpourri of onlookers, mass-dignified as stakeholders would become substitute for genuine governance as it is increasingly the case in Nigeria in recent times. We have had stakeholders' forum on banking sector, forum on aviation sector, forum on census, forum on Niger Delta, forum on textile, forum on cassava, etc, yet the more we look the less we see qualitative value addition for time and resources tied down for hours of alleged stockholding by stakeholders. While on the one hand, stakeholders' forum gives the impression of presidential concern for the sectors under searchlight, your guess is as good as mine as to the efficacy of these unstructured periodic televised sermons on the mountain. For one, these stakeholders' forums often take place after (and not) before abysmal degeneration in the respective sector. The forums are more reactive rather than proactive betraying elementary principle of good governance which is all about envisaging problems and challenges within a broad vision and mission of elected government officials. Judging from the quality of mass media camera coverage of each forum, observers note that the grandstanding has tuned into another public relations exercise under the jack-boot of the President and his Ministers to say we care about the rot even if the problems persist. This observation has been reinforced by the painful fact that every forum is followed by deeper rot in the respective sector instead of advertised promised changes. For instance, the stakeholders' forum on aviation called in the wake of serial air disasters has been followed by greater carnage. Countless gatherings on Niger Delta have been greeted with more deadly raids and hostage takings by the militants. Textile industry and manufacturing sector in general slides from bad to worst under the regime of countless stakeholders' forums. The strikes in Unity schools worsen after a stakeholders' forum and it took good governance via traditional institutions for conflict resolutions to get the striking teachers back to work and not some grandstanding called stakeholders' forum. Of what relevance are mass gatherings that increasingly tie down working executives who should actually be working to solve problems after which the problems get further compounded? Many observers note that the problem with the last census exercise was that governors and other relevant stakeholders who should be working for proper head count at local levels were receiving presidential lectures at "Stakeholders' Forum" in Abuja.
The bane of stakeholders' forums is their false claim to mass participation. Even the arrangement of sitting departs from the time-tested town halls in which local chiefs rob shoulders with their peoples in the great task of communal governance of problem identification and problem solving. The sitting arrangement of a typical stakeholders' forum is imperial: the President and his select ministers predominate in a totalitarian manners dispensing some wisdom to the army of assumed ignoramuses. I have not seen any forum in which all the President and his courtiers did was to listen and listen. On the contrary, in all forums the President did most if not all the talking; introduction and summary and even acted master of ceremony. In not a few forums the President actually banged tables repeatedly. What manner of participation can anybody have in this kind of garrison forum tagged stakeholders' forum? The last presidential forum on education was one huge exercise at presidential top-down talk down. President and Minister Obiageli Ezekwesili consumed five hours out of the estimated six-hour long forum talking to a converted audience about the imperatives of education while the duo determined who should and should not speak and for how long. There was a false impression that all the participants at the forum that included the late Eminence Sultan Maccido were all employees of the Presidency. Nothing could be more patronising for a government who admitted abysmal failure in education and whose officials should do more listening than talking the obvious with poorly-developed power points and twisted statistics. There was nothing ministerial about the Education Minister who was indulged to give repetitive talks and grandstand on what is clearly obvious to all: collapse of education!
The point cannot be overstated that stakeholders' forums should not be substitutes for good governance. There is a false impression that the platforms provide fresh ground for new initiatives and ideas. This further betrays good governance. Nigeria is not short of ideas and policy options arrived at through consensus and mass participation. The ideas and policy options contained in vision 2010 for instance and even the last controversial Political Reform Conference were and are still far more robust than feverish top-down "resolutions" of endless stakeholders' forums.

Original_Search_Title: Luweero Anglican Bishop Shuffles Clergy
Headline: Uganda: Luweero Anglican Bishop Shuffles Clergy
Body_Text:
Luweero — Anglican Bishop, Rt. Rev. Evans Mukasa Kisekka on Sunday shuffled the diocese leadership.
This was during the annual diocesan council meeting at Luweero Diocesan Guest House.
Can. Elidad Nsubuga, who has been serving as the diocesan secretary for five years, was promoted to the position of provost.
Nsubuga becomes the first provost in the diocese.
He will be in charge of Luweero Cathedral and two other nearby parishes.
Rev. Steven Kasozi was moved Wobulenzi Church as Canon.
Kisekka asked the new leaders to start income generating projects to suppliment the little income they earn as church ministers. He said it would soon become compulsory for every priest to own at least three shambas of cassava, bananas or potatoes on the church land.
"I shall move out to monitor the progress of these projects," Kisekka said.

Original_Search_Title: Meat Price Up
Headline: Uganda: Meat Price Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — BEEF, chicken and goat meat prices have gone up due to low supply, a survey has shown.
A kilogramme of beef costs sh3,000 from sh2,700, while goat meat goes for sh4,000 from sh3,500. Chicken costs between sh6,000 and sh15,000, depending on the size.
Musa Tamale, a butcher at Nakasero Market, said, "There is low supply of cattle to Kampala markets and we are yet to establish the reasons. This has forced prices to go up."
The increase in the price of chicken was attributed to the high costs of grinding maize feeds and low supply due to the fear of bird flu.
Food prices remained stable as supplies of matooke and vegetables were maintained.
A kilogramme of maize flour remained at sh900, millet flour sh1,500 and cassava flour sh600.
Beans, rice and maize remained at sh1,000, sh1,500 and sh400 a kilogramme respectively.
Traders said they expect plenty of supplies mid December when the rains decrease and there is enough sunshine.

Original_Search_Title: Ana Marks 25th Anniversary in Grand Style
Headline: Nigeria: Ana Marks 25th Anniversary in Grand Style
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — The celebration of the 25th anniversary of the Association of Nigerian Authors (ANA) at the Gloryland Cultural Centre in Yenagoa the Bayelsa State capital can be described as a reasonable success. Especially looking at the minute human and financial resources available for the Local Organising Committee of the anniversary celebration. However that did not deter the determination with which the committee and other members of ANA Bayelsa, worked to realize their objective. This objective was the successful organization of a befitting anniversary for ANA.
The opening and closing ceremonies were never short of bigwigs in the literary landscape of Nigeria which included the grand old child of the river Nun, Pa Gabriel Okara and Elechi Amadi, our concubine who agreed to share his muse-wife with us.
Without doubt it was indeed a daunting task to have given the hosting rights of the silver jubilee to a young state and indeed a young ANA like Bayelsa. An ANA that is only 11-writer strong. The task was enormous but accomplishable. So it is with heart that I say that they have done marvelously well.
The ultra-modern theatre hall of the Gloryland Cultural Centre was the joker of the organizers. It was near perfect. There wouldn't have been a better place in the whole of Bayelsa that would have comfortably seated and hosted an event of this magnitude and importance. The proportionately large stage was well lighted with shades of flourishing colours and the surface textured to help grasp the feet of actors and dancers alike. The proximity of the stage to the audience was only a fine line of red carpet and a sitting level elevation which could disappear immediately performance was in progress. Although the echo of the dome was a little damp but it was able to carry the voices on stage with an acceptable wavelength. No wonder why the convention play Drums of the Delta held the attention of the audience with music and drums movement and other choreographic modulation in confluence of the poignancy of stage direction and transition, costume and the projection and integration of chorus.
The command performance of Drums of the Delta by Ben Binebei stewed the senses of the informed audience in the social realism of the deltaic regions of Nigeria: The disagreements between the Old and the New; the suspicion existing between community leaders and the generality of their communities. On the whole, the aroma of distrust, mistrust and internal wrangling of destructive tendencies disfigured all groups alike and pervaded the air and it came to an ultimate climax when the leader of the youths is killed and a tremulous reconciliation was reached.
One of my note is that, the gallery of the Gloryland Cultural Centre can seat a capacity crowd without groaning and also wherever any spectator seats to watch the stage is never inconveniencing. This assertion was tested when the play Drums of the Delta lost heat, strength and intensity both physical and spiritual at the middle of the performance. It became too long and dragging and action was slack. The plot became overstretched and windy. Nonetheless, the actors picked up what was left and gave an impressive dénouement. And all these while, the audience was alert on their comfortable folding seats.
The theme of the silver jubilee "literature and history ANA and the development of Nigerian Literature" was aptly spelt out by the keynote address while the sub-themes which were discussed by the seminar papers presented expressed the ardent desires for the way forward for the creative and the ideological direction of Nigerian writing in general.
Gloryland also hosted the ANA annual business meeting where ANA progress report was given and discussed. The trend of discussion showed that the current national executives led by Dr. Wale Okediran has a lot of followership and trust. The representative of the state chapters also affirmed their confidence in the executives since they suggest that it has been transparent enough in all its dealings with the state chapters. It was there that the Imo State Chapter was given the hosting rights for the 26th edition of the convention come next year.
Excursions were planned for Twon Brass Agip Installation, Ox Bow lake and the Niger Delta University. But they did not take place because the trips coincided with the PDP State Convention which took place through out the federation. That Bayelsan afternoon was going to be a little restive and so it was. That also necessitated the last minute cancellation of the venue for the award nite which was originally scheduled for the state house Creek Haven, Yenagoa. The presence of this gubernatorial declaration also made accommodation difficult for ANA members. Approximately, the governor of Bayelsa State, his Excellency Dr. Goodluck Jonathan and the Secretary to the State government Amb. Bolade Igali, all apologized on behalf of the state and the LOC.
In other words, accommodation was adequately applied for until the politicians came to town and made the hoteliers change their minds. They must have been offered something much more than ANA Bayelsa could ever offer and that left the LOC gaping. This development may not be unconnected with the fact that the LOC could not get the N7 million approved by the government for the event as at Saturday, 4th November 2006. But for the show to go on, the committee had to source for loans from financial institutions which amounted to a little over N4 million. Here I say that maybe the event would have fared better that it did if all the resources were available. This cap-in-hand connection ANA has with the government was one of the most regretted issues discussed in the business meeting. But it is true that the organization runs on charity.
The prizes for the awards and all other financial needs are met by the members of the association, government parastatals, private companies and well meaning individuals. For example the three additional prizes for this year's awards namely Neco Teen Author Prize (prose), Mazariyya Teen Author Prize (poetry) and Senator Ken Nnamani Prize for Igbo literature are from individuals and parastatals. These and most of the awards of the 14 categories of ANA literary awards are without endowment and this means that when the donor leaves office, the awards die with their financial absence.
Ultimately,the award nite was also hosted by the main gallery of the Gloryland Cultural Centre. The venue helped the stars to glitter the more. The arena was immensely beautified by the excellent combination of light and decoration was made with colourful and shimmering satin and chiffon. Other shapes were made with ribbon dressing and balloons of different shapes and sizes made there opulent presence and the occasional bust from them gave a startling beauty to the ears. By the time the make up of Gloryland was done. It could easily pass for the banquet hall of Creek Haven.
Together with good food and choice drinks, the Bayelsan rib-cracker Mike Ogbolosinger spiced the night with his social realistic jokes which left guests laughing with contentment.
The publishing companies were ably represented but my wish was that they should be more frequent at ANA conventions. Repesented were Kraft Books press, Cassava Republic press and Treasure books. Of these three, Kraft books enjoyed a lot of limelight as an average number of awards were carted away by writers from their stable.
Out of the 14 categories only one did not have any awardee. It is the chevron prose prize for environmental issues. I hope writers will take up creative writing towards that direction.
Also ANA fellowship award was extended to all the past presidents of ANA and the late Major-General Mamman Vasta, although not a past president, but had done commendably well for ANA in time past. Again, all the partners of ANA and supporters of the literary Arts in Nigeria were honoured with ANA Jubilee awards.
There also was the presentation of a book on 70 contemporary Nigerian poets edited by Nduka Otiono, the literary editor of New Age Newspapers and Odoh Diego Okenyodo, the literary editor of the Weekly Trust Newspapers.
The award winning books include Vincent Egbuson's Womandela; Ahmed Yerima's Hardground; Unoma Nguemo Azuah's Sky High Flames; Uche Peter Umez's Sam and the Wallet; Olabisi Are's Home of the Braves; Odili Ujubuonu's Pregnancy of the gods; Ifeanyi Ajaegbo's Dead men walking and other stories; Tubal Cain's Mystery in Our Stream; Ademola Dasylva's Songs of Adamolugbe.
The event looked from Thursday 2nd November to Sunday 5th November 2006.

Original_Search_Title: In Memory of Uganda's Forgotten Heroes
Headline: Uganda: In Memory of Uganda's Forgotten Heroes
Body_Text:
Kampala — Mzee Aurelio Vuje, whose photographic mind earned him the nickname 'camera', is also for the same reason called kamaera, the Madi word for 'it remembers me.' The octogenarian is truly entitled to such names since nothing can be fitting to describe his memory.
For everything Vuje did, there was a substantive reason and when he rummaged through his metallic box that morning, the village knew he was up to something.
He fished out a khaki pair of shorts and a shirt; that priceless memoir of his soldierly escapades, washed it carefully and ironed it in starchy water left from boiled cassava tubers. He shined his army boots and cleared his bugle as he narrated how they - as veterans - introduced the idea of independence in Uganda and "Suku-leke or women's knickers in the area. "Suku-leke actually means sooka oleke or first wait, a cry by the Baganda women who wore knickers as they restrained the impatient male veterans from the north who were not used to knickers," Vuje said.
Next morning, Vuje's trumpet pierced through the village as he marched to the town's central flag post in front of what was then the office of the colonial district commissioner.
He sang songs of victory in praise of Britain and the allied forces and damnation to Japan and Germany. To the inquisitive crowd of children and a few adults, Vuje talked of the great world war and how Africans helped in winning it. He amused the crowd when he said that by the end of the Second World War, a wasted effort of his resulted into a delicately-moulded baby girl of his, born to a Burmese woman.
Full of knowledge, generosity and humility, Vuje, one of the few surviving ex-service men of the Kings African Rifle (KAR) or Keeya, was celebrating the memory of his colleagues in the ceremony of the 'Three Elevens', when at the 11th hour of the night of the 11th day of the 11th month of the year 1918, the armistice to end the First World War was signed. Since then, that day has been marked as the armistice or Veterans' Day in honour of those who not only died in the two world wars, but also diligently served in the armies of their respective nations to bring honour and dignity to mankind.
In the Commonwealth, it is a day of commending the patriotism of the veterans in serving their motherland.
Politicians all over the world know very well that veterans form a very strong social and political lobby, capable of tilting electoral fortunes. The veterans are regarded with dignity and some countries assign a ministry to their welfare.
Vuje is one of the few surviving Ugandan veterans of the World War who rendered service to the British cause without any terminal benefits.
In Uganda as in other parts of the Commonwealth, come November though not necessarily at the eleventh hour of the eleventh day, the British High Commissioner, military attaches from Commonwealth countries and officials representing the army and the Police will turn up at the 11, 11, 11 celebrations at the memorial tomb at the Constitutional Square, where a handful of the surviving ex-service men will routinely ask for their terminal benefits before retiring for bottles of soda and chicken, as the memory of the veterans fades away.
While Britain is eternally debted to the veterans for fighting alongside them, Ugandans owe much to them for bringing the idea of struggling for independence.
In his book, The Kings African Rifle, Lt. Col. Moyse Bartlett studied the military history of East and Central Africa where KAR served in the pacification and prosecution of the two major wars in which the Uganda Rifle was outstanding.
Formally constituted on January 1, 1902, the Uganda Rifles had been trained and raised long before that by chartered companies like the Imperial British East African Company of Capt. Frederick Lugard and Emin Pasha's troops in the north.
Having great soldiery qualities of courage, cheerfulness, endurance and outstanding loyalty, the Ugandan Rifle soon formed part of the six regiments of the KAR such as the central African Regiment, the East African Rifle, the Indian Regiment and the British Somaliland Regiment," Bartlett said.
Based in Bombo and Jinja, the Uganda Rifle played a major role in establishing British administration in East, Central and the Horn of Africa.
During the First World War of 1914-1918, the Uganda Rifle took part in conquering the vast German territory in central and southern Africa, while in the Second World War of 1939- 1945, they fought outside the continent on the side of Britain and the allied forces in Japan - Burma (now Ceylon) and India.
Rising from scantily-equipped levies with outdated arms employed on local expeditions on ill-armed tribes, the Uganda Rifle worked and fought hard from its nucleus of 270 porters, 75 Sudanese soldiers, each having 11 rounds of ammunition and an old maxim gun. Led by Khedive Ismile under the command of Selem Bey, the Uganda Rifle formed Emin Pasha's troops who joined hands with Lugard to get training under Captain W.H Williams. The number, which grew to 650 men, learnt to respond to the bugle calls, advance with order, aim with care and take cover.
The Uganda Rifle fought hard in defence of Kenya between 1939-1940. Following Adolf Hitler's demand for the lost territories, the British War Office on August 22, 1939, sent a war alert, which was dispatched from Nairobi on September 3, 1939. Thereafter, 1,500 Ugandans joined the KAR depot battalion in Jinja to leave for Iringa.
By the time Hitler invaded France and Belgium on May 10, 1939, Ugandan forces of the 6th KAR batallion at Bombo were ready to join their Kenyan counterparts at Lokitaung to defend Somaliland. With their help, Mogadishu fell on February 5, 1941. Members of the Uganda Rifle of 1-6 KAR batallion under Lt. Col. Blackden assaulted Aba in Ethiopia and on April 6, 1941, Gen. Witheral captured Addis Ababa, with the help of East Africans.
Ugandans helped in securing the Madagascan port of Diego Suarez following the loss of Singapore in 1942.
Apart from providing internal security, the Ugandan Rifle defended the construction of the Uganda Railway, by undertaking punitive expeditions in the Kenyan highlands against the marauding Nandis. They secured the Ugandan border and the northern frontiers.
According to Lt. Col. Bartlett, members of the Uganda Rifle could not be paid terminal benefits because the War Office in London had turned down a request by Governor Collville for Britain to regard the Uganda Rifle as Her Majesty's Forces.
"Troops of this kind are regarded as the Police or constabulary over which the War Office has no responsibility," Bartlett quoted in his book.
In his book, My African Journey, former British Prime Minister and statesman, Winston Churchill, who christened Uganda 'The Pearl of Africa' was full of praise for the forces and commanders of the Uganda Rifle among whom, he talked of meeting the first commander who rode a bicycle from Mombasa to Kampala.
As the memory of these gallant sons of Uganda fades, it is saddening to note that all their efforts were in vain. Perhaps when the Queen comes to Uganda in November next year to attend the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting, she will make 11, 11, 11 a worthy memory for the few surviving veterans who live in abject poverty, relishing a memorable past in partnership with British veterans who are enjoying full benefits.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Disrupt Food Supplies
Headline: Uganda: Rains Disrupt Food Supplies
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOODSTUFF supplies have been disrupted due to heavy rains that have made roads to rural areas impassable, a weekly survey has revealed.
Traders said commodity prices remained stable but said distribution would be affected if the rains continue.
"Roads have become slippery. We expected more beans and maize but we are getting little supplies," Julius Kasangaki, a trader at Nakasero Market, said.
"Prices are still stable, but we are not sure what will happen the next day because if the situation continues, we might have shortages and a price increment," Kasangaki said.
Paul Muyigi of St. Balikuddembe Market said the rains would contribute to more food production that will lead to price reduction.
A kilogramme of maize and millet flour remained at sh900 and sh1,500 respectively.
Cassava flour was sold at sh600 from sh400 a kilogramme while beans and rice remained at sh1,000 and sh1,500 respectively. A kilogramme of beans was sold at sh1,300.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Prices Up
Headline: Uganda: Flour Prices Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — Prices of flour have gone up due to the high cost of operating machines by the grain millers, a weekly survey has revealed.
A kilogramme of maize flour was sold at sh900 from sh800, while millet flour went for sh1,500 from sh1,200.
Cassava flour was sold at sh600 from sh400 a kilogramme, while beans and rice remained at sh1,000 and sh1,500 respectively. Prices of other foods also rose.
The Government recently announced a 41% power increment on commercial industries' which includes grain millers.
Traders in Kampala's major markets said prices are expected to go up further in the next few months and many millers will close.
"Electricity prices have gone up. This has forced grain milling owners to hike prices in order to maintain the tariff costs," Julius Kasangaki, a trader, said.
"Most of them have closed. The remaining ones charge high prices to remain in business. This has forced flour prices to go up, " Kasangaki said.
He said continued demand for food from southern Sudan had also contributed to higher prices.

Original_Search_Title: Kaunda is a Man to Admire, But Africa Doesn't Need Such Leaders
Headline: Africa: Kaunda is a Man to Admire, But Africa Doesn't Need Such Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A photograph on the cover of the October 19 issue of the Daily Monitor, Uganda's main independent newspaper, shows President Yoweri Museveni shaking hands with Zambia's former president Kenneth Kaunda (clutching his signature white handkerchief).
Looking on is Rwanda's President Paul Kagame. The picture was taken at the Sandton Convention Centre, Johannesburg, during the Annual Business Leaders' Forum. What is striking about the photo is that, with their heads and chins clean-shaven, Museveni and Kaunda look to be about the same age. However, Museveni is 62 years old, and Kaunda is all of 82!
In the 1960s, Kaunda was part of a left-leaning group of leaders called the Mulungushi Club, along with Tanzania's Mwalimu Julius Nyerere and Uganda's Milton Obote. Both have since died. Kaunda, however, is still reasonably healthy, good-humoured and keen-minded.
What's his secret? A journalist who has followed Kaunda's routine closely, thinks he has it figured out. One, his diet. Kaunda is a vegetarian. But he goes one better; he only eats uncooked food. Mainly fruits, vegetables and juice.
In the morning, he has apple juice with raw groundnuts. For lunch he will usually eat a banana, raw sweet potatoes or raw cassava. In the evenings, he will add a vegetable and fruit platter - mangoes, pineapple, avocado.
On a continent where we have had presidents hunting game in the national parks for Christmas and birthday treats, Kaunda showed a lot of character by staying away from meat altogether.
Second, he has good physical habits. He's been described as a "great" golfer.
Third, he is a not an excitable man, and he's deeply religious. And we all know that he is a musician (playing an accordion, no less).
In addition, unlike an African strongman like Gabon's Omar Bongo, Kaunda avoided the temptation to amass a football team of wives, and an army of children.
During the debate, Kaunda said Africa was a continent at the crossroads because of its failed economic policies, and conflicts arising from bad leadership. Museveni and Kagame offered two different views of the way forward.
Kagame argued for strength of institutions and against the cult of individuals, and made the case for presidential term limits. Museveni, who had the constitution amended last year to allow him to run for a fifth term in office, argued for the importance of visionary leadership.
It was a legitimate philosophical divide that is nearly 200 years old.
In Africa today, it presents the two contending political traditions. When Kaunda was president, it was whether Africa should follow the "capitalist" or "socialist" path. Related to that, was whether one-party rule or multiparty democracy was the "most suited to Africa's unique conditions." In the mix, was whether military rule could be justified.
ALL THREE QUESTIONS HAVE BEEN largely resolved. The capitalist path, its imperfections notwithstanding, has trumped socialism. Multiparty democracy is the norm in most of Africa. Military rule is totally out of fashion.
We also have early indications as to which fork, at Kaunda's crossroads, most of Africa will take. The highest placed five sub-Saharan African countries in the latest World Economic Forum Competitiveness Report (2006/2007), are South Africa at 48th; Mauritius at 58th; Botswana at 83rd; Namibia at 86th; and Kenya at 96th.
In common, these countries have term limits, rule of law rather than individuals and, except for Kenya, established governing parties. Looking to the future, then, it seems Museveni will lose the bet. After all, with all his individual virtues, Kaunda was still a disaster as president.
Charles Onyango-Obbo is Nation Media Group's managing editor for convergence and new products.

Original_Search_Title: Mukono Classrooms Collapse
Headline: Uganda: Mukono Classrooms Collapse
Body_Text:
Kampala — TWO classroom blocks at different schools in Mukono district collapsed last week due to heavy rains, John Semakula reports.
The schools are Living Hope Nursery and Primary School in Nagojje sub-county and Kiwafu Primary School in Kimmenyedde sub-county.
According to the bursar of Living Hope, the building collapsed on Monday during a downpour.
Deborah Nanfuka said 18 pupils were injuried and rushed to Kawolo Hospital.
The LC1 chairman, Emmanuel Suna, said the heavy rain destroyed cassava, potato, beans and maize gardens.
At Kiwafu, a parent at the school, Hajji Hassan Mugerwa, said the building, which housed four classes, collapsed on Thursday evening.
No pupil was injured as they had all returned home.

Original_Search_Title: Foreign Reserve Now Above $40 Billion
Headline: Nigeria: Foreign Reserve Now Above $40 Billion
Body_Text:
Abuja — Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Prof Charles Soludo, yesterday disclosed that the country's external reserves is now slightly above $40 billion.
But Lagos State Governor, Asiwaju Bola Ahmed Tinubu, is not very comfortable with the news saying, rather than accumulate reserves and keep them in foreign accounts, the Federal Government should release the monies to the three tiers of government for developmental projects and programmes that would pull the ordinary people out of poverty.
Speaking at the 36th Annual Conference of the Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria (ICAN), Soludo said the nation's external reserves net slightly over $40 billion, even after the payment of over $12 billion to exit the Paris Club of creditors.
He also said Nigerian banks are now becoming stronger and are now receiving inflows from foreign banks.
"Our banks are now receiving inflows by way of credit lines from foreign banks. We have our external reserves now slightly over $40 billoin even after we paid over $12 billion to exit the Paris Club. The new banking system is getting stronger and better.
"When we started the reforms, none of them was in the top 1000 in the world. As at the end of last year, nine of them are now in and I tell you, by the end of this year when they now take into account the new capital bases of these banks, my prediction is that no less than 15 of them would be in.
"We are not looking at top 1000. Our goal is not the top 1000, we are looking at the top 50 to 100, and I believe this economy has the capacity to get there.
"The deposits in the banking system have actually more than doubled from last year to now with about 101 percent. All those who predicted doom, I think they better look at the numbers and see that the Nigerian banking system has turned the corner. There is no better time to invest in the economy than now.
"FDIs in Nigeria is on the surge particularly for tenor inflows. We now discovered that all the Federal Government bond issued, all the instruments are now heavily oversubscribed.
"Foreign investors are investing in Nigerian instruments massively to an extent that we are even beginning to worry a little bit about over exposure. Historically, every country that has received 5 percent of its GDP per annum in foreign investments, consistently for a long period of time, has invariably suffered from one financial crisis or the other at some point. And so we are watching the development. This is principally because the returns on investment in the country are actually one of the highest in the world", Soludo said.
However, the apex bank boss canvassed for the establishment of commercial courts that would deal with commercial banks' disputes, especially as it relates to loan default.
He disclosed that President Olusegun Obasanjo would next week convene a special meeting that will seek on the way to establish such.
According to him, "commercial courts that will actually be dedicated to dealing with nothing but commercial issues. When a bank gives you a loan and you refuse to pay, they don't take you to the same court that tries murder cases etc We need commercial courts and the President is convening a special meeting on this next Tuesday because justice delayed is justice denied."
Meanwhile, Governor Tinubu who spoke on "Economic Development and Empowerment: Public and Private Sector Initiatives", during the Plenary Session at the ICAN conference, totally disagreed with the policy of piling up external reserves at the expense of Nigerians whom he said are "wallowing in poverty."
He said: "I want to disagree with the CBN Governor, Professor Charles Soludo, on the issue of reserves. Don't lock up the money in reserves, spend it. Let's spend our way out of poverty. The Federal Government should stop being the store keeper without the inventory card."
He said there should not be anything called excess revenue saying all monies realised should be accounted for, in the spirit of transparency and accountability. If you are talking about transparency and accountability, you must talk correctly. You must practice what you preach."
On the recent performance of the National Economy and the impact on Nigerians, Tinubu said the economic indices reeled by the Federal Government, even though portray positive economic situation, ordinary Nigerians are not feeling the positive impact as they are still groaning in hardship.
"The news from official federal sources, including Mr. President's recent national day broadcast is that the various economic indices point to a considerable remarkable economic recovery, which is projected to be further strengthened and deepened by the plethora of projects in the pipeline.
"There is no denying that there has been some progress in the last seven years. Oil price have been at sustained high and often record levels for much of the period", he said.
"Clearly, there need to be greater involvement in the economy by the people. The key empowerment for people comes in the form of jobs. Therefore, unless and until the economic initiatives undertaken by government create substantial new jobs in addition to retaining existing ones (in the public and/or private sector), people will feel left out, and unconvinced of the benefits of the ongoing economic programme, no matter the rosy picture that the statistics may paint", he added.
In her keynote address, ICAN President, Dr. (Mrs.) Catherine Okparaeke said the institute was delighted by the ongoing economic reform programme and its successes.
According to her, "we note with delight and indeed, commend the remarkable progress so far made particularly with the revolution in the telecommunication industry, the debt forgiveness, the consolidation in the banking sector, the introduction of the due process mechanism, the growth in external reserves, the first ever national investment and risk rating, the privatisation of public sector parastatals, the trend towards the professionalism of the public service, the creation of SEVICOM, the cassava revolution , the anti-corruption crusade, the war against financial crimes, the shift of emphasis from oil to solid minerals."

Original_Search_Title: As NDC Presidential Race Gets Murkie, Ayariga Nails Spio, Annan
Headline: Ghana: As NDC Presidential Race Gets Murkie, Ayariga Nails Spio, Annan
Body_Text:
Mr. Mahama Ayariga, the 32-year-old legislator and Spokesperson for Prof. John Evans Attah Mills, former Vice President and leading contender in the race for the presidential ticket of the National Democratic Congress (NDC) has condemned the campaign strategies of Dr. Ekwow Spio-Garbrah and Mr. Eddie Annan, who are also battling for the party's presidential ticket.
In an interview with The Chronicle yesterday, the young lawmaker who had to resist pressures from his party leadership and risked losing his respected Parliamentary position as Minority Spokesperson on Legal and Constitutional Affairs, to hold on to the new position as spokesperson to the Professor, said the campaign strategies of the two contestants, whom he saw as underdogs, were unhealthy for the party.
Mr. Ayariga described as very unfortunate, the consistent efforts of Dr. Spio-Garbrah to discredit Professor Mills by drumming home to party supporters that the Professor was not a good candidate for the party, since he had had the opportunity to be president of the nation after running on the ticket of the party in two elections, losing on both occasions.
"If he continues with that kind of message, it will not be in the interest of the party since at the end, if it turns out that he fails to win, which will be the case, he cannot support Professor Mills during the main elections since he will be in a serious moral dilemma," Mr. Ayariga, who is the Member of Parliament (MP) for Bawku Central, analyzed.
He said from the way Dr. Spio-Garbrah was going about with his campaigning, it would be very difficult for him to support Prof. Mills if the Professor wins the flagbearership slot, "since if at the time, even if Spio would want to lend his support, the people will question his credibility and ask whether he was not the same person who had discredited Mills and described him as not being a viable candidate."
"What Spio is doing can be likened to what J. H. Mensah did to Kufuor in 1998 when they were both battling for the NPP's presidential ticket and he described candidate Kufuor as a rejected cassava that should not be returned to the market. Eventually when Kufuor won the mandate of the delegates to contest for the presidency, Mr. Mensah found himself in a difficult position to campaign for Kufuor since he himself had described him as a bad candidate just on the grounds that he (Kufuor) had lost to the NDC in a previous election," he said.
The MP stressed that it was important for all the contestants to appreciate that after congress, there is going to be only one winner who would have to be supported by all, including the losers and thus desist from the practice of trying to discredit each other in their bid to secure the presidential ticket.
Turning on Mr. Eddie Annan, an Accra-based business tycoon, who is also vying for the flagbearership, the Mills Spokesperson said the businessman was conducting his campaign in a way that creates the impression that the battle for the presidential ticket was about money.
"Eddie Annan would want people to look at the money he has. But that is unfortunate because people should not be made to use the wealth of candidates as the yardstick for determining who ought to be voted for. I think the basis should be the integrity and track records of the candidates and not their financial wealth," Ayariga stressed.
He expressed the conviction that they on the Mills campaign team were sure of victory for the professor and had therefore decided to run a decent campaign since "we are aware that after congress, we would require the support of all the candidates and their supporters to be able to win the national election."
On the chances of Prof. Mills at the congress of the NDC, Ayariga, a Harvard-trained lawyer, maintained that he had toured several parts of the country with the professor and that based on their interactions with delegates, it had become clear that the rank and file of the party would still want to repose their confidence in the former Vice President and twice-defeated candidate of the party.
He noted that all manner of political analysis points to Professor Mills as the most viable candidate of the NDC, emphasizing, "it is clear from the 2004 elections that Prof. Mills has a support base of 45% of the electorates and would require just about 5% to win power, so why would the delegates choose someone who is yet to be tested?"
"After the 1996 elections, Mr. Kufuor had 38% of the total votes and the NPP rank and file realized that it was politically prudent to retain Kufuor for the 2000 elections since he had secured a base and needed just an additional 12% of the total votes plus one, to win the 2000 elections.
The NPP also saw the need to retain Kufuor because the NDC was to change a presidential candidate in that elections since Jerry Rawlings could not contest for a third term, " he said, to emphasize why Professor Mills would be the best candidate for the biggest opposition party.
Asked about the prospects of Prof. Mills in his home region, Central Region, where he recorded the poorest performance in the 2004 elections, Mr. Ayariga said the party's problems in the region had been diagnosed and that the appropriate measures were to be put in place to ensure that the region was annexed from the NPP in the 2008 general elections.
He emphasized that the party was also working hard to increase its support base not only in the Central Region, but also in all the other regions.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Falls to 6 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Falls to 6 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE annual headline inflation rate for September dropped to 6.2% from 7.5% in August.
"Prices of foods like sweet potatoes and cassava went up by 1.7%, while matooke prices went up in Kampala, Jinja, Mbale, Masaka and Mbarara except in Gulu. Fish, milk and eggs' prices increased in a number of centres due to seasonal and distribution factors. Prices of fruits, some vegetables and cereals went up due to low supply," Steven Bahemuka, the acting principal statistician at the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS), said.
He was releasing the Consumer Price Index at the finance ministry in Kampala.
The monthly headline inflation went up by 0.8% due to an increase in the prices of foodstuffs.
Prices of other commodities like charcoal, local brew, household items, soap and toiletries also rose.
Transport fares and medical charges also increased.
Vincent Musoke, the price statician at UBOS, said the transport fares rose because of speed governors.
"Police restricted tax operators to carrying three passengers per row. The charges had to increase to compensate for that gap," he said.
Musoke said the medical charges shot up as a result of high import taxes for goods from Germany, India and the UK.
Bahemuka said October rates would be similar to September's or slightly higher.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agency Challenges Nigeria On Industrial Development
Headline: Nigeria: UN Agency Challenges Nigeria On Industrial Development
Body_Text:
An agency of the United Nations at the weekend in Abuja challenged captains of Nigerian economy to steer the country's economy towards independent of international support for industrial development.
Speaking at the occasion of signing a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with African Business Round Table (ABR) on one hand and the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) on the other, the Director General of UNIDO in the West African subregion, Dr.Kandel Yumkella said: "No UN agency can help Nigerians to develop the industrial base of their economy. The people need to wake up to the reality of the challenges facing them. The government needs to create enabling environment for private participation in the industrial development of the country for the sake of creating jobs for the mass of jobless youth.
Dr Yumkella told the ministers of commerce and that of industry, Dr Aliyu Moddibo Umar and Ambassador Fidelis Tapgun respectively who were in attendance at the occasion that it is their responsibilities to encourage the government to foster good grounds for adding value to the export of Nigeria through the industrial development of the country." It is only when value to add to the cassava, palm oil, e.t.c exported out of Nigeria that jobs can be created for these teaming jobless youth," he explained.
According to him, China with its 1.36billion population has been able to industrialize its economy through the process of adding value to its resource and not just exporting them for others to create jobs for their youth out of it.
He reckoned that Malaysia is now noted for the biofuel it produces by simply adding value to palm fruit it once obtained from Nigeria.
Earlier, the Executive President of the ABR, Alhaji Bamanga Tutur, noted that no nations can be able to sustain itself without industrializing. He explained that public -private partnership as well as increased private participation is required for a sustained industrial development of Nigeria. Hence, he said, the need for ABR to sign an MOU with UNIDO.
Also the representative of the executive secretary of ECOWAS, Dr. Nan Joof, noted that its secretariat has benefited from a considerable numbers of programmes and activities of UNIDO. Thus, he explained, the MOU has become necessary in other to further cement its relationship with UNIDO.
Dr. Joor highlighted UNIDO is expected to assist towards achieving a number of activities in the ongoing reform programme of ECOWAS.

Original_Search_Title: War Against Fake Drugs . . . So Far So What?
Headline: Nigeria: War Against Fake Drugs . . . So Far So What?
Body_Text:
Lagos — The five-year leadership of the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) received another round of accolades recently at the public presentation of a report on the level of counterfeit and substandard medicines in Nigeria. It was sponsored by the World Health Organisation (WHO) and the British Department for International Development (DFID).
And as usual, it was another round of celebration for an agency that has done so well on the local scene that the international arena is always eager to share in its success. The source of its celebration came from the report itself, which says that the level of counterfeit drugs in the country has dropped from a staggering average of 41 per cent in 2001 to 16.7 per cent this year. But the country representative of DFID in Nigeria, Dr. Cassidy, aptly expressed the caveat on this celebration when he said that NAFDAC must not relent in its war against counterfeit and sub-standard products.
Apparently revealing his grasp of the report even before its presentation to the public, Cassidy, however, commended the leadership and the entire staff of NAFDAC for the work it has been doing over the years, stating that the report "makes it very clear that a lot of improvement have been made in this fight." He also said that the study was necessary so that all stakeholders in drug control and administration would be able to have a clearer direction in making sure that good medicines are available to the Nigerian people. Messages also came from the representatives of the WHO, Pharmaceutical Society of Nigeria, drug manufacturers, Association of Community Patent Vendors, and other stakeholders in the war against drug counterfeiting.
The report started with a brief introduction tracing the history of counterfeit and sub-standard drugs in Nigeria, developing countries and the world. The global situation of bad quality medicine, according to the report, dates back to the 17th century when Cinchonic bark suffered adulteration. Vietnam in the present century has suffered heavily from sub-standard anti-malarial and Artesunate; likewise Myanmar. Developed countries were not left out in the counterfeiting scourge. Or what could one say of a certain Allan Valentine who was arrested in the United Kingdom for faking Diazepan and Viagra in his Wembley warehouse?
The methodology of the study, according to the researchers, used to determine the quality of drugs in circulation in Nigeria was by collecting selected medicines from four major study centres in the country - Kano, Lagos, Onitsha, and Jos, each contributing 25 per cent of the total. Medicines with high consumer rate, high unit cost and a listing on the Essential Drug List of the Federal Government made the selection. The study was also to determine the percentage of drugs registered with NAFDAC. The researchers explained that the choice of the four centres was based on the fact that they are the major drug distribution centres in the country.
"In sourcing the samples, made up of tablets, syrups, capsules and injections, we made sure they were each selected from one batch and one brand. Three hundred and thirty-eight samples were gathered and divided into two portions; one portion was sent for laboratory analysis at a WHO-commissioned laboratory in Thailand while the second portion went to NAFDAC Directorate of Registration and Regulation for determination of their registration status," the researchers said. Consideration was also given to chemical component of the drugs rather than their generic or non-generic nature. All samples analysed contained active pharmaceutical ingredients as indicated on their respective labels. Some of the samples collected are Chloroquine, Artesunate, Arethemeter, Paracetamol, Nifedipine Retard, Ampicillin, Cloxacillin and Piroxican, among others. The study determined what makes a drug sub-standard or fake by using the following globally approved criteria:
Fake drugs fall under the first two criteria while sub-standard drugs fall in the third. And a standard drug must meet the condition specified in the fourth bullet.
In its findings, the research team discovered that out of 60 samples from Jos, only 36 passed while 24 failed the laboratory test. From the samples gathered in Onitsha, 79 out of 137 passed. Of the samples gathered from Lagos, 50 passed living the rest as failures. 56 samples made it from those gathered in Kano while 40 failed. In total, 221 samples from the 385 gathered made the pass list, while the remaining 164 failed the laboratory test, which was repeated by a second independent analyst.
Giving further insights into the result, the research discovered that one in every 15 Chloroquine injections failed the test, six in every 26 Chloroquine syrups also failed, another six in 27 Choloroquine tablets didn't pass, while seven in every seven Nifedine Retard failed. In total summary, sub-standard drugs constitute 35.6 per cent of total drugs in circulation in the country. Another 16.1 per cent went the way of drugs not registered with NAFDAC, while 47.5 per cent is the share of genuine drugs with correct quantity and packaging; 0.3 per cent went to drugs with wrong NAFDAC registration number, and drugs with a different manufacturer's tag take up 2.9 per cent. The highlight is that the level of counterfeit medicines in Nigeria is now down to 16.7 per cent and non-compliance to licensing authorisation has fallen from 68 per cent in 2001 to 19 per cent in 2006.
In this era of Millennium Development Goals, which find a bloodline in the health reform programme as outlined in the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy, President Olusegun Obasanjo could hardly hide his delight. Represented by the Minister of Intergovernmental Affairs and Social Development, Dr. Grace Oguche, Obasanjo said during the presentation: " Today, we are witnessing another bold step of NAFDAC in scientifically quantifying the present level of counterfeit and sub-standard medicines in Nigeria. I understand that no such study, carefully designed, using the best available resources, has ever been carried out in the country. This study I was informed, was made possible with the technical input of WHO, which also provided an international consultant, with the sponsorship of DFID and NAFDAC personnel playing key roles."
The report is the first of its kind, according to the NAFDAC consultants that carried out the study. WHO and DFID also acknowledged this. It is, however, important to trace the history of similar studies in the country in order to affirm the unprecedented tag placed on this particular one. What makes it unprecedented?
Four previous studies had been carried out on the subject matter in Nigeria. Poole, WHO and the Federal Ministry of Health estimated in 1989 that the level of fake drugs in the country was 25 per cent while admitting that 75 per cent was inconclusive. Ochekpe et al gave 41 per cent in 1993. Shakoor, Taylor & Dyrehen reported 48 per cent in 2001. And former deputy Director-General of the World Health Organisation, the late Prof. Adeoye Lambo, gave us 54 per cent. The 2005 WHO report on this issue focused on 20 countries, 60 per cent being developing countries, no emphasis on Nigeria. In the present study, the WHO was also involved. But the fact that NAFDAC, the primary organ here, is the initiator of the study makes it different from every other one before it. One should also note that Dr. Ochekpe, who gave the 41 per cent figure in 1993, is one of the three NAFDAC consultants that conducted this present research; so he should know how unprecedented this report is, having been part of previous researches. The study dug deep to source its samples from the grassroots. Samples were sourced from public and private hospitals, pharmacies, dispensaries and patent vendors in Kano, Jos, Lagos and Onitsha market, where the wholesale distributors are concentrated. It was thorough, with in-depth analysis of the methodology used and the results derived. The recommendations that came out of it will go a long way in directing the arsenals of NAFDAC in the war against fake drugs. Among these recommendations are: Strengthening of control mechanism at points of entry; strict monitoring of distribution channels, and consolidating on the already established quality assessment control at countries of origin.
And from stakeholders present at the occasion came some other recommendations. The president of the Nigerian Medical Association, Dr Dan Gana, recommended the establishment of zonal medical stores for proper monitoring of distribution channels, as practised in Sweden and Ghana. The National Association of Community Patent Vendors also want a collaboration with NAFDAC so that it can efficiently carry out its on-going in-house cleaning exercise - an initiative of the association. It is expected that these recommendations will be adopted by the drug and food-regulating agency. This expectation was confirmed by the Director-General of NAFDAC, Prof Dora Akunyili, when she said: "The result of the study will form one of the benchmarks on which activities of the agency in the last five years will be evaluated. It will also be the platform for future studies that will be conducted on fake and counterfeit medicines in Nigeria." And not one whose dreams for the future will be overwhelmed by the achievements of the moment, Akunyili added: "Our dream is to handover to our children, a country free from fake/counterfeit drugs and other sub-standard regulated products and create a strong regulatory environment that will enable our food and drug industries become major players in Africa and indeed the world."
A dream rooted in the president's resolve, as expressed in his speech: "Nigeria is on the right part to where it should be, even though the present position is still not acceptable. All hands must continue to be on deck so as to ensure an acceptable global level. Even one per cent below universal standards is unacceptable to any responsible government because life is too precious to be lost through avoidable circumstances like taking of fake drugs."
In all, the report was considered unprecedented by all present, despite questions raised on its methodology, especially the markets from which the samples were sourced, the non-inclusion of multivitamins and the little significance placed on antiretroviral drugs.
