Nigeria must indeed be free from chalks in capsules, cassava-enhanced tablets, drugs stored under sunlight, injections with fraudulently extended expiry dates and deaths of drug users who have no reason to die because they are innocent of drug abuse.

Original_Search_Title: Emulating Gani Audu
Headline: Nigeria: Emulating Gani Audu
Body_Text:
THE setting was grand for the Award of Excellence to the best 30 local government chairmen in Nigeria by the management of Daily Independent newspaper, which includes who's who in Nigerian politics at the Shehu Musa Yar'Adu Centre, Abuja.
With the calm weather and friendly atmosphere walked in a friend of mine Professor Godwin Sogolo, formerly of the Institute for Peace and Conflict Resolution Abuja, whom I have lost contact with for some time. As we were exchanging pleasantries came in Mr. Ted Ewere, Editor-in-Chief of Daily Independent newspaper. Seeing these mighty men of the pen was an indication that something serious was in the making. In no time there was an unusual gathering of local government chairmen from different political parties complete with posters indicative of success. Hearing about the event ignited my curiosity as I was informed that the occasion was a meirt award night for the best 30 local government elected chairmen in Nigeria.
With the introduction of Chief Tony Anenih, PDP Board of Trustees Chairman as the Chairman of the occasion and the Minister of the Federal Capital Territory, Matlam Nasir el-Rufai, as the Chief Host, further heightened my desire to witness the event. Soon it was the turn of Gani Audu to receive his award. When it was announced that Chief Anenih would present the award as a mark of honour since both came from Edo State, it was greeted with an emotionally- charged thunderous ovation enhanced by the beating of traditional goge. This marked a milestone in the life of Gani Audu, the Executive Chairman of Etsako West Local Government Area, a place noted for real estate of brilliant political folks.
Like the Biblical Samaritans who did not only believe in Jesus Christ because of what they were told but because they had seen Him and heard him, I decided to collect the list of the projects executed by him. Since most of the towns in Etsako West Local Government are along the road leading to my town Ogbona in Etsako Central Local Government Area that made him among the best 30 local government chairmen. Then came the opportunity for me last weekend on my way from Abuja to my town to see my mother. I discovered that Gani Audu is a social politician of uncommon character and uncommon passion to see his constituency developed. And today he has an impressive array of awards to show for his devotion to service at a time when failure is more common among local government chairmen than success.
The catalogue of projects executed by him include the Ozemoya Cassava Processing Factory in Auchi; a few metres away a road is under construction to give access to the General Hospital that has often been threatened by erosion. To put an end to this he quickly awarded the contract to Systems Construction Company. Also to his credit is the renovation of Etsako West Primary School, Abotse and the construction of the Secretariat-Police access road of about two kilometers.
The renovation of and construction of primary schools and primary health care centres had attracted visits by officials of UBE and National Primary Health Care Centre to Etsako West Local Government.
The tarring of a trunk road inside Agbede town has particularly reaffaimed this writer's belief in Gani Audu. This being in line with author, James Allen's philosophy that: "A man is literally what he thinks, his character being the complete gum of all his thoughts".
Here is a man who believes in Nelson Bosweu School of thought that: "The difference between greatness and mediocrity is often how an individual views a mistake".
This writer is therefore of the candid belief and indeed all well-meaning Nigerians that other elected local government chairmen should emulate Gani Audu instead of engaging in financial "lootocracy". It is this culture of looting that has created a gulf between them and the electorates, thereby leaving them in the Bermadu Triangle of betrayal.

Original_Search_Title: Do We Really Need Speed Governors?
Headline: Uganda: Do We Really Need Speed Governors?
Body_Text:
My friends, things are indeed getting complicated in the city. First, we had the mayor picking a fight with a man who knows more about fighting than anyone else in this country.
Then on Monday, on my way to work, I found the police had staged a big operation and they were impounding taxis. I later learnt that this was because they didn't have speed governors.
I found that very confusing. You see, I've never really understood how those 'machines' work in cars. Are they supposed to make cars go faster or slower? And I can't rely on muyindi to explain such technical things. The last time he explained to me how digital cameras operate, I lost a bet to a customer.
But the way the taxi chaps are complaining, you can know for sure that there is something about those things that is not very clear. I asked one of my customers here to help me out but his explanation didn't help. He said they were meant to reduce the speed of taxis so that accidents due to over speeding are prevented.
But that made me get even more confused. The taxis I see in town barely get out of their second gear before some woman in the back seat shouts wofuna parkingi! After that, they'll see a potential passenger and stop again. I swear taxis in the city don't over speed.
Actually if you looked at it properly, you'll realise that that the taxi people's desire to make money at all costs is actually their natural speed governor. And I don't think they need something to make them go slowly, otherwise more fights will break up in taxi because some man stuck in a taxi is getting late for a deal.
In fact some devices that make these taxis in town go a little quicker should be installed. That way, we'll all be able to reach town faster and get straight to our different jobs. It will save people's shoes as well. If you look at some of my customers' shoes , you'll know how much mileage these people cover. Probably because they had to get out of a slow taxi and walk the rest of the distance.
However there's one guy I know who definitely needs a speed governor. There's this disabled man I usually see speeding on his wheelchair around Kamwokya. That guy needs to be picked up very fast by the traffic cops before he gets involved in a nasty accident at the black spot near the mvule stage. Luckily, I use a bicycle to work and the only speed governor I know is the amount of cassava and soup I eat at some kidukulu that side of Kisekka market. The more I eat, the faster I'll ride but in these tough days, can you really eat that much?
Meanwhile I haven't seen Eva in a long time. Maybe I should send my commando muyindi over to find out if she has a speed governor that's preventing her from running here like she used to. I'll let you know.

Original_Search_Title: Proposed Law Could Outlaw Female Genital Mutilation
Headline: Guinea Bissau: Proposed Law Could Outlaw Female Genital Mutilation
Body_Text:
Bafata — The practice of fanado, or female genital mutilation, could soon be outlawed by a new bill to be presented to the Guinea-Bissau parliament.
Fanado is a traditional initiation ceremony for young girls, in which the vagina's clitoris and lips are removed. Many of the country's 30 ethnic groups practice it, including all the Muslim communities, about 46 percent of the population, especially in the eastern regions of Gabu and BafatÃ¡.
The girls' bodies are painted with rice flour or talc powder, and animist girls, attracted by the drumbeats and dances, sometimes go to be circumcised with their Muslim girlfriends.
Unicef estimates that some 2,000 girls are subjected to the procedure annually, and that 250,000 to 500,000 women suffer its medical and psychological consequences.
The trade of cutting is handed down in families, and daughters proudly use their mothers' knives, already blunted by age. Given the unhygienic conditions and ignorance amongst the practitioners, there is a high risk of HIV transmission, as the same knife is often used in several operations.
Legislation providing penalties and prison sentences for practicing fanado, drafted in 2001 by the Institute for Women and Children (IMC, from its Portuguese acronym) in partnership with human rights organisations, is being re-presented to parliament.
"Without wanting to offend the religion or the culture of one or another ethnic group, we have to involve all social and political players," said Adelina Na Temba, Minister of Social Solidarity, Family and the Fight Against Poverty.
Na Tamba told PlusNews that the new bill would be tabled in parliament during the next sitting, and the country could soon follow in the footsteps of 16 African countries that have approved laws against female genital mutilation.
The Maputo Protocol, an African Union (AU) document condemning the practice, came into force in November 2005. AU chairman Alpha Oumar Konare, ex-President of Mali, where FGM is almost universal, stated in June on African Child International Day that it violated human rights and the dignity of girls and women.
FGM is common in 28 African countries, in some in the Middle East, and amongst the immigrant communities of these countries living abroad.
The fight against fanado in Guinea-Bissau is an uphill one. Despite a barrage of criticism from traditional groups, a local nongovernmental organisation, Sinin Mira Nassique (Think of tomorrow, in the Mandinga language), has been leading the campaign against the practice, but the recent death of its chairwoman, Maria Augusta Mendonça Baldé, has weakened the campaign.
Parliament's inactivity has not helped either. After the 1998/99 civil war, the legislative body was temporarily dissolved in 2002, and was then interrupted after a coup d'etat in 2003 until the new government took office in October 2005. Politicians and legislators are also reluctant to offend voters who consider FGM an important element of their culture.
To overcome cultural issues, Sinin Mira Nassique has adopted a strategy of promoting a symbolic fanado, with all the social and traditional features of the original, but without the cutting. Such "alternative rites" have had some success in various countries, as they still retain the initiation characteristics of the ceremony and ensure cultural continuity.
After conducting awareness sessions, the group was able to perform five alternative fanados in the regions of Bissau, Gabu and Oio. More than a hundred practitioners from these areas have laid down their knives, hoping to organise other income-generating activities, but because these never materialised, they resist completely abandoning the practice because they would lose their source of income, said the IMC President.
Between CFCA5,000 and CFCA7,500 (US$10-15) is charged per child, and if it includes cutting the practitioner receives soap, chickens, rice and other goods.
Bula Baldé has been practising fanado for 40 years in BafatÃ¡. Now in her 80s, she was taught by her grandmother and mother, both from Futa-Djalon, in Guinea-Conakry. She still keeps her mother's 4cm-long knife, but uses a newer one. She grows maize and cassava in her backyard, but said she would only stop circumcising girls if "the knife could be exchanged for an income".
The purpose of fanado is to control female sexuality. Baldé told PlusNews some families are afraid of finding out their daughter is not a virgin on her wedding day and resort to a severe form called infibulation, which prevents any sexual relationships. To reopen the vagina on the day of her marriage, they call a practitioner. The family offers the daughter gold jewellery or a knife as a reward for her suffering.
During the Muslim month of Ramadan, when people fast from dawn to sunset, Muslim men do not accept food from an uncircumcised Muslim woman and she will not be allowed to pray in a mosque. This was why women from non-Muslim ethnic groups who marry Muslim men are forced to undergo female genital mutilation, said Baldé.
FGM is prohibited in the neighbouring countries of Senegal, Guinea-Conakry, Burkina Faso and Niger, so practitioners and Senegalese girls cross the border to perform the ceremony in Guinea-Bissau, where it is not a crime.
Na Tamba points out that Guinea-Bissau has signed international conventions on women and children's rights, and maintains that her government cannot allow practices that offend against these rights.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: President Obasanjo Says Ethanol Will Be Compulsory for Nigerian Fuel
Headline: Nigeria: President Obasanjo Says Ethanol Will Be Compulsory for Nigerian Fuel
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has said the Administration would make the use of ethanol in fuel compulsory, like the cassava flour in wheat for bread.
The President was speaking to Mr. Roberto Gianneti da Fonseca, Director of the Sao Paulo Federation of Industries, at State House, Abuja on September 7.
"Like we did for the introduction of cassava flour into bread, we shall gradually introduce ethanol into fuel soon. This is a serious project for us. NNPC is spearheading the project for now, but it will essentially be private sector driven", he assured the Brazilian delegation.
President Obasanjo said that about six states were ready to commence the projects in their states, including Jigawa, Bauchi, Benue and Ondo.
"Nigeria has the capacity to produce ethanol from both cassava and sugarcane, so we will take advantage of these natural endowments to provide jobs and cheaper fuel", he added.
Earlier, Mr. Fonseca had said his organisation was already talking with NNPC and some private Nigerian companies as part of the effort to introduce ethanol to the Nigerian market.

Original_Search_Title: CBN Seminar: Participants Discuss How Foreign Reserves Should Be Utilised
Headline: Nigeria: CBN Seminar: Participants Discuss How Foreign Reserves Should Be Utilised
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The Central Bank of Nigeria organised a three day seminar on the dynamics of exchange rate management. The various resource persons argued for the effective utilisation of the current foreign reserve accumulation to launch Nigeria development agenda.
Participants at a 3-day seminar on the dynamics of managing exchange rate in Nigeria have profered workable solution on how the nation's foreign reserve should be proparly utilised to achieve maximum development for the country. Resource persons were drawn from CBN, the University community and international organisation.
The conference which held between 28-30 August 2006 had finance correspondents, stakeholders in the finance sector and resource person as participants Alhaji Mohammed Nda, CBN director of Foreign Operation presented a lead paper on the challenge of effective foreign reserve management. He defines foreign reserve as "consisting of official public sector foreign assets that are readily available to and controlled by the monetary authorities for direct financing of payment imbalances and regulation of the magnitude of such imbalances through intervention in the exchange markets to effect the currency exchange rates and for other purpose. He explained that "The accumulation of reserves is a global phenomenon and not peculiar to Nigeria. Global official reserves increased from about $2 trillion at the end of 2002 to over $4 trillion in January, 2006, with emerging and developing countries accumulating the bulk of the world's foreign reserve.
"The rise in oil prices has now shifted foreign reserve accumulation from Asia (excepting China and India) to the oil producing countries. Many of these countries are accumulating reserves on account of the lessons they learnt during the various financial market crises of the 1990's. They have continued to build reserves to shield them from volatile capital movements."
He noted that Nigeria, like other oil producing countries, has accumulated reserves since 2004 following the upsurge in oil prices occasioned by the general tension in the Middle East, regional conflicts, and increasing global demands for oil, especially from China. Nigeria's current external reserves had reached the unprecedented level of $38.07 billion by July 31, 2006. The big challenge before us is how to manage the phenomenal increase in our foreign reserve that we don't feel the impact when the prices come crashing, as they surely will. How do we manage the investment of this reserve in our microeconomic sectors to diversify the economy?
Dr. Ayo Odusola, UNDP Senior economist argued that the accumulation in the foreign reserve has enable the CBN to harmonise the official exchange rate and the parallel market. He noted that the major lesson we can learn from Malaysia and Mexico in 1998 and 2002 is that we have to be careful the way we handle our capital account. He explained that you don't just open your capital account to all and sundry. It must be a capital account that is what we called guided by the policy directive in terms of agenda of government. He said no country ever leaves it free like that and think it will impact positively on the economy. he further clarified that leaving the capital account open has ripple effect that dislocate the whole economy from getting its own bearing for several years because that exactly what happened in Mexico for several years before they could relocate themselves."
Professor Mike Obadan, of university of Benin in his reaction said that the deregulation of the economy that started with SAP targeted the diversification of the oil sector. That the deregulation since 1985 has failed to achieved the set target of generating 1.0billion dollar from non-oil sector. The challenge of the current accumulation is to see how we can diversify the economy to generate revenue and foreign exchange from the non-oil sector that still remain untapped. That governement has started with cassava which is in serious demand in India and China. That there is the need to focus on other sector. Malaysia has oil but they de-emphasise it and concentrate on real production and manufacturing.
Alhaji Nda observed that Nigeria's dependence on oil for over 90 percent of its foreign exchange earnings, which makes its capital account vulnerable to the fluctuations in crude oil prices, poses a lot of challenges for the management of the country's economy. Nigeria's high level import bills have contributed to the fluctuation of our foreign reserves over the years and consequently rendered its management more difficult.
Certain important measures could facilitate the effective management of our huge accumulation of foreign reserve, the most important of which is the enactment of the Fiscal Responsibility Act to provide for prudent management of the reserve and ensure long-term macroeconomic stability and greater accountability and transparency in fiscal operations within a medium-term fiscal policy framework.
Tied to the issue of fiscal responsibility is the recent call for the establishment of a national investment fund. It is an open secret that the recent build-up in the nation's external reserve has mainly been due to unsustainable upsurges in oil prices, which are however already fuelling the search for alternative energy sources, especially in the USA. The national investment fund is intended to invest excess earnings from oil into projects that could shield the nation from the volatility of oil prices. Countries such as Botswana, Venezuela, Norway, Sao Tome and Principe, to name a few examples, have successfully established such funds. What these countries have done is to secure the proceeds from oil and other minerals and finance their budgets through other sources.
The national investment fund serves the twin objectives of stabilising volatile fiscal revenues and creating a capital pool to provide alternative sources of foreign exchange.
As part of its effort to manage our foreign reserve effectively, CBN has outsourced its component parts to external fund managers in line with the best practices worldwide. A recent survey by UBS Investment Bank showed that 70% of central banks around the world now use the services of external managers. A portion of the reserve would be invested in bonds, which yield higher returns than fixed deposits.
CBN has appointed J. P. Morgan Chase to take custody of all securities purchased by its external fund managers. The apex bank has also come up with the idea of allocating the external reserve to strategic assets, the aim of which is to take its risk tolerance into account in optimising long-term reforms of the reserve portfolio. Strategic asset allocation (SAA) would split the reserve into liquidity, investment, and stable tranches to reflect the capital preservation, liquidity, and return objectives of the bank's reserve management.
Foreign reserve is held by a country for a number of reasons. It supports and maintains confidence in the monetary policy and exchange rate management, including intervention in the foreign exchange market in support of the national currency, naira for example. This is evident from the relative stability of our foreign exchange market since 2004 following the historic merger of the wholesale Dutch auction system and bureau de change rates.
Foreign reserve also services the foreign currency liabilities and debt obligations of country. For instance Nigeria was able to pay the Paris Club $12.4 billion from its foreign reserve to secure a debt relief of $18 billion.
Foreign reserve finances government expenditures abroad, e.g. the import of goods and services for government programmes. It provides a defence against emergencies or disasters and could equally be invested in income generating instruments such as treasury bills, bonds, time deposits, etc.
Nigeria's foreign reserve has grown in leaps and bound in the past three years. The trend started in 2003 after the American invasion of Iraq, which resulted in the escalation of prices as Iraqi oil failed to reach the international markets. Some of the participants argued that the windfall of the 1990's cannot be said to have been judiciously utilised. The surge in oil prices pushes countries like the United States to search for alternatives to oil as their major source of energy. If the US succeeds, Nigeria could be in trouble because the US is our major oil trading partner.
During the oil boom of the mid-seventies which resulted in the build up of external reserves, these reserves were diversified into an array of financial instruments including foreign government bonds and treasury bills, foreign government guaranteed securities, special drawing rights (SDRs), fixed term deposits, call accounts and current accounts, providing significant investment income as well as liquidity. However during the glut in the global oil markets which led to the collapse of prices and consequently depleted the reserves, the reserves were largely held in current account and treasury bills. This underscores the need to diversify the sources of a country's foreign exchange inflow.
Other participants also argue that beyond this, however, the fiscal and budgetary violations of the executive arm of government must be treated with more seriousness.
In the last seven years we have witnessed increasing fiscal rascality in the way and manner that members of the executive arm of government handle the budget. This can no longer be condoned if we are to benefit in the long-run from the accumulation of foreign reserve.
In conclusion, the present level of our foreign reserve is a golden opportunity to launch our development agenda. Much as we appreciate CBN's strategic management however the needs of ordinary Nigerians dictate the translation of our excess funds into meaningful life. That boils down to the issue of diversification. We have noted the efforts with cassava, but a lot more must be done to ensure that a country as blessed as Nigeria does not depend on one single but volatile product.

Original_Search_Title: Changes in Food And Nsima Culture Affect Our Lives
Headline: Zambia: Changes in Food And Nsima Culture Affect Our Lives
Body_Text:
Lusaka — SHARING and eating food with others is a sign of social communion, a "being together." The sharing from the bounty of nature's nourishment expresses unity of persons and purpose.
When people share meals, relationships deepen. We may notice that those who are mentally and socially close often would have shared some meal together.
This eating is more than just feeding the physical body. Eating together expresses some spiritual bond amongst the persons involved.
Cultures and habits develop around food and eating. Food affects cultures and behaviour and cultures affect feeding. The contents and preparation of various foods affect life and living in various ways.
The food and culture around the food can nourish us or sometimes limit us.
A common food in this region of Africa is nsima. Some call it nshima, inzhima, buhobe, bwali, ugali, papu, and sadza, amongst many variations.
There are those who feel a strong bond with nsima, especially that made from maize meal commonly known as "mealie-meal." They refuse to consider the starch component of other food types.
When outside Africa, they may feel deprived without maize nsima. This has more to do with attitude and habit than any fact that maize nsima is necessarily the best food in the world.
It also has to do with emotions packaged in nsima. Nsima can be made from various flours, from cassava to millet and sorghum. Some, for such reasons as taste, availability, and economy, use a combination of the flours.
Maize meal only became widespread some few decades ago. Maize was brought into the nsima belt by people from Portugal. Nsima is more than maize flour heated and cooked into lumps. Like many types of food, culture and emotions surround nsima and the way it is eaten.
It is a training ground for character. It is also an observation ground. As towards many foods, nsima eating has culture and discipline developed around attitudes and procedures.
It is a process, or used to be so in many places, from which young persons learn habits and attitudes that help them face life. Around the meal are issues like who washes hands first and who starts to eat first and "opens the floor." A meal is about relating to other persons.
Each meal session will have some captain or meal commissioner that directly or indirectly assists the meal session. This may be linked to age or other seniority of the persons involved.
There is order to the way a person moulds the nsima lump. The accompanying "relish" must relate to the nsima in certain ways. And there is order to the way one eats. To some people, eating habits show discipline rating and whether one is well brought up.
Changes in food and nsima culture are affecting our quality of life. Changes in our social and economic lives affect our nsima and food culture.
In urban areas, many persons no longer eat nsima from one common plate but have lumps given on individual plates. Even then, the pot the nsima comes from is the same. Although it varies in places, the principle of discipline and order still apply.
Crucial to enjoyment of nsima is the cooking. Besides technical skill, cooking is an extension of the emotions and character of the person cooking.
The quality of nsima is in direct relation to the skills and emotions, at a particular time, in the person cooking. The person cooking nsima must at cooking be in a good frame of mind. An angry and unsettled person may not cook good nsima. For that reason, many persons will refuse to eat nsima cooked by an angry or stressed person.
The emotions of the person are moulded in the food they produce.
When some people feel discomfort after eating some nsima meal, they may seek reasons in the temperament of the person that cooked.
The mixing of ingredients must be done in right amounts and at the right time. No nsima is the same. Each is packaged with emotions of the time of cooking and eating. It is not easy to reproduce nsima in a factory. Another delicate food is chikanda, very good for those of us that are vegetarians.
Chikanda requires the right type, coming from the right soil, and the right approach. Otherwise, many persons try but find difficulties in being consistent.
They bring out good chikanda at one time and then a poorly baked one the next. Consistently good chikanda- making requires masters to prepare it.
For some decades, whenever I can, I get my chikanda from a Mrs Ngwira who is found at Kangwa's shopping centre in Libala. The lessons of chikanda apply to nsima. Nsima making is art and craft. It demands deep care. Recently, with various brothers and sisters in various places of Zambia, we have been moaning the decline of good nsima making.
Whether one's palate and taste likes it light or heavy, and hot or cool, like us, good nsima should come off in nice grains. Good nsima tastes so great that it can be eaten without accompanying "relish." Good nsima does not stick to the hand. Neither does it stick to the pot it is made in. Those who cook well were admired and sought for in marriage and families.
It was not a physical thing but something to do with the person's emotional expression and balance. As it seeks to nourish persons, meal preparation is sacred. Cooking is an expression of love and nourishment. For that reason, some people will personally cook nsima and not task the house worker.
Some people avoid nsima that is mass cooked. Under good cooking, good nsima can still be made for a large number of persons.
In places where people preparing or handling food are poorly paid and oppressed, it shows in the quality of nsima and food. There is no joy in the mechanical cooking and there will be no joy in the meal.
Poor nsima is a sign of the changing emotional situation of our society.
The more stressed we are, the poor the nsima. Healthy emotions, aided by good skills, will produce good meals. On other issues, it is not easy to understand why Zambia's new Demographic and Health Survey's national collection of data, scheduled for August 2006 to August 2007, was allowed to take place at peak ofElections 2006 campaign time.
The survey may already have been planned for this period and the elections dates were announced when survey planning and arrangements for logistics were already in place. But in communities, such combination of projects may not help the survey and election process.
Those involved in the survey and elections will have to do adjustments in their work. Ideally, having known about the election dates, one would have expected the survey to be rescheduled to after elections.
Again, on another issue, the signing of the Kazungula Bridge project by presidents Festus Mogae of Botswana, Levy Mwanawasa of Zambia, and Zimbabwe's Robert Mugabe must, although it could have happened earlier, be commended as major action for further removing mental and physical distances and limitations that affect persons in the region, persons who, beyond politics and state boundaries, are foremost brothers and sisters that should be living and working together.
Most often, most solutions to most challenges can be found within and around us.

Original_Search_Title: Venezuelan President Hugo Chavez Ends Visit
Headline: Angola: Venezuelan President Hugo Chavez Ends Visit
Body_Text:
Luanda — Venezuelan president Hugo Chaves Frias ended this evening his five-hour official visit to Angola, meant to reinforce the two countries' cooperation relations.
The South American nation's leader, who left the Angolan capital, Luanda at 8.20pm, headed his country delegation that held talks with the local side, meant at analysing future areas for cooperation, with highlight to oil sector.
After the official talks, both delegations released a final communiqué, in which Venezuela, the world's fifth biggest oil producer, expressed its will to support Angola in oil field, due to its greater potential and experience.
The two countries' officials, at the presence of host President Jose Eduardo dos Santos and Venezuelan leader Hugo Chavez, signed an agreement in oil sphere that will boost the exploration of both nations' potentialities.
After expressing the satisfaction for the climate of understanding that led the talks between the two oil producing nations, José Eduardo dos Santos and Hugo Chaves gave green light to the respective ministers of Foreign Affairs to continue with the bilateral consultations and negotiations toward a cooperation accord to be signed soon by both officials.
Angola and Venezuela started diplomatic relations on 25 September 1986, but a legal cooperation framework has never been defined.
World's fifth biggest oil producer, Venezuela expressed interest to open a permanent diplomatic mission in Angola.
It is a Presidential Federalist Republic with 22 states, one federal district (Caracas) and 72 federal dependencies. The Climate is tropical and until the year 2000, the rate of illiteracy was 07 percent.
The local currency is Bolivar, whereas the main production is sugar cane, banana, maize, rice, orange, cassava and sorghum.
Venuezela is amongst the five main oil exporters. It produces 3.7 million barrels per day and is expected to reach 5 million by 2012.
On 03 July 2000, presidential and legislative elections were held in the country, won by Hugo Chaves with 55 percent of the votes.
A referendum was held in 2004, with 58,25 percent of the voters supporting the continuation of the president.

Original_Search_Title: Hugo Chavez in Luanda
Headline: Angola: Venezuelan President Arrives
Body_Text:
Luanda — The president of the Republic of Venezuela, Hugo Chaves Frias, arrived Thursday in Luanda at 03:07 pm (local time) with a view to strengthening bilateral cooperation with Angola.
Hugo Chavez, who has come for a few hours official visit, was welcomed at Luanda's "4 de Fevereiro" International Airport, by Foreign minister, João Bernardo de Miranda.
Soon after the visiting head of state was driven to the Presidential Palace, for due military honours.
This will be followed by an official lunch host by president Jose Eduardo dos Santos, in the presence of officials from both countries governments.
Meanwhile, official talks between ministerial delegations from both countries are taking place this afternoon with discussions of issues concerning cooperation, with highlight to oil matters.
A private meeting will be held between the two heads of State.
This evening the visiting president will receive separate courtesy greetings from local parliament speaker, Roberto de Almeida, and prime minister, Fernando da Piedade Dias dos Santos.

Original_Search_Title: VP in Bilateral Business Talks With Thais
Headline: Ghana: VP in Bilateral Business Talks With Thais
Body_Text:
Vice President Aliu Mahama on Tuesday discussed with the Thai Government Ghana's desire to boost technical cooperation in the spirit of South-South cooperation.
He used his one-and-half-hour meeting with the Deputy Prime Minister, Dr Surakiart Sathirathai in Bangkok to explain why the two developing countries should share expertise in cassava and fish production with emphasis on tuna in Ghana for the economic advantage of the two countries.
Mr. Yaw Asamoa Buabeng, Special Assistant to the Vice President, disclosed aspects of the discussions to journalists after a closed-door meeting at Thailand's State House.
He said Ghana was also tapping Thailand's expertise in the large-scale production of anti-malaria and anti-retroviral drugs to boost the fight against malaria and HIV/AIDS, respectively.
Thailand is currently producing anti-malaria drugs in Gabon, Tanzania, The Gambia, Senegal and Mali.
Also on the drawing board is to get Thailand to capitalise on its immense expertise in the textile industry to invest in Ghana's Free Zone Project to benefit from the Africa Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA), which ensures a ready market for textiles from Africa in the US.
Mr. Buabeng said the Vice President also sought the assistance of Thailand to boost works on the Accra-Kumasi trunk road and other infrastructure development projects.
Another issue of interest to Ghana is to see the Ghana-Thailand Trade Commission come into fruition in October 2006. Mr. Buabeng said Ghana is also seeking technical assistance to initiate a social housing scheme for inner city dwellers. The scheme involves the provision of soft loans to beneficiaries to be able to rehabilitate existing structures and rebuild or construct new homes.
Vice President Mahama was the guest of honour at the Grand Palace of Thailand's Monarchy, which also accommodates the Emerald Buddha Temple, revered by 85 per cent of Thai Buddhists.
The Palace, which has an area of 218,400 square metres, was built in 1782 and is one of the tourist attractions in the country.
The public monument comprises Government offices, the Royal Chapel of the Emerald Buddha, a reception chamber where royal paraphernalia are kept, a library and galleries.

Original_Search_Title: LRA Ambush Ambulance
Headline: Uganda: LRA Ambush Ambulance
Body_Text:
Kampala — REBELS of the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) yesterday afternoon ambushed and set ablaze an ambulance belonging to Kalongo missionary hospital.
It had drugs and other medical supplies destined for Kalongo hospital in Pader district, the army said.
Northern army spokesman Lt. Chris Magezi told journalists in his Gulu office the rebels opened fire on an ambulance UG 177M, at about 2:30pm.
"About six medics who were on board jumped out of the ambulance and fled in the bush unhurt and were rescued by UPDF soldiers who were nearby. The rebels burnt the ambulance and looted some drugs," Magezi said.
He said the ambush took place at Pacer village on the Pader-Kalongo road.
Magezi said the rebels were led by LRA's Lt. Col. Okuti, who is still roaming Pader district with his troops.
"Our troops are pursuing these attackers and we hope they will be punished in due course," he added.
He said on Saturday the rebels ambushed and killed a cyclist identified as Dennis Ocan near Kitgum high school and stripped him naked.
Magezi said the UPDF 23rd battalion in Amiel sub- county, Pader district, recovered six sacks of cassava that the rebels had dropped.
"If the remnants of the LRA who are in northern Uganda are still carrying out these barbaric acts on the civilians, what moral authority does the LRA leader of their peace team, Martin Ojur, have to talk about unilateral ceasefire? They are busy making ridiculous and unrealistic demands in Juba yet the remnants continue to commit atrocities. An ambulance is a protected vehicle in conflict areas used to attend to the sick," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Cafe Viva's Rice is Worth a Try
Headline: Uganda: Cafe Viva's Rice is Worth a Try
Body_Text:
I first made the acquaintance of Dr. Vali Jamal almost a couple of years ago in the company of a fellow who was slated to be his partner. As providence turned out they parted ways with the chap turning up to be a cad and a duplicitous sort of person.
It is an ill wind that blows nobody any good and the departure of the fellow turned out to be a blessing in disguise for since he Jamal was now forced to sink or swim. The former was never an option and today Café Viva is alive and well and holding it own admirably.
What is surprising is that a Trinity economist decided to become a restaurateur and in Uganda no less! But then again Jamal is not an airborne "parachute" Asian Johnny Come lately.
His grandfather was already ensconced in Arua at the turn of the last century circa 1904.
Starting a restaurant in Uganda of all places is at best a daunting task by any accounts and it has turned to be an arduous quest that has by no means been easy sailing.
Fortunately Jamal having travelled in over 55 countries while working with the ILO has given him a great deal of exposure to different culinary fare and this is amply reflected in the menu at his place. Needless to say he has encountered many a sticky wicket, but with indomitable stamina and courage the worst is behind and Café Viva is slowly making its mark upon Kampala diners. It has greately improved.
The menu is eclectic in nature and for those of us who were there at the start, by all accounts the standard of the food has greatly improved since the inception. Jamal is regularly in the kitchen and while I was there he whipped up a great penne with cilantro pesto that I found to be simply scrumptious.
I am told that Bebe Cool is a regular and on the 26th they will be doing an "Oldies" night with an Indian-African fashion show.
With three floors including a garden and a balcony that is buzzing with revellers in the evening, the potential for growth and improvement is tremendous.
Rating
XXXX UNRIVALLED
XXX EXCELLENT
XX VERY GOOD
X GOOD
NONE POOR TO SATISFACTORY
These views are purely the reviewer's personal reaction to food, ambience and service with price being factored into consideration. Bear in mind that the listings and prices are subject to change without notice.
Place: Café Viva
Rating: XX
Location: Kampala Road, next to Crane Chambers opposite City Sqaure.
prices: Breakfast special 7am - 10 am omelet, toast and tea 2,500/-Starters and snacks available throughout the day from 500/- for cassava chips to 2,000/- for a pizza slice, salads, soups and sandwiches between 4,000/- and 4,500/-, Traditional food from 2,500/- for vegetarian fare to 5,000/- for chicken stew with all the accompaniments. Continental dishes are between 3,500/- for Mexican chapatti with chips to 5,500/- for fish fillet (Nile perch) with chips and salad. Café Viva also offers Indian food for around the 4,000/- mark with curry of the day costing 7,000/-.Stir fries such as gingered chicken are 8,500/- and desserts go for 6,000/-.
Recommended dishes:Penne with cilantro, pilao, tandoori chicken.
Service: The staff appears eager to please.
Ambience: Room for great improvement.
Parking: Problematic during the day otherwise on the street with guards who look after ones vehicle.
Prices: Pretty much on the money
Open: Seven days until late.
Sound Level: With three floors from which to choose you are bound to find a quiet area.
Reservations: Not necessary

Original_Search_Title: Kampala's Smoking Cars
Headline: Uganda: Kampala's Smoking Cars
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kampala's traffic produces more toxic smoke than the average coffee shop in Amsterdam. It is more chaotic than 15 old taxi parks combined in an area half the size of the current place.
It is more dangerous than a blind Boda-Boda driver on a 3000cc motorcycle. It follows less rules than an anarchist rebel stuck on a deserted island and it moves slower than a crippled turtle. All these things make the overall traffic system in Kampala function less efficient than a child who is trying to staple jelly to the living room ceiling.
The result of this chaotic, lawless, dangerous mess is the cost of many lives, the obstruction of efficient commercial transportation, the heavy pollution of the Ugandan sky and the reason for all Ugandans to be at least one hour late for their appointments in Kampala.
Is there a sustainable solution besides turning the roads of Kampala into one massive golf course? I believe there is. Bicycles. Bicycles are safe, bicycles are clean, bicycles are cheap, bicycles don't take up a lot of space, bicycles don't use petrol and riding a bicycle is a good exercise and will help Ugandans to burn off their carbohydrate overdose indulged during their heavy matooke- posho - kalo - lumondi - rice - cassava - lunch.
How do we get Kampala on the bicycle? I propose all government officials to set an example for the rest. The introduction of bicycles for political leaders will create a huge saving in the annual budget normally spent on government vehicles. This spare money can be invested in re-construction of roads with special lanes for bicycles.
What about the people who now find their monthly income in the transportation sector? The taxi drivers could start bicycle repair shops. The Boda Boda drivers could open up bicycle parking venues and the drivers of the government vehicles could switch from driving cars to massaging government officials' legs after they have been riding their bikes all day from meeting to meeting.
One last problem remains, how do we get government officials to move their big butts out of their big cars? The answer is simple. They will receive special bicycles. Bicycles that will give them the status they deserve, bicycles that will give them the comfort they are used to and bicycles that will distinguish them from the rest of the people, just as they have always put themselves above the rest of the people.
Their bicycles will be painted in the colours of the national flag. Their bicycles will have an extra soft seat and their bicycles will even have silver bells. And the president? The president should get an extra special one. Maybe one with a golden bell, or a even a little parasol, to protect him from the sun, but most importantly, the little flag that normally flows in the wind on the front of his Mercedes, will proudly represent his concern for the wellbeing of Uganda from the back of his bicycle.
The author is a Master student, Faculty of Social Science, Free University of Amsterdam

Original_Search_Title: Kitara Secondary School Closed
Headline: Uganda: Kitara Secondary School Closed
Body_Text:
KITARA Senior Secondary School, one of the oldest school in Bunyoro Kitara Kingdom, was on Tuesday closed and students sent home following an abrupt decision by the school's Board of Governor's meeting held at the school.
The abrupt closure of the school follows Monday's strike where students destroyed two houses belonging to Mr Patrick Kugonza, a neighbour of the school.
The school and Kugonza are fighting for 10 acres of land, with each party claiming ownership.
The students on Monday held a demonstration, which ended up being violent, accusing Kugonza of grabbing the school land, saying it was unacceptable to them.
The students led by the school prefect, Mr Denis Sseguya, first marched to the streets of Hoima town in protest and eventually attacked Kugonza's houses. They cut down gardens of cassava, sweet potato, beans and eucalyptus trees.
When Daily Monitor visited the school the headmaster, Mr Christopher Byenkya, confirmed the closure following a meeting chaired by the school chairman Board of Governors, the Rev. Can. David Kaahwa.
Byenkya said 850 students were sent home after the school authorities discovered that the students' continued stay at the school would cause more destruction to both the school and the community around.
Byenkya said the students would report back to school for the third term which commences on September 18 according to the school academic calendar. The official closing date of this term is today. When contacted by Daily Monitor, Kugonza said he had reported the matter to the police.
"I have a list of students who participated in the demolition of my property and it has been handed over to Hoima Police for better management," Kugonza said.
The Hoima District Police Commander, Mr Charles Imalingat, confirmed the destruction of properties and closure of the school.
"We have heavily deployed at the school because we suspect students will come back and destroy property. Investigations are under way to bring the culprits to book," Imalingat said.

Original_Search_Title: Saving Meat for Last
Headline: Uganda: Saving Meat for Last
Body_Text:
Kampala — Since my coming to Uganda, I have noticed something every Ugandan does. Whenever they are served with meat for a meal, they make sure it is the last thing they tussle with. Everything on the plate is devoured, but the meat is pushed aside. Only the impatient and mannerless will pinch a small piece to calm down their provoked appetite.
This practice does not know tribe and the level of wealth. It cuts across the Ugandan society from the north to the south, and the rich and poor all alike.
John Waiswa, a Musoga and a lecturer, explains that meat used to be a scarce commodity in the days gone by and was only eaten on big days. That attitude is still intact. "Even if someone had a thousand chicken or goats those days not even one was touched, unless it died of disease, or it was Christmas. That is when it was slaughtered," he says
In Arua District, it is traditional that before meat is cooked the pieces are first counted by the head of the family. At the dinnertime, the meat is divided amongst the family and each person holds their share in the left hand. It's only after all the kalo (millet bread) has been consumed that they can turn to the meat.
At Makerere University, campusers may even abandon a course work test for lunch the day meat is served. On such days the queue at say the dinning hall of Nkrumah spills over to the outside as the students anxiously wait to be served the prized delicacy.
Even those who normally take "mess" food for granted and pretend to be loaded patiently stand in that queue, with whetted appetites. On such days the ladies who do the serving turn violent, barking at the students. Even if you desire only soup she mistakenly thinks you are agitating for more meat.
Nsubuga, a Muganda, says, "It is bad manners to start your meal by eating the meat first. People may think you were only served soup, which creates a bad impression. I think eating the meat last is the right thing to do," he explains.
Caroline, who comes from Ankole, says it is just like those days we were kids. When we were given a sweet, we did not eat it quickly. I think it is the same thing with the meat.
Ben Bella Ilakut, an Etesot, says people in Teso are trained by their parents to be polite in the face of meat, chicken or fish. "So it is bad manners to gobble up meat first," he explains.
"Besides meat is delicious, so if you start with it, how are you going to finish the rest of the kalo? It is better to wash down the kalo with the meat, so it leaves you with a good taste in the mouth," says Ilakut, who has since abandoned meat, chicken and fish for raw vegetables and fruits "for quality health."
In Buganda, says Lillian, meat is valued a lot. That is why the phrase "the man who does not buy meat does not buy sauce," was coined. When eating, you first get rid of bad things like matooke, potatoes and cassava, then you zero in on the "real food, that is meat," she says.
In Arinaitwe's culture livestock is so highly prized that people eat only the dead, very sick and old animals. "This makes meat so important. And as a head of the family, I take my time. Even if a visitor arrives, they will not take away my portion," Arinaitwe, a Mukiga, says.
That is how important meat is in Uganda and it changes the behaviour of people, bringing out the real them.

Original_Search_Title: Gramu Scoops Global Gold Award for Quality Goods
Headline: Uganda: Gramu Scoops Global Gold Award for Quality Goods
Body_Text:
Kampala — WHEN Charles and Robinah Mutumba of Ntebetebe, Bweyogerere in Wakiso district, started Gramu General Merchants in 1986, they pounded the millet in a mortar to remove the husks. "There was a high demand for millet flour (busera), but the flour on the market had lots of impurities," they said.
"As we consulted on how to start, we linked up with Lutaaya, who was dealing in millet and soya processing, but the partnership was short lived. He went to work in a food processing firm," Charles says.
"We hawked the flour in Bweyogerere and some residents who sympathised with us bought it from our home," they said.
Charles says, due to impurities including stones and cow dung, a lot of winnowing had to be done such that only one or two kilogrammes were sorted daily. And worse still, the grains are tiny and have to be dusted using a damp cloth," says Robinah, adding that when it rains, the work became more tedious.
She says millet from some places like Kisenyi is sub-standard because it has a high moisture content.
"These days, we use an electric fan to blow the dust and sort out the stones manually," they said.
The Mutumbas recently got a golden award from the International Selection Committee of Business Initiative Directions (BID), which is made up of a group of companies and prestigious professionals.
The 20th International Quality Summit Convention (IQSC) BID gave Gramu General Merchants the International Quality Summit Gold Award in New York for quality and excellence out of 9,500 companies all over the world. They did not attend the convention because at the time elections were taking place in Uganda, thus they could not travel. However, their award will be sent soon.
For the past 28 years, BID has been awarding companies in 166 countries, making it one of the largest quality corporate image endorsers in the world, which promotes the corporate and brand image.
The Mutumbas say millet processing undergoes six stages to ensure that no impurities are left.
"The procedure requires specialised machines both for sorting, removing husks, drying, roasting, milling and packaging," they say. From the time they started using a fan, millet production increased. The Mutumbas started mixing millet flour with soya, making about 10kgs per day from one. They now have five brands on the market; pure millet for porridge, millet mixed with soya and millet mixed with cassava.
In 2004, they embarked on the production of soya-rice porridge and soya sauce. They recommend soya-millet for children from six months and above and feeding mothers.
They say it helps in the growth of the brain hence stimulating the intelligence quotient (IQ). Millet flour contains iron, proteins, vitamin B1, B6, pantothenic acid, moisture, minerals; magnesium, zinc, calcium and dietary fibre.
"As the quality of our products improved, the Seventh Day Adventist (SDA) Church tendered us to supply millet flour to Rwanda refugees during the 1994 genocide for six months. This was our first big tender," they said.
They can only process about 40 bags a month because of the technology. "We can process about 100 bags a month if provided with up-to-date technology, including packaging machines and a bigger working space," they add.
In June 2003, the Mutumbas brushed shoulders with President Yoweri Museveni at the Natural Chemotherapeutics Research Laboratory exhibition at Wandegeya, who promised them assistance through Joan Kakwenzire, the presidential advisor on poverty alleviation.
"We tried to reach her, but were told that she was busy or outside the country. We gave up and tried to see Maggie Kigozi, the executive director for Uganda Investment Authority instead, but we were discouraged at the office reception," they said.
Before the Mutumbas took on millet processing, they reared poultry for seven years.
In 1988, they decided to expand the poultry project and got a loan of sh2m from the Women Trust Bank. They bought 600 chick layers. As the hens were nearing laying eggs, a disease struck all of them and they died.
Gramu employs about 10 men, whom he feeds and accomodates. The Mutumbas said they make about sh1.5m per month. From their earnings, they have educated their children and recently, they bought a milling machine.

Original_Search_Title: Gov't Loses Over ¢2 Billion in Smuggling
Headline: Ghana: Gov't Loses Over Â¢2 Billion in Smuggling
Body_Text:
Dabala — THE GOVERNMENT has lost over two billion cedis as a result of rampant smuggling activities going on at the eastern corridor of the country's border posts.
So far, the Dabala Customs, Excise and Preventive Service (CEPS) in the South Tongu District of the Volta Region has impounded over 12,000 pieces of wax prints between January and July this year.
Smuggling activities in wax prints and cigarettes have assumed an alarming proportion along the Eastern corridors, and smugglers have adopted sophisticated methods to outwit CEPS officials as they resort to the appointment of agents, the use of mobile phones and the wrapping of wax prints around children before dressing them up, in a bid to succeed in their illegal acts.
The Chief Collector at the Aflao Sector who is also in charge of the Dabala checkpoint, Mr. Ben Suuri, made this known to The Chronicle at Dabala when CEPS officials intercepted 1,503 wax prints last Sunday.
Mr. Suuri said most of the smugglers have been using unapproved routes like Akakpo-Kope near Dabala through agents, feigning funerals by carrying coffins full of wax prints and cigarettes, and the use of bicycles to carry smuggled goods to the riverside at Sogakope and cross the Volta Lake to Accra.
He also said although the use of mobile phones was good, criminals and smugglers have also taken advantage of the technology to outwit law enforcement agencies.
Mr. Suuri revealed that wax prints and cigarettes were being put in sand, cassava products like 'gari' or 'kokonte', cement, loaded fish baskets and even in vehicle engines, while some female smugglers fold them up and them around their middle and pretend to be pregnant.
He expressed regret that some of the smugglers sometimes attempt to kill CEPS officials, citing last Sunday's smugglers on the Ho-Sogakope-Adidome road as an example, whom he claimed had tried to crash their vehicle into that of the CEPS officials while being chased for arrest. He said the officials managed to maneuvre to escape death narrowly.
Mr. Suuri disclosed CEPS' administration has now purchased an engine boat, which would be used to track down the smuggling activities on the Volta Lake at Sogakope. He said the Service has now decided to use private cars at the Dabala checkpoint because smugglers easily identify the Service's official vehicles, making it very difficult to make any arrest.
The Chief Collector said the smuggling of wax prints and cigarettes were on the increase and pointed out that apart from the wax prints, which were the highest smuggled products, cigarettes were the second highly smuggled goods into the country through the Eastern corridor.
Mr. Suuri attributed the high level of smuggling of wax prints into the country to the big market and demand for the product in Togo, and the greedy nature of some Ghanaian businessmen and women to get rich quick. These, he said, were killing the Ghanaian economy.
Mr. Suuri therefore cautioned those who involved themselves in the dubious business of smuggling goods into the country to cultivate the habit of paying their taxes, which he noted was a major economic means for the growth and development of the country.
The Chronicle has gathered, however, that the major cause of the increased smuggling of wax prints into the country is the restriction that all wax prints should be imported through the Takoradi Port.
Some of the smugglers openly confessed to the paper that it was not economically reasonable and feasible for a businessman to travel from the various parts of the country to Takoradi, the only designated point, to purchase or import wax prints while cheaper wax prints of equal quality could be found in Togo.

Original_Search_Title: Ben Dolsi Malo Lauds Girl Child Education in Sierra Leone
Headline: Sierra Leone: Ben Dolsi Malo Lauds Girl Child Education in Sierra Leone
Body_Text:
Former British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) presenter, Ben Dolsi Marlo, a Ghanaian by Nationality now in his mid 40's, who is now with the United Nations in New York, has said that the Sierra Leone Government has signalled to other nations to give girls priority in the educational sector.
He made the commendation on the Sierra Leone Government on July 20 at the Mammy Yoko Hotel en route to Ghana.
He said he was greatly impressed with Sierra Leone during his visit while in the delegation of the United Nations Secretary General Kofi Annan on his visit to the coastal West African country. Ben Dolsi Malo speaking exclusively to this reporter on July 3, said there had been much improvement in post- war Sierra Leone.
On good governance, , corruption and development, he said , "The government need have their honour in thick blanket and not lay it to be trampled in the dust" About his background, He said he hailed from a poor home where he could remember he used to fetch cassava for feeding and commercial purpose for his family.
On corruption in Sierra Leone Ben Dolsi Marlo said, "Corruption is literal and technical' .
'One has to be mindful, lawful, careful and cautious in using it', he said.
Comparing BBC to the UN Radio where he now works, he said both are sophisticated but the United Nations Radio is more sophisticated.

Original_Search_Title: Bribery, Violence And Violation of Electoral Rules Mar Polls
Headline: Kenya: Bribery, Violence And Violation of Electoral Rules Mar Polls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Claims of bribery, sporadic violence and violation of electoral rules marred Monday's by-elections in five constituencies.
The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) deplored the "wanton violation of electoral rules by the Government" in elections that were characterised by low voter turnout.
At the same time, pro-Government functionaries sustained an onslaught launched a week ago against the ECK, with another round of vitriolic attack on chairman Samuel Kivuitu, whom they accused of being disrespectful to the Narc Government.
But the elections watchdog body vowed it would not be beaten into submission with threats - for exposing yet another flagrant violation of election laws by the State.
Violence reared its head in Nakuru Town and North Horr, an expansive constituency with some polling stations up to 150 kilometers apart, while a woman was arrested in Moyale for dishing out cash to voters.
Later in the day, as frustration began to set in, two MPs found themselves caught up in a scuffle in Nakuru, where polling had generally been peaceful.
Bandits attack polling station
And in an unprecedented attack in North Horr, 10 bandits armed with automatic rifles laid siege on Hula-Hula polling station for over 30 minutes, firing in the air in a bid to scare voters.
Presiding officer, Mariam Barako, said hundreds of voters fled as the brigands opened fire at around 11 am. No one was injured in the attack, but it nevertheless accentuated perennial security concerns in northern Kenya.
Four Administration Policemen also fled. Hula-Hula is some 12km south of Marsabit.
On Monday, the Government appeared unrelenting in its attack on the ECK high command, with Energy assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri daring Kivuitu to resign "for being disrespectful to Narc".
"Kivuitu has been desperate and disrespectful of the Government. He should either do his work or resign. After all he has served for many years, he is old," he said. "We wonder if he is an LDP activist."
Kiunjuri was speaking at Afraha Stadium when he, together with assistant ministers Stephen Tarus and Danson Mungatana and MPs Macharia Mukiri and Jayne Kihara accompanied the Narc-Kenya aspirant William Kariuki Mirugi to cast his vote.
Vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele said he had witnessed a GK vehicle emblazoned with Narc-Kenya posters, campaigning for its candidate Ukur Yattani. It had Narc-K supporters, he said.
At a press conference in Marsabit Town, Mukele said the vehicle had private registration numbers but on closer look, it bore GK registration numbers - which he declined to reveal - on its windscreen and side mirrors.
Little General Election
At Bubisa, the ECK official said he saw voters being given rice and a truck transporting voters to and from polling stations.
Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo and his Subukia counterpart, Koigi Wamwere, were caught up in a melee as the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) supporters beat up three youth they alleged had been ferried to vote from the neighbouring Subukia Constituency.
Police had to whip out guns to save the trio from being lynched by the irate LDP supporters. Ndolo confronted Koigi's bodyguard, who had drawn his gun to keep the attackers at bay, and demanded that he keeps the firearm away.
"Unatoa bunduki ya nini? Hata sisi tuko nazo. (What are you drawing your gun for? We also have guns) Ndolo told the police officer. In the heat of the moment, an aide of LDP candidate, Pastor Mike Brawan, also drew a pistol, sending people scampering for safety.
About 200,000 voters were expected to go to the polls to pick replacements for MPs who were killed in a plane crash last April. This is the first time in Kenya's history that by-elections were being conducted in five constituencies on the same day.
Voters in Nakuru Town (117,016), Saku (17,050), Laisamis (15,610), North Horr (17,281) and Moyale (27,860) began voting as early as 6am in the "Little General Election", whose outcome political analysts argue, would provide a peek into how the battle for 2007 will be fought.
Misuse of public resources
The by-elections were occasioned by the deaths of Mirugi Kariuki (Nakuru Town), Bonaya Godana (North Horr), Titus Ngoyoni (Laisamis), Abdi Sasura (Saku) and Guracha Galgalo (Moyale) when a Kenya Air Force plane ferrying them to Marsabit for a peace mission crashed in foggy weather.
Several civic by-elections were also under way countrywide. By 6pm on Monday, most polling stations had closed, with counting to determine who would carry the day getting under way by 7pm.
In Marsabit, Kanu candidate Sarah Godana complained voters were being given relief food near polling stations at Hurri Hills and other areas that were her strongholds.
Voters, she added, were mostly assisted to vote and those who voted for her rival got relief food on the spot.
And, in an apparent reaction to Government spokesman Alfred Mutua's denial that the State was not using resources to influence voters, Mukele said: "If what I witnessed at Bubisa was not misuse of public resources, then what is?"
"I will speak the truth. If they (Government) sack me, I will go home to eat my cassava," Mukele said.
He said the bandit attack at Hula-Hula had not affected voting "very much". However, when The Standard left the station at 2.30pm, only election officials were present.
Kanu's Saku candidate, Halake Dida, said more than 300 people had not voted at Hula-Hula and Karare because of the incident.

Original_Search_Title: Straight Comedies Take a Break
Headline: Kenya: Straight Comedies Take a Break
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A sabbatical leave from straight comedies and the triple themes of obsession, redemption and retribution marked the theatre scene this week.
That theatre goers pay to watch plays that are a break from the "per minute hilarity" type of farces, shows how mature theatre patronage has become. Not of course, forgetting, the attendant seriousness theatre groups have taken in staging quality productions, starting with the Festival of Creative Arts.
The group staged Suspects, a psychological thriller by Giles Cole at the Alliance Francaise Auditorium, Nairobi.
The thriller was about a seductive housewife, Ellen (Dama Irungu) who meets Allan (Derrick Amunga), an insurance salesman at an exclusive pub who ends up at Ellen's flat in Hurlingham.
A couple of drinks and cosy cuddling later, and Allan blacks out, just enough time for her hammer carrying husband (Victor Ber), to come out of the bedroom and "handle" him.
But the following day, it is Ellen's husband's body that is discovered outside her flat and the police inspector Tindall (Arabron Osanya) and sergeant Goss, (Ken Gichoya) begin their investigations.
Liz (Angel Waruinge), the calculating former wife of Ellen's husband enters the scene to muddle up matters. The plot deepens when police discover that Ellen's husband had taken a Sh20 million life insurance policy and all the suspects were somehow, inching for a share of the spoils.
It is ages since we watched a psychological crime thriller, the last perhaps being Anthony Shaffer's,The Sleuth in the '70s and '80s and Agatha Christie's The Mouse Trap, staged at the Nairobi Institute in 1994.
Suspects, a cerebral whodunit, was therefore a welcome break from our staple diet of bedroom slapsticks. Its strength lay in finding out how, and who killed Ellen's husband, a situation that was compounded when we learn that Allan, in his second appearance, didn't actually die.
Directed by Caroline Odongo and produced by Eliud Abuto, the thriller featured a seasoned cast that understood their roles pretty well.
Waruinge seems to have the roles of the bitchy mistress cast in stone for her. A part from playing auntie in Jimmi Makotsi's She Ate the Female Cassava at the Kenya National Theatre in April, all her other roles have been of the bewitching "other woman". And don't we love her for it. As Liz, she was the sarcastic know-it-all, helping to angle Irungu's character as the greedy, but naive wife.
Gichoya was the cowardly and indecisive cop who almost botched the investigations with his "sissyness". Osanya, a veteran thespian handled the demanding role of inspector well, mixing his assertiveness in solving the crime, with the twin motive of being smitten with Ellen.
The cleverly written, neatly tied up, suspense thriller was spruced up with subtle humour and comic facial expressions from Amunga. Good stuff from Festival and we can't wait to watch what they will present next.
Over at the Phoenix Players is Simon Gray's comedy, Just the Three of Us. It is the story of Enid (Nyambura Mwaniki) who invariably adopts a pet, tells her hubby, Ronnie (Jackson Mwacharo) to take a hike as she retreats in her converted lighthouse.
But this time, her adopted "pet" is a chained up Terri (Nice Githinji), Ronnie's personal assistant.
Millicent Ogutu directs the play which is more of a story than a comedy that pegs the audience's attention span, not on the sparse spasms of hilarity, but more on the strength of brilliant acting from Mwacharo, (not a cubicle of vivacity lost since A Doll's House), Mwaniki (bawdy sense of humour and comical mood swings anchors the play) and Githinji, who crosses over from KNT to the benchmark of how good an actress one is. And she doesn't disappoint as Enid's "domestic pet", I might say.
The play runs till July 29, next Saturday and will give way to Mike Leigh's Smelling a Rat.

Original_Search_Title: Saraki Counsels Opposition Groups
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki Counsels Opposition Groups
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State yesterday said it was high time for the opposition groups in the state to have a re-think and join hands with the present administration in the state to accelerate the socio-economic development of the state.
Saraki who spoke with reporters in Ilorin shortly after the departure of President Olusegun Obasanjo to Abjua at the end of his two-day working visit, said all the frivolous allegations being levelled against the government would not take them to anywhere as the government would not renege on its promise of making life bearable for the people of the state.
The governor who was apparently reacting to allegations raised by Kwara Progressive Movement in one of the national newspapers (Not ThisDay) published during the visit of President Obasanjo to the state on the award of contracts and other programmes of the state government, said there was no iota of truth in the said publication as "this government is prudent in the management of the state resources".
He said, "The group alleged that, we spent over N1bn to construct new terminal building at the Ilorin International Airport whereas the government only spent over N400m and this is highly embarrassing and they should have a re-think in their approach towards the state ".
The governor remarked that "their opposition to our government cannot change things on the ground, it will continue to boost government's determination to do more for the people of the state in the provision of basic things of life". Saraki who commended the people of the state for giving the President a rousing welcome said the hard line posture of the opposition would not deter him from fulfilling all his campaign promises to the people of the state.
He said the visit and the happiness of President Obasanjo to all the places of our projects commissioned has attested to the government's determination to make life worth of living for the people of the state.
" See our state library that was commissioned by Mr. President, it was first of its kind in the federation because the library is of e-library status and this would go along way of assisting the people to gather information capable of boosting the ideas of the populace". He stressed.
He manitained that, " The visit has made the president to access us and confrimed that the Kwara state has moved from dark age to a new age where socio well being of the people has taking a major priority rather than selfish interest".
Saraki explained that, " Look at the Dangote Flour Mills here in Kwara state, the people of the state can now supply at least 10 tonnes of cassava while remaining 20 tonnes needed by the company can now be produced by the people of the state and thereby creating an employment opportunities for the citizenry".
He therefore caslled on the people of the state to continue to support the goverment so as to bring more dividends of democracy to the doorsteps of the populace.

Original_Search_Title: Burundi Inches Closer to Peace
Headline: Zambia: Burundi Inches Closer to Peace
Body_Text:
On Saturday, June 18, 2006, regional leaders, among them Vice-President Lupando Mwape, converged in the Tanzanian commercial capital Dar-es-salaam in their renewed and continued search for lasting peace in Burundi.
The meeting, like others before, marked another milestone in putting the tiny but potentially rich East African state back on the path of reconciliation, peace and national development after years of bloody hostilities in which thousands of Burundians have been maimed, displaced and killed.
Despite the adoption of a new constitution and resultant elections that saw the formation in August last year of a new power-sharing government headed by former Hutu rebel leader Pierre Nkurunziza, the National Liberation Forces, Palipehutu- FNL, the last active rebel group, had remained outside the Burundi peace process and its fighters continued to wage a mild insurgency in areas surrounding the Burundian capital Bujumbura.
After repeated overtures for peace from the Burundi government, the FNL split into two groups late last year with one faction vowing to keep up the fight and the other seeking a negotiated settlement.
But recently, South African President, Thabo Mbeki, offered his country's services as mediator and appointed his Safety and Security minister, Charles Nqakula, to oversee the peace talks.
Expectations were high on the coastal foggy and rainy morning of Saturday, June 18 as dignitaries from the region and beyond arrived to witness what was dubbed "Historic Signing of a Comprehensive Ceasefire" at the plush Movenpick Royal Palm Hotel in the heart of Dar-es-Salaam.
According to the programme, the signing ceremony was due to take place at noon after a brief closed -door consultation among the heads of state and the signatories to the peace pact.
But not unusual, this was not to be as it soon emerged that serious differences had arisen between the Burundi government team and the Palipehutu-FNL. Potential of a deadlock was evident earlier in the day when one of the key players Burundi President, Pierre Nkurunziza, in unexplained circumstances, delayed his arrival for the event fuelling speculations that the signing ceremony would not take off after all.
The real cause of the impasse could not be immediately ascertained but sources said differences on the nature and content of the documents to be signed were at the core of the stalemate.
Later when asked to comment on the deadlock, the FNL maintained that as far as they were concerned, what was agreed upon to be signed that day was a document on the 'principles and modalities' for negotiations to pave way for a comprehensive ceasefire.
"As far as we are concerned, we are not here to sign a comprehensive ceasefire document as proposed by our counterparts. So many peace agreements have been signed in the past but none of them has been upheld and implemented because they have not addressed the root cause of hostilities in our country," said FNL spokesperson Pasteur Habimana.
Pasteur Habimana blamed President Nkurunziza for the deadlock by insisting on the comprehensive ceasefire agreement before ironing out various contentious issues which have been the source of the civil war in Burundi over the years.
These include the demand by the FNL to be recognised as a legitimate political party and provision of immunity and security for its members and leaders in Burundi.
The FNL also wants the Burundi people to speak freely and truthfully on the history and genesis of their society as a way of finding a permanent solution to ethnic tensions.
Head of the rebel group Agathon Rwassa sums up the demands: "In total, we want the government to put in place a system that would respond to the needs of the whole nation as well as a review of the country's constitution."
On the other hand, the Burundi government contended that it would not accept anything short of a comprehensive ceasefire by the time of departure from Dar es salaam.
Chief of delegation Brigadier-General, Evariste Ndayishimiye, said from his government's point of view, negotiations had gone very well under the midwifery of the South African and Tanzanian governments and an agreement was reached on all the issues raised by the FNL.
General Ndayishimiye who is minister of home affairs and public security explained that the signing of a ceasefire was necessary to provide a platform for the restoration of durable peace in Burundi.
"President Nkurunziza means well. He remains committed to peace, that's why he is here and ready to sign the ceasefire agreement as soon as our colleagues are ready," said Gen Ndayishimiye.
The stand-off persisted with neither side willing to back down on their demands.
Closed door negotiations and consultations led by Mr Mbeki and his facilitator Mr Nqakula then intensified late into the night until the following day when a compromise breakthrough was announced.
And this was that the Burundi government and the rebel Palipehutu-FNL would sign a peace accord that would bind and pave the way for a comprehensive ceasefire in two weeks time.
Announcing the development before a weary but anxious audience, Tanzanian president who is also vice-chairman of the regional peace initiative Jakaya Kikwete said the history of Burundi was strewn with blood and violence and it was now time to bring reconciliation and lasting peace to the country.
The Tanzanian president observed that the impact of the civil war on life and property in Burundi was incalculable.
Mr Kikwete however paid glowing tribute to Mr Nkurunziza and the rebel FNL for choosing to tread the path of reconciliation at long last, adding that war cannot be used to prevent another war.
Mr Nqakula speaking before he invited the two parties to sign the peace accord, said the warring parties had shown their willingness to see peace in their country.
Mr Nqakula said the agreement reached that day would set up principles and modalities for moving the peace process to finality.
"The articles in the document to be signed in a moment, create the necessary space in the search for lasting peace in Burundi,' said Mr Nqakula. He said the articles would be signed by Gen Ndayishimiye and FNL chairman Agathon Rwassa.
