Gen Ndayishimiye and Rwassa hesitated to append their signatures for a couple of minutes as they made last minute consultations on certain articles.
After signing, the two leaders shook hands twice to the applause of dignitaries who included Mr Mwape.
The leaders urged the two parties to lay down arms and engage themselves in tackling the challenges of poverty facing their country and Africa at large.
They implored the warring parties to ensure they attain the objectives of the peace accord within two weeks as agreed.
Mr Mwape said after the signing ceremony that Zambia was excited that at long last, a firm foundation had been laid for peace in Burundi.
He said it was comforting to note that both the Burundi government and the FNL were represented at the signing ceremony at the highest level an indication of the seriousness and commitment they attached to the peace process in their country.
"Zambia is happy with this development and we pledge our continued support until full and final results are achieved to end the war in Burundi, "said Mr Mwape.
Zambia's contribution to the Burundi peace process and the region at large is mammoth.
Both President Mwanawasa and his immediate predecessor, Dr Frederick Chiluba, have played a key role in the restoration of peace in that country.
President Mwanawasa has been a member of the Core Group on the Great Lakes peace process which has been behind the peace efforts this far.
"When your neighbour is not at peace, you are not at peace either. So, we are happy that through the tireless efforts of our leaders, peace is finally in sight in Burundi," said Zambia's High Commissioner to Tanzania Professor Royson Mukwena.
Professor Mukwena said Burundi regards Zambia as its leading trading partner in the region with sugar, cement and other construction materials being Zambia's principal exports to that country.
He said he has also received inquiries from the Burundi government and the Chamber of Commerce and Industry in Zambia to consider exporting cassava to that country.
"There is a yawning market for these products in Burundi and with the brightening peace prospects, Zambian entrepreneurs should rise to the occasion and increase exports to earn the much needed foreign exchange," said Prof Mukwena.
Prof Mukwena said the potential for increased trade is strengthened by the fact that Zambia and Burundi share the waters of Lake Tanganyika , making the movement of people, goods and services cheaper and faster between the two countries.
By signing the peace accord, a foundation has been laid for durable peace in Burundi.
The outside world will help as it has always done. But ultimately the choice for peace lies in the hands of the Burundian leaders themselves by translating their signatures into action so that for once, the country can be at peace and take its rightful place among nations.- ZANIS

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Initiative: Flour Millers Donate N500m
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Initiative: Flour Millers Donate N500m
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government's cassava initiative received a boost at the weekend, with the presentation of a cheque of N500million, by the Flour Millers Association of Nigeria towards mass production and processing of cassava flour.
Making the presentation to the Managing Director, Bank of Industry (BOI), Miss Evelyn Oputu, at the Bank's office in Lagos, Chairman, Nigerian Flour Mills Plc, Alhaji Aliko Dangote, said the donation became imperative, in view of the Federal Government's directive to flour millers and bakers to ensure 10 per cent cassava input iof cassava as one of their major raw materials.
Dangote said the N500million was to encourage and assist in cassava production, processing and transformation into flour, as alternative to wheat/sorghum in bread and other confectioneries production.
According to him, "unless we support them, we will not be able to achieve the target of 10 per cent cassava input as directed by the Federal Government."
According to him, "we hope they will assist us to ensure that the required cassava is available to meet the target," he said, addng that they need about 200 metric tones of cassava per annum to meet the target.
In her response, Oputu commended the Association for the donation, saying "they have put their money where their mouth is."
She said BOI so far, has committed more than N10billion towards industrial development and growth in different areas, including cassava production and processing.
President, Flour Millers Association, Mr Emma Ukpabi, expressed hope that with the presentation, cassava producers and processors would be tremendously encouraged and tasked BOI to ensure that the project succeeds.

Original_Search_Title: Interview With Congolese Army Commander in Ituri
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Interview With Congolese Army Commander in Ituri
Body_Text:
Bunia — With a continued fluid security situation in the district of Ituri, northeastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), the Congolese army deployed Gen Mbuayama Nsiona six weeks ago to assume overall military command in the area.
Faced with a variety of problems, including those of logistics; equipment; wages for the troops; and still active militia groups; Nsiona's troops have been taking part in joint operations with those of the United Nations Mission in the DRC, known as MONUC, to try to stabilise the security situation ahead the country's first democratic elections in 45 years, scheduled for 30 July. IRIN spoke to Gen Nsiona on 27 June about Ituri's security situation. Below are excerpts of that interview:
What is the current security climate in Ituri?
The general situation is relatively calm with the exception of a few areas such as Djugu where Peter Karim's militia group seized seven UN peacekeepers on the 28 May. The general base of this militia force was in Tche but it has been dislodged to around Mount Aoyu and government troops are now carrying out operations there to neutralise it.
Has it been difficult for the army to deal with problem of the militia groups still active in Ituri?
Militia do not operate in units. This is why you have bands operating in areas such as the Bunia-Morabo road where joint patrols of government and MONUC troops are active, down to the border with North Kivu. At the moment, however, government troops are not carrying out large-scale operations because we have given the militiamen a grace period to surrender their weapons against a deadline of 30 June. This is why there are currently no operations to disarm them by force.
How would you describe the relationship between the Congolese army and MONUC?
Most of the media reports coming from here are only interested in the negative aspects of the Congolese army and many times have tarnished the image of FARDC [the Congolese army]. Yet the relationship with MONUC is very good, it is a very professional army, which is doing a good job and, from my point of view, as the military officer in charge of Ituri, this needs to be taken into consideration, and credit should be given where it is due.
How serious is the problem regarding the easy availability of small arms and light weapons in Ituri?
The infiltration of light weapons such as the AK-47 is certainly a problem, especially against the background of the fact that six years ago, Ugandan troops were operating here in Ituri. After a peace accord was signed and the Ugandan army left, they left all their weapons in circulation here. There is also the infiltration of weapons from neighbouring countries into the district.
The difficulty is in policing people along the border areas. At the border posts, it is difficult as the authorities do not have the capacity to inspect every piece of luggage, every vehicle, every bag of cassava, beans or rice; everything that is entering the country from outside. Yet when you attempt to carry out such checks, the people complain of harassment, so it is a very complex situation and if it is to be improved, action has to executed right from the point of entry where control measures have to be implemented.
If there were tighter international controls on the production and sale of small arms and light weapons, what difference would this make here in Ituri?
The implementation of controls would be difficult, such as putting a soldier at every border entry point and beginning the systematic search of goods and vehicles coming into the country. But if measures were taken against the countries manufacturing these weapons, this would serve as a good deterrent. Neighbouring countries such as Uganda produce munitions like the AK-47 rifle, yet we do not even know the number of these produced each year. We know some weapons come through [the port of] Mombasa in Kenya, then on to Kampala [capital of Uganda] and on up to Ituri. In a nutshell, greater control measures are required if the situation is to improve.
You have problems paying, feeding and supplying your soldiers with ammunition; how could this situation improve and how difficult is it for you to operate under such conditions?
The question is pertinent and is the nerve centre of everything here. It should be noted that the government has made tremendous efforts to secure the payment of the army; not the quantity or amount of salary, but the timing. Now the troops are being paid on a regular basis. One of the efforts is to know the exact number of soldiers. Once this is known, then better planning can be made to improve conditions.
The country is vast and the transfer of money requires a lot of time and effort but we are now in a position where at least salaries are being paid regularly every month. The other problem [relating] to the total number of soldiers in the army is that we need to know things such as how many uniforms and weapons are needed, once this is concluded things can be handled much better. Also, transport from Kinshasa to Ituri poses a major challenge. Blankets and uniforms for the soldiers have been received, but there is problem of fuel for transport from Kinshasa to this place.
Have your army's logistical problems been a result of government bureaucracy?
As you know, the present government is one of inclusion with people from different groups forming the interim administration. So, to take a decision to execute a particular action requires a lot of time. I hope once the elections are held we will have a government of national unity and things will work out well. I tell my men that brighter days are ahead and good soldiers understand there are difficulties and that these difficulties concern everyone.
Once the security situation in Ituri has stabilised, how many troops will be needed to maintain the peace without MONUC's help?
More soldiers are needed now in order to force the militiamen to surrender their weapons. We need more soldiers on the ground, now. After the climate of conflict, fewer soldiers will be needed; more police will be needed to work, leaving the soldiers to be deployed in order to intervene and secure specific areas. If I am given two more battalions I could deploy them along the border with Uganda along Lake Albert.
What do you think is the reason for the heavy deployment of Ugandan troops along the Ugandan side of the border, near Lake Albert? How much of a concern is this?
It is not only along Lake Albert, but also in Arua in northwestern Uganda opposite the Congolese town of Aru. It is a concern of course, but I do not think they have any intention to attack this country. I see it as a precautionary measure being taken by Uganda to prevent militiamen from spilling over into their own country.
How pertinent to the elections is the deployment of Congolese troops?
It is the duty of the police to carry out operations regarding the elections. The army only comes in as a back up. The strength of the police in Ituri is very small, so in some areas the military is required to secure the safety of voters.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Nkiinzi, the Revolutionary
Headline: Uganda: Nkiinzi, the Revolutionary
Body_Text:
Kampala — It's very challenging to be a girl in King's college Budo," Sanyu Nkiinzi Kagwa says when asked about her memories as a she-Budo and as the first female mess prefect. Now the communications and public relations co-ordinator of the Family Planning Association of Uganda, Nkiinzi describes herself as a spokesperson for the speechless, a career she says started the moment she joined Budo in 1993.
After seven years of studying with boys at Kampala Parents and growing up in a family full of boys, the least of Nkiinzi's fears was being intimidated by the boys of Budo.
She was in for a surprise. "If you are a girl in Budo, you have to be aggressive and fight for what you want. We were 10 girls in our time (1993 to 1998) compared to 40 boys. The boys mercilessly booed us in and out of class. They used to call us terrible names like zoo, Jurassic Park so we also started calling them names. We had to learn how to tease and fight back," she says.
The mess was one of those areas where the boys brought out their worst. Shaking her head, Nkiinzi narrates: "The girls had no tables so they had to share with boys. Three girls would share a table with 10 boys and since the boys were in charge most of the time, we often went without food. They would serve themselves heaps of food while girls walked away with a few beans and a small a piece of posho. And because you are a girl, you would meekly say 'thank you' instead of asking for more.
"The worst part came when you lifted your fork to eat. The boys would turn and stare at you - we used to call it pocketing - until you felt too shy to eat. If you ignored them and ate, they would call you a 'bender' which meant that you were greedy," she recalls.
"And then there are days when they left nothing for us. Once, the boys ate all the chicken. We were very hungry so we cried. I told the cooks that we hadn't eaten chicken but all they said was 'Sorry, it's all finished.'"
Fortunately for the girls, the headmaster then, Samuel Busulwa, was all for girls' rights in Budo. "He was like our father. A girl could go to his house anytime to cry and he would listen," Nkiinzi says.
Sure that this was one of those times, she ran to his house and poured out her hunger. His reaction was immediate. By the end of the day, the girls had devoured more pieces of chicken than they would have if the boys hadn't stolen their share.
By this time, the girls had devised ways of dealing with 'pocketing' and whatever pranks boys chose to play on them. "We decided to start a girls' revolution to fight back. If a boy pocketed you, you had to pocket him back. We agreed to pocket one boy every day until they all stopped pocketing us." She says, triumph written all over her face. Always, Nkiinzi was the ring leader, and the boys learnt to respect her for that. With the girls' support, they voted for her overwhelmingly and she beat four boys to clinch the post of mess prefect.
It was an easy win, but not an easy term. Some boys challenged her authority but she nevertheless succeeded in changing most of the rules the boys often manipulated to take advantage of the girls. The boys started participating in slaughtering the chicken and peeling food, particularly potatoes and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Standing High in a Male Dominated World
Headline: Uganda: Standing High in a Male Dominated World
Body_Text:
Art celebrates the spirit, tradition and culture and communicates to those who understand it. In Uganda, art has been a male thing and often, female artists fail to rise above this. However, some female artists are taking their talent and profession to a whole new level. Elizabeth Kameo spoke to three women who are using this medium to empower themselves and others and earn a living
Maria Naita,
Medium of expression: Wood and Metal fabrication
When did you start working as a professional artist?
I have been doing commercial art since 1991. However before that as a young girl I did a lot of artwork, but it was different, it involved making dolls, colouring and moulding.
What made you choose art?
I think I have loved art right from childhood and was encouraged by my mother. When I joined HSC, I realised I could do it full time, it gives me challenges. Art is very challenging and satisfying.
Who or what inspired you?
My mother. She is an artist. When I was still young she used to paint using thread work.
Is art a recognised career in Uganda?
I think it is picking up. Before, we would only get expatriates buying our work but now more and more Ugandans are buying our too, especially those in the younger generation.
What have your greatest achievements been so far?
A monument about the heroes of Uganda at Kabamaba military barracks, a sculpture and next to that my first major garden piece in the State House in Rwanda about the Intore Cultural dance.
Are male artists more recognised than female ones in Uganda?
Yes I think so. They are many and more active than the female artists. Female artists have more challenges. I am married with four children; it is not easy having a studio at home and looking after the children. If I were not so taken up with art I would have given up by now. It is also manual work, takes a lot of energy and so many females are not up to such work. The female artists are also sensitive, to criticism. When they receive negative criticism, they easily give up.
As a woman, what qualities do you need to succeed in this field?
You have to be able to express yourself well and put your spirit in your work. If you do not do that, you will not succeed. You also have to keep busy.
What career options do artists have in Uganda?
Teaching. Artists are also good managers. They can work in industries such as Nytil, channel into designing, architecture and landscaping.
What are the biggest obstacles?
Before you create a name in the art world, you have to live a tough life and be patient. You cannot get clients easily and have to persevere. Marketing one's work is not easy at times it is not possible.
What are your future plans?
To have a well equipped studio, big enough to share with fellow artists. Currently I work at home and share my studio with my brother (also an artist).
Margaret Nagawa
Medium of expression: Painting with acrylics on canvas using cassava as a starch base
When did you start working as a professional artist?
I went through art school education and started practicing when I completed. I first exhibited at Gallery Café when it existed along Masaka Road. I have been working professionally since 1993.
What made you choose art?
I always wanted to be an artist. I liked art and it was my first choice at the university. My mother was not supportive but my father was. However I applied for an art course and got accepted at the university. I enjoy art, it is an avenue I use to communicate best. It is a medium I feel comfortable with. We always had artwork at home and I gravitated towards it like others did towards law and medicine. Images are something I really like.
Who or what inspired you?
Nothing and no one in particular but I was encouraged by my father. Whenever I said what I liked he would support me. Most people at that point would never have done that even now many parents do not encourage their children to take up art.
Is art recognised in Uganda?
It is getting recognition. There are more avenues showing art and more Ugandans buying art and attending exhibitions. In 1993 when I statred there were very few studios and one of the few was Nyanzi's along Parliamentary Avenue, Nommo Gallery and the University Exhibition Hall.
What have your greatest achievements been so far?
Tough question. Capacity to continue working when surrounded by three children. I have been able to raise my children and still work as a professional artist and merge my art career with my everyday life.
Are male artists more recognised than female ones in Uganda?
I think there are probably more recognised female artists, but the male artists are still more. Many women get lost in motherhood. And since it is not easy to rely on art they turn to other avenues to earn a living. However the few female artists who are practicing are excelling.
As a woman, what qualities do you need to succeed in this field?
Need to have the talent to work with, the drive to keep going, you have to be brave and persevere. Art exhibitions are like presenting oneself in the nude. If you are not brave and the responses you get from people are not what you want, you will give up easily.
What career options do artists have in Uganda?
First and foremost artists would want to be primarily artists. But may have a sideline. They can be teachers, work in advertising as designers and architects.
What are the biggest obstacles?
The audience we have is both positive and negative. Our main audience is the expatriate community and they live here for a few years. So there is a continuous change of audience.
Then there is the perception about artists. Many people think we are not serious; they want to hear one is a lawyer or doctor. If you tell someone that you are an artist they will immediately ask you to draw them and if you can't they think you are not an artist and yet not every artist is into drawing.
What are your future plans?
Being a mother, I choose not to take on big projects currently. But in the future when my children are older, I plan to set up a big display space, which will be an avenue of communication through art. I hope to find a way of merging all forms of art in this space, the theatre, film and magazines.
Stella Atal
Medium of expression: Painting
When did you start working as a professional artist?
In 1997, but since I was in school I used to paint and draw, I do not think I can say this is when I started practicing professionally because I was selling my work even when at school. But in 1997 I set up a studio.
What made you choose art?
I grew up in a family of artists. Art and artists always surrounded me. So I grew up appreciating art and in that way developed my talent.
Who or what inspired you?
My brother, he is an artist and today I work with him at the studio we share.
Is art a recognised career in Uganda?
No. Students are discouraged from taking it on and government has failed to offer support and assistance to the artist community regardless of our contribution to society.
What have your greatest achievements been so far?
Being recognised both at home and internationally as a good and talented artist. That is the greatest achievement to date. In all countries I have been to I always find a person or two who have seen or bought my artwork and that is humbling for an artist, what more can I ask for and what other achievement can one seek.
Are male artists more recognised than female ones in Uganda?
There are so many male artists. While so many females took art at the university and in higher institutions of learning, very few take to practicing art as a career. While there are few female artists, they are immediately recognised when mentioned which is positive.
As a woman, what qualities do you need to succeed in this field?
You have to be willing to use paints and be ready to get "dirty". As a woman you have to stop thinking of beautiful nails all the time, because being an artist will hinder this. To succeed you will also have to respect clients and meet deadlines, reliability is important if an artist is to meet goals and be successful.
What career options do artists have in Uganda?
Most become teachers or interior and fashion designers.
What are the biggest obstacles?
Marketing. We lack skills to market our work or people to help us do this. In Kenya ACFODE helps artists market their work and they are succeeding and entering markets they never thought they would access before.
We really need government support because art is a tourist attraction and generates a lot to the revenue.
What are your future plans?
To spend more time helping vulnerable people in my country using my talent. I love putting smiles on those people's faces as much as I can.

Original_Search_Title: Re: the Death of Ken Moore
Headline: Sierra Leone: Re: the Death of Ken Moore
Body_Text:
Freetown — We deeply regret the wrongful death of Kenneth Moore, the late land ministry official. We express our heartfelt condolences to his family and friends.
Beyond doubt Ken Moore died in the line of duty - we will forever remember him as dying an honorable death in serving his nation.
The P.M.D.C. strongly condemns the savage actions of Ken Moore's murderers. The most important lesson the decade-long brutal war has taught us is to desist from reacting to threats of violence with threats of violence against ourselves, against our government officials and against our country. Ken Moore's murderers must be pursued and brought to justice.
The P.M.D.C. is fully aware of the following maxim amongst Sierra Leone's folk; "The cassava leaves must be put away while restraining the goat." The S.L.P.P.-led government of President Tejan Kabbah and Vice President Solomon Berewa has failed to follow such popular wisdom.
A house is not a temporary tent for recreational use that can be assembled and disassembled without the notice of the authorities especially in a small country as Sierra Leone. Some corrupt officials in the past and present governments have knowledge of these land transactions that have sadly become the prognosis of Ken Moore's death.
This unfortunate state of condition that forced our citizens into committing such an egregious act of violence against their own could have been averted.
The government of Sierra Leone knowing quite well that corruption is its Achilles heel should have had plans in place to rebuild or recompense the homeowners, who are likely victims of corruption, and thereafter pursue the corrupt government officials who might have had hands in these land transactions.
In an article on Awareness Times, "A Seven-Point Rejoinder to Mr. Jacob Jusu Saffa's Laudations of the Sierra Leone Peoples Party Government by Karamoh Kabba-P.M.D.C. USA & Moijue Kai Kai-P.M.D.C. England, March 28, 2006, suggested exactly that, but fell in deaf ears.
In their article, the two men wrote, "One would ask the following question; why is Mr. Saffa not worried about the destruction of houses without compensation that is underway in Freetown in the name of road construction? Berewa has become the demolition man in Freetown-he is going about ordering the demolition of houses without compensation plan." Instead, the Honorable Members of Parliament on Friday 16th June, 2006, according to the Awareness Times newspaper condemned "the present state of lawlessness in the country and blamed the death of Kenneth Moore squarely on two Ministries including the police," in retrospect that the police could have done a better job in protecting Ken Moore. Their actions demonstrate a bankruptcy of knowledge that the strongest crime and violence stopper is not the police, rather a good government that protects the life, integrity and property of its citizens without bias.
Ken Moore died because of corruption by the A.P.C. and S.L.P.P. governments and gross negligence by the S.L.P.P.-led government. The government of Sierra Leone is responsible for the death of Ken Moore. The P.M.D.C. encourages all land and homeowners who have become victims of A.P.C. and S.L.P.P. governments' corruption, now losing their life earned properties to demolition, to file a class action lawsuit against the government officials of Sierra Leone or their agents of demolition or both.
Ken Moore's death could have been averted.
National Interim Chairman Pa Bai Bai Kamara P.M.D.C. National Interim Leader Charles F. Margai P.M.D.C. Interim National Secretary General Ansu B. Lansana P.M.D.C. Interim Chairman-USA Dr. Baimba Kamara P.M.D.C. Interim Chairlady-England Agnes Macauley P.M.D.C. Interim Chairman-Netherlands Abdul Karim John Interim Director of Comm. & Media/Rapid Response Team (RRT) Karamoh Kabba The Members of the P.M.D.C. global RRT

Original_Search_Title: Women Caught in the Soccer Passion
Headline: Ghana: Women Caught in the Soccer Passion
Body_Text:
Accra — It was a beautiful sight in the early hours of Thursday June 21st when Ghana was scheduled to play the United States at 2 pm in the ongoing World Cup championships in Germany. Along one of the dusty roads that lead to a Gbawe suburb in Accra, a Kooko (Porridge) seller had wrapped her huge pot containing the hot porridge in national colours shouting and waving at every car that passed by. Her enthusiasm drew many customers, including males to the little shed that houses the porridge as they responded in the Ga language 'ebaa fee gbeye, America baa kotoshie' literally meaning America will bow as Ghana perform wonders.
Elsewhere in East Legon in Accra at the office of the Institute of Democratic Government (IDEG), a pretty lady said to be a civil society activist and attending a meeting with European Journalist on tour of Ghana to discuss poverty and growth wore a beautiful necklace in national colours with a small football as a pendant.
In addition to that she wrapped a national flag around her pony tail hair style. As she entered the IDEG conference room, the men rose up to receive her all shouting in ecstasy. In front of the World Bank offices at Ridge, Public Agenda spotted some young women in Areeba hats all over Accra.
Since the beginning of the World Cup this June, women have been caught in the frenzy. Although men patronize matches in stadiums than women, women have shown they are better supporters of the national team than men. Even during the unfortunate May 9 stadium disaster women were not left out in the casualties. But for the numerous household chores and other responsibilities, women would have played an even greater role in playing and supporting football in this country.
No wonder the women's national team, the Black Queens qualified for the World cup before their male counterparts. Some observers have even argued that women have done a lot of the singing and dancing at the ongoing tournament than men.
In fact more women were at the road side or beside their male counterparts on the streets, in cars and on roof tops when Ghana won her first match against the Czech Republic.
But what amazed this reporter was the mood of women in Accra Central last Thursday. Since this reporter's heart had often been broken by football results, she decided to take a stroll to Accra Central during the match to observe the stress levels of market women as the match progressed.
At the entrance to the Kwasea Dwaso opposite the Electricity Company of Ghana Offices, a female porter around 17 years was really dressed up. Her dress, headgear, necklace and earrings were all in national colours, while carrying her load.
Some of the market women were in a prayer mood. "Yesu nawo mobo eei," literally meaning "Jesus have mercy on us".
Others were in joyous mood as they blew whistles clad from hair to toe in national colours and sweating all over.
Radios were dotted everywhere, there were the more literate market women who were listening in English commentaries and passing on information to their stressed out colleagues.
Sometimes goals were announced ahead of time and the jubilations were short lived.
However, it appeared majority were listening to Akan commentaries. With ears close to the radio set almost all the listeners announced the first goal simultaneously and the market was thrown into frenzy. A tuna seller stripped off her blouse revealing a beautiful midriff and heavy busts as her contribution to the efforts at goal scoring.
What amazed this reporter was the level of commitment of the women to the soccer fiesta, their familiarity with the players and the rules of the game. ; It appeared every body at the market had a stake in the march. The weak hearted only waited for good news as their faces were bowed on their tables. "My head is aching, one woman confided in Public Agenda as she pulled up her trouser which was down her hips as a result of excessive jumping after the first goal. There were flags, hats wrist bands, necklaces, earrings available for sale and almost every trader and their porters had put on one of these. One would think it was a requirement for doing business at the market.
The market which is sharply divided along political lines prior to elections was this time united and so strong was the unity that it was beyond comprehension. "The boys playing the soccer on the field in Germany are our children, they deserve our prayers and our support," a cassava dough seller told Public Agenda.
As soon as the Americans equalized, the market women started praying and trusting God. Before long the listeners announced their own goal against the background of loud noise, no wonder they came back to correct the misinformation.
As the real second goal came, the market was thrown into frenzy again with loud music from Sidney's hit song "Obiara nye obiara," Grace Ashy's Black Stars song among other praises to God. Others took to dancing with the movement of body curves -wriggling of the waist, bust and sometimes showing off vital parts of the body.
At the end of the match the young porters and their male counterparts all took to the streets with some of the women following them.
As Public Agenda left the market, several women were spotted along the streets of Asylum down beside men and singing and dancing in a festive mood. At Asylum Down many young ladies were seen dancing at Semi's Place, a restaurant and bar to crown it all. Who says women do not enjoy soccer? The gender desk of this paper wishes the women of Ghana a stress free watching of tomorrow's match against Brazil. With the support of all Ghanaian women, the sky is the limit.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Sacks Agabi, Macebuh, 10 Others
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Sacks Agabi, Macebuh, 10 Others
Body_Text:
Abuja — SPECIAL Adviser to the President on Ethics, Chief Kanu Agabi (SAN) and wife of Chairman, Board of Trustees (BoT) of Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Mrs. Josephine Anenih were among 12 top aides sacked yesterday by President Olusegun Obasanjo.
Mrs. Anenih was the Special Adviser on Women Affairs to the President. Special Adviser on Policy and Programme Monitoring, Dr. Stanley Macebuh and Special Adviser on Civil Society, Alhaji Mohammed Abba Gana made up the four presidential aides also dropped.
The sack which came 24 hours after the President effected a cabinet reshuffle also affected eight senior aides of Vice President Atiku Abubakar
Two new ministers; Chairman, Peugeot Automobile Nigeria (PAN) Dr Aliyu Modibbo and Prof. (Mrs) Lesley Obiorah, from Imo State who is a professor of law and until her appointment was a visiting lecturer at the University of Connecticut United States of America, were sworn-in by Chief Obasanjo.
But other aides sacked in a memo signed by the Chief of Staff to the President, Major General Mohammed Abdullahi (rtd).
As two Special Advisers to the Vice President and the six remaining Special Assistants. The Special Advisers are: Professor Mike Kwanashe, SA on Social and Economic Affairs and Dr. Okolie SA on Policy and Programme Monitoring. The Special Assistants are: Mr. Phil Agbasi, Yima Sen, Nath Yadima, Dr. Ardo, Dr. Ajuji and Mrs. Adeola.
Sources in the Presidency revealed however that the President's desire to embark on what is undoubtedly the last of his planned reorganisation and down sizing within his office and that of the Vice President has been long waited, adding that he has personally informed Vice President Atiku Abubakar of this latest development without naming the affected officials before the latter jetted out last week to the United States for his annual vacation.
Administering the oath of office on the ministers, the President charged them to perform above board to see their appointment as a call to serve the people of Nigeria. The president had Wednesday administer oath of office to five new ministers after which he reassigned some of the old cabinet members to new portfolios.
While Dr Modibbo was appointed as Minister of Commerce, Prof. Obiorah will serve as the Minister of Solid Minerals.
Addressing them during the brief swearing in ceremony held at the council chambers of the state House, President Obasanjo directed the new Commerce Minister to continue as Chairman of PAN; and to promote for export the mass production of cassava, cocoa, and cotton.
While advising the new Minister of Solid minerals to work closely with her predecessor Oby Ezekwesili, moved Wednesday to the ministry of Education, he challenged the new Ministers to see their call to serve as a special privilege. According to him "If out of a total of 150 million people any of us are invited to serve as minister we must see it as an honour and you must see it as a challenge because the work ahead of us are enormous.
President Obasanjo also tasked the new Minister of Solid minerals to collaborate with the National Assembly to pass all the bills relating to the operation of the Ministry in the area of mining.
Dr Modibbo who holds PhD in Education had in 2003 served briefly as Minister of State Power and Steel.

Original_Search_Title: Brother Attributes Chiluba's Illness to Prosecution Pressure
Headline: Zambia: Brother Attributes Chiluba's Illness to Prosecution Pressure
Body_Text:
Mwense — FORMER president Fredrick Chiluba's brother Jim Nkonde has blamed the illness of his sibling on prosecution pressure.
In an interview at Chiluba's Kaombe Village in Mwense district yesterday, Nkonde who spoke in Bemba said he was always worried about his brother's prolonged illness and treatment in South Africa.
"I am worried about his health. Even when I am eating, I am still worried about him," Nkonde said. "Icikonko chalemwikata sana echo alelwalila, chilaleta ukutontonkanya."
He complained that the government had troubled Chiluba for a long time, hence his illness. Nkonde, 66, said Chiluba's illness had caused anxiety among the villagers.
"We are worried about our young brother's suffering in health and in life in general," Nkonde said. "Umwaiche (the young man) is ill because of pressure. Prosecution has taken many years."
Chiluba is facing corruption charges for offences he is alleged to have committed whilst in office. And Nkonde thanked the government for flying Chiluba to South Africa for medical attention.
"People are happy that President Mwanawasa took Chiluba to hospital," he said. However, Nkonde said it was sad that President Mwanawasa had caused a lot of problems on Chiluba despite the latter having given him the presidency on a silver plate.
Nkonde said when Chiluba was president, he used to help him and other family members in the village but he stopped after leaving State House. "Chiluba used to help us but now he doesn't. They (government) closed his doors of income so we are suffering a lot," Nkonde said. "But I have some cassava."
He wondered when Chiluba would come back. Nkonde also said when he heard rumours that Chiluba had died, he started crying. "I cried when I heard that message. I was happy to hear that he was still alive though still suffering," he said.
Nkonde observed that people in the area could vote for President Mwanawasa though he had done little for the people. "People here are still supporting the MMD government. But I haven't seen what President Mwanawasa has done for us," Nkonde said. "People here want wells, we draw water from a very far away place."
He said Chiluba wanted to develop Luapula Province but members of parliament let him down because they did not deliver his development goals.
Asked why he had a different surname from Chiluba, Nkonde said they took their surnames from their different fathers. Nkonde's house is situated just metres away from FJT Chiluba School.

Original_Search_Title: Bon Apetit
Headline: Uganda: Bon Apetit
Body_Text:
Kampala — Mention Dewinton Road and one of the first things that springs to mind is the restaurant, Bon Appetit. Bon Appetit has stood the test of time and remains a favoured place for all those who work in the vicinity and the students who visit the National Theatre across the road.
Is this Bon Appetit affiliated to the other Bon Appetit's that are springing up all over Kampala?
That I am not too sure about. What I do know is that this particular one is owned by Gera Mosha, wife of Kampala Mayor Nasser Sebaggala. Bon Appetit is more of a fast food restaurant where, on average, people spend less than 30 minutes on their meal.
Talk us through the place.
On either side of the entrance is the seating area. Beyond that is the cashier's bay, a small office and the kitchen. Like most fast food restaurants, the beauty about Bon Appetit is that you get to see what is happening in the kitchen, especially if you have ordered a take away
Is that all?
They have these huge bay windows which have been darkened so that people walking past do not get to see you but you can see them. That is where most people prefer to sit so they can look at the traffic and those walking by as they eat.
Most fast food restaurants tend to be stuffy and cramped. Is this the same with Bon Appetit?
Not quite. They have overhead ceiling fans and from time to time there is a cool breeze that blows through.
What about the food? Was it to your liking?
Like I said earlier, Bon Appetit is more of a fast food restaurant. On the day we were there most people went in for chicken and chips, sausages and chips or kigere (cow hooves). I had the chips and sausages at sh2,900, and it was to my satisfaction. However, the salad, while fresh, did not look that healthy. That aside, just to give you an idea about what they have on their menu, there is beef and matooke, katogo, and tilapia. For snacks, they serve chaps, kebabs, cassava - that sort of thing.
THE GOOD POINTS: See the prices.
THE BAD POINTS: Trying to find parking during the lunch hour traffic.
THE PRICES: Healthy on the pocket. Average price is sh4,000 for a main course meal, sh700 for snacks and sh1,000 for drinks.
THE KABOOZI: "I did not know that Members of Parliament came here for lunch." And "Isn't our Kampala Mayor over there in the corner?"
THE STAFF: On the day we were there, we saw nothing out of the ordinary. And they did have fresh shirts and blouses.
THE TOILETS: Passable.
THE CROWD: A good number from Nile Bank, WBS Television, National Theatre and Parliament.

Original_Search_Title: To Cane Or Not to Cane
Headline: Kenya: To Cane Or Not to Cane
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Garissa students vow to kill a teacher", "Female teacher in Kakamega stabbed by a knife-wielding student", "Gun-totting student gunned down by Ruai police in a foiled robbery".
These are some of the headlines in the local dailies that drove a teacher in Kitui to sue the Government over the caning ban.
Mr Nicholas Kaloki, a teacher at Ilako Mututa Secondary School, says he could not just sit back and watch as things got out of hand.
Kaloki says indiscipline in schools is chilling and unless action is taken, a whole generation risks going down the drain.
Ever since the Education ministry banned caning in 2001, the children's rights crusader has been watching events unfold. He says as things stand, they can only get worse.
He is a seasoned counsellor and offers his services to his school. He is also a visiting counsellor to 24 secondary schools, spread in the semi-arid districts of Mwingi and Kitui. Kaloki says the talking has not been enough.
"The decision to ban caning in schools was a whimsical political manoeuvre as a result of the bad press coverage the ministry was attracting over a few teachers who went overboard and inflicted physical injuries on their students. It was a unilateral decision made without the evaluation of the case on the ground," he asserts.
When The Saturday Standard visited the school that is set in an offbeat location of Mutonguni Division, the scorching sun overhead and the sun-baked bare earth traversed by gaping craters for roads, make you know that this truly is a hardship area.
We had been travelling for more than a 100kms without seeing as much as a stream and at every market centre we stopped, eager hawkers swarmed around our car, brandishing raw tubers of cassava. We'd later realise why the teacher had insisted that we meet him in the school.
Naturally, the first question we bowl over when he ushers us into his new and clean staffroom is why he took up the enormous task of taking the Government to court single-handedly. "You will not understand my answer unless you have the patience to first hear what I've been doing with my life for the last two decades or so," he says, eyeing me with his sober eyes that cannot belong anywhere else other than a classroom. It is a story of rare passion, unbridled audacity and an inbred dream of daring to conquer the topsy-turvy vicissitudes upon which every young mind is accursed.
Standing at just over four feet, he is a slender frame who speaks with a persuasive conviction. His is not mere talk, he fumbles inside a metal cabinet at the corner to back up his contentions with figures, news reports or Government sources complete with cell phone numbers.
He says he knows where he is headed because, in the same measure, he knows exactly when and where his journey started.
It was a gloriously sunny Sunday morning of 1987 and the 22-year-old Kaloki had travelled to Kitui town to pick his favourite newspaper, a ritual he has observed to this day. "As usual, I sat on a bench at the Kitui bus terminus to first skim through The
Sunday Standard. Suddenly, looking up, a young woman was standing there before me carrying an 18-month-old baby. She asked me to hold the baby for her as she picked up some things from a shop and vanished - for good."
"It was not until late in the evening when I finally realised what had happened," Kaloki continued. "Though I later took the child to Kitui Children's Home, the event changed my life forever. Try as I might, I could not fathom why a person would visit such cruelty to a child.
"That encounter has been my life's driving force because it has stuck in me like a virus. Today, I have three things as my mission statement, children, children, and children," he said.
Kaloki first taught as an untrained teacher before joining college.
He was posted to Mbitini Boys' High school, but it collapsed in 1997, three years after his graduation. He went to Ilako Mututa Secondary School, where he'd make his mark in the children's rights crusade.
Being already a counsellor, his calling would gain a new dimension in 2002 when he witnessed a case in Mutonguni Division, where a man had raped a three-year-old girl and agreed with the parents to settle the matter out of court.
"I could not accept that! I quickly had the man put behind bars and went to speak to the Kitui Officer Commanding Police Division over what was happening in my division. "To my horror, he confirmed to me that Mutonguni had the highest number of cases of rape in Eastern Province. I realised that if I did not do something urgently a similar tragedy could befall the other girls," says Kaloki.
The teacher applied to the Children's department as a volunteer in the district and the following year he was made the children's officer for Mutonguni. Henceforth, he would be teaching half a day on Tuesdays and devote the rest of his time to children's work.
Every evening, he attends to complainants at his office in Tulia market.
Kaloki's reputation as the children's defender has spread far and wide. "Nobody gets away with rape in Mutonguni," he says, showing us a list of filed cases and the convicts he has managed to put behind bars. "Every household in the division has my cell phone numbers and I'm on call around the clock."
Later in the day, when we visit Musengu market, several miles away, people look at us suspiciously when they hear we have been to see Kaloki. When idlers loafing by the shopping centres see his car they beat a retreat and wait until the white Nissan Sunny, battered by the lunarscape roads and scorched by the punishing Ukambani sun, is safely behind the corner.
He has in his car a copy of the Children Act, 2001. "It is my duty to deal with parents who will not take their children to school. I'm mandated by the Government of Kenya to protect children," he asserts. "It is my work to take charge of abandoned children, begging or even displaced children. Those displaced, those whose parents are in prison and those in child labour bring them to me."
True to his word, he is already living with five children in his home near the school - three of whom their parents are in prison for other offences and two whose parents are unknown.
"Counselling is a time-consuming job and requires many follow-ups," he says, "Unfortunately, there are not enough of us in the country and that is one of the reasons I'm taking the Government to court to reverse the decision on caning. I'm doing this for my love for the children."
"Should the Kenyan public watch helplessly as the discipline of their children deteriorate due to a simple administrative order by the Education ministry that lacks the capacity to stand to legal scrutiny?" he poses.
The teacher says the introduction of free compulsory learning has seen massive enrolment of youngsters in public schools. This has reduced the ratio of pupils to teachers considerably. "Considering the requirements of effective counselling, today's teacher is unable to cope with the workload. Add the level of indiscipline brought by children who have been out of school for a long time and you will see a disaster in the making."
"There is a correlation between indiscipline and poverty. That is a fact as old as time, which Kenyans are ignoring. As long as we remain poor we will need the cane. Indeed, Section 127 of the Children Act deals comprehensively with the limits of enforcing discipline on children and the same can be used to gauge what is acceptable and what is not. Regulation of caning is what we need right now."
Kaloki says that some quarters have not taken kindly to his lone crusade. "Some people have accused me of going overboard because I don't have children. This is erroneous. I'm the loving father of three girls," says the teacher, who is also a pastor at the local Full Gospel Church.
"Doing this kind of work needs a supporting family because many times I have to use the family car to chase law breakers around the villages. Fortunately we share the same vision with my wife and I hope my daughters will follow suit when they grow up," he says.
His wife, Agnes, is a teacher at the neighbouring Kiatine Primary School. She teaches children with physical disabilities. "Caring and protecting children is a family affair to us," she told The Saturday Standard.
At 41, Kaloki has been teaching for half of his life. A Tanzanian media house is preparing a documentary on his crusade and he has appeared in premier global publications - among them the New York Times. The teacher reckons we can only spare the rod at the peril of spoiling the children.

Original_Search_Title: Waiting for Taylor in the Hague
Headline: Sierra Leone: Waiting for Taylor in the Hague
Body_Text:
Freetown — I wonder how it would have been like if Europe and the United States would just open their gates for at least an hour and allow Africans to stream in droves without the headache of visa restrictions. The word anarchy would have been an understatement and any attempt to restrain a loved one from going would fall on deaf ears. The idea of most Africans is that it is very easy over there. In fact, many think manna just fell from heaven. The reality is that we do have brothers and sisters out there who literally sleep on the sidewalks , others do barely have jobs to etch out a living while some have to contend with immigration wahala coupled with the uncompromising winter.
For many of those whose minds have been made up to go to the "white man's home," they will do everything humanly possible even to the extent of nakedly risking their lives along the Moroccan borders for Spain. But wait a minute , ask yourself this question ; with corruption growing wings like an over fed bird everyday, with Kabbah tiger generators having succeeding in replacing a moribund NPA (No Power Available) in our homes and with the unsightly spectacle of filth in every available space in the city coupled with ' No Jobs Available,' what would you expect of a desperate man or woman? To them, their only hope is to go out there and start from afresh clean up rubbish, wash dead bodies and so on.
Few weeks back , I decided to go and see for myself what is happening on that side of the world . I did not go to stay. My destination was primarily France but with the prospect of seeing Liberia's former president ,Charles Ghankay Macarthur Taylor having his day in court in the Dutch town of Den Haag, it served as a perfect enticement to go there and just in case ; wait for Taylor in The Hague.
In recent times, the name, The Hague, has become synonymous with "a court". And it is not just an ordinary court; The Hague, which is the administrative hub of the Netherlands, is the seat of the UN International Criminal Tribunal for Yugoslavia (ICTY).
Nobody visits this court without making international headline news. Until recently, it was the trial and death of Sloboan Milosevic, former president of Yugoslavia that made headline news from there. Now, a Dutch businessman is also behind bars and facing trial there for his alleged involvement in arms deal during the Liberian Civil war.
For me , the ICTY was a major landmark that I had wanted to visit ever since I was born but what further strengthened my resolve was the fact that Den Haag had opened her doors for its ICTY facilities to host the trial of Taylor should they find a jail for him if proven guilty of his charges and considering the fact that I come from a country where Taylor is being kept waiting for trial backed by the fact that I also work in his native , Liberia was a motivating factor to have ventured there to see what awaits the former president when he finally arrives.
Thus, when the opportunity came for me to take some time off work from my normal community outreach activities, it dawned on me that a visit to The Hague was almost now a fait accompli . The journey itself started by boarding a flight to Accra Ghana for an almost fours hours and twenty five minutes flight which saw me being much more closer to God in prayers amid the recitation of some holy verses for a very long while . After resting for about seven hours at the Kotakoa airport, I was enroute again but this time round, onboard a comfortable KLM flight whose beautiful air hostesses were almost always on hand to ask , " coffee , tea, juice, wine or snacks ?". In the early hours of April 12, I landed in Schipol airport in Holland but it was not my final stop. Charles De Gaule airport in Paris beaconed and in less than 45 minutes from takeoff at Schipol, I was there .
Picked up by a friend, off we went to the Parisan suburb of Melun on board an underground train. Paris, they say is one of the most beautiful cities in the World and I got the better part of it but alas !
Unlike most of our West African states, there is more life underground her holes and tunnels than above.
In fact there is a lot of fuzz and buzz down there where you meet a lot of people than you can imagine order than on top, literally running to and fro for trains . After enjoying the beautiful landmarks of Paris including her Eiffel Tower and the famous media houses of Le Monde and the Stade de France among others , I was ready to set out again for The Hague.
On a cold and windy Saturday morning in April, I boarded a Thallys ( underground electric train ) from Paris' Gardene Nord for an over four hours journey to Den Haag's central station. As the place was very cold, I had to double and redouble my clothes to keep warm. Upon arrival in Den Haag as it is know in Dutch, one could easily feel the serene and peaceful atmosphere of this small Dutch town which happens to be the country's seat of government. There is some kind of romantic air that looms around it that one can't simply resist its enticement from falling in love with it.
Since it was my maiden visit, I decided to take a look at some of the landmark features, which saw me moving from street to street and visiting a few shops in the process. The Hague boasts of many museums and is definitely a place to be for the lover of arts.
Many of the elegant buildings must have been built several hundreds of years ago and the main streets are dotted with government offices. There is the historic collection of buildings known as the Binnenhof, home to both chambers of the legislature and there is Paleis Noordeinde where Queen Beatrix's administrative offices are housed. The Maduro Dam is also a must see. A major form of transportation there are Trams and bicycles. They battle with taxies and other cars for available space in the city's tiny and narrow roads. It is even muted that the Prime Minister, at times goes to work on his bicycle. Can our own president try it one day? I mean moving from Hill Station to PZ and see for himself how our brothers and sisters trod everyday in search of getting their stomachs filled by 2007?
With a friend as my guide, I decided to satisfy the curiosity of my eyes by visiting the principal judicial organ of the UN, the ICTY. Locating Taylor's next port of call in this small Dutch city was a problem. Despite armed with a map and several stopovers to ask questions, it could not save us from going round and round like a merry- go- round until a Good Samaritan was gracious enough to literally install the direction into our heads and, off we dashed for our gold spot.
Right in an area called Churchillplein (or Peace Palace????) was the imposing building that at first glance reminds you of those medieval churches or a museum in Eastern Europe. It does not have the looks of our Law Courts Building on Siaka Stevens street, nor the Temple of Justice in his home country or even the Special Court premises at New England Vile.
Surrounded by the world Forum Center and KPMG, one of the world's renowned financial auditing firms, the ICTY surroundings has an air of pin drop silence that was somewhat frightening for someone like me from post-war Sierra Leone.
Perhaps, I should talk a bit about The ICTY. It started work in 1946 replacing the Permanent Court of International Justice, which had functioned in the Peace Palace since 1922. The Court is composed of 15 judges elected by the UN General Assembly and Security Council sitting independently of each other.
Unlike the Special Court in my home country that is heavily guarded by battle tested Mongolian troops with raised concrete walls and barbed wires, the ICTY has a relaxed outlook which does not mean security is taken for granted.
Though I walked up to the main entrance of Taylor's next home, I never had the opportunity of passing through the first gate and unlike Sierra Leone where four people were recently held for taking photos at the Special Court, at the ICTY , you can take as much as you want and nobody will cough.
But take your time to ask passersby and even the taxi drivers around whether or not they know that Taylor, appears headed for the building just in front of them , most will ask, " but who is this Taylor? " Need I say more to them? Of course not but again , those guys still have fresh tales of how until recently, the former Serbian president , Slobodan Milosevic was a guest of honor at The Hague.
From what I saw, The Hague is a nice place to be.
Although some people including Taylor may disagree.
There, his worries over mosquito bites, the scotching heat or what can now be best described as the uninterrupted flow of darkness - I mean , electricity shortage and just maybe, conjugal visits may be over.
After the Haag, I also had the opportunity of rubbing shoulders with the Sierra Leonean community in the cites of Helmond and Eindhoven where I attended a Sierra Leonean jam session and a PMDC organized meeting as an observer. The trip also gave me the opportunity of reuniting with two of my former colleagues at Concord Times, Kingsley Evans (Sugar), Alex John and Benjamin Palmer, formerly of For Di People. The latter came all the way down from Germany to see me.
Interestingly , guys kept asking my friend during my stay with him whether I was there for good or not but I am back to base , in Africa , where I enjoy eating my cassava leaves with red oil and of course, bush meat.
The writer was one-time Editor of Concord Times.

Original_Search_Title: What Could Have Influenced Cabinet Appointments?
Headline: Uganda: What Could Have Influenced Cabinet Appointments?
Body_Text:
The cabinet list is finally out. Rodney Muhumuza and Emmanuel Gyezaho look at what may have informed the appointing authority's thinking.
If the list of nominees to Cabinet, made public yesterday, came amid a lot of unpredictability and tension, it was for good reason.
While it gave some credence to the bulk of media speculation that preceded it, the curious mix of new-timers, odd choices and previously unknown persons betrayed the complexities that must have informed its drafting.
But it did not lie on one thing: that the men who previously managed the health ministry and, even more critically, were implicated in the Global Fund scam, had lost their jobs.
Health ministers out
Maj. Gen Jim Muhwezi, Capt. Mike Mukula and Dr Alex Kamugisha, who typically became the embodiment of the biggest financial scandal since the junk chopper affair, will not be in the next Cabinet.
But so will reliable NRM cadres like Prof. Mondo Kagonyera, known for his hard work ethic and Igeme Nabeta, a youthful legislator, who many commentators thought had come to typify an emerging shift in the NRM power base from the old breed to the younger cadres.
That is the same story that Kibaale MP Frank Tumwebaze, who was widely expected to be nominated, will not want to recount.
A closer look at the list shows that while certain disparate issues guided its compilation, no particular script seems to have been followed.
While it rewards the ageing Kirunda Kivejinja for routing FDC rabble-rouser Abdu Katuntu from the House, it also returns the equally elderly Muganwa Kajura, a veteran Munyoro politician, who was defeated at the Bugahya NRM primaries by the energetic Kiiza Rwebembera.
Losing MP seats
That irony explains the fact that while losing a parliamentary seat did not help one's chances of retuning to cabinet, it was not, on its own, a harbinger of 'ministerial doom'.
It is apparent that issues of seniority, loyalty, and competence were some of the key criteria. " I think President Museveni had to give some posts to Bunyoro.
He was considering loyalty, regional balance and not ideological positions," said Makerere University political historian Mwambutsya Ndebesa.
" I don't see the cabinet being chosen deliberately for government effectives or ideological clarity or political effectiveness. These were not his considerations."
Energy crisis
With an energy crisis on the hands of the government, it had been somewhat expected that Lamwo MP Hillary Onek and his counterpart Simon D'ujanga would make it to Cabinet.
Although the portfolios are yet to be defined, it is now expected that Onek and D'ujanga, who have in the past demonstrated their competence on matters to do with energy, will most likely take on the Herculean task of sorting out the crisis.
"I am hoping we can have Hilary Onek to sort out the energy sector; a technical person to know what is being done and not work under mediocres. The NRM has used a window dressing strategy for 20 years now and we are now reaping where it has become full circle," said Salaamu Musumba, the FDC deputy president. "I am hoping we have people with real knowledge in key sectors."
Onek, as the only successful lawmaker from Acholi, had extra protection. The President has consistently had low popular support in Acholi and Lango: that there are only two Acholi (Onek and Okello Oryem) points to a direction that is not inspired by a need to compromise.
Oryem may have lost his seat in the House, but his personal connections are well known. Omara Atubo, a Langi, returns to Cabinet in circumstances that can only be explained within the context of Museveni's desire to run a government that has a semblance of all-inclusion and unity.
The inclusion to the list of Atubo, a former UPC stalwart, completes that picture in the same way Sulaiman Madada's name gives some delight to the Muslims.
In some cases, it is not possible to explain the interests certain nominees represent beyond the ostensible idea of diversity.
Names like Kasirivu Atwooki, Simon Ejua, David Wakikoona, Nelson Gagawala and Lukia Chekamondo do not make much sense to analysts, and were out of most theories before the list was released.
Persons from the west, again, make up the bulk (27 in total) of the list. It remains to be seen what the people of Rukungiri, who now have no one in Cabinet, will react to the list.
Muhwezi and FDC leader Kizza Besigye have been the area's most illustrious sons.
In a district where 'cake-sharing' topics are discussed with so much passion and where the natives have traditionally voted for Museveni with one heart, the absence of a Cabinet minister from there could cause a fallout.
Ms Paula Turyahikayo, who defeated Kagonyera to take the Rubabo Constituency seat, would have been a fair bargain had she not been unfavourably mentioned in the Global Fund mess. But Ms Winfred Matsiko, the Rukungiri Woman MP, is not tainted.
The inclusion of Mr Eriya Kategaya, who fell out with Museveni in 2003 over the proposal to amend the 1995 Constitution to remove presidential term limits, has been traced to some kind of soul-searching within State House.
The return of Kategaya, beyond painting a gloomy picture of his personal convictions, raises the questions: Has there been an unreported agreement between Kategaya and Museveni? Does Kategaya, who considered the third term project an abuse of democracy, know something about Museveni's ambitions that the rest of the citizenry don't know about?
Since it has been claimed that he will be in charge of a portfolio that relates to East African integration, does Kategaya find that East African unity is a vision that outshines the Museveni regime (and legacy)?
Some opposition politicians are saying they have no confidence in the competence of the incoming Cabinet.
Despondency
Such despondent thought is undoubtedly linked to the inclusion of Mr Salim Saleh, the President's sibling who has had a chequered past.
"As a diet, the NRM has given us more carbohydrates of the cassava type, which takes you to sleep. The NRM cabinet is more cassava than anythingâ-oe It looks more to me like another wasted opportunity," Salaamu said.
"You can never point to any background of success in the majority of these people even on a technical basis. What expertise is being brought in? Is it to occupy chairs?" Only time will tell.

Original_Search_Title: US Condemns Niger Delta Crises
Headline: Nigeria: US Condemns Niger Delta Crises
Body_Text:
The United States of America has condem- ned the lingering crises at the Niger Delta region that resulted in kidnappings and terrorism by restive youths against oil companies in the area.
US ambassador to Nigeria Mr. John Campbell made the remark at a press conference during his visit to Kano, last Friday at the Murtala Muhammed library complex.
Ambassador Campbell expressed dismay over the frequent occurrence of crises at the Niger Delta region. He added that youths in the area have no justification to take laws into their own hands by kidnapping foreigners and other acts of terrorism leading to the breakdown of law and order.
"We are deeply concerned with the plight of the people of the Niger Delta. We aspire for a better life for them. Nonetheless, kidnapping, acts of terrorism and destruction of properties are never justified," said the US ambassador.
He called on the federal government to expedite action in regulating the activities of oil companies working in the on-shore and off-shore areas with a view to finding lasting solution to the crises.
Campbell assured of the US government continued support to the region through non-governmental organisations that will ensure the improve-ment of lives by providing them with basic necessities of life.
"We are interested in improving the quality of life of the people there through a multi-partnership programme underway to introduce a variety of cassava resistant to disease for cultivation in the area."
The US ambassador had earlier at the state government house told journalists that with the approach of elections in 2007, the US government will support the country to ensure a free and fair conduct of the election.
He however assured that in as much as the country's democracy is important in the eyes of the world, the US government will not interfere in Nigeria's election uninvited.
"What interests the US is human rights, democracy and rule of law and also a free and fair election in the country."
The ambassador later delivered a lecture on elections with theme "Election: the American Eperience, 217 Yars Snce the Frst American Eection and the Wrk for Uiversal Sffrage Cntinues," at the Murtala Mohammed library complex.

Original_Search_Title: Keeping It Ugandan... in London
Headline: Uganda: Keeping It Ugandan... in London
Body_Text:
It is that kind of lovely Saturday afternoon that makes you wish you were anywhere near the shores of Nalubale soaking up the tropical sun, waiting on a juicy fried tilapiaâ-oen'ebigenderako.
The former is a wish that is at least an eight-hour flight away from this deceitfully early summer South East London sun but the former is a matter of choice; all you need is a visit to Owino or Wandegeya. Then you would have to decide whether you want your tilapia fresh or smoked, and that is without even going as far as Heathrow Airport.
The variety of things on sale is wide enough to include clay, that pottery raw material that is a delicacy for people who are eating for two (pregnant women). The other dilemma may be whether you still really want just fried fish or stewed with matooke, lumonde, muwogo or posho and, possibly mixed with groundnuts.
Owino Supermarket
Inside what could easily pass for a traditional English Off-licence (a small supermarket of sorts that sells alcohol, cigarettes, food and newspapers) with a bold sign of Owino Supermarket, Ugandans in London can lay their hands on any merchandise.
Apart from the famous name in bold green letters , the goods outside are another give away with boxes of matooke, sweet potatoes cassava and avocado staring at you along Mitcham Road. On entrance, the story is told in immense detail. The list runs from fast-selling snacks like pancakes -that bring in throngs on Mondays and Tuesdays when fresh ones come in - mandazi and chapatti to different species of smoked fish and Maganjo maize flour.
This business venture is on such rise that people no longer just go to Owino. Across town is another one with a name way bigger than its size - Wandegeya Mini Market. Set on the residential Conway Street at the back street of Plumbstead, it is probably safer to carry your reading glasses with you otherwise you are bound to miss the miniature sign.
The stock may be just as famous as that of Owino but the diversity is hardly as high. Probably one reason why customers only come in spurts; leaving the young part-time attendant staring a stretch of parked cars between posters of the Easter shows with Julianna Kanyomozi, Bobby Wine, Ragga Dee and General Mega Dee and Nvannungi.
Playing safe would be doing your shopping as close to Thursday as possible when fresh stock comes in from Kampala. Most captivating is a fridge full of East African lagers: Bell, Nile Special, Tuskerâ-oeand whatever comes out of the fridge goes for Â£2 a piece. Never mind that some people do get knocked out on that much (roughly Shs6,500), but this is drinking more for the memory than anything else.
Ugandan CDs
Chances are that every third black person in Forest Gate, East London, is either Ugandan, speaks, understands or can, at least, detect Luganda which explains the size of Pascheal Exotics; the other home to 'ba Mugalu' in Kampala. A name change to something like St Balikuddembe, considering the bustle at Pascheal's would be justifiable.
The corridor-like establishment makes it seem to go on for ever with lines of boxes of matooke, corn, pineapples, cassava. At the back of the stretch lies the source of that booming 'local' music; a stall that vends CDs, DVDs and cassettes of different Ugandan and other African artistes and the money transfer service in another corner.
It seems to get so busy that the person in charge of filling up the boxes is constantly on his feet and Pascheal himself is ever punching the keys at the till cashing in on the home-grown merchandise.
These tiny establishments live up to their names in their diversity of Ugandan merchandise. Who would ever imagine being able to buy those chewable coffee beans and mats in the heart of London! Even the non-Ugandan commodities are as close to our very own as you can get; tinned fish and beans, orange juiceâ-oe There may have been times when leaving 'those' shores was a western-lifestyle sentence of burgers, pasta and - depending on which strand the moonsoons dropped you - ghastly sea-food.
Same lifestyle
It is not necessarily the end of that era but there is unbelievable diversification; coming to London and turning all western is only a matter of choice that hinges on the kind of lifestyle that a Ugandan in London chooses to lead or whether one has the time and skill to prepare those delicacies.
As it turns out, those Ugandans in the British capital with the means live a more accomplished Ugandan lifestyle than many folks in Kampala with probably just their loved ones to miss about home.

Original_Search_Title: Cunene - Province Hosts World Food Day Central Act
Headline: Angola: Cunene - Province Hosts World Food Day Central Act
Body_Text:
Ondjiva — The southern Cunene Province will host the central act in the celebrations of the World Rural Women Day, World Food Day and the Tele-Food campaign, to be marked on 15 October, as stated on the programme received by ANGOP on Wednesday.
nAccording to the note from the organisation of the event, seminars will be held on "The introduction of cassava in the staple dietst o the populations in the southern region of the country " and "Guidelines and elaboration of Tele-Food micro-projects", in Ondjiva city.
In the ambit of the festivities of the World Food Day will be also inaugurated some social undertakings, as well as will take several socio-cultural activities.

Original_Search_Title: Inside the Market
Headline: Sierra Leone: Inside the Market
Body_Text:
Tengbeh Town Market "Congosa" Market as it commonly called, is located at the main Old Railway Line, Tengbeh Town.
The market accommodates on wide range of the Tengbeh Town community including people from all walks of life.
The market comprises of make shift structures with about one hundred traders selling various cooking food and non-food items.
Speaking to this press in an interview, one of the market women, Ya Sento Kabia , said in the midst of some problems which they face, life is normal in their daily business. She pointed out that most of the items sold are bought in large quantity where goods bought from the provinces are sold cheap in large quantity.
Every morning she went on they have to board a vehicle as early as 5:30 to go to "Dove Cut" and Guard Street market to buy goods. Ya Kabia noted that they have to rush up to return as early as 7 a.m to get their item out from stores.
"This has been going on for a very long time. We are use to it because this is the only way some of us find our living" she said.
On some of the problems they are encountering, she mentioned lack of proper market house that can accommodate more traders.
'We have more traders who want to come and join us so that we can together provide more service to the people, but they could not because there is not enough space to accommodate them" she said, adding that the problems normally get worse in the rainy season when we experience flooding and roof leaking. She requested the government and other NGOs to help rescue them from this suffering, more so as we will soon get heavy down pour of rains.
Explaining the reasons why prices in the market are high compared to others, Ya Kabia said it is due to transportation cost from East End Police 'Poda-Poda park, as they are charged exorbitantly for their loads "We sometimes run at a loss most especially when our goods perished on the way to Freetown," she said, adding that they can buy five gallons of palm oil today to sell at a minimum price of Le800 and by the next day they will hear of a lesser price which eventually lead you to a loss" she explained.
There is a poor sanitation at the "Congosa" Market as well as other market such as Guard Street market which happens to be the filthiest in the city.
Unlike other markets in the municipality of Freetown, the Freetown City Council Market keepers continues with their normal sale of market tickets.
Very close to the market is a lorry park for taxis and other commercial vehicles which makes it easier for traders. Other traders are found around the market vicinity, calling different items. The prices of some basic cooking food items include:
Rice Imported white long grain rice is sold at Le 400 per cup, while the short grain called 'fen-fen' goes for Le500 per cup. The locally produced country rice called 'Wala' rice is sold at Le600 per cup while Rough rice goes for Le 800.
Sauces A tie of potato and cassava leaves goes for Le 300 while the medium and big ties of cassava leaves cost between Le500 &1,000. Crain-Crain and Green cost Le500 each per tie.
Seasonings Onions of different sizes go for Le100-500 for both the imported and locally produced leaf product called "Lungai Yabas" wet and dry pepper is packed at Le200 while big pepper cost Le200 and 500. The same prices go for vegetable tomato. The imported can tomato paste is sold at Le500 per tin. Dry pepper is sold at Le100 per small plastic tie, Jumbo magi is sold at Le 200 each and 100 for the star cube maggi. White Ajinomoto small packet magi go for Le100 while the bigger size goes for Le200. English salt is sold at Le100-200 per tie; the locally produced salt is sold per cup at a price ranging from Le100-300.
Oil Red palm oil is sold at Le900 or at times Le800 per print base on the quantum in the market. The price for "mosankay" cost Le 600 which also goes for the palm cannel nut oil. Groundnut oil is sold at 1,000 per pint.
Fish Dry herring is the most common fish sold at the Congosa Market. The price for dry herring depends on the quantum also goes for the wet mina and herring. Good fish such as 'Shine Nose' and others is difficult to get at the Congosa market mostly one has to take the strain to go to other markets.
Others Groundnut is sold at Le600-700 for the raw product, while the milled one goes for Le300-500 per tie; Garden egg is sold at various prices depending on the size, ranging from Le200-500. Ogiri is sold at Le100 per ball. Tola and Gbala are both sold in teaspoon and small cups, the price ranges from Le1, 000-700. White and black pepper, baking soda and power cost Le100 each per tie.

Original_Search_Title: UN Food Drops Reach Some, But Many More Missing Out Due to Lack of Funds
