Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: UN Food Drops Reach Some, But Many More Missing Out Due to Lack of Funds
Body_Text:
Although United Nations-sponsored airdrops into the Democratic Republic of Congo's (DRC) violence-wracked Katanga province have provided enough food for some 34,700 displaced people, a severe lack of funds is preventing a quarter of a million others from being reached, the UN World Food Programme (WFP) reported today.
"We are very pleased to announce the success of this first airdrop into DRC," WFP Country Director Felix Bamezon said of the operation, which began in April and is the agency's first food airdrop outside Sudan in eight years.
"But we are not content with what has been achieved. We need to reach many more people in the next few months, particularly in North Kivu (province)," he added of the latest crises, in which an estimated 220,000 people have been uprooted by fighting between the Government and Mai Mai rebels in Katanga and another 80,000 displaced in North Kivu Province.
With just three months left of its overall two-and-a-half year Protracted Relief and Recovery Operation in the DRC, WFP still has a critical 36 per cent shortfall, or $69 million of the $191 million needed to help up to 1.6 million internally displaced persons (IDPs) and other vulnerable people throughout the vast country.
This leaves very little funding to assist the IDPs in Katanga or in North Kivu, where WFP was forced to cut rations during the last distribution due to the shortfall.
Although expensive, WFP chose airdrops in south-eastern Katanga province because the rainy season, rising overland transport costs and insecurity have made it difficult to use roads. Food trucks were taking up to eight weeks to reach distribution sites from Katanga's provincial capital of Lubumbashi.
To feed people who have fled to the towns of Dubie, Sampwe and Mitwaba, an Antonov-12 aircraft has spent just over three weeks dropping 336 metric tonnes of commodities, at nearly twice the cost of road transport.
"Our village was totally destroyed by armed men. I feed my child with what I have - some cassava - but I am really happy that aid has arrived," Claudine Kambemba said in Sampwe after seeing food airdropped not far from her camp.
WFP's partner non-governmental organization (NGO) last week completed distributing cereals, lentils, oil and salt to more than 8,750 people in Sampwe. Distributions of air dropped food are taking place in the next few days for 9,980 people in Mitwaba and 16,000 people in Dubie.

Original_Search_Title: Theatre: Moving On to Serious Stuff
Headline: Kenya: Theatre: Moving On to Serious Stuff
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Like they promised early this year, local theatre groups have given Western bedroom comedies a break and are instead staging more serious works by African dramatists, beginning with Kenyan playwrights.
Festival of Creative Arts set the ball rolling with Jimmi Makotsi's She Ate the Female Cassava at the Kenya National Theatre (KNT) a month ago. They were followed Phoenix Players with Cajetan Boy's Dead Talk a fortnight ago, while last weekend at the Alliance Francaise auditorium, Pambazuka Productions celebrated their first anniversary with Francis Imbuga's seminal drama, The Burning of Rags.
All were serious productions and, happily theatre groups are taking us back to the memorable 1990s, when an amateur but vibrant theatre satisfied theatre lovers. Of course, there are a few jokers like Arts Africa Productions, who over the Easter weekend staged Taphine Otieno's I Dearly Love Your Wife, an example of how not to write a play.
Shallow script
While local playwrights should be encouraged, it is important that they give theatre goers their money's worth. Otieno's supposed comedy was the group's debut theatrical offering, staged at the Kenya National Theatre's concert hall.
It was a shallow script, worsened by over acting, wanting set construction, drab costumes and lack of continuity. Impressive Grace Dola (Jacky) was the promising actress in the wrong play, which was being re-staged due to great "public demand" - to a largely empty hall!
There is a tendency among thespians to only watch their own shows, but Otieno should write more plays only after watching those of serious playwrights like The Burning of Rags.
Pambazuka gave a remarkable rendition of this play which, hopefully, will make more people take theatre seriously. There were signs that that might already be happening, if the number of companies that sponsored the show is anything to go by.
The play revolves around Denis (Sam Kihiu), a brilliant professor who is in trouble with his community for failing to participate fully in the traditional initiation of his son Yona (Ledema Sempele), due to his demanding career and Westernisation. His father Agala (Charles Bukeko), an arrogant traditionalist, and Babu (Andronico Otieno) the know-it-all village elder, will have none of it. Denis' born-again mother, Elima (Loise Abukutsa), sides with her son, making the situation even worse.
Rough time
Denis is not having an easy time in the city either, what with his girlfriend Hilda (Joy Mureithi), who has a fishy past, is pregnant and determined to marry him. Things get tricky when Denis learns that Hilda had another baby and wants to leave her.
Henrics Fisher (Kanyeki Sinbad), a friend of Denis' and father of Hilda's child, comes back into the picture. He wants to take her to the US where he is a lecturer but withdraws the offer when he learns of her pregnancy.
Charles Bukeko directed a brilliant cast which included Mumba Hinzano as Dr Agbale and Peggy Ngure as Matilda. The cast clearly brought out the theme of generational conflict, which Westernised Nairobians can still identify nearly two decades after the play was written.
All the actors portrayed such understanding of their characters that one can hardly pick a favourite: could it be Joy Mureithi, who convincingly made us empathise with women in her situation? Or Kihiu with his tearfully believable demeanour when his father "died"...Kanyeki and his laughably affected mannerisms?
Commendable too was the superb set construction, complete with real smoke on the village set. Well, Pambazuka showed that a group can hold an audience captive without raucous laughter at end of every punchline.
The only minor lapse was in continuity. At one point Hilda was listening to Denis' poem, From Home with Bananas on the music system, but we didn't see her insert the tape. And when she was dancing to a Salsa tune, Denis switched it off to play traditional Luhya numbers, but again, without changing the tape. So was the tape with the poem also the one with Salsa and Luhya songs?
Imbuga drew heavily from traditions of his Luhya community in scripting the play-within-a-play that had his trademark fool figure, subtle humour and a contemporary issue. He is famous for his political play, Betrayal in the City, which represented Kenya in the 1977 Fespaco festivals in Nigeria.
His other plays include; Kisses of Fate, The Fourth Trial, The Married Bachelor, (written in 1973 but made into a feature film in 1998), Game of Silence, The Successor, Man of Kafira and Aminata, which was commissioned for the UN Decade for Women's Conference held in Nairobi in 1985.

Original_Search_Title: Because of Limited Facilities: Taylor, Norman, Others Angry At Special Court
Headline: Sierra Leone: Because of Limited Facilities: Taylor, Norman, Others Angry At Special Court
Body_Text:
Freetown — Dr. Bubuake Jabbie, lead defense counsel for the first accused, Sam Hinga Norman in the Civil Defence Forces (CDF) trail at the Special Court Tuesday, as a preliminary notice to the court, complained to a Status Conference saying detainees, including Charles Taylor and others are not happy with facilities.
"Detainees are complaining about their visitors, including relatives, being harassed and embarrassed whenever they pay a visit to the detention center. But whilst we intend to take the matter with the Chief Detention Officer and Registrar, we thought that such preliminary notification is important," he deduced.
Jabbie put it to the Presiding Judge, Pierre Boutet that telephone lines are no longer in their earlier better situation when one line was available to each set of indictees.
"Detainees are asking for an increase in the quantity of traditional diet (rice and potato or cassava leaves). They also need a clarification on the issue of compensation or labour fees as compared to international standard. They receive Le 3,000, which is far below a dollar, per day," Jabbie noted.
Also other concerns he raised with the Conference have to do with the general medical care, which needs to be improved upon.
"They are seeking more emergency care, particularly in the case of the first accused, Norman who sustained and still complains of pains to his hips. His doctor recommended his hospitalization with a view that it is now very necessary to take urgent actions with regards his situation," he pointed out adding that because of the inadequate availability of telephone lines Norman has to walk quite a long distance to receive a phone call which also worsens his pelvic pains.
He concluded saying counsels intend going into writing but because of the urgent nature of the situations they'd decided to use the opportunity accorded them by the Status Conference to make a preliminary notification.

Original_Search_Title: ThisDay and the Commoditisation of the Press
Headline: Nigeria: ThisDay and the Commoditisation of the Press
Body_Text:
Last Friday, April 28, ThisDay responded to widespread criticisms of its decision to carry a wrap-around advert in support of the Third Term agenda with a full-page self-advert. The advert, addressed to its readers, was a robust defense of its position.
"Since the publication of our wrap-around advertisement on Thursday, April 20, 2006 placed by supporters of tenure elongation for President Obasanjo," ThisDay said, "there has been a lot of reactions from readers and the general public.
"We continue to welcome all views and reactions as we engage our esteemed readers in making ThisDay a better newspaper.
"However, a few local commentators and columnists with limited understanding of current trends in newspaper publishing have continued to serve their readers with half-truths, founded on ignorance and lack of understanding of the issues.
"For columnists who do not know, whose journalistic ideas are passé, wrap-around advertisement is not new to Nigeria and the print media the world over. It is an effective marketing tool used by the best and brightest newspapers and magazine the world over to offer creative solutions to marketing challenges."
To prove its point ThisDay displayed a wrap-around advert from a New York property developer, carried by the International Herald Tribune on January 27, 2006 and another one from Ritz, a leading global hospitality company, carried by Newsweek International on March 27, 2006.
ThisDay's wrap-around advert was the subject of my column last week. In it, I argued that publishing the advert was unprofessional and unethical. "The wrap-around political advert that ThisDay carried on Thursday April 20, and which the Sunday Vanguard carried last Sunday, was," I said, "like the Atkins' episode repeating itself - only this time worse; Atkins may have been a faceless coward, but at least someone signed his advertisement. Not only did no one sign the advert, it carried the stamp of authority of both newspapers. This was simply unacceptable professionally and ethically."
For those who may not have read my article last week, Dr. Keith Atkins was a, if not the, leading advocate of the extension of military president General Ibrahim Babangida's transition programme. Early 1992 he published a full page advert in almost all the national newspapers urging Babangida to extend his transition from 1993 to 1997. It was in consideration of Dr. Atkins' ignoble role that I titled my article last week as "ThisDay, the neo-Atkinists and the Third Term agenda."
Apparently my good friend, Nduka Obaigbena, ThisDay's publisher, and his editors took exception to the characterization of their decision as unprofessional and unethical. Their response goes to the heart of both the theory and practice of journalism as a profession. It therefore deserves a close examination.
ThisDay says those of us who criticized its wrap-around advert are out of date, at least professionally. Its only proof was that even well-regarded global publications like International Herald Tribune and Newsweek International do it.
To begin with, ThisDay's example was a misleading comparison; the IHT and Newsweek wrap-around adverts were not anonymous. The advertisers were well known names in their areas of business.
In sharp contrast, ThisDay's wrap-around was anonymous. To date no one has owned up to paying for it nor has ThisDay itself revealed the advertiser's identity.
On the contrary everyone, including ThisDay, has tried to disown the advert. Transcorp, President Obasanjo's conglomerate godson and the leading suspect, has since vehemently denied any links with the advert. ThisDay itself, through my brother, Olusegun Adeniyi, one of its editors and divisional directors, has said that in carrying the advert it should perhaps have entered a caveat warning the reader that he read it at his own risk!
However, even if ThisDay's wrap-around was not anonymous, I still think the newspaper was wrong professionally and ethically to have carried it. The fact that others do it abroad does not necessarily make it right.
I cannot presume to lecture Obaigbena and his editors on how to run a successful newspaper. As I said last week I tried running a private newsmagazine, Citizen, sixteen years ago and failed after only four years on the streets. Obaigbena too once tried publishing a business newsmagazine, ThisWeek, and failed. The difference was that he was undeterred. He dusted himself up and tried again, this time with ThisDay which even his worst enemies must admit is one of the best newspapers in the country.
The newspaper company itself, I suspect, may not have been profitable and its assets and liabilities may not have been in balance, but at least its publisher and editors, if not its foot soldiers, have done well for themselves and the newspaper has hardly missed a beat since it started over 11 years ago.
However, even though I cannot presume to lecture Obaigbena and his editors on how to run a successful newspaper, I am sure they know that the real issue at the heart of the concept of wrap-around adverts is not about what is trendy. Neither is it about offering "creative solutions to marketing challenges." Rather it is about the subordination of the purpose of journalism to the profit motive and to other less than wholesome considerations.
As Obaigbena and Company know very well, this idea of wrap-around adverts is a very recent phenomenon in the history of even modern-day journalism. It has arisen because the success of newspapers abroad, under relentless concentration of their ownership especially by Big Business, has become measured more by their earnings than by the quality of their journalism.
This development can only portend ill for society. As Joseph Pulitzer, one of America's most notable newspaper publishers and the founder of the Pulitzer prize, Journalism's equivalent of the Nobel Prize, said more than a century ago, "Our Republic and its press will rise or fall togetherâ-oe A cynical, mercenary, demagogic press will produce in time a people as base as itself."
As the American press became more cynical, mercenary and demagogic with time, is it any surprise that the American people have become more cynical, mercenary and demagogic about its politics and business? Is it any surprise that fewer and fewer American citizens are participating in the country's politics as poll after poll have shown? More specifically, is it any surprise that its politicians thought nothing of lying their way - lying about non-existent weapons of mass destruction in Iraq and about Saddam Hussein's non-existent link to Al-Qaida - into dragging Americans, kicking and screaming, into a war with Iraq that may yet prove more disastrous for their country than Vietnam? If the politicians thought nothing of lying their way into such a disastrous war was it not because they knew they could rely on the media owned by their Big Business cronies and partners to act as their lapdogs instead of as society's watchdog?
The purpose of journalism is not merely reporting or writing news. Its purpose is to serve the public trust and journalists cannot serve the public trust properly if they subordinate quality journalism to the profit or any similar motive. And no matter how you look at it wrap-around adverts are a consequence of subordinating quality journalism to the profit motive.
Once upon a-not-too-distant-time, the front cover of any newspaper or newsmagazine was meant for the headlines of its stories and nothing more.
Advertisements may bring in more money than the cover price of a newspaper or a newsmagazine, but the newspaper cover was not the place for shouting an advert at the reader.
Building a large enough faithful readerships to attract sufficient adverts that would make a publication profitable takes a lot of resources in time, capital and men or women. It also takes mother luck. The easiest way for a newspaper to loose those readers in the long run is for it to mix its adverts with its editorial content. As one, Annie Gerton, said in her 2003 book Press Here!: Managing the media for free publicity, "The closer the advertising and editorial, the less credible the editorial and the less appeal it has to the discerning reader - your customer."
The chaps at ThisDay may think those of us who say that adverts should never take the front page away from news are out of date, but they know, or at least should know, that while fashions come and go, only adherence to the basic principles of an institution can keep it alive and well. And, I repeat, it is a basic principle of journalism that adverts do not take priority over news and certainly you do not camouflage views as news, which is what wrap-around adverts do.
Camouflaging views as news can only create conflicts of interests. Such conflicts are journalism's greatest enemy, as a profession that searches for and publishes the truth as much as it can establish such truths.
As the Washington Post which ThisDay quoted approvingly in its self-advert, said in its in-house Code of Conduct for its journalists, "In the pursuit of truth, the newspaper shall be prepared to make sacrifices of its material fortunes, if such course is necessary for the public good."
Post may not have always practiced what it preached as we could see from ThisDay's example of Newsweek International which Post owns, but the fact that it says it is prepared to make material sacrifices in the pursuit of truth is an acknowledgement of the fact that money, especially in journalism, should not be everything.
And while we are at it, we may as well address another innovation at ThisDay which can only create conflicts of interests. This is its innovation of organising lavish ceremonies at which it doles out awards to personalities and institutions. These awards are even worse than wrap-around adverts as a practice that can only undermine the integrity of Nigerian journalism.
Newspapers do endorse or reject candidates for political office. They also nominate men or women or institutions as the most newsworthy in a year, for good or bad. Such endorsements/rejections and nominations have, however, never been regarded universally as journalism's best practice. Certainly no newspaper has ever organised ceremonies to give out such awards, not especially in the elaborate and lavish manner in which ThisDay has gone about it in recent years.
As one Professor Layi Erinosho of Ogun State University said in a letter to the editor in The Comet of April 11, newspapers' "integrity could easily be compromised once they get in into the business of dishing out honorific awards to the so-called reputable organisations and eminent personalities."
A newspaper or a newsmagazine is not just any commodity like oil, groundnut, silver, or cassava.
Newspapers and newsmagazine are sacred trusts. They should therefore never be commoditisized as if their primary role in society is to make as much money as possible for its owners and editors rather than to serve as society's watchdog.

Original_Search_Title: Ecobank Commits More Support for PSIs
Headline: Ghana: Ecobank Commits More Support for PSIs
Body_Text:
The new managing director for Ecobank Ghana limited, Mr. Samuel Ashittey Adjei has committed to give more support to the president's special initiatives (PSI) on garment and textiles, salt, oil palm and cassava starch.
The Ecobank boss gave this assurance during a courtesy call on Hourable Kwamena Bartels, the minister for Private Sector Development and PSI. According to Mr. Ashittey Adjei, Ecobank, being a sub-regional bank is designed its products and services to support and expand trade within ECOWAS countries and business between West Africa and the world. He added that the bank had already provided substantial financial assistance for working capital and equipments to companies under the PSI- garment and textiles.
He was excited about the progress of other PSI programs and said the bank was prepared to extend more assistance to salt and oil plam industries under the PSI programs including facilitating the marketing of products within and outside the country.
Hounrable Bartels expressed gratitude for the bank's continued support for the PSIs and appealed for more funding for cassava starch projects, which have seen increased private sector interest and participation in recent times. He said Caltech, a Ghanaian-Dutch firm has already gone into the cultivation of cassava on a 5000 hectare land to process cassava starch into ethanol. He announced that four more similar projects to be executed by a Thai-Ghanaian consortium are in the offing.
On PSI salt, the minister lamented that the current capacity of producers made it impossible to deliver on international orders. He spoke of a case where the country had to lose a Cameroonian order of 2,500 metric tons of iodized and refined salt. Mr. Bartels also urged the bank to "handhold viable SMEs with assistance to transform them into global players".
Mr. Ashittey Adjei was responsive to the appeal but encouraged government to also assist SMEs with simple entrepreneurial training to prepare them for the funding they request from the banks.

Original_Search_Title: Women Learn Cassava Processing Techniques
Headline: Cameroon: Women Learn Cassava Processing Techniques
Body_Text:
Santchou (MENOUA) — A two-day provincial seminar on cassava processing, organised by SOCEC - PROB, a women cooperative bank and sponsored by the German Technical Cooperation (GTZ) ended in Santchou, Menoua Division recently.
The seminar which brought together 30 village group leaders and small projects managers was aimed at training the women on the modern methods in processing cassava. Addressing course participants at the end, Mme Kouteu Lydia National President of Société Cooperative d'Epargne et de Credit des Promotrices de Bafoussam (SOCEC - PROB) said that the training is a very vital in the fight against poverty. She thanked Mr. Heinnich Rinke, technical adviser with GTZ, for accepting to sponsor the seminar. According to Mme Kouteu Lydia, the workshop is organised to give the women both at urban and local levels the necessary techniques to be self employed.
Mr. Lazare Naloussi consultant with GTZ and Mr. Didier Fotso expert, taught the 30 women to produce garri, fufu, cakes and puff-puff, etc, how to preserve them, sell them and earn money. Mme Kouteu Lydia assured the women, especially members of common initiative groups (CIGs) that her cooperative bank SOCEC - PROB will give them loans to help them expand their businesses in cassava production and transformation. She called on course participants to practice what they have learnt at home so that their children and family members can benefit, causing a multiplier effect in the Santchou workshop.

Original_Search_Title: Rail Traffic Suspended, Fuel Shortage Hits Brazzaville
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Rail Traffic Suspended, Fuel Shortage Hits Brazzaville
Body_Text:
Brazzaville — The suspension of rail traffic serving Brazzaville, capital of the Republic of Congo (ROC), and at least two weeks of fuel shortages have caused food scarcities and disrupted travel for many of the city's one million residents.
"According to several testimonies, food products such as cassava, peanuts, beans and potatoes are getting rare since the suspension," Dieudonne Moussala, the head of the Congolese Association for Consumers, said on Monday.
"We fear that the situation might continue," he added.
A government minister last week attributed the fuel shortage to damages at the national oil refinery in November 2005. Energy Minister Tatti-Loutard also said the shortage was due to "difficulties" the government was facing in getting supplies abroad.
"Things will soon improve," he added.
The Congolese Ocean Railway Company recently suspended traffic on its PK 493 line between Brazzaville and a locality known as Simon, following flooding when the River Djoue burst its banks during heavy rains. The flooded 10-km stretch of the rail line lies on the outskirts of Brazzaville.
Traders in the city said they usually stocked food supplies like maize and peanuts for one to two weeks.
"Now it is difficult because of the situation," Henriette Elion, a trader, said.
Elion said she feared the current heavy rains could worsen the situation. Several localities along the railway line supply Brazzaville with foodstuff.
The flooding along sections of the rail line is a recurring problem. The rail company has carried out several studies on the possibility of rerouting the rail lines around the flood-prone zones. It launched such efforts in the 1980s but work was suspended due to of lack of money, an official of the rail company said.
Moreover, the suspension of rail traffic in the Pool region has also been provoked by insecurity caused by the so-called Ninja rebels once loyal to Frederic Bitsangou, alias Pasteur Ntoumi, who still roam the Pool region, a war-scarred district adjacent to Brazzaville.
Besides the rail suspensions, the fuel shortages in the city has caused a sharp increase in petrol prices and disrupted long-distance passenger travel.
Following the scarcity, owners of public transport vehicles have been paying informal vendors up to 1,000 francs CFA (US $2) per litre, up from the normal price of 400 francs CFA ($0.7). The vendors buy the fuel from local stations and resell it at profit, with the complicity of some service station agents.
"It is hard for us to arrive early in the morning at the harbour where we usually buy our products," Jacqueline Ibara, a fish vendor at a local market, said.
The fuel scarcity has forced buses to ply shorter routes and taxis to raise fares from an average of 700 francs ($1.3) to 1,000 francs ($2).
Despite being sub-Sahara Africa's fourth largest oil producers, the Republic of Congo's three million people have mostly lived with shortages. Major local retailers of petrol - X-Oil, Total-Congo and Texaco - are unable to meet consumer demands, despite supplies they receive from the National Petroleum Company.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Over the Counter Drugs And Concoctions Facilitate Illegal Abortion
Headline: Ghana: Over the Counter Drugs And Concoctions Facilitate Illegal Abortion
Body_Text:
Accra — Records at the Department of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of the Korle-Bu Teaching Hospital indicate that most of the unsafe abortion cases among adolescents were caused by the intake of high doses of over the counter drugs and concoctions.
These are high doses of paracetamol; chloroquine; ergot; coffee; grounded bottle; 'blue' used for washing; hair dye; grounded ants; cockroaches; bleach; douching with poisonous and caustic substances; insertions into the vagina or enemas with potent herbal preparations from trees and gunpowder.
Some even use trans-cervical insertions of sticks (cassava) and some will have their boyfriends jump unto the abdomen to force the foetus out.
Speaking in an interview with the Ghana News Agency, Mrs Hectorina Yebuah, Deputy Director of Nursing Services, Korle-Bu, said abortion cases were on the increase with most of the cases being incomplete, inevitable and septic, adding, "incomplete abortion cases tops them all".
She said that many women had died out of unsafe abortion while others had had severe long-term complications, which included injury to the genital tract and internal organs, chronic pelvic pain, infertility and psychological trauma.
According to her, 10 women died of unsafe abortion in 2005; seven in 2004; 12 in 2003; nine in 2002; 16 in 2001 and 10 in 2000 at the Hospital.
In Africa, more than 30,000 women die each year of unsafe abortion, four million African women continue to undergo unsafe abortion and millions are getting injured including infertility. Abortion causes about 30 per cent to 40 per cent of maternal mortality and morbidity in Africa.
These women resort to abortion either due to contraceptive failure among the married women; sexual coercion; rape; sexual abuse; out of reach of family planning, social and economic reasons.
Mrs Mercy Offei, Deputy Director of Nursing Services in Charge of Gynaecology Unit, told the GNA that as at March 2006 more than 300 abortion cases had been reported with the ages of patients ranging between 16 years and 35 years.
She explained that with the low patronage of family planning, many women especially the married women found themselves pregnant when they were not prepared "and such women some of whom due to religion, will resort to other illegal methods and end up here at the Hospital.
Mrs Offei explained that most of the abortion cases among the adolescents increased after cultural festivities where merrymaking, indiscriminate and unsafe sex among the youth were at its peak, saying that reported cases started in August.
She said those children, mostly school children, out of school children, house helps and kayayee, upon realising that they were pregnant, sought advice from their peers, who also introduced them to all sorts of concoctions to abort the pregnancy.
"These girls do not tell their parents when they are pregnant for fear of being beaten or thrown out of the house so the only option is to practice what their peers have told them ", she said.
Mrs Offei called for continuous education on the dangers associated with unsafe abortion and the use of contraceptives as a measure to curtail the menace.
"If we are able to accept the use of contraceptives to prevent unwanted pregnancies, then I believe, it will reduce cases of unsafe abortion and there will be no need to legalise abortion."

Original_Search_Title: Dodging KCC's Iron Fist
Headline: Uganda: Dodging KCC's Iron Fist
Body_Text:
Kampala — TO make a living, food vendors who cannot afford Kampala City Council (KCC) taxes sell their delicacies late in the night, away from the prying eyes of KCC officials.
Fruit, shoes and clothes vendors patiently wait for the city cleaners to clean up the city. They are dodging and running away from the iron hand of KCC law enforcers. Thursday, 9:45pm found night vendors and I along Namirembe Road. As shops closed and human traffic thinned, I requested a Muzamilu Mukwasi, one of the night-time public phone operators to let me borrow his stool for while.
I watched as women food vendors dressed in dull-coloured clothes with head wraps, carrying black polythene bags containing charcoal stoves and charcoal arrived one at a time. Then followed wheelbarrow pushers transporting their heavy food-laden saucepans. Not to be apprehended by KCC law enforcers, the saucepans were left hidden in a dark corridor. The vendors kept pacing from one end of the street to the other as they waited for the coast to clear.
Mukwasi is a loyal customer of Annet Namubiru, a food vendor. He said food vendors station themselves along the dark stretch opposite Nabugabo Road, between Mukwano shopping complex and Caltex fuel station. This point acts as both the kitchen and dining area.
"At that point it's not easy to identify who is eating as most diners would rather not be identified," Mukwasi said.
Actually, I came to appreciate his comment. No sooner had I taken a photograph of him getting food from Namubiru, than a group of drunk street kids and other diners with the help of a security guard from Saracen opened my camera and yanked out the film. What really terrified me were the handcuffs that the guard got from his pocket and placed on my wrists threatening to take me to the police station.
Unlike other vendors that spread their stock for sale as early as 9:30pm, food is not served immediately for fear of losing it to impromptu check by KCC men who could be lurking around, dressed in plain clothes.
Mukwasi said more than once, tea and food vendors have lost their merchandise to enforcers pretending to be buyers. So the food isn't served until 10:00pm.
To show that they are ready to serve, benches are arranged in a semicircle, facing the vendor. The food includes, matooke, posho, spaghetti, yams, rice, cassava, potatoes, millet bread, served with beans, groundnuts, beef, peas, fish, eggplants and cabbage.
Mukwasi explains that most of the diners are bus and special hire drivers, boda boda cyclists, streets kids, night builders, bachelors, policemen and traders who come from up-country to do their shopping.
"With the exception of street kids that are served on plastic plates for fear of stealing the breakable plates, other diners eat from breakables while using forks, an added attraction," said Mukwasi.
John Okwinyo, a night-watchman, who said food vending has been on for more than 10 years, added that these women do not leave until the wee hours of the morning when all their food has been sold. They also prepare breakfast for the early risers.
"When it gets very late, they reduce the price of food from sh1,000 to sh500 per plate," said Okwinyo.
Although it is not acceptable to have such business on the verandahs, Okwinyo said they let the vendors operate because most of them cannot afford to rent space in food vending markets.
"We sympathise with them and allow them to use the verandah because they have to earn a living. However, we also know that some operate from St. Balikuddembe, Shauri Yako and Nakasero markets during the day but only come here to sell what was left over to avoid losses," he said.
Most of the vendors reside in the city suburbs of Katwe, Kisenyi, Kibuye and Mengo.
Edison Kiwawulo, another customer, revealed that many retiring single shop attendants and Muslims from Isha prayers also eat from these vendors.
"So other than going home to start cooking in the night, these people prefer to eat here and just go home to sleep," Kiwawulo added.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Remain High
Headline: Uganda: Food Prices Remain High
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE prices of foodstuffs and basic commodities have remained high, The New Vision weekly survey, has indicated.
In Kampala's major markets of Nakawa, Nakasero, St. Balikuddembe,
Nateete and Kalerwe, the price of a bar of soap has remained at between
sh800 and sh1,000.
Maize flour is still at between sh800 and sh1,200 while cassava flour was at sh600 per kilo, the same as last week.
A kilo of salt has also remained at sh500 and groundnuts and simsim
go for between sh1,800 and sh2,000, unchanged from last week's rates.
Beans is at between sh1,100 and sh1,500 and sugar at sh1,500 a kilo,
the same as last week.

Original_Search_Title: As He Steps Inside
Headline: Nigeria: As He Steps Inside
Body_Text:
Lagos — Geniuses, crackpots, dictators and even men of piety have at different times worked relentlessly and may be, selflessly to actualise their phantom world of utopia, that delusion of human perfectibility, which Saint Augustine conceived as the City of God.
From the baroque designs of the Renaissance to the replica of the sexy robot in Fritz Lang's Metropolis, the story is the same. Dissecting the lives of these famous men and women in pursuit of an idyllic land presents us with two principal characters, one the familiar terrain of strong presidents harping on noble issues and two, the typical artistic Joe DiMaggio, who fits into Ervin Gaufman's sociological piece of man and his Managenent of Impressions. We shall revisit soon, Richard Ben Cramer's real life character epitomised in DiMaggio: The Hero's Life. But suffice it to say that Boy Joe was America's, not a Nigerian creature of the outward and profane elevated to high art, the ideal macho character out of the dreamland-Hol lywood.
Still on the men and women whose very lives - past and present - presented the world with alternative view points, the recurring decimal has been a biography of self conceived, worthy triumphs and private pains. Imagine Napoleon Bonaparte wanting to conquer the world. Weigh it against Isaac Newton's foray into Physics and Metaphysics, a life- time project that delivered the basic principles of gravity and levitation (anti-gravity) with the accompanying revolutionary accomplishments in science and engineering. Each was convinced the project on ground was the best for mankind and it was pursued with a cosmic timetable that brought a revolution that moved the world forward, for good or bad. And each for different reasons was admitted into man's Hall of Fame.
While Bonaparte was privileged to reminisce on the vanity or otherwise of presiding over regular meetings where he is President of the United States of the World (USW), assigning ambassadors to another extra-terrestrial planet and signing cheques to complete scientific projects that would involve another great battle in planet Mars, Newton stayed away from the crowd for hours and days without food, toiling to advance the cause of mankind. At one time his dog ate his lunch and gracefully left the remnants for his master who was too happy to celebrate his dinner on those leftovers. In the same dog life scenario, Newton lost a lifetime research when the pet stepped on a lantern that burnt his research materials. This monumental mishap not withstanding, the friendship continued still on a happy note. That vacuum created by a doggie way of life did however come with its blessings, clearing the intellectual block and before long the coast expanded and behold the new impression call ed gravity emerged with all the clarity needed for its creative application. Does that draw some leadership notes to our own heroes on what constitutes the true values of a leader?
Our Nigerian leaders are not different from their counterparts the world over. The pursuit of power by other means is for them, life itself. When President Obasanjo stepped out of power he wanted that same power on the platform of the United Nations as Secretary General. Since ex-President Babangida stepped aside from the warm protection of the cocoon, that opium of power called Aso Rock, he has been giving us notice of his desire to return to the premises as a tenant for perhaps another 12 years of corrective governance, whereas the body language of the current occupant, suggests he wants to buy up the property for posterity. Buhari who was there for one brief moment wants to make a comeback. Neither Ernest Shonekan nor Yakubu Gowon, all men of letters believes the corridor of power is attainable in their life- time again. Gowon tried the idea once and flopped like a pack of cards. Does his inability to assert his consciousness on the power matrix have anything to do with hi s long sojourn into the intellectual realm, carrying plates like any other student to be served lunch? Some say no learned man or graduate has been privileged to rule this country while Chief Ojo Maduekwe in his public dissertations postulates that only those with military background have the birthright to rule this country.
So, the power calculation for 2007 boils down to an unpresidented power calculus, a prime time battle of the military titans, and the heroes of our recent past. At one end is OBJ who is building a start up factory for risky but rewarding ideas. At the other end are the opponents who in the past had remained silent but ready to draw blood from a soundless stone.
From these characters will emerge the pot pourri images of Saint Augustine, the Newton incarnates and our own inimitable Joe DiMaggio.
Back to the monumentally vain and cheap Joe who according to Cramer understood the power of silence, that the less the principal character gave of himself, standing remote and noble and regally aloof, the more the world took it as evidence of dignity.
The off diamond DiMaggio, according to Cramer, was a jerk, high school drop out whose favourite reading material was Superman comics, a lousy father and wife beater who was reluctant to fight in the First World War.
But here was the hero of America. What made him a hero? Handsome suits, silvery hairs and sculpted features. These were attributes that had nothing to do with character but elevated to high art enough to make him a hero. And this was eloquent testimony of the American value than what any one thought of vain Joe.
Our polity has made more DiMaggios out of our leaders and less of the St Augustine persona. And our leaders prefer the outward, self-serving showmanship of a Dimaggio than a foray into the intellect, a buffeting of the cerebral man which disciplines the mind and tames ambition, the Newtonian dialogue with nature. As a result, these leaders have learned to squeeze every drop of privilege that comes their way to satisfy us. Thus by extension they mirror the way we are, the values we have evolved out of our collateral consciousness. And what we consider our cultural heritages are monumentally cheap values, embodied in the Joe DiMaggio mystique.
Babangida after stepping aside from the column of societal conscience, has learnt some lessons, no doubt, that in spite of his milk of human kindness and concern for the good and prosperity of his neighbour, the collective good of society supersedes every thing else. These are lessons he must imbibe as he makes his calculations of stepping in to continue his march to national awareness. The question however is: "Have we learnt anything?" The mirror reflects our images. And if Obasanjo is the mirror image of military discipline, does he tell us what any military man in governance would want to make of the rest of us, as fools and bloody civilians who can only bark and not bite? Babangida at least listened to the voice of reason and stepped aside, while our President has remained regally aloof, in the mould of Joe DiMaggio, exerting the power of silence while Rome is burning. Is this what the country deserves at this time? Up until last December, at Christmas to be precise, Pre sident Babangida was playing the devil's advocate, defending the incumbent, understandably in the tradition of the rank and file of the military, sorting themselves out in house, protected by the famous esprit de corps. It is unlikely he would do same today given the fact that the third term agenda has gone beyond night fall to dawn and would soon be actualised as a full blown project if left unchecked.
For we the citizens who watch those who have appropriated the commonwealth of the republic to their privileged constituency, the message is clear, we need less of the sociological piece in the character of DiMaggio.
We instead want more of such characters manifest in strong presidents harping on the noble issues of carrying the fatherland to the long awaited land of promise. The mythical utopia reduced to the earthly Promised Land of rice and cassava, beyond the self- serving project of third term. This is the lesson as we step inside.

Original_Search_Title: Even Suave Pastor is No Match for Mayor
Headline: Uganda: Even Suave Pastor is No Match for Mayor
Body_Text:
Nairobi — There are very few pastors quite like Peter Ssematimba. A boyish-looking Kampala businessman with his own radio station and production company, Peter is one of the reasons FM stations are so popular in Uganda.
He spent more than 10 years living on the US west coast, and word from there is that he came that close to becoming a doctor, before packing his bags and heading home in the mid 1990s, just when FM radio was about to become more than a fact of life. His fresh and bold ideas, not to mention his American work-ethic generated wonders at Capital Radio and CBS, two of Uganda's biggest FM stations.
He soon quit one after the other to strike out on his own in a very crowded and competitive market. Few expected him to take the fight to the big boys and survive. He has not survived; but thrived beyond his wildest dreams.
About a year or two ago, Peter claims to have "seen the light" and renounced his old ways (he is a dapper dresser best known for a perm in his hair, a diamond pin in his earlobe and the Japanese cologne Issey Miyake on his clothes).
It's not known how many lost souls Pastor Ssematimba has rescued; but he pretty is serious at least about speaking on his salvation. Last October, the pastor suddenly announced that he would contest to become Mayor of Kampala, replacing the economist and losing presidential candidate John Ssebaana Kizito.
Many thought Ssematimba was joking, until he unleashed a series of serious and cleverly produced public service announcements, courted Kampalans with his fresh ideas (not to mention his very clear American-accented English) and surprised all with a sober and concerted campaign.
In the end, the March 3 election was won by former mayor and big-time populist Al-Hajj Nasser Ntege Sebaggala with 53 per cent (or 164,498 votes) to Pastor Ssematimba's 40 per cent (or 125,625 votes).
Four other candidates shared the remaining 6.5 per cent (or 20,532 votes). It's anyone's guess what could have been had the field been less crowded.
What's undeniable is the level of support the young man seems to have received. Indeed, it's been said that Ssematimba ran the cleanest and most professional of the thousands of recent campaigns for political office right from that of President of Uganda, which was one by Yoweri K. Museveni -again.
The incoming mayor certainly took his young opponent very seriously and used his own very colourful lifestyle to try to match up.
Sebaggala, popularly known as Seya (Luganda slang for "one of us"), was famously elected in 1998 by riding a wave of disaffection with the moneyed and connected elite who mercilessly jibed him for his modest education and shaky command of the Queen's language.
A cunning and successful businessman who thrived even during the dark days of Idi Amin in 1970s, Sebaggala convinced enough people - derisively called "lumpens" by some - to latch their star to his rocket for a stunning and well-deserved victory over three very highly regarded opponents. They included Christopher Iga, then incumbent mayor and member of the ruling Movement.
Sebaggala was, however, soon in trouble with the long arm of the law. He was arrested at the airport in New York in June 1998 and charged with dealing in stolen travellers' cheques. He was sentenced to 15 months' imprisonment and, even as his supporters claimed that he had been set up by the Uganda government, it was hard to see how someone could recover from such a sucker punch, especially one delivered in broad daylight. But Seya recovered.
He served his time and was freed in 2000, returning home to one of the most feverish welcome parties ever staged at Entebbe International Airport. To the thousands of his supporters, Sebaggala was not a common thief as some Members of Parliament had dubbed him, but a latter-day Robin - or is it Robbing? - Hood, who stole from the rich (Americans) to give to the poor (Ugandans).
Sebaggala was also saved because his crime, like all white collar crimes, is by nature highly technical and difficult to explain to a lay person. Plus he was never actually in jail incarcerated inside Uganda.
After making a go at the presidency and dropping out almost a week into the campaigns earlier this year, Sebaggala concentrated on Kampala, the city that has embraced him like no other place. This is the man Pastor Peter Ssematimba came up against.
And while the good man of the cloth put a brave face on it, his was a good fight that ultimately came up short. In a recent interview, Ssematimba declared, quite correctly, that Kampala City Council needs a manager, not a politician.
"I looked at the potential that the city has; [which] potential has not been exploited," Ssematimba told Sunday Monitor last week. "For the kind of fulfilment that we are looking for, we need leaders, solid managers and that is what we are lacking in KCC [Kampala City Council]. I contested so that I could offer leadership and management to this city.
We don't need politics but solid management and leadership."
Ultimately though, the KCC mayorship went to Sebaggala mostly because of politics. Kampala is a hotbed of opposition.
President Museveni has not won many friends in the city and the recent parliamentary elections saw only one of his supporters returned as MP.
The president doesn't help matters when he incessantly attacks the city council for all the problems in Kampala - pot-holed and congested roads, illegal construction in wetlands, garbage dust, etc.
The real crisis of Kampala is that it is a ramshackle something masquerading as a modern capital city, with slums right in the metropolis, too many vehicles on too few roads, less than 10 sets of functional traffic lights, and a most archaic sewerage system (designed for 200,000 Asians who favoured a rice diet) and now serving more than 1.5 million natives who love their ugali and cassava.
Those problems were not created by KCC. But the city council has exacerbated them over the years by allowing fly-by-the-night construction, corruption and having no plan either for now or the future.
The government has finally invoked these shenanigans in a plan to significantly trim the mayor's powers and take over many of his functions - something it failed to achieve at the ballot box. The running of the city will not get any easier of course, either today or 10 years in the future. The unsound template on which Kampala as a city is built simply can't allow that.
But in Kampala, you will always find bright lights like that of Pastor Peter Ssematimba, who will no doubt continue to thrive in music and evangelism. Besides him will always be people like Sebaggala, the wily man-on-the-street with native cunning.
Their Kampala will, however, be a city in name only.

Original_Search_Title: NDE Targets 7 Million Jobs By 2007
Headline: Nigeria: NDE Targets 7 Million Jobs By 2007
Body_Text:
The National Directorate of Employment (NDE), has set a target of creating seven million jobs by next year, its Director-General, Engineer S.O. Adelodun, has disclosed.
He said yesterday at the opening ceremony of the third joint NDE and National Export-Import Bank (NEXIM) training programme on Start-Your-Own-Business (SYOB) for ex-corps members in Kaduna that over 450 ex-Youth Servicemen will benefit from the programme.
Engr. Adelodun, said that the unemployment situation has worsened over the years as a recent survey showed that out of about 130,000 graduates coming out of tertiary institu-tions, only about ten percent secured wage employment.
In the address read by NDE's Director of Small-Scale Enter-prise (SSE), Engr. Nnamdi Odoh, the DG said the NDE has been able through its core prog-rammes to provide employment in the formal sector of the economy.
He said the SYOP programme training is being conducted in all the six geo-political zones to give as many ex-corps members from all over the country to become entrepreneurs.
Engr. Adelodun said these opportunities are provided through the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS) and includes the Presidential Initiative on Rice Production and Processing, fish culture and cassava production, adding that NDE is poised to explore non-oil sector for the teeming unemployed.
Managing Director NEXIM Bank, Alhaji Baba Y. Ahmed, said the bank has provided N39 billion as credit support to over 300 exporters since 1991 and assisted Nigerian exporters to generate an average of US$50 billion annually.
He said in the speech read by NEXIM Executive Director Operations, Engr. Edet J. Ekere that NEXIM's initiative with NDE is to address the twin issues of export development and unemployment, tapping the capacity of the NDE in the area of manpower and employment generation.
"Specifically, the bank's support is targeted at providing training and financial support for export-oriented entrepreneurs," he added.
Governor of Kaduna State, Alhaji Ahmed Mohammed Makarfi, who was represented by his commissioner of commerce, Mr. Mikaya Lekyap, said the Kaduna state government will continue to support NDE in training entrepreneurs to reduce the level of unemployment in the state.
He urged ex-corpers wo are yet to register to do so without further delay.

Original_Search_Title: Chief Chisunka Villagers Turn Bush Into Toilet
Headline: Zambia: Chief Chisunka Villagers Turn Bush Into Toilet
Body_Text:
Monze — SOME people in chief Chisunka's area in Mansa have turned toilets
into storerooms for charcoal and cassava and are instead answering the
call of nature in the bush.
And chief Chisunka said friendship based development would create
tension among chiefs and their subjects in Luapula Province. Confirming
the development in an interview, chief Chisunka of the Aushi people of
Mansa district said it was disappointing that some of his subjects had
resorted to animal behaviour where they prefer the bush to VIP toilets
when answering the call of nature. He said some of the 268 VIP toilets
built using part of the K900 million from Zambeef had their roof
sheets, doorframes
vent pipes and doors removed and sold by some of his subjects. "So I
have already apprehended five people in connection with this scam.
Instead of using the toilets for the intended purpose, they have turned
them into storerooms for cassava and charcoal. Some have removed the
roofing sheets. I will not tolerate this. If they don't bring the
sheets, doors, vent pipes and doorframes before I go back, I will
report them to the police for possible prosecution or arrests," chief
Chisunka said.
He said it was saddening that even a headman called Fuya of Konga
village was involved in such activities. And commenting on senior chief
Puta's statement that he had reconciled with President Mwanawasa
because he had promised him to tar the road leading to his palace,
chief Chisunka said many chiefs in the province were not happy with
President Mwanawasa's friendship-based development approach.
"People will start creating personal friendships with the President
for personal interests. Now what about some of us who have no access to
the President to see him? What is he going to do for us since his
calendar is always loaded from January 1 to December 31?" asked chief
Chisunka.

Original_Search_Title: Funding Ethiopia's Growth
Headline: Nigeria: Funding Ethiopia's Growth
Body_Text:
NIGERIA university teachers are in great demand in Ethiopia and
President Olusegun Obasanjo has promised to supply them as requested by
Yohannes Guirida Ginbi, the out-going Ethiopian ambassador to Nigeria.
At a time of high unemployment, even among the highly educated, the
request for 671 Nigerian lecturers would have been appreciated in all
quarters, it would have meant that more Nigerians would be employed and
they could repatriate some of their earnings home. The bitter truth is
far from this. Nigeria would pay for the lecturers and it is a lot of
money.
President Obasanjo has promised to dispatch the lecturers, some of
whom are experts in cassava production, to meet the need of the
Ethiopians, under the Technical Aid Corps, TAC, a 1987 scheme that was
promoted under the "concert of medium powers".
The Federal Government pays TAC members between $700 and $800
monthly, which at the official rate convert to N92,400 and N105,000. It
is instructive our country's minimum monthly wage is N7,500.
Nigeria would spend about N1.69 billion on the 671 lecturers in the
two years that is the usual tenure of the TAC scheme. Other African
countries benefit from the programme which means that the expenditure
would be much higher.
We consider this a waste of resources that could have been channeled
towards more developments at home. TAC has been used in the past to
provide human resources to needy African countries, but it has been
riddled lately with allegations that the allowances arrive late and
that officials back home see that they trade with the money approved
for the scheme.
Other issues standing against the gesture to Ethiopia include the
state of the national purse. Was the Ethiopian request that was made
and granted on the spot part of the 2006 budget? Will the National
Assembly be silent on this too and lament later about the President's
lack of respect for the budget? When would budgetary discipline start?
There is also the effect of this official brain drain policy on our
own higher institutions. Does the country have enough qualified
university lecturers to warrant sending 671 of them to Ethiopia alone?
The National Universities Commission, NUC, weeks ago declared that less
than 45 per cent of those teaching in the universities were qualified.
The NUC followed this declaration with a regulation that only Ph.d
holders would be qualified to lecture in the universities in the next
three years.
If this is the situation in the nation's universities, should the
government be depleting the human capital in the universities by
sending the few qualified ones abroad?
Apart from the damage that the TAC policy could do to the public
universities, the Federal Government is pursuing another initiative of
licensing more private universities which are better funded and are
poaching staff from public institutions.
The entire TAC policy is overdue for review as it is in conflict with our current national interests.

Original_Search_Title: Saru Chief Laments Over Plight
Headline: Ghana: Saru Chief Laments Over Plight
Body_Text:
The Chief of Saru in a remote community, sharing border with the
Ivory Coast in Sawla-Tuna-Kalba District of the Northern Region, who
was captured and molested by Ivorian rebels said he had been sick ever
since the rebel's abuse.
Saruwura Seidu Seidu, who was recounting his ordeal to the Northern
Regional Minister at Saru, when the minister visited the community
said, "I have never recovered from the trauma and ordeal I passed
through in the hands of the rebels. I am old. I cannot walk properly,
so the rebels dragged me on the ground until we got to their camp".
The minister was on a 7-day tour of the western corridor districts
of the Northern Region, comprising Bole-Bamboi, Sawla-Tuna-Kalba, West
Gonja and Central Gonja districts.
The purpose of the visits was to enable the minister to know the
situation on the ground at the rural areas, see how best he could help
solve them and to explain government development policies to the
people. Key among the minister's message to the people in all the
communities is peace, the need for the Representation of the People's
Amendment Law (ROPAL), and the importance of the NHIS.
The chief said "I have always been praying to God to forgive them
because my children are older than them. The only thing I need is a
clinic in the community and tarring of the road from Kalba to Saru to
enable security men to come and guard us".
Saruwura Seidu Seidu said what had happened to him could generate
cross-border conflict in future, if the problem was not solved amicably
and called on government to intensify security on the country's
borders. "The rebels have not regretted from their act, since they
still cross to the community to trade with the people without coming to
apologize to me" he said.
He said ever since Dr. Kwame Addo Kufuor, Defence Minister visited
the community with some soldiers after his capture and release, fear
had gripped the rebels and that they were no more crossing to Saru with
weapons.
"They fear our soldiers.
Any time Ghanaian soldiers come to the community none of the rebels
would enter Saru market even though that was where they depend so much
for their needs. He commended the minister for visiting the community
and appealed to him to use his position to help the community with a
clinic and to construct their road to link them with the rest of the
country, since they had been cut-off from Ghana.
Alhaji Boniface consoled the chief, saying, "I am touched by your
pathetic story and I will do my best to see how fast government can
award contract on your road since there is abundance of food in your
community". Saru is a small community about 10 miles away from Kalba
and the people are engaged in yam, groundnuts, cassava and maize
production but cannot transport them to market centres in Ghana for
sale due to unmotorable roads.

Original_Search_Title: Kribi, a Small Paradise for Tourists And Aids
Headline: Cameroon: Kribi, a Small Paradise for Tourists And Aids
Body_Text:
Kribi — The inhabitants of Kribi, a well-known sea resort on the Gulf of
Guinea, were hoping that an oil terminal linking Chad to Cameroon would
solve all their problems of unemployment, poverty and isolation. But
three years after construction began, little has changed.
Between June 2001 and July 2003, with the financial support of the
World Bank, 1,070 km of pipeline was built linking the oil fields of
Doba in southern Chad to the oil tankers stationed outside the bay of
Kribi. The sea terminal, with a capacity to handle 225,000 barrels of
oil per day, was inaugurated in October 2003.
The economic boom experienced by Kribi has, however, heightened the HIV/AIDS risk.
According to Cameroonian activists, members of the consortium
responsible for the extraction and transport of the oil, had sensitised
their employees with HIV/AIDS education, but neglected to extend it to
the local population.
"The pipeline! It caused great damage and brought us misfortune
rather than happiness," declared Louis NziÃ© Owona, a professor of
accountancy at the Confessional College of Saint Joseph in Kribi, who
also runs the college's AIDS committee.
"Large numbers of girls came from all over the country to follow the
progress of construction, offering sexual services to the work force.
They waited around every corner for them to spend their salaries on
sexual favours," he added.
Valery Nodem, Coordinator of local NGO "Reseau de Lutte Contre la
Faim" (RELUFA), told PlusNews: "With a project of this magnitude, we
were expecting a campaign to prepare locals for such large influx of
foreign workers, most of whom were better paid and generally single."
According to Nodem, Kribi's population has increased tenfold in a
relative short period of time, with many houses transformed into
brothels.
TOURISM: SOLE SOURCE OF INCOME
But contrary to studies undertaken in Chad - which showed a link
between the development of the pipeline and the spread of HIV - no
statistical data can confirm what most health experts were dreading: an
increase of the HIV/AIDS epidemic, particularly among young women.
The small market town and its 50,000 residents are heavily dependent
on the tourism industry, which attracts expatriates, wealthy
Cameroonians from the capital and other large cities.
At dusk, motorcycles zigzag to and fro between nightclubs and
hotels, carrying young girls in search of money or their soul mates. It
is in these chaotic streets, alongside women cooking mackerel and
cassava, where casual relationships are negotiated, observed Martha, a
chambermaid in one of the numerous seaside hotels.
"Girls like the easy life, one day it's one guy, the next it's
another one, all they're after is a quick buck ... it sometimes works,
especially in the hotels," explained Martha who also admitted to being
unhappy with the US $73 dollars she gets monthly for cleaning rooms.
"A woman cannot live without a friend, and while struggling to find
money, one can end up doing silly things ... I don't always use
condoms, it depends if the man happens to carry one on him when we do
it, which is not always the case," she added.
FEW COME FORWARD FOR TESTING
Martha is not alone: very few people in the small market town have
come forward to get tested, and the hospital has the only testing
facility.
In Cameroon a state subsidized test costs US $1.8 dollars, expensive
for most people, although the health ministry regularly sends
technicians across the provinces to offer free testing.
In 2005, Kribi's district hospital was able to test 1,731 people,
compared to 274 in the previous year. Despite the fact that the
infection rate declined from 13 percent in 2004 to 9 percent in 2005,
figures are considerably higher than the national average of 5.5
percent.
This is hardly surprising when "no public financing has been made
available for the 20 or so local committees put in place by the
government's National Committee for the Fight Against AIDS since 2004,"
Professor NziÃ© Owona noted.
He added: "In 2003, I received 500,000 francs [US $900] in order to
purchase information material as well as to train our peer-educators
and to acquire condoms for the pupils. In 2004 I was given another 250
000 francs [US $450 dollars] but I have received nothing ever since."
Nzie Owona acknowledged that countering sexual tourism was difficult
when there was so much poverty. "If a girl earns 10 000 francs [US $18
dollars] with a tourist, what kind of job will she want to do after?"
Joseph, a family man who cleans rooms in a small hotel in the city
centre, said that girls engaging in sex work were helping their
parents, who were struggling to make ends meet "because of inflation
caused by the arrival of the Oil Consortium".
Kribi is surrounded by infertile land making it hard to cultivate.
Consequently all products are shipped from other parts of the country
making basic daily necessities more expensive, even before the
inflationary impact of foreign oil workers and tourists.
"Everything is expensive here. The solution is to cultivate our own
food but to do so you need to get far out of the city, and that
requires time, effort and resources. Nowadays the teenagers want an
easy life, the one the tourists have," explained Joseph, whose 14-year
old daughter just gave birth a few months ago.
"We know we live in a tourist town. I told her school should be like
her first husband, but she does not listen. She knows she will find
foreign tourists in the nightclubs and no work in Kribi. The oil is not
helping. We only see it from afar and then we see it leave."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Remains Stable At 6.2%
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Remains Stable At 6.2%
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE annual headline inflation has remained stable at 6.2% for the
year ending February 2006, the same as last month, Uganda Bureau of
Statistics' (UBOS) monthly Consumer Price Index (CPI) has shown.
Matthew Ssewanyana, UBOS' director of macro-economic statistics,
said the rate had remained stable because of a slight change in
commodity prices.
"Though on average, the prices in February were slightly higher than
those in January, the price movements between February 2006 and
February 2005 were at the same level with those between January 2006
and January 2005," Ssewanyana said while releasing the CPI at the
Statistics House in Kampala.
He said food prices went up by 0.2%, with matooke prices going down
in all centres except Gulu while other staple foods like sweet potatoes
and cassava registered substantial increases in all centres.
Fruits, vegetables and milk also registered increases. Underlying
inflation went down to 4.8% compared to 5.6% in the year ending January
2006.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Assists Ethiopia With 671 University Lecturers
Headline: Ethiopia: Nigeria Assists Ethiopia With 671 University Lecturers
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has agreed to consider the request by
Ethiopia for 671 university teachers in addition to the 31 already sent
from Nigeria to enable that country cope with the new universities
being opened.
The President who stated this when he received the out going
Ethiopian Ambassador, Mr. Yohannes Guinda Ginbi on 28 February at the
State House, Abuja said the request would be considered under the
Technical Aid Corps (TAC) scheme in addition to sending some experts on
cassava.
He described the bilateral relations between both countries as
"very, very good," noting that "Nigeria is passing through an
interesting reformist period which we believe we need'" adding that
"the results are encouraging".
"All of us in Africa need critical self-analysis if we are to get to where we should be" He stressed.
The President said trade between Nigeria and Ethiopia must be
encouraged as Nigeria would consider buying Ethiopian meat and leather
products in exchange for Nigerian products in the spirit of reciprocity.
Responding, Mr. Ginbi said that he had learnt a lot from Nigeria's diversity and complexity during his two-year stay.
President Obasanjo also bade farewell to the outgoing Ambassador of
Cote d' Ivoire, Mr. Emille M' Iingui Keffa after nine years of
diplomatic tour in Nigeria.
The President sad that the events in Cote d' Ivoire were being
closely watched while urging Mr. Keffa to continue to render service to
Africa in his subsequent endeavours.
Mr. Keffa described his period in Nigeria as "enriching and
rewarding", noting that the spirit of reconciliation and
reconstruction, which he imbibed from Nigeria, would be useful in Cote
d' Ivoire.
He thanked President Obasanjo, the government and people of Nigeria for working towards peace in his country.

Original_Search_Title: FG to Help Ethiopia With Varsity Lecturers
Headline: Nigeria: FG to Help Ethiopia With Varsity Lecturers
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has agreed to a request made by Ethiopia, to aid it by sending 671 university lecturers to teach in its universities.
Already, there are about 31 Nigerians already teaching in Ethiopia.
Obasanjo, who said the request would be considered by the Federal Government, however said the lecturers would be sent to Ethiopia under the Technical Aid Corps (TAC) Scheme, in addition to Nigeria sending more experts on cassava.
Receiving the out-going Ethiopian Ambassador to Nigeria, Mr Yohannes Guinda Ginbi yesterday at the State House, Obasanjo said like Nigeria, all other countries in the African continent need to do some soul-searching.
While describing relations between both countries as "very, very good," he said "Nigeria was passing through an interesting reformist period, which we believe we need. All of us in Africa need critical self-analysis if we are to get to where we should."
He emphasised the need to encourage trade relations between both countries, adding that Nigeria was considering buying Ethiopian meat and leather products, while Nigeria will export spirit-related products to the country.
In his remarks, Ginbi said the request for additional Nigerian graduate teachers was to enable his country cope with the new universities being opened, saying he has learnt a lot from the country's diversity and complexity during his two-year posting. Obasanjo, who also yesterday received the out-going envoy of Cote d'Ivoire, Mr Emile M'Iingui Keffa, said political events in the strive- torn French-speaking West African country are being closely monitored. He, however, urged the diplomat to continue to render service to the continent in future, and describe his nine years diplomatic tour of the country as eventful.
Speaking earlier, Keffa described his stay in Nigeria as enriching and rewarding, saying the virtue of reconciliation and reconstruction that he imbibed here would be applied in his country,and thanked government and Nigerians for working to achieve peace in Cote d'Ivoire.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Destroy 200 Houses in Kunhinga
Headline: Angola: Rains Destroy 200 Houses in Kunhinga
Body_Text:
Kuito — Two hundred houses were destroyed this month at Kunhinga municipality, 30 kilometres north of the central BiÃ© Province, due to the recent heavy rains befalling that area, ANGOP has learnt.
According to Kunhinga administrator, Israel ValÃ©rio, large part of the destroyed houses belong to disabled people, built under the project "Directed Self-Construction", of the local department of Welfare and Social Reintegration and some schools built with un-burnt sun-dried brick of primary and secondary schools.
The source informed that the torrential rains devastated equally fields of maize, beans, cassava and vegetables.
The government official stated that the situation is serious due to the fact that the Ministry of Welfare and Social Reintegration, humanitarian agencies and non-governmental organisations have not yet supported the affected population with zinc sheets and first aid needs.

Original_Search_Title: Taking On Healthy Circumcision
Headline: Uganda: Taking On Healthy Circumcision
Body_Text:
David Mafabi is bare-chested with sweat dropping from the beads
hanging around his chest and neck. He looks tired but fearless at the
same time, he has to make it at all costs. It is time for the cultural
initiation of circumcision (Imbalu) that proves that he has grown up
and is a man.
A large excited crowd is chanting and encouraging him to move on and
face the challenges of a man. But Mafabi throws the crowd in silence
for a moment as he stops at the doorway and rolling his eyes, looks
around the crowd as if looking for path to run away from reality.
In a flash, his father appears from the crowd with a goat and gives
it to him. He then shouts "Masaba oyee!" and the crowd replies in
excitement, "oyee."
Mafabi jumps bravely with a sharp knife to the circumcision spot
while the crowd watches curiously to ensure that he doesn't take any
backward step or show any cowardice during such an important ceremony.
On arrival at the spot, the traditional surgeon called Umukyebi and
his assistant are anxious to accomplish the job. The assistant measures
and marks the part to be cut off. The Umukyebi with the help of the
assistant cuts the foreskin off Mafabi's penis.
An onlooker would be surprised for Mafabi didn't show that he felt
pain of any sort. Immediately after the surgery, he went to Buhugu
health centre for a tetanus shot to protect himself from the threat of
the deadly infection. Traditionally however, Mafabi's circumcision
wouldn't have been considered a real Gisu circumcision.
Change from the past
According to an elder Stephen Mayende, 87, of Bushigai sub-county in
Manafa district, the Imbalu tradition has undergone many swift changes
in the recent decades due to the new challenges in the society.
Mayende says that in reality, the Bagisu men circumcised these days
are not truly Basani (a man who is rightly circumcised) but Basinde (a
man who is uncircumcised).
He argues that everything intended in the Imbalu tradition is
actually the opposite today. "In the past, before a man would get
circumcised, he would walk in processions and also dance the Kadodi
dance through the villages for weeks. In those processions, the boy met
all his relatives and collected gifts," Mayende recalls.
He insists that the main reason for making these boys move long
distances visiting different relatives was to strengthen the
relationship with family friends that would help him in future.
On the circumcision day, the boy performs a lot of traditional
ceremonies where he gets blessings and is also presented to the spirits
that will protect him in his new life. On the same day, herbs called
Ityanyi are administered and they are said to give the person to be
circumcised the courage to desire to be circumcised. The herbs are
believed to very strong to the extent that if the candidate isn't
circumcised by anyone, he can do it himself.
Then cassava flour and yeast are smeared on his head as a form of
decoration and identification. During the circumcision, Mayende says, a
very blunt knife would be used in order to inflict enough pain on the
boy since the main reason for circumcision is to prove to the society
that you can stand problems in future.
"Sometimes, a knife was first put on fire until it was red hot then
used to cut," says Mayende. "It was so painful but you wouldn't say no.
Any cowardice prompted a beating and all the gifts given to you would
be taken. This was and is still seen as a shame to the clan and the
parents of the 'candidates' for circumcision," he adds.
Customarily, one knife is used to circumcise all candidates in the
same locality. Local herbs called Nandwasi cure the wound and the
ceremonies go on for some days until the boy heals, which in the past
was tested by him having intercourse with a girl of his choice.
Pockets of conflict
Certainly, Imbalu cultural practices have changed due to the
numerous challenges in the world among which is HIV/Aids. But the old
generation criticise these changes, which they say have led to moral
decay among the young Bagisu generation.
Fred Nangwali of Bunamubi in Manafa District says the existing
problems are a result of the new system. "We should go back to the old
system because nowadays, children don't have morals. You can even see
the way they behave in the community," he says, He reasons that the
"blessings, lessons and pain a Mugisu suffered during circumcision
helped him to stand strong when he met challenges in the future."
"Unfortunately, these days, the youth don't get these good
experiences and thus haven't worked as we used to in our youth," he
notes angrily.
James Maido, the surgeon who circumcised Mafabi agrees that the
tradition has changed but he says it is due to the current health
problems facing the community.
"The government advised us to use a knife per 'candidate' and he has
to bring it himself because of the fear that the surgeon used it on
another person who might have been HIV positive or had other sexually
transmitted diseases," Maido says. "The new system of using one knife
per person wouldn't be bad but in our culture, the knives are made by
special people under the influence of the spirits," he says, adding
that, "These people are very few to make knives for all candidates.
Secondly, to get them (knives), we have to pay a lot of money
especially during the busy seasons of August and December." Formerly,
boys of one lineage or clan were circumcised from one place. But when
HIV spread to the community, things changed.
The elite who had gained knowledge about the ways in which HIV is
transmitted no longer wanted surgeons who didn't use gloves. Some even
started to shun the custom or took their children to hospitals where
the risks were minimal. Others resorted to circumcising their children
a few months after birth in the hospitals to avoid such risks in their
children's puberty stage when they have to be initiated.
Big improvement
According to Dr. James Magomu, the situation has changed and new
methods have to be used to tackle the new challenges. He says that with
the current health standards, a professional medical person must be
present at every circumcision centre to help the patients if there is a
