lot of bleeding and also see to it that the person is given a tetanus
shot immediately after circumcision.
He explains that since the communities are more congested than they
used to be in the past, there are high chances of contracting deadly
diseases like tetanus which is why the shot is given immediately to
reduce all risks.
Secondly, the tradition of using the same knife on all initiates is
only practiced if the knife is sterilised before it is used on another
candidate.
"After a surgery, the boy is examined by a professional medic to see
whether he needs an injection of vitamin K if there is excessive blood
loss. In some cases, he might be in need of a blood transfusion," Dr.
Magomu says. "Now people are used to the new system and in the absence
of the medics, they ask where they are before the ceremony starts," he
explains.
With the modern treatment, it takes only three to four weeks for a
circumcised patient to heal. Not only has the culture been altered in
the health sphere, it has also changed in the way the ritual is
conducted. These days, they rarely hold long-distance processions as
they use to in the past.
Maido blames the problem on the poor economic situation they are
facing. "Today, people don't have enough food even for their own
survival.
The problem is affecting all families that is why people organise
traditional ceremonies individually in their homes to avoid going to
their relatives who are already poor," Maido says. "In the past, there
was a lot of food to feed people for weeks, for instance those who
escorted the candidates in processions locally called bamaayo. The
candidates had all the time to fully engage in the traditional
customs," he recalls.
Most of the candidates these days are between 12 and 28 years of age
who are still in school or in active service. They don't get enough
time during holidays to fully get involved in the culture. So they
prefer the system that takes a short time.

Original_Search_Title: Recalling 2001, Besigye's Year
Headline: Uganda: Recalling 2001, Besigye's Year
Body_Text:
Kampala — David Kezio-Musoke covered the 2001presidential campaigns and brings back to memory some of the events of the Besigye campaign.
WHAT is it about Col. (rtd.) Dr. Kizza Besigye that has so fixed him
in the media's consciousness? Why does he tower over all the other
presidential candidates? Trying to trace answers to these questions,
some of us have dug through memories of our encounter with this man.
The year 2001. It was a year of bitterness, defiance and anger.
My first encounter with Besigye was in January 2001, in a small seminar room at Hotel Africana.
Presidential campaigns had been banned and he had dubbed this
gathering "consultations with professionals". The room was packed,
though there was none of the pomp that one would expect at a
presidential campaign. There were no dignitaries but there was a sense
of suspense, typical of a Besigye function.
Besigye spoke raucously for about two hours. Days later, newspapers would described him as angry and strong-willed.
At this gathering, Besigye recounted his experience of the National Resistance Army war, coining the metaphor, "chicken eaters".
He explained that the "chicken eaters" fighting in the bush made
others eat cassava roots while they feasted on chicken because they
thought of themselves as the brains behind all operations. "This is
selfishness," he said.
Soon after that, Besigye suggested that presidential candidates be screened for HIV before nomination.
What did we make of Besigye's character? Who ordained him? Was it
the soldier in him? This guy was daring. The press referred to him as a
supremely self-confident man with an enormous ego. Others called him
selfless.
That year, two events made us look professionally inept. One was the
arrest of Maj. Okwir Rwaboni and the other was Besigye's disappearance
from the country.
While we covered the SMART summit at Munyonyo, Besigye was busy
putting together his exit plan. Our excitement to interview Sudan's
President Omar el-Bashir diverted all our energy from the Besigye
story. Bashir had not granted an interview in a year and The New Vision
secured an exclusive with him.
Early the next day, we thought we had an edge over our competitors
but The Monitor scored with the headline "Besigye goes missing".
We should have seen that coming. An acid sour Besigye had earlier
warned on Radio One that he could sneak out through any route besides
Entebbe Airport. His warning came just after he had been barred from
flying to Johannesburg on suspicion of being involved in terrorism.
Covering Besigye, we met people we had never thought of meeting. One
such person was CNN's Catherine Bond. We also met a horde of the
international journalists who once spent a cold rainy night at Rock
Gardens hoping to charter a plane to Runkungiri, where Besigye's last
rally was meant to be. He, however, cancelled it, citing violence and
so the foreign journalists spent the whole night drinking liquor till
morning when Anne Mugisha, Besigye's aide, invited us for the last
international press briefing.
At the Grand Imperial Hotel briefing, Besigye was asked if he was in
any ill health. He responded, "I suffer from no ailment that can hinder
me from running the country."
Time magazine had earlier published an article which quoted
President Yoweri Museveni as saying Besigye had HIV. The New Vision
republished this article and this fuelled a hate relationship between
the Besigye camp and The New Vision.
It was very difficult to identify oneself as a New Vision journalist at any of Besigye's functions.
In February 2001, I called up James Opoka, one of Besigye's aides
for a comment and he shouted, "We don't talk to The New Vision," before
slamming down the phone.
Besigye's group intensified calls to its supporters to stop buying The New Vision and its sister papers.
In April after the Supreme Court announced that Yoweri Museveni was
the winner of the 2001 election, The New Vision ran a rather poetic
editorial headline, "The die is cast the contest is done."
That unprecedented case had brought a lot of excitement. Besigye, in
one of the exclusive interviews he granted us, said he had assembled a
team of 40 lawyers. We hurriedly wrote the story but most of the
lawyers listed denied any involvement.
Days later the Museveni's group announced they had a team of 100
lawyers. Besigye then announced the presence on his team of Prof.
Richard Pildes, a former consultant lawyer of then US Vice-President Al
Gore.
Apparently to counter this, John Khaminwa and Eugene Wamalwa both renowned Kenyan lawyers joined the Museveni team.
While 2001 was full of drama, intrigue and conspiracy, we certainly saw humour too.
The encounter between Museveni and Joseph Balikuddembe, Besigye's
lead counsel, is one of those worth remembering. Balikuddembe visited
State House, Nakasero to serve Museveni with the election petition.
Museveni: Are you from the enemy camp?
Balikuddembe: I am looking for a gentleman who was declared winner
of the election. The law requires me to serve him. It is not easy to
trace that gentleman but today my colleagues managed to trace him and
that is why I am here.
Museveni: But I have heard that this gentleman is immune to prosecution.
Balikuddembe: I am serving him in his personal capacity as president-elect and not as president, head of state.
Museveni: Let me accept so that you don't say that I am holding up the process.
Balikuddembe: We also hope that after we have won, the gentleman who was declared winner will give us some money.
In 2001, I met all the candidates save for Museveni and DP's Francis
Bwengye. Nasser Sebaggala had a lofty vision which the media saw as
hopeless. Charles Senkubuge and Aggrey Awori were a pair of comics.
Kibirige Mayanja of JEEMA was very much aware he was losing. All of
Museveni's rivals, apart from Besigye, seemed one-dimensional.
However, Besigye was far too bitter a candidate. Five years later, not much has changed.

Original_Search_Title: Country Reinstated On US Exim Bank Cover
Headline: Ghana Reinstated On US Exim Bank Cover
Body_Text:
AS A Result of the general improvement in the economy, Ghana has
been reinstated on the US Exim Bank cover for both public and private
sector companies, after being blacklisted for six years.
This indicates that American companies that may invest in Ghana
could now access short, medium and long-term financing from the Exim
bank.
"To give a new lease of life to all businesses which, for whatever
problems, have reneged on their tax obligations, government has decided
to waive the penalty incurred from failure to pay the tax when it was
due," President John Agyekum Kufuor declared at the opening of the 10th
Ghana International Trade Fair in Accra on Wednesday.
He said the re-instatement was as a result of the macro-economic stability and the general improvement in the economy.
He said government had continued to embark on various programmes and
initiatives to open up the country and make it an attractive
destination for business, as well as stabilize the economy and prepare
it for growth and accelerated development.
This involved massive infrastructural development in terms of roads,
ports and harbours, telecommunications and improved social services
delivery, as well as the introduction of various Presidential
initiatives in Cassava, Oil palm, salt, sorghum, maize and cotton
production, as a means of diversifying the economy and adding value to
exports.
Besides, investors are also being encouraged to access the Export
Processing Zones to facilitate the development of export-oriented
businesses.
President Kufuor also cited the Trade Sector Support Programme,
which was launched last year as one special programme that aimed to
increase the country's competitiveness in international and domestic
markets and improving the legal and regulatory environment for both
business and consumers.
As part of the programme, trade promotion and frontline agencies,
such as Ghana Civil Aviation Authority, Customs, Excise and Preventive
Service, among others, are being strengthened and resourced to play
their facilitating roles more effectively.
President Kufuor said the financing needs of businesses were being
addressed through the establishment of various credit lines and
facilities such as EDIF and the Venture Fund to micro and small-scale
industries.
To further deepen the contributions of Ghanaian businesses, the
President said government had decided to waive the penalty they
incurred from the failure to pay the tax on time to give them a new
lease of life.
He said this was being done with the hope that prompt and voluntary tax compliance by the businesses would become the new order.
"This is also to allow companies to grow their businesses and take
advantage of new opportunities, which government is making available to
them, especially through the banks."
President Kufuor lauded the decision of the Trade Fair Company
Limited to organize the International Trade Fair annually, but asked
them to ensure that the fairs did not become mere rituals.
"The critical business issues must always be in evidence and must be
handled in a manner that would make participants feel that the fair is
good value for money," he said.
He said the Fair Company ought to know that the global exhibition
industry had become highly complex and competitive and that only
competence, dedication and aggressive marketing would guarantee success.
President Kufuor therefore urged that management systems responsive
to international practices must be adopted for the Ghana International
Trade Fair Center.
On his part, the minister of trade and industry, Mr. Alan
Kyeremanten, said government was in the process of establishing an
e-commerce website to enable Ghanaian products be displayed in the
gallery.
He urged exhibitors and visitors to form linkages to fully benefit from the fair.
The Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Ghana Trade Fair Company, Miss
Esther Ofori, said the Board and Management of the Company was pursuing
an agenda to transform the Trade Fair Center from the present trade
exhibitions focus to a modern multi-purpose trade center complex.
This would comprise a hotel complex, a convention hall, a cinema hall and exhibition halls, among others.
She said they were currently looking for a strategic partner to help
transform the Trade Fair Center, and therefore called for the
recapitalisation of the Company to be able to meet the transformation
process.

Original_Search_Title: Kampala Feels Energy Punch
Headline: Uganda: Kampala Feels Energy Punch
Body_Text:
Kampala — Hardly a month since Umeme announced the increase in load
shedding from four to 12 hours on the advice of the Uganda Electricity
Transmission Company Ltd, many entrepreneurs are already feeling the
brunt of power shortage.
Daily Monitor combed some usually busy areas around Kampala to find
out how they are coping with the power cuts. Kampala Siti Cable Ltd, an
Internet service company in Kamwokya has devised means to cope through
their newly purchased generator.
"We service this generator at Shs300,000 every after 100 hours. This
is very costly," said Mr Shaban Lukooya, the Marketing Manager
yesterday.
Ms Sylvia, the Sales and Marketing Manager of Herbco Colour
Printers, a company dealing in transporting and clearing said, "We can
not afford to maintain a generator because it has to be refuelled".
The Manager of Katwe Fresh Dairy, Mr Lawrence Muwanga, his business
has been 'devastated' because his milk goes bad due to irregular power
surges. "We used to sell 10,000 litres before this problem but now we
sell about 6000 litres," he said.
Things are no better for fuel stations , which are expected to be making alot of profits.
The General Manager of Katwe Caltex Station, Mr Medard Bigyemano,
said: "We use 80litres of fuel to power the generator. Already this is
a huge expense."
Mr Edward Zaake, the Sales and Marketing Manager, Uganda Legal and
General Bureau, a company that deals in stationery, said, "I have to
bring my work in another bureau because for us we never have power."
Meanwhile, Maize and Cassava millers in Kisenyi area appeared
redundant in their factories as sacks of maize and Cassava packed all
over the place with workers seated on top of them.

Original_Search_Title: Dr Besigye Woos Janet Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Dr Besigye Woos Janet Museveni
Body_Text:
Ntungamo — Like a long lost son who has finally come home, FDC presidential
candiddate Kizza Besigye was Friday treated to a rapturous welcome in
this district. During a rally, Dr Besigye urged First Lady Janet Kataha
Museveni to declare that she is ready to work with the Forum for
Democratic Change if she is really bent on developing Ruhaama County.
His supporters lined up along the road to Kyamate playground
chanting "Kyahweire" (it is over) as he was driven in his open roofed
Pajero vehicle. Besigye who had a broad smile across his face, waved
the party's two fingure salute.
Speaking in his native Ruhororo, Besigye asked the people of Ruhaama
County to resist intimidation and voter buying against incumbent MP
Augustine Ruzindana.
Addressing so far the longest rally since he started his campaign,
Besigye said: "If Janet is voted, how will she bring development yet
she will be opposed to the policies of the sitting government (FDC).
Development policies are determined by the government in power. Janet
should declare that she will work in the FDC government."
He added: Ruzindana can oppose bad policies in Parliament, but can
Janet stand up and oppose her husband?" Besigye's remarks excited the
already charged crowd .
Ms Museveni who is vying for the Ruhaama seat, says she was sent by
God "to rescue the people of Ruhaama County". Besigye assured the
Rwandan community living in Ntungamo that he had no plan of
repatriating them. "They are going around telling lies that I will send
the Rwandese back.
At the same time they say that President Paul Kagame's boys are on
standby to fight for me. I have failed to understand this!," he said.
A significant population of Ntungamo comprises people of Rwandan
origin because the district borders Rwanda. "I want to assure you that
our government is not planning to chase anyone. For us in FDC we don't
consider one's tribe or nationality. It is one Uganda, one people,"
Besigye roared as the crowd joined in.
He asked the electorate in Ntungamo to vote him. "President Museveni
is only concerned with members of his immediate family, friends and
in-laws. He has completely forgotten about the ordinary man that voted
him in power.
Ours will be a government of the people. In case you realise that I
have not delivered, please feel free to boot me out of power. I was in
Bulemezi the other day and I saw a man whose cassava we used to eat
still waiting for his compensation 20 years later."
Besigye also said that not true that all the Bahiima had benefited
from the NRM regime. "Many are still grazing cattle. But there are
other corrupt people who have benfited. Is (Minister) Rukutana a
Muhiima?

Original_Search_Title: Three Arrested for Trailing Dr Besigye
Headline: Uganda: Three Arrested for Trailing Dr Besigye
Body_Text:
Nakasongola — THREE men were yesterday arrested in Nakasongola town for trailing
Forum for Democratic Change presidential candidate Kizza Besigye.
Two others suspected of being their accomplices melted away from the
charging mob. Those arrested were identified as Ali Ssentumbwe,
Ssemanda Kyeyune and one Ssentongo, the driver of the special hire taxi
in which they were travelling, a grey Corona registration number UAB
365R.
Ssentongo pleaded for mercy, saying he had only been hired from
Najjanakumbi, a Kampala suburb, and said he did not know that the plan
was to trail Besigye. However he said he was a staunch FDC supporter
known to everyone in Najjanankumbi.
The three produced brand new FDC party cards before they were handed
over to Nakasongola Police for further interrogation. A police officer
searched the car for arms but found none.
The Police Deputy spokesman, Mr Patrick Onyango confirmed the
arrest, but said the trio were later released after recording
statements.
"They recorded that they were Besigye's supporters who were
following their 'beloved' candidate and blamed their arrest on poor
coordination amongst FDC supporters," Onyango said.
Yesterday's arrest of the three men brings to 23, the number of
people who have been questioned for trailing Besigye. In January, two
men, Mr Frank Nyakairu and a common ISO agent known as Banana were
arrested and detained briefly at Mpenja Police Post in Mpigi.
The two claimed to be Besigye's supporters but Banana was quickly
identified. They survived being lynched when Besigye's security
intervened. Another 18 were arrested and detained in Kaberamaido. They
were travelling in a gray Corona registration number UAD 592W, and an
omnibus. They also claimed they were Besigye's supporters. They
followed Besigye's convoy from Soroti, through Amuria, Katakwi to
Kaberamaido where they were eventually apprehended.
Besigye seemed unbothered by the arrest. He continued with his
campaign in Luweero where he urged the people not to borrow money from
any micro finance insititution between now and May when FDC comes to
power if it wins the elections.
"Don't borrow money now. The next government will give you lower
rates," Besigye told the crowd. Micro finance institutions bring loan
facilities closer to the people, but many complain of the high interest
rates they charge.
Besigye criticised the Movement government for having neglected
Luweero its launch pad for the five-year bush war in which Besigye took
part. "I fought with Museveni here in Luweero and we ate your cassava.
But 20 years down the road, he still has not compensated you," Besigye,
who was Museveni's personal doctor in this very area, said.
He continued that there have been a lot of complaints in managing
war debts. In a separate interview with the Daily Monitor, Besigye said
he would look into the problem in May after he has been sworn in. "The
money has been paid to wrong people and the problem has lingered on.
The whole process needs to be streamlined," Besigye told the excited
crowd. He said once sworn in, these are the problems he would look into.
Besigye also had no kind words for the government's plan of 'poverty
for all'. "The government is lying to the people about that programme,
because it is not practicable," Besigye said. "How is it possible when
they cannot pay the little amount they owe you," Besigye wondered.
In Nakaseke, Besigye's supporters turned up with fish bones pinned
on sticks. The scene has become common at Besigye rallies. It was
started in Busia, with the residents there saying they had fallen on
hard times, thus resorting to eating bones rather than the local fish
delicacy.
The Nakaseke residents also carried the bones now popularly referred
to as 'mugongo wazi' as a demonstration of how the government is having
a big share of the national cake yet the peasants have been left to
gnaw at bones.
Besigye told these creative supporters that the very reason for them
to vie for change is right in their hands. He told them that the NRM
has left no mark in Luweero yet it was the base for the war.
"For 20 years, can anybody tell what change they have had in their
lives? Haven't things remained the same? Just look at the bad roads, is
it not time for change?" Besigye asked.
Like he has done on previous rallies, Besigye urged the people of
Luweero to guard their vote jealously come February 23. By press time,
Besigye was on his way to Migyera, still in Nakasongola district.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Won't Stay Beyond 2007 - Aide
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Won't Stay Beyond 2007 - Aide
Body_Text:
Asaba — An aide to president Olusegun Obasanjo, and the chairman, cassava
export project, South-south, Chief Ned Nwoko has said President
Obasanjo has no intention to stay in office beyond 2007.
He said Obasanjo has requested in Abuja to form a group to propagate his ideals long after he has left office.
Briefing newsmen after the launching of a group tagged "legacy",
Nwoko told journalists that legacy group was the brain-child of the
President, adding that the group had a similar thinking like the
President .
He stressed that the ideals of the group was the provision of good
leadership, noting that legacy group "want credible and accountable
leadership, that will provide food, shelter and employment to the
people"
Nwoko hinted that if President Olusegun Obasanjo had intention of
extending his tenure, he would not have been planning to form a group
that would outlive his administration.
The presidential aide assured that Obasanjo would not contest
election in 2007, praying that Nigerians should allow another person
the opportunity to carry on the good works of the president.
On the issue of power shift, he called on the leadership of the
Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) to make its position known on the
controversial issue. He also added that various criteria must be in
place before leaving office, one of which he said was good leadership.
While also communicating on the various reform programmes of
President Obasanjo, the CEP chairman stressed that the reforms put in
place would yield truthful and required results in the next few years.

Original_Search_Title: Col. Besigye Pledges to Give Luweero a Facelift
Headline: Uganda: Col. Besigye Pledges to Give Luweero a Facelift
Body_Text:
Kampala — FDC presidential candidate Dr. Kizza Besigye has pledged to give Luweero a new face if he is voted into power.
Addressing a rally at Wobulenzi town playground on Friday, Besigye
said although Luweero was the base for the NRM liberation struggle, it
had not received the due attention it deserves.
"The first thing I will do when you vote me into power is to give
Luweero a new face. In the bush war Luweero was our base and we ate
people's cassava but it's a pity that 20 years down the road the war
claimants have not been paid," Besigye said.
On his way from Kampala to Luweero, Besigye supporters put
barricades in every small town requesting him to address them. However,
he pleaded with them to let him go because he had to divide his time
between court and campaigns.
He would occasionally wave to them and the crowds would respond 'obewo' (long live).
He asked Ugandans to vote for him, saying President Yoweri museveni does not have anything new for Ugandans.

Original_Search_Title: NDC On NDC
Headline: Ghana: NDC On NDC
Body_Text:
On Thursday 12th January, I listened to the current affairs programme on Metro TV, "Good Evening Ghana" hosted by Mr. Paul Adom Okyere with Mr. Ekow Spio Gabrah as the Guest. Several issues were addressed. To say the least it was appalling for a senior member of our party to say the type of things he said about the former flag bearer of the party Prof Mills.
It is said that "when the anthill allows holes into itself, all animals enter". It is unfortunate that this great party of ours and the largest opposition party in Ghana and at a point in time ostensibly the party in waiting to form a government, if not by 2008 at least by 2012, has allowed several holes into itself and thus facilitating the entry of all kinds of animals: ants, toads, roaches, rodents, reptiles-serpent, wolves etc. Unfortunately we have not had any doves entering our anthill yet, not in the last couple of years.
Firstly I have a few of questions for Spio Gabrah:
1. As the Master Strategist for the former President Jerry John as you claimed to be in 1996, where were you and where did your strategic skills vanish to in 2000 and then in 2004? Why did you not put those skills at the behest of Prof Mills for him to win handsomely in 2000, Even if these were for a fee?
2. Does Ekow want us NDC Activists and indeed all Ghanaians- to believe that he (Ekow) had to be appointed a Campaign Manager for Prof Mills before he could put his skills at the disposal of the Flag-bearer? God save our party if we have people like Ekow Spio Gabrah in our Party
3. Ekow, being outside Ghana in the UK for an assignment with the Commonwealth did not take you to another planet outside the Milky Way of our universe. At least you were and still are entitled to annual vacations. In 2004 why did you not bring your strategies to the fore to help our Great Party to come to power during any of the annual vacations that you enjoyed? Indeed you voted for Prof Mills in 2003 as against Dr Botchwey as the flag bearer of the NDC. Are you saying you were hoodwinked before you voted for his candidature?
4. Ekow, are you sure you are not prompted to make the observations you made from any mercenary or unworthy motives?
Let me now address some of damming, scathing and acerbic statements that Ekow made. Among others Ekow said instead of Prof Mills undertaking Proper Social Science Research to find out why he lost the 2000 and 2004 elections he and his cronies (so to speak) have been engaged in "Chop Bar" Research and the Blame Game.
When did Ekow know that the NDC undertake their research over fufu and Akrantie soup, Green-Green Soup, Kako/Ewurefua and Cassava Ampesi or ewurefua and etew? Is Ekow aware that the NDC has very great minds and a well established Research Department with academics and political luminaries such as Dr. Tony Aidoo and Mr. Kwamina Ahwoi among others who at one time or another were in charge of that Research Department?
Is Ekow suggesting or implying by those caustic and mordant remarks that all the works of Kwamina Ahwoi and Dr Aidoo were based falsehood and day-dreams after they had taken a good fill of Ghanaian delicacy and therefore were without scientific basis? When did you realize this, Ekow? Did you know or learn about the new Social Science Methodologies when you took up the appointment at the Commonwealth Secretariat?
It has been rumoured that you, Ekow Spio Gabrah wants to run for President on the ticket of the NDC. At least your reactions to some questions by Paul Adom Okyere literally offered some clues to any critical political analyst on the matter. But let me remind you, Ekow Spio Gabrah, of the story of Zaccheus in Holy Writ. Conscious of his handicap, and perhaps having several options under his sleeves that could prop him up to catch a glimpse of the Greatest Teacher that ever lived, did not follow the line of least resistance.
For instance he could have subtly asked some of the people around to lift him up and pay less or nothing during the subsequent tax year. He could also have pleaded with other friends to lift him up to enable him see Jesus or the other way round. He did none of these and may several other options that he might have schemed. He rather chose the hard way - he climbed the tree - by his own efforts. Ekow Spio, you don't go to the stadium and climb on the shoulders of your friends to watch a football match. Ekow Spio Gabrah, you want to chart the course of least resistance to the top. Be careful about that, Spio. We no go sit down!
Ekow, are you sure those barbed and sour statements (which as best is qualified to tear the NDC apart further) you made were your own thinking or you are doing the dirty work of the "Founder"? Is he still the undisputed Founder - Lawyer Kwaku Baah should help me!
Ekow, don't think that you are more marketable than Prof Mills. We are aware that you want to pair up with Madam (Mrs Konadu Rawlings) for the Presidency. Do you think the two of you are more marketable than Prof Mills? Some of us can say that Prof Mills has done so much for our Party and this country than most of you so-called Master Strategists and Champions of Revolutionary verve in the party.
Do you know the amount of benefit that Prof Mills' sobriety and decorum and those of other political stalwarts such as Dr Obed Asamoah, Alhaji Mahama Idrisu, Mr. Harry Sawyerr, Mr. Owusu Acheampong, Justice Annan, the late Ms Patience Addow, Hon John Mahama, Mr. PV Obeng among others have gained for Party?
These fine gentlemen come handy when it comes to moderation of issues for the party that was born out of a revolution. We must be grateful for these people. When people like those mentioned are spending time and energy to built the Party, others like you, Tony Aidoo, Victor Smith, Jerry John and their breed use their diarrhoeic speeches (seen by them only as signs of charisma) to scuttle whatever has been built.
Spio should be ashamed that instead of finding ways of mending the Party he is creating another block - the Mills Faction against the Jerry John Faction. In conclusion let it be known to you that neither you nor your team-up with "She that must be Obeyed Ticket will fly in Ghana. I challenge you, Ekow to try your ticket. Spio give us a Break, Peace.

Original_Search_Title: NAFDAC Assures Harsh Regulation On Foreign Products
Headline: Nigeria: NAFDAC Assures Harsh Regulation On Foreign Products
Body_Text:
The National Agency for Food, Drugs Administration and Control (NAFDAC)
said yesterday that it will put in place strict regulations to ensure
quality of foreign products for the benefit of the country.
The Director-General of the Agency, Prof. Dora Akunyili who made the
remark while receiving Thailand's Deputy Minister of Commerce, Preecha
Laonhapongehana, said that the gesture was aimed at removing unfair
competition by counter-feiters in the country. "Our regulations are very
stringent because we want better quality products for our people. We want to
remove unfair competition of counterfeiters so that genuine people will come
into the sector, because you cannot effectively compete with fraudsters
because they will get round."
Dr. Akunyili, who described Nigeria as a goldmine for genuine
businessmen and women, assured Thailand of prosperity if its products make
an in-road to Nigeria.
According to her, Nigeria is strategically placed to launch Thailand
into the African and West African sub-region, adding that the population of
the country will help if Thailand brings genuine business.
Earlier, the Thailand Deputy Minister of Commerce, Preecha
Laonhapongehana, said that he was in Nigeria to solicit the federal
government's cooperation for the benefit of the two countries.
According to him, Thailand has plans to set up a rice factory in Nigeria
and to process Cassava in order to boost export and import.
He noted that Thailand will set up an embassy in Nigeria in order to
strengthen more cooperation between the two countries.

Original_Search_Title: Failed Banks Owners Are Unforgivable - Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Failed Banks Owners Are Unforgivable - Obasanjo
Body_Text:
President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday said that owners of the insolvent banks that failed to meet the N25 billion recapitalisation deadline of December 31,2005 have committed unforgivable sins.
His word: "the owners of the failed banks have committed an unforgivable sin."
The Central Bank of Nigeria, Monday, withdrew the licences of about 14 banks in the country as a result of their failure to meet the deadline issued by the apex bank.
Speaking at the bank's consolidation/stakeholders meeting held at Aso Villa, Obasanjo said however that the CBN owes no apology to the senate over the consolidation exercise provided that the law establishing the apex bank gave right for such restructuring exercise.
At the meeting, the Senate President in his massage to the president delivered by Senator Gbenga Ogunniya, said the senate was in doubt about the legality of the consolidation exercise and questioned 25 mega banks.
Obasanjo said if the exercise carried out by the CBN contradicts the law establishing the banks, he will apologise to the Senate on behalf of the apex bank that the exercise is meant for the goodness of Nigerians and would promote the economic stability of the country.
It will be recalled that the CBN Governor, Professor Charles C. Soludo, said in his speech while withdrawing the licence of the 14 failed banks that the exercise is in conformity with the powers conferred upon it by the Banks and Other Financial Institutions Act.
The president who also spoke on the cassava initiative of his administration, gave July 1st this year as the deadline for all flour millers in the country to use 10 percent cassava or have their factories closed.
He said, the industrialists are not assisting the initiative at the grassroot level, the situation that brought about the delay in its take up.
He said the whole essence of a generate employment and create wealth for Nigerians, stating that the idea build a private/public partnership was to fight poverty.
The president said the meeting will be held every quarter to access the performance of the economy and to find out the contributions of each sector in the area of job creation.
The meeting, which is the first in the history of the Obasanjo administration, witnessed participants from the organized private sector, governors, non governmental organizations, among others.

Original_Search_Title: In Overcrowded Prisons, Survival is a Daily Battle
Headline: Nigeria: In Overcrowded Prisons, Survival is a Daily Battle
Body_Text:
Kaduna — As visitors approach the death row block at Kaduna's central prison in
northern Nigeria, a sea of hands waving tin cups automatically jerk through
the bars of the dark cells.
"Get back!" shouts the prison guard at the 118 detainees crammed inside
a dilapidated building originally meant to house 33. Up to three inmates
live in less than four square metres of space. An overpowering stench of
urine and mould billows out into the courtyard.
In the turmoil of the shouts some of the prisoners draw back to their
spots on a tattered mat on the floor that aside from a few plastic bowls is
the only object in the cell.
But the guard is jumpy and cuts short the visit, prohibiting any further
interaction with the detainees.
Rights organisations working in Nigerian prisons - and even prison
officials themselves - say the conditions of death row inmates do not fulfil
even minimum international human rights standards.
In Kaduna prison, death row inmates are locked up all day long, said
Festus Okoye, executive director of Human Rights Monitor (HRM), a group
based in the northern city.
"They are allowed out only rarely, for a few minutes, one by one," he
said. Meanwhile some prisoners collect the buckets used as toilets.
Most of the death row inmates are utterly alone and never receive
visitors - their families living too far away and having abandoned them for
fear of being associated with their crimes, rights group sources say. Some
simply cannot pay the 'visiting rights' fee charged by the wardens.
Nigeria this year acknowledged the sorry state of its jails, announcing
plans to free some 25,000 inmates still awaiting trial - some for as long as
10 years - in a bid to relieve overcrowding and bad conditions.
The move could ease conditions for those left waiting on death row for
years. Since Nigeria legalised capital punishment in 1999, only one prisoner
has been executed by the state in northern Nigeria, with authorities openly
reticent to carry through with executions, according to HRM.
Nigeria countrywide has 548 prisoners awaiting capital punishment - 10
of them women - among a total 40,000 detainees, according to Ernest Ogbozor
of Prisoners Rehabilitation and Welfare Action (PRAWA), Nigeria's largest
prisoners' rights organisation.
Under Nigerian law, crimes punishable by death include armed robbery,
murder and treason. Islamic Sharia law, in force in 12 northern Nigerian
states, also calls for the death penalty in other crimes such as
adultery.
Lack of food
If conditions for death row inmates are harsh, they are hardly any
better for other prisoners. For the sick and weak, incarceration can be
tantamount to a sentence to death.
"The two main problems in Nigerian prisons are overpopulation and lack
of food," said Hassan Saidi Labo, assistant to Nigeria's prison inspector
general.
Kaduna is a clear example. In December 2005, 957 detainees were crammed
in 10 buildings designed for about 550 people.
Labo says some prisons hold up to four times their capacity.
In such conditions, just surviving is a daily battle, according to
54-year-old Felix Obi who was condemned to 27 years in prison in 1986 for
drug trafficking. He spent 13 years and three months behind bars in the
economic capital, Lagos, before benefiting from an amnesty in 1999.
"You fight for a scrap of blanket, a piece of soap, a bit of food or
medicine if you get sick," said Obi, who now works with PRAWA.
"Prisoners fight for space on the floor to sleep, they fight not to
become depressed, and not to be victims of violence. They fight to survive."
Monitoring by outside groups has had some impact. Since prisons were
opened to religious and humanitarian organisations more than 10 years ago,
the prison death rate has fallen from 1,500 per year in the late 1980s to 89
deaths in 2003, according to authorities.
Still the risk of death in prison remains high, particularly because of
lack of food, said Harp Damulak, the Kaduna prison hospital doctor.
The daily ration generally consists of a bowl of beans in the morning
then cassava in the afternoon and evening. Prisons have a budget of 150
Naira (US $1.15) per prisoner per day.
But this small amount does not necessarily get to all prisoners. Supply
is in the hands of subcontractors who - poorly paid, acknowledge prison
officials - sometimes dip into the goods, according to PRAWA and HRM.
The UN Office on Drugs and Crime says a prison employee earns about
6,000 Naira ($45) per month at the start, earning a maximum of about 40,000
Naira monthly at the end of a career. As a consequence corruption is
common.
Conditions favour disease
Lack of food moreover aggravates already poor hygiene
conditions. Damulak said that malnutrition makes prisoners highly vulnerable
to infectious diseases such as tuberculosis or skin diseases caused by lack
of hygiene.
The situation is the same for women inmates in Kaduna prison, where 18
women live in two cells, sleeping on iron beds stacked one atop another,
some without mattresses. The bathroom has long been without running
water.
"We are devoured by mosquitoes, we all suffer malaria but don't have bed
nets and the hospital has no medicine except paracetamol," said Zainab, 32,
who has been incarcerated since April. "There is nothing. Even sanitary
napkins - we have to share one between two women every month, or even every
two months."
Prison conditions weigh heavily on the detainees, often causing
depression and other psychological problems, according to Damulak. And
prison personnel are not trained to handle such issues, he said.
To survive in their environment, some prisoners have taken things into
their own hands.
"They have created a veritable government," HRM's Okoye said. "One
prisoner is president, another police chief, another head of justice." He
added that some prison officials see the initiative as a positive thing
because it helps foster order in the institutions.
Former prisoner Obi said, "Some [prison 'leaders'] invent rules that are
impossible to follow." Punishment generally comes in the form of an order to
do chores, such as washing the clothes of 'chiefs,' but often prisoners pay
for misdeeds by being beaten or even sexually assaulted.
Despite efforts by inmates to impose some sort of organisation, prison
riots are common, PRAWA's Ogbozor said.
"In the past six months we have seen five riots in prisons across the
country - all linked mostly to the lack of food for detainees."
Under the recently announced plan to release prisoners, those who have
spent three to 10 years awaiting trial will have their cases reviewed for
immediate release. Also eligible will be the elderly, the terminally ill and
those with HIV, as well as people locked up for longer than the prospective
sentence for their crime.
Among those who have languished in prisons for years, human rights
activists say, are some who were picked up by mistake or for very minor
infractions and simply could not pay a fine.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Orders Tariffs Cancellation At Calabar Free Zone
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Orders Tariffs Cancellation At Calabar Free Zone
Body_Text:
Henceforth, all regulatory obstacles and tariffs which may have stifled the activities and smooth operations of businesses at the Calabar Free Trade zone are to be eliminated, President Olusegun Obasanjo has directed.
Against this background, the various security and service operatives, including the Nigeria Customs Service, Immigration Service, the Police and the Nigerian Ports Authority, operating within the zone are to eliminate the high tariff regime imposed on businesses at the zone to allow for smooth businesses at the zone.
President Obasanjo, who was represented by the Minister of Commerce, Ambassador Idris Waziri, at the official commissioning of the SkyRun International Air Conditioners manufacturing factory from China, said that any attempt to hinder business activities in the zone would scare investors. He advised that activities should provide safe haven for investors in order to woo more.
The president explained that by investing in the country, foreign investors help to create job opportunities, thereby strengthening the nation's economy through poverty alleviation.
Obasanjo stated that Nigeria finds its trade relations with China, especially in the non-oil sector, very beneficial. He disclosed that Nigeria's non-oil export to China is about $500m annually.
According to him, the economic romance between Nigeria and China started in 2004 with Nigeria exporting cassava, palm oil, and Siamese beans to China. He announced that Nigeria's cassava has been noted as the best in the world and that it fetches the nation $12bn from exports.
The president explained that the public-private participation policy is one of the best ways to better the nation's economy, adding that the SkyRun International Company was invited from China to invest in the country six months ago and that within a short period of time, it had lived up to its expectation.
According to him, Calabar would give the Chinese easy access to other West African sub-zones, adding that the Chinese are conscious of the population and influence of Nigeria in the West African sub-region, which they can do business with.
He however urged more Nigerians to diversify into the non-oil economy since, according to him, the oil sector had brought instability and corruption.
On his part, Governor Donald Duke of Cross River State said his government would stop at nothing to providing enabling environment for investors to operate in the state. Represented by his deputy, Duke said his government was committed to the completion of the projects, as well as cooperating with investors to create wealth and improve the living condition of the people.
The Consul General of China in Nigeria, Mr. Yuan Wenjin, said China attaches great importance to the growth of Nigeria's economy because of its policy to strengthen relations among developing nations. He added that Nigeria is rich with natural resources while China has the technology.
Wenjin said SkyRun International is a reputable company and urged that it should be protected and patronized to enable its growth, adding that its growth will improve the economy of the country, as well as the West African sub-zone.
Also commenting, the president of SkyRun, Mr. Liu Djindu, described the factory as a maiden project in Nigeria, expressing gratitude for the Nigerian government for the commissioning.
Djindu disclosed that President Obasanjo invited them to invest in Nigeria when he visited China in April 2005, and that equipment were moved to the site before the end of last year, adding that they decided to invest in the country because of the favourable trade policy put in place by the federal government.
The factory, he said, would also produce such other items as television sets, refrigerators and VCDs.

Original_Search_Title: Wabara Rallies Kinsmen, Blames Opponents Over Travails
Headline: Nigeria: Wabara Rallies Kinsmen, Blames Opponents Over Travails
Body_Text:
Umuahia — FORMER Senate President, Chief Adolphus Wabara has blamed those he said do not wish Ukwa Ngwa people well as responsible for the political travails of the people from the area, stressing that his main concern as 2007 approaches is to ensure that Ukwa Ngwa produces the next governor of the state.
Addressing the people of his senatorial zone at his Ohambele country home in Ukwa East Local Government Area of Abia State at the weekend, he assured the people that he was not in anyway weighed down by what happened to him as the Senate President.
His words: "The fact that I have got to this height is divine. I came into politics and rose so fast and that was why some people decided to pull me down. But I want to assure you people that they will not succeed. One thing I want to say is that many of you are the losers for my losing that position because instead of coming here, they now have to travel to Enugu to get what they want. One thing I have learnt is that when one is in power, everybody loves him. But once you lose that power, you are alone.
"On December 25, I was here with my wife and my only son alone in the whole compound and I recalled what the situation was during the previous Christmas when this place was full of people. But let me inform you that nothing will weigh me down. There is nothing that happened to me that has not happened before. I still believe in democracy.
"So, I am overwhelmed by your presence in my house today. When I saw the crowd, I asked my wife whether I am still alive because of the number of people here. If this crowd will remain steadfast, I do not see why the next governor will not come from Ukwa Ngwa. And I want to assure you that I will fight to see that the Abia charter of equity is maintained by allowing the next governor to come from this area." He, however, frowned at what he called dissident elements in the midst of Ukwa Ngwa people, adding that such people must not be allowed to succeed in dividing the people .
At the meeting with the people of his senatorial zone, Wabara, through the Adolphus Wabara Foundation, distributed 100 motorcycles to the youths from the six local government areas that make up the zone. He also gave out N3 million grant- in- aid to all the political wards in the zone for the purpopse of cultivating cassava for export .
Sunday Vanguard gathered that the senator has been empowering the people of the zone since 1999 when he was first elected into the Senate. According to the former Senate President, those in charge of the Foundation have been directed to ensure that everybody from the zone, irrespective of his political leaning, should benefit from the empowerment scheme.
Describing himself as water that does not have enemies, he urged the people to try and win the souls of other political groups in the state so that they can come into the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) for the purpose of winning next year's election.

Original_Search_Title: 3rd Term...the Storm After
Headline: Nigeria: 3rd Term...the Storm After
Body_Text:
Lagos — In 1975, Gen. Yakubu Gowon, having been military leader for nine years reneged on an earlier promise to allow the country's return to democratic governance. His excuse then was that politicians were not matured enough to continue the task of nation building. As events turned out, Gowon was the one lacking maturity in vision and statesmanship. He was overthrown July 29 of the same year, later forced into self-exile after an alleged involvement in the February 13, 1976 coup and ended up in disgrace. Even when he was pardoned several years later, Gowon returned to the country and lived in the shadows of his successors. Nobody remembered his achievements as Nigeria's wartime leader, if not hero. His single-minded re-integration of the Ndigbo into the Nigerian nation after they were vanquished in the 1967-70 civil war became distant tales. His words carried no weight. So humbled was Gowon after his return from exile that he could only take solace in an anonymous association called
Nigeria Prays. Later, a leader who ordinarily should be a national icon accepted the chair of the Board of Trustees of Arewa Consultative Forum (ACF), a regional association. Today, Gowon has no associates, no followers, and no power centre, exerts little or no influence and is almost completely anonymous in a crowd of the country's political elite. Gowon has not recovered from the mistake of 1975; he is obviously still in emotional pain.
Gen. Ibrahim Babangida was military leader for eight years. When his long, tortuous and convoluted democratic transition programme ended in the annulment of the results of the June 12, 1993 presidential polls, Babangida "stepped aside" to save face. A leader ever conscious of his place in history, every achievement of his administration was interred with the post-June12 annulment crises. Although still a darling of the ruling elite, Babangida's every attempt, since he left office in August 1993, to embark on image laundering always ended up a public relations disaster. Babangida would obviously have loved to be a celebrated statesman, but due to the June12 annulment, he is today a celebrated political fraud. Twelve years after leaving office, Babangida is still immersed in the pool of disgrace he fell into due to the unhealthy ambition to stay in office in perpetuity. The late military leader, Gen. Sani Abacha wanted to transmute into a civilian president. He got the five par
ties to adopt him as the sole presidential candidate. Not long after this strange experiment in political manipulation, Abacha could not stop the earth from swallowing his body. And his dreams. Today, Abacha's name evokes memories of fear, assassination, torture, thievery, abuse of office and every ugly thing power could be deployed. Even in death, his name has not been spared from the scum of disgrace.
Gowon obviously had these experiences in mind when, in a message to the Middle Belt Conference in Abuja late last month, generally advised the present leadership of the country to avoid prolonging its stay. In obvious reference to the speculated third term ambition of President Olusegun Obasanjo, Gowon said, "We have to avoid the tendency of leadership prolonging itself or even overstaying its acceptability - my experience, that of Gen. Babangida and Abacha should be a good lesson for all leadership in Nigeria today and tomorrow." However, like a student on a self-destructive path to exam failure, the presidency would hear none of this. Presidential aide, Femi Fani-Kayode, asked Gowon to shut up, that the former leader, having been forced out of power in 1975, does not have the credentials to advise Obasanjo on leadership prolongation. Some few days earlier, Fani-Kayode had cause to join words with Dr. John Onaiyekan, bishop of Abuja Catholic Diocese. Onaiyekan had, at a Chri
stmas homily, advised Obasanjo not to listen to those asking him to extend his stay in office. The cleric said there are over 5,000 Nigerians capable of piloting the affairs of state. Fani-Kayode countered that though there may be so many Nigerians that could be president, none has what it takes to match Obasanjo's achievements in the economy, the polity and at the international scene.
If Fani-Kayode's ill-advised responses to Onaiyekan and Gowon were not enough signals that the third term agenda is real, three ordinarily isolated incidents completely removed the veil of pretence. The first was the Southern Leaders Conference in Enugu, which apparently had the backing of the presidency, obviously to counter an earlier meeting of northern leaders in Kaduna. While the Kaduna meeting resolved that the presidency should come back to the North in 2007, the Enugu meeting threatened confederation should presidential power not remain in the South. With Obasanjo's men like Chief Olabode George and Fani-Kayode shoving their weight and running their mouth at the Enugu conference, it was obvious the presidency had more than a partisan interest in its outcome. Obasanjo and his men, however, seemed to have overlooked the manifest danger of playing the South against the North and causing a sharp division in the country along that divide. The second signal was Obasanjo's p
athetic response to a simple and straightforward question he was asked in last month's presidential media chat. A caller from Sokoto had wanted to know if the president was interested in staying in office beyond 2007. Obasanjo, in what was known as out of point in our secondary school exam parlance in those days, rambled on about cassava production and his administration's successes in the economic sector. The third signal was an interview the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) national chairman, Dr. Ahmadu Ali, granted THISDAY and published last Monday. In a logic as perverted as the reforms going on in the PDP in the last four months, Ali said, "We have seen evidence of good leadership again; do you just change a gown when it is not dirty?... The PDP is proud of President Obasanjo and if the country decides on their own to amend their constitution, who are we to prevent anybody from enjoying it?" The PDP national chairman said much more in what is a direct campaign for Obasanj
o to continue in office beyond 2007.
In my last piece, "Will 3rd Term Agenda Succeed?" (December 18, 2005), I concluded thus: "By the time the veil of pretence is removed from all the shenanigans going on, Obasanjo may just successfully roll the tank of the third term agenda on the country. Opposition to the agenda may be too tepid and uncoordinated to stop him. But then after the calm comes the storm. Getting a third term is one thing, keeping the peace is a different thing entirely." Now with the veil of pretence completely removed, the storm is gradually gathering. And the storm troopers are reporters, men and women who daily write the history of this country. Newspapers have now made third term almost the only issue worth playing up. Politicians whose names have long been forgotten now talk about third term and get front-page treatment. Columnists are lifting the banner of third term on the agenda of national discourse. To everybody Obasanjo is fair game. He's being hit and battered from every angle. The mon
umental achievements of his administration are daily being sacrificed on the altar of third term. What are the people saying out there? Vice President Atiku Abubakar's problem with Obasanjo is not unconnected with his opposition to the third term agenda. When DSP Alamieyeseigha was impeached as Bayelsa governor, it was because he was against the third term agenda. Alhaji Lamidi Adedibu and his thugs are after Oyo State governor Rashidi Ladoja because the governor is pro-Atiku. The Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) arrested Brig. Gen. Buba Marwa because he would like to be president in 2007. These may not necessarily be true but the perception is fast wearing the cassock of reality.
As long as things continue this way, the administration's credibility, already battered by the massive fraud in the 2003 polls, will be eroded the more. Obasanjo may succeed in manipulating the political process in amending the constitution and prolonging his stay in office, but there is no way a post-2007 government he would form would not be weighed down by a moral handicap. And if the June 12 annulment experience is any guide, a government lacking in moral authority is a government dead on formation. That was why Babangida had to step aside after the annulment. That was also why Chief Ernest Shonekan's Interim National Government stuttered and scattered. That was equally why Abacha was, for five years, a prisoner of power, afraid of his own shadows. Indeed, had Chief MKO Abiola, who apparently won the annulled elections not conveniently died in prison, the 1999 polls would perhaps have been indefensible. Such crisis of credibility and moral handicap would rubbish Obasanjo'
s stature as an international statesman. And at home, the administration's successes in anti-graft war, in banking consolidation, in debt reprieve among others may only be recorded as footnotes, if not completely forgotten. If what happened to Gowon, Babangida and Abacha are guiding signposts, Obasanjo's blind ambition to prolong his tenure would only catapult him to a dark alley of disgrace and infamy.
There is also the certainty that Obasanjo's third term agenda would rob Nigeria of peace. That is why long-time enemies like Babangida and Major Gen. Muhammadu Buhari are rediscovering their friendship. That is why all those who lost out in the PDP have regrouped in the Movement for the Defence of Democracy (MDD). Even if they cannot stop the president from realising his agenda, they will continue to talk and to heat up the polity. And make no mistake about it, some of them have the will and capacity to sabotage government, if not instigate bloody protests, in a big way. At the end of the day, when the push comes to shove, the Southern leaders decision to threaten confederation if they lose the presidency in 2007 could still blow in Obasanjo's face. For if the North and the South insist on having the presidency in 2007 or nothing, it could throw up a crisis that could tragically make Obasanjo the last of the nationalists. This may have informed the warning of the Chief of Arm
y Staff, Lt. Gen. Martin Luther Agwai, that politicians should watch their utterances and not create unnecessary tension capable of threatening "the corporate existence of Nigeria as one united country." After the Abacha experience, Nigerians may not accept military intervention in the polity. But that does not mean the soldiers can be stopped in the event of another descent into civil war.

Original_Search_Title: Jaqee Mbabazi is Single, But Still Relates With Her Ex-Lovers
Headline: Uganda: Jaqee Mbabazi is Single, But Still Relates With Her Ex-Lovers
Body_Text:
Kampala — SWEDEN-based Ugandan singer Jaqee Mbabazi recently took Kampala by storm with her 14-track album, Blaqalixious. The album is doing well in Sweden and Mbabazi has been nominated for the Swedish Grammy Awards. She talked to Pidson Kareire and below are excerpts:
How old are you?
I look young, but I'm 28 years old. Anyway, I love growing older. It is good you get more comfortable in your own skills and you are able to identify what you want.
What were you like at 15?
I was shy and quiet. I used to think a lot and contemplate. I knew I would be a writer and I loved writing so much.
Where were you born?
I was born in Mulago and grew up in Mbuya, Kampala.
When did you go to Sweden?
My family relocated to Sweden in 1990. Since then, I have lived and studied there. I'm a trained nurse.
Did you learn singing while in Sweden or did you start your music career in Uganda?
Sweden only encouraged me to sing. I already knew how to sing because I used to sing in churches before we left for Sweden. I knew I had the talent and I only needed motivation, which I found in Sweden.
What does Blaqalixious mean?
It is a way of expressing my feelings about my colour. I'm a black person among white people. I got the word black and the word lixious and joined them together making it Blaqalixious. This is because I believe that black people have to be more proud than they are. They have to show their pride because they are not different from whites, the only difference is the skin colour.
What does Karma, one of your title tracks mean?
Karma is a spiritual thing.
Is there 'detoothing' in Sweden?
Detoothing! What is that? Oh, yeah! I have remembered. It exists, it is everywhere. It is bad, disrespectful and degrading. Those who do it are likely to live a hopeless life.
What do you think attracts most Bazungu to black ladies?
Nothing special. It is their approach that makes them look sharp and smart. Black men have inferiority complex and get intimidated by whites.
What is the difference between African love and Bazungu love?
The difference is in their way of expressing love. The Bazungu express their love anywhere at anytime, which is still difficult among Africans.
What do you think is the major cause of break-ups in relationships?
Not listening to one another. Actually, poor communication.
What do you think is always on women's minds?
It could be anything; but what I know is they are sensitive.
Can you guess what is on men's minds?
Psychologists say that on every man's mind, there is a naked woman. Their thoughts begin from a woman to other issues.
What is your worst bedroom crime?
(Laughs). I don't have any.
Do you have a man in your life?
No. I'm single and not looking for one. When the right time comes I will get the right man. I want to first develop my career and come back to Uganda and settle.
What kind of boyfriend would you like?
I would like a man who is not afraid to love. A man who can actually listen and be affectionate. If all men could strive to do that the world would be a better place to live in.
How do you relate with your ex-boyfriends?
I'm friends with most of my ex-boyfriends because we got to know each other very well and the fact that we disagreed should not make us enemies. I try to make sure that we remain friends.
How would you react to a boyfriend who enjoys vulgar language?
I don't think that is sexy.
What role does money play in a relationship?
Of course money is necessary. However, it should not be the most important thing in a relationship because we have seen many rich people in miserable relationships.
When did you last use a hoe?
During my childhood when I was still in Uganda. In Sweden, I don't dig at all.
What is your best dish?
I like Ugandan local foods like cassava, matooke, groundnuts and fish.
Are you a soccer fan?
No. I watch so many movies that I don't have time for matches.
What do you enjoy wearing most?
Just a piece of cloth; it could be a lesu, whatever
How much do you spend on thongs?
It's not a big deal, but I like the boxer type.
How much do you spend on shoes?
I'm a smart buyer and I spend very little on shoes.
What are your favourite hang-outs in Kampala and Sweden?
I don't have a favourite hangout in Kampala, but it would be where my pals hangout and has to be homey. This even applies to Sweden. I follow friends.
Is there a beauty product you cannot do without?
Water and cocoa butter. I drink a lot of water and apply cocoa butter because they are good for the skin.

Original_Search_Title: Luweero War Survivors a Thorn in Gen. Museveni's Campaign
Headline: Uganda: Luweero War Survivors a Thorn in Gen. Museveni's Campaign
Body_Text:
Luweero — IS President Yoweri Museveni defying the old adage, "You can't eat your
cake and have it too," The answer may be Yes.
Gen. Museveni, a man who has always opposed the multiparty system for
all the years he has been in power, championed the opening up of political
space in a move that surprised everybody.
Save for probably himself, none of his buddies rarely knew that Museveni
meant business. But he is defying the above expression and having his cake
too because he is likely to be a beneficiary of the multiparty system he
supported when he bounces back to State House in 2006.
All indications show that as incumbent, he has an advantage over other
candidates. His main challenger Col. Dr. Kizza Besigye of the Forum for
Democratic Change (FDC) is in jail over treason and rape charges.
Museveni is in the countryside soliciting for votes.
The other political opponents, the Uganda Peoples' Congress (UPC) under
Miria Kalule Obote and the Democratic Party led by Mr Ssebaana Kizito are on
the edge of being torn apart by divisions and disorganistaion.
Independents, Nasser Sebaggala is still haunted by ghosts from his
former party (DP) while Dr Abed Bwanika has an Identity crisis as many
Ugandans do not know him.
For the two days I followed President Museveni in his campaign trail in
Luweero, Nakasongola and Nakaseke districts, which formed the foundation of
the war that catapulted him into power, I landed on one poster of
Col. Besigye pinned on a wooden door of a desolate house in a remote village
of Kangave in Semuto.
Ssebaana had some posters scattered in Wobulenzi, Luweero while Miria,
Bwanika and Sebaggala had none.
Museveni's visit to Luweero came two days after Ssebaana's, which brings
a question.
Didn't Ssebaana take the posters there? Secondly, if he took them, where
did they go? Your guess could be as good as mine.
Affordable material
My assumption is that the posters are the cheapest election material any
candidate can afford. But some residents claimed the posters of other
presidential candidates could have been removed ahead of Museveni's visit by
supporters, an allegation Daily Monitor could not independently confirm as
there was no evidence and police said no one has reported to them such
cases.
However, the Press Secretary to the President, Mr Onapito Ekomoloit who
was on the campaign trail said, the absence of posters for President
Museveni's opponents in the area is an indication of his candidate's
popularity.
"Since this is the opening of the campaigns, there is no strength of the
other political parties and there is no evidence to show that the posters
were removed," Onapito said.
With an illiterate population that relies on how much information they
have accessed in order to make a decision, the absence of posters of the
other candidates should be an issue of concern.
If UPC, FDC, Sebaggala and Bwanika have not sent their posters in the
area then they are making a big mistake because the voters in the
countryside may not know how they look like as many only depend on radios
and a few own television sets.
Unlike the previous campaigns, the huge crowds, which used to
characterise rally venues and became a basis of judging the strength of
presidential candidates, in Luweero this time it was a different story.
Local Council officials said that there was similar low turn up when
Ssebaana visited the area, two days earlier.
Museveni's supporters said that the number of people at rallies had
reduced because his campaign team set up meeting places to reduce on the
distance covered by people to hear their candidate speak.
There could, however, be two or three scenarios to it. One, that people
have become fatigued with endless electioneering from village to national
level that at times do not address their plight or the population has lost
interest all together.
The other scenario particularly for President Museveni is that rigorous
security checks by the Presidential Guard Brigade (PGB) make some people
stay away.
Oblivious of this Museveni at times drove ahead of the guards so that he
can talk to people along the way without interruption.
But the unrelenting guards would catch up with him and he was forced to
shout at them in Swahili.
To the journalists who were hungry for the news, apart from the comments
he made about the UPDF invasion of Congo agaisnt the backdrop of the
Internal Court of Justice ruling, the rest of the speech was usual rhetoric
in the media speak.
His address dwelt on the enumerations of the NRM's achievements which we
have heard overtime, how people can eradicate poverty, his bashing of the
opposition and his vision for East Africa region.
Different agenda
However, the trip was not all about politics. Museveni occasionally
stopped along the way and freely conversed with his former National
Resistance Army (NRA) volunteer fighters as if they were village mates. Both
parties with guards at bay recounted their days during the five-year
war.
Others reminded the president of the cassava they gave his rebel outfit
while others told him they shared a bed with him in their rundown homes as
he hid from the government soldiers. The villagers looking haggard and
wearily, are a big contrast to those they helped bring in power.
Though they are living through hard times, their pride of having
participated in the war has never wavered. Other people here have got
personal problems and wanted immediate presidential intervention.
One girl shed tears before the President requesting him to pay her
school fees after failing to resume school in the past three years due to
lack of money.
"Mzee nkunoonyezza ebbanga ddene, leero nkufunnye" (I have been looking
for you for long but now I have found you), the girl aged about 17 years old
said along the Gayaza-Ziroobwe road meaning,
The girl collapsed on Museveni's feet.
Museveni held her up and directed Col. Proscovia Nalweyiso, his aide on
welfare to ensure he finds a solution to the girl's problem.

Original_Search_Title: Food Demand Down
Headline: Uganda: Food Demand Down
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE demand for staple foodstuffs has reduced, forcing prices down, a weekly survey has revealed.
A bunch of matooke was sold at sh7,000 from sh10,000 at Nakasero Market. A kilogramme of maize flour went for sh600 from sh700, while beans were sold at sh500 from sh700.
"Few people are rushing for the food because of the festive season during which consumers preferred meat, chicken, matooke, sugar, posho, powder milk, cooking oil, rice and irish potatoes," Robert Mugisa, a trader at Nakasero Market, explained.
Mugisa added that there is plenty of maize, beans, tomatoes, oranges, matooke, cassava and maize flour in the market.
He said meat, beef, chicken and turkey prices were still up because they are still on high demand.
Mugisa predicted that prices of chicken, beef, matooke, rice and irish potatoes would go up on New Year's day.
Chicken was sold between sh7,000 and sh15,000 at Nakasero Market. Turkey was sold between sh20,000 and 30,000 while beef was hovering between sh3,500 and sh4,000.

Original_Search_Title: A Night With IDPs
Headline: Uganda: A Night With IDPs
Body_Text:
Opit, Gulu — The bonfire cast the shadows of dozens of people against the walls of mud and wattle huts in the sprawling internally displaced persons' (IDPs) camp.
"The bonfire was the place where elders, the youth and women met to discuss solutions to problems facing the community," Milton Munu, an elder at Opit IDP camp in northern Uganda's Gulu District told the gathering. "But similar meetings were last held here 20 years ago. Because of insecurity, meetings around the bonfire that used to help the community close ranks have not been held since."
Another elder, Korina Odur, who broke down in the middle of her contribution, said bonfire meetings were the principal medium for informal education, where children were taught good behaviour and girls how to run homes. "For 20 years we have been denied this tool," she said.
Before inviting Jan Egeland, the United Nations Under-Secretary- General for Humanitarian Affairs, to address the meeting on Saturday, Munu narrated the history of Opit camp, 32 km southeast of Gulu town. The camp had been attacked 11 times by the rebel Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) before the Ugandan army deployed a permanent unit to guard it.
"We want to help you go home when you think it is safe to go," Egeland said. "We are seeking your views on what the international community can do to help."
The UN official had earlier arrived at the camp without much funfair. Some IDPs were surprised when he announced that he wanted to spend the night with them - becoming the first senior UN official to sleep at an IDP camp in the region battered by years of conflict.
Hordes of children, some barely dressed, followed Egeland as he toured the camp before dusk, walking carefully through the myriad huts and past rubbish that showed how dire the sanitation is in Opit.
Many demands
At the bonfire, the IDPs talked about the desire to return home, but many things were needed to facilitate the journey to their villages - for most, the first time home in years.
"There is no water. We need some food to go with, ox-ploughs and iron sheeting. The government should adequately deploy the police at every sub-county..." The list was long.
The main issue, however, was the role of the International Criminal Court (ICC) in the northern Uganda peace process. The court has indicted LRA commanders, including its leader Joseph Kony - a decision local people said could jeopardise the ongoing talks in the southern Sudanese town of Juba.
The talks have been enhanced by the promise of an amnesty to the rebels made by the Ugandan government if the rebels agree to a peace deal in Juba.
"The international community should tell the ICC that the people of Opit don't want their involvement because they are just holding back the peace process," Nixon Winy said, prompting Egeland to ask what should be done to those who have committed crimes against humanity.
One of the elders replied: "The amount of death in Acholi should not be equated to the pursuing of five LRA commanders," adding that the Acholi people had a system that was capable of delivering justice in the region.
Story time
Forty minutes into the debate, the meeting turned to one of the last items - stories that teach the children about society.
Egeland told one about a mouse that saved a lion from a hunter's snare. The lion had threatened to devour the rodent, which had promised to help the lion if it were spared. "However powerful you are, we need each other and can help each other," he concluded.
Then it was time for dinner. The meal, served on plastic plates, consisted of a local delicacy - cassava, potatoes and a mixture of green vegetables and peanuts. Soon Egeland retired to his hut where a mattress had been laid out for him.
For some on Egeland's entourage, including local and international journalists, the night would be long. Sleeping in a windowless school-room, they endured swarms of mosquitoes. "This area is mosquito-infested. Malaria is the biggest ailment here and it kills many children," Yolanda Olio, the nursing assistant at Opit Health Centre said.
After breakfast of tea and groundnuts the next morning, traditional dancers turned up to bid Egeland farewell. "Thank you for spending a night with us," Munu told Egeland, before the convoy roared away.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Samukai Destests Awarding Of Feeding Contract Of New Army To Foreigners
Headline: Liberia: Samukai Destests Awarding Of Feeding Contract Of New Army To Foreigners
Body_Text:
The Minister of National Defense has strongly detested the action of DynCorp to award the contract of feeding the 109 recruits of the new Armed Forces of Liberia to a Lebanese business firm.
Speaking to this paper yesterday at his Benson Street office when he was asked by our reporter regarding the information of a Lebanese business firm, the Palm Hotel being in charge of feeding the new AFL recruits, Defense Minister Brownie J. Samukai, Jr. replied in the affirmative, adding "my office has just received a confirmed report on the information."
Minister Samukai described the report as very unfortunate; noting that his ministry was not aware from the beginning that the contract to feed Liberian military personnel was awarded to the Palm Hotel. "If I had gotten that information prior to the training of the recruits, I would have resisted it in the strongest term."
Continuing further the Defense Ministry boss said, "the Ministry of National Defense is very disappointed that DynCorp would take such an action; and as the result the ministry would like to state emphatically that henceforth, that the feeding contract will have to be reviewed urgently so that, though we don't want the training interrupted, the next training that will take place at VOA, only Liberian company will provide catering services for the Liberian troops that are in service. DynCorp would do the selection, but it will be a Liberian company.
"The Ministry of National Defense will not accept any foreign company to provide feeding for Liberians in Liberia. The feeding of the AFL recruits would have to be done by a Liberian catering company. Liberians are capable of doing the job. We saw how Liberians provided catering services to about 14 thousands ex-combatants during the demobilization exercise," the defense boss averred.
Mr. Samukai who is also the chief of the national security disclosed that DynCorp contract with the U.S. government states that contracts such as feeding should be awarded to Liberians unless in a case where it is proven that Liberians are not capable to do the job, then a foreign company can be hired.
"Are you telling me that the potatoe-greens, cassava leaf, eddoes, palm butter, the eggs, rice, fish are being brought out of the country. These food stuff are being bought locally and can be prepared for eating by our people. We need capacity building in this country; and by awarding such a contract to Liberian companies will help to build their capacities. To cook our local food in Liberia should we give that to a foreign company, a big NO! The feeding of the soldiers will have to be done by a Liberian company."
Meanwhile Mr. Samukai has rebutted media reports that the Ministry of National Defense has a plan to bring in Ghanaians to be in charge of the Armed Forces of Liberia when the Americans shall have completed the army's restructuring and retraining program.
Mr. Samukai told this paper that the report was false and misleading; noting that at no time did he ever dream or think of saying such. "The report is absolutely untrue. We have a long standing agreement and understanding with the United States that the training of the AFL will strictly be in line with the training doctrine of the United States military. We have no plan to bring in Ghanaians to manage our soldiers. In fact we have plans to ask the Americans for more assistance.
