Like Kityo, many long-suffering pedestrians, residents and motorists keep on wondering whether there is any work going on at all. The section between Sports View Hotel in Kireka and Petro fuel station near Bweyogerere has for several months been closed.
Traffic is diverted to a ring road skirting Mandela Stadium. It is inconveniently longer and narrower - a black spot, that has witnessed several accidents.
The pace of work is slow and angry residents now have to walk long distances to board taxis. Desperate for some action, residents, businesspeople and commuters hopefully glance at the road construction site whenever they pass by. "We would be happy if everything were finished in a month!" Jane Nakato echoes a popular sentiment in the neighbourhood.
With rivulets of sweat already flowing down our brows after only 10 minutes of trekking, we spotted a group of ebony bodies glistening in the hot sun as they dug tunnels on either side of the road.
As we approached Kyaliwajjala, the level of activity suddenly went up. A gang of labourers rapped in Runyankore while making huge cement blocks for the bridges. They chattered endlessly, competing with the noisy motorised concrete mixers.
The labourers digging trenches looked exhausted, but didn't seem to mind working without protective gear like gloves, helmets or shoes. We continued with our trek.
Soon we were at the former Valley Point pub between Ntinda and Naalya. Time check; 12:30pm.
We stopped for a drink before resuming our trek, snaking our way between hills of murram and gravel awaiting deployment along the road stretch.
There were several 400-metre stretches of untarred gravel along the road. Developers are already erecting residential flats and houses on either side of the Northern Bypass.
One o'clock found us in Kiwatule. It is a miniature, normally quiet town, but it was now humming with a sudden burst of activity as parents picked their children from school.
Unplanned houses stuck out like sore thumbs, each facing whichever direction happened to catch the developer's fancy. We took a 30-minute lunch break in Kiwatule, during which we had a meal of poorly prepared matooke and bland soup.
Soon a pattern developed; every 30 minutes would bring us to yet another trading centre. In Kisaasi, we found the same driver whose load of murram had flagged us off in Kireka.
After dumping his load, he again craned his neck, as if to confirm it was the same faces he had seen walking in Kireka earlier in the morning. At least here there was a second lorry with a water tank at work, getting rid of some of the dust.
It is in the densely populated area between Kiwatule and Bwaise that residents have suffered the mother of all demolitions. The prayers said at the numerous biwempe churches along the bypass seem not to have helped. Many more buildings seemed set for demolition. They struggle to stay on their foundations, stagnant water licking at their crumbling walls.
The Government has spent about sh20b on compensating those who lost their land and houses. However, there have also been false claims. Some unscrupulous residents demand for as much as sh65m for structures that were not fit for human habitation.
Although the money is supposed to be split between the property owners and the landowner, who is the Kabaka, most people just pocket the money and disappear.
According to Dan Kitakule Alinange, the public relations officer for the Road Agency Formation Unit, the Ministry of Works has taken the initiative to sensitise the slum dwellers about AIDS too.
Apparently the campaign was a success; near Kawaala, we came across child playing with a used condom.
The stretch between Bwaise and Busega was one monotonous view of the long winding road with just the swamp on either side. What used to be a water-logged swamp has been filled with mountains of murram and gravel. This has forced the water to find alternative routes and left many homesteads swamped in dirty, stagnant water. Besides the murram, there were yam gardens and the occasional mud and wattle house as the road snaked its away through the swamp.
At Masanafu, between Bwaise and Busega, was a mountain of boulders. The road had been raised, leaving swamp on either side. Bodaboda cyclists were making a fast buck shuttling pedestrians from Bwaise to Nansana or Kisaasi at sh500 to 1,000.
Brickmakers were also enjoying a boom as property developers hurriedly set up residential and commercial structures.
By 6:30pm we could see the grey skyline in the distance at Busega, where the Northern Bypass ends a few metres away from the Busega roundabout on Mityana road.
Having come to the end of our trek, we sought out the authorities for a detailed explanation of what was happening, or rather not happening to the bypass project. Alinange had volumes to divulge.
"The Northern Bypass is on schedule and 84% of the work is done. Remember, it is part of the CHOGM security requirement," he explained. "The road is designed such that 17.5km of it is a single carriageway and the 3.5km between Hoima Road and Gayaza is dual carriage. The whole of it should have been dual carriage but there is a shortage of cash."
Asked when Ugandans should expect the road to be completed, Alinange confidently said soon. According to him, the contractor, Salini Construttori, is a reputable company with a good record.
"All the major requirements of concrete for the nine bridges has been done. There will be a bridge on Kawaala, Namugongo, Kisaasi, Kyebando and Old Kira roads, with two more bridges on Jinja Road and two on Bombo Road. Other than that there is one underpass and one foot path in Kiwatule," he said.
When we asked him why work between Bwaise and Busega was slow, Alinange laid the blame on the different nature of the terrain and vegetation there.
Without waiting for the bypass to be completed, entrepreneurs are already sniffing out strategic places where they can construct gas stations, plush residential areas, churches and schools.
But before they invest huge sums of cash, they would be advised to first consult with the authorities to avoid being fleeced by unscrupulous property dealers.
Compensation figures are shooting up. There are some areas that are densely populated, especially Kawaala, Bwaise and Kiwatule," said Alinange. "There is a risk of those already compensated selling their plots to innocent developers. They should consult us before any transaction is undertaken.
"Our laws need to be changed. In the last four months we were summoned by the Human Rights Commission and courts of law over complaints by aggrieved people. Cases drag on for ages. I call upon those aggrieved to come for an amicable settlement out of court," he added.
From the walk along the bypass, it is clear that people are gearing up for the development that a new highway is expected to bring. By connecting major routes, the bypass will serve as a key link for national and regional traffic. When complete, the route will carry 90% of Uganda's imports and exports.
It will also serve as an alternative route to traffic destined for either Burundi or Rwanda that currently goes through the city centre.
The idea for the Northern Bypass was conceived in 2000 to reduce the heavy traffic congesting the city centre. Currently, vehicles from the east of the country, heading to the west and the north have to go through the city centre, causing a permanent traffic jam. These include heavy trucks from Kenya en route to Congo, Rwanda and northern Uganda.
Construction work on the bypass started in May 2004 and is expected to be completed at the end of 2007.
The Northern Bypass will cross directly from the east of Kampala at Kireka to the west in Busega. Perhaps it is worth the wait.

Original_Search_Title: Carnival As Onitsha Celebrates Ofala
Headline: Nigeria: Carnival As Onitsha Celebrates Ofala
Body_Text:
Lagos — As it has become an annual event, the traditional institution of Onitsha in Anambra State recently celebrated its Ofala Day which was witnessed by the cream of the society from far and near. Eugene Agha who was at the occasion writes
On the bank of the River Niger in the eastern part of the country lies the ancient city of Onitsha, generally referred to as the cradle of civilisation to the Igbo man. This is born out of the fact that the city of Onitsha could be aptly described as the melting point of all human and commercial activities in the east of the Niger.
Recently however, both business and vehicular activities were brought to a halt as the people converged on the ancient Palace of the monarch, Imeobi, on Awka Road, to catch a glimpse of the Obi, Igwe Alfred Nnaemeka Achebe as he celebrates his fifth Ofala.
Indeed, the Obi had gone on a weeklong traditional retreat of spiritual cleansing on behalf of his people. The activities culminating into the Ofala day celebration commenced in earnest on Saturday evening of September 30. There after the Obi withdraws into seclusion, and prayers to God for the well being of his people.
The Obi also used the occasion to ask for blessing for his people and the improvement of the entire Onitsha kingdom. While the king is temporarily separated from his people, two things, according to the traditional institution of the people of Onitsha happens.
First, the king's warlords, traditionally known as the 'Ndichie Ume, supported by their able lieutenants will celebrate what the Onitsha man call Ogbalido. This is a feast of atonement and rejuvenation of the king. Towards the end of this, the Obi enters into direct communication with his ancestors
The two-day festival encapsulates the culture and tradition of the Onitsha people. Though the event has traversed many centuries, it marks the re-emergence of the monarch from a weeklong retreat. On the Ofala day, the Obi makes a historic reappearance to demonstrate to the world that he has survived another year and is hale and hearty to witness another New Year.
His emergence on the Ofala day goes to show the people that he had actually atoned for the sins and misdeeds of all the inhabitants of the area, failure of which means that he did not merit the throne, since it is believed that only a man who has made his way right can stand before his ancestors.
Igwe Achebe and his Ndichies, in the presence of visitors from far and near, celebrates this yearly ritual with pomp and pageantry, dressed in a multi-coloured cultural attire and accompanied by the members of his council of chiefs, the Obi jumped-dances his way into the arena in symbolic display of his emergence from the retreat.
Traditionally, the Ofala festival marks the 15th day of the new yam festival known as 'Owuwaji'. While the first day is mainly for the Obi and his red cap chiefs and Ndichies, the second day, also called Azu Ofala allows for wider participation by the people of Onitsha, age grades of different cultural groups and members of the Agbalanze.
From the Ime Obi venue of the ceremony on Awka Road to the major streets of Onitsha, it was a carnival of some sort. There was music, dance and lots of food to eat. Very early friends and well-wishers had besieged the Obi's Palace, adorned in different attires.
Earlier, the traditional drums were heard from all corners of the city by noon when the venue had been fully occupied, the drummers did what they know how to do best, adding a little drama into it to the admiration of the guest.
At about 1pm, the Obi made his triumphant entry, into the Palace, dancing round the venue while acknowledging the salute from his people. From time to time the shrill noise of people could be heard shouting "Igwe Agbogidi" while the Obi at intervals stops to receive wild applause from his people as the audience cheered in unison. After this initial display, the Obi retired to his stool.
Igwe in an address, expressed gratitude to God for another year well spent, even though Anambra State has had its own share of problems.
According to him, the past one year has been challenging globally as well as in Nigeria. In various parts of the world, he said violence and death continued to feature significantly in the lives of the people, particularly in Iraq, Afghanistan, the Dafur region of Sudan, Israel and Lebanon among others.
He explained that the world was also shocked by the attempt by some people to blow up in mid air several flights originating from the United Kingdom. While expressing shock, the Obi stated that it was absolute unbelievable that human beings would go to the extent of sacrificing so many innocent lives just to make a point.
Nigeria, he said had equally had its own share of sadness and grief "We were all shocked some weeks ago, by the air accident in Benue State resulting in the death of 13 senior officers of the armed forces. Late in 2005, there were also two air accidents with hardly any survivors," he added.
The Obi however, hailed the economic reforms of President Olusegun Obasanjo. According to him, the economic adjustment programme was making steady progress. He pointed out that the reformed telecommunication sector was yielding growing benefits to consumers.
He however called on the Federal Government to repair the Lagos Benin Expressway, which has consistently made life unbearable to millions of road users, particularly the Igbo who had always been on the road. The state of the road, he pointed out has an adverse effect on the nations tourism industry.
He also used the opportunity to welcome his visitors for making it to the ceremony. He appealed to both government and the people of Anambra State to put their differences behind and forge ahead.
"It is important for government to bring the message of the reforms programme down to the ordinary people through stakeholders, groups and opinion leaders. I call again on the people of Anambra State to rally together, sink our differences, forgive one another and join hands to restore our dignity and sense of community and commonwealth. We must look inward to ourselves for solution not to others," the Igwe emphasised.
Igwe Achebe commended the cooperation between the community vigilance service and the police for the improved security in his domain.
"Onitsha," he said, "is in the throes of change and transformation. We need to run fast enough to stand still. We appreciate the need to change with the world around us while at the same time preserving pure and innovative norms and features that mark us distinctly as a people.
"Our external challenge is to constantly seek the balance between our customs and traditions and the processes of modernisation. We are not unique in this quest," the traditional ruler stressed.

Original_Search_Title: Yes, Ukwuani Not Igbo!
Headline: Nigeria: Yes, Ukwuani Not Igbo!
Body_Text:
THE debate that flashed through the media recently on whether or not the Ndokwa people are Igbos filled me with amusement as the responses to Omenazu Ofili's piece were reminiscent of the proverbial goat who against all advice on the need for restraint and decorum to earn the respect of his prospective in-laws swooped on yam peelings and exposed his notoriety as a goat before his in-laws. Ofili had, in a well researched and brilliantly written article in the Vanguard of Thursday, September 21, 2006 on p.39, held down Mr Ikebudu for referring to his Ukwuani people as Igbo in the Vanguard of Monday, September 4, 2006 on p.40 in the course of which he cited selfishness and aggression as some of the innumerable mannerisms of the Igbo that could asphyxiate any right thinking man alluded to by Ikebudu, himself an Igbo.
The response to Ofili's piece, which came in rapids, attacked not just his person but his likes, thereby corroborating the aggressiveness he cited. Ikebudu himself fired the first salvo which pinned Ofili as belonging to a horde of mis-schooled persons (Vanguard, September 26), followed by no less a person than the reputable Pini Jason who while dismissing a most germane issue raised by Ofili as sterile considered it infra dignitatem to argue with his likes but nevertheless condescended to engage in the undignified act (Vanguard, October3).
Then came the stern-faced columnist, Nnanna Ochereome who while restraining himself from joining issues still generously threw a punch at Ofili for his timidity in denying his alleged Igboness (Vanguard, October 9). May we ask Ochereome where the bravery of the Igbo has landed them other than inflicting a defeatist siege mentality on them, which is often whipped up as the bane of the Igbo marginalization? Thankfully the respondents all succeeded in their choice of vocabulary to prove poor Ofili right on the issue of Igbo aggression. Pini stretched this proof a mile further when he accused Igbo Ohaneze of fossilized and greedy leadership blinded by opportunism.
Pini while dismissing the Ndokwa claim to a separate and distinct identity as politics rather than anthropology failed to show anthropological proof that Ndokwa is Igbo. Ochereome claimed to have made his point on this in the past with facts and historical records, which he failed to cite, thereby denying his submission of vibrancy. Ikebudu attempted to help out on the anthropological roots by stating that the mere fact that only the Igbo of all Nigerians can explain without prior tutorial the meaning of such Ukwuani names as Omenazu, Ndokwa, Ukwuani, Ndiolu and Ofili makes both people one. I beg to disagree with such pedestrian argument as it is capable of making General Ike Eisenhower an Igbo!
The Igbo, Urhobo and Ukwuani share the same word for "please", biko, with different stresses on syllables but does this make the Urhobo Igbo or the Ukwuani Urhobo? I have met an Isoko woman named Ikechukwu without any trace of Igbo root. Ikebudu would be shocked to learn that his Igbo translation of some of these Ndokwa names would differ markedly with the Ukwuani etymological understanding. There is no Igbo construction anyone would place on the word Ndokwa to give it the same meaning in Ukwuani to whom the name is an acronym. One would have expected these respondents to puncture the historical findings and submission of ebullient Dr S. N. Nwabara, himself an Igbo, in Iboland: A Century of contact with Britain 1860-1960 cited by Omenazu.
The fact remains incontrovertible that the Ndokwa or Ukwuani have never seen themselves as Igbo in spite of some level of lingual affinity. We should be bold enough to call a spade by its name rather than a garden spoon. We did not need a civil war, oil or politics of opportunism to change or reinforce centuries of perception. Neither do we need an English Professor Isichei married to an Asaba man to tell us who we are. A welter of colonial documents exist on our identity as Ukwuani or Ndosumili, the two administrative districts which constituted Aboh division at a time when there was neither war nor oil to prompt any agitation for separateness. Without meaning to be derogatory it is offensive to a freeborn Ukwuani man to be referred to in Ukwuani lingua-cultural setting as Onye Igbo. Though we suffered the brunt of the civil war for our shared affinity with the Igbo, we resolutely maintained that we are a different people and both during and at the cessation of the hostilities we needed not change our names to prove our separate identity like Ochereome alleged the Ikwerres did.
Onye ndidi new mmeli
Victory belongs to the patient
No tu nau bu osukpo
Nwa ugo ne yi ugo ni ucha
Nnunu fefuni ni adamgbugbu beli ni mkpomkpo
The bird that flew
Onye ejikemene eka aba ni onu
Ucha ne gbu nji
Onye eshine nne osa ele osa
Madu amali mili ne zue funi okoshi aboa
Onwu egbune chi dili
When there is life there is hope(99 sayings)

Original_Search_Title: Oshwal Academy's Shah Shines in Age Group Gala
Headline: Kenya: Oshwal Academy's Shah Shines in Age Group Gala
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Sharmee Shah of Oshwal Academy Nairobi starred in the Carnivore age group swimming championships, breaking two national records at the Aga Khan High School.
She won the 10-11 years 100m individual medley in a time of 1min 23.18sec to lower the old mark of 1.23.22 set by Nasra Shehan in 2002.
Shah, 11, broke another national record when she won the girls' 10-11 50m backstroke preliminary round in 38.27sec.
She later won the final round of the event in 38.81 which, despite being lower than her national mark, was a meet record.
Kanyali Ilako held the previous record of 39.56. Shah again won the 200m individual medley in 3.04.31 and the 100m backstroke in 1.21.99.
"I didn't expect to break the national records, but I feel great to have done so," she told Sportfest yesterday.
Shah's coach Ken Mudogo commended the swimmer for her exemplary performance."She has shown improvement this season. We hope to get her to the next world junior championships," Mudogo said.
Swimmers who were in the national team to the African Open Championships held in Senegal last month won several events.
Pina Ercolano from Hillcrest Secondary School won the girls' 16 and over 100m individual medley in 1.19.33, followed by Whitney Pragassa of St Austin's Academy who clocked 1.24.16 and Nairobi Jaffrey's Naila Dass in 1.24.66.
Ercolano also claimed the girls' 16 and over 50m freestyle in a meet record time of 29.84. Rui Yam ashita held the old time of 30.87. Dass was second in 31.18 with Eva Donde of Hillcrest third in 32.40.
Shafiq Manji from Hillcrest was first in the boys' 16 and over 100m backstroke, winning in 1.19.01. Manji also won the 100m breaststroke after clocking 1.19.01.
"The race was not tough because there was no competition in the over-16 category," said Manji.
Martin Kenny from International School of Kenya (ISK) returned 1.25.36 to finish second with David Mawende of St Mary's third in 1.27.19. Jonathan Boffa from ISK won the boys' 12-13 100m individual medley in a record time of 1.11.85.
Joshua Oruya from Brookhouse was second in 1.18.97, with Theo Sotiropoulous, also from ISK, third in 1.21.76.
Oruya had earlier won the 12-13 100m backstroke in 1.28.55. Suhayl Meghji from Aga Khan (1.35.64) and Hillcrest's Charles Gathuru (1.35.86) were second and third.
Soraya Oruya claimed the girls' 10-11 100m individual medley in 1.25.51 followed by Shah (1.26.41) and Loreto's Sellah Makotsi (1.27.51).

Original_Search_Title: Igbo Man Can Become Lagos State Governor - Royal Father
Headline: Nigeria: Igbo Man Can Become Lagos State Governor - Royal Father
Body_Text:
Lagos — EZE Ndigbo in Amuwo Odofin local government Area of Lagos State, HRM Anthony Elui, has predicted that an Igboman can become Lagos State Governor, if Ndigbo themselves are united in such a quest.
The royal father also said that Ndigbo daughters like former Finance Minister, Dr. (Mrs.) Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, and Director-General of National Agency for Foods, Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC), Prof. Dora Akunyili are capable of ruling Nigeria, given their track record of performance.
Elui made this known at a recent celebration of the New Yam Festival in his palace at Amuwo-Odofin.
"With one voice, Igbo can be governor of this Lagos State because, we have 50 per cent of the population of this state", he claimed.
He, however, said Ndigbo would support a God-fearing candidate in next year's election, who has passion for them a man of the people; that would respect Igbo tradition, culture and values".
Elui, who said that Ndigbo generate about 50 per cent of the internal revenue of Lagos State maintained that the ethnic group would back a candidate who would award scholarships to its students and provide jobs for its graduates.
"Having representations in government through appointments as members in boards of parastatals of the state government, appointment as commissioners, if not, an Igbo Deputy Governor of Lagos is not too much. Enough is enough for using us as use and dump tribe", the royal father stressed.
He promised to mobilise Ndigbo in Amuwo-Odofin to [participate in the on-going voters' registration exercise noting that "2007 election will no longer be selection but election for the interests of poor masses as our voter's card is our power".
While lauding Ndigbo for their industry, adventure, and resourcefulness, Elui urged them to eschew unnecessary bickering, greediness and selfishness to maximize their potentials in the country.
High point of the celebration was the cutting of yam tuber by Elui, while different dancing groups entertained guests at the event witnessed by other royal fathers.

Original_Search_Title: Prosecutor Refuses to Reopen Journalist's Murder Investigation
Headline: Burkina Faso: Prosecutor Refuses to Reopen Zongo Murder Investigation
Body_Text:
Reporters Without Borders has said it was stunned by the Burkina Faso public prosecutor's refusal to reopen the investigation into the 1998 murder of journalist Norbert Zongo and three companions on the grounds that the document the organisation gave him on 20 October 2006 did not constitute new evidence as defined by article 189 of the code of criminal procedure.
Public prosecutor Adama Sagnon announced at a press conference on 21 October that the original, unedited version of the report issued by an Independent Commission of Enquiry in 1999, which Reporters Without Borders had given him on 20 October "does not contribute anything new to the case." Attorney General Abdoulaye Barry described it as "information that is already known."
"It took eights years of judicial investigation to drop all charges, and just one night to decide not to reopen the investigation," Reporters Without Borders said. "Burkina Faso's judicial system clearly functions in a strange way. The prosecutor's lightning decision was evidently not the result of serious reflection, but rather a hasty, clumsy and politically-influenced response."
Reporters Without Borders continued: "The prosecutor's interpretation of the code of criminal procedure is astonishing and biased, to say the least. The information in the report we gave the prosecutor showed that the president's brother, François Compaoré, and businessman Oumarou Kanazoé lied to the independent commission. It also showed that the Zongo murder was closely linked with murder of François Compaoré's chauffeur, David Ouédraogo. And it revealed how and why Kanazoé and the then prosecutor, Dramane Yaméogo, tried to silence Zongo prior to his murder."
The organisation added: "Nothing so far shows that any serious attempt was made to shed light on these facts. We therefore insist that they are liable to 'strengthen the accusations that have already proved too weak' or to 'contribute new developments useful in establishing the truth' - the conditions for reopening an investigation."
The version of the Independent Commission of Enquiry's report which Reporters Without Borders gave the public prosecutor on 20 October was the original one, the one the commission drafted before it was toned down on the insistence of two of its members who represented the government.
The final version completely eliminated certain passages about the contradictions in François Compaoré's statement to the commission, and Kanazoé's attempts to silence Zongo prior to his murder. The original report's conclusions were also much more assertive and specific, identifying "six main suspects," all members of the presidential guard.
According to article 189 of the code of criminal procedure, after investigating judge Wenceslas Ilboudo's decision on 19 July 2006 to dismiss all charges against the leading suspect, former presidential guard chief Marcel Kafando, the case can only be reopened if there are "new accusations" liable to "strengthen the accusations that have already proved too weak" or to "contribute new developments useful in establishing the truth."

Original_Search_Title: Buhari And the Hand of God
Headline: Nigeria: Buhari And the Hand of God
Body_Text:
Kaduna — Ask any Buhari basher why he doesn't like Buhari and you would be surprised at his reasons. I have tried it many times.
The latest was recently when I visited a pounded yam joint somewhere in Garki village, Abuja. After my meal, I engaged the proprietress in political discussion centering on the now dead third-term agendum. The woman didn't seem to like the idea of another four years of Baba-Iyabo but could not think of a better Nigerian who could take over. When I suggested Buhari to her, she said she would never vote of Buhari.
And when I asked her why she wouldn't want to, since it is an undisputed fact that the Buhari/Idiagbon regime was one of the most purposeful Nigeria had ever had, she claimed that the credit being attributed to Buhari when he was Head of State was actually supposed to be attributed to his late deputy, Gen. Tunde Idiagbon. I then asked her whether Idiagbon was at PTF when Buhari headed it. Not having an answer to give me, she said she just wouldn't vote Buhari. I left her at this juncture feeling pity for her. I felt pity because it is a pitiful thing to see someone live a life of opinions formed without reason.
But my pity could probably have been misplaced. This woman might have had a reason she could not fathom to give. Such reason could probably be centered on the news item carried by Thisday newspaper on General Buhari on the eve of the 2003 political campaigns alleging he asked Muslims not to vote non-Muslims in the coming elections. I think this is the crux of the matter with the common man who says he doesn't like Buhari. I hold Thisday newspaper liable for this deliberate falsehood by its Reporter against the General and I think they need to make amends if they were ab initio not acting the scripts of some exploitative elitist forces in the polity. For it is only among these forces that I think there is iron-cast dislike for the General and even this is on selfish survival grounds. This group dislikes the general because he represents the opposite of the totality of the crassness it stands for.
I doubt if there is any person living that can build a case of indolence, vice, deceit, immorality, lies, corruption, ungodliness and what have you against the General. Even the most morally bankrupt Nigerian, when he pontificates, doles out the right qualities that are expected of a president in the present day Nigeria. And you would think he is referring to Buhari. Yet it baffles me when I hear the common man says the kind of things my pounded yam woman said against Buhari. If it was a conspiracy by the forces of darkness as characterized by some of our politicians, the media and the clique that holds the national economy by virtue of the less than transparent privatization programme, to paint Buhari a religious bigot, then they have, to some extent, succeeded. However, it must be clear to the kin observer that this conspiracy was solely due to the fear by this clique, of the qualities in Buhari, that have set him apart from the typical Nigerian politician, that make him truly a man of the people. If this were not so, how could one explain why Zamfara State Governor: Alhaji Sani Yarima the progenitor of "political" "Sharia and now an ANNP presidential aspirant is not regarded a religious bigot by this clique?
Those, whose base instincts on religious sentiment have been inflamed by this clique that have held us hostage since 1999, should be wiser by now. They should have by now seen through the deceit, greed, insensitivity and ungodliness of this clique. It is a truism that lies, deceit, greed and their siblings do not bear fruits. It is now self evident that the contradictions between what this ungodly clique espouses and what they say are now manifest in their ever increasing dislike among themselves. They are at each other's throats and knowing that God does not play dice, those of us who have reported to Him, their transgressions against us, are now content that there is an Omnipotent design unfolding which is akin to what happened in the hallowed chamber of the Senate when the third-term Senators could not open their mouths during the voice vote. For those knowledgeable in the Holy Qur'an, there are portions which stated something like: "Allah had sealed their lips". It is my belief now that God has equally caused commotion among their ranks such that they cannot unite to cheat us like they have done in 2003.
Nigerians are living witnesses to the campaign trails of General Buhari before the 2003 presidential elections. They have seen how popular the General is among the ordinary and not so ordinary Nigerians. They have equally witnessed the sham called the 2003 elections and the subsequent court case that spanned over two years with a culmination in the political judgment given by the Supreme Court. What is of interest is that General Buhari has shown his stoicism and strength of character to the eternal shame of his detractors. He has shown he is a democrat per excellence, highly disciplined, meticulous and principled. Above all, he has shown he is a man of peace. Had he wanted, he could have ignited an upheaval of unimaginable proportions in the national body polity. All he needed to do was ask the masses to troop out onto the streets and make the country ungovernable for those who stole the polls. He, in stead, chose to leave the matter in the Hands of God and now God has started manifesting His Omnipotence in ways the polls and treasury looters cannot understand.
Since 2003 these marauders in government have not known peace. And surely peace will continue to elude them until they pay for the sins they have committed in their transgression against the Nigerian masses. They will continue to be bereft of the wisdom of how to maintain the power they have so glaringly stolen. They will continue to fight each other till the day they finally see the Hand of God at play. That would be the day they would stand stupefied and open-mouthed just like the day the third term agendum was killed in the Senate. It would then dawn on them that all their scheming, their pride, their arrogance was allowed by the Omnipotent just so they could be roped in by their ever-ballooning vanities. And it would be too late and impossible for them to do anything about the Buhari phenomenon because if they try it, they would be wrestling with the Hand of God.
Therefore, for those who stand by the truth and those who feel repentant about their past, standing behind the General in this crucial times when only an exemplary leader suffices is a duty to their conscience. If they ponder hard and long enough, they would know who amongst the multitude of presidential aspirants that have emerged or about to, has the truest of all the qualities Nigeria currently needs as a Leader.

Original_Search_Title: Rev King Turned Me to a Sex Machine - Witness
Headline: Nigeria: Rev King Turned Me to a Sex Machine - Witness
Body_Text:
Lagos — A devotee of Christian Praying Assembly (CPA), Miss Kelechi Chikere yesterday alleged that her pastor, Emeka Ezeugo alias Rev King not only turned her into a "sex machine," but forced her to drink his sperm as covenant rite.
She also gave hint on how the embattled clergyman allegedly set fire on six members of CPA that subsequently led to the death of Ann Uzoh King.
Ezeugo is on trial on a six count charge of murder and attempted murder before Justice Olubunmi Oyewole of a Lagos High Court, Ikeja.
Testifying on her personal experience in court, Chikere who is the fourth prosecution witness said she was brutalised during her stay with Rev King.
"He (King) presented me as holy to outsiders and church members but brutalised me and had sex with me at will. In fact, on the morning of the fateful day, he beat me. He has iron rods, sticks and other tools he use in beating people.
"He once threatened me that he would kill me if I ever leave his church or house. He placed me under covenants. "The first covenant was in 2003 when he ordered me to suck his penis and drink his sperm and I did because I had no choice.
"He told me that is the covenant and repeated it for more than one week while he kept reminding me of the covenant. "I had series of abortion for him and even on the fourth occasion, the doctor informed me that I already had an infection in my womb."
Still on her amorous relationship with the embattled clergyman, Chikere said she had been living with the clergy for the past five years after being introduced to the church for a night vigil by her sister in 2001.
The University of Lagos undergraduate who said she used to be called Queen King when Rev King "married" her in the presence of two church members and divorced her again within two weeks after her mother refused the bride price (N200,000) sent to her (her mother) through her (Kelechi's) sister.
On the alleged burning of three CPA members, she said that on July 22, 2006, Rev King was very angry after accusing six church members of fornicating with one another, and after beating them mercilessly with anything he could lay his hands on including knives, rods and stool, he then ordered her to bring fuel.
"The accused person ordered me to get fuel and I quickly went outside, saw a big blue plastic basin and poured the fuel inside." Then he ordered all of them out beside the gate and asked me to pour the fuel on them which I did because nobody dares disobey his orders.
He asked me to get the matches too and when he struck the first and could not catch the fire, he struck the second and threw it at them.
There was confusion and everybody ran towards the boys' quarters. Ann and Kossy were badly injured. Ann could not escape because she was wearing a nylon textured night grown.
"She burnt from head to toe and the accused dragged her to the water tap and later poured palm oil and vegetable oil on her," she narrated.
While cross-examining the witness, the defence counsel, Mike Okoye reminded her that she had lied in a court of law (Magistrate Court) before and so there is no possibility that she couldn't be lying again but she debunked it, saying she had to lie on oath at the court on the order of Rev. King as he had already instructed all the witness in the said case on what to say.
Also at the court to testify as the fifth witness before the court was the father of the deceased Ann Uzuh King, Raphael Uzu who testified that Ann told him at her hospital bed before her death that Rev. King ordered fuel to be poured on them while he lit and threw it on her and some other people.
Uzuh recalled that his daughter packed out of her house upon gaining admission to the university and told him she would be living at CPA where he could come if he wanted to see her.
"She packed all her belonings including pictures after collecting N24,000 from me and bidded me good-bye. "I was there one day and after I was being denied entrance into the compound (6B Canal View), two members of Lord's Army beat me up and said I must not come there again.
"I decided to join the church just to be seeing my daughter at every worship day since I didn't have access to her. She was my only daughter (crying).
Uzuh told the court that when he saw his daughter on the hospital bed just like a roasted yam, he broke down crying while Ann held his hand, apologised and said "Daddy, Rev King had done his worst. He ordered fuel to be poured on us and threw a lit match at us."
According to him the pastor never came to his (Uzuh's) house to inform him of (Uzuh's) daughter's death which he later heard the news from his son Ifeanyi.
"I just prayed then that the murderer will marry her in death and bury her dead body."
He was cross examined by Okoye. Trial continues today (Thursday).

Original_Search_Title: There's Much Ignorance About LG Administration - Ogbu
Headline: Nigeria: There's Much Ignorance About LG Administration - Ogbu
Body_Text:
Igumale — The Carekaker Committee Chairman of Ado Local Government of Benue State is Hon. (Chief) Dennis Ekpe Ogbu. A graduate of Business Administration, he has variously been Speaker, Ado Legislative Council, member, State PDP Exco and elected Chairman of Ado. In a recent interview with SIMON IMOBO-TSWAM, at Igumale, the Ogaku K' Idoma gives an account of his stewardship, among other issues. Excerpts:
You were an elected council chairman and now, you are an appointed chairman. What is the difference? Are there any noticeable operational handicaps?
There is no noticeable difference and there are no noticeable operational handicaps that I know. It's just that when you are elected, you are certain of the extent of your tenure. That gives you the confidence to plan, to make policy or programme projections within definite time frames. But now, I have no defined tenure exactly and, therefore, I don't have the luxury of making long term plans.
But then, everything has advantage. I am now more conscious of service delivery. I want to impress my immediate constituency sharp, sharp since, as they say, no one knows tomorrow. There is no laxity at all. And I may add that may be because one is appointed, one tends to be closer to those who appointed you.
Now, being "closer to those who appointed you," does it extend to the level of being so close that, as it is sometimes rumoured, they take away your allocation through this deduction or that, and then you are "too close" to complain?
Don't let me laugh. There is so much ignorance flying about that you pressmen have to help us educate the people. The fact remains that whether you are appointed or elected, there are certain responsibilities that are statutorily basic. And there is no debate over this. These statutory responsibilities include local government pensions, local government service commission, training funds etc. Don't forget also that the salaries and allowances of traditional rulers are there.
These are the deductions that the Ministry for Local Government and Chieftaincy Affairs (MLGCA), our supervising ministry, irons out with council chairmen, whether elected or appointed, at the monthly Joint Account Meetings. These are statutory responsibilities of councils, but ignorant people go about, saying the ministry is doing this or the commissioner is doing that. So in essence, it's not only what people know that they talk about. And this is not too good.
So, what has it been like, heading the tier of government that is closest to the people?
It's been very challenging. When I came in 2003, I inherited some major problems. There was the royal and non-royal crises. There were sharp, very sharp dichotomies, the we-and-them mentality. I swung into action, seeking peace, reconciling people, securing peace and sustaining it. Securing the peace was one thing, another was taking care of and rehabilitating those displaced or dislodged. We resettled them at Apa. The thing is when they were dislodged in Agila, the people returned to their kinsmen in Apa - they are the same people, we had to embark on the massive opening of 23 roads and bus culverts to the New Layout at Apa. All this, to enhance their resettlement.
And then, there was a long-drawn out communal/boundary dispute between my local government and Aha-Okwu (Ngbor-West) local government in Ebonyi state. We attended joint meetings both here in Benue and in Ebonyi. In fact, on one of the trips back after a joint peace meeting here, the Chairman of Ngbor-West, the leader of the legislative council and one other person died in an auto-crash on the Ebonyi side. Thank God, all the drestructions and the needless blood-lettings are history now. A joint mobile patrol team keeps watch on the common border as we await decision of the National Boundary Commission on the crises. The Methodist Bishop of Otukpo and his Ebonyi counter-part intervened on the side of peace. And I must mention the State Governor, Dr. George Akume - he was and has been most supportive of the peace efforts and the general well being of our people. Besides giving Ado people all the support , he also sited one of the cement companies at Igumale. For over a year now, my council has been hosting 14 Chinese experts, they are working round the clock on the cement company. In addition to two imposing structures on the ground, we have just taken delivery of 10 containers as we await others. When it is commissioned, it would provide jobs for our youths and step up economic activities in the area.
One recalls that when your tenure came to an end several months back, none of you wanted to go. The general belief is that those of you with powerful godfathers were re-appointed, and the orphans, so to say, were sent home.
Well, I will speak for myself. I was re-appointed based on three things: the grace of God, the Governor's discretion and my performance. And my local government is not a place where you will go about shopping for a godfather. There are too many big men. It is the home of David Attah, former Press Secretary to Gen. Sani Abacha; Prof. Ochapa Onazi, a former Vice Chancellor and, Gen. Sunday Ifere. Others are, Mr. Dan Agbese, Deputy Managing Director Newswatch; Cols Ernest Attah, a former governor, Col. J. P. Ubah, Justices George Uloko, former Chief Justice of Plateau state and Justice James Ogebeh; Chief Enyi Obande, Dr. John Ochogo etc. There is also Prof. John Adikwu, who just won the Nigeria scientist of the year award. There are too many big men, so to look for a godfather is to look for trouble. So, if I have any godfather at all, He is God Almighty, and may be, possibly, the Governor himself.
So what exactly did you do to be favoured with re-appointment?
I just mentioned peace and security. These are very important for development to take place. In infrastructure too, we have tried to justify public confidence in us.
I was one of the first chairmen that embarked on rural electrification. We brought electricity to five council wards via nine giant transformers. The Commissioner for Local Government and Chieftaincy Affairs, Hon. (Barr.) Emmanuel Udende, is coming to commission them in December. We also built two prototype clinics, one at Etenyi in Igumale Ward 1 and the other at Ndekma i.e. Utonkon. Both are awaiting commissioning. The council has opened five health posts in various communities to enhance primary health care delivery. And we have purchased and supplied drugs to them via a counter-part arrangement with Partnership for Transforming Health Systems (PATHS). We have actively campaigned against HIV/AIDS and carried out council-wide immunization campaigns.
We renovated the council secretariat and built a fortified block for the Finance Department, - We have only one bank (UBA) at Igumale. We have constant monthly sanitation exercises in and around the local government. There is so much that we did and are still doing.
You have done so much, at least, one can see the Modern Finance Block, the renovated Administrative Block etc. So why does the impression subsist that you chairmen are just sharing allocation and generally having a good time at the people's expense?
I just told you that what appears on the pages of newspapers as allocations do not come to chairmen in block. But people who don't know go to town crying wolf. And again, performance is a relative term, and many who say we are not performing mean different things. Then, take note that our largest involvement at the grass roots is largely unseen, for 24 hours, people are there for you. They are sick, they are hungry, you can't blame them, there is so much poverty. A snake bites somebody, his son has no school fees, his in-laws want to take away his wife it's the chairman that they run to. We attend weddings, burials, thanksgivings, launchings you name it. By the time you've attended to all these people, much of the money is gone, but you can't quantify it, you can't see it and you can't show people or journalists. But then, development is about people. But the snag is that even the people that you help, if for one reason or another you don't sustain it, they may turn round to say: ah! this chairman is a failure - he has not performed.
But in my case, the people have been very cooperative; I am so thankful to them. Like the people of Agila, they printed shirts, their chief, Chief Ochoga led them, they came to thank me, with yams, cows, fanfare. They called it: Ekpe, Thank You, Visit. And then, on July 6, this year, the Idoma Traditional Council, under HRH, Agabaidu Elias Ekoyi Obekpa, conferred a chieftaincy title on me: the Ogaku K' Idoma i.e. A man of influence, a man of word and action. So I thank God for everything.
If you had more time on your hands what would you do?
There are still four wards without electricity. These are: Ekle, Ijigbian, Roungo and Vlai. When we gave light to the other wards, things changed instantly, businesses sprang up: barbing/hair dressing saloons, weldings, electronic shops, video rental clubs etc. The youths are engaged at Utonkon, Igumale and Agila. They have never seen light throughout their history, but now, they are using it to better their lives.
Given more time, I would love to give light to the other wards too, and possibly seek assistance to construct the Otukpo-Utonkon-Igumale-Agila road. In fact, this should rightly be a Federal Road as 20 km further on, you connect Benue with Ebonyi State. And since it passes through the disputed area, it would take care of the boundary dispute too.

Original_Search_Title: Neglect of War Museum
Headline: Nigeria: Neglect of War Museum
Body_Text:
Lagos — AS national war museum Umuahia, Abia State, not a Federal Government Institution or Project? This question has arisen because, in my mind, the museum does not look like what a rich nation such as Nigeria could build or an institution that belongs to a responsible nation.
Nigerians, especially those who garner the nation in their hands, politicize everything and situation on hand, whether it is reasonable to do so or not, it does not matter. Otherwise, the museum should have been contracted and regularly maintained to reflect the commitment by Nigerian government to the immortalisation of the Civil War in which millions of people perished especially the zone that saw the war.
The museum is a very important national monument that symbolises bloodshed for the struggle of self determination as well as to keep Nigeria one. But its poor architectural design and inadequate maintenance, did not show total commitment by the Nigerian government that build the all important historic institution.
It is a relic of the thirty months devastating war that was waged against the former Eastern region by its compatriots for seceeding, to hold their destine in their hands.
Most people, who have not visited the place, may have painted the picture of the museum in their respective minds as a cynosure where people must always crave to visit.
The road to the place has no street lights and not even wide enough to befit such important and historic monument of a nation. The signpost is not big to be sighted and it is mounted in an obscured view from the major road.
One may not believe that the whole of the place is open to the view of passersby, criminals can easily jump over the wall of fence into the premises and perpetrate evil, because the fence is not high enough to cover the premises from the view of passers-by as well as protect the war equipment.
Besides, the war planes that were damaged during the war, Ogbunigwe and warship NNS Bonny are exposed to the direct heat of the sun and rain without roof over them, for protection. The ship, now used as restaurant, is not even refurbished regularly and there is no attraction to the museum as something historic.
Judging from the nation's abundant wealth, the museum can be transformed into a world class tourist attraction and could generate funds.
In fact, many things are going wrong in this country because of deep rooted tribal politics. Those who have been ruling Nigeria since the end of the war in 1970 wanted every development in their own areas at the detriment of other zones.
The moment those who garner the nation into their hands begins to see politics, governance and development in Nigeria beyond ethnic barriers, the rancour in the society would drastically reduce and an enduring peace would reign.
National War Museum, Umuahia, is an important national institution, and for the Federal Government to remember the need to build such relics of the War makes it important. Therefore, government should in its wisdom maintain it adequately to prove that Nigeria is really the giant of Africa where crude oil flows inexhaustibly like the sea.
And government's lukewarm attitude towards the museum may give the impression that government has built the place in bad faith just as President Olusegun Obasanjo accused the old Imo State of building Imo Airport in bad faith therefore does not merit upgrading to the status of a standard Airport.
Nigerians should not allow sentiment or ethnicity to over-ride our sense of judgments in any national matter. Therefore, no matter our tribe and tongues may differ, we should try to do what is right to be done and stop politicising issues of national interest.
Obviously, I do not have the clear view of the nation's direction, whether or not, it moves in the direction of a united, strong and reliable Nigeria where affairs would be treated equally. Also, I cannot see a united, strong and reliable Nigeria if our leaders continue with the policy of divide and rule against ourselves and of grab-grab attitude for only their zone to benefit from the national development.
If it is deliberate by the Federal Government to neglect the war museum or by omission, the wrong could be corrected to maintain and expand the museum to include research centre. And our leaders should also try to encourage things that will unite us than things that would divide us, if we really want an indivisible Nigeria.
A situation where some sections of the country monopolise development by riding at the back of others, and the policy or segregating other sections from tasting the political power in the same country is like a house that divide itself, and a house that divide itself must fall.
This is the situation in Nigeria which must be addressed immediately to actually proof that we fought the Civil War to keep Nigeria one where nobody would be short changed and oppressed. If we insist on our winner takes all attitude, then the peace which Nigeria is trying to achieve may elude us, and it will prove that the Civil War was not actually fought to keep Nigeria one but for something else probably because of the oil.
I hope the current reforms by President Obasanjo would also address the issue of divide and rule as a political tool to weaken the former Eastern region for easy manipulation.
We must be deceiving and cheating ourselves if we continue to politicise development in any part of the country because that part of the country where development is politicised is not alien.
I believe Nigeria would be quiet when justice and equity become the order of the day. Also people may not be denied so much of their right when the centre is decentralised of enormous power it control.
The Nigerian Government should not see the issue of the war museum as a favour to the South-East but some thing of great importance to the nation of Nigeria and what is worth doing is worth doing well, therefore the museum should be given proper attention by the government so that it can create more job opportunities.
In fact, the overall condition of the place is not satisfactory as a symbol of bloodshed because both the attackers and defending Biafrans, lost their lives therefore government should not treat the museum with levity because it concerns bloodshed.
If the museum is well constructed and maintained, it is Nigeria that would receive the honour and commendation, and if it is not, the same nation Nigeria would be criticised.
Also, if the Federal Government encourages even development and industrialisation in every zone of the country, all sizes of business will grow and unemployment would reduce.
Subsequently, poverty would be alleviated; the rate of crime will reduce too, while people will be happy because many must have been empowered to fend for themselves.
But if our leaders continued to restrict development and industrlisation to a particular section of the country without spreading it to all nook and cranny, there will be no hope for enduring peace in the land.
I believe that the Federal Government can redesign, expand and maintain the museum to create employments for Nigerians.
Time has come for leaders of this country, especially the bigger tribes that are controlling the nation, to understand that time changes everything. Without equal opportunities in all Federal Government organs, including police, armed forces, ministries, parastatals as well as even development the nation will continue to wobble.
The time for one section of the country to hold onto power and development has elapsed and we are in a new era, therefore we should allow old things to pass away for a new dawn. So that every section of the country will have a sense of belonging for the nation to move ahead than one or more groups to hold the yam and knife, to cut to others at will.
The museum is a child of circumstance and one of its kind in the country. Therefore we should put sentiment apart and give the museum the attention it deserves, so that it would not make mockery of the nation that has abundant wealth but fails to spend it where necessary.
If the museum should be measured with Nigeria's wealth, then the country would be a laughing stock before the international communities for building such under-rated and inadequately maintained museum.
Apart from books which some people have published and distorted facts, there is no structure on ground to remember the war except the museum.
Nigerian government has been reluctant since the end of the war to establish its presence in South-East and the entire Eastern region through provision of infrastructure for development.
Afterall, East, especially South-East, did not challenge any group for war, it is Nigeria that brought war to the West to forcefully bring them back to one Nigeria against their will, therefore it is not justified for Nigerian government to maltreat the East.
The issue of museum is just one of the many cases indicative of the main objective of fighting to keep Nigeria one. We have assumed that God does not want Nigeria to break, and if God does not want Nigeria to break up but to build a united Nigeria, why must our politicians and leaders chose to go their own way by doing things that would divide us more than things that must unite us.
Don't we know that problem we are having in this country is because we nurse animosity and hate each other and leaders want only their people to enjoy certain position and development minimal to others.
This is contrary to God's desire of what He wants the nation to be, and why must the nation prosper.
My advice to Nigerians and the politicians is to drop hatred, animosity and all kinds of things that would divide us and focus on things that would unite.
Above all, justice and equity should be the watchword. No ethnic group shall be short changed, development and industralisation should spread in all the corners of the country so that every ethnic group would have equal opportunity for employment. Big and small businesses will grow to drive away poverty in the society.

Original_Search_Title: NEMA Lauds Bayelsa Over Bakassi Returnees
Headline: Nigeria: NEMA Lauds Bayelsa Over Bakassi Returnees
Body_Text:
Lagos — The National Emerge-ncy Management Agency (NEMA) has commended the Bayelsa State government over the humane and kind manner it handled the affairs of Nigerian returnees from Bakassi and Cameroun.
The Director-General of NEMA, AVM Mohammed Audu Bida (rtd) made the commendation while delivering relief materials to the State government to compliment the efforts of the state in the upkeep of the 10,333 returnees.
Represented by the South-South zonal coordinator of the agency, Mr. Martins Osuwah, the director-general stated that the tasks involved in the rehabilitation and resettlement of people have always been challenging all over the world.
He said the case of the returnees are more pathetic as some of them have left their communities for several years and rehabilitating them to adjust to their native land is an onerous task which the state have tried to achieve with reasonable success.
The director-general revealed that when Nigerians were repatriated from Cameroun, only 5,633 were evacuated to Yenagoa but the figure increased to the present number due to the migration of Bakassi residents to the state.
Sequel to the state's request to the President for Federal Government intervention, as the state capacity could no longer cope with the upsurge, AVM Audu Bida stated that the following items were approved to aurgument the efforts of the state, they are 600 bags of rice, 600 bags of garri, 300 bags of beans, 2000 tubers of yam, 200 (m)20 litres jerry cans of vegetable oil, 100 bags of salt, 1000 pieces of blankets, 1000 pieces of towels, 1000 pieces of mats, 1000 pieces of mosquito nets, 1000 pieces of bucket, 100 detergents, 100 cartons of bath soap.
NEMA had recently repatriated over eight thousand Nigerians from Cameroun and handed them over to their respective State Officials for eventual reintegration to their communities.
Receiving the items on behalf of the State Governor, Hon. Chubby Benwahsen, and the special Assistant to the Governor on Humani-tarian Affairs stated that the state government gave ten thousand naira each to every returnee, but this, he said, is grossly inadequate to provide reasonable succour to the returnees thus the request to the Federal Government.
The Governor sought for time to resolve some logistic problems before conveying the materials to the respective local government areas for direct distribution to the returnees by NEMA and State officials.

Original_Search_Title: Gov't to Address Youth Unemployment, Says Aliu Mahama
Headline: Ghana: Gov't to Address Youth Unemployment, Says Aliu Mahama
Body_Text:
THE VICE President, Alhaji Aliu Mahama has said that Government is taking steps to tactfully address the problem of youth unemployment in the country.
He therefore stressed the need to re-orient the minds of the youth towards securing jobsin the private sector, which he noted, is vibrant and offers a lot of self-employment opportunities to the youth.
He observed that the recent launch of $50 million micro-credit scheme to provide capital to small and medium scale enterprises would definitely enhance performance of small-scale businesses in the private sector and described it as a, "God sent opportunity," which the youth needed to take advantage of to make life more comfortable for themselves.
The Vice President, who was speaking at the 76th annual Yam Festival of the Chiefs and people of Leklebi Traditional Area at Leklebi-Duga in the Hohoe District of the Volta Region, said while Government was taking the relevant steps to secure jobs for the youth, they should complement such efforts by being disciplined and resourceful.
"Any successful person in life needs to be disciplined, respect the elderly, protect state property, work hard, behave decently at home, school and in the society, which are attributes that we all endeavour to inculcate in the youth, he noted."
Alhaji Mahama said the major challenges facing the country in recent times included how to improve upon the quality of life of the people particularly good health and food, better education and healthy environment, how to reduce poverty among the people, more employment opportunities, greater individual freedom and a richer cultural life style among others.
He said the health status of the people in a country would determine their level of productivity and capacity for wealth creation, hence the increased access to quality health service by introducing the National Health Insurance Scheme to remove the financial barriers to health services.
The Vice President emphasized that one of the pillars of Government's development strategy was through human resource development by means of an effective educational system.
He said that was why the Capitation Grant and the School Feeding Programmes were introduced to prevent parents from giving reasons why they failed to send their wards to school.
Alhaji Mahama has also directed the Ministry of Education, Science and Sports to assess the needs of the Leklebi Secondary School and make provisions in the 2007 budget to address pressing issues, particularly in terms of infrastructure. He also directed the GETfund Administrator to provide the school with a pick-up truck early next year to help improve on teaching and learning in the school.
The Vice President also challenged students and pupils to learn hard in order to justify the huge investment Government and their parents were making in them, as well as to protect school properties, which he pointed out, could be done when they were disciplined.
He announced that Government, in its 2007 budget, would tar the road from Logba-Vunta to Leklebi-Dafor through Liati to Hohoe to open up the numerous tourist centres in the area, which also serves as link roads to the Republic of Togo.
The Paramount Chief of Leklebi Traditional Area, Togbe Agboka VI commended the people of Leklebi for their communal spirit and urged citizens of Leklebi living abroad to contribute meaningfully towards development projects at home.
Those present at the function included, the President of Volta Foundation, Mr. Raymond Okudjeto, Volta Regional Minister, Hon. Samuel Kofi Dzamesi, DCE for Hohoe, Hon Peter Amewu, Hon. Kofi Mensah Damentia, DCE for Krachi-West, D.C.E for Jasikan, Hon. Solomon Donkor, President of the Volta Regional House of Chiefs, Togbega Gabusu VII and his Vice,Togbega Delume VI, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Wetland Row Intensifies
Headline: Uganda: Wetland Row Intensifies
Body_Text:
Kampala — ANGRY residents of Kirundu, Buzibira village in Gaba parish have vowed to resist the encroachment on Kyetinda wetland by army veterans.
Hundreds of residents yesterday confronted the veterans' leader, Lt. Fredrick Walakira and nearly manhandled him during the meeting with area MP Hussein kyanjo and Mak-indye Division leaders at Kyetinda wetland, on Wavamuno Road.
Last Thursday, about 50 veterans embarked on clearing the 2sq.km wetland, about 400 metres away from the pumping system. The system also doubles as a storm water treatment area and a flood regulator for Gaba, Kansanga, Buziga, Kirunda and Munyonyo.
Mike Muwanga, son of the late O.K. Muwanga, the director of Mentesori Nursery and Primary school, said the land partly belongs to the Muwanga family, which they got in 1965. They gave away part of it for the construction of the community centre at the Gaba Mission.
Residents who have been using this place for their livelihood for decades shed tears.
Ssemakula Nambi, an old woman, said, "At the age of 25, I became a widow; sold sugarcanes and yams using this wetland to educate my children. The last one is at the university studying medicine. How do these veterans expect us to survive? Let the government intervene."

Original_Search_Title: Nakasero Soap Works Closes
Headline: Uganda: Nakasero Soap Works Closes
Body_Text:
Kampala — NAKASERO Soap Works, one of Uganda's oldest factories, has closed shop after failing to put up with stiff competition in the market place.
Effective September 18, the soap-making Nakawa-based company, known for its soap brands of Ngoma, Makasi, Nina and Yam Yam sweets, laid off its more than 60 direct employees and another 1,000 indirectly linked to the factory will lose business.
In a letter to employees on September 16, which Daily Monitor has seen, the Managing Director, Mr Suresh Tanna, cited stiff competition as the main reason for the 41-year old company's closure.
"The last few years have been extremely difficult. In the case of soap, competition has been extremely severe with selling prices below our production costs. We have tried very hard to compete in order to retain our market share but in doing so, we have lost a lot of money," he said.
"It with great regret and sadness that I have to announce that as from 18 September, 2006 we will be ceasing operations," Tanna said in the one page notice.
He also said cheaper imports of sweets from Kenya, China and India, coupled with rising costs of the main raw material (sugar) also killed their sweets business.
The move marks the first major bold decision by a company to publicly announce a closure, following recent economic hiccups that the country has faced such as the rising costs of operations due to electricity shortages.
Tanna said the current power problems as well as increases in fuel prices combined with increased transportation costs from Mombasa to Kampala had had their toll on his factory. But he said all the employees would be paid their terminal benefits.
In 2005/06 financial year, Nakasero Soap Works paid Shs715 million in revenue to the Uganda Revenue Authority.
When contacted for a comment, the Executive Director Uganda Investment Authority Dr Maggie Kigozi regretted the development.
"Given the fact that they have the oldest type of machinery where they have to use a lot of energy compared to their competitors and with the current power crisis they were left with no choice," she said.
The Chairman of Uganda Manufacturers Association Abid Alam said he was aware of the development, adding that the association is yet to study the matter.
The factory's main competition is from cheap imported products and Mukwano Group of Companies and new entrant, Bidco Uganda Ltd.

Original_Search_Title: Nakasero Soap Works Closes
Headline: Uganda: Nakasero Soap Works Closes
Body_Text:
Kampala — GREATER competition in the soap manufacturing sector with the entrance of BIDCO has claimed its first victim with the closure of Nakasero Soap Works Ltd (NSWL).
The shutdown of the former makers of soap and Yam Yam Sweets has rendered over 60 workers jobless.
Suresh Tanna, the managing director, cited increased competition, power problems and rise in fuel prices as some of the reasons for the closure.
"It with great regret and sadness that I have to announce that from September 18, 2006, we will be ceasing operations.
"Certain activities will continue for a short period of time. During this period, we will only need minimal staff.
"The purpose of this message is to explain to you why we are closing the factories after so many years of operations," Tanna said in a September 16 communiqué to staff.
NSWL, one of the oldest soap makers located at Mbuya Hill in Kampala, was producing at half capacity at 30 tonnes per day from about 60 tonnes previously.
The company's installed capacity is 80 tonnes a day, Tanna told reporters on Saturday.
He said the company has been employing over 60 people but will maintain 30 workers for few months as it winds up activities.
"We fully intend to honour our contractual and legal obligations in terms of termination payments to all employees.
"We will also try to help staff members with recommendations to other potential employers," the communique said.
The factory was started in 1945 by Tanna's father but was taken over during President Idi Amin dictatorial regime but was repossed in 1992 from the Lint Marketing Board.
"In the case of soap, competition has been extremely severe with our selling prices below our production costs.
"We have tried very hard to compete in order to retain our market share but in doing so, we have lost a lot of money," Tanna said.
Edward Isingoma, the chief accountant, said in an interview that by June 30 this year, the company had recorded a net loss of sh1b.
Regarding sweets, Tanna said cheaper imports from Kenya, China and India crippled their operations while raw material prices for sugar and other items were rising.
"Common to both operations are the current power problems as well as increases in fuel prices combined with increased transportation costs from Mombasa to Kampala," he said.
Isingoma said unless the Government intervenes, more factories were set to close.
"We believe something is not right regarding regulation. Some competitors could be avoiding taxes.
"We believe the Government should come in and save other manufactures because we are the fourth factory to close after Avis, Kengrow and Mbale Soap Factory which closed and has just resumed operations recently," said Isingoma.
Officials said two attempts to meet Uganda Investment Authority boss Dr. Maggie Kigozi to salvage the situationwere futile.

Original_Search_Title: Watching From the Sideline
Headline: Nigeria: Watching From the Sideline
Body_Text:
One of the hardest things I had to endure during my hospitalisation was to watch the whirligig of events pass before me like a blurry charade without my intervention. My isolation from events as they unfolded was made worse by some of the text messages readers were sending me, pointing out issues that were dear to them, on which they solicited my comment. Imagine that from a man who used to complete five laps of the outer perimeters of the National Stadium most Saturdays, I was reduced to a man who was applauded for shuffling the distance from my hospital ward to the reception. In such a condition, you can't but feel helpless as the world outside the hospital was spinning dangerously out of orbit. Two issues of the time stand out for mention because they have become what newsmen call running stories.
One is the saga of hostage taking in the Niger Delta. Between February and May, I wrote series of articles lamenting what I saw as an obfuscation of the real struggle in the Niger Delta. I drew attention to what I called many strands of the violence in the Niger Delta, some of which were clearly criminal. I also condemned what the British Government aptly identified as "glorification of terrorism". In the case of the Niger Delta, it became a "glamourization of terrorism".
Many champions sprouted out to justify hostage taking. If you didn't spit fire or add to the tension in the Niger Delta, you were not a patriot. The erroneous impression was made as if the Nigeria military was no match to the insurgents, thus egging the youths on to even more criminal violence. Yet some leaders were calling on the government to withdraw law enforcement agents from the area as if Niger Delta had ceased to be part of Nigeria.
Meanwhile, many people, from quarters we least expected were cashing in and making fortunes out of crime in the name of "Niger Delta struggle".
My hunch was confirmed the day an Ijaw taunted me with a text message, to the effect that I was envious of the newly found good fortune of the Ijaws. Now is the time of the Ijaws; may be someday it would be the time of the Igbo, he gloated!
I knew then that many people in that region have missed it, as far as solving the problems of the Niger Delta was concerned. It was clear that the Niger Delta struggle had become a matter of money in private pockets.
My view then and now is that if you start rationalising the criminal violence in the Niger Delta with the issue of the perennial neglect of the region, you lose me. The reason is that not one Nigerian is unaware of the problems of the Niger Delta. That the three tiers of government in the region, including the intervention agency, Niger Delta Development Commission, have not found the appropriate strategy for sustainable development in the region did not mean that we all did not understand the problem.
Therefore, I argued, violence was futile. If there were a few who did not know about Niger Delta, they now know. Therefore, I argued, we needed to move to the next level, which is the peace option, under which the region can be developed. When on 18 April the President inaugurated the 50-man Council on the Niger Delta, he was applauded. But what did we see? Those who had made a career out of hostage taking could not take it. They shifted demands and invented more excuses to continue in their criminal acts.
Today we are being told of illiterate operators of the racket having up to N200 million in their accounts. We are being told of people in some state governments being the minds behind the crime. Now that the SSS has cracked down on them, they are on the run. Those who glamourised the invincibility of the hostage takers no longer sing that silly song. We must see the current crackdown in the interest of the Niger Delta, because most of the contractors who will work on the projects approved by the Federal Government have foreign workers on their staff. They could jolly well refuse to work in the Niger Delta. And people should desist from exaggerating this oil thing.
There is more to development than natural resources. As I reminded the chap who taunted me about his good fortune, Sam Mbakwe airport, the forsaken stone that is today the pillar of aviation to the East and Rivers was not built with oil money! It was a product of determination, which more important than oil!
Another issue is the running battle between the President and his Vice. First, I must make it clear that I am all for measures to clean our corrupt society. Those who only see vendetta, selectiveness and witch-hunting in the anti-corruption drive surprise me. They have forgotten that those millions and billions bandied about is our money.
And I feel outraged that some people would appropriate what belongs to all of us just because they happened unto public office or they know people in high places. Since some of these revelations, I have developed two emotions. One is that, first, I get angry that some people do no other job than denying us water, light, good roads, public transport, health facilities and regular jobs by hanging around people in power.
Another is that before any moneybag impresses me, I want to know that he worked hard for his money and not by fronting for public officials.
But having said that, one is worried by the dimension the fisticuff between the President and his Vice is taking. Can we wedge this anti-corruption war without a collateral damage to the nation?
It seems to me that the two combatants are no longer the proverbial "two elephants". They have become two Samsons, each at the opposite end of the temple, trying to pull down the roof. And those who comment must understand the nuances of the issues at stake. I was dumbfounded when a senator opined that the EFCC ought to have charged the Vice President to court. The constitutional means of trying an indicted President, Vice President or Governor is impeachment. However, if the conclusions of both the EFCC and the Administrative Panel set up by the President, are what the President sent to the senate, those who see that as impeachment notice under section 143 of the constitution are wrong.
The National Assembly, not the President, is the one empowered to initiate notice of impeachment. May be the President wants the National Assembly to initiate an impeachment based on the information sent to it. Until then, people should not jump into conclusions or preempt the National Assembly. Let us wait for the next move, now that the Deputy Senate President Senator Ibrahim Mantu and Deputy senate Leader, Dr Jonathan Zwingina have had pounded yam lunch with the President in Ota. But what is important is that our leaders must not bring down the roof on all of us.

Original_Search_Title: How I Left University as Over-Priced Job Seeker - Prof Fajemirokun, Ex-UNILAG DVC+
Headline: Nigeria: How I Left University as Over-Priced Job Seeker - Prof Fajemirokun, Ex-UNILAG DVC+
Body_Text:
ALL he wanted was to have a first degree which would enable him earn a good living and start enjoying his life. But, unlike his mates, the school authority (University of Nigeria, Nsukka) did not allow him to go. Reason: He made a first class!
