While he was imagining driving his dream car and working in his air-conditioned office, his department was imagining how its first class product could be made into a first class lecturer.
"When I was entering the university, I almost vowed that I was not going to go beyond first degree, particularly during my last year in the school because I was fed up. In those days, the first thing you do, on graduation, was to get employed, it was automatic, that was in 1966. The car I even wanted to drive was already in my head, I was dreaming of it, so I was just praying for the last examinations to get out of the system and get a job because I wanted to enjoy my life. I could hardly wait for the day I would graduate from school," he told Sunday Vanguard Lifestyle.
But, without his knowledge and consent, the department forwarded his name, on American government scholarship, to an American school, for his master's degree. He finished the programme in record time and proceeded for his Ph.D, which, according to him, made him out of reach in the labour market. His words: "When I got my Ph.D, it made me an over-priced commodity in the labour market. In those days when you have a Ph.D, there is nothing else for you to do because you have already out priced yourself in many establishments except for you to go back to the university. So I returned to the country and to the then University of Ife in 1971 before I came here (UNILAG) in 1973 and ever since then, I have been working here." Professor Afolabi Francis Fajemirokun, until two months ago when he retired, was the deputy-vice chancellor, University of Lagos.
Beyond academics, Fajemirokun is also an Anglican priest. In fact, he became one almost 10 years ago. Don't be surprised, missionary work runs in the family. His late father was a priest, one of his siblings is also one. "It is true that I am in the ministry but being there, for me, was just a matter of time, it was a matter of when was it appropriate for me to go into the ministry.
What I decided to do while growing was that I was going to obtain all the necessary academic qualifications I could lay my hands on and that I would get to the peak of my career and God helping me at the appropriate time when He calls, I would heed to His calls and it worked out. I went to the university, obtained three degrees, years later, I became a professor, that was in 1982. Ten years after I became professor, I decided it was time for me to help others spiritually and I am still in it till date. But I have always wanted to work for God. If there is anything I love to do most, it is to be in His presence".
His entry into spirituality, naturally, has increased the number of men of God and, correspondingly too, the number of churches. Are there still men of God in the land?, I asked my host. His reply: "There is no gain saying the fact that there are far more crimes and vices at present time and correspondingly, there is increase in religious activities. The problem with religion is that it can be superficial, very superficial. Jesus Christ once told the Pharisees and Sadducees that religiosity was their target and serving God and I want to believe that with the fact that you have mentioned that the motivation of all of people is to make money, that is true but it is not the only thing.
In other words, there are groups that are actually trying as hard as possible to influence people positively through religion. Besides, if we should say because of the increase in the number of churches and vices at the same time, therefore we have not made gains, I don't think that is right. I would want to disagree on that. Souls are being won for Christ even in some of the churches that are money oriented and that is the beauty of it because, the Holy Spirit can empower anybody at anytime at any place to become a child of God. So, the truth of the matter is that you cannot throw away the baby with the bath water. The fact that the baby is bathed with bad water does not make the baby bad. The water may be bad but not the baby. We need a lot of soul searching in this country."
If religious bodies make so much money like they are doing now, is it now high time they are made to pay taxes? "That again, taking off from where I left, is like throwing away the baby with the bath water. We believe that money is raised in the church so as to further propagate the work of God and we believe that if everyone were to be a christian, the world would be a better place. If everyone were to be a child of God, then the world would be a better place and Jesus Christ said "my word would be preached in all the corners of the world". In other words, people would be given the opportunity and choice for salvation but whether one takes advantage of it is another matter entirely. So, to be in position to do all that, you need to raise money in the churches and if in the course of doing that, some organizations turned into a personal use, it is all well and good, they have to answer to the Almighty. It is between them and God. So, I don't think that is a sufficient reason for government to now insist that we should be paying taxes. Of course, the churches are doing something that would help even the government because once you have a morally upright society, the problems of government reduce", he said.
Fajemirokun is a good cook? He is so good at it that the children preferred his cooking to Dorothy's, his wife. "That was some years back when I was still active in the kitchen", he said with a long laughter. He continued: "I was on sabbatical in California, I didn't take my family with me, I cooked my meals, and even when they eventually joined me, the children preferred my cooking to their mother's, that tells you volume but now it will be stupid for me to carry plate to the kitchen and start cooking when all of them can conveniently do that. But I can give recipe of any kind of food you want to cook.
The reason for this simple. In those days, there was no difference between the boys and the girls. Our parents would give all of us the same kind of work. I grew up in Ilesha, my father was a missionary, a priest of the Anglican church. We lived in the vicarage because both the school and the vicarage were in the same compound. So, there was no reason for us to go outside except we were sent on errands, we were all living together including my father's children and relatives. The boys would cook, pounded yam and do so many things, and I think that was what helped as one began to grow because there was and still virtually nothing I can't do when it comes to cooking, yes up till now I can still cook".
Talking about prostitution and immoral behaviour, there is a lot of that in our higher institutions of learning including his former school, University of Lagos. Fajemirokun says he cannot agree less. "You are right. In those days, it used to be just one way, it was the men that sexually harassed, but there is the other side of the coin now, that is, the female students want to show themselves because of one or two reasons, either that they are ill prepared or they don't even know the course or they are unqualified to read the course in the first place and so they try to entice. But I must say that in the final analysis, the fault must be on all sides.
"If a girl tries to entice you, it is your responsibility not to get enticed. If a man tries to force himself on you, I believe it is your responsibility not to yield to it and there are ways to ensure that it does not happen. I always tell students that if you are a good student, if you are not weak, it is going to be difficult for a man to insist on having something to do with you before you pass but if you are dangling around F,E, that is, the last pass grades, and you appeared as if you need help to want to get closer to the lecturer, that teacher, depending on the kind of up bringing he had, may want to take advantage of the bad situation. But, it is still your responsibility as a morally upright lady to refuse and to refuse to the last and if the worse, comes to worst, you bring it up to the head of the department or dean of faculty. If a lady does not want, she really does not have to".
What if the temptation is irresistible? I asked him. "You have a choice, haba. Even if a woman is naked before you, it does not mean you do anything. If you are properly brought up, you will be decent. The worst you can do is to send her away, 'get out of this place, you are not properly dressed'. Not that because you have been offered, therefore, you take it like a beast, because that is what the beasts do. Human beings must be a little more discretionary which makes the difference between us and animals"

Original_Search_Title: Nkonya, Alavanyo Paramount Chiefs Committed to Peace
Headline: Ghana: Nkonya, Alavanyo Paramount Chiefs Committed to Peace
Body_Text:
Kpeme — THE PARAMOUNT Chief of Alavanyo Traditional Area, Togbega Tsedze Atakora VII, has pledged on behalf of the chiefs and people of Alavanyo to protect and defend the hard-won peace enjoyed by the people of Nkonya and Alavanyo to help accelerate development in the two traditional areas.
The traditional ruler said chiefs from both traditional areas were fully committed to peace and unity, as it was the only tool relevant to promote development in any community, and urged the youth in the two areas to learn from the cordial relationship that existed between chiefs and elders, particularly the paramount chiefs of the two areas.
Togbega Tsedze Atakora made the pledge at a congress durbar where an educational endowment fund was launched for the Alavanyo Traditional Area at Alavanyo-Kpeme in the Hohoe district of the Volta Region.
The durbar was attended by the paramount chief of Nkonya, Nana Okotor Kofi III, and other chiefs as well as a large number of youth from Nkonya, who also contributed towards the endowment fund to further deepen the peace process in the area.
Togbega Tsedze Atakora was however unhappy about the attitude of some drivers in the area who refused to ply between Alavanyo and Nkonya, saying such behaviour was retarding the peace process even though the youth from both areas attended social functions in any Nkonya and Alavanyo community.
He assured those drivers that the current peace prevailing in the two traditional areas was not imposed on them, but was a decision that was arrived at by the chiefs and youth from both parties through the support of the mediation and consultative committees and assured the drivers that nothing would happen to them within the territories of Nkonya and Alavanyo because violence was a thing of the past to both areas.
"Any of you can hold me and Nana Okotor Kofi responsible if anything happens to you in our areas," he challenged. "I want to inform you that Nana Okotor Kofi has given me tubers of yam for the celebration of this occasion, which I need not to say in public but I do so to reveal to Ghanaians the extent of unity and peace in our area."
Contributing, the paramount chief of Nkonya traditional area, Nana Okotor Kofi III, commended Togbega Tsedze Atakora for his efforts to deepen the peace process in the area and urged the youth from both traditional areas to love one another and forget about what happened in the past and forge ahead in unity for the development of the areas.
He said he and the paramount chief of Alavanyo had since been living as brothers and asked the youths from both areas to learn from their leaders and see each other as brothers and sisters because the future of the two traditional areas largely depend on them, stressing that there could only be a bright future for the youth if they regarded the past as unfortunate events and pledged not to repeat it.
Nana Okotor Kofi was particularly glad that the youth of Alavanyo and Nkonya have rich ideas and talents, which could be harnessed for the common benefit of the two traditional areas.
He observed that the youth could now attend school in any community of the two traditional areas as well as freely participate in social activities like funerals and other outdoor games, which were vital in ensuring total peace.
A Member of the Council of State and the paramount chief of the Dodome traditional area, Togbega Kpangbatriku III, launched an educational endowment fund on behalf of the Volta Regional Minister, Mr. Samuel Kofi Dzamesi.
In a speech read for him, Mr. Dzamesi commended the chiefs and people of Alavanyo and Nkonya for making peace among themselves, noting that it was difficult for people who once lived under constant fear and threat to live together, celebrate and dance at a function.
"It takes people like Alavanyo and Nkonya who know the effect of conflict to reunite themselves," he lauded. "You have shown the whole world that you are matured and capable of solving your own problems".
Mr. Dzamesi said now that the wind of peace could be felt in the areas, government could now start developing the areas, and that the road network would be tackled first to help open up the place to investors for accelerated development.
The Regional Minister noted that education was the bedrock of every society and commended the chiefs and people of Alavanyo for the establishment of the education endowment fund.
He noted however that the establishment of the fund was not enough unless parents supported in the upbringing of their wards to ensure holistic education of the children.
Togbega Kpangbatriku presented a cheque of ¢5 million on behalf of the Volta Regional Minister to the paramount chief of Alavanyo to support the fund.
Contributing, a member of the mediation committee and the Bishop of the Ho diocese, the Most Rev. Francis Lodonu, said the absence of peacekeepers in the area was a clear indication that the people were in control of the peace process and commended the two paramount chiefs and the youth from both traditional areas for the maturity shown in maintaining peace.
The two paramount chiefs who sat by one another and earlier embraced each other on the arrival of Nana Okotor Kofi II to the durbar grounds to demonstrate to the youth that there was strength in unity.
There was a football match between Nkonya and Alavanyo ladies at Alavanyo-Kpeme to show their commitment to the peace process.

Original_Search_Title: 'The Sun's Rays Have Become Strangers To Our Eyes'
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: 'The Sun's Rays Have Become Strangers To Our Eyes'
Body_Text:
Abidjan — To get a sense of the problems besetting prisons in Côte d'Ivoire, look no further than Building C of the House of Arrest and Correction of Abidjan (Maison d'arrêt et de correction d'Abidjan, MACA).
The 115 detainees crowded together in this building share just one toilet, and barely manage a daily shower. MACA, the largest prison in southern Côte d'Ivoire, is home to over 4,000 prisoners -- even though the 26-year-old facility was built for just half that number, says Director Toha Ouattara.
Two or three residents of Building C can be seen sharing an ear of boiled maize or a braised yam, bought for about 24 U.S. cents. This is the amount of money allocated to buy daily rations for detainees in Ivorian prisons, says one of the guards at MACA.
"Those who are lucky are visited by relatives or friends who bring them good meals every day," the guard told IPS. However, certain prisoners claim that some of this extra food, such as meat and fish, is often confiscated by guards, who take it for themselves.
One of the detainees, Abou Sanogo, has already served five years at MACA for voluntary homicide. Walking in the prison courtyard, he struggles to lift his head. "There is no light in the cell. Sometimes we go for three months without leaving, and when the occasion (to walk) presents itself, the sun's rays have become strangers to our eyes," he told IPS.
The poor condition of prisons in Côte d'Ivoire has come under fire from several quarters.
Love in Action (Amour en Action), a non-governmental organisation (NGO) headquartered in the financial capital of Abidjan, has issued a reminder that detainees have rights which must be respected; but, numerous complaints to authorities about the state of jails have elicited no results, the group noted.
Paris-based NGO Doctors Without Borders (Médecins sans frontières) has also described prison conditions as inhumane, as has the United Nations Operation in Côte d'Ivoire (UNOCI), in a report published last month.
Between June 2004 and July 2006, 475 deaths were noted in prisons, with MACA having the highest death toll (64), indicated the UNOCI study.
Most of the deaths resulted from malnutrition, although some appeared suspicious. Cases of degrading treatment were common, said the report; this could take the form of chaining the feet of two to three detainees together.
U.N. forces help patrol a buffer zone between the rebel-held north of the country, and the government-controlled south. Côte d'Ivoire was divided in two four years ago, when soldiers took up arms to fight against the alleged exclusion of people living in the north.
Of the eleven prisons that were operating in this area before the rebellion, ten are still functioning, according to UNOCI. However, the conflict has taken a significant toll on the facilities, including Bouaké Penal Camp: the largest prison in the country, which catered for long-term prisoners and Ivorian gang leaders.
Bouaké, in the centre of Côte d'Ivoire, is also the headquarters of the rebels -- known as the New Forces (Forces nouvelles).
"In the absence of any judicial system, the prisoners are judged by those with authority in the New Forces. They can be detained as long as these persons (the rebels) want," said Françoise Simard, one of the authors of the UNOCI report, which identified 295 detainees in northern and western jails.
The rebels are also said to have freed prisoners after starting their campaign against the government, to serve as combatants.
Matters are scarcely better in the 22 jails in government-held areas, where over-crowding is rife: meant to house 3,371 prisoners, they currently house almost 9,300 detainees, notes Ouattara.
According to justice ministry estimates, some thirty percent of detainees (almost 3,000 prisoners) are still awaiting trial.
A large number of these detainees should not even be in jail, says John Rose of the UNOCI Unit of Judicial and Prison Affairs. However, "the slow pace of judicial procedures (and) lack of sessions of the Courts of Assize (criminal courts) for several years" stand in the way of their release.
UNOCI notes that funding for prisons decreased slightly last year, despite the substantial needs of jails.
"It (the prisons budget) must be increased as soon as possible for improving the conditions of detention of prisoners, and also working conditions for prison staff -- without which there is a risk of collapse of the judicial system," warned Simard.
The situation is not completely without hope. The Ministry of Justice says it is giving thought to increasing the daily rations of prisoners, and has undertaken repairs to certain jails.
A computer system to assist with management of detainees is also being gradually installed, this under the auspices of a project to fight against abusive detention initiated by Paris-based NGO Prisoners Without Borders (Prisonniers sans frontières). The project is supported by the European Development Fund.
But, enormous challenges remain, as the situation of a guard interviewed by IPS in MACA's Building B, attests.
Dressed in a dirty combat uniform and worn-out shoes, carrying an old submachine gun, the guard was found dozing. "There are not enough of us to have shifts," he noted, adding that the prison was not secure: "We always have problems preventing prisoners escaping."
While prisons require a staff complement of about 1,370 people, they currently have just 700 officers, according to the UNOCI report. Some 3,660 prisoners escaped between June 2005 and April 2006, noted the document.

Original_Search_Title: Chieftaincy Must Serve As a Development Tool
Headline: Ghana: Chieftaincy Must Serve As a Development Tool
Body_Text:
Nana Mprah Besemuna III, Krachiwura has said the chieftaincy institution has evolved in contemporary times to become a tool for socio-economic development and should no longer be an avenue for litigation.
Speaking at the annual Kabrebi Yam festival of the chiefs and people of Anyinamae in the Krachi West District, Nana Besemuna said a new dawn of development was emerging, which demanded the mobilisation of both human and material resources to meet the aspiration of the people.
Nana Besemuna therefore urged chiefs to be always there for their subjects and the government and champion the preservation and protection of their cultural heritage, values and norms.
Mr. Joseph Nayan, Deputy Volta Regional Minister, appealed to chiefs to co-exist and live in peace.
He said government would continue to invest in its citizens in order to tackle the shortcomings and the developmental imbalances.
Mr. Nayan mentioned the capitation grant, which had increased school enrolment, the Ghana School Feeding Programme, the Ghana youth Employment Programme and Community Policing as areas for consideration.
He advised the youth to shun immorality, illicit drug and substance abuse and live above reproach to guarantee them the future.
He told the people that the District Assembly election was non-partisan, urging them to come out to vote on September 12.
Mr. Kofi Mensah Demetia, Krachi West district Chief Executive, called on drivers and fuel station operators to abide by the principles of the activities or face rigors of the law.
He appealed to the people to embrace the National Health Insurance Scheme for a guaranteed healthcare delivery.
Mr. Francis Osei-Sarfo, Member of Parliament for Krachi West, said festivals had become platforms for development and stocktaking and urged the people to unite.
Nana Dandanba II, Chief of Anyinamae called for the provision of amenities including the construction of a landing port at Anyinamae by the district Assembly in collaboration with the Volta Lake Transport Company Limited.

Original_Search_Title: Chief Tells Community - Don't Let Politics to Divide You
Headline: Ghana: Chief Tells Community - Don't Let Politics to Divide You
Body_Text:
Nana Obiri Yeboah Kokroko II, Tuntuhene of Techiman Traditional Area and chief of Bamiri in the Brong Ahafo Region has urged his people not to be divided by partisan politics.
He told them to remain united and concentrate their efforts to initiate development programmes.
Nana Obiri was speaking at his palace at the end the annual Munufie yam festival in the area.
He expressed concern about how partisan politics had created factions among the people in parts of the country and expressed the hope that such "a calamity" would not befall his people.
Nana Obiri advised the people to register with the National Health Insurance Scheme and appealed to parents to invest in the education of their children. With the government's capitation grant to schools, no parent, he said, should allow a child to stay at home during school hours.
Nana Obiri advised the youth to take their education seriously and shun social vices like alcoholism, hard drugs and pre-marital sex.
"As future leaders of Bamiri, you should live above reproach and acquire high knowledge that would enable you to continue with the development of this community and for the benefit of posterity", the chief added.
Mr. John Kwasi Kwao, Assembly Member, commended the people for their support and cooperation throughout the 12 years that he had been in office.
He paid special tribute to the chief, elders, other opinion leaders and members of the unit committee for their support.
Mr. Kwao urged the people to extend such love, support and cooperation to his successor.
Nana Yaa Agyapomaa, queen mother, expressed regret about the low level of female education in the area and called on parents to support and encourage their girls to pursue higher education.
She advised them to minimise their expenditure on clothing and social activities and rather invest their resources in the education of their children.
"Parents should monitor the activities of their children so they can check them against falling into the hands of bad and irresponsible peer groups", the queen mother said.

Original_Search_Title: Talking Point - Between IBB And the Vatsas
Headline: Nigeria: Talking Point - Between IBB And the Vatsas
Body_Text:
Until the bubble burst in late 1985, December to be precise, the relationship between Ibrahim Babangida, self-appointed President and dictator, and Mamman Vatsa was no less than could be expected between brothers. When Vatsa, then Minister of the Federal Capital Territory and an army general like Babangida, was getting married, Babangida played the role of bestman. And if we are to believe the words of Safiya, the woman who has had to bear the brunt of widowhood in the wake of the falling-out between the erstwhile friends, IBB ate, slept and changed his clothes in the Vatsa home. Indeed no part of this home was out of bounds to Babangida.
Such was the relationship between them that, even Safiya, believed the two couldn't be anything other than blood brothers. But in an ironic and unexplainable twist of event Babangida had or, as he would like to put it, was saddled with the unenviable and truly horrendous task of signing his friend's, nay brother's, death warrant.
Everyone aware of the Byzantine character of the intrigues that rule the everyday existence of an idle, pepper-soup suffused soldiery, knows that the punishment for contemplating, not to mention planning a coup, is death. This high crime Vatsa was alleged and finally found guilty to have committed. And Babangida, the 'President' at whose table the buck for punishing such crime stopped, discharged (not without some unbearably agonizing thoughts that left him sleepless, he claims) this ugly duty, made uglier by his connections to the accused, like a true soldier.
Such was the case then that the man who, to paraphrase Jibrin son of the late Vatsa, led Safiya into womanhood also launched her career of widowhood. This, however it is viewed, is a grave charge and definitely dubious distinction, for one man alone to bear. Moreso now that it is being bruited that there was probably not enough evidence for conviction or execution. For Vatsa was hanged along with others for planning a coup to overthrow Babangida.
Babangida on his part has been doing his best, fighting tooth and nail, to stave off the image of a cold-blooded murderer without regard for friendship that is fast being foisted on him. A particularly undesirable image, if you ask me, at this hour of his great need that he longs to return to the cozy ambience of Aso Villa, the power house he vacated thirteen years ago.
As a Yoruba proverb has it, a twenty-year old pounded yam might yet scald the palm (iyan ogun odun a ma joni lowo), for the prologue to this present drama actually started twenty years ago. The denoument nobody knows. The matter was thought all dead and forgotten until Domkat Bali, Defence Minister in the Babangida regime, while answering a routine question during an interview with The NEWS, suggested there might have been a miscarriage of justice in the trial and execution of Mamman Vatsa, thereby opening the Pandora Box that is threatening to undo Babangida and, along with the recent arrest of Mohammed his son, unravel the mystique surrounding him.
The Vatsas want and are demanding justice-no more, no less.
The devious general who heartily proclaimed, once, his love to dominate his environment is now contesting the claim that Vatsa who had no troop at his disposal might have been done in for reasons other than the advertised. His voice is hardly being heard, leading one to ask if Babangida is not now being dominated by that same environment he thought was all for him to dominate? Twenty years since the deed, we might not be privileged to make any eyewitness claims about the events. Which is to say that Babangida (and his men?) might have genuine grounds for believing Vatsa was involved in the coup. But the all-powerful image he projected for himself, an image implicit in his claim about the love to dominate his environment, makes his claims a hard sale and his position morally shaky.
True, we all know that the punishment for planning a coup is death but even at that, Babangida admitted that Vatsa did come to him before his arrest to proclaim his innocence. And Babangida told him to think nothing of it since he didn't believe Vatsa, as his friend, would have a hand in anything intended to harm his interest. What is more, the plan was yet to be executed.

Original_Search_Title: U.S. Politician Given Reception Fit for a King in Kenya Visit
Headline: Kenya: U.S. Politician Given Reception Fit for a King in Kenya Visit
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When he visited Kenya in 1983 and later in 1991, US Senator Barack Obama did so quietly and even squeezed himself in a matatu on the way to his father's home in Alego, Siaya District.
Mr Obama describes the matatu (the rural type where passengers sit in two rows facing one another) in his memoir Dreams from my Father, as "a sad-looking vehicle with balding, cracked tyres".
Travelling long before enforcement of the so-called Michuki rules, the senator ended up carrying his half sister Auma on his lap together with a basket of yams while supporting a crying baby! This time round, though, he is travelling high-class with a big security detail to boot.
In fact his visit has elicited excitement across the country and attracted global interest with a battery of local and international journalists hot on his trail.
And unlike previous visits when he was received by his kinsmen, who had travelled hundreds of kilometres from western Kenya, last Thursday he arrived to a posh reception by US ambassador Michael Ranneberger and Foreign Affairs assistant minister Moses Wetangula.
Smiling shyly at the Vice-Presidential pavillion at the Jomo Kenyatta International Airport on Thursday evening, he waved at journalists but refused to take any questions.
As if preoccupied by memories of 1983 and 1991, when he mingled with other passengers and walked past the immigration desk unnoticed, Mr Obama looked shy and restrained, occasionally tapping his foot on the carpet.
But at successive events, looked more relaxed and cheerful.
On Friday, when he called on leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta, he cracked a joke and burst into laughter as we proceeded out of Mr Kenyatta's office.
"I think I recognise you. You have been following me all along since I landed in the country. I hope you mean well," he teased.
Trailing the US Senator has been a difficult task, with conflicting alerts from the US embassy and ministry of Foreign Affairs officials.
Probably as a security measure, the two offices deliberately misled the media over Mr Obama's arrival.
While all media outlets announced his arrival at 10 pm, the senator quietly landed in the country at 6pm. It is perfectly in order for the senator to have been received by an assistant minister.
But many people had anticipated the presence of high-ranking government officials and opposition chiefs, especially Mr Raila Odinga, Nyanza Province's political heavyweight, where Mr Obama has his roots. The Lang'ata MP actually stayed out of the Nairobi itinerary and only linked up with Mr Obama yesterday morning in Kisumu.
Also lining up to receive the senator were local MPs Anyang' Nyong'o (Kisumu Town) and Kennedy Nyagudi (Kisumu Town West). The Government's repeatedly announced that Mr Obama's tour was a State visit.
But a few days later, Foreign Affairs minister Raphael Tuju, who has a frosty relationship with Mr Odinga, seemed to go easy by re-stating that the senator's visit was semi-official "since he has family matters to attend to". Mr Tuju was not in Nyanza yesterday.
And contrary to expectation, the senator landed in Kisumu aboard a commercial, East African Air Safari Express, flight instead of the private jet he flew into the country in.
Unlike other US senators who come into the country on commercial flights and leave the country unnoticed, his visit is big time.
He enjoyed no ordinary security escort with all roads leading to the airport and the city centre being sealed off. His way was cleared all through to Nairobi's Serena Hotel.

Original_Search_Title: Militants Kill Soldier, Kidnap 2 Expatriates
Headline: Nigeria: Militants Kill Soldier, Kidnap 2 Expatriates
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Barely 48 hours after six hostages kidnapped by militants in Port Harcourt, Rivers State on August 13, 2006 were freed two expatriates working for Saipem, an oil servicing company affiliated with Agip oil were late Thursday night kidnapped.
Also, a soldier described as head of a team in the area simply called "Cracker" was killed and another of his colleague shot in the leg by the rampaging militants who stormed the place from the water front before disappearing again through the same route.
All efforts to get the company to confirm the names of the affected oil workers suspected to be Italians were rebuffed by a security man at the gate of the oil servicing firm on the excuse that following the incident, workers did not turn up work.
However, one of the over 3,000 displaced settlers around the site said that one of the men taken hostage was popularly called 'Sobaja Mario' while the identity of the second hostage was yet to be known.
The militants, who the villagers suspect came from Tombia and Bakana given their tongue and appearance stormed the barracks like settlement about 9.45pm while the expatriates were having fun in the Rumuolumeni joint.
"There were about four expatriates drinking and dancing makossa with girls at Carols Bar when suddenly the boys came through the creeks wearing army camouflage. One of the expatriates who was recently accorded a chieftaincy title seemed to have understood what was happening and told them that they could not hurt him, that after all they gave him a chieftaincy title and waved his hands in the air to showcase the beads.
"That gave him enough room to maneuver and he escaped with another of his colleague but the other two did not understand what was going on and were taken away. It was then that the soldiers realised they were not one of them and tried to do something. The head of the team was shot in the head and another of them shot on the leg before they escaped with the expatriates", an eyewitness said.
Later the soldiers who were taken unawares came back and started shooting sporadically. They poured fuel on the makeshift houses and set them ablaze with all the wares in them.
One Priscilla West who sells yam had a heap of her yam roasted by the flame and hungry residents went there to pick some to assuage their hunger while bottles dissolved by the heat of the flame were scattered everywhere.
West lamented that the people that carried out the kidnap did not come from their area while another Saipem source doubted that people from Bakana and Tombia could carry out the raid without the assistance of people from Ogbakiri.
When contacted, the Commissioner of Police, Mr. Samuel Adetuyi referred us to the Army while the Army Public Relations Officer, Major Sagir Musa confirmed the story acknowledging that a soldier was killed while they in turn killed one of the raider and wounded four.
Although he said he was meeting with some top shots in the army to determine an appropriate response, he would not comment on why the army burnt the settlement long after the militants had left. He, however, admitted that they recovered five rifles from the hoodlums.
When THISDAY visited Saipem, only one expatriate looking tired was directing affairs at the gate while a bus marked AE 901 NEM and loaded with some of the expatriate workers was leaving the premises.
A peep inside the compound showed a high level military presence as soldiers and policemen were seen either in their truck or moving around in twos.
Meanwhile, the Movement Of the Niger Delta People (MONDP) yesterday expressed regret about its departure from its initial aim of protecting the rights of Niger Delta people and called for immediate stoppage to the practice of hostage taking.
It cited the exploitation of the tactics for other gains which were outside the initial plans of protecting the interest and rights of the people of the region from oppressive laws and actions. This was in apparent reference to the monetary demands which now attend kidnap cases.
An online release by spokesman of MONDP, Mr. Preye Amazo said the movement now doubts if they could still achieve the aim of their struggle which are to emancipate the people ad free their leaders, Alhaji Dokubo Asari and former Governor of Bayelsa State, Diepreye Alamieyeseigha and therefore called for a total reappraisal of the agenda of the struggle.
According to Amazo, the group after due consultations arrived at a 10-point agenda, which includes doing everything within its powers to achieve peace in the Niger Delta; to cease with immediate effect all action which could be interpreted as hostile and inimical to peace and to support all group efforts aimed at achieving peace
"That the Movement Of The Niger Delta People hereby calls on all other Groups in the struggle to adopt the option of dialogue and discussion as a means of negotiation instead of the hostage taking method which has now been hijacked to accomplish some other counter productive interests as thus resulted in the violence and crises witnessed in the Niger Delta recently."
The group vowed to protect the interest of all Niger Delta youths who identify with the new move to stop violence since it would create synergy of those who genuinely want to achieve peace as they are handling all matter step by step with concerned stakeholders.
It, however, asked the Federal Government to reconsider the military option it has employed and urged that the Bill at the National Assembly to give legal teething to military actions in the area should be withdrawn as it can only serve to aggravate the situation
It called for restraint on the part of forces already deployed against the usual practice brazenness and occupation mentality in the prosecution of their mandate while the government should on its own, speed up the trial process of Dokubo and Alamei-yeseigha in a judicious manner devoid of government interference.
Meanwhile, the the State Security Service (SSS) yesterday arrested the leader of a militant group, which specialised in abducting expatriates working in the Niger Delta.
The suspect, Nicholas Dickson, an indegene of Akugbene in Bomadi Local Government Area of Delta State and a member of Niger Delta Enlightenment and Expedition Force (NDEEF) was apprehended in Burutu after he successfully abducted one Danny, a Lebanese working for Homan Engineering Company in Port Harcourt, Rivers State.
The syndicate had demanded N50 million as ransom before the expatriate could be allowed to regain his freedom.
The expatriate was held in captivity in a remote fishing town of Enekorogha in Burutu Council of the state and has so far spent about eight days in solitary confinement.
The SSS, which paraded the suspect before reporters yesterday in Asaba, said eight members of the syndicate were still on the run.
The Assistant Director of Operations of the SSS in Delta state, Mr. Brown Ekwoaba, bemoaned the kidnapping of oil company workers, particularly foreign nationals.
According to him, the expatriate was abducted at Ahoada, Rivers State while trying to make a call on his cell phone in a parked white Hilux Toyota Pick-Up van.
The Director explained that the Lebanese was forced out of the vehicle on gun point by one Benadi into a fleeing bus to the waterside enroute Enekorogha town where he has since been kept.
He stressed that unbridled abduction of oil workers in the region could send wrong signals to the international community.

Original_Search_Title: Police Invade Mararaba Market and Destroy Stalls and Goods
Headline: Nigeria: Police Invade Mararaba Market and Destroy Stalls and Goods
Body_Text:
Some Mobile policemen swooped on market traders in Mararaba destroying their stalls and scattered their produce yesterday. The police destroyed the stalls in an attempt to move traders away from the site of the old market, the Traders Association said. Goods such as, rice, beans, yams and fruits were scattered all over the ground by the police who stormed the area early in the morning.
A few traders were lucky to get away with their produce. One of the traders, Mrs Jummai Omo said the garri she sells were all scattered on the ground by the policemen who refused to allow her pack them. "What I lost here is over N5000," she lamented. The dislodged traders after the operation displayed their wares on Aso road and caused traffic congestion for motorists coming from Aso as they had to turn back and follow the road leading to Aku village. Another trader, Christopher Ujoh said the Nasarawa state government is only tryi-ng to force the traders to leave the old market to the new on e. "I learnt that the state government bought the land where the old market is situated and want to use it for something else hence the dislodgement. The government should realise that the new market cannot accommodate half of the traders from the old mar-ket and that is why the whole of Aso road is crowded with traders who cannot find spa-ce to display their wares," he stated. Alhaji Haruna, a fruit seller lamented the high rents charged in the new market. "The new market was built by private individuals, who have contracted estate agents to market their stores for them. Can you imagine that these agents rent big stalls for N120, 000 per annum with ten percent agency fee while the small ones go for N60, 000 per annum with five percent agency fee," he disclosed.
When City News reporter called at the office of Mararaba Market Association, the financial secretary, Nnaji Chukwuemeka attributed the traders woes to the disobedience of government's order to leave the old market to the new site. "Government gave them a directive to relocate and they don't want to obey it. That is why they were raided, "he said. The financial secretary disclosed that the stores built through private initiative are rented out at the amount arrived at after serious discussions between the owner and the intending tenants. "Sometimes the rent can be N56, 000 but all this depends on the arrangement between the owner and the person that want to rent the store," he said. City News learnt that the real owners of the shops in the new market are supposed to pay N12, 000 yearly to the Nasarawa state government and that the latest action of the government in dislodging the traders goes against the earlier decision of Ambassador Polycarp, chairman of Karu Area Development and Planning Authority (KAP-DA). The chairman had declared during an inspection tour to the new market in April that the traders in the old market would be forced out of the old market but persuaded to see what th e new market can offer, for them to make a choice. A KAPDA official told City News that the state government is bent on removing the traders from the road side to a descent and more organised place to market their goods.

Original_Search_Title: Les meilleurs élèves et enseignants à l'honneur dans les Hauts-Bassins
Headline: Burkina Faso: Les meilleurs Ã©lÃ¨ves et enseignants Ã  l'honneur dans les Hauts-Bassins
Body_Text:
Les élèves, enseignants et encadreurs pédagogiques de la région des Hauts-Bassins qui se sont distingués par leurs résultats au cours de l'année scolaire 2005-2006 étaient à l'honneur vendredi dernier. Leurs efforts couronnés de succès ont, en effet, été récompensés au cours d'une cérémonie présidée par le gouverneur de la région, monsieur Mathieu Bêbrigda Ouédraogo. De nombreux prix en nature et en espèce ont été distribués aux différents lauréats, venus des provinces du Tuy, du Kénédougou et du Houet.
Pour cette édition 2006 de la journée de l'excellence, la région des Hauts-Bassins a tout d'abord tenu à remercier les différents acteurs du monde de l'éducation, qui, malgré les multiples difficultés, ne cessent de s'investir davantage pour un meilleur devenir de notre système éducatif.
C'est donc pour permettre aux élèves, enseignants et encadreurs pédagogiques de persévérer dans l'effort que ces manifestations sont annuellement organisées.
La journée de l'excellence vise, entre autres, à créer une saine émulation dans le travail scolaire tant au niveau des élèves que des enseignants, à traduire la reconnaissance des responsables de l'éducation à l'endroit des associations des parents d'élèves, et enfin, à récompenser les différents acteurs du système afin de lui insuffler un dynamisme nouveau.
Cette année, ils étaient plus de deux cents lauréats dont cent cinquante élèves, vingt-quatre enseignants et trois encadreurs au niveau du primaire et vingt-cinq élèves du secondaire à recevoir leur prix. Des certificats de mérite ont été également décernés aux associations des parents d'élèves et associations des mères éducatives.
Cette cérémonie a été l'occasion pour le gouverneur de la région, parrain de la présente édition, de saluer les efforts de tous les enseignants du préscolaire, du primaire, du secondaire et du supérieur qui, dit-il, font aujourd'hui la fierté de notre système d'enseignement, malgré les conditions de travail difficiles.
Ainsi donc, monsieur Abou Diabaté de l'école Colsama D, désigné meilleur enseignant de la région avec un taux de réussite de 100% avec 92 admis sur 92, a été proposé à l'ordre des palmes académiques. Le meilleur élève au CEP dans la région s'appelle Yaméogo Kévin de l'école Saint Vincent de Paul de Koko.
Marlène Sossia Hien du lycée privé Diongolo Traoré de Orodara a été désignée comme meilleur élève au BEPC et Tiendrébéogo Delwendé Maxime comme meilleur élève au BAC dans la région. Ils ont tous reçu des prix en nature des mains des autorités.
Cette journée de l'excellence a aussi permis de reconnaître le mérite de trois inspecteurs dont monsieur Sirima Martial, chef de circonscription de l'enseignement de base de Banzon, province du Kénédougou.
Le moins que l'on puisse dire est que cette journée de l'excellence a connu un véritable succès et devra, selon le gouverneur de la région, aiguiser dans les années à venir, l'émulation et stimuler une concurrence saine.

Original_Search_Title: A Legacy of Decay
Headline: Uganda: A Legacy of Decay
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT is not just a barracks, it is home to thousands, but soon, it will be no more. Nsambya Police Barracks, Uganda's largest has been sold to an investor who will turn it into a housing estate.
"It is no longer a rumour. It has been sold," said Joseph Opio, the acting Barracks Commandant, whose brief includes allocating houses to police officers.
Nsambya Barracks was built in the 1930s with funds from the British government to accommodate about 1,500 policemen working around Kampala city. The barracks occupies 70 acres of prime land and currently houses an estimated 10,300 people. It is a 'self-contained' village with a fuel station, places of worship, schools and a health centre.
Over the years, the barracks gave rise to several businesses, ranging from maize mills to open-air cooked food markets, to malwa and pork joints.
From the 1970s, it also sustained a flourishing market in stolen fuel siphoned from police vehicles.
The barracks has also seen the growth of several other businesses ranging from groceries, to shops and schools.
In spite of its rich history, which includes the attempted assassination of the then president Idi Amin Dada while presiding over a police passing-out parade on June 10, 1976 and leading to death of at least 70 people, the barracks has seen its fortunes dwindle to an eyesore in the city.
Mzee Jingo was a houseboy to one of the senior officers living on block C240 in the 1980s. Currently he is a taxi driver. He remembers the barracks as a place that brought pride to resident police officers.
"Every morning, they would march out of the barracks in their well-pressed uniforms and polished shoes. At the average height of six feet, which was one of the requirements for joining the force, the officers commanded a lot of respect," he says.
"The sewerage system was functioning properly, the lawns were well-trimmed and the buildings well-painted. We didn't have the yams and maize fields we see today.
There was a network of tarmac roads running through the housing blocks. The barracks was a beautiful place to stay," reminisces Jingo.
The barracks also boasted one of the biggest sports grounds in the country.
The area bordering the railway lines, land occupied by Mukwano factory and the maize fields flanking the entrance to the barracks, were part of the sports grounds.
These grounds provided training facilities for sport legends like John Akii-Bua and former head of state Idi Amin, during his heyday as a heavy weight boxing champion.
Police officers resided in a spacious barracks. Houses were allocated in such a way that every officer that lived in a main house had a kitchen outside. All police officers received food rations from the Government and enjoyed sumptuous meals," says Jingo.
Sulaiman Kibuuka, a resident of Kibuli, remembers the barracks for being a source of cheap fuel. "Officers that had old motorcycles would wheel them up to the Shell fuel pump in the barracks. The tanks would be emptied and the fuel sold."
"This gave birth to illegal fuel selling points just after the furniture points along Ggaba Road," he added. To curb the illegal trade, the Police force started dying its fuel blue.
A stroll through the barracks leads one to buildings that are worn out, with brown walls, rusty roofs, with uniports taking up every available space between the permanent structures, all connected by a network of deeply potholed murram roads.
Erosion has eaten the once close-knit tarmac, leaving behind murram tracks. Walkways that once had streetlights have become maize and yam gardens.
Over 70 years after it was built, the population is six times higher without a corresponding addition to the housing units, save for uniports.
"The last permanent structures were erected in the 1960s. More trained officers are added every day, yet accommodation only gets worse, with some buildings threatening to fall apart. The sewerage system is appalling.
Opio says the barracks' state of disrepair could be responsible for the sale of its land.
Structures initially meant for one officer are no more. Except for senior officers, today, a family is forced to share one room with curtains demarcating bedrooms from living rooms.
Newly transferred officers often occupy offices, stores and even verandas with their families. The treasured dining hall is now a home to several families with curtains separating one family from another.
What used to be kitchens are also houses. At one point, even the mortuary was pressed into service as sleeping quarters. Toilets were filled and conveniently turned into houses.
Both married and single police officers are often forced to share rooms within a house.
With the exception of the flats, independent blocks and the unfading aluminium uniports, the once well-painted blocks are now all a shade of brown.
They are thirsty for a layer of paint. David Lwabi, a police constable residing in the barracks says if one is not careful, they can easily get lost among the faceless structures.
For identification, the blocks were once labelled A, B, C, D, E, F from the oldest to the most recent. This is long lost to weathering. The exceptions are Ggaba, Inspectorate and Band flats, which are after the water tank. Uniports are named after police commandants and inspectors in memory of their contribution.
On the other side of the railway are companies A and B. Houses roofed with red iron sheets follow on the left side as one moves in the direction of Police Children's School. "The few that were renovated were in a terrible state with the tiles leaking and some missing," Lwabi says.
Nsambya Barracks is a tale of an enduring legacy. It testifies to the history of Uganda Police. But it is also a sad tale of misery of Uganda's servicemen. Its demise will surely kill one of the monuments of the Uganda Police, but it will also bury one of the most disturbing reminders of the squalid living conditions of the Police in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Obama's Real Feelings When He Last Visited Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Obama's Real Feelings When He Last Visited Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — United States of America senator will be coming to Kenya next week in his five-nation tour to Africa. He carries with him a credential no other US Senator can claim: He is the son of an African.
Twice before, this African connection led him to visit Africa and learn more about his late father, a Kenyan educated at Harvard. Obama's roots can be traced to K'Ogello clan in South East Alego Location, Karemo division, Siaya in Nyanza Province. His quest for identity, which on many fronts stemmed from an incomplete understanding of his father's life, is captured in his 1995 memoir, Dreams from My Father: A Story of Race and Inheritance. It captures the author's fleeting relationship with Barack Obama snr, a Kenyan economist who left his family when his son was two years old.
The book opens with the author receiving a phone call from an unknown aunt Jane, in Nairobi. At the time (1982) Obama was a 21-year-old university student living in New York City. "At the time of his death, my father remained a myth to me, both more or less than a man," he says. Yet this jolting death announcement led Obama to search for knowledge about his Kenyan legacy. Obama enjoyed a comfortable childhood except the nagging absence of his father. "There was only one problem: my father was missing," he says. As a young boy he is shocked and confused with the race related issues and ends up crafting clever stories to explain his African heritage to his peers. Not only does he convince them that his father is a prince in a tribe full of warriors but that he is the next prince. In all this his heart was pricked with guilt. " A part of me knew that I was telling them a lie, something I had constructed from the scraps of information I had picked up from my mother."
As he enters adulthood, Obama learns much about his father from a woman named Auma, one of Dr Obama's children from another marriage in Kenya. Auma's visit to Obama in the United States results in the development of a close sibling relationship. Yet not until he visits Kenya does he encounter the truth about his father. The old man, as Auma and his other children know him, had managed to repair his life before it ended abruptly. Right at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport Obama felt different especially after Miss Omoro, a British Airways official recognised his surname. "For the first time in my life, I felt the comfort, the firmness of identity that a name might provide, how it could carry an entire history in other people's memory No one in Kenya would ask me how to spell my name, or mangle it with unfamiliar tongue." In Kenya the young Obama gains insights and affinities not only of the Luos but also of the Kenyan community at large. He becomes aware of many problems pla guing the country and it's contemporary culture-poverty, corruption and wife beating.
As he walks the streets of Nairobi Obama cannot help but feel different, nothing like his American experience. "Here the world was black, and so you were just you; you could discover all those things that were unique to your life without living a lie or committing a betrayal." On his way to 'Home Square' in Alego he gets a chance to travel in a matatu another main feature in our Kenyan road culture. To Obama it was "a sad-looking vehicle with balding, cracked tires." Due to scarcity of seats he ended carrying Auma on his lap together with a basket of yams and somebody else's baby girl, it was travelling - Kenyan style. As he meets his granny and other relatives Obama could not find words to describe what he felt. "It wasn't simply joy that I felt in each of these moments. Rather, it was a sense that everything I was doing, every touch and breadth and word carried the full weight of my life."
Towards the end of Dreams from My Father, it is obvious that Obama attains a measure of peace and a degree of self-awareness. As he weeps between the graves of his father and grandfather, he experiences what he had all along been searching for. "When my tears were finally spent, I felt a calmness wash over me. I felt the circle finally close. I realised that who I was, what I cared about, was no longer just a matter of intellect or obligation, no longer a construct of words The pain I felt was my father's pain. My questions were my brothers' questions. Their struggle, my birthright."

Original_Search_Title: The Day Obasanjo, Mbeki Others Brainstorm On the Ivorian Crisis
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: The Day Obasanjo, Mbeki Others Brainstorm On the Ivorian Crisis
Body_Text:
The international community has once again reaffirmed its commitment to the Ivorian peace process. On 5th July, 2006 a high-level mini-summit of stakeholders was held in Yamous-soukro to discuss the implementation of the roadmap to peace in Côte d'Ivoire.
Convened at the instance of the UN Secretary General, Kofi Annan, participants at the summit considered in great detail thorny issues in the lingering Ivorian crises as well as the elections scheduled to hold in October this year. Present at the meeting were President Olusegun Obasanjo, Thabo Mbeki of South Africa and the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Congo, Rodolphe Adada, who represented the Chairman of the African Union, President Sassou Nguesso. Also at the meeting were the Representatives of the ECOWAS Chairman, President Mamadou Tandja of Niger, President Amadou Toumani Touré of Mali as well as the Ministers of Foreign Affairs of Ghana and Burkina Faso, representing their presidents.
The mini-summit also attracted Jean-Marie Gueh-enno, Under-Secretary-General, Department of Peace-Keeping Operations of the United Nations, Ahmedou Ould-Abdallah, Special Representative of the Secretary-General for West Africa and the co-Chairs of the International Working Group. The Day to Day Mediation Group members, including the Representatives of the African Union and ECOWAS, the Special Envoy of the African Union Mediator, and the High Representative for Elections in Côte d'Ivoire were also in attendance. On the Ivorian side were the key players in the Ivorian crisis led by President Laurent Gbagbo and Prime Minister Charles Konan Banny. Others included, Guillaume Soro, Secretary General of the Forces Nouv-elles, Alassane Ouattara, President of the RDR, Prof. Djédjé Mady, Secretary-General of the PDCI, representing President Konan Bedie.
In his opening remarks, the Secretary General Kofi Annan expressed his satisfaction that Cote d'Ivoire had never been so close to peace and reconciliation as it is now. He was however quick to emphasize the need to facilitate the implementation of the roadmap necessary to ensure that elections were held as scheduled.
Stressing the urgency of the situation, President Olusegun Obasanjo, of Nigeria warned of the consequences of not holding elections in Cote d'Ivoire this year. He stated that it would not be in the best interest of the sub-region or the entire African continent if elections were unnecessarily delayed, adding that the "world would no longer take us seriously if we failed".
In the Chairman's statement issued at the end of the meeting, participants reaffirmed their commitment to the Ivorian peace process and the need to continue to make all efforts to implement Security Council resolution 1633. They reaffirmed the key commitments made at Yam-oussoukro on 28 February 2006, as regards the media and decided that a compulsory code of conduct should be established for journalists that explicitly forbids inflammatory, extremist, an d threatening emissions and publications.
In particular the statement called upon the media, and especially the print media, to embrace the peace process and reflect the growing spirit of national reconciliation. Recognizing the improved political environment and also noting that important steps had already been taken towards Disarmament, Demobilisation and Reintegration (DDR) and the Identification excercise, the Ivorian parties recommitted themselves to the task of accelerating the DDR process as well as the identification and electoral processes.
"In this context, the dismantling of militias, the redeployment of state administration, the nationwide launch of the audiences foraines, and the deployment of the Independent Electoral Commission (IEC) regional and local commissions were of the utmost urgency" the statement added. In addition, the participants stressed the important role that UNOCI, the impartial forces, the High Representative on Elections as well as other UN a gencies have to play to support the implementation, transparency and credibility of the process. In this context, they affirmed that freedom of movement and the involvement of impartial forces in the military dialogue are crucial to the credibility and transparency of the process.
The impartial forces, as mandated under Chapter VII of the UN Charter, should be granted access to all aspects of the DDR process, and in particular the pre-cantonment sites, as it is their mandatory duty to carry out the verification and listing of soldiers and weaponry. To this end a Monitoring Committee was created to facilitate the DDR process, which would include, among others, representatives of the Impartial Forces, the Chiefs of Staff of the national army, FANCI, the army of the Force Nouvelles, FAFN and the office of the Prime Minister. To curtail impunity, the participants strongly warned that those inciting violence and hatred would be liable to the imposition of targeted sanctions by th e United Nations Security Council. They also urged all those concerned to fully adhere to the new spirit of dialogue and cooperation enshrined in the Yamoussoukro declaration of 28 February 2006, as well as to the spirit of United Nations Security Council resolution 1633.
The parties reaffirmed that the roadmap, as well as earlier agreements signed by the main Ivorian political actors, including the Pretoria accord, must be implemented as such without delay and without re-opening or re-interpreting them. The question of post-election Cote d'Ivoire attracted considerable attention of participants at the summit. Debate on this issue generally centred on the need to fashion out an all-inclusive government to forestall the "winner-takes-all" syndrome. Ivorian politicians were thus encouraged to develop confidence and dialogue among themselves and to foster unity and national reconciliation beyond the elections.
On their own side, the Ivorian parties agreed to take necessary acti on on the technical implementation of the salient points in the peace process. They also agreed to respect a comprehensive timeframe and relevant resolutions of the United Nations, work towards election and the DDR processes, including the dismantling and disarmament of the militias by the end of July 2006.

Original_Search_Title: Collapsed Building - Angry Surviors Attack Developers' Property
Headline: Nigeria: Collapsed Building - Angry Surviors Attack Developers' Property
Body_Text:
Survivors of the collapsed building in Lagos at the weekend attacked two other property owned by the same developer, vanda-lising them even as scave-ngers invaded the site of the collapsed structure.
One of the survivors of the collapsed building at Ebute-Metta, Mrs. Patience Okoh, has narrated how one of the area boys pretending to rescue some victims of the disaster, stole N300,000 from her daughter's room before hitting her with a hammer on the head.
When our correspondent visited Ibadan street, Ebute-Metta, site of the incident, some individuals who clai-med to have lost property in the collapsed building were seen removing valuable items from the already vacated buildings owned by the now fleeing developers.
At the two-storey buil-ding located on 42, Ibadan Street, some men were seen removing materials, doors and window frames. Others were seen trying to remove the water tank hanging be-hind the structure.
The same thing hap-pened at the other building located on 76, Bola street where some of the invading young men were eventually chased away by a neighbour who resides in house number 7a.
The young men who argued that their action was informed by the need to recover parts of what they lost in the collapsed building, insisted on having their way even when the said neig-hbour, an elderly man iden-tified simply as Alhaji, threatened to call the police.
"I have lost all I have. This (pointing to the dirty jeans trousers and singlet which he had on), is all I have left. All my money, properties have been lost in the collapsed building," one of the young men identified as Sema said.
According to him, "we are trying to see whether we can recover some items here to sell so that we will not lose totally." Also, some individ-uals were seen on the debris of the collapsed structure, scavenging for any item they consider valuable. An elderly woman and her daughter were seen picking planks which they considered useful as firewood while others went after iron rods used in the collapsed structure.
Meanwhile, rescue efforts were eventually brought to an end on Sunday.
All agencies involved in the exercise have all vacated the site.
Mrs. Okoli who spoke with our correspondent on her hospital bed at Mainland Health Centre, Ebute-Metta, at the weekend, said while she was gasping for breath and waiting for help, she saw one of the area boys wriggle himself into the room to take the money (N300,000) kept in a bag belonging to her daughter.
"I was gasping for breath; my daughter and her husba-nd are already dead. A heavy block was on my chest when I saw the area boys enter with a hammer. He pac-ked all the valuable things in the house, including the N300, 000 kept in my dau-ghter's bag. He then hit the dead woman on the head with a hammer. He was also hit-ting the wall so that it could fall on us, but other rescuers came in and dragged me out," she nar-rated.
She described her dau-ghter whom she identified as Mordi Ewelle, as her bread winner. "She is my eye. She had been taking care of me and assisting me to take care of my other children. Now, she is dead. I am finished. What am I going to do,?" she wept.
The distraught woman explained that she lived at Ajegunle and only came to visit her daughter and her husband who recently pac-ked into the house. "It was my daughter who phoned me last Sunday that I should buy some yams for her.
"So, I bought the yams and took them to her. We were relaxing in the room when suddenly, I heard a loud noise. The next thing I saw was that the house had collapsed. I was shouting for help underground while my daughter and her husband had died.
"I thank God for bringing me out safely, but I want to appeal to the government to help me. I am a poor widow. There is no one to assist me. My daughter is dead. I want the government to find a house for me to live," she sobbed on her hospital bed.
Investigations reveal that the collapsed building was built by a developer named Collins, working in conju-nction with one Kayode and Alfa. Residents of the street were said to have raised eye-brows at the speed at which the house was built but their cries could not stop the deve-lopers who allegedly bribed Physical Planning Ministry officials to complete the job.
It was learnt that the original approved plan for the house stipulated a one-sto-rey house, but the developers ignored this and built three more storeys and a pent house. It was also gathered that a child fell into a well and died in another three-storey house near the site of the collapsed building.
The house was const-ructed by the runaway dev-eloper. Some residents of the street added a spiritual angle to the scenario when they dis-closed that a white garment preacher visited the street last Sunday and prophesied that tragedy was about to befall residents of the house and that whoever has ears should leave the house.
Apparently, none of the occupants of the ill-fated house heeded the warning.

Original_Search_Title: Ejulieri Nanziri (born 1901, Lives At Bwerenga Off Entebbe Road)
Headline: Uganda: Ejulieri Nanziri (born 1901, Lives At Bwerenga Off Entebbe Road)
Body_Text:
Kampala — At 105 years, it is heart-warming that Nanziri is still kicking, though she is not sure between 1901 or 1911 when she was born. But it is probably the former, since her baptism card reads 1910.
She likes to chat with her great grandson. But she forgets the little boy the instant he goes to the backyard. She welcomes him like a visitor when he returns and how the old lady laughs at herself, when she is reminded that this is her great grandson.
You may not being amused if you brought a mirror before Nanziri's eyes. "Who is this unattractive old woman with those sunken cheeks and wiry hair?" she may say in vernacular. On second thoughts; 'Do not show that photograph to visitors again." Nanziri looks great at her age and she never misses to smile for the camera.
This little 105-year-old woman reads and writes Luganda flawlessly! She asks visitors to write their names on a piece of paper then she deciphers your name from the paper. Age is nothing but a number, after all.
Born in the Ssese islands to a Saza chief's butler, Nanziri grew up on a staple diet of fish, vegetables, yams and the coffee that her father roasted. She studied up to primary four. Seems ageing runs in her blood; her father the butler died at a ripe old age of 102 years.
Nanziri was a chorister-and this seems to be her life-giving secret. Her great grandchildren are now accustomed to waking up to the sound of her singing Yesu mukama wange, a popular Anglican hymn at every cock crow.
She got married to a fisherman and they moved to Entebbe. "That was soon after Girimaani (World War I, perhaps)" One thing is for sure; Nanziri is never alone. Relatives attest that she has always begged to have her grandchildren sent to her so she can raise them. Today, the reverse holds.
Nanziri does not allow herself to get idle. She weaves a new mat everyday and prays for the family at meals. And besides, her healthy sense of humour has endeared her to the villagers.

Original_Search_Title: In the Fascinating World of Berliners
Headline: Cameroon: In the Fascinating World of Berliners
Body_Text:
Cameroonians in Berlin are dynamic and industrious. But they all nurture one dream - return home to build their fatherland.
How many Cameroonians live in Berlin? One thousand, two thousand, three thousand? It is very difficult to say. But one thing is certain; Germany's capital is one of the cities with the highest concentration of Cameroonians in Deutschland. The fact that plenty of work is available, and studies free, makes Berlin a haven for students and those in quest of employment. The majority of Cameroonians in Berlin are therefore university students or former students who have taken up permanent residence while waiting for an opportunity to relocate to Cameroon. There are also a few asylum seekers and business persons. It is a dynamic community.
This can be seen through the number of regional, tribal and even village meetings that flourish within the Cameroonian community. Generally such meetings have a common goal: preserve and promote the customs, traditions, and heritage of the Cameroonian people and their German relations. They also seek to promote contact between Cameroonians in Berlin and Germany as a whole, as well as help Cameroonians who are new in the country through advice, social contact and exchange of experience. The overall objective is to create a strong Cameroonian community which can assist its members, morally and financially in times of need and also to assist their communities back in Cameroon.
In effect, most cultural associations in Cameroon have branches in Berlin. Attending cultural meetings is one of the favourite pass times of Cameroonians. "We organise such meetings because there are a lot of stress factors here - missing our families and the fact that we have to constantly go out to search. Most Cameroonians are not living the best of life, they struggle", Eno Stanley Ndip, National President of Manyu Cultural Association in Germany said.
Sports, social and cultural activities are usually organised between the different associations to bring the different communities together. During the World Cup, for example, two Cameroonian communities resident in Berlin organised a friendly encounter. The Grasslanders FC took on Manyu FC at Strokower Sportsplats. There was a lot of conviviality during the match that saw Manyu FC beat the Grasslanders, 3-1. "Cameroonians love football. That is what has made us popular throughout the world. We in the diaspora always try to stay united, think about our roots and keep the spirit of football high", Fred V, one of the players said. To him, playing during the World Cup was a way to make up for the absence of the Lions.
Partying
Besides the tribal meetings, birthday parties are the other get together pretext for Cameroonians. There is a birthday to celebrate every other weekend. No age group is spared from the celebration, with some people born during the winter period pushing their birthday parties months ahead or behind! Birthdays are generally an occasion for Cameroonians to get out of the daily rice-chicken-potatoes menu. A good birthday therefore is synonymous to lots of Cameroonian dishes and good African music. It is to meet the demand for Cameroonian dishes that the Bantu village was opened. The facility, located in the Kamerunerstrasse in the Wedding neighbourhood can be considered as Cameroon's "embassy" in Berlin. You are sure to meet at least one Cameroonian at any one time in the restaurant. Bantu village is the fruit of the idea of Suzanne N., a Cameroonian resident in Berlin since 1997. "We live far from our country and such meeting places are few. The restaurant is a medium to unite C ameroonians with people from other country as well as make them feel at home", she said. And home indeed she has succeeded to make the place. In the Bantu village, the food you eat, music you listen to and people you meet are mostly from Cameroon.
During the World Cup, the Bantu Village was the hub of activities for Cameroonians. The presence of a giant screen made many people to refer to the place as the Cameroon's "World Cup stadium". Spectators flooded the area each day to watch matches of the World Cup and comment on the performance of teams. The atmosphere is very good; just like in any other bar in Yaounde or Douala. After some roast fish and bobolo, yams and Ndole, the evening of most Cameroonians general ends (or begins) at the Mandingo night club. The pleasure spot is the most famous afro night club in Berlin. It is owned by a Cameroonian and as expected, Makossa occupies an important place in the music programming. There is also good Mapouka, Ndombolo, Zouk, etc.
Work
All is not however rosy for Cameroonians in Berlin. Most of them have to work and very hard to make a living. Those with no valid residence documents are most affected as they are obliged to work clandestinely. Such Cameroonians are general abused by their employers. Others have been victims of xenophobic acts. In effect, every Cameroonian in Berlin has a little story of how he or she has been victim of acts of racism. They range from arbitrary control of documents on the street, outright refusal of certain services, restricted access to certain places and open insults in school or work place. "A girl in my class has been repeatedly calling me black monkey. She and her friends would laugh and point at me whenever I pass. I have to accept the treatment because I know that I am in their country", Carine, a Cameroonian student said.
Fru Aaron Che, President of Cameroon Grassland Cultural Association, Berlin, however thinks people should not be worried by the racist acts. "Many of those who just arrive in Brlin usually have their fair share of racial abuse. I have been here for 14 years. In the beginning it was difficult. But now I know what I want. Generally, life is difficult to those who just arrive", he said.
It is therefore understandable why despite the partying and merrymaking, good living conditions and development, virtually all Cameroonians in Berlin are unanimous that there is no place like home. Most of them are looking forward to when they will relocate to Cameroon. Their greatest problem is however their reintegration into the mainstream of the Cameroonian society. To them, the country has very little opening for those who study abroad. "Graduates from professional schools such as the ENAM have taken the country hostage. There is no space for us. We are considered as outsiders. That is why most of us will prefer to continue living here after our studies", Tchouwa Jacques, a student in his final year in medicine said. As solution, they suggested the creation of a Ministry of Cameroonians in the Diaspora that can look into such issues.

Original_Search_Title: Police Nab 9 Suspects Over Mgbowo Communal Clash
Headline: Nigeria: Police Nab 9 Suspects Over Mgbowo Communal Clash
Body_Text:
Enugu — THE Enugu State Police Command has arrested nine suspects in connection with the communal clash that engulfed the Mgbowo community in Awgu Local Government Area of the state last weekend.
Several lives and property worth millions of naira had been lost in the alleged clash between the police and youths in the community who claimed that some prominent persons in the community had invited the police to stop the planned "Iri iji" (new yam) cultural festival in the area.
The violence that lasted for about four days had been attributed to a tussle between factions in the community over Igweship traditional stool in the recently created autonomous communities in the area.
But speaking with reporters in Enugu yesterday, the State Police Public Relations Officer (PPRO), Mr. Mike Abattam, who confirmed the arrest of the nine suspects who are now cooling off at the state police headquarters, Enugu, denied police involvement in the matter.
"They have been arrested in connection with the trouble at Mgbowo while investigation into the matter continues," he said, while assuring that total calm has returned to the troubled area.
He added: "The Commissioner of Police's quick intervention into the matter by way of visiting the flash spots helped in preventing further crisis. His visit stopped the crisis from degenerating."
Refuting the allegation of police involvement in the matter, Abattam explained that the only role of the police in the crisis was that of "peace making and restoring law and order."
"The police can't be involved in Igweship tussle. The police can't be interested in the creation of a new community or things like that, because that is not the duty of the police. The police have the responsibility of restoring law and order," the PPRO said.
He stated that in a trouble situation such as the Mgbowo case, those who would like to paint the police in a bad image will always like to accuse the police of even things they know nothing about. "People will always accuse the police falsely in situations like this. Such false accusation does not help matters," he said.
