Abattam expressed the hope that ongoing investigations into the matter would reveal the truth, while urging the people to maintain law and order rather than to take the law into their hands. "Trouble does not pay anybody. People should learn to live in peace with their neighbours," said the police spokesman.

Original_Search_Title: 12 Feared Dead in Enugu Police, Youths' Clash
Headline: Nigeria: 12 Feared Dead in Enugu Police, Youths' Clash
Body_Text:
Enugu — NO fewer than 12 lives have been lost while over 15 houses were burnt in a communal violence that erupted in Mgbowo community in Awgu local government area of Enugu state between last Saturday and Monday.
Meanwhile, in another ugly development, Awka, the Anambra State capital was thrown into confusion yesterday following the killing of a former Commissioner for Women Affairs in the state, Mrs. Uzo Obi-Okoli, allegedly by her son. She was shot dead in her bedroom in the family palatial home in Awka.
Ten others who were critically injured in the Mgboro violence are receiving treatment in various hospitals in the state. Scores of residents have fled their homes.
Vanguard learnt that trouble started on Saturday following the alleged shooting of six youths from the community by policemen. The youths were protesting the security agents' interference in the activities marking the community's annual Iri Ji (new yam) cultural festival.
It was learnt that some prominent members of the community who feared that the festival might be turned into a political platform by some of their opponents were said to have invited policemen apparently to halt the ceremony.
An eyewitness said the policemen who arrived the town with in an armoured personnel carrier clashed with the youths when they (policemen) allegedly started smashing canopies and seats already arraigned for the festival at the premises of the Mgbowo Boys Secondary School. The policemen were said to have opened fire indiscriminately on the aggrieved villagers.
After the shooting that lasted several minutes, six of the youths were said to have died while several others were severely injured. "Angered by the development, the youths regrouped in the evening and resolved to deal with prominent natives who were suspected to have invited the police to disrupt the ceremony," the eyewitness said.
The youths then went on the rampage, torched about 15 houses belonging to the alleged instigators of the police action and the violence persisted till Monday when the State Commissioner of Police, Mr. Charles Dawodu, paid an emergency visit to the community to enforce a cease-fire.
The commissioner, leading a team of senior police officers reportedly appealed to the aggrieved natives to stop further violence, promising that the circumstances behind the shooting and killings of the youths would be investigated.
Natives of Mgbowo, who fled the community on account of the alleged "police invasion" have continued to lament the bloody incident, and asked Governor Chimaroke Nnamani to come to their rescue by instituting an inquiry with a view to apprehending those prominent persons behind the attack.
The decomposing corpse of a middle aged man believed to have been trampled to death during the rampage was seen on a major road in the town yesterday while the remains of dead youths were said to have been deposited at a mortuary in the town located on the Enugu-Port Harcourt expressway.
Some of the natives who spoke with Vanguard blamed the situation on the appointment of two "unpopular" natives as leaders of the two autonomous communities, which were recently created in the town by the state government.
They claimed that the two leaders whose houses were also burnt in the rampage and their supporters might have feared that the people would use the Iri ji festival as an opportunity to register their displeasure and, therefore, invited the police to forestall any untoward development.
But the police command and members of the families of the alleged instigators, however, denied this claim even though they refused to comment on the development.
The state Police Public Relations Officer, Mr. Mike Abattam, speaking on the issue yesterday, said: "There is nothing like that at all. The police were not involved at all. We only received reports of violence in the town and we intervened to restore order."
Abattam, however, said the command had been able to restore calm to the area following the visit of the police commissioner, pledging that thorough investigations would be conducted into the incident.
Police in Anambra State confirmed Mrs Obi-Okoli's killing and the arrest of her son as principal suspect. Obi-Okoli served in the government of Dr. Mbadinuju. She was a director of Orient Petroleum Resources, Nigeria's first privately-owned refinery sited in Otuocha in Anambra State. She also ran orphanage homes in Awka, namely "Mother Theresa Foundation" and "Tender Love Orphanage."
The suspect was said to have fled soon after the killing.
The police are investigating the motive for the killing. Sources said the suspect escaped from the house in his Mercedes Benz 500 SEL.
The Assistant Commissioner of Police in charge of CID, Mr. Muhammed Kastina, who confirmed the incident, said the suspect had been arrested. He was said to have been arrested in Lagos and would be brought to Awka soon.
He said: "I received a signal from the Divisional Police Officer of "B" Division in Awka about the incident. When I received the signal, I mobilised my men and officers of the Homicide Department and I led them to the place. The security man at the gate allowed us in and we met one of the sons of the late woman who led us to the actual scene of the crime.
"I saw her in a stiff condition and in a pool of her own blood. So we conducted a step-by-step observation with a view to establishing the identity of the attacker. I was able to conduct some preliminary investigations that assisted me in drawing closer to the identity of the suspect.
"I picked other clues and we are still waiting for the Forensic Department of the Force Headquarters, CID Annex. On observation, I also recovered a very important clue that led us to classify the woman's son as the principal suspect," Katsina said.
He said the suspect, whose name he declined to mention, was later arrested in Lagos by detectives following an alert signal they sent out.
The widower, Chief Obi-Okoli, Chief Executive Officer of Bilante Construction Company, was unavailable. He was said to have returned to Awka on receiving the news of the incident but left for Lagos afterwards.

Original_Search_Title: The Chef Has Superb Beef Stew
Headline: Uganda: The Chef Has Superb Beef Stew
Body_Text:
The original chef first opened at the end of Kampala Road where Commercial Plaza has since risen into a modern high rise and done away with the awfully dilapidated structures that were more of an eyesore than other wise in a modern city. Even at that juncture, some 12 years ago The Chef was a cut above the other local greasy spoons no doubt this being mainly attributable to the founder/ proprietor Allen Mwine an alumnus of the prestigious Utali College that specialises in the leisure and hospitality industry.
Several years later The Chef is holding sway in a fairly spacious establishment along the prestigious Kampala Road and in one fell swoop the move has realised more seating capacity. This in turn has enabled her to set up a buffet for a modest fiver; that just happens to be par for the course in town.
One is generally assured of a good beef stew, the ubiquitous chicken, rice, yams, matooke; all in all the value for money is there. No such place would be complete without the presence of katogo and the price never deviates from one thousand shillings. It seems that people are just not attuned to paying a penny more that this.
The take away side of things is never anything terribly exciting, a concoction of liver or kidney, forget about pan fried and made to order. This says a lot about the dictates of tastes as far as culture goes. It is as if all the take away joints conspired together and said that let us do it the same way.
Liver Creole or Lyonnaise will never be realty in these necks of the wood. What I do highly recommend is their pan-fried fish filet, a worthy choice that has generally turned out to be well prepared. A while back they had offered a weight watchers choice and wonder just how good the response has been? Anyway, in the main the prices are pocket friendly while at the same time there is a high degree of cleanliness and orderliness. The service can be erratic depending on just how busy they are, but patience pays off.
Place: City Bar & Restaurant
Rating: Nothing to really write home about
Service: Sluggish
Location: Near Agip House
Ambience: Excellent prospects
Parking: Limited inside but ample and secure outside
Reservations: Not necessary
Open: Breakfast, lunch and supper
RATING
XXXX: UNRIVALLED
XXX: EXCELLENT
XX: VERY GOOD
X: GOOD
NONE: POOR TO SATISFACTORY
These views are purely the reviewer's personal reaction to food, ambience and service with price being factored into consideration. The menu listing may change without notice.

Original_Search_Title: A Nation At War With Itself
Headline: Nigeria: A Nation At War With Itself
Body_Text:
Lagos — We are constantly at war with ourselves. I am not talking of the civil war or the Biafran-type of war where one part of the country is set against the other. But how else do we describe a situation where we set up institutions and proceed to render them impotent?
How else do we see a situation where we establish courts of law and where we proclaim in abstraction that the judiciary is the last hope of the citizen but we go ahead to disregard the rulings of the courts at every bend? When we establish legislative assemblies and pay little or no regards to the laws they make; when we swear to uphold the constitution of our land but we relish in subverting the provisions of the very constitution; then, of course, it is clear that we are at war with ourselves.
Add to the foregoing, the state of insecurity across the land, where people -- except those in the GRA perhaps -- cannot sleep with their two eyes closed; a situation where less than one percent of young Nigerians desiring placement into our institutions of higher learning actually get such placement and still less than one percent of this one percent get gainfully employed when they graduate. In essence, the original 99 percent that were unable to get into the institutions of higher learning a plus 99 percent of the one percent that got admitted are constructively poured into our streets on a continuous basis. What magic wand do we have to eliminate the resultant effect of this obviously defective arrangement?
Clearly, for a very long time to come, our forward movement as a nation cannot be progressive. We get into office and commit loads of fraud and constitutional breaches for which we must be called upon to account, if not sooner, certainly later. As a result, before we know it, we simply find ourselves beginning to struggle to remain in office so that as long as we live, our misdeeds can remain under cover. That's Africa for you. Where that fails, our successor invariably spends a good part of his first term trying to undo some of the disagreeable things we did while in office. Essentially, instead of starting from where we stopped, as happens in the advanced countries, our successor has to dig deep possibly beyond the point where we originally started. It is like taking one step forward and a thousand steps backward.
There is a greater evil I find in our system. We refuse to grow beyond political considerations. As an instance, any reference to President Obasanjo is invariably mistaken for a reference to the PDP. The political party should be seen as a conveyor belt that helps to convey members into office. Once they are in office, they should be responsible for their actions. We must grow to the level where every body, irrespective of party affiliation, sees bad as bad. After all, the President is a President for all, not just for PDP. When he goes wrong, he offends all Nigerians. It is unnecessary for the PDP to begin to roll out its arsenals in his defence.
When President Nixon of the US got immersed in the Watergate scandal, he received total condemnation of Americans - Republicans and Democrats alike. The Senate Judiciary Committee had voted overwhelmingly to impeach him but being the smart guy that he was, he resigned before impeachment proceedings could commence.
And even more recently, we saw what happened when President Clinton got entangled in the Monica Lewinsky affair. If any thing, the Democrats were more vocal in condemning the entire episode. Misdemeanor has no party colour.
All along, President Obasanjo and the former Finance Minister, Dr Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala had assured us that the excess crude oil account was intact. All of a sudden, we now hear that the President has dipped his fingers into the a account to the tune of $17.71 billion, as accepted by the President. The stand of the law is that under no circumstance should any kobo of public fund be spent without legislative approval. It is not an excuse that the need for the expenditure was urgent. This is because the National Assembly is always around. Even when members are on recess, they can always be reassembled in cases of emergency. President Shehu Shagari provides a good lesson here. In 1982, members of the National Assembly were recalled from their recess to act on the President's Economic Stabilization measure. The bill passed through the entire maze - from presentation to presidential assent - in a record time of two days!
Any infringement of the constitution is an offence against the State and the entire people. Meanwhile, why should it take only the opposition party(ies) and the social crusaders to condemn what obviously constitutes a gross misconduct? Why would the PDP remain stoically silent when a leader of that party is trying to rub everybody with red mud? No party should behave in a way that could suggest that it has dispatched its men to Government on a plundering expedition.
When the executive branch of Government attempts to write its own testimonial, it only succeeds in postponing the evil day. There is no reason to expect that the last has been heard of the sordid affair at the Nigerian Ports Authority (NPA). Rather than deal with the far-reaching revelations once and for all, it has been convenient for our own President to consign the damning report of the EFCC to the wastebasket. Right now, our President has both the yam and the knife. He therefore decides who shares from the yam. But nothing lasts forever.
We also hear that the account of the NNPC stinks. Contrary to the requirement of law that the account should be audited annually -immediately after the end of the financial year - that account has managed to be audited just once since 1999, when President Obasanjo became the Minister of Petroleum. The audit report submitted by the Hart Group, a consortium of auditors based in London, has revealed that between May 1999 and December 2005, not less than $1.514 billion in crude oil revenue and payments has not been accounted for. The report also says, among other things, that during the period, some 10 million barrels of crude oil have remained unaccounted for. We are told that this report has also been dumped in the wastebasket.
I wonder for how long we can continue to behave like the proverbial ostrich that buries itself in the sand, but unknown to it perhaps, that the head is very much on the surface. In just the same way that no matter how fast we run we can never run away from ourselves, it is also true that there is no hiding place for constitutional breaches.
Perhaps more than anyone else, the National Assembly members know that the death of the third term project is not the end, but rather the beginning, of their assignment. Matters miscellany to the dead programme must manifest in various ways such as misappropriation and all that, and the best time to deal with them is now.

Original_Search_Title: Understanding the Onitsha Crisis (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Understanding the Onitsha Crisis (2)
Body_Text:
THESE groups had earlier succeeded in relegating the corrupt local governments, like the Siad Barre regime was sidelined by Somali warlords. Governor Obi is now only trying to reassert the state. (Very soon the Bridgehead market will erupt when Governor Obi begins to implement his pact with NAFDAC to rid the market of fake drug dealers and merchants of death). But the principal antagonists and vested interests on one hand, and the masses of Onitsha citizenry who are constantly violated by corrupt policemen are equally using the opportunity to vent their anger on the Nigeria Police. So this is really a mixed-grill of anti-Governor Obi/Police uprising.
Therefore, it should not be impugned that what is going on in Onitsha is just Police face-off with hoodlums and the MASSOB. It is instructive that some transport unions, market associations and vigilante groups are officially fingered, along with the MASSOB, in the evolving Onitsha crisis. There is indeed a deep rupture - a collapse and disintegration of the political community in the area - that will take some doings to repair. And I venture to opine that it will take a diligent and thorough restructuring of the nation's political edifice, a strong resolve by the Obi government in Anambra State, as well as a commensurate attitudinal change on the part of the Onitsha residents, Ndi Anambra and Ndigbo in general, to reverse the subsisting ferment and to achieve the ultimate solution to the problem. The Igbo must eschew crass individualism and embrace attitudes that make for social cohesion, greater socialization, cooperation, inter-dependence and community.
Why, you may ask, has the Nigeria Police been unable to deal with the crisis in Onitsha and Anambra in general? One, the Federal Government which has primary jurisdiction over the Nigeria Police is too far removed from the local scene and too preoccupied with national affairs to even understand the complex socio-economic and political cleavages and phenomena at work in Onitsha, let alone issue the appropriate political direction, and in time too, to control the deteriorating situation in Onitsha. The state government which is in transition appears bewildered and appalled at the turn of event, while the local governments are simply not there. Two, the people are fed up with the Nigeria Police which over the decades has unleashed a reign of terror on the Onitsha populace. Its high-handedness, brutality, proclivity to extortion and willful suppression of the rights of the individual in the area is now unrivalled and entrenched.
The Police in Onitsha exhibit the characteristics of an occupation army. They routinely arrest masses of hapless citizens and road users in the town on trumped up charges for the sole purpose of extortion. Scores and hundreds of innocent men and women in Onitsha are known to be habitually arrested in frequent Police raids on such mean charges as not carrying the purchase receipts for cellular phones or handsets and such personal items like wristwatches. In some other cases, people are apprehended for all manner of invented charges over vehicular (motor and motorbike) particulars, wandering and even offences unknown to the criminal code. In effect, the way it is going, I am not sure that some individuals are not being arrested and detained for not producing purchase receipts of the very dresses, including underwears, they have on at the point of contact/encounter with the Police. The situation is that degenerated! Who are you to transport a 'container' of goods, truck-load of yam tubers, tomatoes, vegetables, name it, in Onitsha and environs without paying exorbitant 'tolls' at the various numerous Police roadblocks and stop-and-search operations? Most times, it is alleged, the police are more preoccupied with these money-making schemes than the actual work of providing security and safe-guarding the citizenry from marauding hoodlums, robbers and swindlers.
There are also the anti-MASSOB raids in which the Police routinely and arrogantly arrest any able-bodied men and women in sight or that happen to be found within the vicinity of such Police operations.
Often, the police actually abduct innocent youths from their homes, restaurants, sporting arena, etc, label them MASSOB members and proceed to clamp them into detention. Once arrested, hardly does the police release any of these youths without the payment of huge bribes or ransom. Woe betides those who cannot produce the requisite 'bail' money or meet the extortion demand of the police. Such individuals are left to languish in police cells and the Onitsha prison. It is for this reason that friends and associates of such unfortunate prison inmates or detainees, including the MASSOB, often organize raids to rescue detainees of the Onitsha prisons and the various police stations, to free the oppressed and the subjugated. Of course nobody should rule out the fact or the possibility that hoodlums do exploit the ensuing chaotic situation that emanate from these numerous encounter or face-off between the 'people' and the police to loot and cause general mayhem in the town.
Has anybody stopped to wonder why these clashes and crisis are so rampant in Onitsha and to some extent, Aba, the Enyimba city? One reason is that these are the main commercial centres where cash is readily available, and where 'ignorant' traders are more readily prone to part with money to buy their freedom or free themselves from police molestation and detention. These are people who have little or no means and the clout to secure their civic rights by other means or through due process. The police therefore capitalize on this handicap to harass, pummel and intimidate the ordinary residents of these cities for financial gains and sadistic pleasure.
What the police are doing to Onitsha residents and passers-by is indeed heart-rending. The notoriety of the Nigeria Police in the area is underscored by the fact that policemen and women lobby to be posted to Onitsha where a 36-year old culture of police impunity, extortion, high-handedness and willful degradation of the masses have become entrenched. Indeed, every road to Onitsha is now infested with uncultured, ill-mannered disrespectful policemen who block the roads, at times every kilometre of the way, to terrorize and extort money from road-users. In this escapade, no road-user, no matter how highly placed in the social order, is exempt; no class of vehicles and occupants are spared or escape censure; statuses are rubbished, especially those of people of Southeast/ South-South extraction who, to the police, seem to have become second-class citizens in Nigeria.
In fact, some of us now find it extremely unattractive to drive on the Lagos - Onitsha federal highway, not for fear of hoodlums or the so-called armed robbers, but actually to avoid humiliation and extortion from the police. With the numerous police roadblocks on that 'expressway', it has become absolutely unenjoyable and irritating to drive on that road. You hardly get past gear three before you are stopped at the next checkpoint. Given the level of frustration engendered by the activities of the Nigeria Police, it is little wonder that the affected people are at last fighting back, for they have been pushed to the wall. This is to be expected. No honourable self-respecting group would sit back and swallow the humiliation, the rubbish being forced down their throat by the Nigeria Police and, by omission or commission, the Federal Government of Nigeria that established the Force.
Given this background to the crisis, the imposition of a curfew on Onitsha and environs can, at best, provide a respite during which adroit steps should be taken to correct and remove the inequities that have provoked the crisis in Onitsha and beyond, and hence reassure the oppressed populace that are completely disillusioned and alienated by decades of neglect and insensitivity of the ruling political elite in the country, at federal, state and local levels. Needless to add, the expected restructuring should embody the resuscitation of the nation's economic fundamentals, and a reasonable welfare package for the police whom I understand are really poorly paid, and are hence forced by the circumstance to prey upon the citizenry, especially on those at the lower rung of the society. I should also use this opportunity to advise Governor Obi to be more discerning and tactical in his handling of the rotten Onitsha situation. He should not take on all the entrenched interests, gang s and warlords in Onitsha at the same time. He should tackle them one after the other, otherwise they could gang up and take on the state government; a situation which could be quite daunting.
Now that Governor Obi appears to have the ears of the President and the police authorities, unlike his predecessor, Ngige, he should impress on them the need to cutback on the waywardness of the Nigeria Police by whatever means necessary, including of course the decentralization of the force or the creation of state police in Nigeria. The Ohanaeze Ndigbo also has a role to play in this matter and the wider issue of MASSOB's continuing agitation for the creation of Biafra. Okenye adigh ano n'ulo ewu amuo n'ogbili (The adult does not standby while the goat gives birth in her tether). It is true that Ohanaeze has declared publicly that the Igbo is committed to the Nigerian state; that they are not interested in or supportive of anything that is geared towards the dismemberment of the Nigeria nation state. In its recent publication entitled .
'The state of the Nation: The Challenge to Ndigbo', the Ndubuisi Kanu-led Ohanaeze Transition and Caretaker Committee lamented and condemned the pro-Biafra stance of the MASSOB, and I venture to quote the document in extension; "...Worse, some of us have set up structures and organizations, not to fight for the soul of the nation, but to cajole, coax and plot for a separatist nation/state of our own... There is no doubt that many MASSOB members are heroic and selfless sons of Igboland. But their ideas are unrealistic; their hope vain and misplaced. The claim that we are being forced out of the Federal Republic is unacceptable. It is trite and demeaning... Ndigbo should put their act together, not only to salvage their fortunes, but also to help anchor and ratify democratic norms in the conduct of Nigeria's national affairs. Ndigbo can be a force for healing in the nation".
I see a ray of hope in the above Ohanaeze proclamation. It shows at least that the broad Igbo elite is not supportive of secession. And that they are committed to the rebuilding of the battered Nigeria nation-state. I will however urge Ohanaeze to go a step further in its quest for an honourable resolution of the crisis of confidence in the nation. In its forthcoming congress scheduled for 1st and 2nd July 2006 at Awka, Anambra State, the Ohanaeze should resolve to set up an Ohanaeze contact committee to reach out to the MASSOB leadership, with a view to reining in the group. The Ohanaeze should appeal to President Obasanjo to release Uwazurike, the MASSOB leader, to them, so that this discussion or parley could take place in an unfettered environment. It should be impressed on Obasanjo that a political solution, not a judicial or legalistic inquisition or military pacification, is needed in this matter.
Once the president accedes to this request, the Ohanaeze should use the opportunity to tell Uwazurike a few home truths. They should get the MASSOB leadership to abandon any form of secessionist struggle, sloganeering, nostalgia or hallucinations. Indeed, they should help create a new role for MASSOB, especially in the area of checking electoral fraud and making elections credible in the Southeast and beyond. The call for the 'actualisation of the sovereign state of Biafra' should not be interpreted in terms of securing a new republic outside of Nigeria; it should be conceptualized as the realization of a free Nigeria in which no man is oppressed, in which genuine progress and development may speedily be achieved to help bring the black people at par with other races of the world. In this light, MASSOB may retain its name or acronym, but its role and focus would be adjusted, for the good of all. The senseless shedding of innocent blood, on both sides of the divide, over the m oribund Biafra issue must be stopped now! A stitch in time, they say, saves nine!!

Original_Search_Title: I Wear Clothes That Are Peculiar to Me - Princess Latifat Lamoliatte
Headline: Nigeria: I Wear Clothes That Are Peculiar to Me - Princess Latifat Lamoliatte
Body_Text:
When Princess Latifat Momodu-Lamoliatte left Nigeria for educational pursuits in France, little did she know that in less than ten years, she would see her designs on the runway. Influenced by French culture, her label Ravissant mode, which means unique fashion, comprise of sophisticated, ultrafeminine pieces, with a unique line for men and children, all made with a blend of African fabrics.
The Auchi born designer always had a flair for fashion, since her adolescent days, when she used to sketch fashion illustration for friends and tailors to sew for her. Though she studied Foreign Languages at the University of Lagos, her passion for fashion came to the fore at the University Standard Grenoble, France where she studied French literature and did Masters in International Law.
After six and half year sojourn in France, where she got married to a French man, Princess Latifat came back to the country recently to live her dream as a designer. As she set to unveil Ravissant mode to the fashion world tomorrow, Sunday July 2nd at the Eko Hotel and suites, her greatest desire she says is to sell Nigerian fashion to the outside world.
What should we expect at tomorrow's unveiling?
I am going to showcase my work. I will have evening gowns, casuals, bikini, native attires. You will also see wedding gowns made with Aso Oke and ankara. I work a lot with Nigerian wax and the adire from Abeokuta which are really lovely. I'm working with about 30 models.
Do you have an idea of the number of clothes you will be showcasing?
I can't say how many, but there will be a lot.
Do you have a speciality? An area you think you could have an edge over others?
I don't know about others, but I am very good with evening gowns. And boubous too is another area because, I am an embroidery specialist, I could get very creative with boubous..
What fabric do you love working with mostly?
Adire. I love the Adire Eleko from Abeokuta, they are really lovely. I mix them with chiffon sometimes and they come out fantastic. I love them.
Do you wear only your own designs?
Yes, I stopped wearing other designers a long time ago.
What is fashion like for you?
I love exclusive designs, I make sure I look very nice all the time. Whenever I have to go out. I wear clothes that are peculiar to me alone.
Do you always dress African?
I mix them, sometimes African and western. Most of the time African. I wear evening gowns too if I have to go out with my husband. I love boubous, I attend parties in my boubous and stand I out.
What distinctive thing do you love about the French fashion?
I do a lot of window shopping in Paris and I am fascinated with the way they put their designs together. It's just different, you won't find it in London or America.
Do you sew or you only create designs?
I create designs and I sew too. I learnt to sew from the professionals I employed.
Your jewelry taste?
I love jewelry a lot especially unique pieces. I wear gold, silver, beads, anyone depending on my outfit.
One beauty product you can't go without?
My cream. I make sure I apply my cream everyday, else I won't be comfortable.
How do you spoil yourself?
I go shopping and get a total make-over.
What is your signature fragrance?
Escada and Magic Noire.
As a designer what inspires you?
The environment, the people around me, I look at someone and I can tell what will suit that person. Ideas just come, especially late at night. I sketch out all I have noticed during the day. I tend to focus on inexplicable designs that people don't really understand.
How do you spend your 'me' time?
I sleep, I shut up everything and sleep. Sometimes I watch movies or go out with the family. I love reading too.
Who are your favourite shoes and bag designers?
I love Channel and Cartier for bags. But I really don't mind, I could wear anything so far it's nice.
Favourite food?
I love the French Cuisine, I love the French wine, I love the French fashion. I love everything about the French. You know the French people have class. I like pounded yam and efo riro, but I don't eat it all the time, maybe once a month. I eat jollof rice more.
Fitness tips?
I eat three times a day, but I try not to eat after 8 p.m. I go for a walk sometimes, it helps me stay fit and trim. But one thing I can't do without is drinking champaign.
What part of the world do you love most?
Paris -- 'See Paris and die'. I think this phrase is very true. Paris is beautiful. I am never bored of going there.
What challenges do you encounter as a designer?
It's difficult to please people. Some customers are very difficult, you have to be very patient with them. Though I have tailors I make sure I supervise them all the time and it's not that easy.
How supportive has your husband been?
I should say a big 'thank you' to him he has really been helpful else, I would not have been able to cope.
What is in your make-up bag?
Powder, lipstick, eye shadow, liner that's all.
What is your view on the fashion industry in Nigeria?
Nigerian designers are not doing badly at all. I've seen what they do and I believe they are doing very well. But, someone else has to come out with something different, that is what Ravissantt Mode is all about.

Original_Search_Title: Letter From Y'de To...Buea
Headline: Cameroon: Letter From Y'de To...Buea
Body_Text:
Dear Mbella,
I have nothing to gainsay in your observations but for the crude fanaticism you have so lavishly displayed for every decision that your hierarchy takes. In fact, your evangelism for democratic change in the country has become an enigma because contrary views out there are still considered as subversion.
Such a situation, Mola, would have been logical had you guys proclaimed the dogma of infallibility like Pope Pius XI. I lampoon and applaud when it is necessary. That is why I must shout and weep over the nauseating stench of the MINADER embezzlement scandal.
Event though the main protagonist is of the majority fraternity, the tale must be told in very unequivocal diction that HIPC-I funds were used to line individual pockets.
Mbella, let me bore you with the banal cliché that "le Cameroon c'est le Cameroon" before stating very clearly that nothing is absurd about this whole thing.
Since HIPC-I funds are tied to the apron strings of poverty alleviation, would anyone still apportion censure if the Minister and his collaborators were simply fighting their own poverty, that of their family and that of the Bonamoussadi Biancas?
The whole issue is a banality if we consider that corrupt goats always eat where they are tethered. Let us wonder why the cow the upcountry Provincial Delegate was bringing to Minister X in Yaounde died on the way.
What type of omen was that? What was clearly a bad omen was that he still went and forced the corrupt chain of his Divisional Delegate to contribute money for another cow for the Minister. Yet, what happened, happened. This is a thing that should be dumped into the dustbin of gossip. The truth is that the Delegate's boss nodded ministerially and the man was "relevé de ses functions."
I say, Mbella, I hope you know me very well and that I can always get to tree tops to shout down the bitter truth and ram it down the ears of all those who like to listen to pleasant lies. Nobody has cared to analyse how many people have gone to their early graves in the Ngoketunjia Baghdad, where the local Saddam has been operating the guillotine since February 2, 1977.
Over 25 people have been sent home to their fathers rather hastily, yet there are intrigues to liberate Saddam from the dudgeon of nemesis.It is a bloomer that basic legal principles are compromised. Legal "ngong dogs" have been basking in the heat of double fidelity and showcasing elastic morals in the application of the very rule of law.
Oh Jehovah! Money is about to sleep with their consciences. Can we call that rape?
Mola, are you surprised that the Chief Magistrate of the land has been watching through his magic box with relish how jungle justice is being dispensed on alleged criminals? That tells you that the justice system is porous.
It is just balderdash when the owner of the yam and the knife claims that he/she is miscarrying justice because his hands are tied. Fombe and Abenego used their consciences and the law only to deliver justice. On the contrary, they both today are sitting at the right hand of the god of justice at their various levels.
I know what is in your mind about Peter Yabassi, but I want to tell you that he is just taking us back to enjoy the good old days during which DOs enjoyed seizing newspapers and detaining journalists. Nobody should gainsay the old boy for having the nostalgia for those nice days. But he could be in for some suicide because Etoudi seems to have a contrary opinion on the issue.
You can stay in your village and continue to complain. I am waiting to be appointed Minister when Etoudi nods presidentially in the coming days. In such a situation, do you still expect me to be hot about the poisoned chalice called an independent electoral commission? Nobody who fears death will play with poison or walk into the lion's den with his eyes open.
I will be grateful if you let me read from you again soonest.
My regards to your shock absorber, Enangs.
Yours, sincerely,Ngwa

Original_Search_Title: "APC is Climbing the Mountain Backwards," Alikalie Kamara Claims
Headline: Sierra Leone: "APC is Climbing the Mountain Backwards," Alikalie Kamara Claims
Body_Text:
All Peoples Congress (APC) stalwart, Amara Alikalie Kamara Thursday charged that the "APC is climbing the mountain backwards." Speaking in a telephone interview from Sweden, Kamara charged that the current popularity of the APC which is being exhibited by rallies held by their Leader, Ernest Bai Koroma do not in any way mean popularity in its sense for the party.
"You can get all the population on the streets, but these rallies do not mean you are popular. Or you can be popular like the Karefa Smarts or late Thaimu Bangura," Kamara states.
He argued that the current APC leader is showing out his cards so much that it will enable his opponents do him a technical knockout.
"Wae yu yams white, nar for coba ram, if u nor do am, orda porsen go put san san dae." (When your yam is white you should cover it or else someone else will pour sand in it and you will not be able to eat it.) Asked why he did not like Ernest Koroma as Leader of the APC, Kamara responded, "I personally have no problem with Ernest Koroma, but it is high time he realized that with him as leader, the APC will be dealt a technical knockout in the 2007 elections."

Original_Search_Title: Bantu Village : Cameroon's "World Cup Stadium"
Headline: Cameroon: Bantu Village : Cameroon's "World Cup Stadium"
Body_Text:
Most Cameroonians in Berlin watch matches of the World Cup on a giant screen in the restaurant.
The Wedding neighbourhood in Berlin is special to Cameroonians for several reasons. First, it has an entire street dedicated to the country; the Kamerunerstrasse. Out of affection therefore, many Cameroonians have taken residence along the street, wherefore the high concentration of fans of the Indomitable Lions in the neighbourhood. The place to visit in the area is the Bantu Village, a restaurant specialised in Cameroonian dishes. It can be considered as Cameroon's "embassy" in Berlin. The Bantu village is the fruit of the idea of Suzanne N., a Cameroonian resident in Berlin since 1997. "We live far from our country and such meeting places were few. The restaurant is a medium to unite Cameroonians with people from other countries as well as make them feel at home", she said. And home indeed she has succeeded to make the place. In the Bantu village, the food you eat, music you listen to and people you meet are mostly from Cameroon. Roasted fish and "bobolo", yams and "Ndole" , etc are the principal components of the menu
During the ongoing World Cup, the Bantu Village has been the hub of activities for Cameroonians. The presence of a giant screen has made many people to refer to the place as the Cameroon's "World Cup stadium". Spectators flood the area each day to watch matches of the World Cup and comment on the performance of teams. The atmosphere is very lifely; just like in any other bar in Yaounde or Douala. The discussions are general and at times very emotional. The trilingual nature of those present is evident as they discuss in English, French and German.
Last Wednesday evening, focus was once again on the absence of Cameroon. "We are not happy because Cameroon is not present. We just watch the matches with little enthusiasm", one of the Cameroonians said. There were several reactions to his statement, among them this one from George, another Cameroonian who has been in Berlin for 10 years. "It is good for our football that Cameroon did not qualify. It gives us the opportunity to rebuild our football and lay ground works for a solid tomorrow", he said. According to him, Cameroon will bounce back in 2010 when the World Cup will be hosted in the African continent for the first time.

Original_Search_Title: First Farai Releases Smash Hit Album
Headline: Zimbabwe: First Farai Releases Smash Hit Album
Body_Text:
Harare — Tongai "Dhewa" Moyo should watch out for Karoi-based sungura musician First Farai who released a smash hit album that sounds quite like dynamite.
Frankly speaking, I haven't heard an album that is full of energy, as it is entertaining. The latest is the fifth album after the other four that ranged from lukewarm to cold as ice. In fact titling the latest CD Simba Racho (Maximum Voltage 33 Kilovolts) was by no means a misnomer.
I have always liked his style of music, which is a bit of everything. Listening to the album for the first time, one gets the impression that Farai is his own man now. It is hard to liken his music to anyone else's and yet he says he draws inspiration from a diverse range of artistes.
Surprisingly, I played every single song imagining if he was to put the same energy into his videos, I thought we could be having another king of sungura in the making. Songs that will hit you the most are Mhiri Kwemakungwa, Maoresa, Secret Number and Mwana Wedangwe.
It would not be surprising if the album storms into Gramma Records charts at number one and possibly push his previous albums back into the charts. You are not likely to find any of his previous mistakes on this album. He has worked it out and penned some of his best songs to date.
I'm told the video album will be out soon and hopefully things will work out for him. Kudzai Ncube -- Angisweli Lutho Let us welcome a new gospel diva on the music scene. One thing I liked about Kudzai is that every song on this album has something to teach about God's grace. The world we are living in now is full of adversities and challenges but sometimes we only live by the grace of God.
This is perhaps the theme on this album but I think its premature to shower her with praises, as this is her debut. The six-track album is off to a good start though some of the songs need a patient listener. If you stumble upon this album make sure you sample the songs Makabongwe, Musi WeSabata, Ndiri Wako and of course, the title track.
Record and Tape Promotions are marketing and distributing the album. Ringo -- Love Songs Man oh man, this album is a collec tor's item not only for those in love but also those who love sweet music. Undeniably, one of South Africa's stars, Ringo's music needs no introduction in Zimbabwe.
This gem has songs from all his hit albums such as Vukani, Mamelani, Buyisa, Live Greatest Hits, Sondelani, Into Yam, Ntumba and the latest Baleka. But unfortunately, the version of the song Into Yam is the original, the one that features Oliver Mtukudzi is not on this compilation. While there are a lot of favourites, I know most fans are familiar with Sondela, Ndiyagodola, Kuzolunga, Kum Nakum, Buyisa and Hey Ntombi.
If you enjoy love songs, this album is for you. Peter Makomeke -- Vasingadi Kurwa Here is another rookie trying to make an impression on the overly subscribed industry. While I may not be able to tell what the future holds for him, his music needs polishing. This is not to say it's half-baked, but that sometimes sounding different pays in the end. I listened to the album for more than three times an d I simply forgot what it was about.
But I think the six-track album also needs a patient listener before one can fully appreciate the album. Songs to sample include Terera, Mwene, Baba Komboreraiwo and Mary.

Original_Search_Title: Odoki Advises Cabinet
Headline: Uganda: Odoki Advises Cabinet
Body_Text:
Kampala — CHIEF Justice Benjamin Odoki has said new entrants in the Ugandan political scene should prioritise the understanding of Uganda's legal system to contribute effectively to the country's development.
The new entrants are mostly from opposition political parties, joining the National resistance Movement.
Odoki was on Friday speaking during the launch of Prof. George Kanyeihamba's book titled "Kanyeihamba's Commentary on Law, Politics and Governance."
The function took place at Speke Hotel in Kampala. Former rubaga South MP Ken lukyamuzi and judges Stephen Kavuma, geoffrey Kiryabwire and joseph Mulenga attended.
"This book has been published at a time when the country is about to complete the process of transition to multipartyism, following the amendment of the constitution," said Odoki.
"Both the old and the new political players need to be familiar with the law and principles relating to constitutionalism, democracy, human rights, the rule of law and good governance," he added.
Kanyehamba denied having said recently that he would make last minute revisions in the book to give First deputy Premier Eriya Kategaya "a piece of yam".

Original_Search_Title: May 20th Avenue: Pedestrian Tunnel Cleaned
Headline: Cameroon: May 20th Avenue: Pedestrian Tunnel Cleaned
Body_Text:
There are about three passage ways built along the 20th May Boulevard to ease circulation of pedestrians
Created to serve as a passage for pedestrians, the tunnels built along the May 20th Boulevard also add to the beauty of the town. However, over the years, the tunnels have been neglected and have instead served as a home for street children and a hideout for thieves.
As part of preparation for the last May 20th celebration in Yaounde, .Two of the tunnels which had been abandoned with stagnated water, grass, garbage, were properly cleaned to serve the purpose for which they were created. However, the tunnel closer to CAMPOST is still full of dirt, grass and coco yam leaves. As such people cannot pass through as the tunnel is field with water stagnant.
Talking to the Assistant Chief of Service, in charge of Environment and Hygiene, Dzou Bonaventure Dominique, refuted the fact that, the tunnels were kept clean because of May 20 celebrations. He said the tunnels have not been abandoned.
"Yaounde is a very big town and given the insufficient staff which we have to clean the town, some places are bound to remain unclean for long, before they are being cleaned. This has been the case with the tunnels", he said. The duty to clean up the town, he said, is handled by the Park and Garden Service.
The Chief of Service, added that, the council budget is not enough to do thorough clean up of the town so they have to resort to council resources, which he said is still insufficient. Due to lack of staff, they do give contracts to NGOs to do some of the cleaning, such as the Mvog Ada volunteers of Kamdem, etc.
Asked how safe it may be to pass through the tunnels, Dzou warned that passing through the tunnels or entering is dangerous, because it is dark. "You could be beaten by a snake or be harmed by anything because there in no light to lead the way", he said. Asked why the tunnels could not be blocked to keep it safe from thieves, he said, if the tunnels are blocked they will not serve the purpose for which they were constructed.

Original_Search_Title: FPAL Launches Youth Movement in Kakata
Headline: Liberia: FPAL Launches Youth Movement in Kakata
Body_Text:
The family planning Association of Liberia (FPAL) over the weekend officially launched the Youth Action Movement of the Association in Kakata, Margibi County.
The launching program was characterized by a parade in the streets of Kakata.
Launching the Youth Action Movement (YAM), the Executive Director of the Association, Ms. Miatta K. Kiawu, speaking on the theme, "Working For Youth Sexual and Reproductive Health, Rights and Choice" said, FPAL recognizes the need to ensure that young people are involved in a youth friendly organization, as well as the need to address the growing trend of the sexual and reproductive health challenges among adolescent.
She said the Association's mission is dedicated to providing leadership in Sexual and Reproductive Health (SRH) in Liberia through advocacy. With this idea in mind, she noted, the International Planned Parenthood Federation African Regional (IPPFARO) adopted the youth action Movement.
Ms. Kiawu said at the 16th Members Assembly of the Association which was held in August 2005, resolution number 13 says that "the national Executive Committee of FPAL ensures that, the Youth Action Movement (YAM) is established in all the branches of the Association and given the necessary support to its programs and projects".
It is in this light, she emphasized, that the National Executive Committee, volunteers and the staff of FPAL were in Kakata to grace the formal launching of the youth Action Movement in that city.
The FPAL boss commended all those who contributed to the success of the program. She assured the staff of the Kakata Clinic (an FPAL own clinic) that a massive renovation and extension of the clinic would soon begin.
She called on other branches represented at the program to emulate the good example of the YAM members in Kakata City. She added that FPAL remains dedicated to share vision of the International Planned Parenthood Federation (IPPF) of "an African society where every child is a wanted child, every person enjoys good maternal health and men, women and youth live free of HIV/AIDS".
For his part, the development Superintendent of Margibi County, who served as guest speaker, John S. Buway said that good health is a fundamental pillar for national development.
According to him the HIV/AIDS pandemic and other diseases are serious hurdles in the face of carrying out development, adding that the best remedy to getting these diseases is to prevent it.
The Margibi development Superintendent further said that AIDS is real. He believes many persons seemed not to believe that AIDS is real. Mr. Buway noted that the young people are the most vulnerable to these diseases. He cautioned the youth to be more careful how they go about their activities. He described them future leaders.
He however recommended that government encourage young people to enter the medical field through awarding scholarships to deserving students.
He further admonished the government to improve the learning facilities in various schools in the country by hiring trained and qualified teachers to mold the minds of the future leaders of Liberia.
He also recommended to FPAL, especially the Youth Action Movement to decentralize their activities in every corner of the country, so that other people will promote the good dream of the Association.
Giving a brief history of the youth Action Movement (YAM) the National youth Action Movement representative to the National Executive Committee of the Association, J. Aloysius Davies said, the YAM was established in Monrovia in 2005, and subsequently in Kakata and unification Town in Lower Margibi County.
He said the establishment of the Youth Action Movement was against the back drop that the young people were not fully represented at the International Planned Parenthood Federation African Regional Office governing body.
In view of this, he said, the Executive Committee in its 70th session held in April mandated the Regional Office to develop an implementation structure for the youth strategy to be represented at the Regional council meeting.
The decision was endorsed at the 33rd session of the Regional Council held in July 2003, he noted.
The President of the Family Planning Association of Liberia, Dr. Lyndon G. Mabande who also spoke at the program advised youths to be serious and cautious about how they conduct on their lives.
He said the major focus of the Association is to provide sexual and reproductive health care delivery throughout the country.
The Family planning Association of Liberia is a member of the International Planned parenthood Federation (IPPF), which is based in London with the Africa Regional office based in Nairobi, Kenya.
FPAL is non profit, non governmental and voluntary organization established in 1956 to compliment the efforts of the government of Liberia and other stakeholders in the provision of sexual and reproductive health and rights services to the population.
Dr. Tipoteh Decries Violence -Terms It No Alternative Problem Solver The Standard Bearer of the Alliance for Peace and Democracy (APD) in the just ended Presidential and Legislative Elections, Dr. Togba Nah Tipoteh is calling on all Liberians at home and in the Diaspora not to resort to violence as an alternative formula to solving problems, during crisis situations among them.
Dr. Tipoteh made the statement over the weekend during a turning over ceremony of an ambulance jeep and assorted humanitarian materials donated to the Ministry of Justice for use by the prison inmates of Liberia from the Prison Fellowship Liberia - International (PFL).
Dr. Togba Nah-Tipoteh, a Board Member of the PFI, on behalf of the organization presented assorted humanitarian materials to the Ministry of Justice.
The group also cautioned prison inmates and called on Liberians never to use a violent approach as a means of solving their grievances.
According to him, violence had done more harm than good to the nation and its people during the past 14-years.
Dr. Tipoteh, referred to by his followers as the "only man on the ground", recounted the devastating effects of the 14-years old brutal conflict and added that it was now time that the people of the country begin to unite and reconcile their differences, put the bitter pasts behind them for the betterment of the nation.
According to the PFI Board Member, the violent approach to resolving grievances among Liberians during the past decade and a half created serious destruction to all fabrics of Liberian society. He then warned them to take cue from past history of the country whenever they attempt any violent approaches.
For his part, the Executive Director of the Prison Fellowship Liberia, Francis S. Kollie used the gathering to appeal to all Liberians to work hard at consolidating the hard won peace put together by the International Community and the Liberian people.
The PFL Executive Director intimated that if Liberians create the framework for peaceful co-existence among themselves the international community and other countries of goodwill would be willing to positively contribute their quotas to the rebuilding of the country after years of bloody civil war.
The Chairperson of the PFL Board, Mother Comfort T. Niminneh-Logan on behalf of the organization presented the ambulance to the Ministry of Justice.
Mother Logan used the occasion to send an SOS call to the international community, as well as to other humanitarian aid agencies operating in the country to help the nation and its people to recover from the abyss of war by joining rank with the Sirleaf government to bring relief to war weary Liberians.
The Prison Fellowship Liberia also presented assorted humanitarian materials to the Ministry of Health.
The MOH package according to the PFL Executive, Francis Kollie included mattresses as well as medical equipment.
The North West Medical Team at the JFK was also a beneficiary of the PFL gestures and all of the presentations took place at the headquarters of the United Methodist Church on Broad Street.

Original_Search_Title: Judge to Write On Kategaya
Headline: Uganda: Judge to Write On Kategaya
Body_Text:
Kampala — Prof. George Kanyeihamba is to make a last minute revision of his new book in order to include a section on the re-appointment of former deputy premier Eriya Kategaya into Cabinet.
The book, published by Kampala's Renaissance Media, is titled Kanyeihamba's Commentaries on Law, Politics & Governance.
It analyses the process through which Parliament amended the Constitution and gave President Yoweri Museveni chance to contest for a third term in office.
Kategaya opposed the move but recently accepted a ministerial appointment under Museveni's third term.
"Kategaya is my friend. I will make a small revision to give him his piece of yam," Kanyeihamba said yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: 'Positive Force' Launches Musical Album Soon
Headline: Liberia: 'Positive Force' Launches Musical Album Soon
Body_Text:
A new Liberian musical group, The Positive Force, with the title track of their debut album "Liberia, Land of the Free." is scheduled to launch its musical album on June 3, 2006 at the Monrovia City Hall.
The Positive Force is a Liberia based musical group with determination to set a new standard on both the local and international musical scenes.
Speaking to The Analyst last evening, the two young group members introduced themselves as Samuka Kamara and Lester Aquoi, saying that they worked assiduously to realize their premier dream.
According to Lester Aquoi, a singer of the group, the song, "Liberia, Land of the Free," was inspired by the October 2004 crisis of which he was a victim.
He added that the October 2004 crisis, otherwise known as the Rock War affected many people and was fought with an undertone of religion and ethnicity.
Turning to the message of the title track, Liberia, Land of the Free, he said that the song echoes with the fact that all Liberians bear responsibility for the Liberian wars and that the problems brought upon the Liberian nation must similar manner be jointly resolved.
"Accordingly", he maintained, "it will take all Liberians, irrespective of tribal or religious affiliation to come together in finding solutions to all the problems caused by the many years of war." Other mind moving songs of the album include "We Are One," which is sung in Mandingo and Bassa; "Teacher", "Bongo", "Love 4 the Nation".
Additional songs are "Africa In the Century", "Bush Yam Party", and "Susan".
Also speaking to The Analyst, the second singer of the album, Samuka Kamara added that the eight songs on the album are rich in music quality as well as socially conscious messages dwelling on peace, love and understanding.
He said that The Positive Force's new album was recorded at the LNS Studio on Peace Island in the Paynesville suburb of Monrovia.
Kamara also said that after the album was locally recorded, they had to take it to the United States, where it was remixed at the FVK Studio in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, USA. The album was then launched on March 13, 2006 in Philadelphia, becoming an instant hit and one of the hottest musical items at Liberian and African social events, there.
He then disclosed that the Executive Producer and Manager of the group is Nvasekie Konneh, a Liberian writer based in the USA.
For his part, producer Nvasekie Konneh says he was motivated by the positive efforts of the group to serve as their producer.
According to him, the response of Liberians and Africans in the United States, after the launching of the album has so far been positive. He added that he returned home purposely to launch the group's debut album in what is going to be an extravaganza of a musical concert on June 3, 2006 at the Monrovia City Hall.
The album, after the formal launching on June 3, is set for massive distribution locally, well as in Africa and in Europe.

Original_Search_Title: Democracy Has Won Again
Headline: Nigeria: Democracy Has Won Again
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Olusegun Obasanjo is one of the few universally respected leaders in Africa. In fact, he is one of the very few who can be accorded the accolade - statesman - not only because of his brave attempt to adhere to democratic tenets in a country that is as unruly as any on the continent, but also for his many selfless attempts to broker peace in the West African region.
But he was about to make a terrible mistake. He was about to join the cabal of African leaders who believe they are indispensable to their countries' survival.
For his pains, he was given a rather sharp reprimand by his own Senate, and told to prepare to call it a day, go home, and grow yams. That will be next year when his second, and final, four-year term ends as spelled out in the constitution he himself helped promulgate.
President Obasanjo's supporters had been campaigning for years to amend the constitution so that he can run again, and he, in the best African strongman tradition, had kept a studious silence on the matter, ostensibly waiting for the "will of the people" to prevail. Well, it did, but not in the manner he may have expected.
There is no doubt Mr Obasanjo has been good for Nigeria. Never before in its history has the oil giant been this calm, despite pockets of trouble here and there. Its economic growth has been steady under his stewardship, and coups d'etat seem to have been banished.
But as his own estranged vice-president Atiku Abubakar commented, the setback for the third term proponents "confirms that democracy has a teeming army of believers and converts who will defend it" despite harassment and intimidation. This is certainly another victory for democracy in Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Exclusive: Why I Want Third Term
Headline: Nigeria: Exclusive: Why I Want Third Term
Body_Text:
Lagos — Wonders shall never end. After overloading my helpless tummy with several balls of pounded yam, I slumped into my couch, picking my teeth and belching carelessly. Predictably, I fell asleep and began to snore. Then, the president appeared to me in my dream and said: "Son, ask for anything and I will do it for you." I quickly asked for an interview and he obliged, surprisingly answering all the questions with candour. Before I woke up, we had discussed virtually all the questions that had been bothering me. Excerpts:
Simon Kolawole Live!: Your Excellency, it's nice to have you talk to me unscheduled. I thought you hate journalists
His Excellency: and dogs, don't forget. I hate journalists and dogs.
SKL: Are you really interested in third term?
HE: My son can I call you my son?
SKL: I'm flattered sir. I'm not worthy to be called your son.
HE: I will call you my son.
SKL: If you insist
HE: You see, this government has achieved a lot. Everybody now has GSM. I can see that you alone have two handsets with you. Before I came, did you even have a NITEL line at home? You journalists have to be fair to me.
SKL: So you need another term so that you can auction more GSM licences?
HE: I got debt relief and you people did not even acknowledge it. You guys are so unfair. One upstart called Salmon Kola whatever he called himself wrote a silly article criticising the deal. That is why I don't like journalists.
SKL: So you need another term so that you can secure another debt relief?
HE: On the international stage, Nigeria is now more respected. During the time of Abacha, Nigeria was a pariah state. Things are getting better now. You can see the role I have been playing in Liberia, Sudan, Togo, all over Africa.
SKL: So you need another term so that you can resolve the crisis in Iraq?
HE: We have done banking consolidation. Now we have 25 solid banks. They are so solid they can never be distressed again. All small scale industries can now apply for loan and get it in two hours. Lending rates are quite inviting.
SKL: So you need another term so that you can do another round of banking consolidation?
HE: Our external reserves are now $33 billion
SKL: And if you leave next year, your successor will steal the reserves. That's why they want to hurry you out of power
HE (grins): God bless you my son. Flesh and blood did not reveal that to you.
SKL: If you get another term, our reserves will be around $1 trillion by 2011. Because your successor may steal it, won't patriotism tempt you to amend the constitution again to stay back and protect the reserves?
HE: We'll cross the bridge when we get there.
SKL: Let's say you amend the constitution again in 2011 and stay till 2015, the reserves may hit $2 trillion by then. You may have to sit tight until 2019 because your successor may steal it. You're the only honest Nigerian. Every other Nigerian is a thief. Even when you die, your ghost must continue to rule us so that nobody will steal our external reserves. Your hands are very, very, very clean.
HE: To God be the glory.
SKL: Hallelujah!
HE: Are you a Christian, my son?
SKL: You're the only Christian in Nigeria sir. I'm not worthy to share your glory.
HE: My son, don't be cynical. I mean well for Nigeria. Why I want a third term, that's if I want it at all, is to be able to groom a successor. There is no successor in waiting.
SKL: If you could not groom a successor in eight years (1999-2007), what magic will make you groom one in four years (2007-2011)?
HE: My focus was reform programme. Next is succession programme.
SKL: What exactly will be the contents of the successor-grooming programme?
HE: I will teach my successor-trainee the theories of Economics, Politics and Finance. No other Nigerian knows these, except me. I'm a rare blessing to this country.
SKL: You almost forgot to mention continuity and consolidation as one of your excuses. What exact part of your reform programme is your successor going to reverse? What remains of your reform programme that cannot be implemented before May 29, 2007? What companies remain to be privatised and what sector needs to be reformed that cannot be done by May next year?
HE: Can you come again, my son?
SKL: On page iii of NEEDS, you stated: "Over the next few years (2003-07), NEEDS will consolidate the achievements of the previous four years and lay a solid foundation " All the talk about "consolidation" is based on the period between 2003 and 2007, not 2011. NEEDS' projections are based on the 2007 exit date. Generating 10,000 megawatts of electricity by 2010, for instance, is based on what happens with regards to power sector reforms by 2007.
HE: I don't seem to understand you.
SKL: You claim to have met and even surpassed NEEDS targets! Recently, you said GDP growth in 2006 would be 10%, whereas the projection in NEEDS is 6%. NEEDS targets $9.2 billion as external reserves this year, but your government says we would have $50 billion by December. Going by the figures being churned out, we can conclude that you have even surpassed your own expectations. So what on earth is this unfinished business that you want to accomplish with a third term?
HE: Continuity, my son, continuity
SKL: On the same page in NEEDS, you wrote, concerning continuity: "It is this national ownership, together with the results already visible, that will ensure the sustainability of NEEDS beyond 2007". Nowhere did you state that it is only one man who can ensure its sustainability.
HE: Nigeria is like an aircraft that has just taken off
SKL: And when you want to break through the cloud, the pilot says fasten your belt.
HE: You just said that? I can't believe a journalist can be this intelligent.
SKL: I'm flattered sir, but it was not a journalist who said it. It was Georgie, the Lagos Boy who passed through Ondo, who made that profound statement.
HE: Don't mind Georgie. What does he know about flying? He's a Naval man. It is not when you're breaking through the skies that the pilot says fasten your seatbelt. It is before the aircraft takes off at all.
SKL: Georgie also said we are in a stormy weather, so we cannot change pilot until we're cruising at a high altitude.
HE: He was obviously misquoted. We are not in any stormy weather. Yes, we were in stormy weather before I came in 1999. But how can we still be in stormy weather after seven years of my piloting the affairs of Nigeria? Is Georgie trying to insinuate that I am not performing? And he calls himself my loyalist!
SKL: I apologise on his behalf, Your Excellency. He didn't mean any harm. He was just being overzealous on your behalf. He only misfired, as usual.
HE: He's a good boy, so I believe the media misquoted him. Where were we, my son?
SKL: You were giving reasons why you need another term
HE (visibly angry): Here we go again. Have I ever told you I want a third term? When did I say that? That is one of the problems with journalists.
SKL: It seems you're getting upset. Should I take my leave, sir?
HE: Before you go, see my aide for your transport fare. You journalists always collect transport fare after interviews. You think I don't know the secrets of your trade?
SKL: That one is below the belt sir. But I don't want transport fare. I don't want
HE: Is it because of my anti-corruption campaign? Are you afraid of Ribadoo?
SKL: Sort of
HE: Don't worry about that. He won't go after you except I tell him to do so.
SKL: That's not the impression he gives us sir. He says there is no sacred cow.
HE: And you believe him? Tell him to go after the NPA looters. Challenge him to arrest the Fixer over the N50 billion budget for Ministry of Works
SKL: You mean N150 billion, Your Excellency
HE: Yes, N150 million. Thank you for the correction. Ask him to arrest Ali Baba over the dirty deals at UNTH. Let him arrest legislators who are each collecting N50 million "amendment allowance" at the Last Highland Bank.
SKL: Maybe he's not aware of the "amendment allowance" yet.
HE: Wake up, my boy. Ribadoo is a security officer. There is enough information in the newspapers for him to follow up and investigate. Don't forget he nailed Tafa the Thief and Adolfus the Naive. Ribadoo is smarter than you think.
SKL: You're on tape sir. Should I publish that?
HE: No, my son. That is off record.
SKL: Okay sir. Many Nigerians abroad believe you're doing well.
HE: That's very good, but what are they still doing abroad? They should come home and enjoy the new Nigeria. If they're studying, why don't they come and school in Nigeria? If their children are ill, why don't they fly them down to Nigeria to receive first-class treatment? If they are working over there, why can't they come home and take some of the 10 million jobs I've created in seven years? If they truly believe in me, they should come home and enjoy 24-hour electricity.
SKL: Your critics say you hate criticism. When Professor Wole
HE (furious): Just don't mention his name here. I don't listen to unbelievers.
SKL: But you don't listen to God either. When a man of God, Archbishop John, spoke, your spokesman attacked him. Even when Rev. Peter criticised your government, you tongue-lashed him.
HE: That one is a pulpit economist.
SKL: Are you truly God-fearing or you are just pulling my leg? If you're God-fearing, how come you supported the destroyers of Anambra? Why are you still holding on to Lagos State council funds? Why have you not given the IG marching orders to unmask those who killed Ige, Harry and Dikibo, with the same zeal they displayed in dislodging La-dodger in Oyo? Why are you victimising some governors while some known crooks and assassins among them are having a swell time in your presence? Is this part and parcel of your Christian virtues, my father?
HE: You can't understand, my son.
SKL: Who are your modern African heroes?
HE: The three Ms-Museveni, Mugabe and Mobutu, in that order.
SKL: What of the other M?
HE: Which M?
SKL: Mbeki
HE: That one is a black head of a white country. That's why he doesn't want to amend a man-made constitution to continue in office. He's an embarrassment to the black race. In Africa, kings don't quit-they die on the throne. Without me, Nigeria is doomed. I'm the only Nigerian who can rule this country. Tell me, who among 135 million Nigerians can take over from me? Nigeria can do nothing without me.
SKL: How great thou art!!!

Original_Search_Title: Meet Pygmy Who Fought Two Gorillas
Headline: Cameroon: Meet Pygmy Who Fought Two Gorillas
Body_Text:
The stitches on his left lower lip, the indelible scar on his left fore arm and the bruises on his back, give you an inkling of the ferocity of the battle Gilbert Mbwapeh, 54, a Baka pygmy residing in Ngoyla, East Province of Cameroon, fought with two gorillas.
It was a battle Mbwapeh employed his last strength and solicited god's intervention to win. Ask how he managed to emerge intact from the grip of two gorillas and he tells you: "God was my saviour."
This toothless old man, who often moves barefoot, had gone looking for "wild yams" and games in the forest when two gorillas attacked him. "One of them held my neck from behind while the other attacked in front, trying to tear my mouth," Mbwapeh told The Post during a mission to the 3000-hectare Nki National Park.
When the battle got hotter, one of the gorillas made a desperate bid to grab Mbwapeh's legs, after the old man had successfully elbowed the other that walked him from behind.
"I soon found my buttocks on the ground. I had to desperately pull the trigger of my gun, blowing off the head of one of the gorillas," he said. The other gorilla, perceiving defeat, took off after tearing Mbwapeh's lower lip and inflicting a big wound on his left forearm.
"I struggled and got to the hospital, with blood dripping from my torn lip, all alone," he said. Raising his hands towards the sun, the old man once again thanked God for coming to his rescue.
Of all the animals in the Nki forest, Mbwapeh told The Post, the gorillas has a penchant for attacking first. "Though the elephant is the most feared animal in the forest, it would hardly attack you unless it feels provoked or threatened. I have never been attacked by an elephant but I have received several threats and attacks from gorillas," he said.
Unflinching
This visceral attack has not discouraged Mbwapeh from visiting the forest barefoot. "I depend on the forest for survival. I was born in the forest and my parents lived here. They taught me how to walk barefoot while I was growing up."
According to Mbwapeh, he was able to cure his wounds thanks to his knowledge of traditional medicine. "I used herbs to check blood oozing from my wounds." He explained that Bakas are good herbalists and can cure many diseases.
They Believe In God
Contrary to generally held notion that Bakas do not believe in an Almighty God, they are Roman Catholic Christians. "I know God exist. We worship in the catholic church," Mbwapeh stated.
However, they also believe there is a god of the forest called Jengi. According to Mbwapeh, Jengi cures and protects them in times of danger. "Komba (Baka word for God) gives Jengi the strength to protect them," Mbwapeh revealed.

Original_Search_Title: Mbale At Risk of Cholera Over Poor Disposal of Human Waste
Headline: Uganda: Mbale At Risk of Cholera Over Poor Disposal of Human Waste
Body_Text:
Mbale — The National Environment Management Authority has said Mbale Municipality and in particular the industrial division has 600kg of human excreta discharged daily indiscriminately in the open.
According to the Nema health inspection report on the Mbale Municipality industrial division, human waste is discharged into yams, sugarcane gardens and other bushes because of lack of sanitary facilities.
Nema warning
Nema warns that the indiscriminate discharge into the open has a deadly impact on people's health as it makes the industrial division a dangerous spot for the outbreak of diseases like cholera and dysentery.
"An inspection of the Mbale regional Abattoir, food processing stores, eating places, wood works, small and medium industries, reveals that the entire industrial area lacks sanitary facilities," read the report signed by Ms Mary K Mukimba, the Mbale district environment officer.
"This is a clear indication why 600kg of human excreta is being discharged daily into yams and sugarcane gardens and other bushes around the industrial area."
Mukimba says inspection of the above areas revealed that the entire industrial area is characterised by poor sanitary conditions where waste is indiscriminately dumped comprising a lot of polythene bags.
Consumers at risk
The report says that the food processing mills are using crude and untidy processing methods where they use their feet to mix maize together with water before it is milled thereby putting the lives of the consumers at risk.
The report was a response to several complaints by the residents of the area led by Mr Pierre Rwandarugari.
Petition
According to a letter dated September 20, 2005, signed by 26 people, the residents petitioned Nema about poor health in the area due to lack of sanitary facilities, the process of cooking and drying blood, burning of horns and dirty eating places.
When contacted, the Town Clerk, Mr Kenneth Waniaye, said he had not received any letter from the residents.
"I am new in the office but of course it is true that there is uncontrolled pollution in the place and a number of premises lack sanitary facilities like toilets," Waniaye said on April 15. He said Mbale Municipal local government council will inspect the area to asses the health problems related to pollution.

Original_Search_Title: Stationary Vehicles And Fatalities On Our Roads
Headline: Ghana: Stationary Vehicles And Fatalities On Our Roads
Body_Text:
New Jersey — Last year or so, when it was announced that persons who failed to complete basic elementary education will in future be ineligible for drivers' licenses in Ghana, I wrote on Ghanaweb to condemn the proposed regulation as a policy driven by prejudice and ignorance, rather than facts or reason.
Unfortunately, so much of what passes for regulation and policy in Ghana is made that way; no hard facts based on research or primary knowledge; just subjective hunch or speculative thinking.
Supporters of the proposed law, which for me is little more than a blatant, class-based discrimination, claim that the policy is necessary to reduce traffic accidents, implying that somehow 'uneducated' drivers - and a lack of formal education - are responsible for most traffic accidents and fatalities on our roads. I continue to believe there are no facts to back this claim, nor is the claim supportable by common knowledge or history of driving in Ghana.
There are, however, more than sufficient facts and common knowledge to support the observation that there are too many vehicles immobilized on Ghana's roads. Many of our roads meant for moving vehicles have been turned into parking lots and garages for immobilized or stationary vehicles.
