Vehicles that break down on our roads are left right there, sometimes for days, and when mechanics are eventually brought in to repair them, they do so there right on the roads, turning entire lanes into garages.
As the immobilized vehicle sits waiting to be repaired (almost never to be towed), the only notice that passing motorists get about the breakdown ahead is a few branches and twigs from a tree, scattered a few yards of metres away from the vehicle.
Obviously, these tree branched do not illuminate in the dark, heightening the danger of another vehicle traveling in the same lane running into it.
This, sadly, is how hundreds and thousands of lives are lost on Ghana's roads. These 'parked' vehicles, which have commandeered entire lanes unto themselves, have become death traps for unsuspecting drivers.
The tragedy is not that this problem exists, but that no solution appears in sight year after year. And yet, instead of thinking about solving this real problem, we divert attention into some prejudiced-driven discrimination that may or may not have much to do with this problem of immobilized vehicles or of accidents on our roads, generally.
I offer the proposal below for the attention and consideration of government; more specifically, the Ministry of Roads & Transport, the Ministry of Local Government, and the Ghana Police Service. It is free of charge; no consultant fees. All I ask is for somebody with the appropriate authority and responsibility to take this seriously for a change. It is dedicated to the memory of Mr. Ferdinand Ayim and all the countless lives lost before him; all victims of tragic avoidable 'accidents' on our nation's roads and highways.
Ghana needs a no-nonsense, well-regulated towing service industry to rid our roads and highways of these dangerous immobilized vehicles. The challenge is how to do this effectively nationwide.
First, I propose that each district assembly and district police command be given towing rights and authority over the roads and highways that lie within their territorial jurisdiction.
Second, each district assembly should be required to purchase tow trucks and equipment and operate a towing service, or assign the right to licensed private sector operators within their districts. The Ministry of Roads and Transport, in collaboration with the Ministry of Local Government, can pre-finance the initial purchase of the trucks and heavy-duty equipment for the districts, with repayment of the cost to be made by the districts from the revenues generated from operating the tow service.
Third, national law and regulation should authorize and require the towing by the relevant district assembly (with the assistance of the district police) of any vehicle that is immobilized on any road or highway within the jurisdiction of a district.
In cases where towing is not immediately feasible, the vehicle must be officially impounded on the spot, and while it sits there, all of its cargo must be off-loaded or the cargo portion detached and relocated, and the immobilized vehicle monitored ad illuminated by the police at night.
The district assembly must levy fines for every hour or specific fraction of the day the vehicle remains immobilized and impounded on the road. The amount of the fine should be based in the gross weight of the vehicle, with tankers or haulage and timber trucks paying as much as 1 million cedis (or more) in fine for each hour or specified fraction of the day they remain impounded on the road, plus a surcharge if the vehicle remains immobile overnight. Vehicles towed should also be levied a fee for each hour or specified fraction of the day they remain in the custody of the district, subject to the right of the district to dispose of the vehicle by public auction if it has not been claimed after a certain fixed number of days. The district assembly concerned should retain proceeds from the fines and auction for local development.
Fourth, to ensure accountability, the relevant district assembly and district police command should be held responsible, and the appropriate officers subjected to discipline, including discharge and, in appropriate cases, prosecution for criminal dereliction of duty, if a fatal accident occurs involving that was not towed or impounded.
Fifth, motorists would be required under the proposed regulation to make a report o the appropriate district assembly and district police command immediately their vehicle becomes immobilized on a highway.
Failure to make a timely report would be a separate criminal offense. Owners of vehicles immobilized on the roads that cause accidents should be held criminally liable (in addition to the drivers) for any resulting deaths or damage to property, and be subjected to penalties that must include a confiscation of the vehicle and stiff fines. The government should also encourage and advise families of victims in such accidents to bring tort suits, including wrongful death suits, against drivers and owners of such immobilized vehicles. This may require new wrongful death statutes to extend strict civil liability to vehicle owners.
Finally, the existing regulation (I presume there is one) prohibiting vehicles exceeding a certain weight from traveling at night should be extended to and enforced in a decentralized fashion against all vehicles carrying certain exposed cargo, especially timber, charcoal, yams or bags of grains. All such vehicles must be required to park away from the roads after sunset and recommence their journeys at daybreak. The same rule should apply to any vehicle whose tail or brake lights are defective.
Again, authority must be granted to district assemblies to enforce this regulation, in collaboration with the police and private towing services. Criminal penalties, including impoundment and towing of vehicle and punitive fines, must be levied against vehicles that remain on the roads in violation of the 'no night driving' regulation. Also, in the event of an accident involving any such vehicle, the vehicle owners must be strictly and criminally liable.
While the immediate goal of this proposal is to get immobilized vehicles off our roadways, the ultimate effect would be to make it extremely punitive, both financially and criminally, for vehicle owners who allow their 'road-unworthy' vehicles to ply our nation's roads. The system of 'road-worthiness certificates' has obviously failed, and we need to put in place new or additional measures to deal with the risks that vehicles in poor mechanical conditions continue to pose on our roads.
My proposal also aims to apply appropriate 'carrots and sticks' to support decentralized enforcement of discipline on our highways and provide them with the necessary legal authority, facilities, financial incentives and accountability to get the job done.
If government cannot find and implement appropriate remedies to basic, recurring everyday problems like these, what else do we need government for?
My proposal may not solve or prevent every road accident caused by immobilized vehicles, and I haven't aimed at perfection in this initial proposal.
But one thing I do know for sure is that my modest proposal is better than doing nothing or issuing driver's licenses only to speakers and readers of the Queen's English. (Will the Queen's English speakers and readers also be the ones exclusively qualified to drive the 'tro tro's and tankers and tipper trucks?) Development is not rocket science, but it will not happen by chance either. To make progress as a nation, we must begin with the simple things of life and everyday problems first. Along with W. Arthur Lewis' prescient book, Politics in West Africa (1965), Lee Kwan Yew's memoir, From Third World to First: The Singapore Story (2000), must become required reading for everybody who aims to enter politics or public service in Ghana.
Have we not had enough already of the 'blind leading the blind' - leading us all to our avoidable death?

Original_Search_Title: N1bn Property Burnt in Police, Umuaka Youths Clash
Headline: Nigeria: N1bn Property Burnt in Police, Umuaka Youths Clash
Body_Text:
Owerri — Property estimated at about N1 billion was reportedly burnt, looted or destroyed during the clash between the police and youths in Umuaka, Njaba local government area of Imo State, even as the state government has called for immediate ceasefire.
Governor Achike Udenwa who gave the directive when he paid an unscheduled visit to the troubled area Monday evening, equally urged the youths to remain calm, promising that his administration would set up a panel of inquiry to critically look into the matter as well as apportion blames where necessary.
Chief Udenwa also directed the police to stop further crackdown on the citizenry and dismantle all the roadblocks mounted in the community.
In his own contribution, the Deputy Speaker, Imo State House of Assembly, Chief Chuma Nnaji, who doubles as the member representing the area in the State Assembly, appealed for calm, promising that government was very desirous to get at the root cause of the crisis.
Meanwhile, when Vanguard visited the community, apart from the Divisional Police Station, which was allegedly torched by the rampaging angry youths, some policemen living in Achara area of Umuaka, lamented over what they termed as the losses they recorded while the crisis lasted.
In the city centre however, Vanguard noticed that several other private homes and business outfits were similarly burnt, destroyed or looted, while some villagers who spoke on the crisis, accused the police of inflicting varying degrees of destruction on over 200 vehicles, especially those parked in automobile workshops.
A young man from Isiozi and another yam dealer from Obinwanne, simply identified as Jude, were allegedly shot by uniform men during their reprisal attack, while others were seen by villagers, carting away goats belonging to two yam sellers in the community, Andrew Obodozie and Chibueze Osuagwu.
At the accident scene, Mr. Hyginus Uzoma, a relation of the deceased driver, Ekenedilichukwu Bede Uzoma, told Vanguard that two other passengers, Ahamefula Uzoma and a mechanic, died in the accident.

Original_Search_Title: Little Things That Count
Headline: Ghana: Little Things That Count
Body_Text:
Accra — The importance of domestic tourism data harmonization in tourism development
At independence in March 1957, Ghana was a continental show-piece, the Star of Africa, leading the colonial pack with a vibrant economy from her gold, diamond, manganese, timber and, of course, cocoa. Politically, Osagyefo Dr. Kwame Nkrumah led his people to prosecute the independence of Africa because "the independence of Ghana is meaning-less" without the total liberation of Africa. Ghana was then rich, brave, courageous and willing to throw down the gauntlet or pick it up as the case may be.
Almost fifty years on, Ghana is a pale shadow of her former wealthy self: poverty, disease, hunger, frustration, all digging her at the heel.
Ghanaians are rushing out of the country seeking greener pastures no matter what and damn the consequences. Armed robbery is common, bribery and corruption rife, people are doing drugs including "honourable" members of Parliament dishonoring themselves, getting arrested and corroding the country's image. All these go to point to how Ghana's riches have dwindled.
Yet, even religion does not seem to be a panacea of sorts for her recovery. Religion has become opium for the people.
Twenty years on after independence, Ghana realized the need to revise her economic calendar.
Ghana's economy dipped very low in the early 1980s and 1983. Ghanaians became so emaciated because of food shortage that a term was coined, "wearing the Rawlings' chain". This referred to the former Flt-Lt's visible bones around the neck when he became ruler through his overthrow of Dr. Hilla Limann in December 1981.
The international community did little to help Ghana eventually forcing her to go to bed with the Bretton Woods' institutions, the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank.
"Necessity is the mother of invention", it is said, so Ghana turned to what she termed "non-traditional exports" to help the economy revive and survive; this, Ghanaians did, through the export of yams, pineapples, snails, crafts and sumptuous mushrooms.
One other economic means was through tourism in the attempt to resuscitate the economy.
Ghana abounds in tourist sites; these include her beautiful beaches, forts and castles, cascading water-falls, lakes, historic museums, game reserves and the exotic flora and fauna there-in.
But the industry was probably not well-managed despite its resourcefulness largely due to improper data/information collection and harmonization.
It is one reason why, today, tourism has lost its place as third foreign exchange earner to remittances from abroad. Tourism now ranks fourth on that scale. With this realization, the Management of the Ashanti Regional Tourist Board decided to organize a one-day Domestic Tourism Date Collection training programme for workers of various tourist sites in the region.
These included staff from the Prempeh Museum at the Cultural Centre at Bantama; the Manhyia Palace Museum; the Kumasi Zoo, the Military Museum, the Komfo Anokye Sword Site, the Bobiri Forest, where butterflies of different species abound, Lake Bosomtwi at Kuntanase and the Travel and Tour Centre of Anglo Gold-Ashanti at Obuasi that organizes tourists to go down the bowels of the earth to see and learn at first hand how gold is exploited.
In his welcome address, the Regional Manager of the Ghana Tourist Board, Kumasi, Ben Anane-Nsiah, outlined the basis for the training programme.
He said that it was a matter of national importance and objective; "we need reliable statistics/data to manage the tourism sector, have an idea of the trends, customer impressions and use the numbers to measure the impact of the industry on the economy."
He stated that the Board and Ministry of Tourism and Modernization of the Capital City "need to do proper, scientific management of tourism, that is collect data of people coming into the country as tourists, where they originate from, their impressions of the product which all give an idea of our economic pattern; it also gives the country the chance to put a reasonable package for the visitors."
More than this, tourism data/information harmonization and networking was necessary because it would lead to knowledge of customers and since the collection of data relates to people, it would help to deal with them professionally.
Ghanaians, he noted, are usually suspicious of numbers because they think they would be used for tax collection.
"Tourism is a renewable resource but it could never be a magic wand for turning the economy round," he said. "Additionally, it may also have negative effect or impact as well," citing exploitation of tourists, people doing drugs, and human trafficking.
"Ghana's target of one million tourists in year 2007 cannot be achieved if accurate statistics are not collected and collated. It is therefore important for all stake-holders to play a positive part in tourism development and promotion. Such co-operation would lead to tourism having a great impact on Ghana's economy," the Regional Manager said.
Martin Twum was one of the resource persons at the training programme. He defined the tourist as a person leaving his normal place of residence or environment to embark on a leisure or business trip elsewhere, for a certain period of time not exceeding one year and where he would not be renumerated.
Explaining further, Mr. Twum said that in 2005, the Minister of Tourism and Modernization of the Capital City managed to lobby government for an additional twenty billion cedis in addition to the initial eleven billion cedis allocated to the Minister.
This was because he had satisfactory data/information to influence decision from government to increase the allocation from the HIPC Fund. He has, in his submission cited Ghana's participation in the 2006 World Cup in Germany and preparation for CAF's football competition in Ghana come 2008.
On the multiplier effect, Mr. Twum, from the Research and Statistics Division of the Ghana Tourist Board Headquarters in Accra, said in 2005 the multiplier effect on employment was 3.5%. Tourism, in itself, generates employment; for example, a hotel may employ near-by companies to supply these needs; a cleaning company may also take up that job in the hotel thus, hypothetically, five more persons or more may be employed by these two subsidiaries.
In 2005, the multiplier effect on income was 3.4% in Ghana. Direct employment in 2004 in the tourism industry was 46, 502 employees while in 2005 the figure rose to 155,015 people.
Mr. Twum further emphasized the potential of tourism data/information collection as the prediction of future trends, by using the figures, assisting planning in the product development and marketing, making information decision and bringing about efficiency and growth in the industry.
Asked why there was need to standardize data collection formats, he gave various reasons: variables in the industry: uniformity: disaggregating specific variables like domestic tourism contribution as against foreign or external tourism aggregates. Participants were practically taken through existing data collection formats vis-Ã -vis harmonized formats. The required columns in the visitor register drawn accordingly were by nationality, country of residence, sex (male/female), children/adults and place of origin. He said enumerators of data/information collection behave like "garbage-in garbage-out". "If you input wrong data, you generate statistics", he said. This would misinform and mislead policy planners and decision makers: and what a tragedy this would spell for tourism in the country.
Collecting official must ensure that integrity of data: ensure that the correct data or information is solicited, avoid doctoring and such data and information must be carefully collected and analysed. Timely dissemination also ensures immediate use and timely, informed decisions taken.
Very down to earth, frank but entertaining, in his presentation, he noted the challenges workers in the industry face: assuming customer needs: rushing in collection of data: disrespect towards other staff: undermining: going beyond customer need: lack of communication: domineering attitude: stealing and sometimes "selling the product and selling oneself" on the part of lady tour guides.
He advised tour guides to improve upon their skills by learning all in all about their tourist sites to avoid embarrassment: know their environment well: read and learn more to be able to direct tourists, be polite, avoid stealing but above all be generous with their smiles. He also advised them to dress up decently and never to over-promise the clientele.
He said the four categories of tourists are the patrons, who come often to visit: raisers, who would appreciate the guide's efforts: walkers, who would storm off and talkers, who would talk the guide to death.
He prevailed on participants to turn walkers to patrons and talkers to raisers. He advised them to avoid bad odour, over-dressing in certain cases and poor self-maintenance.
Some of the participants complained of under-staffing, lack of logistics and differing gate-fees that arouse visitor suspicion and distrust. They said tourists can sometimes be over-bearing and haughty.
Worse, they complained, the black in the diaspora would refuse to meet whites and at times do want to pick up fights. People from the West Indies and the Caribbean's even blame Ghanaians for having sold their ancestors to the whites. Others refuse to fill data/information sheets.
Mr. Abrokwa advised tour guides to exercise restraint and tolerate what one participant called "smelling tourists".

Original_Search_Title: Gateway Games Special : Ogd's High Stakes And the Festival of Profits
Headline: Nigeria: Gateway Games Special : Ogd's High Stakes And the Festival of Profits
Body_Text:
ABEOKUTA, capital of the ancient Egba Kingdom wears a gay look as it welcomes visitors from across the country and abroad to the 15th National Sports Festival which starts today, with a promise of innovation and better organisation.
The drive for this innovation is the charismatic Governor of Ogun State, Otunba Gbenga Daniel, who has introduced a new dimension to civil governance by way of bringing in private sector efficiency into public bureaucracy. "This sports festival will be the very best ever organised in the history of sports organisation in this country. The Games would be made profitable through a professional drive that is unprecedented in Nigeria."
The Festival in Abeokuta is christened 'Gateway Games 2006'. Apart from the profit intent, it also promises to be the biggest, and perhaps, the most controversial. A key factor of these games is the reintroduction of elite (so-called professional) athletes. Whereas, several people see the inclusion of elite athletes as an aberration to the intent and purpose of the NSF, the pro camps see it as a much welcomed development.
"Who is a professional athlete in Nigeria?," Edo State director of sports, Mr. Brown Ebewele said "we discussed the prospects at the Abuja International Stadium, venue of the ExxonMobil Track and Field Championships. "The athletes are hungry and that is what every person of purpose and intent should be looking at and not whether some are professionals or amateurs."
Ebewele's position of elite athletes introduction is also concurring by the Director of Communication, Igbinedion University, Okada, Prince Victor Omoregie. "Let them open up the festival and stop this pretence about being a developmental event. What have we developed over the years? This is the showpiece of Nigeria's sports and it should be made to be. They can have other developmental events like schools sports festival and other age-grade competitions, not the National Sports Festival which should be to showcase our very best," Omoregie said.
The athletes are very enthusiastic about this latest turn of event, although it is coming with a limited scope of 11 elite athletes per state. Already, states are positioning to get the better of the situations after the early acrimony of the policy shift.
The tendencies, however seem misplaced as states view their successes in the festival by coming tops, and in this regards, they all seem to be talking tough of dethroning Delta and Edo states from the top of the ladder. It doesn't really matter whether they are prepared for the event or not, the focus appears more like, 'these Edo and Delta are the states to beat."
"That is a very wrong concept and it is the very reason they are not succeeding in their drive for excellence. How much do these states, including Lagos spend on infrastructural and athletes' development? Do they know the budget of sports in a state like Delta that they want to wake up one morning and say 'we want to beat Edo and Delta states'?
"We would not fold our arms and wait on them to beat us as they wish. But I can tell you that at the end, the states with the biggest budgets would still excel at the festival. And in this regard, Delta and Edo would still come tops. Never mind the tough talk of Ogun State. They really don't have what it takes to win the festival. Just watch what would happen at the Games because we already see their maneuvering and I make bold to say, no scheming would work magic. Only hard work would enable any state win the games and not political talks," Ebewele said.
Fun and sports in Ijebu-Ode
The Ogun State government would set very high standard for future hosts with the precedence of multi centres with state of the art facilities, which only oil rich Delta could likely surpass. One of the several venues for the Gateway Games, Ijebu-Ode promises to be the 'fun and sports centre' of the games.
"At Ijebu-Ode, the weekend starts on Thursday and ends when your money ends," Weekend Vanguard deputy editor, Kunle Adekoya said.
He should know better, being a son of the soil. He had given me the Ijebu culinary delights with ikokore as the main dish on the menu. It is a water yam base food, cooked with fresh fish; spiced with hot pepper and other condiments. The Ijebu are friendly people whose commerce is legendary. Otunba Mike Adenuga, is a testament of that business heritage. "There is nothing to fear in Ijebu-Ode at night. The athletes would appreciate nightlife here," he adds.
The government recently completed a new stadium which was test run last Sunday when the national women under 20 soccer team over-ran their Kenyan visitors 8-0 in a qualifying game for FIFA's world tournament coming up later in Moscow, Russia.

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan: 100 Days After
Headline: Nigeria: Jonathan: 100 Days After
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bayelsa state which prides itself as the glory of all lands is
unique in so many ways. It has only 8 local government areas, two short
of the constitutional requirements. The 1999 constitution provides for
a minimum of 10 Local Government Areas for each state.
Till date, Bayelsa remains the only state in the federation that is
yet to be connected to the national grid. This is in spite of an N8
billion contract awarded by the Federal Government to Pivot Engineering
Company Limited more than three years ago.
Minister of Power and Steel, Senator Liyel Imoke had promised to
hook the state to the national grid last December. Nearly three months
after the promise, the state which was created on October 1, 1996 by
the late head of state, General Sani Abacha, remains in darkness, and
no one knows when there will be electricity supply in a state that
contributes a significant chunk of the nation's total income. Residents
of the state depend on the services of the Kolo Creek/Nun River Gas
Turbine Power Station, Imiringi for their energy needs.
This is the state where Governor Goodluck Jonathan holds court as
the new lord of the manor. His emergence was by divine intervention. He
did not set out to be the number one citizen of the state. At least,
not in 2005. Perhaps, in 2007. But fate played a crucial role in his
emergence as the second executive governor of the only wholly Ijaw
state in the federation. As a result of their main occupation, fishing,
the Ijaws are scattered in the fishing ports, hamlets and villages of
all the costal states, particularly, Rivers, Delta, Edo, Akwa Ibom,
Ondo, Lagos and Cross River. However, it is Bayelsa they regard as
their ancestral home.
It is the lot of the Otuoke born politician to give them a sense of
belonging and hope by providing the dividends of democracy. To meet the
yearnings and aspirations of the Ijaw in Nigeria and in the Diaspora is
a task that Jonathan cannot handle with kid gloves. Besides, the
circumstances of his emergence as the governor of the wealthy but poor
state presuppose that he must only perform but be seen to deliver the
basic necessities of life.
Jonathan is not a stranger in governance. From 1999 till December
2005, he was the Deputy governor. And only a few will say he did not
blend well with his erstwhile boss, Chief Diepreye Alamieyeseigha.
Reason? Alamieyeseigha unlike other governors, did not have cause to
look for another deputy governor as the loyalty and competence of
Jonathan was legendary. He represented the state well in private and
public functions. Rumours of impeachment were not common, even when the
former governor was not in town. And the days he was not in town were
not few.
But as our elders would say, there is a wide gap between being a
pilot and a co-pilot. What is the situation on ground since the
emergence of Jonathan as the governor of the oil and gas rich state?
How has the people fared since Jonathan took over the reigns of
governance? After one hundred days, has Bayelsa moved forward or
backward? Is it still business as usual?
Some may argue that one hundred days are not enough to assess an
administration. But the fact remains that as the morning tells the day,
the first one hundred days of Jonathan in the saddle would in no small,
way indicate the direction of the government.
Already, there are strong indications that Jonathan knows where he
is going to if the array of appointees is anything to go by. The
cabinet is largely made up of those who have not been tainted by the
oddities of the past. Men and women of proven integrity have been
assigned various roles and responsibilities to achieve what has been
tagged "the Bayelsa Dream".
It is instructive to note that apart from the appointment of a
two-time former speaker, Hon. Peremobowei Ebebi, as the deputy governor
of the state, Jonathan also appointed a technocrat and former
Consul-General in the Republic of Cameron, Dr. Boladei Igali as the
Secretary to the State Government. Until his appointment, Igali was in
the Presidency where he worked closely with President Olusegun Obasanjo.
Within the first one hundred days of his administration, Jonathan is
already showing the colour and character of his administration. A
foundation for the construction of a 1000 housing units for civil
servants in the state has been laid. The state deputy governor, Hon.
Peremibowei Ebebi, performed the ceremony. The Housing Estate will be
allocated on owner- occupier basis.
In a bid to avoid the one city syndrome plaguing its parent state,
Rivers, a model Yenagoa city which will take not less than 100 hectares
is to be constructed. The state House of Assembly has already given a
stamp of authority with the passing of a bill.
The state government is collaborating with a business construction
company from South Africa, comprising Jurong International, Groupgive,
Mvelapanda and Anyiam Osigwe Group (AOG) to make the plan a reality.
The first phase of the project will cost $200,000,000.
Jonathan has given approval for the construction of roads to link up
the three senatorial districts in the state. This is besides the
dualization of half kilometer to either side of the centre of the
East-West Road, Amassona-Torubene access road with a major bridge at
Ebeni, expansion and construction of Emeyal- Otuoke Road, and Swali
Traffic bye-pass to main Yenagoa and Swall Market. Already a take off
grant of N3.2 billion has been given to Gitto Construction Generali
Nigeria Limited to construct a state government-owned Tower Hotel and a
new conference centre.
It is not impertinent to note that the execution of these projects
would stimulate the economy of the state since it would provide
employment opportunities to the large army of jobless youths in the
state.
Many are of the view that if the youths of the state were fully
engaged, the incidence of youth restiveness would be minimized, if not
totally eradicated. This is not unconnected with the fact that only an
idle youth can become a ready tool in the hands of mischief elements in
the society.
Apparently aware that he can not go far in an atmosphere of anarchy
and chaos, Jonathan has made his young administration the rallying
point of the old and the young, men and women, chief and leaders of the
various organizations in the state. These include the Ijaw Youth
Council (IYC), Ijaw National Congress (INC), Bayelsa Elders Forum,
among several others.
These bodies, to say the least, were in disarray in the events that
led to the removal of Alamieyeseigha from office. Not a few are of the
opinion that if Jonathan maintains the pace he has carried on the
reigns of governance in the last 100 days, he would not only achieve
the goal of the 2006 budget which is wealth creation and eradication of
poverty, but also put himself in a vantage position for future
responsibilities.
Known for his simple look and gentle voice, Jonathan is the
threshold of history. He is not only holding the knife but also the
yam. What he does with the yam and the knife will be judged by
posterity.
Time is not on his side. Bayelsans are in a hurry to join the other
parts of the country. They want to enjoy those things the other parts
of the country have already taken for granted. And now that the
honeymoon is over, the doors are open for assessment. And the verdict
will be determined by his actions and inaction in the last 100 days and
beyond.

Original_Search_Title: Chinua Achebe And the Polity
Headline: Nigeria: Chinua Achebe And the Polity
Body_Text:
Lagos — It has been forty "deafeningly silent" years since Heinemann Publishers first released Chinua Achebe's very political novel on post-colonial Nigeria: A Man of the People. It aroused immense interest at its birth because of its uncanny ending.
There, in black and white, was a prediction of the coup d'Ã©tat set to rock the Nigerian polity only weeks later. The book had come out under that frontline publishing outfit's African Writers Series, AWS, of which Chinua Achebe was founding editor.
I have often wondered if Nora Ephron didn't speak the truth after all to the 'men of timber and caliber' (apologies to the late Ozumba Mbadiwe), who were swallowed up in that precedent-setting political earthquake, when he said: "I always read the last page of a book first, so that if I die before I finish, I'll know how it turned out!" Had they read and taken to heart A Man of the People , it would have taken care of them! For we mustn't ever forget that "the stories people tell have a way of taking care of them," according to Barry Lopez.
But I'm jumping way ahead of my thesis. Back in secondary school when I first 'did' it - like I did books of every sort with my peers: oh yes - we did books! - I waved aside A Man of the People as 'the hilarious Achebe.' No doubt about it; the book still sets one rollicking in waves of girth-racking mirth. There's simply no let-up to the fun it packs!
Grown up (which isn't always the same thing as aging), re-reading it now sets alarms clanging in my head. What Mahatma Gandhi said is really true: 'Politics is too important to be left in the hands of politicians'! My thesis: To get things right, who is better than the writer to take them up to pursue right to their logical end, for the benefit of all?
I think Achebe is uncanny, simply put. All right, it's his writing that is. But recall his 'Too Dangerous for Silence' speech just before the crisis in his home state, Anambra, came to a head? Now, that's what I call uncanny! If only people would read, they'd be ready
A Man of the People even has something to say about the current head-count. In a wry aside that has direct bearing on the political permutations in the realm, the writer has the people's thinking-represented by the narrator's father-cast in the following microstory from this political odyssey:
" I know some foreigners think we are funny with figures. One day when I was in the university, an old District Officer with whom my father had worked long ago came to our house [for] a call on his old interpreter. As they talked in the parlour my younger half-brothers and sisters kept up an endless procession in front of the strange visitor until he was constrained to ask my father how many children he had.
'About fifteen,' said my father.
'About? Surely you must know.'
My father grinned and talked about other things. Of course he knew how many children he had but people don't go counting their children as they do animals and yams. And the same I fear goes for our country's population."
The political party in power in A Man of the People is Peoples Organisational Party, P.O.P. Now try 'drawing out' the difference between 'O' and 'D'! Its main opponent (apposite, actually) is 'Progressive Alliance Party', P.A.P, described in the book as 'weak and disorganised'. You know I know what that one is harbinger of! There's yet a 3rd - a mÃ©lange of hotheaded eggheads to which the hero of A Man of the People is drawn: principally for vendetta over the loss of his girlfriend to a minister in whose house he'd been an invited guest! This party is simply interested in securing a beachhead for sometimes to come. But it seems its leadership can only speak from both sides of the mouth. Not so for our hero, who becomes indifferent with the whole scheme of things at the end.
Now, just when will it be the turn of 'the man for the people', one who will truly perform, especially to make power generation and distribution the norm in the land - and damn the bludgeoning generator sets and diesel-vending mafias ravaging the land?
Right now Nigeria stands at a crossroads, a make-or-mar socio-political transition. But maybe that's too drastically hilarious a conclusion to reach yet. But again, time will tell, as it did in 1966.
My work of late is to help engender a culture of reading in the land. Believe me, that's its own reward. And this reading culture must be, I always insist, principally of stories. 'Stories,' said Chinua Achebe, 'are not just meant to make us smile [or, smirk] Our life depends on them.'
According to art critic Kenneth Burke: 'Stories are equipment for living', the spells that compel us to go on. These make up the infrastructure of our 'belief system' with which, says Forbes publisher, Rich Karlgaard, if it's the right one, "we can predict the future." A Man of the People is right on cue here for foretelling Nigeria's future, I submit.
From my recent reading of the book, I'm now really desperate to get its story into the consciousness of the people it speaks so squarely to, to their Ego. For, according to Freudian psychology, this is the part of the mind with which we think and take action. From there it should percolate into the Id, the part of the mind that is completely unconscious but has hidden needs and desires. Desires for change
I'm convinced that the required solution lies in dramatizing the story professionally to yield an audio book. 'Talking book', I think, will be more telling a term for the African! Thus would the cycle be complete: oral (its proto-state) to written (its present form) to aural (the projected format). See? On the archetypical 'Israelite journey' model - a national odyssey precipitated and perpetuated by crippling unbelief and dreadful nonchalance, much like ours - perhaps now there'll be a 'settling in'.
Big time publishers, Random House, have done it for even the most staid writing in their stable to bestsellers, like Michael Crichton's. And I've enjoyed quite a few Left Behind Series by Tyndale House in that format. The art has been 'finished' - no reservations as to 'publishing sense' whatsoever. I hope the powers that be here get the point enough to act on it!
People are naturally curious. If they like the 'talking book', they'll want to see it in print as well. Much like people go all out to secure the lyrics of songs they're taken with, which they then refer to over and over again. At least until the next rave release!
Having proposed a toast to A Man of the People on its 40th anniversary, the other aspect of my present work is to move for its induction into the Channels Outstanding Books (COB) list. I hope you will come to agree with me that the story is both succulent and serious enough for ones mental teeth. As for what's in it for the writer, I'll advise that his literary agent begin pursuing a deal with publishing houses with a history of making a success of audio books - in time for Prof. Achebe's 76th birthday come November 19th.
'Faith comes by hearing', states the Holy Bible (the same way fear makes its entrance into the human heart, mind you). Karlgaard writes: 'Beliefs have consequences, which are sometimes harsh'. But there are some others that are ennobling. The people can be made conscious of what's really at stake when any zealot shows up, promises to romp with their polity and claims to have elixir for its teething pains and so on.
With George Santayana clanging in their ears - 'those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it' - the people will then wag A Man of the People in the face of the political lot to say, caveat actor: 'Let the doer beware.' For, now, we are aware!
In the meantime, three happy cheers to A Man of the People at 40, when new life begins!

Original_Search_Title: Govt Threatens to Halt Relief Food Distribution
Headline: Zambia: Govt Threatens to Halt Relief Food Distribution
Body_Text:
Lusaka — GOVERNMENT has said reports of people selling relief food could cause it to halt the distribution exercise.
Giving an update on the relief food exercise for January and February, Office of the Vice-President Permanent Secretary Bernard Namachila warned communities concerned that the relief food they were receiving was not for sale.
"We are receiving disturbing reports that some beneficiaries of the relief food have started selling the same food while others continue crying out for more food," Namachila said. "This will make us reassess our position to reduce or even stop our supplies. "
Namachila said it was an anomaly for people to be crying for more relief food while others were selling it. "This is a contradiction at different wave lengths. This food is not for sale," he said.
Namachila said it was possible that those selling relief food had their own food now that green maize, yams and pumpkins were ready in the fields.
And Namachila said the Disaster Management and Mitigation Unit did not have appropriate airpower of its own and heavily depended on hired and tendered services from private firms to airlift relief food. He said consultations were still ongoing on how to reduce the time of the actual operation for the expected 21 days and also for the districts targeted to be reached.
Namachila appealed to district commissioners and distributing Non-Governmental Organisations (NGOs) to respond promptly to the distribution exercise to ensure all satellite areas of affected districts were covered.
"It is in those areas that the voices of the vulnerable communities are heard and come from," Namachila said. "It is from these distant rural areas that the most affected live. We shall follow and serve them there."

Original_Search_Title: Bird Flu Syndrome Hits Tamale
Headline: Ghana: Bird Flu Syndrome Hits Tamale
Body_Text:
Accra — A survey conducted by Public Agenda in Tamale shows that the deadly avian influenza is threatening the operations of many restaurants and hotels in the municipality.
For the avoidance of any doubt, customers of many restaurants and hotels are rejecting meals prepared with chicken or its by-products and are rather consuming fish and beef.
In an interview with Public Agenda, some restaurant and hotel managers in the Tamale metropolis who depended on chicken for their cuisines said they were running out of business because their customers have stopped patronizing food prepared with chicken. The problem has gotten to a head as most restaurants, hotels, cold stores and other traders in chicken are gradually running out of business.
As a result, some restaurants have stopped ordering for chicken and have resorted to fish and beef in a move to keep their customers. They are however feeling the heat, since fish is twice more costly than chicken.
"We use to sell 100 pieces of chicken tires a day, but for four days now we have sold less than 40, we now sell fish and beef but people do not buy the fish and beef like the chicken because the chicken is affordable," the manager of Big Byte restaurant, Mr Chris stated. He continued, "we are the only restaurant that operates seven days in a week. People's perception about the bird flu has also affected the sales of gizzard. Government needs to do something about it".
The situation was not different at the Ghana Commercial Bank Canteen. "The bird flu scare has affected our sales, we use to serve a full carton a day, but for the past three days even the half carton consignment has not finished, therefore we have stopped selling chicken. We now serve our customers with egg, fish and beef," the GCB canteen Manager, Issahaku Forgor said.
According to the proprietress of Club Ernesta, Esther Konadu, "anytime we serve our customers chicken, they reject it, therefore we have since stopped selling it". At Sparkles Restaurant at the Tamale Centre for National Culture the story was not very different. Many customers at Sparkles are workers of nongovernmental organizations and expatriates. The manager of the restaurant, Awaafo Desmond said the last NGO he hosted after their workshop, ordered for fish instead of chicken. He stated that though Sparkles uses only local fowls, the bird flu threat has affected his sales. "I am trying guinea fowl which is gradually catching up" he said.
Probably the hardest hit by the avian influenza threat are the cold stores owners in Tamale. The Manager of Enso Nyame Ye Cold Store, Alfred Nii Adjah corroborated the fact that NGOs that used to buy chicken for their workshops no more order for it. He said yam sellers have also resorted to buying fish instead of chicken. In his own words " this time when the restaurants come for the chicken they return it for fish, ..business is going down, I was selling 50 cartons a day, but now I hardly sell 15, .. something needs to be done about it".
Tohazie Hotel used to be a business spot for workshops, where chicken was in high demand. No more. The hotel's restaurant manager, Licious Gam has therefore called on the government to ban the importation of all poultry products into the country. "Our local chicken tastes better even without much work on it; with the foreign chicken you have to season it several times before it gets tasty, if government bans the importation of chicken it would boost our local industry and people will be convinced to consume the chicken," Mr Gam noted. Another hotel that expressed the same sentiment was Alhassan Hotel, situated in the heart of the central business district.
The Northern regional Veterinary Officer, Dr Kakari Agyemang says so far no outbreak of H5N1 Avian Influenza has been recorded. " We don't have a bird flu we are constantly monitoring the situation .we are doing our surveillance at market places, house to house, anywhere we have water bodies, where we know they have potential areas for migratory birds", he assures the public.
He admitted that the New Castle disease has infected many birds in the region, but that is different from bird flu. Dr Agyeman blamed the outbreak of New castle on the people's unwillingness to vaccinate their birds against the disease.
The Northern Regional Veterinary Officer described the common sign of New Castle in a fowl as "the funny robotic tactics of the fowl", which is the turning round of the fowl and continuous whitish diarrheoa and urged the residents not to mistake New Castle for Avian Influenza.
According to Dr Agyeman, his outfit is collaborating with the security agencies to ensure that no recalcitrant imports unauthorized poultry product into the region from neigbouring countries.

Original_Search_Title: Bluntly Speaking: Nana Yaa Ofori-Atta
Headline: Ghana: Bluntly Speaking: Nana Yaa Ofori-Atta
Body_Text:
IT IS with a certain amount of uneasiness, even trepidation, that I
use the term infant terrible to describe Nana Yaa Ofori-Atta, one of
our foremost radio presenters or hosts.
You see, the literal meaning of the French term means " a terrible child".
My daughter in Accra, who has seen Nana Yaa Ofori-Atta, describes
her as somewhat on the small side, physically. However, there is
nothing terrible about her and nothing infantile or infant-like about
her either.
I am not helped by definitions of the term, either. For example, the
1963 edition of BREWER'S DICTIONARY OF PHRASE & FABLE has the
following definition: "An embarrassing person, one who says or does
awkward things at inconvenient times."
The 1995 edition of CONCISE OXFORD DICTIONARY defines the phrase as
" a person who causes embarrassment by indiscreet or unruly behaviour."
THE BBC ENGLISH DICTIONARY states, " a clever but unconventional
person whose unusual behaviour or ideas cause anger or embarrassment
among their friends or colleagues."
The dictionary that came to my rescue somewhat is the OXFORD
ADVANCED LEARNERS'S DICTIONARY. ITS DEFINITION STATES, " a usually
young person whose behaviour, ideas, etc. annoy, shock or embarrass
those with more conventional attitudes."
The Usage Note states that the term is French and is often used
jocularly. That is exactly what I want. In other words, I do not use
the term in a condemnatory sense but rather in a commendatory sense. If
you ask me, Nana Yaa Ofori-Atta is undoubtedly one of our best radio
personalities today.
Nana Yaa Ofori-Atta is the regular host (or hostess, Nana?) of JOY
FM's Saturday programme, Newsfile. In a different context or situation,
what she sometimes says about the people who appear on the panel could
indeed be awkward, shocking, embarrassing or even abusive.
Her panel often consists of people whose yam you cannot cut with a
small knife, and whose palm wine you cannot pour into a small calabash,
as our Nigerian brothers and sisters might say.
They have included intellectuals, politicians of " timber and
calibre"(apologies to the late Nigerian politician Chief Dr. K.O.
Mbadiwe), lawyers, Ministers of State, Members of Parliament, etc.
From time to time, panelists get fractious, even obstreperous. That
is when Nana Yaa can say really terrible things about their behaviour.
Somehow, because of the context and situation and due to the way she
passes her comments, she succeeds in whipping them back into line
without their getting mad at her.
I have had occasion to describe Mr. Kweku Baako as "literally,
intellectually, politically and journalistically big-eyed." As a
regular fixture on the Newsfile programme, Kweku Baako can be
"troublesome". What seems to get his goat is what he perceives to be a
factual inaccuracy in the statement of another panelist.
Mr. Baako will immediately pounce even when it is not his turn and
it takes the whiplash of Nana Yaa to get him back into silence.
Sometimes, in approval or disapproval, Mr. Baako will indulge in sotto voce (in an undertone) interjections.
I have sometimes winced at some of the comments of Nana Yaa, like
the day she playfully described the conduct of Honourable Mahama
Ayariga as "dishonourable" and threatened to take him off the
programme. The following week, Honourable Ayariga was back, mutual sins
forgotten and forgiven. I have heard her tell a panelist, "W'abodam"
meaning "you are insane."
Did Nana Yaa have her tongue in her cheek when she once said in her
closing remarks that Mr. Ken Kuranchie, editor of The Searchlight
newspaper, had contributed to the discussion with the same lucidity
with which he edits his paper?
What are some of the weapons Nana Yaa brings to the programme?
First, she has a voice that can rise above that of the most persistent
panelist.
Nothing can drown that voice of hers. It simply rings distinctively. Next, Nana Yaa's command of English is simply amazing.
There is hardly mother-tongue interference in both stress and
intonation and yet there is none of the irritating, false accent that
characterizes the speech of some people, particularly some disc-jockeys
and sports presenters and hosts of entertainment programmes.
She has no problem reaching for the right word without the intrusion
of such speech mannerism as "You know", "I mean", "Erm", etc. The
language simply flows and rolls off her tongue rhythmically like
products on a conveyor belt. When she is in her element, she hardly
catches her breath. It is sheer delight listening to her.
Boy, does she have humour! as the Americans will say.
It is her keen sense of humour and the way she lets out the humour,
which guarantee that none of her "victims" would take offence.
Apart from the jibe at Ken Kuranchie noted above, she also once
silenced the same Kuranchie with the sarcasm, "Let us pretend that I am
the host of this programme."
When she reads a text message that praises a panelist, she will say
something like, "Kweku Baako, you got your cousin to send this one."
She passes other comments intended to provoke laughter.
Both her preliminary and closing remarks show intelligence and
conciseness. And she has trademarked the expression, "So what?" The
expression explodes as it comes like a bullet.
The other day, I read in one newspaper letter that accused her of
skewing her questions to suit the ruling New Patriotic Party (NPP).
I thought that was an unfair and pretty dumb remark. Of course I do
not know which side of the partisan political divide she is on. Still,
any intelligent, discerning and fair-minded observer would say that
Nana Yaa does not allow her partisan political interests, if she has
any, to intrude into the programme. She tends to distribute both simple
and awkward questions fairly among her panelists.
Well, there is no accounting for taste and there is no way you can please everyone.
I have not heard Nana Yaa stridently speaking like a feminist.
Still, she does her best for womanhood. You see, anytime a panelist
uses the male pronoun "he", Nana is quick to remind that panelist to
add, "or she". The panelist promptly complies.
When her panelists prove particularly troublesome and
uncontrollable, she tells them, "Your microphones are off." That brings
about temporary respite.
JOY FM's Newsfile goes beyond a mere review of news items in
newspapers or a discussion of events of the week. Sometimes, it is very
serious drama with the different "actors" engaged in a serious act of
cut and thrust, with Nana Yaa directing the "action".
Sometimes, it is like a circus of lions and tigers (or lionesses,
Nana?) with Nana Yaa as the ringmaster (or ringmistress, Nana?)
cracking the whip to keep the show on course.
Her awesome power ensures that Newsfile never degenerates into a
farce or a fistfight. In her hands, Newsfile remains a credible
"must-listen-to" programme. To me, she is simply a wonder.

Original_Search_Title: Expert Laments Spread of HIV/Aids
Headline: Nigeria: Expert Laments Spread of HIV/Aids
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — A medical expert and consultant for National Action Committee on
HIV/AIDS (NACA), Dr Alvin Enyi, has expressed alarm over the spread of
the scourge in the communities of South Eastern Nigeria and has urged
that more efforts should be deployed to the rural areas of the zone to
assist those who are already down with the disease.
Enyi, who is the director of Basic Health Initiative, a
non-governmental organization (NGO) who spoke during a workshop on
HIV/AIDS at Okposi in Okposi local government area of Ebonyi state,
said that the prevalent rate of the disease in the rural areas of the
zone is highest in the country and called for a broader response and
sensitization campaign in the rural communities of the five Eastern
states.
He attributed the reason for higher prevalence of the scourge in
these rural areas to the peculiar migration of city dwellers to the
rural areas during ceremonies like Christmas and New Yam festival,
noting that the city dwellers entice the local women with money and
infect them with disease, which blossoms after they had departed to
their various urban towns.
Enyi therefore, advised the people who live in rural areas to shun
the money, which many of those who live in the cities bring to the
villages, noting that as they dole out their money to the poor
villagers, they also infect young girls with HIV.
"In one of the lecture, I laid a lot of emphasis on migration of
those who live in the cities to the rural communities; that rural
dwellers should not be carried away by the money those who live in the
cities bring and because of that compromise a lot of things happen.
They should know that anybody coming into the communities is a potential HIV carrier," Enyi warned.
Enyi, who has taken the work of his NGO to the rural communities
said that Basic Health Initiative has been carrying out sensitization
campaigns in various towns and villages of Ebonyi and other states,
stating that his organization now carries out capacity building
programmes whereby it enlightens and trains those who ago to their
various communities to sensitize others on the dangers of the ailment.
The Basic Health Initiative Boss said that as an obstetricians who
has been carrying out HIV/AIDS tests on expectant mothers he is
convinced that the general rate of prevalence of the disease is coming
down, "but we need to prove it statistically. There is no doubt that
the awareness has reached everywhere; like we are just in the village
now and those who don't know, now know about the scourge".
He expressed the hope that sensitization and other enlightenment
programmes would continue to lead to change in behaviour, so that those
who used to be reckless in their sexual habits will now begin to
realize how they are endangering their lives,
observing that campaigns against the disease have yielded fruits, as
many Nigerians have become conscious of the disease and what to do to
prevent contracting it.
He thanked Ebonyi State Government for its efforts to curb the
spread of HIV/AIDS and for paying its counterpart funds to donor
agencies who support the fight to eliminate the disease and urged
government to support NGOs, saying, "I would like it if government
realizes that while some organizations are providing the drugs, there
are others who are campaigning,
enlightening people to bring about behaviour change. I want
government to help bring these groups together and support their
efforts."

Original_Search_Title: African Woman
Headline: Nigeria: African Woman
Body_Text:
Ontario — With my gele (headgear) looking like National theatre on one side and Niagara falls on the other, I was ready for a special ceremony.
My gele was not as perfect as madam kofo's own in the popular series 'second chance' back home in the 80's. We were on our way to a friend's wedding and stopped for a washroom break at a fast food joint. As I got off the car making my way into the place, I suddenly became the centre of attraction and I gladly accepted the brief stageÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oe.You look gorgeous, awesomeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oeoh, I like your outfitÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oe.it is so colorfulÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oewhere are you from? I replied grinning from ear to ear, ohÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oethank you, I am from Nigeria, we wear this for special occasions. It is called lace, we have lots of it back home. And the headgear, I tied it myself, .yeah, it is lovelyÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oe.All this while, I blocked the entrance into the place, it was my stage and for that brief moment, an African woman was talking and must be heard oh Ã¢-oeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oe.I a m glad to be an African madam. As I made my way back to the car, I felt the eyes staring and I walked majestically so that they will have enough time to look, but I ran at one point because the cold was biting into my skin oh!!!!!!!!!!
As an African woman living abroad, I have found out that once in a while as the weather permits, when I dress in my attire, there is this glow and attention, which I can not really describe. Oyinbo's are in awe of our outfits and they also marvel at out hairdos and can't quite understand some intricacies. There are some parts of our culture, which they admire like carrying a baby on your back with a wrapper. I remember someone told me her church members were taking pictures of her and her child when she backed the baby. They kept asking, "how did you do that"? I have also spoken to a couple of friends here in Canada who back their children. It is actually good for light workouts.
I also remember receiving something from my pastor in Canada and making a little courtesy (slightly bending my knees), at the end of the service someone actually called me to say that I made them feel like kings and queens and they have not seen such in a long time. Somehow it has been engraved in my head that I should not stand like an Iroko tree when greeting my elders or people in authority. Some people have also commented that Nigerian children are very respectful, now that is very positive. Infact, one of our core values in Nigeria is Respect, so it is really no surprise.
One aspect of the Yoruba culture that I admire is the way they kneel or prostrate for their elders, it is a significant part of their culture and we were told that in social studies back in primary school. When we moved to Lagos, it was also one of the first things I noticed and I had to learn.
As a Nigerian woman in Diaspora, sometimes I do have my challengesÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oewill we forget our values and traditions in a foreign land? As long as what I have been brought up with are there to guide me, make me a better wife and mother, friend and colleague, daughter and in-law then I will keep them. I may not be perfect in delivering all but the sincerity of the heart and the willingness to learn is what matters.
My dad is a very firm disciplinarian. He studied in the UK and he is very homely. When we were growing up he would warn usÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oe "all you girls in this house, you better learn how to cook, otherwise your husband will chase you away" , and we will laugh while he said that. He is a very good cook, the best porridge beans I have eaten in my life was prepared by him and he can cook a pot of soup with his mouth. He used to supervise us whenever my mum traveled. He was also very strict on our dressing. And sometimes when we display any stubborn attitude he will remind us of how he used to wash our dirty nappies and change us when we were kids. Now that just brings us back to planet earth. These are the classic memories I will have of my dad. I look forward to cooking his best food: fresh fish pepper soup when I see him. One thing I miss is the look of satisfaction on his face when he says thank you after eating that meal. The baton was passed from my mum to me and now to my younger ones.
I have learnt that as a Nigerian woman no matter the amount of Oyinbo (like the Queens English) and grammar you can speak, or the amount of degrees, masters or Ph.D. you have, or if you have an army under your command, when you enter that kitchen, you must know how to perform. Somehow you should know how to co-ordinate some ingredients and whip up something. It does not have to be a world class exotic meal or international cuisine with all the trimmings and toppings. You don't have to be perfect. I remember growing up with "Maggi Kitchen" by Madam Funmi Adeoye, every Friday. I will sit down with my pen and paper. It was a taboo to sit in front of the TV on Saturday afternoon when my mum was cooking soup. As soon as she comes back from the market, we all entered the kitchen and found something to do: pick the vegetable leaves, pound crayfish and pepper or just pass things to her. As time went on, she passed some of the cooking to us but she always cooked her okazi soup. Of c ourse she always bought tapioca and coconut to keep us in the kitchen.
Sometimes I think every husband's secret and silent prayer before they get married is: "oh God please give me a wife that can cook!!!!!!!they won't ask you up front but they will give you little assignments that will reveal your work with time. However, they are also very willing to teach you if you don't know. When they get married they are signing off from all takeaway joints and they relinquish every shareholding badge. There is something about mama Ngozi's food that will make papa Ngozi run home from work to eat his ofe nsala and pounded yam. There is also something about baba Tola that will make him reject every other food outside because he can't wait to get home and eat his precious efor and Amala by his darling wife. Eating out is fine for the family occasionally but it is not economical on a regular basis. Although out here, we substitute quite a lot of our traditional vegetables with Spinach !!!!!Where can you find fresh ugu, okazi, waterleaf, oha, Ewedu, bitter leaf etc?
I enjoy watching cooking channels just to learn something new because somehow the kitchen is one aspect of the home where God has given women unbelievable creativity and energy. I also think it is the engine of the home. It is also a place where mothers can bond with their children.
Some people might have grown up in autocratic and dictatorship homes and never really saw marriage as teamwork, partnership and fun. A place where laughter and peace can flow, so unconsciously you may carry that mental picture into your home when you get married. Some people never saw their parents hug or give each other a peck. There was really no show of emotions and their conversations were not fun eitherÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oeÃ¢-oe.where is my food? Actually, our culture does not really permit us to show our emotions, which to an extent is not the best.
However the greatest gift you can give to your husband is respectÃ¢-oehe is king in the home. It is simply unique when you see a king serving his family once in a while, it is the strongest and most effective message of leadership.
Life is short and at the end of the day what really lingers on our mind is the quality time we spend in our homes. You can make it enjoyable and fun. You can laugh and play, argue and reconcile, build and encourage each other. We are not perfect people, but we can accommodate each other and help one another to become the best that we can be.
As Nigerian families living abroad, we can be happy, we can stay together with God on our side and with the values and foundation that we have. The pressure of economic demands we shall overcome and remember that God is our source. We will continue to work very hard and be wise in our investments and spending.
God has also given women unique grace and softness, now it is in our hands how we use it to affect the people around us. If you have a husband in a position of leadership, you are that gentle voice that God can use to talk to him in difficult times or at critical decisive moments. You can speak to him to be accountable and selfless in his service. However the most important change can only be achieved through prayer.
History was made in Africa on the 16th of January 2006, when a woman became the President of War torn LiberiaÃ¢-oePresident Ellen Johnson Sirleaf. A 67yr old grandmother, an ex-student of Harvard University, an iron lady set to lead Liberia to the promise land. The first for the African nations and the whole world is watching to see what changes a woman will bring to this nation and the continent at large. There to support her were US secretary of State Condelleezza Rice and Mrs. Laura Bush whom I admire so much for her modesty, poise, elegance and humility.
In conclusion, I will say that being an African woman is a thing of pride, It reminds me of the qualities of a virtuous woman. We may not have had all the privileges and freedom that I see in the western world but I am glad that I still had some boundaries growing up and I will still treasure them. The drive to conform will always be out there but I have to constantly ask myself, will it make me a better person to stand out or to join the crowd and forget where I am coming from?
Onyinye Oyedele is a former BEN-TV London News anchor.

Original_Search_Title: Orlu LG Councillors Set to Remove Chairman
Headline: Nigeria: Orlu LG Councillors Set to Remove Chairman
Body_Text:
Owerri — Councillors of Orlu local government area are angling to remove
their Chairman, Chief Eze Chukwudi Mayor, following his alleged gross
misconduct and fragrant abuse of office, including refusal or complete
neglect to present statements of monthly income and expenditure to the
legislature despite repeated demands.
Already, a 19-point allegation has been forwarded to the Chairman
for his comments within seven days, vide a covering letter,
ORLG/LC/003/25, dated February 23,2006, and signed by the Clerk of Orlu
Legislative Council, Mr. T.C. Onwuegbuchu."
The Executive Chairman of Orlu local government is expected to
respond to the allegations in writing within seven days of the receipt
of the letter to the Orlu Legislative Council in accordance with
Section 26(1) of the Local Government Administration Law 15 of 2000",
Onwuegbuchu directed.
Part of the serious allegation, which was made available to Vanguard
in Owerri include his non-compliance with and utter disregard to the
Federal Government's due process in the award of contracts. "The
Chairman claimed to have spent over N5 million on the Governor's New
Yam Festival without council's approval", the letter alleged.
He was also accused of refusing to explain to the council how he
spent N2 million counterpart funding without the approval of the
council on the building of the overhead tank at Okwuabala Ward and
various huge sums of money on similar projects in other wards. The
councillors equally accused the council boss, who doubles as Imo State
Chairman of Association of Local Governments of Nigeria (ALGON), of
incessant secret employment of staff without approval by the
legislature and despite the touted high wage bill of the local
government.
His "refusal to explain to the Council the composition and purported
payment of over N800,000 monthly to unknown persons called city
fathers", as well as "refusal to send statements of account of Orlu
Local Government Council's banking transactions to the legislature
despite repeated demands by thelegislature."
The lawmakers were not happy that the Chairman had been laying false
claims to projects carried out by international donor agencies, federal
and state governments in Orlu local government area "and using same as
coverage for misappropriation of council funds".
All efforts to get the Chairman's side of the story before going to
press, including trips to Orlu, failed but a staff in his office, who
who pleaded anonymity confirmed receipt of the Council's resolutions.

Original_Search_Title: Councillors Vow to Oust Algon Boss
Headline: Nigeria: Councillors Vow to Oust Algon Boss
Body_Text:
Owerri — Chairman, Imo State chapter of the Association of Local Government
of Nigeria (ALGON), Chief Chukw-udi Mayor Eze, has been given a
one-week ultimatum by Orlu Local Government Legislative Council, to
answer allegations of gross misconduct and flagrant abuse of office
levelled.
The ultimatum was contained in a notice of impeachment served him
over the weekend and signed by Clerk of the Council, Mr Onwuegbuchi
T.C., with the full endorsement of all the 17 Councillors.
ln the notice tagged, "Motion for the removal of the Executive
Chairman of Orlu Local Government Area, Chief Chukwudi Mayor Eze," the
Councillors, who made 19 allegations against the Chairman, said they
had been relegated to the background in virtually all activitie since
the inception of the present administration.
They accussed Eze of "flagrantly disregarding all their
resolutions," adding that he had refused to present statements of
monthly income and expenditure to them, despite repeated demands as
provided by Section 35(4) of the State Local Government Administr-ation
Law No 15 of 2000.
They alleged that he refused to brief the legislature on details and
status of the counterpart funding of International Donor Agencies
Projects in the Council.
"The Chairman ran the affairs of the Council for three monthsas a
sole administrator without carrying supervisors and secretary along,
and operate without a Secretary to the Local Government till date."
He has also disregarded the Fedral Government's policy of due
process in the award of contracts", the notice which was signed by the
Councillors contained.
On alleged misappropriation of funds, he was asked to explain the
rationale behind the spending of about one million naira on the burial
ceremony of a Former Chairman of the Council, late Chief Thomas Ukagba
and another five million naira on the new yam festival of the State
Governor, without the Council's approval.
He was also accused of refusing to explain how he spent two million
naira counterpart funding without the council's approval on the
building of over-head tank at Okwuabala Ward, as well asa refusing to
explain to the council the composition and purported payment of about
one million naira to a group simply identified as City Fathers.
The Legislators also alleged that they were constantly harrased and
embarrased by the embattled Chairman with demands for the submission of
their educational certificates for a re-screening exercise which they
said was intended to question their credibility.
When contacted on phone, the Chairman dismissed all the allegations,
insisting that they were all ochestrated by his political rivals who he
said were hell-bent in truncating his political future.
He however said he was not going to be detered by the allegations,
since he was sure that he remained innocent of all the charges.

Original_Search_Title: Fire Guts Mile 12 Market
Headline: Nigeria: Fire Guts Mile 12 Market
Body_Text:
Lagos — Barely two weeks after Mile 12 market at Ikorodu area of Lagos
was reopened, goods worth several millions of naira were destroyed
following an early morning fire yesterday.
Though the cause of the inferno which also burnt cash could not be
ascertained, but reports said it started from one of the shops at about
6.10am.
Reports said traders who were returning from their journeys with
goods , first sighted thick smoke from a particular section of the
market and alerted other people.
Some lucky shop owners who had arrived at that time managed to
salvage their goods to safer places with the help of some customers.
Some of the goods especially tubers of yams were destroyed in the
process. Efforts by the traders who were assisted by sympathisers to
put out the raging fire failed as it reportedly spread to other shops.
However an hour later the whole environment was covered with cloud
of smoke while the heat emanating from the scene prevented anyone from
going near.
Hoodlums reportedly hijacked the situation as they looted even
unaffected shops pretending to be helping to carry goods to safer
places.
When Saturday Vanguard arrived the scene, affected persons were seen
mourning their loses .One of the victims who simply identifies himself
as Mr Polycap Chukwu said he lost all his goods as well as over
N300,000 cash in the fire.
Polycap said, " Apart from the amount which is well over N300
thousand that I had in there , I also lost clothing materials, wrist
watches, and other jewelry because I run a boutique.I usually keep
amount realised from the day's sales in the shop. If I had known I
would have removed the money yesterday.
He however attributed the huge loss to the late arrival of fighters.

Original_Search_Title: Deputy Gov Commends Assembly
Headline: Nigeria: Deputy Gov Commends Assembly
Body_Text:
Ekiti — Ekiti State Deputy Governor, Mrs Abiodun Olujimi, has commended
leadership of the State House of Assembly for ensuring cordial
relationship between the executive and legislative arms of government.
Olujimi made the remark while commissioning some constituency
projects executed by members of the State House of Assembly at Oye and
Ilejemeje local government areas.
She commended the Assembly for the initiative, which she said has
further demonstrated the commitment of the present leadership in Ekiti
State, to spread dividends of democracy to all nooks and crannies.
At Oye Local Government area, Honourable Segun Owoeye built lock up shops and two blocks of classrooms at Oye Egbo High School.
Olujimi attributed the achievement to the existing understanding between the executive and the legislative arms of government.
She said for several years, elected public officials have neglected
the electorates who ensured their victory at the polls, saying the
present administration changed the trend by advocating for an effective
communication flow between both parties.
She said the constituency projects are meant to be a product of the
electorate's investment in their representatives, whom they voted for
and supposed to be beneficial to the growth and development of
communities.
Owoeye, representing Iye Constituency II at the Assembly, said the
projects should be credited to the leadership of the Peoples Democratic
Party in Ekiti State, and also the efforts of the Speaker of the
Assembly, Chief Friday Aderemi and Governor Ayodele Fayose.
He said as a teacher, he was inspired to embark on a project that
would be beneficial to Ekiti children, who must be educated properly to
take over the state's leadership.
Olujimi also donated a substantial sum of money to the School towards rehabilitation of existing structures in the school.
Lock up shops was commissioned at Isan-Ekiti, and the Chairman,
Honourable Bamitale Oguntoyinbo, lauded Owoeye's vision, and expressed
the loyalty and support of people of the area to the Fayose-led
administration.
At Eda Oniyo in Ilejemeje Local Government Area, Olujimi was
received by hundreds on people, led by Counci Chairman, Honourable Oore
Kupolati and Deji Akinola.
Akinola built a multipurpose hall and also built market stalls for the Ebira community who deals in yam produce.
Akinola thanked the people of the area for supporting the Fayose
administration, and also commended Aderemi for sustaining a peaceful
relationship with various arms of government.
Kupolati and the monarch of Eda Oniyo, Oba Julius Awolola, thanked the state government for the various developmental projects.

Original_Search_Title: Our Beelzebub Democracy
Headline: Nigeria: Our Beelzebub Democracy
Body_Text:
Lagos — GRADUALLY, but very painfully, Nigeria is fast gaining ascendancy on the
ladder of nations where anything goes. Not that we have ever had a history
of orderliness and respect for the rule of law. But it does appear now more
than ever that Nigeria is a massively dismembered carcass oozing with
noxious stench, so toxic that even the vultures stayed away.
Each passing day, we advertise ourselves as a people incapable of life,
of existence and self rule. We totter and dither in all directions, making
endless motions without movement. With the termination(?) of military rule,
I had thought we had seen the last of absurdity, Mickey Mouse chicanery and
government house tom-foolery. But I was wrong. Whereas the military did
project us as a people incapable of good conduct and destitute of moral
rectitude, democracy has actually brought out the beast in us. And we really
are beasts. Within the past six and a half years, the government of
President Olusegun Obasanjo has exhibited nothing but asinine banality,
voodoo gangsterism, political witchcraft and economic heist of the most
benumbing dimension. And episode after episode, the government exposes its
poisonous fangs, threatening to tear the masses of the people into shreds
and shags.
First, the president, an unwilling one at the time, claimed he was out
to fight the bull called corruption. Yet all around him, even within his
household, are tell-tale ensigns of graft. All his actions and those of his
men belie their words. If anything, they underscore the wisdom in an age-old
proverb that you cannot keep a piece of yam in the custody of a goat.
Then the president turned to electricity, mouthing promises of a new
Nigeria where electricity would become the right of the people, not a
privilege. Again, he failed here as he has in recent years plunged the
nation into pitch darkness. Then, he looked to the economy promising to
tinker with it and spring us out of the cesspit of economic sclerosis. I
cannot safely say whether the president succeeded economically or not. But
if you consider that mid-level income earners who in those days could
conveniently afford to buy brand new vehicles cannot afford same today, then
you would better appreciate what success he has made of his pledge to turn
the economy around.
But today, let's not bother about the failings of this government. Let
us even pretend that this government has done very well in trying to burnish
the engine of state. There is yet something more horrendous than a parlous
