economy or a stretch of bad road. It is the shameless effrontery of the
Obasanjo government to promote, as a matter of routine, bizarre acts that
violently violate the constitution to perpetrate monstrous acts of
subornation.
The most manifest trait in this highly complex and convoluted maze of
constitutional infraction is the emergence of the culture of godfatherism in
politics. Nowhere in the history of democracy has the world been treated to
this asinine theatrics of a band of grumpy old men and sometimes a clan of
fairly literate young men who pride themselves as kingmakers or godfathers
in a manner such as we are witnessing in Nigeria's variant of
democracy.
The roll call of infamy drips with names like Emeka Offor, Olusola
Saraki, Chris Uba and Lamidi Adedibu. There are many more but this four
typify the soul and spirit of this tribe of jobbers called godfathers who
rule the roast, politically.
Chinwoke Mbadinuju was the governor of Anambra State from 1999 to
2003. But for every action he took while in office, he had the silhouette of
Sir Emeka Offor, his barely literate godfather bearing down on him. Offor
was not a cabinet member but he was practically calling the shots, holding
both the pulse and breath of Anambra State in his hands. Then, Offor had an
ally in the presidency in the person of Vice President Atiku
Abubakar. However, a time came and Mbadinuju thought he had come of age and
therefore should be his own man. But he forgot that in Nigerian politics,
the godfather always wins except if that godfather does not have any root in
the presidency. At the end of the battle of attrition, Mbadinuju lost, Offor
won. Mbadinuju's nemesis started when his own ally in the presidency,
Mr. President himself, turned his back at him. And clutching at nothing,
Mbadinuju lost his re-election bid. He fled into exile with his tail
in-between his legs. While Mbadinuju's fa ce was draped in ignominy and
shame, Offor continued to swagger and swang in the plush corridors of the
presidency, compensated of course with juicy contracts. Today, as you read
this, Emeka Offor has firmly ingratiated himself to the
president. Presidency watchers say he could barely look in the direction of
his former chief supporter, the vice president. That's politics.
While the Offor Mbadinuju tango lasted in Anambra, somewhere in Kwara
State, Alhaji Olusola Saraki was busy installing his estranged godson,
Mohammed Lawal as governor. As usual, the godfather wanted the godson to
remain a figure-head, all-correct-sir governor. But the godson had other
ideas. He violated the creed. And for daring to challenge the authority of
the master, Lawal lost in his re-election bid. In his stead, Saraki
installed his son, Bukola, a medical doctor. Again, in this case, the godson
lost. Lawal today is being hounded in the state with various accusations
bordering on malfeasance. The most outstanding credentials of the Sarakis is
that both father and son were on the board of Societe Generale Bank, a badly
run bank that become distressed for which they ought to be prosecuted. But
this is Nigeria where chiefs are thieves and felons are festooned in
national honours. Today, both father and son are still Mr. president's
pals.
Let's return to Anambra. With the political eclipse of Mbadinuju and the
negotiated disengagement of Emeka Offor, another barely literate Chris Uba
sauntered into the scene with madcap, vengeful fury. On one hand, he bore a
gun and was full of shooting, shooting ceaselessly at any enemy, both real
and imaginary. On the other hand, he dragged his godson, Chris Ngige, to
take up a four-year tenancy in government house, Awka. But no sooner had
Ngige mounted the saddle when things began to fall apart. Chris, the
godfather wanted more than a foothold in matters of the state. On top of
that, he wanted a certain percentage of the state's allocation in
royalty. But Chris, the godson, had a different agenda. He wanted to be in
charge. And that marked the beginning of the end of their romance. Chris,
the godfather has yet another godfather in Mr. President himself. And
pronto, Uba mustered presidential support, guns, dynamites and all plus a
platoon of armed men of the Nigeria poli ce and stormed Awka. Mission? Get
Ngige. And so it was that in 2003, Chris Ngige became the first governor in
Nigeria to be abducted, sacked from government in a state coup, a
treasonable act for which the perpetrators ought to be in jail. But because
the perpetrator is Obasanjo's right hand man, Uba as you read this enjoys
unfettered access to the president better than Ngige, a democratically
elected governor. Well, even his election is debatable as Obasanjo has told
Nigerians how Uba and Ngige confessed before him that their party (the PDP)
did not win election in Anambra State. The electoral tribunal has also ruled
to that effect. We are still awaiting the verdict of the Appeal Court. And
from that time till now, Ngige has remained both embattled and rattled while
Uba has emerged as one of the president's best friends. We really have no
shame in this country. A felon, a coupist and abductor is the president's
main man. This is a poster that illustrates nothing but m oral indiscretion
and a people without integrity.
Take a trip down West, precisely to Oyo State. A notorious thug, a
jobber, military apologist and barely literate Lamidi Adedibu waved his wand
in 2003 and by a stroke of magic brought his godson, Rasheed Ladoja, to
government house as governor. And like all godfathers, Adedibu, in spite of
the fact that he cannot differentiate between the executive and the
legislature in a democracy, wanted to run things for his godson. But Ladoja
won't deal. And so war broke out. The battle got to an inglorious crescendo
last week when Oyo State House of Assembly, the state judiciary and a
Kangaroo panel of seven impostors masquerading as persons of "unquestionable
integrity" conspired with presidency forces and Ahmadu Ali, the PDP
chairman, to suborn the constitution of the federal republic in the name of
impeachment.

Original_Search_Title: Over 3,000 IDPs Appeal to Pres. Sirleaf
Headline: Liberia: Over 3,000 IDPs Appeal to Pres. Sirleaf
Body_Text:
A group of internally displaced persons, under the banner of "Abandoned
Vulnerable IDPs", have appealed to President Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf to use
her good offices to call on the relevant authorities to repatriate and
resettle them before the rainy season.
In a letter addressed to President Sirleaf, the Abandoned Vulnerable
IDPs, comprising of over 3,386 destitute Liberians, said it has been very
difficult and almost impossible for them to return home in the absence of
resettlement benefits as was done in the case of other IDPs.
The group alleged that since the commencement of the resettlement
exercise on October 15, 2004, they have been abandoned, a situation that has
caused them to live on wild yams and palm cabbage, and under other
constraints which they consider as a deplorable way of living.
"Disappointingly, we were informed by the World Food Programme (WFP)
that our names have been deleted from the feeding log, thereby leaving in
our hands that very ration cards that were issued to us by the WFP." The
IDPs said during the establishment of the six IDP camps within the
Brewerville area, they were registered and given ration cards by WFP and
LRRRC for food and other benefits from which they benefited from the time of
registration up to the period of the resettlement program.
The 3,386 IDPs are from various displaced centers in the Brewerville and
Virginia areas including the Perry, Wilson Corner, Blamasee, Jah Tondo,
Saiyegbeh and Plumkor camps.

Original_Search_Title: Uniuyo Alumni Donate to SOS Village
Headline: Nigeria: Uniuyo Alumni Donate to SOS Village
Body_Text:
Lagos — As part of activities to celebrate yuletide, members of the Lagos Chapter, University of Uyo (UNIUYO) Alumni Association visited SOS Children's Village, Isolo, Lagos.
Chapter Chairman, Mr Clement Akpanobong, who led the delegation to the motherless home, said the association decided to embark on the visit in the spirit of the yuletide, to give the inmates a sense of belonging.
According to Akpanobong, as members of the society privileged to have enjoyed the benefits of parenthood, members of the association felt obliged to extent a hand of fellowship to the less privileged and the orphans, particularly in seasons of goodwill like Christmas and New Year.
On behalf of the association, the chairman presented food items and beverages to the inmates, urging them to accept them in the spirit of the season. The items included bags of rice; cartons of evaporated milk, Indomie Noodles, Bournvita, flavoured vitamin drinks, Cheese balls and packs of sugar. Other items include tubers of yam as well as rolls of tissue paper and other toiletries.
While assuring the management of the village that the association will strive to make the visit an annual thing in its calendar of activities, Akpanobong said his family and some members of the association have already indicated interest to take up the Educational Sponsorship of some of the inmates under the "Child Sponsor" and "Village Sponsor" categories.
Urging privileged Nigerians to endeavour to show the milk of human kindness to the less privileged in the society, he traced the various problems the society is currently facing to lack of love and absence of the spirit of sharing among the people.
Receiving the delegation on behalf of the director and Village Father, Mr Augustine Adetola, the secretary, Funmilayo Olaiya, thanked the association for its kind gesture, saying such support from public-spirited groups will help provide succour to inmates of the home.
Olaiya, who said the village, depends on donations and philanthropic gestures from individuals and groups at home and abroad, said with the growing population of the orphans in the society, what they receive as foreign support was becoming inadequate to cater for the welfare of inmates.
She said the home, which currently has over 100 inmates, has about 64 attending various vocational institutions across the country, adding that since its establishment in 1973, two other such villages have been developed in Ogun State and Gwagwalada in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT), Abuja.

Original_Search_Title: Lagos and a Life of The Poets
Headline: Nigeria: Lagos and a Life of The Poets
Body_Text:
The writer is a product of his own society; and the story of all great writers from ages to ages has revolved around the very environment in which they grew up. In this fascinating essay, told in an undeniable powerful prose, Nwakanma, himself, a poet, explores the arresting power of metropolitan Lagos on him and his own generation of writers.
POETRY walks on the streets in Lagos. It is in the incredible dynamic of life which enacts, like some incandescent power, the moment of each living hour: it is in the sense by which, living in this city, a poet glimpses a whole new form of life, and an alternative way in which to experience it fully. This vast, tense and bristling city, its social tendon tautly held by imprecise impulses, reverberates in the poetic propensities which she inspires.
Lying amidst a network of lagoons, from which it derived its name, from early Portuguese adventurers, in some halcyon distance, there is a very animated kind of drama which flows in the bowels of Lagos, just as the sea flows by it. That, in actual fact, can be felt in that subtle sense of perpetual motion, in which Lagos can be discerned. And motion is logistics which the poet Okigbo says is what poetry is. And motion is the spirit of Lagos. A continuous flow of humanity, trapped in the imponderable minutiae of existence: like the stretch of pilgrims from Dan to Beersheba. A going and coming that goes on forever.
I had been born in that other city, Ibadan, some two-hours of hard driving away from Lagos, and which had been celebrated by the famous Nigerian poet, John Pepper Clark, in his poem 'Ibadan'. Of Ibadan, in sharp epigram, J.P Clark had written, with unbearable love:
Ibadan,
Running splash of rust
and gold-flung and scattered
among seven hills like broken
china in the sun.
But this had been in another age. J.P Clark was living in Ibadan in the 1960s, and was part of the Mbari crowd of Christopher Okigbo, Wole Soyinka, Chinua Achebe and Uli Bier, which had made Ibadan tick with cultural fervour, until the serious political events which marked that decade, and in which they were all so equi-finally involved, scattered them. But between 1965 and 1966, events moved rapidly to subvert Ibadan's creative conviviality.
It was in this period that I had been born, around Ibadan - to a generation which my friend, the poet Ogaga Ifowodo, born also at this time, many miles away in the deep riverine marshes of Isoko in the Niger Delta, has called 'the still-born generation', an echo, it seems of Wole Soyinka's description of his own generation as the 'wasted generation'.
Events had scarred the times deeply. The post colonial mood of euphoria had twisted into pessimism. The coup which Ifeajuna (although Nigerian newspapermen still attribute it to Nzeogwu) led failed, and it created a momentum of political events, the consequence of which snowballed into a civil war. The wild wind of violent events. But I was born in Ibadan, at this time, in the soul of anomie: when the heat of that social crisis, like molten metaphor inscribed my age. My mother remembers the burnings. The city had erupted into the tragic cycle of violence that had marked the 1960s. How the guardian angel of children, had saved me from death: I, a mere sapling, was lying in my cot in Ibadan. And a huge stone had been lobbed at the window of the bedroom in which I was sleeping, by a rampaging crowd in the city, smashing the glass. But, both the broken pieces of glass and the stone, fell just inches away from my bed!
The weakened threshold of our humanity, has been the single most potent inspiration, in the works of my generation - from writers like, Esiaba Irobi, Ike Okonta, Uche Nduka, Maik Nwosu, Chiedu Ezeana to painters like Olu Oguibe, Syl Ogbechie, Chika Okeke, Krydz Ikwuemesi etc; who survived the war and still live with its consequence. It has been a reaching out for certitudes, for the whorl of reason which should explain our conditions, as victims of a still violent, post-war society. The event of the war has driven many of us to the most critical conflict of our lives: we are conflicted on two important question of allegiances - to our nation, which we question because we feel like exiles in Nigeria, and to our heritage, which we question because it seems to have failed us. The poet, painter, and professor of modern African arts at the University of Florida in the United states, Olu Oguibe, captures this dilemma of allegiance in his recent essay on Biafra, titled 'the killing
Fields', in the Transition magazine.
Oguibe belongs to my generation, marked deeply by war. This dilemma has been narrated, but no one yet has told the full stories, for it seems that the true chroniclers of the last war, will not be, those who fought, but it will be those who were wounded by it: these are the children of war, born mostly between the eve of Nigeria's independence and the end of the decade of the 1960s.
Especially also because the triumphant army, swept the whole of Biafra into a mould, and enshrined it into the most villainous form of silence. Years of military dictatorship has made it impossible for the victims to speak. The war ended on paper. And the paper declared a 'no victor no vanquished'. And many of us had flocked to Lagos, in search of a life, and some meaning. Perhaps that is what drew me to Lagos. The search for meaning.
Its most renowned chronicler, the novelist Cyprian Ekwensi, still lives in his old house at Ojuelegba, at the heart of the city, where he wrote People of the city and Jagua Nana. Ojuelegba never sleeps: life here radiates with an uncommon nervousness, at this place, where the Nigerian musician Fela, had sung into legend. Even Fela's 'Kalakuta republic' was in that neighbourhood, before soldiers burnt it down in 1977. It is invested with a tumult of screeching cars, inviolable joys, and Ekwensi's city novels are the metaphors of Lagos in a particular milieu. Of course, the highlife singers in the 1950s and '60s, like Israel Woba Njemanze, and Bobby Benson, had sung in the city, and about its discernibly ostentatious pleasures. They were the kind of pleasures which were sublime and distractive. Lagos was, and remains, the example of Nigeria's own version of the city of a million lights. With its totally byzantine corpulence. In character dissimilar from the urbane remove of Enu
gu, or the aged, culturedness of Ibadan or even the intriguing mystery of Kaduna's power cult. Lagos is feisty and almost amoral. And not exactly photogenic, in the classical sense of the word.
The poet, Odia Ofeimun, was one of those who came to Lagos at the end of the war from Benin city, where he had been a war-time reporter for the provincial newspaper in the city, the Observer, on the same desk with the novelist, Festus Iyayi, winner of the Commonwealth prize for fiction in 1986. Obasi was fond of fat books, beautiful women and cognac. All these, Maik and I discovered, were interests we commonly shared.
And so, we soon hit it off: every Wednesday night, at the lull of production, we were to be found in the Editor's office, smoking endlessly, quaffing beer or brandy or coffee, and speculating on the most arcane forms of metaphor, or proposing some great new literary movement, till the lights seeped through the curtain, and we became too restless with incantenation.
Sometimes, we ventured into the nights with Ely, at great risk, like the one occasion, when he and I nearly got smothered into the great incandescent light of an on-coming car at the Maryland end of Ikorodu road, arguing seriously on the merits or not of commercial writing from 'serious' literature, while returning one morning from Niteshift, at onetime, the celebrity hangout in the city, and under the serious grip of the deity, Bacchus.
Or when he and Maik Nwosu
got so drunk, they could no
longer identify the particular address of the house to which they were originally headed at Yaba, and they parked the car on the kerb and slept till morning! Many of us, were homeless, with impermanent moorings, hibernating from home to home and living out of duffel bags. Craving only the space to write, and write and write ... with our blood.
Our manuscripts were our only possessions, and they had no homes, but in our bags. Some nights, for instance, for lack of where to sleep, Uche Nduka and Izzia Ahmad were known to have sometimes stayed awake with the revelers of Obalende, that part of the city where no one sleeps, till the next morning. Some were luckier. I was lucky to have a room upstairs in the home of an uncle. But I was too regularly late home, and was loath to wake everybody on the street, whenever I got home late in the night. Lagos suffers from a siege mentality, and most streets, like the one in which I lived, have huge iron gates. I had to make a lot of noise, usually in the early hours. On a few occasions, to avoid this, I have had to pay some prostitutes in the Ayilara neighbourhood of Ojuelegba, the red light district of the city of Lagos, just to sleep in their rooms!
We drank, because like all flawed and broken people, we had little consolation from life, in the theatrical, dog-eat-dog mood of Lagos society sweltering under military dictatorship. We were wasting, with deliberate, suicidal relish. Many in my generation, feel choked out of a common heritage, for we have known nothing other than violence and lawlessness and the dehumanisation of military rule. Our anger and frustration, ring like the echo of drums thundering in our writings. But it does not beg for pity, only understanding. As Uche Nduka affirms in his poem, Chiaroscuro:
I won't have to outdrink the fish
and outsmoke the chimney.
I will pull you to where poems
quell the rage of booze and smoke...
Uche Nduka worked, two streets away from the Ilupeju office of TSM, on Coker Street, where Update Publishers occupied a rambling one storey building, full of cobwebbed shelves, and browning manuscripts, and an increasingly frustrated poet, who began to disengage emotionally from his job, at the time I happened upon him one afternoon, and he took me out to his favourite restaurant for lunch.
Story of his generation
He was gathering materials for his experimental prose work, on the lives of the poets, especially the new poets, whom he had encountered, which he had then titled, On the Romp. It seems to me, however, that the materials he got, were utilised in his poems, Chiaroscuro, in which assuming the character, Abaji, he narrates in verse, the story of his generation, and the compelling human drama which drives their existence and their art.
He had been in the same graduating class with Maik Nwosu in the university. Nduka Otiono, who had moved to the Classique magazine would join up with us, every evening after work, at our favourite restaurant, where we normally had raucous arguments. We were normally broke, but never too broke for beer and vegetable stew and pounded yam. Sometimes, we would have to beg for our transport fare back home in the night! Sometimes, the woman who ran the restaurant would pile up our bill till the end of the month when we were paid, and when we would renew our credits! It was at this point, that Maik Nwosu showed me manuscripts of some of his poems, many of which appeared in his book, Suns of Kush, which won the ANA/CADBURY prize later. I showed him the manuscripts of Sonori of a Lilac, my first collection of poems, and I think, it was at this point, that we understood, what we soon discovered to be the central meaning of our poetic vocations, as the attempt to re-invent poetry as the
lost 'language of the enlightenment'. Maik Nwosu thankfully acknowledges this when he wrote in Suns of Kush:
You and I know, obigbo
that there are contentions
larger than the world...
Those contentions are linked inexorably to our artistic quests, and to the way in which we provide the creative testament of our generation of writers. That has been, I think, the real meaning of my poetry, and the poetry of many of my generation of writers: to describe our anguish, and to present them before a pitiless world, as an experience of value.
In Lagos, now, there is a certain calm: Of course, we still venture to Jazzville, on Majaro Street on some Friday nights, we still commit adultery, and wound our livers with alcohol. Many of us have fled into exile, unable to function any more, in the political turbulence, in the aftermath of the June 12, 1993 elections. Some are dead or dying or broken. Even now, many of us cannot publish our works, as a result of the death of publishing in Nigeria, and the subversion of the new Nigerian voice by international publishing concerns. We still console ourselves, however, by reading to ourselves, and by passing our works round to each other. Someday, many of these would be discovered, hopefully, by literary archeologists.
Nwakanma, a poet, now lives in the United States. His book of poetry, The Roped Urn, won the ANA/CADBURY prize in 1996.

Original_Search_Title: NSFR to Commence Yam Storage, Says Director
Headline: Nigeria: NSFR to Commence Yam Storage, Says Director
Body_Text:
Lagos — National Strategic Food Reserve (NSFR) department is set to commence yam storage, Director of the Department, Dr Mohammed Lawal, said in Abuja.
He said approval for the programme was given last year, in conjunction with research institutes for its effective management and monitoring, adding that the institute had produced yam bans on experimental basis for the storage of the produce which would in turn be processed into chips, flour and pounded yam.
Lawal said for now, the bans would be located in two research institutes in Kano and Umudike, near Umuahia.
He said the produce could be stored for nine months, and that the location of the bans in research institutes was aimed at improving the duration of the storage.
He said the success of the department in the storage of garri and other grains informed the decision to experiment with yam, adding that if the programme became successful, then the locations for the storage would be increased.
Meanwhile, the grains reserve had expanded its capacity from 186,000 metric tonnes to 336,000 metric tonnes in preparation for storing more grains in the silos across the country.
Lawal said between October 2004 and April 2005, the NSFR released a total of 10,239 metric tonnes of "Sorghum, Garri and Maize to neighbouring countries."
This, he said, was part of the presidential order placed by the World Food Programme for Chad-Ndjamena, Niger Republic and Sudan, for Dafur refugees.
Lawal said the department released grains from the silos for public sale at subsidised rates to Nigerians, to cushion effect of the high cost of grains and protect the interest of the consumers.

Original_Search_Title: Sweet Christmas Gone Sour
Headline: Kenya: Sweet Christmas Gone Sour
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When Ken Mboi left a party for his house last Christmas, he marveled at stars shimmering in the darkness. Kisii can be a beautiful place at night, and so full of evil.
For a moment, he wondered which among the stars the three wise men saw two millennia ago. Mboi calmly passed by a church where worshipers angelically hummed Christmas carols, and from a bar a few meters away, revellers raised their voices over bottles of spirits ushering in Christmas Day.
"Something told me to enter the church, but my legs hesitated. I could not enter the bar because I'm a teetotaler," he narrates.
"Unknown to me, I was walking straight into trouble that night."
Tragedies befall people in party time
As the Christmas fever rose, so did the animation among others looking for a share of the jinx.
Like a sudden storm in the dry season, tragedies befall people in a party time.
Most victims find themselves in Queer Street - embarrassed and troubled - but trudging on in the walk of life.
Mboi had been away in the city for many years, and he wanted to make last year's Christmas a memorable one. But as fate would have it, all the fanciful plans were rudely interrupted.
"A few metres from my house, a powerful headlight of an on-coming car blinded me before the car stopped. Two men jumped out and ordered me to stop. I thought they were thugs. Before I could say anything, they grabbed me. I was roughed up and bundled like a sack of potatoes into the back of a police Land Rover and taken to the police station," Mboi recalls.
Disappearance caused panic
He was thrown into a dirty police cell among scores of drunkards and loiterers rounded up earlier that night.
"I almost cried. It never occurred to me that Christmas could find me in such an awkward place l," he says.
"The day when I planned to celebrate with my family was ruined. My hungry stomach rumbled and filled with gas from the smell of sweating bodies. That was my Christmas."
Mboi's mysterious disappearance caused panic in the family. He spent two nights in the cold before word reached them that he had been arrested. He was released on a bond of Sh2,000, part of the money he had planned to buy goodies for his family.
Adelaide Mghambi
But for Adelaide Mghambi, losing a dear father was the cruelest thing to happen in the festive season. "Everybody was mourning. We felt as if the sky had fallen on our family," she says.
Christmas came with double tragedy
Her father was a gem in the family. During festivities, he gathered the whole family for celebration.
"It is depressing to know that the one whom you pegged all your aspirations is no more. You will never enjoy life again."
To date, Adelaide is still trying to find her bearings. "The party is over without dad," she says.
For Wanjiru Ng'ang'a, Christmas came with double tragedy. The pain of her grandfather's death, and a night of terror by gun-wielding thugs remains one of the most traumatic and agonising for her.
The family was mourning the patriarch's the sudden death when strangers, posing as family members, entered the compound at Huruma Estate, Nairobi.
"The whole family was in Huruma making funeral arrangements. It was a hive of activity - praying, planning the funeral, cooking with goings in and out. At about 8pm, two guys entered through the back gate and we thought they were relatives. They started comforting people before one of them drew a gun and ordered everybody to lie down. Everybody was caught unawares," she says.
Harassed by thugs
"In the sitting room where we were raising funds, two other thugs took charge as some went where prayers were being conducted. They started emptying purses and wallets. They then took their time eating part of the food and laughing at us."
"I was forced to squeeze next to a sufuria of hot rice. I got burnt. They forced my cousin to sit on the hot hearth. She screamed and the thugs got wild. They hit her, pulled to a cooler place and ordered her to remain silent," Wanjiru narrates with laughter.
"We had never encountered a tragedy during Christmas. It was the worst ever," Wanjiru narrates. "Sometimes, when I look back at the incident, I laugh at myself if only it could help me get over the trauma," she says.
The night was tense and no one slept. The following day, which was Christmas, Wanjiru was nursing burns on her bottoms, and everybody was broke.
"But this year I must revenge," she swears.
Incident changed perception of Christmas
She laughs off the incident, and hopes that tomorrow all will be well. Her plans actually begin today. Time it seems has slowly washed away the fear and as Wanjiru says, she plans to "revenge", not on the thugs but on lost time. "I plan to make the best of it," she says.
While Wanjiru takes the incident on a light note, what happened to Martin Nakali two years ago changed his perception of Christmas.
"Christmas celebration to me has been smooth since childhood. As children we felt good in new outfits and delicious meals. As an adult, it was great being with the family and we took it for granted that at Christmas, all things fall in place. But two years ago, I was caught pants down."
Penniless and staring his mother on a sickbed, Nakali thought his world was caving in. How else could one feel when his source of hope and happiness swung between life and death?
Celebrating each day as it comes
"My mother was very sick in hospital and our hearts were in our hands. There was no money and hospital bills had drained us. There was a cloud of foreboding and uneasiness hanging over the home," he recalls.
At home, there was no food, and sweet smell of delicious food wafting into the compound from the neighbours was a stark reminder that hunger would be their chief guest.
Nakali and his siblings had taken sugarless porridge on Christmas morning, and no meal was forthcoming.
"I realised that life is rarely smooth. I didn't even shower. From that day, I was initiated into a new maxim: celebrate each day as it comes."
For Nakali now, the tradition of going upcountry to celebrate Christmas with family has fizzled and the idea of a fancy day withered away even though his mother eventually recovered.
Pub-trotting expeditions on Christmas eve
For Rashid Juma, alias Popo, his pub-trotting expeditions on Christmas eve earned him a place at the Lang'ata Police Station for two nights.
After having one too many at a local joint, Juma and his friends thought the city centre would be the ideal place to be. So he went out to negotiate with a taxi driver when he bumped on steely hands that grabbed him like a vice.
"I was under arrest for drunkenness and loitering. When I tried to resist, a policeman slapped me so hard that I saw stars and sobered up," he says lightly.
"I thought they were joking. But they pulled me and hurled me at the boot of their car. One of them placed his heavy boots on my neck on the way to the police station," he says.
Suffering a terrible headache and a stubborn hangover over a rumbling stomach, Juma spent his Christmas in a sickening police cell.
Gave three well-dressed women a lift
Interestingly, although he stared death in the face last Christmas dejected and broke, Mercury Wafula says the Christmas tradition of going upcountry remains deeply embedded in his life.
His spirits are high as he recounts what happened to him last Chritsmas.
It began around Naivasha on his way to his Kimilili rural home. He had stopped on the way to buy yoghurt for his daughter when some three well-dressed women initiated a chat, and he offered them a lift to Nakuru. The women were lively but a few kilometers on, the one sitting at his back placed a cold piece of metal on his neck and she calmly ordered him stop.
"One came from the back seat and took the steering wheel. They were friendly and cautioned not to make any "stupid" moves. In fact, my daughter did not realise what was happening.'
Spent Christmas under surgeon's scalpel
"I had about Sh50,000 for shopping and I was pained when I handed over my wallet. Satisfied that they would leave me on the roadside, I was shocked when one of them saw my ATM card and asked her colleagues if they could do with more cash. They said they did and insisted on driving to Nakuru. It was a hair-raising journey as they drove at breakneck speed.
"In her recklessness, the driver ended up ramming a trailer. Two of them died on the spot but I was lucky. I suffered a fracture in the ankle and thigh. When I saw my daughter, my heart skipped a bit. She suffered deep head cuts and groaned in agony, yet I was helpless too."
"I found myself in the Nakuru General Hospital plastered all over, pipes and drips and in vicious pain. All along I cried for my daughter but couldn't see her since she was in the pediatric ward."
"I spent my Christmas under the surgeon's scalpel, syringes and drugs. It was hell," he says, gulping a drink from his glass at a club in Nairobi. "Later in the day, my wife and daughters were beside me. This Christmas will be fine. I'm going upcountry to revenge."

Original_Search_Title: Much Improvement, As Cresta Jameson Nears Half-Century
Headline: Zimbabwe: Much Improvement, As Cresta Jameson Nears Half-Century
Body_Text:
IF you've not looked in at Cresta Jameson recently -- and many folk haven't for a decade or so -- you'll be amazed at changes, immensely for the better, following an ongoing "makeover" due to end just before the hotel's 50th anniversary in 2008.
I was never over fond of The Usual Place bar, but there's now a direct entrance off Samora Machel and it gleams: tile and timber polished to high gloss. A grand piano is due anytime, centrepiece of an Afro-jazz-themed music/piano bar.
The "inn sign" announces: "The Usual Place, est. 1958." Wrong! The pub was unprepossessingly called "The Pit" until circa 1978 and was often confused with The Rat Pit, the all-ranks mess at the Drill Hall.
Lunch with GM Garret Moyo recently turned out to be a meal shared with him, Francis Ngwenya, Cresta group's chief operations officer, Jameson F&B manager, Kudzai Ratisai and new executive chef Mukeem Soomro, whose previous card showed he was recently exec-chef at the Dubai Grand Hotel: one of two Pakistani exec-chefs brought here by Cresta. A colleague is already making a name for himself cooking at Cresta Lodge.
Tiffany's was a family favourite in the days when Gordon Addams, now executive chairman of Inns of Zimbabwe, ran his "ethnic" nights there regularly. Without leaving town you could to try culinary delights, sample the wine and enjoy the art, entertainment, songs, dances and general culture of (say) Australia or Ireland, Greece, France, Italy, Cuba...or whatever country Gordon inveigled the cultural attaché of into enthusiastically supporting.
Tiffany's closed when the land chaos deterred travellers from visiting this country, but re-opened two years ago. It quietly shut again recently; temporarily, to undergo major refurbishment. So, pro tem, a la carte meals are served in the more prosaic Sandawana, named for Zimbabwe's emeralds and painted suitably in a rich green.
Sadly, a pressing afternoon on deadline day, precluded leisurely working my way through the a la carte menu, but the buffet looked good, inviting and still fairly intact at 1:20pm.
Cream of chicken soup was thick, hot, nourishing, certainly creamy, but lacked a certain oomph, taste-wise. It is also inconveniently placed between main course carvery and sweets: a common failing with buffets/carveries. Surely it's logical to display food in the order it's usually eaten? Soup should be bang at the beginning, with rolls within
reach.
This was followed by a grand array of salads, of which pickled fish, tasty tongue and a pasta-and-bean spread stood out.
Vegetables weren't overcooked, potatoes were memorably good and there was rice, sadza or pasta to accompany griddled chicken, steak, pork and fish. No joint, per se, that day but chef carved superb looking racks of smoked pork ribs.
Sweets also looked wonderful but, rather replete, and time ticking away, I enjoyed only a soupçon of fruit salad, lemon jelly and a mini-éclair. Coffee or tea were declined.
The Jameson buffet/carvery was, then (sorry, with hyper-inflation, can't guarantee it is now) $16 000, as opposed to Meikles' Pavilion's spread at $18 000 two days earlier and Rainbow Towers' $25 000 five days later.
My children (now 27 and 25 and overseas) were almost raised believing no weekend was complete without breakfast at The Jameson. When we first went, it cost $10 for adults and $5 for kids. I thought that the wrong way round as growing boys and girls do far more permanent damage to a running buffet than grown ups!
The buffet is superb value as, from the a la carte, soups were $5 000, assorted cold meat platter (no doubt featuring tasty tongue) $6 000, "smocked" rack of pork ribs $12 500, puddings $6 000. Other main courses vary between beefburgers at $6 500, fillet steak $12 500, baby chicken $13 500, gammon steak $12 800, queen prawns $15 500 and tiger prawns $16 500.
Service is slick yet friendly; the place has a pleasant ambience; food is a little more affordable than that served at similar outlets.
* Sandawana Restaurant, Cresta Jameson Hotel, Samora Machel Ave, Harare. On-street parking on Samora and Park Street. Reasonably handicapped friendly. Residential, open breakfast, lunch, supper daily. Tel 774106-12.

Original_Search_Title: Peep Over the Fence
Headline: Kenya: Peep Over the Fence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Authoritative sources give Caroline Mango a graphic account of how the First Family lives and behaves while on vacation in Mombasa
President and Lucy Kibaki open the New Year's Eve dance at State House, Mombasa on December 31, 2003.
It is yet another season when the important, the famous and not so important or famous will be converging on Mombasa to pay their annual homage to the god of rest and leisure.
Being faithful to a familiar ritual, the coastal city will once again play host to the country's most important personages, the First Family.
Any time from now President Mwai Kibaki and his family are expected to travel to the coast for their end-of-year vacation.
The cooling breeze from the Indian Ocean and a relaxed environment away from a busy schedule are only some of the reasons the Kibaki family makes this pilgrimage each year.
Since he took over power, Kibaki's custom has been to spend the Christmas vacation and usher in the New Year at State House, Mombasa. This contrasts with the practice by his predecessor, Mr Daniel arap Moi.
Even at the end of the festive season, it will be unclear even to his aides exactly when he will be leaving the seaside for State House, Nairobi.
More family oriented environment
Burning curiosity set The Standard on a mission to find out just why the President puts everything aside to travel to Mombasa for lengthy Christmas sojourns each year.
Interviews with State House insiders reveal that this love affair with the coast has wrought considerable changes since Moi's retirement.
The State House in Mombasa underwent major renovations immediately Moi retired, to create what a source describes as a more family oriented environment.
It is adjacent to the Indian Ocean and is only separated by a coral reef and a security wall.
The facility has been painted afresh and renovated as part of a major face-lift. The State House stands between the Coast Provincial Commissioner's residence and the Coast Provincial Police headquarters and is also near the GSU camp at Kizingo.
From the main gate, where heavily armed General Service Unit (GSU) officers keep guard, a winding road leads to a pavilion, where the President meets delegations when he is in Mombasa.
The pavilion is a stone's throw away from the main maisonette, which hosts the President's office and resting rooms.
The ground floor also houses the offices of his aide de camp, the State House Comptroller and a private office where the President holds meetings with VIPs like Cabinet ministers, MPs and his aides.
Two special bulletproofed rooms
Cabinet ministers and others dignitaries follow suit.
The maisonette is divided into two parts, with the lower part accommodating the kitchen, the private quarters and offices for his aides.
The upper part has two special rooms that are used by Kibaki and the First Lady either as rest rooms or as private study rooms.
The upper offices and the two special rooms are bulletproofed and are always under guard, with keener emphasis whenever the President is in Mombasa.
To the left of the pavilion are the staff quarters, which house the servants, including cleaners, gardeners and senior security personnel.
A swimming pool that was largely disused and unkempt during Moi's time is now renovated and fully operational.
Another facility that never used to function is the table tennis court which is now operational and a sight to behold with marbles running around it and the pavilion right to the main house.
The two facilities are important for the President's family, especially the sons, daughters and grandchildren who often accompany him during the December vacation.
"Some of Mzee's children stay here. They like it here because of the sea, the quiet environment and the scenic beauty of the Coast. They also spend the nights here at times," says our source.
First Lady jogs a lot
We were also told about the love Kibaki and the First Lady have for the sea, the breeze and the quiet environment.
The First Lady jogs a lot and when she is in Mombasa, the warm weather is just the right environment to do her thing.
President and Lucy Kibaki in a group picture with members of the First Family.
She tucks in her tracksuit for a jog between State House gates and slightly beyond the Provincial Police headquarters.
Security is, however, tight around her as she jogs, usually accompanied by her favourite body guard, a young and focused man from Kaloleni, and a number of plainclothes police officers.
Lucy, according to our sources, is a down to earth woman when it comes to matters of security, just like her husband who will often go to the extent of ordering his security people to keep in the background to allow Kenyans to interact with him without intimidation.
A good example was in August last year when Kibaki visited Kikambala Primary School. Security was extremely tight with a convoy of intelligence and police vehicles erecting a wall between the President and people, probably because of the fact that Kikambala was the scene of a terrorist bombing in 2002, shortly before he was elected.
Preferences include arrowroots and sweet potatoes
Kibaki ordered security officers to stay away from the crowd and remove the wall of vehicles so that the pupils at Kikambala Primary School and their teachers could get closer to him and the First Lady, much to the delight of the boys and girls.
Every time the President is about to jet into Mombasa, State House becomes a beehive of activity - with the workers sprucing up the grounds and buildings to ensure that everything is in place.
Procurement of foodstuff for the duration of the First Family's stay is done locally, although the President travels with his special cooks from Nairobi.
"He never leaves them behind. They are always here to do the cooking. It's a special team that comes ahead of him to ensure what the President
likes is available and in good time," says our source.
The President's list of preferences, we are informed, include arrowroots and sweet potatoes and for refreshment, a drink of Litchi juice from Del Monte.
"He has been on a special diet since his accident and naturally, he is not a fan of food. But those special items must be on his daily diet, especially for breakfast," says our source.
Passion for flowers
The State House budget is undertaken by State House, Nairobi, under the direct supervision of the Comptroller.
"The PC's and the DC's offices will only concentrate on the procurement and mobilisation to ensure that what Mzee needs gets to State House after proper verification," our source adds.
As for Lucy, she has a strong passion for flowers - not just any type of flowers but specifically carnations, which are always to be found around State House and the rooms she uses for study and rest.
During Moi's time, State House, Mombasa, maintained an open door policy, and the local people would flock in to either greet the President or just pay him courtesy call.
The late Kanu supremo and Moi's close ally, Shariff Nassir, used to joke that State House was "open to all".
Nassir used to arrange for dance troupes and Taarab music to entertain Moi and his ministers up to late in the night when he was around.
"The feeling that the President was around was real during Moi's time. Everybody would flock to the State House gates with women's groups being given special preference to go and air their problems," says our source.
This was largely because Moi travelled alone to Mombasa, even for brief holidays.
Hotel provides catering services
But today the situation is different, and the State House has the air of a family residence.
Outside catering is done once in a while by Nyali Beach Hotel - which has enjoyed Kibaki's confidence since he was in the Opposition - under the sharp eye of State House cooks.
The prestigious hotel also provides catering services for the First Family and visitors during functions such as the opening of the ASK Mombasa Show.
When he finds the time, Kibaki and the First Lady cross the ferry to the Indian Ocean Beach Club (IOBC) for what a source describes as a "hearty chat" with old friends. The IOBC is an exclusive hotel that also has a golf course.
Although the President's security is obviously top on the list, the Police Commissioner's priorities, Kibaki has often demonstrated that he does not mind travelling or going about his business at State House without too cloying a detail.
Kibaki's motorcade leaves Nairobi for Mombasa ahead of him.

Original_Search_Title: An Inside Look at Kony's Congo Hideout
Headline: Uganda: The Luxury in Kony's Congo Den
Body_Text:
At his home, Kony is a contradiction of the atrocities he has been accused of committing.
He laughs, cracks jokes, dances with his children and watches Nigerian movies on a giant TV set in the evenings.
A friend once asked me, "What would you do if you found yourself in Joseph Kony's camp surrounded by the rebels?" My answer was that I would commit suicide before the rebels chopped me into pieces.
But last week, I spent three days and nights in the LRA camp in a jungle in Ri-Kwangba, in the Garamba National Park of the Democratic Republic of Congo and left with my limbs intact, so was the delegation of leaders from Acholi and Kony's relatives that we went with.
Unlike the devils I have known the rebels to be over the years, Kony and his deputy, Vincent Otti, would pass a sanity test judging from what they do inside their camp.
Kony wakes up at 6.a.m, plays with his children and at times offers to dance for them as any caring father would do as their mothers prepare breakfast. In the evening, his guards switch on a generator to power the huge Sony television screen in his compound where his family and himself watch movies, especially Nigerian movies.
At his base, Kony is a contradiction of the atrocities he has been accused of committing against the people of Northern Uganda and Southern Sudan where he had taken sanctuary. He laughs and cracks jokes with family members and visitors.
My journey to the camp begun when we arrived at Nabbanga, Western Equatorial Province of Southern Sudan at Sudan People's Liberation Army (SPLA) detach by a United Nations helicopter from Juba on the way to meet Kony in the late hours of Tuesday.
An army of rebels led by Capt. Sunday Achaya received us. The rebel fighters led us to the waiting UN vehicles, which took us to Ri-Kwangba. Here we were received by Otti. After handshakes and few pleasantries, in company of about 20 well-armed rebels, we began the trek to the LRA headquarters.
A protocol officer from the LRA took charge of luggage and called his men to help in carrying it to where Kony was waiting for us across the border, some 12km into Congo. The officer registered the names of handlers to ensure none of our property was lost. Due to the experience I attained on the previous visit I had carried gumboots with me.
Otti ordered his soldiers to surrender the gumboots to the visitors who didn't have. In single file, marching like soldiers heading for a battle, we were led through a small footpath through thickets.
After walking for about five minutes, we met three young boys, probably aged between 12 and 15 years. With mean faces, which betrayed their tender age, they shook our hands and greeted us in Acholi, the official language here in the bush before they waved us on.
About 10 boys moved ahead of us to ensure all was fine. Otti followed with Maj. Gen. Abudema, who had a radio call hidden in his jacket and continuously communicated to their forces.
We trudged on, with people occasionally cracking jokes.
Later, Otti checked his watch and shouted to his escorts to bring him his transistor radio, which was promptly handed to him and he tuned to the BBC Focus on Africa programme. Minutes later, we encountered another rebel outpost, who stood at ease and saluted as Otti passed them. They stood in the same position until all of us passed. After about 20 minutes of walking and jumping over fallen trees, which had been felled by the rains into our path, we ran into a swamp. Then another swamp, until we began climbing a small hill, where another outpost of the LRA was located.
Like the previous one, the soldiers saluted as we passed. Otti asked me about friends in Kampala and how they were doing. I told him they were fine. "When you go back, send them my greetings," he said as we crossed another stream. The journey to this unknown place to most of us was now taking its toll.
The first casualty was Gulu Resident District Commissioner, Col. Walter Ochora but the soldier in him did not let him down. He persisted while sweating and panting as the sun began disappearing in the thick forest. It was getting dark when we stumbled onto a rock covering a wide area and immediately saw rebels emerge from the forest. Some stood at a distance while others came to greet us.
Then we saw a clearing ahead of us with dreadlocked gun-totting young men wearing uniforms similar to that of UPDF, Sudan government soldiers, the Sudan police and torn trousers.
Finally, we were ushered into a well-kept compound, where Otti and Kony reside. In the background was a huge rock on the western side of the camp, which disrupted the endless flow of the canopy formed by the tropical forest around us.
Another rock imposes its massive presence, East of the camp.
There were so many bonfires around the densely forested camp, with occasional cries of babies in the tents surrounding Otti and Kony's quarters. Though there were many people shuffling around the nearby bushes, their voices were inaudible as they spoke in muted tones.
Otti immediately rushed to brief Kony about the presence of the delegation. When we arrived I had managed to look for the exit through the dark forest in case Kony rejected the presence of three of us (the journalists) in the group and we had to find our way out in the night.
"Don't fear, he has already accepted, so you are making history for being the first Ugandan journalists to have slept in his compound," Ochora said probably sensing our unease.
We sat on plastic chairs as Otti paced up and down. Later they began calling us one by one to the shower room. It is a clean grass-thatched place with a pit latrine inside.
There was Yeyo soap, a sponge and a towel. The basin was carefully placed on a platform made of tree stumps to ensure comfort.
The water was warm and clean from the springs unlike in Juba where we used to shower with polluted water from River Nile. Otti removed his slippers and gave them to me and I was led by one of his guards to the shower room.
Congolese music
Later, dinner of sweet potatoes, posho, beans and boo (a traditional Acholi dish) was served. I went for sweet potatoes and boo, which I munched with near vengeance. To accompany the food was Lipua Lipua, a song by one of the legendary Congolese musicians.
It was approaching midnight when Otti showed each of us a tent where we were to sleep. But the party was not yet over in Kony's compound, which was about 200 metres from ours as the music blared till early hours of the morning.
Apparently Kony was a happy man that night as one of his wives, had come with us from Juba. Another one had caused a stir at Juba airport when she wept after the crew of the UN helicopter which took us to Nabbanga refused to take her on grounds that she had a baby. But two days later she was put on another plane and she found us in the bush.
Early in the morning, breakfast of powdered milk, Manji biscuits, bread (buns), mandanzi, blue band, simsim butter, tea and coffee was served.Immediately, the food was brought, we were invaded by bees. But Otti assured us that all was fine since they could not sting. At one time, it would be difficult to sip from the cup as the bees would literally conquer the cup while others perched on our bodies. Save for me who was stung after I accidentally squeezed it in my trousers, no bee casualties were recorded.
Also in our delegation was Otti's mother-in-law, Marta Atto, who had gone to visit her daughter. The grandma she is, several children including those of Kony and their wives came to greet her as she sat under a tree next to us.
All the children and their mothers like the rest of the rebels appear healthy and have soft skins. When I shook hands with the LRA's number three, Okot Odhiambo, also an inductee, they were as soft as those of people who have not gone through hardships.
After holding a meeting with his commanders, Kony joined us wearing full military uniform and dark shades. He was surrounded by about 15 soldiers, one of them carrying a plastic chair. Hugging and handshakes ensued before we settled for business.
During the meeting, Kony occasionally laughed, though at times he appeared nervous as the cameras clicked away but he spoke eloquently in Acholi drawing examples from US, China and local leadership in Acholi.
Story-Telling
"Those who thought I had a tail, that I was a devil, now you can see I'm a human being like you," Kony said as if to reassure his guests. At 1.15.p.m., he postponed the meeting and said we would resume at 3.p.m. A delicious lunch of beef, rice, millet bread, beans, sweet potatoes and boo was served.
Everyday we were at Kony's camp, we went through a routine of breakfast, meetings, lunch, a warm shower in the evening, supper and story-telling around the bonfire before we retired to bed.
Though Kony rarely drinks, he keeps in his house wine made from wild fruits, which we drank and it tasted good. His rebel fighters are barred from drinking alcohol, smoking or eating pork, according to the LRA Commander, Col. Lubwa Bwone.
"A pig is not considered a clean animal, so the spirits do not allow us to eat it," Bwone told me as I took a hike savouring the beautiful scenery around Kony's hideout.
But even if the young rebel fighters wanted alchohol, the camp is so isolated. The nearest village town of Ibba in Sudan is about 98km away. Kony's current base is about 1,700km from Gulu, where he operated from over the years before fleeing into Congo, a journey that can take weeks to accomplish.

Original_Search_Title: Free Lifts Can Be Very Expensive
Headline: Uganda: Free Lifts Can Be Very Expensive
Body_Text:
Getting something free of charge is one of the best things in life. I mean, who wouldn't enjoy the thrill that comes with it? But I have learnt the hard way that there is no such thing as a free lunch. In fact most free things come with a price - especially free lifts.
On a hot afternoon, a lift from a colleague at work comes as a Godsend - or so you think. You swiftly jump into the car, not wanting to delay your 'saviour' for the day. Then it dawns on you that entering this car is one of the biggest mistakes you have ever made in your life. You realise that he is a careless driver; on top of that, he shouts louder than taxi touts at a straying boda boda or bicycle. He doesn't stop there, he curses as he snakes through the traffic. He is getting on your nerves and you just want to scream on top of your voice that it is just a jam, which is slowly, but surely moving!
He surely has had a bad day. You hold back you comments and choose to suffer in silence since he is doing you a favour. Finally the jam clears and your are jerked out of your day dreaming by break neck speed and an abrupt stop at the place where you are to alight. "Hurry, I am late," he snaps. He is worse than an irate taxi driver. And you are itching to tell him off but hey, who told you to enter his car? You promise never to ask for a lift, ever!
But the next day is particularly hot and this time it is a female driver. A few pleasantries are exchanged, followed by your request for a lift and you squeeze into the car. This lady loves loud music and not just any music but the rock which you hate. To make matters worse, she sings along, throwing her arms in the air. You are tempted to command her to reduce the volume or else you walk, like you confidently do with many stubborn taxi drivers only that this time, you dare not open your mouth.
Then there is the silent type. "Can you drop me off on your way to town?" you ask. "Yes." It is hot today. "Yes." Your mind is racing; isn't it obvious that making conversation when in someone's car shows your gratitude? But what if the person isn't interested? Being the talkative type, you are not content. You think that the current rains will break the ice. "It's raining very heavily these days," you say and again your reply is "Yes." You just give up and continue to your destination.
Upcountry lifts
Have you wanted to save money for that upcountry trip? The availability of a free ride gets more appealing as you think about what you could do with the transport money. So you pick up the phone or coins and rush to the booth. You swallow your pride and ask not minding that you could; no, are actually inconveniencing the car owner by making it hard for him/her to refuse.
On the day of departure, you discover that you are not the only one getting a free ride. So you squeeze in with the others. And you can't stop thinking about the comfort of that bus seat you would have booked. Unfortunately, it is too late to turn back. "Do you mind if we add a few more sweet potatoes in the back seat?" the voice of your host cuts through your silent grumbling as you approach a market on the highway. Of course, you can't refuse, though you now have to compete for space with his sweet potatoes.
As you are getting accustomed to the discomfort the sweet potatoes have brought, the several stops are even more irritating. The urge to find alternative means seems the way out but you are in the middle of nowhere. It is even worse when kids who won't stop going for short calls are involved. Sometimes it is not only the children but also the person you are travelling with.
Long stops
It is no longer a myth that men who often travel have homes in every trading centre. Like on this encounter, the driver had cautioned earlier that his boss made several long stops before reaching his home in Kampala.
I insisted on taking the lift anyway. After a few kilometres from the village, we made a stop at his mother's place. "He has to eat the food because this is the only time he gets to see her in a week," his driver reminded me. The fact that we were not invited to eat was only made worse by the fact that he spent a whole hour.
But the winner was when I asked for a lift to save on a Shs500 boda-boda fare. The guy drove straight to a petrol station and asked if a had, "a ka loose 5k note!" I ended up paying for his fuel. That's a free lift for you.

Original_Search_Title: Meikles - Best Venue This Festive Season
Headline: Zimbabwe: Meikles - Best Venue This Festive Season
Body_Text:
Harare — WITH Christmas still five weeks away, it was an early (but pleasant) surprise to be invited to Meikles Hotel to sample what executive chef Chris Gonzo and his team have for their guests this festive season.
Accompanied by two other media colleagues and members of the Meikles management team, I was treated to a splendid meal in the La Fontaine kitchen -- at what is popularly known in the trade as the Chef's Table.
Meikles Hotel always treats the festive season with the greatest care and with the attention to detail one would expect from a five-star establishment.
Starting December 1, the hotel will dress up its foyer and other areas with Christmas trees and colourful festive décor.
Christmas food will be available for private functions from that date, as well as on the table d'hôte menu in La Fontaine.
Special functions are planned for Christmas Eve (La Fontaine only) and Christmas Day (The Pavilion, La Fontaine and the Stewart Rooms), as well as on New Year's Eve (also The Pavilion, La Fontaine and the Stewart Rooms, with the traditional spectacular fireworks display on the roof garden at midnight).
At all of these, there will be entertainment to add to the meals that guests will enjoy, from the St John's College Pipe Band to a team of singers and dancers and from Mother and Father Christmas to a church choir singing carols and other Christmas melodies.
What can we expect in the way of cuisine for this time of eating, drinking and being merry?
Chef Gonzo's treat for us was a magnificent feast of four courses, with each of them allowing us to sample a selection of his planned culinary delights.
The starter featured a trio of melon cocktail laced with port wine and mint, a chicken terrine with vegetables and button mushrooms wrapped in pastry and garlic butter.
The soup course featured two samples of the planned soups -- one was a rich seafood bisque, and the other a hearty ham and pea soup, accompanied by miniature croutons. Such was the pleasure with which this was met by the guests at the Chef's Table, that Chef Gonzo is considering our suggestion of serving it as prepared for us --- with both soups available as an alternative to a choice of one or the other.
The main course was a sample of four dishes: turkey, ham and chipolatas complete with cranberry sauce, mini pepper steak in béarnaise sauce, crumbed kingklip on potatoes with a lemon and caper sauce and the fourth, a vegetarian option of grilled and roasted layered vegetables with a pleasant basil pesto.
There was also a variety of sweets, Christmas pudding in brandy sauce and strawberry cheesecake, coffee, a cheese platter as well as mince pies.
It was a meal fit for the most discerning of guests.
Meikles has a reputation of being one of the finest hotels in Africa and their cuisine speaks for itself.
Chef Gonzo and his team -- some of whom were introduced to us while we ate -- clearly take pride in their work and the artistic and creative nature of this delightful meal reveals the highest standards of preparation and presentation.
The hotel also has a selection of Cape wines that are unique -- in Zimbabwe -- to Meikles and The Victoria Falls Hotel.
We sampled two -- an Avondale Sauvignon Blanc as our white wine choice and a rich red Flagstone Shiraz.
Both were absolutely suited to this meal and are among a wide range of wines that have earned the hotel accolades.
If you can get to Meikles this festive season, you will be doing yourself a big favour.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Up By 0.2 Percent
Headline: Zambia: Inflation Up By 0.2 Percent
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THE Central Statistical Office (CSO) has reported a 0.2 percentage point increase in inflation for this month. The rate of inflation now stands at 8.2 per cent, up from 8.0 per cent last month.
This implies that between September this year and September last year, prices of goods and services have increased by an average of 8.2 per cent.
However, CSO acting director Efreda Chulu said this month's inflation rate was 11.3 percentage points lower than the 19.5 per cent recorded in September last year. Chulu yesterday attributed the increase in inflation to a rise in food inflation.
"Of the total 8.2 per cent annual inflation in September 2006, food products accounted for 0.8 of a percentage point while non-food products accounted for 7.4 percentage points," Chulu said.
"The annual food inflation rate was recorded at 1.5 per cent in September 2006, increasing by 1.1 percentage points on the August rate of 0.4 per cent. Contributing most to the increase in food inflation were increases in the cost of maize grain, maize meal, fresh vegetables, fish, kapenta, meat, sweet potatoes, dried beans, table salt and other processed items.
"The annual non-food inflation rate stood at 15.7 per cent in September 2006, compared with 16.4 per cent in August 2006. This decline was mainly due to decreases in annual inflation rates for furniture and household goods, transport and communications, rent and household energy."
She also said comparatively, the price of roller meal increased by 0.5 per cent to K24,713 per 25 kilogrammes bag from K24,599 while the average price of maize measured in 20-litre tin increased by 7.2 per cent to K11,949 from K11,148.

Original_Search_Title: Iganga the Dirtiest District
Headline: Uganda: Iganga the Dirtiest District
Body_Text:
Kampala — IGANGA town is very filthy. It is so grubby that district health department researchers say it is the dirtiest in Uganda.
Garbage takes ages to be cleared off streets in the town of 100,000 people. The situation worsened after the Uganda Revenue Authority impounded town council vehicles recently over unpaid sh75m tax arrears.
The rubbish is often left to rot away on the streets emitting offensive fumes in the neighbourhood dominated by the Basoga.
Jokes doing the rounds here revolve around the poor sanitary conditions of the area. Commuters talk of, "Nja mu bukyafu" (Am going to dirty territory).
The town also does not have piped water and sewerage flows into unprotected water wells.
Records show that each home has at least one bicycle hence the numerous accidents involving cyclists in town. Even a bicycle bin exists outside the police station on the main street where damaged bikes are dumped because it is not cost-effective to repair them.
Hussein Kato, the clerk to council, says the main commercial activity carried out by the locals in this mainly sweet potato-eating community is small scale trading in fresh foodstuffs.
"The small stalls dotting the entire neighbourhood imply that almost everyone has something to sell like onions, tomatoes or cabbages," Kato adds.
The Asian community dominate wholesale and retail trade businesses. They mainly deal in textiles and electronics.
However, the Indians and Arabs seem to be working overtime to destroy the Asian-style houses put up in the 1920s. These are being replaced by petrol stations and double-storeyed buildings.
Although the place is dominantly inhabited by the Basoga, their local brew made out of bananas is not as popular as ajon, which is brewed by the Itesots in Nkaatu and Kasokoso suburbs.
Lydia Nekesa, a popular ajon dealer in Kasokoso says a unit of the brew costs between sh500 and sh1,500. Ajon drinkers constitute a big percentage of opinion leaders in Iganga town, hence most politicians feel obliged to identify with them.
Iganga also has a range of communal cultural practices which have inevitably been taken on by the non-Basoga.
Beatrice Magoola, the district Woman MP, says the ceremony of welcoming twins in the family is most peculiar. "Those blessed with twins acquire a pot with double openings in which they put placentas and umbilical cords of the toddlers. On the day of the ceremony they take the pot to a secluded anthill," Magoola says.
She adds that if anyone goes near the anthill he or she would be "burnt" by the twins and look like an albino.
"Cutting off hair in respect of a dead parent is another cultural practice that has been adopted by residents of other tribes from the Basoga. Another is the avoidance of calling people by their names at night out of fear that ghosts may learn the names and use them later," Magoola says.
Iganga town has popular persons among whom is Dr. David Muwanguzi, the district director of health services.
The doctor is a football fanatic and usually travels to as far as Rwanda to support Ugandan clubs. He also regularly features on organising committees of most ceremonies in the area.
Dr. Muwanguzi's social nature has also prompted school heads to make him a visiting doctor in nearly all secondary schools in Iganga.
Another such person is Abdu Akiiki, a retailer since 1985 in shop number 18 at Iganga central market on Ngobi Road. He specialises in feminine merchandise including panties, bras, lotions and earrings.
There is no one in town who does not know Akiiki as even men visit his outlet to purchase gifts for their partners.
While in Iganga, those who like to eat and sleep like kings should try Mwana Highway Hotel on main street.
If you leave town without visiting the state-of-the-art Kiss Discotheque on Jinja road you would have cause for regret. It is the only place where one can have unlimited observation of Basoga women gyrating their trademark pumpkin-size bottoms.

Original_Search_Title: Habineza Confident in German Coach
Headline: Rwanda: Habineza Confident in German Coach
Body_Text:
Kigali — Joseph Habineza, Sports, Youth and Culture Minister is optimistic that the newly-appointed national soccer team coach Michael Nees will post results.
The minister spoke to the press yesterday after arriving from Germany, where he watched the World Cup final. While in Germany, Habineza also held a meeting with Nees.
"We held discussions with him (Nees) while in Germany. He has agreed on the terms and expressed willingness a to start his job," said the minister. Habineza said he was impressed with the way Nees has prepared for the Nations Cup 2008.
"I was so surprised when I discovered that he had detailed information about all the Rwandese professionals. I think he even knows what type of sweet potato Desire Mbonabucya ate yesterday," he added.
Rwanda will face Cameroon, Liberia and Equatorial Guinea in their quest for qualification to the Ghana 2008 Nations Cup. The minister said that Nees has analyzed and drafted a plan on how to defeat the three opponents.
Nees requested a one year contract, and promised to quit if he does not deliver good results, the minister said.
Nees will arrive on Wednesday.
Meanwhile, the minister told the press that he held a meeting with Rwandese players based in Belgium, and that they were ready to play for the national team. Former Amavubi skipper, Desire Mbonabucya, has agreed to return to the team, he said.

Original_Search_Title: 'Consider Cost of Living in Awarding Salaries'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Consider Cost of Living in Awarding Salaries'
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Consumer Council of Zimbabwe (CCZ) has urged employers to award workers salaries in line with the cost of living.
This comes in the wake of a surge in the consumer basket for an urban family of six, which is now pegged at $61 million according to figures released last week.
A snap survey carried out by Herald Business reveals that the majority of shopfloor workers were taking home anything between $15 million and $25 million a month, a far cry from the consumer basket.
"Consumer spending and consumption patterns have been on the decline due to the economic conditions.
"Therefore, employers are called upon to give their workers salaries that take cognisance of the cost of living," CCZ said in a statement.
"We are pushing for Government to expedite the setting up of the National Incomes and Pricing Commission (NIPC) as this is the right time to introduce it given inconsistencies with prices of several commodities, for example fuel, bread and other commodities," said CCZ .
A toiletry manufacturing concern admitted that more and more people were buying laundry soap as opposed to the "luxury bathing soaps" such as Geisha and Jade.
This was because they found it more economic to use the laundry soap for both their laundry and bathing needs.
A manager with Harare supermarket confirmed there was a marked decline in the sales of luxury soap and perfume.
Even basic commodities like bread were not immune to consumer resistance. Bread, which now sells for $130 000 a loaf, has already been taken off the shopping baskets of many households as they opt for cheaper substitutes, such as sweet potatoes (mbambaira).
Beer is also becoming a luxury for the majority of workers. A pint (375ml) of clear beer, for example, is now going for $170 000 in most supermarkets.
At $130 000 a pint, Eagle is the cheapest of locally manufactured beers.

Original_Search_Title: Beauty's Memory Amazes Treason Trial Lawyers
Headline: Namibia: Beauty's Memory Amazes Treason Trial Lawyers
Body_Text:
Windhoek — ONE of the most recent witnesses to testify in the main Caprivi high treason trial astounded defence lawyers last week when she claimed she was able to recall an exact date in mid-1999 when she saw two of the 119 accused people in the Caprivi Region - whereas she is unable to remember her own birth date, her wedding date or the year her school career ended.
Beauty Munyandi, who testified before Judge Elton Hoff in the High Court in Windhoek last Tuesday and Wednesday, is the 54th witness called by the prosecution.
The trial continued yesterday with evidence from two Police officers involved in taking photographs of the accused people that were later collected in photo albums.
Proceedings had to be adjourned last week following Munyandi's testimony because the prosecution had again failed to provide the defence team representing all but 31 of the accused with a statement setting out the expected testimony of its next witnesses to enable the defence lawyers to prepare for the next testimony.
Munyandi told the court about events dating back to 1998 and 1999.
In 1998, she related, she attended a meeting in Zambia that was addressed by Conrad Walubita, a former DTA Regional Councillor in the Caprivi Region who left Namibia in late 1998, when an alleged plot to launch an armed campaign to secede the Caprivi Region was first uncovered.
That meeting was called by local traditional leaders, Munyandi said.
Walubita used the event to tell his audience that the Caprivi Region and Zambia should be joined together as one country, she told the court.
In 1999, she continued, she was in the Kongola area when she saw two of the accused in the trial, John Samboma and Britan Simisho, together with 12 other men, passing through the area on foot.
Munyandi pointed out Samboma and Simisho in court.
Samboma was carrying two AK47 rifles with him, while the others were carrying food when she saw them, she claimed.
Munyandi claimed that rumours about the existence of a "rebel camp" in the area had been making the rounds in that area at about that time.
It was also around that time that she found one of the previous witnesses in the trial, Ackson Masule, in the same area.
He was carrying maize meal and sweet potatoes, and she made a deduction that he was taking food to the rumoured "rebel camp", she said.
Masule, who is a witchdoctor, testified in February last year.
He told the court that he "treated" a group of about 100 men on an island named Nabumbwe in the Kwando River during 1999, after Samboma had asked for his services.
His treatment was supposed to make the people invisible.
According to Munyandi, she again saw Samboma, this time with another, larger group of men, in the Singalamwe area north of Kongola on July 30 1999.
They appeared to be crossing into Namibia from Zambia, she stated.
Two days later, on August 2 1999, the Caprivi Liberation Army, of which Samboma is alleged to have been a commander, is alleged to have carried out surprise attacks at Katima Mulilo.
Munyandi told the court that she could remember the date of July 30 1999 because to her it had been both a frightening and a major event that she had witnessed when she saw the group of armed men crossing the Kwando River.
She however does not know in which year her school education - in which she went as far as Grade 7 - had ended, she said when defence lawyer Jonathan Samukange started testing her ability to recollect other dates.
Samukange was incredulous, and exclaimed that she could not be serious.
He continued to ask her about other significant dates in her past - such as when she got married - but she confessed that she also did not know when this was.
"Mr Samukange, I agree, she can't be serious," Judge Hoff remarked when he heard this.
When fellow defence counsel Jorge Neves also cross-examined her, it emerged that she also does not know when she was born - although she said she knew this was in 1974 - or on what date Namibia became independent.
"I don't know because I'm dull," she answered at one stage.
As peculiar as the workings of her memory might have been to the defence, it still appeared that at least someone in the dock was taking her testimony very seriously.
What could be a vague threat had been directed to her just after the mid-morning adjournment, the court heard when its session resumed.
"You'll see at the village," someone had said to her from the dock, she told the Judge.

Original_Search_Title: Opposition Hot Potato
Headline: Botswana: Opposition Hot Potato
Body_Text:
The recent utterances by Botswana National Front (BNF) president, Otsweletse Moupo that other opposition parties should affiliate to the BNF has thrown a cat among pigeons in the opposition fraternity. Days after Moupo uttered the statement, opposition leaders are either tongue-tied or find themselves in a diplomatic conundrum.
At political rallies in Kanye and Gaborone South over the weekend, Moupo reiterated the BNF's position that it would never disband to form a new outfit with other opposition parties. He said the BNF would rather have other parties affiliate to it because it has the goodwill and national appeal. He stated that BNF would not disband in the name of opposition cooperation.
Dumelang Saleshando, the BCP publicity secretary said that the statements shocked him. "I am surprised that he would advocate for a model that was discarded in the Maun meeting with the consent of the BNF," he said.
