Saleshando stressed that a joint press release from all negotiating partners indicated that the models that would be discussed are the Pact and a combination of the Pact and an Umbrella organisation. The Gaborone Central MP who did not want to be drawn into detailed discussions said certain actions of the BNF are disappointing as they have agreed that opposition leaders would not in anyway compromise the talks and would instead give the negotiating teams a chance.
BNF vice president, Dr Kathleen Letshabo contradicted Saleshando's view. She refuted suggestions that the alliance model was discarded in Maun. "No model was discarded in Maun. If our model was discarded, why would we still be hanging around," she asked. Letshabo said negotiating teams agreed to discuss the two models but these did not mean that other models had been eliminated. She decried that the media has misrepresented them on various occasions.
"We have never said any party should join us. People should know the difference between alliance and affiliation," she said. Letshabo added that BNF has always advocated for a structure that would allow parties to be autonomous while affiliating to a single political entity.
The president of Botswana Alliance Movement (BAM), Lepetu Setshwaelo says the noise is much ado about nothing. Setshwaelo has instead applauded Moupo's honesty. He said that opposition talks should not be treated as a fragile teenage affair characterised by sweet-talking and lying to each other to spare the relationship.
"We are engaging in open public negotiations in which people have to be frank and forthright," he said. Setshwaelo is happy that Moupo has expressed his views and that of the party sincerely. He called on other opposition parties to openly listen to Moupo and see whether they agree with him or not. "It should not be a matter of whether we like it or not," he said. Setshwaelo argued that open mindedness could allow parties to bring forth their strengths and remain sensitive to other parties needs. Botswana People's Party (BPP) president, Bernard Balikani was tongue tied and could not discuss the issue that has proved to be a hot potato in some quarters.
"It is an in-house issue and we are still discussing the matter so it would be improper to comment," he said. But the BPP youth have not been held back by the diplomacy of their leader. Party youth leader, Hlonipani Chengeta lashed out at Moupo and dismissed the cooperation model he has advocated for. In an open letter to Moupo, the BPP youth says: "Let me tell you that the cooperation model that you and your compatriots are always singing about will never be accepted". Chengeta contends that other parties cannot accede to the BNF agenda, as they did not go into the talks to bolster the BNF by affiliating into it as small partners.
"We don't care how many years your party took to gain popularity or how big it is. We want a relationship that is aimed at a win-win level and not a big brother to lecture and bully us around," he said. He called on Moupo to desist from attacking other opposition parties as this might indicate that he is not serious with opposition talks. In one of the letters to the editor penned by other opposition supporters, one Boniface Habana says that the BNF's ambition does not stretch beyond being the eternal main opposition party in the country. He calls on other opposition parties to kick out the BNF from the talks for undermining the spirit of opposition negotiations.

Original_Search_Title: Like Father Like Daughter
Headline: Botswana: Like Father Like Daughter
Body_Text:
Tebogo Moribame, the Communications Manager at Kgalagadi Breweries Limited has followed in her father Samuel Moribame's footsteps. Moribame is a retired journalist and the current University of Botswana's Director of Public Affairs.
Having initially chosen to study Medicine, Tebogo abandoned the course mid-way and enrolled for a Degree in Public Relations, a field which she has expressed passion for. The bubbly and vivacious woman recently had a chat with Moses Maruping about her job and her life in general.
Who is Tebogo Moribame?
I'm a very down-to-earth, career-driven and God fearing woman who is passionate about life. I'm also a dare-devil who doesn't fear anything I haven't done.
Could you kindly tell us about your upbringing?
I come from a family of four children- three girls and a boy. I was raised by very strict parents with strong moral and Christian values. Every Sunday we were taken to church and I did not understand why. It's only now that I consider myself extremely fortunate to have had such a religious upbringing.
I also grew up knowing the value and importance of education so I studied very hard and excelled at school. My parents used to tell us that we were the masters of our destinies and that success was only a result of hard work, not luck as some people believe. I'm grateful for all the encouragement and support that I got from my parents. I am the woman I am today because of them. I was also fortunate to have siblings who shared my vision and were by my side at all times.
Where did you grow up and where did you go to school?
I was born and raised in Gaborone, where I completed my primary and secondary education. I also served National Service (Tirelo Sechaba) in the capital city and I enjoyed the experience immensely. I don't know if this is still happening in schools, but my career-path was initially determined by my teachers. Bright students were encouraged to follow science-based careers and I happened to be one of those. All we ever heard from our teachers was science and nothing else. So naturally, after completing my Cambridge, I then enrolled for Bachelor of Science (BSc) at the University of Botswana hoping to finish my education as a medical doctor. In my heart, I knew science was not my line and everyday I literally dragged my feet to class. The programme was not exciting one bit. After successfully completing part one of BSc i.e. year one and two, I left and enrolled for Bachelor of Arts in Public Relations. I was in love with the programme from day one until I completed.
What is your current job?
I work as a Communications Manager for Kgalagadi Breweries (Pty) Ltd, a position I have been holding since 2004. I handle the company's internal communications and am very passionate about my job.
What challenges do you come face to face with in your job?
Honestly, everyday is a challenge. I work for a company which is very dynamic and everyday poses its own challenges which I tackle head on. I do believe that everyday poses a new opportunity for me to grow and build my career.
Is this the job you've always wanted and why?
From as far as I can remember, I have always wanted a high-powered and good paying job. I remember vividly during my first year at varsity when a friend of mine and I used to mimic people in very powerful positions delivering speeches. I have only learnt that to get there one needs to persevere and sweat blood.
So how did you land in the communications field?
I come from a Communications family. My father is the Director of Public Affairs at the University of Botswana and my mother, although retired from Public Service is a natural communicator. My brother and younger sister both have communications degrees and my older sister is an attorney. My father shaped my career immensely. His long hours at work did not discourage me from landing in the field. He has a lot of passion for his job and he has been my core inspiration.
What was your first job after completing your tertiary education?
I started off in advertising. My first job was with Horizon Ogilvy and Mather where I worked as an Account Executive. This was a very exciting phase in my career as I used to deal with various communications practitioners and it just fuelled my love for communications. The advertising industry is very dynamic and that's what makes it so enjoyable. It's a very competitive industry and you have no choice but to be on your toes 24/7. The industry is reserved for extremely hard workers, and if you are not, then you simply fall off.
What is the difference between advertising and communications?
Communications is a vast filed that encompasses advertising, public relations, marketing etc. I'm very fortunate to have had the experience of all the three aspects of communications. To be an all rounded communicator, one needs to understand the advertising, public relations and marketing worlds. I'm still on a journey and new wisdom come up everyday.
Are you proud of your achievements so far?
Of course I am! For me everyday is a new beginning and at the end of the day, I always want to look back and say I contributed to the success of both my organisation and country.
What do you think about the growth or lack thereof of the communications field in Botswana?
The communications field in Botswana is growing like never before. If you open up the vacancies section of any newspaper right now, you will find a vacancy for a PR/Communications practitioner. Organisations are taking this field very seriously now because the value that communications practitioners add to any institution is immense. It's unfortunate that most people still do not know our role in an organisation. They still see the job as one of glamour and limelight and nothing else. But overall, I'm happy that the field is finally getting the recognition it deserves.
Do you think there are enough women practitioners in Botswana?
I don't think so at all. This is an area where a lot of work needs to be done to lure other women into the field. What is encouraging though is that more and more women are enrolling for public relations programmes and are making head way in what has for a long time been a male-dominated field.
Are you a feminist?
To some extent yes, I am a firm believer in the philosophy of feminism. I do believe that women have in the past had their rights trampled upon and their efforts to make strides in any industry throttled. I'm utterly amused at the advancement of women these days. It's quite encouraging that nowadays we have more and more of our women joining politics and also climbing the corporate ladder, something which was seen as a taboo in the past.
What kills productivity in this country?
I do believe that organisations that are not willing to let their workers grow through individual creativity are to blame. If creativity is stifled, a worker gets frustrated and they become unproductive.
Who are your role models in your field?
I have various role models. The first will have to be my dad who continues to nurture and inspire me. If I could mature to his professional level, I would have grown tremendously. My other role model is a friend and a great mentor, Lettie Gaelesiwe. I have known her for four years and it feels like I've known her all my life, she is an all-rounded and balanced communicator who just like my father, has a lot of know-how in the media and corporate communications. Her doors are always open to offer advice and assistance. Isn't that sweet?
What are you most proud of about your life?
I'm proud of my upbringing. My parents did a sterling job on raising me to what I've become today. Although I used to feel they were harsh and unfair at times, they have brought up four very responsible children. I am also proud of the beautiful circle of friends I have. They are just the best part of my life.
What is your favourite holiday destination in Botswana and why?
It will have to be Kasane, because of its beauty. The place feels lovely all the time and it's quite a welcoming environment for one to chill out.
Marriage, kids?
I'm very proud to be single at the moment with no kids. I have a nephew and a niece whom I absolutely adore. They lift me up after a long stressful day at the office.
There's wide speculation that men feel intimidated by career-orientated women hence most of you stay single most of their life time. Is this true?
I don't think so. I do believe that my time for marriage and kids hasn't come yet but hopefully it'll come. I'm still building and shaping my career. Men that I've dated in the past were never intimidated by my position. However I've heard talk like that from my other female friends.
What's your message to budding public relations/ communications practitioners?
The communications world has so many opportunities waiting for you. Take them with both hands and work to the best of your abilities. There will be so many challenges along your journey but also remember that no challenge is insurmountable.
What are your next career plans?
As of now I'm happy where I am and I'm taking each day as it comes. I'm planning to enrol for a Masters degree next year.
How do you relax after a hard day's work?
I read a book, cook dinner or watch television. I read a lot of motivational and inspirational books. I'm currently reading The Road Less Travelled.
PERSONAL PROFILE
NAMES: Tebogo Moribame
D.O.B: 3rd July 1977
FAVOURITE FOOD: Potatoes and Seafood
FAVOURITE MOVIE: A Beautiful Mind
FAVOURITE ACTOR: Russell Crowe
CURRENTLY READING: The Road Less Travelled by M.Scott Peck
HOME VILLAGE: Molalatau
DREAM CAR: A sporty Merc with a powerful engine
MOTTO: Never say you can't when you haven't tried

Original_Search_Title: Ordinary People Bear Brunt of Economic Meltdown
Headline: Zimbabweans Bear Brunt of Economic Meltdown
Body_Text:
Harare — If an uprising begins in Zimbabwe any time soon it might well be dubbed "the Lemonist Revolution".
So important has the lemon now become that it is a part of every family's shopping basket. People visiting neighbouring South Africa return with plastic bags loaded with the yellow-skinned citrus.
Fresh milk, when available, has become unaffordable for most. The same goes for sugar. At breakfast, to enhance the taste of their morning cup of tea, locals now use lemon instead of milk and sugar. They need it in their porridge too, for the same reason.
"Tea tastes good with lemon," said Margaret Gweshe, a housewife living in Chitungwiza, 28 kilometres from Harare. "But that's not why we have lemon tea every day. We would prefer the old days when we had milk we could afford."
Prices of essential commodities have shot up, with inflation reaching 1043 per cent in May. "People now only have money for the very basics," said Gweshe as she cradles a two-year-old child showing the early signs of malnutrition. "Maize meal is our staple, so all the money has to be directed towards it before we can think of buying anything else."
For breakfast she gives the baby maize porridge with lemon in it while she makes do with a cup of tea and a sweet potato from her garden. To preserve maize rations there is no midday lunch. "Maputi is all we will have in the afternoon," she said. Maputi are popped maize, very different from popcorn familiar to film-goers. The latter is made from special corn, while the former are just ordinary maize kernels roasted until they pop.
"For supper we always have sadza [a thick maize porridge] with vegetables boiled in salt. There is no cooking oil. We can't afford tomatoes and onions. Having supper is an ordeal rather than a pleasure," she said.
Zimbabwe's economic meltdown is now virtually complete and ordinary Zimbabweans are bearing the brunt of it.
The price of a litre of petrol increased from 200,000 to 300,000 Zimbabwe dollars at the end of the first week of June. Obviously no foreigner can comprehend, at a glance, what that means.
So bear with me for a short seminar on a country holding two world records - the highest inflation rate in the world and the fastest shrinking economy.
The real value of one US dollar at the black market rate in a country where the formal economy has collapsed is 310,000 Zimbabwe dollars.
President Robert Mugabe's has pegged the official exchange rate at 102,000 Zimbabwe dollars to one US dollar, but US greenbacks are exchanged at this rate only in exceptional, unavoidable circumstances.
To illustrate how far we have fallen, one Zimbabwe dollar was worth more than one American dollar at independence in 1980.
Eighty per cent of all Zimbabweans now live in abject poverty on the equivalent of less than one US dollar a day.
Every rise in the price of fuel has an immediate effect on everything else. Commuter bus fares rise as soon as the price of fuel rises. The prices of basic commodities also rise because grocers have to pay more to bring the commodities to their shops.
Zimbabwe is the only country in the world whose highest denomination bank note cannot buy a loaf of bread. Less than a week after the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe introduced a new 100,000 Zimbabwe dollar bank note at the beginning of June, the price of a loaf of bread shot up from 85,000 to 132,000 Zimbabwe dollars. Last Christmas, we were paying "only" 45,000 a loaf.
A lot of things have become so ridiculous in Zimbabwe that they would be hilariously funny if they were not so deadly serious and causing so many of us to die unnaturally early. Another world record we hold is for the lowest life expectancy for our women. According to the World Health Organisation, the average Zimbabwean woman does not live now beyond the age of 34 compared with 60 years at independence.
The word "trillion" has been added to our basic vocabulary, because that is the monetary figure government departments and businesses work in.
But a recent snap survey showed that only one in ten professionals know how many zeros there are in a trillion.
The Reserve Bank has introduced "bearer cheques" because it has run out of foreign exchange to import the kind of high quality paper necessary for printing standard bank notes. The bearer cheques are printed on ordinary low quality paper, with no security features, and they expire after a given period. The last "quality" bank note printed in this country was for 1,000 Zimbabwe dollars, which these days does not buy you even a single matchstick.
For a single purchase of twenty litres of petrol, one needs 6 million Zimbabwe dollars or 300 twenty thousand dollar bearer cheques. Petrol attendants use money counters for this and as the cheques purr in the machine the poor quality paper sends lots of dust into the attendant's face. They have to wear masks because the dust causes terrible coughing.
The dust pollution is made worse because "some of our customers carry their great wads of bearer cheques in the most unsavoury of containers", one attendant told IWPR at a service station in the Harare suburb of Hatfield.
In the villages, the new money puzzles the elderly. "Why do they give us this new note when it cannot buy anything," asked an old man at funeral vigil in Chivi, 320 km south of Harare. He told IWPR that in his day, "when money was still money", one could buy a pair of shoes using the biggest denomination note. "Now this 100,000 dollar bill buys nothing, but they bring it to us anyway," he said.
The 100,000 Zimbabwe dollar bill has been dubbed the "greenback" by local wits because of its colour. But, unlike a single US dollar, one of these new notes is useless by itself. To buy a single 40-watt lightbulb you need a dozen "greenbacks".
Reserve Bank governor Gideon Gono said on the eve of the launch of the 100,000 Zimbabwe dollar bearer cheque, "It will help reduce the annoyance of carrying loads and loads of money around and make shopping easier." Well, Gono, who lives in a palatial home perhaps second in size to President Mugabe's, is way out of touch with reality. We ordinary Zimbabweans no longer use wallets. They are too small. The country's money is devaluing so fast that you have to lug around plastic bags full of "bricks" of notes if you are going to a small grocery shop. To buy anything bigger, you need a suitcase.
For most low-income urban dwellers whose salaries range from a low of five million to an average of ten million Zimbabwe dollars a month, life has simply become unbearable.
Margaret Gweshe's husband earns ten million Zimbabwe dollars and works near the city centre. He needs 200,000 a day to travel to and from work and the total per month is about four million. Rental accommodation in most working class suburbs ranges between 3 million and 3.5million a month for a single room.
The Gweshes' monthly ten million Zimbabwe dollars is gobbled up just by transport and accommodation. They rent two rooms. Therefore there is no money for groceries, school fees and school uniforms, whose prices are soaring in line with runaway inflation. School shoes now cost 3 million a pair at a shoe shop considered to be one of the cheapest.
"A person earning 10 million is not able to come to work, pay school fees and accommodation. That's what it means," said Fadzai Kaseke who lives in the sprawling shantytown of Epworth just outside Harare. "That person cannot afford to get sick. Imagine if a serious illness falls in the family. The person will be in serious trouble."
Most families are surviving on one meal a day. Things that most people used to take for granted, such as milk, margarine, bread, sugar, meat, cooking oil and tea, have become luxuries. Beef, once a staple along with maize, now costs 1 million Zimbabwe dollars a kilogramme in a supermarket.
Zimbabweans joke grimly that they are "poor millionaires". Like Gladys, a domestic worker who earns more than 3 million a month. That is a salary she could only dream of a few years ago when maids' monthly wages were set at 80,000. But these days her millions do not stretch to a regular morsel of meat. So she eats dried grasshoppers.
"We are getting to the point where people can't take anymore," said Lovemore Madhuku, chairman of the National Constitutional Assembly, a major lobby group for political reform."Poverty and suffering are growing by the day. It's just a matter of time before inflation sparks civil disobedience."
Benedict Unendoro is the pseudonym of an IWPR journalist in Zimbabwe.

Original_Search_Title: Kankizi's Treasured Water Rock
Headline: Uganda: Kankizi's Treasured Water Rock
Body_Text:
Kampala — A young boy swings the handle of a borehole, drawing water through its metallic pipe. The water pump sits on top of a huge underground tank. To the left of the borehole is a huge rock of about 500 square metres. It looks like a small hill.
Residents of Kankizi, about 80kms on the Kampala-Gulu Road, did not realise the treasure they had until four years ago.
Funded by AMREF at a tune of over sh30m, the rock has since become the main source of water for the village. The rock doesn't produce water; nor does it have a stream running from it like other rocks. Rather, its smooth surface was turned into a catchment surface.
Using rocks as a water catchment source is a method used in many countries, but rarely in Uganda. They include Mauritania, Benin, Burkina Faso and Kenya.
"The system is suitable in areas that have a steep terrain and rocks of fairly wide and smooth surfaces," says Edward Matovu, a geologist.
He says for the rocks to be effectively used, they should be hard, flat and smooth so that water is able to flow freely on them. Kakinzi has unusually huge and spacious rocks, which is why this project was successful.
In a simple, but not widely used method of collecting water off flat surfaces, rows of blocks of about two feet high were constructed on the surface forming an enclosure, similar to a short wall fence.
The water, which collects in this wall fence, flows into two main collection sections. From here, the water is filtered through six-inch pipes. It moves through the underground pipes before collecting into two giant underground water tanks. Each of the tanks has a capacity of over 15,000 litres. On top of the tanks is a water drawer. It is not drilled into the ground.
"We get water from here all-year round irrespective of whether there is rain or sunshine," says Martha Nalwanga, a resident. She has a sweet potato shamba a few metres away from the rock.
Outgoing Luwero district chairman Hajji Abdul Nadduli says when the proposal was first made, it looked like a joke.
"The project has since proved that we can use our natural surroundings effectively without necessarily destroying them," he says. The Local Council manages the rock. According to an LC official, the main problem is controlling the quality of the water.
"We have to go on top of the rock regularly to pick off dirt, including animal droppings and rotting leaves," he says. Antelopes and bush bucks love to bask over this rock. it has a collection of reptiles, including snakes, geckos and lizards.

Original_Search_Title: Of Doctors And Daisies
Headline: Zambia: Of Doctors And Daisies
Body_Text:
Lusaka — In all the years that I've known him, "Super Doc" Gupta (BSc. Madras, M.D. Calcutta, Ph.D Lahore) has struck me as a deep, introspective, peaceful and law-abiding individual. The kind of guy who'd turn the other jaw if you gave him a left hook and followed it up with an upper cut.
So you can imagine how shocked I was to hear that our beloved doctor had been busted by cops for assault occasioning actual bodily harm.
When word of his arrest reached us, The Brethren immediately called an emergency meeting, established the Super Doc Gupta Legal Defence Fund and proceeded to work out ways to get him out of police custody before the thugs in jail made a wife out of him. After all, Brother Gupta is a very senior and highly respected neighbourhood stalwart who has worked tirelessly to keep us healthy and medically compliant all these years. Not only that.
Last month, the Brother made what experts say is the most remarkable medical discovery of the last millennium. Super Doc Gupta discovered a fruit that has stronger medicinal powers than the African Potato and has the added reputation of curing cancer, STDs, HIV/AIDS, infertility and impotence. From what I hear, it also has proven aphrodisiac properties. Whosoever eateth the fruit three times a day is well protected against witchcraft, the evil eye and bad luck. I am talking about The African Tomato, which is as black as only Africans can be and tastes like turd. You will recall how folks from far and wide besieged Super Doc's Chipatala in The 'Hood when they heard that The African Tomato had gone on sale there and that the stock was limited. It had to take some quick police intervention to bring the situation under control. But as you can probably tell by now, I am digressing, talking about cops, potatoes and tomatoes instead of telling you how on earth Brother Gupta ended up in a stinking cell full of spivs and petty thieves a few days into The New Millennium.
Apparently, a group of Chinese doctors came into The 'Hood with the intention of muscling in on Super Doc's turf. They made the mistake of trying to open their clinic just two blocks away from the old colonial house that has been Super Doc's Chipatala for the past 15 years. Worse, they started trying to steal Super Doc's patients. That's when The Medical One took off his white dust coat, put aside his stethoscope, washed his hands and kicked some Chinese butt. When he was through with thrashing them, he did what any doctor would have done under the circumstances: he treated their cuts and bruises and sent them a bill.
The cops, when they descended, weren't at all compromising because you see, doctors have been in their bad books lately. Let me put it this way. Cops and doctors have a strange love-hate relationship. Cops bust you up, doctors sew you up. That's how it goes. That's how it's always gone. The English call it - Business! The Americans call it - Home Economics. It all has to do with job creation.
For instance, did you know that wherever there's been war or terrorist bombs exploding all over the place, the only folks smiling all the way to the bank are brick-layers and builders? Take Baghdad, for instance. When the last bomb has exploded and the last building razed to the ground, who do you think will put things back together again? In short, blow up building and then hire builders to put them up again. That's Home Economics made simple, but I suppose y'all too dull to understand the intricacies of economics. Did I say - dull?
Well, these days, it doesn't matter how dull you are. With the right amount of bucks, you can buy yourself a new IQ. If you went to Game, I'm sure you could get yourself a discount on a new brain. Ask Cherry nee Mukundambolo whose wet chicken IQ, it seems, has since been committed to the dustbin of history. All it took was K25m from the lottery to turn her life around and give the Sister some respect. As you know, Cherry's wet chicken IQ has long been a neighbourhood joke. If you dialled the string of 9999s she calls Grade 12 results, (or so the joke went) you'd get the Inspector General of Police himself! But now, you can't go around saying such things about a millionaire, can you?
Not if you know what's good for you. That's something her husband, abena-Raymond has had to learn in a hurry. These days, you won't find him liberating steins of intoxicating cold liquids at the neighbourhood fleshpot and den of sin till the wee hours of the morning. Oh no! The Brother would be home washing his wife's hair and whispering sweet nothings into her ear like he was aspiring for the Husband of the Year award. The issue of his wife's Grade 12 results has been deleted from his memory. As far as he is concerned, Cherry isn't thicker than two planks and an Eskimo's blanket put together. But enough of Cherry, her millions and her husband's new-found love for her.
You know, lately, I have been spending a lot of time at home, drinking tea and watching TV instead of hanging out at the neighbourhood fleshpot and den of sin liberating steins of intoxicating cold liquids with The Brethren. If I weren't so honest, I'd have told you that my resolution for 2006 is to be the perfect father and the ideal husband, the kind every Sister wants to have and to hold. The kind of Brother who will cook, clean and who is too upright to look at another woman, let alone sow the odd wild oat. But the truth is, when you see a Brother hanging around home watching TV, drinking tea and generally trying to win The Husband of the Year award, then there's something very wrong. He's either flat broke or is hiding from creditors or from some irate girlfriend.
Of course, this fact, which I will call Edem's Law of Nature, did not apply to my disobedient forefather, Adam who, as you probably remember from your Sunday School days, never ever slept out. Where would the Brother have gone, you tell me? So Eve could sleep well at night, knowing that no matter where her husband was, he wasn't in some other Sister's bosom. According to reliable sources, when Adam was liberating forbidden fruits from someone else's tree with his accomplice and spare rib, Eve, he spent his time counting trees, giving names to things and discovering aphrodisiacs.
Who do you think discovered the fiery properties of the rhino horn, the Spanish fly, mutototo, or for that matter, Chalimbana groundnuts? Oh yes, take it from me, Adam was one very fast Brother. Come to think of it, he was the fastest man alive. After all, wasn't he the first in the human race?
And talking about races... My good friend and former neighbour, Guften Haachipola (B.A.Hons, MBA, PhD) will next week Tuesday be running what might well be the race of his life. Before the ink of his Ph.D certificate could even dry, the Brother had already started applying for jobs all over the place. Now, he has been called for an interview for this really cushy US$10,000 per month-job with eight other hopefuls to determine the best candidate. It was a very anxious Goofy who turned up on my doorstep the other day to break the news of his impending race (sorry, job interview) to me and The Wife.
"Edem, my good friend, I desperately need this job. Burn some candles, fast for seven days. Pray for a Brother in his time of need." "Pray for you? Are you mad?" I asked him. "Whoever told you that's how guys get jobs? Tell me something. Do you know who you're up against?" "Yes, I managed to find out the competition." "Good. The job is as good as yours, but only if you do what I tell you." "Oh, Edem, get serious! This is a matter of life and death." "I am serious!"
"What the hell are you talking about?" "Divine intervention, you idiot! The best way to land this job is to bewitch the competition..."

Original_Search_Title: Alo, Alo a Splendid Newcomer
Headline: Zimbabwe: Alo, Alo a Splendid Newcomer
Body_Text:
I DON'T usually visit a new restaurant till it's been open three months -- unless specifically invited by the owner.
I was invited to the newly opened Alo, Alo (ex-Le Tam-Tam), however not by restaurateurs, Adrian and Lesley Orford (ALO, geddit?) but by the bubbly blonde bosslady of MWEB, CEO Nikki Lear and her marketing maestro/misstresso (?) Kirsty Exton.
Following that lunch meeting, you'll soon be able to access a much fuller, more frequently updated version of Dusty's Diary and The Standard restaurant guide in deeper detail on the Internet. You'll also eventually be able to read any restaurant review ever written by me and published in The Standard or Zimbabwe Independent to help you decide where you'll take the missus, when you drag yourself way from surfing MWEB!
Alo, Alo was as packed as I ever saw it when the urbane Gilles Perot, a Frenchman from one of the former West African colonies, ran Le Tam-Tam. (He's now at La Riviera Rukova, Borrowdale Brooke and miffed I haven't visitedâ-oe.butâ-oe the petrolâ-oe oy veh!)
Many diners and drinkers were HIFA stars and a pleasant laid-back Gallic ambience (it's owned by the abutting Alliance Francaise) was underscored by arty-parties and sundry culture vultures circulating animatedly amid the distinctive whiff of Galloises and Gitanes cigarettes, reminiscent of Calais, Paris and Marseilles jazz clubs in the 1960s.
We ate on a shady terrace. A compact but competently constructed menu features hot or cold hors d'ouevres platters for two at $900 000. Hot: chicken livers, spicy potato balls, fish and chicken fingers, crumbed mushrooms; cold: Greek-style dips, fleish (meat) salad and smoked beef.
Nikki and I had pleasant plump crumbed mushrooms, redolent of herbs, with tangy tartare sauce ($550 000 apiece); Kirsty homemade deep-fried crumbed Haloumi, with sweet chili sauce ($450 000.) All three declared our appetisers among the best of their kind tasted. Other starters: chicken livers in crisp bacon, served with garlic butter ($550 000) and homemade soup of the day -- chicken -- $350 000.
Kirsty had the blackboard special pasta: bacon and broccoli tagliatelli, $600 000; and Nikki -- who's eating for two, she's due in November -- fish special: a grand meaty slab of hake, perfectly fried in crisp beer-batter, and chips. Fish ($900 000) can also be grilled and comes with lemon butter or sauce tartare.
My lamb cutlets (two) with sweet mint jelly or traditional mint sauce were to die for and at $950 000 with great crispy chips, dusted in paprika, and a wide and interestingly colourful array of roast vegetables, a snip. I would offer a "man-sized" portion of three, maybe four, cutlets, or good lamb chops. That would take the dish over the million mark. Currently only fillet or rump steak with choice of sauces, or gravy, have crossed the "bar" at $1,1m.
I'll definitely be back -- soon. Liked the sound of crumbed chicken Maryland with sweetcorn fritters, fried bananas and spicy relish or pork chops cordon-bleu: grilled with ham and cheese topping, both $900 000.
Regular diners out will remember the Orfords putting catering firmly on the map at Country Club, Newlands; they also ran Borrowdale Brooke Country Club spectacularly successfully, after a brief spell at the larney Sanyati Lodge, Kariba. They were headhunted to put in a winning tender for Alo, Alo.
Full English breakfast is available at weekends; they plan to introduce traditional Sunday roast lunches soon. In addition to meals, homemade cakes are $170 000, crispy bacon and egg rolls or omelette with two fillings $440 000. Homemade burgers (looking really scrumptious) and chips cost $660 000, cheeseburger $40 000 more.
The restaurant is fully licensed. Torn between pudding: selection from dessert trolley $330 000 or ice-cream and chocolate sauce at $300 000, or another refreshingly chilled article of a moderately intoxicating nature, I chose Pilsener. It would have been churlish to ask my delightful hostesses how much.
Five Stars, late April 2006.
Alo, Alo, 32, Herbert Chitepo Avenue. Open Mondays 9.30AM-5PM; Tuesday Saturday 9.30 AM-10PM; Sundays 9.30-2:30PM. Tel 011 602 165 or 011 602 245. Booking recommended.
Serendipity revisited
Returned to Serendipity, just a few weeks after my initial visit.
Pat Bowler, helping owners train staff, select menus, establish prices and organise reliable suppliers, proved a consummate catering pro and a huge fan of the column. Her family owned the Balfour Hotel, Rusape and Marandellas (now Marondera) Hotel. She was involved in running Twin Rivers, Karoi and Homegrown Restaurant in town.
Serendipity's big breakfast at $900 000 was a masterpiece: two really fresh large golden eggs, lots of bacon, splendidly tasty sausage (from Gerry Grobler at Kensington Butchery, I heard), grilled tomato, devilled kidneys with sauté potato, toast, apricot jam, lashings of tea.
Sadly one largish party left; they'd come especially for the superb cappuccino, but Zesa plunged the hissing spluttering machine into Stygian darkness.
I could have spent all day chatting to Pat, but left on a fool's errand to get car number plates updated as cheaply, painlessly and bureaucracy-free as possible. That mission was "a lemon!"

Original_Search_Title: Magical Meals At Meikles
Headline: Zimbabwe: Magical Meals At Meikles
Body_Text:
AS dining out grows ever dearer, the more we receive comments like: "We simply can't afford to go out anymore. The only way we know how unbelievably costly things are is through columns like yours."
So, many people eat out only vicariously: through restaurant reviews!
Meikles was the scene of the annual orgy of self-congratulation by the advertising industry: the Ngoma Advertising Awards dinner.
One day I'll ignore the ticket's instruction on dress: the gala night is described as "strictly black tie/evening wear".
Perhaps 5% of men complied: one wore a black leather jacket and matching polo neck, another a torn brown open-necked shirt, and a prize-winner -- who should have known better -- wore a shapeless, disreputable pork-pie hat throughout the night, even on collecting two coveted prizes, one from myself. I didn't know whether to shake his mitt, or doff a pretend piece of headgear.
However, females present -- whether wearing strictly formal "evening wear" or not -- were mainly exquisitely dressed and fragranced!
Meikles laid on a great cabaret, much of which I was again to see the next night at the Reps/Afdis Awards of Excellence -- you can't get too much of a good thing.
Table d'hote supper was splendid homemade rich mushroom soup. Coming as it did after wearing wordy waffle, presentations and re-running of the prizewinning ads, I murdered it with warm bread rolls and lovely butter.
Then there was trio of lamb -- popular at "banquets" where religious or dietary considerations may preclude beef or pork: leg of lamb, shoulder of lamb and a tasty lamb pasty with mint jelly, lovely gravy, seasonal vegetables and croquette potatoes were served.
Individual "demitos" (demitasse?) "of breathtaking lemon posset" proved pleasant Portuguese pudding that some thought over-sweet, but one I relished. Tea and coffee should have ended the repast; don't recall it arriving. The evening cost attendees (or their employers) $2,1million each.
Pre-show snacks at Reps, next night, were courtesy of The Cheeseman -- a wonderful showcase for its savoury lines. Cheese went particularly well with Afdis wines, served in the new M-Web-sponsored wine bar. Post-show snacks were served on stage to an invited few by the irrepressible Jorika Mhende, caterer at Sables Motor Club.
Back at Meikles, Monday, for high tea, a meal I don't think I've taken other than the almost obligatory ones on mail ships since an aunt socked me one at Harrogate's Majestic Hotel "celebrating" non-spectacular results of O levels; it cost half a guinea (52Â½ p). Meikles conventional high tea (for two) is $1,2 million, but over recent holidays -- when possibly the country's best, most spicy hot cross buns featured -- that was temporarily raised to $1,8 million.
I stress the price is for two covers for this oh-so civilised meal. If any dainty sandwiches, cakes, scones, jams, butter and jam, hot x buns etc are uneaten, customers are welcome to a doggy bag.
"Tea" is either local, or the imported Twinings range, or coffee. At this function, hosted by Meikles and its publicity gurus, I discreetly asked how much the hotel now charged for lager, as an advertising colleague and I sank several the previous Friday. I was shocked to hear $350 000 each.
An open invitation to foodies for Meikles breakfast over the holiday resulted in me arriving Easter Saturday, mainly because Zesa sabotaged planned boiled egg, tea and toast soldiers and I couldn't read or write (on the computer), listen to news or watch a movie.
If you eat but once a week, make that meal breakfast at Meikles or the Sheraton (now Rainbow Towers). Local and imported cereals, hot oatmeal porridge, fresh, canned and dried fruits, eggs and bacon, with steak, liver, kidneys, sausages, boerewors, tomatoes, beans, sauté potatoes flapjacks, griddle cakes; toast, croissants, scones, butter, jam, marmalade, honey, savoury spreads etc could be followed (or preceded) by hams, rare roast beef, salami, polony, several cheeses; with lashings of tea, coffee, milky drinks, fruit juices and mineral waters all beautifully displayed and -- where appropriate -- professionally served.
It was less than a month since I stayed at the Sheraton -- just before it changed hands - and breakfast there, then, was $1,7 million. The city's two five-star hotels almost invariably charge identically but Meikles' magic morning meal is now $2,8 million, effectively beyond financial reach of most Zimbabwean families.

Original_Search_Title: Kigali's Only Nocturnal Market
Headline: Rwanda: Kigali's Only Nocturnal Market
Body_Text:
Kigali — In Kigali, when it comes to 7pm and you have not yet made your purchases from any of the markets around the city, you just drop the idea.Interestingly, of late, at Kamashashi, Kanombe, there has sprung up a market that bizarrely opens in the evenings
-- at around 7pm, when the rest are closing shop - and operates till as late as midnight. The market operates directly opposite Kanombe Hospital. Bernadette Uwingabire, the sector coordinator, told Sunday Times that the market mianly targets both residents who break from work late and people at the hospital. To Uwingabire, the late-night market helps residents and the sick at the hospital, "not mentioning the fees collected from the market that goes to the government".
Asked about the likely inconvenience by the market to residents as it operates during odd hours, she said that people have no problem with the market, emphasizing that it is because they do not have to cover long distances to buy food and other domestic necessities.
"Much as there may be some inconveniences caused by the market, residents consider the advantages of having the market more than the probably would-be bad things about it," she argued.
The market mainly stocks various types of foodstuffs like sweet potatoes, vegetables like cabbages and other varieties, fruits like bananas, manufactured foods, as well as charcoal.
Joseph Uwimana, one of the traders, said operating from the market is comparatively more profitable than elsewhere.
"I have been in this business for long, operated from different markets, but this is unique with relatively high demand although it operates late in the night. But it okay with us," he said.
One shopper, James Kamuri, said: "We I come back from work late, but I am certain that I will find food for my family."
Asked what he thinks the government should do to help the residents, he said: "We wish the authorities could think about tarmacking the Kanombe-Remera road."

Original_Search_Title: Nearest And Dearest of Fordsburg's Curry Dens
Headline: South Africa: Nearest And Dearest of Fordsburg's Curry Dens
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — IT IS fair to say that there is little to choose from among the
curry dives of Fordsburg. My choice of Larosh as my preference is more
a question of convenience, being literally the closest, across the road
from my studio. Thus one gets to know the owners, the chefs, the
waiters, their best shots and their worst, and your chilli threshold.
Medium hot can sometimes take the roof off the palate of the unwary.
I can hear on weekend mornings the slapping of the puris between
deft palms, before being frisbeed into the sizzling oil in the wide
karai and then dipped into chickpea stew, potato curry or sweet
semolina halva, a breakfast speciality from Islamabad.
Before lunch and supper the first wafts of chicken tikka, braaiing
on the pavement, come drifting through my workplace. Tikka is
essentially skinless chickens from a tandoori marinade, grilled over
charcoal instead of in the scorching oven. They do have the latter, but
I seldom see anything else from it than the most delectable naan and
rotis (not the oil-soaked wafers, but blistered flatbreads) used by
most locals, including me, as cutlery, to mop up the curries with your
right hand.
They also grill several other tandoori classics on the braai on the pavement, like the chicken boti and mutton seekh kebabs.
I always wonder how they manage to sell this marvel of chicken tikka
with two tandoori roties, salad, raita and chips, for only R16.
A personal boon is that waiter Santosh jogs upstairs to my loft to
take the order from my guests and return with it, admittedly on Eastern
Time, but one learns not to be taxing between 1pm and 2 pm when
Fordsburg prays at their exquisite mosques.
Fondly christened ZCC by other artists working in the precinct after
the blue sashes over white linen-smocked plastic chairs with fake
flowers and upturned plates on every table, Larosh is endearing in its
larney aspirations. Like most of these eateries, the owners are
Pakistanis who have flooded in since our democracy, and quickly ousted
the local Indians with their fiercely competitive pricing, sadly making
old South African Indian cuisine a thing of the past.
Also typically, they offer Pakistani, Indian and (not very good)
Chinese dishes, which share a joint influence. It is wise to order the
North Indian/Pakistani dishes that include heavenly fragrant biryanis
and rich, mild curries. Of these the mutton and chicken kormas reign
supreme.
For two years I have been requesting a little gravy with my rice,
until one day I asked what it was. Oh, they skim it off the chicken
curry, the simplest curry on the menu, and now one of my all-time
favourites. All their curries are interesting, but alas, the Brain
masala, that once sounded so inspiring, deserves your wide berth.
Most curries are R20-R25, but weekday specials come at gasping
prices, like Friday's platter of Biryani with side dishes @ R18, and
you can request extra meat.
Most curry houses have a layer of oil floating on their curries, but
don't shriek, praise the Lord, because it seals off the food from the
air in the sweltering kitchens. And incidentally, I have never been ill
in the slightest from any Fordsburg restaurant, which is more than I
can say of the north.
Just as important, is what not to order: beef, unless minced or
stewed forever, is out. It also has to be said that I have never had
good seafood nor fresh fish here.
Desserts come and go but a newsstand outside offers Ras Malai: fresh
curd cheese dumplings in very sweet reduced milk, flavoured with
pistachios nuts and rosewater. To add to the Pakistani enrichment of
our pavement culture are the paanwallas behind colourful kiosks,
selling cigarettes and an addictive, mildly narcotic paan (don't tell
the cops), digestive sweet spices wrapped in a betel palm leaf. High or
no high, you'll have to brace yourself to chew this.
GETTING THERE:
50 Central Road, cnr Commercial Street, diagonally opposite
Fordsburg Square. All food halaal, so no alcohol allowed, please.
Beware the appalling toilets.

Original_Search_Title: Love Found Unexpectedly in the Avenues
Headline: Zimbabwe: Love Found Unexpectedly in the Avenues
Body_Text:
Harare — People find love in the most unusual places. A young friend
discovered the man of her dreams in a crowded aeroplane parked on a
runway in Angola, while another couple met at a fuel queue in
Kensington shopping centre.
They chatted, shared a meat pie and a Stoney ginger beer, exchanged 'phone numbers, and have never looked back.
A very similar experience, but in the realms of culinary expertise, befell me last week near the Athienitis Spar in Fife Avenue.
Following a tip off from a Japanese business associate, George
invited me to Lucullus CafÃ© and Restaurant for lunch. Although
initially reluctant to stand in line with my own tray and plate, I
quickly became enthusiastic when I saw the lunchtime spread, piping hot
in bains-maries. Everything looked so good, but I had to choose
carefully, as my plate would be weighed, and charged at $1 400 000 per
kg.
Traditional beef stew looked rich and tasty, but would those
delicious marrowbones add too much weight to my plate? Reluctantly I
also gave the hot and steaming sadza a miss, although I later
discovered it was charged out separately at $400.000.00 per kg.
Eventually I selected a slice of tender roast beef with a spoonful
of home-made chilli jam, and a large spoon of delicious roasted
mbambaira (sweet potato). Roast goat, tasty cousin to the more boring
mutton, was too good to miss, but I made a detour around a
festive-looking stuffed turkey and helped myself to several spoons of a
fragrant mixed vegetable curry. I hadn't tasted even half of the dishes
on offer, but I decided to weigh and pay. For 0.324 g the charge was
$453.600.00.
George's selection included a robust traditional chicken stew,
steaming hot pilau rice, and stirfried noodles with beef and
vegetables. A few slices of roast chicken and a spoon of macaroni
cheese brought his bill up to $534.800.00. Cokes were $120.000 each.
I can count the number of good cooks I've met on one hand -- well,
maybe one and a half -- and I was curious to meet whoever was
responsible for this varied and delicious feast. Maitre d'hotel Colin,
previously working with Frank at the River CafÃ© in Umwindsidale,
introduced me to Mrs Patricia Williams, and her right hand men, Stanley
and Cletus. It was obvious that Mrs Williams used good quality
ingredients, and depending on her inspiration each morning, would
prepare an exciting selection of the very best of bistro food.
Don't go to Lucullus Restaurant looking for Peking Duck or Spaghetti
a la Puttanesca, but if you want an inspired fusion of traditional
Zimbabwean, Asian and English food, you won't go wrong. George and I
looked around, and saw that almost every table was full. Most people
looked efficient and well-dressed, enjoying a tasty lunch before
returning to purposeful jobs and demanding occupations.
If your office is nearby to the Fife Avenue shops, a hearty lunch is
but a short walk away. Alternatively, there is plenty of parking
available nearby. If you don't choose to sit with friends at a table,
you can take your tray to a long, 70s- styled table, which faces the
window and carpark. In between courses, watch the world and his wife
bustling in and out of the shops.
I would never have guessed from the location and sparse appearance
of Lucullus CafÃ©, that a feast fit for a king, or at any rate minor
royalty, awaited the lucky visitor. We ended our meal with a full
cream, steaming hot CafÃ© Latte, $160.000.00. Capuccino coffee is
$100.000.00, and a large slice of chocolate gateau $200.000.00.
Having fallen for Lucullus in a big way, I can't wait for my next visit.
Lucullus CafÃ© and Restaurant
Fife Avenue Shopping Centre
Harare
Tel: 703621 / 704228
Open daily for breakfast, lunch and early supper, 8am - 8pm

Original_Search_Title: The Potato Chips War
Headline: Nigeria: The Potato Chips War
Body_Text:
To many observers it became clear that the Americans were dead serious about going to war with Saddam Hussein when it was reported in the media that members of the United States Congress were considering a resolution that would remove French fries from the menu in congressional dining rooms. The ban was intended to send a message to Mr. Jacques Chirac, the French President, who had threatened to veto any United Nations resolution that would give President Bush the go ahead to wage war on Iraq.
French fries, as everyone knows, are thin strips of potato fried in deep fat. There is a belief that they are so named because it was the French, knowing a thing or two when it comes to gourmandizing, who first thought of the idea of frying strips of potato in deep fat. A more familiar name for French fries is "chips", which is what the English call it when they serve it with fried fish wrapped in last week's newspaper.
The media report was silent on the fate of French dressing, which is made from oil, vinegar and seasoning. My own guess is that French dressing was untouched because Americans have never been partial to salad dressing, preferring to drench their salad with ketchup.
French bread was also spared. Banning it would have been of no effect, as no United States congressman had ever been seen entering or leaving the Sam Rayburn building with one of those long slender loaves tucked under his arm.
There was no word at all about the French letter (condom to you) probably because it was not one of the items on offer in the dining rooms of Congress.
The ban on French fries sent shock waves throughout the culinary world. I was greatly surprised myself. Cooking is a hobby that I have always taken seriously, so I made an appointment to see J. Cunning Angle, the ubiquitous and fearless correspondent of Fable News Network, to get his views on other dishes that are likely to be banned because of their association with countries that did not support America's position on the war. Here is a summary of what he told me:
Spanish onion is in a favoured position, because Spain joined America and Britain in sponsoring a pro-war resolution at the Security Council.
The ban on French fries (whose principal raw material is the tuber that we call Irish potato) had placed the Republic of Ireland was in a difficult position, no matter where it stood on the Iraq question. This was causing a lot of concern in the republic, and it was probably no coincidence that, in the same week that the ban on French fries was announced, the Irish Prime Minister flew to Washington for bilateral talks with President Bush. It was not known if the "linkage" between French fries and Irish potato was on the agenda, and if the Prime Minister had seized the opportunity to seek assurances about the fate of Irish stew, a dish made of, among other things, lamb or beef, with Irish potatoes and onions.
Turkey, that large bird with bare wattled head that is domesticated for its flesh worldwide would definitely be on the banned list. Turkey (the country) had refused to allow the deployment of American troops on Turkish soil, and if this attitude should continue, turkey (the bird) was in danger of being removed from the menu in congressional dining rooms. The only happy outcome of this would be an improvement in the life expectancy of turkeys, especially as there was at that time a ban on the importation of frozen turkeys into Nigeria. The problem was that the absence of turkeys from congressional tables would fundamentally change the nature of Thanksgiving Day, that annual holiday celebrated in thanksgiving to God on the fourth Thursday of every November.
In the event of all things Turkish being banned, members of Congress would no doubt still continue to sweat it out in Turkish baths which would, of course be renamed "saunas".
For the sake of their health if for no other reason, congressmen already tending to obesity would have a patriotic excuse for giving up Turkish delight, a jelly-like sweet, flavoured with flower essences. It is usually cut into cubes and covered with icing sugar.
Next is Brussels sprout, those things that look like tiny cabbages. Because Belgium was seen to be siding with France and Germany in opposing a second U.N. resolution on Iraq, Brussels sprout would most likely be on the list of banned food.
Other comestibles that would be closely watched were Brazil nuts, Danish pastry and Hungarian goulash, the last named being a rich stew, originating from Hungary, and made of beef, lamb or veal highly seasoned with paprika. Brazil, Denmark and Hungary were not strident in their support of the war, so the fate of the aforementioned comestibles was still in the balance.
Germany, as everyone knows, was strongly opposed to a second U.N. resolution. Hamburger (a cake of minced beef, often served in a bread roll) and frankfurter (a smoked sausage made of finely minced pork or beef) are named after important German cities. This should qualify them for a ban, but because Hamburgers and frankfurters are quintessentially American staples, banning them from the congressional cafeterias may prove to be a hard wrench. A decision would require the kind of deft diplomatic moves that the Americans have not shown themselves to be capable of making over the Iraq issue.
On the other hand, three dishes are assured of continued patronage in the dining rooms of congress. They are the oddly named Welsh rabbit, which really has nothing at all to do with rabbits, but is just a savoury dish consisting of melted cheese sometimes mixed with milk and seasoning; Scotch eggs (a hard-boiled egg enclosed in a layer of sausage meat, covered in egg and bread crumbs, and fried); and Yorkshire pudding (a light puffy baked pudding made from a batter of flour, eggs and milk, traditionally served with roast beef.) From the sound of their names they are undoubtedly British, and the least the Americans would want to do would be to retain them on congressional menus as a reward to the British Prime Minister for his steadfast support for President Bush.
I had one last question for Mr. Cunning Angle. With the war in Iraq having become such a diplomatic and public relations disaster for the Bush administration, and the opinion polls showing a big drop in the President's approval rating, would Congress now restore potato chips to its proper place on its dining tables?
I couldn't get an answer from the Fable News Network correspondent. His secretary told me that he had gone to the "war front" in the creeks of the Niger Delta, and would not be back for several months.
A glut of lizards
A little over twenty-three years ago, when I moved into the housing development where I now live, my nearest neighbours were six humans, about a thousand lizards, and an unknown number of snakes.
The humans lived about a kilometre from me, and kept to themselves. Not so the lizards and snakes. They felt free to roam all over the estate, not leaving out the bit of it that was my garden.
Contact between reptiles and humans was unavoidable, and to prevent unpleasant clashes I drew up some ground rules, to be strictly observed by all concerned. Humans (represented by myself and my family) would refrain from stepping on snakes. Snakes, on their part, would stay out of doors and, if possible, out of sight. Lizards may have a free run of the external walls of the house, for the purpose either of sunning themselves or of staying beyond the reach of my dog. Notwithstanding this last rule, any lizard engaged in the amorous pursuit of a lady friend may rush headlong into my sitting room, provided that it makes its stay indoors reasonably brief.
These rules, except the one about my not stepping on snakes, were not always strictly observed. Using every excuse in the book, lizards often strayed into the house, touching off a frantic and uncoordinated hunt by broom-wielding members of my family. The trespassing lizard was seldom found immediately, having hidden itself behind a a settee, or climbed up the folds of a curtain. Later, just when a visitor would be settling down to a drink, the lizard would emerge from its hiding place and make an unhurried exit through the front door, disconcerting my visitor and causing me some embarrassment.
I solved this problem by preparing the visitor's mind for what was about to happen. "I wonder where Lizzy is," I would casually say, as I poured him a drink.
"Who is Lizzy?" he would naturally ask.
"My pet lizard," I would reply. "She's probably having a nap under a chair somewhere."
So, when a lizard did crawl from under a bookcase, its appearance, already expected, would not be such a shock. "A man," my visitor would probably say to himself, "is entitled to keep any pet of his choice in his own house."

Original_Search_Title: Eastern Nite At Steak Out
Headline: Uganda: Eastern Nite At Steak Out
Body_Text:
Following the success of the Luo Night, Face 2 Face Promotions has
created the Eastern Night to take place at Steak Out Bar and Restaurant
on Lumumba Avenue this Friday.
The night promises a lot of entertainment from established and
upcoming artistes from Eastern Uganda. We are talking about a stage
line up that will comprise 2005 PAM award Eastern artiste of the year,
Gen. Mega Dee ( pictured); 2004 multiple PAM award winners, East World;
Desperado, Rocky Giant, Sylvia Kyansuti, Juliet Mugirya, Engineer
Kateregga, Sylvia Kyansuti, Kumi Cultural Dancers and Nile Beat
artistes who will do the sensual Gishu kadodi dance and tamena ibuga
from Busoga. Look out for the surprise guest artiste.
There will be a sumptuous buffet of local dishes like malehwa
(bamboo shoots), embooli (sweet potatoes), maido (ground nuts) and
duuma (maize). There will also be dishes from the various tribes like
the Sabiny, Bakedi, Karimojong, Itesots and Banyole.
Some of the highlights of the night will include a special unveiling
of new songs by General Mega Dee off his forthcoming eight-track album.
East World, will also sing songs off their new album especially where
they collaborated with Bebe Cool.
A couple of bigwigs from region have said they will attend the musical do.
Capt. Kirunda Kiveijinja, Hon. Mike Mukula, former Vice President
Specioza Wandira Kazibwe and officials from the Busoga Kingdom will
grace the high table that night. Gates will be open at 6p.m. and one
will have to part with Shs 5,000, as entrance to the event.

Original_Search_Title: Inside Owino
Headline: Uganda: Inside Owino
Body_Text:
Kampala — Owino Market is one place where you won't need your mobile phone.
If you hear it ring, you will never get round to hearing what your
caller has to say. Not so long ago, you would not even succeed in
getting it to your ear - it would be snatched the moment you pulled it
out of its hiding place.
Officially known as St. Balikuddembe Market, Owino attracts over
200,000 people every day. The first callers start flowing in as early
as 4:00am. These are traders rushing to offload their luggage to bulk
buyers.
By 6:00am, Owino is already packed with customers. The noise is
already deafening. "Lukumi lukumi mulonde," (The price is sh1,000. Pick
whatever you like) a woman shouts as she points to a heap of
second-hand skirts. Right next to him, a man sings "Bitaano byokka
omwana ngayambala (buy cheap children's clothes at only sh500).
It's hard to believe that behind all this noise and confusion is a highly organised system of administration.
Dan Kiwanuka, one of the market administrators, says that Owino is
divided into eight, zones each named after the items sold there and
managed by elected leaders. These sections include Soweto (cooked
food), Police, Kafumbe (clothes), Matooke (raw food, particularly
bananas), Kyebandula (type of sweet potatoes), Nyanya (tomatoes),
Produce (cereals) and Central zone, where you will find a mixture of
items like fruits, saucepans, curtains, bedcovers and soap, among
others.
By noon, you can't believe anyone who associates the word
'organisation' with Owino. It's so crammed with people that it's almost
impassable. At this point, you need to be very careful with the people
you are moving with; before you bat an eyelid, they might disappear
from your sight and leave 100 strangers in their wake.
This Wednesday afternoon is no different. Before we plunge into the
mass of people pushing their way in and out of Owino, Angelina Mrs.
Wapakabhulo, points to her black and white kitenge headdress and
instructs us to look out for it should we lose each other.
Mrs. Wapakabhulo is one of over 50,000 people who work in Owino,
except that hers is a job targeting fellow Owino workers. Known as Mama
by the vendors, she has been co-ordinating the Market Vendors Aids
Project (MAVAP) since 2003. An initiative by the vendors, MAVAP also
runs in Nakawa and Park Yard markets, with hopes of expanding to other
markets in the country.
It aims at raising vendors' awareness about HIV/AIDS and providing
them with free voluntary counselling and testing services. MAVAP also
assesses risky behaviour and environment that contribute to contracting
HIV and looks for ways to lessen its impact.
According to Godfrey Kayongo, the chairperson of St. Balikuddembe
Vendors and Traders Association, vendors' behaviour included sneaking
off in between working hours to have sex with fellow vendors.
"In the early 1980s, they used to do this in the corridors and you had to just look away if you happened to find them," he says.
But even after they heard about HIV/AIDS corridor visits continued
to be a big temptation until Mrs. Wapakabhulo intervened between 2003
and 2004.
"She brought some women and showed us a video about AIDS. We all got
scared and decided to do everything in our power to resist and fight
this disease," Kayongo says.
Mrs. Wapakabhulo started with a pilot project called Afia sokoni, a
Swahili phrase that means 'health in the market'. As is always the case
with new projects, Mrs. Wapakabhulo and her group had heavy stones to
lift before they could make headway.
But after training some vendors as peer educators, positive results
began to show. "Targeting vendors within the market worked because it
is very hard for someone from outside to make as much contact," Mrs.
Wapakabhulo says.
James Rumaza, the vice chairperson of Produce Zone and one of the
peer counsellors, says almost every one knows the facts about AIDS.
"Most people are aware now so they can't misbehave or claim that they
have been bewitched to get AIDS," he says.
MAVAP also pins up posters, supplies free condoms, aprons, t-shirts,
headscarves, umbrellas and literature with messages about HIV/AIDS.
Mrs. Wapakabhulo explains all this as we move towards Nyanya zone.
She's trying to scream something to us when a hoarse voice screams
"Fasi fasi fasi (give way, give way)", right behind us.
We all scamper in different directions to let the man pass. As we
move up the stairs to Kayongo's office, a man taps me on the bum. I
turn to reassure him that my bum is not 'for all to touch' but I decide
that he is not worth one word when he winks at me. Before we left the
market, I meet many others like him. Some are so filthy-hearted that
they don't have to think twice before hurling obscenities at women.
We then head to Marie Stopes and AHF Uganda Cares, MAVAP's partners
in the fight against AIDS. As we walk through Nyanya Zone a heavy man
with an equally heavy box of tomatoes on his head comes springing in
her direction.
He almost rams into her but she manages to jump aside. Her agility
is surprising for a woman her age. She walks as fast, seemingly
undeterred by the horde of people pushing around her.
"We approached Marie Stopes and Uganda Cares," she says, "to bring
services nearer to the vendors. Before, we used to send them to Mulago
or Nsambya hospital for VCT.
They had to sacrifice a day's work to join the long line at the
hospitals and few were willing to do this." She decided to approach
Marie Stopes and Uganda Cares to fill the gap. Marie Stopes provides
free counselling and HIV testing while Uganda Cares gives those who
test HIV-positive free ARVs and also treats other HIV-related diseases
According to Esther Awino, the counsellor at the market's Marie
Stopes clinic, more than 25 to 40 vendors go for VCT daily. At Uganda
Cares, we meet the medical director, Dr. Peninah Lutung who is waiting
for Angelina to check out the rooms Uganda Cares wants to rent.
"More and more vendors are turning up for VCT and ARVs... we need
extra space to keep up with the numbers," Dr. Lutung says as we force
our way through Matooke zone. Kibuuka joins us and from their
discussion, we learn that he is also one of MAVAP's peer educators. In
between their discussion, we jump in and out of the narrow corridors in
an effort to dodge box and sack carriers.
"With all this dodging, jumping, you get tired after spending only
one hour in the market," she says, the first sign that the insanity
does take its toll on her. As we move through Kafumbe zone, ladies use
their sweetest and politest voices to lure buyers.
Ahead is a line of women pedalling away at their sewing machies. To
our right, women laugh at a crude joke, punctuating their laughter with
a high "ha ha haaaaa... wuu" that sounds like a rehearsed soprano.
To our left, another line of men and women, some of them teenagers,
are singing and shouting out different items and prices on sale. These
include bras, skirts, blouses, coats, dresses, sweaters, trousers,
shirts and sweaters. Mrs. Wapakhabulo points at the bras and exclaims,
"God, they've got designer bras of all sizes here. Do you know that you
can fail to get such a selection in shops?"
And unlike the general belief that Owino has second-hand items, you
will find scores of new items as well. From personal items (clothes,
bags, perfumes), to household items and electrical appliances they are
all there and at fairly cheap prices.
With all these assets and people swimming around them, thieves are expected to come with the territory.
But according to Kibuuka, it's too risky for anyone to try stealing
anything within Owino market. "Anyone who risks is courting death.
He/she is beaten up without mercy because people (vendors) got fed up
of thieves," Kibuuka says.
Of course, some cunning people manage to grab a thing or two but,
although Mrs. Wapakhabulo keeps her bag securely under her arm, such
are the least of her worries. "I've never been a target of thieves
since I came to Owino," Mrs. Wapakhabulo says as a lady beams and
invites her to 'see how the sun tortures me as I sell eggs'.
"Habari Jane," Mrs. Wapakhabulo greets her. She's Jane Nabirye
Kasasa, another of MAVAP's peer counsellors. Kasasa's two daughters,
adorning MAVAP t-shirts and headscarves also rush to greet Angelina.
"Mama, women are asking for female condoms because they want to be
in charge," Kasasa informs Mrs. Wapakhabulo as she points at a packet
of condoms strategically placed between trays of eggs.
It's coming to 4:00pm when we finally leave her shop. The afternoon
sun is unrelenting in its efforts to roast human flesh, further
stirring up the madness around us. Angelina's neck is drenched with
sweat but she seems unaware of this as she jumps out of another sack
carrier's way.
The air is a stuffy mixture of gases, dust, spiced foods and smoke;
but somehow, every nostril manages to smuggle in a little oxygen into
the lungs. And the noise! Howling babies, singing and shouting vendors,
buyers haggling over prices...
"Eh eh, mama Angelina Mama... that Kibuuka is very bad. Why do you
allow him to take you away from your people?" Mrs. Wapakhabulo turns
towards Victoria Nume, a tomato seller and also a peer counsellor.
"Mama, (MAVAP is doing very well. More people are coming for VCT
although some wait for their CD4 counts to drop to 30 and some peer
educators have refused to abandon their bad behaviour. They go after
our young daughters in Soweto yet they are sick," she says.
Nume's complaints are many, but Mrs. Wapakhabulo keeps nodding her head with a smile.
We push our way through Soweto, where several women invite Mrs.
Wapakhabulo for lunch. As we move on, we notice several children
playing. One toddler tugs at her mother who is busy frying chapatis.
"This environment is not healthy for babies. We are lobbying for a day
care centre for them," Mrs. Wapakhabulo observes as she points at a
baby sitting near a hot cooking stove.
Now in central zone, we rush passed a group of women selling curtains.
This time, the interruption comes from Kibuka, who pulls her hand
gently and says with a smile, "mama, greet my mother. Mother, this is
one is called mama Angelina. She's the one who is teaching us about
AIDS," he says as the two women smile at each other.
But not everyone has kind words for Angelina. As we force our way
out of Owino at 6:00pm, someone shouts, "Mwe musasule sente za Global
fund zemwalya." (Pay the Global Fund money you stole).
"We've never received a coin from Global fund. Our sponsor right now
is the Norwegian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. But that doesn't
discourage us because we all get accused at one point or another," she
says as we walk away, leaving the Owino jumble far behind.

Original_Search_Title: Why the Golden Rose Stands Out
Headline: Tanzania: Why the Golden Rose Stands Out
Body_Text:
Arusha — At any restaurant, the theme and entertainment value can be a big part of the enjoyment, but the primary draw is the food. Mediocre food would probably not keep people coming back and again, and Golden Rose restaurant is a favourite of many guests and residents of Arusha.
A few days ago the Golden Rose restaurant became my preferred dining spot, and while there I noticed nearly all day-long the restaurant boasts of a congenial party atmosphere. Everywhere I looked diners were in animated conversations, telling many good stories in a jovial and social way. It is the type of restaurant which seems to foster this type of interaction emanating from conference delegates repeatedly using the hotel's facilities.
As you walk in, the lounge area sets the tone. The restaurant is open for lunch and dinner daily, and priority seating are recommended. Although the crowd is classy, the ambiance is not pretentious. Priority seating is Golden Rose's version of a reservation, with the condition that the party will be seated at the next available table after their priority seating time.
The food at the Golden Rose has a broad appeal, simple, straightforward and satisfying a wide range of palates. The waiters are part of the allure of the place as they are efficient at cleaning the table and doing all the rudimentary chores, but they also add a bit of their own personality. Their approach is familiar, but strike the right note for this type of restaurant.
Each day there is a "special" menu offered, but the most popular menu items are the golden fried chicken, traditional grilled meat, and the roast meals. The chicken meal served with mashed potatoes, rice or French fries with seasonal vegetable are incredibly flavourful.
At dinner fried chicken, meatiness and goat roast entrees are available, as is the vegetable stuffed pepper. Items exclusive to dinner include various steaks, pork and seafood choices.
The menus include several "comfort" as well as health-conscious foods. In almost many servings there is a huge platter of thinly sliced onion rings that are tasty, as well as French fries with your choice of gravy, cheese, or chilli and cheese.
For anyone with room for dessert, there are several great items to choose from. Deserts at Golden Rose are strength of the kitchen and their stylish presentation wins the chef's profession. In fact, it would be worth a visit just for dessert if you have a sweet tooth.
Be adventurous and sample Golden Rose cuisine, it is light, flamboyant and crowning fusion for food, and you will be pleasantly surprised. Health conscious diners or those with resolutions to improve eating habits - can happily head out to the Golden Rose where the meals are preservative and additive free.

Original_Search_Title: Uwec's Classy Animals
Headline: Uganda: Uwec's Classy Animals
Body_Text:
The standard of living at the zoo is high. The animals eat food that
is not different from that of humans. Their breakfast menu includes
powder milk, porridge, bread and eggs. When it comes to lunch and
supper, they go for cabbages, carrots, mangoes, jackfruit, yellow
bananas, French beans, pineapples and tomatoes
You have probably read about former Big Mama, the crocodile that
made news last year when it was discovered that after 50 years of being
known to be female, the mysterious creature is actually male. Big Papa,
now recovering in the veterinary unit at Uwec (Uganda Wildlife
Education Centre), following an operation in which his unresolved sex
issues came to an end, is not the only interesting thing at the
Entebbe-based nature centre.
This time it's nothing close to sex issues. Rather, we are looking
at the stylish lifestyle of the animals at Uwec, specifically their
menu.
While some of us may regard certain dishes like chips and chicken
highly, the animals at Uwec would include the same too on their menu.
And they wouldn't stop at that; they would also acquaint themselves
with certain foods that most of us either regard as food for the well
heeled or have even never heard of.
These animals' way of life is high roller; no wonder their keepers
relate with them at a personal level. The keepers actually refer to the
animals as guys, babes, individuals and the like. "Take care brother,
that 'guy' is tricky and can snatch your camera if you get too close,"
says Uwec's Edward Kabagyo of Shaka, a chimpanzee named after South
African Zulu King, Shaka Zulu.
The menu
What exactly constitutes these animals' daily menu? Most of us, if
sent to buy beef for animals, would go camping at abattoirs and
butcheries to lobby for those rejects at low prices. But the guys at
Uwec won't settle for less.
To begin with, all the food brought in for the animals is put under
thorough scrutiny, technically known as screening or inspection by a
veterinary doctor. If anything is considered to be of poor standard, Mr
Expedito Nsubuga, the supplier, would have to take it back, lest the
animals reject it.
"Our conviction is that a right-thinking human being must as well be
able to find the animal food edible and tasty for it to qualify as food
for our animals," says Dr David Hyeroba, Uwec's veterinary doctor. This
is the reason one has to actually taste the food.
Food is supplied every Monday and Thursday. On Monday, the supply is
entirely raw stuff like beef, liver, chicken, fish, mukene
(hypochromines), white ants, rabbit pellets, dog food, chicken mash and
the like.
On Thursday, you find fruits and creams such as yellow bananas,
mangos, jackfruit, eggs, bread, cerelac, powder milk, avocado, papaws,
oranges, soya flour, tomatoes, watermelon, sugar, nakati, cabbage,
French beans and several other fruits. In all, 58 items have to be
delivered for the animals every week.
Good business
The perishables are kept in big refrigerators to keep them fresh for days. A month's supply goes for Shs7-10 million.
For Nsubuga, who has supplied food to the animals since 1998,
business is booming. According to his purchasing officer, Mr Hussein
Balikoowa, it costs them about Shs4 million worth of working capital to
supply the animals in a month. Balikoowa says that with the many years
