in this business, they have grown to love their work and supply the
animals as if they were human beings, since their preferences are very
close to those of humans.
The primates, especially the chimps, pride themselves as the high
profile and civilised animals at the centre. They eat cooked food and
walk with a swagger. Then other primates like baboons, colobus monkeys
and the red-tailed monkeys follow in terms of profile and respect. But
regardless of the type, all the primates regard themselves highly.
They play ball during their free time - usually a blend of
basketball and football. They also get sick and suffer from flu just
like humans. No wonder they are so interactive. But that's only when
they are having a good day. "You don't have to take the jokes too far,
as you might find them moody and they hack you," warns Mr Jimmy Awany,
the primate keeper.
Their standard of living is high and most of their food is akin to
that of humans. They consume sugar and powder milk mixed in porridge
along with bread, eggs and the like for breakfast.
And when it comes to lunch and supper, they go for cabbages,
carrots, mangoes, jackfruit, yellow bananas, French beans, pineapples,
tomatoes, papaws, sweet potatoes other such foods. But these are only
snippets from the lot.
There are other entries that leave you wondering who eats what. Take
sugarcane, green pepper, foetus, cerelac, guava, French peas, cucumber,
white ants, honey and several others.
Other items scare you stiff. While most people would love to
jealously protect chicks from any harm, Ugachick does a generous
donation of one-day-old chicks to the wildlife centre to be preyed on
by the Fish Eagles. Then there are a couple of several other animals
bred for the purpose of being fed on by others.
They call it establishing feeding colonies for the animals. While
the guinea pigs and rabbits are busy consuming the centre's carrots,
pellets and nakati, they are only beefing up their bodies to make
adequate fodder for the snakes, mostly pythons and birds. They used to
breed rats for the same in the past.
Meal times
Interestingly, the animals know their weekly feeding timetable and
the exact times they are supposed to eat. "For instance, Bonge the lion
starts moving towards the den whenever dinnertime is approaching," says
Hyeroba.
And even though you drive the food van towards their cage when it's
not time for meals, none of the animals will bother you. Only the
keeper is allowed to feed the animals and no other person is allowed to
come with food from outside to give the animals.
The administrators however say they are planning to give an
opportunity to the public to start feeding the animals, especially on
festive or recreation days, as many have shown interest in feeding the
animals whenever they visit.
Festive and recreation days are usually the best for the animals.
For instance the primates feed between five and six times a day but on
a festive or recreation day, they are fed up to about seven times.
These are the times the animals get sponsorship opportunities. "We give
opportunity to people to sponsor and rename some [animals]," says
Hyeroba.
The sponsorships differ in terms of how much is spent on an animal
per year. Shs20,000 is a Bronze sponsorship, Shs100,000 is Silver,
Shs500,000 is Gold and Shs1 million is Diamond sponsorship.
Others simply adopt the animal(s) by paying their year's upkeep such
that they are able to visit them any time they want. Renaming an animal
also depends on a situation, for instance one Servel cat is called
Aggrey because it is aggressive.
Minimum funds
Though these animals have everything they need now, their World Bank
funding ends in 2007. The World Bank has been funding the Wildlife
Education Centre under a project: Protected Area Management for
Sustainable Utilisation (PAMSU). Currently, Uwec meets only 21 percent
of its expenditure from self-generated revenue. This means a
sustainability deficit of 79 percent surfaces after the World Bank's
withdrawal.
Uwec is appealing to the general public to make contributions
through actual visits, annual or single contributions into a Uwec
Endowment Fund and physical or financial support toward the operations
of the centre. There are Uwec donation boxes at the centre and in areas
around Kampala for simple cash donations. Better still, you can adopt
or sponsor an animal.

Original_Search_Title: Peace At the Cost of Freedom?
Headline: Somalia: Peace At the Cost of Freedom?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — After more than a decade of brutal factional fighting, the roadblocks and gunmen have been cleared off the streets of the Somali capital, business is thriving and Mogadishu is being rebuilt.
But strict standards of religious and behavioral discipline are being introduced, and questions are being asked about the vision of the new authority, the Union of Islamic Courts (UIC).
For now, gratitude for security and freedom of movement takes precedence for most Mogadishu residents. Others have fled to refugee camps, complaining of persecution and loss of business.
"There are two sorts of freedoms," pointed out a Mogadishu businessman. "Going about our daily lives is the most important freedom we can have now."
But he said one freedom may come at the expense of another, adding "People do not want to lose their personal freedoms."
The extraordinary turnaround in security means people can go about their daily life; traders can safely take home their earnings; children can go to school regularly and without fear; clinics and hospitals can concentrate on primary healthcare instead of constantly dealing with the trauma of conflict.
A recent visitor from the diaspora, who has not been to Mogadishu in six years, was astounded by the changes.
"I drove through areas no one has driven in 15 years - like Bermuda (named after the Bermuda Triangle, previously one of the most dangerous areas) - without any security escort or even a gun. Five months ago, this would have been unthinkable, even with a heavy security escort," he said.
Stability under the UIC has opened up new prospects for humanitarian assistance. For the first time in more than a decade, food aid has successfully been brought into the newly opened port for distribution outside the city.
According to Leo van der Velden, deputy country director of the World Food Programme, for Somalia, the new authority had "done the right things and said the right things" to encourage humanitarian access, and that good security allowed transporters to safely carry and deliver food aid.
CARE International confirmed that a consignment of sorghum from the United States had arrived in the port in October, and was successfully handled, transported and delivered to areas outside the city - a logistical achievement impossible for more than a decade, when the port and its resources became a flashpoint for factional fighting.
Freedom of movement has also allowed access to the displaced camps in Mogadishu.
For years, thousands of internally displaced people have suffered "a forgotten tragedy" in the city. Now the displaced are also benefiting from safety and small-scale community assistance. In October, the Al Bayan court militia escorted small consignments of food to the camps, donated by the local community. "We are doing what we can," said the chairman of Al Bayan, Mohamed Ibrahim Bilal.
But despite the change, security fears resulted in the humanitarian community withdrawing from Somalia in September. The move followed the murder of a foreign cameraman and an Italian nun in Mogadishu, and the assassination attempt in Baidoa on the president of the Transitional Federal Government (TFG).
Since the withdrawal, talks between the UIC and the TFG have stalled. The two sides met in Khartoum twice since the UIC victory in a reconciliation meeting sponsored by the Arab League and supported by the international community. The third meeting scheduled for late October did not take place after the two sides failed to agree on fundamental issues. Fears of regional conflict increased after the UIC declared ihad on Ethiopia for deploying troops inside Somali territory - which Ethiopia has consistently denied.
In November, the US embassies in the region issued a warning of "reports of terrorist threats emanating from extremist elements within Somalia, which target Kenya, Ethiopia and other surrounding countries." It warned of suicide attacks, particularly in Kenya and Ethiopia - seen as regional US allies and supporters of the TFG.
The US accused the UIC of ha-rbouring alleged terrorists and extremists, while the US in turn has been accused of supporting the defeated warlords.
On the streets of Mogadishu, there is much debate about "moderates" and "extremists." A local journalist said his personal freedoms had changed in many ways since the takeover, "good and bad."
"In some areas there is no music or cinemas, radio stations have closed down, we have no freedom to write, and they are carrying out public executions."
He says there is a "wait and see" atmosphere in the city.
"When it comes to security, we feel freedom. When it comes to personal freedoms, we feel worried."
Since coming to power in June, the UIC has implemented Sharia law, but avoided issuing official directives on details of social and religious conduct. Instead, it has used punishment and propaganda to set new standards.
Public floggings have been meted out for men and women accused of selling drugs, chewing khat and "immoral behaviour." There are public lectures and radio appeals addressing religious commitment, behaviour and morality, and the interpretation of the Koran.
But the new climate of punishment and restraint may not sit comfortably in a culture known for its personal freedoms and egalitarianism -and the UIC is wary of launching an assault against certain aspects of Somali culture.
The UIC's new ban on khat will prove a critical test of acceptance of the new restrictions.
During the holy month of Ramadan in Mogadishu, when the sun had gone down and the last prayers said, women sold bundles of the narcotic leaf from small street stalls. Angrily, they talked about the number of times they had been stopped from selling khat. Young men push and shout around shadowy markets, complaining their cinemas and entertainments had been closed down.
Khat bans were first announced in UIC-controlled Kismayu and Jilib in southern Somalia.
The new authority is pragmatic about the popular impact of an official ban. "It is a public challenge," the chairman of the Islamic Courts, Sheikh Sharif Sheikh Ahmed said.
The daily importation of khat from Kenya and Ethiopia is costly, and its narcotic effects have contributed to conflict and lawlessness.
It is seen as a social evil by many. But it provides countless small traders, mostly women who are heads of households, with a livelihood, and its daily use is socially and geographically widespread. No Somali government has been successful in abolishing the trade.
There is also consternation over new standards of dress code. In UIC offices, men who dress "Western-style" are frowned upon.
Visiting businessmen and women say they sometimes struggle to conform and do not understand the disapproval around certaindetails of behaviour and dress.
Change is visible. Increasingly, women in Mogadishu are adopting a full veil, more associated with Saudi Arabia and the Arab states than "Somali-style" modesty. Over the last few years, black, brown and grey hijabs have been steadily replacing the vibrant and colourful Somali scarves.
The trend has accelerated since the takeover, note Mogadishu residents.
Sheikh Sharif emphasises that the increase in "modesty" is not the result of any official declaration by the UIC. "The Islamic Courts has made no decision about (this) because Somali women dress the way it is intended in their religion. I do not wish to impose anything like that."
Some of the UIC leadership says the debate over dress is provoked by pro-Western critics.
But there is much debate - in private- among Mogadishu residents as to whether people are changing their habits through religious choice, or just to protect themselves. "Women are already taking up the veil not out of choice but as a precaution, so that no-one singles them out," said one businessman. As a supporter of the UIC, he said the focus on dress and behaviour was "not a priority problem in Somalia - and shouldn't be for anyone.
"The UIC should be concerned with more important things," he added, street humour reflects the fact that the new path to power is through religion. "Everyone is becoming a sheikh these days," observed one hotelier. Red and white checked headscarves are used fairly ubiquitously by the UIC - from the leadership and the militia to their supporters.
The scarves are seen as a badge of loyalty. For those who want to demonstrate strict religious commitment, musical ring tones on mobile phones have been replaced by recordings from the mosque.
Some of the social confusion comes from the absence of policy. Soon after the takeover, cases were being reported of women being ordered to wear the hijab. But the UIC leadership explains these incidents as the challenge of centralisation. There are 27 branches of the courts in Mogadishu, and 11 outside the city, all used to operating autonomously.
Different local courts were imposing different standards and implementing different punishments. Centralisation of the courts did not take place until late September.
Once the courts had been brought under a central authority, the UIC officially established a judicial system under sharia law.
In October, the Supreme Court of Benadir region was opened, and an appeals court set up. Public floggings and executions can now only proceed when approved by the Supreme Justice Committee, representing all the courts. Police stations are also being established, although not yet fully functional. Sentenced prisoners are being held in the central Mogadishu jail.
The UIC also recently banned photographs and film of executions, aware of the impact on its critics and the wider world.
The confusion over codes of behaviour illustrates the struggle between the moderates and hardliners within the UIC. Since the takeover, there has been increased emphasis on "jihad" and visible militarisation of society - including seminars on jihad for men and women, and training camps to unify the militia into a centralised force.
Some UIC leaders see security and "defence of country and religion" as the priority, and have made it the rallying call.
The hardliners - known as the Shabaab group - invest in militarisation, advocate strict religious codes and punishments, and shun contact with the non-Muslim world. They include key figures in the UIC - such as chief of security for Mogadishu, Sheikh Abdullahi Mo'alim Ali alias Abu Utayba, who stated publicly, according to local journalists, that people who failed to pray five times a day should be shot.
Prominent UIC figures, including Abu Utayba, appear in a jihadi video doing the rounds in Mogadishu and the diaspora. The video glorifies Osama bin Laden, shows military training camps in Mogadishu, and calls on "any marginalised Muslim to come to Somalia." It calls Somalia "the new Afghanistan of the world." The video is not officially endorsed by the UIC, and there is no indication of who authored it.
Jihadi propaganda experts believe it was probably made for fund-raising in the Somali diaspora, as it is predominately in the Somali language.
"The courts never authored such a thing ? it is the work of our enemies," Professor Ibrahim Hassan Adow, head of the UIC foreign affairs department, said.
Key members of the UIC leadership are working to secure international support and assistance in rebuilding Somalia. There is consternation in the movement that, having successfully delivered peace and unity in Mogadishu after nearly two decades of chaos and conflict, the humanitarian community has pulled out.
The moderates are concerned that isolation from the international community is serving to strengthen extremism and undermining access humanitarian assistance.
Sheikh Sharif said the UIC wasmore than willing to provide security for international organisations to work in Mogadishu, and had encouraged humanitarian groups to take advantage of peace in the city.
"We had started negotiations and the process was going well, but then the humanitarian community declared it was leaving the areas where the UIC was in control. We regret that because we see it as a violation of people's rights," he said.
According to Adow, the greatest challenge for the UIC is to meet basic needs. "Our biggest challenge is to go beyond peace and provide social services, to provide the basic needs, - food, medical care, shelter, education, employment.
"While we do this, we have to simultaneously show the world that we want to pacify Somalia and defend the Somali people, and establish a working relationship (with the international community),"
To date, humanitarian organisations are unsure about the implications of the takeover. According to Philippe Lazzarini, head of the UN's OCHA-Somalia office, many of the Somalis most in need are in south-central Somalia, most of which is controlled by the UIC. He said there was need, for dialogue and engagement.
"In order to get access to those in need it is imperative to engage with the authorities in control, including the UIC," he said.
Engagement was important to "build trust and to get commitment for the protection of aid workers and unimpeded access to those in need," he said.
As a "broad church," the UIC is at a critical stage in centralising itself administratively and politically.
"I think it is good for Somalis to start debating the jihadists' position and the need for the moderates," said a member of the diaspora, Awale Ali Kullane, who was in Mogadishu for an exploratory visit. Kullane, like thousands of others, is watching developments closely, eager to return home and help rebuild his country.

Original_Search_Title: Kiyonga and Gen. Aronda Brief MPs On Karamoja
Headline: Uganda: Kiyonga and Gen. Aronda Brief MPs On Karamoja
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Chief of Defence Forces, Gen. Aronda Nyakairima, yesterday flew from Karamoja to brief MPs on the defence committee on the progress of disarmament and the current situation in Karamoja.
Aronda, flanked by defence minister Crispus Kiyonga, said the government was still committed to voluntary disarmament but forceful means would continue if the Karimojong warriors refused to hand in their guns.
"We are still committed to the voluntary means of disarmament but if the Karimojong fail to bring the guns, we shall not hesitate to use force," Aronda said.
He said the insecurity was no longer limited to cattle rustling in Karamoja but had extended to neighbouring districts.
"I am told that the warriors have started doing funny things. They are burning people's sorghum and stores, they are forcing teachers and reverends to put on sukas, saying that is their tradition wear. We are not going to tolerate this."
Aronda also said three groups of forces had been created to deal with Karimojong infiltrations.
"We are creating three forces: a zonal force, a mobile force and a chopper force to protect the areas, right from Kapchwora to Kitgum. They will help us in controlling the area, even in the most remote hills."
Kiyonga told the committee that the Government was trying to improve its forces and intelligence, while continuing to engage in dialogue with the elders.
The MPs wondered why the Government was using forceful means when 80% of the population was committed to the disarmament exercise.
They also wanted the minister to explain why the Government was not using the local authorities and involving the area MPs.
In his reply, Kiyonga noted that they had recovered more guns than ever because of the new method.
He also remarked that some local MPs were not fully behind the disarmament exercise. "If they were, the issue would be finished by now," he said.

Original_Search_Title: How Safe is 'Cheap' Alcohol for the Poor?
Headline: Kenya: How Safe is 'Cheap' Alcohol for the Poor?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — I read with interest the report that MPs had rejected several budget proposals. The one that captured my attention was the refusal to increase tax on fortified wines from 45 to 65 per cent.
The debate on this proposal was reduced to a defence of the Naivasha-based Keroche Industries, which manufactures fortified wines targeting the low end of the alcohol market.
In fact, Keroche had already gone to court to complain. The Finance minister, on the same day of raising excise tax on wine, including fortified wine, had also zero-rated non-malt beer sold in kegs to augment access to a safe alcoholic drink to low income Kenyans.
The court case, which has now been overtaken by events, was a complaint about how the two acts would operate in tandem.
The thrust of the argument by Keroche was that their fortified wine products have established themselves as "cost-effective, good quality and safe bottled alcoholic drinks" serving the very market the minister was seeking to assist.
Legislators trivialised issues
Raising excise tax on the one product, fortified wine, and zero-rating another, non-malt beer, while the two serve the same low end market, is what our MPs would seem to have resolved in favour of Keroche Industries. But in their debate, they trivialised issues that are much wider.
The increase was on wines, including fortified wine. I doubt that our MPs intended to protect wine imports in general for there would be no reasonable rationale for such action. Therefore, the loss of revenue from taxing other wines is totally unjustified.
We understand our MPs merely contrived to protect a local manufacturer of fortified wine on the logic that these products have been certified by the Kenya Bureau of Standards. If this certification is understood to mean that such products are, ipso facto, safe to drink, such logic is wide off the mark.
Alcohol, especially in high concentrations, is toxic to the body and is obviously "not safe". The body breaks down consumed alcohol as quickly as possible in the liver, which takes a heavy toll in the effort.
Here, it becomes necessary to distinguish fermented products from distilled products in any determination of how "safe" these products are to the body.
Fermentation is the primary process of producing alcohol in which sugar molecules derived from a variety of sources - barley, malted barley, wheat, maize, sorghum, grapes, sugar-cane or even sugar itself - are provided as food substrate to a family of organisms called yeast.
When yeast feeds on the food substrate, it produces ethyl alcohol as a "fortune" by-product of the fermentation process. Beer, busaa and wine are fermented products.
But after fermentation, a further process of refining alcohol is possible. This, essentially, involves heating the fermented morass under controlled conditions, collecting the resultant alcoholic vapour at specified temperature, cooling and bottling the liquid alcohol.
This process is called distillation. Gin, Vodka, and chang'aa are "spirits" in reference to the vapourisation stage during distillation.
Cheap liquor or any cheap distilled product is, by definition extremely dangerous. Fortified wine is made by arresting fermentation through the addition of raw alcohol. How much is added defines the potency of the product.
Finance minister Amos Kimunya's rationale was to reduce tax on a fermented product which makes sense, but extending similar rationale to reduce tax rates on any type of liquor would operate in the opposite direction.
Distilled liquor does not render itself well to the low end of the market precisely because liquor, in contrast to fermented brews, ought to be expensive to mitigate blatant alcohol abuse.
Therefore, if MPs thought that they were helping Keroche as a local enterprise, then they must also understand that they are aiding and abetting the gross consumption of dangerous levels of potent alcohol products on the cheap.
Sustainable business model
We would argue that the Government has a duty and an obligation to raise barriers against all distilled alcohol products.
Martel Brandy, no doubt, is certified by the Kenya Bureau of Standards as indeed by many other such institutions worldwide, but that is not to mean that we should strive to offer it as cheaply as possible to our citizens. There are very good reasons why MPs are not themselves Keroche customers!
The most successful companies are those that have the best sustainable business model. It is perfectly legitimate for East African Breweries Ltd to solicit for and obtain policy decisions to augment their entry into the low-end of the market, but the production of non-malt beer is not the exclusive preserve of any one company.
EABL sells distilled products too. Parliament ought to increase excise tax on liquor based products and reduce the same on fermented products. The market would then respond accordingly and, together, we shall save lives.
At the same time, and for the same reasons, we urge Parliament to urgently review the law prohibiting traditional African brews.
We have no doubt that these brews have their rightful role to contribute in the production, distribution and supply of safe, affordable alcohol at the low end of a well-regulated market place. It would also provide a valuable income-generating activity in poor urban and rural settings.
Mr Ngugi is a biochemist and trained brewer

Original_Search_Title: A Question of Balance
Headline: Somalia: A Question of Balance
Body_Text:
Mogadishu — After more than a decade of brutal factional fighting, the road-blocks and gunmen have been cleared off the streets of the Somali capital, business is thriving and Mogadishu is being rebuilt. But strict standards of religious and behavioral discipline are being introduced, and questions are being asked about the vision of the new authority, the Union of Islamic Courts (UIC).
For now, gratitude for security and freedom of movement takes precedence for most Mogadishu residents. Others have fled to refugee camps, complaining of persecution and loss of business.
"There are two sorts of freedoms," pointed out a Mogadishu businessman. "Going about our daily lives is the most important freedom we can have now." But he said one freedom may come at the price of another. "People do not want to lose their personal freedoms."
Humanitarian prospects
The extraordinary turnaround in security means people can go about their daily life: traders can safely take home their earnings; children can go to school regularly and without fear; clinics and hospitals can concentrate on primary healthcare instead of constantly dealing with the trauma of conflict.
A recent visitor from the diaspora, who has not been to Mogadishu in six years, was astounded by the changes. "I drove through areas no one has driven in 15 years - like Bermuda [named after the Bermuda Triangle, previously one of the most dangerous areas] - without any security escort or even a gun. Five months ago this would have been unthinkable, even with a heavy security escort," he said.
Stability under the UIC has opened up new prospects for humanitarian assistance. For the first time in more than a decade, food aid has successfully been brought into the newly opened port for distribution outside the city. According to Leo van der Velden, Deputy Country Director of World Food Programme, Somalia, the new authority had "done the right things and said the right things" to encourage humanitarian access, and that good security allowed transporters to safely carry and deliver.
CARE International confirmed that a consignment of sorghum from the United States had arrived in the port in October, and was successfully handled, transported and delivered to areas outside the city - a logistical achievement impossible for more than a decade, when the port and its resources became a flashpoint for factional fighting.
Freedom of movement has also allowed access to the displaced camps in Mogadishu. For years, thousands of internally displaced people have suffered 'a forgotten tragedy' in the city. Now the displaced are also benefiting from safety and small-scale community assistance. In October, the Al Bayan court militia escorted small consignments of food to the camps, donated by the local community. "We are doing what we can," said the chairman of Al Bayan, Mohamed Ibrahim Bilal.
But despite the change, security fears resulted in the humanitarian community withdrawing from Somalia in September. The move followed the murder of a foreign cameraman and an Italian nun in Mogadishu, and the assassination attempt in Baidoa on the President of the Transitional Federal Government (TFG). Since the withdrawal, talks between the UIC and the TFG have stalled. The two sides met in Khartoum twice since the UIC victory in a reconciliation meeting sponsored by the Arab League and supported by the international community. The third meeting scheduled for late October did not take place after the two sides failed to agree on fundamental issues. Fears of regional conflict increased after the UIC declared Jihad on Ethiopia for deploying troops inside Somali territory - which Ethiopia has consistently denied.
In November, the US embassies in the region issued a warning against "reports of terrorist threats emanating from extremist elements within Somalia, which target Kenya, Ethiopia and other surrounding countries". It warned of suicide attacks, particularly in Kenya and Ethiopia - seen as regional US allies and supporters of the TFG. The US accused the UIC of harbouring alleged terrorists and extremists, while the US in turn has been accused of supporting the defeated warlords.
Establishing standards
On the streets of Mogadishu, there is much debate about 'moderates' and 'extremists'. A local journalist said his personal freedoms had changed in many ways since the takeover, "good and bad". "In some areas there is no music or cinemas, radio stations have closed down, we have no freedom to write, and they are doing public executions." He says there is a 'wait and see' atmosphere.
"When it comes to security, we feel freedom. When it comes to personal freedoms, we feel worried."
Since coming to power in June, the UIC has implemented Shari'a law, but avoided issuing official directives on details of social and religious conduct. Instead, it has used punishment and propaganda to set new standards. Public floggings were meted out for men and women accused of selling drugs, chewing khat and 'immoral behaviour'. There are public lectures and radio appeals addressing religious commitment, behaviour and morality, and the interpretation of the Koran.
But the new climate of punishment and restraint may not sit comfortably in a culture known for its personal freedoms and egalitarianism - and the UIC is wary of launching an assault against certain aspects of Somali culture.
Banning khat
The UIC's new ban on khat will prove a critical test of acceptance of the new restrictions.
During Ramadan in Mogadishu, when the sun had gone down and the last prayers said, women sold bundles of the narcotic leaf from small street stalls. Angrily, they talked about the number of times they had been stopped from selling khat. Young men push and shout around shadowy markets, complaining their cinemas and entertainments had been closed down.
Khat bans were first announced in UIC-controlled Kismayo and Jilib in southern Somalia.
The new authority is pragmatic about the popular impact of an official ban. "It is a public challenge," the Chairman of the Islamic Courts, Sheikh Sharif Sheikh Ahmed, told IRIN.
The daily importation of khat from Kenya and Ethiopia is costly, and its narcotic effects have contributed to conflict and lawlessness. It is seen as a social evil by many. But it provides countless small traders, mostly women who are heads of households, with a livelihood, and its daily use is socially and geographically widespread. No Somali government has been successful in abolishing the trade.
Dress code
There is also consternation over new standards of dress code. In UIC offices, men who dress 'western-style' are frowned upon. Visiting businessmen and women say they sometimes struggle to conform and do not understand the disapproval around details of behaviour and dress.
Change is visible. Increasingly, women in Mogadishu are adopting a full veil, more associated with Saudi Arabia and the Arab states than 'Somali-style' modesty. Over the last few years, black, brown and grey hijabs have been steadily replacing the vibrant colourful Somali scarves. The trend has accelerated since the takeover, note Mogadishu residents.
Sheikh Sharif emphasises that the increase in 'modesty' is not the result of any official declaration by the UIC. "The Islamic Courts has made no decision about [this] because Somali women dress the way it is intended in their religion. I do not wish to impose anything like that." Some of the UIC leadership says the debate over dress is provoked by pro-western critics.
Badge of loyalty
But there is much debate - in private - by Mogadishu residents as to whether people are changing their habits through religious choice, or just to protect themselves. "Women are already taking up the veil not out of choice but as a precaution, so that no-one singles them out," said one businessman. As a supporter of the UIC, he said the focus on dress and behaviour was "not a priority problem in Somalia - and shouldn't be for anyone. The UIC should be concerned with more important things."
Humour on the streets reflects the fact that the new path to power is through religion. "Everyone is becoming a sheikh these days," observed one hotelier. Red and white checked headscarves are used fairly ubiquitously by the UIC - from the leadership and the militia to their supporters. The scarves are seen as a badge of loyalty. For those who want to demonstrate strict religious commitment, musical ring tones on mobile phones have been replaced by recordings from the mosque.
Centralising policy
Some of the social confusion comes from the absence of policy. Soon after the takeover, cases were being reported of women being ordered to wear the hijab. But the UIC leadership explains these incidents as the challenge of centralisation. There are 27 branches of the courts in Mogadishu, and 11 outside the city, all used to operating autonomously. Different local courts were imposing different standards and implementing different punishments. Centralisation of the courts did not take place until late September.
Once the courts had been brought under a central authority, the UIC officially established a judicial system under Shari'a law. In October, the Supreme Court of Benadir Region was opened, and an appeals court set up. Public floggings and executions can now only proceed when approved by the Supreme Justice Committee, representing all the courts. Police stations are also being established, although not yet fully functional. Sentenced prisoners are being held in the central Mogadishu jail. The UIC also recently banned photographs and film of executions, aware of the impact on its critics and the wider world.
Balancing act
The confusion over codes of behaviour illustrates the struggle between the moderates and hardliners within the UIC. Since the takeover, there has been increased emphasis on 'jihad' and visible militarisation of society - including seminars on jihad for men and women, and training camps to unify the militia into a centralised force. Some UIC leaders see security and 'defence of the country and the religion' as the priority, and have made it the rallying call.
The hardliners - known as the Shabaab group - invest in militarisation, advocate strict religious codes and punishments, and shun contact with the non-Muslim world. They include key figures in the UIC - including Chief of Security for Mogadishu, Sheikh Abdullahi Mo'alim Ali 'Abu Utayba', who stated publicly, according to local journalists, that people who failed to pray five times a day should be shot. Prominent UIC figures, including Abu Utayba, appear in a 'jihadi video' doing the rounds in Mogadishu and the diaspora. The video glorifies Osama bin Laden, shows military training camps in Mogadishu, and calls on "any marginalised Muslim to come to Somalia". It calls Somalia "the new Afghanistan of the world". The video is not officially endorsed by the UIC, and there is no indication of who authored it. 'Jihadi' propaganda experts believe it was probably made for fund-raising in the Somali diaspora, as it is predominately in the Somali language.
"The courts never authored such a thing it is the work of our enemies," Professor Ibrahim Hassan Adow, head of the UIC Foreign Affairs department, told IRIN.
Key members of the UIC leadership are working to secure international support and assistance in rebuilding Somalia. There is consternation in the movement that, having successfully delivered peace and unity in Mogadishu after nearly two decades of chaos and conflict, the humanitarian community has pulled out. The moderates are concerned that isolation from the international community serves to strengthen extremism and undermines opportunities for humanitarian assistance.
Sheikh Sharif said the UIC could provide security for international organisations to work in Mogadishu, and had encouraged humanitarian groups to take advantage of peace in the city.
"We had started negotiations and the process was going well, but then the humanitarian community declared it was leaving the areas where the UIC was in control. We regret that because we see it as a violation of people's rights."
According to Adow, the greatest challenge for the UIC is to meet basic needs. "Our biggest challenge is to go beyond peace and provide social services, to provide the basic needs, whether it is food, medical care, shelter, education, employment. While we do this, we have to simultaneously show the world that we want to pacify Somalia and defend the Somali people, and establish a working relationship [with the international community]."
To date, humanitarian organisations are unsure about the implications of the takeover. According to Philippe Lazzarini, head of OCHA-Somalia, many of the Somalis most in need are in south-central Somalia, most of which is controlled by the UIC. He told IRIN there was need for dialogue and engagement.
"In order to get access to those in need it is imperative to engage with the authorities in control, including the UIC," he said.
Engagement was important to "build trust and to get commitment for the protection of aid workers and unimpeded access to those in need", he emphasised.
As a 'broad church', the UIC is at a critical stage in centralising itself administratively and politically.
"I think it is good for Somalis to start debating the Jihadists' position and the need for the moderates," said a member of the diaspora, Awale Ali Kullane, in Mogadishu for an exploratory visit. Kullane, like thousands of others, is watching developments closely, eager to return home and help rebuild his country.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Gasabo People Donate Frw 15 Million to Returnees
Headline: Rwanda: Gasabo People Donate Frw 15 Million to Returnees
Body_Text:
Kigali — Gasabo District authorities led by the mayor, Claudine Nyinawagaga, on Sunday October 8, donated an assortment of goods worth over Frw15 million to 327 families of Rwandan refugees who were expelled from Tanzania, now living in Mwiri village of Kageyo Sector in Kayonza District.
The donation consists of 1835 iron sheets valued at over Frw8 million, clothes valued at over Frw3 million and food items that included beans, maize, sorghum and sosoma flour for children.
Delivering the assortment of goods, Nyinawagaga acknowledged that much more was needed to meet the needs of the refugees in the camp.
"We bring you a message from fellow Rwandans from Gasabo district. This is a first step in our endeavour to help people who are in urgent need of assistance like you. Accept the little that our people have managed to put together to bring you," she said.
Nyinawagaga castigated previous regimes that were bent on keeping its people in exile. She added that the people of Gasabo will never associate themselves with politics of discrimination, and that is why they were receiving them back as their very own.
In his speech, the mayor of Kayonza District, Damas Muhororo, thanked Gasabo authorities for the gesture of love they extended to the people in Mwiri who are in need of help.
"You have practically demonstrated what it means to be a good neighbour. Coming to the rescue of a neighbour in times of difficulties should be a culture that Rwandans must embrace," he said, adding that other people should emulate Gasabo's gesture because the people in the camp need urgent support.
Mayor Muhororo urged the refugees to get acclimatised to the environment, strive to know the names of their leaders and the area in which they live as this will help them settle in their new homes.
Mwiri is home to 327 families with a total number of 641 people, all of whom are returnees from Tanzania. More 60,000 Rwandans who have been living in Tanzania for more than 20 years are expected soon after negotiations with the Rwandan government for their repatriation are complete.

Original_Search_Title: Let's Popularise Air Travel!
Headline: Botswana: Let's Popularise Air Travel!
Body_Text:
Batswana love to travel, to visit relatives in distant places or just to see previously unknown places. They appear to be born tourists, born to gallivant and explore the natural world around them and to enjoy its munificence and the hospitality of fellow-humans that populate it. What restricts Batswana in their peripatetic tendencies to the limit, so far, is obviously the resources to undertake these.
If one strayed on railway stations, bus and taxi ranks at the start of long weekends or the end of such weekends in Gaborone, Lobatse, Selebi-Phikwe and Francistown or watch by the roadside, particularly the highways, the traffic jams that occur, one cannot miss the point made. Travelling is in Batswana blood. I guess other people may claim that the same culture is to be found in their communities. Granted. But I would continue to demur because I have lived in other parts of the world and have never noticed the culture to the same extent.
I look back to the days of the foot, the sleigh, the ox-wagon, donkey-back or donkey cart and the bicycle and I see Batswana's undying habit of enjoying their peregrinations. My old man, who seemed to have had relatives all over the show - Sefophe, Tobane, Serowe Maunatala, Kgagodi, Mawaza (Zimbabwe), and Blouberg/Bochem (Transvaal) - once he had finished thrashing sorghum and happened not to be on contract to the mines in South Africa, he would be off to one or some of these relatives, go ba lekola. It was something to engage my juvenile curiosity! He was not an exception. His relatives in the village were just the same, travelling to one place or other when they had time, which they had at every off-season.
With the advent of the locomotive and the motor vehicle, travel opportunities have grown and we have become tourists-plus. We look destined to boost further our own burgeoning tourism industry within our country, incomes permitting in the near future. If one visited a place and came back to tell another Motswana one had visited a place and enjoyed it, her/his appetite is immediately whetted for the same place! Regrettably, the modes of transport available are inadequate and un-diversified. There is too much congestion on the roads leading to fatal accidents and long hours of travel. Road transport at the moment dominates other modes of transportation. It is not clear why the railways are not giving stiff competition to road transport for long distance travel.
The transportation system we learn is a type of business that will not be cost-effective if it conveys goods and people to a destination without bringing any freight or passengers on return. Obviously that must remain the big problem for freight transport services; but why should it be the same for train passenger services? Trains should be assured of return passengers at all times.
At this juncture air travel appears to be reserved for a certain class of Batswana, largely due to its cost. I do not see why the cost of air travel could not be reduced to make it attractive and affordable to an ordinary Motswana. In this day and age of the internet, the cell-phone, the audio-visual conferencing and the supersonic jet, I see no sense in continuing to relegate, confine and maintain transport at the donkey-back mode level. The powers-that-be should begin to popularise air travel.
It can be done if internal air fares are slashed - if senior citizens and children are offered special fares. This is a category of citizenry which could be favoured by air travel but cannot always afford it, either because the skimpy Tandabala pension is impossible to accumulate to the level of an air ticket fare or even where mdala owns a vehicle, he/she may not have strength or the eyesight to drive it. That is why the alternative convenient travel would be by air for the oldies!
What the air companies will lose by sacrificing higher prices, they will definitely recover by passenger volume conveyed, which is likely to increase manifold with the lowering of the air fares. Senior citizens will find flying more convenient to their physical endurance of distant travel. Instead of enduring the rattling, shaking and occasionally premature fracture of their brittle bones, they can be flown safely and in style, all expenses paid by doting children or grandchildren to distant places and relatives. Moreover, the senior citizens would enjoy the pampering by the flight crews who are trained to help any passenger who need help unlike the bus/combi conductors who shove and shout at passengers irrespective of their age or physical condition.
Recently, I flew with a senior citizen, more senior than myself from Francistown and was impressed by the attention she got from air hostesses. I had watched this lady in the departure lounge, unescorted, walking-stick in hand and wondered whether she was not lost. She appeared to be unacquainted with the processes of going through the customs and immigration formalities and I had to play a minor role to help. Had I not been around anybody would have played the role or the immigration officers themselves. When we walked to board the flight I followed behind to assist if she faltered in her steps but taking care not to be forward when she did not need my forwardness. At the steps boarding the aircraft, an air hostess was waiting to take her by hand and lead her to her seat. Absolute delight! I do not know whether this kind of quality service is to be found at the shout-and-shove bus and combi ranks?
Air travel should be a treat to senior citizens. It was not available in their prime of life and there is no reason why they should not enjoy it now, even at the sunset of their lives. After all, they contributed to the current developments; and they should not be denied the glitter we have accumulated since independence.
Diversifying transportation , one of the fastest growing service industries in the country will enable other potential diversifiers to piggy-back on this form of diversification. Popularising air transport should also help promote punctuality which is an important element towards enhancing the much-desired productivity which the nation is still very far from achieving.

Original_Search_Title: Karamoja Not a Wild Jungle
Headline: Uganda: Karamoja Not a Wild Jungle
Body_Text:
When I informed friends and family members that I would go to Moroto, most of them got concerned and said "make sure you miss the bullets and arrows."
From the window of a plane, misty clouds enshrouded the towering mountain and hills of Moroto. They were edged below by untapped light-green expanses of land punctuated with shrubs and scattered trees. There was also an occasional introduction of a lone, darker and clearer space, suggesting human settlement-the manyatas (Karimojong homesteads).
Sometimes, there were deep cuts into the land that formed patterns like a child had playfully doodled the ground using a sharp stick, which cut through it. But at a closer look revealed that these were dry riverbeds. The one hour and 10 minutes flight saw us land at Moroto air strip, a levelled murrum stretch of land with almost no building except for what looked like a small office and a pit latrine that came to one's view.
Cattle, sheep and goats
Cattle, sheep and goats grazed nearby oblivious of the plane. Some of the boys and men herding the animals came close as if to welcome us. With brilliant smiles, nodding heads and hands, they wore clothes or at least had something to cover themselves except for a few children who were naked.
This was contrary to the impression I had of the Karimojong; I expected nothing short of a scene like in the The Gods Must be Crazy movie.
Here, no Karimojong scampered for their dear lives on seeing us and neither was a whole village fascinated at the sight of a plane. Although there was evidence of hot weather, Moroto didn't appear like a desert as many people who have never been there imagine. And contrary to my P4 Social Studies where we were told that the Karimojong were basically cattle keepers who only ate milk, meat and blood, I saw fields of sorghum, maize and sunflower.
But unlike the road from Entebbe to Kampala where there are several billboards welcoming you to Uganda or wishing you a safe journey out, there was nothing of the sort from the airstrip to Moroto town. It was only in the town that an MTN and Cocacola billboards are placed. Hmm! So the Karimojong drank Coke and used mobile phones.
Rings of beads in various colours rested lazily on the girls' and women's waists, necks and wrists. Most men wrapped themselves in thick and brightcoloured sheets like the Masai of Kenya and held portable stools. Some of them may have learnt early to take exception to newcomers as they declined visitors to take their pictures or engage them in conversations unless they were given money as motivation.
Moroto town situated on the slopes of the towering dark blue- green Mt. Moroto, had an Independence Monument based at its district headquarters, something I had never seen in the other districts I had been to.
When I informed friends and family members that I would go to Moroto, most of them got concerned and said "make sure you miss the bullets and arrows." They added " Don't over interview those people, when they get annoyed, they can shoot."
To them, it seemed I was going into a wild jungle. I partly felt the same way because I heard that the place was very remote, hot and dry like a desert. Even in my childhood, naked children used to be teased by being called Karimojong. Besides a government minister for Karamoja Affairs Mr Tom Butime had resigned, a move that almost made the area seem worse than it had been painted. You add to this the street kids in Kampala who storm the Shoprite-Clock tower roundabout to beg from taxi passengers during traffic jams. The kids have always been refered to as abakoromojo (the Karimojong).
The common saying "we shall not wait for Karamoja to develop" didn't help either. Yet in reality, the physical appearance of the town, the bustle of life and the calm atmosphere there was a far cry from the desert, insecurity, backwardness and all I had heard about Moroto. Could it be that day the Karimojong decided to put up a show because they were hosting guests and were impatiently waiting for them to leave so that they became their true selves again?
But think of it. I went to attend a conference on Education in Karamoja and the official launch of the United Nations Girl Education Initiative (UNGEI), an event fostered by UNICEF and other partners, recently.
From various sources, I heard tales that residents didn't like pens. That to them, a pen was reminiscent of colonial masters who used it to implement their interests. And it said that in a ritual, the Karimojong elders did bury a pen as a protest against foreigners. But it is also said that in a bid to change this attitude, a ceremony was later organised to exhume the pen.
I learnt also that a certain dignitary from Karamoja only went to school after he tested sugar. When he inquired from the person who gave it to him where he got the sugar, the dignitary was told that he had to get it after going to school. Such tales, true or false, were evident enough to reveal the indifference the Karimojong had about education.
Little wonder therefore that the conference-raised issues like long distances to school, female genital mutilation, early marriages, preferring cattle keeping to education as obstacles to achieving education in the region. The conference stressed the urgency and need for communities to take their children to school.
Embrace education
Speakers who addressed the huge crowd during at the launch in both English and Akarimojong urged residents to embrace education.
In Karamoja, many people have not embraced formal education. It is common to find pupils who are far older than their classmates. For instance, in Nataikwae Primary School, the headmistress Ms Rose Illukor said that some students had joined school through Alternative Basic Education for Karamoja (ABEK). Through ABEK, the Karimojong are taught informally before they can join a formal school.
With the excitement, a series of music, dance and drama the town organised for the girl-child education campaign, it was soon evening and time to return. Near the airstrip, the animals were still grazing and nothing seemed to have changed. But there was hope and conviction that this time round, the Karimojong had taken the education message seriously and would pursue it with utter zeal.

Original_Search_Title: Assistance Extended to Flood Victims in Tessenei Town
Headline: Eritrea: Assistance Extended to Flood Victims in Tessenei Town
Body_Text:
Tessenei — The inhabitants of Tessenei sub-zone extended of 170,000 Nakfa and 20 quintals of sorghum in assistance to Tessenei town flood victims.
Each of the 172 families was given 600 to 1,000 Nakfa depending on the number of their family members.
Speaking on the occasion, the Administrator of the sub-zone, Mr. Tekleberhan Gebrewold, pointed out that the victims have been settled along the shorelines of rivers and that decision has been made to resettle them in a safer place with the needed support to be extended.
Moreover, one hectare of land has been given to each of them by the Government. The victims commended the support extended to them by the people and government.
It is to be recalled that the Gash-Barka administration has distributed a 3-month worth of grain, blankets, kitchen utensils and tents to the flood victims.

Original_Search_Title: Transit Cargo to Southern Sudan Rises By 25,000 Tons
Headline: Kenya: Transit Cargo to Southern Sudan Rises By 25,000 Tons
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Transit traffic from Mombasa to Southern Sudan has increased by over 25,000 tonnes in the first six months of this year, a report by the Kenya Ports Authority (KPA) says.
A total of 77,684 tonnes comprising imports and exports was handled at the port in the six months compared with 51,407 tonnes handled in the same period last year.
Imports grew from 49,583 tonnes in 2005 to 70,701 tonnes while exports grew from 1,824 tonnes to 6,983 tonnes.
The main imports to Southern Sudan were: Grain sorghum, 22,799 tonnes (31.3 per cent); gas oil, 10,853 tonnes (14.9 per cent); assembled motor vehicles, 4,240 tonnes (5.8 per cent); cereal flours, 3,576 tonnes (4.9 per cent); and jet fuel, 3,116 tonnes (4.3 per cent). The region exports mainly wood and wood products.
Total transit traffic through Mombasa improved from 2,890,597 tonnes in 2004 to 3,535,714 tonnes last year.
Uganda led with 75.8 per cent of the total traffic followed by Tanzania with 8 per cent. Rwanda recorded 6.2 per cent, Sudan 4.2 per cent, Congo 3.8 per cent, Somalia 1.2 per cent and Burundi 0.8 per cent while others accounted for 0.1 per cent.
KPA says the port of Mombasa has the capacity to handle up to 22 million tonnes of cargo per year.
"Only about 60 per cent of the capacity is in use. We have asked the government to provide a road link between Kenya and Ethiopia through Moyale and Isiolo to help increase our business. We have hosted Ethiopian trade delegations in the recent past with very fruitful discussions," said KPA senior public relations officer Bernard Osero.
The Authority will set up special desks at the port to serve transit clients, including those from Ethiopia, he said.
The Authority's corporate services manager, Gichiri Ndua, said the improved performance was due to marketing of the port.
"The combined performance of transit markets mirrors marketing efforts that the authority instituted," he said.
He said the transit market registered an average growth rate of over 10 per cent for each individual country last tear.
"The performance is also attributable to the improved economic performance in the port hinterland countries," he observed.
The total average annual throughput for last year is 13 million tonnes. Container boxes amounted to 430,000 teu, or 4.3 million tonnes, while conventional cargo was 1.2 million tonnes. Liquids and dry bulk handled amounted to five million tonnes and 2.5 million tonnes respectively..

Original_Search_Title: Goods Imported, Exported Through Ethio-Sudan Border
Headline: Ethiopia: Goods Imported, Exported Through Ethio-Sudan Border
Body_Text:
Gondar — Over three million birr worth goods have been imported and exported in the recently launched Ethio-Sudan border trade, the Northwest Customs Coordination Office said.
Office representative Tesfaye Haile told ENA  that the goods have been imported from and exported to Gelabat and Menza towns of the Sudan through Metema and Mankush in the current year.
Tesfaye said more than 220 Ethiopian businesspersons have been engaged in the business activity in accordance with the economic and trade agreement concluded between the two countries.
He said the businesspersons have exported goods worth over 2.6 million birr while goods valued at over half a million birr have been imported.
The representative said the business persons have supplied beans, sorghum, honey, vegetable, garlic and butter to the Sudan while onions, salt, soap, matches and perfume, among others, were imported.
Tesfaye said border trade exchange strengthens the existing relationship between the peoples of the two countries while it also contributes a significant share in economic development.

Original_Search_Title: LRA Abductees Cry for Home as Rebel Leader Agrees to Release Children
Headline: Uganda: LRA Child Abductees Cry for Home
Body_Text:
Nabanga — With tears in her eyes, the 18-year-old girl (name withheld) stood before a small group of rebels and aid workers, pleading: "We want to go back home, to be reunited with our families and go to school."
She was part of a group of more than 100 children paraded by the rebel Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) to officials of the United Nations Children's Fund (Unicef) in a camp near the border between Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) on Sunday.
The rebels, led by Capt Sunday Ochaya, inhibited the children from talking openly. "I am from Gulu [northern Uganda] and have been here for two years. I want to go back home," the girl added, as more tears rolled down her scarred face.
Among the children were 18 boys aged from eight years, 20 mothers carrying babies aged from two months to two-and-a-half years and more than 60 girls aged 12 and over. Most were dressed in rags and begged for medical help.
"More than 500 boys and girls from various places in northern Uganda and southern Sudan are still in our camps," said Ochaya, who is also the coordinator between the LRA and the southern Sudan government. "They will be brought later, [because] they will have to walk for four hours."
A few metres away, a 16-year-old boy (name withheld) stood quietly in army trousers and a dirty white shirt. "I was abducted six years ago from Kitgum [northern Uganda]. But I got tired after the grass cut my feet and I could not walk so other boys carried me through the bush. I saw six men who failed to walk. They were chopped to death."
He was hesitant to discuss the details under the watchful eye of the rebels. "One of the commanders was my uncle. We walked until we came to a river where we stayed for seven days, eating only boiled sorghum. Then we were led to a place surrounded by mountains. Eventually a group came from Sudan and led us to a town.
"Here we were taken by car across a bridge to a place where we walked to where we are now. I have since been [a soldier]," he added, just before the interview was cut short by one LRA officer, who ordered him to stop talking to strangers.
As the meeting ended and the cars carrying the Unicef team, reporters and mediators in the peace talks revved up their engines, some of the children wept uncontrollably. "We wish our leaders could sign a peace agreement, so the suffering ends," one of them sobbed.
Una McCauley, Unicef's child protection officer in south Sudan told the children: "We are ready to support you to go back to school when you rejoin your families in southern Sudan or northern Uganda."
Kony to release children
The meeting between the children and Unicef officials was part of an ongoing peace process that started on 14 July in Juba, southern Sudan. It followed a decision by the Ugandan government to begin peace negotiations with the rebels.
Several meetings are being held in jungle locations between the rebels, Sudanese officials, led by the Vice-President of the south, Riek Machar, and various Ugandan groups, including religious leaders, chiefs and government officials.
"I have many children [in camps] who were abducted from both northern Uganda and southern Sudan," Joseph Kony, the LRA leader, told leaders from his Acholi region in a separate meeting on Sunday. "We are ready to release them so they can continue with their studies, with their families. We are sorry for what happened [to them]."
He added: "Our people in northern Uganda have suffered for a long time, especially those in IDP [internally displaced persons'] camps. Now we want peace. If we don't change the situation, our brothers in the camps will continue to be killed by HIV/AIDS." The prevalence rate for the disease in the districts of Kitgum and Gulu is more than nine percent, according to health ministry data from a recent nationwide study - three percent higher than the national average.
The leaders, including religious leaders and elders, travelled to meet Kony on Saturday night and stayed on until Sunday at a hideout near the DRC-Sudan border. They were led by the Archbishop of Gulu diocese, John Baptist Odama, and the Acholi paramount chief, Rwot Achana II.
Kony was scheduled to meet Machar but sent his 14-year-old son instead. But Machar remained upbeat, saying he would probably meet the rebel leader on Monday. Another group of leaders from northern Uganda, led by the Gulu district chairman, Norbert Mao, is also due to meet the rebel leader.
On Saturday, Machar, who is the chief mediator in the Ugandan government-LRA peace talks, had met an LRA team led by Kony's deputy, Vincent Otti. "I wish to stay here until these people sort out all their things," the Vice-President said.
The LRA has fought for 21 years, claiming it wants to replace the Ugandan government with one based on the Ten Commandments. Almost 2 million people have been displaced in what has been called one of the world's worst crises. More than 25,000 children have been abducted either to fight for the rebels or to become sex slaves for the commanders.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Nelson Mandela Village a Brigde - SA Envoy
Headline: South Africa: Nelson Mandela Village a Brigde - SA Envoy
Body_Text:
Kigali — The Peace Village that was named after Nelson Mandela is a bridge to enhance co-operation between the people of Rwanda and South Africa. The observation was made Friday, June 23, by the South African Ambassador Ezra Sigwela...at the Peace Village in Ntarama of Bugesera District. The envoy said he was impressed that the village was named to depict democracy in South Africa and Africa as a continent. "It's impressive to have this village in the names of Nelson Mandela because he is more than a politician; he is a father of democracy," Ambassador Sigwela said and appealed to the residents of the village to maintain cordial relations with their neighbours.
The envoy presented gifts to members of Association de Femme Rwandaise (ASOFERWA), an association of widows benefiting over 380 families in the area. The gifts included balls that were donated to the Mandela Primary School and hair saloon equipment worth Frw 600.000.
"It's not a big donation, but just like what good neighbours do when they even decide to share tea, this is what we have done; to Africans, the spirit of receiving is our strength," he added.
The Embassy rendered support in the construction of a clinic and primary school in the area and also provided a grinding machine to the association.
"I love seeing good maize and sorghum growing and seeing animals grazing because I used to graze after school hours," he said.
The area residents promised to work hard to develop small business in a bid to improve their livelihood.
Later while visiting the Ntarama Genocide Site, the Ambassador sneered at what could have motivated the militia to kill fellow Rwandans during the 1994 genocide.
"Something else motivated the killing; it is not the question of difference (ethnic) and this should be established," he said and asked: "Which soldier would kill or fight with a baby or helpless women and still feel proud as a soldier?"
Comparing the apartheid in South Africa to the genocide in Rwanda, Ambassador Sigwela said: "What happened in South Africa is not a miracle, but how Rwandan leaders are preaching against what happened in 1994 is a miracle."

Original_Search_Title: Firms Feel Pinch As Consumers Get Price-Sensitive
Headline: Zimbabwe: Firms Feel Pinch As Consumers Get Price-Sensitive
Body_Text:
LOCAL companies are experiencing a decline in volumes as consumers become more price-sensitive, a set of financial results released recently has revealed.
Numerous firms reported that consumers are now cutting on spending as the country's galloping inflation continues to erode disposable incomes.
Zimbabwe's inflation recently surged to 1 042%, further straining consumers.
Beverages manufacturer, Delta Corporation, said its volumes for the year ended March 31 slumped 15% due to consumer resistance to price increases.
Robbie Mupawose, Delta chairman, said the decline in consumers' disposable incomes had resulted in reduced demand for the company's products.
"Volumes for the year are 15% below those of the previous year reflecting relatively soft demand for beverages as a result of the tightening of disposable income," Mupawose said.
"With rising inflation, real incomes will not keep pace," he said.
Mupawose said the decline in the volumes also reflected sensitivity to pricing in sorghum beer as the cost of grains rose considerably faster than inflation and a most disappointing occurrence where the market went without carbonated soft drinks.
Despite having posted a pre-tax profit of $475,8 billion in the half-year ended March 31, Red Star Holdings (Red Star) is also feeling the pinch as it reported that consumers had become more price-sensitive.
The retail firm, which was recently listed on the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange, said this could be due to erosion of consumers' disposable incomes by the country's galloping inflation.
"Consumers have become more sensitive to price changes, probably due to declining real disposable incomes," Patison Sithole, the chairman of Red Star, said in a statement accompanying the company's results for the half year to March.
Sithole added that the situation had been worsened by suppliers who have reduced credit payment periods.
"On the other hand, suppliers reduced their credit with the group," he said.
He added: "This demonstrates how the business environment had become challenging in the period under review."
Sithole said industry capacity utilisation remained low at an estimated 40% due to fuel problems, erratic input supplies and working capital constraints.
Red Star is the holding company of Advance and Red Star Wholesalers.
Supermarket group, OK Zimbabwe, said operations were restrained throughout the trading period.
"Disposable incomes were eroded by hyperinflation," OK chairman Eric Kahari said in the group's results for the year ended March.
Kahari said business activity was generally subdued throughout the period under review.
OK registered an operating profit of $890,3 billion, an increase of 1 270% from $65 billion made in the previous year.
Newsprint and stationery manufacturer, Art Corporation, also reported lower volumes due to a decline in the demand in the same period.
The group's sales volumes dropped significantly by an average 30% throughout the business units.
"The loss in local market sales volumes, particularly in the second quarter, was due to a decline in aggregate demand," the board said in a notice signed by company secretary Franklin Mukarakate.
"Post March trading conditions in the local market have continued to deteriorate due to pressure on disposable incomes," Mukarakate said.

Original_Search_Title: Beer Demand Escalates
Headline: Uganda: Beer Demand Escalates
Body_Text:
Kampala — Demand for beers has risen by 20% over the last 18 months due to the introduction of value for money beer brands made from locally grown barley and sorghum.
The managing director of Nile Breweries, Nick Jenkinson, said like their competitors (Uganda Breweries), they were running at full capacity but had unbelievably realised an acute shortage countrywide.
"The scale of the shortage cannot readily be established. We are doing everything possible to increase production capacity," Jenkinson said, adding that Nile Breweries is set to inject $4m(sh7.4b) into expansion.
He said since introduction of local raw materials to make Eagle Lager (Nile Breweries) and Senator (Uganda Breweries), production volumes had increased from nine million cases in 2003 to over 12 million this financial year.

Original_Search_Title: Funding Shortfall Threatens Millions With Hunger, UN Food Chief Warns
Headline: Sudan: Funding Shortfall Threatens Millions With Hunger, UN Food Chief Warns
Body_Text:
A shortage of steady and timely funding for food aid to Sudan risks increasing malnutrition among millions of people and threatens pacts to end conflicts in the South and in the western region of Darfur, the head of the United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) warned today.
"We aim to help 6.1 million people in Sudan in 2006, but we are falling short at times and in places," WFP Executive Director James Morris said at the end of a five-day visit to the capital, Khartoum, the South, the East and Darfur.
"We must reach people in Darfur cut off from WFP rations this year because of insecurity. We must give everyone the minimum that they need. Not to do so is a terrible betrayal," he told a news conference in Khartoum of his agency's largest operation in the world. As of 5 June, the $746-million relief drive was less than 50 per cent funded.
He noted that he met people torn from their homes by violence in three different parts of Africa's largest country: families in Darfur driven from their homes by violence since 2003; those displaced in the East the 1990s and as recently as 2000; and - "a huge cause for hope" - returnees to the South who are now flooding home after a peace accord last year ended 21 years of civil war.
"They fled different conflicts, at different times, in different areas," Mr. Morris said. "But they all desperately need our help so that, as the returnees to the South are proving, one day they will finally get home and start rebuilding their lives. With our help, they can support themselves and build for the future," he added.
In May, a critical shortage of funds forced WFP to take what Mr. Morris described at the time as "one of the hardest decisions of my life," cutting rations to half in terms of energy content for 2 million people in Darfur and the East.
A series of new donations, including 20,000 metric tons of sorghum from the Government of Sudan, has allowed rations to be increased to 85 per cent of the full amount from June to September in the Darfur states, but the earliest full rations can be restored is October.
"We are extremely grateful to those donors who stepped in at the last minute," Mr. Morris said. "But in Sudan it takes up to six months for a confirmed contribution to materialize as food in the hands of people in Sudan. We know this, and we must all plan ahead for it to end these breaks in the supply."
To ensure a consistent supply of food for full rations throughout the year, the operation should have received nearly $600 million, or 80 per cent of the total required by the start of April. Mr. Morris said it was critical that contributions, preferably cash, be furnished now to ensure a consistent supply of food to the end of 2006, "and more importantly, to avoid a similar problem occurring in the early months of 2007."
Yesterday, Mr. Morris visited the town of Habilah in Darfur where WFP feeds 11,500 displaced people and 5,500 original residents affected by the conflict. In all scores of thousands of people have died in Darfur and more than 2 million people displaced by three years of fighting between the Government, pro-Government militias and rebels.
On Sunday, he visited a returnee village near the town of Rumbek, southern Sudan, where he met 'One O'clock,' a three-year-old boy named after the time when he was found two years ago by a woman as he hid in tall grass following an attack by gunmen on returnees. His parents were never seen again so Noura Sawa Abu rescued him and raised him as one of her own.

Original_Search_Title: Delta, Ok Find Profit in Lower End of the Market
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta, Ok Find Profit in Lower End of the Market
Body_Text:
Harare — THE country's largest brewer and soft drinks bottler Delta Beverages, encouraged by a better than expected performance of its budget beer Eagle, is launching a new carbonated soft drink targeted at the lower end of the market.
And OK Zimbabwe, seeing even higher inflation and weaker consumer spending ahead, is also expanding its range of products for lower income earners.
Delta's new brand of soft drink will be launched in July, while the company will continue a drive to get more drinkers on Eagle Lager, hoping to boost the malt beer's contribution to lager volumes to 15 percent from the current 10 percent.
"Coca Cola has agreed to allow us to launch a more affordable brand of soft drink at much lower foreign currency cost than the current carbonated soft drink," Delta Corporation chief executive officer Joe Mtizwa said.
Delta plans to use the 750ml bottle -- the quart -- as the growth driver for Eagle. The beer had appeared to struggle after launch, but Delta pushed 30 000 hectolitres of the beer in April alone. Eagle took 2-3 percentage points off other lagers, especially Black Label, officials said.
Overall, lagers make up 22 percent of beverage volumes, but they are dwarfed by sorghum beer -- Chibuku -- which accounted for 58.5 percent of volumes in the year to March, up from 53 percent the previous year.
Aggressive pricing as a strategy to drive earnings is out of fashion. In comes a new strategy of offering poor people just what they need, which is an affordable product. For OK, this means stripping goods of the fancy packaging and branding that add big and unnecessary mark-ups on goods, and marketing them under brands such as OK's Pot o' Gold and Bon Marche's Premier Choice. In-house brands contribute seven percent to OK's turnover.
"People are looking for the cheapest possible alternative. Value brands for the lower end of the market have proven to be very profitable for the company," said Willard Zireva, OK Zimbabwe CEO.
"Consumption will obviously continue to decline. Our people are getting poorer because salary increments are always lagging behind inflation."
Both companies expect a spike in sales this month as civil servants spend more after receiving salary hikes as high as 300 percent, but Zireva says that this spending is unlikely to be kept up for too long.
"The effect of the civil servant salary adjustments will filter through this month, but inflation will soon catch up with that," Zireva said.
The OK boss also sees government giving out more tax concessions in the form of further changes to income tax bands before the end of the 2006 budget year, and says it is unlikely that inflation, at 1043 percent in April, will slow down as quickly as forecast by the central bank.
But while admitting that the salary hikes will hurt the economy, retail businesses are not complaining.
Said Delta's Mtizwa: "Although the civil service salary hikes are inflationary, we are also happy that the increments will have a positive impact on beverage volumes."

Original_Search_Title: Widows Fight Poverty
Headline: Rwanda: Widows Fight Poverty
Body_Text:
Kigali — Widows of the 1994 Genocide grouped in Association Dushigikirane, based in Masoro Sector in Rulindo District, have been hailed for engaging in developmental activities.
In an exclusive interview last week, the association president Constance Bangire, said, "Before, we had numerous problems including malnutrition. Our children were developing kwashiorkor and widows suffered due to poverty".
Consequently, the members set up an income - generating project of livestock rearing to produce milk for sale.
"The founding members began contributing Frw 20cents as early as 1994 and by 2002, this had accumulated to more than Two million francs and with financial assistance from our partners, we were able to buy 10 exotic breeds." Though 4 of the cows died immediately, the association now boasts of 22 herds of cattle capable of producing more than 40 litres daily.
So far, 16 associations, all affiliated to Dushigikirane, have been formed and deal in different activities such as cattle rearing, handicrafts, sewing, tailoring, bakery, fishery, beekeeping, growing of sugar cane, coffee, pineapples and tomato growing.
The association that began in 1994 has also managed to set up a small scale scheme manufacturing wine from sorghum. According to Bangire, the association now has 516 widows, 16 widowers, 309 self-reliant orphans and other 1,000 vulnerable orphans.
She further revealed that 723 of the orphans have been facilitated with at least one goat.

Original_Search_Title: Delta, Ariston Marriage Bears Fruit
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta, Ariston Marriage Bears Fruit
Body_Text:
Harare — THIS week you will allow me to stray a little and mourn in deepest sorrow the passing away of nearly 40 innocent souls in separate but gruesome road accidents that befell Zimbabwe in the last week.
It was certainly one of the bloodiest weeks on our roads in recent years.
