Whether the road bloodbaths were a result of negligence on the part of public transport drivers (whose rogue antics on the highway are well documented) or it was God's will is another matter altogether. But today Zimbabwe mourns in empathy with relatives of those who passed away. In their moments of longitude and emptiness we are in this together. May the souls of all our departed friends rest in eternal peace in the full knowledge that with the grace of the Almighty, we shall be reunited one day. Drivers on the roads you have done enough harm to our wrenched souls. Zimbabwe and its citizens deserve better treatment for we have treated you with kid gloves because we love you and we love our lives. Please protect the travelling public through improved care on the roads. Life is a gift of all time that cannot be replaced or bought with money. Delta-Ariston marriage pays off The beverage producer this week released an exciting set of figures for the year to March 2006, spurred by Delta's association with tea and coffee grower Ariston Ltd.
Last year, Delta acquired 53 percent of Ariston to become the controlling shareholder. Delta reported a sharp increase in net profit to $2,8 trillion in line with average inflation for the period at 427 percent. This translated into EPS of $2 434,70. The group says it will splash $325 billion in dividend payments, representing 225 percent growth on prior year. The final dividend per share stood at $500. Last year Delta pumped in over $615 billion (outside the Ariston acquisition) into maintaining and expanding current operations and managed to maintain a positive cash flow of $155 billion. But volumes were 15 percent down on the previous year due to softening demand for beverages, given weakening disposable incomes. High inflation also chipped in with a significant chunk of misery, as pricing of sorghum beer and the cost of grains rose faste r than inflation plus the disappearance of carbonated soft drinks from the market for almost three weeks. Demand for Ariston products remained strong with production and price levels having improved considerably although exchange rate distortions hamstrung profitability. Delta said: "From a group point of view this acquisition has yielded a steady flow of foreign currency, which was largely applied to clearing outstanding creditors." Gross margins improved marginally on previous year but overheads increased ahead of inflation due to higher wages and impact of foreign currency on fuel and spare parts. Going forward, Delta will seek to contain costs, enhance cash flow generation "and a review of the product range with the intention of introducing a lower cost and more affordable range of carbonated soft drinks". But what the Delta-Ariston relation has done is to improve Delta's capacity through a constant supply line of raw materials as opposed to imports, which require vast s ums of the elusive foreign currency. FEEDBACK:jgogo@zimpapers.co.zw

Original_Search_Title: Liquor Makers, Distributors Urged to Renew Licences
Headline: South Africa: Liquor Makers, Distributors Urged to Renew Licences
Body_Text:
The Department of Trade and Industry has urged macro manufacturers and distributors of liquor to apply for new licences in accordance with the law.
This follows the passing of the Liquor Act 59 of 2003 almost two years ago, repealing the 1989 Liquor Act.
All persons whose provincially issued licenses were valid and involved in this trade were required to apply for conversion of the licenses to national registrations by 11 November 2004.
The department says the licenses of those persons who did not apply for a conversion have expired.
"Consequently they need to make new applications with the National Liquor Authority (NLA), which is housed within the Consumer and Corporate Regulation Division of the dti," it said.
Meanwhile, the distinction between macro and micro manufacturing is made on the basis of the annual liquor production volume of any entity.
Entities whose annual liquor production exceeds the set volume threshold are regarded as macro manufacturers of liquor and are regulated through a national system of registration by the department.
For example, beer is set at a volume threshold of 100 million litres, wine is set at a volume threshold of 4 million litres, sorghum beer is 50 million litres and spirits is 2 million litres.
For more information, contact the dti Consumer Contact Centre at 0861 843 384.

Original_Search_Title: Disarmament: 15,000 Herders Flee to Uganda
Headline: Kenya: Disarmament: 15,000 Herders Flee to Uganda
Body_Text:
Nairobi — About 15,000 Kenyan herdsmen have fled to Uganda fearing a military operation in West Pokot District, a church minister has claimed.
Rev Abrahim Domongile of Kasei African Inland Church said yesterday the herdsmen have crossed to Eastern Uganda with more than 30,000 head of cattle in the past one week.
He urged President Kibaki to call off the operation and seek an alternative solution instead.
"Failure by the Government to protect the community had prompted them to acquire firearms, there is no need to disarm them, who will protect them against raids from neighbouring communities," said Domongile.
"Possibilities of them being raided by Pians, Matheniko, Rupa or Tepes, the Karamoja sub-tribes is imminent, as their security is not guaranteed," Domongile told The Sunday Standard in Kapenguria on arrival from Amdat after visiting the herders.
He said some of the herders were going without food, while others relied on food aids from relatives.
Domongile said the herders were from Kasei, Kacheliba and Alale divisional headquarters, West of Kapenguria. Efforts to contact local DC Stephen Ikua were fruitless as he was said to have travelled to Alale on the Kenya-Uganda border.
Intelligence reports from the district confirmed the migration of the herders but could not give the figure.
"Food production is likely to be affected as most homes have been deserted," said Domongile.
Tarket, Kapturomwo and Korokowo areas in Kasei and Kalapat, Lorusuk and Sasaki regions of Alale are viable for production of maize, sorghum and finger millets. The herders have settled in Nakapirpirit and Moroto districts.

Original_Search_Title: Matsiloje Lights Up With Spiritually Healed
Headline: Botswana: Matsiloje Lights Up With Spiritually Healed
Body_Text:
When members of the popular Zion Christian Church (ZCC) and International Pentecostal Church (IPC) visited their South African holy shrines of Moria and Silo respectively, thousands of SHC pilgrims converged at Matsiloje. It was obvious that something extra-ordinary was taking place as evident from the large numbers of tents of all sizes that were pitched in most of the homes and the white and dark blue uniform brightening up the village.
The leader of the SHC Bishop Joseph Gotlaadirwang Motswasele, revealed that his late father Bishop Jacob Mokaleng Motswasele started the church in 1952. The 51-year-old Motswasele revealed that his father who was a prophet was initially a Methodist church member but in 1923, the holy spirits started tormenting him. "My father was born prophet but by then he hadn't realised it. He continued praying and started not only healing his church members but everyone who sought his help," revealed Motswasele who is also a prophet. The Thabancho-born founder of SHC built the first church at Matsiloje in 1955. In 1968, he built another church in Gaborone. "Today we boast of more than 35 church buildings in Botswana. We have more than 15,000 members including those from neighbouring Namibia and South Africa. We have a church building in Windhoek but we still don't have a church building in South Africa," the soft-spoken Motswasele disclosed.
The founder of the church died on January 1, 19 80. The late prophet's successor was his elder son, Bishop Israel Motswasele who passed away on March 1, 1999. The current bishop was quick to explain that his late brother Israel unlike him was not a prophet. On why he succeeded his late brother Israel, he preferred the answer to be given by one of his pastors. Pastor Patrick Maake from Gantsi attributes Motswasele's ascendancy to church leadership to the fact that Motswasele is a prophet and their church as a healing church so they saw it fit to appoint him as leader. "The church members chose him as he had grown up in the church and was a natural prophet like his late father. Apart from that, he has every quality to lead the church," said Maake. Like his late father, the holy spirits started tormenting him when he was a seven-year-old boy and he was subsequently blessed in 1972 when he was doing Form 3 at Kanye. That was when he became a prophet. Motswasele also revealed that his church is independent and has bishops, pas tors and marriage officers.
They do all this work voluntarily. Motswasele disclosed that SHC heeds the government's plea about social ills such as passion killings and the HIV/AIDS scourge. The church, which boasts of a demographically diverse membership are praying to God for a cure to be found and the end of social immorality. There is Sunday school which comprises the under 12s followed by Makgabeya (12 to 18 years old) and youths which includes people from 19 to 35 years. This year's Easter holidays were attended by about 55 pilgrims from Namibia led by Bishop Ananias Kozondunge. According to Kozondunge, from Windhoek they travelled about 1,300Km to attend Easter in Matsiloje. The 78-year-old Kozondunge became a Spiritual Healing Church member in 1953. He was attracted by the fact that the church was led by the late prophet Bishop Mokaleng Motswasele. As a 47-year-old spiritual protégé he started as an ordinary member and rose to his current rank of Bishop in Namibia. "W e started a church on August 15, 1966 in Windhoek with around 20 to 30 members.
Today we have about 2,000 members from all over Namibia," Kozondunge revealed. He said they usually celebrate Easter Holidays in Namibia and it was the first time they came as a group to attend Easter at the headquarters in Matsiloje. Last Thursday there was a church service from 6pm until 7.30pm. On Friday there was a 9:30am service that ended at 12midday. There was also an afternoon session, which started at 3pm and ended at 4pm to specifically cater for those who could not partake in the Holy Communion ceremony during the morning session. The same evening, church choirs from across different congregations put up a sterling and inspirational performance. On the last day, it was an ordinary church service, and towards the end of the service Bishop Motswasele gave his short Easter message to his congregation. After the service the pilgrims had the mammoth task of un-pitching their tents and trave lling back to their different destinations.
Every time this year, village headman Moipolai Seleke is a happy man. He disclosed that it was an honour for Matsiloje village to be the headquarters of a denomination like SHC. "It is very exciting to see masses of faithful in our village. It shows that our village is very popular for a good reason and members of Spiritual Healing Church will further promote it to their different communities," revealed the jovial pint-sized Seleke. The pilgrimage did not only bring a smile to Seleke's face but also to the local business owners. Enterprising vendors took the opportunity and erected makeshift structures to sell food to pilgrims. One of those who seized this economic opportunity was 25-year-old Onkemetse Setime who sold cooked food, ranging from chicken claws, fresh chips, fat cakes, cooked vegetables, mealie meal and beef among others. Setime normally runs a tuck shop in his parents' yard and it is not making much on an average day, but during Easter, she made about P300 on a single day at her makeshift stall outside SHC gathering.
"I cannot complain because I made enough every day. I wish it could be Easter everyday," she said jokingly. The pilgrimage also proved beneficial to the unemployed youths of Matsiloje like Phosi Segopolo, who pitched tents for the pilgrims at a fee. The 21-year-old Segopolo did not want to disclose the amount he charged, but looking at his bright face, he was making a killing. At the popular Itereleng General Dealer and Bar, the mood was very festive. It was apparent that the owner of the establishment would smile all the way to the bank after the holidays. The locals who were also home from their different workplaces throughout the country were in a celebratory mood drinking at their local bar. At a nearby Chibuku depot, some were busy taking turns drinking the sorghum-brewed beer and even though the carnival-like atmosphere prevailed, there were no alcohol related fights b etween guzzlers.

Original_Search_Title: Otema Dismisses Rupiny Report
Headline: Uganda: Otema Dismisses Rupiny Report
Body_Text:
Kampala — The UPDF Northern Uganda intelligence coordinator, Col. Charles Otema, has dismissed a report by Rupiny newspaper that 400 LRA rebels had entered the country from Sudan as 'a complete lie'.
Rupiny yesterday reported that 400 rebels entered through Ngom Oromo in Kitgum district and caused a lot of panic among the people.
"We have had no reports of any LRA entering Kitgum district," Otema said yesterday at Gulu barracks. "We also did not hear of any incident in that area. On the contrary, a group of 15 to 20 rebels is being pursued by our troops in Palabek, as they are trying to cross into Sudan to join their leaders in Congo," he said.
"The remnants of LRA in Congo, Sudan and northern Uganda do not exceed 400," Otema said. "Of these, not more than 100 are in Uganda, split up in small groups of a maximum of 20. Let those 400 be availed to us. It would make it much easier for us to fight them," he said.
He warned 'irresponsible journalists' of fuelling the conflict and stopping the return of internally displace people to their homes. "Reporters should stand warned not to mislead the public," he said.
"This is the time when people are cultivating. Others in Teso and Lango regions are going back to their villages. Such irresponsible reports are sending fear into the population. People have suffered enough. Nobody should feed them with false information to stop them from cultivating or going home. This sounds like propaganda," he said.
Meanwhile, the UPDF said three rebels were killed and one captured from Nyarabonga, southern Sudan on Monday. They were part of a group of 20 which was trying to cross River Nile to Congo. The army confiscated sorghum and drugs.

Original_Search_Title: Delta's Eagle Lager Fails to Reach the Skies
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta's Eagle Lager Fails to Reach the Skies
Body_Text:
Harare — DELTA Beverages, the country's top brewer, will soon launch a
campaign to promote its newly introduced sorghum-based Eagle Lager to
boost volumes amid concerns over its uptake.
Although the beer, the newest addition to the Delta lager family on
the Zimbabwean market, currently sells at a 48.5 percent discount to
Delta's mainstream brands, official sources say the company is
dissatisfied with sales of the product.
Eagle, which is targeted at low-income drinkers, has been on the
market for seven months and Delta had hoped the beer's cost advantage
would translate into bumper sales. But Zimbabwe's seven-year economic
crisis -- marked by inflation of 613 percent -- has seen a persistently
sharp drop in disposable income.
Delta marketing director Etherton Mupisaunga confirmed that the
company was laying the ground for a promotion, to be launched this
month.
"We are looking at running a promotion on the brand," said
Mupisaunga. He, however, maintained sales were strong. "Its (sales) are
slightly ahead of our expectations," Mupisaunga said.
Last year, Delta reported that initial sales of the Eagle
approximated 10 percent of total lager volumes, warning it would take
more months to measure the level of sustainable demand. Mupisaunga said
total sales of the recently introduced 750 ml quarts were neck-on-neck
with the 375 ml pints.
Delta introduced Eagle in September 2005, bringing the number of the
group's lager brands to eight after its flagship Lion and Castle, Black
Label, Bohlingers, Zambezi, Pilsener and Milk Stout lagers.
Eagle Lager is brewed from a combination of the finest locally grown
low-tannin sorghum, sugar, hops and water. The use of locally grown
tannin sorghum as the main ingredient rather than expensive imported
barley malt has given the lager a favourable price.

Original_Search_Title: Bojalwa
Headline: Botswana: Bojalwa
Body_Text:
"If you do not discuss liquor, which is the ruin of chiefs, I shall never join you." Khama to Isang 1921. Bojalwa, taken in its wider sense to mean alcohol rather than strictly, traditionally brewed sorghum beer, has had an up and down history in this country and the current attempt by the government to get control of it is far from being the first to be made here, just as it will not be the last.
The first non-traditional alcohol to appear in this country was a cheap South African brandy, which was introduced by European traders, and travellers of one sort or another. Its effect on the local population was rapid and devastating. In 1877 the Bangwaketse, for instance, were described as 'a tribe which is fast being demoralised by drink from the Chief downwards' and an official report of 1885 referred to the enormous amount of liquor that was being sold to them.
When the three Dikgosi were in the UK in 1895 to try and ward off Cecil Rhodes, a major part of their case was their wish to keep their respective tribal areas free from European settlement and thus from hard liquor and its associated ills. By and large, the British Administration, in the years that followed this visit, was sympathetic to this wish but it had no intention of interfering with local preferences related to the production and consumption of sorghum beer and other local alcoholic brews.
Several Chiefs were particularly severe on defaulters. Khama III roasted both European traders in his territory who flouted his laws by drinking brandy and members of his own tribe who drank bojalwa. In 1926 Tshekedi went as far as thrashing a number of women in the kgotla for violating his tribal beer law but he was heavily criticised and never repeated it. Khama tried to control drinking by levying heavy fines, by seizing cattle and even by banishment. When none of these steps proved effective, he opted for complete prohibition and having done so, immediately took action against the three youngest mephato for their wholesale disregard of his earlier order. He fined each individual one ox and in all collected 2,000 beasts, which he then sold for Â£14,000. Despite these measures, Khama was still unable to control the situation and in 1902 Willoughby remarked that 'he has seen that he cannot prohibit it and retain his tribe'.
Although the Chiefs adopted a variety of approaches in controlling the drinking of beer, none was particularly effective. Bathoen I and Sekgoma Letsholathebe prohibited its sale or purchase, Isang forbade people to drink it at night or on Sundays and prohibited the young from drinking it at all whilst Molefi in Mochudi made the one and only attempt, other than Khama III's, at total prohibition. The attempt did not last long. A few months later, Molefi lifted the ban for his marriage celebrations. In Kanye, Bathoen II made repeated attempts to discourage people from drinking excessively. One reason why the Chiefs so often failed in their efforts to control people's use of beer was that they themselves were frequently known defaulters. The three Kwena Chiefs, Sebele I, Sechele II, and Sebele II were all heavy consumers, not least of kgadi and Molefi in Mochudi was an habitual drinker.
Taking the country as a whole, the situation was, of course slightly ridiculous because whereas there was some degree of control exercised over alcohol in the tribal areas, the non-tribal areas where 'whites' were settled, and along the railway line, were both prohibition free. The one stricture, which prevailed in those areas, however, was that European liquor could not be sold to the 'natives'.
Inevitably perhaps, the sale of liquor was a central element in the maintenance of the system of racism, which dominated so much of the country's pre-independence society and was most evident in the hotels and clubs. Two of the highest profile incidents resulting from this caste system were those involving Seretse and Ruth, in 1957 when together they had a drink in Francistown's Grand Hotel and earlier when Ruth had been admitted into the Lobatse Hotel because she was a member of the European community but was then denied service because she wasn't. Routinely non-whites, which in Lobatse and Gaborone included Indians, were obliged to go around the back of hotels and be served there with tea or water in cheap tin mugs. The third and most celebrated incident occurred in 1965 and involved Kgosi Linchwe who, pushing for change, asked for a whisky at the bar of the Mahalapye Hotel. The bar tender refused to serve him. Linchwe left the bar, collected his gun from his parked car and returned with it to the bar where he repeated his request to be served. He got it. This particular occurrence was surprising because all forms of discrimination had begun to be dismantled as soon as the Legislative Council set up a Select Committee to look into all forms of racial discrimination, the first to change being the railway which opted to introduce a desegregated service on the route between Ramakgwebana and Ramatlhabama. Somewhere in those months, when so many of the in-built prejudices of the past began to fall away, the old stricture that white liquor could not be sold to black chaps fell away. Kgosi Linchwe remembers Timothy Dintwe and Johnny Lebotse seizing their new opportunity to dash from Mochudi to the Gaborone Hotel to buy the stuff openly for the first time in their lives. But, as ever, change comes unevenly. At the wedding of Tshekedi's daughter, Semane to Kgosi Edward Molatlegi in 1964, the only liquid refreshment available to guests was ginger beer and photos taken (in Johannesburg) a year later of the wedding of Linchwe and Mma Seingwaeng show only Coke bottles on the tables.
Hard liquor, even the more innocuous imported South African beers may have become available to everyone but in the tribal areas there were no bars where this could be sold. Local entrepeneurs moved in rapidly to fill this gap. Avery rough estimate was that in Mochudi in 1965, there were at least 30 shebeens where enterprising ladies, with or without a paraffin fridge, provided customers with any sort of mix, the most popular and the most lethal being a combination of warm beer and brandy.
Since then, the brewery has long been established, bars and bottle stores have sprung up almost everywhere and the consump tion of alcohol, by all age groups and in almost any situation, has dramatically increased. There has also been that almost representative story about the individual who drank too much at an Independence Day Reception and was supposedly found dead the next morning, under a bush in the State House garden.
There is now an urgent need to look back at the country's earlier drinking habits and patterns, and at the attempts which were made to control or alter them, to assess what worked better and what didn't work at all and perhaps to learn from the remarkable correspondence in 1921 between the elderly Khama III who detested all alcohol and the young Isang who trod a middle road. We need your experience and advice on many important issues in the African Council, Isang told Khama. But Khama was having none of that soft talk. For him, there was just the one issue and until this was considered, there was no point even thinking about the others.

Original_Search_Title: We Need Brains Not Ranks, Says Ssebaana
Headline: Uganda: We Need Brains Not Ranks, Says Ssebaana
Body_Text:
Kibaale — LACK of military knowledge cannot stop one from running for the highest office in the land, Democratic Party presidential candidate John Ssebaana Kizito has said.
He told a caller during a live programme on Kagadi-Kibaale Community radio on Tuesday, that the UPDF is a professional institution, subject to civilian rule.
The caller had asked Ssebaana whether he would be in position to control the military, if voted into power. The caller had also challenged him to name all military ranks in their order.
"Army work is a professional job. For us we are civilian leaders, but the army has professional leaders. They know they have a commander in chief and they are supposed to obey him," Ssebaana said. "Unless you are saying it's soldiers who should lead this country, that point should not arise."
Two candidates, incumbent Yoweri Museveni and Forum for Democratic Change leader Kizza Besigye have military backgrounds. Besigye is a retired colonel. Other candidates, Ms Miria Obote Kalule (Uganda Peoples Congress), Ssebaana and Abed Bwanika (independent) are civilians.
Earlier in the day Ssebaana had addressed small gatherings in Buganganzi and Kibaale counties on his first campaign day in Rwenzori region. On Wednesday, he addressed rallies in Bundibugyo and he proceeds to Kamwenge today.
At Kagadi Town Council, Buyaga County on Tuesday, Ssebaana also pledged to start a rural industrialisation programme.
"The industries shall employ citizens, reduce unemployment, process and add value to Uganda's products," Ssebaana said, adding that "Uganda's products will then be able to compete in the international markets which will enable the country earn huge foreign exchange."
"If you elect me, I will set up a brewery in Kibaale district," Ssebaana said. He accused Museveni for stifling industrial development, saying he lacks the will to industrialise the economy.
"Kibaale produces alcohol, sorghum and wheat which are key ingredients in beer brewing. These raw materials shall be inputs in the brewery," Ssebaana said. He promised to train technical experts who will work in these industries.

Original_Search_Title: Rufaro Monopoly Not the Answer
Headline: Zimbabwe: Rufaro Monopoly Not the Answer
Body_Text:
Harare — RUFARO Marketing is a holdover from the days when blacks were banned from buying alcohol or, when finally allowed, were banned from drinking in almost every hotel and bar.
Rufaro can trace its roots to the early 20th century when what is now Harare Municipality found that its ban on the sale of alcohol to blacks was being circumvented, sometimes quite legally by people selling ground sorghum and sugar for people to make their own beer.
The "location" superintendent, who also had to look after the cemetery, was given the third job of brewing "wholesome" beer to sell in limited quantities to Mbare residents. This operation grew and flourished, thanks to the municipal monopoly on alcohol sales in the high density suburbs coupled with the Government ban on blacks drinking in the rest of the city after business hours or most city bars refusing to serve blacks at any time.
Some good was done by using the beer profits to fund social amenities, from libraries to swimming pools.
After independence, all these tiresome racial restrictions ended. Unfortunately, neither Rufaro Marketing nor the city council realised what the dramatic changes meant and neither took the action that was required.
Rufaro Marketing might well have been seen as redundant. The city properties it was using could have been rented out for redevelopment on long leases, or converted to other uses. At the same time, the council could easily have lobbied for a city-wide tax on all sales of alcohol, or dramatically raised the fee for a licence to sell alcohol, and then using the proceeds of this "sin" tax to maintain and expand the social amenities so long funded by beer profits.
But no one seemed willing to grasp the nettle, and no one seems willing to do so now, least of all Rufaro chief executive officer, Mr Caleb Mutsai.
His latest idea is to travel back in time and give Rufaro back its near monopoly by banning the sale of beer and spirits in supermarkets and grocery stores.
He cannot, of course, lobby for such a change on the grounds that Rufaro outlets, some of which have more goats grazing than people drinking, are decayed, outmoded and need a monopoly just to stay open.
Instead he argues that teenagers buy alcohol in supermarkets. If that is a problem, then the obvious solution is to enforce the law, and force supermarkets to stop breaking the law. Those that break the law can lose their licences. Some modern supermarkets are already taking active steps to obey the law fully by having the alcohol area barred off from the main supermarket and having its own till. No underage people can then buy the stuff. So the problem is soluble without making life very inconvenient for thousands of people who enjoy a glass of wine or a bottle of beer with their supper.
We would urge the city council to take a long hard look at Rufaro and decide whether it still has a role to play in post-independent Zimbabwe. Some bars and beer gardens may be potentially flourishing businesses, but the "bar business" tends to rely for success on personalities. The owner needs to be behind the bar and a big impersonal organisation like Rufaro cannot supply that personal touch.
This implies that the potentially viable outlets, probably a minority, should be leased out, the rent being a percentage of the turnover rather than a fixed sum.
The rest can be converted to other vital social amenities, such as meeting rooms for women and youth groups, creches and the like.
At the same time the council should be looking at preserving the one part of the old regime that did make sense, using a part of city liquor profits to fund these same social amenities. This can be done by hiking liquor licence fees or having a modest tax imposed on every drop of liquor sold in the city.
The first can be done by the council acting on its own; the second should be acceptable, with proper lobbying, to both the central Government and the city's MPs, ensuring its smooth passage through Parliament with support from both sides.
Returning to some form of apartheid is simply not the answer.

Original_Search_Title: Brewery Firm,Ibbi, Invests N1.5bn for Job Creation
Headline: Nigeria: Brewery Firm,Ibbi, Invests N1.5bn for Job Creation
Body_Text:
Lagos — EMERGING brewery giant, International Beer and Beverage Industries (IBBI) has said that its N1.5 billion investment was meant to create jobs and add value to the economy.
Marking Manager of IBBI, Mr. Olukayode Shittu who said this during the public presentation of its new premium lager beer "Tigre Bock" in Abuja explained that new investment drive billed to commence next year will make the beer present all over the country.
Mr. Shittu pointed out that the new beer "Tigre Bock" is a 100 per cent Sorghum beer of 70 per cent malted Sorghum and 30 per cent plain Sorghum which makes it a unique and fine Nigerian premium lager.
He said the beer is poised to claim at least 30 per cent of the nation's premium lager market within the next five years and keep the position with well thought out strategies.
Speaking on the benefits of the brand to the nation, Shittu said already lots of jobs have been created by the over N1.5 billion invested into the brewery through production of new bottles, crates, a new packaging line, new automation equipments among others.
"IBBI has and will recruit more people in relation to the Tigre Bock, we have so far increased our sales force and our operating staff in the brewery for the new packaging line and will continue to do so as the brand is rolled out in the Nigerian market".

Original_Search_Title: While Some Flourished Others Fizzled Out
Headline: Zimbabwe: While Some Flourished Others Fizzled Out
Body_Text:
Harare — THE year 2006 will go down in the annals of history for various reasons. While some flourished others fizzled out as "one-hit wonders".
Cephas Mashakada was diagnosed with diabetes and had his leg amputated while a virtualy unknown teacher from Mhondoro Jospeh Garakara became the toast of sungura music with a song with the unlikely title of "Idya Banana".
While Garakara was enjoying his place in the sun, others like dub poet Albert Nyathi, Busi Ncube and Cde Chinx were fading into obscurity.
The recording companies came in for their fair share of flak for what some aggrieved artistes described as "daylight robbery".
I shall start with the obscure artistes. And one of them is Adam Chisvo. He is certainly not a household name because he has for years been backing other musicians.
He has played with and for Andy Brown, Ilanga, Busi Ncube and the Band Rain and in between he has found time to do his own projects.
His latest project is with a Zimbabwean based in Europe that seeks to help children whose parents died of HIV and Aids could mark some kind of breakthrough for him.
When Nyathi talks about his time in the United Kingdom during which he was asked to be part of a campaign meant to kick out racism from football, he brims with excitement.
Of course, he also talks about white children who would look at him and then mimic monkeys.
Although Nyathi has not had any new recording, he has a DVD that was done in the UK and must now be available in the country.
It's not clear why he has not recorded or even held shows as extensively as he used to do. His group is still intact but has now mutated into a dance ensemble by the name Amavithikazi.
And talking about Amavithikazi, its manageress went through an acrimonious divorce with her husband complaining that she now had little time for him.
In order words, she was now married to the dancing group.
While Amavithikazi can claim to have been the pioneers in the dance arena, it was offshoot Mambokadzi that hogged the limelight.
As the year went by, the number of dance groups mushroomed. Now we have Girls of Peace, Toxic and Apama, among others.
For sungura king, Alick Macheso 2006 must have been a very disappointing year indeed. Not only did he not release a new album, but parted ways with Gramma Records.
He wanted to feel the waters before making his next move. But Gramma Records decided they were not going to wait and went ahead and released a compilation album.
They claimed that they could not wait for Macheso who had turned the idea of a compilation album. The company argues that Macheso's fans wanted something to listen to during the festive season.
Besides this disappointment, Macheso proved that when it comes to live shows, he reigns supreme. Of course, he did not win anything at Zima this year but that does not mean he is now a spent force.
Allan Chimbetu proved that he could stand on his own without his late brother Simon's guiding hand when he released his album Sonny. Although it received positive reviews and favourable airplay, it is not clear whether this translated into sales.
He learnt it the hard way when Arthur Janjawa, who later dumped him, asked him to perform in the UK.
But Allan himself was no saint in the early days of his career. There were allegations of him double booking shows.
Elder brother Simon had his own weaknesses but he never double booked or operated without a manager.
But if ever there was a moment Allan is unlikely to forget in a hurry it was when he was mobbed on stage by the Speaker of the House of Assembly, Cde John Nkomo, and Cde Munyaradzi Paul Mangwana -- the Acting Minister of Information and Publicity -- during the Unity Gala held at Chinotimba Stadium in Victoria Falls last week.
He proved too that he had learnt the right things from Simon.
The youthful Afrika Revenge, who burst onto the music scene with "Wanga" failed to live up to their promise of releasing a second album.
Neither did they hold as many shows as they promised at the beginning of the year.
Of course, last year they got two awards during Zima but whether they will come back to reclaim their glory is the million-dollar question.
Muzukuru Andy Brown, who has a new album Goga, now has a new hairstyle. Gone are dreadlocks.
It appears he has been maintaining a low profile this year. His presence was only felt only during the galas, the latest being the Unity Gala.
Amai Charamba has proved that working with a committed mentor and husband pays off.
She released one of the greatest gospel albums to be released by a female artiste in the country, titled The Gospel, which does not only deal with Christian matters but also links them with some of the challenges afflicting the world today.
And winning the Best Female Gospel Artiste at Zima was a fitting tribute to her blossoming solo career.
As an elder, this year saw Baba Mechanic Manyeruke taking the centre stage when tackling moral issues. He castigated gospel artistes for dances which he described as out of step with gospel music.
He has been singing about this for ages and perhaps, realising that some people were not listening, he decided to preach about them this year.
He not only talked about moral issues but also took part in projects to help the community. Together with Macheso, Hosiah Chipanga and Cephas Mashakada, Baba Manyeruke fund-raised for Chitungwiza General Hospital.
For her, this year must have been very painful but then music does not pay a day after it's played. The sister must be patient.
Gospel music had a new find, Bethan Pasinawako -- whose album Ndinzverei was a hit -- brought her right at the centre of competition.
She is young and like others before her, could be one of Zimbabwe's greatest singers if she keeps the momentum.
But then considering that her album contains only one good song, she has to prove that this popularity and success was not a fluke. People will be watching and waiting with bated breath next year.
There was a time when Busi Ncube ruled the roost. She was Zimbabwe's own version of Brenda Fassie.
But over the years she has been slowly sinking into oblivion. Her only contribution this year was the project Hupenyu Kumusha/Impilo Ekhaya involving Chiwoniso and others.
Does this mean she has lost her creative juices that made her a great name when she sang True Love?
Cde Chinx has not recorded anything for a long time. Neither has he been holding live shows. He was, however, a very permanent fixture at all the galas held this year, where he was backed by the Police Band or Cde Max.
While others are maturing in song, Cde Chinx seems to be fading away with age.
What happened to the man who brought us the Ngorimba beat?
For Cephas Mashakada who suffered from diabetes that resulted in the amputation of his left leg, 2006 means that he has to be determined or he loses it.
His situation, however, showed how Zimbabwean musicians have matured and can stand by each other. Perhaps because of his ill-health, "Moto Muzhinji" did not release a new album this year.
"God willing", he will be back in the studio next year.
Pastor Charles Charamba had a whirlwind year. He is probably the only gospel musician who can fill up the Chitungwiza Aquatic Complex without a supporting act. His countrywide tour proved how popular the brother is. He has also managed to survive accusations that almost marred his career and this year saw him proving that when one believes nothing comes their way.
He did not win an award this year. Neither was nominated for any. But he remains the uncrowned king of local gospel music.
The mbira strumming sister, Chiwoniso Maraire made 2006 one of her busiest years by holding live shows and appearing at different fora across the world playing the instrument her father taught her when she was only five years old.
Still trying to market her last album, Timeless, Chi is the epitome of Zimbabwe's female mbira players.
Clive Malunga is not very popular with his colleagues as well as some sections of the media. But this year brought out the real Malunga when he took Gramma Records and Zimbabwe Music Corporation head-on after discovering that his music was being sold online.
His efforts are, however, being hampered by lack of support from some musicians who do not see the importance of fighting the system that robs them of their due earnings.
Besides the struggle to make life for musicians better, Malunga has been to Japan on several occasions where he played traditional music and is understood to have sourced equipment to set up a radio station.
Daiton Somanje does not only have a passion for releasing albums but holding live performances as well. This year has not been very good for him because the attendance at most of his shows was poor. He was also threatened with a split after some of his group members claimed that they had not been paid.
But the man has soldiered on whether playing before a handful people or when faced with a split.
What happened to David Chifunyise? The young man who came up with his own brand of urban grooves where he remixed old songs has been very quiet this year and one wonders whether he is still in the country or not.
After touring London with Oliver Mtukudzi, Decibel decided to stay over in the UK doing the club circuit. Maybe the brother found life back home a bit hard and he skipped. Is he a student or an economic refugee?
There were strong indications that Devera Ngwena would reunite after years of playing as separate entities. They played at a gala in Bindura and an album was said to be in the offing.
But then other guys who trade under the name Zhimozhi went on to record their own album in the absence of Jonah Moyo. That put paid to the dream of a reunion. Today Jonah is playing in South Africa while the others are still trying to find their feet here.
There was Dino Mudondo doing his own thing with Sister Flame while Willom Tight said he would follow suit. Dino recorded two albums with Corner Studios. The next thing they were back together again.
By the end of the year, the two were holding shows and making it appear as if they had never split. They were said to have split over money.
But if that was the case, then it is laughable because how can people split over "peanuts" when the future is paved with gold? Hopefully, their case will not be that of now-we-are-together and then tomorrow-we-are-apart like an bickering couple.
It's not often that Diva Mafunga holds a live performance. His videos are excellent but when it comes to live shows, the gospel artiste develops cold feet. But to his credit, the Unity Gala was one of his best performances.
Maybe 2007 will see him challenging the likes of Pastor Charamba when it comes to holding live shows.
Dudu Manhenga has been singing for some time now. She used to have her own band but the last part of 2006 saw her backing other people. She was there beside Oliver Mtukudzi and was also there when Philbert Marowa launched his album. Where is her band and is there any new album? Is she going to be just another jazz musician who plays renditions?
Extra Large have matured. They are no longer the stage loafers but real performers and musicians who know what the people want. This year was their year, what with the song Small House or Amai Linda that captures the real life situation today.
After two long years in the wilderness, Ephraim Patai and the Voice of Prophecy returned this year with a new album. The man who, like Charles Charamba, sings with his wife promises to make it very big next year. Who can doubt him?
If ever there is a musician who should get full marks for trying it is the Chinhoyi-based First Farai who was in the studio early this month. Farai has also been wreaking havoc in his home area. His album Tiri Tose Mundima is more of a challenge to big names reminding them that he is also enjoying the same fruits.
Fungisai Zvakavapano-Mashavave has outlived the negative publicity, which threatened her career over the past years. She has become a shining example of what a gospel artiste should be. She has not let a year pass without a new album.
This year has not been different and hopefully next year brings better things.
Gorden Nzira came. He sang and his music received favourable airplay. But then he quarrelled and took a case to the courts.
He got nominated for two Zima awards. For some time people thought he would grab both because the brother is just good. But when the big night came he went home empty-handed.
To him, this year is both great and disappointing but then he should be patient because if he is good, his time will come.

Original_Search_Title: Godwin Agbroko's Last Moments
Headline: Nigeria: Godwin Agbroko's Last Moments
Body_Text:
Lagos — Exactly one week ago, I walked into his office at about 5.30 pm. He was working on the paper's editorial for the Christmas day. As was his habit, he was trying to supply the planner of the editorial page enough material to last till Thursday, December 27 so that she wouldn't have to be at work during the public holidays.
That evening, Mr Agbroko wouldn't let me leave his office without throwing a banter as usual. On this occasion I started it all when I grabbed a banana from the bunch on his desk. "I thought old men no longer eat such things like banana," I teased. He laughed and then said: "but they can drink the dreg of the palmwine."
"I have gone through the National Honours list ," I further teased, "and your name must be the only one missing from it." We both burst into laughter. Then he joked: "Who de give monkey banana for your country. They would rather give it to villains than to people like you and me." Then we had more pep talk about the honours award which took place the previous day. He then shared some information with me about his recent trip to Adamawa state where he had gone to see his friend, Governor Boni Haruna. If he had any premonition about the cold hand of dead that grabbed him later that evening, he hardly betrayed it.
When he was through with producing the editorials, he returned my visit. As soon as he entered my office he raised the issue of a leave roaster for members of the editorial board. He would like members to take a few days of rest between Christmas and the end of January. I reminded him of my own plan to be away for one week so that I could attend to some family matters. We agreed to take another look at the matter when the editorial board reconvenes on December 27. Altogether, our discussion lasted about an hour during which we covered many subjects, including the political condition of the country, the high state of insecurity in the land and Lagos in particular.
Agbroko and I then left my office together to walk down the hallway. We were heading to the administration block. On the second flight of the staircase, we ran into Mr Eniola Bello and Mr Kayode Komolafe, managing director and deputy managing director of THISDAY respectively. A few banters followed. Then Agbroko said it was time for him to go home. He returned to his office to pack while I returned to mine to read my e-mails. Before that, we had agreed to meet in the office at 11 o'clock the following morning. It was then well after 9pm. Ten minutes later, the editor of the Sunday paper, Mr Yusuph Olaniyonu who had seen both of us in the hall way, came into my office with another staff to enquire if Mr Agbroko had gone home for the night. I advised them to check his office to see if he was still around.
At 10.30pm I packed my own things and headed home. I arrived home at about slightly past 11pm. I was barely through with my dinner when my phone rang. Initially I thought it was my daughter who was coming home from school. When she boarded a vehicle in the morning of that Friday at Nsukka, she called to let us know she was heading to Lagos. However, when I called the house around 5 pm to know if she was back, the answer was no. I tried to reach her by phone but her phone was dead. On getting home I was surprised that she was still not back. All efforts to call her failed. That got me worried. It was in that state of mind that I received that phone call. My assumption was that my daughter was probably calling from Jibowu for me to come and pick her up. But lo and behold, the caller identification showed that the call was coming from Mr Komolafe. Again my presumption was that he was calling to pass on a message from the publisher. But I was far off the mark. KK, as we fondly call Komolafe, had shocking news instead. "Have you heard what happened?" he asked me. "What happened?," I retorted instinctively. " Godwin Agbroko was killed this night on his way back from work on the Isolo bridge," he said, his voice laden with pain. I momentarily lost my power of speech. Who was killed this night? Was that a joke or what? How could this be true? Oh! No. A riot of thoughts coursed through my mind.
For more than five minutes I held on to the phone, completely dazed by this tragic piece of news. Even though I had had that sort of challenge before, this was completely devastating. Only moments ago Mr Agbroko was telling me quite a few intimate things about his plans for the future, the sort he had never before. When we both walked out of my office, there was nothing about his conduct to suggest that danger lurked in the corner for him. His gift of witty anecdotes ( for which Monday Ekpe and I had nicknamed him 'The King of Urhobo Anecdotes') was in full display. It was a gift that compensated adequately for his quick temper.
Agbroko was the sort of editor that few newspaper houses can boast of today. He was exceedingly thorough. He demanded the best of any copy. If he failed to get it he would explode on the writer. He had zero tolerance for sloppy writing. A few younger members of the editorial board who faced his chastening for sloppy writing thought he was unduly harsh but I had to explain to them that all he wanted from them was good writing. The editorial board, he insisted, was not a place for journalistic rookies. He was, as far as journalism is concerned, a full General. He would have been nothing less than a senior editor in the best newspaper in the world.
As chairman of the editorial board, he was firm and well-informed. Whenever he displayed excessive anger during debates, he would spend the next couple of minutes ruing his behaviour. He was evidently a man of good conscience. He wasn't particularly religious but his moral conscience was all too obvious. He was a raconteur par excellence and took delight in sharing jokes. I once asked him if he had ever given a thought to compiling his many humorous anecdotes into a book. He laughed and answered the question with yet another anecdote.
In the nearly four years that I worked with Agbroko, he never betrayed any fear of death. In fact, I recall him sometimes saying that life in Nigeria was almost like dying many times over. He was sometimes too cynical for me and on such occasions we would try to reason things out. He would often insist that this generation of Nigerians has failed the nation. Then he would add that the reason why he still wrote a regular column was to free his conscience. He called it catharsis. He seemed to believe that nothing was changing, after all.
He might not have realized it but Agbroko was an agent of change-the sort of change that cannot be reversed. Those who killed him last Friday obviously sought to extinguish the great light he had become through his pen. But a thousand bayonets cannot extinguish that. As they say, what is not worth dying for is not worth living for. Agbroko has left big footprints in the sands of time. He therefore lives on. This is enough consolation for me and all those genuinely saddened by his passage. May he rest in peace.

Original_Search_Title: Death Robs Soccer Fraternity of Stars
Headline: Zimbabwe: Death Robs Soccer Fraternity of Stars
Body_Text:
Harare — DEATH robbed the sporting fraternity of its players, administrators, coaches, referees and supporters - some of whom put Zimbabwe on the world sporting map - in 2006
Francis Shonhayi
Shonhayi died at the age of 39 on March 30 at Victoria Hospital in Cape Town, South Africa after suffering from meningitis.
He was buried at his rural home in Chirimanzu and was given a befitting send off at Rufaro Stadium where former teammates, administrators and football fans gathered to pay last respects.
At the time of his death, Shonhayi had been a personal driver to his former boss at former South African Premiership side Cape Town Spurs, after hanging his boots due to an injury five years ago.
Mike Mhlanga
Former Warriors goalkeeper Mike Mhlanga, who was 47, died on December 17 and was also declared liberation war hero for his liberation contributions.
Mhlanga played for the national team alongside Japhet M'parutsa and Bruce Grobbelaar.
He also played for Zimbabwe Saints, Olympics and Arcadia where he played alongside the likes of Joey Antipas, Boyce Malunga and Hamid Dhana.
Maxwell Cheche Billiat
Former CAPS United and Blackpool defensive midfielder Maxwell Cheche Billiat died at the age of 38 October 4 at Parirenyatwa Hospital.
He was serving a 15-year jail term for raping his daughter.
Billiat burst into prominence in 1988 when he graduated into CAPS senior team and was part of the club midfield department, which included the late Joel Shambo, one of the best linkmen to emerge from this country.
Billiat also had the opportunity of rubbing shoulders with the club's greats such as Shacky Tauro, Friday Phiri, the late Tobias Sibanda, the late Ephraim Dzimbiri, the late Never Chiku, Silver Chigwenje, Eddie Muchongwe and Brenna Msiska.
Lewis Kutinyu
Former Black Rhinos and Manning Rangers dribbling ace Lewis Kutinyu died on September 5 at the KGVI Barracks Hospital. He was 32.
Kutinyu wrote his own piece of history in September 1998 when he became the most expensive player on the domestic scene as he joined the now defunct Premier Soccer League side Blackpool from Highlanders.
He was signed by Blackpool from Bosso for a then club record fee of $132 000.
Kutinyu played one match for Blackpool and joined South African side Manning Rangers in October 1998.
After a short spell in South Africa, Kutinyu returned home and was signed by Black Rhinos for the 2001 season.
He was later loaned to Motor Action in 2002 before he decided to hang his boots.
David Muchemwa
Premier Soccer League treasurer David Muchemwa died on April 28 at a private hospital in Kwekwe after failing to recover from an operation for tonsillitis. He was 43.
Muchemwa broke into mainstream football administration when he led Kwekwe Cables into the Premiership in 2003 before they were relegated.
Nani Mchiwa
Midlands also lost one of their sons, former Kwekwe Cable's and Chrome Stars coach Nani Mchiwa. He died in August after being ill for some time.
A former goalkeeper with Chrome Stars and Kwekwe Stars, Mchiwa was a winner of the Mailnet coach of the month award for July in 2004.
Benjamin Zulu
Former Mhangura and State House Tornadoes striker Benjamin Zulu died after a long illness on November 9 at his home in Bindura.
Zulu, 38, had been an assistant coach at Bindura-based Premiership newcomers Mwana Africa.
Zulu's football career started in the late 1980s when he was playing for the now-defunct Mhangura where he rubbed shoulders with the likes of Shabanie Mine coach John Phiri, Abraham Mkanga and Jani Milanzi.
He then moved to Harare after being lured by former Zimbabwe President, the late Canaan Banana to Tornadoes.
After retiring from active football in 1997, he joined Ashanti Mine as a player coach in Division Two and helped the goldminers gain promotion into Division One.
He was then appointed assistant coach to Arthur Tutani with whom he worked with to lead Mwana Africa into the Premiership on their first attempt.
Wonder Kalota
Referee Wonder Kalota. Kalota, who was 45, died on March 18 in Harare's high-density suburb of Mbare after a short illness.
He was one of the top referees in the country and was on the Fifa panel and handled a number of international matches outside Zimbabwe.
Faith Mupaso
Death also robbed the country of women's soccer and athletics administrator Faith Maenda Mupaso.
Mupaso, 45, died on February 17 after a short illness.
A tireless administrator, Mupaso first served as an athletics and netball administrator before venturing into women's soccer in the late 1990s with the likes of Susan Chibizhe, Pauline Matute and Elizabeth Moniwa.
In the early 1990s, Mupaso assisted Eva Chirwa in running the Zimbabwe Netball Association before she became the first national chairperson of the then Amateur Athletic Association of Zimbabwe in 1995.
Munyaradzi "Kissinger" Chazika
The country lost one of the most colourful and vocal soccer supporters -- Munyaradzi "Kissinger" Chazika.
Chazika, 52, died of meningitis on September 7 at Harare Central Hospital.
Chazika, a larger-than-life-character, was born and bred in Harare's high density suburb of Mbare where he grew up supporting Dynamos like all the other youngsters in that area.
He also travelled outside the country with the senior national team on numerous occasions.

Original_Search_Title: Unity Gala Goes According to Script
Headline: Zimbabwe: Unity Gala Goes According to Script
Body_Text:
Victoria Falls — TWO things that were expected to happen at the Unity Gala held at Chinotimba Stadium in Victoria Falls last Friday did not happen, but everything else went according to the script.
The rains that pounded the resort town in the morning did not wash away the gala while the Ikalanga musician from Botswana, Ndingo Johwa, failed to turn up citing delays in his own country. There is something about Unity Galas and the rains. Last year's event that was held in Kadoma was preceded by heavy rains but on the day of the event, the skies were calm and dry.
This year's event opened with the national anthem done by popular outfits Iyasa and Black Umfolosi with prolific drummer Douglas Vambe beating the famous drums.
Later on, Black Umfolosi -- the guys have grown now -- played the theme song when they sang about the importance of unity. It's not a new song but then the group gave it loads of meaning.
The artistes' line-up had a few big names like Hosiah Chipanga, Bob Nyabinde, Allan Chimbetu, Baba Mechanic Manyeruke and Jeys Marabini. Baba Manyeruke delivered songs that encourage people to unite and to be morally upright. It appeared as if he was following up on his message to the gospel musicians and the nation about being morally principled.
Chimbetu was the toast of the night when Speaker of Parliament and Zanu-PF national chairman Cde John Nkomo led other dignitaries onto the stage where they sang and danced along to the late Simon's compositions that were beautifully done by Allan.
It was clear from the way Allan performed that years of playing alonside his brother are now paying off. When Cde Nkomo took to the stage he told the audience that the music Allan was playing and the knowledge he had were a legacy from the late Simon.
Humble and shy as usual, Allan accepted the honour and went on to show why he has kept Orchestra Dendera Kings going strong up to now.
Unlike Allan, another younger brother to a great musician, Chase Skuza, who played before Allan, did not do justice to his late brother's songs. He can neither dance nor sing. And when he sang "You Don't Love Me Anymore" it was so pathetic that his backing vocalists did not know where to begin. But then it was not only the late Solomon's songs that he messed up. Even his own songs were a disaster. Chase rose to fame when he featured on the Iyasa video of "Banolila".
But on last Friday's performance it is clear he cannot make it on his own. This is hardly surprising as, unlike Allan, he did not serve an apprenticeship under his late brother. As such, he had no training and is therefore not the right person to sing Solomon's songs.
Cosmas Muponda is virtually unknown but he was the first artiste to arouse the audience. Although he is not a good dancer, his country-flavoured gospel was well received.
Extra Large -- now with the backing of a live band -- were also received well. These guys do not waste time and their humour and stage work were enough to make the whole stadium rise to its feet. Unlike Ex Q and Stunner who came on earlier, Extra Large showed some maturity and their songs carry powerful messages.
This time, Ex Q was rather slow and sang for himself rather than for the people.
Bob Nyabinde -- who announced that he had borrowed Cephas Mashakada's guitar -- paid tribute to the recovering maestro by playing the hit song "Samson". In an earlier discussion, Nyabinde revealed that he had fund-raised for Mashakada during some of his shows.
Zima award winner Joseph Garakara came out for the first time and it is beyond dispute that he has no stage presence. Neither can he dance. Of course, he has the voice but no stage work.
That makes him a musician and not a performer like Oliver Mtukudzi or Alick Macheso even Tongai Moyo.
It's not clear what he would have done had he not invited the three girls to dance for him. In any case, sungura music is a fast beat and to have Garakara waving and kicking lamely was not enough. And that Macheso-like word dragging and voice changing.
Then that Njerama trace in the song Idya Banana. Where is it coming from?
He might have won two awards at Zima but Garakara has a long way to go if he is to come anywhere near his predecessors.
Now it is clear why Sandra Ndebele, who snubbed the previous gala, accepted the offer to play at the Unity Gala - she is finished and her career has hit rock bottom. One wonders why she had to crawl and throw herself onto the stage because that does not translate into dance any more. Andy Brown has been known to complain vociferously whenever the media dares mention that he performs under the influence of alcohol. It was clear to every one present that Muzukuru had had one or half a dozen too many. Of course, alcohol, over the years, has become the greatest bane to Brown's once lustrous music career.
He struggled to keep his eyes open and his voice was hoarse. But on the plus side, the few songs he managed to play were vintage Brown.
Cde Chinx had the time of his life when he he was given free reign on the stage. He was also one of the few musicians who received the support of the top brass present at the gala.
Diva Mafunga has always been a mature musician. His music is calming and the message sobering. Friday night was no exception.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Factor in Ford-Kenya Wars
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Factor in Ford-Kenya Wars
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Trade minister Dr Mukhisa Kituyi flashes the three finger salute as he acknowledges greetings from supporters shortly after opening Narc-Kenya offices in Bungoma town. Picture by Peter Ochieng
The petrol bombing of Narc Kenya offices in Bungoma on Saturday highlighted the battle for supremacy that is taking shape in Western Province as the country enters the election year.
Previously, Western Province was taken to be a Ford Kenya zone.
Today, even as Ford-Kenya claims a stronghold in western Kenya, the party remains a widely divided house. And the "Kibaki factor" is largely to blame for the party's political woes, which have led to the walkout of 12 MPs - more than 70 per cent of Ford-Kenya legislators in the ninth Parliament.
And contrary to popular belief that "election irregularities" were primarily responsible for the boycott of last Wednesday's polls by legislators allied to Housing minister, Mr Soita Shitanda, The Sunday Standard has established that the bone of contention may actually have been the party's apparent decision to warm up to President Kibaki.
Kibaki factor cannot be wished away
Shitanda allies have all along been spoiling for a fight over the Kibaki factor since last year's referendum, where they claim to have been "bulldozed into selling bananas."
No doubt, last Wednesday's polls provided a perfect opportunity for retaliation.
According to our source, the MPs have all along wanted to jump off the Ford-Kenya bus on suspicion that the driver, Local Government minister, Mr Musikari Kombo, was leading them to the direction of President Kibaki.
"We are just being realistic, for while Kombo can comfortably market the President in his constituency and Bungoma District, most of us cannot," says an MP from
Kakamega District who is among those who have fled the party.
"Our constituents rejected the pro-Kibaki campaign during the referendum and we are not ready to put our jobs on the line again."
Indeed, the Kibaki factor is one that cannot be wished away as MPs from Western Province carefully calculate their steps ahead of next year's general elections.
MPs have since formed a political entity
Mr Musikari Kombo
Incidentally, all the MPs from the region who have broken ranks with Kombo - except Trade minister Dr Mukhisa Kituyi - are from constituencies outside Bungoma District where the Orange team carried the day.
The Sunday Standard has reliably learnt that the MPs have since formed a political entity under the chairmanship of Shinyalu MP, Mr Daniel Khamasi and David Were (Matungu) as Secretary. Shitanda himself serves as patron.
The notion behind the formation of the outfit is to build a wider base from where to stage political bargain with other "like-minded forces" come next year.
"We are in the process of reaching out to all Ford-Kenya supporters who felt cheated by last week's sham elections across the country with the view to strengthening our new-found entity before anything else," says Shitanda.
While there have been pointers that the group might join the Orange Democratic Movement, Shitanda refutes such claims.
Sh2m requirement for chairman's seat
However, according to our sources, critical consultations and positioning are ongoing and will most likely be concluded before the New Year.
Shitanda explains that his team stayed out of the polls because members felt the exercise was unlikely to be fair.
"But even more importantly we thought it unwise to rattle over a political party, more so a dying one," states the Malava MP.
Dr Noah Wekesa
Science and Technology minister and Ford-Kenya's director of elections, Dr Noah Wekesa thinks otherwise: "These are purely hollow excuses by colleagues who fled from the party on sensing defeat."
The requirement that aspirants for the chairman's seat pay two million shillings is one that was heavily criticised by Shitanda and co-candidates, Public Service minister Mr Moses Akaranga and Health assistant minister Dr Enoch Kibunguchy.
However, Wekesa points out that the figure was arrived at during the party's National Governing Council meeting and was "in fact seconded by none other than (Bonny) Khalwale (a key ally of Shitanda)".
"Why would they be the ones turning around to claim the polls were rigged? In 2003, I equally paid half a million shillings alongside Kombo and Kituyi to run for chairmanship - this is our tradition," says Wekesa.
Claims of external hand in Kombo's ouster
Nonetheless, Ford-Kenya woes do not seem to end with Shitanda, as there have been claims of an external hand in Kombo's dramatic "ouster" as party chairman early this month.
Mr Cyrus Jirongo
Reacting on the incident where a group of delegates gathered in Nairobi and replaced Kombo with Shitanda in the wee hours of morning, a host of Kombo allied MPs blamed it on Kituyi and former Lugari MP Mr Cyrus Jirongo.
"It is common knowledge that Kituyi has told anybody who has cared to listen that he is poised to be the next Vice-President," claimed one. The Kimilili MP has repeatedly denied any involvement in Ford-Kenya's wars.
Vice President Moody Awori equally astonished many last weekend when he dismissed efforts by local leaders to unite members of the Luhya community. Such a move, he claimed, was tantamount to practising tribalism.
Both Awori and Kituyi are firmly in President Kibaki's political corner and, therefore, pointmen in Western Province. However, this fact alone creates discomfort to both politicians, as they are likely to engage in quiet wars for the number two slot.
President has a soft spot for Kituyi
The possible entry of the Ford-Kenya boss complicates the competition, a development that is likely to open up Kombo as a clear target of his political adversaries.
Apparently, the President has a soft spot for Kituyi. In fact, politicians closely allied to the late Vice President and former Ford-Kenya boss Mr Michael Wamalwa, say his appointment to the Cabinet was purely a result of President Kibaki's kind gesture.
The Kimilili MP's name was reportedly not on the initial list of Ford-Kenya legislators Wamalwa presented to the President for appointment to the Cabinet, but Kituyi was nonetheless given a slot at the expense of Kombo - Wamalwa's priority.
Kombo only found his way into the Cabinet much later in September 2003 following Wamalwa's demise and after his election as Ford-Kenya Chairman.
Of course, the President was not going to condemn the leader of a crucial coalition member party to the backbench.
Narc-K rotational chairmanship has stopped at Kituyi
And is it not curious that the rotational chairmanship position of Narc-Kenya, has finally stopped at Kituyi? In fact, sources privy to the party's leadership claim that the President had all along been desperate to give the party a face - one that is not from Central Kenya - the region largely associated with the party.
Indeed when it came to Kituyi's turn, the rotating slate stopped. Narc-Kenya is momentarily convinced that in the Kimilili MP it has finally found the face of the party.
Kituyi might indeed have the national face, but if Narc-Kenya and President Kibaki have hopes of fishing from the populous Luhya vote then the Ford-Kenya leader is the favourite pick as he has a comparatively larger pool to fish from.
Even so, Kombo has a couple of challenges to overcome. Ikolomani MP, Dr Bonny Khalwale, for instance, observes that the Ford-Kenya leadership has over the last three years failed to engage in any structured negotiations with any likeminded political parties - a factor that he claims is solely responsible for its current shrinkage.
Party has lost a host of legislators
Those who blame Kombo for this kind of retrogression observe that since his predecessor's demise, the party has lost a host of its legislators including four ministers - Kituyi, Dr Newton Kulundu (Labour), Akaranga and Shitanda.
Curiously, most of those who are still hanging on bear names that start with the letter "W" - a factor that many including Kituyi have openly chided as not just Luhya but a Bukusu party.
Except for party Secretary General and Special Programmes minister John Munyes and Turkana Central MP Ekwe Ethuro, the rest of the MPs solidly behind Kombo include Wekesa, Wafula Wamunyinyi, Bifwoli Wakoli , Moses Wetangula and Raphael Wanjala.
Wanjala maintains that of all Ford-Kenya MPs, particularly those who have been keen on leading the party, Kombo is still the best man for the job.
And while conceding that the party has had its fair share of its problems and may have shrunk in the course, the Budalang'i MP accuses those heaping all the blame on Kombo of not being fair.
Still commands a heavy following in western Kenya
"If Ford-Kenya has truly been managed badly or has been reduced to a Luhya party, then we are all to blame, he says. Wanjala poses: "What have we done to rescue the situation? What contribution has Shitanda and group, for instance, made to remedy the situation?"
MPs allied to Kombo were particularly irked by the manner in which the Shitanda group tried to take over the party's leadership.
"These Kakamega guys just decided one night that they want to lead the party and organised a discreet coup. This was very cruel and shameful," says one MP.
The obvious challenges notwithstanding, Wekesa maintains that Ford-Kenya as a party still commands a heavy following in western Kenya and a considerable one across the country. The party managed to pool delegates from 200 out of 210 existing constituencies.
According to the Kwanza MP, Ford-Kenya is actually contented with playing its small but useful role in the current political dispensation. Even small parties, he argues, are very crucial in a coalition arrangement.
Shitanda group is raring to go
Over the years, Ford-Kenya has indeed played the stabilising role between the two extremes - Government of National Unity and Opposition. The most delicate moment unfolded immediately after the referendum when Kombo led his MPs into declining to take up ministerial appointments.
Had the MPs not yielded to fresh negotiations and joined Government, chances are that a vote of no confidence in the Kibaki administration would have easily sailed through because the Ford-Kenya MPs would have probably voted with ODM colleagues. The timing for a vote of no confidence was ripe, especially after the Government's referendum loss.
"Ford-Kenya shall continue playing the middle ground role. It does not matter how small we are but our goal is to be part of the next government," he says.
Meanwhile, the Shitanda group is raring to go: "I have personally tasted and felt the Luhya unity calls. We are accordingly moving in that direction and even Kombo should stop worrying because he is fully accommodated in our plans," remarks Khalwale.

Original_Search_Title: Alex Mukulu On Restaging 30 Years of Bananas
Headline: Uganda: Alex Mukulu On Restaging 30 Years of Bananas
Body_Text:
Renowned playwright Alex Mukulu will restage his acclaimed theatrical work 30 Years of Bananas at the National Theatre starting tomorrow in commemoration of local theatre's Golden Jubilee.
Is the play still relevant today 15 years since its premiere?
It is in the sense that those 30 years from 1962 to 1992 were a vital part of our history but we have also included "banana" aspects of the years dating 1992 to today.
What are the bananas in the title?
Bananas is an American slang for crazy but we also have our own bananas, the ones we grow which scientifically are 90% water and I think 1% protein. There is a lot of nothingness in a banana and combined with the American slang, I try to explain the period from 1962 to 1992, which was really crazy because during that period Uganda was tiptoeing politically. Not that much has changed in terms of good governance today either.
You said in the local press this week that, "the play will trace Uganda's history which is a path that the country and its people took voluntarily or otherwise." What did you mean by that?
Otherwise means forcefully. For example during the Amin era some took it forcefully, others voluntarily and others spiritually. Even 20 years after Museveni took power, some are taking it voluntarily, others forcefully while others have resigned themselves to whatever may happen.
Your plays aim to make people ponder but do you at times feel your efforts are going in one ear and out the other?
Definitely yes and that is natural! I believe people have the choice to take the good thing out of it and leave the rubbish much like in the distillation process.
You have advocated for civil disobedience before. Are you disappointed Ugandans are too meek to even demand for better roads and protest increasing taxi fares even when fuel prices are falling?
In the play, I say we are all cowards considering that we cannot even strike because of the black outs yet power is an essential factor to production and work. That may explain why we let people like Tito Okello, Binaisa, Lule and Obote rule us. Letting this regime rule us for 20 years is also an act of cowardice and I think our mwami culture where we do not talk back to the chief is to blame.
Museveni watched the play in 1992. Did he take heed?
At that time he said he would not come into the museum [in the play] without his ears. Today there is still some debate as to whether he has them or not and my disappointment would be him breaking his promise.
Why is your lead character Rwandese?
I wanted a neutral person who could see our history with neutral eyes and I felt a Rwandese refugee would do that well. These people are an integral aspect of our history. I grew up with them and I know exactly what their feelings about us were.
Is there anything to celebrate about theatre's 50th year?
I think it is more about we the old guard training, developing and giving our experience to a younger generation than looking back to what we have or have not achieved.

Original_Search_Title: ODM-K Leaders Endorse Plan
Headline: Kenya: ODM-K Leaders Endorse Plan
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is now official: Kenyans will know the ODM Kenya presidential candidate in the next six months.
Three of the ODM-Kenya leaders interested n the party's presidential ticket, from left, Uhuru Kenyatta, Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka when they turned up in a Nairobi court yesterday for the hearing of a case over Kanu leadership. Photo/Paul Waweru
But the party's torchbearer could even be known by April, if most of the MPs allied to ODM-K agree.
Five ODM-K presidential hopefuls resolved in Nairobi yesterday that the candidate will be picked by 350,000 members countrywide who will vote in their sub-locations.
The decisions were reached after an hour-long meeting at Nairobi Club.
The meeting was attended by Mr Raila Odinga, Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta, Mr William Ruto and Mr Musalia Mudavadi, who have declared interest in vying for the presidency. The sixth aspirant, Mvita MP Najib Balala, did not attend.
Also present were interim secretary-general Peter Anyang' Nyong'o and MPs Julia Ojiambo and Kenneth Marende. Mr Marende chaired the committee that prepared the ODM-K constitution and nomination rules.
The party now appears close to resolving the dispute over when to pick its candidate to face President Kibaki in next year's election.
Interim chairman Henry Kosgey said the middle ground decision was reached to balance the interests of two groups - one favouring the choice of the candidate by March and another proposing that the decision be delayed until September.
Mwingi North MP Musyoka's supporters wanted the candidate elected by March, while those backing his Lang'ata counterpart, Mr Odinga, favoured September.
Mr Kosgey said they had agreed that the candidate be picked between April and June next year.
Whereas the Kalonzo group felt that early nominations would give the presidential aspirant enough time to campaign, the Odinga camp wanted it delayed to cushion the candidate from "opponents like the Government and fatigue."
The report was presented and approved by the aspirants. It will now be tabled at a meeting of MPs allied to ODM-K for ratification early next month.
Mr Kosgey added: "The nomination rules for the presidential candidates will be ratified by members."
The party members will elect 50 delegates in each sub-location who will choose the ODM-K flagbearer. There are 7,000 sub-locations, hence a total of 350,000 delegates.
It had been proposed that only areas that voted for the Orange Democratic Movement during last year's referendum pick the candidate to keep out "votes from enemies and other competitors".
If this was to be accepted Central Province, parts of Eastern, Rift Valley and Western would not have participated.
It had been feared that opponents from the "Banana areas" could infiltrate and rig the poll in favour of a weak candidate to enhance President Kibaki's re-election chances.
But ODM-K, some argued, would be starting from a disadvantage if it left out certain regions.
Yesterday, Mr Kosgey said: "We are a national party and don't want to discriminate or balkanise the country."

Original_Search_Title: Former Army Chief Odongo Retires to Growing Fruits
Headline: Uganda: Former Army Chief Odongo Retires to Growing Fruits
Body_Text:
