Have you wondered about where in this world the former army commander has disappeared? Well Inside Politics has "discovered" him in his home village of Amuria. The Lt. Gen. Can best be described in military language as a man on Katebe.
Q-Were you surprised at being dropped from cabinet in June?
A-I was not surprised. You see then I was a minister before we went into a multiparty system of governance. As an army officer I don't belong to any party so if my name does not appear on the list of ministers I don't care.
Q-How did you learn of your sacking?
A newspaper reporter rang me to ask if I was aware that my name was not the list.
Q-Are you happy now that you are on katebe?
A- I would say I have mixed feelings. This is because partly I am not serving the people and believe Ishould be. On the other hand I am happy because it has afforded me time to be myself.
Q-What lessons have you learnt from this life on katebe?
A-The truth of the matter is that, this is my time to reflect on many things. First it has given me a chance to reflect on how I would like to live my life and also reflect on the political future of our country . I am looking critically on what is happening in the country and am trying to draw a road map to it.
Q-Have you talked to the president since May?
A-Yes I have talked to him once. But I must say it was my own initiative. I must mention that even then we did not talk about Jeje we were on different things.
Q-What are you doing apparently to earn a living?
A-I am growing oranges, which I sell and get something.I also get money for the rank I have in the army. I spend part of my time trying to rehabilitate my home which was destroyed by Kony when he attacked Teso.
I had 3acres of banana, 3acres of pineapple, 6400 trees of oranges [Which were destroyed].
I have been doing research on a fruit called Sheer-Nut, which grows wildly in Teso. This oil is used in most creams and I have been looking on how we can involve the people of Teso into it so that they can sell this oil and improve their economy.
I am also trying to write some books on topical issues like the challenges facing the African Union, my Life and the Northern Conflict.
Q-Do have any plans of retiring from the army in the near future?
A- I don't have any plans as of now. But when I feel that the time has come for me to do so I will definitely.
Is it true you are linked to the Forum for Democratic Change (FDC)?
Its not the first time I am hearing this, but all that is trash. Many of these colleges in FDC were people I knew before they formed FDC.
We had developed friendship at personal level for example Gen. Muntu was friend , I had known Hon. Elija Okupa much earlier. For Besigye, actually he was my boss; it was official so I never got to know him on personal terms.
I can't loose my friends and brothers just because of mere loose talks by some people.

Original_Search_Title: Ajimobi Plots to Break Oyo Political Cabal
Headline: Nigeria: Ajimobi Plots to Break Oyo Political Cabal
Body_Text:
Lagos — Oyo State Chapter of All Nigerian Peoples Party (ANPP) has unanimously adopted the sitting Alliance for Democracy (AD) Senator representing Oyo South Senatorial District at the National Assembly, Senator Abiola Ajimobi as the party's governorship candidate for the 2007 general elections.
Ajimobi's election came even as the State's Action Congress (AC) announced Professor Taoheed Adedoja as the party's consensus gubernatorial candidate for the 2007 polls.
At the ANPP State Congress held at its secretariat in Ibadan, party members from all over the 33 local goverments in the state elected Ajimobi by acclamation, describing him as the much-deserved saviour for the State should he emerge the governor in the next political dispensation.
The ANPP congress was virtually turned into a carnival as party delegates jubilated openly while dancing to melodious tunes.
Oyo State ANPP Chairman, Alhaji Folorunso Razaq, had earlier acknowledged Senator Ajimobi's outstanding performance at the National Assembly and concluded that his antecedents both as a renowned accountant and former managing director of National Oil Plc had positioned him effectively for the governorship seat of the State.
In his post-election speech, Senator Ajimobi told the mammoth crowd that he felt highly elated for the confidence reposed in him saying he would do everything possible not to disappoint the people of Oyo State if elected into the office.
Addressing journalists shortly after the election, Senator Ajimobi declared that his vision and mission as a governorship candidate is "basically to rescue Oyo state from the oppressive tendencies of the cabal that has all along been retarding its economic and social growth, as they steal and loot the state's treasury indiscriminately for their selfish goals."
According to him, "for years on end, Oyo State has remained a banana republic despite the fact that people of means and quality abound all over the different senatorial districts."
"To change the status quo, we need quality people in the saddle of governance, not only at the state level but in all the tiers of administration," he posited.
On his chance of emerging Oyo State Governor come 2007, Senator Ajimobi maintained that he had the same chance as he did when he contested for the Senate about four years ago, since he enjoyed the support of the masses of the State.
Meanwhile, the Elders Committee of the Action Congress (AC) in Oyo State has named Professor Taoheed Adedoja as the party's consensus gubernatorial candidate for the state in the 2007 polls.
However, the announcement has caused a furore as some groups within the party, especially those routing for the candidature of Dr. Adebayo Adewusi have staged a protest, describing the choice of Adedoja as an imposition. And beside Adewusi, other AC gubernatorial aspirants in the State include Hon Soji Akanbi and Dr. Laolu Adepoju.
Vowing to resist the imposition even up to the national level, a spokesman of the party, Evangelist Olaoye Abimbola, who pledged his loyalty to Dr. Adewusi told journalists that the purported idea of consensus as announced last Friday was a repeat of what happened in the Alliance for Democracy (AD) where a group called stakeholders polarised the party.
Abimbola remarked that Professor Adedoja who until about three weeks ago was a member of the Peoples Democratic Partry (PDP) could not be the AC's consensus candidate.
His words: "I want to tell you with all sincerity of purpose Professor Taoheed Adedoja cannot emerge as the consensus candidate of the AC in Oyo State. It is not possible. He joined AC some 18 days ago. What of those people who came from AD who have transformed into the AC now? Those who have worked assiduously for this party, who have beeen promoting this party in the last three years? What of Dr. Adewusi who has visited all the 33 local government councils of the State and the 351 wards in Oyo State? What should have been done is to allow all the aspirants to go to primary. If such is allowed, I am sure Dr. Adewusi will win hands down. That is sure."

Original_Search_Title: ODM to Push for New Law On Voting
Headline: Kenya: ODM to Push for New Law On Voting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Party will push for a new legislation to allow voters to produce either voting cards, national identity cards or passports during voting.
Currently, a voter must produce both the voting and identity cards to be allowed to vote.
ODM's presidential hopefuls Kalonzo Musyoka and William Ruto said the current rules were discriminatory and had in the past denied some Kenyans the right to vote.
Mr Musyoka said Kanu and the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) will push for the legislation in Parliament.
"We will also invite like-minded MPs to vote alongside us as the legislation would benefit all Kenyans irrespective of their political affiliations," Mr Musyoka said.
The leaders were speaking at Nunguni market in Kaiti constituency, Makueni District, during a funds-drive in aid of women's groups. More than Sh1.2 million was raised.
Mr Musyoka, who was accompanied by MPs Musa Sirma, Mutinda Mutiso, Gideon Ndambuki and Reuben Ndolo, addressed several gatherings on the way.
However, supporters of Kilome MP Mutinda Mutiso and his main challenger, Mrs Regina Ndambuki, both in LDP, clashed forcing Mr Musyoka to intervene.
He said no-one will be favoured during the party's nominations for civic and parliamentary candidates.
Mr Ruto claimed the Government had lost direction and was now promoting violence.
He caused laughter when he urged the crowd to pray for former president Moi's quick recovery so "that he could come home and see how the "true Kanu led by Uhuru Kenyatta and himself was preparing to give Kenyans the government they have been yearning for."
Mr Sirma said ODM-K supporters in the Rift Valley were ready to support Mr Musyoka if he emerged the party's flag bearer.
Mr Ndolo said Narc-Kenya had sensed defeat and was applying unorthodox tactics in a bid to win the 2007 polls.
Mr Ndambuki said Nark-K would be defeated the way the Banana camp was shown dust during the referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Time Up - Besigye Assures Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Time Up - Besigye Assures Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — FORUM for Democratic Change (FDC) party president Col. Kizza Besigye has asked President Yoweri Museveni to 'begin packing his bags because his time of rule is up.'
He said this on Sunday at the launch of the sale of party cards in Mbarara district at the Mbarara golf course.
"Museveni should know as he packs his suitcases, that he is the last dictator in this country. And in case he has not yet finished, he should hurry," he said.
"A dictator is a scarecrow. If you meet a scarecrow and confront it, you find they are only banana leaves. Little wonder that NRM chose their symbol as banana leaves," Besigye scoffed.
Besigye was welcomed in the town by FDC flag waving fans and cheering bodaboda riders.
He said FDC and other opposition parties would find it easy to fight the NRM because it has pressed its own self-destruction button.
"Museveni cannot say that 'I still have five more years (to rule).' I guarantee you that it will not happen if you have not helped the people," he added.
FDC publicist Wafula Ogutttu said the party estimated to raise sh5b from the sale of the party cards. He said the next launch will take place in Gulu district.
Present at the launch were organising secretary Maj. Gen Mugisha Muntu and FDC chiefs from Kabale, Mbarara, Bushenyi, Rukunguri, Isingiro, Kiruhura and Ibanda districts.

Original_Search_Title: UN Food Official Recalls the Horrors Depicted in the Film Blood Diamond
Headline: Sierra Leone: UN Food Official Recalls the Horrors Depicted in the Film Blood Diamond
Body_Text:
At a time when the film Blood Diamond depicts the efforts of the United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) to feed the hacked and mutilated victims of Sierra Leone's savage civil war during the 1990s, an agency officials intimately involved in the battle for survival recalled how the smugglers used the names of food for their contraband.
When WFP regional manager for West Africa from 1997-2000 Paul Arès fell into conversation with diamond traders in hotel bars in Sierra Leone, they would tell him they dealt in melons and bananas.
"They were referring to the colour of the uncut diamonds," Mr. Arès said in an interview on the agency's web site. "But for me and many others, those diamonds weren't yellow and orange, but red with people's blood."
It's ironic they chose a food metaphor because food was one thing that was hard to come by in this diamond-rich, dirt poor west African country for the civilians terrorized by rebels and driven from their homes.
WFP's recovery project in Sierra Leone, Liberia and Guinea is currently facing a $35 million funding shortfall and Blood Diamond's stars are helping the agency raise much-needed awareness of the hunger and poverty with Djimon Hounsou, and Jennifer Connelly appearing in public service announcements.
Mr. Arès recalled that during the decade-long civil war, Revolutionary United Front (RUF) rebels hacked off people's limbs, raped women and killed tens of thousands of people as they enforced their operation 'No Living Thing.' In just a few months, 250,000 people in the east of the country fled to the so-called Parrot's Beak area of Guinea.
"These refugees were in dense, mountainous jungle with very little food," he said. "It presented WFP with an enormous challenge. Airlifts were impossible so we appealed for six-wheel drive trucks which could tackle any situation."
Mr. Arès said the true horror of the war hit him when he visited the camps and saw people whose limbs had been chopped off by the rebels. These people were entirely dependent on WFP and other aid agencies. Perhaps one of the most shocking trends to emerge from West African unrest in the 1990s was the use of child soldiers.
"The way they were inducted into the ranks of the RUF made them the most vicious little killing machines around," he explained, recalling that after the war was over, he saw these child soldiers in rehabilitation centres with scars etched into their shaven heads. He was told that this was where their 'big brother' (the rebels) had injected them with drugs when they went on the rampage across Sierra Leone with their AK47 machineguns.
Even though the war ended in Sierra Leone some four years ago, the country is still reeling from the devastation and poverty is rife. Despite its diamond wealth, unemployment is high and the country is 176th out of 177 countries in the UN Human Development Report index. WFP is helping its people to rebuild their lives through school feeding, food-for-work and food-for-training programmes.

Original_Search_Title: Pinochet's Death - a Lesson in the Wages of Sin for All Dictators
Headline: Zimbabwe: Pinochet's Death - a Lesson in the Wages of Sin for All Dictators
Body_Text:
ADOLF Hitler 's death in the Berlin bunker ought to have taught all future would-be dictators that their day of reckoning would be messy.
Call it the "wages of sin", "the revenge of the gods", or God's vengeance.
Augusto Pinochet, the former Chilean dictator who died last week, may have had his loved ones around him. But, like Hitler who killed himself and his lover, he must have felt relief that, at last, he would escape the pain he had endured for so long.
Before that, he must have felt the world and its brother would persecute him for his sins until he expired, from entering and leaving this or that court.
His own country was determined he would not escape punishment. Even if a dozen specialists certified he was senile and had Alzheimer's disease, they would not let him off the hook.
Pinochet's demise ought to warn other dictators that their inevitable comeuppance will entail the same excruciating pain that this 91-year-old former dictator endured.
Yet we all know, life is not that simple; people will commit acts which they know will bring them grief. That is the way of fallible humankind.
Slobodan Milosevich must have gone to meet his Maker knowing what he had done to God's innocent people would earn him the Almighty's eternal wrath; yet up to the end, he protested his innocence, as Pinochet did.
Those who believe in the existence of a Supreme Being will assert, comfortably, that Pinochet is an example of how Humankind must know the terrible reward for going against the tenets of The Supreme Teacher - Jesus Christ, Mohammed or the Buddha.
The atheists will ascribe Pinochet's demise to a heavy dose of guilt. His mind and body were terrorised incessantly by memories of innocent people being flung from helictopers into the ocean, or being smashed into smithereens by the gun butts of his soldiers.
In the end, the man cracked, his body giving up under the anvil-like weight of this guilt: moreover, he was 91 years old. How much longer can an immune system last after that age?
Most Africans will not bother themselves with Pinochet's death: after all, they have their own dictators' death to worry about. Will they hang on to life until they are 91? Will they expire before then, saving the taxpayer a lot of money in medical bills?
Pinochet came to power in 1973 after overthrowing the Marxist regime of Salvador Allende. In those years, Africa had not yet cornered the entire market of military coups; it's true that the former Belgian Congo, Ghana, Nigeria, Uganda and a few former French colonies had been taken over by soldiers.
Yet South America, with its "banana republics", led the field. Africa would take over soon enough.
For me, the coup in Chile was played out in the Hotel Rusia in Moscow, at that time the largest hotel in Europe. Three Chilean women were huddled in a corner of the massive lounge, crying quietly. Our interpreter told us they had heard of the coup; they were Salvador Allende's supporters, visiting Moscow as the guests of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union; they would not return to their country, until democracy was restored.
Like most South American republics, Chile was hardly a model of democracy before Allende.
His problem was that the Cold War dictated the geopolitics of the world then. The United States government, under the soon-to-resign Richard Milhous Nixon, was livid. The Central Intelligence Agency is reported to have plotted Allende's downfall.
As with most things Uncle Sam did in the pursuit of beating the Soviet Union, the success in Chile turned into something of a pyrrhic victory: Pinochet and his brutal regime were hardly what they had bargained for.
Still, that the killings and disappearances continued for 17 years would seem to suggest Washington was not in a hurry to "demo cratise" its client state - as it did not do with Mobutu Sese Seko's Congo, which the former colonel renamed Zaire as the LSD of power got to his head.
Incidentally, Mobutu himself provided one of the most salutary lessons for all dictators: it may take long, but God or Allah or Something or Someone can guarantee that your end is going to be ignominious, whichever country offers you sanctuary.

Original_Search_Title: Revealed - Mzee's Poor Health Was Kept Secret for 10 Years
Headline: Kenya: Revealed - Mzee's Poor Health Was Kept Secret for 10 Years
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In this third and final part of Duncan Ndegwa's book he tells us more about Mzee Kenyatta's personality
Mzee - at the peak of his glory - was taken ill at Tiririka in Mombasa. It was an event hidden from the public. I saw him bed-ridden breathing quite heavily and almost in a coma. Of the ministers, only Koinange, Njonjo and Gichuru visited him. Dr Mungai took personal charge of the situation. He spoke to me of a diagnosis; something like a blood clot needing utmost care to dissolve. Soon Mzee awoke to his normal routine and to his loyal subjects, nothing awkward had occurred. The public was told of his continued "busy working holiday" as soon as he could manage to walk about.
Revelation to Kenyatta
The story would not be told here were it not for a revelation to Kenyatta of the existence of an obituary prepared by George Githii on the "passing away" of the Great Kenyatta. It was intended for publication soon after his expected death. Nothing new. Editors of newspapers often prepare obituaries of their heroes. How Njonjo came by it immediately, I dare not guess. Charles and George were friends. Maybe the copy had received Njonjo's clearance. In any case, George Githii came from a known family with a long history with Mzee. Githii's father went to school at Thogoto with Mzee and George himself served as Mzee's first Personal Assistant in 1964. However in a rare demonstration of ferocious emotion, Mzee ordered the offending article together with drafts and photographs destroyed by burning under the supervision of the CID.
On orders of the Aga Khan, George Githii lost the job of Editor-in-Chief at Nation Newspapers. He had yet to learn the wishes of all great men from Alexander the Great, to Julius Caesar to Napoleon, was always to be seen by their subjects as indestructible and invincible.
He lived 10 more years
Kenyatta lived 10 years more but never wholly recovered the energy and stamina of his robust past. My successor often complained of a host of challenges; being met by far-away stares, arranged briefings and meetings being put off, or key decisions being unduly delayed. Kenyatta's sharp memory had disappeared as well as his mastery over facts and issues. He leaned much more on the AG for support.
* * *
Kenyatta, the man whose attention many forces were trying to reach and in some cases dislodge, was aware of human frailty, particularly his health. He was, however, very sensitive about publicity concerning his health. I became aware of that fact - almost to my peril - when a delegation from Kitui, led by Cabinet Minister Ngala Mwendwa, went to Kenyatta's Gatundu home for a scheduled courtesy call. As was common with such delegations, it included traditional dancers and, for most of that day, they danced away long moments waiting for Mzee. They might have as well been waiting for Godot because Kenyatta was indisposed and could not, therefore, come out to receive them.
I thought that the news of Mzee's indisposition should be announced to the delegation which by then was exhausted and so I informed him as much over the telephone. His reaction was both immediate and angry. "Are you mad? Has something gone wrong with your head?" I could imagine his eyes in flames.
Mzee wanted his state of health kept secret. Although his eyesight was failing him more and more as the years went by, he would not heed advice to wear reading glasses in public. Instead, his speeches were typed in bigger and bigger letters as his age advanced and his eyesight deteriorated. Eventually, the trick could not work any more and he had to accept reading glasses.
Heart transplant surgeon
When the renowned South African pioneer heart transplant surgeon, Christian Bernard, visited Kenya at the invitation of Njonjo, he caused a political furore when an announcement went out that he would meet Mzee Jomo Kenyatta. The foreign office opposed the visit arguing it could have passed for a gesture in support of the apartheid regime. Those in favour of it added that this surgeon would strap a pace setter on Mzee. Nothing could have been further from the truth. Mzee would have been the last person to allow his body to be encumbered with mechanical devices to support his heart, no matter how fragile.
That ailing heart was perhaps the cause of the blackouts that Kenyatta suffered from time to time. On one occasion, he suffered such a blackout at his Mombasa Tiririka residence, and remained in a coma for three days. They were three days of uncertainty and when he came out of it, I was among the first people to visit him.
I asked him what had happened. He replied that he had visited what he called "Weru wa Mukaaga," meaning an expansive plain where he was all alone. He recalled that one could not see the end of that serene plain where he had been at complete peace and where the sun never set. Many people of his age had told me precisely of such visits. It must be a universal occurrence to old people. Kenyatta toured Weru wa Mukaaga on other occasions but the final one was on August 22, 1978 when he went, never to return. Though concerned about public knowledge of his health, Kenyatta did not worry about his age although he was given to the joke that he was not as old as some people were wont to imagine.
In the meantime, some of those near him lived in fear about the possibility of his death and his lapses of memory. Kenyatta's memory loss was at times so severe that he could not recall his signature until a copy of the same was shown to him to jog his memory.
Could not recall signature
Though a Deputy Permanent Secretary in the Treasury, rightfully the citadel of government planning and financial administration, my mind had not been prepared at all for Kenyatta's visit. Furthermore, it could not have dawned on me that he would make a beeline to my office and single me out from among several other colleagues to serve as Permanent Secretary for his Economic Planning and Development docket. But he did. It was hard to describe what I felt just then.
Here was a man who had been painted in the colours of the devil by the colonial forces and the settler community. A man seen as an ogre whose hands dripped with the blood of innocent children and women; a man accused of being a leader "unto darkness and death". To his detractors, Kenyatta was a despicable person who had allegedly led his people back into the dark ages; one who deserved death by hanging if not by the firing squad, and one made to pay for those crimes by serving a long jail sentence only 10 years earlier.
On that day, as I walked Kenyatta back to his car, I could see faces of Europeans peeping from their office windows to get close-ups of the man they loved to hate. The one thing I was sure of was that in their minds, they were not looking at an angel hot from heaven. Rather, they were staring at deviltry itself; gawking at a fiend whose place was behind bars.
Ease and charm
But the Kenyatta I saw that day was certainly not the bubbly man I had seen in 1952 addressing tens of thousands of people with enviable ease and charm. Then, he was a towering figure donning a bushy beard and haranguing robustly in a voice that spoke volumes about his favourable health and also anger. He was not quite the same Kenyatta of July 1952 whose beaded belt shone in the sun as he addressed crowds of eager people who had turned up at Ruring'u Stadium in Nyeri to hear the last word on whence their salvation would come.
This was a Kenyatta who had suffered bad health in prison.
Then the day of hope and glory for the Kenyan African, too long forgotten in the shadows, came. It was June 1, 1963, the day of self government. The scene was outside Harambee House. As I sat among people of all races to witness this significant event I thought to myself, what a wonderful day!
Soon after, the Prime Minister appointed me his first Secretary to the Cabinet in the new Government. It was my moment to serve not only Kenya but also Kenyatta, a political colossus and a man separated from me by a big age difference.
A political colossus
This marked the beginning of a journey I was both anxious and excited about. Within a short time, Kenyatta, in a most welcome turn of events, gave me a chance to discover who he really was. He let me see through the big hero that I considered him to be.
Soon after, I was to learn a number of things about my boss. For example, he had a long memory and was of particularly sharp intellect. He liked to approach issues from a grass-roots perspective, yet always blending sophistication and simplicity with amazing ingenuity. I also learnt that he hated bureaucracy and was never rash at making decisions. Kenyatta's timekeeping was immaculate. As a leader, he considered himself a worker leading other workers. Kenyatta was a good listener. With time, I noticed too that he loathed paper work and that files were not part of his office desk. He liked information presented in notes and letters prepared for signature, pronto. A man of simple habits, Kenyatta loved roast bananas, well-baked and rolled into a ball.
There were goofs as well as opportunities for on-job training as time went by. We, for instance, learnt sooner rather than later that the protocol measures that we designed did not go well with everybody, least of all the former freedom fighters who felt that they deserved direct access to Kenyatta. I remember personally restraining Field Marshall Muthoni, one of the most distinguished women freedom fighters, from forcing her way into Kenyatta's office. Foremost among tasks tied to my docket was to guarantee the best possible welfare and administrative support to Permanent Secretaries whose work translated directly to the success of the government. Periodical meetings were held with Permanent Secretaries who reported practically everything to do with the public to my office.
What precipitated my wish to leave my job at OP were small matters, but like the proverbial camel, my back was weighing under the last straw. Charles Njonjo and Bruce Mackenzie had made my job redundant. They had created new channels so that decisions to appoint civil servants were sometimes taken behind my back. The foregoing piled upon manifold State affairs tasked to my docket was indeed a donkey load. I could have latched onto my job since Mzee still had confidence in me, but there were now other bridges between the President and the running of the country. My space had been infringed and so had my ability to excel. I had no doubt that I could not perform as well as I would have wished with such cross-currents blowing midstream against a well established rhythm.
Old age and arthritis
I suspected that he (Kenyatta) was prompted in his impatience to have me take up my job (as Governor of the Central Bank) by the fact that its caretaker, Dr Leon Baranski, was being affected by creeping old age and arthritis. He had been loaned to Kenya by the World Bank on assurance that he would not stay for long. A man of 79 and frail looking, he would come to the office late in the morning and only when he was needed. I had to cut my leave short immediately and join Dr Baranski who handed over to me the day I reported to work. I was to get help subsequently from Dr Simiensky who headed the research department, and Mr Abdallah, my deputy.
The currency function of the Central Bank caught my attention immediately. The issuance of notes and coins had been transferred from the East African Currency Board to the Central Bank of Kenya. Attendant to that was the issue of subdividing and paying foreign currency dues to the former member states of the Board.
There were new currency notes already but with my arrival, a new set had to be issued bearing my signature. This may sound trivial and an effort to pander to the vanity of the new Governor. However, that action had substance in that it enhanced sovereignty and marketing. With the signature of the new African Governor on notes, Kenyans could now say, "Now there is the thumb mark of one of our own!"
To make the new notes even more Kenyan and acceptable, we embossed the portrait of the President on them with a caption saying that he was the first President of the Republic of Kenya. That went down very well with Mzee and also with the people. Kenyans then - as today - thought that a large amount of money used in exchange is in bank notes. Notes are actually a small amount of money used in exchange of goods and services. The larger part of paper money consists of paper money in bank deposits.
That people saw the face of money only in bank notes was illustrated many times when new bank notes were transported aboard open flat bed trailers under guard from the Nairobi Railway Station to the Central Bank as was the practice when such notes arrived from Mombasa. Bystanders would look with awe at the trailers under the guard of General Service Unit sharp shooters and say, "How can the Government say that there is no money? Where are those twelve trailers taking all this money?" What they did not realise was that the lorries were transporting pieces of paper to the vaults of the Central Bank. The pieces of paper would only become money after commercial banks bought the notes from the Central Bank.
No less than Kenyatta was caught in the same illusion the first time he visited the Central Bank. We took him to the bank vaults and on seeing the cartons and crates of bank notes stacked all over, his mouth seemed to sag. He then looked over his shoulder at his security detail. He waved them away impatiently. A sight so privileged could unsettle such humble minds!
When his security men were gone, he asked me in almost a whisper, "Ndegwa, is all this money?" I replied in the affirmative. Well, I had told a "lie" because all those currency notes were mere paper. We would not have incurred a loss if all of it had been consumed by fire. Money, in reality, gets value only after it is issued to banks and used as a means of exchange, store of value and unit of accounting. The so-called bank notes are in essence a simple IOU.
Man who signed currency notes
The myths about what went on behind the closed doors of the Central Bank knew no bounds. No wonder there was a public story about old notes. They were said to end up in the hands of the favoured at the Bank and not in the furnace where they were officially supposed to end up.
The myth about "the man who signed currency notes" soared, at times to my embarrassment, least not when I was introduced in those words in public meetings during the Gikuyu-Embu-Meru Association (Gema) meetings. In one incident in Kiambu, a speaker waxed lyrical about the wisdom of Mzee having appointed "one of our own, a man from Kiambu District to be signing every bank note in the country." My only qualification for being a Kiambu man was that I lived in Riara Ridge near Kiambu. Another man expressed disgust with what he thought was my failure. He claimed that golf was taking so much of my time that I did not have adequate opportunity to sign enough bank notes - hence the shortage of money.
In my own village, I was expected to dispense some of the bank notes that were supposed to have come my way in the process of signing them. The people there thought that, like Abunwasi - the trickster from Baghdad in stories of old who ate the food of the poor just by sniffing it - I could acquire bank notes by just seeing or sniffing them. They would not have believed it if I told them that the smell of new notes was not inviting at all since it reminded me of raw timber and even caused me migraines at times.
"Walking in Kenyatta Struggles, My Story" by Duncan Ndegwa. Published by Kenya Leadership Institute, Nairobi, 2006. To order, ring: Nairobi 343199/218673. Available in bookshops from tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: Thorny Issues in Race for ODM Ticket
Headline: Kenya: Thorny Issues in Race for ODM Ticket
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange leaders were in the week at pains to paper over fractures in the party, even as Mr Kalonzo Musyoka launched his presidential bid.
The wedge issues this time round were the boycott of Mr Musyoka's function by the other leaders and a controversial proposal to lock out "Banana zones" from the ODM presidential nominations.
The Sunday Nation has learnt that a section of leaders want the areas which voted for Banana in the referendum not to take part in the presidential primaries.
Sources who attended a meeting on Tuesday at the Nairobi Safari Club said arguments arose on whether the party would want to open its candidates to "votes from enemies and other competitors".
The meeting was convened by a committee mandated to draw the party's constitution and presidential election rules. If the proposal is accepted, only about 150 constituencies will participate in electing the ODM-K candidate.
Mr Odinga
The proposal, the Sunday Nation learnt, was first presented by Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga who was supported by Mvita MP Najib Balala and his Mbita counterpart Otieno Kajwang'.
However, MPs Marsden Madoka and Kiema Kilonzo, a key ally of Mr Musyoka and committee members Donald Kipkorir and Samuel Chepkonga opposed the proposal arguing that all parts of Kenya should participate in electing the party's candidate. Mr Mudavadi and Mr Marende did not support either of the two sides, it was said.
Banana areas
Those opposed to the "Banana areas" participating in choosing the party's flag bearer argue that opponents of ODM-K will infiltrate the party and rig the elections in favour of a weaker candidate and in so doing enhance President Kibaki's chances of returning to State House.
But those opposing the suggestion said ODM-K would be starting from a disadvantaged position if it left out certain regions during its presidential primaries.
The committee, however, has stuck with its initial proposals that all areas participate in the ODM-K presidential election primaries and that sub-locations be the voting centres. If the proposal is ratified by MPs allied to ODM-K in a retreat in January then, a total of 50 delegates in the 7,000 sub-locations will vote for a candidate of their choice.
As to the failure by rivals to attend the launch of his campaign, Mr Musyoka told the Sunday Nation that he understood the predicament of other presidential hopefuls considering that they were also in the race.
He dismissed the suggestion that his colleagues boycotted the function saying: "They wouldn't want to be seen to endorse one candidate. We spoke and we are all together."
In the race
Eldoret North MP William Ruto said they wanted to allow Mr Musyoka to "sell himself" without them appearing to endorse him when they were all in the race.
"It is a principal of fair play to give a competitor a chance to give his vision," the Eldoret North MP said.
The Mwingi North MP said he launched his vision on Monday because the elections were approaching and wanted Kenyans to judge and evaluate his pledges early enough before the main contest among the six presidential hopefuls.
He also encouraged his colleagues in the race to launch their blue print so that Kenyans can evaluate them.
"Next year begins next month and that is why I have launched it. We are preparing to fight for a ticket for this two-horse race," he said in a telephone interview.
Mr Musyoka defended his record arguing that he had fought for the current democratic space which he said was being ruined by the Kibaki administration.
He said he had even stood up against former President Moi by declaring he was going to contest the presidency on a Kanu ticket in the 2002 General Election.
Stand for democracy
"How many of us were able to stand for democracy the way I did in 2002? Mr Moi chastised us but I told him I will not change my mind," the Mwingi North MP added in reference to the former President's insistence that they back Mr Kenyatta.
He said that because of his firm stand on issues, he was sacked from the Kibaki administration and he also spearheaded the campaign against the proposed new constitution.
"Is it sweet music to be sacked from the Government? The year between 2002 and 2006 brought out the real me," he said.
He argued that if it were not for his firm stand, he would still be serving as a minister and would even be President Kibaki's running mate. "I have struggled a lot. I could be a very comfortable Foreign Affairs minister in the Kibaki government today, even his running mate."
His background, he added, was unique as he came from a poor family and experienced life as many Kenyans have. "We are stressing equal opportunity. I represent the aspirations of millions of Kenyans. Some have gone through poverty, others through detention while others through lavish lives but whatever path, we are all in ODM-K," he said. The former Foreign Affairs minister described his relationship with Mr Odinga as cordial.

Original_Search_Title: Chagga Christmas: Always Fun-Packed Season
Headline: Tanzania: Chagga Christmas: Always Fun-Packed Season
Body_Text:
Arusha — The annual Chagga holiday ritual is on - it's a few days away. It is a yearly ritual observed by most of the 'Wachaggas' "As a tradition I do visit my home area in Marangu every year during the X-Mas and New Year holidays. Every year come mid December we will find ourselves back home," said Augusta Mboya a resident of Njiro in Arusha.
Often described as "the hidden jewel of the Chagga land," Marangu boasts many attractions for the visitor. First on your itinerary should be a visit to the KINAPA gate, where climbers of the rooftop of Africa start - Marangu route - to acclimatize the mountainous vegetation. Then at least take in the beauty of the rolling countryside.
Market days conducted on rotation basis from one area to another are a highlight for visitors. At Kinyange, nestled away in peaceful, idyllic area surrounded by grass thatched huts and the sprawling village of Sembeti - the market is quite a spectacle for the unsuspecting visitor. The aroma of fruit, veggies and bananas hangs in the air and the buzz of the market throughout the gigantic space. Hundreds of stands advertise everything from fresh produce, live poultry and used clothes [mitumba] to "yebo yebo" sandals.
Yet all this only scratches of the surface - there are waterfalls and for the slightly more adventurous Mandara hut perched on 2700m offers great chances of exploring the mountains topography and scenery.
Having done a tour of the area visitors are keen to find a place to lay their weary heads. Marangu has a wide range of accommodation on offer - from simple budget self-catering to more upmarket bed-and-breakfast establishments.
Many hotel establishments say up to 90 per cent of their guests during X-Mas and New Year book well in advance. Many families from Dar es Salaam, Arusha, Nairobi and other areas are known to spend their festive season in Marangu.
It's doubtful if Hans Meyer and Mzee Lauwo would recognize the area today, however much they loved it in their travel in 1889. Hans Meyer was the first foreigner who conquered the summit of the Kilimanjaro and Lauwo was his porter.
During the X-Mas and New Year holidays the sky in Kilimanjaro region would be invariably overcast, the showers, wind-chill factor in some place, horde of people arriving and unpacking, drinking beers and 'mbege' [the traditional drink]. For some reason it is always a fun packed time!
Christmas and New Year holidays are used to be largely excuse for excesses. Being naturally pleasure-seeking, many a people spend most of the holiday in an alcoholic haze. And the Wachaggas are not exception - they really drink to their fill!
During Christmas last year in Marangu it turned out to be a highly sociable experience for my family and I, even when none of us dared to drink the famous "mbege". The Wachaggas best-known local food - Machalali - is the must inclusion of the holiday sumptuous menus nearly always during this time. We had a feel of the local traditional food, unknown to us earlier, but turned out to be a delicious meal.
While merrymaking with a family up in Mamba Kotela courtesy of Mrs. Yesse Moshi and her family, we found almost all homes busy as families moved from one house to another to either have a meal or enjoy a drink. Every one in this place was generous. As a person from the Lake region I still consider the North in particular the Wachagga areas to be the most beautiful during these holidays, whose populations equally soars and holding up better.
The Chagga population seemed to have emptied itself out en masse, and it was exciting, lively crowd with chattering loudly over music of the season most of us hear during this time round.
Our ravenous appetites were soon satisfied as endless plates of food were served to us on beautifully laid tables. We relished in the luxury and ate much more than is usually considered respectable.
During the year, Kilimanjaro Mountain Resort in Marangu Kyalla is a Mecca for families looking for odd-world hospitality and peace. "Guests come from different regions and they are very excited to see one another every year" said Mrs. Elizabeth Njiu the Director of the resort. "They come here for freedom in our gardens and good service. Our biggest advantage is our price-friendly policy, safety and comfort of children" she added.
Strengthened we admired the architecture at the resort, wandered down through walkways and eventually made our way to the gardens. Walking really needs nourishment and in all the gardens, the options are so attractive.
No wonder Marangu has been a unique holiday destination for so long perhaps the only surprise is that more people have not yet experienced it for themselves.

Original_Search_Title: Vatican Acts On Kenya's Political Priests
Headline: Kenya: Vatican Acts On Kenya's Political Priests
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Catholic clerics now have orders linked to the Vatican warning them not to dabble in politics or even accept public appointments.
The five-point edict decreed by the Kenya Episcopal Conference on behalf of the church's 27 bishops, demarcates fresh no go-zones for the Catholic clergy, including Constituency Development Fund, trade, and appointments to State corporations and boards.
Against some of the orders are caveat: they must secure permission from their bishops. The circular by the church's most powerful organ comes against the background of increased recruitment of clerics by the Government to serve in public commissions and boards for which members draw sitting allowances.
It is believed the edict was forced by fear within the religious arena that the Government was trying to smoothen the ground for next year's election by roping influential members of the church.
Although the pastoral letter does not single out individuals, one clergyman easily comes into picture, Father Dominic Wamugunda, the Dean of Students, University of Nairobi.
Outraged by open support for Government
Dr Wamugunda not only participated actively in the Banana campaign at the referendum but also served as Head of Communications at the 'Yes' secretariat. If he was paid allowances for his services, then this contravenes the priesthood canon law.
But even more recently, the clergyman was appointed a director of the Postal Corporation. The church, in the circular, could be also combating perception that its shrunken criticism of the Government could be influenced by the fact it counts among its members President Kibaki and his principal assistant Mr Moody Awori.
The Saturday Standard established the office of Pope Benedict XVI might have had a hand in the move. Outraged by open support for Government by some of the church's clergymen and failure to reprimand those seen to have transgressed the church-state divide, a section of the Catholic Bishops petitioned the Pope through his Kenyan representative.
"This happened immediately after the referendum because we wanted direction from Vatican on how to go about the situation locally," divulged our source.
Archbishop Allain Lebeaupin accordingly consulted with the Pope, who reportedly directed that action be taken, hence the latest pastoral letter released to priests through the church's dioceses across the country.
"Priests are not permitted to accept positions on State or Corporation Boards," states one of the so-called practical guidelines.
Efforts to engage Church in Government programmes
Another decrees: "In the area of party politics, priests should not take sides, as it will divide the local community. Canvassing or even urging people to vote for particular candidates or parties leads to confusion and division."
The warning comes in the wake of efforts by the Government to "fully engage" the Church in its programmes. Recently, provincial administrators in Nyanza were asked to furnish the Government with the lists of all church leaders in their jurisdiction.
A circular released by the Nyanza Provincial Commissioner Mr Paul Olando to all the District Commissioners wanted them to state the number of all churches in each sub-location.
Pastor Isaiah Owak of the Church of God the Eternal in Migori District said they had handed over the names to the chief.
"Chiefs and their assistants have been going round collecting the data and we have responded to their demands."
However, Owak said it was not made clear why the Government was seeking information from them.
Another church leader, Bishop Walter Ogwada of the Migori Gospel Church said: "It is true we have received the requests and we are yet to know what the Government wants because they have taken our contacts."
Catholic priests lined up for jobs
Migori MP Mr Owino Likowa alleged the Government wanted to enlist the support of the churches in the next year's campaigns. He further claimed that the PCs, DCs, DOs and chiefs and their assistants was secretly executing a plan to use the administrators to shore up support for President Kibaki next year.
"We are already aware of the underground schemes and we have devised a counter strategy, "he claimed.
The circular, dated November 19, comes weeks to the election year and following increased participation of Catholic priests in Government political projects, including last year's constitutional referendum.
Wamugunda is not alone. The Saturday Standard established that there are a host of Catholic priests lined up for jobs, in what looks like a campaign strategy by the Government for next year's elections.
There also appears to be an effort to have them included in the CDF committees - a plot that could well project the Government's development record.
Bishop John Njue, Chairman of the Kenya Episcopal Conference, would rather treat it as a "mere house-keeping routine".
"We are entering an election year and we have a mission of ensuring that our clergymen remain focused on their divine duty," he told The Saturday Standard.
"This is a pastoral exercise and clergymen have a specific mandate of serving their flock. This is why we circulated the pastoral letter as a reminder," he explained.
His deputy, Bishop Philip Sulumeti of Kakamega Diocese, contests the notion advanced by Opposition politicians that they (clergy) abandoned their flock during the referendum.
Church has been involved in various exercises
Church leaders, he argues, read the document thoroughly and accordingly pointed out its strengths and weakness but left the final decision to their worshippers.
However, on Wamugunda, Bishop Njue who is also the Bishop of Nyeri, said Archbishop Ndingi Mwana a' Nzeki, who is in charge of Nairobi, was better placed to respond.
"That I cannot deal with. Ordinarily before one takes up a government job, which we highly discourage, one has to seek permission from the head of his diocese," he added.
The Catholics constitute an estimated 40 per cent of Kenya's population. Sulumeti himself maintains that he is a neutral servant of God who supports no specific politician - a factor that has endeared him to many over the last 40 years of his priesthood.
"Even for the position of President, as a Church we do not affiliate ourselves to given candidates and will not do so in the coming year. However, ours is to point out at qualities of a good leader and leave it unto the people to decide," he explained.
The Church, not necessarily the Catholic, has been involved in various exercises, especially those related to the constitution review where their arbitration skills and non-partisan faces help break the ice between competing forces.
Bishop Sulumeti was a chairman of the committee at the Bomas Constitutional Conference, selected to identify and harmonise the so-called contentious issues.
"If one is committed to serve God, he should only do so alone because "as Jesus Christ aptly said you cannot serve two masters at a go," argued the former seminarian.

Original_Search_Title: Arts Education to Be Pushed in Schools
Headline: Namibia: Arts Education to Be Pushed in Schools
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Namibian arts have enormous educational value and potential, presenting a multitude of learning and teaching opportunities to learners in the country.
So said the arts educational officer of NIED, Calvin Martin, last week during a one-day consultative meeting on the importance of arts in education between representatives of the Oruuano Artists Union and academic art experts from Finland.
According to Martin, learners directly involved in arts are more motivated, more engaged, more sensitive, more focused, creative and responsible.
"The question is are we offering our learners an arts curriculum that allows them to grow in totality? In my view the possible significance of arts in the education of the Namibian youth is largely unrecognized, often ignored and generally underrated. It is a fact that the arts are being pushed to the curricula periphery," Martin seriously charged.
He reflected on changes in the existing Namibian arts curriculum in schools.
"The reason for these changes is that art is first and foremost a developmental subject because it is difficult to prescribe a rigid progression. As the learner develops, the command of the art topics and expression should become more sophisticated. As a result the duplication of learning objectives and basic competencies that was evident in the old syllabuses have been avoided," he said.
According to the Finnish delegation art and skills subjects have equal positions with mathematics and science in their education system.
The Minister of Education, Nangolo Mbumba, urged Namibian artists to take up the challenges offered by arts in education.
"We encourage and support efforts to promote arts and culture in our schools so much so that we are all the time expanding our efforts. We need to get more Namibian artists involved in our efforts," said Minister Mbumba, who received copies of four old books as a gift from the Finnish delegation.
"I see this one-day workshop between Oruuano Artists Union and the Finnish delegation as an important starting point for introducing arts back to schools in Namibia," said the Finnish Chargé d'Affaires, Seija Kinni-Huttunen at the same occasion last Friday.
According to her the Finnish government feels strongly about arts and culture and has been supporting art efforts by the local union and other community art and culture promoters for a number of years and will continue to do so.
Banana Shekupe, president of Oruuano Artists Union, emphasized the fact that Namibian school-going children are still marginalized because they do not have access to proper arts education.
"It is imperative that the whole education system in the country be readjusted to accommodate the creative hopes, dreams and aspiration of learners. My union can do much more to assist in these efforts, if we are financially supported by government and other stakeholders," Shekupe said.
A formal agreement of assistance to the Oruuano Artists Union was signed between the two delegations. The union will be assisted by the Finns to help implement arts education in Namibian schools.

Original_Search_Title: The Battle for New Districts in Bushenyi
Headline: Uganda: The Battle for New Districts in Bushenyi
Body_Text:
Bushenyi — Bushenyi is divided over the split of district into five districts.
Ministers Tarsis Kabwegyere for Disaster preparedness, relief, Richard Nduhuura (State for Health, General duties) Kahinda Otafiire (Local government) and the LC5 chairman Longino Ndyanabo are opposed to the division of Bushenyi.
Mr Kabwegyere recently said the areas that were granted district status when he was minister for local government are now limping in terms of development.
"RDCs don't have vehicles, district councillors don't get sitting allowances and many other problems," he said.
Mr Kabwegyere held this position until early this year.
During his term in office, Mbarara district was split into four districts of Isingiro, Ibanda, Kiruhura and Mbarara.
Maj. Gen. Otafiire said he would not table the proposal before the Cabinet should it come to his desk. Back home his Ruhinda County has also joined the fight for a district.
Ruhinda's seven sub-counties have already endorsed the idea. The move has been spearheaded by the area LC5 chairman Benon Karyaija.
The Minister of State for Industry and MP for Sheema South Ephraim Kamuntu is agitating for the split. He wants his county to become a district. Others in support of the idea are Dr Elioda Tumwesigye of Sheema North, Mr Deus Bikwasizehi of Buhweju and Gadioza Kabondo of Bunyaruguru County.
Buhweju county leaders are angry with Mr Kabwegyere. Burere LC5 councillor Biraro Nganshanga recently called a press conference in Bushenyi town to dispute Kabwegyere's ideas on the creation of new districts. "Prof. Kabwegyere's refusal to split Bushenyi district is selfish. How many areas did he grant district status when he was a minister of local government?" Biraro asked.
In Buhweju county, the district status search has united the opposition party FDC and NRM members. The former FDC parliamentary candidate Francis Mwijukye has joined his opponent area MP Deus Bikwasizehi in the quest for a district. He is mobilising the youth to support the creation of new districts.
Residents of Bunyaruguru county argue that they should be granted a district status because they speak Orunyaruguru whereas other people in the district speak Runyankole.
Led by their MP Gadioza Kabondo, Bunyaruguru residents argue that they are financially capable of running a district.
They say they grow bananas, fish from over 20 lakes in the county, and have Queen Elizabeth National Park for tourism and many other income generating ventures.
"The split is the wish of most people. Revolutions are demand driven," UPC's Maj. Edward Rurangaranga said.
Many are hoping that President Museveni listens to the residents' plea and not the ministers.

Original_Search_Title: Theatre's Golden Celebration Starts
Headline: Uganda: Theatre's Golden Celebration Starts
Body_Text:
Kampala — Celebrations to mark 50 years of theatre in Uganda begin in earnest this weekend with the opening of Theatre La Bonita this Friday.
His Royal Highness the Kabaka of Buganda, Ronald Muwenda Mutebi will cut the ribbon for what will become the new home of The Ebonies. The theatre group's new play, Enigmatic Palpitations- Gutujja premieres the same day.
The other highlights during this golden celebration will be a Grand Comedy Show by Theatre Factory on Thursday December 21 at the National Theatre's upper gardens. The show will feature the best comedy skits of the year.
Ndere Centre will also open its theatre on Friday December 22 while renowned playwright Alex Mukulu will also restage one of his most critically acclaimed works, 30 Years of Bananas at the National Theatre starting Saturday December 23.
The play, released 15 years ago to mark Uganda's 30th Independence anniversary, takes a peek into the calibre of leaders Uganda has had since the British handed over power in 1962.
Another feature of theatre's golden jubilee will be the introduction of the Theatre Artiste of the Month initiative in January 2007. The celebration will climax into the World Theatre Day celebration in March next year.

Original_Search_Title: Shitanda Camp Cries Foul Over Ford-K Grassroots Elections
Headline: Kenya: Shitanda Camp Cries Foul Over Ford-K Grassroots Elections
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Housing minister, Mr Soita Shitanda has given Ford-Kenya election board up to Monday to sort out irregularities in the party's grassroots elections.
Shitanda said he had a list of 14 MPs who will walk out of the party.
He said the MPs would also boycott the national elections set for Wednesday next week if the anomalies are not rectified.
The party's Deputy Secretary General, Mr Otieno K'Opiyo, said Shitanda, who was beaten in his Malava constituency by a rival group siding with Local Government minister, Mr Musikari Kombo, would run for chairmanship in his capacity as an MP.
K'Opiyo said they intend to finish the "mission" against Kombo at the national polls and warned the minister to prepare for a titanic duel.
The former Kasipul-Kabondo MP, who is an ally of the Housing minister, declared Kombo a Narc-Kenya sympathiser and warned that his days in Ford-Kenya were numbered.
"Some of us backed the Banana camp unknowingly after Kombo entered a deal with Kibaki without consulting anyone.
"He is again trying to drive the party into the Narc-Kenya tribal axis and we cannot accept this," K'Opiyo declared.
The politician, who was speaking to The Standard, claimed that his camp was holding talks with a number of delegates countrywide to ensure a shocker for Kombo at the polls.
"I am telling you, by next week, Kombo will be no more. We shall set him free to join Narc-Kenya," K'Opiyo said.
The official denied that a group of politicians opposed to Kombo were sponsoring the Shitanda camp financially.
He said the allegations were far fetched and explained that Shitanda was capable of financing his political activities.
On Thursday, Special Programme minister Mr John Munyes, was quoted as saying they would reveal Shitanda's bank rollers if he did not stop his war against Kombo.
However, Kombo remained focused to the national election on December 20.
The chairman is reported to have denied knowledge of any irregularities he has influenced in the registration of the party officials, who have won at the grassroots.
Kombo instead said that the election board was handling the matter and he was not involved at all.
Shitanda maintained that ODM-Kenya officials have been registered as Ford-Kenya official in some areas.
He added that the irregularities would affect the party's popularity at the grassroots.
Shitanda said they have to meet the Director of Elections, Dr Noah Wekesa by Monday to sort out the irregularities. Wekesa is currently out of the country.

Original_Search_Title: A Year Later, a Step Backwards
Headline: Kenya: A Year Later, a Step Backwards
Body_Text:
Nairobi — IN THE RUN UP TO THE 2005 referendum, there was a significant body of opinion that counselled a delay, pleading with the protagonists to allow room for dialogue so that unresolved issues could be negotiated before subjecting an unfair referendum on the citizenry.
This group was informed by the realisation that the nation was being called to referee what was essentially a political contest, and that the sacrificial lamb was going to be the constitution.
But drunk with recently acquired power, the Banana team could not hear any talk of postponement. As for the Orange team, it was show-time; they could smell blood and they were determined to win the battle they had lost over the MoU.
Fortunately for the Orange crowd, they had also acquired new allies, those that Narc had vanquished in 2002 and those that the new Government had disappointed in its first year in office.
And so the battle-lines were drawn, and the ever-trusting Wanjiku naively assumed the fight was about her interests and so fought hard on either side.
The referendum was won by Orange. They celebrated "their" win only to realise later that we were back to square one. As far as the constitution reform process was concerned, we may even have taken a step backwards.
A year later, Wanjiku has realised that she was conned all the way. There is no new constitution in sight. The autocracies of the existing constitutional order rear their ugly heads every so often, and she enters into another election year hoping that the next breed of political leaders will deliver on the reform promise.
The only problem is that on both sides of the political divide lie reactionaries who have age-old anti-reform credentials. It is to these people that she must place all her hope on a new constitution.
And now the talk about minimum and comprehensive reforms gives the impression that we are making progress. But all this is political talk, and though interesting, is not clothed in reality. It is now accepted that constitution reform has become hostage to our dysfunctional politics, and that to expect the same political class to resolve the constitutional impasse is to be unbelievably naive.
In any event, the constitutional reform agenda has never really been driven by politicians in Kenya's illustrious history. It has been driven by the Church, civil society and the media.
Unfortunately, having taken a beating during the referendum, these reform parents abandoned their infant baby. Its new adoptive parents appear incapable of bringing it up!
Wanjiku needs to disabuse herself of the notion that the political class, in power now or their erstwhile competition, have the capacity to take this process any further.
And so, the religious community, civil society and the media must retake the onus of leading this process. The Government's facilitative role must continue, but it cannot have the final word on the process.
As for the minimalist agenda, there is no doubt that to go into the 2007 elections under the current electoral rules would be a fraud. But I can think of 10 other reform issues that are just as critical as balancing the electoral field. And any Kenyan must be worried that once the electoral issues are resolved, the clamour for comprehensive reforms will be forgotten.
And so if there are to be any minimums, they must start with the entrenchment of a revised process in the Constitution. If the politicians cannot deliver that to Kenyans, then all other minimums which resolve political concerns should wait their turn.

Original_Search_Title: Maka Helps Girls to Stay in School
Headline: Uganda: Maka Helps Girls to Stay in School
Body_Text:
Amid the noise of children playing in the afternoon sun is a softer sound - as a lady crushes fresh papyrus reeds into finer fibre with a hammer.
She is glad she is playing a big part in the retention of the girl child in primary school - who does not only lack affordable sanitary pads but also faces a host of patriarchal cultures, physical and psychological problems in her quest for education.
With beads of sweat dripping from her face under the shadow of her one-roomed house, she tells me with confidence: "I am a woman and a parent and I know the importance of sanitary pads. The cheap ones made out of these papyrus reeds will cater for the needy girl child."
She adds that she feels more useful to the community as a result of her craft to have more poor girls stay in school by processing papyrus reeds into finer fibre to manufacture low cost sanitary pads. Seated on a wooden stool in Factory Zone, Natete, Rubaga in Kampala, Florence Namuwonge, a mother of two does not disguise the passion for her work.
"I love this work and besides, I also earn some income to contribute to the welfare of my family."
Namuwonge processes 4 kilogrammes of papyrus per day with each kilogramme going for Shs1,500. Her skill involves peeling the outer skin off the fresh papyrus reeds, leaving the soft inner part that she crushes, as the fibre drops onto the clean tarpaulin in front of her house to dry.
She has no kind words for the rain that makes it hard for her to dry the "daily bread". "Whenever it rains, I cannot dry the fibre," Namuwonge says. Her processed papyrus including that of others' in this project is later taken and mixed with waste paper to form absorbents used to make the Maka pads.
Affordable
The pads are made from 95 percent local materials and the absorbent is from natural materials except for the non-woven material and polythene paper used to wrap, under the Provision of Affordable Sanitary Pads to the Disadvantaged Primary School Girls in Uganda project.
The project now manufactures sanitary pads which are 75 percent cheaper and thus more affordable to the rural primary school girls, and their absorbent capacity is three to eight times that of other pads on the market. One pad can be used for 8-12 hours.
A packet of 10 Maka pads costs Shs650 compared to Shs1,500-Shs2,800 for conventional sanitary pads. With the majority of Ugandans living below a dollar a day, this is out of reach and not a priority for some.
In addition to producing safe and cheap sanitary pads, other project achievements include developing appropriate technology of simple, small and easy to operate cottage machines which are locally manufactured. The project has also enabled skills and knowledge transfer, allowing decentralisation in outsourcing its services.
The making of the papyrus sanitary pads under the trademark "Maka pads" is within the Increasing Retention Through Improved Literacy and Learner Friendly Schools in Uganda Project, supported by the Rockefeller Foundation, aimed at creating a leaner-friendly environment in schools.
A pilot project has been rolled out in 12 primary schools in the districts of Kampala, Mpigi, Luweero and Nakasongola where girls receive Maka pads free of charge and future plans are to have it spread nation wide both in primary and secondary schools, and replicated in the Sub Region. Unfortunately, Shs650 is still high for an average family as Esther Kojo and Eunice Ajok told.
Prior to joining the Maka pads pilot project, both their mothers asked them to use soft cloth or cotton as sanitary pads, a common practice among the poor. "My mother told me to use soft cloth as pads because she couldn't afford the expensive ones," said 14-year-old Kojo.
"Blood would seep through the cloth pads and the Maka pads are far better although they itch sometimes," said Kojo whose mother is a nurse in Kampala while her father is unemployed.
Kojo who is in P.5 and Ajok in P.6 at Railway Primary School, Nsambya in Kampala are examples of the urban poor that cannot afford other pad brands on the market, and who are the main targets of the Maka pads sanitary pads project. Both Kojo and Ajok did not hesitate to recommend Makapads for other girls.
"I have not received any complaints by the girls using Maka pads since the project started two years ago," Miss Catherine Musisi, Railway Primary School's Senior Woman Teacher, chipped in. "They are comfortable and last a long time. My suggestion is that the packet should contain 12 pads because some girls' menstruation periods last six days or more," Musisi said.
Dr. Moses Kizza Musaazi, the brain behind Maka pads noted that the reactions to the project vary. "Some people believe it's too good to be true, and others are sceptical. The majority, mainly women and girls are excited because it's timely and long over due. While on the other hand I thinÃ¡S¡W9Æ;þ-ôt know that such a problem existed."
Musaazi, the principal investigator, Appropriate Technology, Faculty of Technology, Makerere University said the entire production process is labour intensive by the communities and the only cost is electricity needed to seal the pads.
"We want to involve the beneficiaries in the production process so that the Shs650 is divided into a number of items. The beneficiaries should absorb the profit as well as the labour component for we don't make a big profit."
Easy to make
The papyrus which is the main raw material is processed manually and a school can absorb this cost, Musaazi said. "Then we get the processed papyrus and mix it with free waste paper to dry. And here with 90 percent done, nothing is costly. The absorbent has been made and after that, it's simply cutting, softening and integrating (sealing) the non-woven with polythene paper," he explains.
Uganda is home to vast expanses of wetlands whose services and products contribute millions of shillings to the economy. More importantly, in the informal sector, millions of people depend on wetlands and their products to sustain their livelihoods.
About four years ago, the Rockefeller Foundation realised that sanitary facilities were poor and inadequate and approached Musaazi to design an appropriate toilet facility for primary schools, having constructed one for Makerere University Primary School which lacked disposal of sanitary pads.
"The Rockefeller Foundation wanted a disposal mechanism for sanitary pads, which I did by designing an incinerator that burns used sanitary pads to ash and any other waste apart from glass and metal," Musaazi revealed. "But then it said that was not enough because if a girl does not have sanitary pad, then the incinerator was only partially useful."
An earlier study by the Rockefeller Foundation had proved that many girls missed school during their menstruation periods due to lack of affordable protection materials on an average of four days a month accumulating to 12 days in a term (10 percent) out of the 90 days of a term.
This means a girl who cannot afford sanitary pads will miss 36 days of school in a year. Lack of protection meant that girls feared stigmatisation due to soiling themselves while at school. Some girls were already using unhealthy materials such as banana fibres, grass, leaves, old newspapers and old pieces of cloth.
"So many girls find it difficult to concentrate and perform well, or drop out of school completely due to lack of sanitary pads. The reason is that they cannot get appropriate sanitary protection during menstruation periods so to avoid embarrassment and stigma, they stay at home," Musaazi noted.
In Sub-Saharan Africa, poverty, tradition and ignorance deprive millions of girls even of an elementary school education, and the lack of toilets and water is one of many obstacles to girls' attendance. And until recently, the subject was considered unfit for discussion. In some rural communities in the region, menstruation itself is taboo and girls are prohibited from cooking or even banished to the countryside during their periods.
Research has documented that lack of sanitary pads, a clean, girls-only latrine and water for washing hands drives a significant number of girls from school. The United Nations Children's Education Fund (UNICEF), for example, estimates that one in 10 school-age African girls either skips school during menstruation or drops out entirely because of lack of sanitation.
The average schoolgirl's struggle for privacy is emblematic of the uphill battle for public education in Sub-Saharan Africa, particularly among girls. With slightly more than 6 in 10 eligible children enrolled in primary school, the region's enrolment rates are the lowest in the world.
According to UNICEF, beyond that, enrolment among primary school-aged girls is 8 percent lower than that among boys. And of those girls who enrol, 9 percent more drop out before the end of sixth grade than boys. The Rockefeller Foundation wanted Musaazi to design pads that would not cost more than half a dollar per packet of 10.
"Two years ago, I started on the project and I am glad to report that we now have simple machinery that makes affordable sanitary pads," Musaazi said.
The UN-Habitat has recognised this initiative, terming it as one of the excellent innovations in recent years. Musaazi is confident he has made a contribution towards girl child education: "I have solved a problem of having affordable sanitary pads and offering employment because the beneficiaries can be the users as well as sell off the excess."

Original_Search_Title: Maka Helps Girls to Stay in School
Headline: Uganda: Maka Helps Girls to Stay in School
Body_Text:
Amid the noise of children playing in the afternoon sun is a softer sound - as a lady crushes fresh papyrus reeds into finer fibre with a hammer.
She is glad she is playing a big part in the retention of the girl child in primary school - who does not only lack affordable sanitary pads but also faces a host of patriarchal cultures, physical and psychological problems in her quest for education.
With beads of sweat dripping from her face under the shadow of her one-roomed house, she tells me with confidence: "I am a woman and a parent and I know the importance of sanitary pads. The cheap ones made out of these papyrus reeds will cater for the needy girl child."
She adds that she feels more useful to the community as a result of her craft to have more poor girls stay in school by processing papyrus reeds into finer fibre to manufacture low cost sanitary pads. Seated on a wooden stool in Factory Zone, Natete, Rubaga in Kampala, Florence Namuwonge, a mother of two does not disguise the passion for her work.
"I love this work and besides, I also earn some income to contribute to the welfare of my family."
Namuwonge processes 4 kilogrammes of papyrus per day with each kilogramme going for Shs1,500. Her skill involves peeling the outer skin off the fresh papyrus reeds, leaving the soft inner part that she crushes, as the fibre drops onto the clean tarpaulin in front of her house to dry.
CLOCKWISE FROM LEFT: Florence Namuwonge crushes Papyrus into fibre; factory workers work on the fibre; the finished product. Photos by Morgan Mbabazi
She has no kind words for the rain that makes it hard for her to dry the "daily bread". "Whenever it rains, I cannot dry the fibre," Namuwonge says. Her processed papyrus including that of others' in this project is later taken and mixed with waste paper to form absorbents used to make the Maka pads.
Affordable
The pads are made from 95 percent local materials and the absorbent is from natural materials except for the non-woven material and polythene paper used to wrap, under the Provision of Affordable Sanitary Pads to the Disadvantaged Primary School Girls in Uganda project.
The project now manufactures sanitary pads which are 75 percent cheaper and thus more affordable to the rural primary school girls, and their absorbent capacity is three to eight times that of other pads on the market. One pad can be used for 8-12 hours.
A packet of 10 Maka pads costs Shs650 compared to Shs1,500-Shs2,800 for conventional sanitary pads. With the majority of Ugandans living below a dollar a day, this is out of reach and not a priority for some.
In addition to producing safe and cheap sanitary pads, other project achievements include developing appropriate technology of simple, small and easy to operate cottage machines which are locally manufactured. The project has also enabled skills and knowledge transfer, allowing decentralisation in outsourcing its services.
The making of the papyrus sanitary pads under the trademark "Maka pads" is within the Increasing Retention Through Improved Literacy and Learner Friendly Schools in Uganda Project, supported by the Rockefeller Foundation, aimed at creating a leaner-friendly environment in schools.
A pilot project has been rolled out in 12 primary schools in the districts of Kampala, Mpigi, Luweero and Nakasongola where girls receive Maka pads free of charge and future plans are to have it spread nation wide both in primary and secondary schools, and replicated in the Sub Region. Unfortunately, Shs650 is still high for an average family as Esther Kojo and Eunice Ajok told.
Prior to joining the Maka pads pilot project, both their mothers asked them to use soft cloth or cotton as sanitary pads, a common practice among the poor. "My mother told me to use soft cloth as pads because she couldn't afford the expensive ones," said 14-year-old Kojo.
"Blood would seep through the cloth pads and the Maka pads are far better although they itch sometimes," said Kojo whose mother is a nurse in Kampala while her father is unemployed.

The majority, mainly women and girls are excited because it's timely and long over due. While on the other hand I think men did not know that such a problem existed."

Original_Search_Title: Pay Attention to Your Children's Pleas, Naggings
Headline: Uganda: Pay Attention to Your Children's Pleas, Naggings
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kids grow faster than corn! With Tendo making five early next year, sometimes I feel like life is moving too fast and I cannot stop it! As my kids grow older and as we plan to celebrate more birthdays. I know that the childhood days are slowly going away forever. It hurts a bit sometimes.
The other day, Tendo returned from school very excited because he had been taught how to peel a banana -- it must have been part of the etiquette class. He took every opportunity to show me the proper way of peeling a banana. He also showed his brother Imani and he was really excited.
The next day he returned from school excited about tying shoe laces. That really excited him and in the morning before going to school, he spent 30 minutes trying to tie his shoe laces. He talked about shoe laces until I was tired of listening. In fact I was too busy to pay attention to his stories.
Sometimes, we are too busy to listen to such stories but we do not realise that these good times our children share their stories or discoveries with us really mean a lot.
