I often ask myself: Have I given my best to my kids in their childhood days? These days of asking endless questions, calling and crying for mummy all the time may seem very demanding and endless but the truth is, they quickly vanish like mist. We long for them to come back and they can never do so.
My prayer is to make use of these years and to count them as precious.
The other day, an older friend of mine, who together with her husband have achieved successful careers, told me, almost in tears, that if she were to reverse the clock, she could not trade anything for missing her children's childhood days -- I know what she was talking about.
No wonder I am obsessed with listening to talks about balancing life and work and I try as much as possible to pick a practical piece of advice from them.
Many times I am amazed by the number of solutions you can get by listening to others telling you how they manage their lives. I think we parents of young children very much have to ask ourselves what our priorities are and I certainly know that part of that priority is raising and mentoring those children.
So next time your toddler drowns you with questions and repeated demands for attention, answer them with a smile and know that these questions, like rainbows, will not stay long. Soon they will be over and you will have independent individuals who care less about your opinions.

Original_Search_Title: Servants of the Rich And Their Glamorous Lives
Headline: Uganda: Servants of the Rich And Their Glamorous Lives
Body_Text:
Luxurious life It is the dream of every house help to move up the income ladder and work in a rich neighbourhood; something like moving from serving tea in Kikoni, Bwaise, or Kyebando to pressing a button in Kololo and tea simply appears.
By now, we have settled into the reality that there are men in this Banana Republic who lead a life of kings. And from their wealth, they have made several other people rich, respected and acquire high status in society.
It is not about their friends and business partners; we are talking about their servants: The maids, stewards, valets, barmen, drivers, cooks, chefs, gardeners, interpreters and errand boys to mention but a few. These work for rich folks and that in a sense makes them rich too. Their salaries and fringe benefits could turn a primary school teacher green with envy.
Although they are always at the rich family member's beck and call, these workers lead a different lifestyle from the average househelp or shamba boy. They are always clean, dress smartly and will yank out a flattering and stylish phone.
If they have not bought it from their savings, it is a 'work phone' which they will have to give back after work. Sometimes the phone has been handed over to them by the boss after he purchases a more sophisticated one.
They speak fluent English and are conversant with Kiswahili, and could well know another international language. Their status in society is elevated in a sense that they rub shoulders with their bosses. This privilege has sent some of them places and interacting with other wealthy families. Thus it is not uncommon to find them dropping names here and there and talking big about their bosses and themselves.
They also have cars, usually the oldest car in the parking, the jalopy was once a favourite of the boss before the advent of the Prado. And when the boss is away on a business trip abroad, the driver doesn't stop driving the Prado and Mercedes Benz because he has to drop the children at school. Infact many campus girls have ended up having relationships with rich men's drivers because the girls assume that these drivers are loaded. After all they can speak good English, they are stylish and change posh cars.
The job
The next time you are around Kololo, Nakasero, Muyenga, Ntinda or Upper Naguru Hill where most of these wealthy people stay, don't despise any shamba boy, driver or housemaid you see there. He may be having a CV somewhere; he may even have a certificate or formal training for his job.
SUPERB RESIDENCE: A servant converses with a colleague by the small gate of an impressive house of his boss in Kampala. Photo by Ismail Kezaala
He could have actually done an interview and passed it before he was employed. And even before they started work, they could have gone through an orientation. For instance, the cooks and chefs are sent to professional cooks and catering schools so that they can cook internationally fitting meals.
Among such trainers is Chef, Kadumukasa Kironde II. "Their bosses usually invest in only those ones who they have been with for a long time or those who are highly paid," Kadumukasa says.
According to him, the training ranges from how to prepare soups, salads, starters, main courses, rice dishes, pastry dishes, or and any other specific cuisines. Kadumukasa, whose clients range from diplomatic missions and the middle class who can afford, charges at least Shs400,000 for 20 hours training.
While many women hassle looking for helpers and sending relatives to bring one from upcountry, the wealthy guys use the services of 'house help bureaus'. Alternatively they are handed over a worker from a colleague who is leaving the country.
These servants are paid handsomely and even given tips. In one instance, a boss pays her workers' Social Security to NSSF and gives them loans for their private businesses. These servants have the privilege of living in lavish servant quarters in the leafy suburbs of Kampala. Their children go to good schools. Forget about the house-helps whose children are enrolled in UPE schools deep in a kyalo (village).
The wealthy valet's child attends good boarding schools, private primary schools and fair UPE schools like Shimoni, Nakasero , Kitante or Buganda Road. As they drive the wealthy man's child to Greenhill, Kabira or the French school, these drivers also find a way of dropping their own children at their schools.
While some stay with their families in the rich man's premises, others have rented homes for their families in the suburbs where the middle class usually leave. Thus on their days off (by the way they all must take an off like any other worker) they visit their families.
The neighbours, and sometimes the wife and children do not get to know that he is a servant. What with the nice car parked and a boot full of shoppings and hand me-downs from his 'office' given by the rich person's children or wife.
On duty
Woe unto you if you visit or call the rich person's home. His servant, usually wearing a uniform and headgear, scrutinises you and asks you a million questions as a way of assessing whether you are fit to see his boss. If you fail the 'test', he tells you his boss has gone swimming, bird shooting, playing golf, or is taking a nap.
One reason they are long serving is that these servants are sitting on the secrets of their bosses. Behind the backs of their bosses' spouses, they know their financial secrets, their girlfriends or boyfriends, where they stay, details of one's journey, among others.
Often the drivers and errand boys who go everywhere the boss goes have developed a strong relationship. They are very loyal and will not divulge any secret. In exchange, they get more favours, tips and salary rises.
Civic leaders
In rich suburbs, most of the rich men don't have time for community leadership. Not their servants. In such places, it is the cooks, drivers, gardeners or house helps who constitute the civic leadership.
The advantage is that they know each other well, so voting even gets better. A story is told of one rich man who sent his children to the LCI chairman in the earshot of one of his servants. The children started to choke with laughter because they knew that the LCI chairman was their servant. Actually that was the reason many visitors frequented this home, to see the LC chairman, not the boss!
FINE JOB: A richman's driver changes posh cars and can even hoodwink people that he owns them. Photo by hoto I. Kezaala
Wealthy pets
Tugging after these helpers are the wealthy family's pets. Those animals that have a more balanced diet than you probably have.
Actually, these animals don't eat leftovers picked from kitchens of restaurants, or from the bin. They have special meals and homes to stay.
Obviously the envy of other animals their food is picked off the shelves of plush supermarkets like Game, Shoprite and Uchumi.
In these supermarkets, the price of a pets' food is enough to buy a week's meal for a humble family. Otherwise what do you make of dog food at obscene prices? While people never spend money on pet foods, others part with as much as between Shs6,000 and Shs15,000.
These pets range from dogs, monkeys, birds, and cats, among others. The dogs, especially are a far cry from the dogs in Kivuulu.
While some are flown in from abroad, others are specially bred here. Otherwise, how would you explain a bulldog, a sheep dog, a German shepherd, Labradors, Dalmatians, or poodles in Kampala? And you don't get them for less than Shs300,000.
By virtue of their species, they need to be handled with care. That is why they need personal or sometimes a private doctor to attend to them.
Dr Ronnie (Mich) Egwang says that it costs Shs30,000 to vaccinate a dog. This is mainly done annually against rabies canine distemper and parvo Virus. Besides, they need to be de-wormed at least once a month, and a general medical check up. This means constant visits to the doctor and constant spending.
Dr Egwang admits, "It is only the people who can afford these services that make the majority clients." And that is not enough. These pets need exercise and they must be taken for a walk every evening.
These pets also have their own pets with which they join the rest of the family to watch TV. These servants and pets are the inspiration of their less privileged counterparts.
It is the dream of every house help to move up the income ladder and work in a rich neighbourhood; something like moving from serving tea in Kikoni, Bwaise, or Kyebando to pressing a button in Kololo and tea simply appears. What with spending your entire life between silver and gold.

Original_Search_Title: Ford-Kenya - More Trouble Than Messy Party Polls
Headline: Kenya: Ford-Kenya - More Trouble Than Messy Party Polls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A tumultuous Christmas awaits Ford-Kenya. Owing to the party's contentious national elections that kicked off on Friday, two sets of party officials are likely to emerge by December 20 - the date slated for the National Delegates' Conference.
The final blow to the anticipated falling out was evident from day one following parallel polls by a group allied to party chairman, Mr Musikari Kombo and that of his main challenger Housing minister, Mr Soita Shitanda.
"Of course, you do not expect us to circulate membership cards bearing the image of Kombo. We have printed out new cards and other materials and we are carrying out the elections in the best way we know how," Shitanda, told The Sunday Standard.
In a quick rejoinder, however, his Science and Technology counterpart who is the party's director of elections, Dr Noah Wekesa, warns that Ford-Kenya organs will not recognise a parallel exercise carried out with "unknown material other than that supplied by my office."
Hopes of a truce between the Kombo and Shitanda camps were dashed from the day the Malava MP staged a dramatic "ouster" of his party boss. The High Court has since prevailed upon the Registrar of Societies to disregard the move.
The development has left both Cabinet colleagues and their supporters in rage. In essence, Ford-Kenya's history is bound to repeat itself, as was the case between Lang'ata MP, Mr Raila Odinga and the late vice-president Michael Wamalwa in 1996.
While Raila resolved the impasse following an aborted election in Thika Stadium by quitting the party and his parliamentary seat to join the National Democratic Party, Kombo and Shitanda are "holding on tight" and could stretch the Ford-Kenya wars to the very last days of the General Election due next year.
In the meantime, a host of political hawks are watching from the wings with keen interest. Former Lugari MP, Mr Cyrus Jirongo, for instance, does not hide his keenness on Ford-Kenya affairs.
"I am monitoring their elections very closely because I believe they are crucial considering that Ford-Kenya is the giant party in Western Province," he told The Sunday Standard.
Although Jirongo denies interfering with the party's polls, as chairman of Kenya African Democratic Development Union (Kaddu), no doubt a stronger and united Ford-Kenya does not augur well for the future of his latest outfit. There are other stronger players within Government and in the opposition eyeing the Luhya vote, which according to the latest statistics from the Electoral Commission of Kenya, stands at 1,414,030 million.
Though a national party with a presence in every corner of the country, Ford-Kenya enjoys bedrock support among the Luhya, who often provide the swing vote.
There are serious competing political interests in Western Province and the recent dramatic events, especially in Ford-Kenya, have exposed the intentions of some political players.
Initially, few had doubts that the Government may have had a hand in Kombo's political woes. This was particularly evident from the rival's group's confidence about the registration of its "new officials".
But the subsequent court rulings in favour of the Kombo group have created even more confusion with Shitanda now claiming that State House was interceding for the Webuye MP.
Either way, the delaying tactics may just be part of the game given that it is in the interest of Ford-Kenya's enemies in Government and opposition to keep the party's house burning.
Separately, Mwingi North MP Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, sees a window of opportunity for the Orange Democratic Movement as well.
"My brother Kombo now clearly knows where the Government stands in the Ford- Kenya matter. He should read the signs and do the honourable thing - abandon the Government and join the ODM forces instead," he says.
The very notion that the minister trusts ODM-Kenya vice-chairman Mr Mutula Kilonzo, as his defence lawyer, observes Musyoka, should be a pointer to where his real friends are. Curiously, Musyoka has been leading in opinion polls in Western Province as the most preferred presidential candidate.
The decision by the Local Government minister to hire the services of Kilonzo is understandable. When the Shitanda group struck, the move looked like a well-choreographed operation with support from some "higher authorities".
Incidentally, it came barely two days after another onslaught on Leader of Official Opposition, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta. Interestingly, the Kanu takeover involved key players including Kombo's perennial challenger Mr Joash Wamang'oli, who was elected one of the party's vice-chairmen. Wamang'oli is a former Webuye MP.
Unknown to many, ethnicity is a crucial factor in the Ford-Kenya politics in Western Province. Politicians from the area confess that the entire battle has degenerated into a sub-ethnic contest. The Luhya has 16 sub-tribes.
When the elections kicked off on Monday, Shitanda told The Sunday Standard that sales of membership cards by his agents were doing well in Butere, Mumias, Lugari, Vihiga and Kakamega - areas that encompass the former wider Kakamega District.
And although the MP suspected that Kombo's agents must have been doing well in Bungoma and Trans Nzoia districts - home to the populous Bukusu - Dr Wekesa maintained that all was well and that party agents were circulating membership cards evenly.
"This to me is a healthy exercise for even when we are striving to promote democracy it does not mean that we shy away from competing at home," says Jirongo.
Nonetheless, the raging sub-tribal war is worrying to many. Judging from the support the key competitors are enjoying, it clearly paints a picture of what one MP claims "a scheme where other Luhya sub-tribes are ganging up against the Bukusu."
For some an unexplained reason, the latter has over the years produced powerful and acceptable political leaders for the Luhya community. Before Wamalwa, there was the late Masinde Muliro, a leading opposition figure and former minister in the Jomo Kenyatta government.
However, the quiet battle of visibility and authority among the Luhya community goes beyond politics. Members of the community confess that their counterparts from Kakamega District have always held themselves as more Luhya (read superior) than others. That Kakamega town is therefore "capital city of Luhyas" is not an accident.
"Anybody who tries to create segregation among the Luhya does so at his own peril, otherwise we are a community," reiterates Wekesa.
Jirongo further adds that the Luhya Elders' Forum is watching the current mess and will address the matter after the Ford- Kenya polls.
Another point of departure among the Luhya politicians is the political direction that the party should take next year. For while Kombo and Trade minister Dr Mukhisa Kituyi - now Narc-Kenya chairman - seem destined to incline towards President Kibaki, Shitanda happens to be warming up to ODM-Kenya.
Kombo might not have disclosed as much, but Shitanda has repeatedly accused him of "taking the community to the Banana side" during the constitutional referendum "against the wishes of our people".
Essentially, Shitanda's worry is that the chairman may yet again "forcefully take us to a wrong side against our wish."
However, Kombo's position has all along been that the party strengthens itself first before shopping around for a political partner ahead of next year's polls.
"We are doing a good job on this end and after this month's polls, I can assure you Ford Kenya will join a winning outfit. We shall be in Government next year," declares Wekesa.
Apart from Kombo and Shitanda, the Ford Kenya race has also attracted other contestants including Public Service minister Mr Moses Akaranga and Health assistant minister Dr Enoch Kibunguchy.
Unlike the main contenders, Akaranga (Sabatia) and Kibunguchy (Lugari) are faced with stiffer challenges at constituency level and appear keener on strengthening their political curriculum vitae than the Ford Kenya race per se.
Akaranga, for instance, faces former vice president Mr Musalia Mudavadi. Apart from his ministerial position, an added feather as party chairman will indeed boost Akaranga's chances.
Kibunguchy faces Kaddu party Jirongo. A political schemer per excellence, Jirongo is former chairman of Youth for Kanu '92, a lobby group that successfully campaigned for retired President Daniel arap Moi's election in 1992.
In the meantime, Ford-Kenya is at a crossroads as parallel polls are actually underway, a fact that Wekesa vehemently denies.
"For all I know, only one election is under way and come December 20 we shall, as one team, elect our set of officials," explains the party's director of elections.
For Shitanda, though, the elections are already flawed considering that separate election material is being used and that the party's director of elections, has allegedly taken side in support of one candidate.
However, this is just the beginning of the mess as The Sunday Standard has separately established. Some of the candidates, including Shitanda are participating in an exercise where their fate has already been sealed.
"They are simply wasting their time as they have all failed to meet the set requirements," said a source. Only Kombo is reported to have paid the two million shillings required from those contesting the chairman's seat.

Original_Search_Title: Of Noise And Urban Waste
Headline: Ethiopia: Of Noise And Urban Waste
Body_Text:
The Reporter of October 28th, 2006, in its Life and Art column, carried Mr. X's article entitled "This Noisy City", conveying his personal opinion on the issue of noise polluting Addis Ababa.
Also a couple of years ago, a quintessential columnist and journalist, Yacob Wolde-Mariam enabled us to read about the effect of noise let loose in the city. He too suggested that concerned authorities grapple with the problem before it adversely affected the mental health of the citizenry.
Nonetheless, let alone being curbed, noise at present is spreading like wildfire to regional states, and even small villages.
This writer agrees with Mr. X. However, what is very perplexing is the writer's lack of prioritizing issues.
As I see it, Mr. X could have expressed his opinion regarding other major pressing environmental pollutions affecting Addis Ababa. Of course, our constantly sprawling city has become the epicenter of the cholera water-borne diseases like malaria, and typhoid fever and other virulent diseases.
As a matter of fact, the inhabitants of the city are being made to bear the brunt of pollution as a result of rickety regulations, lack of a strong policy and directives and absence of agents that can contain challenges hampering urban development.
On top of this, the city dwellers' lack of concern to determine the causes of pollution has, by and large, exacerbated the problem. In spite of this, the magnitude of solid waste, liquid waste, and smoke emitted from households, industries, vehicles and other sources is becoming life-threatening.
In the name of making the city clean we the citizenry have not discovered any shift of attitude. The utmost they do is to organize task forces, to recruit personnel and to try to deliver the business of cleaning and burning waste without significantly changing the problem of pollution.
In spite of this retrogressive attitude, at present it is not uncommon to encounter human faeces being simply left to drain over the tarmac, carcasses left for days on end in the bowels of improperly designed ditches, sewers uncovered manholes. We are witnessing in this turmoil, the pungent smel of abattoirs, the effluent of small-scale to medium-size industries pervasively affecting ecological balance, which is pernicious to the existence of mankind.
Under these circumstances, with the speedy sprawling of the city, proper care is not taken, even in the course of constructing the mushrooming, scintillating multi-story buildings and gorgeous chateaus amidst squalor and filth, to accommodate the causes of pollution.
To enable itself discharge its responsibilities, the city government of Addis Ababa has to streamline its one-way modus operandi (tax collection and land lease only). The office has to design strategies in conformity with the science of urban planning and management.
In fulfillment of this, the City Administration is required to establish and organize a well-trained bureaucracy with a strong enforcement arm, coupled with increasing public funds and mobility, to mobilize financial resources and to ensure proper implementation of the rudiments of urban planning and management.
In the process, prior to any action, the organized bureaucracy has to act upon the design, refurbishment of networks of existing and new infrastructure for water, electricity, telecommunications, and roads. It must tackle the sewerage system, treatment of solid waste and the removal of waste to counteract pollution.
On top of organizing its bureaucracy, the city government has to dutifully mobilize the inhabitants and conduct workshops, seminars and capacity building training by segmenting the city in an equitable and transparent manner to protect the city from any undesirable causative and degrading factors.
Much to our dismay, despite widespread advocacy work conducted internationally by multiple CSO and NGO groups set to safeguard developing countries from the ordeals of environmental consequences, it is embarrassing to see our fellow Ethiopian environmentalists, sanitarians, health professionals remarkably silent to at least offer a minuscule contribution. In this regard, this writer pays tribute to Gash Abera Molla's constructive endeavor.
In general, if we wish a propitious future for our city, apart from the above-cited prescribed solutions as a means to combat pollution, the city government has to:
· Put a check on excessive wretched immigrants who can barely cope with the standards of city life. These citizens are inundating the metropolis with their remote cultural and traditional behavior that degrades and exacerbates pollution. This problem can be tackled through the coordinated efforts of the central government and the migrants' respective regional states.
· Put in place building codes, and duly establish construction standards and techniques, to be monitored and supervised solely by registered professionals involved in the fields of construction engineering. However, if, on the contrary, construction in the city is to be conducted by 3rd class people, like carpenters, masons and others, as it is being done now, for sure this is ultimately destructive. Basically, in the system of city development, these class of people lack greater awareness of the negative impacts these have on infrastructure and the assessment of environmental problems prior to construction.
· Relocate industries, garages and the like supposed to be the genesis of pollution to alternative industrial zones by providing subsidized credits.
· Formulate and implement strong directives that rigorously prohibit the movement of domestic animals on the roads of the city as they are contributive factors to the increasing rate of pollution.
· Put a prohibitive measure to the sales of seasonal food and other items like maize, bean, khat, banana, oranges alongside roads
· conduct construction on a par with the master plan of the city.
· Increase densities of population in the existing areas by decreasing the fast expansion of the city to control pollution causes.
· Finally, this writer urges fellow Ethiopian intellectuals and the city government of Addis Ababa to fully commit and extricate the impoverished city dwellers wallowing in pipe-dreams to make the impossible possible.

Original_Search_Title: Garakara Set to Perform At Unity Gala
Headline: Zimbabwe: Garakara Set to Perform At Unity Gala
Body_Text:
Harare — THE line up for this year's Unity Gala scheduled for Victoria Falls' Chinotimba Stadium is out and it includes debutant -- Joseph Garakara famed for the hit Idya Banana.
Garakara who scooped two ZIMAs for Best Upcoming Artiste and Best Sungura Artiste at a glittering ceremony held last month is arguably this year's biggest find.
He will join a host of other performers at the gala slated for December 22 in the resort town.
Other artistes on the bill are Hosiah Chipanga, Tanga WekwaSando, Bob "The Headmaster" Nyabinde, Jeys Marabini, Allan "Sonny" Chimbetu, Cde Chinx & Police Band, Black Umfolosi.
Chipanga, Nyabinde, Marabini, Chase Skuza, Iyasa, Extra Large, Diva Mafunga and Allan were each nominated in different categories at the ZIMA ceremony.
But this is just the tip of the iceberg, as there are other performers who include Baba Mechanic Manyeruke, LMG Choir, Andy "Muzukuru" Brown, Mbira DzeNharira, Beater Mangethe, Phabian Muchakwa, Sandra Ndebele and Cosmas Muponda.
Muponda has taken country music to greater heights and those who saw his performance at the Mzee Bira held in Mutare would remember how he wowed the crowds.
It was there in Mutare where he left lasting impressions on the music scene.
Muzukuru Brown has been quiet for some time and he is expected to thrill revellers with hits such as Mawere Kongonya, Mapurisa, Tichangoshaina and Daisy.
And if that line up does not entice you then you should watch out for the urban grooves artistes -- the likes of ExQ, Stunner and Extra Large popularly known as Maroja.
Currently Maroja are doing well with an album called Small House while Stunner is also making waves with his debut -- Hameno that features a "diss" track targeting some celebrities. ExQ is arguably one of the smoothest rappers of our time.
He enjoys singing about mazirudo (love) and his syrup-laced lyrics have won him hearts of many. Some of his popular tracks include Staira and Mazirudo.
Agatha Marudzwa from Mutare will be there to represent gospel divas.
Gala co-ordinator, Mavis Gumbo said they are expecting three groups from Zambia and one from Botswana.
"The groups that we invited from Zambia have not yet confirmed but as soon as they do that we will give you the names. However, we have had responses from Zimbabwean artistes and we're hoping to deliver the show of the year on the day," she said.
At least 27 groups will take to the stage a development, which is seen by critics as positive because it gives enough time for each group to showcase their talents.
The Unity Gala is held annually to celebrate the signing of the Unity Accord by President Mugabe and the late Vice President Dr Joshua Nkomo in December 1987.
Last year, Kadoma had the honour of hosting the gala.

Original_Search_Title: Guebuza Opens Acp Summit
Headline: Africa: Guebuza Opens Acp Summit
Body_Text:
Khartoum — Mozambican President Armando Guebuza on Thursday reaffirmed his conviction that the eradication of poverty, sustainable development, and the gradual integration into the world economy of the members of the ACP (African, Caribbean and Pacific) group are attainable objectives.
Speaking in Khartoum at the opening of the Fifth Summit of ACP heads of state and government, Guebuza recognised that the challenges facing the ACP states, particularly with regard to their relations with the European Union, are "complex".
He argued that the group needed to preserve the gains accumulated over three decades of ACP-EU cooperation, but also take measures to improve the ACP's own relevance and visibility.
He claimed that, since the last summit, held in Maputo in 2004, there have been important steps to implement the decisions taken then, and a strengthening of the ACP's internal cohesion.
"The technical meetings held between Maputo and Khartoum had an important role in our mutual understanding, as well as in harmonising positions of common interest", said Guebuza. He believed that intra-ACP cooperation was strengthening the group's position in its negotiations with the European Union.
This had brought positive results in pledges to attain the Millennium Development Goals, and in the programme of the revised Cotonou Agreement, which Guebuza urged all ACP members to ratify.
Among the major challenges that had to be faced, he said, were the negotiations of Economic Partnership Agreements with the EU, the erosion of the ACP's preferential position in trade in primary products, and the massive emigration of citizens of ACP states to Europe.
Guebuza warned that without due consideration to development issues, and if structural constraints linked to supply were not solved, then the Economic Partnership Agreements (EPAs) will bring no substantial benefits to ACP countries. On the contrary, they could marginalise them, in an ever more competitive world.
He argued that the liberalisation of the markets of ACP countries (the EU's key demand) should be preceded by additional financial support "to strengthen our countries' supply, production and export capacities".
As for the erosion of the ACP countries' preferential access to EU markets in goods such as sugar, bananas, cotton and rice, Guebuza believed that funding was needed to diversify the economies of these countries, so that they would no longer be so dependent on these products.
Guebuza praised the return to stability in some ACP members, notably the Democratic Republic of Congo, but condemned this week's military coup in Fiji, and demanded the restoration of constitutional order.
As for the war raging in the eastern Sudanese region of Darfur, Guebuza called on the Sudanese government to continue negotiating with the African Union and the United Nations for a solution. So far the Sudanese regime has been refusing to allow a UN peace-keeping force into Darfur, and has downplayed the scale of the atrocities committed there.

Original_Search_Title: Liberians Falling Apart Again
Headline: Liberians Falling Apart Again
Body_Text:
Speaker Edwin Snowe, has called on the Executive branch of government to work harder in reconciling the Liberian people, and emphasized the need for the government to restore the happiness of the Liberian people, adding there is a need to "put a halt to divisiveness, the divide and rule system and work with everyone as Liberians."
According to him, the current politics of targeting certain individuals or a particular group instead of reconciling and unifying everyone won't in anyway help to solve any problem in the war-torn society.
Hon. Snowe as the third man in the government he is calling upon to give reconciliation a premium above anything.
He, however, fell short of saying who is targeting who and for what, but noted that the country's leaders took oath to serve all Liberians whether good or bad citizens, illiterate or educated, partisans or oppositions.
"The idea in Liberia where leadership is now drawing a platform that only the so-called good people can sit on and the perceived bad people must perish must be denounced now, and let us reconcile Liberians because Liberia is in dire need of total peace and reconciliation to move forward", said the Speaker.
He made the call over the weekend at the Paynesville Town Hall when he held a development meeting with citizens and residents of District #5 in Montserrado County. He told his constituency that several tactics including armed robbery were being deployed by some leaders to get at perceived foes in society, stressing that the issues of armed robbery is very serious.
Whether he had any intelligence linking some officials of government to myriad of armed robberies across the city is a guess but his statement is against the background of an armed robbery over the weekend at the home of his Special Assistant, Andy Quamie.
None of the armed robbers who attacked the home of Mr. Quamie was arrested, and not even an ounce of information gathered from any of them; so the question being asked by analysts is from whence comes the assertion.
Speaker Snowe has from to time been uneasy with the issue of armed robbery since assuming the office of speaker. He some times ago scolded UNMIL for not providing inadequate security for citizens against armed robbers and other criminals. With no signs of improvement still, he told his constituents that crimes have increased again with an alarming rate.
Giving reasons behind his assertion, he noted that some of the armed robbery issues were confusing because the robbers were not seeking money, but going after individuals. He used the recent armed robbery at his Special Assistant home as case in point, saying the robbers wanted to eliminate him (special assistant).
The robbers, according to Speaker Snowe placed Mr. Quamie's entire family under gun point and demanded his whereabouts. "My Special Assistant had money, cell phones, jewelry, but the armed robbers only took the phones and jewelries and left the money while demanding to see the special assistant.
The armed robbers insisted that the family must produce Mr. Quamie, my Special Assistant and so some times it sounds confusing," he said. As if it was a systematic attack against him staff, Speaker Snowe further revealed that on Friday night last week, another group of armed robbers attacked the home of another staff of his office and demanded to only see his office staff.
He said the robbers took away the staff's lover and threatened to kill her if she failed to disclose the whereabouts of a staff only identified as Touray. "It is my hope that the armed robbers, in as much as we denounced them, I hope they are indeed armed robbers.
But they (armed robbers) begin to look for human beings; not money or jewelries. I don't think this is a way to move Liberia forward", lamented Speaker Snowe. He promised to request UNMIL to beef up security for Liberians particularly residents of Monrovia, stressing "I call on UNMIL to protect our people because UNMIL is in charge of security to protect our people so we can live in peace".
However, Speaker Snowe averred that the old political tactics of targeting and hunting people nowadays won't solve any problem because "We Liberians cannot afford to go back to where we are coming from, but rather let us build Liberia by working together; let us stop looking here and there for people." Speaker Snowe insinuated that political powerful few were hurting and settling scores with others like in the days of old.
"During the Tubman days, when President Tubman had problem with Barclay, they (security, etc) look for the Barclay people. When the President had problem with Coleman, they look for the Colman people. When Charles Taylor came, they looked for the Krahn people, when Samuel Doe came to power, they look for the Gio people", the Speaker revealed to Paynesville citizens and residents.
Today, let us stop looking for the Taylor people, Bryant people, the Doe people, let everybody be the same in Liberia", he pleaded. He said Liberians are one family so they should stop hunting one another again, adding "Because we are all the same people from one banana tree".

Original_Search_Title: EPC's Bid to Deepen Exports to Europe
Headline: Kenya: EPC's Bid to Deepen Exports to Europe
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Exporters eying the European Union market have been urged to seek trade information that would broaden market opportunities.
Export Promotion Council managing director Mr Matanda Wabuyele said the lucrative EU market remains the most challenging for the local exporters.
Kenya and the other 76 African, Caribbean and Pacific (ACP) countries have traded with the EU trade block for over four decades now under various conventions, notably the Lome Convention and the Cotonou Agreement.
But export volumes from the ACP countries have continued to decline mainly because most of the exports are raw materials.
The bulk of these exports are coffee, tea, cocoa, copper, bananas and horticultural products.
"Trade information is one of the most important tools in international business today," Wabuyele said during an export sensitization seminar organised by the Swedish Chamber of Commerce in Nairobi yesterday.
"Exporters must be prepared to diversify the range of exports with more value added products."
The Swedish Chamber of Commerce is calling on the exporters to establish links with Swedish companies to expand the opportunities.
The chamber is particularly interested in encouraging joint ventures between Swedish and Kenyan small and medium enterprises (SMEs).
'The big companies are doing everything for themselves and may not require our support but we need to establish linkages for the SMEs," said Ms Beata Wiberg, commercial counselor at the Swedish embassy in Nairobi.
She said upcoming SMEs in her country were interested in new investment opportunities within the East African region.
She said her Government is supporting the Gateway project between the chamber and the East African Business Council.
Wabuyele said trade between Kenya and Sweden had risen marginally in the last five years though it was still in favour of Sweden.
"Kenya's exports to Sweden have been on the increase growing from Sh900 million in 2000 to Sh1.1 billion in 2004," said Wabuyele.
"Some of the leading exports include coffee, fish, fruits and vegetables, plastics, paper and paperboard."
Imports from Sweden include telecommunications equipment, aircraft and associated equipment and mechanical handling equipment.

Original_Search_Title: Old Trade Beefs to Resurface at ACP Meet
Headline: Africa: Old Trade Beefs to Resurface at ACP Meet
Body_Text:
Port of Spain — Not many Caribbean leaders are expected to attend the two-day summit of the African, Caribbean and Pacific (ACP) Group starting in Sudan Thursday, but the region will follow its agenda quite keenly, including a proposed Economic Partnership Agreement with the European Union and a new threat to its ailing banana industry.
Founded in 1975 to promote closer ties with Europe, the ACP Group has 79 members, including 48 countries from sub-Saharan Africa, 16 from the Caribbean and 15 from the Pacific.
Over the last 30 years, its mission has evolved to include greater cooperation among member states in areas such as trade, economics, politics and culture.
This year's summit in Khartoum, Sudan, which is currently riven by violent conflicts in the south, west and east of the country, will look at how to achieve the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs), the Economic Partnership Agreement (EPA), Doha Development Agenda and cross-cutting issues of HIV/AIDS and gender.
The MDGs cover a broad range of internationally-agreed targets, including cutting extreme poverty in half by 2015 and forging a global partnership for development between rich and poorer countries.
President Omar Hasan Ahmad al-Bashir, the host of the summit -- which coincides with the 50th anniversary of Sudan's independence from Britain -- said it will address "our global commitments and those that the group shares with its partner, the European Union".
ACP leaders plan to weigh the future of the bloc, made up mostly of former European colonies, and their prospects for survival in a changing world characterised by new trading patterns for the majority of their commodities and products.
The Caribbean, for example, has already raised a red flag against efforts by Ecuador, which last month formally requested consultations with the European Union under the World Trade Organisation (WTO) rules governing the settlement of disputes as they relate to the export of ACP bananas to Europe.
The dispute has pitted the so-called Most Favoured Nations (MFNs) -- many in Latin America -- against the smaller countries of the ACP, whose banana sectors have for years enjoyed preferential market access to the EU.
Ecuador, in its request circulated to all WTO members, said that it considered the new EU tariff of 231 dollars (176 Euros) per metric tonne to be too high, causing the MFNs to lose market share to ACP suppliers.
The EU began applying the banana tariff in January 2006 as part of a commitment to Ecuador and the United States in 2001 to introduce a tariff-only regime from that date. The commitment was part of an understanding to bring an end to the banana disputes of 1997 and 1999 between the EU and the Latin American banana producers and Washington.
But Caribbean states argue that when the actual volume of bananas exported by the two sides is shown, a huge disparity remains.
Dominica's Trade and Industry Minister Charles Savarin said that from January to June 2005, the Latin American producers exported 1.58 million tonnes of bananas to the EU, a figure that hit 1.64 million tonnes in 2006.
"This is compared to ACP countries' exports of 367,571 tonnes from January to June in 2005 compared to 436,161 tonnes for the same period in 2006. Some fear that this action by Ecuador will only have the effect of reigniting and reviving the more than a decade-old banana war," he added.
The banana issue aside, the proposed EPA agreement with Europe is another key issue for the region, particularly as the Caribbean negotiators have insisted that any new accord must go beyond trade to embrace genuine development.
Caribbean countries want to ensure that development is placed "on the front burner" during negotiations, even as the EU and the Caribbean Forum (CARIFORUM) countries ended two days of talks in Brussels last week indicating that there were still areas of disagreement.
CARIFORUM groups the 15 member states of the Caribbean Community, the Dominican Republic and Haiti.
Prior to the Brussels meeting, Richard Bernal, head of the Caribbean Regional Negotiating Machinery, said a question mark remained about the inclusion of sugar and bananas in the accord.
"Sugar and bananas are really exceptions. They were dealt with in the old Lome Convention by special protocols and the banana issue has assumed its own momentum in the WTO dispute settlement mechanism and that may not be resolved in time for us to include it in the EPA text," he said.
"It may be in a side agreement or a special arrangement which complements that. Sugar also is a special arrangement and it is an open question at this stage if it will be included and how," Bernal said.
In a joint statement, both sides said progress had been made on a draft text, but added that three major challenges remain. These include the specific nature of commitments within the EPA; the negotiation of tariff liberalisation for goods; and the negotiation of commitments for trade in services and investment.
The joint EU/ACP parliamentary assembly held in Barbados last month agreed that undue pressure should not be placed on ACP countries as they seek to craft the new accord with Europe.
"Now is the time for a radical and rigorous rethink, and not for what is being perceived as complacent reassurance from Europe's negotiators," warned EU parliamentarian Glenys Kinnock, the co-president of the Assembly, who said that the expiry of the WTO waiver should not compel the signing of an EPA agreement that is perceived as detrimental to ACP development interests.
A resolution adopted at the end of the week-long assembly also said steps should be taken to ensure that if negotiations are not completed by the Jan. 1, 2008 deadline, existing ACP exports to the EU will not be disrupted until a final settlement is reached.
However, there is quiet optimism that a deal can be forged before that date.
Barbados' Foreign Trade Minister Dame Billie Miller, who is also chair of the ACP Ministerial Trade Committee, told the Barbados meeting that while the divide between Europe and its former colonies appeared insurmountable at this point, she was hopeful that a mutually acceptable agreement could be found.
"The eyes of the world are on these EPA negotiations. The multilateral trading system needs a success story if it is to maintain its credibility. The ACP/EU can show the world that it is possible to forge an EPA between 79 developing countries and 25 developed ones," she said.

Original_Search_Title: 2007: Garlands for Ken Nnamani
Headline: Nigeria: 2007: Garlands for Ken Nnamani
Body_Text:
Last weekend, the Peoples Democratic Party [PDP], the biggest and unquestionably the most corrupt political party in all Africa and much of the civilized world held its congresses into the upper legislative chambers with surprising ease given the rancour and violence which characterized similar elections in the past.
At the last count, no fewer than fifty incumbent senators out of the total of 109 failed to make the cut this time, a thing unheard of in 2003. In Anambra State, the incumbent Governor Nnamani who may yet be a guest of the EFCC coasted home virtually unopposed, same with Governor Mu'azu of Bauchi thought to be interested in contesting the presidency.
In Niger State, my big sister and wife to the governor waltzed through to pick the ticket ahead of a contest that is sure to challenge as much as it is also guaranteed to generate excitement. In Katsina, my former boss and big brother, Alhaji Ibrahim Ida stunned me with the ease of his victory being a relative 'greenhorn' in the political arena. Ida, it would be recalled, was the Secretary of the Pius Okigbo Panel which probed the Central Bank of Nigeria [CBN] and was also the Secretary of the Vision 2010 Committee which crafted the well-regarded economic blue print for the country which the current administration decided to cast aside. What he brings to the table are unparalleled organisational ability and years of experience as a well-rounded technocrat.
But I was not at all surprised by the decision of the Senate President Ken Nnamani not to seek re-election to the National Assembly. Much has already been written about the incumbent governor of his state bearing the same surname, but in my opinion, he took the right decision to avoid the wrath of the vultures in the Wadata headquarters of the PDP over his principled opposition to the president's third term project. In doing so, he also took a leaf out of the book of one of his predecessors, Pius Anyim, which their counterpart in the lower house Ghali Umar Na'Abba apparently failed to read.
Both Anyim and Na'Abba, it would be recalled, fought a valiant battle to uphold the dignity and honour of the National Assembly during their tenures in the face of a rampaging president to whom the principle of separation of powers counted for very little. Since the days of John Lock, the parliament in representative governance had always been the difference between absolute tyranny and the government of the people for the people and by the people. Apparently, such niceties meant nothing to Olusegun Obasanjo in those days when Anyim and Na'Abba threatened to remove him from office for countless impeachable offences. It was an atrocious battle which left a sour taste in the mouth of the president who's infamous for his unforgiving spirit and tendency for Sicilian class vendetta.
Anyim clearly saw the handwriting on the wall and declined to seek a return to the Senate populated by mercenaries on the payroll of the Presidency. It was not long that Nigerians began to appreciate just how much our democracy missed his outstanding leadership. First, the president assaulted our sensibilities when he paved the way for the now repentant Adolphus Wabara to emerge as Senate President at our collective cost as it later transpired following his subsequent indictment for graft. It is a testimony to how well he served the president for the few months he was allowed the desecrate his lofty office that he is still a free man today, having apparently enjoyed a 'soft landing', another unfortunate phrase the Obasanjo clique introduced to our political nomenclature.
These then are why Nigerians need to appreciate and celebrate the uncommon courage of people like Senator Ken Nnamani. Were he an Adolphus Wabara, the president would probably have had his way on the tenure extension project in spite of our wishes, because the Senate's standing rules would have been abused with impunity with people like Ibrahim Mantu in diabolical form. In an atmosphere that reeked of greed and naked ambition, the nation may well have joined the ranks of banana republics that dot the political landscape of the developing world - if we were not one already, but for the patriotism and integrity of Ken Nnamani who stood up to be counted when it mattered most.
And so as he has signified his desire to quit the Senate in 2007, the challenge to Nigerians is to ensure that we elect more of his type next time we return to the polls. We must begin to ask questions about the calibre and character of those who seek to represent us in parliament, because in a democracy, their conduct could be the difference between freedom and tyranny and between good governance and absolute misrule. I am aware of course that some self-serving people hoodwinked by the president's theatrics could point at the ongoing social and economic reforms to justify the president's draconian style of governance, which quite unfortunately, is also characterised by unpardonable abuse of the rule of law, but I beg to differ.
For reforms of any type to be acceptable and meaningful in any society, they must be centred on the people, and must take into full cognisance the needs of the various strata of society. Granted, some people have argued that Nigerians have problems of prudence and social discipline, but both can hardly justify the type of hardship visited on them in the past seven years of this administration.
Even in advanced societies when such far-reaching reforms are contemplated, sufficient safety nets are provided so that the weak will not suffer from needless adversity. What the current reforms have achieved is to create a class of nouveau riche who have done very little to deserve the local protectionism they now enjoy at the expense of the teeming masses left to face the music in an economy rooted on the demands of the Washington Consensus, completely oblivious of the peculiarities of a third world nation such as ours.
These again are why we need to be vigilant to elect the right parliament in 2007. Next year, we must do away with the likes of Arthur Nzeribe and Ibrahim Mantu if the people are to have a say in the way their nation is governed. We need a National Assembly that is patriotic to the totality of Nigerians and not one hung on absolute loyalty to a despot who preaches good governance and the rule of law abroad only to exhibit the most reprehensible intolerance and brutality at home. We need a parliament that will ensure that the president remains bound to the dictates of the constitution and one who will uphold the rule of law and the principle of separation of powers.
Finally, as we access the character of the potential parliamentarians who picked their party nominations these past few weeks, let us also pause to pore over their antecedents, especially over the past seven years. Let issues and competence be brought to the fore in our choice of representatives, but above all, let us avoid the lure of money as we cast our votes, because parliament and not the executive arm, is the only voice of the people in a genuine democracy. It is only when we have done all these can it be said with any degree of satisfaction that Nigerians have done justice to the courage and legacy of people like Ken Nnamani.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Fibres New Raw Material for Paper
Headline: Uganda: Banana Fibres New Raw Material for Paper
Body_Text:
Kampala — MAKING paper from banana fibres will reduce deforestation, one of the major causes of global warming in developing countries, said Prof. Hiroshi Miroshima, from Nagoya City University, Japan.
This was during a workshop on production of paper from banana fibres at Makerere University, recently.
Miroshima, who is training 46 Ugandans, three Kenyans and one Tanzanian in the technology, says the innovation would help the country reduce on paper importation.
"We developed an environment-friendly and chemical free banana paper and cloth, using traditional Japanese paper-making art and simple technology," Morishima said.
The government of Japan provided a grant of $91,220 for the development of the technology under the Grant Assistance for Grassroots Human Security Projects that promotes the technology.
Joseph Byaruhanga, the general manager of Uganda Gatsby Trust said, the project would not only see provision of paper, but the banana fibre wastes would be recycled to get cloth and manure.

Original_Search_Title: Odm-Kenya Takes On Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: Odm-Kenya Takes On Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Five ODM-Kenya presidential aspirants were in Malindi to kick off the first of a series of rallies under the banner, "In Defense of Democracy."
The leaders who will on Sunday troop to Tononoka Grounds, Mombasa, linked President Kibaki to last week's internal coups in Ford-Kenya and Kanu.
At the Malindi rally, the ODM-Kenya leaders vowed to hold mass demonstrations to the offices of the Registrar of Societies on Tuesday to "counter the Government's efforts to finish ODM."
"The Government has now picked 'professors' who are experts in finishing political parties," Leader of official opposition, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta said.
"They are now determined to finish multiparty politics, which Kenyans sought for so long. We shall not allow them to ride on other peoples' backs," Uhuru declared.
"Democracy is not on sale. These are the days of clear and serious concerns for transparency. Although the Banana team have disguised themselves as flowers, we shall cut them with the same blade we used during the referendum," Uhuru added.
Law relating to abuse of office
He described the Kibaki regime as "government of mamluks (mercenaries) and traitors".
This, he said, would not stop ODM's "journey to State House."
The presidential hopefuls present are Mr William Ruto, Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, Mr Musalia Mudavadi, Mr Najib Balala, and ODM-Kenya chairman, Mr Henry Kosgey. The other aspirant, Mr Raila Odinga, spoke from Geneva.
MP Marsden Madoka, former MP, Mr Chris Obure, MPs, Dr Oburu Oginga, Mr Joseph Nyagah, Mr Ramadhan Kajembe and Mr Lucas Maitha were also present.
Former Mombasa Mayor, Mr Taib Ali Taib, and his Malindi counterpart, Mr Gedion Mungaro, also attended.
"There will be two horses in the race. Those who want to build Kenya and those who want to destroy," Uhuru said.
The leaders warned the Government to refer to the law relating to "abuse of office". He said if ODM takes over power "they shall be charged under the law".
"They are creating coups in political parties so that we are divided. They are looking for a shortcut to State House. This is a dream they will not achieve," Mudavadi said.
Referendum was just a warm-up
The Government, Mudavadi added, created a crisis by registering Mr Nicholas Biwott's faction in Kanu, but predicted that in that crisis, ODM would unite faster and shock the Government.
"We shaved them during the referendum. We have the same blade and we shall shave them again next year. The Government is not fighting Uhuru by imposing a party faction on Kenyans. It is it fighting ODM, but this is the party that will form the next government. The Kanu war is our war because anybody who tries to break Kanu want to break ODM," Balala said.
The leaders said that the referendum was just a warm-up to the General Election and that a similar pattern would be repeated.
"If by next Saturday the Biwott faction would not have been deregistered, we shall meet at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, with less than one million ODM members and head to State House," Ruto said.
Judiciary accused of corruption
The leaders claimed that the way things are, ODM leaders could find themselves ousted by the same crooked means.
Balala assured Kenyans that ODM presidential aspirants were united and that the party was the only threat to the Government.
Oburu, on his part, warned Mr Musikari Kombo to abandon the Narc-Kenya horse that he said had kicked him. He invited him to join ODM.
Kalonzo said that the Judiciary is corrupt and had no respect for the highest court in East Africa.
"How can the President challenge the highest court in East Africa?" asked Kalonzo.
"We invite all ODM-Kenya members to join us on Tuesday, to force the registrar to deregister these fake Kanu officials. People say Kalonzo is a coward but I want to be the first to lead the demonstration on Tuesday," said the Mwingi North MP.

Original_Search_Title: Njau Finally Signs His Paintings
Headline: Kenya: Njau Finally Signs His Paintings
Body_Text:
Nairobi — After a 50-year wait, veteran artist Elimo Njau has finally appended his signature to the murals at St James' Anglican Cathedral in Kiharu, Murang'a.
In a relatively low-key event presided over by Vicar Jesse Ngure, Njau, the founder of Paa Ya Paa Arts Centre, put to rest doubts regarding the person behind the six paintings that tell the story of Jesus Christ.
"I painted these works during the colonial era and I feared the colonial government would sabotage them because they were so good that the white man would not believe that they were the work of a 'native'," he says. "In fact, the colonialists believed that the murals were the work of one Harper Jackson.
Elimo was then an art student at Makerere University, Uganda. At that time he was living with the late Obadiah Kariuki, the first African Anglican bishop, at his Kiharu home. "I did the work for free and my aim was simply to tell the story of Christ from an African perspective, away from what the colonialists were ramming down our throats."
Consecrated by the then Archbishop of Canterbury, Edward Knapp-Fisher in December, 1969, the murals have over the decades earned international fame, if the number of publications that refer to them is anything to go by.
They have been quoted in African Art, a self-explanatory tome by Marshall Ward while Elimo was the subject of Portrait of An Artist: Elimo Njau, The Antelope-man, produced by URTNA.
Interestingly, these references acknowledge Njau as the creator of the murals, even though they did not bear his signature. "There are no such pictures in the whole of Africa," claims Njau.
Not unexpectedly, plagiarists are using the murals for commercial gain without acknowledging the painter. Though his aim was not to make money from the murals, Njau says, anybody seeking to reproduce them as a photograph, book or magazine cover should seek his permission first.
"I saw one distorted version of the murals, which was depicting the story of Christ's baptism in reverse," says the jovial 74-year-old.
He took about seven months to complete them (from sketching to painting), which is a record by any standards.
Besides painting, Njau is also a poet, carver, sculptor and initiator of the banana fibre technique, which he developed during his years at Makerere in the 1950s.
Paa ya Paa Arts Centre, which he founded in 1961, is located at Ridgeways, off Kiambu Road. It has several paintings and art works that portray Elimo's deeply religious spirit.
"My spirit has been reborn. I feel I can create again," he said after signing the paintings at Murang'a.
Paa Ya Paa survived a mysterious fire in 1997, which destroyed a good number of pieces by artists from all over the world. Njau runs the place with the help of his American-born wife, Philda.
He is also credited with having started the Kibo Arts Centre in Tanzania. Born in Tanzania, he first came to Kenya in 1979 while teaching at Makerere. He began by managing Sorsbie Gallery, then pitched tent at Sadler House (now Consolidated Bank House, Koinange Street), which was then a favourite haunt for the likes of novelists Ngugi wa Thiong'o, Okot P'Bitek and poet Jonathan Kariara.
Paa Ya Paa, which was founded in 1961, now houses freedom fighters' creative and meditative gardens, love garden and an artist's residence.
It has also played host to several notable personalities among them former US president Jimmy Carter (in 1998) and actor Sydney Poitier in 1970.

Original_Search_Title: Teacher, Poet And Musician Rolled Into One
Headline: Zimbabwe: Teacher, Poet And Musician Rolled Into One
Body_Text:
Harare — IN FOOTBALL they say Gwekwerere Bhora, but in music we can now say Garakara Song!
Or if you want to go ahead and add Garakara Idya Banana Kechi-One because this is likely to be the title of Joseph Garakara's third album and that release will prove whether the 2006 Zima award winner is genuine or a fad.
Or even whether he is just another teacher trying to crawl his way out of the classroom into showbiz like Bob Nyabinde.
Or if he is going to be like the Tula Bechulude teachers who disappeared when the echoes of their song died down.
"I am not new to the music industry. In fact, this album -- Tapinda Tapinda -- is my second. My first was Uyu Neni, which we recorded with Record and Tape Promotions in 2004. Of course, it did not do well," he says.
But even the album that did not do well was not his first time into music because he used to play with Detembira and Chirikure Chirikure.
"I come from a musical family. I grew up playing mbira with my siblings -- Daisy, Sekai, Norman and Lovemore -- as Mhuri Yekwa Garakara. We played in all night ritual ceremonies for a fee," he explains.
Both Garakara, who was in Grade 5 then, and his siblings were not trained to play mbira. They used to watch their elder brother Lovemore playing the instrument. When he was out, they would sneak into his hut where they taught themselves how to play on his mbira.
When he found them out, Lovemore was not angry but worked with them instead.
The next stage was when Garakara's father, who was also a musician who played kwela when he was in South Africa, started making guitars for sale. Once again, the youngsters would wait until he left home and then pounce on the instruments.
When the father discovered their ploy, he did not angry either but encouraged them by showing them how to play.
Thus Garakara's fate was sealed.
"After school, I did some part-time teaching until I got a place at a college in Harare. I did not take music but after training, I became my school's choirmaster in Mhondoro. But I longed for music and when I saw an advert in the paper for studying at the College of Music, I applied and attended a two-year full-time course.
"I graduated last year with two distinctions in performance studies and organology. I believe that the success of my album is largely because of the knowledge I acquired from the college," he says.
The songs on Tapinda Tapinda were composed last year and the album was supposed to have been recorded in September last year but was put on hold following the death of his father.
Recording was then set for October but again there was a family bereavement as Garakara's sister passed away and the album was deferred once more.
"I was not sure of the album's success until I was given a contract," he confesses.
Idya Banana, Garakara says, does not mean what most people think. He says he chose banana as a symbol of something soft and by using it, he was, in actual fact, pleading with a girl to soften her heart.
"I was just saying to a girl: 'Wadii kurerutsa ndima?'" he laughed.
He added: "Of course, the other song on the album that could do well if given a chance is Ndege Yamashanga. In the song I sing about people who make foolish decisions."
As the Zima most promising artiste, Garakara says there is pressure on him already to prove that he is not just passing but here to stay.
"I know people are expecting a lot from me but then I am a poet. I am also a trained musician. I use notation and understand quite a lot of things. With this knowledge, I do not think that there are problems.
"We are already rehearsing for our third album which is going to be called Kechi-One," Garakara says.
He admits that his music carries traces of Njerama's beat but is quick to say that in music one cannot pinpoint exactly where one's inspiration comes from.
"It could be true but I regard Richard Mafuwamhandu as the man who inspired me. I used to listen to his music quite a lot. I like his lyrics especially," he reveals.
The late Mafuwamhandu was the lead vocalist of the now defunct Nyaminyami Sounds.
The 37-year-old teacher, who has since transferred to an Epworth school where he has been now for a month, says initially his workmates did not know that he was a musician.
"Nevertheless, they support my efforts," he says.
For spawning the Zima 2006 Song of the Year and for being crowned the Most Promising Artiste, Joseph Garakara is The Herald Star of the week.
As usual, The Herald gives out prizes to readers who answer the following questions correctly.

Original_Search_Title: No Surprise Winners At Zima Awards
Headline: Zimbabwe: No Surprise Winners At Zima Awards
Body_Text:
Harare — IN previous years, the Zimbabwe Music Awards generated so much debate and criticism on the selection process that was flawed and presided over by a panel that either had no idea of what music is or just did not want to be neutral arbiters.
This year's process managed to reach out and embrace musicians who deserve the awards, not so much because they have been in the business for a long time but simply because they are doing well.
Who can begrudge Amai Charamba, Oliver Mtukudzi, Jeys Marabini, Clive Malunga and Iyasa for emerging the best in their respective categories?
Who will question Joseph Garakara's double accolade -- Best Song of the Year and Most Promising Artiste? This man from the backwater of Mhondoro managed to make short work of the "city slickers".
His Idya Banana, off the album Tapinda Tapinda, is a cheerful, comical and happy-go-luck song, which oozes energy that sucks you in. It is a song any one can sing along to, a song for any occasion.
The Most Promising Artiste has always been a tricky award because those who have won it in the past proved to be the most disappointing artistes.
Unlike in soccer where the likes of Evans Gwekwerere come in from nowhere and become instant hits, in music the majority of promising artistes become history soon after their second album.
Maybe this is because they put so much energy into their debut album that they become spent forces by the time they return to the studio for a second bite of the cherry.
In most cases, the so-called most promising artistes' debut albums carry one plug track whose death precedes the artiste's. Hopefully, this gentleman from Mhondoro will be inspired to go beyond Idya Banana.
Who would question Victor Kunonga's award as the Best Traditional Contemporary because he is steady, sober, consistent, determined and, above all, very calculating in his approach.
He is one young artiste who regards his musical career as a profession that needs to be managed well. He is head and shoulders above most of his peers in the industry.
This award is an endorsement of his undisputed presence in the industry.
Another no-surprise winner was Alexio Kawara, who was named the Best Male Urban Grooves Artiste. Alexio is a different breed from his counterparts who hang aimlessly around town and often make it into the newspaper pages, but for the wrong reasons, like assaulting their girlfriends in public places.
His lyrics are deep, pregnant with meaning, memorable and appeal to a wide cross-section of society.
Like Victor, Alexio sees his musical career as a profession on which he depends for his bread and butter.
Philbert Marowa, the Best Jazz Artiste, is a surprise. Here is a man who spends most of the time behind the scenes and then "experiments" with Kariba Bream and what happens?
When Chiedza Chavatendi's albums hogged the top positions at the Record and Tape Promotions' weekly charts, it did not make much sense. After all, there are various such groups who ply their musical trade much like the pioneers Vabati VaJehovha.
Then the group was on television, in the streets of Harare and eventually everywhere. Comprised of young people, Chiedza Chavatendi's lyrics are about what is happening now. They do not sing about hell waiting when people finally die. Rather, it's about giving hope and subtle enouragement to the "sinner' to change their ways.
Allan Chimbetu shed tears when he was named the Best Sungura Artiste for his album Sonny. It's not clear why he cried but it could be that he was overwhelmed with joy or the memory of his mentor, the late Mukoma Simon, was too much for him.
Or it was because of pressure that comes with high expectations from the group's fans? But this is his award and he should not try to be Simon or anybody else, but himself.
That way he will be able to think clearly and produce better albums than Sonny.
As for Tia, Iyasa, Jeys Marabini and Mawungira Enharira it's no surprise that they won.
It's worthwhile to mention that while Malaika was awarded the best appreciated group outside the country, there is debate in South Africa today on which of two groups is better than the other.
But then if awards are anything to go by, Mafikizolo deserves this award after bagging six awards between 2004 and 2005.
They scooped the Metro FM 2004 Best Afro Pop Album, Best Group and Best Album. Then came the 2004 Kora Award for Best New-comer Group. Next was the 2005 Channel O Music Video Awards for Best Afro Pop Group.
In 2005, they were back again as the Kora Best Southern Africa Group. They scored a double in the South Africa Music Awards for Best Selling Album and Best Afro Pop.

Original_Search_Title: Left to Fend for Siblings At 10
Headline: Uganda: Left to Fend for Siblings At 10
Body_Text:
Isingiro — AT 14 GIBSON Niwarinda is already heading a family in Nyabugando village, Isingiro district.
"Both my parents Mr Christopher Kamaniini and Ms Hope Kankore died of HIV/Aids," Niwarinda says.
"I can't remember the year. We were staying in Kiiha village in Hoima district."
His parents had sold their family land in Nyabugando in Isingiro and relocated to Hoima where they died in the 1990s leaving behind six children.
"After they were dead, my maternal grandfather Bernard Kibiiho came to Hoima and sold the land we were living on. He took us to his home in Nyabugando," Niwarinda says.
"I did not know how much was got from the sale of the land but the land that our grandfather bought in Nyabugando was just enough for a small house."
At 10 Niwarinda was faced with a task of taking care of his five siblings.
He says his grand father's wife was uncomfortable with them staying with them, so he moved his siblings to the small plot the grandfather had bought for them.
"I built a make shift fibre hut commonly known as eki bus and shifted with my brothers and sisters," he says.
His siblings are Herbert Owunshaba, 13, Livius Kyensi'rikora, 12, Wilberforce Byamugisha, 11, Stevenson Kansiime, six and Tukamuheebwa Moderate, four.
Niwarinda then started doing casual work to support his family.
"We never had source pans or plates. I used to borrow a source pan from the neighbours and we would use green banana leaves as plates," Niwarinda said adding, "This went on for long until good samaritans gave us one sauce pan and two plates."
"Then I was in Primary four at Nyabugando Primary school 2003. I would go to dig for food and money to pay fees for me and my young ones" Niwarinda says.
He would dig for three days in a week and go to school for two days.
But this method could not work for long as pressure for domestic needs from his young ones mounted.
"I later dropped out of school in Primary four as I could no longer raise fees for all and enough food through casual labour."
All this happened as neighbours helplessly watched. Because of the poverty people were unable to help the children.
Niwarinda's sister Kyensi'rikora later dropped out of school in order to cook for the family. The others kept in school. Owunshaba is in Primary seven, Byamugisha and Kansiime are in Primary four while Tukamuheebwa is in nursery school.
"My sister also dropped out of school, she now acts as the mother and I as the father," said Niwarinda.
Hope
"It is in 2005 that I started seeing hope in my life."
When talk of our plight reached the area LC3 councillor Penelope Byarugaba, she visited and registered us in her Kamubeizi Efuuzi Twimukye group."
Byarugaba later took Niwarinda to a Unicef agent at Ngarama sub-county who gave him 18 iron sheets and 3kgs of nails to roof their house.
At Kamubeizi Efuuzi Twimukye group, Niwarinda started a tailoring course.
He hopes to get his own sewing machine so that he can make ends meet.
The children were also helped by the Agency for Co-operation and Research in Development (Acord). The organisation introduced the Parish Orphans Care Associations which deals with the community's responses to HIV/Aids and its impact on households.
"Because of Niwarinda's experience, I have stopped helping people who think they can live on begging" said Narathius Asingwire of Acord.

Original_Search_Title: The Way Forward in Naming Issue
Headline: Botswana: The Way Forward in Naming Issue
Body_Text:
The debate on the naming of public institutions and landmarks in honour of outstanding individuals - be they local and foreign - resurfaced last week in Parliament. From the time of independence, Botswana's policy on this issue has always been clear - no naming of institutions and landmarks after people who are still alive.
Paradoxically, this policy did not apply to foreigners, hence we have an embarrassing situation of streets named after dubious characters like Africa's foremost purveyor of kleptocracy in the person of former Zairean (now Democratic Republic of Congo) dictator Mobuto Sese Seko, iron-fist Malawi ruler Kamuzu Banda and wayward tyrant Robert Mugabe of Zimbabwe. Incidentally, Mugabe's misrule has contributed to soaring crime in Botswana. It must be said that the likes of Julius Nyerere of Tanzania and Nelson Mandela of South Africa were named after landmarks in Botswana yet they did not live to embarrass us. It is not clear why our policy was crafted in such a way as to honour foreigners and not locals. Perhaps, at that nascent stage of our development, we were eager for recognition from our neighbours, most of whom were then dominated by one-party dictatorships.
The first institution to be named in honour of a living Motswana, Julia Molefhe clinic in Gaborone, raised eyebrows.
Last week's parliamentary motion had sought to recognise Sir Ketumile Masire's contribution to the country, and it was amended to include other deserving Batswana. For the record, we have nothing against Masire and other Batswana being honoured. In fact his contribution has been immense. Masire and his contemporaries such as Sir Seretse Khama, as architects of modern Botswana, had it in their wisdom that the policy of naming institutions and landmarks is likely to be open to abuse. And we agree with them on this.
What we propose is that the policy should be applied across the board. No living foreigner or local should be named after a landmark or institution. This is not all. The names of nasty individuals like Mobutu, Mugabe and Banda should be removed from our institutions or landmarks. We are a respected democracy and we should not honour despots, murderous and kleptomaniacs. We should strictly stick to the policy of not naming institutions and landmarks after living individuals to avoid a situation where an egocentric leadership will christen everything after himself and his family. The current policy has served us well, so let us maintain, and at the best make it uniform by applying it to foreigners.
We should not encourage the naming of everything in honour of some demented, banana-republic type leader and his or her sycophants.

Original_Search_Title: 'Tintin Goes Bananas' At National Theatre
Headline: Uganda: 'Tintin Goes Bananas' At National Theatre
Body_Text:
Kampala — Play: Tintin goes Bananas
Playwright: Patricia Kleeb
Director: Paul Adlam
Group: Kampala Amateur Dramatics Society (KADS)
Showing at: The National theatre, this weekend
Preview by: E Ssejjengo
Remember phrases like " billions of blue blistering barnacles"? They come back, but this time on a stage right in front of our noses.
KADS' year ends with a pantomime of an adaptation of Tintin's comic strips that everyone who had a childhood will remember.
For this well-known story with a fantastical plot, the director should have less work, as the audience will shout out the instructions.
And who would not like the intrepid boy reporter, Tintin, to get out of trouble when his work sets him up in those difficult situations? And what do you do when you realise that fevered Snowy, Tintin's faithful four-legged companion, is painstakingly going to hit rock bottom? You direct him.
All the singing and screaming should bring the house down, especially that the kids are back home. That confusing character of the dame that always adds humour to pantomimes could provide a highlight in the production.
This should be a season of laughter and KADS are having the last laugh.
Tintin has fascinated the world for the last 70 years and our very own Tintin with the missing matooke is, in a word, funny.
If you had not started your Christmas season, it is a worthwhile experience to start it here as this is a family show.

Original_Search_Title: Garakara Scoops Two Zimas
Headline: Zimbabwe: Garakara Scoops Two Zimas
Body_Text:
Harare — MHONDORO-BASED sungura artiste Joseph Garakara achieved a rare feat by taking home two trophies in the Zimbabwe Music Awards yesterday at a ceremony held at Meikles Hotel in Harare.
Previously unknown, Garak-ara's sizzling debut album Tapinda Tapinda saw him being voted the Most Promising Artiste and his song Idya Banana becoming the Song of the Year.
Mai Charamba and superstar Oliver Mtukudzi also walked away with two awards each.
