Mai Charamba won the Best Gospel (Female) and Best Female Artiste Awards while Mtukudzi won the Music Ambassador Award and Best Ringing Tone for Handiro Dambudziko.
Versatile gospel star Tawanda Mutyebere won the Best Gospel Artiste Award while sultry Tia scooped the Best Urban Grooves (Female) Award.
Best Urban Grooves (Male) went to Alexio Kawara of the Pfimbi Yangu fame.
Victor Kunonga won the Best Traditional Contemporary Group/Artiste award while Clive Malunga's Jenaguru won the Best Chimurenga award.
Bulawayo-based theatre mogul Cont Mhlanga was in charge of the whole production.
The classy red carpet treatment and top-of-the-shelf arrangement gave the awards, whose theme was "The Essence of Celebration", a whole new flair matching international standards.
All the categories with their winners are as follows, l for winner:
Best Gospel Artiste (Male)
Best Gospel Artiste (Female)
Best Urban Grooves Artiste (Male)
Best Urban Grooves (Female)
Best Traditional Contemporary Group/Artiste
Best Jazz Artiste
Best Mbira Group
Best Choral Group
Best Chimurenga Musician
Best Marabi Contemporary Musician
Most Promising Artiste
Song of the Year
Best Live Performer
Best Producer of the Year
Best Ringing Tone
Best Sungura
Best Appreciated Group/Artiste outside the country

Original_Search_Title: Same-Sex Law Makes South Africans Seeth
Headline: Kenya: Same-Sex Law Makes South Africans Seeth
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Twelve years after emerging from apartheid, South Africa continues to be considered the torchbearer in the "African Renaissance" through its renowned respect for civil liberties, but a recent decision to legalise same-sex marriages is threatening to torpedo this reputation.
Last year, the South African Constitutional Court ruled that the country should by Thursday last week introduce legislation allowing for same-sex partnerships.
The country's National Assembly passed the Civil Union Bill, which now awaits the endorsement of the National Provinces Committee, and eventually President Thabo Mbeki's signature.
The court ruled that if the Thursday deadline was not met, the Marriage Act would automatically be changed so that it applied equally to same-sex partnerships and heterosexual marriages.
This meant that the country becomes the first on the continent and fifth in the world to legalise marriage for gay and lesbian couples when lawmakers gave their final approval to the controversial legislation.
The legislation has sparked debate in Southern Africa and beyond with most critics labelling it "un-African and unnatural."
Former South African vice-president and a front-runner to succeed President Thabo Mbeki when he steps down, Mr Jacob Zuma, was forced to beat a hasty retreat after passing comments that were considered to be degrading to gays.
Zimbabwe's President Robert Mugabe, a respected figure in the Southern African Development Community (SADC) for his liberation war credentials, describes gays as "worse than pigs."
Last week, one of his lieutenants, Mr Didymus Mutasa, who doubles up as the minister of State security, made known Harare's feelings about the legislation when he described it as "shocking."
Mutasa was speaking during a reception he hosted for Zimbabwean and South African delegations attending a meeting of a Joint Permanent Commission on Defence and Security in Zimbabwe's resort town of Victoria Falls.
He challenged South Africa's minister of defence, Mosiua Lekota, to explain to Zimbabweans what the legislation was meant to achieve.
"Recently, you passed legislation to allow men to marry other men and women to marry other women. Good heavens! I find that very difficult. To be quite honest, I find it very difficult because, how can I be attracted to another man sexually? How will women view me afterwards?"
"Our president does not like it and when he spoke against it, he was not speaking for himself alone but for all of us," Mutasa said.
Mr Lekota explained that when the South African legislators passed the law, they did not look at whether it was right or wrong, "because only God can judge".
"The issue was whether we would deny this man who wants to marry another man and give him the right to inherit his property, or whether to deny a woman who wants to marry another woman of her choice."
At the end, we stood guided by the words of our foremost leader Nelson Mandela, who said: "Never never and never again will it be that one South African or some South Africans suppress other South Africans," he said.
Mr Mutasa said although he was aware that some Zimbabweans were gay, "the country would never legalise gay marriages."
In Zimbabwe, same-sex relationships are outlawed and for a number of years, the Gays and Lesbians Association of Zimbabwe was denied permission to participate in trade fairs across the country.
A survey conducted in Zimbabwe last year indicated that the majority of the respondents were opposed to the recognition of the rights of gay people.
However, homosexuality is not as strange a phenomenon in the country as the authorities would want to believe. Former Zimbabwe President Canaan Banana was tried for allegedly sodomising his bodyguards during his tenure.
In South Africa itself, the Bill has sparked fierce opposition, with the Business Day newspaper reporting that traditional leaders were mobilising against its passage. The traditionalists, who wield a lot of power in rural communities, declared that they would not allocate communal land to gay couples.
Christian groups have also held a series of demonstrations against the legislation, which many people believe is a product of minority white South Africa - a symbol of the brutal apartheid system.
More gay than other
Summing up the feelings of the growing band opposed to the legislation, prominent Zimbabwean historian Phathisa Nyathi said South Africa risked being viewed as an extension of Western influence in a conservative continent.
"The mere fact that South Africa is more gay than other African countries, shows just how much white influence is in that country. South Africa is certainly leading the way in Africa. But leading where to? It is clear whose values are being promoted here. In the First World, there is nothing wrong with being gay. It is just part of what they call freedoms," Nyathi said.
Despite protests by religious groups and opposition parties in a region where homosexuality remains largely taboo, the Civil Unions Bill was powered through by the ruling African National Congress.
South Africa's Cabinet approved the Bill in August after the country's highest court ruled it was unconstitutional to deny gay people the right to marry.
The country will become the first country in Africa to accord homosexual couples over the age of 18 the same rights as their heterosexual counterparts, borrowing a leaf from countries such as US (2004), Belgium (2003), the Netherlands (2001), Spain and Canada (2005).
Mr Nyathi is a journalist in Harare, Zimbabwe

Original_Search_Title: Old, New Horses Vie for Honours
Headline: Zimbabwe: Old, New Horses Vie for Honours
Body_Text:
Harare — THIS year's edition of Zimbabwe Music Awards slated for the Meikles Hotel in Harare tomorrow are a case of the old voice against new ones.
In the best gospel male category, the old horse Diva Mafunga faces Taurai Gorden Nzira and Tawanda Mutyebere.
This time, Kudzai Nyakudya, who held onto the award for two years, did not make it.
Those who have had the opportunity of seeing either Mutyebere or Nzira on stage bear witness to the boys' talents and as such, Mafunga, despite having been in the industry for some time, has a difficult situation to deal with.
Donna Chibaya, Mercy Mutsvene and Amai Charamba are competing in the best female gospel artiste category where the real competition could be between Mutsvene and Amai Charamba.
The best urban grooves male artiste category is an exception because all the three artistes are young and relatively new and here Alexio Kawara, Nzira and Extra Large jostle for the award.
Nzira is good but it is not clear whether he could stand much ground against Extra Large and Kawara, not because the two have been happening before him, but simply because he does not sell his music as urban grooves but gospel.
His appeal is therefore not so much to the hardcore urban groove fans but to the churchgoing young people who prefer a "clean act" characterised by uplifting lyrics.
Cindy, Tia and Sku are vying for the best urban grooves artiste female award. While Tia and Sku can claim having been present on the scene for a few years, Cindy sounds new.
Competition in this category is tight unlike in the best traditional contemporary category where Victor Kunonga stands up against Lwazi Tshabangu and Max Mapfumo of the Dopiro fame.
Tshabangu has been doing the club circuit for some time now dishing out both his original creations and Lovemore Majaivana's. At one time he was touted as the new Majaivana.
Mapfumo has been a case of "now-am-in and now-am-out".
His return this time accompanied by the live show onslaught has helped his curriculum vitae and made him a competitor but not when he is pitted against Kunonga whose act has been consistently perfected over the last two years.
The outcome in the best jazz artiste category depends on who listens to what and where.
Bob "Headmaster" Nyabinde is the most popular of the three -- the other two are Mono Mkundu and Philbert Marowa.
Although the fact that Mkundu and Marowa are best known as session musicians does not make them lesser musicians, pitting them against Nyabinde is a tall order.
Even in this category, Nyabinde has been considered for some musical awards before.
If ever there is going to be a headache for the judges, then it has to come from the best mbira group category where Mbira Dzenharira meets Mawungira Enharira.
It is not so much because the other group is a splinter but because one of the albums -- Gonamombe Rerume -- that is being used as a standard measure was the last recording done by the original Mbira Dzenharira.
The album carries all the markings of Mawungira Enharira, which means comparing it with the splinter group's self-titled album, is like comparing one group's product.
Vabati VaJehovha returns to compete with Impumelelo Shining Stars and Chiedza Chavatendi in the best mass choral group.
This is another case of the teacher competing with his able pupil. The outcome is not easy to predict.
Thomas Mapfumo's Unplugged marks the maestro's return to his genuine roots where the sounds of mbira ring out clear like water but whether the master will keep his crown when Clive Malunga and Gwindingwi Rine Shumba stand up to challenge him in the best Chimurenga musician category is something else.
Maybe, the question would be whether Chimurenga music is mbira or a combination of mbira and guitars or just some sounds akin to mbira music being produced from whatever source.
Will Chase Skuza ride on his late brother Solomon's fame and clinch the best marabi contemporary musician award?
This time, the young man has to be on his own and it's not going to be that easy.
Maybe it's going to be but not when there is Jeys Marabini who, like Kunonga, has been building his career through great videos and lyrics.
Trevor Dongo and Joseph Garakara of the Idya Banana fame have a fight in the most promising artiste category.
The two have penetrated radio and thus predicting who is going to be crowned the prince is difficult.
This time around, Zima winners are a closely-guarded secret and tomorrow will bring the answers.

Original_Search_Title: Tintin Goes Bananas
Headline: Uganda: Tintin Goes Bananas
Body_Text:
Title: Tintin goes bananas
Director: Tom Adlam
Written by: Patrick Kleeb
Cast: Kristen Boe, Michael Melvill, Tangee Griffin, Samuel Lutaaya , Kat Tucker, Courtney Preston, Tom Adlam, Alex Porter, Colin Barnard.
Venue: National Theatre
Date: 29 Nov - 3 Dec
Kampala Amateur Dramatic society (KADS) is back in yet another production, Tintin goes bananas.
The play centres on the adventures of the popular comic strip hero Belgian boy journalist Tintin, who was created by Belgian cartoonist Herge in 1929.
This time though, the adventures of Tintin are set in Uganda. In true style, Tintin, played by Kristen Boe, races against time to solve the mysteries of the missing matooke and disappearance of the gorillas from Bwindi impenetrable forest.
With the assistance of his friends Captain Haddock (Tom Adlam), Professor Calculus (Colin Barnard), Snowy the dog (Michael Melvill), an intrepid Ugandan safari guide (Samuel Lutaaya) and his fiancée, fifteen brave children and a silverback gorilla, Tintin confronts his old enemy, the criminal mastermind Roberto Rastapopoulos (Kat Tucker) in a struggle that has only one outcome - mad musical mayhem!
This play provides interactive family entertainment that will enchant the young and old alike with the all too familiar music and plot.

Original_Search_Title: New Brewery Coming
Headline: Rwanda: New Brewery Coming
Body_Text:
Kigali — After putting up a magnificent building in town, given a tender to construct a new stadium in preparation for the 2011 Under-19 All African Championship bid, the tycoon is at it again. Tribert Rugijiro has bought the local industry that brews Tarama, Sabana, Susuruka, Akarusho all of which are made from banana juice. He, apparenyl, has been given piece of land along Nyabarongo River to construct a modern brewery that will add value to the local brews to suit international standards.
Besides this, our snoops tell us that the Union Centre Complex will house a new radio station courtesy of the same tycoon. The said FM Radio is proposed to be called Star F.M. Meanwhile, the best presenters in Rwanda have vowed to resign from their stations which offer peanuts and join Star F.M which they expect to pay handsomely given the person who owns it.

Original_Search_Title: Giving Hope to Women
Headline: Uganda: Giving Hope to Women
Body_Text:
Kampala — She picks up a trouser and places it under the needle of the sewing machine. Its wheel spins, producing a rhythm that attracts your attention even before you see it. Her legs move up and down, with her hands pushing the trouser forward. Stella Aine is at work.
It is 10:13am on a Tuesday, the market day in Mukono town. Before Aine completes her task, two other women enter her shop, located in Mukono Central Market.
"Mending jeans is not easy. I have to press it hard on the sewing machine just because I need your money," she tells a waiting customer.
Tailoring is what Aine does to earn a living to take care of her two siblings.
"My father died in 1992 and in 2003 my mother also died. That shattered our lives," she says with difficulty.
It marked a turning point in her life. "I wanted to go on with my studies but none of my relatives came forward to assist me," she says.
"I tried getting a bursary as an orphan but it just didn't work. I dropped out of school in S3," says the 24-year-old former student of Progressive SSS, Kajjansi on Entebbe Road.
The second in a family of four, Aine's stepmother took her in. She was reduced to doing only house chores. "I didn't think I would go back to school," she says, sadly.
Aine saw her hopes of graduating with a Bachelor's degree in Education wane as years passed. One day, however, a friend told her of a place she could study for free.
"Was it true? Many questions crossed my mind," she says. The Aine's friend took her to Mukono Women Skills Development Centre. This vocational school, which offers free training for girls in the district, is the brainchild of the Mukono Town Council Mayor, Johnson Muyanja.
"I conceived the idea of starting this training centre during one of my visits to Europe. Local governments in Gran municipality, Norway, for example, have special programmes for orphans and needy children," Muyanja says.
He says being the chairman of Mukono Young Men's Christian Association (YMCA) also spurred him to start the programme.
In conjunction with NORAD, a Norwegian aid agency, Muyanja started the school, which is adjacent to the town council offices. The centre has several secondary school dropouts. This two-year-old institution has so far held three graduation ceremonies in which over 500 students have graduated with certificates in catering, salon and hair dressing, tie-and-dye, tailoring and computer skills.
The institution has 40 computers connected to the Internet and 20 sewing machines. Each course runs for eight months.
"We also advise the students on how to steer clear of HIV/AIDS. Being youth, they are adventurous. We advise them to abstain from sex or use condoms when they just can't avoid sex," Muyanja says.
The centre has become a hunting ground of sorts for young men in the town. They track down these young beauties like hyenas stalking prey.
Smartly dressed, some of them even hire bodabodas to take them there. They lay ambushes at 5:00pm when their prey come out of classes.
The students get regular counselling from the district health staff and free blood testing from the Uganda Red Cross Society (URCS) officials during their periodical visits.
"I joined because I want to get computer skills so that I can start my own computer bureau," says Shadia Nanono, a student.
Nanono is one of the 356 students who enrolled during the July intake.
"We received over 500 applications for the September intake," Muyanja says.
While at the centre, students are always busy. Those doing classes use manila papers for their practicals. "Pass me that shirt," says one student to another, referring to a dummy cut out of the papers.
In the computer room, Viola Nakafu, a computer instructor, is explaining how to type using Microsoft Word in Luganda.
"I have a serious problem here," she says. "Some girls have not been to school at all. I have to teach them separately," Nakafu says. This is even harder, because computer commands are in English. "This makes my work more difficult. Even during exams, I separate those who speak and write English from those who don't," she explains.
The former bursar at Highway Nursery and Primary School says she is keen on her new job because she earns more money. The centre is congested because it only has three classrooms. Muyanja, however, says there are plans to put up more structures.
"We expect about 400 more students in our next intake. This means putting up more classrooms and other facilities," he says.
Because of the big numbers, students doing the salon and hair-dressing course study in two shifts. "Even with this arrangement, the students can't all fit in one classroom," says Ronald Yiga, an instructor at the centre. As a result some students are forced to have their lessons outside the classroom.
Aine graduated in 2004. "I chose tailoring because I knew that I would make money as soon as I got a sewing machine," she says.
Under this scheme, students pursuing the tailoring course contribute 50% of the cost of a sewing machine to carry away one after graduation. Freda Atulinda, 17, a former student of Hoima High School, joined in 2004. "I am doing a course in computer skills because I want to open a secretarial bureau in Mukono town," she says.
"I am now independent. I am able to pay rent and school fees for my siblings," Aine says. To supplement her income, Aine sells fruit salad. She buys oranges, bananas, watermelon and jack fruit from Mukono Central Market. The fruits are sliced and packed in containers, then sold at sh500 each.
"I sell as many as 30 in a single day. Sales usually go up during the dry season," Aine says.
She intends to return to school after saving enough money. "I had given up on life, but the training I got has helped me. Going back to school will be hard, but I know I accomplish that. I want to be a successful woman," she says.

Original_Search_Title: Peter Takes a Dig At the Girls
Headline: Zimbabwe: Peter Takes a Dig At the Girls
Body_Text:
Harare — IT sure feels good to be back. I have been missing you since I went away two weeks ago. I could not bring you last week's instalment due to pressure of work. I was running around with the Top Companies Magazine, which is in today's issue.
The powers-that-be at The Financial Gazette decided that we had to come to work over the weekend, during which I normally write, LTV, Level The Vibes to the uninitiated, to beat the printing deadline for Top Companies Magazine. I couldn't say no, being the low man on the totem pole at the paper where there are more chiefs than Indians, if you know what I mean.
You know what it's like under such circumstances folks. You are always expected to put your shoulder to the wheel without minding the squeeze.
The good thing though is that I am back and raring to go. I thought I was probably the only one hooked to reggae music like a junkie to his fix.
And boy, oh boy, was I wrong. That is if the list of mail I received requesting me to come up with what I personally think are the reggae songs of the 1980s and 1990s is anything to go by. The list is a mile and half long.
Well even though I cannot give you the full list this week,
I think I would title the imaginary double CD Best Of The 80's and 90s-- Taking A Fleeting Glance Down The Corridor of Time. The first CD would have strictly lovers' rock and the second one would have dancehall.
I know that the suggested title of the CD is a bit longish. But that's the way I would like it.
Such a title reminds me of the controversial Irish singing priest, Sinead Oconnor's mouthful of a 2003 CD titled She Who Dwells In The Street Place Of The Most High Shall Abide Under The Shadow Of The Almighty.
Best Of The 80s and 90s--Taking A Fleeting Glance Down The Corridor Of Time will, as a compilation collector's item surely include Super Cat's Sweets For My Sweets and Boops, Pam Hall's Dear Boopsie, Beresford Hammond's Groove A Little Ting and Honeycomb, Trevour Sparks' Wings Of Love, Leroy Gibbons' Four Seasons Lover, Luciano's One Way Ticket, Shabba Ranks' Wicked In Bed, Gregory Isaacs' Substitute and Front Door, Dennis Brown's Joseph's Coat of Many Colours, Buju Banton's Destiny, Tenor Saw's Chill Out, Chill Out, Macka B's Don't Drink Too Much, John Holt's Sweet Come Brush Me, Cocoa Tea's P.O.S.S.Y and of course Level The Vibes by Mr Lindon Roberts better known in the reggae business as Half Pint. As you know it is after the last song that this column is named.
These are some of the best tunes from the era, according to yours truly. And like I always say, there is no accounting for tastes. So there is no need for any of my fans to argue with me. In matters of opinion debate is useless. I will give you the full list of what I think should be songs on the imaginary compilation album next week.
So much about that and now to this week's feature. It was a toss between Norman Jackson, better known as Tiger of the hit No Wanga Gut, or Donovan Harris. Yes, the one you know as Peter Metro of Debi Debi Girl fame. You might remember that this song--which talks about debi debi girls who keep the house untidy, cannot peel bananas, mix flour or wash white shirts--was such a big hit in Zimbabwe that it became one of the most requested dancehall tunes alongside Sophia George's Girlie Girlie and Michael Palmer's Television. The song, which Peter Metro did with Sister Charmaine, also takes a pot shot at men who have what I call a limping sex life. Those who have been through the dreaded mid-life crisis know what I am talking about.
There was a girl who lived in my neighbourhood when I lived where the people live, 1440 Lusaka in Highfield. She was beautiful, the kind of girl the singer says you can only see in dreams. A-picture-come-to-life, if you will. Her easy-on-the-eye looks are not the reason I remember her. But it is more to do with two things. The way she danced to Debi Debi Girl every time she came to the Agony Centre or Zimbabwe Hall where reggae music was happening in the 1980s into the early 1990s and the fact that she seemed to be a free spirit; unconventional and adventurous. Indeed, Millicent, who has since been spoken for, did not feel limited by the usual rules of social behaviour. This is as appealing to men as power and money are to women.
I remember one particular evening in 1987 at Zimbabwe Hall, the buxom, earthy attractive girl wearing a sexy bluish décolleté lace blouse with a mini-skirt to match, turning heads when she danced to Peter Metro and Sister Charmaine's Debi Debi Girl. As if the fact that her skirt was incredibly short was not provocative enough, she made it flare out every time she danced about. Of course they will not admit it, but many men in the dancehall had their imaginations running wild with the way she suggestively wiggled her well-put-together bottom. It is moments like these and of course the artistic merit that usually make it easy for a song to leave an indelible mark in your mind. It did for yours truly and many others as far as Debi Debi Girl is concerned.
When he started off as a DJ Peter Metro whose other tunes are Water Jelly, Fisherman Connection and Water Pumpee was known as Peter Ranking. He however dropped it when he realised that yet another DJ was using the same name. He assumed his current stage name while working with a sound system known as Metromedia. The DJ recorded his debut song, Jamaican Salute while still at Metromedia and after that worked with a number of producers and singers, including the DJ sensations Yellowman and Fathead. With Yellowman, Peter Metro recorded a version of Michael Jackson's The Girl Is Mine. It was a big hit in reggae circles. He also worked with a white English DJ known as Dominic to release the hit tune, Yardie And Cockney.
Peter Metro's success inspired his younger brother Squidly Ranking, (now known as Squidly Cole) to join Gemini Sound System where he frequently clashed with Yellowman. You may also want to know that Tanto Metro (real name Mark Wolfe) of the Tanto Metro and Devonte DJ sensation was introduced to Jimmy Metro, the owner of Metromedia by Peter Metro.
Although he emerged in the late 1970s, the 46 year old Peter Metro has never looked back. And he is still going strong. On July 19, 2006 he performed at Summerfest Productions-organised Top Ranking, a show that was meant to highlight some of the best years of dancehall music. He performed alongside artistes such as Gregory Isaacs, Frankie Paul, General Trees, Courtney Melody, Admiral Bailey, Flourgan, Pam Hall, Professor Nuts and Pinchers. The show was staged club style at Pier One at the Waterfront in Kingston.
Prayer
Just remember folks that no matter how difficult the situation you are in might appear, God's mercy will see us through. He will never let us down, which is why you should always give thanks and praise. I love you and you and you baby. All the best and God bless.

Original_Search_Title: ODM Plans to Paralyse Parliament
Headline: Kenya: ODM Plans to Paralyse Parliament
Body_Text:
Nairobi — MPs allied to ODM Kenya yesterday vowed to disrupt and paralyse parliamentary business to force the Government to accept minimum reforms.
They said their main agenda in the last session of the Ninth Parliament, which ends in two weeks, would be to ensure the passing of a Bill proposing "necessary changes to the Constitution" to level the playing field in next year's General Election.
The more than 40 MPs attending an ODM-K parliamentary group meeting at the Nairobi Club resolved to support the Bill being drafted by the Paul Muite-led committee on Administration of Justice and Legal Affairs.
As the ODM lawmakers announced their course of action following last week's collapse of the review talks, Ford Kenya MPs also agreed to back the minimum reforms Bill.
The Ford-K decision was reached at a National Executive Committee meeting chaired by party boss Musikari Kombo, who is also the Local Government minister.
Mr Kombo said: "It is surely unrealistic to expect the country to afford a referendum and a General Election in the space of three months."
But the Government side insisted that it would publish Bills in the next two weeks for debate in Parliament on what had earlier been agreed on by the Multi-Sectoral Review Forum before last week's stalemate.
Justice and Constitutional Affairs assistant minister Danson Mungatana accused ODM-K leaders of insincerity for arguing that comprehensive reforms cannot be achieved by mid next year.
The forum's members were drawn from the Government, political parties and civil society.
Mr Mungatana said they had all agreed that Section 47 be amended to entrench the review process in the Constitution.
Announcing the ODM-K position, party interim secretary-general Peter Anyang' Nyong'o said: "We will ensure that this reform package and a Bill to that effect is given priority and precedent in Parliament over any other business."
And he added: "We will not sit by and see Parliament transact any other business until the passing of this Bill is guaranteed."
However, it is House Business Committee chaired by Vice-President Moody Awori, as the leader of Government Business, which sets the agenda for Parliament.
ODM-K MPs might find it difficult to have their way in bringing the Muite Bill faster as the 20-member committee has a majority of Government MPs.
It meets every Tuesday evening to prioritise Bills, motions and questions which should be taken to the House, giving first priority to urgent Government matters.
But both sides of the House lack the requisite 65 per cent or 145 MPs of the 222 necessary to pass a constitutional Bill, and therefore need each other if minimum reforms touching on the Constitution have to be realised.
The ODM-K claim that they have on their side 110 MPs - even if Ford-K MPs were to join - would not attain the 145 mark. The Government also claims to have a slim majority of 112. Yesterday's ODM-K meeting was attended by MPs Raila Odinga (Lang'ata), Kalonzo Musyoka (Mwingi North), Billow Kerrow (Mandera Central) and William ole Ntimama (Narok North). They said the Muite Bill must be given priority once it is published and read first time in the House.
The package the ODM wants before next year's General Election includes independence of the Electoral Commission, Parliament to control its calendar and a provision for independent candidates.
They also want the next president to garner 51 per cent of the majority vote, a provision to block the Head of State from poaching MPs from the Opposition and representation of women, youth and the disabled through affirmative action.
Except for the Political Parties Bill to grant funding to parties, which they also want passed, the rest of the package involves amending the Constitution - which no side can do it alone.
Prof Nyong'o added: "We support the reform package prepared by the departmental committee on Administration of Justice and Legal affairs to guarantee a level playing ground in time for the next year's elections."
ODM-Kenya also said it would ensure that the country gets a new constitution along the recommendations of the Bomas Draft. Prof Nyong'o was speaking after the PG meeting at Nairobi Club to mark the Orange referendum victory exactly a year ago. The referendum vote was taken on November 21.
ODM Kenya garnered 3.5 million votes to the Government's (Banana team's) 2.5 million to defeat the Draft Constitution.
Yesterday, the party accused President Kibaki of "not being interested in a new constitution", citing Justice minister Martha Karua's rejection of minimum reforms proposed by the multi-sectoral forum.

Original_Search_Title: Prices Affect Banana Flour Market
Headline: Uganda: Prices Affect Banana Flour Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — Banana price fluctuations have affected the market of Uganda's banana flour, a Makerere don from the department of food science and technology has said.
Dr. Florence Isabirye Muranga who is also the project director for the presidential innitiative on banana industrial development said climate and seasonal changes have affected the exportation of banana flour.
"Uganda had ready market in Europe, but we could not maintain it because of our unstable prices," Muranga said while meeting leaders of Bushenyi district where government has constructed a banana processing factory.
"We need to maintain the pricing because they (foreign countries) donot want us to increase prices day after day."
However, Muranga said Uganda's banana flour is on high demand in the United States whom she said they want constant supply.
She also said children in primary schools from the district of Kabarole and Bushenyi are going to feed on banana flour as a way of promoting it.
She said the Government has committed enough money for the programme (banana industrial development).
The district chairman Longino Ndyanabo said, "we can stablise the price after improving the storage system."
The chief administrative officer, Charles Kiberu and district resident commissioner Sam Tibigweisa attended the meeting.

Original_Search_Title: Turns And Twists in Search for New Law
Headline: Kenya: Turns And Twists in Search for New Law
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is one year today since the Orange Democratic Movement handed the Government a resounding defeat in the referendum on the Constitution.
Over that period, Kenyans have witnessed dramatic developments in the fight for a new Constitution, but whose realisation has remained a mirage.
The Orange No-vote won the referendum on whether to adopt the proposed constitution with over 3.5 million votes, against 2.2 million 'Yes' votes.
The contentious issues in the draft had to do with the Executive, where the President still retained powers to appoint people into constitutional office.
Also, the Prime Minister's powers as proposed in the Bomas draft were watered down and the independence of the office undermined.
Seven rebel ministers dropped
Similarly contested was devolution of power after the Government removed some tiers, including a proposed Senate. Affirmative action, land and religious matters also brought out major differences.
Despite the No vote, intrigue over a new constitution has continued to dominate politics. President Kibaki responded to the defeat by dropping all the seven rebel ministers from his Cabinet two weeks after the vote.
Mr Raila Odinga, Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, Prof Anyang' Nyong'o, Mr Najib Balala, Mrs Linah Kilimo, Mr Ochillo Ayacko and Mr William ole Ntimama were shown the door.
During the reshuffle, Kibaki also sacked Dr Chris Murungaru, a powerful lieutenant and promoted people like Assistant minister, Mr Kalembe Ndile, whose antics had spiced up the otherwise low-key Government-sponsored banana rallies.
Government ignored calls for revival of reform agenda
It was also during the same house cleaning that Kibaki removed Mr Kiraitu Murungi from the Justice and Constitutional Affairs docket replacing him with Ms Martha Karua.
The Government then ignored calls from the Opposition and civil society for the revival of the reform agenda. Religious leaders from the then vibrant Ufungamano Multi-Sectoral Forum tried to revive the review and met the President at State House several times.
Civil society was operating from a weaker position, because most of its leaders like Prof Kivutha Kibwana, Mr Gichira Kibara and Justice Martha Koome were now in Government.
Serious effort towards the renewed interest in the constitution came from the Paul Muite-led Parliamentary Committee on the Administration of Justice and Legal Affairs (PCAJ).
'Government used recent talks to spy on ODM'
The committee agreed to introduce minimum reforms that would include the introduction of the Constitution Review Act, its entrenchment under section 47 and the referendum law.
The team also received input from the public seeking the independence of the Electoral Commission of Kenya, an affirmative action Bill and an independent calendar for Parliament.
It was at that period that Karua emerged with the inter-parties agenda with the Government insisting at its first Windsor meeting that it was for comprehensive reforms.
It later softened its stand, broadened the composition of the committee to bring on board the civil society whose agenda was also for comprehensive reforms.
Some critics have argued that they were brought on board to bolster the Government's position in a vote like the one that led to the chairman, Mr Dalmas Otieno's walkout, last week.
Debate on the review continues with claims from the Opposition that the Government used the recent talks to spy on the ODM-Kenya plans.
Kilonzo dismissed proposal for piecemeal reforms
Shadow Attorney General, Mr Mutula Kilonzo, says Government sources had confided in them that it does not want a new constitution.
"It was both cynical and arrogant for the Government to use constitutional talks to penetrate the opposition," said Kilonzo.
He dismissed the proposal for piecemeal reforms. He is convinced that neither side of the House will marshal 148 MPs to pass Karua's Constitution Amendment Act (2006). However, the PCAJ chairman Mr Paul Muite warned those fighting minimum reforms that they would not succeed.
"We will amend section 47 to ensure that whoever takes over leadership completes the process," said Muite.
"I was surprised by the fallout because the State supported the November Package that was to be handled concurrently with comprehensive reforms."

Original_Search_Title: Memories of November 21 Referendum - Lessons Learnt And Those Ignored
Headline: Kenya: Memories of November 21 Referendum - Lessons Learnt And Those Ignored
Body_Text:
Nairobi — If leaders could change history by deleting or abolishing the day, time and date on which it was made, President Mwai Kibaki would have done that with November 21.
But it cannot and so on Tuesday, November 21 will be on the calendar as the day Kenyans went to the referendum and defeated the Government on the proposed new constitution by more than one million votes.
An anniversary can be sweet or solemn, but either way, it is only the echo, not the cry, someone wrote recently.
As November 21 approaches, the Opposition is basking in the sweetness of the date, having defeated the Government at the ballot. The Government, on the other hand, has to contend with the solemn aspect of the anniversary, hoping the General Election next year will turn out differently.
In the exercise held last November 21, Kenyan voters rebelled against President Mwai Kibaki, handing him stinging defeat in the referendum on a new constitution.
President Kibaki had staked his reputation on the exercise, and rejection of his plan by 58 of the voters was widely seen as vote of no confidence in his three-year-old administration.
But as the anniversary approaches, opinion is divided between those who think the event changed everything-or nothing.
There are those who say there were no lessons learnt out of the exercise.
Yesterday, Lang'ata MP Mr Raila Odinga, who led a vicious campaign against the Wako Draft, said the Government had not learnt from the defeat.
"The Government has continued to live in denial. They still think Kenyans never knew what they were doing and never meant what they did," Raila said.
The MP said that when he started calls for minimum reforms, the President asked what the hurry was when Kenyans had rejected a new constitution during the November referendum.
"We in the Opposition learnt that Kenyans want a new constitution. They want a particular type of a new constitution and we are prepared to wait and give it to them later."
Raila said had the losers learnt anything, President Kibaki would have gone out of his way to reconcile the nation.
"He never did. Up to now, there has been no dialogue. If Kibaki had accepted the olive branch we extended, the country would not be this polarised. But we ended up in a situation where winners were chased away and losers let to run the country. It is a kind of dictatorship. It is spit on the faces of the people who rejected the leaders and what they stood for," Raila said.
The anniversary comes a week after President Mwai Kibaki reinstated Mr Kiraitu Murungi to the Cabinet after an eight-month suspension over corruption accusations.
Kiraitu was in charge of the search for a new constitution then.
As Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister then, he promised an earth-shaking campaign machine that would shake all corners of the nation in search of the 'Yes' vote.
The anniversary also comes just days after the fresh search for a new constitution collapsed largely as a result of disagreements over the same issues that led to the defeat of the Wako Draft.
Although the referendum was on the Wako Draft, issues like the reform credentials of those pushing it and their ages became critical issues.
Raila repeatedly said Kenyans had come a long way in fighting for democracy and would not allow "one or two bad elements to derail our dream."
Mr Uhuru Kenyatta asked old guards in the Government to step aside and give youth a chance to rule.
Yesterday, lawyer Kibe Mungai, who campaigned and voted for the Wako Draft, yesterday said nothing much has changed, especially for the better.
"We are more polarised than ever before. The possibility of this country experiencing ethnic-related violence is getting more real as we approach the elections," Kibe said.
"Some people are still saying they were short changed and hope to teach others a lesson at the polls. But elections in Africa never heal the differences. The old issue of the Kikuyu question will come up even more prominently next year. We are headed for hard times, but I pray for the best," he added.
November 21, 2005 was seen as President Kibaki's date with destiny, it was his first major test after he led a coalition of opposition parties to electoral victory three years earlier and promised a new constitution within 100 days.
An estimated 11 million people voted to decide whether to accept the proposed new constitution or not.
The President led the "Yes" campaign under the symbol of a banana against seven of his Cabinet ministers, who broke ranks with the Government and opposed the draft.
The rebel ministers who championed the "No" vote - symbolised by an orange - were led by then Roads minister Raila, the man credited with Kibaki's 2002 election victory after he endorsed his candidacy for the opposition coalition.
The major bone of contention between the "Banana" and "Orange" activists was the structure of Government.
The "Orange" side said the proposed constitution vested too much power in the President, creating an imperial presidency.
The rebel ministers favoured a constitution where a powerful prime minister shares executive powers with the President.
The "Banana" side, however, said that was tantamount to handing over the powers of a popularly elected President to an unelected prime minister.
The referendum was also seen as dress rehearsal for 2007 General Election.
A win for Kibaki's "Yes" side was expected to greatly enhance his reputation and give him the mandate to kick out rebel ministers from his Government.
At least nine people were killed in violence that erupted mainly during campaign rallies organised by the "No" team. Four of the deaths occurred in Kisumu.
Since the referendum, the political landscape has narrowed. The defeated Banana team has taken refuge in a new party, Narc-Kenya, which they say is President Kibaki's party.
The triumphant Orange team has formed the Orange Democratic Movement Kenya (ODM-Kenya) and is struggling to come up with a single candidate against Kibaki come next year.
A South African think-tank attributed Kibaki's defeat to a number of "betrayals" after sweeping to power in 2002 at the head of a reform-minded coalition.
That event was heralded as "an African velvet revolution."
"Forty years of cronyism, graft and autocratic rule ended with promises to deliver effective free public services, fight the cancer of corruption and limit presidential powers through a new constitution," the organisation reported.
"Yet the fairy-tale triumph of good over greed has stubbornly eluded Kenyans. Kibaki started well, delivering on promises of free education and healthcare and sacking much of the judiciary and all of the Government's procurement officers for corruption. In the euphoria of those early days taxi passengers - believing the era of corruption would end -leapt from vehicles and beat policemen seeking bribes. However, reformist zeal soon encountered a tenacious return of the ethnic cronyism of the old. When corruption began to taint his own Government, Kibaki did little in the face of vehement warnings from donors and the public. His own anti-corruption czar quit and went into hiding."
Yesterday, Mr Mutula Kilonzo, the Orange Team Leader during the referendum, said only the Opposition and the public had learnt a lesson from the referendum. The Government never did.
"Kibaki is behaving exactly the way he acted before. He is acting with contempt and disregard for the opinions of the people," Mutula said.
He cited the return of Kiraitu and Prof George Saitoti to the Cabinet as signs of contempt for public opinion.
"The AG has gone to court to appeal against the ruling that cleared Saitoti. In respect for the rule of law, the President should have waited for the case to be heard and determined. The evidence Githongo compiled against Kiraitu is so compelling that to reinstate Kiraitu smacks of nothing but arrogance," he said.
"We are witnessing a continuation of that arrogance. But Kenyans have learnt. They have learnt they have the power of the ballot. Kenyans have learnt they have the power to say no, and they are waiting," he added.
Kibe says politicians have learnt little about legitimacy issues, which he attributes to the collapse of the Wako Draft.
"There were questions on whether an unelected body like Bomas could make a constitution for the people. That is yet to be resolved. The only thing we agree on is that we need a referendum. Civil Society is more divided now. It is hard to tell who stands for what," he said.
President Kibaki reacted to the defeat by dismissing his entire Cabinet and appointed a new one a day later, which he said was "aimed at making the Cabinet more cohesive and better able to serve the people of Kenya." The new Cabinet began to crumble as soon as it was named.
Many of the ministers and assistant ministers the President appointed refused to take up the positions because they said he had not adequately consulted with dissenting lawmakers. The resulting political standoff left Kibaki struggling to govern.

Original_Search_Title: African Pride Rises At PAM Awards
Headline: Uganda: African Pride Rises At PAM Awards
Body_Text:
Kampala — PEOPLE in Kampala are known for jumping at any trend, going along with it as long as it lasts and then moving on to whatever else comes in. Now anything that has a tinge of African-ness to it is considered very trendy, and not only in the fashion sense. All these African things have been around for ages, but people seem to be more excited about them now than they were before.
African garb was considered fashionable for politicians - who had a lot to put forward about patriotism - or the married ladies. It was considered old-fashioned and unmarried people would only be forced into it if it was for traditional functions like introduction ceremonies or burials and last funeral rights.
This is not the case any more because more people are choosing African dressing and not only for traditional functions. Go to any corporate function and there is a big chance that the MC will be wearing an African outfit. Many of the people who were clad in African outfits at the recently concluded PAM Awards were considered to be impressively turned out - flowing in perfectly with the theme of the night.
It is no longer a surprise for a bride to change into an African outfit like umushanana or omwenda or to have her maids outfits made out of African material. The groom might pull it off in a nicely done tie-and-dye outfit teamed with linen pants exuding an air of sophistication and a true African spirit.
Although women are felt to be the trend chasers, the African fashion craze has also taken the men by storm. The guys have not only gone for the clothes but the jewellery as well. It was once off-putting (and still is for some people) for a man to wear silver or golden necklaces. Not so for the African necklaces or bracelets, which tend to supplement their masculinity.
The reasons people chose to dress in African fashion range from desiring to be unique to being respectable, patriotic and, above all, decent. Fashion critic Keturah Kamugasa believes that it is because of the increased awareness that Ugandans have about fashion trends. "They know what exactly they want and that is what they ask their designers to make for them," she says.
Justin, a seamstress from a boutique in town, attributes it to the rise of designers in Uganda. "People used to import outfits from abroad but we now have professional designers who are very creative and will make anything beautifully African from a fabric."
The African craze has also extended from the fashion scene to interior designing. It has become popular for homes or hang-out places to have wall hangings and tablemats made from banana fibre or barkcloth, African stools, African candleholders and small decorations like drums or African animals that give the place an African ambience.
Also on the increase is the use of African names, even more popular among the Christians.
Moses, a 35-year-old father of two, says that the African names are very beautiful and that it makes sense if people use names such as Mwesigwa, Kisakye, and Kwagala instead of English nmes. "It is not just about patriotism or repulsion of what is foreign but rather about giving children names that make a lot of sense to us as Africans."

Original_Search_Title: Nsam - Plans for a Befiting Market Afoot
Headline: Cameroon: Nsam - Plans for a Befiting Market Afoot
Body_Text:
The Yaounde III council is looking for patners for the construction of the new market.
The absence of a befiting market for the Yaounde III district has become a major worry for council authories of the locality. Over the years people living in the Nsam neighbourhood are forced to sell their goods in their verandas or just any place along the road sides. The Nsam main junction is fast becomining a market especially for the sale of fruits. Most of the fruits found in the area are Bananas, pineaples, pears, leamon,, water melons, limes, etc. Despite efforts by the council to prevent them from selling in the place the traders have continued selling.
Madame Jacquline is a fruit seller who has been selling at the Nsam junction for the past five years. According to her, sales are good in the area but the problem they face is with the council authorities who have been chasing them from the place and are asking them to go and sell in the new Nsam market which has not yet been constructed and is located in a valley. She said formerly they were asked to pay the sum of CFA 500 to the council but for some time now the council does not want them to pay any money but to leave. "We are not selling food stuff why should we go and sell so far off," she said. She expressed the wish that a market should be constructed for the Nsam district as is the case with the other districts such as the Melen, Biyem-Assi, Mvog-Mbi, etc so that traders can sell their products. Another fruit seller, Monique said that the traders only come and sell in the evenings because the council authorities do not allow them sell during the day. "We wait for the council to close work before coming to sell. They chase us away during working hours," she said.
According to the First Assistant Mayor of the Yaounde III District, Mme Mballa Bessala Rosine, plans are underway to construct a befiting market for the district. She regretted the fact that Yaounde III is the only District in the Mfoundi Division that has not got a market. However a peice of land of about four hectres has been aquired for the construction of a market. Also the Yaounde III council is looking for patners for the construction of the new market. "The absence of a market for the area is a major preocuppation for us. We are not chasing the women we are trying to organise our market. We want all of the traders to have a place in the new site. We are merely encouraging them,"she said.
According to Mme Mballa Bessala, the women have been asked to sell their products by the road sides along the road leading to the market while waiting for the market to be constructed.

Original_Search_Title: 'Mad Dog' Ribadu and History
Headline: Nigeria: 'Mad Dog' Ribadu and History
Body_Text:
The last time the phrase "mad dog" was used in describing public office holders was in the days of IBB when some Air force hellcats dealt with the late MKO Abiola.
When he complained, he was admonished to shun the guys who gave him hell on the grounds that their training entitles them to behave wildly like dogs with rabies. It's all history now. But Nuhu Ribadu was recently accorded the same title by the Atiku Campaign organization, which saw in the man the excellence of fiendish attributes. I don't know how the Campaign arrived at that and they are entitled to their considered opinion. Yet CP wishes to state that Ribadu will be remembered with mixed feelings when the annals of our country are finally written. For many he represents the type of Nigerian this nation wants: trustworthy, fearless, imaginative, principled, dogged and prepared for service. Personally I like the guy. I'm grateful for the hell is giving men (and women) who thought our country has become a banana republic, where the creed is corruption. I love the drama of corrupt commissioners fleeing their state capitals where they once strut around like gods. The fear EFCC instills in our omnipotent governors is refreshing, considering that they now talk and walk like the rest of us. There appears to be a modicum of decency in society. We now know that people can pay for their crimes, thanks to Ribadu. Ok, you argue that he is being selective and that he can't touch some people. Well, he has started and the little he did is praiseworthy, if you appreciate the level of filth he is dealing with. The other day, Uba Sani, the defender- in- chief of Obasanjo and the latest Fani Kayode told me that Ribadu has become "a force for decency and a strong factor in determining the political future of Nigeria." How? Well, Ribadu himself did swear to me that "no corrupt Nigerian will ever rule Nigeria again." Looking at his rigid face, I know he meant every word of that solemn submission.
This life, my brother. Can you imagine men who held sway just a few weeks ago, calling the shots suddenly turned fugitives on the run. What is this thing called power which mortals crave for, only for it to turn sour in the long run? Why can't one ride on this tiger without ending in its belly? Truth is that I don't sympathize with either Dariye or Fayose as both men deserved what befell them. When a governor thinks the state's coffer belongs to himself and his girlfriends, he should await the sad repercussions. What most Nigerians would love to see is many more of their clan cooling off behind bars. It would be the ultimate revenge for these eight years of looting, debauchery, bestiality and fetish preoccupation of some of our revered Excellencies.

Original_Search_Title: Illusions of Democracy
Headline: Nigeria: Illusions of Democracy
Body_Text:
NIGERIANS understandably jubilated at the collapse of the Constitution Amendment (Third Term) Bill at the national assembly in May, 2006. That development signalled a defeat of the powerful neo-feudal forces experimenting on reducing Nigeria to a blighted state and banana republic. To the extent then, that the abortion of this Bill upheld the will of most Nigerians on a constitutional limitation of two terms of executive office, a political but not necessarily, democratic victory was achieved. If this must be considered a gain for Nigeria's floundering democracy, it seems to have been obtained at a rather great price, thus, questioning the actual value of that gain.
We may compare this scenario with that of an anti-terrorist operation in which a group of commandos secure fewer lives of hostages than are killed. Such will hardly call for celebration. It may well be that in our own circumstance, we have not quite come to terms with the notion that we are still on a long journey to democracy.
Our point of departure, as some other commentators have hinted, is that the 1999 Constitution is such an obnoxious document in dire need of extensive surgery. The unitarist and anti-federalist character of the Constitution makes it one of the greatest impediments to the emergence of a democratic culture. The product of a tiny fraction of Nigerians with dictatorial mindset, the 1999 Constitution saddles us with an all-powerful Presidency at the expense of the mass citizenry, elected representatives and democratic institutions. No wonder that even the conservative, lbrahim Mantu-led committee proposed over one hundred amendments to the Constitution.
That we had to give up this opportunity of ameliorating the harsh provisions of the Constitution tells how desperate our situation is. In other words, that this wholesale termination of Constitution review became the only guaranteed way of aborting tenure extension, suggests the nature of this democracy-fragile, vulnerable and shallow.
The disturbing implication is that an option of exercising the third term clause out of the amendment bill was fraught with the risk of playing into the hand of the adversaries. If this is to be assumed correctly, then, it is a sobering statement of a democracy victory that has not particularly deepened democracy.
We can extend the scope of this discussion with an evaluation of the effectiveness of the legislature in this democratic dispensation. We would find that whether at the federal or states level, the legislature is still subservient to the executive. In theory, the legislature is a powerful institution.
Yet, notwithstanding the enormous legislative and oversight powers invested in the third arm of government by various sections of the 1999 Constitution, the reality is that the legislature lives under the shadow of the executive arm of government.
Where we encounter instances of parliamentary independent-mindedness, they are exceptions to the rule of submission. Consider, for instance, that in spite of the overflowing dossiers the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission has accumulated on some state chief executives, not one governor has been impeached by his state house of assembly for corruption or other offences, excluding of course, Bayelsa, Oyo, Ekiti and Plateau states, cases teleguided by the Federal Government.
Correspondingly, consider the fervour with which state houses of assembly conducted the impeachment of deputy governors who had fallen out with their governors. In many cases, the exercises were concluded within one week of the three months allowed by the Constitution! The proceedings were so ridiculous that you kept wondering if the legislators were political illiterates or had no sense of self-worth.
And while we are all witnesses to how desperate regimes have invoked the atavistic Public Order Act to circumscribe our constitutional rights of movement and association, it is a measure of our journey since 1999 that the National Assembly is just now working to repeal the law.
Since the historic Supreme Court ruling in 2001 to the effect that the INEC cannot prescribe new requirements other than those contained in the Constitution for associations seeking to be recognized as political parties, the number of political parties has been steadily rising. In the reckoning of Professor Maurice lwu's INEC and some others, plurality of political parties is an index of democratic freedom. A Sunday Vanguard news analysis quotes lwu as having told journalists in Calabar recently: "We need to register more parties ... to open the political space".
On the surface, this would appear to be a logical statement but it is not a self-evident claim. It is a highly conditional assertion which flies in the face of actual political experience.
Where incumbent regimes decide to manipulate the election process as happened in 1964, 1983 and 2003, it makes no difference whether there are six or one thousand political parties. With the ruling party clinging on to power at each flawed election, the political space is constricted into an unwritten one-party system.
Reports about the INEC's assurances on a successful 2007 election would have been cheering if they did not indicate a misplaced focus on less problematic issues. According to Sunday Independent, May 28, 2006, the INEC chairman while announcing the timetable for the 2007 election on Wednesday, May 24, 2006, said that 'automatic devises will be adopted to ensure the elections are not fraudulent.' Such electoral malpractices as multiple registration/voting and snatching of ballot boxes, we are told, will be foreclosed in a new arrangement being put in place.
Our concern however, is that these and other known measures proposed by the INEC for a free and fair polls do not address the new heights to which election rigging has been taken. Can the INEC assure us that the kind of voodoo officiating that produced two sets of election results in Anambra state in 2003 will not be experienced in 2007?
Mr. Afuba , a public affirs commentator, writes from Nimo, Anambra State.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers to Reap From Banana Fibre
Headline: Uganda: Farmers to Reap From Banana Fibre
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Project for Paper from Banana Fibre Production was on Monday commissioned at Makerere University's Faculty of Technology.
The sh167m project is funded by Japan.
Under the project, banana fibres will be processed into paper.
The technology state minister, Ephraim Kamuntu, said if implemented, the project would curb environmental degradation through the use of non-biodegradable plastics.
Ryuzo Kikuchi, the Japanese ambassador, said the project would boost economic growth at the grassroots.
Prof. Morishima Hiroshi invented the project.

Original_Search_Title: Former Mhangura Striker Benjamin Zulu Dies
Headline: Zimbabwe: Former Mhangura Striker Benjamin Zulu Dies
Body_Text:
Bindura — FORMER Mhangura and State House Tornadoes striker, Benjamin Zulu has died.
He was 38.
Zulu, who until recently had also been an assistant coach at Premiership outfit Mwana Africa, died after a long illness yesterday at his home in Bindura.
Zulu's football career started in the late 1980s when he was playing for the now defunct Mhangura where he rubbed shoulders with the likes of Shabanie Mine coach John Phiri, Abraham Mkanga and Jani Milanzi.
He then moved to Harare after being lured by former Zimbabwe President, the late Canaan Banana to Tornadoes. It was during the same period that he also attended Churchill High School.
After retiring from active football in 1997, the former Zimbabwe youth international joined Ashanti Mine in Bindura as a player-coach in Division Two and helped the goldminers gain promotion into Division One.
In 2002 Zulu, who was also the secretary of the Zimbabwe Soccer Coaches' Association, Mashonaland Central chapter, was appointed assistant coach to Arthur Tutani with whom he also worked to lead Mwana Africa into the Premiership at the end of the 2005 season.
Mwana Africa assistant coach Tutani yesterday paid tribute to Zulu and described his death as a huge loss to the local game.
"Benjamin Zulu's death is a big loss for the province and the nation at large as this man was playing a pivotal role towards the development of football in this province. His life has been taken away at the time I thought we were going to work together towards the re-strengthening of Mwana Africa Football Club.
"To him I say rest in peace," said Tutani.
Zulu is survived by a wife and four children and mourners are gathered at House Number 721 Mount Darwin Flats in Bindura.
Meanwhile, Mashonaland Central was also robbed of administrator Phillip Nyamwela who died in Bindura last week and was buried at Chipadze Cemetery.

Original_Search_Title: Now Ruto Tells of Ouster Plot in ODM Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Now Ruto Tells of Ouster Plot in ODM Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kanu secretary-general William Ruto yesterday accused his detractors in Kanu of plotting to have him dropped from the ODM Kenya leadership.
And he declared that he was ready for any ODM-K post for the sake of the new party's unity as some Kanu members urged chairman Uhuru Kenyatta to reconcile the various factions and have dialogue ahead of a planned national delegates meeting.
"I am ready to be an ordinary member of ODM-K, a party that cares about the development of Kenyans instead an official in Kanu," the Eldoret North MP said at the Mulot Africa Gospel Church in Kajiado South constituency.
He spoke as reports showed that some Kanu leaders were planning to introduce the position of party leader to monitor the activities of MPs and other leaders.
Mr Ruto urged Kenyans to get prepared for what he termed unfinished business between the Orange and the Banana teams, "who have taken the new shapes of the ODM-K and Narc Kenya."
"Parties like Kanu, LDP and Ford People should not bother you, but the match will be between the unity that we are yearning for and Narc Kenya," he said.
Members of Kanu out to fight it in court do not mean well for the party, he said, arguing that by joining hands ODM-K had become a formidable force that would offer better leadership.
"ODM is the reinvention of the original Kanu, and I don't see why some of our friends are grumbling," Mr Ruto said.
In Nairobi, Kanu officials from North-Eastern province said it was important that the oldest party in the country show unity of purpose.
Speaking at Chester House, they expressed fears that should the differences in Kanu continue, it was likely that each faction would hold its own national delegates conference.
The leaders accused Mr Kenyatta of showing disrespect to former party leaders, and demanded that he apologise to Mr Moi. They said that the former party chairman deserved respect "and participation in the activities of the party, just like (former South African President Nelson) Mandela of ANC."
In a statement read by Mandera West party chairman Hassan Ibrahim, the more than 20 leaders said that whatever the leaders said or did, the motto had to be maintained.
They described Mr Kenyatta and Mr Ruto as Mr Moi's students who should not criticise him publicly.
They were reacting to Mr Kenyatta's call to Mr Moi to keep off the party and allow the current leaders to do their job.
And Cabinet minister John Koech and assistant ministers John Serut and Isaack Shaaban as well as MPs Anthony Kimetto and Nick Salat told the Gatundu South MP and his team not to insult their "mentor."
Elsewhere, Mr Moi was accused of financing top Kanu politicians to break up ODM-K.
Interim ODM-K national chairman Henry Kosgey asked him to stop financing the Kanu leaders.
But Keiyo South MP Nicholas Biwott asked Kalenjin MPs to show respect for the former head of state.
Speaking to the Nation by phone, Mr Biwott said it was a curse among the Kalenjin for one to speak ill of the elderly.

Original_Search_Title: KQ Cancels Flights to Kisumu
Headline: Kenya: KQ Cancels Flights to Kisumu
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The national carrier, Kenya Airways, has suspended flights to Kisumu beginning today citing the poor state of the runway.
"Due to concerns about passenger safety, we will resume our operations into the Kisumu airport when repairs to the runway have been done," chief executive Titus Naikuni said yesterday.
Business at the Kisumu airport was normal yesterday, however. The Kenya Airways plane landed at 6:15 pm and the East African Airlines one landed at 6:25 pm and both picked Nairobi-bound passengers.
Efforts to reach the airport manager, Mr Joseph Yego, were fruitless.
Kenya Airports Authority spokesman Dominic Ngigi declined to comment on the issue saying he was on safari out of the country.
The Government has in the past promised that it had set aside some Sh2.1 billion for the expansion of Kisumu airport.
A complete design for the expanded new look airport was expected to be finalised two months ago courtesy of the Netherlands Airports Consultant Company (NACO), Mr Yego said in July.
"Once the design is ready, a suitable company would be contracted to start the expansion work which is expected to cost Sh2.1 billion," he said.
He said because the runway is short, large aircraft can neither land nor take off from the airport. The runway is 2.1 kms long while big aircraft require a minimum of 3kms, Mr Yego said.
He said the airport was strategic in the region as Kisumu could in future host the East African Legislative Assembly if government plans to upgrade and expand the facility were completed soon.
At the same time, Mr Naikuni said Kenya Airways was taking measures to address the delays being experienced on the Nairobi-Mombasa route.
He said: "We recognise that there has been some challenges on the Mombasa schedules. We have purchased three new Boeing 737-899s which will be used on the regional routes and will allow us to replace the ageing 737-800s currently deployed on the Mombasa route."
The airline took delivery of the first of the new aircraft early this week while the other two are scheduled to arrive towards the end of this month and next month.
The expansion of the Kisumu airport is expected to boost horticulture, fish processing and banana business besides tourism in Nyanza, Western and parts of Rift Valley provinces.
"Kenyan fish processors will be able to airlift several tonnes of fish destined for Europe rather than use the traditional means of transport -road and rail," Mr Yego said.
It is also expected to enhance speedy transportation of other perishable commodities like flowers.

Original_Search_Title: Train Ride to a Glorious Era
Headline: Kenya: Train Ride to a Glorious Era
Body_Text:
Nairobi — There has been a gradual but systematic decay of services at Kenya Railways Corporation for the past 15 years. For those who have watched the services deteriorate, it will be good riddance to the government.
For others, it will be wistful nostalgia as they relive the trips they took in passenger trains to Mombasa or Kisumu.
Starting yesterday, a new consortium took over operations of the Kenya-Uganda railway after winning a 25-year concession. The new group is headed by a South Africa company, Sheltam Rail, and will operate as Rift Valley Railways. It will invest at least US$500 million (about Sh36 billion) to modernise infrastructure and equipment.
Many Kenya Railways (KR) workers will be taken up by the new company, while KR itself will have a lean workforce of about 40, mainly to supervise the concession contract.
Before grinding to a halt, KR could manage only three trips to Mombasa and Kisumu every week - from two daily trains to Mombasa and Kisumu up to the early 1990s.
At independence in 1963, KR was one of the most affluent and prestigious organisations in the country. Many school leavers wanted to be employed there as clerks, locomotive drivers or technicians. Even in the late '70s and early '80s, KR was considered a prime employer for fresh university graduates. It had perks such as housing, medical and insurance that rivalled those offered by major private sector employers.
All this changed when the services started deteriorating. From a corporation that owned huge swathes of land all over the country, KR is now a mere shell. This despite being a monopoly since it started operations 100 years ago.
In Nairobi, half of what is now Upper Hill, and parts of Parklands, Ngara, South B, Industrial Area and Jogoo Road, belonged to KR. In Mombasa, the corporation owned half of the industrial area and, in Kisumu, all the land around Lake Victoria. The same could be said of parts of Nakuru, Eldoret and many other smaller towns which dot the railway line.
Besides land, KR enjoyed some of the best housing facilities in the country, with its top managers accommodated at Upper Hill, Kileleshwa, Ojijo Road in Parklands, Ngara and South B, among other places.
The corporation also had expansive housing estates for the lower cadres, the most famous being Muthurwa, Makongeni and Kaloleni. These estates were for manual labourers. Each of the one-roomed houses had a radio monitor that was controlled from the headquarters and a siren that sounded at 6am everyday to wake up the workers.
In the early years of Kenya's independence, students at renowned schools like Maseno, Alliance and St Mary's Yala relied on the railway network to report to school. Admission to any of these institutions included instructions on the day to arrive at railway stations either in Kikuyu, Maseno or Yala. Students would then be picked up by their respective school buses. Parents only needed to purchase return tickets for their children - and transport hassles would be taken care of.
Those were the days when KR was very organised. It even laid on special coaches and offered discounted rates for students at the beginning and end of each school term. But services started deteriorating in the 1980s. Slowly, schools stopped relying on KR for transport of their students. Not only were the trains unreliable, but they would be packed with passengers by the time they reached the various schools. Many students would be stranded.
For those who grew up in Eastlands, Nairobi, a trip by train to western Kenya or the Coast was a great experience. Stratified into three classes - first, second and third - it could also be very harrowing for the poor.
The categories were a throwback to the colonial era where Whites were first, Asians second and Africans third class citizens. This system still persists at KR, with those travelling first class paying Sh3,160, second class Sh2,275 and third class Sh400 to Mombasa. To Kisumu, the rates are Sh2,190, Sh1495 and Sh300 for the three classes, respectively.
Traditional English food
On board the train were two categories of restaurants. Those in first and second classes enjoyed a sitting arrangement where traditional English fare was served, complete with silver cutlery and white starched napkins. The waiters were in snow-white uniform. In the third class coaches, however, one ordered snacks while standing and was quickly ushered back to their seats.
As KR services deteriorated, those wishing to travel in third class would buy tickets as early as 7am for the 5.30pm or 7pm trip. This was followed by a long wait for the train to be brought to the platform. The station opened at 3pm for the 5.30pm trip. This led to a free-for-all scenario. The nimble and agile took off at great speed to occupy the best seats on the train. Woe unto those who had luggage and children. They ended up on the corridors or in the toilets, depending on the number of people on board.
The 13-hour journey from Nairobi to western Kenya started at 5.30pm. When the train reached Uplands station, hawkers of pork meat shouted themselves hoarse to get customers. And at Nakuru, Molo and Njoro stations, traders in petty goods (sweets, cakes, soda and boiled maize) rushed in and did roaring business. It was well past midnight! When the train reached Koru and Kibos, it was time to buy peanuts and ripe bananas.
Food hawkers did roaring business the entire trip. But these stops were agonising for many people as new passengers would board - though they could not find seats, let alone space to stand. The most notorious month for this was December.
As the train became unreliable, many travellers resorted to using buses. Nakuru, which was one of the cleanest railway stations in Kenya, is now falling apart. Mombasa, which did brisk business transporting tourists to the coast, is now forlorn and dilapidated. Tourists now fly directly to Mombasa, or drive all the way there. Most of KR's godowns are no longer in use, as even those who used to transport goods through the railway line have resorted to other means.
But it is not just train services that went down. Houses and land belonging to KR were also targeted in the twilight years of the Kanu regime. Prime land and houses on Ojijo Road in Parklands were sold for a song and later re-sold to State corporations at astronomical sums.
Similar fate befell houses of KR's top management - at Upper Hill area, Kileleshwa, Ngara and South B. These areas are now teeming with private apartments and office blocks. But grabbing of land belonging to Kenya Railways started long before President Moi took over in 1978. In Nairobi's South B, estates like Golden Gate and Plainsview sit on land that was hived off the Railway Training Institute. Estates like Muthurwa and Makongeni are drab and run-down, thanks to neglect of roads and lights that the corporation used to offer. They are no-go zones from dusk to dawn.
The corporation also had some of the best equipped workshops in Nairobi, Mombasa, Kisumu and Nakuru. At Nairobi, next to Landmawe estate, all the workshops have been sold out. In their place are bars and stalls dealing in clothing and wood.
The Nairobi Railway Station godowns have also been disposed off. Among the companies that have offices there is Easy Coach. Other godowns have been subdivided and rented out as cyber cafes, kiosks and offices. Attempts to run daylight train services between Nairobi and Mombasa, and Nairobi and Naivasha, have failed frequently.
Now KR employees are waiting with bated breath as the new company takes over.

Original_Search_Title: Thousands Welcome Besigye in Rukungiri
Headline: Uganda: Thousands Welcome Besigye in Rukungiri
Body_Text:
Rukungiri — Thousands of people jammed Rukungiri streets on Thursday as they welcomed FDC party president Kiiza Besigye. Despite the heavy down pour people filled the streets singing FDC praise songs.
Trading centres of Kebisoni, Buyanja and Nyamayenje 16 kilometres to Rukungiri town along the Ntungamo-Rukungiri Road were decorated with green banana trees, balloons and tree branches as school children stood by the road to wave to Besigye.
He later addressed a rally at Rukungiri stadium.
He asked FDC supporters not to lose morale but fight on until the NRM is defeated.
"Don't abandon the struggle just because we have been defeated twice. Carry on victory is ours," Besigye said.
"The NRM I knew used to work for the people but not this one which fights people.
Corruption within the NRM party has made so many local people poor. Join me in the struggle to fight this bad leadership."
Besigye said FDC had gained a lot of local and national support because of attempts by the NRM to prosecute him and his supporters.
He called on the Rukungiri district NRM party chairman, Maj. Gen. Jim Muhwezi, to abandon the "drowning party" (NRM) and join FDC in its struggle to fight corrupt officials.

Original_Search_Title: Why Kibaki Survives
Headline: Kenya: Why Kibaki Survives
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki appears not to have a political party or to be ready to be wooed into one, but, say analysts, that could all be part of his political strategy.
Indeed, having wrong-footed his opponents, a seemingly witty President Kibaki is headed for the homestretch and on course to complete his first five-year term.
That is the verdict of five university professors The Standard interviewed, prompted by the ease with which President Kibaki appears to confound both ally and foe on a fast-changing political landscape.
