Professors Philip Nyinguro, Richard Akong'o Oyugi, Macharia Munene, Peter Wanyande and Dr Adams Oloo unveil a subtle and wily political operative who has not only built a formidable client base but also taken advantage of State resources to survive.
To ensure total loyalty to himself, say analysts, President Kibaki acquired the allegiance of MPs, including his most virulent critics, by allowing them to set their own salaries and control the Constituency Development Fund (CDF).
They also suggest that he has taken advantage of divisions in his Cabinet, especially those between ministers associated with Narc-Kenya and those allied to Narc and Ford-Kenya. This way he has ensured there is no united front against him.
It has also emerged that the President has survived because after the embarrassing debacle that was the referendum on the Draft Constitution last November, he personally took control of State affairs.
With a weak Constitution that cannot allow for his impeachment or even replacement and backed by a Parliament over which he enjoys near absolute majority, the President has comfortably hit the home stretch, with analysts declaring him a serious contender for 2007.
Ironically, his hands-off style has worked to his advantage as it has wrong-footed those who hoped to patronise him.
Analysts say the President has survived the various political storms through patronage of Members of Parliament, both in government and the opposition, who no longer have the moral authority to openly censure him.
"He has survived by deliberately cultivating a patron-client net-work oiled by state resources. He has 'bought' the loyalty of MPs by, for example, allowing them to set their own salaries and control the Constituency Development Fund (CDF).
"These people cannot pass a vote of no confidence against him. All MPs are benefiting, including those in ODM," says Nyinguro, the head of the Department of political science at the University of Nairobi.
Oloo says the MPs, currently enjoying huge perks in salaries and allowances, cannot be expected to vote against the President because of "selfish interests" and the fact that some may not be sure they will get back their parliamentary jobs.
The President had to take full control of State matters after the referendum when his lieutenants in the Banana camp made serious blunders that led to the Government's lose to the Orange Democratic Movement to revitalise his leadership.
"Since then, the President has been more visible. He has shown his activities better. He has achieved a lot and people resonate with that", says Munene.
Having accepted defeat the President moved to reorganise his government, sacking his entire Cabinet and forming a new one of loyalists, including those from the opposition, while consolidating his majority in Parliament.
And as he gets close to a second term, analysts say he is cleverly crafting a re-election agenda centred on himself as an individual while keeping his old Democratic Party and the open courtship Narc-Kenya guessing.
His hands-off style and unpredictability have worked in his favour. He has refused to be patronised by loyalists and disappointed politicians who wanted to shield him.
President Kibaki has lost key allies to death in Karisa Maitha, Geoffrey Parpai, Mohammed Abdi, Mirugi Kariuki and Liberal Democratic Party strongmen among them Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, and Najib Balala, who helped him ascend to power.
Former Cabinet Minister Oloo Aringo, speaking by telephone from London yesterday said: "Kibaki's style has been described by others as hands-off, but he has given ministers freedom to be independent and to serve their dockets constitutionally this is great progress and (President) Kibaki must be commended for it."
Said Aringo: "(Former President) Moi was interventionist who curtailed our powers when we served as ministers. He always wanted to control ministers, in the Kibaki regime, there is great freedom."
Aringo, however, points an accusing finger at some Cabinet ministers who have used their offices to amass wealth and foster their personal agenda.
"These are the people letting down the Kibaki administration, they have failed to measure up to their tasks and are still living in relics of patronage politics," says Aringo.
However, President Kibaki thinks big about macro-managing matters of the State rather than micro-managing small issues, says Munene.
"His strategy is macro-delivering many good things instead of thinking about small things", he says.
The President has thrived on the divisions in the Cabinet, especially those between ministers associated with Narc-K on the one side, those in Local Government minister Musikari Kombo's Ford-Kenya and those allied to Narc chairman and Health Minister Charity Ngilu.
"There is no united front against the President. Even those against him are also against themselves", says Wanyande, adding that the frequently emerging issues were changing the political dynamic each passing day.
To survive, he has managed to control and eliminate the previous contradictions in Government, which started immediately after he took office and were orchestrated by the opposition from within.
"Opposition to his administration started when he took office. There was serious internal opposition from the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) than from Kanu," says Munene.
But analysts also think that President Kibaki's silence over his second term is a hollow approach.
"Everybody knows he will run for a second term. He should be able to openly tell Kenyans he will defend his seat. Kenyans should start thinking about it.
"It is not a crime to declare that he is running for a second term. This is another weakness", says Wanyande.
Nyinguro says recent activities by the President reveal that he will contest the presidency for a second term, but keeping quiet about it is "not a good way to assess the intelligence of Kenyans. It is a hollow strategy".
Munene thinks the President's approach to remain silent over his second term in office is politically strategic.
"Politically, it is very wise to keep quiet. It is in line with Kibaki who is a very patient man. Patience pays. It gives the other side (read opposition) time to make a fool of itself and make blunders".
Oloo agrees with Munene and describes President Kibaki's silence as a "weapon" to avoid being pushed by ODM-K hopefuls eyeing the presidency.
"Silence is a weapon. He is waiting for the right time. The moment he is announces he is running, he will get another push from other presidential hopefuls. There is no hurry for him," says Oloo
On Kibaki's recent improved and high rating by pollsters, all the analysts were cautious about their credibility given that Kenya is a heterogeneous society and in every province there are people likely to be sympathetic to the government and push their own agenda.
They faulted the methodology used by the pollsters as doubtful, especially on matters of sample sizes and margins of error. The pollsters should also be courageous to tell Kenyans how the samples were picked.
"President Kibaki will be ill advised to rely on these opinion polls", said Wanyande, while Oloo had reservations about the opinion polls. "Many issues in these polls do not make sense," said Oloo.
But Munene says however flawed the opinion polls are, they are indicative of something and should not be ignored outright.
All agreed that it was not legally right for the President to openly associate and campaign for the nascent Narc-Kenya while the party that took him to office, that is Narc, is still in existence-at least by name.
But they argued that there is a unique political culture in Kenya where politicians do not respect party ideals and rules. Kibaki is no exception.
Wanyande especially took issue with President Kibaki's open by-election campaigns in Nakuru last July where he publicly threw his weight behind Narc-Kenya candidate William Kariuki Mirugi.
"A serving president should not openly support a party that is not in power," says Wanyande
Nyinguro said it was wrong for President Kibaki to associate with Narc-Kenya but added that it was not surprising because many Kenyan leaders, including ODM- presidential hopeful Raila, and Ford-People leader Simeon Nyachae do not respect political parties and their ideals.
The analysts say the President's political survival as in next year's election hinges on ODM-Kenya remaining united and the fact that Kenyans have despaired on vision-less opposition politics.
"The ODM is the main challenge to the president but they do not seem to have a different vision from Kibaki," said Nyinguro.
Oloo says it would not be easy to beat Kibaki to the presidency if the ODM-K splits.
"If ODM-K sticks together, they will remove President Kibaki. If they do not stick together, it will not be any easy task," says Oloo.
He adds that the emerging two-horse race between Narc-K and the ODM-K was a healthy development in an ethnicised society such as Kenya.
"This forces a healing and accommodation process because Kenyans can coalesce around the two parties rather than group in ethnic entities," said Oloo.
Nyinguro however said Kenya was not unique because re-cycling old politicians with huge financial resources obtains in the entire African continent every election year.
But chances for the ODM may be complicated if Ford-Kenya and the National Party of Kenya under Ngilu finally play ball with Narc-Kenya.
Oloo says there are high chances that Ford-Kenya will work with Narc-Kenya.
Oloo says despite Kibaki's visible hard work since the referendum, this had not however made him popular.
"President Kibaki has worked very hard since the referendum. He has enhanced public relations. He looks confident. On face value things look okay. But this has not made him popular", says Oloo.
The only advantage the president and government have is that they can tell the people what they are doing without giving the people a chance to pass a verdict as they did at the referendum.
"Only the government has the opportunity to win MPs and tell the people what it has done. People have no opportunity to pass a verdict on the government as they did at the referendum", says Oloo.

Original_Search_Title: Magomago Over Approval of State of Emergency
Headline: Nigeria: Magomago Over Approval of State of Emergency
Body_Text:
IT was supposed to be a simple case of voting against or supporting the presidential imposition of a state of emergency in Ekiti State, which had gone bananas in the last couple of weeks. And what the members' needed, if they would want the state of emergency as declared by Mr. President to survive, was two-thirds. But the magomago which attended the deliberations rendered the case tough. Ekiti State wouldn't have generated much furore, but members mainly looked at the situation there from the binoculars of reincarnation of a third term, the issue became larger and sensitive.
Most members, especially those who fought hard to kill and bury the third term, now thought that if the state of emergency in the state was allowed to survive, then it was like granting a tacit approval to His Excellency to carry go. The wisdom is that once Ekiti sails through, the others on line like Anambra, Plateau and others would provide the ingredients for the president to muster enough point to say because the country is not safe for election, the 2007 poll should be postponed.
These were their fears. And they worked hard and mobilised, thinking it was going to be a fair thing. After series of meeting to make them support the state of emergency, the leadership decided to exploit its power. When Masari came out to announce the result of the session, wahala started. While Speaker Aminu Bello Masari, for instance, announced that 295 members were present at the executive session where the decision was taken, the opposition claimed only 251 members attended, with about 40 members against the declaration.
Said he: "we discussed the issue of state of emergency in Ekiti State, and it took us almost four hours to debate the issue. We examined the pros and cons of the declaration, the need for it and the fears for it. The need for it is that at present nobody can claim authority in Ekiti State, Fayose, who is in hiding is claiming to be the governor, the deputy and speaker of the House also claimed to be in charge. So whichever decision we take, we have to take the interest of the people of Ekiti State in consideration. Now if we say the status quo should be returned to, the people will not accept, so we are constrained. When we look at the state of emergency something should follow. Where there is a case of omission or commission, they should be addressed.
"Whatever decision we take on the matter should not violate the litigations before the court of law. Based on the figures available to me, 295 members attended the session..." Masari stated. But members saw a clear breach of transparency and they shouted the man down. Said Farouk Adamu Aliyu "It's not true, that's a lie". But "we have won, we have won", chorused the others in support of the declaration. Now there is something spectacular here. The members who spoke to the press about the issue were not in unison. Take for instance, the group in support. Though with a little rehearsal they had learnt to stick to the speaker's 295 attendance list, they clearly went their ways at the point of how many members actually voted for or against.
He said out of the 295 members that attended the meeting, 270 supported the imposition of the state of emergency in Ekiti, while 25 were against it. This means that we have secured the constitutional two-third required for the approval, which is 240", he said. And came the members representing Ekiti State, who also said out of the 295 who were present at the session, 268 voted in support, while only 25 were against it.
Indeed, until someone I guessed was one of the aids of the woman scribbled something on the page for her, Ette was saying that out of the 295 members present, 261 voted for, while 34 voted against it.
The opposition group? Said Yerima, "there were 259 members, and 30 of us voted against the declaration of state of emergency" Honourable Nadabo said what was true was that about 251 members were present and the opposition secured about 35 votes. Which meant that the required two third did not scale through.
For Bugaje, he wouldn't talk about the number present, but what was important to him was that "my friend there was simply no voting. No voting at all. It is unfortunate that we have set the clock of democracy far backwards.
What you require to legitimise the state of emergency is the National Assembly. "Importantly there was no voting, because you need that to determine those who support or disapprove of the state of emergency. And when we met at the plenary there was no voting also, the speaker just went on to announce a spurious figure.
"Let me tell you that those of us who were against the imposition of state of emergency in Ekiti State were about 40. We took our list there, so even if we were up to 259, they didn't make up the two third, because when you subtract 259 from 40, what you get is 219, which is less than 240 required for the approval" he said.
So who is saying the truth? For me, I didn't scientifically take the number of members present, but I was quite sure that the members gathered there that day were not more than 260 and I could count as many as 40 members that were against the declaration.
I think it is important that these men learn to be transparent or be seen to be transparent, in whatever they do no matter their political interests. Otherwise, it would turn out to be a disaster tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: Politicians to Be Hit By Ban On Choppers
Headline: Kenya: Politicians to Be Hit By Ban On Choppers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Early election campaign activities by several top politicians could be paralysed by looming helicopter hire freeze aimed at State corporations, The Standard has learnt.
And it has also emerged that Members of Parliament, a number of them high profile personalities who hire State corporation helicopters, have been flying them at their own risk.
An aviation watchdog, the Kenya Civil Aviation Authority (KCAA), has warned it would cancel aircraft operation licenses of State corporations that lease their aircraft for commercial purposes.
KCAA Director General Mr Chris Kuto warned:" The licensing board (read KCAA) will write to these State corporations to stick to their licences, which are for private purposes not commercial ones. If they do not comply we will revoke them (licenses)."
The decree, which appears to target politicians both from Government and the Opposition, comes in the wake of a damning report by the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights (KNCHR) citing abuse of the services.
In the eye of the storm is the Kenya Pipeline Corporation (KPC), whose helicopters are hired out regularly for private use. It costs Sh80,000 per hour to hire a helicopter, according to rates set by KCAA industry insiders say.
The issue of safety and insurance cover is of particular concern, considering that apart from the numerous reported aircraft mishaps involving top Kenyan political personalities, the Kibaki administration has been hit by at least two fatal accidents.
The April, 10 Marsabit plane crash claimed the lives of five MPs among them Dr Guracha Galgalo (Moyale), Dr Bonaya Godana (North Horr), Titus Ngoyoni (Laisamis), Abdi Sasura (Saku) and Mirugi Kariuki of Nakuru Town. The MPs, among other leaders on a peace mission, were in a Y-12 military aircraft when the tragedy occurred.
On January 24, 2003, a plane carrying top Government officials crashed in Busia killing Labour minister Ahmed Khalif, the two pilots and injuring other ministers. A total of six people were reported to have died in the crash.
Four Cabinet ministers were in the ill-fated plane, which was carrying 10 passengers and two pilots. Justice Minister Martha Karua, Foreign Affairs Minister Raphael Tuju and former minister Linah Chebii Kilimo, Assistant minister Robinson Githae and MP George Khaniri were injured.
Another victim of the crash the director of Abantu for Development Wanjiru Kihoro, died this month, four years after the tragedy. Kihoro had been on a life saving machine for three years and nine months. The dignitaries had just returned from Vice President Moody Awori's home in Funyula constituency when they boarded the plane from Nairobi.
Other than engaging in an illegal activity, KCAA is concerned that the helicopter hire services were also putting lives of key personalities at risk.
Said Kuto, "It is risky to hire such aircraft because their licence does not indicate that they are chartered airlines. That would make it impossible for insurance to compensate victims should there be accidents.
He added: "Let Kenya Pipeline Corporation know that the services they are offering are illegal and if they continue, we will ground their operations".
Speaking on Monday, on the occasion of an open hearing for new airline licence applications at the East African School of Aviation in Embakasi, Nairobi, Kuto said KCAA would cancel the licences of State corporations found to be hiring out their aircraft for commercial purposes.
The Executive Director, Kenya Association of Air Operators, Col (Rtd) Eutychus Waithaka kicked up a huge storm, when he complained that his members who are licensed to operate charter airlines, were losing business to State corporations now busy leasing their aircraft for private use.
"We want to know whether these corporations are allowed to use their aircraft for private purposes," said Waithaka.
In response, Kuto said: "It is illegal for State corporations to allow their aircraft to be used for hire. Let me warn them to stick to their license. We did not license them to operate as charter airlines, they were licensed to carry staff only."
Kuto insisted if the parastatals wanted to fly commercially, they would have to apply for charter licences.
He gave the example of the Kenya Wildlife Service (KWS, which was earlier operating as a charter contrary to the provisions of its licence and was forced to stop the flights.
KWS, Kuto said, has since applied for a license to operate a charter airline and the application is being scrutinised by KCAA.
The warning comes just over a year to the 2007 General Election when politicians would be going for helicopters and other aircraft from private and public companies.
Early this month, KNCHR released a list of ministers owing the Kenya Pipeline Corporation Sh3.6 million for hiring their helicopters.
The ministers hired KPC helicopters during campaigns for the referendum on the draft Constitution last year, but have not yet paid the corporation.
During the campaigns last November, both camps - Orange and Banana - chartered choppers to ensure they combed the entire country. The Orange group wanted the draft Constitution rejected; it won at the ballot on November 21.
KNCHR chairman, Mr Maina Kiai, has recommended that the ministers pay the money or be sued for misuse of public resources. Kiai said the amounts were outstanding as at March when his team collected data on the company.
The report said the ministers hired the aircraft for private use on various dates, but had not paid for the services by March 8.
Again this year, KNCHR censured some Government officials for the alleged misuse of State resources, including military aircraft during the July by-election campaigns in Nakuru, Moyale, Saku, Laisamis and North Horr constituencies.
Among those listed in the commission's report for alleged misuse of military helicopters were Cabinet ministers John Michuki, Karua, Kivutha Kibwana, Mohamed Kuti, Mohamed Abdi, Gideon Konchela and Mutua Katuku.
Several assistant ministers and MPs allied to Narc-Kenya were also named for use of aircraft belonging to government agencies.

Original_Search_Title: Queries Linger As Priest Takes Up Top Posta Post
Headline: Kenya: Queries Linger As Priest Takes Up Top Posta Post
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Queries are being raised on the appointment of Catholic priest Dominic Wamugunda as director of a State corporation. The church, by its own laws, does not interfere in Government matters lest it loses its independence. Fr Wamugunda was appointed to the board of directors of Postal Corporation of Kenya for three years.
A cross-section of theologians and lay people have criticised the appointment, saying the clergy should not accept political or civil appointments.
The Pope's representative to Kenya, Archbishop Alain Paul Lebeaupin, however, said there were exceptions to the rule - but only after consultations with respective bishops.
Fr Wamugunda, who is working in the archdiocese of Nairobi, accepted the appointment and has been attending board meetings.
Critics say this compromises the church's independence in national affairs. But Fr Wamugunda dismissed the argument "as baseless."
"What is wrong with it. Are people who send letters through the post office not 'souls'? Shouldn't a priest work with them?"
He said his work was not confined to churches, and that he was happy to spread his pastoral duties "to people sorting out letters." He confirmed having attended the corporation's board meetings.
On whether he had informed his superiors at the Nairobi diocese, he said: "I have not told Archbishop Ndingi (Mwana a' Nzeki). He is not the appointing authority. When you are appointed, you attend meetings."
Politics, the priest says, is about truth, justice, human development and protection of human dignity. To limit a priest to spiritual responsibilities is abnormal.
Many theologians, the clergy and lay people have criticised Fr Wamugunda, who is also the chaplain and dean of students at the University of Nairobi, for accepting the appointment. They said the priest has been acting as an activist and organiser of a political faction close to President Kibaki.
The church hierarchy said its clergy served only in commissions and committees to which the church had been invited.
Retired Archbishop John Njenga of Mombasa said priests cannot accept any type of appointment or employment without first obtaining permission from his bishop.
"The first employer of a priest is his bishop, under whom he performs his core duties, the rest of the things come second," he said.
Archbishop Njenga said exceptions and waivers to this rule can be made after thorough consultations but only in special circumstances. "There has not been any such need for Kenya," he said.
"Like the Nuncio said, the invitation should have come to the church first, which would then have nominated somebody. Such an appointee would be least interested in material gain," Kenya Catholic Secretariat secretary general Vincent Wambugu said.
He added that other civil appointments of Catholic clerics followed agreements with individual bishops.
In an exclusive interview at the Nairobi Nunciature, Archbishop Lebeaupin said the clergy could be consulted on issues where consensus was lacking. But the church does not allow its clergy to take up civil employment. "These jobs are for lay Christians," he said.
"A priest or a bishop cannot accept a direct appointment by the government. They cannot be elected as members of parliament, President or cabinet ministers. Neither can they take up duties in public administration."
Archbishop Ndingi said he had no problem with the appointment, although Fr Wamugunda had not officially told him about it. He said he would study the Postal Corporation of Kenya Act to see what the job entails. "If it will not interfere with Fr Wamugunda's work as a priest, nor impact negatively on the church, "I have no problem with it."
He added: "The gazette notice seems to say he has been invited to do a specific job at a specific government body."
The prelate said the appointment was not unique, and gave an example of past appointments of himself and Archbishop Nicodemus Kirima (Nyeri) to public commissions by then President Daniel arap Moi.
"I was paid sitting and travelling allowances for two years. Archbishop Kirima was a member of the Devil Worshippers Commission, but these were special jobs with specific terms of reference."
In the old days when there were not many educated people, priests performed civil jobs, as well. But now the church prefers to support lay people with Christian values to take up civil jobs. The duty of the priest is to promote them. These changes were effected in the Second Vatican Council held in Rome between 1962-1965.
Said the Nuncio: "The first responsibility of the priest is to serve his church. You don't need priests to instil discipline and values in public offices, there are many Christians of moral uprightness who can do that.
However, the Pope's envoy said priests were entitled to their own opinion. But they should refrain from being too close to political associates, lest they be misunderstood.
Without referring to any specific case, he said there were exceptions where bishops had been appointed to certain commission whose terms of reference were specific.
He said bishops could serve in Constitutional review committees - to articulate the church's view and guard against any conflict between the country's supreme law and the church's Canon law.
"As spiritual fathers, they should keep keep away from political powers. The Kenya Episcopal Conference is united on this, for the common good of the country," the Nuncio said.
The director of theology at Tangaza College, Fr Ambrose Mutinda, asked: "Has he accepted? The normal practice is to decline such offers. Otherwise, there should be consultations with the bishop or one's superior."
But Government spokesman Alfred Mutua dismissed sentiments that the appointment threatened the independence of the church.
Information and Communications Permanent Secretary Bitange Ndemo confirmed Fr Wamugunda's appointment, saying the priest had not only accepted it but had attended a board meeting.
The PS said Fr Wamugunda was selected for his rare qualities and valuable experience in administration, as well as his leadership qualities.
"The Postal Corporation has had many problems, it is threatened by technology and we needed somebody with understanding to unite the staff. Some of them were not willing to share information even when survival of the corporation was at stake."
The PS said they had found in Fr Wamugunda rare leadership, a man of understanding who could early unite people of extreme points of view.
"We need him to instil discipline and appropriate moral values, particularly among the management," he said.
"The Postal Corporation has had many problems. It is threatened by technology. We need somebody with deep understanding of many issues," he said.
Although the church does not allow its clergy to join active politics, Fr Wamugunda raised eyebrows last November during the Referendum campaigns when he pitched camp at the Government-backed Banana secretariat. Archbishop Ndingi said the priest did not inform him of his activities.
"He has never told me he was interested in politics. There are many lay people qualified to enter politics. I don't see the need for priests to join the fray," he said.
Priests can speak on any political and developmental issue so long as their stance is not taken to be that of the church.
"We normally allow priests to teach at universities, or to be members of schools' board. But we scrutinise such appointments carefully to make sure there will be no interference with one's pastoral work. Only then is permission to take up such jobs given.
He said that Fr Wamugunda's qualifications may have put him in good stead. "The Government might have seen in him qualities which would help the State corporation and the country."
A minister can appoint any priest to any position he is qualified for. No church law hinders priests from taking up such jobs. Unlike missionaries, diocesan priests do not take the vow of poverty.
Further reactions to the appointment were received from Fr Joakim Omollo, patron of the People for Peace in Africa (PPA). He praised the appointment, saying it was a surprise only to those Catholics who think the primary duty of priests is pastoral work, administering sacraments and celebrating mass.
"Priests are properly qualified. They can handle any job. But pastoral duties are demanding, and are part and parcel of evangelisation. It is a big sacrifice on one to take up secular jobs, particularly now when the church has a shortage of priests."
Dr Francis Kuria of the Inter Religious Council of Kenya said the Catholic Church plays an important role in the lives of many people. It is an anchor on which life, despite the buffets, remains steadfast and doesn't go adrift.
Being a strategist for a partisan group in a political contest is not a normal duty for a priest, he said. "It may be legal. It may even be within Fr Wamugunda's rights. But normal, it is not."
If it is okay and normal for Fr. Wamugunda to dabble in partisan politics and get appointed as a director of state corporations, what of the Bishop? The Archbishop? Up to what level is it okay? he wondered.
Fr Wamugunda, who in 2003 shed tears in public while paying tribute to his mentor, the late Maurice Michael Cardinal Otunga, is the Dean of Students at the University of Nairobi.
Another such high profile post - University Registrar - was held by the late Fr Zacheus Oganda, who had to quit priesthood to marry his student.
Fr Wamugunda is a professional journalist with a PhD in communication. He was born in Matara in the present Thika district.
His father taught at Matara Holy Ghost Mission School, where he had his basic education up to 1968.
He joined the Queen of Apostles Seminary in Nairobi, before proceeding to Saint Augustine's Seminary in Mabanga, Bungoma, to study philosophy.
In 1979, he went to Rome's Urbanian University for a course in Theology.
He was ordained priest by Cardinal Maurice Otunga in 1981 and posted to Mangu Mission. He served as chaplain of Mary Hill in Thika, and lectured at the United States Internationa University, Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Makwavarara Faces Contempt of Parly Charges
Headline: Zimbabwe: Makwavarara Faces Contempt of Parly Charges
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Parliamentary Portfolio Committee on Transport and Communication yesterday blasted the chairperson of the commission running the City of Harare Ms Sekesayi Makwavarara for lacking seriousness and for being contemptuous of the august House.
Ms Makwavarara incurred the wrath of the committee after snubbing its oral evidence session for the third consecutive time yesterday.
On each occasion, she has been sending her juniors to represent her.
The committee, chaired by Makonde Member of the House of Assembly Cde Leo Mugabe (Zanu-PF), immediately gave Ms Makwavarara until next Monday to avail herself or face charges of contempt of Parliament.
Ms Makwavarara was supposed to give oral evidence before the committee on the implementation of a report on rehabilitation of roads and traffic lights.
Instead, Ms Makwavarara sent officials, led by acting town clerk Dr Stanley Mungofa, council strategist Dr Tendai Mahachi, acting director of works Engineer Michael Jaravaza, and acting operations manager for traffic lights Engineer Mark Kurebwaseka.
Initially, Dr Mungofa, who apologised on behalf of Ms Makwavarara, told the committee that she was not feeling well.
But after being quizzed, he made a U-turn and said Ms Makwavarara had not been advised on time about the meeting.
Cde Mugabe said the committee took great exception over the conduct of Ms Makwavarara.
He ordered her to appear before the committee next Monday, failure of which she would be charged with contempt of Parliament.
He said the in past two meetings they have had with the city, the now suspended town clerk Mr Nomutsa Chideya represented the municipality and had indicated that he would take full responsibility of council activities, and the committee even tabled a positive report before Parliament based on his representations.
Cde Mugabe wondered how the committee could now properly evaluate progress since there was now an acting, rather than substantive, town clerk. Because of this, it was important for Ms Makwavarara to appear in person before the committee for consistency and to answer questions authoritatively.
"These are issues of policy and should be answered by policymakers like (Ms) Makwavarara. Her failure to avail herself is regrettable.
"Last time it was (Mr) Chideya who
appeared before this committee and has since been suspended, and now it is you. You can again be chucked away, so we need to deal with someone who makes policies and not officials," said Cde Mugabe, adding that officials could only aid and advise their boss and not represent her.
"We view this as a ploy by the chairperson not to come, so the committee will send you back and give (Ms) Makwavarara the last chance to appear before this committee. If she fails to come again, we will take it that she doesn't want and that she will be contemptuous of Parliament."
Chitungwiza Senator Cde Forbes Magadu (Zanu-PF) said appearing to testify before Parliament must be treated the same way as being served with summons for a court appearance.
"Being called to appear before Parliament is like appearing before a judge because it takes precedence over all other things like attending a funeral.
"(The late) former President (Canaan) Banana at one stage had to attend to a court case before he attended the funeral of his mother and this shows how serious these proceedings should be regarded," said Cde Magadu.
Zhombe Member of the House of Assembly Cde Daniel Mackanzie-Ncube (Zanu-PF) said it was ironic that Ms Makwavarara had no respect for legislators yet she had invited the same people to give support at her fund-raising tea party to be held next month.
"The mayor has invited us to a tea party to support her on November 4 this year but she does not want to support us in our parliamentary business," he said.
Harare Central Member of the House of Assembly Mr Murisi Zwizwai (MDC) said upon being notified that Ms Makwavarara was coming to testify, he strove to attend the hearing despite the fact that he had not been feeling well.
"Personally I was not feeling well, but when I was advised that Makwavarara was coming, I got out of my bed because I appreciate the seriousness of parliamentary business," he said.
Pumula-Luveve Member of the House of Assembly Mr Esaph Mdlongwa (MDC) urged Ms Makwavarara to take the business of Parliament seriously.
"Makwavarara should respect this august House. When we are here to deliberate critical issues affecting the nation, she should not behave like this," he said.

Original_Search_Title: South Sudan Slumbers Still
Headline: Sudan: South Sudan Slumbers Still
Body_Text:
Kampala — The signs of war are fading. The military trucks are gone and so are the military fatigues. In the pubs, civilians have replaced the military men. From Gulu to Nimule, on the border with Southern Sudan, life is creeping back.
Back in December 2002, Gulu had as many soldiers in military fatigues as people sleeping on verandas at night.
Travelling with the then First Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Disaster Preparedness, Moses Ali, and West Nile legislators to meet President Yoweri Museveni in Gulu Barracks on the way to West Nile to witness the signing of a peace deal with the West Nile Bank Front rebels, we were sandwiched between anti-aircraft guns. And we were warned against straying in the night. Not any more.
However, on the road from Gulu to Nimule into South Sudan, life is still a matter of luck. Dangers lurk on the road: Bad drivers, dangerous corners, landmines and suspicious soldiers.
"Are you seriously going to Juba in this car?" asked photojournalist James Akena, who met me at a petrol station in Gulu. "Unless you have a back-up car, which you can call up in case of an emergency, please don't."
"The road is littered with landmines. You can't afford to skid off the road," said Frank Nyakairu, a Daily Monitor reporter, travelling with Akena. "We drove over one," said Akena.
Silence. We were puzzled.
Akena read the bewilderment on our faces. "It's one of those mines meant for heavy tanks," he said.
And then, they said, there were gun-totting soldiers on patrol. "Even if you have a letter from the President's Office, they don't care," said Akena, "First of all, they don't know how to read."
To get to Juba, we were told, we could drive to Arua and then through Yei to Juba. We were told it was a safer route.
Another option was to drive to Nimule, three hours away, spend the night in a lodge, and wake up to drive another 10 hours. Or perhaps we could load the Nissan onto a truck, which would ferry it to Juba.
"If you are lucky to reach Juba," Nyakairu told Emma Okello, who was driving, "you can rent out this truck. I could use it for $50 (sh90,000) a day."
We set out at dawn the following day, a Saturday.
Mission? To explore life in the land that was, until a peace agreement in 2005, a Sudanese army-SPLA war zone and through Joseph Kony's Lord's Resistance Army, a Sudanese army proxy-UPDF war zone. War was just a game here.
The 20 years of the LRA war left the vast stretch of savannah from Nimule to Juba more or less deserted, leaving only a few villages.
Public transport barely existed: the only two buses from Kampala to Juba through Nimule only started travelling a week before our visit.
So we took the bumpy, potholed, dusty road out of Gulu, leaving the tarmac far behind, and headed for Nimule.
"This place used to be bad," said Okello, who hails from Gulu, as we drove through the Keyo Central Reserve Forest.
Each of the towns we left behind had seen terrible times. At Parbongo, two pickup trucks lay burned by the roadside, a grim reminder of the war days.
Atiak, where the blood of 209 people was shed in 1995, bustled with life. However, the signs of new life ended at Nimule, a muddy stretch of no-man's land with a stream in which the residents bathed in the open.
We crossed the small bridge - wooden bars suspended on metallic poles across a fast running stream. Kids jumped five miles down into the brown clay waters rushing down the valleys. Grey-haired men kicked off their pants, tore their shirts off and walked into the stream to have a bath.
A wooden pole in the middle of the road separates Uganda from Sudan. The Nimule border post is lifeless. There are no people, no livestock, no huts and no tarmac.
The customs point on the Ugandan side was once bombed and is now deserted.
After crossing the border, save for Nimule town, there is nothing to see but shrubbery, streams, warnings about landmines, hills and valleys interspersed with military detachments in addition to burned tanks and military trucks.
A Sudanese writer, Ernest Woderif Marbaga, writing in the Khartoum Monitor put it quite candidly, saying, "Southern Sudan has been reduced to the stone age."
Nimule lies on the banks of the Blue Nile and grazes the western Rift Valley.
The narrow road from Nimule into the interior of Southern Sudan wound round rocks, skirting dangerously at their edges. The corners are sharp. The line between staying on the road and rolling into the valley below is thin.
Even at our low speed, we were losing pressure in the front tyre and would have to change it. We, however, were afraid to stop anywhere.
Fortunately, after about an hour, we came upon a Ugandan-registered truck stranded about three kilometres from a UPDF detachment.
The banana-carrying truck had been stranded from the previous evening.
All they needed was a spare tyre, but the drivers of trucks pasing by were not willing to help.
One truck, carrying crates of beer and five Sudanese, stopped. However, the driver was too angry to help. The Sudanese he had offered a lift had been busy having a party, helping themselves to his cargo. He left them in the middle of nowhere.
We drove on, over several stretches of land littered with mines, the warnings - notices bearing the skull crossbones in red - sometimes appearing for stretches of up to a kilometre.
One detachment we passed had tanks surrounding it, pointing into the hills.
The silence was eerie. Only the car engine hummed.
Okello was agitated. At each detachment he asked whether we were about to reach our destination. Sunset approached. We were running out of fuel and no longer sure that we would get to Juba.
Then we saw lights. It was a military detachment, the last before we reached our destination.
Candlelight and lamps welcomed us to Juba, which, so much for being the capital, is no better than the rest of Southern Sudan.
Joseph Akim visited Juba in July after 13 years in exile. "Juba is still the largest village in the world," he said.
But home is home. "Whether people call it the biggest village in the world, it doesn't bother me," Akim said. "It was political manipulation that has kept Juba and South Sudan in its present state, and once we get the right political leadership the path to development will be achieved."
Lt. Simon Taban, who served in the Sudan military, was one of those who settled in Juba upon retirement five years ago.
He says members of international terrorist group Al Qaeda were behind the destabilisation of Southern Sudan.
Holding up a pen he said, "Those days, if a child picked something like this, we would tell them to throw it away (for fear of an explosion)."
They lived in fear in the world's largest village. Juba, though, is easily worse than a village. Flies swarm like locusts, dust swirls as if it's under the power of a twister and lamps light the city. But it is hardly unexpected after so many years of war.

Original_Search_Title: State of Emergency in Ekiti -Parties, Falae, Others React
Headline: Nigeria: State of Emergency in Ekiti -Parties, Falae, Others React
Body_Text:
Abuja — A cross section of Nigerians and organisations yesterday reacted to the state of emergency which the federal government has imposed on Ekiti state.
Lagos-based lawyer and human rights activist, Chief Gani Fawehinmi (SAN), said Obasanjo should be applauded for taking the step.
"I support the declaration in its entirety because the erstwhile governor, Chief Ayo Fayose, had terrorised Ekiti for too long," he said.
Chief Olu Falae, a former presidential aspirant, said the declaration was undemocratic and capable of bringing about anarchy in the state. He blames the federal government and the Ekiti state lawmakers for what has happened in the state.
Chief Falae who is the national coordinator of the Democratic Peoples Alliance (DPA), said the EFCC was hell bent on removing the state governor and incited the lawmakers. He blamed them for failing to follow the due process in the impeachment saga.
He explained that the impeachment threat going on in Anambra and Plateau states and the development in Ekiti state cast doubts on the readiness of the federal government and INEC to conduct free and fair elections next year.
Chekwas Okorie, APGA national chairman: "I have earlier proposed it and the President got it right this time by declaring that state of emergency. The Plateau state own was horribly dab and now that he has intervened by making this declaration, it is a worthy thing. You noticed too that the president carried us along by discussing it with the nation in an address. That broadcast was a land mark as you saw the president going into the details and niceties of the constitution."
Ibrahim Coomassie, former IGP: I would advise the judiciary and the Nigerian Bar Association (NBA), to rise up to the occasion. This is something that needs a lot of clearance from the judiciary and I think they should, as a matter of urgency, go to court and demand an accelerated hearing on the matter. Unless that happens, this situation might lead to a break down of law and order. It would also spread to other places like Plateau. So, the courts should hurry and declare it null and void."
John Oyegun, Former Edo state governor: It is tragic and I see it as part of a whole grand plan to deliberately engineer a complete break down of law and order. It is a ploy to prepare the grounds for an extension of this regime, the much talked about plan B. If you view it in consonance with the Anambra case and that of Plateau and possibly Abia, you'll get the picture. This is nothing but the countdown to chaos. The fact is that the nation is not prepared for an election and this crisis will ensure there is just no election in 2007. It will be the grand excuse needed for the extension of government. In the particular case of Ekiti, there was no need for that emergency. The courts would have successfully done a great job of it."
Lisa Olu Akerele, PPA Director of Publicity: From PPA's point of view, it is a welcome relief. The emergency proclamation in Ekiti state will stem the descent to anarchy which could tear the nation apart if not nipped in the bud. The party is only reaping what it sowed by what they called "victory"at the 2003 polls. The Ekiti saga will also send strong signals to other states where laws are being misapplied.
"The Attorney-General of the Federation and the National Judicial Institute should as a matter of urgency, sack the Ekiti state Chief Judge for dereliction of duty. The whole saga is unfortubate and unconstitutional and should be considered null and void."
Isyaku Ibrahim, Frontline politician: The president should exercise caution and not drag us down. The nation is already being seen as a banana republic. He must not turn us into a water melon republic. States of emergency is not a flippant issue and you can only declare one when there is crisis. Like Plateau, it made some sense. But in Ekiti, there was peace and no one was breaching the peace. So this whole exercise was done in a hurry without any show of maturity.
ANPP: The All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP), has blamed the leadership of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), for the political crisis in Ekiti state which culminated into the imposition of a state of emergency yesterday by President Olusegun Obasanjo.
In a statement signed by its national publicity secretary, Emma Eneukwu, the party described the crisis in Ekiti state as another sad event in the nation's political history with a tendency to gradually snowball into the sad events of the '60s.
"The ANPP believes that the 1999 constitution has laid down clear-cut procedures for the resolution of any political crisis, if political actors play by the rules. But what we see in Ekiti state is a violation of the constitutional process with a predetermined aim in view," the party said.
The party further expressed curiosity about how the PDP failed to call the Ekiti state House of Assembly to order when it decided to sack the chief judge of the state without following the laid down procedures but decided to jump at the state of emergency option when it was obvious that the crisis was full blown.
"Where was the presidency when the first act of omission was committed by the Ekiti legislators who sacked a sitting chief judge? How come the presidency failed to act when it ought to have acted by restoring law and order in the state? Maybe the crisis was instigated to cause anarchy in the system," it stressed.
The party therefore urged Nigerians to be on their guard as no amount of pressure should make them give up on democracy as it was still the best form of governance that would lead the nation to development.
It then enjoined the Ekiti state people to ensure that they vote the PDP out during the 2007 election as the party has failed to deliver democracy dividends to their people in over three years of the PDP administration in the state.
DPA: The Democratic Peoples Alliance (DPA), on its part, said the declaration of a state of emergency in Ekiti state by President Obasanjo was a violation of the constitutional provisions on state of emergency.
The party said there was no need for such a declaration because the situation in the state was not out of control and did not involve such antecedents that may warrant the imposition of emergency rule.
Answering questions from the press yesterday in Abuja, the Deputy National Secretary of the party, Dr. Amaobi Uwaleke, said the situation is still controllable and as such, there is no need for such an action.
"There is nothing to warrant it. There is no breakdown of law and order. There is no rioting or loss of lives. So, there is no need for the presidency to declared state of emergency," he added.
PAC: Progressive Action Congress (PAC), said the recent constitutional crises in some parts of the country would degenerate into a national chaos if urgent measures were not taken.
National chairman of the party, Chief Charles Nwodo, who said this in Abuja while reacting to the declaration of state of emergency in Ekiti state, said Nigerians never bargained for this when they elected for the return to democracy in 1999.
"There is the distinct possibility that these trends may degenerate into national political crises thereby casting doubts on this government's ability to conduct a free and fair elections come 2007," Nwodo said.
Senators yesterday also lent their voice to President Olusegun Obasanjo's declaration of a state of emergency in Ekiti state.
Senate's spokesman and chairman, senate Committee on Media and Public Affairs, Senator Victor Ndoma-Egba, said it is within the powers of the president to declare a state of emergency in any state especially when law and order is being threatened.
Asked what such an action portends for the system, Senator Ndoma-Egba refused to comment, stressing however that the matter has not yet reached the senate, "but when it reaches us, we would know how to tackle it."
Senate Deputy Minority Whip, Senator Abiola Ajimobi, who described the impeachment process of the former governor as "a badly managed one," said the state of emergency is "a welcome development."
He said: "considering the fact that you now have three governors in Ekiti state, the president had no choice but to declare a state of emergency. But the question is, should the President have waited this long?
"Do we need that to resolve the problem? If the problem had been well managed in the first place by the PDP, there wouldn't have been any state of emergency. It reflects on the management style of this government. It is the same military mentality."
Senator Ajimobi added that both the state Assembly and the President, had in the past, commended Fayose for doing well. He has been there for three years. The money they said he stole, did he steal it last week? So, it is the hypocrisy in the system and the mismanagement of governance."
He however made a case for the Nigerian democracy, saying that "we are still in our learning process. I think in the process of correcting some of the things we have done wrongly; we would continue to have problems...
"But we should complain more; the more we complain, the more things will improve. We should allow democracy to continue. The military should not come in; let us learn our lessons, we will get there," Senator Ajimobi assured.

Original_Search_Title: Is Nigeria a Banana Republic?
Headline: Nigeria: Is Nigeria a Banana Republic?
Body_Text:
"NO form of society can be reasonably stable in which the majority of the people are not fairly content. People cannot be content if they feel that the foundation of their lives are wholly unstable." -- James Truslow Adams.
DURING my undergraduate days at the Lagos State University Law Faculty, Professor Mike Ikhariale, current Editorial Board Chairman of Daily Independent Newspapers was my Constitutional Law teacher. Considered to be intellectually haughty, he was fond of reciting United States Supreme Court Justice Marshal's statement in the landmark Supremacy of Constitution's case of Marbury V. Madison. It goes: "Let the end be legitimate: Let it be within the scope of the constitution and all means which are appropriate; which are plainly adapted to that end: which are not prohibited but consistent with the letters and spirit law of the constitution are constitutional". Several years after passing through the learned Prof's constitutional law class, I still cherish and recite this quote. It has become relevant today in view of the abuse the nation's Constitution is being subjected to.
It has been my belief based on one's academic training that no constitutional government can ignore the significant purport of Justice Marshal's statement in that case. When our nation was under military rule which during my undergraduate days, I had no qualm resigning to fate on military decrees and the military's totalitarian ways of doing things. Then, we were told by our radical lecturers who taught us elective courses in Sociology and Political Science that the military way of doing things was an aberration. They inculcate in us the belief that under a democracy, such high handed way of doing things can never be attempted.
I and others in my class then believed them. Because we truly cherished and desired a government that Abraham Lincoln in Gettysburg described as "government of the people, by the people and for the people"; we dreamt, slept and anticipated the birth of democracy in the Niger-Area called Nigeria.
June 12, 1993 nearly presented us an opportunity to have a feel of full blown democracy but its annulment by the Ibrahim Babangida junta killed that dream. Our university fantasy did not materialize until 1999 when on May 29 of that year, a "democratically elected government" with President Olusegun Obasanjo at the saddle came on board.
We looked forward to seeing in reality all the things we were told about democratic government in our Constitutional and Political Science classes. I was looking out to observance and respect for rule of law, for a respectable and truly independent judiciary. I craved for a vibrant legislature; for a true operation of the principle of separation of power and the doctrine of checks and balances. I desired seeing a genuinely democratic executive arm of government. To me, these are non-negotiable ingredients of a democratic society that the military's incursion into governance in our clime had denied the nation.
But now, I am disappointed in the system and those operating it. I am yet to see traces of the democratic features my lecturers taught me to be inseparable characteristics of a constitutional government. Without being immodest, the Obasanjo-led Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) government is worse than the military. Things that are better imagined than to be real are happening in the country today. From Bayelsa, Oyo, Ekiti and Plateau States, gun-point democracy not constitutional democracy is in practice.
There is no separation of power while doctrine of checks and balances have taken flight. The Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) manifested obvious signs of partisanship in the Plateau State matter by declaring positions of its 14 House of Assembly members vacant. In 2003, it looked the other way when Oluseye Ogunlewe and Wahab Dosunmu crossed from Alliance for Democracy (AD) to the PDP. Worse still, the integrity of the judiciary with the happening in Ekiti State regarding the unconstitutional impeachment of Governor Ayodele Fayose has been dragged to the mud. Unfortunately, in Ekiti, a state of men and women of letters, the Speaker of the State House of Assembly in flagrant violation of provisions of the 1999 Constitution suspended and sacked a serving Chief Judge and solely appointed an "Acting Chief Judge."
Also in Plateau, six members of the House of Assembly conspired with the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) to sit and commence impeachment proceeding against Governor Joshua Dariye against the provision of the Constitution in Section188(4) on quorum.
In the country today, the signs are ominous. Those in power are jettisoning the rule of law for rule of man who has beastly tendency. These people have forgotten that there is no fundamental human right to be a president, governor, legislator or chairman of a local government. They are solving a problem with a more difficult problem. No one is presently bothered about the on-going voters registration exercise. Nobody talks about how a credible election conduct can be achieved next year. Nigerians should be wary of this unconscious diversion that may styly deny us a change of government in 2007.
These are salient issues that can move our nation and its democracy forward, not vindictive politiking that very soon might give birth to a state of anomie, a banana republic where anything goes.
As rightly decided in the case of McFoy V. UAC, nobody can "build something on nothing and expect it to stand". To Obasanjo, PDP and its opportunistic members, they should not think they can use a wrong and unconstitutional method to achieve a right purpose. I know this cannot be regarded as lawful.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni On the Role of Leadership in Growth, Stability
Headline: Uganda: Museveni On the Role of Leadership in Growth, Stability
Body_Text:
Kampala — The following is the text of a speech by President Yoweri Museveni.
Your Excellencies: President Paul Kagame, of the Republic of Rwanda, President Kenneth Kaunda, Premier Mbhazima Shilowai, of Gauteng Province, fellow participants, ladies and gentlemen.
The subject of my brief speech today is: "Transitional Leadership, Stability and Growth". I am not entirely certain what the word "Transitional" is intended to mean here. Nevertheless, I suspect that what you need to know may be captured in a question using the words of Lee Kuan Yew, the former Prime Minister of Singapore: Why is it that much of Africa has delayed in transitioning from "Third World" to "First World" compared to some of the Asian Countries?
Instead of confining ourselves to transitional "Leadership", it is more illuminating to talk of the type of leadership that can cause this metamorphosis, this social transformation whereby Africa transitions from being a peasant society to a middle class, skilled working class society.
Initially, the tasks inside Uganda were confined to minimum economic recovery. Since this was achieved some years ago, we have been engaged in a debate concerning the strategic bottlenecks that held Africa back the fact that the cradles of the human race and civilisation are in Africa without any doubt, notwithstanding. I have attached two long speeches to this short introduction.
One was a speech I gave at the G8 Summit in Sea Island - Georgia on the June 8-10, 2004; and the other one is a speech I gave recently to our NRM MPs. You should ignore some limited dosage of our internal polemics and go for substance.
In these speeches, I have identified eight strategic bottlenecks that have bedevilled Africa's transition for the last 500 years.
One of them has been the stifling of the private sector by the leaders of Africa after independence. Uganda suffered a lot from this problem. The late President Obote confiscated shares of private companies in 1970. Two years later, Amin completely uprooted the small Asian middle class that had contributed so much to the growth of Uganda's modern sector.
When our Movement defeated the dictatorship, this is one of the strategic bottlenecks we addressed. We gave back to their owners the 4,000 properties that the late President Amin had taken from our Asian citizens and residents. We privatized most of the Government parastatals. We liberalised both the current and the capital accounts.
As a consequence, our economy has been growing at an average GDP rate of growth of 6.5% for the last 19 years. Much of this growth, however, is in the type of enterprises that are low investment, low technology and high returns. These are, mainly, in the sectors of real estate, transport, professional services, hotels etc.
However, enterprises that require investment of the magnitude of US $10-$15 million and above have not attracted investments.
Yet they should. We have, therefore, decided to have some interventions by the Government to kick-start some of the strategic enterprises although we still prefer the private sector. Accordingly, the state is now funding our scientists that have invented modern ways of processing bananas into flour, starch and juices.
Uganda is the second biggest producer of bananas in the whole World with a production of 10 million tones per annum.
Hitherto, 40% of these bananas get lost on account of having no technology of processing them. Fortunately, our scientists have found the solution and we are funding them. We are also funding the development of some larvicides against the mosquito that causes malaria.
The point here is that while the private sector is preferable, we cannot wait forever for them when it comes to the strategic sectors of our economy such as textiles, coffee processing, the steel industry etc. We need to assist the market forces to kick-start some of them.
One of the eight strategic bottlenecks we have dealt with is the development of the human resource through education and health for all.
Enrolment in primary schools has gone from 2.5 million to 7.7 million on account of free education provided by the State. On account of improved health services (immunisation, safe water etc), the population of Uganda is now 28 million people. This is a great resource if it is educated and skilled.
Another strategic bottleneck that we are dealing with is the balkanization of Africa into the 53, mainly small and sub-optimal, countries that are now members of African Union (AU). One of the problems with these small units is a small market. A small market cannot support large-scale production. We have been working hard to ensure the integration of these markets through COMESA, East African Community(EAC) etc.
In COMESA, we have a market of about 190 million people. In the East African Community (EAC), we, potentially, with the joining of Rwanda and Burundi, have a market of 110 million people. In the case of EAC, we are also working for the creation of an East African Political Federation.
Apart from the regional markets, we also have tariff-free, quota-free access to the USA for 6,500 manufactured products as well as access to the European Union (EU) market. Access to larger markets is an important stimulus to growth and transformation.
Therefore, our view of leadership, whether transitional or otherwise, is that it must address the strategic bottlenecks of a given society. In order to understand leadership, we must know something about the people who are being led.
Leadership has meaning, mainly in terms of the people whom the leader is leading. It must be understood that the people in Uganda, for example, have fundamental needs and interests at any given moment in their history. These fundamental aspirations cry for fulfillment. Leadership should be the answer to the people's cry for emancipation and fulfillment.
In the Book of Exodus, in the Bible, we read about the children of Israel crying for freedom from their slavery in Egypt. Moses was the answer to their cry. In South Africa here, the Black South Africans' oppression under apartheid gave rise to Comrade Nelson Mandela and other leaders.
The point is surely clear that the fundamental cry of the people is the basis of the rise of leadership. When leadership does not arise, the oppression continues. When leadership arises, the struggle for freedom begins.
It is perhaps self-evident that the conditions giving rise to leadership are dynamic and are undergoing continuous change. Opposition arises within the country and from outside the country. The struggle for freedom is never without opposition. The freedom fighters will meet resistance from the oppressor both from within and from outside.
For example, the struggle against the colonial powers in Africa took many forms. The Mau Mau bloody struggle against colonialism in Kenya in the 1950s differed from the largely peaceful anti-colonial struggle in Uganda.
Our armed struggle against colonial institutions came later in the 1970s and 1980s as we struggled to subdue the colonial army under the late Presidents Idi Amin and Obote. The requirements of leadership in Uganda in the 1950s took the form of political party organisation, trade boycotts and mass demonstrations.
Later, in the 1970s, the struggle metamorphosed into a military struggle in which the progressive elements in Uganda were aided by the late Mwalimu Julius Nyerere and the people of Tanzania to remove Amin's dictatorship and later that of Obote.
The fundamental aspiration of the people of Uganda was for personal security, democracy and economic development.
The National Resistance Movement (NRM) responded to the cry of the people of Uganda. The people of Uganda are fully aware of what the NRM has done. We have liberated our people from colonial and quasi-colonial dictatorships; and our people know it. That is why they overwhelmingly vote for the Movement, election after election.
It is because the NRM represents transition from oppression to freedom, from economic destitution to free enterprise, free trade, universal education, democratic choice, personal security and genuine hope and anticipation of a better future.
Transitional leadership is, therefore, the tactical implementation of some phases in the long march on the journey to address, first and foremost, the strategic bottlenecks facing a society. Lee Kuan Yew, correctly, diagnosed the strategic bottlenecks facing Singapore as well as other Third World countries: to move from Third World to First World.
In my view, it is not very useful to dichotomise the challenges between the strategic and the tactical. It is better if the latter enriches the former. Therefore, it would be more profitable if the transitional leadership also served to enrich the solutions to the long-term solutions.
Apart from the identification of the stifling of the private sector as one of the eight strategic bottlenecks and resolving to put in place, pro-private sector policies, we must develop a comprehensive code to do so, as well as appropriate mechanisms to implement it. In Uganda, we have had three Presidential Investors Round Table (PIRT) meetings.
In these fora, the private sector evaluates the policies of the Government and recommend solutions for the way forward. Most of the time, we adopt their ideas wholesale, without any alteration, because they are right.
Once Government withdraws from production to regulation, then they must ensure consumer satisfaction. What better way of ensuring that the consumer is satisfied than to ask him or his delegates in a Presidential Investors Round Table (PIRT)? At the last round table, they made the following recommendations, among others:
lImproving the regulatory framework by reviewing the old commercial laws and enacting new ones;
lTransformation of Uganda Investment Authority (URA) into a one-stop centre;
lFast-tracking the reforms in Uganda Investment Authority;
lImproving infrastructure in East Africa to reduce transport costs to the sea;
lComputerization of the land registry.
By nurturing the private sector thus, we shall cause growth and transformation in our economy.
Stability should be understood in two senses: political stability (absence of disruption) as well as economic stability (e.g. low inflation). Stability in the two senses is, of course, a sine qua non of growth and transformation.
Without controlling inflation, for instance, it will be impossible to maintain sustained economic growth. The effects of political instability are obvious and have caused the stagnation of quite a number of African countries such as Somalia, Congo, Uganda in the past and many others. I salute you and thank you for listening to me.

Original_Search_Title: Challenge to Mboweni And Textbook Economists
Headline: South Africa: Challenge to Mboweni And Textbook Economists
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — FRAME Textile MD and International Textile Manufacturers Federation president Walter Simeoni has challenged Reserve Bank governor Tito Mboweni and other "textbook economists" to visit the textile maker to clear up perceptions that it is an "old and dying" industry in SA.
Mboweni and other economists claimed recently that plans to introduce import quotas for the clothing and textile industries did not make much sense, because they believed the industries were uncompetitive in SA.
"I beg to differ," Simeoni says in an interview. "These commentators do not seem to understand the world of 'textile value chain' and how SA fits into it."
Frame, which made 39% of Seardel Investment Corporation's operating profit in the year to June from 32% a year before, is one of sub-Saharan Africa's largest textile groups.
"Throughout the world the textile industry has been classified as sensitive, and in many countries the import-protection tariffs are huge. In some countries in Asia and South America there are also additional taxes levied on textile importers," he says.
Countries that have quotas in place on imports from China include the US, Turkey, Brazil and some in the European region, all leading textile producers.
But local manufacturers face additional costs compared with other countries. In China and India funding for capital equipment for textile makers can be obtained at 1,5%, while France, Korea and Israel's textile industries receive billions of dollars a year in financial assistance.
"What do we get in SA? Nothing! The limited advantages of the duty credit certification scheme comes to an end in March 2007," he said.
A volatile exchange rate also affects competitiveness.
Frame's exports in the year to June slipped to 7,6% of revenue from 9,3% the previous year due to the rand strengthening.
Other additional costs included security (1% of the cost of sales) while in some European countries "they've even done without the receptionist".
There is also a cost in managing the HIV/AIDS pandemic.
Emigration of experienced staff, additional costs associated with black empowerment and employment equity, the costs of losing skilled black staff once they were trained, even though Frame paid the highest salaries in the industry, were expenses overseas competitors did not have.
"In certain skills categories we've reached the stage where white people work for 30%-50% less than blacks," Simeoni said.
The cost of employment for local textile operators averages about $600 an employee a month, while some local retailers are being supplied from manufacturers in the east, which pays workers $30-$40 a month.
"They are complaining how unproductive we are, yet they are supporting that kind of slavery."
The distance to export markets also affects profitability. For instance, Frame supplies denim to some of the top global brands in Italy, but these buyers specify 35 days from inquiry to delivery, while it took 27 days for a South African manufactured product just to be shipped to Italy.
Factors that make local textiles competitive are an ability to change product lines quickly and a reputation for producing quality products that are also of a high technical standard.
The fact that fashion lines change every four to six weeks in Europe, which is shorter than the shipping time from the Far East, safeguarded the industry to an extent.
In addition, it might be strategically short-sighted for local retailers to resort to importing all their apparel, says Simeoni.
He believes the future expansion of Frame lies in complex, value-added textile manufactured products using exotic, renewable fibres such as nettles, flax, sisal, bamboo and banana leaf. The company is entering a period of renewed investment in equipment after two years of consolidation.
The manufacturing activities for these types of products require the sophisticated infrastructure available in SA, but in the medium to long term, simple textile products might be sourced from neighbouring countries, where the labour cost is $70-$100 a month, says Simeoni.
"Globally, the textile market is determined by six or eight retail groups. It is the governments which take the decision to support their industries.
"In SA we need a meaningful basket of government support structures to aid us with capital projects," he says.
Government is working well with all the stakeholders on the customised-sector programme, which could be vital for the industry's growth, he says.

Original_Search_Title: KCC Seizes Stray Cattle
Headline: Uganda: KCC Seizes Stray Cattle
Body_Text:
Kampala — KAMPALA City Council (KCC) law enforcement officers yesterday seized over 50 head of cattle that were loitering in the city.
The animals were impounded from Nakawa division, said a KCC law enforcement officer who declined to reveal his identity.
Mostly long-horned, the cattle were herded into the KCC bonded warehouse on Sixth Street, Industrial area.
This was the highest number of cattle to be seized at a go in the last six months, the officer said.
There was disruption of traffic that caused a heavy jam as the animals were led to the KCC bond.
Some of the owners followed closely.
A female KCC official in charge of the warehouse declined to comment on the operation, saying it was her seniors who should do so.
But another officer who participated in the operation said owners would be fined for letting their animals loiter in the city.
"the owners have to pay for each day the cattle spend in KCC custody," he said.
He said KCC pays someone to ferry banana peels on which the animals feed everyday.
KCC director of public and health services Dr. Mesach Mubiru recently said cattle, goats and dogs that roam the city centre cause traffic jams and accidents.
KCC by-laws do not permit grazing of animals in the city, although it is allowed in a few city suburbs. In August, KCC started an exercise to rid the city of stray animals.

Original_Search_Title: Malawians Must Change Their Mindset
Headline: Malawians Must Change Their Mindset
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Ms. Ntshadi Tsheole is South African High Commissioner to Malawi. She speaks to Moses Kaufa in an exclusive interview on Malawi's Mothers' day and expounds on Malawian women in the process of democratic and social development.
How did you find your way to the position of High Commissioner? And for how long have you worked with the government of Malawi?
As a woman to become a High Commissioner I think it's a developmental process. I am meant to believe that my employer must have seen something in me but usually you don't ask people what they saw in you to send you to work in a particular country. But what I can say is that there have been challenges in life and I think those challenges at times help to refine one's outlook towards life's challenges and it could be even the manner in which one dealt with those challenges which could have contributed to people or my head of state and my government in South Africa selecting me as somebody who can represent South Africa.
During my stay in Malawi (for two years now), I have learned a lot; the exposure, the culture and also the political scenario. First, in coming to Malawi from South Africa, one has to come with a mindset that they should not expect to find South Africa here. I came here with an open mind that I will be able to live in Malawi and where possible, I will try my best to contribute positively to the development of the people of Malawi and to women in particular.
Having worked with Malawian women, how can you analyse their working environment in the process of democratic and social development?
The challenges that Malawian women face are not different from the challenges that South African women are facing. I grew up in a village, although it may be different from the villages in Malawi. I went to a rural university because the time when I went to university was the time of apartheid when there were special universities designated for blacks in South Africa. May be the conditions could have been even worse than the condition that students in Malawi are currently going through because it was not far from Pretoria.
Challenges are the same, working environments are the same, even the mindset. The only advantage is that in South Africa since 1994 the mindset in the working environment has changed exceedingly because women stood up and participated in changing their own environment. And in taking the stand, women were faced with many social challenges changing the mindset of men - to make them understand. So women in Malawi must take the challenge themselves to make men understand because there is no way in which the social development of women can take place without the men changing their mindset. Men have a very important role in helping women to develop and once women have developed Malawi is going to change.
Men and government must try to help the women in the rural areas to change their social environment; the social development depends on both men and women. Men and women are also custodians of our culture so it is imperative on the women not in trying to change the mindset of men to attack culture because culture cannot be changed by writing a law. It is an evolutionary change, people have to learn, have to understand and say indeed we need to change here. Culture does not remain stagnant, we believe that culture is alive, it is dynamic and changes with a change in environment and, as the world around us changes, culture also must change.
As Malawians are celebrating Mothers' day, what message can you share with Malawians in general and Malawian women in particular?
I would like to congratulate Malawi for having decided to have a special day for mothers.
