We know there is an international mothers' day which is something that we borrowed from the west but, for Malawi to have their own mothers' day and celebrate and commemorate it in a special way - I find it very positive. My message is that everyday is mother's day in the sense that everyday a mother gives birth. So to Malawians and to the women in particular, I would like to say that they must really find a relevant message for themselves to think and say 'I am a woman and a mother but I also have a mother'. What I want others to do for me as a mother I would like to see myself doing for my mother. I think everyone knows the difficulties that his or her mother went through to bring him or her up. And also to those who abuse mothers, every mother, does not have to be your own mother, how would one feel if such treatment is done to their mother. It can be a husband treating his own wife badly, how could he feel if his own mother was treated that way, if his own sister was treated that way.
Women, mostly girls, are being criticised for concealing births by way of committing abortions, dumping their new-borns or killing them. What can you comment on this?
The situation that you are raising is very sad and unfortunate in that it happens and it is a mother who dumps a child. It means this woman or young girl is not ready to be a mother. She is only a mother biologically, because being a mother means having maternal love. It means this young girl got a child when she was not ready. Maybe it also means that this young girl was rejected.
It is unfortunate that nature has made it that way that it must be a woman who carries a foetus. Where is the father of this child? Unfortunately, this question is rarely asked. What is it that has driven this girl to do that, what role did the community play when this girl was pregnant? Maybe she was scorned; she was looked at as immoral. You know, when a girl falls pregnant she is looked at as 'cheap'. People never go to ask where the boy or man is who made this girl pregnant? She was not alone, at times it is parents who will start to say; 'you have disappointed me my daughter, how could you fall pregnant?' But where are the parents of the boy or where is the man who made this girl pregnant?
It is unfortunate that this whole scenario is never examined critically. What are the things that have let her to be so heartless to deliver and dump that child in the field wrapped in banana leaves? When the police come, or traditional authorities come, do they ever go into looking at who made the girl pregnant? Did the girl tell her parents? Did the parents go to this boy's home? What role did the parents of the boy play in giving this girl support?
Let us assume both the boy and girl are not ready to be parents, what are the two families doing to give this girl support? We never look at those situations and you can imagine if it is your sister. If it is your daughter, maybe the boy raped her; maybe the girl was forced into that sexual act which led to her pregnancy; maybe both of them were exploring as children do, but the blame remains with the girl.
Unfortunately the blame always rests with the girl. All the nine months when she was pregnant probably she was hiding it. The pain of hiding that pregnancy; every night thinking about who was there to support her. These are things that I think communities should look into closely. And now when we say Mothers' Day do we mean women just giving birth or women giving birth with love with the expectation of being a mother?
What is your advice to such girls who are facing various hic-ups in the development of their lives and the single young mothers who are burdened by premature motherhood?
Girls must abstain from sex. But abstinence at times is talked of as if girls do not have feelings. It requires education and brings us back to the same fact that it takes two to tango. I think it must not be addressed only to girls but to boys as well. As parents we must teach our boys to respect girls as if they were their sisters. I think boys need to be educated early in life to know and understand that they are going to grow up and they are going to feel attracted to girls. They need to be taught that when that feeling comes they will have to control themselves.
If we are against early sex when it is taught at school it means we must teach it at home. We must tell the boys that when the time comes when you have sexual feelings it does not mean they must just get a girl and relive themselves. These are the things that, unfortunately we have to teach our boys and girls.
So, on one part it is the responsibility of the boys, our own sons to respect the girls. The feeling of getting attracted to girls is a normal feeling and a parent is always proud that his or her son has those feelings but they don't talk about it enough.
We are called to understand that gender equality is a common word to every nation, particularly in Africa. Do you think African women have the potential to succeed in their struggle for empowerment?
We all have a potential to achieve in gender equality provided we are given a chance. Achieving also depends on the mindset. I would encourage women in the rural areas to learn how to appreciate things so that they don't come out as negative. Somehow we think if a person is assertive and is doing things that are positive and are developmental that person becomes a man, you are no longer a woman.
It is just the mindset that has to change for Africans so that we can know that there is nothing wrong in people being equal; there is nothing wrong also in a man seemingly being weak. Unfortunately we expect men to be strong even though they are weak.
What can be your comparison to the rate of gender-based violence in Malawi and that of South Africa? Are the two countries making any strides? Are you satisfied with what is being done to uproot the problem and what do you think could be the way forward?
The only thing that I notice is that gender-based violence in Malawi is no different as compared to that of South Africa. Because people are now able to speak out and media now are writing about gender-based violence, it might look like it has increased. Yes it may have, but my feeling is that people are talking more about it now than they used to in the past.
The concealment around gender-based violence is not only peculiar to Africa. In Europe and America there has always been gender-based violence. So it is prevalent all over the world. It might be less in other developing countries but in South Africa and Malawi we hear more about it now because women have learned to talk about it and papers have learned to write about it. It looks like the nature of violence now has changed because maybe men now feel threatened by this equality. And they are trying to frustrate women because they don't understand what equality means and they want to prove their power by inflicting pain on women.
Like renowned Opra Winfrey, she was abused by her own uncle and she has written about it. So you imagine how many other women in developed countries went through that. However, definitely something must be done to discourage it. and I think it goes back to what I said about how we bring up our children.
In South Africa, it seems, most political challenges have been defeated without prejudice. Can you share with Malawians how South Africa has managed to survive its challenges?
Yes we have made progress but what has helped us I think is the reconciliation stand the ruling party adopted in regard to political challenges. The issue of saying we cannot change the past; talking about issues, not covering them up for people to come out to say yes, I was part of the wrong that happened in the past and am very sorry about it. This is necessary.
Although Malawi looks like one country on the continent which never had problems in the past, it had its own problems. The fact that Malawians did not fight, they did not have a civil war, does not mean that they didn't have problems. And I think Malawians are harbouring the pain but don't want to talk about it. Unfortunately the anger comes in different ways, which often can delay progress.
Until such a time when Malawians can openly deal with their history, accept and acknowledge that; yes we never had a civil war but we suffered and we can deal with the pain and move forward despite the differences. So I think what helped South Africa is openly saying we can still leave the bad things behind.
The call for women participation in politics and decision making is a human right whose legitimacy is sanctioned by the existence of various legal provisions at the national and international level. Why do you think women should still fail to achieve their set goals in their empowerment?
There is a difference between putting a law and implementing that law. Things don't change just because there is law but because people know of its existence. So the fact that the constitution of Malawi, like that of South Africa talks of the ideal of equality does not necessarily mean that there will automatically be equality. It means the constitution has put a law and government has to make sure that people understand why it has been put there and what has to be done to make sure that it serves its purpose.
So there is a process to that and it does not only mean talking in the newspapers but going into the rural areas discussing with people and chiefs so that there is understanding of what equality means. And if the traditional authorities do not understand what it means the men who are around the chief will understand and can discuss it in the villages. So gradually it means there will be implementation.
This means there is a lot of work to be done. If we can accept that men and women are equal in the sense that there are times when a woman can earn more than a man because she is more qualified; that the man and the woman must accept that she should not look down on her husband because she earns more. Unfortunately most people understand gender equality as bringing men down but actually it is making things more balanced so that if I have a brother who is less successful than I am, I will still respect him. It just means there is a need for a change in mindset but unfortunately it is not understood.
Is there any word you would like to share with Malawians on democratic and social development?
Democratic and social development are words that are being sung everywhere. Democracy also has to be looked at in terms of the conditions in a particular country. As Africans, my own view is that we are too idealistic, we want to be perfect. Yes the basic concept is there but democracy differs from one country to another because it has to be featured into the conditions and cultures of a particular country.
Shortcomings should not interfere with the progress of the nation. People don't criticise the governments' activities properly. Yes criticism is good but it has also to be patriotic. We don't have to compare our democracies with countries like America whose democracy is over 300 years old. South Africa and Malawi's democracies are just 12 years old but we want to be like America. The government should ask itself if it is leading or confusing its people in democracy.
Another thing is the issue of development. What is it that we talk about development? Is it changing our culture or improving on our culture? Also we need to learn to use our local resources better. For instance if people cannot afford the cost of iron sheets, why can't we improve our local methods of roofing?
The problem is that we are not able to adapt development to our own conditions.

Original_Search_Title: Editors Slammed for Running to Cabinet
Headline: South Africa: Editors Slammed for Running to Cabinet
Body_Text:
Cape Town — The chairman of Parliament's home affairs committee, Patrick Chauke, has slammed media editors for in effect passing a motion of no confidence in the parliamentary process by engaging the cabinet on matters before Parliament.
At issue was a top-level meeting last week between the South African National Editors Forum (Sanef) and Minister in the Presidency Essop Pahad over the Film and Publications Amendment Bill, which many fear will muzzle the press if enacted in its present form.
After the meeting, the cabinet said the bill, which could impose self-censorship on the daily news media, would be processed only next year once the concerns had been addressed.
When the committee met yesterday for hearings on the Civil Unions Bill, Chauke began by saying that it was unacceptable that while Parliament was often described in the media as weak, lacking teeth and a rubber stamp for the executive, his committee was bypassed by Sanef.
He warned the editors that whatever consultations they had had they would still have to appear before the committee in order to make their presentations.
"When we are supposed to do our work as the legislature they engage with cabinet and ignore the committee," Chauke said, noting that he sensed Parliament was not trusted to do what was right.
He said Parliament was not a rubber stamp for the cabinet and "when matters are before Parliament they should deal with Parliament". Parliament took all public submissions into account when dealing with legislation.
The committee was supposed to hear submissions on the Film and Publications Amendment Bill at the same time as it heard opinion on the Civil Unions Bill. Chauke said the committee would put the bill aside as indicated by the cabinet but remarked: "There is something wrong here; we are not a banana republic."
He said that the committee would defend press freedom and would not accept legislation that interfered with this constitutionally protected right.
The Star editor Moegsien Williams appeared before the committee yesterday with regard to an advertisement that offered sexual services of a 12-year-old.
Last week, Chauke waved the advertisement before the committee, saying that it was precisely to stop such things that the bill was framed. Government has insisted that the primary focus of the bill is to curtail child pornography and the sexual exploitation of women and children, and not to muzzle the press.
Williams, in response to a question from ANC MP Francois Beukman as to whether it would have been better to engage the committee rather than the cabinet, said that while he was no longer an office bearer in Sanef, "it is very important for us as a society to recognise and uphold the importance of Parliament and I am sure that there was no intention on the part of Sanef to undermine the importance of this committee".
ANC MPs railed against the publication of the advertisement and Chauke said that it was a very serious matter. Williams said that when the advertisement was taken down, the age was transposed from 21 to 12 and the error was not picked up before publication. He said it had been corrected in a subsequent issue of the paper and that disciplinary action had been taken against the individual who had made the mistake.

Original_Search_Title: UNMIL Contains Violence in G/Gedeh
Headline: Liberia: UNMIL Contains Violence in G/Gedeh
Body_Text:
There are reports that forces of the United Nations Mission in Liberia (UNMIL) have contained a violent incident which occurred in the evening of Friday, 13 October 2006, in Zwedru, Grand Gedeh County. UNMIL, in a release, explained that the incident occurred when a mob attacked the local headquarters of the Liberia National Police (LNP) following the discovery of a body of a 25-year-old man, Jerome Karyoe, commonly known "pen-pen" or "Motorcycle Taxi in Zwedru. During the commotion, stone-throwing individuals injured a number of people, including two Ethiopian UNMIL military personnel and two of their vehicles damaged, as well as a number of shops belonging to local residents looted and others damaged.
Independent sources told this paper that no one was hurt or killed during the fracas and that the cause of Karyoe's death has not been established. However, UNMIL said the body of the late Jerome Karyoe was found between the towns of Diahn and Banana Village on the Zwedru-Monrovia highway Wednesday, 11 October 2006. Subsequently, the LNP, with the assistance of the UN Police, launched an immediate investigation and two suspects were arrested and detained by the Ivorian authorities in Zouhoujah Town in neighboring Cote d'Ivoire.
The investigation is ongoing and the suspects were never brought to the LNP office in Zwedru according to the release. UN Police, together with the Ethiopian UNMIL military police and the LNP in Zwedru, succeeded in restoring order Friday evening and presently the town is said to be calm.
However, UNMIL says its military personnel have intensified patrolling activities in the town and nearby areas and remained vigilant to deal firmly with any public disorder and violence. According to UNMIL, the LNP last Saturday afternoon arrested and detained 26 individuals believed to be ringleaders of Friday mob attacks in Zwedru. UNMIL is strongly urging members of the public in Zwedru not to take the law into their own hands and wishes to encourage the public to work closely with the LNP and UNMIL so that cases and incidents of mob justice could be avoided.

Original_Search_Title: Bluntly Seaking - Is Ghana in Distress?
Headline: Ghana: Bluntly Seaking - Is Ghana in Distress?
Body_Text:
I ADMIT that I had not intended to write a sequel to my last week's article that bore the same title as this week's.
However, there are a few more things that should be mentioned because they create the impression, rightly or wrongly that our country is, or could be, in distress.
When the new Vice-Chancellor of the University of Ghana, Legon, Professor Clifford Nii Boye Tagoe, conducted the Matriculation ceremony for new students of the university, he told the heart-rending story of how more than half of qualified applicants could not be admitted this academic year.
The reasons were familiar: lack of space, lack of personnel, particularly academic staff, and lack of other teaching and learning facilities. Where do the eight thousand or so "unlucky" applicants go?
Of course, as has been happening for sometime now, Legon is not the only State-sponsored University in distress. The University of Cape Coast, the University of Development Studies, Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology, the University of Mines and the University College of Education have also had to lament their inability to admit all the applicants qualifying for admission.
There is No guarantee that, having failed to obtain admission in a particular year, these "unlucky" souls could be admitted in subsequent years. Where do they go?
It is possible that a few of them might have parents rich enough to send them abroad or even pay fees locally. Some might also decide to "try their luck" at some of the private university that have sprung up in the past few years. Unfortunately, there are constraints for those opting to go to a private university locally.
The first is the higher fees charged, as compared to what students in State-sponsored universities have to pay.
Secondly, almost to a university, these institutions concentrate virtually on business and computer courses. The mission-established ones are also known for programmes in Religion and Theology, with obvious emphasis on the Christian aspects of the programme.
It is only recently that the Presbyterian Church has made known its plans to establish a medical school.
What all that means is that, for a number of programmes of study, there is no alternative to the State-sponsored Universities, for example, Medicine, Pharmacy, law, Engineering, Accountancy, Art Architecture, Building Technology, even the Pure Sciences and Mathematics, etc.
There is a further problem for applicants who want to attend a State-sponsored University. There may be six State Universities. But, for a number of programmes, there may be only one university to which the applicant can apply. I can think of Pharmacy, Architecture, Art and such engineering programmes as Electrical, Mechanical, Geodetic and Chemical Engineering.
If you cannot gain admission to Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology to study Pharmacy, for example, there is nowhere else to go for that programme within the country.
There are three medical schools, but everybody knows that the one at the University of Development Studies is currently so ailing that students have to continue the course at either KNUST, Kumasi or Korle Bu. The same thing applies to Mining.
It means that for a number of courses or programmes, we have only one State University, not six.
Is it any wonder that there are always so many qualified but unsuccessful applicants? Is it any wonder that there are desperate applicants who resort to criminal means to gain admission, especially when, to us, the ultimate is a university education and qualification?
If I have dwelt at some length on University education, it is because, as the late President John Kennedy of the United States reportedly said, 'Universities are the midwives of progress.'
But if universities are the midwives, the pregnancy must take place first. That means that the basic level and the second-cycle level must do a proper job of producing the pregnancies.
One has to admit that, at the moment, what takes place generally at the basic level in State schools cannot be the best.
In the first place, officialdom says that about 17,000 (Seventeen thousand) teachers are needed at the basic level. Our teacher training colleges simply cannot supply that number, even when all their final products for a particular year are put together. Meanwhile, some already in the system retire voluntarily or compulsorily, leave for more lucrative jobs or simply die.
Secondly, the same officialdom and other concerned citizens keep complaining about the very poor or virtually non-existent supervision that leads to laziness, absenteeism, unpunctuality and general malingering on the part of teachers.
Thirdly, while it has become the proud boast of Government that enrolment at the basic school level has improved considerably because of the Capitation Grant and the feeding programme, the adverse side is overcrowding in classrooms that makes teaching ineffective.
Fourthly, apart from the overcrowding, teaching and learning materials are hopelessly inadequate, particularly in rural schools.
One consequence, as bemoaned once again by officialdom and others is the heart-breaking inability on the part of the basic level pupils to read a text in English or a Local language. In some cases, they cannot even write their own names.
If so much is spent on education and there is little to show for it at all levels up to university what are we to do?
I have dwelt at some length on education because I was once a teacher and educational administrator. It is also because, for lack of knowledge, the people perish, it is said. But there are a few other matters.
Whatever happened to efficiency and business ethics? Resigha Company in Holland opens a bag of cocoa beans and discovers such "impurities" as nails, unserviceable wristwatches, hammers and other things that could foul up their machinery.
A dangerous chemical like Sudan 4 is added to palm oil to produce the right colour.
It is, of course rejected. But purple beans are found in our cocoa, our major money earner. Are we or are we not exporting so-called "blood diamonds"?
They say Europe is rejecting our timber products on grounds of poor quality. The adulteration of food meant for human beings is the order of the day, not to talk of the kind of water used to grow such "European" vegetables as carrots, cabbages, spring onions, spinach, etc.
It is said that carbide is used to make tomatoes and bananas ripen faster though that is not healthy for us.
The concrete block maker steals your cement, the painter steals your paint, and your auto mechanic pretends to buy a new part for your vehicle and steals your money. You can add to the list. Whither Ghana?

Original_Search_Title: Ministers Owe Firm Sh3.6 Million Over Chopper
Headline: Kenya: Ministers Owe Firm Sh3.6 Million Over Chopper
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Four ministers are among government officials who hired a Kenya Pipeline Company helicopter on various occasions during last year's constitution referendum campaigns, but have not paid the State corporation about Sh3.6 million.
A report by two human rights watchdogs to be released today, says the ministers hired the aircraft for private use on various dates, but had not paid for it by March 8.
But Pipeline managing director George Okungu said some had since paid up, while others had given commitments. He did not, however, say who had paid.
List of defaulters
Justice minister Martha Karua tops the list of defaulters with a Sh1 million debt, followed by her Foreign Affairs counterpart, Mr Raphael Tuju, who owes the firm Sh971,355.
The others are Mr Musikari Kombo of Local Government with Sh778,200, Finance's Mr Amos Kimunya Sh605,462 and assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri Sh242,000.
Mr Maina Kiai, the chairman of the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights (KNCHR), said yesterday that the amounts were outstanding as at March when his team collected data on the company.
The report, compiled by the KNCHR and the Kenya Human Rights Commission, recommends that the ministers be compelled to repay the debts or be surcharged. "The Government is under obligation to ensure that public property is not misappropriated and that those who hire the aircraft pay for it at the authorised rates," the watchdogs say.
The report also recommends that an audit of the University of Nairobi accounts be carried out to determine if payment was received for the hiring of the former Kenya School of Law for political party activity.
The premises, on Valley Road, Nairobi, were the official secretariat for the Banana group during the referendum.
The Orange camp was based at a private building in Upper Hill, also in the city. The report says that the university had a lease agreement for the building, although it did not show evidence of payment.
"The commission has not been successful in verifying whether any payments were made or whether this transaction was proper and lawful," it says.
It adds: "A letter from the secretariat asking for confirmation of these (hire contract) particulars has gone unanswered to date."
The report names several other leaders whom it asks to apologise for using language against women that it describes as not sensitive.
They include Orange Democratic Movement heavyweights Joseph Kamotho and William Ruto as well as Sports minister Maina Kamanda.

Original_Search_Title: War On New And Old Graft Only Path to New Kenya
Headline: Kenya: War On New And Old Graft Only Path to New Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans are tired of what is emerging as a losing battle in the war on corruption. New political outfits have emerged, we have a Government that has abandoned the party that brought it to power and supports a new one!
We also have a strong opposition, ODM-Kenya, which did not involve members of Kanu and the Liberal Democratic Party members in the merger decision.
It is crucial that Kenyans remain focused and keep leaders on both sides of the political divide on their toes. It pains me to see laxity in investigation and prosecution of corruption suspects.
Current and former Cabinet ministers and MPs are walking free, tall and arrogant and confusing Kenyans on comparative corruption, witch-hunt and mudslinging, shamelessly clad in the naked robes of guilt. Should the waheshimiwa claiming innocence over corruption be taken seriously if public money was lost under their watch? They cry foul and offer ridiculous excuses - that they were in the previous Government and ministers were not allowed to sign contracts!
Others have no shame as they proclaim that investigations are tantamount to a witch-hunt, never mind that there is all likelihood that they dipped their hands in the cookie jar. Without doubt, there is an element of witch-hunt to crush the Opposition, but investigation of corruption must be done.
Guilt is guilt irrespective of what side of the political divide one is, no matter how old or new the crime and no matter how powerful the position you held or hold now. In the hue and cry, the only valid question is: How innocent are those investigating the suspects?
How can we ensure free and fair and investigation and prosecution against those implicated and expose those not yet implicated? Sorry Madam Martha Karua, we will not accept amnesty. It is not the way out of the corruption quagmire.
Anyone caught with their hands in the cookie jar should step aside, whether in Government or the Opposition, to allow investigations. Corruption is corruption regardless of the political spectrum it manifests itself.
It is especially important that Opposition leaders be above reproach, for how else do we point fingers at the Government when we carry muck in equal measure. Otherwise, the people will lose confidence in it.
The wave that brought Narc to power, promises it made and its failures have created a new breed of wananchi. The wave did not create a democratic Kenya, but a historical evolution of a population conscious of the truth and falsehood of politicians.
It is unlikely for another wave to work. The people will neither vote out of euphoria nor a popular party be it oranges, bananas, rainbows or flowers. Instead, they will scrutinise individuals seeking office and what they portend for the nation.
We must learn from history. Moses did not lead his people to the Promised Land, but they went there anyway. It is the people who count not individual interests.
Let us watch as politics unfolds: Act One came with the last General Election, Act Two the referendum, Act three is the corruption game of hide-and-seek and the final one will be the next election.

Original_Search_Title: Obote's Many Masks Unveiled
Headline: Uganda: Obote's Many Masks Unveiled
Body_Text:
Kampala — Title: Apollo Milton Obote, What Others Say
Editor: Omongole R. Anguria
Publisher: Fountain
Price: sh10,000
Available: Leading bookstores
UGANDA would be different if Obote had been more decisive and jailed Idi Amin, been smart and not given Museveni a job, been less power hungry and not attacked the Buganda king's palace, or less tribal and not appointed Brig. Apon Acak to head the army, or suspicious enough to know that Paul Muwanga would betray him. That is what some writers wish in a new book, "Apollo Milton Obote, What Others Say".
But to others, Obote's feats and foibles, his legendary pan-Africanism and his tribal-centric myopia, were babies of his times. They point to other African countries to paint a pattern and a mixed legacy for Uganda's founding president.
The publishers were looking for someone to write a conclusive book about the man who ruled Uganda twice to mark the first anniversary of his death. Instead, they hit on an ingenious editor, Omongole R. Anguria, who compiled newspaper articles published around the time he died last year.
The compilation reveals Obote to be many things to different people. The articles by over 45 writers are grouped according to their impressions of him in four parts; those who saw him as a hero, those who saw him as a villain, and those to whom he was a victim, and finally those to whom he was human; a mixed bag. No single author could have produced this exciting mix.
The eclectic ensemble also has ensured that fact, fiction, and plain rumour about Obote are now in book form for posterity to judge.
Did he really ever say that "a good Muganda is a dead one"? Did former president, Godfrey Binaisa really advise Obote to kick out Mutesa? Or did the then British Prime Minister, Edward Heath warn him at the Commonwealth Summit in Singapore that, "some of those who have spoken most might not be able to return home"? Why did Obote hire Museveni, a man who even in 1970 looked eager to be president?
This book raises these questions and more from people who claim they were either there when they happened, heard them from credible authority, or are merely opining. In a way, the book is a challenge for those who know Obote and his times to write objective books.
Take the case of lawyer Peter Mulira's article, "Obote did not fight for Uhuru", something many consider indisputable. Not so, says Mulira. According to him, Obote opposed the fight for independence and even wrote in a newspaper that the clamour for it was a Buganda thing!
Mulira also claims that, according to the Lukiiko standing orders, the Buganda kingdom Lukiiko in 1966 did not pass any motion ordering Obote's government off Buganda land. Many claim this motion precipitated the 1966 crisis, which marked the first coup d'etat in Uganda, and sent the Kabaka scampering into exile in London.
But Andrew Mwenda, a journalist who met Obote in his later years in exile, also has an article titles, "Buganda is equally to Blame for the 1966/67 Political Crisis" in the book. In it, Mwenda quotes the Lukiiko record for May 20, 1966, and concludes that the motion was passed despite advice from the then Katikkiro, Mayanja Nkangi. Mwenda is a distinguished journalist but Mulira is also an honourable gentleman; so what is the truth?
Obote's widow Miria explains why Obote fought the Buganda monarch in 1966 but the issues surrounding the crisis are so central to Uganda's political history that any contradictions need to be resolved by independent scholarship which is lacking in this collection as the writers scribbled emotion, perception, and bits of knowledge.
Not surprisingly, the most interesting articles are the more personal ones by people who either worked for or were close to Obote. Kenyan academic and politician, Prof. P. Anyang' Nyong'o, who was the student guild president at Makerere University in 1970, describes Obote in terms that are difficult to believe today.
Only Onyango-Obbo's story, about how Miria, First Lady then, used to cruise around the city in a posh Mercedes delivering gifts of endagala (banana leaves) to her friends, competes with Anyang's.

Original_Search_Title: It's Either Pact Or Pack Up for Parties
Headline: Kenya: It's Either Pact Or Pack Up for Parties
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Just as in 2002, a new trend is emerging. Although a pre-election coalition is still in fashion, individual membership has been adopted by Orange Democratic Movement-Kenya in addition to corporate membership to avoid the pitfalls that befell Narc.
Earlier on, Narc-Kenya was registered, after most MPs aligned to the DP faction failed to convince affiliate parties of Narc to allow the registration of individual members.
Narc-Kenya was formed on the presumption that other parliamentary parties would "go alone" in the General Election due next year. In such a situation, the party would have a headstart against a polarised opposition in the same way Kanu did in 1992 and 1997. This is in fact what happened during the by-elections in which Narc-Kenya won three out of five parliamentary seats. But with the formation of ODM-Kenya, Narc-Kenya does not have a headstart anymore. It is ODM-Kenya, if it holds together, that now has leverage.
The registration of Narc-Kenya was meant to capture the spirit of Narc's win in 2002 by psychologically retaining the name that rooted out Kanu overwhelmingly. On the other hand, the registration of ODM-Kenya is meant to psychologically rekindle the referendum victory. While the Orange group and followers so far are intact, with the exception of a few MPs who were co-opted to the Government of National Unity (GNU), Narc-Kenya is now distinct from its partners during the Banana campaign. Without its partners - Ford-Kenya, Ford-People and NPK- Narc-Kenya would attract less votes than the Banana group attracted during the referendum. This is a statistical reality that Narc-Kenya has to deal with. Another reality is that although the party has poached MPs from Kanu, LDP, Ford-Kenya and NPK, MPs' allegiance and the people's loyalty do not always correlate.
In the countdown to the referendum, the Banana camp won the battle in Parliament by passing the so-called Wako draft but lost overwhelmingly when the same document went to the people. It must thus be clear that even as Narc-Kenya uses "incumbency" and control of Government to attract more MPs to its side under the GNU, that is not the same as getting the support of the people that these MPs represent outside its central Kenya stronghold, as the referendum proved.
In any case some parties in the GNU, especially Ford-Kenya and NPK, have clearly stated that they will be on their own or form a "third force" for elections.
The registration of ODM-Kenya thus means that Narc-Kenya has to go back to the drawing board. Their single-party solo agenda has to change if the party is to make any impact. The party appears to have come to this realisation, as the very day that ODM-Kenya was being registered, the Government of National Unity was on a retreat in Naivasha to discuss how it will work in unity when Parliament reopens. But a new agenda came up, that of coalitions. At the end of the meeting, the VP stressed that the GNU will continue to work together but each party would retain its identity. The statement was a pointer to what is likely to unfold in the political arena in the days ahead and that is what can be called "electoral pacts."
Electoral pacts occur in an environment in which political parties zealously guard their identity, yet realise that individually a dominant opponent will trounce them. They do not merge or form a pre-election coalition (party) distinct from their parties. Instead, they go into the elections as distinct political parties but with one presidential candidate from the stronger/incumbent party, but with a running mate from the second strongest party. They agree on how they will distribute parliamentary and civic seats. Another characteristic of electoral pacts is the recognition of strongholds of the "pacting parties" in which the party dominant in a particular area gets a free hand to nominate parliamentary and civic candidates. This is replicated for each party in their respective strongholds.
In the areas in which none of the parties is peculiarly strong, joint nominations are held and the winning candidates are supported. This is the route Narc-Kenya is likely to take. ODM-Kenya will not take this route as it has provisions for both individual and corporate membership. The parties that Narc-Kenya will be looking out for a pact will be Ford-Kenya, Ford-People, and NPK.
During the referendum, the Banana calculation for a win was based on bagging central Kenya votes. In addition, Ford-Kenya was to deliver Western, Ford-People, Kisii, and NPK a section of Kambaland. Nairobi, Coast and North Eastern were considered swing votes that Banana and Orange would split. Luo-Nyanza and some sections of the Rift Valley were considered hostile the latter especially after Moi supported Orange. But when the results were finally tallied, Ford-Kenya failed in Western Province while Ford-People failed in Kisii. Ngilu's NPK was drowned by the "Kalonzo wave" in Eastern Province. While Nairobi was split, Coast and North Eastern overwhelmingly voted Orange. In the end, for the Banana camp, it was only the DP faction that delivered central Kenya votes. This is the reality that Narc-Kenya has to deal with and the aborted meeting in Afya House should be seen in that light.
Narc-Kenya is currently able to 'govern' comfortably only with the assistance of Ford-Kenya, Ford-People, NPK and rebel Kanu MPs. Without this support it would not have the numbers in Parliament to push through its agenda. The same applies to the elections. Without a partnership with these parties, the party will find it difficult to beat ODM-Kenya if it stays intact. Since Narc-Kenya is a party and not a coalition, it can only enter into electoral pacts with other parties unless it changes its constitution or unless its joins the original Narc as a corporate member. Its first route however will be electoral pacts.
One of the parties Narc-Kenya will be reaching out to is Ngilu's NPK. Although it won't be desperate for all her NPK lieutenants - Mr Mutua Katuku, Mr Kalembe Ndile, Ms Cecil Mbarire, Ms Adelina Mwau, Mr Kyalo Kaindi and Prof Kivutha Kibwana - who are now aligned to Narc-Kenya, have abandoned her.
Ngilu also failed to deliver even her own constituency to the Banana team during the referendum. Her value to Narc-Kenya is, therefore, minimal. However, in the event that a pact formula fails to yield and Narc-Kenya resorts to a coalition (this is very unlikely), Ngilu would come in handy in securing the Narc party umbrella that would make Narc-Kenya the senior corporate partner. Conflicting statements that she will not be joining Narc-Kenya and later that she will be supporting Kibaki should be seen in this light
Ford-People is the second party that Narc-Kenya will be targeting for an electoral pact. Since being incorporated into the GNU, FORD-People has ceased to be an opposition party. All indications are that Mr Simeon Nyachae will be retiring from politics. This does not portend well for the future of Ford-People, as he is its chief financier and leader.
The pact between it and Narc-Kenya might take one of the following two routes: In the first one Ford-People gets dissolved and merges with Narc-Kenya. In the second, the party retains its identity and pacts with Narc-Kenya and reserves the right to filed candidates in its stronghold. Given that Nyachae's hold on the Kisiiland has been on the wane - he lost in his constituency during the referendum - and the fact that he could be heading to retirement, Narc-Kenya will have a huge battle with ODM-Kenya in this region in spite of a pact or merger.
The last and most important party Narc-Kenya is gong to reach out for a pact is Ford-Kenya. Although the MPs aligned to it have dwindled, Ford-Kenya still provides the numbers that tilts the votes in favour of the GNU against a combined opposition in Parliament. Although Narc-Kenya stabbed Ford-Kenya in the back when it was registered, the presence of ODM-Kenya will now force Narc-Kenya to go back to the negotiating table with Ford-Kenya. Despite Vice-President Mr Moody Awori and Cabinet ministers Dr Mukhisa Kituyi and Dr Newton Kulundu being firmly in Narc-Kenya, they are unlikely to penetrate western Kenya, which will be a battle zone between ODM-Kenya and Ford-Kenya.
During the referendum, the VP's own constituency voted Orange as they did too in Kulundu's constituency. Kituyi then still in Ford-Kenya delivered, but not due to his personal clout. Bukusuland is the bedrock of Ford-Kenya.
Ford-Kenya is unlikely to enter into a pact with Narc-Kenya that gives them anything short of a vice presidency. The latter will be reluctant on this quest given that they are not sure whether Ford-Kenya can deliver the rest of Luhyaland outside the Bukusu sub-tribe.
The running mate should ideally deliver, as a bare a minimum, their province or community vote. Ford-Kenya does not have any tangible evidence to show that next year will be different from the referendum except through the Luhya unity talks. Another factor that will come to bearing is the fact that the Luhya currently share the highest number of Cabinet ministers in the GNU - together with the Kikuyu - in addition to the Vice-Presidency.
For the Kikuyu, apart from the President, the community's Cabinet ministers are Mr Njenga Karume, Mr John Mchuki, Ms Martha Karua, Mr Amos Kimunya, Mr Mutahi Kagwe and Mr Maina Kamanda. The Luhya Cabinet ministers are Mr Musikari Kombo, Kituyi, Kulundu, Mr Moses Akaranga, Dr Noah Wekesa, Mr Soita Shitanda and Awori. It is hard to see how the Luhya can get better representation in a future government other than going for the presidency itself. But the irony is that despite the VP's position and six Cabinet positions to boot, the grassroots community members are yet to embrace the Government. A huge section of Luhyas do not appear to have been swayed by the heavy presence of their leaders in the GNU. Narc-Kenya stands very little chance of penetrating western Kenya. It has to go through Ford-Kenya but at the same time it is not sure that the VP post under the party can deliver Western Province. Ford-Kenya doesn't appear interested in a pact. Instead, it is talking of taking the lead in the crafting of a "third force" to counter Narc-Kenya and ODM-Kenya.
The survival of the GNU against an ODM-Kenya onslaught demands that Ford-Kenya stays on board since the party provides the 'swing vote' in Parliament. This is why every time Ford-Kenya rebels, the GNU has been forced to give in. The first time was immediately after the referendum when the party 'forced' President Kibaki to give them more Cabinet seats. The second time was when the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) report on Anglo-leasing was tabled. The party made it clear that there would be no collective responsibility on corruption. Narc-Kenya became immediately aware that without the Ford-Kenya numbers the Opposition would triumph. It, therefore, accepted the report despite its indictment on the Government. This is the muscle that Ford-Kenya has.
The original Narc should not just be ruled out yet. It could turn out to be the 'third force' or even be the umbrella party of Narc-Kenya. If electoral pacts prove to have too many teething problems, Narc could still provide a fallback position for NPK, Ford-Kenya, Ford-People and even Narc-Kenya. This was the initial position taken by Kombo before he abandoned it after the by-elections.

Original_Search_Title: What the Governors Should Do
Headline: Nigeria: What the Governors Should Do
Body_Text:
Unless the nation wants to tell itself a lie, the manifest truth is that most governors are unarguably corrupt. They have fraudulently enriched themselves beyond imagination and, although there is fear in the land and most Nigerians cannot openly talked about it, especially in the state capitals where such utterances could earn you the assassins visit, the facts remain that the average Nigerian now hold these men in contempt, cursing them at night and praising them by day.
Lamenting to CP recently a seasoned politician of the second republic fame wondered aloud how these set of governor got away with such looting. He recalled how he returned the "change "from a fund he was given for an assignment. He said he did the job and realized there was some money left "so, I returned it to the treasury of the party," he said.
But that seems like light years away. The governors became a metaphor for crass primitive accumulation. In most of the state governors who managed to tar the road and build hospitals are seen as heroes. Never mind that they used the state's money to accomplished the projects. They even go on air to announce their "achievements". But you can't blame them in a land where non performance has become first nature.
We watched as they become small gods and strut the landscape with the swagger of men of opulence. Today, they want to entrenched themselves by playing the godfather's role, bent on anointing their successors before step aside. This could mean only one thing: the beginning of the governors' dynasty- a situation which will resemble what obtains in some banana republics in Africa. For the anointed could be worse than his "oga".
What the governors should be doing now is simply penance for all the atrocities they've unleashed. They should engage in heavy duty confessions and apologize for all these years of the locust. By doing this, they may find forgiveness and some peace of mind after 2007. Their robust sons and daughters may not live with the blight and reproach of the memories of a pilfering father. Any one of these governors, who truly repents and asks for pardon, or even resigns, will earn my respect and admiration for life. I swear.

Original_Search_Title: Explore the Windhoek Show's Magic
Headline: Namibia: Explore the Windhoek Show's Magic
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The Windhoek Show opened its gates to the public last Friday and, as expected, has many stalls displaying a wide array of local and foreign-produced goods.
For many visitors, the show offers a myriad of choices to buy or even just to window-shop for something that they desire.
When the New Era team visited the showgrounds yesterday, people were drawn to the various exhibiting stalls, one of them selling the "Afro-Chic" products.
Situated in one of the main halls, the Cane Craft Lighting business from South Africa offered uniquely-designed lamp stands at reasonable prices.
Looking around, one notices that there's a great pull towards naturally made goods. "They are ceiling lamps, wall lamps, table light ranges, floor lamps and up-lighters," said the Head of Marketing, Erick Eckhard.
Most of these products are made from seagrass, banana reeds and brightly-coloured beadwork, giving them that serene, natural look. "It's called Afro-Chic" said Eckhard showing off one of the lamps he was holding in his hands.
It turns out that the ethnic African type of field products or merchandise is very much sought after in Europe. Many African countries go back to their roots by making these natural products. This likeable trend is unlike in the past when there was no cultural feel or attraction for these goods.
Lately, there has been a massive export to the international world of African-made furniture, spelling a unique liking for nature's goods from the mother continent.
Explaining more about the business, Eckhard said although it's a South African self-help scheme for empowering the disadvantaged, plans are underway to sell this range in Namibia.
Through arts and crafts of this nature, unemployed people learn how to weld, weave and eventually become trained in starting up their own businesses. "It's five to six days of intense work and prices range from N$300 to N$2500 each," he added.
Walking into the Health and Beauty Hall, one is engulfed by the pleasant aroma-therapeutic goods.
At the stand of Shadonai Beauty School, (an internationally accredited school) are three young girls waiting to show you more about their work.
What's unique about this business is that it's the first of its kind in Namibia where young people can learn how to give beautifying facials, massages, waxing and other therapeutic services to clients.
First-year students Isabel Bacalhau, Natascha Reichstein and Marie Pieterse are all very excited about venturing into this career, knowing that they even study the very same course at home.
Currently, there are five full-time students, 37 part-time, and 24 new students are expected to enrol next year. On the other hand, there are those organizations in the country that focus mainly on the disadvantaged sectors of the community, like the single mothers, the street kids and the homeless.
One such organization, run by the Ministry of Gender Equality and Child Welfare, is the After-School Centre in Windhoek.
Catering for between 200 and 250 street children and jobless mothers, the centre is geared towards offering counselling services, physco-social support for orphans and vulnerable children as well as sports and recreation.
"We also offer income-generating projects to help unemployed single mothers," explained Social Worker, Rachel Freeman, who was at the stand.
"The money that is generated goes back to these mothers to buy food, clothing and other items for themselves and their children," she added.
The centre, which was established way back in 1994, lives up to its mission's ideal of lifting the vulnerable out of poverty into self-reliance and social reintegration.

Original_Search_Title: Cranes in Danger?
Headline: Uganda: Cranes in Danger?
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT'S a strange country we live in. FUFA recently paid sh48m upfront in annual rent for Cranes' coach Laszlo Csaba's lavish residence in Bugolobi, and they are forking out sh3m for his daughter's education at Kabira International School.
That, of course, is on top of paying the German a monthly salary of over sh10m. Wait until the MPs hear this!
Yet, according to FUFA president Lawrence Mulindwa, Cranes are in danger of not travelling for the African Nations Cup qualifier in Niger due to a lack of funds. Wouldn't it be so typically Ugandan to have money to fund a coach's luxury and none to transport him and his charges to their station of duty?
Not too long ago, I remember reading a headline saying: 'Mulindwa to bankroll Cranes campaign'.
Then, more recently, there was a story in the papers in which National Council of Sports general secretary Jasper Aligawesa assured all who cared to listen that funds to transport Cranes will be available. It was at Regency Hotel, where FUFA was congratulating a clearly over-age Under-20 team for bringing home the top CECAFA honours.
"I assure you that Cranes will travel to Niger," Aligawesa said with all the usual pomp.
Now, the time for words is up, and everyone must back up their promises with banknotes. Then the drama!
That is also typically Ugandan.
Sometime ago, when golfer Stephen Birungi was scratching for a sponsor, a gentleman stepped out of the crowd offering to write off the issue. His sponsorship offer wasn't just good, it was breathtaking. He didn't stop there. He expounded on how Birungi, whom he liberally compared to Tiger Woods, is such a phenomenal golfer who can proceed to conquer the world.
Asked what he does for a living, he said: "I am an international businessman, I am always flying New York, Washington, London ." That came with an accent to match.
Pens were fully engaged, and cameras were clicking as the horde of journalists in attendance were visibly impressed.
Cue the headlines the next day.
Then reality!
Birungi benched the dude for days, then weeks, before finally conceding that it was a hoax.
The reasoning is easy. Don't let reality stand in the way of a good promise. If the occasion is ideal, stand up and steal the show, grab the headlines, and soak up all the praise. If you cannot make good, it will just prove that you're Ugandan. But they probably already knew that. So, no sweat.
So when Mulindwa promised to "bankroll" Cranes, he sure enjoyed the next day's headlines. Now the reality!
That's where the government comes in. Similarly, Aligawesa, being true to his national identity, drew a cheer promising to fund the Niger trip. Now reality bites.
Of course Cranes will travel to Niger, but the run-up to the trip had to follow a script we've come to expect in this Banana Republic.
And the match
Down to the match; Cranes are firmly in line to win away for the first time in six years. The last time we managed that was when Malawi went down 2-1 in Lilongwe in 2000.
This is a good time for Cranes to break all existing barriers.
The 3-0 victory over Lesotho was Cranes biggest victory in eight years. The last time we managed such a glorified scoreline was when Magid Musisi's hat-trick brought Rwanda down 5-0 at Nakivubo.
But for Cranes to get the required away result, the players must be told that we are favourites. The West Africans are 30 places behind us on the FIFA rankings and they must have that in mind.
Six points from two matches, if it happened, would have Nigeria running scared.
Schumi v Alonso
The words started flying two months ago and it has now reached fever pitch.
The run-up to this weekend's Formula One Grand Prix in China wouldn't be misplaced in boxing.
No love lost between two rivals, Michael Schumacher and Fernando Alonso, two points separate them, three races left.
Alonso and his flamboyant Renault manager Flavio Briatore have suggested that the championship is fixed in Schumi's favour. "There are some financial considerations," Alonso told his Spanish fans. He went on to say that Schumacher is the "dirtiest" driver in history. On his part, the Ferrari boss said: "I think he missed a great opportunity to keep his mouth shut."
The one man who can shut Briatore's and Alonso's mouth is Schumacher; by winning in China to take the lead for the first time this year.
But Renault will fight him with all their might.
Whatever you do, do not miss the Chinese Grand Prix. You can catch it at 8:30am on Supersport two on DStv.

Original_Search_Title: Racist Taunts Hamper the Beautiful Game
Headline: Africa: Racist Taunts Hamper the Beautiful Game
Body_Text:
Soccer is the most-watched sports event in the world. It draws millions of views and viewers in every part of the universe. It's most prestigious competition, the FIFA World Cup, commands massive publicity and interest from almost every nation. Soccer brings together different groups of people and is a universal brand.
No matter where one resides and regardless of language, fans enjoy soccer matches without having to utter a word. Definitely, the game draws a lot of excitement from both the spectators and players - even those who are keen to promote racism. For a very long time, in some parts of the world, mainly North Western Europe, the beautiful game has been turned into angry encounters because of racist taunts directed at black players. The misguided racists have been most prominent with their despicable acts and taunts in Spain where Cameroonian striker Samuel Eto'o of Barcelona has been a particular.
At one time, he threatened to walk out of a match after racist taunts by Real Zaragoza supporters. The match referee had to stop the proceedings to make announcement over the PA (Public Address) system for the crowd to stop making the monkey-like sounds. The referee's request did not yield results as monkey chants continued even when Barcelona and Brazilian midfield, Ronaldihno lined-up the ball to take a penalty. Ronaldihno later said that if Eto'o had walked out of the game, he would have followed suit. In the past, Eto'o used to take the taunts in his stride but recently, he has shown that he is fed up. Last year, he celebrated a goal by imitating a monkey. "They paid to see a monkey so I did it," he said. The racial slurs are not just restricted to opposing crowds. Even a club's supporters racially abuse their own players. The crime extends to coaches and club mates. In this order of things, even star players are not spared. French and Arsenal striker, Thierry Henry was racially abused by Spain's national coach, Luis Aragones who referred to him as negro de mierda (black shit). Aragones was caught on television in 2004 at a training session talking to Henry's former striking partner at Arsenal, José Antonio Reyes. It is alleged that Aragones said: "Show them that you are better than that piece of 'negro de mierda'".
He later said that his intentions were to motivate Reyes but this did not spare him from the criticism he received from all quarters. "I have black, gypsy and Japanese friends, and I have nothing against blacks," he claimed then. Aragones who remained unfazed was fined a measly £2,000 by the Spain's soccer governing body who was pressured into doing so by the Spanish Commission on anti-violence. Surprisingly, Spanish President Angel Mar'a Villar took sides with Aragones and criticised those against him. A recent book published by Manchester United and England defender, Rio Ferdinand, revealed racial abuse that one of his team mates suffered at West Ham. In his book, the player discloses that in England soccer, there are 'pockets' of racial discrimination directed towards black and mixed race players. "If I name the player who came out with the most racist abuse, I've ever heard on a football field, no one would believe it. I guarantee you'd be astonished," Soccernet reported a statement from the player's biography.
Last year in November, Italian side, Messina and Ivorian defender, Marc Zoro threatened to leave the pitch when he suffered racial abuse from the visiting Inter Milan supporters in Italy. The incident left Zoro in tears even though Inter's Brazilian striker, Adriano tried to ease the pain by showing compassion. In 2000, UEFA banned Lazio defender Sinisa Mihajlovic, for two Champions League matches for calling former Arsenal midfielder, Patrick Vieira a 'black monkey'. When Costa Rican striker, Paulo Wanchope got fed-up with racial abuse from his own club supporters at Malaga, he engaged in a tussle with a number of fans. Monkey noises and taunts of 'black shit' echoed from some spectators. Ron Atkinson's comments a few years ago that French legend Marcel Desailly is 'a fucking lazy thick nigger' raised a ruckus. In 2000, Bari coach Eugenio Fascetti shouted: 'The nigger Diawara spat in Garzya's face and the spit might even be infected.
Why don't these niggers just stay home?' after Torino and Senegalese defender Djibril Diawara's nose was smashed by an opponent's elbow. In 2002, Nigerian and Treviso player, Oluwashegun Omolade was booed and racially abused during his first game by the club's fans. The players smeared black polish over their faces to show solidarity with the player. The ever energetic, Holland and Tottenham Hotspurs star Edgar Davids once said in the Holland team camp, "black players are not consulted when strategic meetings are held." He was sent packing from the camp when he voiced his concerns.
In a UEFA match against Ajax, Auxerre's Ivory Coast striker, Bonaventure Kalou was shown banners that read: "Kalou is invited to a banana tree". Trabzonspor club chairman once said that Kevin Campbell is a "coloured cannibal who calls himself a striker". Moenchengladbach's Brazilian forward, Kahe was racial abused by Aachen's fans and called an "asylum seeker". FIFA has come up with new measures like deducting points, fines and relegation for teams whose supporters are engaged in racial abuse. Part of the reason why European crowds racially abuse players is the 'white consciousness' factor. It is said that the white-dominated and male crowd feels intimidated in places they feel are their domain because of the presence of star black players.

Original_Search_Title: Mixed Response to Country's Biotech Progress
Headline: Uganda: Mixed Response to Country's Biotech Progress
Body_Text:
Entebbe — Uganda has been both praised and criticised for its efforts to implement national biosafety policies, as it and its East African neighbours take steps to adopt a common approach to biotechnology.
Fee Chon Chong-Low of the United Nations Environment Programme Global Environment Facility (UNEP-GEF) made the comments at a meeting last week (19 September) in Kampala.
Fee was reporting on the progress of four African nations in implementing national biosafety frameworks, as required by the UN Convention on Biological Diversity's Cartagena Protocol to protect biodiversity from any potential harm posed by genetically modified (GM) organisms.
She said Cameroon was leading the way, followed by Kenya, Uganda and Namibia, adding: "However, Uganda has performed a lot better than the rest in public sensitisation and encouraging public participation."
She praised Charles Mugoya for leading the development of Uganda's national biosafety framework despite a lack of cooperation from some sections of the government.
But she expressed disappointment with Uganda's failure to set up a biosafety 'clearing house' to share information on the movement of GM organisms.
She pointed out that US$20,000 had been allocated for this purpose but that there had been "no results".
Uganda and its neighbours in the East African Community -- Kenya, Tanzania and Uganda -- are moving towards a common political, legal and regulatory framework on biotechnology and biosafety.
The three nations are at different levels of biotechnology development, with Kenya carrying out confined field trials on GM maize, cotton and potato.
Uganda is preparing a confined field trial of bananas genetically modified to resist black sigatoka disease, a serious fungal condition. Tanzania, meanwhile is still developing its national biosafety framework.

Original_Search_Title: Political Parties And the National Ethos
Headline: Kenya: Political Parties And the National Ethos
Body_Text:
Nairobi — On the face of it, asking whether we have political parties in Kenya sounds odd, because we have several parliamentary and non-parliamentary parties in existence.
The parliamentary parties are Narc, officially headed by Mrs Charity Ngilu, Kanu of Mr Uhuru Kenyatta and Mr William Ruto, Mr Musikari Kombo's Ford Kenya, Mr Simeon Nyachae's Ford People, Narc Kenya and the LDP.
Other parliamentary parties are Safina, Sisi Kwa Sisi, Ford Asili and the National Labour Party.
In all, we have about 50 registered political parties. Increasingly, however, Narc Kenya and ODM Kenya are emerging as the two political forces to watch in Kenya's unfolding political drama.
Usually, political parties have certain attributes. They have a defined, loyal membership, ideology and vision, democratic institutional infrastructure, a constitution and by-laws, an unfolding political culture, an elected leadership, an agenda, periodic meetings of party organs, a credible outreach programme, an accountability system, international cooperation and a solidarity mechanism.
Consigned to cold storage
In Kenya, political parties have had a faltering experience because the political elite have used them basically as instruments of organisation for power.
The general citizenry is tokenly made part of party activity around election time. Afterwards, the parties are consigned to cold storage, waiting for the next elections. The owners and officials of the parties tend to recruit members on ethnic basis.
Many Kenyans, including the elite, believe that a party headed by their tribespeople will deliver to them maximum development. However, factually, some of the poorest people in the country hail from the regions of past presidents.
Since 1982, former President Moi organised his Kanu politics around the so-called minority tribes. The Luhyia, the Kamba and the Coastal people, and, of course, his native Kalenjin community, gave him the numbers required to emerge victorious in 1984, 1988, 1992 and 1997.
At some point, Mr Raila Odinga re-mobilised the Luo people to support Moi in the hope that Moi would anoint him successor. His National Development Party (NDP) was swallowed by Kanu. However, for the most part, Moi chose to de-emphasise the Kikuyu and the Luo in his politics.
The Narc experiment has failed. President Kibaki's gallant attempt to contain the LDP, DP, NPK, and Ford Kenya together proved to be a Herculean task. He had presumably thought that Kenya's political and other elite would see the sense in coalescing into a national party capable of transforming Kenya within a short period.
The collapse of the ruling party forced President Kibaki to craft a government of national unity. He decided to work with all politicians of good will so as to create a broad-based government.
As a result, the distinction between those from the old regime, those who left Kanu in 1992 and the 2002 entrants, had to be abandoned. Pragmatism took centre-stage.
ODM Kenya is currently organised on the same key principle as the NAK and Narc of 2002. By recruiting principal leaders from key communities, it is expected their tribal voters will give ODM-K victory in 2007.
On the other hand, Narc Kenya wants to revisit the politics of independence in which Kenyans were mobilised around vision, ideology and the national interest.
Narc Kenya's current transitional leadership is young and sourced from all parts of Kenya. It has no Big Man or Big Woman because it is rotational. It provides a training ground for national leadership.
ODM Kenya hopes to capitalise on both the momentum it built during the Orange-Banana referendum tussle, its ethnic alliance, international contacts, sway regarding the media, propaganda machinery, and the time it has on its hands as the opposition. Moreover, there is a tendency for many to support the opposition merely because opposition politics are laced with pomp and drama.
Narc Kenya must organise for the 2007 elections in the context of a most formidable opposition. The party must assume that the Kalonzo-Mudavadi-Ruto-Uhuru-Raila group will lead ODM Kenya.
However, Moi is uncomfortable with Kanu dying in his lifetime. Unless a Kanu leader is ODM-K's presidential candidate, 2007 could bury Kanu.
Just as Mr Odinga and company exited from Kanu in March 2002, so too is Moi's and Biwott's Kanu likely to reject ODM Kenya. More critically, the Moi political and biological family could at best only trust Mr Mudavadi and Uhuru to protect their interests.
Extensive political network
Those who support Mr Kalonzo Musyoka argue that their candidate must be on the presidential ballot box come what may. They further add that President Kibaki tried from 1992, 1997 only to be successful in 2002. A try in 2007, they say, would further popularise Mr Musyoka for 2012.
Mr Odinga has popularised his candidature aggressively. So far, he has probably spent more money than any other presidential aspirant. He has the most extensive political and social network within ODM Kenya. If he doesn't stand now, he would have to wait for 15 years or so to make it to the presidency.
Mr Ruto, by all indications, does not mind being a vice-president. He seems to have an advantage due to both his mobilisational acumen, which he perfected in Youth for Kanu, the search for a post-Moi strongman in the populous Kalenjin country, and the resources he amassed during the last regime.
Even with his Foundation, Mr Musyoka may yet discover, as Mrs Ngilu, Mr Wamalwa and the early Raila did, that a presidential candidature requires real money, as well as intellectual and other resources.
Mr Kibaki decided early in his presidency that he would work with all political and other elite. He wanted a transition strongly built on national consensus. In my view, even after 2007, this dream must be pursued. We must create a nation, not tribal enclaves.
Prof Kibwana (Narc-K), is the minister for Environment and Natural Resources

Original_Search_Title: Each Chimp At Ngamba Consumes Shs5.5m Annually
Headline: Uganda: Each Chimp At Ngamba Consumes Shs5.5m Annually
Body_Text:
Mukono — THE Executive Director of Ngamba Island Chimpanzee Sanctuary, Ms Lilly Ajarova, has expressed concern over the growing number of chimps, saying the cost of maintaining them is high.
She said $3,000 (about Shs5.5 million) is spent on feeding and treating each chimpanzee at the sanctuary.
Ajarova was on Thursday addressing the Minister of State Minister for Tourism, Wildlife and Antiquities, Mr Serapio Rukundo, who visited the sanctuary.
Ajarova said the population of orphan chimpanzees had increased from 19 in 1998 to the present 39.
The sanctuary is located on Lake Victoria.
The Veterinarian and Sanctuary Manager, Dr Lawrence Mugisha, said the chimps have four special meals a day in addition to their natural feeds.
"We ensure that there are supplementary foods such as carrots, mangoes, pawpaws and bananas. A balanced diet is just as important for chimps as it is for people," Mugisha said.
"The surrounding forest also provides meals essential in building strong bones, muscles and promoting health."
Mugisha said they had resorted to subjecting the chimps to contraceptive implants called implanon to control their birth.
Chimpanzees are members of the family of great apes, which are solely in Africa.
Rukundo commissioned two teacher's residential buildings constructed with funds from the sanctuary at Koome Island Primary School.

Original_Search_Title: Coca-Cola FA Cup - Dicey Ties for Enyimba, Rangers
Headline: Nigeria: Coca-Cola FA Cup - Dicey Ties for Enyimba, Rangers
Body_Text:
Lagos — Former African Champions, Enyimba FC, will this afternoon attempt to return to winning ways when they take on El-Kanemi Warriors of Maiduguri in one of the last set of round of 32 matches in the Coca-Cola FA Cup.
The encounter, which comes up at the Jos Township Stadium, was initially scheduled for last week but was put forward till today because of flight problem the Peoples Elephants encountered on their journey back home after crashing out to Orlando Pirates of South Africa in the CAF Champions League group match in Johannesburg.
Coach Fatai Amao knows fully well that another banana slip this evening to ambitious Warriors in the heart of the sahel Savannah may go a long way in determining how long he will stay on the job considering the anguish that is still on the ground following the team's exit from the premier club competition at the group stage for second consecutive year after winning the competition in 2003 and 2004.
"We know fully well that many of our fans are not happy with the exit from the continent while the players are still picking the pieces together and our opponents El-Kanemi are no push over in the tournament, hence we have a lot to do. The management has come to the rescue to ensure that we sail through in this round," the coach said yesterday.
In other round of 16 matches, Gateway FC of Abeokuta hopes to extend the ambition of being the first team to play in the final of the competition as host when they tackle Bayelsa FC of Yenegoa at the Samuel Ogbemudia Stadium, in Benin.
The team, which received a N1.2million boost from the state Governor, Otunba Gbenga Daniel, knows fully well that a win in Benin today will translate into more largess for them as the tournament reaches the crucial stage.
However, with the exit of League champions, Ocean Boys, the Bayelsa lads are aware that a defeat will mean that the state which kicked off the competition with two representatives up to this stage will have none left.
Enugu Rangers will be hoping to end their long trophyless spell (they have not won any trophy since 1982), when they tackle Fountain FC at the Gombe Stadium, while in Owerri, Dolphin FC of Port Harcourt battles Akwa Starlets of Akwa Ibom just as El-Cruzero square up against Kaduna United at the Sani Abacha Stadium.
The Onikan Stadium, which hosted the exit of Heartland FC of Owerri last week will stage the match between Offa United and FC Abuja just as University Teaching Hospital Jos FC will tackle University of Nigeria, Enugu Campus FC at the Enyimba International Stadium, Aba.
The next round of the competition will resume next month while the final match will come up at the MKO Abiola Stadium, Abeokuta also next month.

Original_Search_Title: Strength of a Woman
Headline: East Africa: Strength of a Woman
Body_Text:
Nairobi — ANGIE RUIZ, ONE OF Hollywood's younger stars, is developing a documentary to celebrate the achievements of the East African woman.
The documentary, to be titled Women Warriors, will feature six women achievers - four Tanzanians and two Kenyans - aged between 15 and 55. The women will be from different backgrounds and spheres of life.
"I am hoping to produce a documentary with an inspiring story of women in East Africa," Ruiz said in Nairobi recently. "I believe there are very many intelligent women here with incredible stories to tell. I'm really excited at the prospect of making the documentary and sharing the stories of the women here with the world." The film will focus on educational background of the women, their careers and how they came to be what they are.
The documentary, estimated to cost between $50,000 and $100,000, will be produced by Wandering Films, Ruiz's production company. It is currently at the pre-production stage and is expected to be ready by June next year.
"It will be a beautiful thematic piece of art that I will feel proud to share with everyone," Ruiz, 29, told The EastAfrican at the National Museums of Kenya in Nairobi, where she had gone to see the priceless collection of original fossils.
Ruiz, who also has an interest in the cradle of mankind and supporting communities on the eastern shores of Lake Turkana - the Dassanech, the Elmolo and the Gabbra - came to Kenya on August 28 and flew to the Koobi Fora prehistoric site - a world heritage site.
She is especially interested in research into human origins and the contribution of museums to social and cultural development of different communities.
Simple and passionate about people's wellbeing, the actress, who first caught the attention of moviegoers with her role (Girl eating banana) in the Miramax romantic comedy, 40 Days and 40 Nights, has been involved in charity work in Tanzania since 2004 through her Wandering Star Foundation.
She has worked as a volunteer with Wamata (Walio na Pambano Dhidi ya Ukimwi Tanzania - People Fighting Against Aids in Tanzania) - a grassroots non-governmental organisation that raises HIV/Aids awareness through a drama group that performs stage plays and songs. While working with Wamata, she was stationed in Tengeru, a rural village in Arusha.
She visited orphans and people living with Aids as well as gathering relevant information for Wamata.
Ruiz says she witnessed inner beauty and a sense of community that everyone shared. "Beautiful humanity like I have never seen in my life. I became personally connected to each person," she says.
She has since visited Tanzania four times a year. "Every time I come to Tanzania, I meet so many wonderful people, especially women. Because of this, each time I go home feeling inspired." Her foundation has set up an educational centre in Tengeru that enables adults to acquire secondary education. The centre currently has 60 students.
For Ruiz, engaging in humanitarian work not only keeps her busy but has become something of a mission. "I think one of the biggest responsibilities of an actor or actress is to really be aware of the human conditions around the world. You learn so much when you interact with people, since you get the opportunity to learn everybody's story. This is what inspires me as an artiste."
But what inspires her in African women? "They are strong-willed. They have a strong personality. That moves me. It is something I admire a lot because I don't see a lot of it in other parts of the world," she says. "I hope to get that across in the documentary."
Ruiz is married to Mark Terbeek, a venture capitalist in the US.

Original_Search_Title: Magona to Retire After I. Coast Tie
Headline: Uganda: Magona to Retire After I. Coast Tie
Body_Text:
Kampala — RUGBY Cranes inspirational captain Peter Magona plays his last international today.
Magona, who is the team's most capped player(28 caps), plans to retire into coaching.
The 28 year-old pharmacist and marketeer has somehow managed to juggle it well with a hectic rugby schedule, never once missing an international fixture. But he eventually had to call it quits to concentrate on his professional career.
The Rugby Cranes are in Ivory Coast for their last group fixture, results of which will have no bearing as Morocco have already qualified for the next phase as the winner of Group B.
The game is more about pride and of course a chance to climb up the IRB rankings. The Cranes will also be without their starting kicker and scrum half Robert Seguya who hurt his knee in the bruising encounter against Morocco.
Anthony Kinene has been slotted in as the replacement scrum half and will share the kicking responsibilities with full back Simon Wakabi.
In the first leg in Kampala the Cranes mauled Ivory Coast 32-7 before getting knocked out of the qualifiers after narrowly losing to Morocco 5-3. The West Africans will thus be looking for their first win when they host the Cranes.
Neighbours Kenya also face Tunisia in Tunis in a match that is a potential banana skin, with all teams in Group A still with a chance to qualify for the next phase.
