The Ivorians who failed to assert their physical superiority against Uganda in Kampala will look to get it right this time as their backs were no match for the fluid and speedy Ugandan backs.

Original_Search_Title: Nema to Demolish Mbarara Park
Headline: Uganda: Nema to Demolish Mbarara Park
Body_Text:
Mbarara — The National Environment Management Authority will demolish Treasure Valley Park, that was set up on the banks of River Rwizi in Mbarara town.
"Those are illegal structures and the law is clear. Any development should be 100 metres away from the water bodies, we shall demolish it," said the Nema public awareness focal officer for western region, Mr Jeconious Musingwire.
The park was set up in July and has been operating on Sundays. Mbarara Municipal Council on September 14 resolved to temporarily close it due to lack of an operation license and sanitary facilities like toilets.
Residents of Mbarara town have been flocking the park every Sunday to watch Kakoba Water Falls. It has cottages and huts made up of dry banana leaves.
Rwizi is already under threat of drying up due to environmental degradation upstream in Bushenyi and Mbarara districts.
Efforts to get a comment from the owners proved futile. When Daily Monitor visited the site, we witnessed poor waste disposal as bottles of beer and mineral water floated on the river.

Original_Search_Title: Crises Presidency and the Media
Headline: Nigeria: Crises Presidency and the Media
Body_Text:
"What is happening now, as unpleasant as it may be, is that the PDP led-government of President Olusegun Obasanjo is bold and courageous enough to be involved in a comprehensive process of self-correction in which no one is spared. This historic moment should be saluted by all who wish Nigeria well."
- Ojo Madueke, PDP Secretary-General

If the president's light-fingered cousin Makanjuola, Olabode George, along with Chris Uba, were not free men today, then the unwarranted assault on our intelligence quotient above would have made some sense. But Ojo Maduekwe apparently must be an extraterrestrial in a unique way.
He also doubles as one of the president's valets in the PDP, and on account of the unprecedented injury being inflicted on the polity today by tango between the president and his vice; the way the PDP has responded to the crises, far from being courageous or salutary, is indicative of the total absence of credibility on the part of our leaders today. The current efforts of the Obasanjo administration to pass itself for what it is not is equally a pathetic manifestation of the total depravity in the thinking and morality of our leaders who have chosen to behave as clowns even if the generality of Nigerians can no longer see any humour in their circus show of shame.
The net effect, I dare to add, is that as things stand today, the institution of the Presidency has never been more diminished in stature and prestige. Along the way, Nigerians were not only being taken for absolute idiots, our leaders have also demystified their offices in the eyes of the world. But the greater tragedy is that Nigeria, on account of our size and resources and overwhelming potentials, remains the last hope for the black race to salvage something out of a world scientific advancement that had long being reduced to a village saturated by naked greed, injustice, unfair and uneven trade resulting from the ravages of unbridled globalisation, for which Nigeria, like much of the developing world, remains non-starters.
Instead of our leaders to harness our God-given resources to uplift the nation from the misery of archaic and dilapidated infrastructure, hunger, disease and unemployment, they have decided to play on the legendary gullibility of the average Nigerian to further polarise the nation along its predictable fault lines.
The tragedy is that there is the potent possibility that not for the last time, our long-suffering masses who sadly also constitute a sizeable portion of Franz Fanon's notion of the very wretched of the earth would be left to bear the full brunt of the naked tango for power between two over-pampered Goliaths who betrayed them at the polls in 2003, and would, given the slightest chance, do the same in 2007.
Far from correcting itself or strengthening democracy which it continues to mock and abuse with reckless impunity, what the ruling PDP government has done since its own version of the 'night of the long knives' when it purged its ranks of its last remaining men of honour and integrity, was to reduce the nation to a banana republic in which the president's massive ego was massaged with comical exhilaration even as Nigerians watched with consternation as the likes of Chris Uba and Olabode George, both of whom should be in the warm embrace of the law, assume the status of saints who sadly are also jostling to decide our future!
Of greater worry to me is the reaction of Nigerians to the latest crises rocking the very foundations of the Presidency. I had earlier hinted at the high gullibility of Nigerians who seem to believe anything depending on their medium of information. That attitude, unfortunately, explained why in our chequered history since independence, several politicians like the late Ahmadu Bello and Tafawa Balewa, not to mention the recent example of Umaru Dikko, all of whom posterity proceeded to absolve of their alleged crimes, were nevertheless tried and convicted for corruption by the media when they were in power!
Indeed, the media is crucial as we come to terms with the sordid events in the Presidency today. Now, there is no suggestion here that either Atiku or Obasanjo is on the same moral pedestal with either Ahmadu Bello or Tafawa Balewa, but it seems to me that the media is yet to rid itself of the mistakes of the past when it allowed pettiness and prejudices to compromise the ethics of the profession.
That tendency, it must be added, invited the first military coup in 1966 and eventually truncated the Second Republic. For instance, last week, ThisDay newspaper carried bold headlines with photos of four cheques allegedly cashed by Atiku from the contentious Marine Float Account only to proclaim that it was suspending the practice when the VP's camp responded in kind with the release of even more damaging fourteen copies of cheques they alleged linked the president and the PDP to the same account.
The question is why the newspaper did not deem the publication of copies of the initial cheques which tended to damage the VP's case improper in the first place. Was it because it did not anticipate the potency of the damaging information available to the Atiku camp? We may never know the reason for the editorial volte-face, but like some of our lawmakers who have chosen to redefine the oldest profession in the world, the media owes a duty to Nigerians to uphold the truth at all times to ensure balance and equity in the dissemination of information. Whether we accept it or not, the media has a gigantic role to play ahead of 2007, which, assuming we avoid the mistakes of the past, will witness our first transition from one civilian administration to the other.
The media can either decide to take sides with the longsuffering Nigerian people or risk the contemptuous verdict of history in the service of unquestionably the most discredited politicians to ever pollute our political space. For example, I expected the media to take advantage of the ongoing revelations from the operations of the Marine Float Account to do an incisive inquest into the identity of those who benefited from the huge sums withdrawn from it to no avail. So far, we have only heard from Samuel Ogbemudia who claimed the forty million naira he collected was expended on party activities, but that, I am afraid, should not be the end of the story.
We need to know the nature of party activities involved to warrant the huge withdrawals and subsequent expenditure. Were INEC officials or even law enforcement agents compromised in the process? Were the payments receipted? What about campaign funding in general? Do we have sufficient laws to regulate campaign funding? Were any laws broken? In the United States, for instance, there are strict laws on campaign funding borne out of the desire to prevent the gangsters or external powers from hijacking the government.
But while the current crises suggest that our government has been hijacked by crooks, it is also obvious that we have no proper laws in place on campaign funding, or if they exist, they must be clearly deficient, which explains the preponderance of godfathers and characters such as Chris Uba. These, certainly, are posers which should concern the media, even if as we already know from the antecedents of the hunters and the hunted in this instance, they curiously escaped the deserved attention of the EFCC.
These are the issues which should concern the media in no small respect if only it were aware of its responsibilities to civil society. Sadly, the current crisis suggests that the media has also become the principal casualty of Obasanjo's desperate divide and rule strategy. It has allowed itself to be compromised and effectively politicised, which is a dangerous thing for any society, given our history of post-humus vindication of supposed political felons like Balewa. If you believed most commentators, we should simply accept the reports of the EFCC and the kangaroo panel, which rubber-stamped it, and put the noose around the neck of the VP.
But that tendency is flawed in many respects because it ignores other fundamental issues which border on the legitimacy of the government itself. Indeed, I have been petrified at the inability or disinclination of the media to seize the opportunity provided by the current revelations to unravel the mystery - if at all it could be called that - surrounding the 2003 elections. In that respect, and quite pitifully, the media stands condemned as accomplices to our collective shame. Beyond any reasonable doubt, what the battle between Atiku and Obasanjo has proved is that their presidency is founded on sleaze. That quite unfortunately should not be acceptable to any reasonable Nigerian, least of all the media even in a rotten republic such as ours.

Original_Search_Title: Step Down for Me, Ruto Tells Raila And Kalonzo
Headline: Kenya: Step Down for Me, Ruto Tells Raila And Kalonzo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Eldoret North MP, Mr William Ruto, has asked legislators Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka to give up their presidential bids and support him.
Eldoret North MP, Mr William Ruto, expresses a point during his visit to Kapsokwany in Mt Elgon District on Sunday. Picture by Peter Ochieng
Ruto on Sunday said both Raila and Musyoka had supported former President Daniel Moi and President Kibaki, allowing them access to State House.
He said by supporting the two, Raila and Kalonzo had squandered their chances for the presidency.
"Both Raila's and Musyoka's time was used up by Moi and now Kibaki, whom they supported to win their respective presidential bids. They should support me on the grounds that I am young compared to them," he said.
Ruto said the Kenyan youth were to blame for voting older politicians into leadership positions. He accused the youth of ignoring their agemates, who could address their plight, in favour of older politicians. Kalonzo is 52 years old, while Raila is 60.
Kalonzo served as Cabinet minister in the Moi Government for many years, but broke away in 2002.
Raila, the Lang'ata MP, contested the presidency in 1997 and lost to Moi. In 2002, he threw his weight behind Kibaki to dislodge Kanu.
"Elderly people like Kibaki and (Simeon) Nyachae have served this country for too long. A befitting reward would be to retire them," he said.
Two-horse race
Ruto said that at 40, he was the most suited candidate for the presidency.
Speaking in Kisii on Sunday, Ruto said Kenyans would choose the most suitable candidate to vie for the presidency on the ODM-Kenya ticket.
The Kanu Secretary-General said Moi was his personal friend and that he (Moi) could support ODM-Kenya at an opportune time.
"Moi and I are still good friends and I can assure Kenyans that the retired Head of State will support the ODM-Kenya at the right time," he said.
MPs who accompanied Ruto included Mr Omingo Magara (South Mugirango) and Mr Phillip Rotino (Sigor).
They were speaking at various trading centres during a tour of Kisii and Gucha districts.
Ruto later presided over a funds drive in aid of 23 churches at Turwa Primary School in Bobasi. He said the General Election due next year would be a two-horse race between ODM-Kenya and Narc-Kenya, with no room for a third force.
He said the election would be reminiscent of the referendum on the draft constitution held last November.
Presidential ambitions
Ruto said there was no room for a third force in the current political setup.
In an apparent reference to Ford- Kenya, Ford-People and the National Party of Kenya, Ruto urged political parties to join either of the two blocs or risk being marginalised.
"Narc-Kenya is composed of those who supported the Wako draft, while the Orange team has regrouped under the ODM-Kenya party. We will use the same tactics used at the referendum to win the election," he said.
During the referendum, the Orange team floored the Government-backed Banana team by defeating the proposed new constitution.
Ruto said ODM-Kenya presidential aspirants would not let their ambition override the desire to give Kenyans good leadership.
"Many of our enemies are predicting that our presidential ambitions will see the ODM-Kenya dream go up in smoke, but I can assure Kenyans that we will rally behind one of us and make it to State House," he said.
He said the nomination of the party's presidential candidate would not kill the spirit of unity in the party.
"We have declared interest in the seat, but we have also agreed to recognise that only one person can be president at a time," he said.
Ruto said ODM-Kenya would rally Kenyans to demand minimum reforms ahead of the General Election.

Original_Search_Title: Questions Which Remain Unanswered Over ODM-K Rival Party's Registration
Headline: Kenya: Questions Which Remain Unanswered Over ODM-K Rival Party's Registration
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Even after ODM Kenya finally got registered last week, one protagonist who is not amused is weighing his options about whether to sue regarding the matter.
The person at the centre of the intrigues is 40-year-old lawyer and political activist Mugambi Imanyara. One curious thing about Mr Imanyara is that he undertook the task of organising the registration when, in fact, he belongs to New Kanu of Keiyo South MP Nicholas Biwott.
There are two parties involved in the name sharing - ODM-K and the ODM Party of Kenya which Mr Imanyara registered on December 22. Central to Mr Imanyara's tale is his assertion that he registered ODMPK at the instigation of two Orange leaders.
He claims that not only did the two politicians directly approach him to carry out this task, but besides, they provided the names of interim officials - Mr John Miriti, reportedly a pilot, as chairman; businesswoman Alice Wangui Muringi as secretary and Mr Joseph Bunei of Eldoret as treasurer.
If indeed he had been acting in tandem with two Orange principals, why has Mr Imanyara come out so strongly to resist ODM-K's registration? Why not leave them to their own devices now that they have decided on an alternative? "They were acting contrary to the law," he says. "The law does not allow copy-cat societies."
As if to acknowledge that this explanation is not so convincing, he adds, after a pause: "I was acting on behalf of the current office-holders. The objection I wrote to the registrar specifically stated that."
In his account, the Orange chiefs had not been excluded from membership. "If they wanted to take it over there would have been no resistance," he explains.
The matter was given a stranger twist when an ODM-K interim official told the Sunday Nation last week that in August, he approached Mr Imanyara with the proposal that he "sell" the party to them. Nothing apparently came out of it. Still, it is not clear why the two senior Orange frontmen Mr Imanyara talks about, if indeed they were involved, would want to disown a party whose registration they had initially promoted. When the registration became public in January, there was an outcry in the mainstream Orange ranks.
Mr Imanyara claims to have been "very shocked" at this reaction, especially coming from his two collaborators who, he says, joined in the condemnation. "They even called me a stranger and said party names were not important as were personalities behind those parties," he argues. "Yet a few weeks ago, they suddenly decided they wanted the registration and the name. Between January and now, there had been no controversy."
There appeared no urgency on the part of ODM-K until recently to register their brand. From the beginning of the year, Kanu maintained a stand-offish pose over an alliance with LDP, which was preoccupied with its own internal problems.
Only after the July by-elections in northern Kenya and Nakuru did they realise that they had to swim together or drown. The two parties suddenly accelerated the unity machinery. And only then did it occur to them that they should register as a political party the Orange name, which happened to be the shared glue in last year's referendum battle.
Mr Imanyara is wise enough not to disclose who the two ODM principals are. Asked if they are in the ODM cast of declared presidential candidates, he says that, for example, there are people like Mwingi North MP Kalonzo Musyoka who everybody knows is in the race, but has not officially declared the intention.
It is not clear why the ODM people would want to specifically use Mr Imanyara's services when the ODM-K side is full of top-notch lawyers of its own, among them senior advocates such as ODM "team leader" Mutula Kilonzo.
Mr Imanyara says that probably his two collaborators were not acting in concert with ODM colleagues, or that they may have jumped the gun. Maybe, he adds, they backed off as a result of the heat the news of the registration generated.
He further expresses doubt about an assurance reportedly given much earlier by Mr Kilonzo to the effect that he had petitioned the registrar to reserve the ODM name. If that had been the case, ODMPK's cursory registration would likely have met some procedural hurdles.
The Sunday Nation first got wind of ODMPK's registration a few weeks after the referendum. Curiously, it was not through either Mr Imanyara or the officials he lists, but through a prominent Central Kenya MP in the Banana camp. At around the same time when a new Cabinet was being constituted, the MP had called on the President at State House, Nairobi, where they discussed various matters.
Towards the end of the session, the MP is said to have mentioned the new party's registration, about which the President reportedly appeared surprised. What was interesting about the whole matter was that the MP, when he mentioned his meeting with the President to the Sunday Nation, gave the impression that the registration was a sleight of hand against the Orange principals by a certain Banana cabal to which he belonged.
Even as the lawyer was registering ODMPK, he remained alligned to New Kanu, where he is the secretary. He had left the mainstream Kanu in a huff following last year's Kasarani elections when he and a group of party activists he was leading from South Imenti were locked out of the venue. A rival team of officials from the area, led by former Central Bank deputy governor Eliphaz Riungu, was allowed in instead.
That the ODMPK interim officials are "unknowns" is hardly unique among the new parties. ODM-K used a not-so-dissimilar cast of obscurities for its registration - chairman Daniel Kitonga Maanzo, a Nairobi lawyer; secretary Abraham Chepkonga Kibet and treasurer Lilian Alunga, who has been featuring in ODM processions.

Original_Search_Title: One Million New Voters Register for 2007 Poll
Headline: Kenya: One Million New Voters Register for 2007 Poll
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Around one million new voters have been registered in the past month.
And more than one million have switched to different places in a move that could have a bearing on the result of the Presidential election, next year.
Mr Nelson Shabaan, a registration clerk at Muguga Green station in Westlands, gives Ms Janet Nyambura her voting card. Many people turned up at registration centres countrywide to beat the deadline.
Details of the just concluded registration push - obtained independently by the Nation - show that Rift Valley has most voters in Kenya, with more than 200,000 new voters raising its total voting power to 2.8 million.
It is among three provinces which registered a high turn-out, especially in the last two days before the registration roll was closed on Thursday.
The other two are Coast, which registered more than 70,000 new voters from the previous 967,518 taking its total vote to more than 1.03 million, and Nairobi where more than 80,000 new voters registered taking its total to 1.04 million.
Those three provinces account for 350,000 of the new one million votes, although new totals for the remaining five provinces have not yet been completed, so it remains unclear exactly how the additional 650,000 votes of the new one million are distributed.
But it is known that Coast and Nairobi join the five other provinces that each have more than one million votes - Rift(2.8m), Eastern (1.9m set to hit 2m), Central (1.7m), Nyanza (1.5m) and Western (1.3m) - leaving arid and sparsely populated North Eastern as the only province with fewer than one million votes. It had only 237,321 voters before the registration exercise.
The new voter registration results are likely to be used by politicians in both Narc Kenya and the Orange Democratic Movement Kenya when seeking new alliances to negotiate power-sharing formulae.
Narc-K, packed with President Kibaki's supporters from Central Province, are likely to evaluate which provinces among Health minister Charity Ngilu's Eastern, Roads minister Simeon Nyachae's Nyanza, and Local Government minister Musikari Kombo's Western will put the most votes in the basket.
On the same basis ODM-K's leading politicians William Ruto (Rift Valley) Raila Odinga (Nyanza), Kalonzo Musyoka (Eastern), Musalia Mudavadi (Western), Uhuru Kenyatta (Central) and Najib Balala (Coast) will be choosing a single Presidential candidate based on who among them and their allies can bring in the most votes.
The full report will be released on Tuesday by the Electoral Commission of Kenya, said its vice-chairman, Mr Gabriel Mukele.
"We will answer all questions at that time and explain those concerns some of which I have also expressed my reservations," he said.
He was referring to some reported cases of voters being transported in Mombasa and Nairobi.
But the surprising trend revealing that more than one million voters who had already registered had transferred to vote in new constituencies - the highest number on record - was being seen as targeting not only the Presidential candidates but also aimed at people's enthusiasm to decide on who should become an MP.
Those who have transferred are being seen as serious voters who want to be in the right place to vote when elections are called next year.
A voter who, for instance, works in Nairobi but is registered in Kwale or lives in Kisumu and is registered in Murang'a, would rather remain where he or she would not need to travel long distances to have a say in who should be President.
Travel hitches are normally experienced one full week before the day of a General Election, with buses fully booked making many voters miss voting where they have registered.
It was not possible to confirm if the one million transferred voters were of those people who found they could not vote during last November's referendum on the new Constitution.
Had those one million people been able to vote it might have been produced a tie in the referendum, instead of the result that favoured the anti-Draft Orange side by 3.5 million votes to the Government-backed Banana side's 2.5 million.
Equally, it might have produced an even more resounding victory for the Orange side.
The movement trends of voters was part of what the ECK officials were analysing yesterday and will only become clear when ECK chairman Samuel Kivuitu releases the results next week.
The ECK had hoped to register 785,000 new voters but it had reached 840,000 before the two days' final rush countrywide.
The ECK had deployed a clerk in each of the 20,448 registration centres to carry out the exercise.
Yesterday, the ECK officials were trying to detect anyone who might have been transported to new places for double registration. Their names will be detected by computer, using their Identity Card numbers or those of their passports.
Their names will then be compiled in a different register and they will be barred by election officials from voting.

Original_Search_Title: Turf War As President Visits Western Province
Headline: Kenya: Turf War As President Visits Western Province
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki is in Western Province today to attend celebrations to mark the 40th anniversary of the consecration of Kakamega Catholic bishop Phillip Sulumeti.
Although this is a purely religious function, the President's visit comes at a time when political alliances are shifting, and the fight for supremacy in the province between Narc Kenya and ODM Kenya before the General Election is likely to take the centre stage.
Ford Kenya, a major player in the province's politics, is also positioning itself for a slice of the cake.
As far as the Church and State House are concerned though, the President is on a solemn visit during which politics may not be on the agenda. But to political observers, the tour entails much more than just a church function.
Functions by the Catholic Church are normally tightly structured, and politicians are only allowed to speak outside after the main Mass is over and the congregation dismissed.
But with ODM-K heavyweights expected in town soon, observers say that the President may grab the occasion to woo the Luhya to his side.
There is a third voice, the one fronted by former Cabinet minister Cyrus Jirongo which has been calling for Luhya unity but whose affiliation remains unknown.
Different political scripts
The timing of the visit in the politically turbulent Luhyaland is likely to change the local landscape.
Alliances may shift, and political observers are waiting to see if the country's chief executive will grab the opportunity to drum up support for himself and the party.
The visit comes at a time when Luhya leaders are sharply divided over whether to support Ford Kenya or join forces with either Narc-K or ODM-K.
But Cabinet ministers from the region will be reading from different political scripts as they turn up to welcome the boss.
In the local politics, Vice-President Moody Awori and Cabinet colleagues Mukhisa Kituyi and Newton Kulundu are the Narc-K torchbearers.
Mr Awori decamped from the Liberal Democratic Party, while Dr Kituyi, of Trade and Industry, and Dr Kulundu, his Labour counterpart, abandoned Ford-K, citing leadership wrangles.
And Ford-K chairman Musikari Kombo, who seems to have broken ranks with his Cabinet colleagues when he refused to join Narc-K, has made it clear that he will join neither that party nor ODM-K.
Gaining root in the province
Other Luhya ministers in the Kibaki Government are Mr Moses Akaranga and Mr Soita Sitanda, while the assistant ministers are Dr Enock Kibunguchy, Dr Bony Khalwale, Mr Moses Wetang'ula, Mr David Were and Mr Raphael Wanjala.
Mr Kombo's stand is causing ripples in Ford-K, and the people are waiting to see who between him and Dr Kituyi, Narc-K's pointman in Western Province, will play host to the President.
The President's visit also comes barely a week before an ODM-K rally scheduled for the Muliro Gardens in Kakamega Town on September 23.
As the Church and the Government put the final touches on the plans for the President's visit, ODM-K luminaries, led by nominated MP Julia Ojiambo, have been preparing for their rally.
Will President Kibaki steal the limelight from the ODM-K team?
Narc-K supporters in the area who talked to the Nation, said they want him to play hard politics to brush down ODM-K, which they claim is fast gaining root in the province.
It will be interesting to see if the Luhya leaders put their differences aside to welcome the President to Kakamega as a team.
A recent meeting called to discuss the elusive Luhya unity was skipped by several leaders, among them the Vice-President, Mr Kombo and former VP Musalia Mudavadi.
It will also be interesting to see if President Kibaki uses the visit to convince Mr Kombo to abandon his hardline stance and support Narc-K, a party seen as enjoying State House support.
It is understood that the President will be accompanied by his predecessor, Mr Daniel arap Moi, on the one-day visit.
There has been a flurry of activities and excitement among the church faithful and residents in general as they await the big visit.
For the faithful, the occasion will serve to reunite them with Bishop Sulumeti, who has kept away from the public limelight, preferring to devote his time serving the Church.
President Kibaki last visited Kakamega in October at the height of campaigns for the constitution referendum, in which his Banana (Yes) camp lost to Orange (No).
He met leaders at the Kakamega State lodge who brought in delegations to drum up support for the Wako Draft.
Today's visit is likely to rekindle memories of the last tour because of the unfolding political scenario pitting ODM-K, which was born out of the Orange group, and Narc-K as the General Election draws near. It could touch off a political duel to determine which of the two parties has the upper hand in the tricky political terrain, as witnessed during the referendum.
Mr Moi is visiting the region for the first time since the 2002 elections, and this could give the region's volatile politics a new twist. At the Church's diocesan headquarters, preparations have been under way ahead of the celebrations.
But a senior priest denied that the President's visit had political overtones.
"Our invitation to the President has nothing to do with politics," he said. "This will be purely a Church function, and those reading politics in it are misguided.
It was alleged that the local leaders had attempted to have the President start the visit on Thursday to give him more time to tour other parts the province. But the plan reportedly failed because the President had other engagements.
The last few weeks have witnessed a series of campaign meetings spearheaded by Mr Jirongo, a former Lugari MP, and the Luhya Council of Elders, led by Mr Burudi Nabwera and a former Cabinet minister, Mr Joseph Daniel Otiende.
One organised by the group at the Muliro Gardens two weeks ago was the climax of the preparations for President Kibaki's visit to try to unite the local political leaders and have them identify a single candidate for the presidency.
Although the meeting was well-attended, top leaders from the province skipped it, giving a false start to the initiative to have them bury their differences.
But Mr Shitanda, Dr Khalwale and MPs Daniel Khamasi (Shinyalu), Wafula Wamunyinyi (Kanduyi), Davies Nakitare (Saboti) and Wycliffe Osundwa (Mumias) attended the meeting.
But before the dust on the quest for the elusive Luhya unity could settle, LDP officials in the province organised theirs at the same venue, but it was later cancelled to pave the way for an ODM-K campaign rally on September 23. The rally will be in Mr Mudavadi's political backyard, and it is expected to push the political temperature a notch higher.
At the same time, the Jirongo-led crusade for Luhya unity heads for Mumias Town this weekend to pay homage to the 19th century Wanga king Nabongo Mumia, whose powerful reign was felt in western Kenya and beyond. They will later address a rally at the Boma grounds in Mumias Town.

Original_Search_Title: Analysing Olusegun 'Mobutu' Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Analysing Olusegun 'Mobutu' Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Nigeria entered a frightening phase in its leadership crisis recently. At the National Executive Council (NEC) meeting of the ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), the party unanimously endorsed President Olusegun Obasanjo as "founder, father and leader of Nigeria". It was the first time in the nation's almost 100 years of existence that a political party or indeed a ruling clique would have the audacity to so name someone. The deliberation at the PDP NEC meeting was a study in affront on public sensibilities.
The party's highest ruling organ was convened against the backdrop of national outcry over political assassinations that stalk the land. Between the last meeting of the PDP NEC and the one in question, at least three high profile political assassinations were recorded. In Plateau State, Patrick Anuku, a gubernatorial aspirant of the ACD, was murdered in cold blood. A few weeks after, Funso Williams, a gubernatorial aspirant of the PDP in Lagos State was assassinated. Dr Ayodeji Daramola, another gubernatorial aspirant of the PDP in Ekiti State was murdered.
A common denomination in these assassinations and several other high profile political assassinations was that all the victims were either PDP members or had a tragic liaison with the party. Funso Williams, Ayodeji Daramola, and Chief Dikibo were PDP members. Anuku was killed a few weeks after he decamped from PDP in the acrimonious atmosphere of intra-PDP crisis in Plateau State. Chief Harry Marshal who was killed as Vice Chairman of the ANPP was a decampee from the PDP and was indeed speculated as warming himself back into the party at the time of his brutal assassination. Chief Bola Ige was a chieftain of the AD who was serving the PDP government as Attorney General.
Against this infamous background, a responsible and respectable party would have devoted the last NEC meeting to deliberate on how to deliver itself from its unenviable brand signature as a "nest of killers". Rather than edify itself as a party worried by the crisis of insecurity it has unleashed on the polity, the PDP deluded itself by celebrating ill-digested achievements that can only be gleaned from the vocal chords of its thinning rank. President Olusegun Obasanjo took the time to talk about his new pet project, the railway reforms. It did not matter to him that the frenzy about the railways in the twilight of his eight-year regime is the boldest admission of failure by a leadership that could not appropriately identify national priorities.
That it took Obasanjo, a former military head of state, seven years to realise that a railway system is a necessary condition for economic revival confirms that his abilities were exaggerated by the nation which thought his experience would put him in good stead to redress the damage of the locust years of his fellow military generals, Babangida and Abacha. However, the most frightening of the charade at the PDP meeting was the unanimous endorsement of Obasanjo as the "founder, father and leader of Nigeria". It is inconceivable that in the year of our Lord 2006, some adults will sit in a serious meeting and proclaim somebody a life leader. It is inconceivable that in this age, some adults will crave for the madness of the 1960s and 1970s that left Africa out of serious contention in world economic and technological advancements.
This is more depressing because while other parts of Africa imbibed the madness of life presidents and father of the nation in the 1960s and 1970s, Nigerians prided their nation as too decent to be one of the "banana republics". Africa, in consequence, became one large graveyard of national ambitions ruined by "founding fathers" and "life presidents. Is it not ironic, therefore, that it is after the life presidents and fathers of the nation across Africa have led their respective countries to ruin that Nigeria is romancing the hemlock?
The appellation of life president and life leader are common role images, praise titles and symbols adopted by ambitious African leaders in their blind drive for the total domination of their polities. In the words of Victor T. Le Vine, "African leaders bring idiosyncratic personality-oriented elements to their roles; ego demands, ambitions, phobias, varieties of self images that serve to create or maintain (or at least, so they hope) affect between themselves and their followers to induce submission, acquiescence or support, as well as to satisfy their own role-recognitions. One common form of such projections is praise-titles, much akin to traditional heraldic symbols that were chosen for their evocative power." President Houphet Boigny dominated Cote D'Ivoire as president and "single leader".
Jomo Kenyatta was Mzee (the wise elder) and Babu (Father of the Nation) in Kenya. Kwame Nkrumah of Ghana was Kantamanto (the infallible), Nygeno (the invisible), "the Messiah" and the "Immortal". Dr Hastings Kamuzu Banda was "Little Messiah" of Malawi. "Emperor Haile Selassie of Ethiopia was "Elect of God", Son of Solomon", "Lion of Judah" and King of Kings". Albert Margai of Sierra Leone was "Big Albert" symbolising his powerful authority. In Guinea Conakry, Sekou Toure was "Kelepit" (One of a kind) and Silly (the elephant).
Samuel Doe adopted the title of "the redeemer" of Liberia". El Hadj Ahmadou Ahidjo's official title as president of Cameroon was "Father of Independence" and "Hero of the Nation". Idi Amin Dada of Uganda enjoyed the appellation of "Field Marshall" and C.B.E, "Conqueror of the British Empire". In Central African Republic, President Bokassa enthroned himself as Emperor. Mobutu Sese Seko who, perhaps, most symbolised this craze among this generation of African leaders adopted about twenty praise titles, many of them appropriated from dreadful animals.
These included "the leopard" and "the bee". In virtually all these countries, the role images and praise titles proved to be the initiation of the leaders into the cult of tyrants and dictators. There was hardly any of these countries which survived consequent economic and political ruin. Many of them like Liberia, Zaire, Cote D'Ivoire, Uganda, and Ethiopia ended up as theatres of war. Does Nigeria deserve this fate simply because it entrusted its leadership in the PDP? Akinlola wrote from Ogun State University, Ago-Iwoye.

Original_Search_Title: Artists Union to Join NUNW
Headline: Namibia: Artists Union to Join NUNW
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Ominous indications are that the country's only legally registered artists' union as a government preferential partner is to join the ruling-party affiliated National Union of Namibian Workers.
This became abundantly clear on Monday during the opening of a four-day workshop of the Oruuano Artists Union in the capital. As allies for such a turnabout in the arts and culture setup in the country, both the government and the NUNW were formally represented at the event at the Tabitha Center in Khomasdal.
"Only a few technicalities have to be ironed out before the Oruuano Artists Union will be joining the NUNW," said the national chairman of the union, Dominique Lunenge, who formally welcomed the Minister of Youth, National Service, Sport and Culture, John Mutorwa, and the National Education Coordinator of the NUNW, Stalin Maherero, at the event.
The implications of the union joining the NUNW, a proposal submitted at the union's last congress at Tsumeb, can have far-reaching effects for the promotion of arts and culture in the country.
"The Oruuano Artists Union is viewed and regarded by the Namibian government as a dependable partner, not only to advocate, but also to promote and actually implement our broad national arts and culture policies, programmes, projects and other related activities," Minister John Mutorwa, who officially opened the workshop that is being attended by regional artists, said.
Mutorwa emphasized unionism during his speech encouraging the union to recruit more and credible Namibian artists, something the body has miserably failed to do until now.
The minister also once again implicitly intimated that the Oruuano Artists Union might one of these days be the only artist body that might be recognized by the government.
Many de facto and respected Namibian artists have over the years looked skeptically at the Oruuano Artists Union as being a front for self-enrichment, own trumpet-blowing and a political tool in the hands of others.
Echoing similar sentiments in encouraging the artists union to join the NUNW Maherero said: "As workers your rights need to be protected just like all other workers. Therefore, there is a need to create a legal framework to cover the sector and the existing ones must be strengthened."
At the same occasion Lunenge also announced that legislation is currently being prepared for an Artist Protection Bill is to be tabled in the National Assembly towards the end of this year.
This announcement was not at all disputed by Minister Mutorwa whose ministry is responsible for the promotion of arts and culture in the country.
"Artists like the Zimbabwean dance and traditional song group appearing here on the programme today might be here for the last time without any strings attached. Next year, once the Artists Protection Act becomes law, the situation will dramatically change for all foreign artists coming to work in Namibia," union national chairman Lunenge professed.
He specifically referred to foreign artists not paying taxes to the Namibian government.
At the same occasion an agreement of cooperation between the Canadian Actors Union and Oruuano was formally signed by the president of Oruuano, Banana Shekupe, and a Canadian representative, Ken Burns.

Original_Search_Title: Eastern Highlands Still Perfect Getaway
Headline: Zimbabwe: Eastern Highlands Still Perfect Getaway
Body_Text:
Harare — While burning issues such as carbon emissions, greenhouse gases and renewable energy continue to occupy our thoughts and affect our daily lives, more and more individuals are thinking 'green' and re-cycling wherever possible.
Most of us take our own shopping bags to the supermarket, and buying local and seasonal products helps reduce the "food miles" goods have to travel before they reach your plate. When it comes to going on holiday, you need only to compare the cost of fuelling up your car with the price of an air ticket to Cape town, Beijing or London, to realise that you're better off taking a local break at one of Zimbabwe's beauty spots. And you can pay all expenses with your own hard-earned local moolah!
A few weeks ago, just as the Msasa trees were turning russet, I made a road trip to the beautiful border town of Mutare, haven for tourists, naturalists and lovers of local history. Freewheeling down Christmas Pass in an attempt to conserve fuel, I glanced over my shoulder into the valley below, where Zimbabwe's third largest city lay encircled by mountains and trees.
There's always a sense of excitement when arriving at a new destination, and I wanted to look around Mutare before driving on to the nearby Bvumba Mountains. The National Gallery in Upper Third Street resides in a tastefully restored colonial building which previously existed as Kopje House, a boarding hostel for boys.
Farther back in the mists of time it served as a hospital for soldiers wounded in the war. Currently on display at the gallery was a travelling exhibition of some of Zimbabwe's best contemporary artists and sculptors.
Leaving the Gallery and driving north towards Cecil Kop, I found myself five minutes later on the edge of the suburbs and at the entrance to Thompson's Vlei and a Game Park. Paying an entrance fee of $300, I climbed some rustic steps and came upon a small dam (at one time supplying Mutare's municicpal water), where the Wildlife Association has constructed a wood and thatch building to be used as an educational resource. Picnic tables, chairs and braai grates make this a pleasant picnic spot, where you can look out for game, or gaze at the wooded mountain slopes which come down to the water. Apart from a desultory crocodile, which eyed me with mild interest from the middle of the dam, I saw no wildlife.
It was time to check in to the Inn on the Vumba, situated a few kilometres beyond the Prince of Wales viewpoint, as the road to the Bvumba Mountains winds and climbs up towards the mist belt. The welcome was warm, and I looked hungrily at the complimentary bowl of fruit and slab of mint chocolate at my bedside.
Later, I thought, and walked down to a crystal clear swimming pool at the bottom of the garden. From here one can see for miles into Mozambique, and in the evening flames preparing fireguards burned brightly on distant hillsides. Later I made my way to the Inn's well-stocked bar for a pre-dinner drink. The barman would have mixed any drink I desired, but I couldn't resist a long, cool Malawi Shandy.
The aroma of roasting beef lured me before long to the dining room that was beautifully decorated with roses and glowing candles. My visit to the Bvumba happened to coincide with that of a group of food writers from Harare. They were seated at the table next to me, and were eating as if there was no tomorrow. And who could blame them? The meal was delicious.
On returning to my room later that evening, I realised the importance of living in harmony, not only with Mother Earth, but also with the animal kingdom. Although I'd been advised to close all the bedroom windows, the bathroom window had been open a fraction. It could only have been one of those cute Vervet monkeys seen earlier hanging by prehensile tails from the trees in the garden, which had gained entry. A banana peel lay on top of my neatly turned-down bed, and the apple was missing from the fruit basket. Luckily the mint chocolate was still there!
I awoke early next morning to watch the sunrise. Within minutes the valley was bathed in a rosy glow, and the night watch, ending his patrol waved out in a friendly manner. After breakfast it was time to leave the Brachystegia Woodland of the lower Bvumba, and climb up towards the Bunga forest.
My next sntop was the White Horse Inn. Eco-friendly or "green" travelers, whose preference is to walk rather than to drive, will have many opportunities to roam the lush forest surrounding White Horse Inn. Several calories may be expended climbing the nearby Castle Beacon, from whose summit on a clear day you can discern the Indian Ocean. The Bvumba Mountains are teeming with birdlife, and the White Horse Inn's resident birder, Robert Masvongwe, will take you on the ultimate bird safari. Traversing Miombo woodland, ravines and cliffs, you may encounter Hoopoes, Yellow-Bellied Sunbirds and Lanner Falcons. You may even spot the rare Swynnerton's Robin, which twitchers from as far afield as Europe have sought out when visiting the Bvumba.
Returning to base, enjoy tea and scones in the Inn's beautiful garden, ablaze with azaleas, clivia, wisteria and tree orchids. Dinner is a five star affair, a gourmet experience to be savoured with a bottle of wine from the extensive wine cellar. Do not oversleep the next morning, as breakfast is something special. Fruit juices are freshly squeezed, and there is a splendid selection of homemade jams and marmalades made from locally grown fruit, in addition to a full English breakfast.
While you may not save the planet by holidaying in Zimbabwe, by cutting out the gas-guzzling air miles you'll minimize air travel carbon emissions and be well on the way to becoming an ethical traveller.
Sustainable holidays are about to become an important aspect of the travel market, so make a start by visiting the Inn on the Vumba and the White Horse Inn, in the Bvumba Mountains.
Inn on the Vumba
$15,420 per person per night sharing, d,b&b (until end Sept)
Tel: 263 20 60722
White Horse Inn
$22,000 per night, for two people, d,b&b (until end Sept)
Tel: 263 20 60138

Original_Search_Title: Mandela Was Never a 'Terrorist'
Headline: Kenya: Mandela Was Never a 'Terrorist'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A few days before the world got taken up with the fifth anniversary of the September 11 attacks in the USA years ago, in the UK, the leader of the opposition Conservative Party, closed the book on a shameful period in its history.
Mr David Cameron, fresh from a meeting with the grand old man of South African politics, former President Nelson Mandela, wrote an article in The Observer dramatically denouncing former Conservative Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher for having branded the African National Congress as "terrorists" and opposing sanctions against the apartheid regime in South Africa.
Describing Mandela as "one of the greatest men alive," Cameron wrote: "The fact that there is so much to celebrate in the new South Africa is not in spite of Mandela and the ANC, it is because of them - and we Conservatives should say so clearly today."
For those who weren't yet born, Dame - then Mrs - Thatcher, in alliance with American President Ronald Reagan, championed a policy of "constructive engagement" with the racist regime in Pretoria to urge reform on a government which they saw as a bulwark against Soviet-backed socialism.
To the great annoyance of the Anti-Apartheid Movement, Thatcher described the ANC as "terrorists". In 1987, she said that anyone who believed the ANC would ever rule South Africa was "living in cloud-cuckoo-land."
How the Lady must be eating humble pie! Now we shouldn't let Prime Minister Tony Blair's disastrous adventure in Iraq blind us to the fact that as a younger man, he and his former Foreign minister Jack Straw, were leading anti-apartheid activists. On that one, Blair has been vindicated.
While Cameron serenaded Mandela, from the US came the story of another great human being: Jane Tomlinson, a 42-year-old woman who completed an epic 6,720-km ride across the US.
It might not be much, if you consider that at about the same time, Mr Edward Genochio, a 29-year-old British writer and web designer completed a 41,600-km journey to become the first person to cycle solo from Britain to China and back. Young Genochio started out on his adventure in 2004.
The difference is that Mrs Tomlinson, whose journey lasted 63 days, was diagnosed with terminal cancer six years ago and told she had only six years to live.
However, reports The Times, she was determined to prove that a diagnosis of cancer did not mean the end of an active life.
Since then she has completed a full ironman event - a 4km swim, 180km bike ride and full marathon - in less than 17 hours and run the London Marathon three times. She has also completed the New York Marathon and three London triathlons.
I didn't want to bring this up, but I can't help but say we Africans don't get up to these kinds of things. I wonder why.
The Times also reported that a powder-puff dog named Bebe became the first pet in New York to be awarded a domestic protection order.
Bebe is Mr Derek Lopez's dog. His ex-boyfriend, Mr Fredrick Fontanez, went to Lopez's apartment, to dog-sit Bebe. He seems to have become frustrated at some point and kicked Bebe so hard, neighbours heard the dog yelping. The animal suffered minor injuries.
The judge ordered Fontanez to stay at least 100 metres from Lopez and Bebe.
New York recently joined the states of Maine and Vermont in enacting laws specifically empowering courts to issue protection orders for animals. Fontanez faces up to a year in prison and a $1,000 (Sh7,200) fine if convicted of torturing and injuring an animal.
In Oklahoma, the laws are even stricter. People who make "ugly faces" at dogs may be fined or jailed.
And talking of bad guys, last week the BBC reported that Nigeria's anti-corruption body made its largest single seizure of illegally acquired assets belonging to a dead electoral official and pastor.
The $55m (Sh7.5bn) estate of Timothy Olufemi Akanni came to the attention of the authorities after his two widows fought over who should inherit it.
It was only after his death in a plane crash last October that it was discovered he had a secret second wife. The ex-electoral commission director's assets now pass to the state.
The BBC's Sola Odunfa in Lagos said Mr Akanni, who was a Pentecostal Church pastor in Abuja, was eulogised at his death for being a patriotic and selfless Nigerian.
The posthumous discovery of his second wife and fortune has shocked many of his friends and family. That's not uncommon on this fair continent. What's unusual is that even governments that are portrayed as the least tolerant of corruption rarely seize crooks' assets.
Also, from the neighbourhood of the Artur brothers, Kyrgyz's former opposition Speaker Moure Tekebayev was detained last week at Warsaw airport in Poland after authorities found 600 grams of heroin hidden inside a wooden Russian doll in his luggage. Tekebayev insisted he had been framed. A very familiar defence.
So we shall end as we started, with another story about admirable men and women. A few days ago Bob Mathias died aged 75.
Old sports hands, of course, know Mathias as a great Olympian. As the Daily Telegraph tells it, in the early summer of 1948, Mathias's athletics coach at his high school in California suggested that he take up the decathlon, having been impressed by his winning performances in the discus and shot putt in school competitions.
Two weeks later, he was taking part in his first 10-discipline event, at the Southern Pacific Games. He had never thrown a javelin, pole-vaulted, attempted the long jump or run 1,500 metres. He won easily.
Later that month, he travelled across America to take part in the national athletics championships in New Jersey. He defeated the three-time victor, Irv Mondschein, and found himself on the boat to London.
When he arrived at Wembley, Mathias was greeted by heavy rain and wind. His technique, too, was found wanting by the experts: "He gripped the spear like a guy killing a chicken," wrote one journalist. "He went over the vault like a guy falling out of a moving car, and his high jump looked like a guy leaving a banana peel."
Two months earlier, when his coach had suggested he take up the decathlon, Mathias had replied: "Sounds great. What's the decathlon?" Eight weeks later, he won the Olympics title.
Mr Onyango-Obbo is Nation Media Group's managing editor for Convergence and New Products.

Original_Search_Title: Sexual Violence, Lack of Healthcare Spreads HIV/Aids Among Pygmies
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Sexual Violence, Lack of Healthcare Spreads HIV/Aids Among Pygmies
Body_Text:
Chombo — The forest people of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), marginalised at the best of times, were easy targets for the marauding soldiers and militiamen that reduced the eastern provinces to one of the world's bloodiest war zones.
Rape was a weapon of war in the decade-long conflict, with the local civilian population the overwhelming victims of the fighting and chaos that sucked in combatants from throughout the region.
Over a million Congolese are HIV-positive, according to the United Nation's Children's Fund (Unicef), but there is next to no data on the country's isolated pygmy communities. The minority rights group, Refugees International, estimated in 2000 that there were about 80,000 pygmies, also known as the Batwa.
The pygmies of South Kivu Province say there was little, if any, HIV/AIDS in their community before the war. HIV prevalence is still believed to be lower among them than in the wider Congolese society, but their poverty, social isolation and lack of healthcare services means little help is available for those who contract the virus.
Kahuzi-Biega National Park with its world-famous silver-backed gorillas, once home to the pygmies' forefathers, has now become the base of the Interahamwe militia who perpetrated the 1994 Rwandan genocide and are one of the main groups vying for control of eastern DRC's mineral and logging wealth.
Over the years the villagers of Chombo, a Batwa village set in lush green banana groves downhill from the park, have come to know, and fear, the Interahamwe's demands for food, labour or sex.
The militia came upon Chiza Mwemdena, 36, and a mother of three, when she was tilling the fields near the village in 2002. "I looked up, saw them and ran as fast I could, but they caught me," she said.
"There were about 50 soldiers and I think about 30 took it in turns to rape me. It happened to all the women who were stolen." The Interahamwe kept her for two weeks before she managed to escape. When she returned to the village her husband abandoned her, ashamed of his wife's fate.
"It was four years after the rape that I started to feel very strange," Mwemdena said, stroking her bony arms. "My urine was different; my stomach was very painful and I had no energy in my limbs. I used to be very big but now look at me, I am so thin."
Mwemdena was tested for HIV in 2004 by a local nongovernmental organisation, Union Pour l'Emancipation de la Femme Autochtones (UEFA). She was positive, and she is not alone. According to Salome Ndavuma, 28, four Chombo villagers have died since May, three of them women raped by the Interahamwe.
One of them was Mwemdena's sister. "It's an awful way to die. We know when it's AIDS; all the parts of the body stop working. We are very worried about what might happen to the village if it spreads, but what can we do?"
British medical journal The Lancet published a paper in June arguing that the Batwa had consistently worse access to healthcare than neighbouring communities, in a country the size of Western Europe.
"Even where healthcare facilities exist, many people do not use them because they cannot pay for consultations and medicines, do not have the documents and identity cards needed to travel or obtain hospital treatment, or are subjected to humiliating and discriminatory treatment," it said.
Antiretroviral (ARV) drugs are a rare find for any HIV-positive Congolese, but The Lancet's evidence suggests that if ARV provision is ever to reach Batwa communities, special outreach measures will be required.
For those who contract HIV, Chombo is not a good place to be. Landless after being evicted from their ancestral forests to make way for the national park, most villagers are undernourished, and poor hygiene gives opportunistic infections lots of scope.
"AIDS is a sickness that costs lots of money," said UEFA's Esperance Binyke. "You need good food. The pygmies eat once a day - if that - and that means that they die very quickly."
Access to healthcare is also very poor. Chiza Mwemdena was given medication, but only until the local UEFA's funding ran out, and soon her stomach cramps returned. The nearest free ARV programme is run by the international medical charity, Medecins Sans Frontieres, but that is a prohibitive 25km walk and taxi ride from Chombo.
Marhegane Lukhera, a Chombo elder, believes the first villager contracted HIV in 1998, probably from visiting a sex worker, and died in 2002. Now, he says, villagers are more prepared to openly discuss the problem of HIV.
"We teach the children from 12 years old not to have sex; we teach the girls not to take money for sex," he said in the village's only classroom. "The chief gathers us men together and advises us to stay with our wives, and his wife counsels women not to go outside with other men."
"I think we realise that it's important for everyone here to become aware of AIDS," Lukhera added. "We need to educate our children and test the adults."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Yesterday's Villains Are Today's Heroes
Headline: Kenya: Yesterday's Villains Are Today's Heroes
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya is a hostage state, it can now be confirmed. The hostages are 30 million Kenyans and the hostage-takers are political mandarins in ODM-Kenya and Narc-Kenya, the two dominant political forces that now suffocate the political landscape.
Nowhere is this clearer than in the bizarre, perverted, and twisted logic for the so-called minimum constitutional reforms ahead of the 2007 elections. The country's political class believes that it can do almost anything against Kenyans, no matter how foolish and destructive, and get away with it. This is what is called a culture of impunity. For almost two decades, Kenyans have relentlessly fought for a new democratic constitution in the belief that it would end impunity and autocracy. But at every turn, the political elite has thwarted the people's demand for a new constitution.
Former President Moi and his Kanu cohorts, later joined by Mr Raila Odinga and the now defunct National Development Party, successfully fended off constitutional reform until 2002. One of Mr Moi's last acts in office was to scuttle the review process by dissolving Parliament just as the National Constitutional Conference was about to commence.
Political greed and myopia
Then enter President Kibaki and Narc. In a bitter struggle for power, both LDP and NAK sabotaged Bomas because of political greed, myopia, and skulduggery. Then Mr Justice Aaron Ringera, in a convenient, though well-argued judgment, saved the day for Mr Kibaki in the Njoya case.
Last year, the Wako Draft, itself a doctored version of the Bomas Draft, went down in flames at the referendum. But the referendum campaigns by both the Banana and Orange factions of the elite were an object lesson in hypocrisy, anti-reform, and hateful lies and propaganda.
Now, almost a year later, the same principle actors are at it again kicking up dust about constitutional reforms. The only difference is that this is Round Three between these protagonists. Bomas was Round One and the referendum Round Two.
To be sure, there have been some realignment, but the script remains the same, as do the motivations of the key factions. It is just the same old tired song, with one or two new verses to give the impression that this is a new, serious, and credible effort.
One side paints the other as obstructionist while other accuses their opponents as hypocritical. Except one thing is strange: most of those calling for minimum constitutional reforms are marked by an undeniable history of anti-reform.
One interesting fact is that most of the genuine constitutional reform advocates in the past decade - both in civil society and those in government - are opposed to minimum reforms. In contrast, those calling for minimum reforms are mostly in ODM-Kenya, and have generally been opposed to a new democratic constitution since the 1990s.
Although civil society has largely severed its relationship with the Kibaki state, it has stuck to its historical demand: comprehensive constitutional review or nothing. In this, civil society has taken the same position as the Kibaki side by coincidence, not co-ordination. This is the same position that civil society took in the IPPG debacle of 1997. But it is the political histories of those calling for minimum reforms that is red meat for analysts. Consider the leading lights of ODM-Kenya.
Anyone with a brain can remember where Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, Mr William Ruto, Mr Mutula Kilonzo, Mr Henry Kosgey, and Mr Musalia Mudavadi stood in the long struggle for a democratic constitution. These men stood steadfastly with the Moi-Kanu regime as it brutally persecuted reformers.
Prof Anyang' Nyong'o, an ODM-Kenya leader, has a more consistent record of reform, although lately he has lost his independence to Mr Odinga. But this is mainly due to the matrix of Luo politics which Mr Odinga rules with an iron hand, and out of which Prof Anyang' Nyong'o would be summarily excluded were he to stray from ODM-Kenya.
The other curious supporter of minimum reforms is Mr Paul Muite. He opposed IPPG in 1997, but today supports a similar effort because he wants to position himself from the Kibaki regime.
Kanu's Uhuru Kenyatta is caught in a quandary between ODM-Kenya and Mr Moi. That is why he is doing a yo-yo dance in the middle. He is a decent man, but not a reformer. A Moi protégé, he is wondering what the Moi-Kibaki co-operation against ODM-Kenya means for his ambitions. That is why he supports minimum reforms, but is unsure whether he should allow ODM-Kenya to swallow up Kanu.
Thoroughly discredited
The other heroes of yesterday have either squandered their stellar histories or become pathetic apologists for a failed regime. Some, like Mr Kiraitu Murungi were driven out in ignominy. Prof Kivutha Kibwana, once the voice of reform, is today not recognisable. No one can make sense of where Dr Mukhisa Kituyi, the once articulate Young Turk, is headed.
Mrs Charity Ngilu, the one-time darling of feminists and reformers, is a sad shadow of her former self. Ditto for Mr Musikari Kombo. Ms Martha Karua, a decorated gender warrior, is today an embarrassing defender of Kibaki's regime.
The truth is that as between ODM-Kenya and Narc-Kenya, the people of Kenya are caught in a fix. Both factions of the elite are thoroughly discredited, and none intends to institute true constitutional reforms.
That is why the clamour for minimum reforms must be rejected. For how long does Kenya's political class think it can continue to hoodwink the public, even as the country's slides further into lawlessness and poverty? It is time to think outside the box.
Prof Mutua, who chairs the Kenya Human Rights Commission, teaches law at the State University, New York at Buffalo.

Original_Search_Title: Why Not Provide Alternative Leadership?
Headline: Kenya: Why Not Provide Alternative Leadership?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Rev Timothy Njoya says the options Kenyans are being offered by the two warring political blocs are between evil and evil. There is no option of a lesser evil, he says.
The good man says an alternative political leadership for Kenya is imaginable and possible. But who will constitute that leadership? Who will spearhead it? Has the time for such an alternative come?
I address Prof Wangari Maathai because I am convinced she is part of that alternative political leadership. I also address her because I believe she has constantly refused to be the pivotal point of this leadership.
I also think that through her, we can concretise that debate about alternative political leadership.
Let us take the issue of constitutional reforms. Neither of the two political blocs wants a comprehensive constitutional review. If either was serious, would it not be easy to agree on minimum reforms? If they were serious, would it not be possible to agree on the contentious issues in both the Bomas and Wako drafts?
Imperial presidential powers
If, after this, there were still areas they did not agree on, should they not consider subjecting them to a referendum during the 2007 elections so that the issue of constitution-making is put to a close?
Mr John Githongo is right. Imperial presidential powers in the Constitution are what each bloc wants. None want those powers dismantled, but each wants a level playing field to freely, fairly and peacefully compete for that prize. And the so-called international community agrees so that its interests can be protected.
Does anyone believe that the US would want its military interests to be subjected to even the narrow democratic oversight that Parliament could exercise? Are Kenyans blind to how the ruling elite protects itself? Have we not seen how this elite, since 2003, has come together to stifle reforms and promote the ideology of ethnicity to ensure issue-oriented politics never take root in Kenya?
Look at how well the elite is represented in both warring political blocs. Look at how the reformers of yesteryear have been enslaved by the elite.
The elite is multi-racial, multi-ethnic, multi-religious and multi-regional. It is well resourced, vicious and intimidating. It also has smart think-tanks. The imperial presidency is, therefore, the political weapon of the ruling elite.
Prof Maathai has been known for her "middle-ground" crusades. She can, I hope, graduate from these crusades to thinking about alternative political leadership.
The middle-ground crusades are what the Rev Njoya has dismissed as options between evil and evil. There simply is no middle ground between the two warring blocs. It is all political lies, hypocrisy, and negative racial and ethnic politics to hoodwink Kenyans.
Prof Maathai has a legacy of pro-people protests and courageous agitation for human rights and social justice. She is the Kenyan version of India's Vandana Shiva. Her reform credentials nationally, regionally and internationally are impeccable. Foreign interests in this country would expect a principled contribution from her, and not just subservience.
Yet she, at times, comes through as politically ensnared by her Tetu constituency that literally forced her to eat bananas on the eve of the referendum. Her great constituency of reformers in Kenya and the world over does not matter to her. Or so it seems.
The idea of an alternative political leadership is here with us. The warring political blocs do not represent that idea. Prof Maathai does. And there may be others like her who are afraid of these blocs and stay enslaved by the ruling elite.
Prof Maathai, look beyond those warring blocs and behold the masses of women and youth who are crying out for alternative leadership that will destroy corruption and de-marginalise regions, communities and minorities, while using the nation's resources in the interests of the majority of Kenyans.
Courageous soldiers of change
Look at the multi-racial and multi-ethnic middle class in Kenya that resents the misuse of resources and wants an alternative political leadership. Look at the potential constituency waiting to be mobilised into courageous soldiers of change.
Do not reject this leadership. Take it up and start answering the burning questions that I have posed here. Take up the challenges that this project poses. You have the courage and you have shown before that you can sacrifice your life for the welfare of Kenyans.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Has Demeaned the Presidency - Isyaku Ibrahim
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Has Demeaned the Presidency - Isyaku Ibrahim
Body_Text:
Isyaku Ibrahim, a frontline politician has said that the attack on the Vice President by President Obasanjo amounts to demeaning the office of the presidency. He spoke to Emmanuel Bello. excerpts:
Since the Atiku impeachment saga started, what has been your thoughts on the matter?
First of all, my thinking on this subject is that President Obasanjo has revealed an ignorance to the way the presidential system operates. He is just there and does not understand the nation's constitution. His Attorney General inclusive. Because if they had read the constitution properly they should have come to section 143 whereby it says that he (Obasanjo) can not initiate an impeachment proceedings against the Vice President and vice versa. When you read the EFCC report on Atiku, it is replete with contradictions. There is nothing specifically that shows that the VP has breached the constitution. So, he has sent it to the National Assembly and I'm sure the distinguished senators are brilliant to decipher the document. The sad thing in all these is that President Obasanjo wants to reduce our country to a banana republic. We have a president who betrays an ignorance of the nation's constitution, personalizing everything for his selfish aim. He does not even respect our sov ereignty. He is demeaning the presidency. Otherwise, I can't understand how a department in the USA, the FBI, will communicate with the EFCC (which they erroneously believe they are one) to find out what the VP knows about an issue. A simple memo from the presidency would have done it. But now it is been blown out of proportion. We are a sovereign nation, for God sake! No department of any nation has the right to bring our respected institution into disrepute. The president did not protect the nation's sovereignty because of his quarrels with his vice. For that reason, and why,? because Atiku withstood the third term project. Now, the president wants to drag the whole system with him to the lowest level. It is sad.
What is your view on the president's war on corruption?
In the first place, who established the anti-corruption outfits? The President. And for me, personally, the president hasn't got the moral grounds to fight corruption. He is not qualified or fit to wage the war. He knows this very well and if he decides to deceive himself, fine and good! You cannot fight corruption unless if your hands are clean. Let me tell you a story and I want Nigerians to note this. I had the privilege of meeting an English man by name Raymond Archer who had worked in the Barclays Banks (later Union Bank). He retired to become a consultant for the Beirut/Riyald Bank. That is how I came to know him. We had lunch and he told me an outstanding story. He said to me: "You Nigerians, both Muslims and Christians, you have to be praying for forgiveness." I asked him why and he told me that he was the one in charge of the personal account of Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa. He said: "When you killed the Prime Minister, he had only 25 pounds in his account and I was in charge. If you kill your leaders like that, you need God's mercy or else your country won't move forward." Now, we have a leader in this country whom there are speculations that he has a 200 million naira worth of shares in Transcorp. What's the meaning of that? And he feels he cannot or does not have the moral responsibilities to explain to Nigerians how he has such shares. Then he parades himself as a fighter for transparency and accountability. I'll like him to educate me whether it is true he has those shares. If he is the owner of the shares, he owes me an explanation of how he got them. Otherwise the should shut up on his war on corruption unless he explains how he came about owning 200 million shares in Transcorp.
Are you suggesting that because the President is not entirely clean, that entitles Atiku to corrupt practices?
I beg your pardon. I did not say the vice president has the right to be corrupt. What I,m saying is that if at all there are charges against Atiku, there are due process. It is not for Obasanjo alone to take action and say the vice should leave the executive council meeting. He does not have that sort of a right. The members of the FEC, apart from Atiku are all appointees of the government. Atiku, on the other hand, was elected and given a mandate by Nigerians. Obasanjo is a president today because of Atiku's goodwill. He knows it. Atiku practically made Obasanjo president. Obasanjo is there because of the goodwill of Atiku who is a founding member of the PDP. It is not the other way round. Obasanjo knows the history. And it is wrong for him to ask Atiku out. He and Atiku are the only two people on that council who have the mandate of Nigerians. It was therefore wrong for Obasanjo to violate our constitution and embarrass Atiku before appointees. Millions of your (interviewer ) generation don't have this information and I'm saying that Obasanjo is a president today because of Atiku.
What are the wider implications of the president sending out his deputy from a FEC meeting?
There should not have been such a thing if not for the fact that the president is personalizing matters. He should not ask the vice to leave, when he knows that in normal circumstance, it is Atiku who will preside in the president's absence. Now, you try to bring your vice low before your political appointees. It is wrong. Our constitution has not given Obasanjo such powers. He is not insulting Atiku. He is insulting the mandate of the Nigerian people. He is telling Nigerians that, he, President Obasanjo is not conversant with our constitution and that he is alien to the concept of the "substance of power." It is sad to ask an elected vice president to walk out in the glare of appointees. He is creating a bad image before the world. You see, presidency is an institution, a revered one at that. It should be highly respected.
On the contrary sir, the president's war on corruption is giving the country a good image, especially as it is obvious the president is prepared to deal with anyone on this issue?
It is a mockery and it makes nonsense of the whole issue. I just asked a simple question, can the president explain how he got 200 million Trabscorp shares? Can he, in all good conscience, explain that? Will his friends around the world, Tony Blair and Bush have inexplicable shares? As far as he is concerned, he is not abreast with the conflict of interest. That is why he could acquire such shares.
But even you sir, have to admit that the president has heightened the fight on corruption. Unlike past leaders, he has distinguished himself in this sector, by not taking huge loans
What are you talking about? When a nation takes a loan it is an act of negotiation. If loan taking is corruption, then Obasanjo started the trend. When he was Head of State, he took a loan of 2 billion dollars. Jumbo loans! General Gowon left 8 billions in the account and Obasanjo cornered that money. They could not account for it. 8 billions were squandered and they took 2 billion jumbo loans. Even recently he took more loans from the Chinese government. So, before he leaves, he is taking more loans. Me, I don't talk about a nation taking loans; perhaps they have need for it. But when you come to tell me that corruption has reduced, what about the INEC official who died with 7 billions in his account. Tafa Balogun got 17 billions. Under whose regimes are all these happening? How can you explain when all these are going on? Has Obasanjo declared his asset?
In the second republic, we never heard of this type of relationship between Shagari and Ekwueme, what was the secret?
Both Shagari and Dr. Ekwueme were statesmen who went into power to effect changes. They were not greedy folks and I supported Shagari because he was a fine man of sterling qualities. They had a different code then and were true leaders. Shagari got his nomination at the convention because of his qualities. After four years, he did not leave with anything. Dr. Alex Ekwueme was a well-made man., He had his money before coming into politics. He was not there to acquire money and when he left, he lost more than he got.

Original_Search_Title: Sata Adopts New Campaign Strategy
Headline: Zambia: Sata Adopts New Campaign Strategy
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Patriotic Front president Michael Sata has changed his campaign tactics and opted to stay in Luapula, Northern, Eastern, then Copperbelt provinces and would only resurface in Lusaka a day before elections.
Before leaving for Chilubi Island on Lake Bangweulu and Malungu, Chisamba, Bwalya Mponda and Kalima Nkonde islands on Thursday, Sata said he was doing the last mop-ups traversing rugged terrain, the bush, water and heading for remote areas. Sata said he would not give MMD a chance this year and that victory was certain.
"I feel sorry for President Mwanawasa but I can't help it," he said. Sata said he would go to some places President Mwanawasa had never been to and rally behind his supporters and that his last national rally would be in Lusaka on September 27 after holding one in Kitwe.
"If by that time he (President Mwanawasa) wouldn't be sick, then I don't know. I won't give them space this time," said Sata. On Thursday, several people lined up the swamps leading to Kasoma Lungu Islands shouting his name with some mistaking him for a Republican President.
