Sata, who travelled by banana boat, nearly abandoned the mission to campaign on Lake Bangweulu Islands after the engine failed to start.
The coxswain struggled with the boat before it could start. Sata is expected to pass through some of the worst and unforgiving terrains in Zambia as he connects through Isoka's Muyombe area to exit through Chama in Eastern Province. And thousands of people turned up for Sata on Wednesday at a rally in Samfya boma.
Sata wondered what type of people Reverend Dan Pule and Ben Mwila were who would abandon leadership of their parties and show allegiance to another President who had not even adopted them to stand as members of parliament.
Sata said instead of Rev Pule building a church in Samfya as a man of God, he went to build a lodge on the shore.
He said it was strange that even Mwila, who people were supposed to look forward to as a saviour could relegate himself to a level where he stands as member of parliament.
"It's just money, which is working. If they buy people who are supposed to look after you, who are you?" he asked. Sata said President Mwanawasa's government arrested him on several occasions with the hope that he could be blocked from filing in his nominations but that did not work.
"Without Northern Province and Luapula, he would not even know what leadership is," he said. "Mwanawasa, the way he has kept us, it's like step children. It's because he did not come here to tell you what he would do for us."
Sata said people had suffered long enough and could not afford to be illiterate this time.
Sata said once in power, he would scrap off basic schools and make them high schools, reduce tax for people with six children and above, reduce tax for those with twins and those getting below K1 million.
"It's up to you. If you are not feeling the poverty vote for Mwanawasa," he said. Sata said currently, people were so poor that they even begged from strangers.
Later in an interview, Sata said he had received reports that some security companies were confiscating voters' cards from the guards. Sata said apart from reporting the security firms to police and ECZ, they should stop because PF would deal with them firmly after elections as they were hindering democracy.
Sata also complained that police had been confiscating tapes and CDs containing his campaign songs being played by traders in Lusaka.
He said though some could be pirating his music, he had no copyright and should therefore be allowed to play the music. "That music is not making noise as some bars are making. That is a direct political manoeuvre of the MMD. The Inspector General of Police should not drift into behaving unprofessionally. So far he has behaved well," said Sata.

Original_Search_Title: Union Spreads Its Creative Wings
Headline: Namibia: Union Spreads Its Creative Wings
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The Oruuano Artists Union is currently involved in an ongoing music development programme for pre-school teachers in acquiring guitar skills for the National Early Childhood Development NGO Association in the capital.
"Taking into account that this was the first workshop specifically for kindergartens, I am very pleased with the progress in basic skills-training of the sixteen participating individual teachers. In fact, the project will continue in November," said music tutor and president of the Oruuano Artists Union, Banana Shekupe.
His union had been formally approached by the NEDCD-NGO Association to assist in its activities, which include music and movement in pre-schools.
"The workshop is proving so popular and important that we are considering also staging it in the regions, finances permitting. Obviously we have to find a sponsor to continue with the project in other places. I see it as a very important creative outreach programme, which can serve the nation in one of the most important sectors of education - pre-schooling," said Shekupe.
According to him, many of the participants in the workshop have no formal qualifications, but they have a wealth of natural talent to acquire the necessary guitar-playing skills.
Shekupe also staged a theatre and acting workshop at Walvis Bay recently.
"The idea was to strengthen and enrich the local community theatre groups at the harbour town. There are many actors who need to be trained and supported to ply their skills on stage and in films, many international ones being shot at the coast," Shekupe said.
He further stated that his union is looking at possible ways to work more closely together with the Namibia Broadcasting Corporation (NBC) television to assist in this mammoth task.
"There is even interest for the making of short movies by the theatre group. This is an excellent creative idea that needs to be developed," said Shekupe of the four-day workshop in which he was assisted by students of the College of the Arts.

Original_Search_Title: 'They Tricked Us'
Headline: Senegal: 'They Tricked Us'
Body_Text:
Dakar — A record number of illegal migrants reached the shores of the Spanish Canary Islands last weekend. Authorities reported that between Saturday and Sunday, 1,400 migrants, mainly from West Africa, arrived in eight canoe-style fishing boats that they believe left from Mauritania.
The longer and riskier maritime route to the Canaries became increasingly popular this year after the shorter route through Morocco was cut off by increased patrols. Another route from North Africa into Sicily was stymied by Italian navy patrols.
The journey from Senegal to the Canaries is approximately 1,500 km and can take anywhere from five to 20 days. Upon arrival, dehydrated and exhausted, those who have made the crossing face the possibility of repatriation to their respective countries.
Yet despite the risks, many young West Africans, frustrated by the limited opportunities at home and tempted by the stories of those who have made it, borrow from friends and family to pay for their passage.
The sandy alleys of Yarakh, an old fishing quarter in Dakar, are dotted with modest houses of varying styles. In front of the homes, women tend to small businesses and unemployed youths loiter about. With the waters depleted of fish, few young Senegalese consider following in the footsteps of their fathers as fishermen.
They do, however, look to the sea.
This is the first of a series of three profiles of Senegalese migrants.
GALASS LETTE, 26
Galass Lette had heard the stories. The risk, he decided, was worth it. In Tenerife, they told him, there was plenty of work and banana fields as far as the eye can see. If not on the fields, then perhaps he could get a job on one of the many construction sites. In Spain, he was told, there is work to be had.
If he could get a job, he thought, he could help his mother with money. Then, he thought, he could go beyond the Canary Islands, onward to more opportunity, to the United States.
"When your mother's means are very limited, and she is supporting children who barely knew their deceased father, it begins to weigh heavily on you, especially when you are the eldest," he says. "Without any assurance of work there is nothing left to do. And I've always wanted to go to the United States, and barring that, Europe, to make my life there," he said.
Word eventually reached Galass that some men were organising a boat trip for the Canary Islands. Galass lacked the means to pay the fare but his family got together to help him. An aunt had put aside nearly US $2,000.
"It was enough to send three of us," he said.
Galass, his younger brother and cousin paid traffickers for a place on a boat to the Canary Islands. In preparation, Galass said the traffickers consulted a clairvoyant to ensure a safe trip.
"Everyone departing does this to see if the conditions for the voyage will be favourable. But what we didn't do was consult the internet to really understand what the conditions would be like," he said.
Although the voyage was rough, Galass and his companions made it. But the journey ended there.
"The Spanish police were sympathetic towards us," he said. "They said that they would prefer it if we Senegalese stayed and worked to help them catch up economically, the way Italy did using illegal migrants. They advised us that the minute we got out, to just work and not get involved in anything shady. They told us that we were qualified professionals and could succeed here," he said.
Galass was among the first 99 Senegalese to be repatriated this year. He and the others were put on a plane that they believed was bound for Madrid or Malaga, but that landed in Dakar instead.
"We started to realise that the flight was too long, and that the Spanish might have tricked us, which turned out to be the case," he said.
Galass said the migrants heckled Senegalese authorities when they arrived. "I don't trust them anymore, especially since all their promises meant nothing. The minister of National Solidarity promised us money but the hoops you had to jump through to see the money discouraged us," he said.
Although Galass failed in his journey, his brother and an uncle eventually succeeded and now live in Barcelona. "They have already sent home their first shipment of money so that we can pray for them and make offerings to bless their stay," he said.
Galass works in Dakar making deliveries for a local business but is paid daily and some days there is no work. Otherwise, an uncle gives him a bit of work at a sugar processing company.
"I don't think about becoming a fisherman," he said. "If that was an option, you wouldn't have all these boat owners going over to Spain."
Galass says he is prepared to make the journey again, even though his cousin died attempting the trip.
"How else can I help my family? And what about my aunt who paid for my trip? Nothing can hold me back," he said. "I'm just waiting for the next departure."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Personality Cult Stifling Democracy
Headline: Kenya: Personality Cult Stifling Democracy
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Looking at 2007 we, of course, have to use the crystal ball, although we know a lot, or at least we think so. I am willing to bet that we will see a fast growing economy next year. The export sector will continue to lead. The main internal, economic and social problem will continue to be the weak domestic demand, caused by the glaring inequalities and the widespread poverty.
Africa will continue with reforms, increased growth, intensified regional collaboration and spreading of democracy, that is a fair guess. In Kenya, elections will dominate the political scene and debate.
Will next year's election constitute another leap of democratisation? We don't know. Do we get any guidance from looking in the rear mirror? I think so.
It is easy to see that Kenyans are increasingly aware of their rights and power to influence politics. The 2002 election was important, a milestone - Kenya at the crossroads was making a bold decision. But it was the beginning of a transformation, we have to remember that, it was not the transformation.
The referendum process represented another step in deepening and fermenting Kenyan democracy. I am not so naïve as to believe that the campaigns in 2002 and 2005 were free from excesses, lies, propaganda and from fear factors. Having said that, we did witness discussion at work places, small and "big debates" in media, civic education of considerable insight and impact.
There were local discussions during the referendum campaign about the role of chiefs, about the possibility to get one's voice heard at local and national levels, about the power of the president and about control of land. The by-elections in 2006 seemed to be a step backward; at least there are numerous reports on vote buying and violence.
But progress has not embraced women, half of the people. Although more women than before were both nominated and elected to Parliament in 2002, there are clear backlash tendencies for women.
The referendum process was an alarm signal. One reason for many men to vote No to the proposed constitution seemed to be the fear of women making claims on land. The right of everybody to inherit land already exists in the Kenyan laws but it was clearly spelt out in the proposed constitution that these rights also include women. This scared many men. And their No vote was a vote against the rights of women. Quite understandably, women today own only 5 per cent of the land in the country.
Women continued to be marginal in major events. Oranges, Bananas and now Narc Kenya and ODM Kenya all display male parades. The new Government of January 2006 digressed rather than progressed as female Cabinet ministers went down from three to two. The new ODM Kenya structure is in the same deplorable league: 18 out of 19 leaders are men!
There is a risk that the number of women in Parliament will go down in 2007. According to a recent study, qualified women in urban areas shy away from participating in politics because they do not want to be subject to hooliganism, harassment and deceit.
Democracy needs not only democrats, and more women, but also stronger parties. In all likelihood we will see a mushrooming of new parties the coming year. To what extent do present and future political parties base their participation, in coalitions or movements, on programmes and principles, not on personalities and power?
That parties differ is not remarkable, the idea with parties is that they should reflect various opinions and groups. That coalitions break up is not remarkable either. But for an outside observer, the ease with which politicians move from one group to another is a rather peculiar aspect of Kenyan politics. In most other countries, an individual MP going from the opposition into government would be seen as disregarding democracy.
This is what I would wish to see happen on the political arena in Kenya during the coming 15 months:
Public disclosure of assets by candidates. There is no need for a law on that, any party has the right as part of their party constitution to decide on such an issue: If you want to run on our ticket, declare, and do it public.
Public disclosure of campaign contributions and transparency in how funds are used.
Actively increase female participation in the political arena. In Nairobi only 40 per cent of the women are registered. Women candidates should be nominated and supported by parties, not harassed, aiming at more fair representation.
An election that has its focus on ideology and issues, and respects the various roles of institutions, players, agents. Free, active and responsible media; independence for institutions overseeing the election process; fair and far-reaching election education and access to information.
The greatest risk I see for Kenya's future is the division of the people, by its leaders. The risk is obvious, and it is a risk with enormous costs.
Kenyans, like all other people, want democracy. There is no doubt about the will of the people in Africa; two thirds prefer democracy to other forms of government according to a recent survey. There is no doubt that Kenya and Kenyans are forerunners in this. But to stay in the forefront, Kenya needs not only more democrats but stronger political parties - and more women in politics. On the issue on minimum versus comprehensive review, why not prioritise reforms that guarantee women better chance in the election process and a higher representation in Parliament?
Mr Goransson is Swedish Ambassador to Kenya.

Original_Search_Title: EFCC Seizes $54 Million Assets of Dead Elections Commission Director
Headline: Nigeria: EFCC Seizes N7 Billion Assets of INEC Dead Director
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) has secured a court order, confiscating alleged ill-gotten assets, worth about N7 billion, belonging to a deceased Director of Finance and Supplies of INEC, Pastor Timothy Olufemi Akanni.
The assets include cash in local and foreign currencies; landed properties in choice areas in both Lagos and Abuja; shares in bluechip companies; registered schools, bakeries and expensive cars, among others.
The order was granted ex-parte by the vacation judge, Justice Anwuri Chikere, upon a request by the anti-graft commission on behalf of the Federal Government.
The Commission had told the court that it was investigating Pastor Akanni for various weighty complaints touching on conspiracy, abuse of public office, diversion of public funds and money laundering.
Besides, EFCC alleged that relations of the late Akanni and the deceased's cronies who were still being investigated over some petitions were making frantic attempts to withdraw mony from the accounts of the companies sought to be forfeited, being alleged proceeds of fraud.
The anti-graft commission proffered arguments before the court on why all the assets must be confiscated, supporting same with affidavit evidence.
The high court judge ordered all the assets and properties listed on two separate schedules by EFCC belonging to the late Pastor Akanni to be temporarily forfeited to the Federal Republic of Nigeria.
The deceased director stripped of all his assets by the anti-graft commission was a pastor of a pentecostal church in Abuja until his death in the October 22 Bellview Flight 210 crash at Lisa in Ogun State. He was one of the 117 passengers that lost their lives.
The interim order confiscating his assets, two weeks ago, was one of the last set of orders granted by Justice Anwuri Chikere of the Federal High Court, Abuja before she was transferred to the Kaduna Division of the court.
Pastor Akanni was a director of INEC between 1996 and 2005 during which he allegedly acquired a huge amount of money. No petition was raised when he was in office as INEC director.
Trouble started shortly after he died when his assets were to be shared. Pastor Akanni was said to have married another woman legally without the knowledge of the only wife known to the family. The new wife who claimed to be legally married to Pastor Akanni with children showed up in Akanni's home, claiming to be his wodow.
The only wife at home never believed herself, given the fact that her husband was a respected man of God. But when the new wife tendered documentary evidence to back up her claim, the first wife known to the family vowed never to recognise her and that the alleged new wife and her children would not benefit from the deceased's assets.
All efforts to make the first wife see reason proved abortive, a development that provoked petitions to EFCC for confiscation of huge sums of money totalling one billion in two local accounts maintained by the deceased before he died.
When EFCC moved in, it was discovered that the assets the two women were fighting over were insignificant, given others owned by Pastor Akanni in and outside the shores of Nigeria.
Assets worth N7 billion were uncovered by the EFCC. Investigations are still on-going in the matter since some accounts were traced beyond the shores of the country.
But while investigations were on-going, efforts were made to tamper with the res of the petitions, a development that made the Chairman of EFCC, Mallam Nuhu Ribadu, to apply to court for confiscation of various assets already uncovered and believed to have been acquired with proceeds from ill-gotten wealth.
The uncovered assets, belonging to the deceased pastor, now confiscated, and listed in two different schedules include a house at Victoria Garden City located on Plot H 70, Road 42, VGC; a plot of land measuring 3,510square metre at Banana Island, Foreshore Estate, Ikoyi; a plot registered as 25/25/26 of plot 4, block 5, measuring 1145.5sq metre; 4 terrace houses at Parkview Estate, Lagos; property on 11, Olaosebikan street, off Alli Street, Okota; Victory Plaza located at Area 11, Garki, Abuja and two duplexes situated at 13, Mississippi street, Maitama.
Others include a two-bedroom flat at Gaduwa street, Abuja; four bedroom duplex located on Plot B 10, Prince and Princess Estate, Duboyi, Abuja; plot of land at Danube street, Maitama; two-bedroom bungalow at Plot E 16 at Prince and Princess, Duboyi, Abuja; a unit of duplex at Abuja Model City (Adkan Estate), Gwarinpa II, Abuja and four-bedroom detached duplex at Gwarinpa II, Abuja.
Also seized are his Unity High School on Plot 31, Phase 4, opposite General Hospital, Kubwa, Abuja; Unity Montessori Nursery and Primary School, Phase 4, Kubwa, Abuja; Unity Bakery in Kubwa and a property located on Plot 46, Ajose street, Ilasamaja.
Also on the schedule of seized items are his properties on Plot 342 Dakibiya {P&P}, Abuja with right of occupancy; plot of land at Gosa; plot 1031B, Kaura District in the name of Victory Bakery and Confectionary Limited; plots 3114, 3044, Asokoro extension; plot MP/54155, Mpape layout, Abuja and plot 1016 in Katampe District, Abuja.
Also temporarily forfeited are Land Rover Jeep with registration number BH 559APP; Volkswagen Touareg with registration number KF 242AAA, Lagos; Toyota Camry 2.2L automatic with chassis number 200060090; Toyota Hiace bus with chassis number 7005913; Toyota Hiace bus with chassis number 7005050 and Toyota Hiace bus with chassis number 7006224.
Frozen by the court are 23 different bank accounts (deposit and current) opened in the names of Femak Nigeria Enterprises; Victory Bakery and Confectionaries Limited; Mountain Ventures Limited; Olusegun
Akanni; Femak Nigeria; Ajakaiye Adeola Oluwakemi; Akanni T Olusegun; Unity Montessori Nursery and Primary School; Cumet Nigeria Limited and Molamos Nigeria Limited.
Shares acquired by late Pastor Olufemi Akanni with First Bank; Oceanic Bank and Zenith Bank valued at N4,556.200; N5,000,000 and N3,600,000 respectively during his lifetime among others were also confiscated.

Original_Search_Title: Shift Political Base to Nairobi, Uhuru Told
Headline: Kenya: Shift Political Base to Nairobi, Uhuru Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Gatundu South MP Uhuru Kenyatta has been asked to shift his political base to Nairobi.
ODM-Kenya leaders led by the Lang'ata MP, Mr Raila Odinga (second right), a former Vice-President, Mr Musalia Mudavadi (third right), and political activist, Mr Tony Gachoka , in a show of unity at a rally in Kibera, Nairobi, on Sunday. Picture by Rebecca Nduku
Specifically, the Kanu national chairman has been told to move to Dagoretti Constituency to take on his cousin, Education Assistant minister Beth Mugo, the incumbent.
On Sunday, Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga and other Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) Kenya leaders said it would be difficult for Uhuru to garner adequate support in his home area because of the hostility towards the new party.
Addressing a rally in Kibera's Kamukunji grounds, the former Roads minister claimed that the Internal Security minister, Mr John Michuki, had hatched a plot to remove him from his Lang'ata seat using the Provincial Administration.
However, Raila told the rowdy crowd that ODM-K leaders would not be distracted from national politics.
And that was why Raila asked Uhuru to vie for "the vacant Dagoretti parliamentary seat" in the elections.
Amid chants of Toboa! Toboa! (reveal), Raila stated: "We are playing national politics while they (Narc-Kenya)
are engaged in tribal politics. They cannot succeed. We have asked Uhuru to leave Gatundu South and take up Dagoretti."
Switching constituencies
He went on to state: "Achukue kiti ile haina mtu. Tuko na dawa yao. Mnataka tufanye hivyo? (He (Uhuru) should grab this vacant seat. We know how to handle the tribalists. Do you want us to proceed?)"
Kasipul Kabondo MP Paddy Ahenda also backed calls for Uhuru to switch constituencies.
"We have seen that Uhuru should vie in Dagoretti," Ahenda said.
Mr Musalia Mudavadi, Mr Lucas Maitha, Mr Peter Odoyo, Archbishop Stephen Ondiek, Mr Tony Gachoka and a Kajiado North parliamentary seat aspirant, a Mr Ole Mositet, also addressed the rally.
If Uhuru picks the gauntlet, he would take on his paternal cousin, Mrs Beth Mugo. Incidentally, it is Mugo's brother, Mr Ngengi Muigai, who is Uhuru's main rival in Gatundu.
Ngengi, a former assistant minister, nurses ambitions of re-capturing the seat once held by Uhuru's father, Mzee Jomo Kenyatta.
During the referendum on the draft constitution last year, Banana scooped Gatundu South with 19,848 against Orange's 11,032 votes.
Uhuru would join a list of politicians who have switched constituencies. Historically, those who have dared make the switch are known to have succeeded at first attempt.
Delay in registration
Among them is President Kibaki, who relocated from Bahati to Othaya, and Mr Norman Nyagah who vied in Kamukunji. Nyagah, then the Gachoka MP, gave way to his elder brother Joseph, the current area MP.
The late Kihika Kimani was a serial mover. His expeditions started in Laikipia in the 70s, took him to Subukia in 1997 before he attempted the Naivasha seat in 2002 and lost.
On Sunday, Mudavadi challenged the Government to explain the delay in registration for ODM-Kenya. And Raila chimed in: "We took the application on August 21. It would be 14 days on Wednesday. We will go to the Registrar of Societies to receive our certificate".
Gachoka, of the National Party of Kenya, said he would contest the Kamukunji seat on an ODM-Kenya ticket.
Maitha said ODM-Kenya members were steadfast. "Mzee ameangusha Nyavu pwani. Title deeds wanapewa. Lakini hakuna kitu imebadilika. Hajatoa kipande cha Rift Valley akawapa. (Kibaki has landed the nets. Coast people are being given title deeds. But nothing has changed. He (Kibaki) has not taken a piece of the Rift Valley land to give to them.) The land has been ours for years," stated Maitha.

Original_Search_Title: Exciting Times Ahead As Parties And Leaders Seek Realignments
Headline: Kenya: Exciting Times Ahead As Parties And Leaders Seek Realignments
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With the seeming collapse of Narc, political realignments are in top gear, and next year's General Election is bound to be a two-horse race between ODM Kenya and Narc Kenya.
Even as the scenario takes the shape of rivalries in some of the world's major democracies - Conservatives and Labour in Britain, Republicans and Democrats in the US or Christian Democrats and Social Democrats in Germany, a battle royal awaits political heavyweights in Kenya as they jostle for supremacy in the new outfits.
Due to fears that they were lagging behind their Narc-K counterparts in opinion polls, the ODM politicians recently hurriedly assembled in Lukenya in Machakos District for secret talks which culminated in the launch of the new party.
However, the Sunday Nation has since established that unease and suspicion gripped interim power sharing plans, including the nomination of regional representatives to the party's special committee.
More friction lies ahead as both ODM-K and Narc-K put their houses in order and woo other political players onto their side.
The emergence of Mr Henry Kosgey in the ODM-K leadership circles, for instance, appears to be at the expense of Kanu secretary-general William Ruto.
Initially reluctant
The Kanu Tinderet MP, who alongside his party chairman Uhuru Kenyatta was initially reluctant to join the ODM grouping, seems to have eclipsed - at least on paper - his Eldoret North counterpart.
Mr Kosgey, the chairman of the Rift Valley Parliamentary Group and Mr Kenyatta's key ally, is destined to take up the position of ODM-K chairman.
Should this happen, it would achieve two goals - soothe the rather hesitant Kenyatta camp and, at the same time, check the larger Rift Valley's growing preference for Mr Ruto.
Of course, the Ruto camp is bound to be suspicious and cautious of the development. For besides Mr Kenyatta, Mr Kosgey enjoys cordial relations with retired president Moi and Narc-K leaders, including President Kibaki.
Mr Kosgey and Mr Kibaki suffered political relegation at the hands of President Moi in 1988 - Mr Kibaki was dropped as VP, while Mr Kosgey was kicked out of the Cabinet.
Mr Kosgey, a former Education minister, is equally close to Vice-President Moody Awori, who worked under him for several years as an assistant minister.
Tension building up
In Nyanza Province, for instance, tension is already building up over the region's representatives to the ODM-K special committee. While Mugirango South MP Omingo Magara, who comes from Kisii District, appears to be agreeable to all, the nomination of Mr Dalmas Otieno has caused discomfort in some quarters.
It is unlikely that the region's political heavyweight, Mr Raila Odinga, and his allies will sit pretty with a Kanu man watching over the Luo Nyanza political base. Yet still for purposes of fair play, the Kenyatta camp insists on Mr Otieno - their sole pointman in Nyanza.
To address this concern, LDP's Dr Oburu Oginga, the Luo Nyanza Parliamentary Group chairman, has been seconded to the committee, while Mr Magara has to content with the treasurer's post.
But the entry of Mrs Charity Ngilu into ODM-K may not rekindle the old political turf wars between her and Mwingi North MP Kalonzo Musyoka. The former Foreign minister's popularity has in the recent past grown, overshadowing the Health minister's.
However, Mrs Ngilu might ruffle some feathers in Narc-K should she opt to join the party.
Just as happened during the run-up to last year's referendum when she sought the President's intervention to spearhead the Banana campaign in Ukambani, the Kitui Central West MP will have to battle it out with Water minister Mutua Katuku for supremacy in the region. The Mwala MP currently leads the area MPs in popularising Narc-K.
The possibility of Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo teaming up with ODM-K could change the political equation in Western Province. At present, former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi virtually leads ODM-K's operations in the region, assisted by Kanu vice-chairman Chris Okemo and Labour Party leader Julia Ojiambo.
It is unlikely that, as the leader of a party with a base in the province, would want to play second fiddle to the three politicians. Yet, if Mr Kombo, the Webuye MP, opts to go the Narc-K way, he has to do battle with none other than the VP, the Funyula MP.
Mr Kombo may also have to contend with his perennial political nemesis, Kimilili MP Mukhisa Kituyi, who has joined Narc-K ahead of him.
At the Coast, Bahari MP Joe Khamisi, who is perceived to be warming up to Narc-K, has to contend with the reality of playing in the wings of Tourism minister Morris Dzoro and Justice assistant minister Danson Mungatana.
The latter is a senior party operative, and it will be interesting to see how Mr Khamisi, who has resigned as LDP vice-chairman, humbles himself before him and still keep his political ambitions alive.
In ODM-K, the long-time political foes - former Mombasa mayors Najib Balala and Taib Ali Taib - are both regional representatives. But day-to-day friction notwithstanding, it will be interesting to see how the party leadership resolves the row in Mvita - a constituency being eyed also by Mr Taib.
But there is the possibility of a third major force emerging - mainly made up of middle-ground parties such as Mr Kombo's Ford-K, Ford People of Public Works minister Simeon Nyachae and Mrs Ngilu's National Party of Kenya.
This group would also attract politicians who are either eclipsed within the "big two" or hounded out altogether after failing to get political space. Such a scenario would serve as a deciding factor on who actually carries the day, depending on which group it allies itself with.
The times ahead promise to be extremely exciting.

Original_Search_Title: In Ford-K, Things Fall Apart With Former Allies Lining Up to Challenge Kombo
Headline: Kenya: In Ford-K, Things Fall Apart With Former Allies Lining Up to Challenge Kombo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Sample this. Dr Mukhisa Kituyi walks out of Ford Kenya, Cabinet colleague Soita Shitanda and assistant minister Dr Enoch Kibunguchy unleash stinging attacks on chairman Musikari Kombo as Luhya politicians gather in Kakamega to lay strategies on the community's political destiny. Sheer coincidence or an orchestrated move to undermine Ford-K leadership?
Curiously, the successive revolts have unfolded at a time when the Local Government minister is away on a foreign trip. Prior to the current spate of insurgencies, former Cabinet minister Cyrus Jirongo pulled out of the party to form the Kenya African Democratic Development Union (Kaddu).
But this unfolding drama notwithstanding, a number of Ford-K MPs are putting on a brave face. An optimistic Bifwoli Wakoli, for instance, considers the on-going moves a "healthy democratic exercise".
"There is absolutely no crisis. The party's national elections have been announced and everyone is either declaring interest in selected seats or simply positioning themselves," says the Bumula MP.
The MP, a key ally of Mr Kombo, faults those attacking Mr Kombo's leadership: "It is all malice. The truth is that Mr Kombo does not run the party alone and if we have failed, then the entire Ford Kenya leadership should take the flak and not one individual."
Declaring that he would oppose Mr Kombo in the race for the party chairmanship, Mr Shitanda, the Housing minister, claims the current Ford Kenya leadership lacks vision.
"It is visionless and incapable of reading the latest political trends. The leadership should predict changing times and prepare for any eventuality," charges the Malava MP.
Dr Kibunguchy on his part terms the party's leadership "tired and lacklustre" while Dr Kituyi believes Ford-K has "diminished from a lion in 1992 to a cat".
However, speaking to the Sunday Nation from Maseru, Lesotho, the Local Government minister asked his challengers to hold their horses until the November elections.
"It is their democratic right to vie for party leadership but let them hold their horses and face me in the November elections," he said.
This, he said, would give everyone a chance to seek mandate from party members "on who is capable of leading the party".
Interestingly, Mr Shitanda and Dr Kibunguchy are some of the beneficiaries of Mr Kombo's push to have party MPs appointed to the Cabinet, following last December's reshuffle in the wake of the referendum debacle.
Then, Mr Kombo and the entire Ford-K brigade named by President Kibaki declined to take up their ministerial positions claiming to have been short-changed. Following State House consultations with Mr Kombo and his National Party of Kenya colleague, Mrs Charity Ngilu, the President gave in by appointing more Ford-K MPs to ministerial positions.
Apart from Mr Shitanda and Dr Kibunguchy, other beneficiaries included Dr Noah Wekesa (Science and Technology), Mr John Munyes (Special Programmes) and assistant ministers Dr Bonny Khalwale (East African Cooperation) and Raphael Wanjala (Water).
Coming in the wake of the emergence of Narc Kenya and ODM-K, the apparent impatience among Ford-K MPs is understandable. The recent by-election instance where the President opted to campaign for the Narc-K candidate in Nakuru Town rubbed salt into a fresh wound.
One other factor that is doubtlessly responsible for the political fireworks in Ford-K has more to do with the folly of its own constitution, especially the clause on the party's presidential flag-bearer.
The clause states that the chairman shall automatically be the party's presidential candidate - a factor that is obviously responsible for the jostling for the top post.
Separately, Ford-K's concerns are also about galvanising the Luhya voting bloc - an indeed tall order. Probably because of the various sub-tribes, numbering 14, political unity among the Luhya community has proved elusive. During the 1992 multi-party polls, for instance, residents voted along sub-tribal and district lines.
Members of the Luhya community emerged the most "politically generous" voters accommodating all principal parties; Kanu in Vihiga, Busia, Teso and Mt Elgon districts, Ford Asili in Kakamega, Lugari and Butere-Mumias districts, while Ford-K reigned supreme in Bungoma district and the neighbouring Trans Nzoia in Rift Valley province. The voting pattern did not change much during the 1997 polls save for the fact that Kanu inherited the Ford Asili territory.
However, unity was finally realised during the 2002 polls and only because the regional political bigwigs of the moment, the late Vice-President Michael Wamalwa (Ford-K) and his successor Moody Awori (Liberal Democratic Party) were pulling in the same direction - the Narc direction.
Political observers opine that had the community gone the same direction during last November's referendum polls, as it did in 2002, its political cohesion and bargaining power would considerably have been boosted. Although the situation on the ground was initially Orange, Ford-K opted for Bananas - a push which they altogether lost.
"One of the reasons we decided to vote Banana is because I personally felt that we were bound to isolate one major community. A caged tiger is dangerous," Mr Kombo told the Sunday Nation in an earlier interview.
Right now, it is a free-for-all affair as key politicians from the region push the agenda of different political entities. Other than Mr Kombo's Ford-K, which currently enjoys the lion's share, VP Awori and ministers Dr Kituyi and Dr Newton Kulundu are destined to popularise Narc Kenya.
Former Cabinet minister and Kanu vice chairman Chris Okemo, on the other hand, doubles up as the face of Kanu and ODM Kenya, alongside LDP's Mudavadi and Labour Party of Kenya boss Dr Julia Ojiambo.
While acknowledging that Ford Kenya is "on fire", Dr Khalwale maintains that Mr Kombo is not the problem. The Ikolomani MP blames it all on the entire team of party officials, some whom he says are "too old to lead".
On the party's apparent indecisiveness on whether to go the ODM-K or Narc Kenya way, Mr Wakoli explains that a decision was made by the party's Parliamentary Group.
"We all agreed not to jump into any alliances as yet until we hold national elections and put our house in order. I am surprised that some colleagues are now criticising this position," says the Ford-K Parliamentary whip.

Original_Search_Title: I Am Yet to Meet the Politician I Can Die for
Headline: Kenya: I Am Yet to Meet the Politician I Can Die for
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kibwezi MP Kalembe Ndile irritates me - the way he talks, the things he says, and the political rascal's face he exhibits each time he encounters TV crew.
I change channels whenever I see him lurking in the corners of the screen. His talk is crass, his words squeak in the ears and send the teeth gritting. I may be biased, but that is me.
But I can never wish him a repeat of what he went through on Saturday. The rat race, the stoning and police intervention.
I hate violence, against anyone and by anyone, because it lowers the rating of the perpetrator and raises the profile of the victim, even if he's a political scoundrel.
One, violence begets violence. Two, it's irrational for supporters of our politicians, even if they are top in opinion polls like Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, to hurl stones in their names. This only helps to make the word "stone-thrower" their middle name.
That is the perception Mr Raila Odinga is fighting today. It's a tag emblazoned on his political T-shirt by vicious supporters. Mr David Mwenje, too, faces a similar problem. He has been accused of mobilising street battles so much that many think they are watching the Red Korna when they see him on the screen leading the Bunge CU Choir.
Perhaps he can use his skills to organise a mass choir to perform at Uhuru Park on national days. We shall then say he finally walked down the road to Tarsus while the good Lord was fishing for a disciple.
My low perception of Kalembe isn't so much about his lowly background and sparse education, for how can I not respect a man who rose from a cleaner, a charcoal burner, to MP?
How can I, in this season of lectures on how to be proud of my country - as if I have another orbiting the demoted planet Pluto, not admire a man who has secured Government land to settle the squatters of Kibwezi?
Land is land, it does not matter if it was in exchange for Banana votes, the line 'referendum freebies' won't be printed on the title deeds.
Achievers are simply that. Former British Prime minister John Major failed an interview to be what the Brits call a revenue collector, or in Kenya a bus conductor.
But the man rose to be UK's Chief of the Exchequer, or simply a Finance minister in the league of our own suave former chairman of the Institute of Certified Public Accountants, Mr Amos Kimunya.
The latter was also the Muthaiga Golf Club chairman when President Kibaki had a corner for sipping that cool drink that foams if amateurishly poured into a glass.
Yes, it's unlikely that those who saw Kalembe doing odd jobs along Mombasa-Nairobi Highway would recognise him when he steps out of his four-wheeler, complete with tinted windows.
Not even those who once met him cleaning, and probably gave him a condescending look.
My judgement of him also has nothing to do with his education background. His CV may be the shortest, but he's made up for the miserable lines on the 'A4' page, by making political capital out of it.
He, too, has got people swearing he'll only lose in 2007 over their dead bodies. They don't care what he does in his Assistant minister's office.
They only know what he's done for them, even when as a councillor, the same police beat and harangued him. But the sun has set for Kanu and Kalembe these days is happy to declare sisi katika serikali! (we in Government).
He can also pull strings at Ardhi House where his friend, Prof Kivutha Kibwana, is the acting minister. He, too, tasted teargas in the Kanu era, but lived to join the new Government.
Despite what appears to be contrast of character, both rhyme well, and that is the beauty, or is it absurdity of politics?
It is not about who went to Harvard or Cambridge. If that were the case, Prof Wangari Maathai would be our President and Prof Anyang' Nyong'o wouldn't have to look up at the Nyanza's political windsock to avoid the road to 1997 defeat.
The thing is that everyone has his or her hero, even Kalembe. Let us disagree with him, dislike him, shut him off when he comes on TV, but always remember he, too, has his supporters.
With the General Election nearing, it is actions like throwing stones at Kalembe that will churn such headlines as: "Ten killed, scores injured at Mwingi rally".
A family would have lost a breadwinner, a dozen children orphaned. Out of political exigency, the politicians would likely be in one coalition.
Kalembe will probably be with Kalonzo, Mr William Ruto could be chummy again with Mr Uhuru Kenyatta. Raila, too, may change strategy and team up with Kibaki.
Unfortunately, that is not what the youth like to hear. I know one of the youths who plunged into the KICC Fountain with shoes muddied at Uhuru Park during the 2002 hand-over. He was on TV scooping the algae-infested water with his hands. He drank the dirty water shouting maji yetu!
Yes, Kibaki had won. Today, he doesn't want to hear the name. His hero parted ways with the man they called Obako. Now he's just huyu mtu! They see in him just another Kalembe. This vindicates me, I'm yet to meet a politician worth dying for!
The writer is The Standard Managing Editor, Weekend Editions

Original_Search_Title: Now Shitanda Wants Kombo Removed From Ford-K Chair
Headline: Kenya: Now Shitanda Wants Kombo Removed From Ford-K Chair
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A Ford Kenya Cabinet minister yesterday called for the removal of Mr Musikari Kombo as party chairman.
Housing minister Soita Shitanda said the Local Government minister and the entire national office should be rejected.
He declared he would go for the party chairman's post: "I've thought about our party, and the reality is that we have lost touch with emerging political events.
"We are neither in ODM Kenya nor in Narc Kenya, but the reality is that come next year, the fight will be between those two parties."
His call for Mr Kombo's removal came a day after Trade and Industry minister Mukhisa Kituyi announced he would abandon the party and indicated he would join Narc-K.
It also came as a rally is scheduled for Kakamega's Masinde Muliro Gardens on Saturday to rally the Luhya community towards taking a collective stand on the political developments that have seen ODM Kenya leaders close ranks.
Luhya elders
The rally has been convened by Western MPs and the Luhya Council of Elders, led by former ministers Burudi Nabwera and James Daniel Otiende.
Yesterday, Mr Shitanda told the Nation that the entire national office had failed to give the party the necessary leadership that would withstand the turbulence of coalition politics.
"Let us not pretend that all is well, when we pursue singular existence as we did and failed in 1997," he said. He was referring to that year's elections, when each of the leading opposition parties fielded candidates and lost to president Daniel arap Moi's Kanu.
Mr Shitanda said every political party was reaching out to one another in building alliances ahead elections, which he described as healthy. The minister said the rally would be used to help set the agenda of Western Province.
"We'll be calling for Luhya unity which can help us have a say and be heard by other communities," he said.
Simmering fallout
The latest call for Mr Kombo's ouster is the climax of what has been a simmering fallout in the party founded by Kenya's doyen of opposition politics, Jaramogi Oginga Odinga.
Mr Odinga founded the party after parting ways with Mr Kenneth Matiba, who formed Ford Asili. He then gave it a strong presence at every election in 1992, 1997 and 2002, when it was led by Vice-President Michael Wamalwa.
Assistant minister for Health Enock Kibunguchy announced this month that he would also take on Mr Kombo for the party leadership.
But even as Dr Kituyi was saying that he was through with Ford-K, there have been grumbles within the party, with the more youthful politicians feeling that Mr Kombo's middle-line approach to politics might be detrimental.
His detractors argue that he took the party to the Government-backed Banana campaigns to support the proposed constitution last November, ignoring the prevailing mood in Western Province at the time.

Original_Search_Title: IMF Economic Review Team Begins Work
Headline: Cameroon: IMF Economic Review Team Begins Work
Body_Text:
The team which is led by Dhaneshwar Ghura, Division chief, arrived in Yaounde yesterday.
A team from the International Monetary Fund (IMF), led by Dhaneshwar Ghura, Division chief, arrived in Cameroon yesterday for a two-week evaluation visit, August 28 to September 11. The team, which is coming to the country for the first time after Cameroon attained the Completion Point of the Heavily Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC) Initiative, is expected to carry out the second review under Cameroon's economic reform programme, which is supported by the IMF through the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF). In this context, Mr Ghura and his team will together with Cameroonian authorities review economic and financial developments, as well as structural reforms implemented at the end of June this year. The team will equally seek to reach understandings with the Cameroonian authorities on economic prospects for the short and medium terms.
During their last visit to Cameroon, the IMF team carried out a detail assessment of Cameroon's commitment to the implementation of the medium-term economioc programme approved by the IMF Board of Directors that met in Washington on October 24, 2005. The programme was conceived as another dimension in the economic reform effort after the country failed to reach the completion point of the HIPC initiative as earmarked. Among the issues examined in February, were the budgetary execution for July-December, 2005, external debt servicing, privatisation programme for 2005 and prospects for 2006 with particular focus on the Cameroon Airlines, the National Water Corporation (SNEC), the Cameroon Development Corporation (CDC and reforms in the Cameroon Postal Services. Also of interest was the implementation of the National Good Governance Programme, structural reforms, debt viability within the framework of the HIPC Initiative, public service reforms, reforms in the judiciary, reforms in the forestry sector etc.
While discussing with Cameroonian authorities, the IMF team will certainly discover a lot of milestone covered in the implementation of the post completion point exiggencies. CAMAIR which was of particular interest to the team when it last came to Cameroon is well advanced in its privatisation process with the Belgian company soliciting to take over. A new water management company, CAMWATER, has been born out of SNEC, while modalities for the privatisation of the Rubber, oil palm and banana sectors of the CDC are under discussion.
In the mean time, following the attainment of the completion point, much has been cancelled in debt relief and the hustle and bustle for making good use of the resources therein is being hightened. Several projects have been identified particularly with the French Supplementary Debt Relief and Development Programme (C2D).

Original_Search_Title: Police Look for MPs Over Attack On Minister
Headline: Kenya: Police Look for MPs Over Attack On Minister
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A potentially dramatic showdown between the police and two top politicians associated with the newly launched ODM-Kenya loomed large on Monday night.
National Assembly Deputy Speaker David Musila and Mwingi North MP Kalonzo Musyoka face arrest on Tuesday after they failed to turn up at the Criminal Investigations Department (CID) headquarters in Eastern Province to record a statement over what they know about an alleged assault on an assistant minister at the weekend.
Wildlife Assistant minister, Mr Kalembe Ndile, is rescued from an irate mob at Musila Gardens in Mwingi District on Saturday. Picture by Boniface Mwangi
Last night, police said they would on Tuesday apply for a warrant of arrest for the two, after lawyer Mutula Kilonzo announced he would be acting for the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) politicians.
Terse statement
In a statement faxed to newsrooms early on Monday, Police Commissioner Maj-Gen Hussein Ali summoned Musila and Kalonzo to appear before CID officers in Embu over the alleged attack on Assistant minister for Wildlife, Mr Kalembe Ndile.
The terse statement was signed by Police Spokesman Mr Gideon Kibunja.
"In the event those summoned fail to report as ordered today (Monday), appropriate warrants of arrest will be issued," the statement read in part.
But both Kalonzo and Musila expressed surprise when contacted by The Standard, saying they were not aware of the summons.
'Political mischief'
The two quickly read political mischief in the move. Police rescued Ndile, who sprinted to break free from a rowdy mob that was surging forward and baying for his blood at a cultural fete. He has since called for a probe on the incident.
This is the second time in under a year that police are seeking to arrest Opposition politicians in what has been roundly condemned by pro-Government functionaries as a politically motivated attack on a rival associated with the ruling class.
Last year, in the run-up to the referendum on the draft Constitution, Embakasi MP David Mwenje - then a harsh critic of President Kibaki - and his Makadara counterpart Reuben Ndolo spent a night at Parliament Buildings to thwart attempts by the police to arrest them after a scuffle with politicians aligned to the Banana camp, who had gathered at a city hotel.
Dramatic cat-and-mouse chase
Ndolo and Mwenje were in the Orange camp that was campaigning against the so-called Wako Draft. The MPs stormed a Banana strategy meeting at the City Cabanas on September 28, where they allegedly broke chairs and tables. Violence then erupted between supporters of Orange and Banana sides. Guns were drawn as the rumpus spilled over into the streets.
Finally, after a dramatic cat-and-mouse chase, the two were arrested and charged on October 1. They spent three nights battling the cold and swarms of mosquitoes in police cells.
Makadara Senior Principal Magistrate, Ms Grace Nzioka, freed the MPs on Sh10,000 cash bail after they denied breaking two chairs and a table at the hotel.
'Pure intimidation'
On Monday, Kalonzo and Musila told Ali to stop being misused by Narc-Kenya politicians to intimidate them.
They described the police summons as 'pure intimidation', which they vowed they would not succumb to.
Musila said he was surprised by reports conveyed to him by journalists that he had been summoned by police to record a statement in Embu over the weekend incident in Mwingi.
"Ali knows I am a national figure and he knows how he can get me. If there was a problem, there are police stations in Mwingi and another one in Parliament. So, if this is not intimidation what can you call it?" he posed.
The Mwingi South MP said he had done nothing wrong to warrant the police summon.
"But since he (Ali) is not acting professionally and he has allowed himself to be misused by Narc-Kenya politicians, that is why he is summoning me in the wrong way," he said.
'Action designed to embarrass'
The legislator said he knew his rights and would not be intimidated by the police.
"If they want to arrest me they can go ahead and do so, but I will not be intimidated."
He described the action as designed to embarrass him, but warned that in a way it could also bring the Police Force into disrepute.
Expressing dismay, Kalonzo said procedure demanded that he be served with the summons so that he could decide whether to accept them or not.
"I have not seen any summons and I will decide on what action to take once I see them," he said on Monday.
The former Environment minister, however, warned that they would not be intimidated after years fighting to free Kenya from excesses by those in authority.
Minister called for arrest
Kalonzo recalled seeing TV footage in which Education Assistant minister Mrs Beth Mugo had called for their arrest.
"I hope the Police Commissioner is not being directed on his job by Narc-Kenya politicians because he is supposed to be a professional who is not influenced by politicians," said Kalonzo.
The MP said he abhorred violence and he did not support the use of violence to settle political scores. He denied there was any violence in Mwingi on the material day, adding that Ndile left the venue of his own volition.
'Barbaric act'
The Kibwezi MP, said Kalonzo, had confessed that nobody had touched him and wondered on whose complaint the Police Commissioner was acting.
"Who is the complainant here?" posed Kalonzo. "If Kalembe himself said he had not been harmed, so for what are we being sought?"
But on Monday, police asserted that the two had been ordered to report to the CID in Embu and said warrants of arrest for councillors Musyoka Wanzwili and Geoffrey Munuve Musyimi had also been issued.
Separately, Water minister Mutua Katuku condemned the attack on Ndile. He termed the act barbaric and called on the ODM-Kenya leaders to apologise for the shameful incident.

Original_Search_Title: Let's Be Wary of Change Crusaders
Headline: Kenya: Let's Be Wary of Change Crusaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Since the early days of our independence, Kenya has always needed comprehensive review process. That is why the likes of Jaramogi Oginga Odinga, Koigi Wamwere, George Anyona, Ngugi wa thiong'o, Raila Odinga, Kenneth Matiba and Charles Rubia risked their lives and suffered detention.
For about two decades, now since 1996, Kenyans have consistently and scientifically agitated for constitutional change, climaxing in the 1997 minimum law reforms. In that year, the National Convention Executive Council (NCEC) led massive popular street protests to demand change.
The end result was the Inter-Parliamentary Parties Group (IPPG) consensus that changed the compromise reforms by the political scene in a remarkable way. At least the changes helped to level the playing field ahead of the the General Election later that year.
This was later followed with comprehensive constitutional review that culminated in last year's referendum, where the final Wako draft was rejected. at the November referendum. Those of us who supported the so-called Wako Draft for bananas got a rude shock, when what we thought was a good document, was overwhelmingly thrown out at the ballot box.
Level playing field
With another election around the corner, politicos have dusted their boots ready for another review-inspired confrontation. What do the change-the-constitution now advocates want this time round?
Predictably, minimum reforms to level the playing field ahead of next year's poll. In other words, we are back to 1997, only that the players have changed roles. This time, it is the 1997 (Kanu) government playing the firing squad, and the former Opposition (DP) at the receiving end.
Unlike 1997, today's Opposition lacks the support of the civil society and religious groups. Why? Because they feel the characters fronting for the review are not sincere. I watched recently when some of those previously in Kanu government went on air them on my screen threatening mass action if the Kibaki administration does not yield to demands for minimum law changes.
Yet, some of these people were Kanu's strategists, who schemed to ensure that little was done to change the political equation. They fought hard to resist Opposition's moves to alter the constitution. They wanted to maintain the status quo and entrench themselves in power.
The least the Kanu team should do is to apologise for the sins their government committed against Kenyans. The memories of the ethnic clashes in various parts of the country, including Rift Valley and the Coast, grabbing of public land and other resources, torture of Opposition politicians and stealing of taxpayers' money remain dark marks in Kanu's history.
These are acts that the party will have to pay for at some point, even if not in this generation. With those who were involved in the Kanu scheme now coming out to champion for democracy is a mockery. The latter day crusaders of constitution change do not have any credibility to stand anywhere and proclaim that they want change. Why couldn't they think of it when they were in power?
And while at it, what, pray is Raila Odinga doing in that group? Kenyans may never elect Agwambo their President - we are not known to reward heroes. That is why he does not perform well in s less than well in opinion polls. But like many Kenyans, I have respect for him. Isn't it possible for Agwambo and the less tainted in that crowd to initiate a different pro-change movement?
Mr Odinga does not need to be president for his glorious legacy to hold. Mahatma Gandhi never did. But at this rate, there is the danger of the man and his long-term investment in change in Kenya going down the drain. How can he be salvaged?
That said, it should be emphasised that Kenya still needs comprehensive constitutional review for democracy to take root. Kenya will never move beyond personal rule and the associated misuse of resources, dictatorship and underdevelopment with the current constitutional order. And this is not about a particular government or regime.
President Kibaki may be a fine man, but history teaches us not to trust individuals with power, but institutions, with a good constitution as the bedrock. One needs to observe how intoxicated some of the current ministers are to appreciate this point.
Shoving for State power
Tragedy is, for nearly two decades, Kenyans have been going round and round this issue, with no hope of ever getting anywhere. One suspects that the reason for this is that the contending forces (the political elite) do not have a serious problem with one another beyond pushing and shoving for State power. As long as this is the case, no Kenyan should expect a new constitution during this generation.
That is why the re-emergence of the clamour for minimum reforms is going nowhere. Those at the forefront in this crusade are not strategic. They even say why they want change now: to level the political playing ground ahead of next year's elections.
Kenyans have every reason to reject those calls for minimum reforms because they are not well-meaning. But they must not lose sight of the prize, search for a comprehensive and people-driven constitutions.
Mr Mwalulu is a former MP for Taveta

Original_Search_Title: The Tragedy of Our Politics is Tribal Leaders
Headline: Kenya: The Tragedy of Our Politics is Tribal Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Liberal politics is called so because two or three or a thousand parties freely vie with one another for power. That is why it is called multi-party. Its "liberalness" is made manifest by numbers, not by whether those parties have any basic policy differences. They are liberal because they all define "liberty" as political abstention from all economic activity.
They are all dedicated to the social status quo. That is the reason it is so easy for two or three or a thousand of them to enter into a working arrangement - a memorandum of understanding, for example - with one another.
And that is why it is utterly foolish for any one of them to swear at the top of its voice that it will never enter an election alliance, say, or a governing coalition with any other party.
In Central Europe, it is the done thing. Due to a hundred and one factors - including such a proliferation of parties - no party ever manages to garner a big enough number to be able to rule alone. It then becomes necessary for the Head of State to invite a party with the biggest number to try to find a working majority by negotiating with some other parties to form a coalition government.
We saw a good example in Germany recently, when Angela Merkel's party defeated its closest rival by a mere seat. It then took donkey's years for her to convince other parties to join her in a ruling coalition.
In the Third World - not excepting Kenya - tribe and tribalism are among the most important of such divisive factors. Thus, invariably, our multiplicity of parties are almost always tribal war-formations. Thus at crucial moments - such as the eve of a General Election - we all gird our loins "marching as to war" between the big tribes or between various alliances of them.
For hot war actually erupts, sometimes spontaneous, at other times fuelled by the tribal leaders themselves. You cannot have forgotten Molo, Burnt Forest, Olenguruone, Nyali and the alleged "Kalenjin Warriors".
But the events of last November should have taught every party that - no matter how massive its tribal rearguard may be - it is well-nigh impossible for it to capture the presidency or Parliament single-handedly.
In the referendum, the Gema tribes voted almost to a man for a set of constitutional proposals. They did marvellously well. But, in the end, the rest of Kenya coalesced successfully and defeated Gema.
The vital lesson was that neither the other side nor Gema - so called because these consanguine tribes once formed themselves into something called Gikuyu, Embu and Meru Association - was voting for or against the proposals.
Few of the voters ever read the proposals. Both sides were voting only for or against what their leaders - all of them tribal helmsmen - had told them to be in their interests as tribes.
This is the tragedy of our politics. The most useless and or most dangerous leader - if he has the gift of the gab - can always prevail upon his tribe to vote for him even when it is obvious that personal power and wealth are his only quest.
Yet this is the reality that every politician must live with. The reality is two-fold. First, Kenyans will vote only for their tribal leaders or parties. Because of it, secondly, those leaders or parties can never win alone.
To win - or to be part of a winning group - they must sue for electoral or governing alliances or confederacies.
This is what is surprising about Mwai Kibaki's Narc-Kenya and Uhuru Kenyatta's Kanu faction. Both are renegades from winning entities - Kibaki from the Narc that won him the presidency in 2002 and Uhuru from the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) that carried the victory in the referendum.
And - rightly or wrongly - both can be seen as tribally motivated. Both have been accused of being but two latter-day versions of Gema. And, as reflections of Gema, the rest of Kenya are likely to vote against the one and the other.
Yet they may have something up their sleeves. What if Kibaki and Uhuru join electoral forces? Uhuru's vacillation may be linked to the speculation that - in his own long-term interest - former President Moi is busy behind the scenes trying to work out a Kibaki-Uhuru platform. It would be a really and truly formidable force.
But - if seen as Gema in disguise - would it really succeed where Banana failed against Orange in last year's referendum?

Original_Search_Title: Reforms - Boot On the Other Foot for Karua
Headline: Kenya: Reforms - Boot On the Other Foot for Karua
Body_Text:
Nairobi — She is not one known to shy away from challenges.
At one time she walked out on retired President Moi when he went visiting her Gichugu constituency after organisers had slighted her by omitting her from the speakers' list.
At another function, during a swearing-in ceremony at State House when she had been appointed to Water ministry before being transferred to the current docket, she was the only Cabinet minister who didn't seem to bow to President Kibaki.
State House lawns
She appeared to have just acknowledged the seated President and walked to the swearing-in stand and then back to her seat on the State House lawns.
That's Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua for you.
Neither will she shy away from bragging about her being in government (Narc or Narc Kenya?) and the government of national unity will not shy away from employing a few lessons from Kanu's days in power .
During a joint appearance on a live TV programme last week with Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo, who tried to pin her down on the government's poaching of MPs from Opposition benches, Karua retorted: "Kanu co-opted National Development Party MPs into its Cabinet. The IPPG deal allowed the President to appoint members of the Cabinet from among MPs, not necessarily from his own party.
"What is the fuss over the government of national unity? I agree there is need to define government but the GNU is not illegal.
"It is perfectly in order and it is a happy position for us."
Karua exudes confidence when she declares that the government is committed, able and willing to facilitate comprehensive constitutional review.
Her predecessor, Mr Kiraitu Murungi, made the same pledge, even promising to rock every village in the country to ensure the Yes vote in the referendum carried the day. Mr Murungi was the face of the Narc government's failed attempt to deliver a new constitution last year.
When the Banana supporters were resoundingly subdued by the Orange side, the entire defeat was hung on his neck and the rest is history.
A new constitution was a major election pledge by Narc in 2002, and which cost retired President Moi a great deal of votes in the 1990s and eventually Kanu's banishment from power.
With the renewed clamour for reforms, Ms Karua is increasingly assuming the face of the State in the second attempt to push the tedious review process forward.
Being the minister for Justice and Constitutional Affairs, the review process falls squarely in her docket.
She is viewed as the woman on the spot and and the process will succeed or flop depending on how she shepherds it.
The eloquent and combative third time MP, is also the deputy leader of government business.
Trodden both extremes
But then the MP for Gichugu has trodden both extremes.
When the National Executive Council (NCEC) caused former President Moi sleepless nights with its highly charged pro- reform street protests in the 1990's, Ms Karua was both a Democratic Party MP and senior consort of the well-oiled pro-democracy civil society brigade that came together under the NCEC.
When Kanu eventually buckled under intense pressure, and agreed to discuss reforms, Ms Karua was part of the negotiations that took place at County Hall and which gave birth to the Inter Parliamentary Parties Group (IPPG) minimum reforms deal. Among concessions IPPG managed to wrestle from Kanu was to have opposition parties nominate representatives to the Electoral Commission of Kenya.
The boot is now on the other foot and Ms Karua is now the government face in the review process.
During the TV interview on Friday, Ms Karua revealed some of her IPPG experiences thus: "We (IPPG) rammed the Gender Parity agenda down the Kanu throat and that is how the Kenyan parliament got more women (formally nominated by political parties)..." This time round, however, unless she plays her cards well, she and President Kibaki may be the ones having things shoved down their throats. Ever since former Cabinet minister and Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga launched the clamour for minimum reforms - which seems to have caught the government flat-footed - the political spectrum has been inflamed by high-sounding and polarised-exchanges.
For their part, the minimum reforms brigade bringing together mainly the Liberal Democratic Party, Kanu factions loyal to Uhuru Kenyatta and Eldoret North MP William Ruto, Ford Kenya, among others, say a comprehensive review is impossible, hence the need for minimum reforms.
This is to reduce the Executive's powers on such pivotal institutions like the Electoral Commission of Kenya and and the Judiciary.
They have proposed a raft of reforms they demand should be passed before the 2007 elections, failure to which they have threatened to mobilise street protests.
Among the demands is a financially independent Electoral Commission and Judiciary which they say are currently at the mercy of the Executive.
ECK has been on the spot lately, and fears are rife that the government was out to saturate it with its own nominees, in disregard of the 1997 IPPG deal. President Kibaki has already appointed two Commissioners without consulting other parties.
Nine Commissioners are due for replacement next month while ECK chairman Samuel Kivuitu's term elapses in the first week of December, 2007, three week's before the election.
Although not legally binding, the IPPG deal allowed the party of government to nominate 10 of the 22-member commission while opposition parties nominate the rest. The chairman is a presidential appointee.
Since the referendum defeat, followed by the damaging Anglo Leasing scandal that claimed three Cabinet ministers, then the embarrassing Armenian mercenaries saga, the government has lost a great deal of face.
With President Kibaki having publicly dismissed the minimum reforms drive off hand, another round of confrontation was afoot.
Ms Karua seems to have read the signs of the times, and pulled a quick one when she invited all political parties to Windsor Golf and Country Club for consultations.
And with that the brewing storm was stemmed for two reasons: one, all parties were invited with Ms Karua sending the message that the government wanted to talk with all players.
And two, by inviting all political parties, including moribund ones that only surface during elections, Ms Karua knocked the wind out of the sails of the ODM/LDP/Kanu brigade.
The Windsor Golf and Country Club meeting ruffled the feathers of the civil society sector, who have warned any process that is driven by the political class at the exclusion of other sectors will suffer illegitimacy.
However, explaining the Windsor meeting with political parties, Ms Karua said progress in the constitutional review process has always been made difficult by disagreements among the political class and which, she said, needed to cultivate a common ground.
"The civil society should not fear they will be excluded or ignored. After political disagreements are addressed, the process will be opened up to other sectors and stakeholders. No one individual or group should be allowed to dominate the process" she said.
Mistrust and doubts about the government's intentions still abound.
A Nairobi lawyer and a civil society activist, Mr Haroun Ndubi, says Ms Karua should publicly state that she is not the one to deliver a new constitution but only facilitate the review.
"She (Ms Karua) will only succeed if she limits her role to creating an enabling environment by way of a legislative framework within which the review should be conducted.
She should never make it a government project. Parliament should consult with civil society, workers' unions and professional groups on the way forward. Ms Karua is a confessed Narc-Kenya loyalist and the process needs an independent shepherd," Ndubi said by telephone.
Efforts all week to interview Ms Karua were unsuccessful.

Original_Search_Title: Kampala On Channel O
Headline: Uganda: Kampala On Channel O
Body_Text:
To many, Kampala is synonymous with dust, potholed, gridlock and dark alleys. But beneath these eyesores lies a vibrant capital that beats to the rhythm of partying residents as Channel O's Urban Massive show revealed last week. The show, now in its second season, provides publicity for the African artists and the cities they reside. The focus is on the trend-setting, music-loving, young people. Urban Massive aims at showcasing lifestyles using the common medium of music to create a connective link between people living in Africa's most vibrant cities.
Kampala was chosen yet again in a 13-city showcase. Channel O presenter Kabelo had singer Bebe Cool as his guide to take African viewers onto an armchair tour of Kampala. He was a better choice mostly because he was not as removed as Juliana, last year's half-hearted host.
Bebe Cool's tour started with his humble abode (he keeps a poodle), then his mum's humbler dwelling in Kanyanya, the Wandegeya "ghetto city' as he called it which led us to the one-room shack he writes his songs from. Bebe went on to claim having been the first Ugandan artiste who crossed a border to become successful. He had better be right about that.
Urban Massive is divided into segments to help the viewer navigate through the maze of footage easily. "Street Cred" features music videos played all through the hour-long show. Rachel Kiwanuka's Every Time, Obsessions Jangu, Juliana's Nabikoowa, Ngoni (featuring Buchaman)'s Nakupenda, Chameleone's Bomboclaat and Bebe Cool's Guns N Bombs were some of the videos played. That though does not necessarily mean they are the best we have on offer.
For the "Food" segment, Kabelo got Aunt Sara, Sabrina's Pub buffet chef to give him a crash course in preparing matooke. The apron-wearing Kabelo went on to prepare chicken luwombo, meticulously wrapping of the poultry pieces in smoked banana leaves. He should have tried out the savoury "rolex" too.
The "style" segment gave designer Latif a chance to show off his signature denim fabrics. Who knows, his craft may catch the eye of an interested fashion guru out there. Herbert Musisi of Samaki also explained why fish inspire his colourful designs.
Kampala's new breed of urban artistes got featured on the "Beats" segment. Rachel Kiwanuka was one of them and she lamented about how hard it is for R&B artistes to breakthrough. The scantily clad Obsessions girls also took time to explain the breast cupping in their Wekuume video.
The "Hype" segment had radio personalities Alex Ndawula, JKazoora and Gaetano as the human encyclopaedia to consult about Kampala's nightlife and entertainment. Bebe Cool tour ended with a "street party" at the Old Taxi Park and in his usual flamboyant style climbed on top of a commuter taxi belt out one of his tunes.
For his tour guide efforts, Motorola, the show's sponsor handed him the ultimate freebie, a sleek Motorola phone, an ideal replacement for the antiquated one he had. I guess some viewers were as puzzled about the presence of a camel in our hilly city as I was. Kabelo stood beside one against a backdrop of Kampala's skyline at the beginning of the show.

Original_Search_Title: The Art of Comic Disrespect
Headline: South Africa: The Art of Comic Disrespect
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — WE MEET at the News Café in Hatfield, Pretoria. I've just ordered a cappuccino. He walks in, tall, dreadlocks, faded denim jacket, and orders "the biggest draught beer you have". The waitress is slightly baffled. It's noon. Later he orders another one. The beer doesn't seem to affect him. Life is here to enjoy. Oh, and to annoy and provoke.
Occasionally, when he's in a contemplative mood, 27-year-old artist Vusi Beauchamp wonders where he'd be if he'd grown up in less generous times. "If I was 25 in the early '80s, what would my life story be like? Maybe I'd be working as a miner. Or as a gardener."
A horrible thought. He's part of the generation of young black South Africans who have the sky as their limit. He's a comic artist, a fine artist, a playwright and a musician. He studies animation and graphic design. And on top of all that, he has a fashion label called Feil Poppy. "That's Corruptible Chick," he chuckles.
Understandably, he's not quite sure what to call himself. "I'll tell you in a year's time. I don't wanna get too comfortable in one medium. If I box myself in, I'll be very restricted to say things. And if I don't do all this, I'll just sit at home, not shaving or taking a bath, and drinking Black Labels."
Another horrible thought: being the token black. "A certain lady told me about this job offer as a graphic designer. Wow, I thought, I might like that! Then she said they just want to have a black face there. F**k that! That's the reality of the business world. They look for a dustbin man who doesn't have a clue and put him there."
So Beauchamp has become a rebel, a post-apartheid maverick who is acutely aware of the traps that have been set for bright ModelC kids like him. With so many opportunities and so many expectations, they're in a tricky position. Some, like author K Sello Duiker, can't cope and take their own lives. Others resort to drugs and alcohol. Beauchamp nods.
"There's huge pressure on black males, to drive a nice car and have a nice C-cup lady next to you, with very long, stretched, shiny hair, educated and black. If you're with a white lady in a small car you get a lot of hate from your own."
His defence mechanism has been to confuse people, or rather, to piss them off. Earlier this year, together with his art school buddy Eric Rantisi, he self-published the comic Kaffer Paradys. On the cover is a drawing of Robert Mugabe with a penis-shaped nose, eating a banana. On the back is a drawing of Jacob Zuma, wearing a T-shirt that says "Anti-women abuse".
Inside are all sorts of extremely politically incorrect drawings featuring Mac Maharaj, Mo Sheik, rural blacks, priests, foreskins, sheep shaggers, penises, Orlando Pirates fans, coloureds, Afrikaners and handicapped girls.
A good comic, asserts Beauchamp, should be on the edge and very disrespectful. "No borders on religion, sex or politics. No holding it back. But at the same time they must be entertaining."
Pretoria galleries shied away from launching the book. Pretoria bookshops didn't want it. "They basically told us that this is rubbish, and that they don't want to be associated with such vulgarity."
Eventually they approached Gordart Gallery in Melville. "They loved it. Joburg has a kind of free-spirited vibe and openness about art, unlike Pretoria."
