Beauchamp grew up in an upper middle-class family in Mamelodi. His French surname has its origins in a meeting of Xhosa and French in the Cape, centuries ago. His father is an attorney, who wanted his son to become a lawyer.
But Beauchamp had different plans. At high school he was mesmerised by fine arts. "In the townships you'll only find posters of Jesus Christ, very kitschy. Never any real art on the walls. That propelled me to do something and stick it on the wall."
Black New York graffiti artist Jean-Michel Basquiat became an icon. And in an attempt to emulate Basquiat's reckless lifestyle, Beauchamp went through his own version of sturm und drang. "I was an artist, and believed that you have to live and look what you are. I started messing around with my hair, smoking a bit of the herb, and experimenting."
When he became too involved with a scruffy, religious Rasta community in Northern Cape, he decided he'd ventured deep enough.
A workshop by Bitterkomix artist Mark Kannemeyer came as an epiphany.
"Bitterkomix shocked me and blew me away. They made fun of everyone: Afrikaners, blacks, Indians. Normally it's hard to say what you really feel about the state of the country, particular people, or the way they do things. A comic gives you that opportunity. Kinda like a free-speech box."
THE provocative title came to him after a night out with an Afrikaans friend. "We were chilling, having a braai with his friends. Then he told us how he 'saw a kaffir with funny hair one day'. He kept on talking about this kaffir. And after the conversation he looked at me and started apologising for using the K-word. He didn't see me as a black. He kept on apologising. The whole night."
Beauchamp wasn't so much offended as surprised. It was the first time he had been associated with the K-word. "It was kinda very interesting. Also the way he kept apologising." He takes a gulp of his beer and grins. "I thought: there must be something about that kaffir thing."
Indeed, given the power shift in the new SA, it could be a wonderfully ironic title for a black comic book. "Yeah, it's the new BEE-people paradise. They're spending money hectically, going crazy, forgetting about the shack people. The former kaffir is now the boss."
The problem with being shocking and provocative is that you must always better yourself. He and Rantisi have started thinking about Kaffer Paradys No2. The title will be Pretoria se Poes. Beauchamp says friends have advised him against making jokes about Jews killing Christ, or about Mohammed sniffing gun powder.
And maybe the thought of famous prisoners having sex on Robben Island isn't such a great idea either.
He shrugs. "It will irritate many people, even dangerous, suicidal people. It'll be fun if I see a riot against me one day, people chasing me around with knives. It'll be a rush."
That sounds way too tough for someone who hasn't yet shown Kaffer Paradys to his father or girlfriend. It's when we talk about life in Pretoria and Mamelodi that a different Beauchamp appears. Like many young people, he leads a schizophrenic existence, shuttling between town and township, an outcast in both worlds.
"People in Pretoria seem to suffer from all kinds of disorders. The town is like a case study for psychology books. Also, they don't know how to classify me, black or white.
"In Mamelodi I'm an alien as well. I drag this big canvas and they stop me and want to buy it for 50 bucks. It's like a journey, a very interesting mission. It gives me ideas to use in a play , a comic book or a painting."
But it's when we touch upon heroes and influences that a different Beauchamp appears, a sentimental, vulnerable young artist who adores beauty. "I love classical music. When I start a painting I listen to hip-hop. Then I change to mellow jazz. And for the final touches it's Tchaikovsky to calm me down. Then things are clearer and more relaxed. Details appear.
"It's about emotions. As an artist you are very sensitive. At my first exhibition I didn't even want to sell anything because I was so attached to my paintings. They were little pieces of me."
He quickly orders another beer.
CV:
1979 Born in Mamelodi
1999 Studies fine art at Pretoria Technikon (now Tshwane University of Technology)
2005 Studies animation and graphic design at Damelin
2006 Publishes Kaffer Paradys
2006 Launches an exhibition at Gordart Gallery, Johannesburg
2006 Joint exhibition, Stones and Glass Houses, with Eric Rantisi in the Liesel Manjies Gallery, TUT, Pretoria, until August 28
Heroes and influences:
Basquiat, Spike Lee, Quentin Tarantino, Dennis Rodman, Nelson Mandela, Conrad Botes, William Kentridge, Kendell Geers, Fela Kuti, Winnie Madikizela-Mandela, Dorothy Masuku, John Matshikiza

Original_Search_Title: Making a Place for All in the Cities
Headline: South Africa: Making a Place for All in the Cities
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — THE monotonous design of low-cost housing was highlighted in the press recently. People are now living in the settlements that have been delivered so rapidly over the past 12 years that international attention has swung this way. But many of the settlements look like a visual manifestation of a financial spreadsheet: one household, one subsidy, one plot and one house -- all the same.
The housing department's policy statements now include an intention to transform the Reconstruction and Development Programme (RDP) landscape into something more humane. Also at issue is that so many low-income settlements are on the edges of cities and towns. New developments appear to mimic apartheid planning with its segregationist intentions. So the social and economic integration of society has become a clear call from the presidency downwards.
Suggested solutions to the monotony and dislocation of human settlements are many and varied, and depend on the viewpoint and interests of those making the proposals. But what are the real issues here?
On the first point, we should remember that our observation of monotonous settlements is based on a snapshot taken just after the developers have finished building the houses or, worse, only the toilets.
Within another 10 years, most RDP houses will probably be subsumed by the new building efforts of the residents. This process of transformation sometimes needs to be assisted. For this to happen, competitive local materials suppliers and small builders need to be present. So do draughtspeople and local authorities who can assist by making available and approving plans for house alteration. And also, according to several research pieces commissioned recently by the Finmark Trust, both a functioning market in second-hand houses and entrepreneurial activity taking place in those houses are absolutely vital to driving improvement.
When these conditions are present, areas that start off as low-income settlements will soon become at least lower-middle-income "suburbs" as they do in most other developing countries. That is, if the main elements of a vibrant local economy are present and local authorities support residents' projects.
But what about the second point, the so-called "dislocation" of the poor to the periphery? Here we cannot avoid discussing affordability of land and how urban land markets operate.
In low-income housing debates this is what we most often avoid talking about. Is there a place for the poor in the city? How do individuals and households get a toehold in urban economies? And how do lower-income and middle-income households get on the housing ladder?
Until now the main ways to do this have been to live in very crowded and often degraded inner city apartments, to secure shacks in informal settlements, or to wait until your name comes up on an RDP housing waiting list.
Why is it so difficult for people with lower incomes to buy or rent affordable and well-located land?
Urban land markets often operate in ways that close out opportunities for the poor. They draw clear lines between classes in society.
We have words that carry clear connotations depending which part of the world you are from, such as ghettoes and estates, suburbs and neighbourhoods (or 'burbs and 'hoods), and projects and townships.
Other words describe processes such as urban decay, renewal, regeneration and gentrification -- or the latest: reverse gentrification.
We even have planning acronyms such as NIMBY (not in my backyard) and BANANA (build absolutely nothing anywhere near anything).
These terms all unwittingly or intentionally describe situations that exclude or include certain groups via the property market. This inclusion/exclusion is mediated through who can afford to live in certain areas, and is increasingly signified by the use of boom gates and walls.
So how can we open up this thorny issue of how urban land can be made more accessible to the poor through the market?
In SA, the definition of who is poor is quite broad because of the previous conflation of race and spatial dislocation. The gap between rich and poor is notoriously wide, but the "poor" are not all of a piece. There are many and varied household situations in which each has its own aspirations and livelihood strategy.
Perhaps we should not begin addressing these issues, as some have, by suggesting the building of RDP houses on the polished doorsteps of Sandton. Most people agree that RDP settlements, when newly built, are too monotonous even for city-edge development.
Rather, we should start by opening up better-located places to stay for people who are shop assistants, students, petrol attendants, domestic workers, bank tellers and their families. We should start with rooms over shops, terrace houses bordering security estates and walk-up flats near shopping malls and car showrooms. We should start with employers becoming landlords, and investors backing mixed developments with substantial residential components. There are many creative ways to open up the urban land market so that social integration does not remain forever an experiment.
Not only should we look for ways to open up the urban land market, we also need to honestly address attitudinal barriers which often arise from classes in SA being so used to living apart. Or, as someone has said, we need to change NIMBY to WIMBY (welcome in my backyard).
The consequences of not spatially integrating a society in which there is a growing urban poor component will be negative for all concerned. For example, given the increases in the petrol price it will mean constantly increasing government transport subsidies, increasing transport costs borne by the employer, and the considerable financial and time costs people bear daily in travelling long distances to work.
A land and property market that works for all is crucial in the transformation of South African society.
Napier is programme director of Urban LandMark, a recently launched DFID programme aimed at making urban land markets work for (and with) the poor.

Original_Search_Title: Even 'Tom And Jerry' Would Love Our Politics
Headline: Kenya: Even 'Tom And Jerry' Would Love Our Politics
Body_Text:
Nairobi — One of my secret passions is watching good cartoon strips and I cannot help seeing in our leaders either a Tom the cat or Jerry the mouse.
However, I cannot tell who is Tom and who is Jerry between Narc-Kenya, which dons the inscrutable and at times sullen face of President Kibaki, or the Orange Democratic Movement, which embodies Raila Odinga's rebellious face. I believe they are both Tom and Jerry depending on the political season.
ODM and Narc-K keep melting into the mould of the luckless Tom and Jerry's antics at different times, depending on the subject at hand and the mood of the country.
In their words and deeds, our top guns fit the picture perfectly. In both the rat race and the political chase, you see somersaults and about-turns.
If you pay attention, every step is a stroll down Hilarious Avenue. Like when Tom lights an explosive the size of candle at the hole in the wall Jerry has just entered not knowing the mouse got out through another exit and is igniting a candle as big as a wine barrel near its hairy tail.
Jerry takes off in time, and Tom is blasted up the skies, engulfed by the obliterating black smoke. Then he lands with a thud, shrivelled and the fur burning like dry banana leaves. At another instance, he's chasing Jerry only to smash into the brick-wall and crack his skull.
To put the members of the cast in the right place, here is what a chronologist wrote on the theatrical short animated films by animators William Hanna and Joseph Barbera.
"Tom is very quick-tempered and thin-skinned, while Jerry is independent and opportunistic. Though very energetic and determined, Tom is no match for Jerry's brains and wits."
President Kibaki walks to the microphone in Mombasa and declares that if you want minimum reforms, you can as well die because it won't happen.
It didn't matter that his Justice minister, Ms Martha Karua, had sent out invitation letters to the likes of Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, Mr William Ruto and Mr JJ Kamotho.
Like Tom, he hit the wall, but picked himself up two days later and declared he supported the negotiations. But too late, he had shown his heart was with Narc-K, and minimum reforms weren't on the agenda. And so the time moved to 1997, retired President Moi resisting reforms, and the Inter-Parliamentary Party Group being born.
So as in the cartoon strip, the chase began in the streets and boardrooms. Then abruptly the lead resister dropped his guard and Jerry had his way. We are where we began, and today's Tom is tomorrow's Jerry. The leading pro-minimum reform activists then included Kibaki and Raila. Today, the two political predators are on opposite ends of the pitch.
Luckily the Executive powers remain intact and you can dish out jobs and keep 16 seats in the Cabinet and council of assistant ministers vacant so that those outside can keep ogling and salivating.
By the way, where are Mr Najib Balala (if he's still in Kenya) and Mr Joe Khamisi headed? The carrot tied to the stick Kibaki is holding is shining in the sun!
Back to the pre-1997 elections, there were defections and in today's rat chase the lessons are handy, only that the President doesn't need to receive them in public. You know it has happened when the most vocal go quiet and finally end up on a list the President is reading on television.
That is why a reader corrected me recently. Apart from Kanu-Ruto, Kanu-Uhuru, Kanu-Biwott and Kanu -Tungojee, there is also Kanu-Narc led by Ministers Njenga Karume, John Koech and Gideon Konchella.
But why be a Jerry, always on the run, when you can join the pack that hunts? Even if the prey is elusive, the boss is also the Commander-in-Chief and he can order them to use Weapons of Mass Destruction.
But the lessons are often bitter as we saw in the humiliation of the post-1992 defectors: Mr Benjamin Magwaga, Mr Apili Wawire, Rev Javan Omani, Mr Tom Obondo, Mr Mbuva Ntwiga, Mr Ochola Ogur, Mr Kiruhi Kimondo and the first to jump ship, Prof Protus Momanyi of Bonchari. They set a date with the political hangman himself.
Luckily, today no one is foolish enough to defect in writing, no matter the black and brown briefcases. They defect by mouth and sit on the Government side and there is nothing the Honourable Francis ole Kaparo can do. It's no longer easy to tell the guinea fowl from chicken.
But in the rat race, some appear to have a knack for the dark alleys, always being chased. Many belong to what yesterday became ODM Kenya. Until the elections, they will keep panting and jumping from one hole to another, to escape the jaws and claws of Tom. But even the elections might not deliver victory and so Jerry may continue being Jerry.
The soft belly is that some in the ODM ranks can decide to be Toms when the unity string snaps. After all, Raila told us Uhuru is of "sentimental value", while the son of Jomo called ODM "amorphous" and rudderless.
As for Ford-Kenya's Musikari Kombo and National Party of Kenya's Charity Ngilu, they don't have to run, their ships are stuck in the high seas and they will soon be sending out desperate SoS signals hoping any other ship that could be close by will pick them up.
Next time you chance on Tom and Jerry cartoon strip, try and put on them the faces of our politicians.
The writer is The Standard Managing Editor, Weekend Editions

Original_Search_Title: How ODM Came Into Limelight
Headline: Kenya: How ODM Came Into Limelight
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement was formed in Kisumu at the peak of the referendum campaigns.
This was after Kanu and the Liberal Democratic Party closed ranks to oppose the proposed constitution, commonly known as the Wako Draft. Kenyans voted on the Draft on November 21.
Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman, Mr Samuel Kivuitu, had released the symbols to be used in the referendum. Those opposed to the Draft were handed the symbol of an orange and the supporters a banana.
The 'No' team, comprising Kanu and LDP MPs found themselves more of allies than enemies and jointly addressed a series of rallies. At the Kisumu meeting, they adopted the name the Orange Democratic Movement, which was to cause ripples in the political arena.
The Orange Revolution in the Ukraine could have driven the choice of name. This was a series of protests and political events that took place throughout Ukraine in response to allegations of massive corruption, voter intimidation and direct electoral fraud during the 2004 presidential election.
ODM brought together key names like Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Uhuru Kenyatta, William Ruto, Musalia Mudavadi, Prof Anyang' Nyong'o, Billow Kerrow and Linah Kilimo. Others were Ochilo Ayacko, Chris Okemo, William ole Ntimama, Najib Balala and Mutula Kilonzo.
Party registered
Most Kenyans may have been opposed to the Draft for their own reasons, but the unity of the ODM and the way it electrified the country could not be overlooked in the resounding defeat that Banana proponents suffered at the referendum.
After its heady victory, the movement took advantage of the public goodwill and announced that it would transform into a political party before the General Election, due next year.
But before the members could do so, the rug was pulled from under their feet. Three people, not connected to the movement, registered a party, whose name was almost similar to the movement, on December 22. This legally prevented any political party from using the name. The three were Mr John Miriti Mbarire, who was registered as chairman, Mr Joseph Bunei (treasurer) and Ms Wangui Muringe (secretary).
Deputy Registrar Lucy Waithaka signed the registration certificate for the Orange Democratic Movement Party of Kenya. The 'No' team cried foul and accused the Government of having a hand in the registration of "outsiders".
Internal wrangles
They vowed to fight for the name, with Mutula saying they would go to court.
This was never to be, and with time, ODM suffered internal wrangles. At one point, Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and retired President Moi dismissed the movement as an amorphous organ that had outlived its usefulness after the referendum.
They said Kanu would not join ODM in the General Election, saying the Official Opposition party was strong enough to go it alone.
But as Uhuru rubbished the Kanu/ODM partnership, Ruto, Kanu secretary-general, vice-chairman Chris Okemo and other party MPs broke ranks with him and threw their weight behind the union.
The issue threatened to split Kanu until the recent push for minimum constitutional reforms that saw the ODM luminaries and Uhuru quickly close ranks again.

Original_Search_Title: Poaching of MPs Does Not Scare US, Declares Raila
Headline: Kenya: Poaching of MPs Does Not Scare US, Declares Raila
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) yesterday said it was not scared by Narc-Kenya's poaching of MPs.
LDP dismissed defectors as Government sympathisers who had long ceased being in the party. Mr David Musila, the party chairman, and Lang'ata MP, Mr Raila Odinga, said Narc-Kenya was welcome to poach ODM members.
"The list is of people who defected a long time ago. They were in the Banana camp. There is nothing new. We are least worried by people voting with their stomachs," said Raila.
And Mr Gor Sunguh, the Kisumu Town East MP, dismissed defectors as politicians who had lost direction.
Speaking at Kibos Township, near Kisumu Town, Sunguh faulted colleagues who dumped their parties.
MPs Hussein Maalim, Abu Chiaba, Abdalla Ngozi and Fahim Twaha defected to Narc-Kenya during President Kibaki's tour of Coast Province last week. Raila claimed Kibaki was dangling the "Cabinet carrot" before would-be defectors.
"ODM is stronger than it was last year," he declared.
Musila said the party was not scared of members defecting to Narc-Kenya. "We would rather have a few loyal members than have people who are disloyal," he said. Bomet MP Nick Salat, who was listed as among those being reached out by Narc-Kenya, declared he was firmly in Kanu. "I am not even in the so called New-Kanu, but in the original Kanu," he said. "While we agree that there are differences in Kanu, this can only be to a certain level."
He criticised Mr William Ruto, the Kanu Secretary-General, and Mr Musa Sirma, the Eldama Ravine MP, for pushing Kanu chairman, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta, to join ODM. Mr Najib Balala, the MP for Mvita, said he was not headed for the new party. "I am firmly in LDP. I am not moving anywhere," he said.

Original_Search_Title: No to ING
Headline: Nigeria: No to ING
Body_Text:
The Interim National Government debate burst on to public discourse about three weeks ago, when former Anambra State governor, Dr. Chukwu-emeka Ezeife, who was also former political adviser to President Obasanjo canvassed the idea in what he said would be the last option, should the public cynicism about INEC's ability to conduct the 2007 election persist. He has actually denied that the ING was not his main advocacy at the press conference where he claimed to have broached the idea only in passing.
However, since Dr. Ezeife "accidentally" mentioned ING, the idea seemed to have taken a life of its own and has even began to flourish in public discourse like the ill-fated and now rested third term for President Obasanjo.
Thinking back, Nigeria's romance with Interim National Government was brief and equally unpalatable. when the transition programme of former military president, General Ibrahim Babangida, hit the wall in 1993, he set up the makeshift Interim National Government. The contraption was headed by a technocrat, Chief Ernest Shonekan, whose near six months reign was buffeted with public opprobrium. To cap the fate of the Shonekan-led ING, a Lagos High Court declared it illegal. As a contraption without any foundation in law or commonsense, it fell to the plotting strategem of the military.
To therefore refurbish such a monster in whatever form or guise when Nigeria is clearly out of any political emergency or expediency is unacceptable to say the least.
The call for ING for whatever reason or circumstance should be viewed as subversive of the constitutional process which has navigated the country from 1999 to date, its handicaps notwith-standing.
We call on government and other concerned Nigerians to not only repudiate the idea but condemn it as self-serving, unpatriotic and even treasonable. it is totally wrongfooted that a country that is a serious aspirant to the permanent seat of the United Nations Security Council should contenance an idea of government that is available to coup-prone banana republics. The 1999 Constitution has adequate accommodation for political succession through periodic elections and only such option is acceptable to us.
With the strongest words we can muster, we denounce ING as a criminal enterprise that should attract the attention of the guardians of our Constitution and its enforcement agencies.

Original_Search_Title: ZTA Orders Closure of Over 100 Restaurants Says Tour Operators
Headline: Zimbabwe: ZTA Orders Closure of Over 100 Restaurants Says Tour Operators
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Zimbabwe Tourism Authority (ZTA) has ordered the closure of more than 100 restaurants and tour operators countrywide that were operating without registration.
They had been given until August 17, 2006 to register in accordance with the Tourism Act.
Statutory Instrument 106 of 1996 requires designated tourist facilities, including restaurants and car hire agencies, to register under the Act and ZTA had called on all such facilities to comply with the Act.
In Harare alone, over 50 well-established city restaurants have been blacklisted for breaching the Act and most restaurant owners are crying foul, saying ZTA was imposing a second value added tax (VAT) on them.
In his letter to the concerned operators, ZTA chief executive officer Mr Karikoga Kaseke notified the businesses that according to records, they were operating illegally since the facilities were not registered and licenced in terms of section 36 of the Tourism Act of 1996.
"It is our observation that no action was taken to regularise your operations by the set deadline of August 17 2006," read part of the letter signed by Mr Kaseke.
"We are now instructing you, by copy of this letter, to close down your operations as your continued operations violate the provisions of the aforesaid Act."
The letter further warned that continued operations would result in prosecution.
However, a number of facilities were operating normally over the weekend with the owners professing ignorance about the authority's registration deadline while others said they saw no need to register when they were operating in the city and catering for ordinary Zimbabweans.
"We never received any letter for registration. We are operating a bakery and a fast-food outlet. Our clients are mostly local people," said Mr Victor Prandini of Italian Bakery, which sells confectionery, beverages and other light dishes at Avondale Shopping Centre.
"With our kind of outlet, it is difficult to tell who is a tourist and who is not and we don't know how we are going to charge a tourism levy on locals buying bread and cakes. But if it's the law, then we will register and pay." Another operator accused the authority of charging them a second levy.
"Our operations are remotely related to tourism. We are just city restaurants with a mixed client base," said one operator, who declined to be named.
"ZTA does not provide us with any service. They just want to raise money from our operations."
He said restaurants were already paying tax to the Government and municipalities, which provide them with direct services, and had already acquired licences from the Zimbabwe Revenue Authority (Zimra) and the Harare Municipality.
However, other outlets like Chelsea's in the Angwa City complex and St Elmos Restaurant in Avondale said they had registered with the ZTA and were already paying the tourism levy, although their names were still on the list of defaulters.
Full ZTA list of unregistered facilities:
Black Steer, Great Wall Restaurant, Espresso Café Restaurant, Home to Home, Sheila Restaurant, Phenicienne Restaurant, Curries Court Restaurant, Kebab, Numero Uno Restaurant, Adrianne's, The Base, Gogo's, Mokador, Le Jardin, Five Star, Manchurian, Midlands Hotel, Red Diner (Harare, Bulawayo), Keg and Maiden, Sopranos, Matowe Lodge, Roots of Africa Restaurant, Jacaranda Lodge, Mangwana Lodge, Lomagundi Association, Mutare Lodge, Talk of Town Lodge, Earlside Hotel, Paw Paw Lodge, Pentagon Guest House, Reason Travel and Tours, Kopje View Lodge, Lowveld Executive Lodge, Mteri Lodge, Hippo Valley Country Club, New Cheche Complex, Nongo Overnight Lodge, Muonde Lodge, Mkwasine Country Club, Triangle Country Club, Le Canard Teviotdale, Melange, Wombles Restaurant, Alexander's, Allo Allo, Baobab Grill, Barbours Tea Room, Beijing, Blue Banana, Café Monde, Café Med (Sam Levy), Giovanni's, Italian Bakery, Karma Restaurant, Leonardo's, Libby's, Mama Mia, Mimi's, The Point, Priscilla's (Mu kuwisi), Upper Crust (Batanai), Urban Bliss (Golden Stairs), Shangri La, Sherrols in the Park, Sitar, St Elmo's, Bulawayo Hotel School, Birchenough Bridge Hotel, Masvingo Hotel, Players, Chelsea, Hakuna Matata (Vumba) Lodge, Ngundu Rocky Motel, Beehive Lodge (Falls), Dunelark Travel Adventures, Villa de Afrique Lodge, Livingstone Lodge Tranquillity, Royal Lodge (Falls), Richway Guest House, Adonia Lodge, Villa Nova Guest House, Noddy Transport Tours, Joy Peace Lodge, Matopo Rock Motel, Khaya Elihle, Imperial Car Hire, Circle Court Tours, Naledi Adventure, Pulse of Africa, Southern Link Travel and Tours, Sunshine Tours and Travel, Zimbabwe Itinerary, Zimba Co-operative, Gondola Restaurant, Ascot Mews Lodge, Dwala Safaris, One-Stop Shop Restaurant, Grass Hut Coffee Bar, Cape to Cairo, It's Fishy Restaurant, and Ethnic Pot Restaurant.

Original_Search_Title: Do the Academia Matter in Our Economic Revival?
Headline: Uganda: Do the Academia Matter in Our Economic Revival?
Body_Text:
On August 15, 2006, it was a University Day at Makerere University. The day reminded me of the 1980s when every Friday, we had public lectures at Makerere with renowned sons and daughters of Uganda as main speakers. The Makerere Main Hall would indeed see people of all walks of life who would come 'enmasse' to share the knowledge of the great institution, indeed the Harvard of Africa.
On this good Tuesday August 15, 2006, Makerere hosted Prof. Ali Mazrui whom I want to describe as the "father of scholarship" in Africa. The good professor gave an exposition on "The Role of Academia in Politics and Economic Revival with special focus on Africa." After the good professor concluded his submission, I could not wait because I had been captivated to the core. In his exposition, Prof. Mazrui contended that African intellectuals contributed significantly to the political decolonisation of African States. He gave various examples spanning from Jomo Kenyatta, Mwalimu Julius Nyerere etc.
This is the point I would wish to focus on my comment. I argued in the lecture that while the African intellectuals contributed significantly to the political decolonisation of the African states, the change was more or less in the name of political leaders and not a fundamental change.
Most African leaders simply reinforced the colonial State and all its machinations. I further submitted that the political decolonisation never translated in economic de-colonisation. This is manifested in the current poverty levels in most African countries spanning from the Karahari to the Horn of Africa and from Southern Africa to the margrebs.
Currently, poverty in Uganda stands at 38% of the total population of 28 million people (Uganda Bureau of Statistics 2003) with the number of the chronic poor (people that have no hope of ever coming out of abject poverty) staggering at 7,000,000 (Chronic Poverty Report 2005).
In some countries such as Somalia, they have become total failed states. Most other African states largely remain fragile with no semblance of any robust political or economic institution one can talk of. Here you have Zimbabwe, DRC, name it.
I told the audience that most African leaders have failed to build robust economies and have thus remained as leaders of politically independent "banana republics" which are mere appendages of foreign metropolitan western states. In extreme cases, the local polity are calling for recolonisation, the greatest absurdity of the 21st century.
I would like to state that with the current debt burden of $4.6 billion with a debt amortisation of $115 per financial year (Ministry of Finance 2006) and the over 50% donor support to the national budget, it would be bizarre to talk of any independent state with such economic quagmire.
The transformation and the so-called dismantling of the colonial state by many an African state, was therefore an artificial event that currently manifests in serious economic contradictions with resultant increase in either civil conflicts and abject poverty of the majority of the population in these countries.
Now, turning to the role of the academia and intellectuals in influencing political and economic change, Prof. Mazrui correctly submitted that if our intellectuals are indeed to be relevant, they should play a significant role in influencing both political process and economic change.
I would wish to add that this is the very aspect that distinguishes developed countries from developing countries. The configuration of most African states does not allow flexibility for intellectuals to participate in decision making.
There is rampant mistrust of the intellectuals by governments and this has suffocated not only intellectual growth but also constrained contribution of the intellectuals to national development. There is no need for any examples since this is almost the character of most African states.
I would wish to see the more relevance of the academia and intellectuals in influencing political process and economic change. The academia and intellectuals are the "power house" for any serious economy.
I was in the UK in 1992 pursuing my MSc degree at The London School of Economics and I was amazed at how the state profitably uses the academia and intellectuals. There is virtually no monetary policy that can be passed by the Bank of England or any fiscal policy that can be submitted to the British government without the Chancellor of the Exchequer consulting professors at The London School of Economics.
And this "best practice" is replicated in all political and economic decisions of the British government and indeed other governments in the entire European Union.
There is no any other explanation that can best explain the relevance of the universities than the participation of the intellectuals (academia) in the political process and economic management of their countries.
How I wish that our academia and intellectuals can be viewed as people who matter.
The author is a member of the African Regional Panel of Experts on Poverty Eradication.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Likely to Reach Out to Northern Kenya Politicians
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Likely to Reach Out to Northern Kenya Politicians
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Were President Mwai Kibaki to effect a reshuffle, he would most probably reach out to northern kenya politicians in a bid to cement his support in that part of the country.
Residents of the upper part of Eastern Province are particularly keen to see whether the President will retain Youth Affairs minister Mr Mohammed Abdi Kuti as his point man in the region or will sacrifice him for one of the newly elected MPs from Marsabit District.
Kibaki, sources say, could reach out to MPs allied to Kanu and the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) as he lays the ground for the General Election.
Sources say he might be forced to sacrifice some of his Cabinet ministers in a move to undermine Kanu - the Official Opposition party - and ODM.
Those likely to earn a place include North Horr MP, Mr Ukur Yattani or his Laisamis counterpart, Mr Joseph Lekuton.
Ukur is a former DC in Ijara and Kilifi while Lekuton was a university lecturer in the US.
As the President prepares for what some sources say could be a snap election, residents of Isiolo, Marsabit and Moyale districts are anxious to know whether the Youth Affairs minister can rise to the occasion and get Narc-Kenya the necessary votes.
Kuti was elevated to the Cabinet after last November's referendum following the massive defeat of the Banana side. He spearheaded the 'Yes' campaign team in upper Eastern but was rewarded with the Youth Affairs portfolio despite the residents overwhelmingly voting against the proposed new constitution.
The six constituencies in Isiolo, Marsabit and Moyale voted against the draft constitution overwhelmingly. Kuti and the late Abdi Sasura, Titus Ngoyoni and Dr Guracha Galgalo were allies.
Isiolo South MP Abdul Bahari Ali (Kanu) was left in the opposition with his North Horr counterpart, the late Dr Bonaya Godana.
The minister again flunked the test in the recently concluded by-elections in Marsabit and Moyale. His colleagues, Martha Karua, Prof Kivutha Kibwana and Mr John Michuki replaced him in the campaigns in North Horr, Saku and Laisamis.
Prior to the replacement, both Kuti and the minister for Regional Development, Mr Mohammed Abdi Mohamud had differed on who should spearhead the Narc-Kenya campaign in upper Eastern.
Kuti felt he was the regional leader and should therefore lead the campaign while Mohamud restricted his activities to North Eastern Province.
Following protest from Narc-Kenya candidates and their supporters in the four constituencies, both ministers were replaced by colleagues from 'neutral' areas.
He had earlier backed the wrong horse in Saku against the eventual winner, Mr Hussein Sasura, and was forced to take a back seat by Narc-Kenya supporters.
In Laisamis, Kanu's Lekuton waged a campaign against Kuti and this forced the Narc-Kenya candidate, Mrs Mary Ngoyoni, to ask the minister to keep off her campaigns.
In Moyale, the minister could not even reach Golbo division, where his native Sakuye clan resides. He instead dispatched a team of Sakuye elders led by former Isiolo county council councillor Hassan Galma.
Residents of Marsabit's North Horr and Saku constituencies also feel they deserve Cabinet post.
Cabinet ministers, led by Prof Kivutha Kibwana, had promised at several campaign rallies in the constituencies that Yattani and Sasura would be appointed to the Cabinet.
At the same time, residents of the three districts feel it was not in the best interests of the Gabra and the Borana to give either Yattani or Sasura a Cabinet post.
"As it is not possible to give both of them Cabinet posts, it is better to make them assistant ministers," said a resident from Marsabit.
Giving one of them the Cabinet position, he argued, would antagonise the other. Should Yattani, a Gabra, get the post instead of Sasura, the Borana would feel cheated.
The late Abdi Sasura and his Laisamis counterpart, Titus Ngoyoni had complained in the run-up to last year's clashes that their North Horr counterpart, Godana, had used his ministerial position to marginalise the Borana and Rendille.
However, chances are high that both Sasura and Yattani would be appointed assistant ministers.
Narc-Kenya allied ministers, sources say, have also approached Lekuton to persuade him to join the Government side.
The Kanu MP was in Maralal last weekend and met Samburu East MP, Mr Sammy Leshore and other leaders. Last week Lekuton was also reportedly at State House for a meeting with President Kibaki.
Lekuton said he talked to the President about the flooding in Loiyangalani, where six people died.
When asked by The Sunday Standard whether the Narc-Kenya luminaries had approached him, he dismissed the alleged Maralal meeting as propaganda.
"I was sponsored by Kanu and my right place is with the Opposition," he said.
However, Narc-Kenya is determined to woo Lekuton to its side considering that he overcame the Government machinery to beat Ngoyoni's widow in the by-election.
"Narc-Kenya is interested in MPs who can retain their seats during the next elections. Lekuton is one such MP," said an MP, who sought anonymity, from the Maa community.
Chances of the President sacrificing some of his Cabinet ministers would also be high should he think of reappointing Kajiado North MP, Prof George Saitoti and his Imenti North counterpart, Mr David Mwiraria, to the Cabinet.
The Isiolo North MP is said to be close to Information minister Mr Mutahi Kagwe and the assistant minister for Education Dr Kilemi Mwiria.
There are many Tigania voters in his Isiolo North turf, Kuti is now allied to MPs from Meru North as opposed to Meru Central where the Imenti are dominant. His supporters say the minister should keep away from MPs mentioned adversely in the Anglo Leasing scandal.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Doubt Minister's Sincerity
Headline: Kenya: MPs Doubt Minister's Sincerity
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Ms Martha Karua's invitation to all parties to a meeting to discuss the Constitution has elicited mixed reactions.
Liberal Democratic MP Mr Najib Balala yesterday said the party would state its stand collectively.
Speaking at Aga Khan Hall, Kisumu, Balala, who is the MP for Mvita, said the party would deliberate on the issue.
And the chairman of the parliamentary committee on Administration of Justice and Legal Affairs Mr Paul Muite, said Karua's idea was 'positive' but added that she should not set an agenda for the meeting.
Muite said his committee had selected a sub-committee of four MPs and invited party leaders to a meeting on September 5.
Elsewhere, Home Affairs Assistant minister Mr Samuel Moroto yesterday called on Kenyans to ignore those calling for minimum reforms and support President Kibaki's development initiatives.
Speaking in Eldoret during the launch of a malaria campaign, Moroto dismissed proponents of minimal reforms as cowards who have no moral ground to ask Kenyans to go to the streets.
But Kisumu Town West MP Mr Ken Nyagudi, who accompanied Balala at a funds drive in aid of Al-Mu'uminun Integrated Academy, said Karua has no moral ground to chair such a consultative meeting.
Kisumu East MP Mr Gor Sunguh dismissed Karua as partisan and opposed minimum reforms.
"She is not genuine. Let us go the Inter- Parties Parliamentary Group way of 1997," Sunguh said.
And East Africa and Regional Corporation Minister Mr John Koech yesterday termed those calling for minimum reforms as insincere.
Koech said such people are out to bring back the tension and hatred that marked the Orange and Banana campaigns during the November referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Kituyi's Stand On Minimum Reforms
Headline: Kenya: Kituyi's Stand On Minimum Reforms
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Cabinet Minister Mukhisa Kituyi yesterday said he would support minimum reforms ahead of General Election next year only if they are going to create a level playing field.
Anything else, he said, would be unnecessary, as it would recreate the kind of animosity witnessed before last year's referendum on the proposed new constitution.
"Piecemeal reforms on the Constitution would only make sense to me if they would ensure democratic elections next year," Kituyi said. The Kimilili MP was speaking during the official launch of the Childrens' Legal Aid Programme (Clap) at the Imperial Hotel, Kisumu.
Kituyi spoke after lawyers, led by West Kenya Law Society of Kenya Chairman, Mr James Mwamu, challenged him to state his stand on the constitutional stalemate. "We want to know your stand on reforms as Ford-Kenya, your party, has already made its position public," Mwamu said.
Kituyi said demanding for a mere amendment of what were referred to as contentious clauses only months to the general elections would spell doom for the country. "We do not need the battles witnessed before the November 21 referendum in an election year. Reviving the Orange and Banana tugs of war would be dangerous," he said. The National Council of Churches of Kenya (NCCK), the Catholic and part of the Anglican Church of Kenya (ACK) have opposed the piecemeal reforms. The churches have argued that nothing short of comprehensive constitutional review should be allowed.
However, the Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya joined the ODM in demanding for minimum reforms and urged all Muslims to support it. The umbrella body for Muslims, Supreme Council of Kenya Muslims (Supkem), is yet to declare its stand.
Kituyi spoke as Justice Minister Martha Karua said she had convened a meeting for key party leaders to come up with a Draft Bill for completion of the stalled constitutional review.

Original_Search_Title: How Big Money Shapes And Corrupts Local Politics
Headline: Kenya: How Big Money Shapes And Corrupts Local Politics
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In the days before Mr William Mirugi won the nominations for the Nakuru Town by-election, a bitter debate raged between those who supported him and those who wanted a Nakuru businessman to get the ticket.
Subukia MP Mr Koigi Wamwere recalled that key party members argued that Mirugi was young and did not have money. The businessman they wanted had the money.
"We parted ways on who to support. They told me to go to hell with Mirugi's son, and I told them to go to hell with their businessman. I told them to support their candidate with their money and I would support mine with my presence," Wamwere recalled.
At the end of the campaign for party ticket, the businessman was said to have spent Sh12 million and lost the nomination.
Wamwere may have been lucky to have his candidate win without a campaign war chest. But the controversy they had highlights the central role of money in Kenya's politics today.
Key party leaders, citing clear instances, say money no longer buys victory in Kenya.
Kanu secretary general, Mr William Ruto, cites the 2002 elections to support this.
"I believe Narc never spent even half of what we used in the elections. Yet we lost," Ruto said.
Liberal Democratic Party's secretary general, Mr Joseph Kamotho, cites the referendum and the Nakuru Town by-elections to support this stand.
"The Banana team spent billions and lost. In Nakuru LDP put a really strong show without much money," Kamotho said.
"LDP does not believe in politics of money. We believe that to weed out corruption, money politics has to come to an end. We want to help Kenyans move away from electing money. Politics of money makes a clique hold the nation at ransom," Kamotho added.
While these instances indicate that the ordinary voter is becoming harder to buy, there is worry that money still buys MPs in the House and party support.
By the time elections are called next year, most Members of Parliament will have contributed between Sh600,000 and Sh1.2 million to their parties through a check-off system that deducts between Sh10,000 and Sh20,000 from their pay every month.
These contributions may be small but they tell a big story of the increasing role of money in Kenyan politics.
Those already in Parliament use their contributions to force the party to rally behind them against rivals.
Big businessmen who also double as civil servants and party leaders use money to help their business colleagues come to Parliament where they make more money and preside over matters touching on their businesses.
Those who aspire to go to parliament sometimes finance the party through membership contributions from outside, hoping the party will notice them.
Parties, hard pressed for money in the absence of public funding, are often hard pressed to go against big time contributors who can finance whole elections.
In the competition between those who want to come in and those already in and not keen to come out, it becomes a fundraising match.
MPs want to raise huge sums of money to prevent huge sums being spent against them. Raising money becomes a full-time pre-occupation in the House.
In the process, lobbyists pushing partisan agenda take over the legislators and use them against the people they represent.
That scenario looms with every passing day towards the 2002 grudge elections.
An official of one of the parties estimated that one by-election costs at least Sh50 million. The Electoral Commission of Kenya approximates the cost of a General Election to be at least Sh4 billion.
Without public sources of money, parties stick with the men and women who can help raise this amount.
In the match for survival, the desire to pay for the growing cost of political campaigns, how the money is raised ceases to be an issue.
The voters, armed only with their cards, may end up being asked to vote for the person who finances the party and not necessarily the best they would have chosen.
The men who end up in the Cabinet or key parastatals and diplomatic positions may not necessarily be the best the country could afford, but the biggest givers to the party.
The men and women who spend millions trying to get to Parliament embark on how to get this money back and more for re-election immediately they get to the House.
That trend has paved a new road to corruption.
Former MP, Mr Martin Shikuku says it has given birth to mediocre politics. But Wamwere says it has compromised MPs in their work.
In the 1970s, Shikuku recalled, Kenya had a law stating how much politicians could spend on campaigns.
Those campaigning then were not allowed to spend more than Sh40,000. "That law was meant to ensure that those who had stolen money did not use it to buy their way into Parliament. It made politics better. It ensured that ability and not wealth, made one go to Parliament. When that law collapsed, Parliament was opened to mediocres," Shikuku said.
"Today, whoever has the money, wins. Nobody asks where the money has come from," he added.
The more parties stick with those with money to spend, the more politics get corrupt and mediocre, the former MP said.
"If the party tells the people to elect a person they would not have chosen, that person has to work hard and spend a lot to win. How he recovers the money he spent is a different story," Shikuku said.
Wamwere who campaigned under that law in 1974 remembers that he kept receipts of every coin spent. At the end of the campaign, he had to file returns with the District Commissioner.
The law was not very respected. He recalls that a day before elections, he saw his main rival, Mr Kihika Kimani, campaigning in a convoy of 100 vehicles while he had only two. The 100 vehicles must have consumed more than Sh40,000. But the law served a useful role.
"The logic behind the Sh40,000 limit was that election was not an auction where leadership went to the highest bidder. Campaign rallies were for selling ideas, not buying people. Somebody must have feared that leadership would go to the wealthiest and not necessarily the best," Wamwere said.
"That law was an acknowledgement that the wealthy are not necessarily better leaders. Today, we don't even try to distinguish the difference," he explained.
The worry has gone beyond whether the best and not necessarily the richest end up in parliament. The worry is what having money does to Parliament.
A strange thing happened in May 2001. Kenyans reacted with shock when at the peak of the lobby for cheaper Aids drugs, it emerged that companies lobbying against introduction of cheap generic drugs had selected some MPs for a retreat at the Coast. Major firms selected members of the Health, Trade and Education Committees to a three-day retreat to put their case against the introduction of cheap Aids drugs.
In the middle of public protest, the firms denied arranging such a meeting, but some members of that committee went ahead to defend the firms.
A member of the committee said the government had not given the firms a chance to put their case forward and the committee wanted to do that.
At that time, activists targeted Fluconazole, then the only outpatient cure for Cryptococcal Meningitis which killed 10 per cent of HIV patients.
The drug was costing $18 per pill. Patients needed two pills a day for eight weeks and one daily for the rest of their lives.
A generic version was to cost 60 cents a pill. In South Africa, protests had forced Pfizer to offer the drug for free. As a similar scenario loomed in Kenya, drug manufacturers targeted MPs.
Today, Wamwere says, this kind of thing goes on all the time.
"If you spend millions to get to Parliament, you try to get it back immediately you get there. MPs do everything to get this money back," the MP said.
"I sit here, and somebody tells me 'if you give me frequencies, I will give you Sh100,000'. It has happened to me and I believe it happens in a lot of offices and parliamentary committees," says Wamwere who is also an assistant minister for Information.
Wamwere recalls an incident where an outsider approached him with Sh30,000 as a donation towards a harambee. He later learnt that the man had been sent by a minister who needed support for a Bill in the House.
"Immediately I learnt that, I returned the money. I asked myself, 'if the minister is paying everyone this amount, how is he going to get it back? I believed in the Bill. But I withheld support because of this. This kind of business goes on everyday."
Wamwere says he has heard "from corridors of Parliament" tales of MPs being paid to table Bills or run away from them.
"We have had cases where we sit in committees, discuss issues and agree this is a clear case of corruption. But when we come to discussing what punishment to mete out, people just change positions or say that the individual or firm should not be punished. How do you explain that?
"The money candidates spend is investment. They will recover it at all costs. If presidential candidates spend their own money to get to State House, be sure they will want to recover it," Wamwere reiterated.

Original_Search_Title: Arua Park; the Multi-Ethinic Travel And Trade Centre
Headline: Uganda: Arua Park; the Multi-Ethinic Travel And Trade Centre
Body_Text:
Welcome to Arua Park; the open space between Ben Kiwanuka and William streets in Kampala. Seemingly lifted from the pages of a history text book, Stephen Abili says the park reminds you of West African markets where traders sold herbs , copperware, camels.
The group of darker-skinned, mairungi-chewing and bare-chested men whose fun I momentarily interrupted to make a few inquiries, was not impressed. I received the kind of welcome a leper gets at a Jewish marriage festival. Visibly irritated, they quietly examined me with varying degrees of disdain, before dismissing me.
Confused, I sidled up to a pair of middle-aged men sitting on a bench few paces away and explained myself. Fortunately, I made a breakthrough. Also thanks to them, (Zuma Kidi and Hussein Adrisi, both Alur from Nebbi district) I got to know why the mostly wiry men I had earlier met and who I later learned were Sudanese, had behaved in such an unfriendly way. "Just play it cool. They were only reflecting what they are by culture. Among the Dinka, it is unacceptable to disrupt someone who is eating," explains Zuma, a plump businessman who shuttles between Juba in Southern Sudan and Kampala. Interestingly, the men regarded their khat (mairungi) as food.
Nearby, sweaty men were quickly loading and offloading goods from heavy-duty trucks and trailers like they had just stolen them. Others were hauling merchandise into shops with fluid ease. Women wearing white and blue aprons walked up and down selling foodstuffs in the dust while boda boda cyclists winked suggestively at everyone they thought was a potential client.
Music was blasting out from shops and restaurants that one would be excused to imagine the whole area was a wild discotheque of sorts. Some idlers were seen taking a nap on the back of pickup vehicles in the parking stretch and on the alleyways-looking completely aloof from the drone of activities taking place around them.
Brisk business transactions take place at every turn and money swiftly change hands. The pool of traffic that thunderby also add a mix to the noise poolution, and the resultant buzz could be likened to the booming of surf on a distant shore.
Welcome to Arua Park; the open space between Ben Kiwanuka Street and William Street in Kampala. Seemingly lifted from the pages of some off-the-syllabus O'level history text book, Arua Park reminds you of those West African markets where traders came to sell herbs, copperware, camels, iron products and everything-the stuff of ancient mythology.
Arua Park is an all-embracing multi-ethnic place that draws people from as far as Southern Sudan and the DR Congo just to get involved in the trade here. However, a big number of people in the park hail from Arua (from where it derives its name), Nebbi, Moyo and Adjumani districts. It is a predominantly an enclave for West Nilers or 'home boys' commune of sorts.
No sooner had I stepped into the park than I was impressed by the trading that goes on in the area. Within a short radius, you could find virtually anything-from household wares, hardware and state-of-the-art satellite communication equipment. Indeed, the array of goods on display make Kikuubo lane downtown look like a roadside kiosk.
In a stationary and electronics shop I visited, I asked the shop owner, Peter Ondia, the source of his stock.
"Very many places," Ondia answered.
"And how do they get here?" I asked.
"We bring them," he answered.
With these cut answers, I rested my case.
I later found out that being close to DR Congo, the Arua boys (comprising the Alur, Madi, Lugbara and Kakwa), find it easy to get to the vibrant border market of Ariwara to purchase electronics, cheap fuel and just about anything else, load them on trucks and drive through the bushes back to Arua and enroute to Kampala.
Fortunately, the Uganda-DR Congo border is vast and porous, making it difficult for Special Revenue Protection Service (SRPS) to effectively monitor the movement of goods. This explains why Arua district and Arua Park are one of the most flourishing business hubs in the country. Besides, there is no reason for forgiving anyone who takes advantage of the near nil tax trade across the border to be poor. What with the illegal brisk fuel business in Arua Park where smugglers have even acquired celebrity status.
As I explored the commercial activities at Arua Park, I met Lucy Munduru, business woman of Madi origin, who I asked about the history of Arua Park.
"I think this place started in the 1970s," she said as she looked at her friends for endorsement. She got none. And depending on who you ask, the answers vary. I found out that very few people know the real history of the park and many of them are not even keen to know the history. However, they know that the park's strategic location offers a lot of business opportunities.
Arua Park and President Museveni's NRM regime came at around the same time-which means the park has been in existence since 1986.
Before 1986, there were two state-owned bus companies, Peoples Transport Company (PTC) that operated mainly in Jinja, and Uganda Transport Company (UTC), then based in Kampala. But because of mismanagement, corruption and embezzlement of funds resulting in heavy losses, the government decided to divest the two companies. But still realising no profits, the two companies had to close in the early 1990s.
When Mr Museveni came to power, the then UTC bus park situated in the space now occupied by Mukwano Shopping Arcade on Ben Kiwanuka Street, was banned. This meant that only one park-Baganda Bus Park-was left in Kampala. But the buses were many for the park to accommodate.
Matters have even been made worse by ambitious businessmen's scrumble for the bus park; looking at it as a very lucrative and rewarding investment.
And because the Baganda bus park could no longer accommodate all buses, the owners of West Nile-bound buses, seeked sanctuary at Nebbi park, a stone throw away from Arua Park. However, this park also turned be very congested with buses. Tired of the inconvenience, the Arua boys established a new home at the current location.
Over the years, Arua Park has transformed from an enclave occupied by Arua natives to a multi-ethnic-binding business and commercial centre. Non-West Nilers with a commanding presence here include the Baganda, Itesot and Acholi. There are also some Kenyan and Tanzanian patrons at the park. Because of this motley of tribes and nationality, the main lingua franca at the park is Swahili. Even the young boys and girls selling roast groundnuts and sweet bananas try to bubble a brand of Swahili that would make a Tanzanian native or Kenyan envious.
Speaking in heavily accented English, Tobias Bebe, a young southern Sudanese who owns a restaurant at the park, told me that he started the business after receiving Shs5 million from a relative who is living in the UK last year. Bebe said he went through bureacratic hardships to get a license to start operations. He said his business has enabled him live a better life than he would back home in Sudan.
Thanks to his business, Bebe has managed to educate his children and sisters, including one who recently graduated as lawyer from Makerere University.
Interestingly, Arua Park is no longer a bus park though it still carries the tag. Now a thriving business locale, the only relic to that effect in Arua Park are the scores of offices of buses that ply the West Nile and northern Uganda routes such as the Nile Coach, Gaaga, BTC, Luxury transport companies, among others.
The park also boasts of several small lodges and guesthouses at almost every turn you take-and many more are still sprouting overnight, a clear indication that the people behind the business are making a killing.
Food served in eating houses, restaurants and verendas at the park is exceptionally pocket-friendly. That is especially if you enjoy West Nile and northern Uganda delicacies such as kisira, mulukya, bamiya, malakwang and kalo. The price for meals meander between Shs500 and Shs800, save for fish and chicken, the most expensive items on the menu priced at Shs1,000.
Kampala City Council (KCC) has plans to unleash a pleasant surprise at Arua Park. According to KCC Chief City Planner Charles Kyamanywa, plans are underway to redevelop the park. The development entails putting up modern structures such as offices and shopping malls, complete with adequate parking.
Surely, Arua Park enthusiasts cannot ask for more.

Original_Search_Title: Pope Benedict XVI Makes Six Ugandan Priests Monsignors
Headline: Uganda: Pope Benedict XVI Makes Six Ugandan Priests Monsignors
Body_Text:
Kampala — THEY have something in common. They have served for over 20 years in the priesthood and have been appointed monsignors. Last Sunday Archbishop Emmanuel Wamala announced that Pope Benedicto XVI had elevated six Ugandans priests from Kampala Diocese to the title of Monsignor.
At a one-week synod held at Pope Memorial Centre, Rugaba, little did they know that at the end of the synod they would hold new titles.
Monsignor John Baptist Ssebayiga Kiwanuka, the parish priest of Mitala Maria who was ordained as a priest on June 19, 1983 says since childhood he wanted to wear an alb and this inspired him ito join the seminary.
His turning point was when he joined Nyenga Seminary in 1976, since then his life changed and he has served in several capacities until last Sunday when he was elevated to that title.
Msgr. Joseph Njala, the Parish Priest of Busega, says that because he was the first-born in his family, his parents urged him to become a priest.
Born 54 years ago, Njala says that he received the news with shock because it's one of the things that he had never dreamt of becoming.
"I approached Cardinal Wamala who later encouraged me saying that this is an honour from the Pope, that is when I got courage and praised God for this wonderful gift," Njala said.
Namugongo Parish Priest Father Charles Kato Katongole says, "My grandfather was a friend of Saint Balikuddembe, one of the martyrs. This made me a religious person. So it came as no surprise when I joined the seminary in order to serve Jesus."
On receiving the news of his elevation, Msgr. Kato says, "I was surprised and kept on asking myself, 'why me of all people?' and I still don't have the answer. I just have to thank God and accept what the Pope decided."
Msgr. John Wynand Katende, also a Parish Priest at Foyer Namungongo, says that when he was a little boy he used to admire the alb and this made him live up to his expectation.
Katende says, "When we were young I used to wear a fresh banana leaf to emulate the fathers and I would preach to my colleagues. At times we would organise small wedding occasions and I would be the preacher.
"Being elevated to a monsignor isn't something you work to get. What I prayed for was that God enables me to be his servant. What I didn't pray for, God has given me. I am surprised because out many, I was chosen," Katende said.
The 44-year-old priest says that, unlike during the times of Emmanuel Cardinal Wamala when the young priests used to be confined to the seminary, today they are no longer confined to the seminary.
Msgr. Paul Ssemogerere has been holding this title, but he, too, was officially honoured. To him the news humbled him.
"I was grateful to the Pope for recognising my humble service," he said.
Ssemwogerere will clock 25 years in the priesthood in December 2008. He says, he was inspired to join the priesthood by Emmanuel Cardinal Nsubuga, who was his mentor.
"Father Katongole also prepared me to take up the priesthood vocation and I thank them for this," Ssemwogerere says.
Father Gerald Kalumba, the Parish Priest of Rubaga, says he was not surprised because in the Catholic Church it is not a post, you remain serving like any other priest, it's only the dress code that changes and at times the Cardinal can delegate you to officiate at some of the church functions where he may not be available.
"I respect the title and want to encourage Christians that nothing will change. It's a mechanism in the church which has its purpose. It's only a title," he said.
Kalumba says he was a bit surprised but thanked God for the gift and thanked those who honoured him.

Original_Search_Title: Constitution in the Spotlight Again, As Elections Near
Headline: Kenya: Constitution in the Spotlight Again, As Elections Near
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The fraught process of constitutional review in Kenya marked another chapter this week, with an announcement by President Mwai Kibaki that there would be no partial reform of the constitution ahead of general elections next year.
This came after certain parliamentarians proposed changes, dubbed "minimum reforms", saying another whole scale review of the constitution was not feasible before the 2007 poll; the reforms include having the president stripped of his exclusive right to appoint members of the 21-person Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK).
With the tenure of most current commissioners coming to an end next month, the fear is that Kibaki might include government supporters amongst the new appointments, who could exercise undue influence over next year's vote. The ECK oversees presidential, parliamentary and local government elections in the East African country.
Another key demand was for presidential authority to be reduced, and for the head of state to share power with the newly-created post of prime minister, and two deputies.
This echoes the wishes expressed by Kenyans to the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission, a body appointed in 2000 to discover what form citizens wished their new constitution to take. In nation-wide consultations, the commission found that Kenyans wanted presidential powers to be curtailed, apparently in reaction to abuse of these powers by previous heads of state.
Reduced authority was included in the initial version of the new constitution that was subsequently drawn up: the so-called "Bomas draft". It was named after "Bomas of Kenya", a cultural venue outside the capital of Nairobi where the National Constitutional Conference (NCC) met in 2003 and 2004 to hammer out a new constitution. The NCC comprised delegates from a cross-section of Kenyan society.
However, ruling party legislators later changed the Bomas draft to maintain strong powers for the head of state. Under their revisions, a non-executive prime minister would have been appointed by the president.
Alterations to the Bomas draft divided the ruling National Rainbow Coalition (NARC), prompting one of its factions, the Liberal Democratic Party, to join forces with the opposition in lobbying for the draft constitution to be rejected during a referendum held in November 2005 -- this under the auspices of the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM).
Reports indicated that Kibaki and Liberal Democratic Party leader Raila Odinga had agreed Odinga would be given the post of prime minister, in return for supporting Kibaki in elections that brought NARC to power at the end of 2002. A number of politicians pushing "minimum reforms" have also expressed interest in the premiership.
Those who opposed the draft constitution adopted the orange as their symbol, while constitutional supporters chose the banana -- this in a bid to assist illiterate Kenyans during the referendum campaign, which claimed several lives.
Almost 60 percent of people who voted in the poll rejected the draft constitution.
"People made a statement by rejecting the draft constitution. People were not for the constitution that the government was proposing because it was geared towards government interests and not the interests of the people," said Hilda Obyerodhiambo, deputy director of the Citizens Coalition for Constitutional Change -- a Nairobi-based non-governmental organisation.
Parliamentarians who have spearheaded the latest "minimum reforms" are drawn largely from the ODM.
But, the proposed changes have been dismissed by civil society organisations.
"This clamour for minimum reforms is aimed at demobilising the national momentum for the long desired comprehensive reforms," said a statement issued this week by groups involved in overhauling the existing constitution, which has been in place since Kenya gained independence from Britain in 1963.
These organisations have instead called on government to present a plan for how a new constitution can be provided.
In March this year, authorities appointed a commission to advise on just that. But Obyerodhiambo has little faith in the body.
"The commission was just to pass time. There is no political will because the government has reneged on previous pledges to deliver a new constitution," she noted.
Kibaki initially promised a new constitution within 100 days of coming to power.

Original_Search_Title: Baywatch And K4 Spoke!
Headline: Ghana: Baywatch And K4 Spoke!
Body_Text:
The president of the Republic of Ghana, His Excellency Mr. John Agyekum Kufuor, over the weekend put his boot in the debate regarding who is likely to be culpable in the ongoing investigations into missing drugs, both from the custody of security agencies and a vessel, M.V. Benjamin, at a time that it was under state security surveillance.
The President, as was to be expected, received flak from the National Democratic Congress (NDC)'s Rojo (Robert Joseph) Mettle-Nunoo, the man whose association with the business of a Venezuelan, suspected to be a drug baron, the President had used as his premise for concluding that the NDC had brought drugs into the country.
But that was not all. News analysts, politicians, social commentators and serial callers as well, attempted to tear the president to pieces for what was considered his bad verses, even as his party's General Secretary, Nana Ohene Ntow and a few others tried desperately to tidy up the mess.
He would not be restrained The Chronicle had called on the President to zip up his mouth at least for as long as Mrs. Justice Georgina Woode's committee, tasked with finding the circumstances under which cocaine got missing under the watchful eyes of state security agents, continued its work.
But the President would not be restrained. Rather than listening to good counsel from The Chronicle and well-meaning Ghanaians who would not want to see their leader come under a hurricane here (not in Louisiana), the President decided to step up the elephant dance he had started.
A few days later, he went the extra mile to tell Ghanaians through the Ghanaian Times that there are credible security dispatches to him indicating that an opposition party's movements suggested complicity in drugs dealings!
Currently, all judges except for a few are enjoying their annual legal vacation. But what do we have here? Mrs. Georgina Woode is sacrificing her holiday, presiding over a Ministerial Committee striving to get to the bottom of an issue of missing drugs, some of which may already be flowing through the bloodstreams of sons and daughters of the soil!
But the Interior Minister's boss, John Kufuor, in whose domain rests all the executive powers of the state, has declared that culprits who have brought or attempted to bring drugs into the country belong to the camp of his political opponents!
At least as we sit now, the only person in party colours facing court action on drug charges, fortunately outside the jurisdiction of this country and for which reason no one can attribute the trial to political 'witch hunting,' is Mr. Eric Amoateng, New Patriotic Party (NPP) Member of Parliament (MP) for Nkoranza North. How about that?
The question that immediately arises is: Was the president being briefed about the movements of Amoateng before his arrest?
If so, what did he do about it?
And in any case he has not told Ghanaians what he has done about the reports he received about opposition party elements' 'druggy' movements.
If indeed he received information about movements of Amoateng that could be interpreted to be engaging in drug peddling and he did not do anything about it, then he has done a great disservice to this nation.
He failed to prevent this international disgrace of having a member of our important law-making body languishing in an American jail.
Monkeys and Bananas But for now, the Chief Executive of the land has pronounced his verdict and re-emphasized it that "we know who brings cocaine into the country". Does Mrs. Woode need to continue sitting, since the President has the answers?
By the way, since the drugs we are searching for got lost under the watchful eyes of state security, how can we trust the same security to find it?
Can we trust monkeys to search for, find and declare missing bananas?
The Attorney General's Orders Already, the arrests of some witnesses who had appeared before the Georgina Woode Committee even before the Committee was through with them, clearly undermines the work of that Committee no matter the pronouncements to the contrary.
After all, the Committee is a fact-finding one and if facts remained undisclosed to it due to the decision of the Attorney General to arrest principal witnesses very early in the day, the Committee becomes a scarecrow!
It becomes dangerous to volunteer information to it, and since it lacks any powers to compel witnesses to appear before it, why would any sane person risk an appearance? Let all who are unsure of motives behind this drama hold their peace.
Does it not make sense that you leave these witnesses with tails as they appeared before Justice Woode to rope in more members of the wider net in which they operate if indeed they are key culprits?

Original_Search_Title: Patriotism, My Foot!
Headline: Zimbabwe: Patriotism, My Foot!
Body_Text:
No — AT independence in 1980 it was a piece of cake for the self-serving ruling ZANU PF to pull the wool over the eyes of the people and masquarade as patriots driven by the desire and passion to bring about a Zimbabwe many sacrificed life and limb for.
I remember vividly that amidst that patriotic fervour and state of euphoria it was common to see seething masses of people cheering and patriotically waving flags and banners at ZANU PF officials. It was easy because then, various sections of the population were united by a common hatred for the white minority regime.
But not any more. As already observed by Michael Barone, Senior Staff Editor of Reader's Digest -- major changes in society sometimes announce themselves with a bang and sometimes tiptoe in silently -- as did the new political realities which took ZANU PF, arrogant enough to think that it is the only capable political force in the country, by surprise. Thus gradually, inevitable questions began to emerge over the socio-political orientation of the country as well as the quality and pace of political reforms as ZANU PF pushed for a one-party state.
This was against the grain of the aspirations of the nation. And it not only ignited a dog fight within the ruling party's own ranks but also cost ZANU PF popular support. The ruling party's claims to patriotism and commitment to upholding democratic principles that would transform Zimbabwe into a land of unlimited opportunities where each and everyone of the country's citizens could pursue their wildest dreams, were in the process exposed for what they are -- cheap politicking that seeks to narrow the definition of patriotism to mean fanatical support for ZANU PF. I don't want to be uncharitable but since then, if any of the speeches on patriotism by ruling party officials are memorable for anything, it is for their polemics rather than their substance.
My small dog-eared dictionary defines patriotism as love for your country and willingness to defend it. But is this what the wealth-accumulating Zimbabwean politicians for whom it is difficult to accept criticism without rancour, mean when they repeatedly importune journalists to be patriotic? Hardly. To the hypocritical politicians, known for their patronising arrogance, the interests of the nation are the same as those of ZANU PF. Thus ZANU PF is Zimbabwe and Zimbabwe is ZANU PF. Hence their narrow perception of patriotism, which is defined in terms of political party affiliation. Yet nothing could be further from the truth and I find the politicians' sentiments ninety-nine times in a hundred singularly repulsive! It is absolutely sickening.
The bottom line is that the politicians would like Zimbabwe to be a place where many areas of public life should remain opaque and unfriendly to scrutiny or some utopian society that should emphasise differences quietly with the utmost of subtlety. Under such circumstances, a crusading private media is grossly misunderstood and treated with suspicion. This explains why the private media has always proved to be a banana skin for the authorities for piercing through the government's self-serving veils of secrecy.
Thus the main reason behind this chorus of patriotism (read support for ZANU PF) is that the unaccountable politicians now view the small but vibrant private media, which does not shy away from debating essential political and social problems in the country, as part of the problem in Zimbabwe and not their policies. And the politicians now seek to guide journalists in search of the truth and informing the country's poverty-stricken citizens who are expecting commodities back on the shelves and peace back to their souls.
It was author Thomas Blanton who said that motivations for curtailing freedom of information have been as varied as the circumstances in each country that has sought it. But in Zimbabwe it has more to do with the desire to keep a tight lid on government failures. And these calls for patriotism, which have been repeated ad nauseam, are part of the broader political machinations to muzzle the media.
I was therefore not surprised when ZANU PF spokesman, Nathan Shmuyarira added his voice to the annoying cacophony of calls by ruling party politicians who loathe a no-holds-barred debating society, for journalists to be patriotic. Indeed, that calls for patriotism must come from those responsible for the waste, abuse and plunder of public resources and whose disastrous and sometimes irrational policies have spawned a socio-economic crisis of nightmarish proportions, would in any other country, be nothing short of a curious paradox. The question begs, if patriotism means love for your country and willingness to defend it, how did the "patriotic" ZANU PF reduce the erstwhile regional breadbasket into a banana republic? How do you destroy something you love?
