There is no arguing that economic progress in Zimbabwe is being held back by the dead hand of the ruling ZANU PF's obsession with revolutionary mantras and hatred for the West, Band-Aid approach to serious issues and wasteful pork-barrel projects such as the unbudgeted-but-politically convenient payouts to former freedom fighters and military adventurism in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Yes, I know that success has no orphan. But just like they take credit for liberating Zimbabwe, which nobody denies them, ZANU PF must also take full responsibility for plunging the country into an unprecedented crisis.
Thus those who have visited misery upon millions of Zimbabweans are in the forefront of preaching patriotism through their virulent nationalism. And when Shamuyarira and his colleagues say journalists should be patriotic they mean to say journalists who should, by virtue of their profession be in the eye of the storm and be brave enough to state opinions that might even upset certain people, should lose their objectivity in a hear, see and speak no evil manner.
The message is loud and clear: if you are patriotic stop highlighting the irony of a black underclass under a supposed majority black government, the stifling of democratic space through the arbitrary closure of newspapers as happened under Ian Smith's Rhodesia when the Daily News of which Shamuyarira was the first black editor was closed down in the 1960s in much the same way the incumbent government closed down the later-day Daily News co-founded by exiled journalist Geoffrey Nyarota, the unprecedented economic melt-down and the resultant disappearance of jobs and cycle of broken families.
In other words journalists, whose work should represent things as they are in real life, should paint a rosier-than-real picture of the depressing situation in Zimbabwe. To the politicians who see journalists as ravening wolves, the media should hold political bias in favour of course of the sacred cow -- ZANU PF. They are, to all intents and purposes, saying journalists should do the bidding of ZANU PF! This explains the politicians' unconcealed satisfaction with Zimpapers where scribes practise uncritical journalism that went out with Noah's ark and unashamedly attach the names of the authors to the articles. But wouldn't it be absurd for journalists with conscience, values and opinions and who know that honesty is the best policy, to debase themselves in that way?
That ZANU PF, not only as the ruling party but also the author of the deepening crisis which the politicians prefer to call challenges, cannot by any stretch of the imagination escape the harsh glare of the media spotlight is conveniently lost on the politicians.
What the politicians should realise is that when we (journalists) say that we follow the flag in journalism, we mean that the broader national interests take precedence above everything else. It does not mean that journalists should gloss over issues and pander to the whims of powerful politicians in line with parochial and self-serving patriotism like that espoused by ZANU PF. Facts are sacred in journalism and that it is why the media has to tell it like it is -- warts and all. And that is the bottom line!

Original_Search_Title: On Kigali Streets And People's Love for Kagame
Headline: Rwanda: On Kigali Streets And People's Love for Kagame
Body_Text:
Kigali — After leaving Saint Famille I asked the driver to take us to where I could look for some Rwandan clothes. He spoke very good English and on the way to the shopping area we discussed the Genocide, the subject having come up after the visit to the church.
He wondered how it came to be that we were in Rwanda and so I told him that on my website I monitor events all over the world, searching for the truth, and toward that goal I had written about Rwanda and President Kagame and now wanted to see for myself the nation famous throughout the world for having climbed out from below ground-zero economically and socially to now being the lone example of peace, prosperity and good governance in Africa. He agreed with that perception of Rwanda, saying that it was a million times better than it had been before the Genocide and that what Kagame had done to improve the country was amazing. He believed in Kagame's wish that the Hutu and Tutsi forgive and learn to live as one. But he said that there were still some people who did not want to admit to their role in the Genocide and were afraid of witnesses who could expose them.
I showed him my "Orwell Today" card with the All-Seeing-Eye logo and explained that it was symbolic of what Orwell meant by BIG BROTHER IS WATCHING YOU in his famous book "1984". At that point the driver turned around and said, "And it's symbolic of YOU watching everything that's going on in the world and telling the truth about it, and being an 'all-seeing-eye' yourself". I thought that was pretty hilarious as I had never thought of it that way before.
By this time we had arrived at an area where I could find some clothes shops and he parked in a garage parking lot where he said he would wait for us to finish. Bob, my husband, said he would go to give a soccer ball away and so we split up - me going down one side of the street to look in stores, and he going down the other side to look for kids.
The first place I went into sold only material, no ready-made clothes, and I was just about to walk out when I noticed a picture of President Kagame on the wall behind the counter.
I asked the shop owner if I could take a photo of the picture and he said "Yes" and then asked if he could be in it as well. I said "Sure", and then another man came out from behind the curtain and said he wanted to be in the picture too. I asked them if they liked President Kagame and they said "Yes, Yes" and then happily posed with him in between. The reflection of my orange blouse can be seen in the glass under the counter as I snap the photo. I thanked them very much and told them they would be famous one day because I would be putting that picture on my website for all the world to see.
I did not notice, until I got home and developed the picture, that the photo of Kagame is on a soccer poster celebrating the KAGAME CUP 2004, which is so appropriate as at that very moment Bob had found some recipients for a soccer ball.
When I stepped out of the store I saw him across the street talking to three young boys in various steps of height.
He explained later that he had approached them walking toward him and asked "Do you boys play soccer?" to which the boy in the beige outfit patted his chest a few times saying "goal keeper". Bob asked him if he could take a picture of him holding a soccer ball. They all looked bewildered. Then he reached into his backpack and pulled out the pump and started putting it together. The kids were still bewildered. Then he pulled out the deflated soccer ball and their eyes lit up when they realized what the pump was for.
The three boys and Bob are lost in the crowd, but on the ground in the circle formed around him, the little boy in the beige is pumping up the ball. Afterwards, Bob organizes them into a semi-circle for an official portrait.
Notice how tightly the little boy is holding on to the ball, and how his friend has his hand on it too? But also notice that there is another boy there who seems to be holding a slightly controlling hand on his shoulder. As soon as I stepped back from taking the photo, and thanked him for posing, he sprinted away and up the street so fast that he was yards away before anyone noticed he was gone. Then everyone started chasing him and Bob yelled "RUN!" which got a laugh out of the crowd.
We talked about it afterwards saying how it was almost reminiscent of the little boy running from Saint Famille into the UN refugee truck, all those years ago. We were pretty sure that with those running shoes on and his attitude, there wasn't anyone who could catch him.
When we got back into the taxi and told the driver about it, he said he had watched the whole thing. In the back seat of his car I found a baseball-size ball made of twine which he said was what many children played with, and he gave it to us as a present, and it is a very valued memento.
After that we went to another section of town where I found a souvenir shop full of fantastic Rwandan crafts, including a motor-cycle and rider made of banana leaves and a similarily made three-tiered mobile depicting seventeen characters in various Rwandan activities.
When we got back to the hotel we arranged for the driver to pick us up later as we were going out to dinner with Cecile, the friend who had given me the sari. But that gave us a bit of time to relax and unwind by the pool with a bottle of beer for Bob and a maracuja for me.
Genocidal saint famille
On Wednesday, the day after the July 4th Liberation Day celebrations, we caught a taxi outside the hotel and asked the driver to take us downtown and to Saint Famille, the church where thousands of Tutsi had run for safety after the start of the Genocide, only to be handed over to the genocidal Interahamwe ("those who attack together") by the priest who was in charge there.
The breathtaking view in the top photo below is what a person sees as they come down the hill from the main commercial area in Kigali. That is the Saint Famille church on the right.
We would have gone inside but the huge doors were locked tight. There was hardly anyone around. As we stood in the courtyard in front of the church I told my husband a story I had read about how the United Nations came one day to evacuate a refugee exchange between the Hutu Government and the Rwandese Patriotic Front. They would only take people whose names were on their list and desperate people were begging to not be left behind, as the Interahamwe came regularly to take them away for massacre. A five-year-old boy broke through the barricades and made a run for the UN truck as it was pulling out, jumping on the back and getting away. The crowd left behind all cheered.
I recalled as well the story of how the Rwandese Patriotic Front had made a daring raid, in the middle of the night, into what at that time was enemy territory, and rescued 600 Tutsi refugees from Saint Famille, similiar to how they had rescued hundreds one time at the Amahoro Stadium.
I also recalled the story of how General Dallaire, the commander of UN unpeacekeeping in Rwanda had held meetings with the leaders of the Interahamwe, the gangs of murdering youths who manned all the road blocks and slaughtered those with Tutsi identity cards. He had forced himself to be polite and shake their hands (thus the title of his book, SHAKE HANDS WITH THE DEVIL). On that same day - which was May 1st - after getting back to UN headquarters at the Amahoro Stadium, he heard on the radio that some mortar rounds had hit the Saint Famille church. At this time the then Government and the Rwandese Patriotic Front were fighting for control of Kigali and people and buildings sometimes got caught in the crossfire. When he got to Saint Famille there was chaos, with people terrified and lying bloody all over the courtyard. The refugees, seeing him and his men in their blue berets and uniforms, asked why they never did anything to stop the Interahamwe who were coming to the church all the time to haul them away. He said he explained that his soldiers were there to be "peaceful" not to "fight" but the people could never understand that concept.
To be continued
Jackie Jura is an independent researcher monitoring local, national and international events

Original_Search_Title: Reading Project to Improve Critical Thinking
Headline: Eswatini: Reading Project to Improve Critical Thinking
Body_Text:
Mbabane — Pre-school children in Swaziland are the focus of a novel large-scale reading project that aims to push literacy levels to 100 percent among the adult population and encourage independent thinking.
Although the country of about one million people has a literacy rate of more than 80 percent - relatively high compared to other countries in the region - this figure is misleading, according to the Swaziland Reading Association, as the ability to read does not necessarily translate into critical thinking.
"The way to promote literacy is to encourage the thought process. The more you learn, the more you question," said Spangle Mmema, head of the association. He believes that developing critical thinking will lay a foundation for future leaders to find creative local solutions to Swaziland's problems, rather than relying on foreign expertise.
"Literacy means understanding, and in some cases going beyond the text. Adults taught by the old ways don't do that. We want critical thinking from the earliest age, at the pre-school level. Children need to ask 'Why?' more often," said Mmema, who is also a primary school deputy principal in the capital, Mbabane.
In Swaziland, a conservative society ruled by executive monarch King Mswati, the concept of child rights is sometimes viewed as a challenge to customary disciplinary measures by which children are raised.
Mmema said it was a delicate path to tread in such an environment, but adults should also be taught not to feel threatened by a child's independence of thought.
The project, launched in the central region of Manzini last week, has the backing of the education department and textbook publishers Macmillan.
It will mount the country's first nationwide alphabet- and word-recognition initiative. Workshops were held to instil pre-school teaching skills in rural educators, with practical sessions using children and word posters.
Five year-old Tebenguni munched on a banana given to her by her educator; when prompted, she responded by pointing out a banana bunch on a fruit poster and then worked out the pronunciation of the word with her teacher.
"We are excited by this approach. When we first went out to meet the teachers and pre-schoolers, we didn't know what to expect, but the reception has been overwhelming - the kids are fascinated by books," Macmillan's sales manager for Swaziland, Lesotho and Mozambique, Chris Ntshangase, said in an interview.
"We felt the pre-school side of education, which is vitally important, has been neglected. Rural schools have no access to literature and books," he said.
According to the United Nations Development Programme, two-thirds of Swaziland's people live below the poverty line.
Donating books to children who have yet to learn to read appeared premature, so the publisher opted to give away posters worth R30,000 that link illustrations of the alphabet, food and animals to their English names. Pre-school textbooks written in SiSwati, the local language, will be donated to daycare centers and other children's institutions.
"Children must not forget where they came from, nor their mother tongue," said Gwendolyn Simelane, an early child development official with the education ministry. "English is the second official language in Swaziland, but the curriculum simultaneously teaches in SiSwati."
The project will fan out to the country's other three regions next year.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: The Tragedy of Being Pregnant in Rural Africa
Headline: Rwanda: The Tragedy of Being Pregnant in Rural Africa
Body_Text:
Kigali — At the heart of every human experience is the desire to survive and prosper. To live without fear, hunger or suffering. To imagine how your life could be better and then have the means yourself to change it. Yet, every day, 1.2 billion people - one fifth of the world's inhabitants - cannot fulfil their most basic needs, let alone attain their dreams or desires.
Being pregnant in Africa is so tragic if you come from a poor family. Either God will help you deliver safely or by bad luck you or the baby will die during childbirth. Only those who have the money to afford the hospital and clinic fees can be cared for by specialists. A minister can send a close relative to have her baby overseas whereas the poor women are expected to deliver in the remote village under the supervision of local medicine women. It is only because of the strong beliefs in some kind of spiritual power and the natural desire to have children that keeps the hope of a poor pregnant woman alive. Being pregnant in Africa is like watching the sun going down before you get home. It is all gloomy and there is no light shining down the way - only uncertainty and desperation. African nations must fight against all Pregnancy is a fearful and life-threatening thing to most African women especially those living in remote areas. Lack of maternal education is one among many reasons. Age is a factor too. It is not uncommon to find very little girls pregnant, they themselves still children. Often long distances must be travelled to reach health centers. Women close to giving birth start walking the long distance to the clinic but then some end up having their babies along the way. This is obviously unsafe and many die in the process. One cannot deny that poverty is behind maternal deaths. Here poverty can be looked at in two perspectives. One is poverty at the family level where services at health centers cost more than what they have and secondly it is seen at government level. In Africa a lot of the time, you find that there are few hospitals or sometimes even none at all in remote areas even though many women live there. Poor economic development especially in villages discourages midwives and doctors from working in those areas and as a result women fail to have qualified people attend to them.
Get this moving story from an experience of a young town boy who had gone to visit his grand mother in the darkest corner of rural Africa. This boy is nicknamed the 12years old midwife. He narrates it as thus:
One morning I woke up only to be a midwife or a medical doctor if you like. How did this comes up? I had gone to visit my grand mother in the village. You know my grand father used to have multiple wives and therefore many children. The children could not easily know each other, as they were scattered al over. So one of my sisters in the village was pregnant and due. One morning she woke up complaining of stomach pains and she looked un-confortable. She was in her last stages of pregnancy. At first my grand mother in her late 90's and I, did not care the complaints of the expecting mother. However, when she complained for long up to 5.00pm, I guessed she was experiencing labour pains from my primary six science. I quickly went to tell my grandmother who was half blind what I suspected. She smiled a bit and told me not to worry as everything was going to be all right. I was not satisfied and went back to the lady and asked her what I could do to help her. "Is there any small d ispensary or clinic where I can go and call a doctor?" I asked. She shook her head to tell me that there was no such a place. I was so frustrated and did not know what to do. I sat down next to her in a small grass thatched house. We were getting light from a candle that used paraffin and we were running short of it. It was at 5.00pm but because the house had no windows, it was already dark inside the hut. So I assisted her to get out of the house and go to the nearest banana trees where we enjoyed natural light from the moon.
I quickly rushed to the house to get some bed sheets or a blanket for her to lie on. I searched everywhere in the house without success. An Idea came that I should use mine that were still in my suitcase I carried. However, when I was still looking for the keys to open the suitcase, I heard a cry of a baby outside. Fidgeting, I run outside only to find that the poor woman had delivered a baby on the ground. I picked a panga, cut some banana leaves, and made a bed for her and the baby to lie on. It was horrible and I believe that people reading this story may think it is a fiction. From my little scientific knowledge, I started imagining what I could do next. I vaguely remembered about the placenta but I could not figure out what it was all about and how they handled it during and after pregnancy.
Well I assisted the mother to slowly walk back into the house while I carried a baby in the other hand. I got the pair of bed sheets I had carried and put them over the baby.Then, I asked her what else I could do and she said this ( in a very low and tired voice); Get me a cup of water and it is only because it is late and at night, I would send you to the next hill where you can see fire when you stand outside. There you would call me a woman called Suzan who would give me more help". I told her that I had to find my way there at all cost. But she was concerned about my safety and warned me against hyenas as you go to Suzan's. You know it is far and down there is the home of hyenas and you are too young to confront them. So just keep cool and wait until morning". She advised.
I told her to wait for some minutes and I would be back. She raised her eyes feebly and warned me; "be very careful" I went for my dark jacket and shoes and walked outside. Standing on top of an ant-hill, I contemplated about my setting of, especially having the idea of hyenas. How can I fight a hyena? Can I run faster than a hyena-No. can I beat it-No. Should I leave this mother to die? No I cannot afford to set my eyes on the dead body of this young beautiful woman. An idea came up. I had a heavy whistle that I had accidentally carried and a torch. I risked my life and went for Suzan and fortunately reached safely. She came with her herbs and stopped her breeding. But she was completely dehydrated and was almost dying.
The above story of this young man who actually instinctively saved this pregnant woman has far reaching implications. But in a nut shell, it implies that a pregnant woman in a poor remote area of Africa has one foot in a grave.
Every minute of every day, somewhere in the world a woman dies as a result of complications arising during pregnancy and child birth. This according to UNICEF.
More girls and women die from the complications of childbirth in developing countries than from any other cause, UNICEF said today, calling the figures "a shameful record of discrimination and apathy toward women and their rights."
Speaking on the eve of International Women's Day, UNICEF said that some 1400 girls and women die each day from causes related to childbirth, 99 per cent of them in developing countries.
"The same number died yesterday, and the same number will die tomorrow - most of them in silence," UNICEF Executive Director Carol Bellamy declared.
According to estimates in the Maternal Mortality Report 2000, a new global report published by
the World Health Organisation (WHO), UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) and UN Population Fund (UNFPA), a woman living in sub-Saharan Africa has a 1 in 16 chance of dying in pregnancy or childbirth. This compares with a 1 in 2 800 chance for a woman from a developed region.
For women in developing countries who do not have access to qualified medical care, childbirth is an unpredictable ordeal, which carries with it the risk of death or permanent disability.
"Africa is a latecomer to the revolution in reproductive behavior sweeping across the developing world, but is now poised for positive change with respect to childbearing patterns and sexual health," says Shanti R. Conly, PAI's director of policy analysis and co-author of the study. "Meanwhile the needs are urgent; Africa must learn from successes in and outside the region to accelerate improvements in reproductive health."
Why poor countries?
There is a need for a re-examination of 'the politics of priority': health or wealth? Many of the heated policy discussions around growth versus poverty reduction as the priority are had without reference to the scale of the crisis in maternal well being. Maternal mortality is not simply fatal but is often a cruel and harsh lived experience for Africa's women. And yet reducing maternal mortality in Africa can be done without the need for significant increases in the wealth of the continent. It is a matter of organisation and part of that organisation is the provision of transport facilities and hostel provision for those in need of, or likely to need, emergency obstetric care.
•Ironically, maternal mortality - death because of distance from health facilities - is increasing in Africa at the same point as there is a global discourse on the 'death of distance'.
•The mapping of mobility entitlements and accessibility patterns against gender, and the consequences of these patterns, is not adequate.
•Similarly the measurement of maternal mortality and women's health has been under-resourced with the consequence that current overviews are inadequate and operational and evaluative knowledge on best practice interventions is weak.
•There does seem to be a strong relationship between poor transport organization and high levels of maternal mortality.
•There is a clear ground in which improvements in information technology could help reduce rates of maternal mortality.
•A set of mobility and empowerment factors need to be considered and addressed in any campaign to reduce maternal mortality.
•The reduction of maternal mortality is a Millennium Development Goal.
•The policy discussion is short on suggestions on how to realise this goal - and the contribution that safe motherhood, transport plans could make to this reduction is under-operationalised.
Reality on the ground
Maternal mortality in Africa is a crisis and it is a crisis which is likely to worsen.
'Africa has the highest maternal mortality rate in the world. Many African countries have been hit by an exodus of medical personnel to overseas destinations in recent years.
"Only 42% of births in the African region are attended by skilled personnel," an expert at the regional conference on maternal and new-born health in Zimbabwe emphasised. Unsafe abortions are also high among adolescents, according to him. Experts, who are drawn from various international organisations, are examining the extent of the problem on the continent and will suggest ways of reducing the death rates among mothers and infants. African governments' health budgets were also identified as inadequate to deal with obstetric cases.
"The percentage of GDP (gross domestic product) devoted to health in sub-Saharan Africa remains at between one percent and 3,7% compared to the large percentage spent on arms," they conveyed. "If nothing is done to arrest the trend (of high and growing maternal and child deaths), it is estimated that there will be 2.5 million maternal deaths, 2.5 million child deaths and 49 million maternal disabilities in the region over the next 10 years", Prof. Joseph Kasonde noted. He states that more than half of the 600,000 women who die every year from pregnancy-related causes were in the African region which constitutes only 12% of the world's population and 17% of its births. Maternal mortality ratio in Africa remains the highest in the world with the average actually increasing from 870 per 100,000 live births in 1990 to 1,000 per 100,000 live births in 2001.
According to a WHO-sponsored study made available at the regional workshop on improving maternal and neonatal health in Zimbabwe, neonatal morbidity and mortality rates is currently estimated at 45 deaths per 1,000 live births and contribute about 50% of the infant mortality rate in the region. The findings of the study, presented by Dr Office Chidede, a Consultant Neonatologist at the University of Zimbabwe, also shows that stillbirths and deaths within the first seven days of life in the Region was estimated at 76 per 1,000 live births.
He also indicated that 70% of deliveries take place in the community where maternal and newborn births are usually not recorded. Eight countries were covered by the study conducted between February 2001 and August 2002. Its goal was to develop or recommend evidence-based strategic interventions and establish sustainability in the institutionalization and implementation of identified remedial measures.
The study documents some of the causes of death as provided by health providers and facility records. These include: birth asphyxia (suffocation during birth), 40%; prematurity and low birth weight, 25%; infections, 20%; congenital defects, 10%, and acute surgical conditions, 3%. Other findings relate to unavailability of basic supplies and equipment, staff shortages and low morale, bad roads, long distances between two referral points, continued use of traditional birth attendants (who are still popular and highly regarded) and preference of mothers to deliver in health facilities, although these are still largely perceived as not user-friendly.
Pregnancy in adolescence presents a unique and frightening picture," he highlighted, adding that 13% of all maternal deaths occurred in adolescents, 14 million of whom gave birth annually worldwide. Prof. Kasonde conveyed that in spite of the somber picture, two major initiatives launched in the past two decades had helped to stem the tide of maternal and child deaths in Africa. These include the Safe Motherhood Initiative launched in 1987 which drew attention to the multifaceted nature of the problem and the need to invest in five key critical areas: human rights, empowerment of women, education, socio-economic development and the improvement of health systems. The Making Pregnancy Safer Initiative, launched in 2000, focused on the health sector and its crucial role in accelerating maternal maternity reduction. The aim of the Initiative was to ensure that women and their newborns have access to the care they need through the strengthening of health systems and appropriate co mmunity-level actions."
Is there any hope?
In spite of the harsh economic environment prevailing in Africa, the application of appropriate policies by governments would lead to improvements in the outcome of pregnancies irrespective of the economic status of countries. Checking poverty anywhere, reorganizing health systems, strengthening of midwifery skills, and increasing the number of skilled birth attendants, would mean a lot. Action to place maternal and newborn health high on the agenda of governments and partners; to review policies, guidelines and programmes; to allocate and release resources and action to harness resources from communities and partners."

Original_Search_Title: Critics Say Clamour for Reforms Selfish
Headline: Kenya: Critics Say Clamour for Reforms Selfish
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Politicians have been criticised for demanding minimum reforms before next year's elections.
Critics said the clamour was self-conceited and not in the interest of the public, while those in favour said it would create a level playing ground before the 2007 elections.
The National Council of Churches of Kenya Secretary General, Rev Mutava Musyimi, said the council would object attempts by MPs to hijack the constitution-making process.
"One section cannot purport that it will resolve the contentious issues, which all Kenyans could not agree on last year," said Musyimi.
He said the Ufungamano consultative group requested that the Wako Draft be looked into again before the referendum and that both the Orange and Banana camps rebuffed their plea for an extra six months to iron out contentious issues in the Draft.
"I have not consulted my colleagues in Ufungamano, but I think the call for minimum reforms lacks in consistency with the position we made last year," said Musyimi.
"The review process should not be pegged on any event," he said.
Agitation for minimum reforms
Cyprian Nyamwamu of the National Convention Executive Council said they would mobilise the masses to press for minimum reforms.
"We are mobilising the masses to ensure that a national referendum is held by May 30 next year," he said. But the Kenya National Commission of Human Rights had a different view. Mr Maina Kiai, the chairman, said it was important that the Electoral Commission of Kenya Act be amended.
This, he said, would ensure that ECK operates without intimidation and frustration like is the case now. The reactions followed demands by Langata MP Raila Odinga and the support he received from Kabete MP Paul Muite agitating for minimum reforms before next year's election.
Six areas identified by politicians for review include the Presidency, the Judiciary, the Electoral Commission, Parliament and gender and affirmative action.
Kiai said the civil society feared that politicians could hijack the process. He said there is anxiety over ceding the process to politicians.
"We also need to repeal the Official Secrets Act and the Free Access to Information Act before the elections," said Kiai.
Many fear that the impending election may reduce the review exercise to another political contest between ODM and the Government of National Unity.

Original_Search_Title: AD Petitions IG Over Crisis
Headline: Nigeria: AD Petitions IG Over Crisis
Body_Text:
Osogbo — Alliance for Democracy (AD), Osun State, has petitioned Inspector General of Police, Mr Sunday Ehindero, over the recent political crisis in the state.
It would be recalled that supporters of the state Governor, Olagunsoye Oyinlola, and another gubernatorial aspirant, Mr Rauf Aregbesola, who is also Lagos State Commissioner of Works, clashed recently during Oroki Festival in Osogbo.
The Petition titled Re: Political Clash at 2006 Oroki Day Celebration, jointly signed by the state Secretary of the party, Barrister Adewale Afolabi, and its Chairman, Alhaji Moshood Adeoti, said the crisis was pre-meditated and a plan for extermination of Aregbesola.
The petition in its prayers, called on the Inspector General of Police to post Mr Sulaiman Fakai, Osun State Commissioner of Police out of the state, saying he can not be trusted.
It frowned at a situation where mobile police officers attached to the governor's convoy threw stones and shot at civilians, saying the action has helped to further erode the confidence of the people in the Force.
The petition read, "we request a thorough investigation of that ugly event and a compulsive, unbiased report made available to all interested parties, which should also be caused to be published in major newspapers as done in the earlier hasty report.
"A special investigation team without any link with Osogbo state government should be constituted by the Inspector General of police, to unravel the truth."
It described the arrest of Aregbesola as "an act only possible in a banana republic, as witnessed under the tyrannical military regime of Sanni Abacha, from where Oyinlola had his first taste of public life."
It urged Ehindero to urgently do something in the interest of peace in the state.
In a similar development, the Oodua Peoples Congress (OPC), has admonished political leaders in the South-west to always put Yoruba nation first in all their undertakings.
The statement, signed by the Osun State Coordinator, Chief Rafiu Akinwale, said the efforts would help to avoid Yoruba race been put into disrepute, and shun going into alliance or agreeing to terms that would jeopardise the much needed progress and unity of the South-west and Nigeria at large.

Original_Search_Title: 12-Year-Old Hawker Now HIV Positive
Headline: Nigeria: 12-Year-Old Hawker Now HIV Positive
Body_Text:
Kaduna — At nine, this girl sold bananas to feed her four siblings. In the process she fell prey to several randy men over a period of three years. Now she is HIV-positive in a poverty-stricken home.
Amina now shuttles between Jabi and Gwagwalada to collect anti-retroviral drugs for her daughter. But curiously enough, Onwumere who was also tested for HIV, tested negative.
Adama, 12, has been subjected to sexual abuse since she was nine. After the death of her father, she has assumed partial responsibility as a bread winner for her family of four siblings. She did this by hawking bananas from the little orchard left by her late father.
But in the course of hawking, she has been raped thrice by men who pretended to be buyers. Now, Adama is HIV-positive, but her latest 70-year-old bed mate tested negative.
Looking forlorn and sad, Adama told Weekly Trust "I was raped the first time by a man who dragged me into his room and locked the door. The second time I was raped together with my younger sister, Tabawa, by two youths who lured us into a room and tore off our clothes. They later threw us out, leaving us weeping."
These two horrors were probably one-off experiences for Adama. Her third rape encounter turned her into a regular for a 70-year-old over a period of about a year. This time around the rape was subtly carried out with cunning, deceit, psychological manipulations and coercion by Mr. Christopher Onwumere.
Onwumere was careful in selecting his prey and knew how to silence Adama-he made sure he paid her for every rape session.
"Every time I go to sell bananas to him he would look round when people are not watching and drag me quietly into his room. He would push me on the bed and remove my clothes and underwear. When I begin to scream he will gag me until everything is over. That is when I usually start crying and he will give me N50 and beg me not to tell anyone," Adama said.
With every such act Onwumere got away with, he grew bolder. He began to lay ambush on 12 year-old Adama in the night when she leaves home to run errands for her mother.
"He will lay ambush on me and tell me to follow him. If I show reluctance he will bring out his handset and give it to me to play with. He will use the ring tones of the phone to hold my attention, from where I will follow him to his room," Adama said.
Onwumere's days as a randy rapist got to an end when Adama was getting fed up with the sexual abuse and was beginning to question the nature of her relationship with him. One day in March this year, when she went hawking bananas and he successfully got her into his room and stripped her naked, she made sure she screamed loud enough for neighbours to hear.
A long-drawn case
When the whistle was blown on Onwumere, Amina Saleh, Adama's mother, brought in the police. Amina told Weekly Trust "I feel my daughter's life has been ruined; she has been violated and taken advantage of."
Obviously because he has been caught red-handed, Onwumere confessed to the police. Amina was now advised to have her daughter checked in the hospital for any probable ailment. She went with Adama to the Life Camp General Hospital and the Maitama District Hospital before they were at last referred to the Gwagwalada Specialist Hospital. There, they got the bombshell: Adama is HIV positive.
Amina now shuttles between Jabi and Gwagwalada to collect anti-retroviral drugs for her daughter. But curiously enough, Onwumere who was also tested for HIV, tested negative. Medical experts, however, say it is too soon to conclude about his HIV status as he could read positive on a second test since he could have contracted the virus from his rape victim.
So, who among Adama's previous rapists infected her with the dreaded virus?
Amina had a clue. "Actually, the first time she was raped, her late father and I knew about it. It happened just when he was stricken with the illnesses that will finally kill him. He said we should forget the matter as he didn't have the strength to pursue the case. Besides that, the rapist was someone very well known to the family. So, we kept the incidence behind us and left it at that," she said.
But it is possible other men, more than she or her mother had mentioned, had taken advantage of Adama's vulnerability. In two interview sessions, during which Weekly Trust had to encourage the mother and the daughter to speak, information from them only trickled. While Amina was understandably embarrassed and ashamed of what had happened to her daughter, Adama on her part was partly jittery and at other times could not recollect properly how many times or occasions she had been raped.
Cell no. 3, Jabi Divisional Police Headquarters
Christopher Onwumere has been in detention at the Jabi Divisional Police Headquarters for about five months now. When Weekly Trust asked for him at the police office, the officer on duty suddenly started smiling saying, "So, you are looking for 10-10?"
Apparently Onwumere had achieved some level of notoriety as a result of his act. '10-10' could probably be a nickname from his inmates in cell number 3.
But Onwumere is a master of deception, the girl's mother said. "Nowadays, he pretends to be a mentally ill person. In the cell where he is held, he will often be found speaking nonsense to himself and laughing into empty space," Amina said.
Weekly Trust tried to contact Onwumere's kinsmen and learnt that they had completely dissociated themselves from him. None of them had visited him at the police cell, sources said. They consider his conduct shameful and horrifying.
'The law is a cobweb'
For a poor widow like Amina, five months awaiting justice is painful and financially exhausting. She told Weekly Trust: "I have spent a lot of money since this case started and to be frank, I am tired and fed up."
Amina pays for virtually all the transportation of witnesses to the police station and even the transportation of Onwumere himself to the hospital. she described it as in conformity to the nasty system of police investigation and justice administration in the country. Because of this and Adama's ailment, the mother said she was thinking of withdrawing from the case.
"I am no longer interested in pursuing the case further. I am leaving everything to God. Whatever happens to the abuser of my daughter, I have been cheated already," she said.
What would be the implication of Amina's withdrawal from the case? Weekly Trust spoke to the investigating police officer in charge of the case who said he would not want his name in print.
"Please, see if you can beg her to change her mind," the officer said. "We are at the point of charging him to court. If she withdraws from the case the police will have no choice but to release him. It is not right that a criminal should be allowed to go unpunished. That man deserves at least fourteen years in jail. It would not be setting a good example if offenders go unpunished for their crimes. Punishing them is a way of discouraging others who are similarly engaged or had similar designs on their minds."
But Onwumere's freedom has a wider and more dangerous implication for the society at large. He is a paedophile who might search for another victim like Adama. This is in addition to the fact that he would soon be carrying the HIV virus with him. Adama told Weekly Trust that when "I refuse to allow him sleep with me he usually tells me that if I refuse there are other girls like me who will come to him and agree to sleep with him."
Bitrus Musa, a guide to Weekly Trust in Daki Biyu, shook his head sadly on learning about Onwumere's likely freedom. "I read somewhere that the law is a cobweb-too strong to catch the weak and too weak to catch the strong," he said.
A ray of light
Twelve-year-old Adama has a painful challenge ahead of her when she would gradually come to a traumatic realisation of her HIV status. During all the time Weekly Trust spoke to her, she did not show the slightest sign of awareness of carrying a deadly infection. This may be understandable; after all the girl is just 12. The sad thing is that even Amina, her mother, did not show the slightest understanding of knowing what HIV is all about. Though she said she knew that her daughter had an infection since she regularly visited Gwagwalada Specialist Hospital to get drugs for the daughter, Amina seemed ignorant of HIV and AIDS. How would mother and daughter help each other?
Sister Elizabeth Harris, a catholic nun with the Abuja Catholic Archdiocese and a front runner in HIV/AIDS counselling and assistance told Weekly Trust "Both mother and daughter should be counselled to know what they will be facing in future. They need to be educated. They need to know what the virus does to her body and how she can be helped because usually when people find they are positive it is scary and that even if they have the virus they can be helped. If they eat well and take ARV drugs they can live a healthy life."
Adama could be paying a heavy price for her ignorance about sex and what it could lead to, a reason why she had probably been silent over her abuse for some time. Harris said, "Parents are afraid to talk about sex to their children leaving them vulnerable and easy to be taken advantage of. We need to go into counselling and sensitisation so that parents will be able to help their children and ensure they are protected."
Just how prevalent is the case of HIV/AIDS among children? Harris said, "It is probably more common than we know. We cannot test children without parents giving their consent. There are probably more children with a case like that. They could probably be afraid to talk about it."
Harris has therefore offered to provide free counselling and free anti-retroviral drugs to Adama.

Original_Search_Title: Fresh War Over Review
Headline: Kenya: Fresh War Over Review
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya's political arena is again on the swing as another battlefront opens up with the new clamour for minimum reforms ahead of the General Election next year.
The combat, mimicking last November's 'Yes' and 'No' referendum war, has on the one side the joint stewardship of two traditional political rivals - Kabete MP Paul Muite and former Roads minister Raila Odinga. Raila's influential colleagues in the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM), Mr Najib Balala, Mr William Ruto and Mr Kalonzo Musyoka are also firmly on this track. The battle is taking the shape of the Orange versus Banana duel last year, with all ministers interviewed opposing the proposed minimum reforms.
But as interviews by the Saturday Standard revealed, Justice minister Martha Karua is the new face of VIP resistance to the reforms championed by Muite in his capacity as the chairman of the House committee on administration of justice and legal affairs.
Karua set the tempo for ministers when she declared those pushing for the minimum reforms won't take Kenyans anywhere, an open frontal attack on Muite and Raila. She also argued all that is required is a legal framework to conclude the constitutional reforms.
Ministers interviewed gave responses, which clearly defined the Government's position on the clamour for minimum reforms as a big 'No'. The emerging scenario on the review war also seemed to paint the picture Kenya had gone full-circle to pre-IPPG 1997 period when street protests were the order of the day. Yesterday the National Convention Executive Council (NCEC), which was instrumental in the 1997 protests, hinted they could be back on the streets. "Mass action is a human right, which can be exercised freely to ensure change," said NCEC activist, Mr Davinder Lamba. NCEC Executive Officer Cyprian Nyamwamu called a news conference in Nairobi to say the clamour for minimum reforms was "absurd".
He added: "This absurd clamour for minimum reforms is aimed at demobilising the national momentum for the long desired comprehensive reforms."
The battle also began taking the shape of the pre- Inter-party Parliamentary Group (IPPG) period as the civil societies went on the "No' side.
But the high-stakes game appeared to be tilting towards the independence of the Electoral Commission of Kenya. ODM leaders Raila, Kalonzo, Ruto, Mutula Kilonzo, Joseph Kamotho and Otieno Kajwang' said the law must be amended to give autonomy to ECK and Parliament.
Muite maintained his committee would soldier on despite the opposition by ministers. "This is not a matter that should be handled with exchanges in the media as it affects the stability of the country."
"And anybody with such views should give them to that sub-committee. It is in public interest that chest-thumping be avoided and sincere and honest views be deliberated at the forum," Muite added.
On Wednesday Muite's committee formed a sub- committee chaired by Emuhaya MP Kenneth Marende to organise for a consultative forum of all parliamentary political parties with a view to coming up with the minimum reforms. It is fashioned along the lines of the 1997's IPPG.
Raila said President Kibaki had gone against the IPPG deal that required consultation with other parties before appointing ECK commissioners. The President, he added, quietly replaced five commissioners without consultations with other parties.
"He wants to create an ECK made up of his loyalists. We will not allow it to happen. The law must be amended to protect the autonomy of the ECK," Raila vowed. He added that President Kibaki was a player in the political field and should not assume the role of the referee by appointing commissioners.
The Langata MP said the Opposition would also push for the reintroduction of the Keter Bill, that sought to make Parliament autonomous, as part of the minimum reform package. He declared that if no reforms would take place, there would be no elections.
Kalonzo insisted the minimum reforms were mandatory. He argued the reforms were necessary to ensure that Kenyans got the President of their choice. Ruto, on the other hand, called for the independence of ECK. "Autonomy of the ECK is the crux of the matter and the least we will accept," he declared.
"It is clear that the Government is hell-bent on using ECK to rig elections next year. We insist that either the law be amended to give Parliament powers to vet those being appointed to the ECK or the tenure of the ECK officials as currently constituted be extended," he added.
Yesterday, six cabinet ministers - Njeru Ndwiga, Prof Kivutha Kibwana, Mutua Katuku, Morris Dzoro and Suleiman Shakombo - rejected calls for minimum reforms.
Ndwiga termed as "a mere ploy" plans by Muite and Raila's team. Kanu nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo said his side would push for the law governing ECK to be included in the Constitution to protect its autonomy.
"We specifically want to amend the Constitution to deal with the appointment of ECK officials," Kilonzo said.
Currently, the law says the ECK shall comprise not more than 21 members appointed by the President.
According to Kilonzo, ODM and the other parties want an amendment to make it mandatory for the President to appoint ECK commissioners, with the approval of the House.
Housing minister Soita Shitanda said amendments should target areas that would not cause acrimony as the referendum did. He suggested that the House narrows down to ECK's autonomy. "If there was time, we would have overhauled the whole document but after what happened last year, it is not possible and so the document, as majority would agree, can only be looked at comprehensively after the next General Election," said Shitanda.
"The issue of the Constitution cannot to be left to Parliament. It must include everybody. That is the only way forward," said Ndwiga.
The Cooperatives minister said if it would not be possible to amend the whole document before the general elections due next year, then the task should be left for the Tenth Parliament.
The Minister termed as "hypocrites and busy bodies" Raila and Muite. Water minister Mutua Katuku said the calls for minimum reforms were part of a ploy by Raila to bulldoze a 50 per cent rule. "Let them tell us what is new in the 20 per cent of the rejected constitution they now want to amend," said Katuku. He also rooted for comprehensive reforms.
Dzoro said Raila and his group were crusading for amendments that would "suit their wishes and not the interests of the people of Kenya."
"If these people would have agreed to endorse the draft Constitution last year, we would not be discussing what they are now talking about. There is nothing new from what we were telling them," said Dzoro.
But Balala said minimal changes were vital because they were issues touching on governance and the executive. "The 20 per cent minimal amendments in question touch on very important issues that cannot be overlooked. They are the contentious issues," said Balala.
He insisted that minimal reforms were inevitable if a level-playing ground was to be achieved in the elections next year.
Kibwana - who as opposition activist led Kenyans in protest marches in demand for constitutional reforms in 1997- opposed calls for minimum reforms.
He claimed those pushing for minimal reforms were only interested in power. "In November, we were all fighting for a complete new constitutional dispensation and I wonder why some of us are exhibiting a change of heart," he said.
Kibwana argued the remaining 16 months before the elections were sufficient to give Kenyans a new Constitution if all the interested parties could agree on the contentious issues that led to the rejection of the Wako Draft.
"There is adequate time to sort out the 20 per cent contentious clauses identified in the draft so long as politicians and other stakeholders can agree on the issues", said Kibwana.
The Central Organisation of Trade Unions (Cotu) joined calls for an overhaul of the Constitution as opposed to minimal reforms.

Original_Search_Title: Rwanda in Image Boost At Swiss Fair
Headline: Rwanda in Image Boost At Swiss Fair
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwanda's image was recently boosted during this year's Swiss Africa Forum (SAF) Fair held in the Swiss capital Berne, on August 5-6.
According to a release from the Rwanda Embassy and Permanent Mission in Geneva, Switzerland, the Rwanda stand was the best in decoration and presentation, which made it receive many visitors. The event is used to showcase the potential of Africa.
'The annual event is both an African cultural festival and a trade fair and exhibition, organized with the aim of bringing the African and Swiss peoples together. The event is also an occasion for the African countries to showcase and promote their products and services and for cultural exchange,' the release dated August 7, indicates.
The release further states that Rwanda exhibited promotional materials showcasing the good political and macroeconomic situation in Rwanda, and the tourism potential of the country. Also displayed were handicrafts, maracuja juice, banana beer (urwagwa), coffee and tea, all of which were obtained courtesy of a Rwandan national who operates a business dealing in Rwandan products in Geneva.
'The embassy organized free testing sessions for participants to taste some Rwandan products, mostly coffee and Maracuja juice. Rwanda coffee was very much appreciated, and its strong and good aroma attracted even by-passers, who confirmed that the taste and aroma were unique' the release indicates.
It adds: 'The Trade Fair was also a good opportunity to distribute pamphlets on Rwanda, displaying tourism and investment opportunities in the country'.
Among the African countries represented at the Fair by their Embassies in Switzerland and/or the private sectors were South Africa, Ghana, Ethiopia, Côte d'Ivoire, Kenya, Uganda, Senegal and Rwanda .
Besides African participants, the fair was visited by Swiss people from Bern and the neighbouring towns and from French, Italian and Germany towns sharing borders with Switzerland.

Original_Search_Title: Behold, the Season of Anomy is Here Again
Headline: Kenya: Behold, the Season of Anomy is Here Again
Body_Text:
Nairobi — We are in that dull political season. There is a lull before the election storm. Our year of national elections, 2007, is on the horizon. Until a few months ago, the election year sounded light years away and so we kept saying " in 2007." Now it is just over three moons away.
Hearts are surging, but the political captains are in the changing rooms with their technical teams and the players. In the sweaty rooms are the mid-fielders, the wingers, the goalkeepers, the defenders and the strikers.
Don't mind that some of the players have not decided on the uniforms they will don, the teams that they will play for and their positions. The referee, Mr Samuel Kivuitu, is dusting his registers and shouting himself hoarse that time is running out and there is still too much work to do. So fast is the time running that it may now be a pipedream to expect the 40-plus new constituencies because Parliament, which was to do the work, is on recess.
But wait until the House resumes in two months. Our MPs will give the green light to the creation of new constituencies. So many of them want to curve away the clusters that do not traditionally support them and remain with the so-called "political safe havens". For the captains of the politicking industry, it is time to fight for more constituencies in their strongholds.
A wag told me 10 new constituencies in Central are 10 new seats for Narc-Kenya; 10 new parliamentary electoral areas in Nyanza are 10 new LDP/ODM seats! This reflection of how skewed ethnicity has made our politics will delineate the line the debate will follow when the House resumes. It will also set the level of the political temperature in the country.
But who cares? The politics of coalition building is more urgent. That is why the Liberal Democratic Party mouthpiece, J J Kamotho, is saying his party will form the next Government "on its own or with like-minded partners". I am tempted to add the Kenyan rib-cracker phrase, "But Do I Say", after his bila wasi wasi declaration.
Because it is serious business, the war councils are at work. And because Kenya is not the only trove for strategy and campaign funds, the captains often have to fly out to "campaign" abroad.
I am talking about those who have once woken up in pitch dark, startled to find that it was but a sweet dream that they were rocking on a chair in the green lawns of State House, cracking spare ribs and sipping orange (sorry banana) juice.
All around were friends pushing this or that name into the plain new Cabinet list on the table gleaming under the 2008 January hot sun. Presidential hopeful William Ruto is on a meet-the-people tour in the US where Mr Raila Odinga and Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, who will fight hammers and tongs for LDP stewardship in September, have been. The global meet-the-people tours have also taken the two to Germany. Then Agwambo flew to Australia before the results of the Nakuru Town, Marsabit and Moyale fiasco were announced.
One of his ardent followers later assured me the trip was so important as one of the items on the agenda was the acquisition of a chopper as part of his 2007 war-chest. I am not sure he was pulling my leg.
The other team in the 2007 league, Narc aka Narc-Kenya is busy growing rose flowers. So many bunches will be needed in 2007. And they are not as cheap as the bananas or oranges sold outside Wakulima Market.
The party could be sending the message that its premium has risen. Unless, of course, someone with the nostalgia of the days of Ford comes up with Narc-Kenya Asili, Narc-Kenya Kamili or even Narc-Kenya People.
So as the storm gathers, all eyes and ears remain riveted on State House. The question on the lips is: When will President Kibaki walk onto a lighted hall to face the PPS crew, and their cameras ready, to record the birth of yet another form of Government of National Unity?
Buoyed by the latest opinion polls, the unease in the house of ODM, his success in balkanising Kanu and Ford-People by sucking some sections of it into Government, and the rising perception that it may after all be too early to write him off, President Kibaki could actually restructure his Cabinet and declare "Five More Years" the slogan for Narc-Kenya.
Some of the team captains are not worth this space, so we will read about them elsewhere. Still others are mere disciples, never the saviours. They are good so long as they are followers, not leaders, even if on temporary hire.
So as battle 2007 takes shape, the promise of the battle of the titans draws closer. With each dawn the game totters towards fever pitch. And when the day comes, Kenya may never be the same again. Many others may in time get the courage to jump into the ring.
Because the next season is brutish and bloody, I will tell you the story of one of the most scary fishes in the seas - piranhas. I see in their thirst for blood and flesh, and their ferocity, the face of the politicians who will tell us about justice and democracy and still hire stone-throwers, jaw-dislocaters and skull-breakers to protect them in the campaign rallies.
They smell blood and if you dip your leg with a small wound into the Mississippi, in three seconds it will be some pieces of dangling bones. Those who have studied them wrote: "They are often called the cannibal fish. They sink their sharp teeth into their victim's flesh and devour it as fast as they can. Within minutes, their victim is reduced to nothing but bones."
Dear Kenyans, the season of the piranhas is coming, exercise caution or you will end up a bag of bones.
The writer is The Standard Managing Editor, Weekend Editions

Original_Search_Title: Bong Citizens Launch Scholarship Fund Drive
Headline: Liberia: Bong Citizens Launch Scholarship Fund Drive
Body_Text:
Gbarnga — Bong County Superintendent Ranney Banana Jackson has urged fellow kinsmen to support the educational needs of the county.
Superintendent Jackson said, it was time that Bong citizens begin to utilize their God- given talents to develop the county and help their less fortunate kinsmen.
He made the statement recently the formal launch of a Scholarship Fund Drive to assist needy youths in the county.
Superintendent Jackson also called on Bong citizens to join hands in rebuilding the devastated county, saying, "if we are not together the development of our county is far from reaching".
Buttressing President Sirleaf's call for national reconciliation, Superintendent Jackson reminded his kinsmen to support the Liberian leader in achieving total reconciliation by putting aside their tribal and political differences to unite as one people.
Performing the official launch of the scholarship, the President of sthe Cuttington University College Dr. Henrique Tokpa appealed to sons and daughters of the county who are fortunate to share their blessings with their less-fortunate brothers and sisters by supporting the fund drive.
Mr. Tokpa then offered two scholarships to the first and second Valedictorians of every high school in the county to enroll at Cuttington with full benefits including tuition and dormitory fees for the first dux while fifty percent (50%) tuition and dormitory fees to the second dux excluding cafeteria and breakage fees and other requirements.
The program was attended by chiefs, elders, and youths as well as several eminent citizens including former Bong County Senator Francis Y.S. Garlawulo, Deputy Health and Social Welfare Minister S. Tornorla Varplah and Deputy Post and Telecommunications Minister Estelle Liberty, among others.
The launch generated US$1,027.00 and L$9,540.00 in both cash and pledges.

Original_Search_Title: NaCSA Projects Crumble At Mama Beach
Headline: Sierra Leone: NaCSA Projects Crumble At Mama Beach
Body_Text:
Residents of both Banana Island and Mama Beach along the Peninsular have accused the Comfort Best Construction Company of 1 Hannah Benka Coker Street, Freetown of wrecking two NaCSA projects.
The projects, according to sources, involve the sum of Le 425,259,822 for the construction of a health and community centre for the two communities.
One of the residents of Banana Island, Moses Taylor disclosed that the company, which is managed by a certain Fatmata Bert Cole, won the contract to construct the health centre on the island in May 2005 to be completed in December 2005, costing Le218m.
He lamented however that despite the collection of a major part of the funds for the construction, the company has abandoned the project unfinished.
Another resident, Ellis Wray disclosed that workers have abandoned the site over four months and the contractor has also refused to brief the community about the status of the work.
Mr. Ellis Wray said Banana Island is the only place that has failed to benefit from NaCSA health support programmes, adding that the contractor has deprived them of health centre.
When the NaCSA project manager Information Education and Communication officer, Mr. Unisa Sesay was contacted, he disclosed that, both contracts were indeed awarded to the contractor to be completed in a six-month period, adding that both Islamic Development Bank (IDB) and African Development Bank (ADB) funded the Public Works Programmes (PWP) and community development projects (CDP), that supported the projects.
Speaking on the allegation of wrecking the projects, Mr. Sesay opined, "sometimes a delay is coursed by either the contractors, NaCSA or the community itself." With regard to the displeasure of residents of Mama Beach over the delay in constructing the health centre by the same company costing over Le206 million, Mr. Sesay confirmed the company got the contract in June 2005 to be completed in December 2005 but has failed to measure up and has abandoned the site.
When the Manager of Comfort Best Construction Company, Mrs. Fatmata Bert-Cole was contacted, she said, "I have no business with a journalist but the people of Banana Island community," adding, "I stopped the work because of the rains and have asked the village heads to give me more time."

Original_Search_Title: Seeing is Believing
Headline: Cameroon: Seeing is Believing
Body_Text:
Frankfurt — President Paul Biya, Chairman of the ruling CPDM party, recently told the party's extraordinary congress that he will intensify his drive against fraudulent conversion and corruption, and to hand out the dividends of democracy to the commons.
Many militants are still enthusing over the president's tack shifting. So far, so good. However, "seeing is believing", as the saying goes.Good intentions are useless unless they are swiftly implemented.
The truth is that someone putting on the thinking cap and using his or her mother and native wits at this juncture will take the laudable new orientation of the head of state with a pinch of salt. Remember, "Once bitten, twice shy", as the popular wisdom puts it.
The President is apparently focusing only on fight against peculation - read: embezzlement of public funds - and graft. The third scourge that has always bilked Cameroon's take-off is that of mismanagement. It is mind-boggling and appalling to realise that folks without social skills and emotional intelligence have mismanaged many parastatals, statutory companies and corporations.
These sophomoric felons have failed to put to use the basic economic or rational principle that states that, the duty of a manager or executive is to achieve a sizeable return out of a modicum of resources and to yield a maximum return on the basis of sizeable resources. This is one of the first principles a fresher in business studies or administration learns.
The next point is that "emotional intelligence" and "social skills" are a must today in managing human resources in organisations. Emotional intelligence refers to knowing and factoring in the idiosyncrasies of your co-workers and colleagues, while "social skills" are good manners. "Emotional intelligence" and "social skills" are part and parcel of a strong moral sinew.
Unfortunately, many decision-makers, executives, civil servants and high-ranking officials lack these skills, despite the fact that they are indispensable in this new millennium. The rational principle, "emotional intelligence" and social skills are the mainstays of successful latter-day management and administration.
If a chief executive officer does not routinely practise these pillars, then he will ruin the organisation.President Biya, announced his party would change into higher gear. Only time, the days, the weeks and the months ahead will really tell the punters the rest of the story.
If he means business, if he puts his acts and deeds where his statements are, then one may safely say that a new spirit of the times will grip Cameroon. Otherwise, his recent keynote speech would have been a glib talk. So, only deeds will speak for themselves.
The keynote speech of the Head of State would be rated as groundbreaking if the national and international business community is induced to create jobs, values and wealth by a crystal-clear economic policy drawn up by our government.
If the Cameroonian Civil Service does away with processing files at a snail's pace, if some civil servants forbear from being on the pad, if some individuals leave off battening on the public purse or lining their own pockets, if politicos at long last start doing politics and shun politicking, these will pave the way for the country's economic resurgence.
By the way, there is a wrong perception of politics in some quarters in Cameroon. This is due in part to trickery and villainy peddled by some - not all - of our French colonial administrators who used to dissuade our ancestors from going into politics.
They used to tell our freedom fighters that politics was a dirty, dangerous and objectionable business; that is politicking. So, some politicos in Cameroon are still ready to whip out that dodge in order to hog for them the whole political space and to reap as cruddy time-servers the fruits of democracy. Politics is good but politicking ruins the country.
The besetting shortcoming of many politicos in Cameroon is that they never queried the politicking practised by many colonial masters, instead they co-opted it and have applied it ever since on their compatriots. An adept in politicking is a bestial human because he or she is grasping, selfish and serve himself or herself.
Politics is about tending to the country's and the inhabitants grievances, hardships and anxieties. It is about serving the public, offering solutions to the pressing plight of Cameroonians. It is not about pulling them through the hoop or fobbing them off with hot air. People of value know what it means to serve and to identify one's interest in the midst of the public interest.
Also, politics is about good management and administration of scarce resources. What is scandalous is that the Cameroonian state has got various sources of revenue. Cameroon is endowed with natural and mineral resources: think of the forest, cocoa, tea, banana; oil, diamond, bauxite, among others. Besides, the state derives a part of its revenue from taxes.
If the powers that be appoint executives, officials and civil servants who set great store by the rational principle, social skills and emotional intelligence, then the country will take off in the short run.

Original_Search_Title: Cabinet Reshuffle Looms
Headline: Kenya: Cabinet Reshuffle Looms
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Mwai Kibaki is set to announce a new Cabinet that will determine the next shape of national politics, The Standard can report.
Buoyed by opinion polls and with hardly 16 months left to the next General Election, Kibaki is expected to announce his new Cabinet - in essence his tag team for 2007 - any time soon.
This is the first time Kibaki will craft new positions without a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) around his neck and without the ghost of blackmail as happened after the referendum last year.
But so tricky is the issue of the next Cabinet that Kibaki's inner circle is said to have been strategising on how to coalesce friends and scatter the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) and Kanu.
Plotting and planning
While keeping an eye on regional balancing, Kibaki is expected to take advantage of the collapse of Narc - after most of its members scattered their loyalties to other parties - by expanding the Government of National Unity (GNU).
By last night the plotting and planning among Kibaki's strategists is said to have reached a crescendo.
Waiting in the wings for a windfall are the so-called Young Turks, who managed to catapult the dwindling fortunes of President Kibaki by launching a new party, Narc-Kenya, which has gained the tacit support of the Government.
A source told The Standard that the Young Turks are not clamouring for posts in the short-run, choosing to sacrifice such posts as bargaining chips to swell the Government's ranks in Parliament.
Opposition zones
Insiders say that with 16 positions still vacant, Kibaki will finally reach out to areas hitherto considered as opposition zones - and those that identify with ODM - by dangling the Cabinet position carrots.
When Kibaki agreed to host a delegation last week from Pokot and Baringo districts pundits were quick to point out that Kibaki had began to dangle the carrots and lay strategies - for yet another term.
It will be recalled that before the plebiscite last November, Kibaki received such delegations at State House during which his then Banana agenda was reinforced.
On July 28 at State House, Nairobi, and in a move calculated to reach out to north-western Kenya, Kibaki had an audience with a strong Pokot delegation led by assistant ministers Samuel Chumei Moroto and Asman Abongotum Kamana who had gone to lobby for more positions for the community.
But while the low-key ceremony did not attract much attention or scrutiny, it is worth noting that State House invited media houses to cover the event rather than rely on the Presidential Press Service.
Church delegations
And there were promises too. That Kibaki promised to look at the issue of positions means that the forthcoming reshuffle will have all the ingredients that can swing the area to vote for him.
Interestingly, both Moroto and Kamana are not members of Narc, having been elected to Parliament on Kanu and Ford-People tickets respectively.
In the last month, Kibaki also appeared to be reaching out to churches- hosting two delegations at State House Nairobi and Nakuru in a bid to coalesce groups, which during the referendum, urged their members to vote against the proposed constitution.
He had also attended functions hosted by the Methodist church, which has strong roots in the greater Meru districts.
At State House two of his critics during the referendum, Bishop Mark Kariuki of Deliverance Church and Bishop Silas Yego of African Inland Church had a meeting with Kibaki in the company of American bishop Eddie Long.
While the church delegations may have had nothing to do with politics, the presence of Internal Security minister John Njoroge Michuki during both functions indicates that it was more than a social encounter and could be part of the 2007 reaching out.
Embarrassing outcome
The referendum aftermath is now considered "history" at State House, and hardly bothers Kibaki. His handlers and confidants are also no longer musing over the embarrassing outcome, which forced Kibaki to go low and accommodate demands by Musikari Kombo and Charity Ngilu.
The two had refused to take Cabinet positions until their demands were met. To Kibaki's hilarity, the recent five by-elections eclipsed Kombo and Ngilu from national politics.
But while the Ngilu-Kombo factor is no longer considered strong, Kibaki's supporters know it is politically fundamental to keep Kombo and Ngilu by the President's side.
Two months ago Kibaki hosted a delegation from the greater Meru district. The delegates pointed their desire to get back Cabinet positions they lost when David Mwiraria resigned as finance minister over the Anglo Leasing scandal and Kiraitu Murungi stepped aside over the same.
While the two have disappeared from the limelight, Kiraitu emerged last week as he declared that he has launched a Kibaki re-election campaign in Meru on a Narc-K ticket. That was interesting since it emerged when Kibaki is about to make some changes within the Cabinet.
Corruption allegations
Apart from Nyeri, Meru was the bedrock of Kibaki's support and the new-look Cabinet will have to appease the community - although there is no pointer that they are about to bolt elsewhere.
It is openly said that Mwiraria, a long time ally of President Kibaki, may return to the Cabinet, although not to the Treasury.
On Sunday, Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga said the President had embarked on "cleaning those of his friends tainted by corruption allegations" before reappointing them to Cabinet.
In the last few months, the parties that warred in public and privately around the Presidency have been thrown into a spin by the rise of Narc-K.
While both old and the young politicians have joined the new party, the public face of the party is first-timer in parliament Danson Mungatana.
But behind the scenes are powerful ministers Njenga Karume, Michuki and a coterie of ex-DP financiers who would hardly show their face.
Last Friday, former Narc activist, Mary Wambui - now Narc-K activist - also threw in her lot with the new party indicating which side of the bread is buttered.
Expectations are high
Anyhow, Mungatana is set to be rewarded in the new Cabinet for his role in the new party, especially considering that Ali Chirau Mwakwere is yet to galvanise Kibaki support at the Coast or tame the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) wave.
Interestingly, it is openly said Kibaki might reach out to Joe Khamisi, the renegade LDP vice chairman who has launched his own Kadu-Asili party, prompting LDP to institute disciplinary action against him.
Since the recent two victories in the arid north, expectations are high that Kibaki will use this opportunity to reach out to the pastoralist regions.
With Simeon Nyachae recently hinting he may leave politics, the future of Ford-People - which has its support among the Abagusii - will be in doubt. Therefore, it will be interesting to watch how Kibaki courts the Kisii votes without Nyachae's presence. Nyachae is said to be one of Kibaki's new key confidantes.
With the once powerful Murungaru-Kiraitu axis out of the way, Kibaki might eventually come up with a Cabinet that does not portray the intrigues witnessed when he came to office in 2002.
A key factor to consider in the forthcoming reshuffle is how Kibaki has reshaped his inner circle - with most of his allies electing to stay silent on the issue.
Core members of Kitchen Cabinet
While Michuki has become a constant face in State House - some say because of his internal security portfolio - it is now difficult to know how much he can influence politics inside the Kibaki Presidency.
Still, in drawing his new Cabinet, Kibaki will rely on core members of his Kitchen Cabinet - Prof Nick Gatheru Wanjohi, Michael Waweru, Eddy Njoroge, Solicitor General Wanjuki Muchemi and Stanley Karuthai Murage, who is "officially" the President's adviser.
In Central Rift, there is word that Kibaki may court politicians allied to former Cabinet minister Nicholas Biwott. That the name of Tinderet MP, Henry Kosgey, is constantly mentioned is not in doubt. But with many seats to dangle, Kibaki is not short of supply.
The cleansing of George Saitoti will now mean that he will become once again a principal player in Maasailand politics.
Last Saturday, Kibaki was in Maasailand to create a new district despite opposition. During the function, Kibaki was pressured to reappoint Saitoti into Cabinet.
It will also be interesting to see whether Kibaki will retain Murage as his adviser and what other changes he will make in the Administration.

Original_Search_Title: Kombo - I Have Never Shifted Ground
Headline: Kenya: Kombo - I Have Never Shifted Ground
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With leading political players wooing or shunning it - depending on the political mood of the moment - Ford Kenya has emerged as the political bride of the Ninth Parliament. Sunday Nation's OSCAR OBONYO interviewed the chairman, Mr Musikari Kombo, on his party's political standing and destiny. Below are excerpts.
The ruling coalition came out empty-handed in the recent by-election. Isn't that odd?
