The performance of Narc was not particularly odd. The result was a reflection of what has happened over the years. With most member parties having left the coalition, only a smaller membership, Ford-K and National Party of Kenya, participated in the elections as Narc.
What lessons have you learnt?
I do not feel betrayed by anybody as this is the practical reality of transition politics. Much as we came together as Narc in 2002, true alliances are yet to emerge. I can only interpret the President's decision (to back a Narc Kenya candidate for Nakuru Town seat) as part of the dynamics and positioning within multi-party politics.
And what New Kanu's win in your Butere stronghold?
When we won a civic seat in Wycliffe Osundwa's (LDP-allied MP) Mumias constituency in the same (Butere-Mumias) district, the media failed to make mention of it. Why is this an issue?
Since 1992 scenario Ford-K had at least an MP in each of the country's eight provinces. The story is a bit different today, isn't it?
That's true. Again, this is as a result of the challenges and dynamics of multi-party politics. After the 1992 polls, most youthful and vibrant politicians including Raila (Odinga), Paul Muite, Kiraitu (Murungi), simply abandoned the party and receded to tribal cocoons and parties, for political survival. This notwithstanding, Ford-K remains the sole surviving and strong multiparty era political party of the early 1990s. Where, for instance, are Ford-Asili, NDP, DP and others?
But the party has succeeded in holding on to the Luhyia community?
Ford-K never set out to be a Luhyia party, only that it has a strong political base in Western Province. However, unity of the Luhyia community is elusive because we are a target of all manner of hate and divisive campaigns. Because of our huge population and the fact that Western Province always provides the swing vote, there is a tendency of being targeted by political leaders who have already pooled their communities. I think, the Luhyias must realise that there is strength in unity.
Has Ford-K been reduced to a Bukusu party?
This is not true. We are a national party, with a secretary general from Turkana, treasurer from Nyanza and my deputy from Coast Province. The astonishing fact, though, is that our people sit back and watch as non-Luhyias label us a Bukusu party.
Why does Ford-K hang on to its posts in government though it has quit the coalition?
What we have today is a Government of National Unity which has delivered in a number of sectors, including education, health and the economy. Ford-K is happy to have provided stability in Government to enable it deliver accordingly. One reason, for instance, why I opted to champion the Banana campaign during the referendum is because I felt there was a danger of isolating one major tribe. A tiger in a cage can be very hostile, and so for the sake of a peaceful Kenya, Ford-K decided to go the way it did.
There have been claims that you are not a principled politician..
I have never shifted ground. I have all along remained steadfast and articulated a consistent position of the party, which is fostering peace and fighting graft. I am not the wavering type as I served (the late Masinde) Muliro, (the late) Jaramogi Oginga Odinga and (the late) Michael Wamalwa. I remain faithful to my cause.
Have you been holding night meetings with Mr Kalonzo Musyoka?
I haven't. I believe in structured talks. I have not met Mr Musyoka to strategise on anything although we have, of course, been rubbing shoulders in the corridors of Parliament.
Do you believe in coalitions? And which way will you go in 2007?
I believe in coalitions. However, our immediate mandate is to build a stronger Ford-K after which political alliances will follow naturally.

Original_Search_Title: Ruto Calls for Kanu, LDP Merger
Headline: Kenya: Ruto Calls for Kanu, LDP Merger
Body_Text:
Nairobi — KANU Secretary General William Ruto yesterday proposed that Kanu and Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) should merge and face Narc-Kenya in 2007 General Election to remove President Kibaki from power.
Ruto said political parties that were under the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) during the last referendum should re-group and come up with a single political unit to face Narc-K.
He said Narc-K was formed on the basis of the principles of the Banana campaigns in last year's constitution referendum and " I believe they uphold the same".
" We (ODM) must come out and form a single unit similar to Narc-K and uphold the same principles we campaigned for during the referendum," Ruto added.
Speaking from the US on phone, Ruto said the advent of Narc-K was a positive move that should be emulated by political parties within ODM for the 2007 battle.
"We in ODM stood for better reforms to fight corruption, set up a new Judicial system, introduce checks and balances in the executive through power sharing and handle land issues," he added.
He said those are the principles that the parties affiliated to ODM should come up with and form a single unit to battle it out with Narc-K.
"If we go this way, we are definitely going to beat President Kibaki and his Narc-K team in the 2007 General Election," added Ruto.
He said through the single unit formed by parties which supported ODM, an ideology which was developed during the referendum would be adopted to win the General Elections.
"This is the time, we can reduce all these monkey business of many political parties without an impact," added Ruto.
He said that the political parties on the ODM side should them engage President Kibaki's side in constructive politics and " Let us see who will win".
He added: " I challenge all those who were on the side of ODM to forget about political parties based on individuals and come together to force the changes we campaigned for at the referendum."
Ruto's new move is likely to be resisted by Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta, who has always vowed that the party would go it alone during the 2007 General Election.
Uhuru has already dismissed ODM as an amorphous group whose sale by-date expired after the defeat of the proposed constitution during the referendum.
Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) leader Raila Odinga supports the union of the political parties through ODM arrangements to remove the current Government from power.
Narc-K leaders, led by acting chairman Cabinet Minister Raphael Tuju, have challenged LDP and Kanu to move a motion in Parliament for snap election.
Tuju and Assistant minister Danson Mungatana said that LDP and Kanu have no " spine" to shake Narc-K.

Original_Search_Title: Ida Christin Ssali, a Passion for Children
Headline: Uganda: Ida Christin Ssali, a Passion for Children
Body_Text:
Kampala — A deep passion for children is the first thing that one notices when Ida Christin Ssali walks onto the compound of Nsambya Police Barracks Nursery and Primary School.
Smiling, she greets the nursery children who run up to her by lifting them up and holding them close to her chest. Those that kneel down to greet her, she will carry. At 78, she is still interested in seeing that the school is well run and that children get a good education, a major reason why she founded the nursery school in 1967.
After crosschecking with some long-serving teachers with whom she has shared the history of the school, Ida revealed that this was the first kindergarten in Kampala.
"I started it in a big hall, which now serves as the recreational hall/canteen for the barracks. We began with only eight children in 'tomato class' now known as baby class.
"To me the term 'baby class' carries little meaning. Why would a parent take a baby to school? Babies should stay home. I came up with names like pineapple and orange for top, banana and cucumber for middle and tomato for the first group, baby class. Because in nursery school you have very young people who are very new/fresh in the field of education, names of fruit symbolising freshness conveyed a better message."
Margaret Nakigudde, who has been the school's bursar for 16 years, agrees. "Getting the school to take on baby, middle and top class was not easy. It took the combined effort of parents and teachers to get Ida to accept naming different levels as baby, middle and top classes," she says.
Ssali began with one teacher helper whose job was to see to it that the children didn't fight and remained focused. Children have a very short attention span so to ensure that they learn or that if they fell asleep there was someone to carry them away, there was need for a helper.
After a couple of months, Florence Awino joined as a second teacher and so did more children. Since I was a teacher as well as headmistress, in charge of inspecting the nursery-teaching plan for each week, I would set off on foot from my home in Nakawa Housing Estate very early in the morning so as to be at school by 7:00am before the children arrived. Although I used to walk all the way from Nakawa, I was always on time," Ssali recalls.
Children came from as far as Kyambogo, Bukasa, Port Bell, Ntinda, Luzira, Kabojja, Mukono and many other places.
"By that time the Inspector General of Police, Erinayo Oryema, had heard a lot about the good the school was doing for the community. He gave the school the land it occupies today since the hall had become too small," she reveals.
Now that they had the land, it was time to start buidling and this meant raising money. A building fund of six cents (6cts) per term was introduced and led to the building of the first two classrooms.
The fees enabled the school to feed the children well and pay the teachers too.
"Apart from looking after and teaching the children, they were also to ensure that the classrooms were properly cleaned and the walls scrubbed every week. As the headmistress, I received a salary of 30 cents per month," Ssali says.
As enrolment increased so did the need for space. A uniport became the headteacher's office and a store for scholastic materials. This had its own challenges.
Ssali recalls that "some days, the tinroof would get so cold and other days hot, forcing me to use the verandah of the first two classrooms as an office. I left the verandah in 1989 when two new classrooms were built after the parents, pleased with the school's performance, requested that a primary section be added."
A room that was previously used to store the children's food containers was converted into an office. Furnished with a table and three chairs, the room still serves as an office for both the nursery and primary sections. Additional blocks for Primary Three to Seven were also built.
"On days when my health permits, I come to school to see how everything is being managed and check on the teaching plan for the nursery section.
"The sight of children playing, the sound of their voices repeating after the teacher in unison brings back all sorts of memories.
"And when the old pupils of the school meet me and say hullo, it gives me great honour to know that I touched their lives at some point and they remember me for that," this lover of children and education says.
Moses Sserunkuma Kibuuka, the current headteacher, says Ssali is a person who loves children.
"As she walks around the school she makes it a point not to walk past a child without saying a kind words to them. The children call her grandma and to the teachers, she is regarded in high esteem," remarked Kibuuka.

Original_Search_Title: Women Acquire Modern Business Skills
Headline: Rwanda: Women Acquire Modern Business Skills
Body_Text:
Kigali — Ten women from Kimironko Sector recently underwent a one-week training at ETO Centre in Kicukiro District, in which they acquired various business skills and tips on poverty reduction strategies.
According to the Executive Secretary for Kimironko Sector, Robert Habimana, the workshop aimed at training trainers who will impart the skills to their colleagues who sell foodstuffs in Remera and Kimironko.
"We did this because we wanted to equip women with up-to-date business knowledge and the government policy on socio-economic development. This will reduce the number of hawkers on the street," Habimana said.
He added that the sector will initiate women into the different economic activities in order to provide them with employment opportunities as a way of reducing poverty within the community.
"The Sector is going to come up with a health project which will integrate many women. They will clean around Kimironko. Many women are to benefit from the funds raised during the one-week seminar, and we hope this will stop many from doing business on the streets," he added.
Marceline Musengimana, one the trainees vowed never to sell tomatoes on Kigali's streets, saying the seminar equipped her with various economic alternatives for income generation.
"I will never go back to roadside to sell potatoes and bananas. I am going to put up a small boutique and I believe it will help me to generate income," Musengimana said.
The seminar that is to benefit hundreds of needy women from 35 Sectors was organised by Kigali City Council (MVK) in an effort to train women in business practices and encourage them to form economic associations.
According to reports from MVK, Frw7 million was raised by good Samaritans during the women's seminar, and the money is to be channeled to the 35 sectors in order to help associations in the fight poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Currency of Style Powers Fashion Week
Headline: South Africa: Currency of Style Powers Fashion Week
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — WITH her razor-sharp red bob, Lucilla Booyzen resembles Anna Wintour, the editor-in-chief of American Vogue. The similarity doesn't end there; Wintour has been in charge of the uber famous Vogue since 1988 and wields considerable influence over American fashion. Runway shows don't start until she arrives, designers succeed because she anoints them, trends are created or crippled at her command.
Booyzen has 10 years in her clutch purse at the helm of fashion week. Only air-kisses and caviar, some say. But now that the glitz and glamour of the shows have passed, it's time to start building the future.
Recent weeks have seen a profusion of fashion extravaganzas across the country, with two by Leisureworx and the one Booyzen organises, which is purportedly bigger and better -- "we have been around longer than anyone else".
During the run-up to the Sanlam SA Fashion Week, the catwalk claws were out. Some designers were banned from showcasing in all the events and forced to choose sides. Others were involved in all three.
In tennis-match fashion, Leisureworx MD Paul Jackson declared that the more fashion weeks there were, the merrier. Booyzen said Leisureworx had sabotaged talks and had not been co-operative. Jackson said his fashion weeks were a great success. Now it's Booyzen's return.
"Sanlam SA Fashion Week was a success on many levels," says Booyzen. "Firstly, from a buyer's perspective, it was a success because they bought. And not only did they buy clothes, they have bought into the idea of SA. We have received constructive feedback so we can continue building on that front. They were particularly excited about the collaborative work between the designers and the craftspeople."
This year's fusion workshops linked 270 crafters with 27 designers, encouraging creativity and stimulating job creation. Labels such as Stoned Cherrie, Sun Goddess, Ephymol, Holmes Brothers and Black Coffee participated in the nine-city workshops to build sustainable regional fashion and craft hubs.
International media too were impressed by Sanlam SA Fashion Week, comparing it to London and Los Angeles from a production point of view, says Booyzen. "All our shows were on time, the media had immediate access to all the footage -- in LA, people sometimes have to wait for an hour for a show to start."
Booyzen is the epitome of calm and cool, and always appears as if she wouldn't break a sweat even if her plane was crashing.
There were 22 shows at this year's fashion week, compared with last year's 16. Each show was filled to 500 capacity, which means 11000 people walked through the doors of the Sandton Convention Centre. But it was the workshops and seminars that really took off. "That is the level where the industry needs to grow," says Booyzen. "We invited speakers; the arts and culture department was there; the export council was there to give advice to designers. Government is ready to help those who are willing to help themselves."
Sanlam SA Fashion Week is set to grow even further, says Booyzen. The regional tours of the collection kick off this week, when Cape Town, Pretoria and Durban get to see the shows. Booyzen is also planning the South African Fashion Awards for November next year, and a smaller winter week to be held in March, though the host city is yet to be decided.
There are still many areas that need to be addressed in SA's fledgling fashion industry, mostly to do with manufacturing. "I think a gap exists between the textile manufacturers and the designers," says Booyzen. "The textile companies and designers just don't know each other. We are helping bridge that gap. We will beat the Chinese imports soon.
"We are putting the right people in touch with each other. For example, the French buyers really loved Lunar," she says, referring to the local designers. "We've put them in contact with the export council, and the trade and industry department is also providing support. Buyers were also impressed with Sun Goddess and Loxion Kulca.
"We cannot help designers become successful and sustainable. What we can do is link them to the right people. We have
noticed designers are not business-oriented, but Sanlam has provided the answer with their business fitness assessments."
It's easy to see why Booyzen will take the business of fashion seriously. In Italy the fashion industry is second only to the engineering industry in size.
She is careful not to overestimate the export potential of South African designers. "It's important not to push it before a designer is ready. The next step will almost come naturally, it shouldn't be forced. I do think Loxion Kulca is ready and so is Sun Goddess, they both have strong design and business sense. But it's important to do your research."
SA's industry cannot compete with, for example, Paris fashion week in terms of size. Whereas one designer here may employ five people, top designers abroad employ up to 500 people to stock their stores all over the world and fill orders.
"We can compete on a creative level -- you cannot put a price on good workmanship," says Booyzen. "It's true we can't compete with the Gap and the Banana Republics of the world on a manufacturing level, but we can compete in a niche market where we distribute only 50 garments, for example."
Booyzen quickly dismisses the idea that SA can align itself with southern hemisphere locations such as SÃ£o Paulo or Sydney to create a secondary fashion circuit. "I need to do one thing at a time," she laughs. "Right now I think we need to focus on SA and Africa. Besides, it's easier to set up trade with Europe and Africa. I want SA to be seen as the gateway to Africa."
As for consensus on the profusion of fashion weeks in SA, Booyzen says she is keen to try again. "Just not today," she says.
But aside from the glitz and the industry bickering, what does fashion week mean to the ordinary person in the street?
And, just as important, what has been achieved?
"It gives designers the opportunity to express themselves and inspires the average person to do something and gives them something to be proud of," Booyzen says.

Original_Search_Title: Artists to Strategize
Headline: Namibia: Artists to Strategize
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The Oruuano Artists Union will be holding two emergency meetings with its members this weekend in the capital and at Walvis Bay.
This was announced by the union's president Banana Shekupe, in response to the Filmmakers Association of Namibia's (FAN) planned meeting in the capital Monday and Tuesday with the Minister of Broadcasting and Information.
"It is important that our members meet to strategize and prepare for this all important meeting with the minister. We need representation, too, not only FAN, because our union also has an interest in the country's film industry," said Shekupe.
It is general knowledge that FAN is the de facto bargaining power on behalf of all filmmakers in the country with the Namibian government through the Namibian Film Commission (NFC).
A panel discussion will form part of Monday's meeting with minister Netumbo Nandi-Ndaitwah at a local hotel. All FAN members are expected to attend the watershed meeting, aimed at resolving the many problems within the country's film industry.
"The idea of our meeting is not to neutralize filmmaking efforts in the country, but to encourage our union members also to be actively involved in all spheres of the evolving film industry," said Shekupe.
The union executive will meet tomorrow with members at 10h00 at the Katutura Community Arts Centre (KACA) and on Sunday morning at the Kuisebmund stadium in Walvis Bay. All union members are encouraged to attend both meetings.

Original_Search_Title: Mugabe is the Big Zero That Must Go!
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mugabe is the Big Zero That Must Go!
Body_Text:
THE government on Monday made a brazen admission that it had failed to run the country when it decided to slash three zeros from our currency in what must be a daylight attempt to short-circuit the economy.
Gideon Gono has simply confirmed that Zimbabwe has become a banana republic. Slashing the zeros is what failed governments always do, and Zimbabwe is simply following in the shameful footsteps of Peru, Argentina and Angola where the same solutions failed to rescue unwanted and doomed governments desperate to enhance their political fortunes. Shoplifting failed political and economic ideas from banana republics has become this government's favourite pastime.
Slashing the zeros has never been the panacea to a crisis of governance. Slashing the zeros can never bring relief to a battered economy that is a hapless victim of a failed state and a failed government, which has no respect for human rights and the rule of law.
Re-basing the currency cannot be the panacea to a crisis that squarely lies on an illegitimate government that has failed to govern. It is not the zeros that are at the core of the Zimbabwean crisis. The MDC believes it is not simply the zeros that must go. Mugabe is the big zero that must go!
The government will burn more midnight oil printing the so-called "new" family of bearer cheques which this regime thinks will solve Zimbabwe's economic problems in one fell swoop.
It is such kind of thinking that has led to this fool's paradise because printing money is a sign of failed economics. It represents a last resort by a failed government that has failed to lay the necessary economic groundwork to a functioning economy; a government that has failed to put in place the necessary political climate and environment that makes the supply side of the economy thrive.
Slashing the zeros will simply help in lessening the amount of money people carry around. But it does not address the visible signs of a failed economy such as low production, high government expenditure, the value of the Zimbabwe dollar and the acute foreign currency shortages that have affected fuel and power imports, which in turn have paralysed the productive sector.
The MDC believes Gono's monetary statement is a desperate attempt to rig the economy; it was a statement full of sound and fury but signifying nothing; a shrill statement full of catchy-phrases and sound bites but with no significance to the real demands of the economy and the suffering people of Zimbabwe.
Gono's statement was a crybaby statement with no solutions. Bold statements condemning corruption without the necessary political will do not take us anywhere. If this government's war on the zeros could be turned into a real and purposeful fight against corruption, bad governance and human rights abuses, Zimbabwe could be a different story. The national crisis is deeper than the regime's fixation with zeros!
The MDC believes the final solution to the problems facing the country is political. There must be a concerted effort for a political settlement of the national crisis. The Save Zimbabwe Convention agreed to such a political settlement.
President Mugabe and his government must be made to agree to a peaceful resolution of the national crisis.
The MDC's roadmap is our humble submission to such a resolution. The regime must agree to a new, people-driven constitution, a transitional authority, free and fair elections under international supervision and a reconstruction and stabilisation programme in a post-transitional era.
The people are ready to save Zimbabwe. And the people will achieve their vision of a New Zimbabwe.
Tapiwa Mashakada,
MDC Shadow Minister for
Finance and Economic Affairs.

Original_Search_Title: Ja Rules East Africa
Headline: East Africa: Ja Rules East Africa
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — Akon's show was Tanzania's biggest event of the year," said most Tanzanians after the Senegalese born US-based hip-hop artiste rocked the coastal town of Dar es Salaam in June.
But when the US rapper, Ja Rule took to the stage during the Fiesta Festivals held at the Leaders Club grounds in Dar last Saturday, everybody was convinced that Ja Rule rules and his was one of the biggest performances in the history of entertainment in Tanzania.
The sun shone, crowds that were dressed to kill came in their thousands and everybody said they wanted to see Jeffrey Atkins a.k.a Ja Rule performing alongside Ashanti though she didn't turn-up.
Carnivore in Nairobi
Ja Rule had confirmed Ashanti's absence just after touching down in Nairobi, Kenya on July 26, two days before headlining his first East African concert at the Carnivore grounds in Nairobi.
As rapper cum actor, Ja Rule was rocking East Africa, the 25-year-old Ashanti couldn't make it because she was trying to establish herself as an actress by promoting her romantic comedy, John Tucker Must Die, which is already in theatres and wrapping up scenes for her upcoming movie, Resident Evil. However, she was not missed because when Ja Rule took on the stage he pleased everybody.
While at the Carnivore show, Ja Rule handed over the key of a brand new Porsche during the Celtel Kenya Win a Porsche World Cup promotion.
In a press conference preceding the Carnivore show, Ja Rule said that he wanted to put up a show as memorable as the one he had given to the US troops at Germany's Ramstein Air Force Base in 2001 that left soldiers dancing on top of tanks and airplanes.
When he ended his performance, he left the audience yearning for more of him.
Kenyans got a chance of listening to Ja Rule's upcoming songs as he promised that, "this year is mine, last year was for 50 Cent."
La fiesta in Tanzania
Most of the partygoers who turned up for the Dar es Salaam show must have woken up with hoarse voices on Sunday after a Saturday night of screams as they cheered the rapper who put up an energetic performance.
The organisers followed the saying, "Save the best for the last," by bringing Ja Rule at 12:30 a.m. after performances by local stars, TID, Dully Sykes, Twanga band, Pepeta band, Chipolopolo band, Acudo Sounds, Matonya, Ray C, Joselyn, Mchizi Mox, Banana Zorro, Joe Makini, Mr Ebbo and Kenya's Nameless.
When Joe Makini of the Wanainama fame, Acudo Sounds and TID moved the crowds crazy, a few could predict a better performance than theirs but the American rapper easily got the crowd's mood hyper and stole the show.
When the emcee announced that Ja Rule was next on stage, the entire crowd gave him a rapturous reception. Even without all the other stars who feature in most of his songs, Ja Rule's one-man show proved that an emcee needed nothing but a microphone to have the crowds singing along. Before he could utter any word, the audience had already started chanting his hits.
When he started singing; the audience sang along, song after song. Like any smart gentleman, the 30-year-old rapper who is married to model, Aisha Murray Atkins and father of Jeff Junior and Britney Atkins plastered all his quite vulgar lyrics with softer words. He would replace words like "whore" or "bitch" with "woman" and "niggers" with "brothers" and so forth to the audience's delight.
Getting topless
The singer cum actor of the Half Past Dead movie who came wearing jeans and a black T-shirt became topless as it got hotter and hotter on stage.
At one time, the crowd tried to lure him into jumping into their arms but his bodyguards who travelled with him from the US repeatedly blocked him.
He ranted, talked and sang, and the songs that attracted wildest cheers were Holla Holla, Thug Loving that features Bobby Brown, Livin' It Up, Put It On Me and Always On Time that features Ashanti.
However, his hit New York, which features Mystik and Fat Joe, seemed to be the crowd's favourite as it got the wildest applause.
The loud speakers and his loud voice helped to cover the shouts from the audience as they sang along in chorus, "Nigger (brother) I'm from New York..." The New York song never corrupted Ja Rule's belief of being an African as he kept referring to the audience as his brothers and sisters.
The artiste who physically appears rather lighter than he looks in photos and videos had told Kenyan press that his being in Africa was his return to the motherland. "I'm proud to be a black man."
The two days Dar es salaam Fiesta, where Ja Rule performed started on a low note, but people were optimistic about a world-class show as the emcees kept reminding them that the "world's number one rapper Ja Rule" was coming on shortly.
Starting at around 3p.m. with a football match, bands began their performance at around 4 p.m.
The security was as tight as it could have been when an American superstar like Ja rule made his first appearance anywhere outside the states.
The Leaders club was filled to capacity with barely enough room for anything else apart from dancing.
However there were several power outages before 10 p.m. and people were anxious, thinking that it could disrupt Ja Rules highly anticipated, but things normalised thereafter.
This is the second superstar in Uganda's neighbourhood in the space of just one month. Uganda is yet to get a big star after Sean Paul's performance in April 2004.

Original_Search_Title: Nkumba Beats Deaf, KCC Hands Down
Headline: Uganda: Nkumba Beats Deaf, KCC Hands Down
Body_Text:
Kampala — ALBERT 'Banana' Gayi was the star as Nkumba University men volleyball team recovered from a loss to champions Sport-S to beat Old Kampala Deaf (OKD) and KCC hands down in the national volleyball league at Nsambya over the weekend.
Gayi enjoyed good form in partnership with Moses Sebidde, Denis Amukun and team captain Joseph Apuuli as Nkumba beat KAVC and KCC in straight sets.
Sport-S coach Nason Bwesigye was forced to don the team jersey for the first time in a competitive match to make up for players who arrived late as Sport-S hit Nkumba team into submission.
"I confess Sport-S is superior. They have beaten us on the trot but we have to work to break their block format," said Nkumba coach Anthony Ndaula.
In the women category KAVC continued their supremacy when they beat long time rivals KCC 3-1 to stay on top of the table.
Sport-S 3 Nkumba 2
Cobap 3 Tigers 1
Police 3 JVC 1
OKD 1 Nkumba 3
Veterans 3 Tigers 1
Nkumba 3 KCC 0
Women
Nkumba 3 Cubs 0
KAVC 3 Sparrows 0
KCC 3 Sport-S 1

Original_Search_Title: City Father Who Mixed Freely With the Low And the High
Headline: Kenya: City Father Who Mixed Freely With the Low And the High
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Born near Banana Hill, Limuru, 75 years ago, Mr John Peter Mbogua had an illustrious career as a diplomat and a public servant before his death at Nairobi Hospital on July 26. He had been admitted there after a short illness.
Mr Mbogua was educated at Kiambaa Primary School, then joined Kagumo and Alliance High schools. In 1953, he was admitted to Makerere college, Kampala, from where he graduated in 1958 with a Bachelor of Arts degree.
He attended a post-graduate course at Canada's McGill University in 1959 and left in 1961 with a master's degree in Economics and Geography.
During his stay in Canada, Mr Mbogua did intensive research in urban communities, which took him to Barbados for a six-month investigation. He taught sociology at Plattsburgh State University in New York between 1961 and 1962. On returning to Kenya, he taught at Kangaru Secondary School in Embu district.
In 1963, Mr Mbogua joined the City Council as a trainee administrator and went on to become director of social services and housing. In 1967, he became the second African after Mr Kimani Waiyaki to be appointed the city's Town Clerk; a position he held until his retirement in 1976. The following year, President Jomo Kenyatta appointed him as Kenya's ambassador to the US.
He also served as permanent secretary in the Ministry of Local Government and, since 2004, as chairman of the Institute of Public Policy Research and Analysis (Kippra) in Planning and National Development.
Mr Mbogua was also the chairman of Josem Trust Kenya Ltd and Dr Thomas Barnados Children's Home in Nairobi, director of Del Monte (Kenya) Ltd, Kenya Roads Board and Limuru Tea (a subsidiary of Unilever Tea (K) Ltd).
He had also served as chairman of the Kenya Institute of Management and had been a member of the Nairobi Interim Oversight Board. The board was formed in 2000 following revelations of corruption at City Hall. It was dissolved three years later by former Local Government minister Karisa Maitha.
In a message of condolence to family and friends, Planning minister Henry Obwocha said Mr Mbogua was "a person of good virtue who was respected by many people".
He added: "During his tenure at Kippra, Ambassador Mbogua distinguished himself as an individual not only committed to his duty, but a team player with a passion for success."
A friend of Mr Mbogua described him as a "humble and down to earth person" who mixed freely with both the low and the high in society. Mr Mbogua is survived by his widow, Sarah, and children Caroline Njeri, Angela Njeri, Anthony Thuo, Naomi Wairimu and Judie Wanjiku.
Family and friends are meeting daily at the Mbogua residence - 7819 Kyuna Road in Nairobi - from 6pm.
A family spokesman said funeral arrangements will be announced later.

Original_Search_Title: A 'Kiss of Life' for Saitoti
Headline: Kenya: A 'Kiss of Life' for Saitoti
Body_Text:
Nairobi — By clearing him over the Goldenberg scam, the court gave former Vice-President George Saitoti's political future a veritable kiss of life.
He promptly reciprocated his wife's support during the trying period with a passionate kiss. It is expected that the court decision will clear the last hurdle to the Kajiado North MP's political rehabilitation that should at least take him back to the Cabinet.
He had been hounded out by persistent calls to resign over the multi-billion scandal that involved export compensation for fictitious jewellery.
The resignation was itself the subject of controversy with theories that the minister never really tendered his resignation as stated by President Kibaki when he formally accepted Saitoti's exit alongside that of then Energy minister Kiraitu Murungi on February 13.
The President has set a precedent of rewarding an innocent verdict of public servants forced to resign over various allegations. High Court judge Philip Waki was reinstated immediately after a tribunal cleared him of corruption charges. It is expected the same script will apply for Saitoti.
Apparent demotion
But an eventual Cabinet recall could await other considerations such as a similar not-guilty conclusion by the Kenya Anti-Corruption Authority on Kiraitu and former Finance minister David Mwiraria, who were forced out over alleged involvement in the Anglo Leasing scandal.
President Kibaki could also opt for the right opportunity to fill 11 Cabinet slots that have remained vacant from resignation and refusal of acceptance. Until his resignation, Saitoti was the Education minister, a portfolio he handled superbly despite it being a climb-down from the vice presidency he had occupied for 13 years during Moi's reign.
His willingness to agree to the apparent demotion under the Narc Government was probably informed by his knowledge of latent public judgement for his alleged role in the scandal.
Thus Monday's verdict should, theoretically, be a shot in the arm to his expected quest for the presidency. Whether the decision will translate into a public opinion about-turn on his assumed guilt remains to be seen.
The Mathematics professor has kept count of his days in agony. On Monday, he talked of 16 years of sorrow when his heart, haunted by the albatross of Goldenberg, hardly knew peace.
Political career
Before then, the last time the former don betrayed naked happiness was in 2002 when the Narc top dogs finally managed to agree on Mwai Kibaki as the joint opposition candidate.
Then, he appeared clumsy in raising the locked hands of his colleagues to the "Yote yawezekana bila Moi" tune but it was clear he had put behind an even more acute sorrow that had been gnawing his political soul.
Moi had fished him from academic obscurity at the Universtiy of Nairobi in 1983 before fast-tracking his political career with appointments as Finance minister and later, vice-president in 1988.
He served faithfully as Number Two for 13 years. One of his memorable events involved an alleged nearly-fatal poisoning in 1990 by the "same people who tried to kill Robert Ouko".
Another was a 14-month demotion to minister in 1997. If Saitoti thought his patience and humiliation would be rewarded, he realised he had been mistaken when Moi dropped the bombshell.
During the hustling for 2002 elections, the former President told Saitoti to his face he was not presidential material. He twisted the knife in the professor's heart by anointing a political neophyte, Uhuru Kenyatta, as his preferred successor.
Post-election spoils
The humiliation was too much and the excessively guarded Saitoti finally found the courage to abandon Kanu and join other renegades unhappy with Uhuru's candidature.
The rebels later morphed to the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) but only after Moi had sacked him as the VP for daring to show his unhappiness. His overplayed loyalties and forced departure from Government may have cost him a chance to drive a harder bargain for post-election spoils. Save for the presidency, it would have been difficult to adequately "reward" a long-serving VP.
But if he felt short-changed under Narc, Saitoti did not show it. In fact, he seemed to have perfected a disdain for politics and somehow managed to place himself above the high-volatile politics of the broken memorandum of understanding that strangled Narc right from the onset.
Although he had joined the Government through the LDP, he did not seem to share the Narc faction's noisy displeasure at President Kibaki lopsided sharing of key public jobs.
Genuine changes
Instead, he buried himself in work by making the most of the President's laissez faire freedom to his Cabinet to make genuine changes in the Ministry of Education. One of the changes was to let public universities be run by new chancellors and hire vice chancellors competitively.
But with time, Saitoti who had the added cap of being the Deputy Leader of the Government Business in Parliament, severed his umbilical cord with the LDP and became a staunch basher of the party.
He was a key campaigner of the Banana team in the rejected Draft Constitution, giving significance to his changed loyalties. But much as he tried to change his political skin in Narc colours, the Government's promise to bust the ghosts of Kanu still haunted him.
Nothing represented the debauchery of that era than the Goldenberg scam and the public seemed convinced (wrongly, the court said) that Saitoti had been a major player in that game.

Original_Search_Title: Kombo's Style May Be His Undoing
Headline: Kenya: Kombo's Style May Be His Undoing
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Musikari Kombo cannot be blamed for his sense of grievance. But, as usual, he has taken too long to make up his mind, and this could blunt the impact he wants to make.
Narc became history much earlier than last week's by-elections - it was over soon after the last General Election when the partners started quarrelling over almost everything.
It may be that the Ford Kenya chairman's preferred style was to wait to acknowledge Narc's death only after President Kibaki finally showed his hand in the Narc Kenya business. Yet one gets the feeling that Mr Kombo has no clear strategy - instead he sulks and issues veiled threats whenever he is put in a box.
The Local Government minister appears to be a very hesitant politician who is unlikely to go for grand gestures such as resigning from the Cabinet and rejecting the Kibaki government outright. The last time he did this was immediately after last year's referendum when he and Cabinet colleague Charity Ngilu gave an effective ultimatum to the President to stop taking them for granted.
At the time, President Kibaki was on the ropes, with his Banana referendum camp trampled into a mess. And having just kicked the LDP brigade out of the Cabinet, he was in no mood to see Mr Kombo and Mrs Ngilu take a walk, both having stuck with Banana.
The question now is if the morale booster the President has scored in Nakuru Town and northern Kenya warrants turning his back on Mr Kombo and Mrs Ngilu. On balance, he is now probably convinced that working the Narc Kenya project is more crucial to his presidency than perpetually keeping the two happy.
Mr Kombo's modus operandi has tended to weaken him politically. His belief that dangling an ethnic card to people in power is the way to greater things is now being challenged directly on two fronts - Narc Kenya and Kanu.
In their different ways, each party wants to show that building a grassroots national network is a better investment for gaining power than calling together a small cabal of ethnic chieftains to make a coalition. True, we are a long way from realising if this ambition will succeed in Kenya's already cannibalised polity, but at least somebody is trying.
The main problem with Mr Kombo is that he operates as if everybody else owes him a favour. Basically, he had stuck with President Kibaki in the hope that the President would back him as the successor.
There is nothing wrong with this, but the only problem is that there is more to becoming president than imagining that the incumbent will anoint one.
For one, President Kibaki's Central Kenya power base, which Mr Kombo dreamt would be delivered to him, is never one that the President can casually "bequeath" as he chooses.
The assumption that this bloc will follow, or has ever followed, the President blindly is flat wrong. So it was up to Mr Kombo to go on the ground to campaign like other candidates to woo that constituency, as with all the others beyond Bukusuland. Unfortunately, one always gets the impression that he expects to sit pretty and everybody will scramble to anoint him president.
This may be quite true, especially if you demonstrate that you have a compelling hold on a huge bloc such as the Luhya that is capable of taking you extremely far in the Kenyan politics. The trouble, though, is that Mr Kombo is really not the chieftain he pretends to be. All the other political players know this. He has been leading Ford-K on the premise that it is the "natural" party of Western Province, but this is an argument that is shared only by himself.
To be honest, Kakamega, Vihiga and Busia districts do not quite dance to his tune. There are enough MPs from the other parties in the province whose presence amply confirms that Mr Kombo is not the master of this house.
The succession of civic seats his party has been losing to LDP and other parties in the region further drives this point home. The latest such loss, in Butere, was to New Kanu - of all people!
Mr Kombo's obsession with ethnic-based coalitions would be more at home in LDP, where he has belatedly sought to pitch camp. But he will be welcomed more as a casualty than a hero. If the truth be told, LDP does not think much of him. The party considers his previous sucking up to President Kibaki a cheap strategy aimed at personal aggrandisement and that has now backfired and sent him scurrying for refuge elsewhere.

Original_Search_Title: Birth of New Outfit Led to Slow Death of Coalition
Headline: Kenya: Birth of New Outfit Led to Slow Death of Coalition
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The disintegration of Narc has its roots in the birth and growth of Narc Kenya, which is now eight months old.
After the November referendum - in which the Orange group defeated the Government-backed Banana team - six politicians allied to President Kibaki met to analyse the results.
The meeting at first involved Water minister Mutua Katuku and assistant ministers Danson Mungatana and Stephen Tarus, who met at City Hall. The team was later expanded to six after Cabinet minister Kivutha Kibwana and assistant ministers Mwangi Kiunjuri and Cecily Mbarire were brought on board. They met at the Boulevard Hotel in Nairobi but Ms Mbarire later pulled out.
Mr Kiunjuri said: "We were bitter that we had been humiliated and we wanted to start a new life in politics."
He also blamed Health minister Charity Ngilu for failing to let go of Narc, the party they used to win elections in 2002.
Mr Katuku said: "We had been driven to the wall and some people had kept Narc in their handbags."
According to Mr Katuku, the post-referendum period called for a new beginning with a new party that would seek grassroots support.
Mr Katuku and Mr Kiunjuri were given the job of thinking of a name and arranging for the registration of the party.
Last December, the group held a bigger meeting at Merika Hotel in Nakuru. The purpose was to bring together those who had stuck with the Government in the run-up to the referendum. The meeting came up with the name of Narc Kenya - and the party was born.
"We had to retain the Rainbow identity which was the slogan that helped us to win the last elections," Mr Kiunjuri said.
They registered the party in January with officials who are not MPs for legal reasons. MPs are required by the Constitution to vacate their seats once they decamp to another political party.
Except for Prof Kibwana, the pioneers were all youthful politicians.
The party's emphasis on younger politicians was also evident in the just concluded by-elections.
Narc Kenya has put in place a structure in which the chairman's post is rotational after between one and two months.
"We want everyone from every province to feel its warmth before we hold national elections as this is part of owning the party," Mr Katuku said.
Mr Tarus was the chairman for the duration of the by-elections and has since handed the baton to Foreign Affairs minister Raphael Tuju.
The party's next agenda is to embark on a national recruitment drive.
Buoyed with the success of clinching three out of five seats contested in the by-elections, plus two civic seats out of 12 Narc-K politicians think that the party has enough time to prepare for 2007 elections to be able to win.
The party won seats where younger politicians campaigned - Nakuru Town, Saku and North Horr - while it lost where older politicians camped like Moyale and Laisamis.

Original_Search_Title: Arturs Could Tap Police Radios
Headline: Kenya: Arturs Could Tap Police Radios
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The sheer extent of the security threat posed by the Artur brothers became evident yesterday, after it emerged they could tap police communication.
The brothers were also never licensed to have guns, the Kiruki commission was told two days after police displayed the deadly fire-power recovered from the foreigners' residence.
The communication equipment held by the brothers could tap into the police radio network even from as far as the US, Communications Commission of Kenya director for frequencies Stanley Kibe told the commission.
Mr Kibe, however, described the equipment as rudimentary, and said the security arms of government could avoid such vulnerability in future by upgrading their systems.
The source of the firearms and bullets recovered from the Runda residence of the Artur brothers remained a mystery after chief firearms licensing officer Peter Ingolan said that none of them were licensed to carry guns.
Mr Ingolan, however, did not tell the commission whether the serial numbers on the assault rifles could have revealed how the brothers acquired them.
Two AK-47 assault rifles recovered from a banana bush two metres from the perimeter wall of the Runda residence bore serial numbers, but those of four Ceska pistols had been tampered with.
On his part, Mr Kibe said the communication equipment branded SGC Power Talk HF Transceiver found in the residence were of 63 frequencies in the 1.8 MHz to 29.7 MHz band and could be used from the air, land or sea.
Mr Kibe said CCK's HF database showed none of the 63 frequencies had been assigned, and that it was not illegal to possess them, but it was contrary to the law to use them without a licence.
It was impossible to tell whether the equipment had been in use, Mr Kibe said, as he confirmed that they were in good working order.
A different set of equipment, a Motorola VHF Transceiver, also found at the Arturs' residence, had been allocated to Nairobi's Express Automation, Hepco Builders, Raju Estate Agency, Nandlal and Co, Skyhawk and Prince Communications and Mombasa's Peraly Waters, the inquiry heard.
Yet another Motorola Portable VHF Transceiver, is shown at the CCK's database to have been allocated to Kensta Ltd, Gibb (E.A) and Mombasa's Ancrata Construction.
Also in the collection of equipment were Motorola "family radios" which are two-way, very short distance voice communications gadgets for facilitating family and group activities.
One does not, however, require a licence to own the family radios, the commissioners heard.
"The frequency spectrum is scarce, and is often shared, hence the reason why some of them are assigned to more than one user," he said.
Mr Kibe said holders of SGC Power HF Transceiver like the Arturs could listen in on other frequencies with similar kinds of receiver, including police communication. "It is possible to listen to 63 frequencies," he said.
And using the other Arturs' gadgets, one can overhear communication if he or she gets to a service area of particular licensee. Mr Kibe said CCK had never assigned any of the 63 HF frequencies like the ones held by the Arturs for use in Kenya.
"The entire frequency band of VHF is 1.8 megawatts, and is meant for long distance communication," Mr Kibe said.
The official said the frequency is for long distance and can be transmitted to as far as the US.
Asked by lawyer Jane Ondieki for suspended CID chief Joseph Kamau what could be done to prevent security being compromised through hacking police communication, Mr Kibe said: "I expect security agencies to use something better than this."
He said the defunct Kenya Posts and Telecommunications Corporation prohibited possession of radio equipment, but he had not seen such a clause in the CCK Act.
Earlier, Mr Ingolan said the Arturs and seven others were not licensed to own firearms and ammunition. The others are Messrs Arman Dimidri, Alexander Taschi Pak, Lucas Makena, Nyakasha Ntwenya, Shifana Alarakiya, Sarkissyan Artak and Sarkissyan Arman.
On Wednesday, the commission heard that deadly arsenal, special communication equipment, camouflage gear and a number of video tapes were recovered from the Runda residence of the Artur brothers.
The spine-chilling fire-power held by the brothers and their associates before they left the country was exhibited at the Kiruki commission along with an assortment of special communication equipment.
The inquiry was told that the arsenal was found hidden in a banana bush.

Original_Search_Title: Libby's Works Wonders for the Inner Man On a 'Zesa-Less' Night
Headline: Zimbabwe: Libby's Works Wonders for the Inner Man On a 'Zesa-Less' Night
Body_Text:
I HAVE rarely eaten supper so early, but a call from a neighbour on an unrelated matter revealed Mount Pleasant was again without electricity and had been since before lunch.
Couldn't face yet another meal of cold meat, cheese or pilchard sandwiches by guttering candlelight, washed down with a glass of milk or a can of lager, I was off for hot sustenance.
Newlands called, but the whole of the east side of Enterprise Road was in total darkness, which left the grossly overpriced food court on the west side, the rather good Chinese/Thai takeaway or -- the old standby -- Libby's Restaurant.
It's a very long time since I'd been there for supper and, before, it's tended to be a late-ish sort of afterthought, rather than planned early to mid-evening gastronomic sortie.
Indeed the last time I recall going there reasonably early goes back to the days when it was run by the eponymous Libby (nee Malachias), a Greek lady (in every sense of the word) from Malawi and her Irish husband, martial arts expert Dermott McQuillam. And that was for one of their famous Wednesday night prawn specials around eight years ago when four members of the Miller clan could eat crustaceans, rice and chips, salad, great crusty bread and butter to almost bursting point, plus sundry drinks and puddings and have much change out of $500. (I kid thee not!)
I got the impression most if not all other conventional diners were also escaping from Zesa cuts but -- a cultural shock -- initially three presumably "regulars" in the bar, bellowing at each other as if there were 30 present, then their number grew to five, screaming as if there were 500, shared platters of Greek meze snacks.
A very sophisticated sound system played a non-stop medley of hits of the 60s and 70s, which could sometimes be heard over the row from the bar; a mute TV showed soundless, mindless Yankee soap operas.
Apart from conventional diners eating conventional two or three course dinners, I mentally mused over a couple of strikingly pretty 20-somethings gliding in and ordering simply two plates of samoosas and cane and cokes and a middle aged couple at the next table whose order was for waffles and ice-cream and cappuccinos.
Had they enjoyed soup and steak and chips at home, or in a restaurant across the road, when Zesa plunged them into darkness, meal unfinished? I wondered. Or had they just incredibly sweet teeth?
Libby's is a Mediterranean-style eatery, slightly favouring the Hellenic cooking of the original owner whose family, I believe, hailed from Cyprus. Xana Colquhoun (nee Botha) has owned it for several years and maintains the successful culinary traditions.
I had done serious damage to a basket of toasted continental bread and good salted butter before a really delightful, piping hot, mushroom soup, fragrantly filled with fungi arrived. I'd omitted to say earlier, the dark, cheerless, Zesa-less, robot-less, traffic chaos-filled night, was also near freezing. The $500 000 deep, nourishing bowlful worked wonders for the inner man.
Other starters include souvlaki at $550 000, deep-fried Haloumi $750 000, dolmades $450 000.
Service was so pleasant and professional in all other aspects that one tends to accept reasonably cheerfully that they'd sold out of chicken breasts ($1, 45 million) at lunchtime and forgive the fact that the second choice half roasted chicken, ordered with lemon and herb sauce, without cooked vegetables, but with an optional salad, arrived piri-piri style (which I can live with), without salad and followed promptly by what looked suspiciously like butternut or pumpkin (take it away!) and French beans.
Salad was smoothly and quickly substituted and, perhaps down to pigging out on the bread earlier, I found the $1,5 million platter a bit of an epicurean challenge.
Salads as a main course range from $850 000 to $1,25 million, grills $1,65 million (pork chops) to $2,5 million (T-bone) with fillet steak $1,85 million. Accompanying sauces (the meat usually doesn't need them) are $200 000- $350 000. A generous portion of ice-cream and chocolate sauce was $395 000, Pilsener lagers $300 000 each and an excellent cappuccino $200 000. Thus the bottom line for a three course dinner, two drinks and coffee was $3 195 000.
Between chatting and dipping into a book (you can actually read for pleasure at Libby's without people seeing you as a possible social miscreant) I made the meal last as long as possible. The bar-flies lowered noise level to a steady bellow and it was easier to hear the sort of music one would expect at Billy Fudpucker's pub over the road on a Thursday night (if they had any power!)
Billy's and Trax and The Sitar, Mama Mia's, Blue Banana and Baobab Grill were all still in darkness or operating by flickering candle-light and faltering generators when I left two hours plus after arrival.
I was almost killed by a lunatic in a Pajero or Prado failing to give way to the right at the Churchill/Borrowdale Road "lights" and -- Sod's Law! -- seven minutes after clambering into bed by almost empty cigarette lighter (I quit smoking the day they went up from $2,50 to $3,80 a packet: they now cost $250 000!) the lights, fridge, deep-freeze and short-wave radio came back to life.
Recommended family restaurant, booking not usually vital. Libby's, Mon Repos Centre, Newlands. Closed Sundays. Tel 746471.

Original_Search_Title: Opposition Chief Accuses State of Rigging Elections
Headline: Kenya: Opposition Chief Accuses State of Rigging Elections
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Rigging by the Government cost Kanu and the Liberal Democratic Party seats in the Monday by-election, not opposition disunity.
Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta said rigging made the opposition lose three of the seats, which were won by the government-backed Narc Kenya.
LDP leader Raila Odinga had said failure by Kanu and LDP to approach the by-elections through a united front made them lose the seats.
Mr Odinga said if the two parties had gone to the polls under the banner of the Orange Democratic Movement they would have won.
"It was not about unity; it was the rigging by Government and flouting of electoral rules," said the Kanu chairman.
He added: "Kanu condemns in the strongest terms possible the manner in which the Government rigged the elections in favour of Narc-K candidates in five constituencies."
He also accused the Electoral Commission of Kenya of legitimising a flawed election instead of nullifying it.
Mr Kenyatta argued that the Government misappropriated its resources to manipulate the by-elections in favour of Narc-K.
The Government, he added, "committed numerous violations including constitutional, criminal and electoral malpractices in full public glare."
It included using the provincial administration to campaign and act as Narc-K agents.
The Government, he claimed, was slowly turning the country into a "banana republic" where laws were not respected.
He also dismissed suggestions that internal wrangles in Kanu between him and former President Moi on one side and secretary-general William Ruto contributed to the poor performance that saw Kanu lose two seats it previously held.
"To us Kanu, we did not lose neither did Narc Kenya win - the losers are Kenyans, democracy lost," he said yesterday.
He warned that the abuse of power and State resources witnessed in the polls were a precursor to what would happen in 2007.
The leader of the Official Opposition said: "In 2002 Kenyans celebrated democracy, they talked about increased democratic space but last Monday will go down on record that all this was lost."
Mr Kenyatta said unless electoral laws were amended to give the Electoral Commission enough powers to deal with irregularities, then elections in the country will not be free and fair.
He said Kanu was willing to unite with other opposition parties as far as ensuring there was a level playing field.
However, the party will not collude in the theft of public office as this rendered democracy useless.
"Unless the Electoral Commission puts its foot down, what we have witnessed is a prelude to next year's election," he added. "The Government has shown it is ready to rig the elections and impose leaders on Kenyans."
He asked why the ECK did not take action though it had warned that it would nullify the elections because of the use of State resources.
He was speaking at a news conference at Parliament Buildings in company of 10 MPs among them Justin Muturi, Henry Kosgey, Nick Salat, Billow Kerrow and Amina Abdalla.
Also present were losers in the by-election Sarah Godana and Dida Wako Halakhe.
Mr Kenyatta said Kanu did well by capturing the Moyale and Laisamis seats and six of the 12 civic seats.

Original_Search_Title: Red Tape Killing Banks -- Survey
Headline: South Africa: Red Tape Killing Banks -- Survey
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — BANKS globally have identified too much regulation as the biggest risk to their financial wellbeing, while rising interest rates are also becoming problematic.
That is according to the Banking Banana Skins Survey, released yesterday by Price- waterhouseCoopers.
The survey, conducted by the UK's Centre for the Study of Financial Innovation, polled 468 banks, nonbank businesses, regulators and observers in 60 countries. Six South African banks were included.
Regulation was perceived as the biggest risk for the second year running, while credit risk came second, also for the second year running. The risk of derivatives moved from fourth to third place, while commodities -- largely due to the rampant oil price -- moved from 14th to fourth place. Interest rates came fifth, up from 12th last year.
"People are saying a lot of management time is being taken up by regulation and it perhaps takes the focus away from other things," said Tom Winterboer, lead banking partner for PricewaterhouseCoopers.
Some of the regulations introduced in SA in the past few years included the Financial Advisers and Intermediary Services (Fais) Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act (Fica).
Banks are also preparing for the introduction of new international capital accord Basel 2 in 2008, with parallel runs next year. These regulations mean additional training and system requirements.
However, despite the financial and time cost of regulations, the banking industry saw the regulations as positive and appropriate.
"Clearly one has to say regulation must be good and it must be there for the protection of the system overall and for the consumer. But one has to ask whether the level we are seeing now is really appropriate."
He said the extra layer of costs could be a barrier to entry to smaller banks trying to get into the system.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Must Conduct Credible Elections, Says Nnamani
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Must Conduct Credible Elections, Says Nnamani
Body_Text:
London — The greatest legacy President Olusegun Obasanjo can leave for Nigerians after his tenure is a credible general election that will usher in new leaders in 2007, the President of the Senate, Chief Ken Nnamani, has said.
Nnamani spoke in London on Tuesday night at the Pan African Parliamentary Dialogue and said unless the 2007 general elections were free and fair, the government that would emerge from it might lack legitimacy, warning that it would not be acceptable to Nigerians.
The Senate president said the credibility of the impending elections was important because any government that would emerge needed the legitimacy to push ahead with the reforms of the Obasanjo administration which he said had achieved significant mileage in the efforts to revive the nation's economy.
Responding to a toast by Baroness Howells of the St. David, Nnamani said: "The greatest legacy which the present administration, particularly our president can leave is the institutional legacy of credible elections in 2007," adding, "I say credible because, if the presidential elections and other elections are free and fair, Nigerians both at home and in the Diaspora would be supportive of that administration. If you notice that we have done very well by conducting credible elections and handing over to a credible government, chances are that people would be supportive of the government."
He illustrated his point with the process of his emergence as the Senate president: "I gave a little example, without sounding immodest, I am the first Senate president in Nigeria in recent times that went through a rigorous election and my colleagues, the senators themselves felt that I should be the one to lead them. And that is why the concept of Banana peels is far fetched as far our administration in the Senate is concerned."
According to him, "Every senator makes sure that our administration succeeds. By extension, if we are able to elect a president and everyone feels that it is credible and the elections are free and fair and everything is done properly, chances are that Nigerians, both at home and in the Diaspora will support that administration."
Nnamani told the audience, which included parliamentarians and diplomats and other Africans, including the Mayor of London, Ken Livingstone, that the support of Nigerians and friends of Nigeria was critical now because the country was very vital to the survival of democracy Africa.
"If democracy must succeed in Africa, it must take root in Nigeria," he said, adding, "If for instance the unfortunate incident happening now in Beirut, if anything near that happens in Nigeria, we have no country that can absorb the teeming population of Nigeria."
He warned that if anything went wrong with the 2007 elections, it could undermine the efforts to attract more foreign investments into the country.
On the reforms of the present administration, the Senate president said the Senate would continue to support the President and the need ensure the continuity of his social and economic reforms.
But he clarified that continuity of reforms did not mean continuity of the present regime. "What is critical is our ability to sustain the tempo of the economic growth in Nigeria as ushered in by the present administration. There must be a change," he said.
Nnamani said the National Assembly supported the campaigns against corruption. "We recognise the need to curtail corruption and corrupt tendencies in Nigeria and that is why the EFCC is doing every thing humanly possible," he stated, explaining that the "EFCC is an act of the National Assembly, which implies our support to the administration in fighting the cankerworm, which we now call corruption."
The Senate president said the National Assembly was particularly interested in stamping out graft because "corruption in Nigeria is drainage, it does not add anything to the economy."

Original_Search_Title: The 'Real Mccoy' of Piri Piri Chicken
Headline: Zimbabwe: The 'Real Mccoy' of Piri Piri Chicken
Body_Text:
Harare — FEW readers will recall the 'Great Kitchen Debate', which took place between Vice President Nixon and Premier Khrushchev at a model American house exhibition in Moscow in the 70s. As the lively exchange centred on such topics as domestic appliances, rockets and capitalism, it would appear that political concerns today have remained fairly constant.
But closer to home, kitchen debates have less to do with hot air and more to do with 'Where is the best place to eat out in this town?' or 'Who's the chef in so-and so's kitchen currently?' Since most of us have visited Mozambique at some time, or have relatives and friends who were weaned on 'sumo de piri piri' on the other side of the border, we all have our own ideas about the 'real McCoy' when it comes to piri piri chicken. Many theories and debates abound concerning authentic 'galinha de piri piri', and last Sunday George and I visited Westgate Shopping Centre at lunchtime and made an exciting discovery.
Following a tip-off, we made our way to the Delicious Monster, previously known as the Garden Café, where a three-course Sunday lunch special was on offer. For $2.5 mil one could enjoy soup (two choices), a main course (difficult choice between roast beef and Yorkshire pudding, or a half piri piri chicken and chips) followed by strawberries and ice cream.
George and I both chose the Caldo Verde soup, a delicious hearty peasant broth made of potatoes and covo, seasoned with olive oil, salt and black pepper, and garnished with authentic chourizo sausage. The alternative was mixed vegetable soup.
The waiting service was efficient and friendly, and we munched on crispy potato skin snacks while awaiting the main course. George and I usually try to order different dishes, but on this occasion neither of us could resist the chicken. New owners of the Delicious Monster, Ralph and Bela, are both great cooks, and as they attend regular cookouts in Mozambique, we knew something special was in store.
Our chicken portions were generous, perfectly grilled, and succulent beneath a spicy, crispy skin. Abundant and golden French fries (no stale oil used here) were piping hot, and a side salad of lettuce, onion and tomato was dressed in the classic Portuguese style with Azeite olive oil and balsamic vinegar. Ralph's relatively mild home-made chilli sauce made from a combination of paprika and chilli peppers, slow-cooked on the Aga, provided a subtle contrast to the chilli heat of the grilled chicken.
Breakfast at the Delicious Monster is said to be a special affair, and caters for all tastes. One can enjoy a fluffy scrambled egg made with fresh garden herbs on wholewheat toast for $650 000, or American pancakes topped with banana and pecan nut sauce, served with bacon, for $1 mil. If you really mean business, go for the definitive Delicious Monster breakfast consisting of egg, bacon, sausage, rump steak, black pudding, mushrooms, potatoes, grilled tomatoes, toast and coffee, at $1.8 mil!
As the restaurant has only recently opened, menus are still being developed, but will eventually offer a comprehensive choice including fish dishes, quiches and salads, burgers, and steak and chips. The resident chef, who goes by the name of Heaven, and is ably assisted by his sous-chef Blessing, does a particular line in pickled fish, which is proving much sought-after. As summer approaches, a cold pickled fish salad enjoyed at an outside table, surrounded by Ice Berg roses and flowering shrubs from the adjacent Garden Centre, would be just the thing.
But for the next few weeks, at least, Heaven's kitchen will be concentrating on serving hearty warming dishes, and keeping up with the demand for piri piri chicken.
The Delicious Monster
Westgate Garden Centre
Garden Ct, Westgate Mall
Tel:332844
Open Tuesday - Sunday, 9am - 3.30pm
(Awaiting liquor licence)

Original_Search_Title: How Sympathy And Culture Swayed Voters With An Eye On 2007
Headline: Kenya: How Sympathy And Culture Swayed Voters With An Eye On 2007
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Sympathy votes, cultural beliefs, tribalism and next year's General Election were among factors that influenced poll results.
Voters were sympathetic in Nakuru Town, Saku and Moyale constituencies where relatives of the deceased MPs won.
The 27-year-old son of former assistant minister Mirugi Kariuki won in Nakuru Town, while his age mate Wario Galgalo Malla took the Moyale seat, which fell vacant after the death of his elder brother, Dr Guracha Galgalo.
And the elder brother of Mr Abdi Sasura, who died in the March 10 Marsabit plane crash together with his four colleagues, inherited the seat. The leaders were going to attend a meeting to broker peace in the volatile region.
But it was different for widows Mary Ngoyoni and Sarah Godana of Laisamis and North Horr respectively, who lost their bids due to cultural beliefs. Voters felt that women could not measure up to such tasks; they cannot lead.
Since their husbands also perished in the plane crash, it was widely believed that they could get sympathy votes.
But the campaign against President Kibaki and Narc Kenya helped the party snatch three out of the five seats - Nakuru Town, Saku and North Horr - plus two civic seats from Olkalou constituency in Central Province and Fafi in North Eastern Province.
In the run-up to the polls, Kanu leaders led by chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and a Liberal Democratic Party group led by Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga ganged up against Narc-K, challenging the President to declare his position in the party.
But Narc-K succeed - at least in Nakuru Town - to woo a high number of voters to the President's side as a way of insulating him against what was seen as a threat to his presidency.
The polls were also a replay of last year's November referendum on the Constitution that pitted pro-government Banana campaigners against Orange supporters allied to Kanu and LDP.
Ethnicity, too, reared its ugly head in all the five constituencies - in Nakuru, voters from Central Province were on the government side, against other communities who were supporting either Kanu or LDP.
The town is cosmopolitan, with a sizeable number of voters from Central, Rift Valley, Nyanza and Western provinces.
Voters from Central Province rallied behind Mr Kariuki, as the LDP leadership rallied theirs from the rest of the provinces to support Pastor Mike Brawan.
Mr Odinga, addressing rallies in Nakuru, sent shivers among Narc-K supporters, who turned out in large numbers to block the LDP candidate.
Among the pastoralists from the Upper Eastern, inter-clan rivalry influenced the outcome.
In Moyale, the campaign by Narc-K ministers using government resources appears to have incensed voters.
This was evident on Monday night in Mr Malla's victory speech: "They have said no to intimidation and harassment by voting wisely. They fought for their rights and made the right decision."
He said the campaigns by ministers using State resources had angered the people of Moyale who decided to vote for the opposing side, Kanu.

Original_Search_Title: Widows' Defeat Blamed On Cultural Prejudices
Headline: Kenya: Widows' Defeat Blamed On Cultural Prejudices
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The defeat of Sarah Godana in North Horr and Mary Ngoyoni in Laisamis seemed to re-affirm the perception among pastoralist communities that women cannot rule.
Hopes that a woman from Northern Kenya would be voted to Parliament for the first time were dashed when Ukur Yattani (Kanu) was declared the North Horr MP-elect and Joseph Lekuton (Kanu) the victor in Laisamis.
It was a great disappointment for Godana's supporters since the widow of the late Dr Bonaya Godana had been tipped to win.
Government interference partly contributed to her defeat but then, Mary Ngoyoni, who vied on a Narc-Kenya ticket, lost to her Kanu rival, Lekuton.
Yattani entered the contest on the platform that a woman could not rule and won support from Gabra elders.
Dr Godana helped Yattani to get into the provincial administration, where he rose to a District Commissioner.
Yattani admitted that he was a close associate of the late MP and benefited from his generosity, but added that it was his democratic right to run for an elective post. The mentality that women are weak and cannot rule was enhanced by the prevailing insecurity in the region, where the Gabra were technically at war with their neighbours especially from southern Ethiopia.
In the lead to the campaign period, over 20 people residents had been killed by Ethiopian raiders.
The massacre at Turbi in Marsabit last year also worked against the widow.
The late Godana and the late Abdi Sasura (Saku) did not see eye-to-eye. To counter his rival in North Horr, Sasura abandoned Kanu and joined the Banana side during last year's referendum. He later "co-operated" with the Government side and was appointed the deputy Chief Whip in Parliament.
When the by-election date was set, Hussein, the elder brother of the late Saku MP, decided to vie on a Narc-Kenya ticket.
But to the Gabra in North Horr, Sasura joined Narc-Kenya to counter their "activities". The Gabra therefore felt they too should be on the Government side to neutralise the Borana move in Saku. This worked well for Yattani who capitalised on it.
He also managed to win over elders and residents worried over the security situation.
During his campaign, Yattani, considered a radical, publicly said the Gabra must join the Government "to be safe".
In Laisamis, the men openly opposed being led by a woman. "If we are attacked at night and bandits take our livestock, can a woman pursue them?" posed an elder at Logologo.
But Lekuton's personality and his development record contributed mostly to his victory.
He had set up a scholarship fund that benefited over 300 secondary and 20 university students, and he assisted women and youth projects.
Meanwhile, Nobel laureate, Prof Wangari Maathai, has blamed cultural barriers for the defeat of women candidates in the by-elections.
"The women are victims of the cultural barriers that are unfavourable to the female gender in Northern Kenya," said Maathai.
She commended Mrs Godana and Mrs Ngoyoni for putting up an impressive performance.
"Those were bold steps that will encourage other women to seek leadership in future," said Maathai.

Original_Search_Title: A Momentous Opportunity That Slipped Away
Headline: Gambia: A Momentous Opportunity That Slipped Away

Original_Search_Title: Lawmakers as accomplices to wrong doing?
Headline: Nigeria: Lawmakers as accomplices to wrong doing?
Body_Text:
Legislators have the first place in the temple of glory..--Voltaire, 1694-1778.
LUCKY Voltaire, he lived in an age and a country, France, where the legislators were decent and honourable people. In Nigeria since 1999, legislators, from the National Assembly to the State Houses of Assembly and the Local Government Councils, must have the first place in the National Hall of Shame which we should consider setting up. If the Executive branches in the three tiers of government have been mostly rapacious and evidently corrupt, it is because the legislators permitted them for personal gain. Nigerian lawmakers at all levels can as a minimum, be called outlaws and in some cases, it might even be possible to call their actions treasonable as I intend to demonstrate. Thus, if the president, the governors and the local government chairmen enter the dock as accused fellows, right next to them as co-conspirators to every charge of grand larceny must be the legislators who constitute the Second Estate of the Realm.
Later, I will come to how the Third and Fourth Estate - the Judiciary and the Media - should join them in the indictment. But first, let us educate ourselves about the constitutional role of the legislature regarding public finances. Only by doing this can we know to what extent our lawbreakers, sorry lawmakers, have contributed to the looting of a lot of the N8 trillion estimated to have passed through the hands of the president, the governors and the local government chairpersons. According to the constitutions of real democracies, instead of banana republics like Nigeria, the legislature has the power of the purse, not the executive. But, in Nigeria, that constitutional provision has been, and is still being subverted at all tiers of government. The executive branch has seized the purse! And the results have been tragic.
