James Madison, 1751-1836, one of the three, including Alexander Hamilton, 1755-1804 and John Jay, 1745-1829, considered "the Fathers of the American Constitution" from which we have borrowed the words but not the spirit, in the Federalist Papers, had set out the principles of separation of powers in a democratic republic such as we are trying to nurture in Nigeria. Unlike America, we are trying to foster democracy but with anti-democratic politicians. But before setting out the roles of each branch of government, Madison had issued a warning which is pertinent to my column today. According to Madison: "The accumulation of powers, legislative, executive, and judiciary, in the same hands, whether of one or a few, or many, and whether hereditary, self-appointed, or elective, may very justly be pronounced the very definition of tyranny."
Take a look around Nigeria today and what we observe is the "very definition of tyranny" staring us in the face. From the Federal Government to the state governments and even local governments, the executive branches have seized all powers. Yet we know, or should know that "Power tends to corrupt, and absolute power tends to corrupt absolutely. Great men are almost always bad men," said Lord Acton, 1834-1902. How we expected to allow several loci of absolute power to develop without the accompanying corruption is a mystery to me. And that is something for future historians to address. Several elective and constitutional coups have taken place right in front of our eyes. Federal powers are concentrated in Aso Rock and that explains why the President could defy a Supreme Court order and not get impeached. No American president can do that and remain in office.
State powers are concentrated in the governors' offices. That explains why the governors could, unless the Federal Government intervenes as in the case of Oyo and Bayelsa, do as they pleased with state funds and get away with their crimes. All the other elected public officials at all levels accused of funds mismanagement did not start by stealing money, they started by subjugating the legislators who actually had the power of the purse and forcing them to surrender them to the Executive branch through bribery and intimidation.
The first brazen act of executive lawlessness could be credited to President Obasanjo and his accomplices with all the PDP members of the National Assembly. Shortly after arriving in the office, President Obasanjo disbursed N10 billion from the Federation Account for what he called Poverty Alleviation Programme, PAP. The PAP money was released on account of what the president called anticipatory approval. In other words, the president illegally and willfully spent the money before seeking legislative approval as he was constitutionally expected to do.
The stage was thus set for robbery of Nigeria by all its elected officials. Fish, we know, starts rotting from the head. The Great National Robbery got underway in 1999 with N10 billion and with the blessing of the National Assembly who became accomplices to the crime after the fact. Now, we must ask questions about N8 trillion. "A stitch in time saves nine" according to the old proverb. We failed to stitch when N10 billion was taken illegally, we will spend the rest of our lives stitching. Next week, we will examine how the state governments also set about capturing their states' purses and progressed to loot them mercilessly.
House No 7 in the Presidential Villa of intrigues
POOR Chief Yomi Edu, he was a former worshipper at the Aso Rock shrine where the "god" they worshipped, and which some still do, demands absolute loyalty to himself while giving none in return. He finally saw the light. In that interview titled: Obasanjo: I tasted his bitter pill, Edu sounded like most others who have become disenchanted with Baba Iyabo. Yet, I have been telling anyone who cared to listen since 1998 when he joined the PDP that OBJ would wreck the party established by the G-34. Yomi only has half my sympathy though. I have no tears to shed for anyone who allowed himself to be hoodwinked by Obasanjo.
I have mounted the pulpit this Sunday, as usual, not to praise the President but to lay on him more indictments. As many of the readers of this page would recall, I had warned the President that if he failed to disclaim the third term agenda, he stands to be disgraced out of office. But because the number one among Aremu's less than admirable traits has been stubbornness, he has ignored all those who counseled him against self destruction, thereby proving, once again, that: "Those whom the gods will destroy, they first make them deaf.....".
Today, his reputation is on slippery slope going furiously downwards. Unfortunately, there are more charges to erase more of the little that is left as this piece intends to demonstrate. Everybody is aware that the president recently publicly made a categorical statement that he never sought the third term. Like most Nigerians, I was skeptical but given the exalted position of the president, it was necessary to give him the benefit of doubt until all reasonable doubts are removed. But, even while making allowances for the high office held by Obasanjo, I was bothered by one irrefutable fact. Shakespeare, 1564-1616, had asserted that: " I hate ingratitude more in a man than lying, vainness.....". Fair enough.
Penultimate week, not only Yomi Edu, but the great AGIP (Any Government In Power ) himself, Professor Jerry Gana, provided some facts which point to the fact that Obasanjo might have added another aspect to his lengthening list of human failings. According to a report in the Daily Independent of Tuesday July 4, 2006, Professor Jerry Gana, another former worshipper at the Aso shrine, made an incredible disclosure to an absolutely stunned audience in Minna. If Gana is to be believed, the president himself insisted on going for a third term and that he (Obasanjo) failed to heed advice that it was a dangerous gambit that could split the country. This is serious. And for now I will tell you only one implication of this revelation.
What both Yomi Edu and Gana have said in plain words amounts to this: President Obasanjo lied to the whole world when he disclaimed any interest in the third term. Perhaps, nobody ever told Uncle Sege about the nature of truth as explained by Emile Zola, 1840-1902. "If you shut up the truth and bury it under the ground, it will but grow and gather to itself such explosive power that the day it bursts through, it will blow up everything in its way." The truth they thought they have buried is rising from the graveyard to torment them. One more former worshipper singing to the media is all that is needed to destroy whatever is left of the co-conspirators.
From now on, Obasanjo and his co-conspirators will have to invent more stories to support the first one. I pity him because this is not the way to repay God's mercies. As the Yorubas say: "Won fi e j'oba, o nwu awure, o ma je Olorun ni? Meaning: You have been made the paramount ruler, yet you keep on performing rituals, do you want to become God?"

Original_Search_Title: I Was Embarrassed By the Arturs Saga - Kituyi
Headline: Kenya: I Was Embarrassed By the Arturs Saga - Kituyi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The gun drama by Artur brothers at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport might ruin Kenya's reputation and upset delicate trade talks with the US, Cabinet minister Mukhisa Kituyi said yesterday.
Dr Kituyi revealed how he flew into JKIA on June 8 upbeat that the US would restart direct flights to Nairobi but left dejected and fearful that the commotion would upset negotiations.
The minister told the Kiruki commission how upset and embarrassed he was to find that "goons and people of dubious origin" had caused havoc at the airport after all the efforts to convince the world that Kenya was not a banana republic.
He told the commission how he walked into the baggage hall just moments after the Armenian brothers had left, and into an agitated public waiting for their guests to arrive.
The Trade and Industry minister was then briefed on what had happened by his aide, sergeant Maxwell Masinde, before he in turn called the CID director, Mr Joseph Kamau "and told him I expected him to get to the bottom of the story", the commissioners heard.
"I told the director that I was angry and embarrassed and that this should not be allowed to continue. I also promised to call him the following day," Dr Kituyi said.
Still agitated, the minister then called his Internal Security counterpart, Mr John Michuki, and narrated to him what had happened, asking him to demonstrate who was in charge of the country's security.
"Mr Michuki said it was the first time he was hearing about the incident and that no one had briefed him. I explained that it would be good for the country as they (the Arturs) could not be tolerated any more. I expected the matter to be dealt with summarily and expeditiously since they were taking a lot of adrenaline for nothing," the minister testified.
Moments later, he called Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua repeating the story and giving her an example of how the Swiss once threw out Eastern European underworld operatives in one swoop without reverting to the courts.
Dr Kituyi - the 46th witness to testify at the inquiry - said he was informing his colleagues and suggesting what should be done because the incident did not fall directly under his docket.
From the minister, the Kiruki commission heard for the first time that the Artur brothers wore bullet-proof vests on the night they caused the commotion at the airport.
Dr Kituyi said that his aide, Mr Masinde, had attempted to rescue a customs officer from the affront by one of the Armenians.
"My aide told me that he went to assist the customs officials and one of the Artur brothers drew a pistol. As my aide went to draw his, he also noticed that the Armenian was wearing a bullet-proof vest," he testified and expressed surprise that the officer who briefed him and Mr Michuki, corporal James Kimuhu, was among those suspended over the saga, but said he had not found out why.
He said Mr Kimuhu was reluctant to talk to Mr Michuki but he ordered him to do so adding that it was strange that the policeman was later suspended.
Dr Kituyi said lawyer Philip Murgor was among the people at airport who were waiting for their visitors when the fracas occurred but did not know whether he had witnessed what had happened.

Original_Search_Title: Mischief is Fast Getting the Better of Kimunya
Headline: Kenya: Mischief is Fast Getting the Better of Kimunya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Events unravel so fast in Kenya that one needs supernatural powers to keep up with them.
In the past few months alone, we have witnessed massive corruption scandals involving very senior people in Government.
Anglo Leasing or more accurately, fleecing, threatened to push aside the Goldenberg scandal as the most brazen act of public theft in our history. Both scandals occupied us for months before the former Permanent Secretary for Ethics, John Githongo, fled in a blazing trail of surreptitious wiretaps and audiotapes to London, England. Githongo's heroic tales kept us busy for one month or so.
For a while, we were engrossed with the nation's forever stalled constitutional review. Before the Government put that on a temporary reprieve, Kenyans battled it out in the historic referendum where bananas and oranges assumed mythical meanings.
The result of that political duel left a thoroughly bloodied and tattered Government. Subsequently, familiar faces that had walked the corridors of power limped away to nurse their injuries on the sidelines as new regiments replaced them.
Suddenly, two ghosts emerged on our landscape and claimed that they were the Arturs. They bragged about their bottomless pockets, glittering jewelry, magical connections to high places in our society, fierce dogs that never barked and a platoon of celebrity women at their beck and call.
It took their commando-style assault at the Jomo Kenyatta International Airport and a bungled "deportation" by the Government before Kenyans saw two parallel inquiries into these international con-men formed. By the look of things, we are yet to experience another breath-taking drama surrounding those low-lives before the curtains fall.
Just as we were trying to recover from the soccer World Cup fever, another spectacular political event was engineered by Raila Amolo Odinga in the form of a biography. The spectacular manner in which Raila Odinga: An Enigma in Kenyan Politics official launch was reported by the print-media literally shoved asunder all the other news. News reports and opinion commentaries plummeted Kenyans with all aspects of Raila's past escapades, as if Raila's life had previously been hidden away from them.
Then Government ministers and MPs scrambled to out do one another with demands that Raila should face the law for treason allegedly committed against former President Moi's government in 1982. Even Moi could not resist the temptation to join in the fray by warning people to be wary of "this man from the lake."
Kenyans have been watching with amusement as lawyers, politicians and ordinary people who cannot say precisely the last time they read a book, argue about "allegations" they know absolutely nothing about.
Because of political expediency, those threatening Raila with prosecution forget that for any prosecution to be sustained, the Government must have credible, reliable, relevant and admissible evidence to prove all the elements of an alleged crime in court. For some mysterious reason, these "hang Raila" clarion calls have conveniently assumed that rumours, hearsay, speculations and innuendoes are enough to convict a man for treason.
But as we were deeply engrossed in the treason threats and counter arguments, other tragic-comedy troupes have also surfaced.
We have seen the chaotic pictures of the circus that was the mayoral elections in Nairobi yesterday. We saw chairs flying, fists landing and people scampering in what should have been a dignified and orderly democratic exercise.
Rather than use their heads, mouths and votes, local politicians we rely on to manage the city turned into vigilantes and thugs. Very troubling was a scene in the front-page of The Standard showing the Nairobi City Inspectorate Director, J. Wambugu, tucking away a pistol he had drawn during the MÃlÈe.
I have always wondered why the Government allows prominent people to bear firearms in public. Perhaps it is my ignorance, but I do not believe that ordinary Kenyans have access to these weapons or that if they do, they can flagrantly draw them at will at the slightest provocation. Do Kenyans believe that bearing firearms deter crime? For if this was true, how come the visible lethal weapons did not prevent the raucous from occurring yesterday?
In many Western countries, excluding the US, only licensed law enforcement personnel are authorised to bear arms in public. This explains the very low homicide rates attributed to firearms in Canada as compared to the US where bearing arms is a constitutional right.
Another thing I have never understood is why we do not conduct direct elections for the mayors of our cities and towns. Most, if not all, western jurisdictions conduct mayoral elections through popular vote. In Canada, for example, a mayor is elected directly by all voters within that particular jurisdiction.
That way, the mayor is directly answerable to the public. Direct voting completely eliminates the bribery of councillors. Had this been the case, I doubt that we would have witnessed the flying chairs and fists.
On a final note, there is an interesting report about Finance minister Amos Kimunya threatening to take over all "independent" bodies like the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights, Kenya Anti-Corruption Commission and the Electoral Commission of Kenya. Intriguingly, Kimunya argues that these bodies were set up by Government to serve Government interests.
He reasons that these organisations can only enjoy their "independence" to the extent that they kow-tow to Government. Kimunya either does not know the meaning of independence, or he is too arrogant to care that the bodies he is miffed at can only serve the public interest by acting as free accountability watch-dogs untethered.
After all, Kimunya and the current Government hawks may need these institutions to keep close watch over the next Government, should Kimunya and his friends find themselves in the cold after next elections. Not too fast, Bwana Kimunya.
The writer is a lawyer based in Toronto, Canada

Original_Search_Title: Pat Utomi's Presidential Bid
Headline: Nigeria: Pat Utomi's Presidential Bid
Body_Text:
Lagos — WHEN the news came through that the Nigerian lawmakers had rejected the constitutional amendment bill, Nigerians across the world rejoiced and jubilated. Many people saw the third-term bid as an attempt to breach the contract between the elected and electors, a violation of the rules of engagement by the promoters.
Even those of us who had identified and defended some of the present administration's accomplishments feared that the third-term agenda risked relegating the key actor to being merely one of those Third World rulers who cling to power and simply refuse to go. Many more of us were anxious for Nigeria. The concern was that, far from being perceived as a growing democracy, Nigeria risked being definitely categorized as a "banana republic" run according to the whim of a small self-serving monetarily wealthy, but morally famished and intellectually lacking, corrupt clique.
With the abortion of the amendment, Nigeria - through its media, its civil society at large and it s lawmakers-has shown that it still has some of the decorum and self-respect necessary for a prosperous, dignified future. Or, as a senior London City-based lawyer put it to me, "This shows there is still hope."
As one might expect, what has followed has been a rush and search for candidates and nominations but the news on these activities has not excited us. Rather, the quest for new leaders in the country has generated nothing but monolithic , mind-numbing petty news unworthy of a great country like Nigeria. The same people, the same party, the same lack of ideas-all are moving the same conflicts towards a pettier and duller Nigeria. Reactions have been understandable apathy, cynicism and skepticism among the people.
The general comment amongst Nigerians all over the world seems to be, "Here they go again No point in trying Only can help us I have given up." All these reactions become even more worrying when even those who (by virtue of their age, achievements and experience) should be automatically politically, or at least socially, engaged are engulfed by apathy.
To all those who care about the future of Nigeria, I bring good tidings. Something exciting has happened again in Nigeria. Pat Utomi has declared his intention to contest next year's presidential polls. Most people reading here will certainly know who Pat Utomi is. For the uninitiated, Utomi is - among many other things - a university professor, founder of the Centre for Values in Leadership, chairman of the former Platinum Bank, director of the Centre for Applied Economics and Lagos Business School, was CEO of Volkswagen Nigeria.
In his declaration to contest for the highest public office in Nigerian politics, Utomi identified the fast-developing economies of the so-called Asian Tigers as his economic models for emulation. We will urge and guide him to look towards the West for models in liberty and democracy, but this will come later. Utomi's main vision for Nigeria can be safely summed up as creating jobs and wealth and to transform the country from a state of underdevelopment to a fast-developing country. To achieve these goals, Pat Utomi has rightly identified natural allies for this project in the youths, organized labour and the middle class.
To this list I will include all men and women of goodwill and quickly add here that, for anybody who fits into these categories, not supporting Utomi's bid can only be put down to omissions due to social pathologies such as political myopia or total blindness to self-interest or pure masochism and proclivity for self-destruction.
Those who know Pat Utomi will describe him as a political and economically sound, cultured, informed, motivational and charismatic leader in tune with the Nigeria of today. This is certainly more true of Utomi than for many other aspiring candidates, and this is good for the many Nigerians who need inspiration and charisma. We have been on the block too long to believe in, and to invest our emotions in, a superhero for Nigeria, we have been disappointed too many times to believe in a political messiah; we are too conscious of the magnitude of Nigeria's problems to rely on charisma.
Our support for Utomi's bid is rational and pragmatic. It goes beyond the man himself. For us, the Utomi is an embodiment of ideas, strategies and tactics that can turn Nigeria into free, prosperous and safe country where people from any background can realistically dream, strive and achieve.
For all those interested in the progress of Nigeria, the advantages of supporting Utomi's bid are many-they are concrete, immediate and measurable. In this forum, the advantages and effects of the Utomi bid will be grouped in two phases.
The first phase is that of the candidacy, and this period will be from now till some time in 2007 just before elections take place. In this period, the main advantages of the Utomi bid will include a raise in the level of the Nigerian political debate, a modernization of the rules of engagement, appearance and intervention of fresh dynamic ideas and people in the political arena. Pat Utom's campaign and canvassing, it is hoped, will be all about programmes, ideas, ideals and policies. Utomi has no recent political baggage to justify or defend. He is not known as anybody's boy or godfather. All we will have to discuss with him will be Nigeria's business.
Citizens and journalists will be asking what plans an eventual Utomi administration has in stock for Nigeria. They will want to know how these plans will be achieved, and the man will be seen giving answers to these questions. It will then become inevitable for other aspiring contestants and candidates to be faced with the sam e questions. Even when they leave home with the intention of flaunting their Babariga, and their wives' and mistresses' headgear, Nigerian citizens and journalists will now expect them - and even force them, if necessary - to think and talk about Nigeria's business in a reasonable and fruitful manner.
Due to the Utomi effect, everybody aspiring to rule Nigeria will have to talk in detail, and clearly, about inflation, unemployment, tax, interest rates, infrastructure, education, investment, GNP, etc. Nigerians are creative people and the political class has a remarkable capacity for adaptation and transformation.
Many will copy Utomi's ideas, get people to prepare speeches aping or reacting to Utomi's initiative. Although these might appear to be personally unfair to Pat Utomi because he will not be able to copyright his ideas, it will be personally unfair to Pat Utomi because he will not be able to copyright his ideas, it will be great for Nigeria.
It will confirm our theory that Utomi's bid is an asset for the whole of Nigeria and, as such, everybody will benefit from it - even those who will not vote for him. In phase one, anybody who has the interests of Nigeria at heart should support Utomi's bid. I am appealing for tactical support here. Regardless of our voting intention or patter, all those who are about the future of Nigeria will have to treasure this asset. We will need to support his bid, make sure it gets the right relevance, and commit and engage ourselves to make sure Utomi is perceived as a credible candidate.
The media and everyone that cares for Nigeria will be useful in making sure that (with all due respect to them) Utomi's bid does not end up like Tunji Braithwaite, M.D. Yusufu or Chris Okotie's attempts. The second phase of Utomi's bid will be fully discussed in a later stage. For now, let us just note that it will start from the voting and last till the post-election period.
In this first phase, my invitation to all men and women who care about the future of Nigeria is to do everything we can to treasure Utomi's bid. We have to make sure that he becomes a prime candidate in the 2007 race. Let us talk about the Utomi bid, write about it, tell people about it, and do everything in our means to make sure that this precious asset we have in our hands is not wasted.

Original_Search_Title: Butter Importer Accuses Rivals of Unfair Competition
Headline: Ethiopia: Butter Importer Accuses Rivals of Unfair Competition
Body_Text:
A Yemeni exporter of vegetable butter, Sheno Lega, and its local partners have taken a business grievance against East Africa Holdings and three individuals to the federal Trade Practice Investigation Commission, claiming that the defendants are engaged in unfair competition.
The exporter, Hayel Saee Anam & Co., and its local partners submitted revised charges to the Commission on July 13.
The Commission was established in 2004 and is presided over by Hareka Haroye, former minister of Justice. The case entered is its 33rd. The Commission has judicial power to hear business disputes, initiate investigations and pass enforceable rulings. It is a government arm created to fight monopolistic market behavior and punish those it finds practicing unfair competition.
It has ruled over 14 cases since it began operation in February 2005.
This latest case involves the importation and distribution of various brands of edible butter: Sheno Lega from Yemen, Sheger Lega from Singapour, Welaita Kibe from Malaysia and Jeldu Kibe from Egypt.
The Yemeni exporter claims that its competitors use Sheno Lega's trademark packaging technique, wrapping the butter with a leaf of false banana, when they import and distribute their competing products. As a result, it claims, they deliberately confused consumers.
Hayel Saee Anam & Co. registered the trademark for Sheno Lega at the Ministry of Trade and Industry in June 2003.
The exporter's local representatives claim that the company has spent one million Birr building brand awareness in the Ethiopian market.
Although the original charge was brought against East Africa Holdings, one of the largest domestic trading houses in the country, and two retailers, Hayel Saee Anam & Co. had to reframe it following a verdict by Commissioner Hareka who decided that those charged in connection with the Welaita Kibe and Jeldu Kibe brands should not only be the retailers, but the importers as well.
In the later charge, the plaintiff requested that it should be initially awarded 600,000 Br until it can calculate the total loss incurred due to alleged unfair competition. It has also appealed to the Commission to suspend the marketing of the competing brands in Ethiopia.
All the brands in question are made from palm tree. Various importers from far eastern and Arab countries imported about 15,000 vegetable butter and oil products in 2004.
The Commission has adjourned the case until August 4, 2006, to allow the defendants time to respond to the allegation.

Original_Search_Title: Khamisi Coast Grouping Now Political Party
Headline: Kenya: Khamisi Coast Grouping Now Political Party
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A lobby group that fights for the welfare of Coast Province residents has been turned into a political party.
The Coast People's Forum (CPF) is now called Kadu (Kenya African Democratic Union)-Asili, and will sponsor candidates in next year's General Election, the chairman, Bahari MP Joe Khamisi, said at the weekend.
The Narc MP said the move was caused by the fact that there was no party at the Coast with a national outlook.
The application for Kadu-Asili's registration had been sent to the registrar-general, he added.
Mr Khamisi said the party was born after consultations through the Coast People's Forum, which champions the cause the residents.
"Forms have been completed and handed over to the registrar of societies," he said. "It's only a matter of days before we have our own party.
Addressing a rally at the Ukunda Liberal Democratic Party office, he said the only way for Coast residents to have a strong political bargain chip was to have their own party.
"None of the existing political parties have interest in fostering development at the Coast," he said. "They only come here during elections to get votes to further political agendas for their regions.
He said the CPF chose to name the party Kadu-Asili (original Kadu) as a sign of respect for its founder, Mr Ronald Ngala, a political luminary at independence.
Kadu was the only other party at independence in 1963, but it later merged with the Kenya African National Union (Kanu). "Even the new Narc Kenya, although it is fronting Coast leaders such as Garsen MP Danson Mungatana, has no agenda for us," Mr Khamisi said.
"All the other parties before it had no agenda for this region, that is today among the least developed despite having massive resources.
He said his position as LDP vice-chairman was meaningless since "LDP and all the other parties have their owners", and urged the residents to reject Narc Kenya and vote out leaders who supported the Banana camp during last year's referendum.
Mr Khamisi who urged them to take voting cards in large numbers, said presidential candidates such as Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga and Gatundu South's Mr Uhuru Kenyatta must declare what plans they have for the Coast people should they form the Government if they expect to receive votes from them.
"This time around, we will interview them and they must put our stake on the table before they receive any vote from this region," he said. "We have said we won't be used any more by others who ascend to power at our expense.
Mr Khamisi said the Coast has about 2.1 million legible voters, but that only 900,000 have voting cards. "Kalenjins have 1.3 million votes," he said. "That is why they can offer Mr William Ruto for the presidency. Why don't you register and vote like them?"
He said Mvita MP Najib Balala's bid for the presidency would flop, arguing that the area has only a small number of voters.
The host, Msambweni LDP secretary Kassim Choka, declared he would vie for the parliamentary seat.

Original_Search_Title: Katatumba's Success a Corner Away
Headline: Uganda: Katatumba's Success a Corner Away
Body_Text:
Success is around the corner. That is an expression many, if not all, people have grown up hearing. A motivation speech is incomplete without it. The pursuit for success itself is a never-ending activity.
But be that as it may, it encapsulates the philosophy that has made Mr Boney M. Katatumba. He shares it with the hope that the youth, whom he thinks need it most, would adopt it and create an even better world. This, however, is dependent on whether that is their agenda.
Katatumba's success is fait accompli. One of Uganda's business magnates, a diplomat of a foreign country (he is the honorary consul of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan), a happy husband of two women and a father of eight successful children, success is familiar terrain therefore making his commentary a worthy one.
His book spanning 214 pages reads more like an autobiography in which he records his life from diaper days to the present ones overflowing with double-breast suits, unceasing flights, diplomatic dos (and don'ts you might add).
Long journey
But it is not his life he wants to let the reader in on but rather it offers a sublime specimen for the subject he undertakes. In these pages is his journey from being a herdsboy to a banana juice seller at nine, to operating a hostel for the Ankole Kingdom Administration, to washing cars, to operating a laundry service, to running a construction company, on to real estate, entrepreneurship and then the pinnacle, at least as of now, being a diplomat.
This reads like the typical rags to riches story. But let's face it; Katatumba's is not. By his own admission, his father was well to do, affording more than the family required.
Katatumba confines this in the background though. Everything he touches turns into gold. Even when he encounters glitches (which instances are very few in the book) he never runs to the opulent father, which would seem a natural thing to do.
While this may not inspire some people, it is important to ask why the son of an opulent man would forfeit guaranteed comfort for hardship. Katatumba's statement is clear; you do not need a godfather to succeed. He carefully avoids the snare of spoon-feeding readers, conveying his nuggets on success by intertwining them with personal experience. He concurs with those before him who have made commentaries on success that you are your greatest hindrance to success.
Polygamy
In-between weaving his experiences and the subject matter, he does not lose out on the opportunity his six decades of existence afford him to add his voice to issues that are not intrinsically related to success. He makes a case for polygamy, attributing it to "our beautiful African culture" without sparing flaks for "the white man's culture", which he labels a carrier of distortion.
He skirts politics with a commentary on the Idi Amin era, which saw him dash into exile. He is also very candid and extends it to the current President Yoweri Museveni with whom he attended school and was rapt by his approach especially to economic matters. About him he says, "But alas, my dear brother might have wrecked it for himself. Instead of going down in history as the greatest leader Uganda has ever had the fortune to have, I am not quite sure now. My brother might have clung to power one day too long..."
Katatumba is conversational in his writing. This, coupled with the layout, makes reading the book easier and enjoyable. However, in an attempt to achieve simplicity, he sacrifices cohesion. His sentences do not follow convention, breaking off haphazardly. You either have to read slowly or twice in order to understand what he is saying. As an autobiography, it is only ankle-deep and you get the feeling it moves at breakneck speed for its own good.

Original_Search_Title: MP Bahati to Educate Poor Children
Headline: Uganda: MP Bahati to Educate Poor Children
Body_Text:
HE is the treasurer general of the National Resistance Movement Parliamentary Caucus and MP for Ndorwa West constituency.
Orphaned at the age of three, David Bahati grew up with hope that he would one day make it to greater heights despite all odds.
"I sold banana leaves for 10 years and slashed Kigezi High School compound to raise school fees after losing both parents," the legislator who defeated NRM's Stephen Bamwanga in the party's primaries and FDC's Alex Akampurira in the general February elections said. Bahati said after the death of his parents, his grandmother took care of him up to Primary Six when she died.
"Life was harder from then," he said.
At that young age, Bahati started a journey to find his destiny. He sold banana leaves in Kabale town to raise money for his primary education at Kitumba Primary School.
He later joined Kigezi High School and during holidays he would remain at school slashing and digging the school compound to raise money for the following term.
"I did a lot of things and when I made a flashback in 1994, I got saved," he says.
During his second term in S.5, the Strommy Foundation under Kigezi Diocese came to his rescue and paid his fees for Senior Six.
After completing S.6 in 1991, he joined Makerere University and did a Bachelors Degree in Commerce.
He taught at Seguku Business College on Entebbe Road for a week and got a job with Diamond Trust Bank and eventually joined the Population Secretariat as Director for Finance and Administration.
"While at the Secretariat and even today, I help many children to get quality education. I don't want to see children suffering like I did," he added.
Bahati said it is because of the passion to help that he decided to join politics.
Aged 33, Bahati is married with two children.
A few years ago, he joined Cardiff University in Wales for a Masters degree in Business Administration.
Bahati has started Ruboona Scholarship Fund to help poor and brilliant children in his constituency. The fund will sponsor two students for 'A' level since Universal Secondary Education only caters for 'O' Level.

Original_Search_Title: Ribadu: Govs Campaign With State Funds
Headline: Nigeria: Ribadu: Govs Campaign With State Funds
Body_Text:
Abuja — Chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), Mallam Nuhu Ribadu, has accused some governors campaigning for the nation's presidency of using their states' allocations to pursue their personal aspirations.
Ribadu, who spoke yesterday at the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) National Summit on the Challenges of Transition in 2007, said any individual who presented himself for elections should note that he was exposing himself to scrutiny and that the EFCC would ensure he was scrutinised.
He denied an allegation that the EFCC had been selective in carrying out its activities, stressing that those that accused the commission of being selective were the guilty ones.
"It is wicked that a state governor took out N150 billion out of the N250 billion meant for his state for himself. There are governors who take their state's money abroad, buy houses for themselves and stash others away for their comfort and that of their family. Is that fair? Should we allow such to continue? And when you go after them they say you are sectional. When we were going after 419, bank officials, government officials, etc, we were not selective, selective my foot!
"If you are doing this job, you have to forget your life. Nobody should arrogate to himself/herself that he/she is better than others, we are all human beings. If you have been given opportunity to serve and you fail then you should bear the consequences of your failure.
"Leadership is important, it determines what happens in a country. We have had our fair share of bad leadership and our country has been reduced to a Tokunbo country where everything is dumped here. We have had to occupy the number one position as the most corrupt country and you say we should not change that? I don't think it is right.
"Take the Niger Delta issue, for instance, the people are reacting because they have been neglected despite the fact that their states have been collecting allocations. We have it on record every local government chairman in the Niger Delta region has at least N200 million in his account and the same goes for the governors and yet the people are impoverished. Why will they not be angry?", he asked.
The EFCC boss emphasised that Nigeria can only have credible election come 2007 if corrupt politicians were prevented from contesting.
"Politicians have all the money to build an army of thugs, they have the money to rig the elections, bribe the electoral officers, buy votes from the electorates, etc. Why will the electorate not sell their votes when they are hungry? When they have no shelter over their heads. We will never have free and fair elections if we have corrupt person as candidate because they have the money to subvert the process.
"It is time for us to go after corrupt officials. Enough talk. We have to act now. We know where the problem is but we need the courage and fear of God to clean them out. These same people campaigning were the ones that piled up N30 billion debts for us. Now they have seen that it has been cleared and we now have N30 billion in our foreign reserve, they want to come and throw a party with it. We will never let them, they will never touch it. Government reforms might not be perfect but we are getting there.
"Nigeria is denied the means of e-commerce, we cannot shop on the Internet, we can not use credit card because of the activities of these corrupt individuals in government or outside government. We have now cleaned the system they want to come back and rule us. What moral rights do we have to prosecute the 419 when these individuals are walking free? Unless we address these, we cannot have free and fair elections. Until we can prove that those we are entrusting our monies to are worthy we will continue to be slaves in our country.
"If we don't fight now, how can you present good candidates to contest? Honest Nigerians will never win elections because it's all about spending money they have ensured it is so. We will continue to take the heat, we will continue to take the abuse it does not matter but good people in Nigeria must stand up and say enough is enough. We might not be doing it perfectly but we can tell you we are doing it correctly", he said.
The Senate President, Ken Nnamani, in his remark said what was critical in the Nigerian polity was for the process to be right.
He said: "What is critical is the process and not necessarily the outcome in a democracy. What matters most is the process which you go through before arriving at the answer. If you just write the answer people are going to question how you arrived at the answer.
"I have an example of what happened in the United States between President Bush and Al Gore. Most Americans believe Al Gore won the elections as far as Florida goes but because he went through the process in a democratic procedure, what I call democratic due process. He went all the way to the Supreme Court and the Supreme Court gave the verdict and the Americans reluctantly accepted it because it went through all the processes.
"That is what I mean when I say the process in a democracy is more important than the outcome. So a gathering of this nature and the topic is central to our survival as a nation. It is important it is well articulated because if we move properly and handover to the proper individuals to mange our economy, to continue the continuity, continuity here has to do with the political reforms, structural reforms and economic reforms. That one is so much needed that it should continue. I see the light at the end of the tunnel, anybody who has traveled to the Asian tiger countries will notice that Nigeria is on the right course."
On the issue of corruption, the Senate President stressed corruption did not add to the economy but rather it took away its potentials for development.
He said: "So a man like Nuru Ribadu is a man that we should pray for that God should give us more Ribadus. If we must make real economic and social progress, the way we transit from one government to another is very critical. If we conduct free and fair elections, credible elections, chances are Nigerians and the world will believe us but if we manipulate, that is the reason why we have so many social unrests. If we conduct the elections properly and we know that the man whom we have elected is the one governing us, we will do everything possible to make him succeed."
"Without being immodest, the Senate of the Federal Republic of Nigeria is a recent example, I stood for an election and I was elected by my colleagues, and because I was elected (and) not appointed, the concept of banana peels is not there because all Senators are consciously looking at the banana peels (and) any one who sees it will remove it because the leadership was elected by them and that is why I enjoy incredible support from them.
Also in his remarks, the Chairman of the occasion and President, Court of Appeal, Justice Umar Farouk Abdullahi lamented that the Nigerian judiciary has battle an embarrassing situation where election petitions arising from the 2003 elections were still being addressed.
He said as at March 2006, the breakdown of the election petitions shows that a total of 519 petitions were received but additional retrial of cases scaled it up to 570.
He said the number of appeals decided were 568 and that a total of 360 appeal were filed while 345 have been decided remaining while 15 appeals are still pending as at today.
He however advocated that political parties should be disciplined and abide by the provisions of the constitution and that the Electoral Act should contain a provision to allow court to intervene in any case of breach of political parties constitution.
NLC President, Comrade Adams Oshiomhole, also said at the Summit that to ensure post-2007, notwithstanding the party in power and the social character of the actors, the challenges of peaceful and issues-based, not ethnic-driven, electioneering between now and the election period must be pursued, that of organising credible, transparent, free and fair elections such that all actors (INEC, Police, Judiciary, governments at all levels) discharge their statutory functions effectively and impartially and that all parties enjoy level-playing field and the will of the electorate determines the outcome of the elections at the end of the day.
He said the reforms that must be sustained irrespective of the party in power come post 2007 are: the anti-corruption agenda, the return to due process of the procurement of goods and services, transparency and fiscal responsibility in public and corporate finance, especially guaranteeing public access to information about revenue accruing to various tiers of government and commitment to a living wage based on free collective bargaining to encourage productivity.
Other reforms, according to him, are financial sector reform especially eradicating the foreign exchange black market and non-proliferation of mushroom, parasitic banks which do not add value to the economy and which endanger depositors fund, banks consolidation and sustained high growth rate, build external reserves with focus on saving for future generation, funded pension scheme, eradicating the debt overhang and some elements of public sector reforms as monetisation to reduce wastages and renewed emphasis on service delivery through the servicom.

Original_Search_Title: Military Incursion Wasted Many Generations
Headline: Nigeria: Military Incursion Wasted Many Generations
Body_Text:
The Chinua Achebe Foundation Interview Series
Disclaimer: The views expressed in the interview are not necessarily those of the Chinua Achebe Foundation. The Chinua Achebe Foundation, an intellectual and cultural organisation, believes in the right of every Nigerian to express their opinion.
Continues from Yesterday
Mrs. Oyibo Odinamadu, an icon of politics, civil rights and the women's movement, made a name for herself as a leader of various women's organizations and as a public servant in Nigeria. She was especially active in the founding of the National Council for Women Societies (NCWS) and was president of the Eastern Nigeria council from 1958 until she joined active partisan politics in 1978. She was the First National Vice-President of the Unity Party of Nigeria, and contested as the Deputy Gubernatorial candidate for the party in Anambra State. Educated at Lincoln University, Jefferson City, Missouri and at Columbia University, New York, Mrs. Odinamadu worked in the government of Eastern Nigeria, and retired voluntarily from the public service in 1971. Mrs. Odinamadu talked to Obi Nwakanma in Raleigh, North Carolina, where she was visiting.
About Obi Nwakanma
Obi Nwakanma was educated at the Government College Umuahia, and studied at the University of Jos and at Washington University in St. Louis. He has completed work on the biography of the poet, Christopher Okigbo. Obi Nwakanma also won the ANA/CADBURY prize for his collection of poems, The Roped Urn in 1996. He has worked as a journalist in Nigeria as Group Literary Editor of the Vanguard and correspondent for Newsweek and for the Nue Zurcher Zeitung. He continues to write a weekly column, "The Orbit" in the Sunday Vanguard. Obi Nwakanma currently teaches Literature of the Black Diaspora at the Saint Louis University, St. Louis, Missouri.
I asked earlier about the men you worked with. What was Ojukwu like as an administrator; how do you rate Ukpabi Asika?
Well, Ojukwu loved Ndigbo, and loved Biafra. And he was very clear about what he wanted for Biafra. He was clear about the fact that the treatment the Easterners received in Nigeria was immensely wrong, and that something should have been done about it. But nobody was listening. As an administrator he was good. I think he was trying to carry out what he felt the people wanted, what was good for the people. But Ojukwu was misled in certain ways by his advisers. Some of his advisers were not as good and as honest as they should have been.
They misappropriated money; all the money that was being raised and handed over to them did not come to Biafra, or was even employed for Biafra. For instance, Akanu Ibiam worked like a jackass to collect money from the World Council of Churches and other such organizations, and he'd come and hand over that money to people like Mojekwu and others. But the money never all arrived where it should have.
That really hurt him very much. Even here in the US, people who collected money in the name of Biafra my brother, Dr. Akwuba, for instance, I asked him about it. He said that, at that time, he had donated a car and there was much fighting over this car; Mbadiwe and others fought about who would drive the car, and eventually he took back his car, because they couldn't reach an agreement. So sad The man I mentioned earlier that I had met in Zurich, Switzerland, one Dr. Ndukwe, simply pocketed whatever was collected. He spoke their language. So, Ojukwu you see people thought that towards the end, that he should maybe have accepted the confederation rather than insist on secession. And Sir Louis (Mbanefo) worked hard on the secession move, and then to get the terms for confederation in 1968. In the last minute, the Biafran delegation left before the people who were resolving the issue could come up with a resolution. So we lost all that, and anyone in that position I don't frankl y know whether he should be blamed for not taking the counsel of his advisers.
But the question of what has become of Nigeria must certainly bother you at this stage of your life.
A lot.
In what ways?
One could say that Nigeria has just gone to the dogs. Military intervention I blame the military for all the ills of Nigeria. If there had not been that coup d'etat which forced a break, I don't think that Nigeria would have degenerated so much. The military came in with their dictatorial methods, their brutish methods. They came in with their kill-and-go impulses, without much of any training most of them. They were not disciplined, and certainly not a nationalist army. They came in acting like government resources were actually war booty; that everyone could take as much as he wanted. They were not polite, did not know anything about government, they just came and thought that everything was to be taken by brute force, as in the field. It was when they came in that all this money started to flow about in Nigeria. Those who had never had much access to money, didn't know how to manage it, saw this, and went mad. They became common thieves by just grabbing the resources and w ealth of the people. In Igbo we call it "nkita tala okpukpu anya wa la nya n'olu" the dog that ate the bone in its care. No morality.
When you see all these ex-military rulers and those who collaborated with them talking about nationhood, about governance, what is your reaction to them?
They are not good people. They are not honest people. They are unpatriotic. They don't love the country. They do not love their people. They are products of group psychosis. They see how things are going well in other countries; yet, they don't want the same for Nigeria.
What do you think about Obasanjo's government, his economic policies since he came in 1998; especially given the context within which it is said he negotiated our debt relief?
But does he really have any policies? Look, Obasanjo is just as bad as the worst of them. The fact is that he is negotiating with thieves, with terrorists, with people who destroyed the country, stole billions and billions. How can you negotiate with such people?
But what are his alternatives?
His alternatives are to demand an account from those who had ruined the country. Tell them to return all they've stolen, and the people will be behind him. Right now, the people are not with him, because they don't understand what he is doing. It appears he's one with the Abacha family bring out so much and keep the rest. Why should they keep anything? Or this Chris Uba; why should he have supported Chris Uba's activities in Anambra, be linked in anyway to the man responsible for the incredible destruction that occurred in that state? I mean Nigeria is completely broken, and that is quite sad. Of course, Nigeria did not begin to crack today. It started earlier, even with Obasanjo's first administration, when he finished up the tenure of Murtala Muhammed. People thought he was honest, because he had said he wouldn't stay one day longer. He held elections, and handed power. But even then, Awo would tell you, that Obasanjo called him and told him not to contest the election.
He told Awolowo not to contest the election?
Yes...
Why?
Chief Awolowo told us that General Obasanjo invited him to Dodan Barracks in 1978, and warned him to stay out of politics or seek candidacy as President. That he would not win, if he tried. Awo said he shouldn't tell him that, because he was a Nigerian, and that he would stand for election if he wanted, and if his party put him up. Then he told Obasanjo that he thought it was quite audacious of him to try to tell him a full fledged citizen not to participate in partisan politics. He asked why he thought he would not win if he tried to run for president; and was told that he would make sure that he did not win because of his stance against the Military.
This was all because, when Chief Awolowo was asked by the mass media how he would handle the Nigerian Military if he became President, he said that he would probe them. It was Obasanjo who alerted the Military to the reality that Chief Awolowo would carry out his plan if he was allowed to win the election; and that they should guard against it. It was he who proceeded to organize the rigging of Nigerian elections against Chief Awolowo in 1979 which was carried over to 1983 in favour of the Northern oligarchy by awarding the victories to Shehu Shagari's ticket. Akinjide's Arithmetic nullified the possibility of a run-off. It was not until after the death of Chief Awolowo in 1987, when it was much too late that everybody, even some Northerners and Ndi Igbo started confessing that the direction and affairs of the Nigerian Government would have been much different and much more meaningful had Chief Awolowo been allowed to win any of the Presidential Elections.
Why did the PPA fail? Here seemed a chance in 1983 to create a progressive alliance and a progressive government. What happened? You were involved in those negotiations.
I was, indeed. I was involved in getting in papa Awo to attend the meeting, and for Zik to attend the meeting, as well as Waziri Ibrahim Aminu Kano was still alive at the time. It was Jim Nwobodo who said that Zik had no powers when he was Governor-General, and even when he was president; that this time he was going to get power and all that. And I said, if you give Zik all the power that fills this world, he's not going to do anything. He wouldn't want to stand and fight. I said to them, what you're leading Nigeria into now is what you'd all regret. Leave Zik alone. Let Awo lead the government, because the people are ready to follow him. But Jim Nwobodo refused.
Does it seem peculiar to you that most Igbo people did not understand Awolowo.
Well, they had some grouse against him. He was the Federal Commissioner of Finance in Gowon's government, and was instrumental in the Twenty pounds policy, and all that. But I had this to say: you people are not living on twenty pounds. Look ahead, you know, forget. Ndi Igbo si rapu mfe fele efe, k'anyi sobe nwem n'ewe ife (The Igbo say, leave what is past to the past, and follow what is new and profitable). Let's look forward, in the politics of Nigeria, in the condition of Ndigbo and Southern solidarity, and what the Northerners are doing to us. If we don't pass this bridge now, we're all going to drown in this water.
And you were confident that Awolowo was the one to accomplish this?
I believed that he was the only one who could do it at the material time. He had the program, he had the temerity, he had the strength, and Zik would not be able to do it. He was not only too old, he had gone too soft. Apart from making speeches and so on, Zik was no longer prepared to do any work, or make any sacrifices. Every time, he would just walk away and deny the great things he had done. Once, at the Nnamdi Azkiwe University, somebody in fact stood up to say that Zik in the later stages did not rise up to the strength he had shown in the anti-colonial struggle, to give Ndigbo a direction especially after the war, and that he had disappointed the Igbo. His wife was there, and got up to defend Zik; but, I told her: "You'd better leave this thing alone (laughs); yes, that Ndigbo knew Zik long before she knew him.
From what you know about Obafemi Awolowo, what would he do today, were he to be present?
About what is going on? I'm sure he'd want to opt out of the presidential system. Chief Awolowo and the Unity Party of Nigeria had a program for the whole country the Four Cardinal Program which covered Free, Universal and Compulsory Education up to University level; Free National Health Care; Economic Advancement; Social Security and Pension for the Elderly; etc. If he had taken charge of the Government, the resources of Nigeria would not have been scattered and stolen by the Military Administrators and their operatives, and they would have been applied to the development of the country. Even though the Military Administrators tried to alienate and implement the Four Cardinal Program, and even pull some of the UPN operatives into the Military Government, they could not do accomplish that. They failed woefully, because they did not have the philosophy, mind, spirit and direction of Chief Awolowo.
So, your experiences as a public servant and politician finally came together; what, at this stage, are your greatest regrets about Nigeria?
(Pauses) My greatest regret about Nigeria is that the military ever came into the governance of Nigeria. Secondly, that even the military was not honest with Nigerians and with themselves, and this is what ruined everything. Thirdly, the rulers of Nigeria have made themselves puppets of foreign governments. I don't believe they are acting in their right minds; that what is happening in Nigeria is actually what they want for Nigeria. I don't believe that. And then let's talk of the wasted generations. Wole Soyinka talked about a wasted generation. But I tell you there is not only one, but several wasted generations in Nigeria who have now turned armed robbers, thieves and drug couriers. Young men and women with good training are graduating from schools with no jobs, no opportunities to translate ideas into work, for self-determination, and self improvement.
Governments fail, because they are unable to provide jobs. Even American governments have failed, because they were unable to create jobs. Even with the Bush government, despite talk about so many thousands of jobs here and there, the people still say it's not enough; that they are exporting some of their jobs overseas!
But it does not seem to bother Nigerian governments that there are no jobs, no opportunities for employment or for self-improvement for young people coming out of Universities, not to talk about secondary schools. There are so many universities now, and yet they say Obasanjo is involved in building a private university! Building a university for what; who is going to attend that University, and when they are trained, after graduation, what are they going to do for Nigeria and for themselves? That's why most people leave the country and stay away. Our people come to the US and stay, working at jobs that are not exactly, or even below what they were trained for and hoped to do. But they are making a living.
So, what is to be done? What can we do as a people to reverse the condition?
My own suggestion is that the military serving military, retired military and its cohorts should never govern Nigeria again! They should all leave Nigerian government alone.
How is this enforced?
By not supporting them. When they go into what they call elections which are really no elections they don't hold elections in Nigeria; what they do is intimidate people, buy people up, distribute money and rig elections, and they pronounce whatever result they like. In the 1999 elections, my son was one of the returning officers in a polling station. He said he was there, and just about the time the polling station was closing, and they were carrying the polling box to go and count, Jim Nwobodo showed up with his thugs. Some were dressed like soldiers, with boxes of ballots that were already stuffed. They took away the ones at the polling station, and sent everyone running.
Were the votes ever counted?
We were just told a number and had to be content with that. So what we have in Nigeria is no government; it's no democracy where there is no election. My son-in-law was just telling you the story of a fellow who used a jeep to carry around naira notes. Our naira is now more useless than the leaves falling off the tree outside. And when you give an elderly woman a bundle of N50, 000, would she be thinking about democracy? I don't know where we are going to start or stop. I understand that Babangida is getting ready to contest elections. He has no business coming out ever again to govern Nigeria. What for? Perhaps, because he really sees Nigeria as a Banana Republic? I don't know. Whoever put Nigeria in this position should really, I don't know what America can do about it. But it has been gaining from it.
Must we depend on America to do it?
Well, since they are the ones telling Obasanjo and the others what to do, they should call them to order.
You've had quite distinguished private and public lives; how much support would you say your husband gave you in your professional and political career, and what kind of person was he?
He was a very nice man, very, very gentle. Very supportive, very accommodating; he never one day opposed my duties, either in my professional career or in my political activities. In fact, we went into the Unity Party of Nigeria together. My husband was very, very supportive. Even in my voluntary activities, when I became tired, he would say: "You'd better go, they are waiting for you!" (laughs). And all my trips abroad oh my God, I made trips abroad, attending conferences here and there; this delegation to the Queen of England, and that delegation. He never one day showed disapproval or disagreement. When I was not at home, he minded the family. We were both in the civil service; he did his job, and I did my own. No conflict, whatsoever.
For instance, when he was presidential system in this country, gives too much power to the individual at the top, always a dangerous thing. And this has become too expensive, in more ways than one, for a corrupt place like Nigeria.
Thank you very much, Mrs. Odinamadu.

Original_Search_Title: Country Considers Fruity Import Ban
Headline: Nigeria: Country Considers Fruity Import Ban
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerian President Olusegun Obasanjo has said that his country was considering banning fruit imports in an effort to boost the local industry.
The ban would look at increasing local production of "tropical fruits, particularly pineapples, bananas, mangoes, tomatoes, papaya and avocado," said Obasanjo.
The president said that Nigeria could be self-sufficient in tropical fruit production, and that the ban would increase exports too as the production picked up.
However, the president did not factor in the possibility of neighbouring countries issuing protectionist policies in response to Nigeria's ban. This could unnecessarily raise the cost of living in Nigeria. The other consideration was that the ban would leave local producers with no incentive to be efficient.
In 2002, Obasanjo slapped a 120 percent import tax on rice to promote local production. While figures suggest that imports dropped by 50 percent after the tax was introduced, importers suggest rather that the drop was simply the result of a large amount of rice being smuggled into the country to avoid the tax.
The ban on fruits would follow a ban placed on the importation of packaged fruit juices in 2003. The ban resulted in a boom in the local industry.

Original_Search_Title: It's Time for Serious Politics, Kibaki Told
Headline: Kenya: It's Time for Serious Politics, Kibaki Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki was yesterday told to start high profile politics if he hopes to retain the presidency next year.
But politicians from Bungoma assured Kibaki they would still vote for him during next year's elections as they did during last year's referendum.
Bumula MP, Sylvester Wakoli, told the President he must now commence the same high profile politics as those competing for the presidency. Politics is about perceptions and hope, Wakoli told Kibaki, and Kenyans were forming an impression of a politically reluctant Head of State. "Politics is about perceptions and hope. Change the scenario now and put strategists to talk for you if you cannot talk for yourself," he said in Bungoma, yesterday.
The MP said Kibaki is doing very well on the economic front and on matters of education. "But you must do politics (sic) to survive," added Wakoli.
Wakoli spoke at the burial of the late Martin Wanyonyi Kandawala at Mabanga village, Bungoma, where Kibaki witnessed a rare burial ceremony.
The late Kandawala is the immediate younger brother to Cabinet minister Musikari Kombo. He died in a road accident along the Webuye-Eldoret road two weeks ago.
The Balunda and Bafumi clans of the Bukusu bury their dead in a sitting position, so that the dead can "continue supervising the activities of the family rather than in a lying position, which signifies defeat".
When Kibaki addressed the mourners, he steered off politics saying he had come to show his solidarity with the family of the deceased.
It was not however lost to the mourners, local leaders and political observers that Kibaki's attendance at the burial was his own way of thanking people from the area who overwhelmingly voted for the Banana side during the constitutional review referendum last November.
When Kombo addressed the mourners, he told the President that Bungoma people would continue supporting him.
"Bungoma people will remain with you as a friend and work with you for the sake of the country," said Kombo.

Original_Search_Title: Mbabazi, Hyuha Defend Positions
Headline: Uganda: Mbabazi, Hyuha Defend Positions
Body_Text:
Kampala — NRM Secretary General Amama Mbabazi and his deputy Dorothy Hyuha were appointed minister for security and minister without portfolio respectively so they could focus on reorganising and consolidating the party ahead of the 2011 elections.
"The idea that I am the minister for security and she is the minister without portfolio is by design. We have worked together for sometime now. Our job will be to start the process of re-organising the party so that come 2011, the NRM will be home and dry. Those of you who did not know me and those of you who have only heard about me should be prepared. I will be there," Mbabazi said.
He was speaking as chief guest on Saturday at celebrations to thank President Museveni for appointing Hyuha as a minister. Mbabazi, once the defence minister, said the party would start preparing for the 2011 elections shortly after village council elections next month.
"Once the LC1 elections are over, we shall immediately embark on our mobilisation programme for the 2011 elections," Mbabazi said.
Ministers Dr. Stephen Malinga, Daudi Migereko, Gabriel Opio, Dr. Emmanuel Otalla, Jennifer Namuyangu, David Wakikona, Dr. Beatrice Wabudeya, Mrs. Lydia Wanyoto Mutende of the East African Legislative Assembly and more than 20 MPs and former MPs and officials from the NRM party headquarters attended the ceremony. Banyole cultural leader Dawson Muloki bestowed honour on Hyuha, giving her a spear and shield as symbols of power. "God gave us Dorothy and Emmanuel Dombo as special gifts," Muloki said.
Mbabazi said of Hyuha's positions, "You are yet to see the best."
He said Hyuha was the second highest-ranking woman leader in the NRM after deputy speaker Rebecca Kadaga. "Dorothy is one of the most powerful persons in Uganda. The ministry without portfolio is the most powerful. To be successful, you must be able to discharge the responsibilities of all other ministries. The President assigns you duties anywhere," Mbabazi said.
Hyuha attributed her success to her up-bringing as an orphan. The first minister from Butaleja, Hyuha was also among the first graduates from the district.
She said she sold ripe bananas at Budumba railway station as a child.

Original_Search_Title: Glamour At Virgin Fashion Show At Makerere
Headline: Uganda: Glamour At Virgin Fashion Show At Makerere
Body_Text:
Kampala — The timing could have been wrong owing to the World Cup fever but the evening was a worthy one especially because the show was organised, with a receptive crowd and above all, the remarkable innovation by the university students.
We are talking about the maiden Virgin Show exhibition that was hosted at the Margaret Trowel School of Industrial and Fine art at Makerere University.
The theme of the exhibition was Contemporary yet Cultural and the creative wear that was on display said it all. The works were a combination of the students and their lecturers.
The show kicked off two hours later than scheduled. Some of the night's highlights comprised a showcase of creative, beach and evening wear as well as performances from Priscilla Kalibala.
State Minister for Gender, Rukia Isanga represented the Gender Minister, Syda Bbumba. The students modelled as they showcased a variety of designs drawing cheers from their peers and guests as the emcee gave descriptions of different creations, designers and the models.
The designs brought out the relevance of the theme with the fusion of both African and modern materials like backcloth, banana fibre, calabash as well as cotton.
Meanwhile, the crowd was kept informed on the progress of the match.
Kalibala who was apparently the only musical act at the exhibition lit up the house with performances of her songs Mwana Gwe, Gumikiriza and Yonno that attracted a round of applause from the crowd.
The show went on till approximately 11p.m.

Original_Search_Title: System Failure in Kenya Government?
Headline: Kenya: System Failure in Kenya Government?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — DESPAIR AT THE MANNER IN WHICH THE Kenya government is conducting some of its public affairs is growing as revelations of fumbling, ineptitude or, worse, criminal negligence on matters touching on the national interest emerge.
While many of the errors of commission and omission committed by the Kibaki administration when it assumed office in 2003 could have been excused as part of the learning curve, this is no longer acceptable.
Each new revelation points at the frightening prospect of real systemic failure in the government. Such failings include the humiliation inflicted on the nation by two shadowy foreigners, allegedly from Armenia, who managed to circumvent all security checks in a manner unprecedented in post-independence Kenya.
The very first day of the government-appointed tribunal inquiring into their activities saw Kenyans shocked to learn that the police had been tipped off by Interpol as early as April that the two were travelling on stolen passports. That no action was taken until their deportation three weeks ago is a grave indictment of Kenya's internal security apparatus.
As if this were not bad enough, it has emerged that the government was warned of the imminent collapse of Uchumi Supermarkets as far back as 2004, only to move in after it declared itself insolvent last month. We must also cite the incredibly unprofessional manner in which matters involving a local bank that is alleged to have been involved in money laundering and tax evasion have been handled.
The government only intervened after the matter surfaced in Parliament. An inescapable conclusion is that there is a high-level failure of governance systems. Kenya is not a banana republic. And the sooner those in government accept this, the better.

Original_Search_Title: So How Much is the Presidential Jet?
Headline: Nigeria: So How Much is the Presidential Jet?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Let me recollect a story. About 20 years ago, Dele Giwa was killed via a parcel bomb. Who killed him? Of course, we don't know-officially speaking-up till today. But we have our own suspicions and conclusions because of the events leading to his death.
Chief Gani Fawehinmi, his lawyer, decided to pursue the matter legally, seeking the trial of two of the nation's intelligence chiefs because of too much co-incidence. Bizarrely, the security chiefs sued Fawehinmi for libel (for accusing them of murder) and instantly got a judgement awarding them N6 million or so in damages. Welcome to the judicial system of Nigeria.
The arraignment of two journalists-Gbenga Aruleba of the African Independent Television and Rotimi Durojaiye of the Daily Independent newspapers-for "sedition" by the federal government has again brought this issue back to my mind. I am really not interested if sedition still exits in our laws or not. I believe the lawyers will argue that far better than I can do. Their offence is that one of them reported that the newly purchased presidential jet may be "brand new second hand" after all while the quoted price of $79 million may be an exaggeration taken too far. That is "sedition", according to the federal government. Is it "sedition" against the aircraft or against Boeing or against the pilot? Or is it "sedition" against the president to query the age and cost of the aircraft which almost caught fire on a trip abroad while in its "infancy"? Again, that is a matter for the lawyers.
What I am really interested in is this: how much did it really cost to procure this aircraft? Let the government tell us the actual price, and then we can go on to verify from Boeing. How old is the aircraft? Let us know so we can verify. That is the best way to decide if the journalists are telling us the truth or not. It is very cheap to arrest journalists, detain them beyond the constitutionally approved 24 hours without trial and then charge them with sedition. This government prides itself in transparency in order to hoodwink the highly gullible Western donors. When it comes to the crunch, it is always found wanting. To the best of my knowledge, this aircraft was "smuggled" into Nigeria. There was no official announcement from this transparent government. In the United Kingdom, the prime minister is planning to buy a jet (sarcastically nicknamed "Blair Force One" by the British media in reference to American president's Air Force One). It has been the subject of interest since 2005. How much will it cost the tax payers? What are the features? When will it be delivered?
That, to me, is transparency. The Nigerian government plays games with transparency. Its own definition of transparency is to publish council allocations in newspapers in order to paint state governors as thieves. While I have no serious problems with that, those who come to equity will do well to come with clean hands. Transparency shouldn't be at your own convenience; it should be for the benefit of Nigeria. The country is bigger than any individual. A government that buys an aircraft without informing us of the vital details shouldn't turn around to make scapegoats of journalists who are only trying to fill in the gap. If the information is available in the first place, there would be no need for speculations. And I have not even concluded that they made wild speculations. I am only working based on a worst case scenario. I tend to believe most of the things that I hear about this government because of some scandalous things that I know about it myself.
Take, for example, one of the new airlines in town. Who really owns it? How was it able to acquire those vital, invaluable assets of ours without Nigerians raising an eyebrow? Are we so laid back in Nigeria now that we don't ask questions anymore? Could it be true that the airline is owned by THAT jet-loving governor? Or, to put it more directly, does the airline belong to the governor or is it a joint venture partnership between him and his almighty mentor? Or is the governor only fronting for his mentor? Why then was Governor Orji Kalu's airline stopped from doing business in Nigeria? I insist that there is no justification for any sitting governor running businesses by the side, but what is banana for the monkey should also be banana for the chimpanzee. And if the rumours surrounding this "beloved" airline are true (I hope they are false), then the time has come for us as a nation to march to Sani Abacha's grave in Kano and duly apologise for demonising him all this while.
We can now see that nobody has monopoly of greed and corruption.
To compound the situation, the Minister of Injustice, Chief Bayo Ojo, caused us international embarrassment recently with his explanation for the removal of Mr. Bukhari Bello as Executive Secretary of National Human Rights Commission (NHRC). According to media reports, Bello's "sack" letter from the minister accused him of anti-government activities, namely criticising Guatanamo Bay and denouncing the arrest of "seditious" journalists. The minister has come out with a clever denial and an explanation: Bello was not sacked; he was only redeployed. That is, I did not kill the deceased with a pistol, I used a knife! Such a clever clarification. When Ojo visited THISDAY last year, we asked him why the federal government only released part of Lagos council's withheld funds instead of the entire allocations. He said: "It is to avoid inflation. If everything is released at once, it will affect the economy." Almost one year after, Obasanjo is still running away from inflation.
Ojo is comfortably presiding over injustice and he does not feel bothered. People go into government and say they want to make a difference, but when they get there they become something else. Ojo is now "ministering" the trial of journalists for asking questions over the presidential jet. Shouldn't we know how much the aircraft cost our national treasury? We have not even asked "silly" questions like: who handled the transactions? Was there any kick-back involved? If yes, how much was the kick-back? What was the revenue sharing formula? Did it go round?
I am aware that these questions are "seditious". They are capable of causing hatred against the presidential jet. So help me God.
The 2007 Presidency
The hostage-taking issue dominated a discussion I had with a friend last week. He is an Ibibio from the South-south. "Our geo-political zone has to produce the next president," he ordered. "If the North insists on having it, then the MASSOB guys should keep fighting and the Niger Delta boys should continue taking hostages. Everybody has a right to be president of Nigeria."
