By the way, my wife is half-Ibibio, so I automatically had to fall in line with his argument. "There are over 30 parties in Nigeria," I told him. "The South-south should give its votes to the party that fields a credible candidate from the oil-producing region. This is how it will work: any South-south candidate with the right national political connections should be supported by the region. The candidate doesn't have to be in the PDP, because PDP is not the only party that can produce the president." Of course, I don't believe in zoning or stuffs like that, but I always enjoy discussing "our turn" matters.
It's my favourite pastime.
I was, however, disgusted to read in THISDAY yesterday that some "Nigerians Abroad" had written to the European Union to make a case for a Southern president in Nigeria. What's that all about? Is it EU that will choose our president for us? The general perception in the West is that we are incapable of ruling ourselves, that we are a bunch of incompetent people who spend more time stealing credit cards and executing 419 tasks. The view of Africa in Europe is so pathetic, so dehumanising. We are seen as asylum seekers, money launderers, backward people and everything imaginably negative. What are "Nigerians Abroad" trying to achieve by writing to the EU? Is it that the EU should issue a proclamation to the effect that Nigerians must elect a Southerner as president next year? Why do we keep ridiculing ourselves in front of those who enjoy ridiculing us?
Equally ridiculous is the report that a Northern governor, one of the many candidates reportedly anointed by President Olusegun Obasanjo for presidency in 2007 (I wonder how many presidents we will have), was planning to launch his presidential ambition in the UK. We are a laughing stock in Europe. Our public officers will steal money and come to buy mansions here. They laugh at us. As if that is not bad enough, a governor who wants to be Nigeria's president is coming to the UK to launch his campaign. No wonder white people believe Africans have the lowest IQ on planet earth. Even though we know that this is not true, they are justified to cite instances like this to back their claim. Can you imagine Hilary Clinton or Gordon Brown choosing to come to Abuja to launch their campaigns? The most sickening aspect is that scarce state resources that should be channelled towards development will be spent on this senseless jamboree. My heart keeps bleeding for Nigeria.
I'm sure Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, the new Minister of Foreign Affairs, will have her hands full in her new portfolio. One of the issues she has decided to address is the way Nigerians are treated-mal-treated, to be exact-at foreign embassies in Nigeria. This issue really needs urgent attention, but it happens mostly because we have ridiculed ourselves. Unfortunately, innocent Nigerians are sharing in the humiliation. The conduct of our public officers and their retinue of aides has not helped our ratings abroad in any way. Any airport you are in the world, you can always tell when you see one or two Nigerians. It is only Nigerians that I see repacking their bags at check-in counters because of excess luggage. The extravagance and disgraceful conduct of many Nigerians are embarrassing. Thanks to the democratisation of corruption, everybody is now travelling abroad on the ticket of oil money. I'm sure a large percentage of oil revenue has gone into funding foreign trips for gov ernment officials in the last seven years. Everybody wants to travel abroad-which is not a bad idea, only that it is funded with public money that could have been put into productive use for the sake of development. Why on earth would a governor choose London as the venue of his declaration?

Original_Search_Title: Employment and Growth As Main Targets in New Budget
Headline: Mauritius: Budget - Employment and Growth As Main Targets
Body_Text:
Port Louis — The first budget of the new Government was awaited with much apprehension and anxiety for a number of reasons. It came at a time when all the indicators are in the red and the sick patient is almost waiting for drastic surgery. It came at a time when we are still dazed by the triple shocks leaving little room for manoeuvre. It came after a time when certain popular decisions that did not necessarily have a sound economic rationale were taken but which did have an impact on the national budget. It came after a long time of atypical silence on the part of the Minister of Finance.
This budget puts an end to the period of procrastination when we were left to conjecture in what direction would the ship be steered. Finally, the ice is broken and we see a strong will to take "timely and bold" measures so that the economy remains on course for recovery; admittedly, this will be a slow and long process. This strong signal was lacking in the past and the budget has filled in this gap.
The right priorities
This budget attempts to mark the beginning of a new era. It has a new focus, an entirely novel approach and a strong economic rationale. It calls for a dramatic shift in mindset to meet the daunting challenges the country is facing. Perhaps we had made our opinion prematurely in a fatalistic manner that every thing would be the same and the status quo will always prevail. But the budget has come to belie that notion. It wants to inculcate a culture of discipline in Mauritians, a discipline in the management of public finances, a discipline in public service and a discipline in the private sector as well.
This is a very tall order and only time will tell whether it will succeed. It has many facets and undertones; not all of them are explicit. There are more in it than meet the eyes, either favourable or unfavourable. Many of the measures still remain to be well defined. But this article will tackle one particular aspect of these measures, namely, the implications of the budget for employment and growth.
The budget identifies the right priorities for the country. There is a major preoccupation for the creation of jobs which pervades the entire budget speech. It is repeatedly summed up in these words: create jobs, promote employment and return to high growth. The Government is obviously concerned with unemployment which has reached 9.5 per cent. Nobody will deny that unemployment is high although I have always maintained that the figure is on the higher side because there is a high degree of underemployment.
It recognizes our dismal economic performance compared to Southern Africa Development Community and Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa. The entire African continent has a growth of 5 per cent in 2005 which provides food for thought as to the appropriateness of the business environment in Mauritius. If we compare our performance with Asia, we are in a worse predicament. Asia grew by 7.8 per cent. We are trailing behind when many countries are making big leap forward.
The priorities are right for sustainable development and the constraints well-diagnosed paving the way for targeted measures and action. Low investment, low savings, high unemployment, colossal balance of trade deficit, balance of payments deficit, huge budget deficit and excessive public debt are surely signs of an ailing economy.
The situation is akin to the late seventies which culminated in two devaluations. In fact, this is precisely what the Minister of Finance wants to avoid although the rupee has been consistently depreciating over the past two decades. Of course, the budget refers to numerous difficulties on the external front but it recognizes the need to put our own house in order and many of the causes for our current situation is endogenous.
To start with, it calls for action to improve the investment climate, to instil confidence in business and to encourage initiatives. The business environment has deteriorated over the years because policies had not been adapted to meet the mutations taking place locally and internationally.
There had been a high degree of complacency as if the world would be waiting for us. The budget goes at length to improve this environment for business which is important for creation of jobs, promotion of employment and economic growth. Some of the measures are very harsh but it is a signal that economic realities are also harsh. They require a new mindset and, above all, sheer hard work.
Never has a budget in the past referred so much to the need for openness, to adapt to global competitiveness, to integrate the global economy, to productivity, efficiency, competitiveness and flexibility. These are brutal realities we have to face. The waves of globalisation have swept our shores and it is important to flow with the current and not to be tossed around in the ocean. "No man is an island," said Alexander Pope and we are not shielded from international developments.
Mauritius has to learn this lesson since we are heavily dependent on the world economy. The bitter truth is that we have not kept pace with developments on the world economy. Over the years, we have lost our market share. We are still in the grips of the triple shocks, namely, decline in sugar prices, erosion of trade preferences and rise in energy prices.
For small countries there is no option but to become fully integrated into the global economy. From an exporter of bananas and coffee two decades ago, Costa Rica has been transformed into an exporter of computer chips and is dependent on tourism. Costa Rica's President Sanchez said: "Only if we open up our economies we will be capable of attracting direct investment flows that will make up for our own chronically low domestic saving rate and benefiting from the most advanced technology and know-how that, in the end, will help our own employers. Only if we open up can we develop dynamic productive sectors, capable of competing internationally. But, above all, only if we open up will we create sufficient high quality employment for our youth."
Towards a business friendly environment
The budget attempts to create a business friendly environment. An enabling environment is a prerequisite for investment, employment and economic growth. Some of the conditions for an enabling environment already exist in Mauritius while we have to make significant improvement on others. The budget focuses on some of them which are crucial at this stage of our development and which require immediate attention. We will only highlight a few of them here.
Four ways in which the budget wants to create a business friendly environment is by increasing investment, undertaking labour reform and eliminating bureaucracy and red tapism and investing in training. It is well known that our investment ratio has been on the decline, reaching an alarming rate of 21 per cent in 2005, the lowest rate in two decades.
Diverse ways are explored to boost investment and attract foreign investment. It calls upon Mauritians abroad to invest in the country and attempts to attract foreigners as well. The amount is kept relatively low to attract funds and to democratize the economy. However, an important consideration for such investment lies in our income tax system and here the attractiveness is mitigated.
While the objective is to attract know-how, technology and funds, it takes cognizance of the obstacles on the way and announces measures to facilitate residence and work permits and the granting of visa. The second aspect of this strategy applies to the long list of permits and licenses required for business and takes the bull by the horn. We consider the cumbersome procedures as the biggest obstacles that discourage entrepreneurship, initiative and enterprise creation. This requires a new mindset in Ministries, parastatal bodies, municipal councils and district councils. Perhaps this is the biggest challenge of this budget.
As mentioned earlier, we operate in a globally competitive environment. If our product markets are open, our labour market cannot be closed. This is the missing link in the entire reform process that has taken place over the past two decades. An open economy needs an open labour market. On this score, the budget rightly sets out one of its priorities to render the labour market flexible.
Our actual labour legislation is not compatible to the needs of the country with the multiplicity of institutions determining wage increases without due consideration to productivity and performance, when increases are determined by all yardsticks except economic ones. The performance of these institutions has contributed to an inflationary wage policy that has been detrimental to our exports, employment, investment and economic growth and ultimately to workers themselves. Our soaring labour costs have made the Mauritian industry uncompetitive. The wage system is a major cause for our rising unemployment.
A high degree of government intervention in industrial relations has led to wage drift and more dangerously out-priced our products on world markets. Collective bargaining should be the cornerstone of our industrial relations system. The National Wage Council will no doubt help to promote tripartism and social dialogue and ensure a more rational and structured wage policy taking into account productivity which is the main factor to ensure sustainable wage increases and higher real income.
There was a dire need and urgency to instil a degree of flexibility in the system to encourage investment and by extension employment. This has been the experience of many countries. In an article in the Economist of 17-23 June 2006 on Organisation for Economic Co-Operation and Development (OECD) countries, it is stated that: "Several countries have tried to ungum labour markets by deregulating them, by trimming marginal tax rates and by making benefits less generous. Broadly, these policies have raised employment."
This is concrete evidence that flexibility is the route for the creation of new productive jobs, for the generation of income, for combating poverty and improving the lot of vulnerable groups. There are no short cuts for sustainable development. "The cornerstone to create employment must be flexibility in employment regulations." In the past, too much lip service has been paid and policies have been harmful to those they were intended to protect.
The budget also aims at investing massively in training and reskilling, training of entrepreneurs, the unemployed, redundant workers, vulnerable groups, training of apprentices. It will encourage both on the job training and formal training. In this respect, it is worth mentioning that we should not throw the baby with the bath water. Institutions that have been involved in training and have a good record should be empowered to do more training.
We should not reinvent the wheel and lose the experience and expertise of institutions like the Industrial and Vocational Training Board and the Ministry of Education and Human Resources that have already effectively managed skills development programmes.
In terms of training, it is important that there should be complementarity among institutions rather than duplication. The budget advocates efficiency and best utilization of resources. Training should also be complementary between public and private sectors using the expertise, experience and comparative advantage of each sector. The training of consultants to advise entrepreneurs is a good idea but in a world of business and entrepreneurs what is required is practical guidance and support.
The country has been successful in establishing four pillars namely, textiles, sugar, tourism and financial services. The budget calls for further diversification with the emergence of new pillars and many areas have been identified with progress achieved already in some of them. Small and medium enterprises (SME), information and communication technologies, knowledge hub, medical hub, seafood hub, pharmaceutical cluster have been identified. The potential is there but has to be exploited.
The budget has a mission to double per capita income in 10 years. It is an ambitious target but attainable. This presupposes high growth although it is not quantified. One thing is sure that with 3.5 per cent growth we wont reach there. We also have to create 50,000 jobs. We need to have appropriate policies and actions to achieve this goal. The budget shows that there is a will to effect the right reforms. It will be presumptuous to assume that with the budget everything will change immediately. The platform has been set. Much depends on implementation. We hope that action will follow. The taste of the pudding is in the eating.

Original_Search_Title: Don't Expect Constitution Change Soon, Says Muturi
Headline: Kenya: Don't Expect Constitution Change Soon, Says Muturi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A member said that Kenyans would not go to the polls next year with a new constitution.
Mr Justin Muturi (Siakago, Kanu) said he had studied the Budget and did not see any funds allocated for a referendum.
He said there was no way the country would get a new constitution before a referendum was carried out.
Last year, the Government spent Sh4 billion on the November 21 referendum, which was rejected by voters, forcing the authorities to go back to the drawing board.
Two public vote created two camps - the Yes (Banana) and the No (Orange).
The Banana group was led by the Constitutional Affairs minister at the time, Mr Kiraitu Murungi, while Mr Raila Odinga and Mr Kalonzo Musyoka of LDP as well Kanu's Uhuru Kenyatta and Mr William Ruto spearheaded the Orange camp.
The campaigns split the country into the two groups.
The President has since reshuffled the Cabinet and sacked all ministers and assistant ministers who had identified themselves with the No camp.
The Government then appointed a committee of "eminent persons", led by former Foreign Affairs permanent secretary Bethuel Kiplagat, to receive views from the public on the way forward.
The Kiplagat team compiled a report for the President, which was released to the public last month and tabled in Parliament.
It is due for debate soon.

Original_Search_Title: When I Scared the 'Police' Away
Headline: Uganda: When I Scared the 'Police' Away
Body_Text:
When I was living in Nairobi during the 1980s, I came to learn that the police in Kenya had a motto that assumed the servicemen were patriotic to the level of being on standby to serve everybody. Their motto was 'utumishi kwa wote' - service to all.
Moreover, I used to have respect for policemen (and women), always smart in their uniform with a businesslike look as they went about their work. Unlike some of my friends who had a brush with the law, for all the time I lived in that country, I never experienced those people's wrath for any reason.
Going by this experience, or the lack of it, I had a very liberal view of our own Uganda police servicemen when I returned home...for is it not universally acknowledged that Ugandans have legendary warmth of the heart?
One evening, I picked a friend who was living in a flat on Platinum House in the city for an outing and dropped her back just after 11p.m. after driving through Kiyembe lane from the Nakasero Central market side.
When I saw two policemen skulking in the shadows, I read nothing more in the action than taking cover since they were on guard duty.
However, when I let my friend out of the car and I was pulling away, they approached me and one of them, equipped with a Kalashnikov rifle duly informed me I had breached the law!
Astounded and fearful of the consequences of contradicting the Penal Code Act, I turned off the engine and listened, first to my thundering heartbeat, then to the man when I had regained my breath.
As if he had not scared me enough already, he opened the co-driver's door and sat himself besides me (with a gun in one hand), and ordered me to drive to the Central Police Station as he closed the door.
As we crawled along Burton Street, he produced a 'charge sheet' from which he read a litany of the possible charges I would face the following day after spending the night as a 'state guest', in the cells.
"You are suspected of being idle and disorderly," he said as he looked at cycle styled stapled papers and read with the aid of the roof light he had asked me to turn on.
"You might be a vagrant or a vagabond, if you are not a hooligan," he said dramatically.
"You are suspected of looking as if you have an inclination for harbouring criminal tendencies. You are also suspected of acting in a manner that is likely to suggest you have intentions of not only contemplating a breach of peace, but also of being a danger to yourself," he concluded and paused like a seasoned poet.
It was upon this acting that it occurred to me that either a game was on, or the fellow was a criminal with intent to kidnap me and I told him as much.
At this moment, we were about to cross Kampala Road, not far from our destination.
When we were in the middle of the road, I suddenly stopped, turned off the engine, pulled out the ignition key, opened the door and made as if I was running to the police station.
To my amazement, the fellow not only got out of the car, but also took off in the direction we had come from and thus saved, I reclaimed my car and sped off homewards to Bakuli.
As I reflected on the events of the evening, scarcely believing I had survived a seasoned conman, I drew parallels of the litany of accusation with that Ex-President Canaan Banana, now deceased, had faced not long before.
Banana accused
The old man, Comrade Canaan Sodindo Banana, the first president of independent Zimbabwe, was a former professor of Theology and a diplomat.
But this did not stop his former aide, a policeman, Mr Jefta Dube, from making public allegations of sexual exploits against him.
The Dube-inspired charge sheet had the alternative accusations of sodomy, attempted sodomy, sex against the order of nature, sexual assault, having carnal knowledge of a man, and having the carnal knowledge of a man against his will.
Other charges were sexual abuse, indecent assault and unnatural sexual conduct, illegal sex and offering unsolicited sex to a man, rape and attempted rape of a man.
A deeply traumatised Dube was later sentenced to a 10-year jail term for murdering a policeman who allegedly derogatively called him "Banana's wife". His plea in mitigation that he had been repeatedly raped by Bwana Banana did not stop court from finding him guilty of murder.
Other accusations were to the effect that the President, at State House, had attempted sodomy and indecent assault on bodyguards, gardeners and cooks employed otherwise at the head of State's official residence, to look after his welfare.
Conviction
According to the records of this 17-day trial in June 1998, witnesses described how Mr Banana had invited them into his library where they played cards, had drinks and listened to music.
The trial included lurid testimony of how he drank, danced and played cards with his bodyguard before drugging and raping him on a carpet at State House in the 1980s.
The president then insisted on teaching the men to dance, where upon he groped and propositioned them. Chief Justice Anthony Gubbay said, Banana used "his immense superiority of status to beat down the resistance of a young and inexperienced" police bodyguard.
He was convicted of 11 counts of sodomy and abusing his power to sexually assault and carry out "unnatural acts" with men, most of whom were on his presidential staff. He was sentenced to 10 years in prison, although nine were suspended, on condition that he paid compensation to his victims.
The former president appealed against the conviction but in May 2000, Zimbabwe's supreme court upheld his conviction for sodomy and jailed him for a year. Banana insisted that he was not a homosexual and that he had been framed!
Rammed in the back
It was not long after this that I got another brush with the police not far from the spot where I suffered the first experience.
On another night, I had stopped and parked just after the Luwum Street and Burton Street junction, in the direction of Namirembe when a drunken woman driving a Carina came hurtling down Luwum Street.
She rammed into the back of my pick-up truck, lost all her lights, radiator grill and suffered a mangled bonnet.
When I got out to investigate, I was pleased that the iron guard on my pick-up had protected it from damage. She however accused me damaging her car. My response was to remind her she had had more than an evening she could accommodate and I drove away.
More than three weeks later, my vehicle was arrested with the driver I had engaged and towed to the Central Police Station in the City. On presenting myself there, I was informed of a 'hit-and-run' offence of which I pleaded ignorant.
The alternative offences included reckless driving, inconsiderate use of a motor vehicle, and inconsiderate use of the road, driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, driving an unlicensed motor vehicle, driving without a valid driving license, driving a vehicle in a dangerous mechanical condition (DMC).
The Police Officer, the complainant and I closely inspected the vehicle to establish whether it had recently been involved in an accident or undergone panel beating and found no evidence.
There was however some small anomaly in that on the evening of the ram-in, its road license had expired, so I had to compensate the drunk for the loss she had suffered.
When the Police Officer insisted on charging me with driving a motor vehicle which had no valid road license, I relented and paid but told the woman there would be occasion for us to meet under altered circumstances.
Three years later, we indeed met. She was haggard, I inquired about her welfare, to be told she had actually lost her business along Luwum Street through the many fraudulent associations she was party to. What goes around comes around, doesn't it?

Original_Search_Title: Ousted Fabcos Chief Will Not Stand Down
Headline: South Africa: Ousted Fabcos Chief Will Not Stand Down
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — TENSION inside the deeply divided Foundation for African Business and Consumer Services (Fabcos) escalated yesterday as a defiant Sam Buthelezi refused to stand down as president after being suspended at the weekend.
Fabcos was started in 1988, and even though it has signed up about 3000 small black busi-nesses as members, it is facing the worst credibility crisis in its history as infighting escalates.
The crisis came to a head at a meeting in Durban on Saturday when a majority of Fabcos' nine provincial arms suspended Buthelezi as president over allegations of poor governance, including financial impropriety. But yesterday a defiant Buthelezi refused to go, saying: "I still regard myself as president.
"They had no authority for what they did. You don't oust a president like that, even in a banana republic," he said.
He said the Fabcos constitution set out a process for holding special meetings and procedures, which were not followed in this case. Buthelezi has called for a general meeting of Fabcos membership because, he says, he still has strong support on the ground.
Pre-empting the result of Saturday's meeting, Buthelezi fired off a letter to the provincial chairmen last week, saying that because he had not "arranged and sanctioned" any meeting it would be considered "unconstitutional", and any resolutions would be "invalid".
Buthelezi claims he is being targeted by a "a conspiracy of certain Fabcos members seeking to get their hands on the organisation's assets", which include Premier Foods.
Lesole Mogorosi, who chairs the Free State chapter of Fabcos, said Fabcos' constitution did allow for the secretary-general to call Saturday's meeting.
"I think (Buthelezi) does not believe it now, but the fact is he has been suspended," he said.
Mogorosi said Buthelezi was asked to attend the meeting to explain the allegations against him "because we are responsible to our membership". Buthelezi denies getting an invitation.
Forensic investigators will be appointed within seven days to investigate the claims. Their report is expected to be finalised within three weeks.

Original_Search_Title: Canada in Awe of Uganda's Art
Headline: Uganda: Canada in Awe of Uganda's Art
Body_Text:
Kampala — GAZING at crafts made in Uganda is as good as the best adventure nature can provide, says Peter, a Cambodian social worker. As his hand cuddles a curvaceous sculpture of a woman carrying a baby, it is evident this is his favourite.
The stout man cranes his neck above several delegates and his eyes glow as they look at the items. He is among the thousands attending the world urban forum conference in Vancouver, Canada.
Peter is among those struck by Ugandan products, a country many have never heard of, but have fallen for its products.
"I have not heard of woorganda (sic) but these products are beautiful . now I have what to tell people back home," says Ting Pan, a Chinese student as she lifts a sculpture of a wood-carved gorilla.
The Ugandan stall is making heads turn. "We are doing well our stand is the most busy especially in the evenings as the delegates are retiring to their hotels," Ann Aling the Ugandan selling the products says.
Gorgeous sculptures, footwear made from local material and crafts depicting life in Uganda are being bought in dozens by people from all over the globe.
Maureen O'Neill, President of the International Development Research Centre in Ottawa, says such items are very competitive on the international market and could fetch a lot of money, before buying crafts from the stand.
Uganda's wildlife is well-documented here. The gorillas, the graceful antelope with its trademark horns and the crested crane embodied on bark-cloth are some of the items being sold. They are the work of gifted craftswomen determined to achieve their dreams.
The crafts cost between $2 and $100 and are made from durable materials like banana fibre, bark-cloth and wood.
Aling's dress is attracting compliments. She is draped in a blue kitenge with a matching head gear. One delegate said, "Her dress code stood out. I would love to dress like that; it's so beautiful,"
The goods depict Uganda's culture. From the women in traditional wear, wildlife with sculptures of the gorillas to casual shirts embodied with the colours of the national flag.
You would also get a feel the simplicity of life in Uganda. One elderly Argentinean bought a dozen of table mats made from dry banana leaves. Another lady picked two bead necklaces and requested me to make a choice for her. Apparently she found the two necklaces beautiful and when I made the choice, she still paid for both!
All the products are made by women, under the umbrella of the Uganda Women Entrepreneurs Association Limited. "These are grassroots women who have come together to eke a living," says Aling.
Other stands arealsorun by women. The exhibition also showcased severalinnovations in sustainable urban development and housing.

Original_Search_Title: Artist's Dream Finally Comes True
Headline: Kenya: Artist's Dream Finally Comes True
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Best known as Mbatia Njoroge Joseph back home in Waithaka, where he got his start in graphic arts - drawing cartoons of cars, buses, and boxing bouts - he's now become a world-renowned artist simply known as Bertiers.
The bastardisation of his Kikuyu name came quite recently, as has his lightning-flash ascension into international acclaim.
Mbatia a.k.a Bertiers is not only the first prize winner of the recent Kenya Contemporary Artist Competition held in May at the Alliance Francaise, but has also won the top prize at the just ended 7th Dak'Art Biennial of Contemporary Art in Senegal where Kenyan artists, including Richard Kimathi, Ingrid Mwangi, and Jak Katarikawe, only began presenting their work the other day.
During the Dakar competition, Bertiers was not even in town to receive his award. He had to hear the news second-hand from local art critic Kimani wa Wanjiru, who covered the Biennial, courtesy of UNESCO.
Bertiers could not be there because he was about to take off on a two week whirlwind tour of Europe: the prize accorded him as a result of his winning the contemporary Kenyan artist competition.
Sponsored by Alliance Francaise and the Goethe Institut, Bertiers was on his way first to Germany then to France. With only one week in each country, Bertiers' hosts wasted no time.
When he was not attending exhibitions where his work was on display, in Heidelburg and outside Paris, he was being escorted around sundry museums, galleries, and historic monuments to European culture, such as the Louvre, according to Goethe Institut's Barbara Reich.
Everywhere lauded as the esteemed Bertiers, the 43-year-old Kenyan artist is not done with the Europeans. In fact, after completing a two-week workshop in Dar es Salaam, where he is currently the guest of the Rafiki Arts Collective, he will again head back to France. This time he will be taking up a three-month artist-in-residency in Marseilles, the prize for his winning the Dak'Art.
Speaking to Review just hours before departing for Dar es Salaam, Bertiers (who will always be Mbatia to me) expressed sheer delight for his new-found fame. Yet he admits, art has always brought him happiness, even when he has seemed to pay a heavy prize for pursuing the one thing that gives him satisfaction in life.
"I grew up being ridiculed by my family and some of my teachers for not being a serious student, since I was always drawing rather than reading my school books," he says.
Growing up near Dagoretti Corner, smack in the middle of his mother's seven children, Bertiers was often punished by his big brothers as well his mother.
"But however much they abused me, I couldn't be stopped from doing my art," he says.
Saying he was saved more than once by art teachers who provided him with pencils and papers with which to draw, he somehow passed his CPE and even finished secondary school (O levels) to the amazement of his mother.
"I would have been kicked out of school once if mother hadn't pleaded with the headmistress to forgive me."
Yet, even though he was always an eccentric, Bertiers was also popular among school mates. They were often tickled by his uncanny skill for seeing the satiric and witty aspects of everyday life and capturing them in his art.
"I love making fun out of current events," says Bertiers who was initially inspired by news reports featured on local radio.
Never satisfied with simply copying the cartoons he saw in the daily papers, he also drew inspiration from Western cartoons and the local cartoon art he saw in bars, butcheries and tea rooms around Dagoretti Corner.
Bertiers has also been keen to embrace action in his art. "I loved drawing Muhammed Ali boxing contenders like George Foreman," he says.
That knack for capturing action, emotion and ironic elements of current events is clearly evident in his award-winning 12-foot metal sculpture currently on display in the backyard of the Alliance Francaise.
Referendum Boys features four, short, scrappy acrobats, doing backbends, stretches, and handstands on one another. Made out of scrap metal and painted in KANU colours, Bertiers' satiric poke at current Kenyan politics and politicians is transparent.
He has even got a banana and an orange dangling from one acrobat's key chain.
"They are all meant to be small men with big mouths, making promises they never keep," he says. His work was judged as the best produced by Kenyans in the past year.
Trained in a wide range of artistic and graphic techniques at the YMCA Craft Training Centre in Shauro Moyo, Bertiers admits his training was meant to qualify and equip him to be a commercial and not a fine artist.
"I worked professionally for a while with Chibuku, making signs and graphic designs all over the Kenyan countryside."
Then he met an American art dealer named Ernie Wolfe from Los Angeles who saw some of his "bar art" and wanted to be his dealer in the United States.
"Ernie paid me Sh300 per drawing," he recalls.
"We worked out a deal where I would get art materials from his friend, Martin Dunford of the Carnivore, then paint and mail the work to him in LA."
Paid peanuts, he was nonetheless content to supply the American art market, oblivious of the large profits his friend was making off his art.
"I only stopped that arrangement after Ernie suspended the contract," he says. The timing too was right, he says since he moved on to new ventures after work for Ernie came to an end.
He began making his way around Nairobi and started taking part in local group exhibitions. The rest, as they say, is history. And while 2006 is his red-letter year, one gets the feeling that whatever he is called, and wherever he paints, Bertiers at heart will always be Mbatia, the man from Dagoretti Corner, who gets a kick making fun of politicians and public affairs.
However acclaimed, however world-travelled, one can't help feeling that Mbatia Bertiers will always take pride in being a Kenyan, first and foremost.

Original_Search_Title: Lawyers Were Paid Sh72m, Says AG
Headline: Kenya: Lawyers Were Paid Sh72m, Says AG
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Attorney-General Amos Wako stunned Parliament on Thursday with confirmation that the Government paid six lawyers Sh72 million to act for it in two referendum cases last year.
There was a storm in the House after Wako revealed that the firm given the initial brief, which then went ahead to seek other lawyers to partner with it, was linked to the then Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi.
By virtue of his Cabinet portfolio Kiraitu was among the captains of the Banana campaign during the referendum in which the Government was handed a heavy defeat. Wako described the payment as peanuts, especially when looked at against the huge amount of money pumped into the constitutional review by the Government, and which was now at stake.
He argued that the Government was facing the prospect of seeing Sh10 billion it had spent on the review going down the drain if the referendum was stopped.
Advocates Remuneration Order
The six lawyers who acted for the Government in the case were Mr Fred Ngatia, Mr Waweru Gatonye, Ms Lucy Kambuni, Mr Njoroge Regeru, Mr Kioko Kilukumi and Dr Gibson Kamau Kuria, who once ran a legal firm with Kiraitu.
The advocates, led by Ngatia, represented the Attorney-General and the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC) in three cases seeking to stop the referendum. The first case pitted Bahari MP Joe Khamisi and 21 others against the AG and the CKRC. The two other cases were filed by the Yellow Movement, while the third was lodged by activist Paddy Onyango.
Lawyers James Orengo, Mutula Kilonzo, Pheroze Nowrojee and Amena Amendi represented the other parties.
On Thursday, Wako told the House that had the lawyers not followed the Advocates Remuneration Order, they would have asked for up to Sh150m.
Members accused the Government of single-sourcing the legal services and giving those who were "politically-correct" the lucrative jobs.
Wako, who was answering a question by Rongo MP Ochillo Ayacko, said the money was shared among the six lawyers.
Barrage of points of order
Ayacko had wanted to know the criteria used in picking the lawyers, how much they were paid and what other public bodies they acted for.
Khwisero MP Julius Arunga wondered whether the AG was satisfied that the money paid to the lawyers was commensurate to the job done. Wako said the Solicitor-General identified the lawyers after he requested him to do so. He, however, declined to state other public bodies the lawyers acted for, saying it was a matter between the lawyers and the retaining parties.
Ayacko tabled a letter allegedly written by Kuria asking the Solicitor-General to pay Sh12m a few days before he was officially appointed to represent the Government in the case.
The MP also tabled another letter allegedly written by the Solicitor-General claiming he had been instructed by the AG and Kiraitu to appoint Kuria's firm to represent the Government.
The question prompted a barrage of points of order, with MPs from both sides of the House expressing dismay at the Government's extravagance.

Original_Search_Title: New Brand of Coffee On Market
Headline: Uganda: New Brand of Coffee On Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — A new brand of coffee, Elgonia One Café, is now being produced in Uganda, targeting the export market.
The coffee packs bare tags with the 'Gifted By Nature' slogan that was launched to market Uganda abroad last year.
"It has been received well in Europe. We have so far received orders from restaurants and hotel chains in Denmark and Sweden," said Mr Lars Bendix, the Chairman of the Elgonia One Café Board.
The coffee, which has a unique blend of Arabica coffee, is packed by women at Elgonia Industries in Tororo and was exhibited by Uganda at the Ethnic Foods Show that run from June 14-15 in France.
The London-based Food Processing Awards for Packaging of the year nominated the brand.
The coffee is packed in small coffee bags, (à la tea bags) and a drip cup, to allow for easy brewing of the coffee and to enhance packaging. This is wrapped in an air tight plastic bag and then wrapped in banana fibre sac.
"If Uganda is going to promote her exports, it should be from a branding point of view. Gifted by Nature is strongly connected to Uganda, it is a Ugandan quality stamp," Bendix says. One Café is first being produced for export with the aim of finding a niche in the world market. However, local buyers can access it through the factory.
"The brand is the first of it's kind, we have no competitors, there is no one producing ground coffee in coffee bags and with this kind of packaging," Bendix said.
Currently, an estimated 18,000 packs are made everyday. Bendix said Elgonia One Café is also an initiative to promote gender equality, ensure environmental sustainability and pursuing global partnership for development, which are all UN Millennium Development Goals. "We decided to have UN Millennium Campaign 2015 as a focus to support some of the goals, which promote our values. We are doing this mainly because we want to help and make the world a better place," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Sikota Explains Hichilema's Role
Headline: Zambia: Sikota Explains Hichilema's Role
Body_Text:
Lusaka — UPND acting president Sakwiba Sikota yesterday said Hakainde Hichilema participated in the party affairs from the background.
And Sikota said there was need to address the issue of leadership in the United Democratic Alliance (UDA).
Featuring on MISA-Zambia's Face the Media programme on Radio Phoenix, Sikota said UPND presidential aspirant Hichilema had been funding the party like many other persons.
"There have been times when he has donated to the party. I am one of the people who went round to lobby funds from donors and that's when I met him," Sikota said.
He said Hichilema was among the many people who thought that what the late Anderson Mazoka was building was some
thing worthy investing in. "He has been a contributor to the party like hundreds of others. As to whether he was coming to sleep in the trenches with us in the battlefront, he and many others opted to remain in the background but everybody decides in a way they should participate. We used to sleep in villages on the floors, travelled around the country and moved in banana boats but during this period he was never there. We were getting donations from him like many others, it wasn't anything extra-ordinary," Sikota said. When asked how true it was that Hichilema chartered an air ambulance to evacuate Mazoka to South Africa when he fell ill, Sikota answered: "Yes, since it was an emergency, but the insurance company ultimately did reimburse. Mr Mazoka was a very prudent person, he had an insurance cover. But that's the privacy of the Mazokas and we need to respect it." Asked whether it was true that Hichilema paved way for him to contest the Livingstone parliamentary seat in 20 01, Sikota said: "Nobody brokered anything, it was an extra demand from the people of Livingstone themselves." Asked whether he would go for the UPND presidency, Sikota responded: "The position I am going to take and the announcement I will make tomorrow (today) will impress the majority of the Zambian people. This made me to go into deep reflection and deep prayer. I have come out of the period of reflection and prayer, that's why tomorrow I will come out and tell the nation what I have seen. Those who know me know that I am not an excited person. I don't easily get excited." Sikota said the fact that people have become interested in leading UPND was evidence that what Mazoka built was something worth it. "It shows that many people price UPND, that's why people are showing interest. What Mr. Mazoka and others built for eight years is attracting people, some people thought we won't go far," he said. Sikota said there had been an overwhelming and enthusiastic call for him to contest the UPND presidency. "I have had offers of campaign materials and vehicles, T-shirts and posters, that's the pressure I have been talking about from all over the country and offers of assistance even from across the country," Sikota said.
Sikota said he was succeeding as acting party president. "The unity among our leaders is beginning to show. I have been able to calm the rocky waters in UPND. Those rocky storms which were there are no longer there," Sikota said. "I don't see any scramble for power in UPND. I see a democratic process; we haven't stopped anybody from contesting. We are having an orderly contest."
Sikota said UPND never had a quota system.
"It's interesting to note that a lot of people that are talking about the quota system were not even there when we were forming the party. They were in government and some of them were in opposition," Sikota said. "Probably I will be the first person who was there when we were forming the party who would talk about it. There has been no such a thing as quota system. After Mr Mazoka's death, that's when some people who were not there when we were forming the party tried to mislead the people."
Sikota appealed to UPND members to start selling the party and its programmes to the public.
"We have two and a half months to go, we have to move quickly in selling the party. We have to talk to our colleagues in UDA, we need to go round and see the people as UDA," he said.
Sikota said there was need to address the leadership of UDA. "There is no time, we need to go round the country as UDA. At the moment there is a lot of people who don't know which way we are going. So in order for people to start campaigning there is need to lay their fears to rest," said Sikota.

Original_Search_Title: The burden of citizenship
Headline: Nigeria: The burden of citizenship
Body_Text:
I love the genius of President Olusegun Obasanjo. Yes, I must confess. Each time I run out of ideas, the old man just comes on the scene and bingo, ideas start flowing.
For one, I think the man's impervious skin allows him to take in everything but not to assimilate anything. I'd give anything to have a skin like that, even the elephant would, I guess. But can you imagine what just happened to Nigeria? It's a wonderful thing. Fani-Kayode has just been made a minister, thank God for the 'heroes' at the Senate who invoked Biblical and Qur'anic injunctions to bind us and goad us to accept him as he would be and not as he is. Barely a week after Baba castigated us brainless critics, he chose one of his brainless hinds to be appointed as minister and he had his way - as always! I mean, if those who abuse have no brains as Baba said in his penultimate broadcast, and Fani is a natural abuser, then my mathematics is as good as yours.
A diplomat friend saw me the other day and wondere d what kind of a comic Nigeria is - so heavily endowed but such a let-down in vital areas. I mean a country with such a population can find anyone for anything - AGIP people who saunter in and out of government; third term apostles being rewarded for their effrontery at spitting in our faces and now a Fani-Kayode as minister. By God he would now be called honourable minister! And the rest of us would have to do with the miserable and unenviable title of Mister! For a people crazy about titles, that's a lowly place to be relegated. But the one that did not shock me is the Bakassi issue.
I mean, it's a wonderful thing to be excised. We had Biafra. Gideon Orkar, a fantastic soldier once broadcast it but for some reason, Sani Abacha stopped him right in his tracks. Today we have MASSOB, MEND and the Niger Delta Volunteer Force to deal with. Secession, is a very difficult virus to deal with except through the world court - over to you Uwazurike! And this is the genius in Mr. Pres ident. A few years back, he was swearing his born again head to the people of Bakassi that, as long as God lives, not an inch or a jot of Nigerian soil would be relinquished. But it appears that that part of him was the sinner wallowing in darkness. One bright morning, our born-again President saw the light. He packed his bags and baggage and got into his new plane (well that's relative by all records from Boeing) and to New York he went.
We were not told beforehand so that we won't tell Gani Fawehinmi who is biographing his travels. Once in New York, Baba sat down under a green tree and drank lots of tea with Kofi Annan and Paul Biya. Of course, there were interpreters in tow because Biya does not speak Ibibio - not yet, (would learn). And so, after a few cups of tea and lots of cream crackers, Mr. President ruminated at the thought of the people's rejection of his greatest enterprise - to be the President for life and how to punish us one by one. And knowing how ungrateful we critics are of his wonderful contribution to the suffocation of Nigeria and its Naira, he decided to give us a parting gift - the excision of an integral part of Nigeria. To my mind, that's a wonderful thing to do on the eve of your departure.
Obasanjo inherited Nigeria with a British-written national anthem, he nationalised it! Now, he has altered the map of Nigeria without as much as recourse to the National Assembly and has been rewarded with an affirmation vote for all his ministerial nominees! Now our children sitting for common entrance would have to wait for cartographers to know how to properly draw the map of Nigeria. At least, the excision has reduced the number of presidential agitators from the South South. In future, critics like us would simply be excised out of Nigeria with the relative intoxication of Mambilla tea; we are learning to take life easy. It is sure that no-one is a citizen of Nigeria until he wakes up, listens to FRCN and has not heard the pre sident's croaking voice excising him out of the nation.
The anatomy of the excision is what actually interests me. Historians say that, except otherwise agreed, either by convention, war or plebiscite, the map of Africa remains as they were pre-independence, the lawyers who argued Nigeria's case at The Hague did not know that. The requirement of Nigerians queuing daily for visas to greener pastures is annoy Baba and see themselves excised. This is why I am angry that Frank Nweke Jnr is assaulting the CNN for doing a report on so-called Nigerians in Houston when all he needed to have done is say they are from Bakassi. Charity may not begin at home, but it must obey her foreign suitor. Trust Baba, if America or The Hague asks him to annihilate us all to pave way for new specie of invaders from Mars, he would have done it - I swear by Kofi Anan.
I think we should beg the Ghanaian tea server each time we want the President to implement the budget with the relative ease he excised Bakassi. I think we should take Baba to The Hague to explain why he lied after stealing money from our foreign reserves. If the court rules that he was wrong, he would return the money with interest. To those of you who still believe you could protest, every time you see Baba doing what you don't like, report him to either George Bush or Tony Blair and ask them to threaten him with visa denial and you will see the relative ease with which he would comply. I appeal to local contractors to take Baba to The Hague and obtain a ruling that they must be paid - pronto, it shall happen!
To those of you who are still smarting from Bakassi's excision, many of my compatriots believe that it would return when the English speaking parts of Cameroon, treated currently like second-class citizens by the French rulers wake up and ask for excision. If they go to The Hague, they shall have a favourable ruling. Unfortunately, you should pity me because I drank my last bottle of optimism on Ob j in 1999. I swear by all the cream crackers in Walmart that I will take Baba to The Hague to tell Nigerians how much it cost them to pursue the case. I think it must be smaller than it cost Okonjo-Iweala and Muktar to secure a debt relief for Nigeria. Nobody should blame Baba for thinking differently from Abacha although his policies are called Abachanjo. Abacha chose to fight war and actually sent a few undesirable soldiers to die staving off the attack on Nigeria by Cameroonian gendarmes instead of just signing off the peninsula the same way Gowon signed it off after a few grasscutter peppersoup and Cameroon brewed banana beer.
On his own, Baba decided to send some retired lawyers on a holiday to The Hague for them to bring the ruling. Some unpatriotic Nigerians said that if we had not contested the case with Cameroon, the judgement could not have been binding. Stupid people, why should we not contest the case - how would some Yoruba lawyers earn estacode if we had failed to contest the issue?
There is one thing I know about Nigeria and international conventions - there is enough petrodollars to waste on such global jamborees; the delegates do not read the treaties, they simply sign and go shopping. Unlike other unpatriotic people who have critiqued Mr President, I support him lest he should excise me. I have subscribed to the Nigerian constitution and I have recited the National Pledge, I am not in a hurry to be excised to Oduduwa Republic, I love being in Arewaland. If all it takes to remove my citizenship is a pronouncement at The Hague followed by a presidential broadcast, then what is the burden of citizenship? Why would anybody want to be a citizen of a land of plenty in which fools suffer in the midst of want? Why would anybody want to be a Nigerian if they can wake up in the morning and be a Cameroonian? Why would anybody want to be a Nigerian when your president excises your part of the country by broadcast fiat and above all, on th e twilight of his exit from the presidium?
Why the president would choose this time to hand over a part of the country he vowed to protect beats me silly? We understand the hosting of international conferences and seminars as the best way to keep busy while the clock ticks off May 29, 2007 - what we had expected is that if the president could not add anything to our country in terms of value, he would at least leave the entity the way he met it. But no! I told you all before, beware of the lashing tail of a wounded scorpion!

Original_Search_Title: The U.S. Boomerang Effect
Headline: Somalia: The U.S. Boomerang Effect
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — PLAYING with a boomerang is not an easy art to master, even for people who spend a long time practising.
The tricky thing for most practitioners is that they tend to focus more on how they will manage to catch the banana-shaped instrument. They forget that it is the way you throw it that will determine its trajectory, making it easier for them to determine where it is headed.
Then, despite all precautions, any distraction, miscalculation or overconfidence ends up in that awful moment when the boomerang, having described an unpredictable ellipse, returns to hit the thrower in the face.
Such lack of judgment must have been equally awful for American policymakers when troops from the Islamist courts last week took control of Somalia's lawless capital city of Mogadishu -- driving out of it hordes of troops loyal to the country's warlords.
For the last couple of months the latter had been armed and backed by the US in an effort to neutralise the court's leaders, who allegedly have links with al-Qaeda, Osama bin Laden's terrorist organisation.
The logic of the war on terror seemed to command such an alliance, even though a decade ago the same warlords hardly hesitated when shooting American soldiers involved in a humanitarian operation in the east African country. But the gamble did not pay off.
It would have been much easier for the US to empower the Somalian government to carry out such a task.
First, it is a legitimate and inclusive government, accepted by all -- it was formed after lengthy negotiations backed by the United Nations and the African Union -- though its authority remains restricted to a small portion of the country.
Second, some of its members are former warlords, whose troops could have been used as the backbone of a police and military force charged with establishing the stepping stones to a gradual return of legal normalcy in the country.
This may sound simplistic, but after Mogadishu's fall, there remains a fledging government, further weakened by the US snub, Islamist courts growing in confidence and defeated warlords who are yet to have their last word.
If the US decides, now, to carry on with its policy of arming them, a war would resume that would lead to more chaos for Somalia than it might have been able to imagine in the last 15-year rule of the warlords.
It could have been avoided, but the Americans seem not to be thinking straight.

Original_Search_Title: 'No, Mr President'
Headline: Kenya: 'No, Mr President'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The last six days have been humiliating for this nation. Our sovereignty has been violated. Our nationhood has been insulted. Our borders have been encroached by hostile forces. And our hospitality has been abused.
What is even more humiliating is the casual manner in which the Government has treated genuine public concern over national security.
We are concerned that the President and his minders are conveniently becoming impervious to threats to national security, prosperity and unity and are exposing the Presidency to ridicule.
Monday's suspension of CID Director Joseph Kamau and the formation of a commission of inquiry into the Armenian saga is a good example of this for it is too little too late. As a nation, the Presidency is the one institution we all identify with, one that is supposed to be above reproach.
While the President and his family have the right to privacy, that privacy is not the single most important issue in Kenya today. Many things have gone wrong in this country, which the President's voice - only his voice - would help straighten. Yet the only time we hear the President's voice is when he is clarifying his family situation.
The President has failed to take unique opportunities to address much weightier matters of national concern. In the light of recent events, security is top among them.
Your Excellency, when the Armenian saga broke out in March you held your tongue, and just when Kenyans thought you had your fingers on the national pulse, you took to the podium on national television last Sunday to clarify the composition of your family. You seem to have forgotten that when you assumed office four years ago, your family grew from a nucleus to encompass all Kenyans.
As the Leader of the Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta said on Monday, the blame for the Armenian saga and political and security paralysis rests squarely with the President. What Kenyans want urgently is reassurance about their safety, national sovereignty and competence of institutions and the rule of law.
We have heard for a long time that the police force is divided , with the Commissioner Major General Hussein Ali trying his best to run a disciplined outfit but with the now suspended CID Director meddling. That the Commissioner was kept in the dark about the raid against the Standard Group in March - a raid planned and executed in the name of State security - is evidence of those divisions. Why do you sit by and wait for such a key institution of governance, one tasked with maintaining law and order, to degenerate?
That police received conflicting instructions as they tried to arrest the alleged Armenians last week is further evidence of these divisions. Your Excellency, don't these things bother you? As a company we have taken unjust blows from your Government. Only in March did hooded policemen, in an operation that was patently illegal, raid us. We have sought the return of equipment confiscated from us then to no avail.
Now we have reason to be concerned at reports that tapes similar to the ones taken from our studios were found in the Armenians' house last week. We have reason to be concerned that hoods similar to the ones worn by those who raided our premises were found in the Armenians' house. We have reason to be concerned that the key person who ordered the raid against a legitimate business - Mr John Michuki - one who is on record defending the Armenians, continues to serve without as much as a bother about the serious implications of these revelations.
In March we recorded a statement with the police about suspicious surveillance of our offices by the Artur brothers in an unmarked car, which we saw in their compound when the police raided the place recently. Nothing was done.
As a conscientious corporate citizen, we called for the deportation of the alleged Armenians before anybody did after they asked your minister for internal security to shut up. Nobody heeded us. In fact, the minister in question subsequently defended them in our own Parliament!
When Kenyans voted for you they did so because they trusted you. In you, they saw the embodiment of national aspiration, growth and all that is good for their prosperity.
Sadly, Your Excellency, your Government has progressed from managing by crisis to thriving in paralysis. For how long can a nation operate this way? When foreigners ambush our national airport, the internal security minister goes underground. Yet the same minister does not hesitate to raid The Standard Group or other media houses critical of impunity. Indeed, the minister only a few weeks ago threatened "to do it again".
Does Government only act at the behest of foreigners? Until they drew guns against us at our most respected airport! And what a cache the police found on subsequently arresting them! Guns reserved for the presidential security unit, documents declaring them deputy police commissioners, bullet-proof vests, Government vehicles or vehicles bearing Government registration plates, gun holsters, passes giving them access to all parts of all our airports. If this does not warrant the intervention of the President what does?
Because the Government sat back as Armenians turned our national airport into poodle, the country risks isolation from the international community. The British Government is demanding an explanation for the security breach at the Jomo Kenyatta International Airport by the Armenians and is concerned over the safety of their airline.
The breach could lead to suspension of British Airways flights to Kenya. Other international airlines might follow suit, and the tourism industry would be hardest hit. Should Kenyans suffer such loss for the comfort of a family, a few individuals and foreigners?
Does it occur to the Government what last week's airport drama has done? Kenya as a transit point for serious investment and as an investment destination is severely compromised by this gangland behaviour. And all we can do is clarify family ties?
With these banana republic antics, is it difficult to see why foreign missions will now be coming to this country with their own security detail since they can no longer trust the Kenyan security agents to guarantee their safety? Imagine the number of job opportunities thus lost!
The Government appears rudderless today, and this hurts millions who invested so much hope and trust in you. We speak plainly because we believe that there is time to redress the mess. You took an oath to protect the country and the Constitution, an oath you must live to.
No Mr President: We respect your fourth clarification about your family. But, Your Excellency, it is time you looked at those who advise you with honest eyes. Michuki, his Defence counterpart Njenga Karume, Permanent Secretary Stanley Murage and Head of Public Service Francis Muthaura are leading you into an abyss.
You must also take action against the Managing Director of the Kenya Airports Authority George Muhoho for he has failed to protect our airports. And you must take action against your associates - or those using your name for ulterior motives - for their behaviour has besmirched your good name and that of your family.
And if nobody else will tell you this, we will.

Original_Search_Title: This is Taking a Facade Too Far
Headline: Kenya: This is Taking a Facade Too Far
Body_Text:
Nairobi — We are a banana republic. And this was conclusively proved by the drama that took place at the Jomo Kenyatta International Airport on Thursday night. A bunch of foreigners storm what is supposed to be one of the most heavily guarded installations in the country, defy requests to have their baggage checked, draw guns and terrorise and assault customs staff and security officers, hurl racial epithets at horrified by-standers, and then drive away as if they own the place!
There is no doubt that if I pulled a similar stunt, I would not just be arrested. I would be shot. And the police would be quite justified in resorting to extreme prejudice when there is a security threat at such a sensitive facility.
More drama was played out when the police finally got the courage to enter the residence of the dubious foreigners. They recovered what can only be described as contraband.
Yet instead of the characters being taken to court and facing the full force of the law, somebody very high in government decides to offer them safe passage out of the country.
Yes, safe passage. Don't be fooled that the fellows were deported. They were helped by those in authority to escape, to flee the short arm of Kenyan law.
What has been played out should make us very angry. And very scared. We have witnessed a situation where the Government is complicit in dangerous activities perpetrated by extremely dubious characters.
We have seen that President Kibaki's Government would be willing to open our borders, airports and seaports to arms smugglers, assassins, drug traffickers and terrorists.
We know that the Moi government was guilty of extremely criminal actions against the people of Kenya. It looted with wild abandon, brought down all institutions and promoted all manner of criminals to take charge of our governance, security and the national coffers.
But it at least did not go so far as to jump into bed with characters that are such a grave threat to national security. It knew its limits.
This government knows no limits.
President Kibaki owes us all an explanation. I don't know whether he had any direct dealings with the Armenian brothers. I don't know whether he had ever met them. But I do know that he is my President, and that a lot of evil things are being done in his name. The buck must stop with him.
So it is his duty to summon national security minister John Michuki and demand to know why fellows who so brazenly compromise security at Kenya's premier airport are helped to escape instead of being thoroughly investigated and brought before a court of law.
The president must also try to establish why even as police were preparing to enter the Runda residence, they were being given instructions from outside the chain of command to call off the operation. Who wanted the operation called off and why?
The president must also summon the Managing Director of the Kenya Airports Authority, Mr George Muhoho, and demand to know why foreigners of extremely suspect inclinations were holding passes to have free run of all airports in the country, privileges that even Cabinet ministers do not enjoy.
From the minister for Immigration, Mr Gideon Konchellah, the president will surely want to know why the Armenians had what appear to be very genuine Kenyan passports.
The president must also summon the Commissioner of Police, Maj Gen Hussein Ali, and demand to know who still is trying to block investigations into the Armenians.
He must also seek from Maj Gen Ali an explanation why such a serious security breach was allowed at the JKIA. Somebody is guilty of criminal dereliction of duty and does not deserve to wear the badge of a police officer.
The president must also have the Commissioner explain why one the Armenians were holding a Kenya Police identification cards in the rank of Deputy Commissioner.
Another person the president must summon is the Commissioner-General of the Kenya Revenue Authority. From Mr Michael Waweru, the president will want to know why customs officers stood by helplessly as visiting foreigners defied requests to open their baggage for routine inspection. And why they did nothing as the foreigners insulted them, threatened them and drove away with uninspected baggage.
He must also demand an explanation from Mr Waweru whether the foreigners have been routinely bringing goods into the country and evading customs checkpoints.
He might also want to know why some of the foreigners have routinely been allowed to fly into the country and offload cargo at a section where they evade customs. The president will ask the same question of Mr Muhoho, as the man in charge of our airports, and Mr Michuki, as the man in charge of our national security.
He will want to know, also, from Mr Waweru and Maj Gen Ali, why the foreigners were holding a large stock of car number plates, why they were allowed to routinely switch their car registration plates or even to drive around without the plates.
And finally, the president must summon any of his relatives who may have been consorting with the Armenians, and allegedly misusing proximity to State House to guarantee them protection and access.
By doing so they badly compromise the image and good name of the president, who in the near future will be asking for our votes and assuring us he remains the same decent, honest and upright man we elected in 2002.
Mr Gaitho is the managing editor in charge of Special Projects.

Original_Search_Title: Truth Behind Narc-Kenya FaÁade
Headline: Kenya: Truth Behind Narc-Kenya FaÃade
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Niccolo Machiavelli, writing in his wily masterpiece, The Prince, notes that men are so simple and so much creatures of circumstance that the deceiver will always find someone ready to be deceived.
So, will Narc-Kenya succeed in concealing its true image behind that rosy faÃade of freshness and the one Kenya-one nation-one people sloganeering? I have my doubts.
For starters, it must greatly worry the party's architects that the whirlwind they expected to arouse by the much-hyped launch of Kenya's latest political clone did not quite materialise.
The "coronation" in Eldoret of William Samoei Ruto as the new chief of the Kalenjin kraal, and Daniel Moi's now boring ranting, somehow contrived to steal the thunder from the green spectacle played out in Nairobi.
Exactly why is the Narc-Kenya bubble not flying? The answer lies in the question: Because Narc-Kenya is, in fact, a bubble! A clever, but not really smart, ploy to hoodwink Kenyans that there is a fresh beginning while what we have here is only plastic surgery on a face desperate to conceal some atrocious blemishes.
Tragically for the party principals, though, Kenyans have seen so much of the fiend beneath the faÃade that a mere change of face, name and slogans will not cut the ice. There are at least five "issues" that could well torpedo the rosy odyssey.
One, the party has a fatal image problem. Everyone knows Narc-Kenya is just the old Democratic Party shedding its traditional regional tag for a national veneer. But that is not even the problem here.
The real devil lies in the paradoxical attempt by the "new" party to disown the evils of Narc, while at the same time seeking to reap political mileage from the collapsed coalition's supposed gains.
As successor to Narc, complete with a name inheritance, Narc-Kenya wants to gloat over stuff like free primary education. But sprint from the serpents of corruption, tribalism, nepotism, media repression and the constitutional review debacle, which were not only sired by Narc, but have been lovingly breastfed, weaned and nurtured by the same guys now waving roses all over town.
How does Narc-Kenya preach nationalism when all its principals shamelessly trumpeted the tribal horn only months ago in their bid to shove that poor imitation of a constitution down our throats?
How can they claim to be reformists when they killed the reform momentum with their power-drenched arrogance?
How can they venerate press freedom when among their ranks are self-declared anti-media rattle snakes? And the questions, Charlie, are plethora!
Two, Narc-Kenya will find it extremely difficult to win the public trust. Forget that faÃade of the Konchellas. The new kids on the block are mere decoys, even though they are learning pretty fast from their mentors. This party's real owners are ominously lurking in the shadows.
And they are the same fellows who guillotined Narc. The guys who sabotaged the Kenyan dream of December 2002. Indeed that those fellows are uneasily hiding behind the veil is proof enough that even they know the heinous breach of trust they have visited upon the people of Kenya. But this should not fool you. The unveiling of the party last weekend smelt of old money. And we know the trail to that money. One thing these people forget is that Kenyans are not really that pumbavu!
Three, the party has the challenge of proving that it does not mistake motion for action, and that it knows the distinction between rhetoric and substance.
While Narc-Kenya goes around spewing commitments to democracy and a break from personality cults, it has preserved the position of party leader and presidential candidate for a well known somebody, without the slightest of competition! What is democratic about that?
Four, Narc-Kenya should find a better selling edge besides that deception of "detribalising" our politics. As the party shouts itself horse about "the end of tribal politics and the birth of a truly national party", it is run by a committee of tribal representatives deliberately selected to ensure regional balancing.
Finally, Narc-Kenya must realise that scoring political goals takes much more than fat bravado. Wole Soyinka would tell them that a tiger does not proclaim its tigritude it pounces!
Instead of so many noises, which sound eerily similar to the empty banana referendum threat of "shaking all corners of the country", the party should prove that it indeed is a fearsome political juggernaut that can mobilise nationally. By the way, somebody remind me, what did they say their ideology was again?

Original_Search_Title: Mr Moi Has Had His Day in the Sun
Headline: Kenya: Mr Moi Has Had His Day in the Sun
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In a rare burst of patriotism, I took time yesterday to read the Kenya Times. Gone are the days when we had provincial and district competitions to judge who bought and hopefully read the paper most, and was therefore most loyal. Western consistently took the top prize, if memory serves me right.
But there are still vestiges of the past lodged deep inside those of us who've been around long enough to remember the days when the Baba na Mama paper was so trendy it was the place to be if you considered yourself a true nationalist.
I didn't set out to be particularly patriotic when I woke up. It is a sad admission, I know, at a time when we have just declared the winners of that song-writing competition hosted by the office of the Government Spokesman. It is sadder still that I can't seem to recall who won it, singing what.
You can blame that on Raila Odinga and the Orange Democratic Movement. They have the dubious honour of scrambling the thinking of Kenyans, so much so that we've had to call on former President Moi to unleash his special artillery on their ranks.
There isn't too much of that nationalism thing going around these days. Many of my friends and I - not to mention our enemies - have retreated into the comfort of home-made political parties, complete with home-made leaders spewing fire and brimstone in the interests of their piece of the homeland.
This development has spawned many cottage industries in recent times, and you can see for yourself just how well Bethuel Kiplagat and his band of eminent persons did out of the latest one to do with a way forward for the remaking of the Constitution.
It turns out that we really didn't need them, but that didn't stop the team telling us what we already knew - delivered in the rosy tones of breaking news. This is vintage Kenyan politics, and Kiplagat & Company did not disappoint.
They went on about that dreadful Memorandum of Understanding that, we now learn, led to rebellion in the referendum, which turned Kenyans into banana and orange fruitcakes.
Now they are calling for an even more complicated thing called national healing. Where have these people been in the past three-and-a-half years?
They even had the guts to ask the President to lead the way in the search for a new constitution, as if they don't know how vexed that issue is. What's a man to do when some people, who are known to suffer delusions of grandeur, refuse to be led?
You'd think that they'd let us enjoy some quiet time ahead of the political battles that are bound to dog our lives next year. But there's no accounting for taste, especially when the word eminent is used in such elastic terms that you don't know three-quarters of the members of the team.
The thing with Kenya today is that you can't trust those people from the other ridge or those who live beyond a certain river. They always have an agenda. If they are not trying to wrest power from the legitimately elected government, they are sleeping with the enemy.
They have taken the term "multi-party" literally, and hopping from one political bed to another has become a free-fall activity across the land.
It is more fashionable than in the days of our polygamous forefathers, who had so many wives they even ended up proposing to their own daughters, having lost track of who belonged with which family.
And so I was flipping through the day's papers when the unlikely headline "Moi runs into hostile crowd" caught my eye in the most unlikely newspaper.
A woman is allowed to get nostalgic just thinking of the days when there was only one political party worth speaking of. You expressed the kind of "hostility" that the ex-president ran into at your own peril.
They could lock you up and throw away the keys for even imagining, in the privacy of your mind, the possibility that there could be an opinion other than the one handed down by His Excellency.
That man, William Ruto, who is in the rare position of being both a youth and an elder at once, would have long been hauled off to detention for daring to disagree publicly - even privately, actually - with our man at the top. The hostile crowd in Eldoret North, which was shouting "Ruto pekee", would have lived to rue the day.
