But Mr Moi is a reformed man these days, and he has gems to impart to Kenyans who have been missing him so desperately that he has decided to come out of retirement.
I have reservations, and I propose a commission of inquiry into Mr Moi's reincarnation as a democrat. Only a short while ago, someone prominent in Government threatened to withdraw his pension if he continued straying into active politics. These days he has free run of the country and not a whimper of protest. Do they know something we don't?
Listen to some of the new wisdoms spewing out of the man's mouth: "Some of these youthful leaders think leading the country is an easy task like leading goats and cows which can be shepherded to a direction of your liking."
This came just before he urged the crowd in Eldoret North to stick with Kanu. It was wrong, he argued, for some leaders to give themselves the role of shaping the destiny of Kenyans instead of leaving the people to chart their future without undue influence.
And then came words of wisdom for the Kalenjin peoples, whom he described as nomads who would not prosper politically because of their "disunity" - which presumably refers to entertaining thoughts of belonging to parties other than the revered Kanu. I don't get it. If they did not grow rich politically - whatever that means - in the 39 years he walked the corridors of power, what chances do they have now that we are waving red roses and talking of tribe-free politics?
I have a few words for Mr Moi and his minders: Kenyans have endured decades of being patronised, insulted and taken for granted. We have been turned into a country of neurotics constantly looking over our shoulders at an imaginary enemy, seeing and hearing evil - even saying it aloud. Mr Moi was lucky to get away with barely a smack on the wrist for some of the atrocities committed in his time.
There's a reason why Mandela remains a dearly loved figure not only in South Africa but also globally. He knows when to keep his silence, so much so that when he speaks his words echo right round the world.
Ms Oriang' is the managing editor in charge of magazines

Original_Search_Title: Lead the Way in Cooling Tribal Tensions
Headline: Kenya: Lead the Way in Cooling Tribal Tensions
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When Narc-Kenya was launched last Saturday, nothing so brilliantly captured the jockeying of our political parties as did Gado's cartoon featuring the horses of our four main political parties.
The leaders of Kanu and Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) were feuding internally, but their horses were rearing to go at the starting line.
There was no rider on the new party's steed, its leader still unknown. And on the fourth horse was seated the chief of the Narc ruling coalition, President Kibaki, who is also Narc-Kenya's putative leader. He was blissfully asleep, as was his horse.
The launch of a major new party without a leader must surely be a first in recent world history. But that weakness aside, I thought its strategists were very savvy, having come up with a potentially winning strategy by profiling Narc-Kenya in large billboards and advertisements with a simple and compelling message harking to "the end of tribal politics and the birth of a truly national party".
High-minded party credo
But as the big launch proceeded on Saturday, that high ground came crashing down to make way for the ethnic-oriented politicking we know all too well.
The person who took the lead in subverting this high-minded party credo was Narc-Kenya's interim chairman, Prof Kivutha Kibwana, among our most learned and progressive MPs. Much of his speech in launching the party on Saturday was on track, but then he lurched into divisiveness.
What political party can help defuse or liquidate the ethnic hatred sowed especially against the majority tribes of our country? he asked those attending the launch.
Majority tribes? There is no such thing in Kenya, so I presume that this fascinating new formulation is a euphemism for a brazen appeal for ethnic support from Gema, the country's largest and more powerful community (but still not a majority).
So Prof Kibwana was continuing the old practice of pitting people against each other. It is not for nothing that most politicians resort to it: there are votes to be won in dividing people.
As we saw in Naivasha the following day, other party leaders are stirring up the ethnic pot like everyone else.
Of all the challenges we face, ethnic divisions are the most worrying and destabilising. But the divisions are not inherent in our body politic; they only surface strongly when Kenyans are pushed that way by our leaders' actions or words.
These divisions' worst manifestations were the large-scale ethnic clashes that rocked the Rift Valley in particular and also the Coast during the Moi era.
The divisions had ebbed dramatically by the time of the 2002 election, when both the leading presidential candidates were Kikuyu. Kenyans had absolutely no problem with that.
But three years down the line, during the referendum on the constitution, things had once again unravelled terribly, with most Kenyans on one side and the Mt Kenya region on the other. What went wrong?
Many Kenyans blame ODM leaders for preaching divisiveness and for rallying the country to unite against Kikuyu hegemony by defeating the constitution and the Government.
Certainly, there is truth in that assertion, as there is in its reverse: public appeals were made by the Banana camp to the central Kenya region to ensure that they did not lose the seat.
And both sides demeaned opponents by condemning practices such as circumcision and land inheritance by women.
But this post-2002 round of ethnic polarisation had actually begun long before the constitution entered the picture. There were a host of reasons for this, but one of the most important was the decision by the newly-installed President Kibaki to surround himself with a group of advisers almost entirely from his own region, the Mt Kenya Mafia.
At various crises points in Narc rule, it was embarrassing to read in the press the names of those who trooped to State House to advise him.
In addition, a visibly disproportionate number of key positions in the Cabinet and elsewhere have gone to the Mt Kenya region. Such favouritism was wrong in principle, but it was also a serious impediment to President Kibaki's ability to govern.
In a multi-ethnic society like ours, you need a wide spectrum of the rainbow behind you to govern effectively. So, strategically, the President's most critical failure was not the corruption that seeped into his inner-most circles, but his inability to politically hold together the coalition that had made him president.
Ethnic tensions erupted
There seemed to be a complete lack of awareness about the intense damage the widely remarked ethnic partiality was doing to the President's authority.
As columnist Macharia Gaitho wrote astutely in these pages 12 months ago, Mr Moi tried unsuccessfully for two decades to isolate the Kikuyu, but Mr Kibaki had managed that in just two years.
To go to a constitutional referendum under such circumstances and suspicions was a terrible mistake.
A constitution should unite a country and only be presented if there is a high level of consensus among the political players. It was easy to predict the ethnic tensions that would erupt.
In his panel's report on the way forward on the constitution, Mr Bethuel Kiplagat pointed to the ethnic tensions that boiled over in the referendum and boldly told the President that only he could heal this nation, and he should reach out to the opposition.
The buck does stop with the President. This is a historic moment. The President must take charge and lead the way in addressing the causes of our ethnic crisis. The opposition will also have to rise this challenge and join him in this particular struggle.
Mr Lone, a veteran journalist, writes on topical national and international issues

Original_Search_Title: Zimra Condemning Musicians to Poverty
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zimra Condemning Musicians to Poverty
Body_Text:
Harare — A piece of chewing gum costs $10 000 today. A banana is $50 000. A loaf of bread is now $130 000.
But a musician gets $8 000 whenever his/her song is played on radio! To be precise, it's $8 374,59. And what does $8 374,59 buy these days. Nothing, you are right. Whatever the circumstances and whatever defence is put across, paying $8 374,59 is cursing and condemning musicians to poverty.
It becomes painful when musicians are made to wait for 12 months to receive amounts that are paid by denominations that are effectively not in use. Freddie, the late James Chimombe's son, received $11 000. If he did not get it in thousand-dollar notes, he got two fives and a thousand-dollar note or he was given a ten thousand-dollar note and a thousand-dollar bill. It does not matter that his song was not played on radio because for him to wait for $11 000 for 12 months is like waiting for the rain in winter. And asking him to travel from wherever he was coming from to collect $11 000 is leading him into debt because he needed at least $200 000 to travel from any suburb to the city and then from the city to Avondale where musicians collect the royalties.
What it means is that Freddie lost $189 000 in order to get $11 000! Simple calculation shows that in order for a musician to buy bread at the current rate, their song has to be played 17 times and for them to buy a banana their song has to be played six times. A schedule of payment I have shows that one musician received $4 092 828,20. His 14 songs were played a total 472 times and earned him $3 952 806,89. The other $128 930,51 was paid for the use of the same 14 songs as cellphone ringtones or played as video on television 93 times. If he were paid on a monthly basis, the musician would have earned $341 067,24.
This is enough to buy two and a half loaves of bread today. According to the Zimbabwe Music Rights Association's 2005 chairman's report, the organisation grossed $2 555 512 145 and incurred $1 247 997 774 in expenses. And about 1 454 members shared $1 307 514 371. There is $59 516 597 difference bet ween expenditure and the amount paid out to the people whose music had been paid for. The report notes that postage alone cost $118 608 592 while stationery was $129 340 080 for the year ending December 2005.
This means that every month, the association used $9 884 049,30 in postage and $10 778 340 in stationery while most musicians got a paltry $341 067,24 a month. It's not clear what the chairman meant by saying that they had adopted various initiatives that include "keeping a watchful eye on our expenses" when there is only a $59 516 597 difference between expenditure and money disbursed. If musicians are paid $1,5 million for funeral expenses, what will they get when they retire from the music industry? And what kind of coffin costs $1,5 million these days?
Now compare the $1,5 million that a family gets when a musician dies with $1 million that is given as directors' fee? This is just not it guys, no matter the reasons such as inflation and low rates. If the national br oadcaster pays low rates, then the association that has nine employees at the moment should be leaner and effective. One would ask why with nine people do we still have talk about unclear data collection regarding songs played on radio. One would also wonder why expenditure was just $50 million short of the amount paid out to musicians. And who received the letters whose stamps gobbled $118 million?
A number of musicians said they did not receive any letters. And again what would be the logic of writing letters that cost so much when communicating with a musician who would get $11 000? Cephas Mashakada was right when he said that the Government should intervene.

Original_Search_Title: ODM Vows to Reject Kiplagat Review Plan
Headline: Kenya: ODM Vows to Reject Kiplagat Review Plan
Body_Text:
Nairobi — MPs allied to the Orange Democratic Movement will reject the Kiplagat report aimed at jump-starting the Constitution review when it comes up for debate in Parliament.
They said yesterday that the report was not offering anything new and only made suggestions that ODM members had themselves urged repeatedly; that President Kibaki should initiate fresh talks about the review.
However, the Catholic Church said it had taken the report's recommendations seriously and was studying them.
The chairman of the Kenya Episcopal Conference, Archbishop John Njue said the Catholic church will issue a comprehensive statement today.
He explained: "The Kiplagat position is quite forthright. We believe it requires some kind of reflection before we respond."
For President Kibaki, the director of Presidential Press Service, Mr Isaiah Kabira, said: "Just as the President promised when he received the report, there will be wide consultations to ensure that the country gets a new Constitution."
The report's fate now rests with Parliament after Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua tabled it in the House on Tuesday. It awaits either adoption or rejection by MPs.
Kanu secretary general William Ruto, ODM spokesman Mutula Kilonzo and Emuhaya MP Kenneth Marende for the Liberal Democratic Party yesterday separately rejected the report, arguing it made recommendations which they have made all along.
The LDP gave its position during a news conference at the party's headquarters in Lavington, Nairobi, attended by Mr Raila Odinga (Langata), Mr David Musila (Mwingi South), Mr Najib Balala (Mvita), Mr Joseph Kamotho (Mathioya) and Mrs Jebii Kilimo of Marakwet East, all of whom had campaigned vigorously against the Constitution draft put to a national referendum last November 21.
Mr Marende said the President should have initiated dialogue but had failed to do so.
"Our position in respect of the committee of eminent persons is that they are not saying anything new," he said of the 15-member team headed by former envoy Bethwel Kiplagat.
"They were treading on grounds walked on and what Kenyans need today is political good will. We cannot endorse such a report," he declared.
Mr Ruto said that after the referendum defeat, the ODM urged President Kibaki to call leaders from the anti-constitution Orange campaigners and the pro-Banana team to discuss how to restart the review.
"The President did not have to form a committee to give him suggestions that were already there," he said.
Mr Kilonzo said the Kiplagat committee was giving President Kibaki a soft landing when they recommended the review should be separated from elections.
Consult the Opposition
Others who spoke included a member of Law Society of Kenya council, Mr Evans Monari, the director of the local branch of the International Commission of Jurists, Mr Samuel Mbithi, and Prof Githu Muigai who was a member of the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission.
Mr Mbithi said: "It has nothing new. The Government has been asked in the past to consult the Opposition ... this is something people have said through and through."
Prof Muigai however agreed with the proposal that the President should lead the way.
"I believe the President must bring to bear on the process his personal prestige and authority," the university don said.
Prof Muigai dismissed as "unrealistic and unworkable" proposals that a constituent assembly and a team of experts be used to complete the review.
Prof Muigai said a small group of local Constitutional experts should work with Parliament to complete the review.
Mr Monari said the Kiplagat committee was a reflection of a deliberate move by the Government to waste time.
In Mombasa Anglican bishop Dr Julius Kalu and the secretary of the Council of Imams and Preachers, Sheikh Mohammed Dor, prefered the idea of an assembly, among three suggestions in the report.
Professional bodies
This would mean having another assembly with representatives elected nationwide plus others representing interest groups such as manufacturers, religious groups and professional bodies.
Though the committee saw this as the ideal, it nonetheless warned that it would be costly to the public purse.
Yesterday, Bishop Kalu said he would prefer this plus a referendum and congratulated the team for doing a good job. However, he said he agreed with the team's recommendations that President Kibaki held the key to ensuring that Kenyans get a new Constitution.
Sheikh Dor said: "We do not want the repeat of what happened to the Bomas Draft and that is why the President should ensure the review is going to be people driven and all inclusive."
In Kisumu Law Society of Kenya (LSK) Western Kenya chapter chairman Aggrey Mwamu said lawyers in the country had all along supported the formation of a constituent assembly supported by a group of experts as the way out of the impasse.
He added: "Politicians should not be the ones given the driving seat in this process. We should bring in other parties as well so as to make the process more inclusive," he said.
In Nakuru, Bishop Abraham Gitu of the Apostolic Church Ministries said that the proposal of a committee of experts to complete the review before taking it to a referendum was the best idea.
Reporting by: David Mugonyi, Patrick Mayoyo, Dave Opiyo and Olivia Owuor

Original_Search_Title: Pride Gives Out Sh20b Loans
Headline: Uganda: Pride Gives Out Sh20b Loans
Body_Text:
Kampala — Pride Microfinance has given out loans worth sh20b, the managing director, Paul Musoke, has said.
"The outstanding amount of money being held by clients is about sh20b, but we are giving out more loans. This time, we are targeting people doing business below the small and medium enterprises levels," Musoke said.
He was launching a seven-month campaign, "Osobola Ne Pride Microfinance," at the company's offices on Entebbe Road recently.
The campaign is aimed at inspiring customers and potential customers to improve their businesses. It is also aimed at promoting a savings culture among clients.
"We want to support a person selling bananas from the street to set up a stall. This is done through the loans we offer that start from sh150,000 and above according to one's business plans," Musoke said.

Original_Search_Title: Karongi Residents Want Market
Headline: Rwanda: Karongi Residents Want Market
Body_Text:
Kigali — The residents of Karongi District who have been forcefully evicted from the former market at Nyakariba and tentatively taken to Murwanvumba want the government to construct for them a modern market.
The residents are complaining that the current place they are occupying is in a poor state and is inconveniencing for many residents. They want a modern market constructed along the shores of Lake Kivu where business between Rwanda and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is booming.
"The District authorities relocated us from Nyakariba market to this place. We have been depending on this market for Life; this is where residents of Kibuye get cheap bananas. Even other people come from as far as Kigali, Gitarama and Butare to buy cheap bananas here. We appeal to the government to put up a market place for us," Michael Bizimana, a businessman in the area, told The New Times.
François Gashaija, a tax officer in the department of finance at Karongi District, who was found at Murwanvumba market emphasised the need to have a modern market in the District. He appealed to the Ministry of Infrastructure to intervene.
Some DRC traders also expressed their disappointment at the lack of a planned market in the area.
Musafiri Kabatende, a Congolese trader, decried the frequent change in the market places observing that the changes disrupt and affect the trading activity and called for a permanent market place.
Apparently, the said market operates on a piece of land that belongs to the Seventh Adventist church in the area.
The market operates on Fridays, a day when all activities should end at 6pm in the Adventists faith in preparation for the Sabbath which is on Saturday.

Original_Search_Title: Results of Polls Not a Threat to LDP - Raila
Headline: Kenya: Results of Polls Not a Threat to LDP - Raila
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The just-concluded Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) grassroot elections will not break the party, Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga has said.
He said the top LDP organ will review the situation and possibly order fresh elections in areas where irregularities are proved to have occurred.
"And this aside, our party emerged stronger from the elections, with representation in every corner. Most parties fear conducting their grassroot polls because of prospects of fallouts," Mr Odinga said in Migori District.
He was on his way back to Kenya from Tanzania after attending the burial of benga music maestro Daniel Owino Misiani in Shirati Kabwana village, Tarime District.
Mr Odinga downplayed the quit threats issued by Mbita MP Otieno Kajwang' promising that the row will be resolved soon.
He pointed out that he did not sponsor any candidate although he admitted that some people used his name in Nyanza to get sympathy from the voters.
At the same time, Mr Odinga asked President Kibaki to "summon courage and dissolve parliament now that the ruling coalition is dead".
"The entire Cabinet is now subscribing to the ideals of Narc Kenya...a party that did not sponsor them to parliament. Mr Kibaki is left standing between Narc, Narc Kenya and DP - not knowing which direction to go to," he asserted.
Mr Odinga addressed crowds at Isebania and Mabera centres where he asked the local community to remain steadfast in ODM.
Meanwhile, South Mugirango MP Omingo Magara has dismissed Narc Kenya as a non-starter.
He said future of the country lay in ODM and asked Kenyans to support it in removing the Government of President Kibaki from power.
The banana team
Speaking to journalists at Awendo, the Ford People MP told voters not to be duped by the banana team that was "now wearing a new uniform".
MPs will not be allowed to contest branch seats in the newly formed Narc Kenya party, assistant minister for Transport Njeru Githae said yesterday.
Mr Githae said the party's draft constitution bars MPs from seeking party posts at the branches. Constituencies are the branches, according to the proposed constitution.
Mr Githae was speaking to journalists at Sagana in Kirinyaga District.

Original_Search_Title: Parties Welcome Alliances Bids
Headline: Kenya: Parties Welcome Alliances Bids
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Political leaders yesterday welcomed moves to form alliances ahead of next year's General Election.
But former Roads minister Raila Odinga said parties should seek the nod of their top organs to engage in formal talks.
Ford Kenya and the National Party of Kenya said, however, that any discussions should be carried out within the legal party structures.
The two parties, which were in the Banana camp during the referendum campaigns late last year, said also that they would not engage in talks with the Orange Democratic Movement.
Said Mr Odinga: "It's premature to talk about us coming together with other political parties to form a coalition. We in LDP (Liberal Democratic Party) have to talk among ourselves first, and we have not done that. I will talk to my colleagues soon about it."
The Lang'ata MP said it was crucial for leaders with a common vision to come together and seek an end to what he termed sectarianism, regionalism and ethnicity in Kenyan politics.
"You cannot just wish the problems of sectarianism, regionalism and ethnicity away," he told the Nation. "I believe there's a need for leaders to come together and form alliances that are inclusive of all Kenyans."
East African Community assistant minister Bonny Khalwale of Ford Kenya said his party was ready to negotiate with others to form what he termed working alliances.
But he was categorical that it would not engage in talks with ODM which, he said, had lost meaning after the November 21 referendum.
"We'll negotiate with other political parties, including LDP and Kanu," the assistant minister said. "They all must have structures in place, but not the ODM which is an amorphous organisation. Its promoters should know that."
The leaders were referring to revelations that informal talks had started among Ford Kenya, NPK and LDP after the formation of Narc Kenya.
Sources said representatives of the three parties had been meeting to work out details of a political alliance to challenge Narc Kenya, which was launched at the weekend by 16 Cabinet ministers, including Vice-President Moody Awori.
Yesterday, the new party's interim chairman, Prof Kivutha Kibwana, said other parties should leave them alone to build a "fresh home."
The Environment minister dismissed other parties as tribal outfits which, he noted, had kept the public out of mainstream politics.
"We're not interested in politics of confrontation," he said. "They (leaders of other parties) should concentrate on building what they want to build as we take our party to the people. It's Kenyans who will judge." But Health minister Charity Ngilu and Soita Shitanda of Housing said movers of Narc Kenya would be judged by Kenyans.
Mrs Ngilu, the NPK leader, said she would have confirmed that President Kibaki identifies with the new party had he attended its launch. "I believe the President would like to be in Narc," she said. "Had he wanted to join Narc Kenya he would have attended its launch."
She reminded MPs who attended the inauguration that they would not be in government now if the President and Vice-President Michael Wamalwa had not teamed up.

Original_Search_Title: Alliances Set to Shift As Kombo And Ngilu Eye ODM
Headline: Kenya: Alliances Set to Shift As Kombo And Ngilu Eye ODM
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Leaders of two key Narc parties have started talks with the Orange Democratic Movement in a move to form a broader coalition ahead of the 2007 elections, it can be revealed today.
Ford Kenya and the National Party of Kenya leaders, smarting from defection of several Narc MPs to Narc-Kenya, now want to team up with the ODM to counter the new party.
Insiders confirmed that there have been talks involving Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo, National Party of Kenya's leader Charity Ngilu and Langata MP Raila Odinga of the Liberal Democratic Party, who represents the ODM.
Also, emissaries from the three camps have been meeting to work out details of a political alliance with the aim of fighting the Narc-Kenya in next year's elections.
The discussions, which started as informal, will now be taken before the respective national executive committees of parties for sanctioning, the sources confirmed. In fact, the sources indicated that the matter was likely to be discussed at the LDP's NEC meeting slated for tomorrow.
Other parties will follow suit in the next few days. Yesterday, Mvita MP Najib Balala who is a key member of LDP, said there was mutual progress out of the talks and that there were signals that all sides would formalise the deal soon.
The three parties, he said, needed each other and that their coming together would assist in the building of a better nation. "We believe in coalitions, we also know their value and collectively we can transform the lives of Kenyans," he said.
He said there was agreement among members of the three camps and cross party emissaries who have been talking on the sides that they should unite to form a formidable union.
And yesterday Mr Odinga, though not directly making reference to the talks, exuded confidence of the ODM's future. While addressing the public at Isebania border post along the Kenya-Tanzania border, he dismissed the new Narc-Kenya as inconsequential saying it was nearly composed of Banana proponents during the last year's referendum.
He said: "This new party does not threaten us. We will beat it at the next General Election if it gets support of President Kibaki and Mr Moi." He reiterated that the installation of Eldoret North MP William Ruto as Kalenjin elder in Eldoret on Saturday was a sign of bigger things to come, adding that ODM was solid despite disquiet from some of its leaders.
Education Minister Dr Noah Wekesa, who is the Narc coordinating committee chairman, was non-committal about the proposed alliance, but said Kenyans should expect "a big announcement" from the talks soon.
Dr Wekesa said there were also discussions with some Democratic Party (DP) leaders, who have declined to join Narc-Kenya.
Principle of coalition
The minister added that the remaining Narc parties would be making a decision in due course on how to work with LDP. "We are open to the idea of teaming up with people who share in the principle of coalition," he said.
Stating that the era of one-party governments ended in December 2002, the Kwanza MP said future coalitions will be formed after the elections to avoid the mistakes that cost Narc the much needed unity. He said their alliance ensure that coalition delivers its pre-election pledges.
LDP deputy secretary-general Andrew Ligale, said they will be talking with other political parties to secure a future for Kenyans. The Vihiga MP said: "We are now prepared to talk to anybody and look at all options in the light of changing political scenarios."
"Our priority is the ODM and if others are prepared, we can come together," he added. A Kanu MP, who is associated with Mr Ruto, also confirmed that there have been discussions between ODM, Ford-K and NPK and that further negotiations will take place with a view of forming an alliance.
The latest revelations come shortly after Mr Kombo's visit to South Nyanza where he was quoted as having called for close working relationship between his party LDP.
Mr Kombo recalled that his party was instrumental in the formation of Narc coalition but pointed out that they had learnt lessons from the crises that dogged the Narc Government.
"We must establish workable alliances before next year," Mr Kombo repeatedly said in Migori and Rongo towns. It also comes after Kanu secretary general William Ruto was installed as Kalenjin elder at a ceremony attended by ODM luminaries and which retired President Daniel arap Moi has dismissed as inconsequential.
Should the talks be finalised, a new political front to give Narc-Kenya competition would have been born ahead of the 2007 General Election.
And if the parties join with the ODM, that would leave Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta's wing supported by Mr Moi as the third factor in next year's elections.
While LDP pulled out of Narc last year, Ford-K and NPK have been left alone in the coalition following the decision by most MPs linked to President to join Narc Kenya.
Political homes
Mr Kombo, Mrs Ngilu and some DP officials have dismissed Narc-Kenya and opted to stick to their current political homes. Revelations of the new political realignment comes a day after Vice President Moody Awori and 16 Cabinet ministers launched Narc-Kenya and declared the party the true heir of the original Narc.
Speakers at the Narc-Kenya launch on Saturday said they had formed the party to end the squabbles in the ruling coalition, Narc. They described existing political parties as tribal outfits which were yet to come out of the 'big man' culture.
Narc-Kenya, which asserts that it has 88 MPs on its side, draws its membership from Narc and some from Kanu, Ford People, Safina and Sisi Kwa Sisi MPs who side with the Government.
It was notable that senior Meru and Mbeere politicians were absent during the launch of the party at the Kenyatta International Conference Centre (KICC).
Sources said some MPs from the region who were uncomfortable with Narc-Kenya, were ready to join the alliance being fashioned by ODM and Mr Kombo and Mrs Ngilu.
Meanwhile, Ford Kenya's youth league has dismissed Narc- Kenya as a fraud and demanded that President Kibaki comes clear on his alleged links to the party.
The chairman of the league, Mr Kepta Ombati, described Narc-Kenya as a fraud because it was taking over the Government yet it did not participate at the elections.
Since Cabinet ministers and even the VP, who were elected on Narc ticket, have joined Narc-Kenya, which was never voted for in 2002, what they have done is a fraudulent," said Mr Ombati.
"The President should come out in the open and declare whether he is the principal in the newly launched party Narc-Kenya," he demanded.
The youth accused Mrs Yvonne Nambia, widow of former Vice-President Michael Wamalwa for joining the new party.
Additional reporting by Elisha Otieno.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Are Living in Interesting Times
Headline: Kenyans Are Living in Interesting Times
Body_Text:
Nairobi — "May you live in interesting times" is an ancient Chinese curse that is often bandied about as a blessing by those who are too lazy to brush up on History 101. That said, aren't Kenyans living in the most interesting of times, of all the countries on the continent?
Today, the country is at political crossroads like it's probably never been in 42 years of independence. In 2002, an unprecedented wave of popular discontent brusquely swept away Daniel Toroitich arap Moi, along with more than half of Kanu's power-brokers. It's a testament to the man's mojo - and the dynamics of Kenyan society - that today, Bwana Moi is once again arguably the most feared politico in the country. To that we shall return.
First to England, where I've been reading up on divorce (don't ask!) A week ago, the House of Lords (Empire's highest court) allowed a woman to keep a £5 million divorce pay-out after just 33 months of marriage and no children. Alan Miller had petitioned the Lords to complain about a lower court ruling that put Melissa's cut of his £17.7 million at almost one third even if the marriage was as brief as a typical love affair. Apparently, the lower court judge had been convinced by a clever lawyer that Mrs Miller had entered the marriage with "reasonable expectation" of a future wealthy lifestyle.
The moral of that tale is "reasonable expectation". Last week in Kampala, the news was mostly about Cabinet appointments, and the return to the political high table of Eriya Kategaya, President Yoweri Museveni's childhood friend and formerly one of the most powerful men in the country. The two had a spectacular fallout nearly three years ago after Mr Museveni intensified his presidency-for-life project through his coterie of praise-singers. When Ndugu Kategaya suggested publicly that the president was acting like a typical tin-pot dictator, he was sacked from his ministerial power via a radio announcement (typical Moi style). He has since been linked with the Forum for Democratic Change (FDC), the party headed by Dr Kizza Besigye.
There was predictable anger, dismay and disappointment after Kategaya's re-appointment. But it's the FDC that took it hardest. The party claimed that Kategeya was a member and had even led one of their delegations to Tanzania to meet then President Benjamin Mkapa last year. The FDC apparently had "reasonable expectation" that after being sacked and humiliated, Kategaya would remain in opposition for as long as the man whose third term he had so publicly rejected was in power. So you can imagine.
So we're back in Nairobi, where there is very little that needs to be imagined, since most of the men (and woman) who want to be president are well-spoken for, not least the incumbent. Through their own utterances and actions, as well as those of friends and supporters, Kalonzo Musyoka, Raila Odinga, William Ruto, Uhuru Kenyatta, Nicholas Biwott, Charity Ngilu, Prof George Saitoti and you wonder who else, have all indicated a past or present desire to be on the short-list of interviewees for the top job.
But first they had to shake off Moi. His spectacular loss of influence was a function of being a reluctant democrat who, even after 24 years in power, was determined not to fade into the kind of benign obscurity Nelson Mandela, Jerry Rawlings and Kenneth Kaunda now enjoy. The entire "Project Uhuru" was what a judge in Britain would have called a breach of the "reasonable expectation" that Mr Moi would retire and stay so. But such was his power and influence that it took the combined efforts of a lot of grown men and women to dislodge him. Some of these people, for example Ndugu Odinga, had "reasonable expectation" of constitutional reform which would pave the way for them to finally ascend to the top of the food chain.
We're all familiar with what has happened over the last three and half years. The brazen corruption scandals, riots, a death of six, parliamentary walk-outs, frenzied battles over a new constitution, and bananas and oranges turning into iconic symbols of struggle. It all culminated in the crushing defeat of the government in last year's referendum on the new constitution.
Whether it was the expectation that there would be a constitutional deal within 100 days after the election of President Kibaki, Raila becoming Prime Minister, creating half a million new jobs every year, re-engaging with donors, tackling corruption, or bringing common thieves to heel. It's indeed rather ironic now that the original name for the post-Moi government was the National Rainbow Coalition (Narc). It's been anything but a rainbow.
Through all this chaos and upheaval, however, Kenyans have kept the faith, with the economy showing a real kick for the first time in half a generation and wananchi voting with their cash when KenGen's initial public offering was over-subscribed by about Sh18 billion (or 337 per cent), a figure that was as shocking as it was unexpected. In fact, one wonders what would have happened if politicians in neighbouring Uganda had similarly disposed of public assets, instead routinely - and corruptly - giving them away to preferred political and filial cronies.
KenGen is just the latest in a series of "feel-good stories" - the growth figures and the incredible appetite and growth in ICTs (especially mobile telephony), the real estate boom - that give the clearest indication that Kenyans are willing to shrug off the rapacious corruption scandals and political uncertainty of the last few years. Actually, the politics has mostly been played out in the open, making Kenya more like Britain or the US, and less like a typical African country.
It's in this interesting melting pot that former president Moi has once again thrown himself. Everyone was sure he was not "dead" yet, but the man whose hand Mwai Kibaki was even reluctant to shake at the January 2003 inauguration ceremony, has made such a spectacular comeback that he's even a preferred guest at State House.
Ever wily, the "professor of politics" seems to operate best during times such as these. That might explain why the Orange Democratic Movement, once poised to seize power, is now an acronym for, er, whatever.
Whether it was Moi who dashed ODM's "reasonable expectation" of replacing Narc in government is anyone's guess. For now, however, Kenyans are living in interesting times.

Original_Search_Title: At 74, Nyachae Has Had Enough
Headline: Kenya: At 74, Nyachae Has Had Enough
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The imminent departure of Roads minister Simeon Nyachae from the political scene may not strictly be about age, but rather his waning national popularity.
His party - Ford People - has since the last elections been wavering in stature and since joining the so-called Government of National Unity, Mr Nyachae and his party have been reduced to mere appendages of Narc.
The party failed to present candidates for the Kasipul-Kabondo and Kisauni seats which were left vacant after the deaths of MPs Peter Owidi and Karisa Maitha. And with the by-elections scheduled to be held in North Horr, Saku, Laisamis, Moyale and Nakuru Town, the party is not expected to field candidates either.
The Ford People leader cannot be faulted in his ability to read and interpret the "danger signs" aptly. His timing of the announcement on the eve of the by-elections may have been meant to prepare for his party's inability to field candidates.
Since the 74-year-old joined the government, the Nyaribari Chache MP has been rather shy to play competitive party politics.
By accepting to join President Kibaki's Government, the minister seems to have politically driven himself into a tight corner. He is neither a member of the Narc coalition nor is he known to support the newly-formed Narc-Kenya party.
"Our party died way back when Mr Nyachae and his cronies joined the Government. After getting the ministerial flag, which he had all long been looking for, he lost interest in the party and stopped revamping it," says Wundanyi MP Mwandawiro Mghanga.
The former Ford-P secretary general adds that Mr Nyachae's decision to quit active politics was long overdue. He argues that his latest move was meant to pre-empt the eventuality of being kicked out of Parliament.
"I had a lot of respect for the old man. However, I found his reason of joining Government and abandoning his party very selfish," claims Mr Mghanga.
During the November referendum, Mr Nyachae was among the ministers who failed to deliver the Banana vote to the Government in their own constituencies.
Mr Nyachae's failure in Abagusii districts was even more deafening considering that he headed the campaigns on the government side.
The anticipated vacuum to be left by the Ford People leader gives room to speculation on his possible successor, especially in Kisii. While Economic Planning and Development minister Henry Obwocha stands out as Mr Nyachae's preferred successor, the star of Ford-P "rebel" MP Omingo Magara has been rising by the day.
The South Mugirango MP, who broke ranks with the party to spearhead the Orange campaign in Nyanza Province, emerged victor.
As if haunted by the loss, the minister has lately been chiding Mr Magara. Speaking recently at a Nairobi hotel during a funds drive, he hit out at some MPs for allegedly pursuing national glory at the expense of their electorate.
The minister accused such leaders of playing to the gallery instead of championing the interests of their communities. He told Mr Magara to his face not to pretend to be a nationalist but to instead look for grassroots support.
Mr Nyachae's retirement disclosure seems to be a strong message on fellow septuagenarians still hanging onto power. Prominent among them are President Kibaki (75), Vice-President Moody Awori (78), Internal Security counterpart John Michuki (75) and Defence colleague Njenga Karume (78).

Original_Search_Title: Marsabit Town Critical in Saku
Headline: Kenya: Marsabit Town Critical in Saku
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The battle for Saku is actually one for Marsabit Town, also considered as the political capital of upper Eastern Province.
The constituency, about 15sqkm, is the smallest in northern Kenya but among the most densely populated.
It is home to more than 150,000 Borana, Rendille, Gabra and the Burji among other dominant rival tribes. Some of the voters in the neighbouring North Horr and Laisamis also live here.
Residents prefer it because of its mountain climate and arable land.
Actual registered voters as of last February, stand at 15,877. This is up from 14, 211 in the 2002 General Election. Eligible voters this time around, who failed to register due to lack of identity cards are estimated at 20,846.
The major issue in the constituency is the frequent clashes between the Borana and Gabra. The Borana and Rendille had been at loggerheads with their neighbours since 2003.
The differences led to the death of five Rendille school children, the brutal murder of Chief Ali Nyencho and the Turbi massacre of last July 12. The other major issue is that of plot allocation in Marsabit Town.
The late Sasura revoked the allocation of prime plots and sale of council houses. He had accused the late North Horr MP of the irregular sale.
The seat has attracted four heavyweights - the late Abdi Tari Sasura's elder brother, Hussein, Jarso Jillo Falana and Halake Dida Waqo, all eyeing the Narc-Kenya ticket and Umuro Wario who will be on a Kanu ticket.
Wario accompanied retired Moi during his recent visit to Marsabit where he had gone to console families and constituents of the four area MPs who died in a plane crash last April 10.
The three contenders from Narc-Kenya are however, considered stronger than their Kanu counterpart Wario, who hails from the minority Gabra.
Others considered, as minnows are Aila Danabo Woshe, a Burji and Abdirizak Adichare from the Rendille.
All the aspirants place security from within Marsabit and external one from southern Ethiopia as their priority if elected.
Following a marathon meeting involving Borana, Burji and Rendille supporters, the elder Sasura this week identified Narc-Kenya as his people's party of choice. Not even former President Moi could woo him to run on a Kanu ticket.
"It is final. I have met senior officials from both Kanu and Narc-Kenya. After consultations, my supporters asked me to vie on the Government party ticket," said the elder Sasura.
Falana, a perennial rival of the late Sasura, had also indicated he would vie on Narc-Kenya.
Sources said that Hussein is backed by the minister for Regional Development Mohammed Abdi Mohamud (Wajir East), while Falana is a key ally of the minister for Youth Affairs Dr Mohammed Abdi Kuti (Isiolo North).
Kuti is reportedly supporting Falana (from Borana's Digalu-Matari clan) because he personally wants the Digalu-Matari votes in his home turf of Isiolo against Hussein's Dambe-Nono, a minority compared to their rivals.
Mohamud is a Somali from North-Eastern Province and there seems to be no political mileage for him in his constituency because there are no Borana votes in Wajir East.
Waqo, who ran against Falana in the 1992 General Election on a Kenya National Congress party, comes from the dominant Karayu clan. His clan who call the shots in Isiolo and Moyale districts have never produced an MP in Saku. In an interview in Saku, Waqo said he would also contest on any of the Narc factions.
"It could be Narc-Kenya, Narc or any other affiliate of the party that is currently members of the Kibaki Government," he said.
Hussein was a political ally of Falana, just like his younger brother between 1992 and 1997. He was Falana's chief political campaigner and was rewarded with a post of a DO.
Prior to that, the elder Sasura was a primary school teacher and senior official of the teachers union and cooperative society.
After the late Sasura ousted Falana as Saku MP, Hussein went for studies at the Kenyatta University where he graduated with a Bachelor of Education degree in 2001.
He joined the City Council of Nairobi as a lecturer at the City Centre for Early Childhood Education for pre-school teachers, a position he is yet to leave.
Sources say that Hussein crossed with his late brother and had actually planned to contest against him in 2007.
But Hussein denied that there was a major rift between him and the deceased. "It was a minor differences. We differed politically. But no one can say there was bad blood between us, I actually assisted him start a business in Maralal," said Hussein.
By and large, a cross- section of elders from the late Sasura's political camp settled on him to continue with the mantle of Abdi.
The elder Sasura expects to garner votes previously enjoyed by the late MP as sympathy votes.
He also chose Narc-Kenya to get the financial muscle and the goodies that trickle down to the voters from the Government.
The late Sasura, a former chief of Dirib Gombo location earned his support from the non-Borana voters after wittingly acting as a voice of reason between the warring Borana and Burji.
Then the MP was Falana when the Burji were threatened with expulsion to Burji Hills in southern Ethiopia and the Borana were asked to take over their property in Marsabit Town.
Here the Burji and Rendille always provide the necessary swing votes. Their candidates, this time Danabo and the son of the late Haji Adichare are not expected to stage a fierce battle as was their predecessors'.
Their respective community cannot win on their own and prefer to go into alliance with a Borana candidate.
In the last two general elections, the campaign periods were marred with violence and scores of people were injured. This time around the same trend is expected.
A recent meeting by Borana elders to discuss the by-elections was marred with violence after Falana asked the District Security Committee to disperse the gathering. Supporters of Hussein and Falana clashed forcing anti-riot police to disperse the combatants.
The voting pattern in the 2002 General Election saw the late Sasura getting most of the Rendille votes and a sizeable portion of the Borana share.
Falana got some Borana votes and most of the Gabra votes.
Like his younger brother, Hussein has gone into alliance with the Rendille and Burji knowing that without them, it would be near impossible to see the door of the Parliament. Hussein will sail through if he manages to retain the alliance pattern crafted by his late brother.
The late Sasura polled 5,718 votes as Falana got 4, 470 votes. But in the referendum, the voting pattern was different where the Orange team led by Falana got 4,583 while the Banana side where the late Sasura spearheaded the fight managed a close 3,418 vote.
Falana has a modest education background and also benefited immensely from political and financial support from the late Dr Godana to get to Parliament in 1987.
After the 2002 general election, he was appointed as a director of the State-owned Kenya Broadcasting Corporation.
He became famous through the Inter-Parties Parliamentary Group that came with the minimal amendment to the Constitution prior to the 1997 general elections.
Falana must do all he can to get at least three-quarters of the Borana votes then make sure that the Gabra vote for him to a man and hope that some Burji and Rendille deny Hussein their votes to be certain that he recaptures the seat.
The third candidate, Waqo holds a Masters degree in Sociology from the University of Nairobi and a B.Ed from Kenyatta University. He has been in the NGO world for the last 15 years and worked for among others Oxfam (GB) and Action-Aid International at management level. His field of specialty included community development, conflict resolution and advocacy.
"I am an experienced leader with political experience. Unlike my rivals, all who are tainted I am clean and experienced in conflict resolution. That's why Saku people urged me to contest," he said in an interview.
However, he is criticised for being aloof and out of touch with the local community especially last year when the constituency was engulfed in clashes.
If all these three heavyweights stand on Narc-Kenya ticket, then one of them can as well be counted as the next MP for Saku because Wario, who will ran on Kanu comes from the minority Gabra.
Umuro holds an MBA in Finance an accountant by profession. He has worked with Price Water House Coopers among other institutions and currently runs his own accounting firm in Nairobi.
Wario's hope of getting the seat would be possible only in a scenario where Borana divide their votes and the non Borana-Gabra, Rendille and Burji vote as a block for him. That would be a tall order to achieve.
Chances are that the losers in the Narc-nomination would seek to go into the election proper on another party's ticket.

Original_Search_Title: How Mzee Kategaya Got His Groove Back
Headline: Uganda: How Mzee Kategaya Got His Groove Back
Body_Text:
Kampala — In the late 1990s, celebrated American writer Terry McMillan gained new fame after she published a fictionalised account of her romance and marriage to a charming man 23 years her junior.
How Stella Got Her Groove Back became a bestseller, but Americans-- and readers elsewhere--- were shocked last year when it was reported that Jonathan Plummer, McMillan's Jamaican husband of six years, was gay after all.
It obviously became a made-for Hollywood story after McMillan filed for divorce, saying the marriage was a "fraud". McMillan, who for a while seemed to have got her groove back, had lost it all over again.
So news that 'comrade' Eriya Kategaya is back to the NRM fold has rekindled my memories of the rich-in-shame McMillan story.
After three years in the opposition, the old man probably feels he has his groove back with his childhood friend, Yoweri Museveni. But while McMillan had the opportunity to walk out and maybe find her groove elsewhere, it is unlikely that Kategaya would return to find solace amongst the opposition ranks should Museveni turn out to be the political equivalent of Plummer.
It is, therefore, understandable when opposition politicians and some commentators begin to say that Kategaya is politically dead; that he has made it clear he will forever serve Museveni, even when it is evident all is not well.
Of course, most of the commentaries have been unfair to Kategaya, considering that he has an inherent right to join a political organisation of his choice.
But his U-turn from the FDC, where he served as special party envoy, makes much sense when it is put into a more refined context. One of the legacies of the Museveni regime has been to psyche Ugandans into the belief that the 'revolution' that culminated into the capture of Kampala in January 1986, was in fact the best thing that ever happened to this country.
Ugandans of my age, who have known only one president - Museveni- have been sucked into comparisons between his 20-year reign and the alleged rogue governments that preceded the NRM.
Museveni has largely been marketed as a fatherly figure who, while he may not be nearly as tall and jolly as dictator Idi Amin, cares enough for children to send them to school en masse.
Many Ugandans will not mind if Museveni threatens to close down an independent media house because they believe he is a charming man who will soon have it reopened anyway. For two decades, it has worked well for Museveni, effectively creating a citizenry that is given to so much make-believe.
The results have been devastating for the young generation who find that Museveni is the hero where there had never been one before. Most of us believe in Museveni as a bread-winner even when we don't have money to spend on dinner or transport to work the next day.
We consider FDC leader Kizza Besigye a potential hero even when he shares Museveni's militaristic belligerence. Because we have no real heroes to look forward to, we have been reduced to a society of political believers waiting for those imaginary goodies that may never come. Museveni, as we are now told, is supposed to be our Mzee, a bush war hero whose bonna bagaggawale (wealth for all) plan can take us to El Dorado.
Ugandans, in the quest for 'salvation', have ended up wearing essanja (dry banana leaves) and idolising Mzee.
Why would Kategaya-whether he made the move for material reasons or not- embarrass himself and his family by rejoining a government he spoke so passionately against?
Why would a respected man throw away, in a few days of political illusion, all his integrity before a populace that once treated him in the highest esteem? Many critics have given it to Museveni, saying he pulled off a political masterstroke.
I agree with such assessment, but not to the extent to which Kategaya is accused of betraying his conscience. He did what the average Ugandan would have done. As former de facto No 2 of a perceived success story, who was Kategaya not to believe in what Museveni's vision can achieve in the next five years and beyond?
We may never get the details of what happened in those State House meetings that made way for Kategaya's return. I can imagine him sharing a good cry with Mzee after being reminded of those good old days. We, once again, cannot know what promises Museveni made to him, but can only conjure up images of vows between the two childhood pals. Oh yes, we believe something must have happened.
The author is a journalist

Original_Search_Title: Mantu And His 'Humpty Dumpty'
Headline: Nigeria: Mantu And His 'Humpty Dumpty'
Body_Text:
THERE is a cynical tinge in the way the Nigerian media portrays the Deputy Senate President, Alhaji Ibrahim Nasir Mantu, that evokes the image of that Nursery rhyme persona: "Humpty Dumpty". The fabled character was a patchwork put together by its creator. It suffered the indiscretion of sitting on the wall where it dozed off, forgetting its fragile status. It lost its balance and came crashing, headlong.
The image that the Nigerian media is painting of Alhaji Mantu, whose third term project crashed on the floor of National Assembly on May 15, 2006, is pitiable to say the least. For a man who openly told Nigerians that the third term project was the wish of millions of Nigerians, the interplay of the events that heralded the aborted constitutional coup d'etat and the post mortem arising thereafter tends to make a funny joke on the distinguished Plateau State born-Senator . In fact, it will be proper to play back the video recording of Alhaji Mantu's presentation of the Joint Constitution Review Committee report, after its controversial sitting in Port Harcourt.
Millions of Nigerians had watched with subdued frustration as Alhaji Mantu and his fellow third term campaigners hoisted a bizarre theatre of absurd on the proposed amendment of the 1999 Constitution.
It was like a Picaroon character hoisting a terrifying nightmare on the psyche of its sleep walking victim as Alhaji Mantu claimed that millions of people fully and whole-heartedly endorsed the proposed amendment of the 1999 Constitution and in the process approved the idea of giving President Obasanjo extended tenure in office. What Alhaji Mantu tried to sell to the world, was the smartest political scam that has yet emerged in Nigeria's political history. It must be remembered that the work of JCRC was characterized by street protests, deep-seated divisions within the JCRC team, allegations of financial impropriety, legal intrigues and open intimidations of opponents of the dubious agenda. This did not deter Alhaji Mantu from hurriedly putting in place a report which formed the basis for the debate on the amendments of the 1999 Constitution in the National Assembly.
To be fair to Alhaji Mantu; Nigerians had since 1999 clamoured for a review of the document which did not meet the aspiration of the country and its citizens.
The military-sired Constitution was flawed in several ways which prompted the setting up of several constitution review committees by both the National Assembly and President Obasanjo.
The Senate and House of Reps Committees traversed the country to collate views of Nigerians on the subject matter. There were advertisements of the entire constitution on the pages of newspapers and magazines to afford Nigerians the opportunity to make their input into the document. Then the Clement Ebri Committee surfaced and equally re-enacted the entire scenario. In the end all the views, data and vital materials collated were dumped on the shelf, to gather dust. The political conmen recognized the hubris of Mr. President. That was the genesis of the Humpty Dumpty project. The National Political Reform Conference was hurriedly put in place and financed from unknown quarters. The plot to smuggle in the Aso Rock-authored constitution into the conference agenda met with stiff resistance which prompted the emergence of the JCPC led by Ibrahim Mantu.
Before Mantu's Humpty Dumpty project crashed, no fewer than 40 Senators allegedly collected gratification while at least 200 members of the House of Representatives had also collected their own loot. The nation's treasury had bled out over N18 billion on a venture that was designed to promote the personal ego of public office holders.
In the words of Hon. Abike Dabiri, "if third term project was a the popular wish of Nigerians, why was the Chief Priest of Peoples Democratic Party the President, and states Governors dolling out huge some of money to bribe law makers to endorse it".
For a project which Alhaji Mantu described as a popular wish of the people, it is outrageous to think that the entire process of selling the scam became a money guzzling and brutish affair.Bayelsa State Governor Dr. Goodluck Jonathan was so enthusiastic about Mantu's political gamble that he bought brand new Peugeot 607 cars far each of the Bayelsan Lawmakers in the National Assembly. Some State Governors in the South South were made to cough out as much as N1billion between March and April, to finance the third term project.
Transcorp, an amorphous business group became the revving third term to finance the third term project. In fact, it would seem that the National Assembly has gone against "the popular wish of the people" in killing the Constitution Amendment Bill. Ironically, Alhaji Mantu could not raise his voice in the Senate chambers, to support his pet project. Where then lies scruples and integrity in Senator Mantu's political career?
First, let it be stated here that the Deputy Senate President ran a dirty battle with Malam Nasir El-Rufai over N50 million bribery allegations to get a confirmation of Malam Rufai's ministerial. The name of Senator Mantu featured in another bribery allegation of N50 million to influence the approval of the Ministry of Education budget. This was the banana pill that tripped Senator Adolphus Wabara from the Senate President's job. The Governor of Plateau State Mr, Joshua Dariye, mentioned the name of Alhaji Mantu as a recipient of N100million from the contentious ecological fund to the Middle Belt State. The 2006 Haji Operation which Alhaji Mantu was the Chief Co-ordinator was shody and tainted with further allegation of mishandling of funds amounting to over N240 million. There is a storm from his home front where members of his constituency are calling for his recall At the Port Harcourt sitting of JCPC, another eyebrow was raised over huge sums of money running into million s.
Even in the heat of the third term debate, Senator Mantu was the cynosure of his colleagues who tried to establish cases of wrong doing in the N90 million he received for his accommodation and furniture. Granted that these allegations have not been proven in a court of Law, the truth is: Why should the distinguished Senator constantly be seen in dim light in every of his activities? One question that Alhaji Mantu should ask himself is why must his name and political career be trailed by sleaze and inglorious pre-occupations with dirty deals as we saw in his days as the National Publicity Secretary of the defunct CNC and now in the Obasanjo political catacomb?
Like the Haji operations, the third term project anchored by Senator Mantu, failed woefully.
But the painful aspect is that the entire scheme was nothing but a civilian coup plot, designed by the ruling PDP to illegitimately hold political power. Yet, Alhaji Mantu whose political pedigrees in the days of General Sani Abacha did not glorify the role the embattled Senator played in the ill-fated Obasanjo third term charade. Senator Mantu who chaired the Palliative Committee, did not see "the popular will of the people", that political office should be used to uplift the living conditions of the people. Rather his understanding of the entire concept of power and the use of it, is that it should be used, to impoverish the masses that elected public officers into office.
After this political fiasco, where lies Alhaji Mantu's in Nigeria's political history? It is a worrisome prospect to those who saw Alhaji Mantu's face on television when the third term Amendment Bill was quashed. The Deputy Senate President's puffy eyes and dropping lips cut a pitiable image of a man whose name is destined to conjure a dreadful picture in public memory in years to come. It is the same fate that has engulfed past Nigerian leaders with a track record of trying to hoodwink the people. For daring to fool Nigerian's with this Humpty-Dumpty project, Alhaji Mantu's place in Nigeria's political history is bound to be uncharitable.

Original_Search_Title: MP's Bodyguard Shoots Rival in Election Chaos
Headline: Kenya: MP's Bodyguard Shoots Rival in Election Chaos
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Liberal Democratic Party members restrain an official after a group of youths tried to force their way into the Rainbow House, Nairobi, where they had gone to file election results.
The Liberal Democratic Party elections nearly turned tragic when an MP's bodyguard shot a man to rescue his boss from a hostile crowd.
A bullet fired by former Trade assistant minister Zaddock Syongoh's bodyguard struck the hand of Sammy Aoro, one of the men who had defeated the MP in Gwassi. District Officer Tom Siele said the bodyguard had been arrested over the incident.
Aoro, who was elected the branch vice-chairman, was treated and discharged. Syongoh's camp lost to a group allied to Langata MP Raila Odinga's political strategist, Caroli Omondi. It was the second incident after a bodyguard turned to the gun to rescue another MP besieged by an angry crowd at the weekend. Karachuonyo MP Dr Adhu Awiti's bodyguard fired in the air to rescue him from opponents who had floored him with kicks and blows.
In Mvita, confusion reigned with presidential hopeful Najib Balala insisting he was the legitimate winner despite his rival, Ms Afia Rama, being declared winner. Balala blamed the LDP Coast provincial co-coordinator Michael Tinga for his defeat. He claimed Tinga was a "Banana supporter" in last year's referendum on the proposed constitution and had no business in LDP.
Newly elected Lurambi branch chairman David Malala is carried shoulder-high after he was announced the winner at Bukhungu Stadium, Kakamega.
In the North Rift, the election turned out to be a popularity contest between Raila and his Mwingi North counterpart, Kalonzo Musyoka. In the Eldoret North, Julius Kitur lost to Moses Kiter, while in Eldoret South John Sambu, was elected the chairman, Daniel Simatwa secretary and Eliud Mutai as treasurer. In Emgwen, rtd Maj-Gen Augustine Cheruiyot and his team were elected branch officials, while former Cabinet minister Lina Kilimo swept the board in Marakwet East.
Parallel exercise
In Likoni, former Mombasa mayor Masoud Mwalimu Mwahima was elected branch chairman in relatively peaceful elections a day after Hamisi Mwidau was elected unopposed in a parallel exercise at the Anglican Church of Kenya (ACK) Hall in Likoni.
Poor turnout marred Kiharu branch elections as two rival camps conducted the polls at different venues. The camp allied to Rosalind Wairimu Kori convened at the official General Ihura Stadium, while that of Murang'a LDP chairman Karuri Mwangi was at Court Yard Bar.
In Mathioya branch, the election of delegates went on smoothly with those allied to MP Joseph Kamotho carrying the day. A Kamotho ally and former Gitugi councillor, Mwangi Kabuba, was elected chairman, Njumbi councillor Teresia Wanjiru Kiura women's leader, Walter Ngari youth leader, and Gachanja Kiragu treasurer in an exercise supervised by Maina Wanjii.
Kalonzo's allies won in Machakos District. In Kangundo, area MP Moffat Maitha was elected branch chairman in chaotic polls that left two people seriously injured. Maitha trounced his main rival, Nduya Muthama, during the stormy polls at Tala Boys Primary School.
Chaos broke out when youths allied to Muthama forced their way into the venue before police repulsed them. In Machakos Town, MP Daudi Mwanzia was elected chairman, while in Masinga MP Itwiku Mbai met stiff challenge from former MP Ronald Kiluta.
In Yatta Constituency, MP Charles Kilonzo beat former Knut national treasurer Peter Mutulu to become the branch chairman. In Kilome, area MP Mutinda Mutiso joined the list of MPs who have lost the grassroots elections.
The party's election co-ordinator in Eastern Province, Peter Mutinga, declared Mutiso's rival, Regina Ndambuki, the winner.

Original_Search_Title: Kanu's Secret Minutes On ODM
Headline: Kenya: Kanu's Secret Minutes On ODM
Body_Text:
Nairobi — These are excerpts of the verbatim report of the proceedings of Kanu's May 24, 2006, national executive committee meeting.
Present: Hon. Uhuru Kenyatta (Chairman), Hon. Henry Kosgey (Vice Chairman, Party Organisation), Hon. Chrysanthus Okemo (Vice Chairman, Finance and Administration), Dalmas Otieno (Vice Chairman, Research and Policy), Katana Ngala (Vice Chairman, Information and Publicity), Hon. William Ruto (Secretary General), Hon. Billow Kerrow (Treasurer), Hon. Gideon Ndambuki (Organising Secretary), Joseph Nkaissery (Deputy Secretary General), Hon. Maoka Maore (Deputy Secretary General), Albert Ekirapa (Deputy Treasurer), Hon. Musa Sirma (Secretary, Environment), Howard Mburu (Secretary, Legal and Constitutional Affairs), Badi Ali (Secretary, Youth Affairs), Dr Josphine Ojiambo (Secretary, Gender Affairs), Manson Nyamweya (Deputy Organising Secretary), Shem Onyango Kwega (Secretary, International Affairs), Esther Njogu (Women Representative), John Kihumba (Provincial Representative, Central), Hon. Ali Wario (Provincial Representative, Coast), Basil Khalumi (Secretary, Labour), Siyat Osman (Provincial Representative, North Eastern), Hon. Justin Muturi (Chief Whip and Parliamentary Group Secretary).
The report of the committee mandated to look into possibilities of the party entering into coalitions before the next election was tabled by Dr Josphine Ojiambo before the council for adoption.
She elaborated the recommendations of the committee that the party was not yet fully equipped to enter into any coalition. The chairman proposed that the report be adopted and opened the floor for contributions.
Hon. Ruto: Mr Chairman, I don't identify with some aspects of what the committee has recommended. I think what we need to develop as a position is that Kanu will not go into the next elections when there is no chance of us winning.
(Ruto went ahead to take great exception to party members' reactions of to his declaration that he will vie for the presidency through Kanu and then ODM. He said the mood in the country at the moment does not allow for a definite position as that will divide members. He asked those opposed to him to stop their insulting language. He said Moi was doing a lot of damage in the pretence that he was helping the party. He emphasised that Kanu is finished without ODM.)
Hon. Ndambuki: The proposal that we come out of ODM is very dangerous for several people. Regional politics are different from national politics. In Ukambani, it's difficult to survive politically if we say we are out of ODM. I personally do not think I can come back to Parliament. Let's be serious about the political reality (on the ground).
Hon. Okemo: Mr Chairman, I fully agree that we need to strengthen ourselves before we enter into a coalition. But we are doing nothing about it. We just sit there and say we are a national party. Where I come from, the idea of ODM has sunk into people's minds. It is difficult for me to preach another story because I represent a constituency. Let us not just hit out at ODM. It is noble.
Nyamweya: I think several things are not clear. First, we are already in ODM. As much as I don't see the wisdom in moving out, I also cannot understand how we got there. Second, there is the issue of Ruto's candidature which I don't want to comment on. We need guidance. If we are moving out of ODM, someone must tell us why we will do so. If we are staying, then we need to position ourselves well so that we can remain relevant in the entity.
Onyango: Mr Chairman, we need to know where we have come from. During the referendum, Kanu people on the ground were completely forgotten. In Luo Nyanza, we could not speak because the entire system was LDP (Liberal Democratic Party).
For sure, people like me will be uncomfortable with LDP because it means I will never get a nomination certificate. We need proper consultation before certain actions can be taken. Mr Chairman, is it too late to build ourselves?
Dr. Ojiambo: Chair, I think the debate is becoming interesting. We are officials of Kanu. How can we go to the electorate and tell them that we are now not able to fulfil the mandate for which they elected us? We are a strong party. I think we go wrong on two things. One, officials sitting with us have gone and announced major decisions of the party without consulting us. We are supreme as an organ of the party. I think Chair did well to call us. We must emerge from this meeting with a position. Secondly, the candidature of our secretary general was not in good faith. A lot of bad language has come out as a result. We are dividing the party because of personal ambitions! I think we need to unite for the sake of Kanu.
Hon. Maore: There is a club of mad men to which we are being lured. The political theatrics of certain characters are now open for all to see. We are told that Kanu is weak. No one is telling us where we need to improve, all they want us to believe is that we cannot win elections. That is rubbish. What coalition are we talking about? Let us prepare ourselves to win. We have 19 months. If we are near the tape and we see someone ahead of us, then we can negotiate. For now, what are we fearing? I was in DP (Democratic Party) and we were 22 and we believed we could win the General Election. We need to be positive about ourselves.
Dalmas: We are wrong in many areas. First, we are unwilling to walk the legal rope. Clearly any coalition before the election must be the result of negotiations so that a stable structure can result. The 2002 merger with NDP was a result of many months of talks. Then parties dissolved into one. Everything was going well until that bad October. The issue is, coalitions are only formed after elections, for heaven's sake. If it is before, it has got to be a merger. There is no record anywhere in the world of where coalitions were formed before elections. The only example is Kenya, and it failed. Those who contributed to its failure are now coming to us for the same thing. I'm bitter when I remember 2002. We were building a big party. I spent my own money to establish candidates in Luo Nyanza. When they decamped they swept along every one. I lost money and hope in that year. Kanu was destabilised and there was no time to salvage anything. Guys, we are joking!
Hon. Kerrow: First I'm not happy with both the Chairman and the Secretary General. They are the two topmost officials. When I see the Secretary General insulting the Chairman, I feel sorry. Why should a presidential bid be the reason to insult your boss? Chairman has also not been kind to all of us. We should not be where we are now. The reason I have supported ODM is because I don't see ourselves moving anywhere. What do we tell our members? Is it what the Secretary General says or what the Chairman says?
Hon. Ruto: I think I need to clarify something. People have mistaken by declaration to run for the presidency. When I did that, it was because Kanu was losing Rift Valley to Kalonzo Musyoka. I could not sit and watch. I met the Chairman later and tried to explain. In fact, I also went to (retired President Daniel) Moi to explain, only to be turned away at the gate. Chairman stopped calling me or even picking my calls. So if I had sat quietly, Rift Valley would be with Kalonzo by now. Is that what we want? I am willing to lay aside my ambitions so we build the party. We are still friends with Chairman so we still can sort out where we are wrong.
Hon. Muturi: I think we need to capture the truth at this point. ODM is not a creation of mwananchi as alleged by some. It is someone's gimmick to throw us down. We are riding on a wave that no longer is - the referendum wave. I have heard people asking, how many vote did you bring? That was not a General Election. In Siakago, I won a civic seat not withstanding the fact that many people voted Banana. Orange and Banana were ECK (Electoral Commission of Kenya) symbols, not a mwananchi invention. I've heard that ODM was given the mandate to write a new constitution. Where was that mandate given and by whom? I insist that ODM is amorphous and represents the unknown. I'm sorry we are not alive to the reality.
Hon. Kosgey: I think we are being misled about what the situation on the ground is like. People have been going round preaching a certain message, then they say people have spoken. Which people have spoken? Even in Rift Valley, they have found it difficult to preach the ODM message. That is why they are attacking Moi. But Moi must also respect our capacity to make decisions. He must not come too much or he will face a backlash. He should be told to go slow. The young boys singing about ODM should know they will get tired. Let us dialogue.
Hon. Dalmas: I propose as a way forward that the top officials - the four vice chairs, the treasurer, the organising secretary, the secretary general and the chairman - meet and reconcile and then report in two weeks.
Hon. Ruto: I have no problem with that. The sooner the better. I don't want people to keep saying that Ruto is killing the party.
Uhuru: I have kept my mouth shut since January. I have not commented at all about ODM, and about Ruto's declaration.
When Muturi issued a statement, I denounced it because I did not think it was the best thing to say at the time. But when the debate raged, I opened my mouth. Now there is fire all over, as if I was supposed to play the fool.
It is true that Ruto spoke to me, but I could not reconcile his story with what they were saying on podiums. Honesty is important.
Kanu and LDP came together to defeat a bad constitution. As a party, I felt that was the best thing to do. I sold that position to my people in Central Province even when the ground was hostile. That was a sacrifice.
People have said that Kanu cannot win alone. If as Chairman I persuade myself that that is so, then, honest to God I'd rather resign rather than fail the people who elected me! Every one of us in support of that school of thought must be willing to resign so that delegates can elect people who can lead them to government. What is so hard for us? We were 68 in 2003. We only need another 40 members plus half-a-million votes and we are there! Is that too difficult?
On the by-elections, I remember we failed to field a candidate at Kasipul Kapondo to favour LDP. Now they are saying we field ODM candidates in Upper Eastern. These were Kanu seats. They should leave them to Kanu then we can debate about Nakuru Town.
Moi should be left alone to exercise his democratic rights. Of course he should speak as Moi but not as Kanu. People are saying that Kanu will fail because Moi is bad. If he is that bad, or that unpopular, why are they not celebrating the fact that he is destroying Kanu? If he is destroying ODM, then that ODM is worth nothing as a bad man can bring it down. Let us be honest.
On ODM, I know what it was during the referendum. But what is it now? What is its agenda to mwananchi? That amorphous thing is empty of any substance. We have time to build Kanu. If the need arises, we will talk to any party that wants to join us. But we should not cry to join others. We need a strong party. We must build it.

Original_Search_Title: Nnamani As an Anti-Third Term Hero
Headline: Nigeria: Nnamani As an Anti-Third Term Hero
Body_Text:
BEFORE the historic Senate debate that consigned the Constitution Amendment Bill, a.k.a tenure elongation, into the dustbin of history, the tenure of Senator Ken Nnamani as President of the Upper legislative chamber of the National Assembly was largely regarded as dull and unremarkable. The Senate President was seen in many quarters as a man treading rather too carefully so that he would not slip on the so-called "banana peel" that floored his predecessors.
