For some observers, the ignoble and indeed humiliating crash of his immediate predecessor, Senator Wabara, was more than enough warning signal to Senator Nnamani that a similar fate awaited him should he step out of line. By this, he must be willing and prepared to do the Presidency's bidding at all times, especially with respect to the whims and caprices of an overbearing President Olusegun Obasanjo who was accused in several quarters of nursing third term ambition.
With this in mind, the Senate President was expected to toe the line by pandering to the wishes of the President and his overzealous yes men. But while this was the thinking among some observers, there were several others who expected Senator Nnamani to be his own man by taking a stand that should call the bluff of the Presidency on matters that pertain to the defence of democracy and the rule of law in the overall interest of the Nigerian nation and its people.
This expectation was based on the realisation that unlike his predecessors, except the late Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, this Senate President was not an imposition of the executive arm of the government. It will be recalled that following the removal of Senator Wabara as Senate President after he became enmeshed in a bribery scandal, the Senate acting through a greater majority of senators had shunned all tempting overtures from the Presidency and the ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) to elect as their President a man they gave the impression they could trust.
Senator Nnamani has since then been trying to give the impression that he understood the enormity of the confidence reposed on him by his fellow senators. This he has shown by both the diplomatic and direct manner he distances himself from the executive on policy decisions generally perceived as being anti-people.
So, it was little surprising that when the contentious issue of tenure elongation for President Obasanjo became a subject of public discourse, the Senate President did not hide his objection to it. Disturbed by Senator Nnamani's position in this respect, the third term campaigners had zeroed in on majority of senators with the intention of influencing them to support a pro-tenure elongation bill whenever it was tabled for debate before the National Assembly.
Using the carrot and stick method, these bigwigs of the Presidency and the PDP were able to divide the senate along the line of those in support and those opposed to tenure elongation which they cleverly couched as a Constitution Amendment Bill to hoodwink majority of senators to support it. Where mere persuasion based on party affiliation failed to work its desired magic, money, huge amount of it, was reportedly deployed to encourage senators to mortgage their conscience and betray majority of Nigerians who had made it clear that they were stridently opposed to any tenure extension for President Obasanjo.
With the widely publicised antecedent of the Senate as a gathering of greedy senators who are prepared to sell their conscience for a plate of pottage, most observers and indeed Nigerians were understandably apprehensive that the pro-third term campaigners may have their way in the National Assembly. Still fresh in the memory of quite a lot of Nigerians was the case of senators making outrageous demand for furniture and other allowances at the early stage of the Senate as well as several cases of bribe scandals involving some senators.
It was against this backdrop that many felt that the anti-third term battle was heading for a defeat in the Senate, especially with the huge amount of money being dangled before senators. So, came Tuesday May 16, 2006 when the Senate began debate on the Constitution Amendment Bill, the whole country had waited with bated breath and apprehension for the upper legislative house position on the contentious matter.
But Nigerians did not have long to wait. Just before deliberations commenced on the matter, the Senate President had delivered an eloquent and moving speech which signposted the direction most senators may vote. In that speech, Senator Nnamani had enjoined his colleagues to vote according to their conscience in the overall interest of Nigerians without fear or favour.
According to him: "I have thought carefully about the process and politics of amending the Constitution. I have come to the conclusion that the historic responsibility, which the Almighty God has placed on my shoulders as the President of the Senate at this moment in our history, is to uphold the truth, justice and principles of the rule of law".
Senator Nnamani had followed this up by taking up the noble responsibility bringing further proceedings on the Constitution Amendment Bill to an end, thus sending millions of Nigerians into wild jubiliation. With this development, many have come to see the Senate President as a national hero. In fact, it is reported that he is already been courted as a prospective vice presidential material. Only recently, it was reported that he was a possible running mate to former military president, General Ibrahim Babangida who is nursing presidential ambition against 2007, though he has denied this. But the prevailing feeling is that in politics anything is possible.

Original_Search_Title: Weighing Museveni's Cabinet On Wrong Scale
Headline: Uganda: Weighing Museveni's Cabinet On Wrong Scale
Body_Text:
Finally, the official list of nominees for President Yoweri Museveni's new cabinet reached Speaker Edward Ssekandi on Monday, ending weeks of endless speculation and unnecessary fevers.
As usual, it now transpires that most of the nominees were in fact not contacted before their names were sent to the Speaker. This says quite a lot about what the President thinks about his team. It is mostly a bunch of impoverished fellows at heart who would jump in joy at hearing that they have been named ministers. It doesn't matter which ministry.
Oh why, when Dr Speciosa Wandira Kazibwe was appointed Vice President, she told the nation she was happier on that day than on her wedding day (I don't know if the fact that she is now divorced confirms that).
The way both the public and politicians (and perhaps the media) have come to look at ministerial appointments is quite problematic.
We have reduced our judgements of these appointments to criteria such as old faces vs. new faces, gender, region, religion, and party loyalty.
Granted, all these factors should perhaps be important considerations (except old faces vs. new faces, which is peculiar to those countries where Presidents rule for more than 10 years), but lost in these criteria is the more important question of the ability of the appointees to deliver.
If Museveni had served for only 10 years, he could as well have retained 90 percent of his cabinet in his last term without many people raising eyebrows because the new president would have been expected to come in with his own team. Because he has made the presidency a life-time affair, Museveni now has to be judged on his ability to change his team.
But then if such change is in fact important, why is "no change" okay when it comes to the president?
Then there is the talk about regional balancing. While others are crying foul that western Uganda is overrepresented on the list of nominees, some in the western district of Bushenyi were reported to be demonstrating because their "woman", Mary Karooro Okurut, was not on the list. Meanwhile, the people of Rukungiri may soon send a delegation to State House, as the exit of their "patriotic" son Jim Muhwezi has left them with nobody in Cabinet.
But let's face it: Do districts really benefit from having one of their own in the Cabinet?
If they do it is because those ministers have abandoned their national duties and reduced themselves to local chiefs.
Unfortunately, the president continues encouraging this talk by assuring the endless delegations that visit him from the districts that he made their sons and daughters ministers. The message is if their districts remain embroiled in poverty and all the usual problems of our banana republics, they should blame it on their sons and daughters.
The same can be said about religion. I don't see how the Catholic community would seriously benefit from having several of their brethren in the Cabinet, unless those ministers decide to be sectarian in the exercise of their duties.
The only thing achieved by regional, religious and all sorts of balancing then, (at least ordinarily) is a sense of placating the diverse interests that want a piece of the national cake, but not actual delivery of the cake.
Those who care more about actual delivery of service, about how the president's election promises could be fulfilled, would pay more attention to the qualification of the appointees.
And here I am not referring to academic qualifications, which is unfortunately the key thing the Parliamentary Appointments Committee appears to consider. I am referring to the ability of nominees to deliver by virtue of their training, experience, competence, and integrity.
From my reading of the President's list, it appears that the first 25 nominees will be the substantive ministers (who sit in Cabinet) while the rest will be the ministers of state.
The first 25 include, in Museveni's order, Kirunda Kivejinja, Amama Mbabazi, Beatrice Wabudeya, Eriya Kategaya, Henry Kajura, Adolf Mwesige, Hilary Onek, Crispus Kiyonga, Tarsis Kabwegyere, Geraldine Namirembe Bitamazire, Ezra Suruma, and John Nasasira.
Others are Khiddu Makubuya, Daudi Migereko, Ruhakana Rugunda, Syda Bbumba, Janat Mukwaya, Maria Mutagamba, Omara Atubo, Stephen Malinga, Sam Kutesa, Ham Mulira, Kahinda Otafiire, Dorothy Hyuha, and Jane Kabakumba Matsiko.
Do these men and women have what it takes to deliver on Museveni's promise of prosperity for all? That is where the debate should focus.
To his credit, the President appears to have listened to those who were calling for the appointment of people with expertise and experience in certain areas, thus we have Ham Mulira, an ICT expert, who will most likely head the new Ministry of Information and Communication Technology.
Hopefully, Hilary Onek will take over from Syda Bbumba at energy. We should not care where he comes from. He brings more expertise on energy issues than Bbumba. Period!
It is a pity that the Appointments Committee vetting the nominees was given a list without the specifics of the ministries where the men and women who have been chosen by the President will work.
If Parliament really cared about service delivery, MPs would have made it a requirement for the President to submit the ministry where each nominee would work. Then the committee would make more informed decisions.
mwesige@yahoo.com

Original_Search_Title: Ogungbure Appeals to FIFA
Headline: Nigeria: Ogungbure Appeals to FIFA
Body_Text:
Lagos — Former Nigeria international Adewale Ogungbure has appealed to Fifa to use the World Cup to combat the scourge of racism.
The 24-year-old, who plays for German fourth division side FC Sachsen Leipzig, has been the frequent target of racist abuse from fans since joining the club last year.
Ogungbure hopes the staging of the World Cup finals in Germany will provide Fifa with the opportunity to crack down on racist fans.
"Now that all these nations are coming to Germany, to play together as one, this is the time to make clear that humanity and respect for others are part of the game," Ogungbure said.
Having been pelted with bananas and greeted with loud monkey chants when he steps on to the pitch, Ogungbure admitted that coping with the horrendous treatment has taken its toll on him.
"I am human, I am not an animal or a bimbo. I like my job, I want to play football but that's just got nothing to do with the game any more," Ogungbure said.

Original_Search_Title: Epitaph for a Blind Ambition
Headline: Nigeria: Epitaph for a Blind Ambition
Body_Text:
Lagos — And the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria 1999 (Amendment) Bill, 2006 died! We saw the death coming but there were a few things we did not know. We did not know that the process could ever have travelled on such a fast track. We did not also know that the death would be so comprehensive - from head to toe, more so, as our minds were already prepared for the type of thing they call straight line veto in budgeting which enables you to retain the items you like on a sub-head and reject the ones you do not like. What's more, the National Chairman of the Largest Party in Africa (LPA) had virtually threatened earlier to go to the Senate, chase out the boys and pass the Bill himself. One obvious advantage inherent in that type of Bill is that it would not require any presidential assent to become law. After all, is the party not supreme? What an innovation in legislative engineering!
Admittedly, it was the attempt to alter section 137 (1) (b) of the 1999 Constitution to extend the maximum tenure of the President from two to three terms of four years each that people were opposed to. This item had been cleverly sandwiched among 115 other items, some of which were absolutely desirable for the smooth operation of the Constitution. Among other things, the Bill had sought to enhance the autonomy of the Local Government Councils instead of leaving them as mere appendages of the State Governments.
Again, since February 2005 when the press stumbled into the tenure elongation proposal in the working papers of the National Political Reform Conference, that single item had succeeded in distorting the entire process and had also dwarfed all other issues in the Constitution. By throwing out the Amendment Bill, all items on the Bill died. To some, this action of the National Assembly is simply analogous with the case where you throw away the bath water, the baby and the bath tub.
It is also true that all other items on the dead Bill owed their existence to the elongation clause. Knowing what we know now, it is crystal clear that the author of the Bill, the 2005 Confab, was established purely with the tenure elongation agendum in mind. Since the President had no serious stake on the other items on the Bill, chances are that if the other items were passed, the President would look for one reason or another not to sign them into law. In essence, the National Assembly is right to assume that whoever is dying should be allowed to die quickly.
There is hope for this country. It is difficult to believe that even at the edge of doom; even in the face of all the heavy bribe money that was in circulation with other mouth-watering promises of green chip plot allocations in Abuja and all that, there are Nigerians who would say, WE STAND FOR THE TRUTH EVEN IF THE HEAVENS FALL. All those members of the National Assembly who stood relentlessly on the side of truth and who were, indeed, willing to die so that Nigeria might live, we salute you. We salute your sense of patriotism and we salute your courage.
In his "grave side oration", President Obasanjo dwelt exhaustively on media excesses as possible causes of the untimely death of the Amendment Bill. There is no gainsaying the fact that from the very beginning the war was a media one. For the 15 months or so during which the war raged, the media were in the forefront agitating most relentlessly that the will of the people must prevail over the oppressive desires of a cabal, thanks to the liberalization of media ownership in this country. The media lived up to their constitutional charge to "...uphold the fundamental objectives of the Constitution and uphold the responsibility and accountability of the Government to the people" (section 22 of the 1999 Constitution). In this singular act, our media men have amply demonstrated that the pen is mightier than the atomic bomb.
The war we just went through succeeded tremendously in making nonsense of President Obasanjo's anti-corruption crusade. From the very beginning, it has been asserted that the anti-corruption fight has been targeted at some selected audience. The tenure elongation bid unmasked this fact. It is difficult to understand how the EFCC could block its ears with cotton wool to all the allegations of bribery in the open market place of the National Assembly.
In the same way as a man should not be seen as writing his own testimonial, maybe we should help in re-establishing the very valid point raised by Senator Adolphous Wabara. Wabara was thinking aloud and wondering that the same sum of N50 million that himself plus four other Senators plus other members of the House of Representatives had their fingers chopped off is now being offered to each of hundreds of legislators to gain their support for the tenure elongation clause. And now, the watch dogs or rather, the anti-corruption crusaders have gone to sleep!
I overheard an argument the other day. Some held the view that the Naira has lost its value. If just a few months back, N50 million could put say 25 members of the National Assembly in deep trouble but today one member of the same National Assembly can just walk up to the lobby and pick up the same N50 million and walk away, then the Naira has gone bananas. Others argued that it was issue centredness. While the first N50 million was to facilitate budget passage for which no one had a strong stake, the other N50 million was meant to rig the President into power for life. The issues were not the same and that's the total difference. It has nothing to do with the value of the Naira.
One good question deserves another. We are aware that the bribe money in the Wabara saga was fully refunded to the treasury. How far have we gone with the bribe-for-Constitution-Amendment? We hope the EFCC will seek the refund of this money with the same speed it applied to the bribe-for-budget recovery. How much refund are we expecting? In this last leg of the bribe saga, we are expecting a minimum refund of N31.3 billion. The mathematics is quite simple. It assumes that there is no way they could have bribed less than the number needed to pass the measure. The calculation is, therefore, as follows: N(2/3 X 109 X 50) + (2/3 X 360 X 50) = N31.3 billion Q.E.D. Further investigations will beam some light on the slush fund involved at earlier stages. By the way, what was the source of the money? It is clear that the money was extra-budgetary. The Finance Minister has denied on her honour that the money came from the excess crude account. We are tempted to believe her. But what i s the source? It is clear that in the months ahead, the National Assembly has a lot of work in its hands. Whichever direction we look, we see full support for our earlier call for the establishment of a separate Federation Accounting Office as distinct from the Federal Government Accounting Office.
OBJ has addressed the PDP. We hope he does not stop at that. The era is gone when what was good for the PDP was good for the entire nation. The President owes this nation an explanation and a full apology for taking us on a wild goose chase for 15 months during which period, governance was put on hold, with the attendant rot and waste of billions of Naira of the tax payers' money.
Now that the tenure extension bid is off the way, the time has come for the President to point the INEC Chairman, Prof Iwu to order. We hear that after the National Assembly has thrown the e-voting system through the window he is quoted as saying that he is going ahead with the system since the issue of the method of voting to be adopted is purely administrative. God loves this country. That is hardly a good reason for tasking His patience by courting catastrophe to ourselves!

Original_Search_Title: War On Terror: U.S. Hypocrisy Astonishing
Headline: Kenya: War On Terror: U.S. Hypocrisy Astonishing
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is not entirely surprising that the self-righteous Bush regime should accuse Kenya of doing too little in the war on terror. Nor is Washington's bloated arrogance ever concealed when it accuses our courts of failing to convict the Kikambala bombing suspects of terrorism charges or for closing down the extra-judicial Joint Task Force on Terrorism.
For a democratic country such as ours, the US government's statement smacks of hypocrisy and is a blatant contempt of our Judiciary.
Kenya is not a banana republic that can be micro-managed from Washington, even though, as a nation vitiated by massive corruption, our rulers pester the US for handouts to feed its hungry. Even then, we cherish our dignity as a nation.
In my view, our Government has gone out of its way to harass its citizens in the past two years by arresting, detaining, beating and violating their rights, under pressure from Washington.
Many Muslims in the Coast, North Eastern and Nairobi have been persecuted on the flimsy excuse of being terror suspects. A ragtag outfit - the so-called Anti-Terror Police Unit - has been fleecing businesses belonging to ethnic Somalis and Arabs on the claim that they finance terrorists.
No one ever gets charged in court and, when they do, there are no convictions because the charges are fundamentally based on prejudice and stereotyping, not facts.
Kenyans rejected the anti-terrorism Bill because it was unconstitutional, discriminatory and an unnecessary burden. For a country struggling with major insecurity problems - robbery, murder, cattle-rustling and ethnic conflict - it can ill-afford to fight a proxy war for the Bush regime.
The gross violation of human rights by the US government under the pretext of fighting terrorism has eroded its international image as a defender of freedoms.
The Guantanamo Bay torture camp, where hundreds of terrorist suspects have been detained without trial for the past five years, is a dark blot on its image. It demonstrates America's utter disregard for international law.
How many have been tortured to death in US army prison camps in Afghanistan and Iraq? How many more have been subjected to dehumanising, degrading and demeaning conditions in interrogation camps?
What about the so-called "black sites" across Europe, to which suspects are shipped illegally for torture?
Amnesty International, the International Committee of the Red Cross and the United Nations have all documented these atrocities by a state supposedly in pursuit of a world free of terror.
The US is blind to the simple fact that it is this kind of state terror, injustice and persecution visited on innocent citizens of sovereign countries that inspires many more to defy submission to a life of persecution.
Clearly, the US should by now know that there must be better, more civilised, ways of dealing with the underlying causes of terrorism.
The Israeli aggression on the Arab world, global poverty, autocratic puppet regimes supported by the US and its intolerance to alternative forms of governance are some of the causes. Thanks to the US, the world has become more insecure today than ever before.
The US must learn to respect other human beings' right to life. It must also appreciate that it does not have a monopoly of leadership of the international community simply by virtue of its superpower status.
Its might has not helped "smoke out" Osama bin Laden or make the world a safer place for mankind. It must embrace dialogue to solve international issues. You cannot do it by issuing ultimatums, only by seeking to listen and understand.
No nation is more ignorant of the rest of world than the US, courtesy of its inward-looking and isolationist policies. It champions free speech but is more intolerant than our own leaders, who admittedly are often rattled.
In 2004, when I wrote an article critical of the US government's policy on terrorism, ambassador William Bellamy responded harshly, ordering certain US marines not to undertake any project in Mandera Central.
The marines were carrying out rehabilitation projects worth up to Sh8 million in each North Eastern the constituency. A contemptible attempt to blackmail me?
America's actions belie its oft-repeated claim to civilisation. Long ago, George Bernard Shaw put it succinctly: "America is the only country in the world that has gone directly from barbarism to degeneration, without the usual intervention of civilisation".
Mr Kerrow is MP for Mandera Central and the Shadow Finance minister

Original_Search_Title: Who Won The Test Match?
Headline: Nigeria: Who Won The Test Match?
Body_Text:
During the afternoon of Monday, 3rd April I telephoned a few friends to ask them what I thought was a simple question. In the process I managed to puzzle some of them and irritate others. I also probably left wondering if my old age was beginning to tell on me. The question I asked was: Who won the Test match between Australia and South Africa?
Let me begin the story properly. For some years now, I have tried very hard not to miss watching cricket matches on television. This means sitting in front of the box for the best part of the daylight hours of five consecutive days. Cricket is not like boxing in which everything can end in ten seconds plus the time it takes to deliver the knockout punch. It is also not like football, in which a match lasts for ninety minutes plus the half time interval and whatever the referee may add as "injury time." Cricket is a more unhurried affair, and a Test match is stretched over five days, with breaks for drinks, lunch and tea.
The Test matches that I try not to miss are the ones involving India, Australia, Pakistan and (lately) Sri Lanka. I used to be enthralled by the performance of the West Indies cricketers, especially Vivian Richards, Gary Sobers, Clive Weekes and of course Brian Lara. So great was my admiration for Clive Weekes in particular that I felt absurdly flattered one hot summer day in London when, as I walked along King's Road, Chelsea, I was accosted by two boys who asked if I was Clive Weekes. They probably were autograph hunters who had been cruelly deceived by my hat. It was, a soft, white, wide brimmed hat, the type that cricketers put on when fielding. Adding to the boys' confusion was the coincidental fact that the West Indies cricket team was then in London to play the opening Test match against England at the Oval.
Now let's go fast forward, to Monday, 3rd April this year. I was watching the third Test match between Australia and South Africa on television when a large momentary increase of NEPA/PHCN's voltage (also known as a "surge") left my satellite TV decoder in ruins. It was almost 2 p.m. by my watch, and as I was due to attend a six o'clock meeting at a club on Lagos Island, I decided to set out immediately. If the traffic was not too bad I would get to Lagos by 3.30 and be able to watch the rest of the Test match on the club's television set.
I knew that the club has a wide-screen television set that is located in a big room that has facilities for dining. The set seems to be entirely dedicated to the CNN channel, but nobody ever pays the slightest attention to what is on the screen, since everybody is busy talking. Conversation often takes the form of informal and noisy debates that go on from lunch time to early evening. Sensing that there would be shouts of protest if I should just walk into the room and switch the television to the sports channel to watch cricket, of all things, I beckoned to a steward and asked him if there was another room where I could watch television without interfering with CNN.
"There is a TV set in the Billiards Room," the steward replied, "but it is a small one."
"Would anyone be playing billiards at this time?" I again asked.
The steward looked at his watch, and assured me that the Billiard Room would be empty. "Then lead me to it," I said.
In the Billiards Room I sat in splendid isolation, sipping beer and watching cricket. The TV set had a twelve inch screen, but it was better than nothing.
Australia was trailing by 44 runs, with four wickets in hand, when stumps were drawn for the day, and it was obvious that the match would be concluded within the first hour of the following day.
My first concern on Tuesday morning was to get the decoder fixed. "It will take a week," the fellow I called in said, "but you can get a replacement for a small charge." "When will the replace arrive?" I asked.
"It will be delivered and installed in two days."
The next thing I did was try the local TV channels to see if I could get information about the Test match. I couldn't. What I got was plenty of "miracles" broadcast by local channels that devoted several hours to evangelism and faith healing by smooth-faced, smooth-talking pastors who made the lame to walk and the dumb to talk. They didn't raise the dead, but that was probably reserved for the following week's crusade. With television devoting so much air time to evangelical crusades I wondered, not for the first time, why is there so much ungodliness in this country? Having failed to get any news of cricket out of the local TV channels, I resorted to telephoning my friends to ask: who won the third Test match between Australia and South Africa. The answers I got were varied,
"I haven't the foggiest idea," someone told me.
"I am in a traffic jam in the middle of Third Mainland Bridge, so don't bother me."
"If it were soccer I would have told you. I have no interest in cricket."
"Don't you have something better to do with yourself?"
"You are asking me about cricket when there hasn't been electricity in my area for the past one month?"
"You say it was shown on television? Sorry, I didn't tune in to the sports channel. I was watching Big Brother."
These answers did not tell me much about the Test match, but they told me a lot about how Nigerians spend their leisure time; the extent to which NEPA/PHCN has been taking people for granted, and how little the content of TV programmes in this country has improved in the last fifteen years.
Three days after the Test match had ended, I finally got the answer to my question via a text message sent by my son. What the message said was "South Africa was sent scurrying back to the drawing board as Australia won 3rd Test by two wickets,, to make a 3-0 victory." I always back Australia against South Africa, so it was a satisfactory ending to what had started as a frustrating three days.
A cautionary tale
Here is a story about a lady whose husband was a good cook, who couldn't keep it a secret from her friends. Her husband was very, very good at preparing curry dishes. He could make them hot, medium or mild, and serve them up with such accompaniments as chutney, sliced peppers and tomatoes, raisins, grated coconut, sliced bananas and rings of spring onions. Hardly would a week pass without his disappearing into the kitchen to prepare his specialty, which was curry chicken. Their house permanently stank from roof to floor of curry, but that was a small price to pay for the pleasure of sitting down to delicious meal of curry chicken every Sunday. He had learnt to cook curry, it was said, from an Indian with whom he had shared a flat during his student days in England.
So delicious was the man's curry that his wife made it a practice to invite her friends to lunch on Sunday, at which curried chicken cooked by the husband was the main attraction. While the wife chatted with the guests over pre-lunch drinks, her husband would be busy in the kitchen, bent over a steaming, bubbling pot, the aroma of whose contents permeated the entire house.
No one saw anything wrong about this role reversal. All agreed that the chicken curry was scrumptious. The wife listened with smug satisfaction to their exclamations of delight, telling herself that it only proved how smart she was to have married a man to whom she could delegate what was, after all, the most tiresome of her domestic duties.
Then, almost inevitably, something went wrong. As the fame of the man's curry spread, his wife's friends began to pester him, first to reveal his special recipe, and later to give them practical demonstrations in his kitchen. At first the wife had no objection, for she saw it as conferring on her a sort of superiority over these friends.
But the situation got out of hand. One of her female friends, in inviting the curry expert and his wife to a Saturday lunch, requested that they arrive early so that the man could advise on (and possibly supervise) the final stages of the preparation of the chicken curry she planned to serve. The wife reluctantly agreed. Things turned sour the very next week when, after that, another woman whom she barely knew telephoned to say that she too was planning a big luncheon party at which chicken curry would be served. "May I borrow your husband?" She asked.
Borrow? After puzzling over for a while, the curry expert's wife decided that that was carrying a good thing too far. She therefore summarily banned her husband from ever stepping into the kitchen again, even to boil an egg.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerian 'Chai' is Delivered in Boxes, But Obasanjo Still Lost
Headline: Nigerian 'Chai' is Delivered in Boxes, But Obasanjo Still Lost
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Olusegun Obasanjo, or OBJ as the Nigerians call him, was defeated last week when his country's Senate voted down a bid to amend the constitution and allow him to stand for a third term next year.
There have been several attempts in Africa recently to amend constitutions and remove terms, allowing the Big Men continue in office. They failed in Malawi and Zambia, but succeeded in Namibia and, lately, the embattled Idriss Deby in Chad and Uganda's President Yoweri Museveni, to name a few of the luckier ones, pulled off theirs.
OBJ missed Museveni's inauguration last week, but dropped in the next day to personally congratulate the president. He visited Kampala late, when the battle at home was all but lost. Perhaps if he had come last year, he might have learnt how to craft a sure formula for victory.
THE MUSEVENI formula, what one might call the Pearl of Africa's Five Steps To Getting a Presidency for Life, was fairly straightforward.
Step One: The Reluctant Big Man. A president must never declare openly that he wants a third term. He gets delegations from the villages to clamour for it. Then the districts pick up the call; then parliament; his ministers begin claiming he's a gift from God, and the ruling party adopts a resolution demanding he "heeds the call of the people."
This strategy lulls opponents into not taking early action to foil him, giving him a crucial element of surprise.
Step Two: Purge internal opponents from government and party; crack a few journalists' skulls; and make the cost of opposition too high.
Step Three: Make the reward for personal support very attractive: Bribe MPs with cash and public property.
Step Four: Throw a bone to the masses. Museveni nearly doubled the number of new districts and scrapped several taxes.
Step Five: Pull off a dramatic international gambit or spectacle. Museveni bagged next year's Commonwealth Summit.
OBJ actually deployed most of these strategies. Though analysts think it's too early to say he's been comprehensively defeated, observers have applauded the result as a triumph for Nigerian democracy. However, it has far greater Africawide significance.
If a ruler succeeds in making himself president for life in Uganda or Chad, it's only a minor setback in continental terms. However, if it happened in Nigeria, South Africa or Egypt, it's no small matter. It's a major reversal, because these are countries with tremendous regional clout and some global influence, and what they do has the potential to create a ripple effect.
So after Hosni Mubarak changed the constitution to perpetuate himself in office, Africa was desperately in need of correction from a major country to avoid being written off. Nigeria has provided it - for now at least.
WHAT MAKES it more remarkable is that, with its reputation for corrupt politicians, most observers were not putting their money on defeat for OBJ's third term project. There were reports of Senators being bribed with land and money. Now Nigerian "chai" doesn't come in small brown envelopes containing $3,000 that Ugandan MPs got. It's delivered in boxes.
That the country now has politicians who can turn it down is something close to a revolution. Even more remarkable, OBJ had just done Africa proud by making Nigeria the first country on the continent to pay off its foreign debt.
His government actually wrote a cheque of over $6 billion. Ironically, by so doing, OBJ might have sealed his fate. A country that can write a $6 billion cheque to foreign debtors can only make its citizens proud. Suddenly, they feel theirs isn't a banana republic.
And if you ask them to allow you hang around State House past your sell by date, they are very likely to tell you, "No Sir."
Charles Onyango-Obbo is Nation Media Group's managing editor for convergence and new products.

Original_Search_Title: MUK Makes PADS From Papyrus
Headline: Uganda: MUK Makes PADS From Papyrus
Body_Text:
Kampala — MAKERERE University Faculty of Technology has made a breakthrough in producing affordable sanitary pads from papyrus and elephant grass.
The pads, under the brand name "MAKA PADS", which stands for Menstruation Administration Knowledge and Affordability, will be used by disadvantaged primary schoolgirls and rural women, who cannot afford the expensive pads on the market.
"We want to help disadvantaged girls manage their menstruation periods such that they don't miss school as has been the case," says Dr. Moses Musaazi, a researcher at the faculty and the brain behind the pads.
Musaazi says research shows many girls have over the years been missing classes during their periods due to ignorance and the ever-increasing poverty among the rural and urban homesteads.
He says imported pads range from sh1,500 to sh2,000, depending on the quality.
"Where do you expect schoolgirls, especially those in rural areas to get that money?" he asked.
Musaazi says many girls had resorted to using unhealthy alternatives like cloths, newspapers, leaves and banana fibres.
To minimise such suffering, the Rockefeller Foundation gave a grant to Makerere University to develop cheap sanitary pads through local materials.
"I was put to task to produce the pads from local material and machinery. I have achieved it by using simple machinery, operated by girls trained to handle the whole process," he says, adding, "We do not use chemicals. We use papyrus and elephant grass and the machines are locally-fabricated."
To make the pads, raw papyrus is pulped and turned into paper using machines. The product is later mixed with duplicating papers, dried and softened before cutting out the pads. Non-woven material is then added onto the pads, taped and sterilised before they are packed.
A packet of 10 pads costs sh650 and one pad can be used for a period of 8-12 hours.
Musaazi says they have close to 40,000 pads in stock, ready for sale.
He says their major challenge is making enough pads to meet the over-whelming demand from schoolgirls. They also have to carry out publicity campaigns such that the pads can be accepted by the target group.
"The pads have successfully been tested and certified by the Uganda National Bureau of Standards, but we have to carry out regular sensitisation campaigns to market them," said Musaazi.
Asked whether sh650 is a fair price, he says, "Compared to the higher prices of the imported pads, this is a fair deal."
Musaazi says they will train more entrepreneurs in different parts of the country to produce the pads.
"So far, some women in Kawempe have adopted the technology and are making the papers which they sell to us for value-addition," he says.
To venture into the business, one needs a team of three girls to run a set of machinery that costs close to sh1m.

Original_Search_Title: African 'Van Gogh' Who's Not Even Known in His Homeland
Headline: Kenya: African 'Van Gogh' Who's Not Even Known in His Homeland
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Anthony Wakaba Mutheki may well be referred to as the African Van Gogh but, interestingly, in Kenya, the land of his birth, nobody in the art world seems to have ever heard of him.
During the past week as the Lifestyle team worked feverishly to get more information on this mysterious genius, phone calls and conversations with local art galleries and artists provoked blank stares and far too often the response: Anthony Who?
Sources at the Kuona Trust, Gallery Watatu, the Banana Hill Artists colony and the Ramoma Gallery all claimed to only have heard that the man is being referred to as Africa's Van Gogh and that he was originally from Kenya. None of them, however, could say that they have ever seen the man or met him in the flesh.
A source from Kuona Trust said over the telephone that they suspected the reason for there being an "X-factor" quality may be the fact that he did not begin to do any serious art work until after he had already emigrated to South Africa.
A Kenyan contact in Durban, South Africa, where Mutheki is based, came closest to bringing us closer to the man.
In a text message, the contact, a Kenyan woman married to a South African and living in Durban, said: "I know him and have met him once. My friends, however, know him better. He is also quite well known because of his paintings which are very expensive and very good."
We did learn, however, that the artist was in Kenya and would be here for a short while, presumably visiting family and friends and perhaps gathering more inspiration for his works.
His gallery in South Africa and his colleagues there also came across as very protective of their man. They would on no account pass on his telephone number or whereabouts in Kenya.
This suggested that the painter takes his privacy quite seriously and is perhaps somewhat averse to media interest in him or perhaps that his promoters like to have control of their discovery's media availability.
Gallery forwarded pictures
That said, however, the gallery in South Africa did very kindly provide us with pictures of the paintings that have earned the Kenyan-born artist such a huge reputation everywhere except, apparently, his homeland.
Meanwhile, for those who may not be too sure of whom the original Van Gogh was, a little potted art history lesson might be in order.
Vincent Willem van Gogh was born in Holland on March 30, 1853 and died on July 29, 1890.
The Dutch painter was classified as a Post-Impressionist, and a century later is still generally considered one of the greatest painters in the history of European art.
According to one critic of Van Gogh: "His work shows the objects, people and places in his life with bold, usually distorted, draughtsmanship and visible dotted or dashed brushmarks, which are intensely yet subtly coloured."
In his entry in Wikipedia, the internet encyclopaedia, it says further: "Vincent Van Gogh is probably known as much for his embodiment of the myth of the tortured romantic artist as for his work, which is seen as the visual expression of his life."
According to the entry, three of the most widespread myths about him are that he cut off his ear (it was only the lobe), that he killed himself because no one recognised his talent (in the last six months of his life he received generous accolades which he found very disturbing), and that he painted as he did because he was mad (he painted during his lucid periods).
What is factual is that Van Gogh produced all of his work (some 900 paintings and 1,100 drawings) during the 10-year period before he committed suicide. Most of his best-known work was produced in the final two years of his life. In the two months before his death, he painted 90 pictures.
He was afflicted with increasingly recurrent periods of mental ill health, spending time in a sanatorium. His state of mind was not helped by overwork (especially as he did much of it outside in the hot sun), bad dietary habits, and dependence on tobacco, coffee, and alcohol. It is widely believed he suffered from a severe case of bipolar disorder. A major show of 71 paintings was held in Paris 11 years after his death.

Original_Search_Title: Michuki is a Liability to Law Enforcement
Headline: Kenya: Michuki is a Liability to Law Enforcement
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Internal Security minister John Michuki's threat to the media early this week serves as a perfect example of why he is a serous liability to the Government as far as the administration of justice and the rule of law are concerned.
While I was Director of Public Prosecutions, word had it that it was difficult, if not impossible, to reason or argue with Mr Michuki once the police had convinced him to take a particular line. This attitude would soon lead Mr Michuki to State House to complain that all the good work of the police was being undermined by the AG and the DPP who were terminating "very good cases" instituted by the police.
One of the cases complained about involved senior Cabinet minister, who was infuriated by an article in the Press that described the sudden new wealth of some Narc politicians. Following which the minister issued his infamous threat -Tutawasaka, tutawasanya na tutawasiaga. (We'll seek them, sort them and crush them). Shortly afterwards, Kamau Ngotho, the writer in question, was hauled to court by police without any investigations being conducted and charged with criminal libel. The AG promptly ordered the termination of the case.
Similar if not radical approach
The much older Michuki came into the Office of the President with a similar, if not more radical, approach to law enforcement and the media. Shortly after his appointment he got into trouble with human rights groups with his "shoot to kill" order to the police. During the referendum campaigns, he would often confuse his role as a Banana supporter with the ministerial security docket by suggesting that his Orange opponents were committing treason in some of their legitimate challenges on the draft constitution. Under Mr Michuki, the police force has been transformed into a law unto themselves, with the AG only being consulted when convenient.
Last week, looking rattled in a televised function in Kibwezi, he threatened any media house with the same treatment meted out against the Standard Newspaper and KTN if they dared threaten what he describes as "the security of the state." Without a particular transgression by the media in mind, this was pure and simple unlawful terror and intimidation. We were reminded of his justification of the Standard raid when, trembling with anger, he shouted his now infamous "rattling a snake" remark. As minister in charge of various coercive arms of government including the police, Mr Michuki needed to control his temper.
From a legal perspective, Mr Michuki does not have the legal or statutory authority to direct the police force in the discharge of its statutory mandate. The AG or his subordinates, on the other hand, are required by law to oversee and direct the investigations and prosecutions by the police.
Mr Michuki should take time to be updated by the AG on the current state of the Penal Code and, in particular, the repeal of the sedition laws which previously dealt with "the security of the state," the statutory requirement to allow inspection of police custody facilities by the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights and the need or legality of conducting of searches, raids, damage to citizens' property, arson, etc.
From a policy perspective, Mr Michuki should spend more time creating a synchronised relationship with the professional prosecutors in the State Law Office to ensure the creation of a criminal justice system that works. Almost daily, we read of acquittals by courts on grounds of poor, shoddy and corrupt investigations by the police.
In turn the police blame the rise in serious crime on the acquittals by the courts. The blame game continues and the public continues to suffer. The presidential directive in September 2004 to bring police prosecutions unequivocally under the AG should be implemented without further delay.
The unchecked excesses of the Kenya Police have dominated the news for the past weeks through the investigations into the death of Robert Njoya on Soysambu Ranch belonging to the Delamere family. This happened in investigations into the Sh6.4 billion cocaine case and last year's death of an undercover KWS officer on the Delamere Ranch where the police took it upon themselves to leak or issue highly prejudicial and sometimes misleading statements to the public through the media.
In the first incident which occurred on April 19, 2005 the police committed various prejudicial errors including arresting the suspect and booking him in "to be charged with the offence of murder" even before the investigations had started.
On April 21, 2005 the Naivasha police boss informed the press that the suspect would be charged with murder, before he had even recorded a statement from him or most of the witnesses. Eventually to pre-empt a perusal of the file by the AG, on April 27, 2005 before investigations were complete, he rushed him to court on a charge of murder and asked the court for time to conclude the investigations.
At the time of charging the suspect, neither the AG, the DPP or the Provincial State Counsel had been satisfied that there was adequate evidence to sustain the charge of murder.
The mischief intended by this conduct is to make it impossible or difficult for the AG to make a decision that may place him in conflict with public opinion. Politicians, including government ministers, in ignorance of the facts, have similarly misused the unfortunate incident for a variety of reasons. Fortunately, the AG has declared that such politicians should be ignored, and that his decision on whether or not to prosecute will depend entirely on legal considerations including adequacy of evidence.
Indeed by Section 26 of the Constitution the AG enjoys security of tenure and in deciding whether or not to institute a prosecution in any case, he is not subject to the direction or control of any person, or authority, including the President. However the President can show his displeasure by firing the DPP which, in itself, undermines the independence of the AG, because his subordinate officers will naturally avoid making decisions that place them in conflict with powerful political forces.
The regular police investigator at present appears completely ill-equipped to appreciate the law relating to homicide (the killing of a human being) and the legal distinctions between various categories of homicide including: Justifiable homicide, excusable homicide, murder and manslaughter. For this reason, without professional prosecutorial guidance an investigator will blindly compile an investigation file and recommend a prosecution for murder, notwithstanding the absence of the necessary ingredients for an act to constitute the offence.
Criminal responsibility
Additionally section 17 of the Penal Code provides that criminal responsibility for the use of force in the defence of person or property shall be determined according to the fairly complex principles of English Common Law.
The police may be firmly convinced of the guilt of the suspect, but if the evidence is insufficient in terms of the probability of a conviction the policy of the AG should be to oppose the initiation of a prosecution. Any substantiated indications that the AG or his subordinates are prone to succumb to police pressures in making their decisions instead of adhering to the principle of fearless impartiality would surely contribute to the erosion of public confidence in the process.
I remind Kenyans, as a former prosecutor, that preferring charges against a suspect is the easy part, proving a case, particularly investigated in the present circumstances, is the difficult part. Publicity seeking individuals should allow the process to take its course and avoid mob justice.
Mr Murgor is an advocate and former Director of Public Prosecutions

Original_Search_Title: Third Term Buried... What Next?
Headline: Nigeria: Third Term Buried... What Next?
Body_Text:
ALL lovers of Nigeria and the survival of democracy in the land must have heaved a sigh of relief Tuesday as President Olusegun Obasanjo's bid for an unprecedented third term in office was buried at the Senate on Tuesday. As the Senators voted against any form of review of the 1999 Constitution, it became clear that the opposition had won the battle. For any section of the Constitution to be amended, it must receive the support of two-thirds of members of the two Houses of the National Assembly by the unanimous decision of the Senators, the bid can not be renewed in the next six months. And, given how close we are to the 2007 elections, it is doubtful that the bill could be represented by November or December.
However, the opposition should realise that it is not yet uhuru. Going by what the government succeeded in imposing on the people of Oyo state, how the process was maniacally manipulated, it should be realised that the plotters of the coup against Nigerians could still come up with new tricks. They could precipitate a crisis and insist that the President could continue in office for six months "in the first instance." It might appear far-fetched, but not impossible. Nigeria has become a country of anything goes.
If the President agrees to bow to the voice of reason and drop the bid for a third yterm, we should expect a man out to take his pound of flesh against those who had opposed his continuation in power. He would likely deploy all the resources at his disposal to work against the interest of certain people. In that case, having freed himself from direct participation, less attention might be paid to clear infraction of the constitution.
This is the time for all those opposed to the President's brand of politics to close ranks and constantly monitor the process. Every move of the establishment must be watched for the motives and intentions. We all must insist on scrupulous adherence to processes and system and any attempt to sideline rules must be fiercely resisted. This is the only way to sustain democracy.
As General Danjuma pointed out in one of his recent outbursts, this country was not built for one man. One thing is certain, God loves Nigeria and Nigerians. He has demonstrated that love over the years. He is still on the throne. He has not changed and has promised not to leave his people or abandoned them. But, we, too, have our responsibilities, Far too many people, more than just soldiers who fought the civil war have put so much into the Nigeria project. Many paid the supreme sacrifice during the June 12 struggle. Many lost so much to the NADECO resistance and many others in the media, in the civil society groups and the general society have pledged their lives to the struggle for a better, democratic Nigeria. The price of liberty is eternal freedom.
And AIT was shut
Yes, there's no room in a democracy for rule of the thumb and capricious decision making. But, last Sunday, security agents were said to have invaded the Abuja centre of the Africa Independent Television, made away with a master tape of a documentary being aired by the station and ordered them temporarily off the air.
Time was when Nigerians would describe some African countries where unusual things were taking place as banana countries. At the time, it appeared inconceivable that Nigeria could ever experience the same things. But, today, we've come full circle. After military desecration of the House of Politics, militics has taken over. A civilian with military background who believes that some measure of military dictatorship is required to govern Nigeria is now on the throne.
It is the first sign. When a dictator loses the argument, he guns for his opponents' heads. Chief Raymond Dokpesi, AIT proprietor's house in Agenebode has been bombed. After AIT, one might progressively expect a degeneration in government-media relationship. As it was under General Sani Abacha that many media houses were shut with impunity, the Obasanjo regime might try the same method.
But, as it was under Babangida, Abacha, barefaced despotism helps no one. For one, the people have now seen that the administration's castle is not impregnable. The media has a duty to watch out and take pre-emptive steps to protect their hard-won freedom. No one has the right to withdraw that which even the military could not.
Agagu's second term
One state to watch closely in the build up to the 2007 governorship election is Ondo. If the election could be taken for granted in other states, it can't be so in Ondo. First, even in 1999 when the results of the election could be foretold in all the other states of the South West, Ondo was different. While the PDP barely made the 20 per cent mark in some states, it had close to 40 per cent in the sunshine state. The feat was credited mainly to the Agagu magic. It was therefore no surprise that the party was able to unseat the AD in 2003, albeit with a narrow margin, too.
The next election would be tough. Both AD and PDP are talking tough, but what could make the difference is the unity or lack of it in the ruling party. How united in the PDP in Ondo State? How popular is the governor? How well has he performed, and what is the general perception of him?
These and many more questions would be addressed on this page in subsequent weeks.

Original_Search_Title: Triumph of Unity
Headline: Nigeria: Triumph of Unity
Body_Text:
Lagos — When the Senate gunned down the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria 1999 (Amendment) Bill, 2006 last Tuesday, my mind shifted from the euphoria of the moment to something more sublime. The way discussions often go about Nigeria at various fora, one would think that at the slightest provocation, the country would split into bits. But now, I am convinced, more than before, that although many people do not consider it a strong attribute, unity of purpose may turn out to be one weapon we have as a nation.
But, before I go on, I must salute the Senate President, Ken Nnamani, for leading the National Assembly to enforce the will of the people. While all anti-third term legislators deserve a pat on the back, we must not lose sight of the strength of character demonstrated by the leadership of the legislature. For, it is practically impossible to have a sick head and a healthy body at the same time. Even nature recognises the importance of the head. How else would you explain the presence of sensitive organs like the brain, eyes, ears, nose and mouth there? The story of the banana peel and the Senate Presidency is not new. This Fourth Republic has the largest turnover of senate presidents - their rising and falling traceable in most cases to the favour or disfavour of the presidency. But when the time came for Nnamani to choose between personal aggrandizement and the wishes of the people, he did what great leaders would do.
Permit me, at this point, to reproduce in part his address to his colleagues which I believe historians will never forget: "Today, every decision of this Senate on the amendment of the Constitution is in the full glare of Nigerians who gave us the mandate to represent them and whose Constitution is being amended. Today, every decision of this Senate is made according to procedures in the Constitution and the standing rules of the Senate. We are following due process because we want to ensure that Nigeria survives this crisis.
"We are following due process because without due process, political conflicts easily graduate to political warfare. I will continue to strictly follow the letters of the Constitution and the Senate Rules as long as I preside over this congregation of senior citizens. This is, perhaps, the only way we can save the nation from the crisis of political succession.
"Distinguished senators, now that we are at the threshold of concluding this historic debate on the proposed amendment to the Constitution, I urge every senator to rise up to the challenge of patriotism. We should not become political entrepreneurs who pander after personal gains but rather delegates and trustees of the people who will preserve democratic governance. Let us remain guided by the highest ideals of legislative democracy. Let us protect democratic procedures no matter where our votes fall. In this circumstance, the process is more important than the result."
The strength of all he said would be lost if they are not situated within the context of survivalism that marks the overriding motivation for present-day politics in Nigeria. At the risk of personal safety, at the risk of becoming the target of anti-corruption agencies, at the risk of ceasing to be the Number Three citizen of the country and at the risk of falling onto the wrong side of the all powerful president of the Federal Republic, he presided over the congregation that stamped the supremacy of the constitution and the spirit of the people. It is indeed unfortunate that serious issues like revenue derivation formula, states creation, restructuring, redefinition of political apparatus and judicial reforms have been nailed together with their more notorious counterpart -tenure. Well, let them be for now. They can be sorted out later.
Back to the question of unity. Never since June 12, 1993 had Nigerians been so united politically like the desire to see to the demise of power elongation. The nationwide jubilation that greeted the May 16, 2006 declaration of the Senate is indicative of deep-rooted nationalism, the kind that was demonstrated when the late Chief Moshood Abiola swept the polls 13 years ago. Desirous of seeing the back of soldiers then, Nigerians voted massively for the defunct Social Democratic Party (SDP), not minding the Muslim-Muslim presidential candidature. It wasn't only the wall of religion that fell. Ethnicity and regionalism did also not mean anything to Nigerians. What mattered was putting someone with national appeal, someone who had paid his dues, someone who could hold the country together. That the overwhelming mandate was not actualised is another matter entirely.
Again, the third term issue has united the people. It is instructive that most of those who kick against the attempts to shift the goal post do not do so because they hate the president. They are motivated by the urge to do the right thing, to keep to the books. Just like what obtains any time the national football team play, people ignored their differences and fought against a common enemy. It would be sad to think that that enemy is Obasanjo. The enemy is actually the demon that easily works its way into the souls of African leaders and turns them into eternal presidents.
If the third term project had succeeded (one can only hope that Nnamani's gavel has sealed its fate), Nigeria would have joined the club of conquered countries. It would have lost the moral grounds to be the leader that providence has designed it to be on this continent. It would have brought itself down to the level of places like Chad, Zimbabwe and Uganda where the presidents love their people too much to let them go into wrong hands.
It is to the credit of the ability of Nigerians to act as one that the country can hope to maintain its pride among the comity of nations. It is too early, after the Sani Abacha era, to relapse to a pariah status. The drama that brought this government's selfish dream to its knees could become a reference point to forge a more cohesive society. It could be a springboard for a new Nigeria. The message is simple: The divisive tendencies in the country are surmountable afterall. With the right music, Nigerians can dance at the right rhythm. They can sing similar, if not same, songs. They can lay aside the fears of the past and look ahead with a better sense of mission, the mission to make the country great again. This lesson must not be missed.
mondayekpe@yahoo.com

Original_Search_Title: Between Nigeria and Jamaica
Headline: Nigeria: Between Nigeria and Jamaica
Body_Text:
The op-ed piece titled "Think Jamaica is bad? Try Nigeria", by one Ms Dianne Abbott, first published in Jamaican Observer of Sunday, April 9, 2006 and reprinted by The Guardian, Wednesday, April 12, 2006, is not only offensive, but also in bad taste. I suppose it was published by The Guardian (a respected Nigerian daily) out of respect for freedom of expression and to show us (for good or ill) what other nationals think of our nation and its teething problems. Still, as a patriotic Nigerian who is tired of the gang-up of a horde of tiny and inconsequential countries against Nigeria, I could not help being nauseated. As far as I am concerned, The Guardian has inadvertently helped to give currency to hate propaganda on Nigeria.
It is common knowledge that Nigeria-bashing is a very flourishing business for the foreign media. Writing negative things about Nigeria increases your chance of getting published while publishing such appears to have a cash value. Nigeria is one country the foreign media (following the unfortunate lead of the Western press) loves to hate and lampoon. It fits into the mould of Nigeria as a compulsive critics' choice.
For the international media, Afro-pessimism is normal and a way of life. When the issue centres on a proud, assertive and activist global player like Nigeria, there is a tendency to write the country's virtue on water and carve its vices on marble. Coming from somebody of Caribbean descent, the ill-informed attempt at Nigeria-bashing is probably understandable even though not justifiable. It can be explained as phobia for Africans by Blacks in the Diaspora arising from a sense of betrayal by our forebears who sold them into slavery or at least acquiesced to their being taken away as slaves. So, present-day Africans are to this extent paying for ancestral sins.
Moreover, our Diaspora brothers and distant cousins have a tendency to want to show us that being victims and products of slave trade has elevated rather than diminished their status as human beings. So they act as more knowledgeable, more sophisticated and more civilised. It is the "we-are-better-than-you syndrome" that inspired the myopic opinion in question.
Perhaps more important, some small countries have underdog complex in their relationship with Nigeria. For some of these tiny countries, irritating Nigeria is a way of gaining relevance or creating an air of importance. Hence, what they cannot condemn in Nigeria, they simply ignore. For them, the Nigerian cup is one-third empty rather than two-thirds full. It fits into a familiar pattern. The story of a clay-footed giant (perceived or actual) is a good copy any day.
While I sympathise with the petty nationalism and rabid patriotism that impelled such passionate but uninformed opinion, I must state that it smacks of intellectual dishonesty to compare a banana republic and near-failed-state like Jamaica with Nigeria. While Jamaica lacks several attributes of a sovereign and effective state, Nigeria is by all standards a global player and a giant of Africa. Comparing Nigeria with Jamaica is like comparing apples with tomatoes. Even the illusory theory of equality of states should not deceive one into comparing a country of Nigeria's power potential and actuality with a micro-state like Jamaica.
Jamaica, despite its popularity for its reggae and music tourism, is better known for its widespread crime and poverty. It is instructive to note that Jamaica, a country of less than three million people and about 11,000 sq. km, recorded more than 1,600 assassinations and murders in 2005 alone. In Jamaica, extra-judicial killings by law enforcers are common. Governments frequently deploy army units to suppress violent unrests. With one of the highest murder rates in the world, Jamaica is perpetually struggling to overcome lawlessness. At the worst of times, Nigeria does not experience the level of violence and gangsterism in Jamaica.
While I can understand the warped sense of nationalism of our dear Ms Abbott, I believe it is chasing the shadows to sweep Jamaica's problems under the carpet or diminish their importance just because of the perceived bigger magnitude or complexity of Nigeria's problems. Such selective perception and deliberate self-delusion cannot move Jamaica forward.
Those who have made the underdevelopment and poverty of Nigeria a singsong should remember that Nigeria's population, heterogeneity and its manifest destiny to lead and assist all African countries, as well as other Blacks in the Diaspora, is a serious constraint to its rapid economic breakthrough. Given Nigeria's domestic imperatives and international responsibilities, the country has not fared so badly as to merit being compared to Jamaica.
At any rate, it is offensive and in bad taste to seek to gloss over the failures and problems of a particular country by magnifying and exaggerating the problems of another country. It is a self-defeatist and escapist approach that does no one any good.
Blacks in the Diaspora like Ms Abbott who thinks that they have "moved on" and who are in privileged positions to do something about the crisis of our underdevelopment should not appear to take delight in lampooning one African country or another. What some of us expect from the likes of Abbott is that if they choose to write about our failures, they should do so with sympathy or patriotic understanding and not write like some unconcerned foreign tourist who is anxious to satisfy his readers' appetite for stories about Africa's poverty, hunger, wars and diseases.
Ms Abbott is indeed in a position to influence help for both Jamaica and Nigeria to enable them to get out of the troubles which she seems to celebrate with some glee. The likes of Ms Abbott have a lot to learn from the Diaspora nationals of some countries who use their talents, their time and their treasures in their adopted countries to help their original countries. Asian-Americans, for instance, will not engage in the very unprofitable act of comparing the unflattering life in Bangladesh with that of Indonesia. Rather, what they will do is to see what they can do to improve things in those countries. We commend this kind of ethnic, national or racial nationalism to Ms Abott.

Original_Search_Title: NBA Asks FG to Release Transition Programmes
Headline: Nigeria: NBA Asks FG to Release Transition Programmes
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Nigerian Bar Association (NBA) yesterday said with the defeat of the Third Term plot at the National Assembly, the federal and state governments should immediately release their transition programmes towards next year's general elections.
In a statement jointly signed by its President, Prince Lanke Odogiyon and Secretary, Nimi Walson- Jack, the association said failure to do this would give credence to insinuations that the Federal Government might embark on illegal means to remain in power beyond the May 29, 2007 terminal date of its tenure.
While expressing satisfaction on the rejection of the third term agenda, the NBA noted that while the bill contained some useful provisions, its relevance was rendered useless by the many obnoxious sections including the provision for tenure elongation from two to three four year terms.
"It is unfortunate that the Federal Government of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, prodded by self-seeking individuals and selfish interest groups insisted on amending a weighty document like the Constitution for the benefit of the incumbent President.
"We congratulate the distinguished senators of the Federal Republic of Nigeria for heeding the voice of the people and voting against tenure elongation despite the intimidation of the leadership of the ruling party. This is one of the few occasions when our senators have put the interests of the people above personal aggrandizements.
"On this occasion, we commend the senators for rescuing the nation from the abyss of political and national calamity, and international odium.This beauty of democratic action has ensured that once more Nigerians can walk tall in the comity of Nations and loudly proclaim that Nigeria is not a banana republic", the statement read.
The association also enjoined members of the House of Representatives to bring to an end the on-going deliberations and consideration of the bill in the House of Representatives, adding that by the Rules of Legislative proceedings, a bill must pass in one chamber before it is considered in the other.
"Since the bill failed to pass in the Senate, its continued consideration in the House of Representatives is an exercise in futility. Six months must now elapse before the bill can be re-presented to the Senate, if Obsanjo's third term supporters wish to" the statement adds.
While expressing confident that the House of Representatives, which has provided foot soldiers for the opposition to tenure elongation, would put the final nail on the coffin of third term the lawyers noted that when that is done, then we all can say that the blood of those who died fighting for democracy has not been in vain.
"Like Abraham Lincoln, we too can proclaim that government of the people, by the people, for the people, shall not perish in Nigeria.
"The Nigerian Bar Association would like to use this medium to appeal to Nigerians not to embark on any form of harassment of legislators who proposed and supported tenure elongation. They must be seen to have acted in exercise of their freedom of _expression as citizens and the right to hold opinion as legislators. The beauty of democracy is that all those persons who said Nigeria will cease to exist without a particular ruler, would enjoy democratic rights in a new Nigeria with a new leadership. "However, we believe that the electorates are now wiser and would ensure that Governors, Ministers, and legislators who championed and supported tenure elongation would not eat their third term cakes and still have them.
"In the same vein, we call on the government to resist the temptation to use the instruments of state against individuals, groups and organizations who opposed the elongation of tenure. We look forward to an end in the undemocratic practice of using of the police and other coercive institutions of state to stop political meetings and rallies, and prosecution of known political opposition and groups holding positions that are at variance with that of the ruling party and government.

Original_Search_Title: 3rd Term: Edo Group Hails Danjuma, Arigbe-Osula
Headline: Nigeria: 3rd Term: Edo Group Hails Danjuma, Arigbe-Osula
Body_Text:
Benin City — A group of political leaders in Edo State under the aegis of Edo Restoration Group, has commended the member representing Edo South Senatorial District in the senate, Senator Daisy Danjuma and the member representing Oredo Federal constituency in the House of Representatives, Mr Emmanuel Arigbe-Osula for rising against the third term plot,declaring that they have saved the state from embarrasment.
The group led by the first civilian governor of the State, Chief John Odigie-Oyegun in a statement yesterday in Benin City, asserted that the nation and its entire population stood threatened by the inordinate political ambition and adventurism orchestrated by power hungry persons, whom according to them, are hell bent on manipulating the constitution to perpetuate themselves in power.
It therefore stated that the position of Senator Danjuma and Osula reflects the stand of the people of the state, adding that they have been able to save the state from being cursed by the younger generation if they had allowed themselves to be influenced by the inducement, like other representatives of the state in the National Assembly had done.
According to the statement, "we wish to express our deep sense of gratitude to our distinguished and honourable members who have proved that they have no price tag and could not be intimidated or blackmailed.
"We salute particularly Senator Daisy Danjuma and Hon. Emmanuel Osula who have saved our State from total shame. At the proper stage, we shall accord them the honour that this singular act has conferred on them.
"For democracy to become enduring, it does not depend on the length of rule of one man. We do not need continuity of personality but rather of political, economic and social reforms that are people and not regime oriented.
"Dictatorship is not only retrogressive but it is downright criminal. We hope that the largest party in Africa would have learnt from the ongoing debate that Nigeria is not a banana republic, poor as we may have been reduced to, there is a limit to what the people would tolerate", it stated.
The group further appealed to the National Assembly to ensure that the process of appointing judges should be made even more rigorous rather than leave them to the "whims and caprices of president and governors."

Original_Search_Title: Kivuitu Team Seeks Control of Polls Cash
Headline: Kenya: Kivuitu Team Seeks Control of Polls Cash
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission says its independence is threatened unless it is financed directly from Treasury.
Chairman Samuel Kivuitu said the commission could only be strong if it had control of its funds and asked Parliament to allow it to draw its funds from the exchequer and account for it to the Controller and Auditor General.
"It is very important for the country to have a strong electoral commission...if it is not strong, it is better it goes home," he said, and proposed that the team be given powers to ban candidates who disobeyed the electoral code of conduct from taking part in elections.
Mr Kivuitu, who spoke at a post-referendum workshop at Panari Hotel, Nairobi, said the case for the commission to control its finances was made stronger during the public vote when a meeting between him and the Finance minister was misunderstood by those opposed to the proposed constitution.
He said the Orange team had questioned the meeting, saying the Government had wanted to influence him.
"Many electoral commissions get their funds from the consolidated fund and they account it to Parliament," Mr Kivuitu said, adding that voter education was underfunded with a provision of only Sh6 million a year.
"We should not negotiate for funds under pressure....with ministries telling us we overspent. Democracy is expensive," he said.
He was with commissioners Jack Tumwa and Abuya Abuya, Institute of Education and Democracy director Koki Muli and Muslim Consultative Council official Abdularahaman Wandati.
Mr Abuya said: "In order to enhance the independence of the ECK, its finances are very important."
He said the current practice of the commission negotiating for funds with Treasury was not good because in future the Government in power could refuse to release the finances, incapacitating it.
The meeting, which included members of the civil society, political parties and donor representatives discussed the aftermath of the referendum.
Mr Kivuitu complained that although the referendum was held, there was no water-tight law governing it, which had invited serious legal implications.
He accused Parliament of abdicating its duties of making laws and said both sides of the campaign had shown contempt to the ECK by disobeying the code of conduct because the commission could not legally punish them.
The commission called for the passing of the Political Parties Bill -currently in the attorney-general's office - to empower the organisation to conduct elections.
He said the Orange and Banana teams had engaged in propaganda, rendering voter education useless.
"It was difficult to deal with the groups because of the regulations we were using. It was shoddily planned not by us but by others...It was a nest of litigation," said Mr Kivuitu.
