Other proposals include that the ECK should acquire a helicopter for its work instead of hiring from the police and army and that voter registration be continuous and the inspection period extended.

Original_Search_Title: Conference Hall : Tenure Elongation --the Heart of the Matter
Headline: Nigeria: Conference Hall : Tenure Elongation --the Heart of the Matter
Body_Text:
IN Uganda, President Yoweri Museveni, is in his 21st year as President. They derisively called his most recent tenure, sad term. The African record for tenure extension is in the sight of Albert Omar Bongo, who is 38 years old on the seat. If he finishes his new mandate, he would have done 45 years, one year clear of the 44-year record that death stopped His Imperial Majesty Haile Selassie, from extending. Our panelists looked at all the reasons that could suggest tenure extension, and why there should be none. As the debates continue on constitutional amendments, our panelists are concerned about the lack of transparency and the poor attention that matters that would benefit Nigerians, are getting.
NB: Some of those invited who could have added to the broad, views did not attend. We note the apology from Chief Florence Ita-Giwa, presidential National Assembly liaison aide, who said she was busy arranging the visit of the Chinese Premier to the National Assembly.
Are people in the National Assembly concerned and vocal enough about these changes and how they ought to be made? How can the civil society help in directing what is going on?
Ugwunebo: I believe that in democracy, no matter how strong your views are, you should appreciate that the other person is entitled to his views. The process is not being followed. This is an amendment that is supposed to be done by the legislators, no matter the party they belong to. There is nothing that says because you belong to party A, whatever majority members of your party support, you must support. Some parties have started issuing public threats of expulsion of members, who do not support their views on third term. So many people are interested in continuing with their offices. Some people feel that when they are expelled from their party, that that is the end of their political career. So there is no way they can now be free to debate the matter freely and fairly. Ordinarily, I do not see anything wrong with anybody having the other view. If majority of the people say this is what we want, then it can be done, but not in our system, where some people are afraid of getting out of government. There are people whose business is government, in fact, even if the present President leaves, the next thing they will be struggling to do is to see how they will get close to the new man coming in, so that they can remain around government. Some of them are professionals who cannot find something else to do, their business is government. If they are not special advisers, ministers or party leaders, they seem to think they cannot exist, because they know the kind of resources they are getting from there. I am not against anybody getting interested in amending any section of the Constitution, provided it is done through due process. If it is done by due process and majority of the people say they want even a life President, so be it.
But in our case, we are insisting that due process be followed and that if the government wants to blackmail the society to force its opinion on the society, the civil society groups can educate the people. Some of the newspapers are doing a very good work and when the pressure continues and they find that people are resisting the move, then they will either allow due process or drop the idea. Students can get involved, as well as ASUU and the trade unions. In other societies, the civil society groups and trade unions, including students, will continue to put pressure on them. People can even go to court to challenge it and if you have a judiciary that is free, the judicial opinion can assist in changing this kind of situation. I do not believe that the best way is to keep quiet for the tenure elongation to be forced on Nigerians, but if anything has to be done, why the rush? If this amendment is not done between now and the end of this tenure, what will happen? What is wrong with a new government coming in and continuing with the constitutional amendment? Is it not possible? Must it be done by this government?
Why did it wait till now to consider constitutional amendments? Does this tell us why there may not be due process?
Kua: I agree that this problem is actually foundational, because, the heat that this issue is generating simply says that as Nigerians, we have no faith or complete trust in our electoral and legislative processes. The people that are there today are, to a large extent, not the real representatives of the people. Therefore, they are not accountable to their constituencies as it were. The idea here is that, if you belong to the winning party, whatever method they use is as good as made. Therefore, if a political party says I will suspend you, the man keeps quiet. If that man knew that people had voted him because they believe he can deliver service and wherever he goes, the people who voted him in will go with him. So once the fever for change starts to come towards election, there is a tendency to try to strategise to perpetuate business (if you like) as usual, so that the people who have advantages in government will not be thrown out.
The people who were agitating for a five year term in 2003, were looking for another one year to go on, because they were coming to the end of four years and some of them were thinking that they may not have another go at elections. Now, when those issues were resolved and some of them were re-elected and were coming close to another time, there was a strategy to change the rules of the game again and to move on. I know that in the State and National Assemblies, people have constituency votes. They are supposed to actually have offices where they interact with the electorate, address issues and educate them. We were even talking of whether the people know if they have a right to amend the Constitution. This is the work that if people that were voted for will do for the people in their constituencies, they will have a feel of the people they represent and that will become their own voice and position during voting and the views that they propagate during debates. We have a cou ntry where everybody knows the answer and practises what he believes is an answer. And when we are unable to collate views and get them to function, we have disordered tunes. The people agree with themselves to have a distinctive vision, another group there has another one and because of how big we are, it becomes very difficult to co-ordinate and manage.
People crossing into government do not want to get out and all of these are strategies, underground moves from whatever level to try and perpetuate interests that are personal. We need to increase vigilance, we need to let people know the power of their vote, we need to let the people hold the so called representatives accountable for the actions they take as individuals, how they affect the people they purport to represent. If these roles are not clearly marked out and there is no check point, I do not see how we will survive, whether we are amending the Constitution or whether we are creating a new grundnom or not. Look at the scenario of how a Constitution affects you if it was in place while it was in place while you were in office. So, you are in an office today and we modify the Constitution, we amend it.
Post amendments, when promulgated, will it become the best Constitution? If you put a clause that we as the people have amended and today promulgated as the new Constitution, then nobody has ever won an election, we are starting the country on a clean state. If we say we are amending a certain sections, we are trying to have a better ruling document, therefore it is the same old Constitution with these amendments. All these are not clear. There are bogeys everywhere because people know that at the end of it all, no matter what we say, somebody is trying to put a trap up and he is going to bend it. At that stage, we will be unable to do anything.
The best time to talk is now, those of us who will not be inside the room for debate, those of us who were not called to present papers in this zonal places. I know of people who wanted to present papers at the zonal hearings whose papers were not received. Somebody walked out in Maiduguri, somebody actually walked out because a Governor shouted him down. I think we have to re-establish faith in the system. Let the people know that when there is an election, they will be allowed to vote, so that when something goes wrong with the people who are supposed to represent them, they can vote them out of office. Let the people who are going to make the laws be accountable to their constituencies.
They do not have a voice of their own but they have the voice of the people who they represent. If these things are put in place, most of the fears will not be there because, if the people know that things are going to change, they will at least contribute to it. It is the fear that these things can be done overnight, back dated and corrections made after the decisions have been taken. If the due process is not followed and the people are not talked to, if they do not even know you are doing it for them, how do they follow you? Are we going to create leaders who necessarily do not have followers? I think we should go back and establish in the mind of people what is right. I do not think it is a question about reforms or what government is doing. The question is about the people not knowing anything, the ones you spoke about. There are some people who control the President.
Johnson: Nigerians are very lazy people, intellectually. If you go to bankers, for example, and you raise this issue of third term, you will see that most of them will not be able to discuss with you based on any fact or figures, not even the section of the Constitution. The problem arises that those who are termed activists are seen as trouble makers. I am not even talking about the government. I am talking about the people who at the end of the day, have to lead. I am not a journalist, but an editor in England once said he had to take a stand. Unfortunately we seem to be fighting the battle because the people themselves ask no questions, require nothing from their representatives or government, they do not personally. I think the centre is too powerful.
If President Obasanjo loves this country, I believe he loves Nigeria really, and wants this country to develop properly, I believe this issue of derivation should be increased to 50 percent or more. It should also include all natural resources. Everyone will start to work . Some states will fold up and go back to where they came from and say "look we have to be part of you, we cannot fend for ourselves and we are not contributing to the commonwealth of nations". Nigeria is a commonwealth of nations. So let us know, if we cannot break up like that, there should be more independence and more determination everywhere, that way, the centre begins to become unattractive. Even the Governors have too much power. We need to look at our culture. Does your son or niece have an idea of what you are saying at home? If no, then a person who is controlling a battalion will not listen to you. Once it is in our culture, we should look for a system of government that does not place too much p ower in the hands of one individual.
If by May 29, there is no third term, there is no election,. There is a long civil strike all over the country and the Constitution does not provide for a caretaker committee. How do we run our country, from May 29, in that kind of situation?
Uwazurike: Under Section 135, the tenure of the President is clearly specified by death, impeachment or resignation. Then it gives an exception under Section 135(3) when the nation is at war, the National Assembly can extend the tenure of the President by six months at any given time. It did not give a limit to the number of extensions . In other words, Mr. President can be there for the next 10 years - with six months extensions. But there is a lacuna. It says for six months as may be extend by the National Assembly. It only means if it is in the opinion of the President it will not be stable to hold an election because of the war. Now the tenure for members of the National Assembly is four years. After four years, they are out. If the National Assembly decides to extend the tenure of the President in case there is a war, there is no constitutional provision for the extension of the tenure of the National Assembly, at the end of the four years, they will stop being legislato rs.
The confusion starts from that point. The Electoral Act is supposed to guide us in the coming elections. It has not yet been amended. If on the long run, the third term agenda fails, the Electoral Act may not see the light of day, then the procedure for election will not be there and that means another stalemate. The President cannot just suddenly leave . We will then have a dictator ruling directly. Just like Section 135(2). We have various things to do and one of them is what happens next. If you have an all-powerful President, who can control this situation then what he will do is to stay there endlessly. So as it is, Nigerians are in a quandary. The only prayer is, please Mr. President, stablise the system of succession.
Other countries have done that. You do not need to put this country in any serious crisis before you say, "yes, I believe in two terms, I am going". In Benin Republic, Mr. President was special guest of honour when the young banker took over. In Ghana the opposition candidate defeated the government candidate. Nobody created any crisis in that country and they handed over. I do not see that happening in Nigeria. If that happens, we will use the instruments of coercion in the state beginning with EFCC and ICPC to get at everybody, just to create a crisis. So, I think we should show example and if we cannot show example, let us emulate other countries. Mr. President today, can leave a legacy that nobody can overtake, which is: continuity of the constitution, continuity of the political system, which is certainly of election time. If you want to amend the Constitution, there are other important provisions that can be amended and Nigerians will doff their hats for you.
I will give you some examples. In the Niger Delta, he has done some cosmetic interventions, like what he is doing now, trying to exclude Abia and Imo States, which are also oil producing states, from the menace of the oil producing companies. Those people are there because, those in the Izombe, Asa and Owaza, are not militants, so they are not considered for employment and job opportunities and amenities put in the areas that their neighbors will enjoy. If he wants to help this country, he can amend the power of the President to appoint INEC chairman, transfer it to the National Council of States. As it is, the National Council of States can only advise when an issue is presented, it is not compulsory that the President must abide by the advice he gets. The Chairman of the Police Service Commission, the Attorney General of the Federation, the Accountant General of the Federation, the Auditor General of the Federation, among others - remove the powers of the President from app ointing these people, transfer them to the Council of States, subject to a confirmation by the Senate, then we will have a true separation of powers.
The same goes for the appointment of the Inspector General of Police. He is there at the whims and caprices of the President. Normally, it is the Police Council that says "we appoint this person, now go to the Senate". The retirement or resignation of Alhaji Tafa Balogun was received by the President. Nobody knew what happened. Whatever he has done was immaterial. The nomination of the present IG was done by the President. That is not what the Constitution says. The same thing goes for the military chiefs. There is a way you devolve powers in this country so that the local governments will get their allocations straight from the Federation Account without going through the States. The local governments will now be truly independent and no State Governor will sack an LG chairman and appoint a caretaker chairman in his place. These are things that have been observed in the Constitution. If we want to straighten this country, direct revenue allocation will help INEC and the Judi ciary, they should not be looking up to the President to release funds for their operations. It is very wrong. If he wants to emasculate them, he will do that. The same goes for the National Assembly. They have had some proposals for the past two years, there was no money to execute them. Now that their votes are needed, the money is released. No reasonable man will simply think the President is acting fairly.
We have ICPC and it says no contract will be awarded except there is a cash backing for it. If you award contracts without cash backing for it, you are guilty of a criminal offence. I am sure ICPC has not persecuted anybody on this issue. Yet contracts are being awarded at federal and state levels without cash backing and nobody is being prosecuted.
If the President wants to help Nigeria, EFCC should be removed from politics. Today, EFCC cuts the image of being used to threaten all those who are not seen to be the President's friends. If EFCC is seen to be going only after the opponents of the President, it will be seen as political persecution.
The President can help this country and stop it from being a "Banana Republic". The President should be concerned with what history will say of him. He will not want go down in history as an enemy of Nigerians, this can happen if he bows out now. If he wants to soil his image, let him continue to listen to those hawks around him, who are manipulating the system.
Shenge: It is quite scary. I have been genuinely worried about this problem for quite some time. It is not enough to just go to the roof tops and abuse the President on tenure elongation. What manner of people are we? What manner of people are trying to humanise this system? Nobody is really attending to these questions. Why is it that people want to hold on to power? Nobody is asking the question. Why should a presidential commission take charge of the Niger Delta, when they have the resources? Some people are not even thinking of the need for a transparent and orderly succession system in Nigeria and all it will take, for example, the kind of structure that Nigeria needs that will actually make it unnecessary, in fact, unattractive for anybody to want to continue to be in power at the centre.
If you have greatly reduced the power at the center and then you give greater autonomy, greater space for the people in zones and states to really govern themselves, they will develop. I strongly suspect that the reason we are where we are, is that, we are not ready to attend to the fundamental issues. We have corruption in Nigeria because we have a system and structure that really breeds it. Nobody is loyal to Nigeria. The pretences that runs through the pages of newspapers aside, deep down, most of us, because of the way the peculiar issue of nationalities has been handled, and also because of the injustices upon which Nigeria is built. Most of us, if we get to power, will nearly run Nigeria dry. Instead of the President thinking he can really fight corruption, no matter how genuinely he wants to do it, he should have known that the structure of Nigeria is not really allowing it. He should give the people of Niger Delta control over their resources.
Those of us who do not have oil can see how we can start developing ourselves. There are many countries that do not have these natural resources. Our people are indolent. There are people who want Nigeria to continue the way it is. Perhaps, the only hope they have is that the President will come from their part of the country. And then it is so easy for people to continue talking about very abstract concept of democracy, succession and others. The fundamental problem about Nigeria is that, the structure of Nigeria is so tilt and lopsided. It does not encourage honesty and hard work. I am from the Yoruba part of the country. In the 70s when I was about finishing my secondary school education, if somebody suddenly came from Lagos and started to exhibit affluence, parents will call on their children and say "My son be careful about this Lagos boy. He just left secondary school two years ago".
It is not a matter of this devolution of powers being arranged from top or centre. It has to be a popular thing. It has to do with people at the regional level. Why must we run a uniformed system in Nigeria? Why must we run a uniformed legal system in Nigeria? It is not compulsory. Since the majority of the transactions that affect most of our people are things that have to with Customs. Now judges at the State level are being queried and disciplined by the National Judicial Council. You have policemen who feel free to do whatever they want in their own way simply because they know that anything goes. Nobody can really do anything about you. I think we have to go back, even if the third term fails, 2007 and beyond, you know Nigerians have short term memory, we suffer from collective amnesia, do not be surprised that by 2008 they will now say, "oh we probably made a mistake". That is the way people are already complaining that perhaps the Abacha's regime was better, when compa red with Obasanjo's. Why is it that we are repeating this trend as it is in the imagination of the people that the subsequent regime is better than the succeeding one? That simply means there is something defective in our structure and I think it needs fundamental restructuring - a popular national conference which must be actively supported by the government is imperative. Otherwise, we will continue to have corruption everywhere
Ugwunebo: I will say God forbid, that we will not have anywhere to go by May next year. I want to recommend to the government to put the machinery in motion for election for next year, because as at now, there is nothing that shows that the government is interested in transition. INEC should be funded so that it can start taking steps. The electoral law should be put in place. The way it is, I think that the third term supporters are thinking that once the campaign succeeds, the President will be the only candidate for the elections. I am urging the government to put the machinery in place, so that it will be real that we are going to have a transition next year. Nigerians should be exemplary, we are the biggest in Africa. Many African countries are settled about their succession, Nigeria should not be the only country where questions about succession should continue to be controversial. I believe that if we are the giant of Africa, which we are, we should not only stabilise our succession system but show example to others. I also want to say that we should have sense of history. Why is it that the same Nigerians who had the experience of Abacha yesterday want to repeat history? I will appeal to the President to make his position clear. He has good policies, no doubt, but I believe that, if a good structure is in place for a good successor to take over, the successor may even improve on the policies, so that we will get somewhere.
Kua: The Constitution we have is supposed to be based on separation of powers between the legislative, executive and the judiciary. Now, if we are almost halfway into the year and we are going to be looking at transition to a new government post mandatory two terms as enshrined in our current Constitution by May 29, 2007 and a lot on the ground needs to be done which is left undone because the National Assembly is supposed to handle the amendment of the Constitution. We will end up with a situation where people are planning and preparing to play a game whose rules we do not know. Many people have printed posters about being President or Governor in 2007. The setting now is that, if you are not on one side, you ought not to be a Nigerian. People are still forming new political parties. When will we know presidential candidates? Are we preparing for Utopia? I doubt so much. Beyond approving a budget, the funds will not be released based on a condition.
The President is not expected to teach the Assembly how to apply the votes that he sends to it. I think as Nigerians, we are getting to the point where in various areas of life and particularly with who governs us, we should be telling anyone and we should be standing for ourselves to say that "look, the power to appoint and to elect is in our hands and we will not be sitting when selection, appointment and what manner of methods are going on". It is very difficult to say this at this stage. But it is only in this country that a man who never contested an election can win. You can win an election in this country if the election did not hold. I do not think all these are done by the President. So, while we decry the fact that there is too much power in the hands of these people, the problem here is that we are not asking them to be accountable to anyone. The checks and balance that should exist has been crashed, systematically. And if I understand our country very well, it is no longer a question of availability of procedures, it is a question of our ability to insist that what we have agreed, what has been arranged and stated, is followed. Yes, the hands of the people who should ensure these things are followed are tied. The judiciary gets its allocation from the President.
Johnson - In every country, there is a law to provide and to protect the generality of the public from an individual or from a group. Come May 29, 2007, I sincerely believe things will be right. I do not think that there will be fighting and all the anticipated troubles. There is too much money for Nigerians to fight. There is too much money in this country for it to cause any problem. Rather, we should come to a round table and settle these issues. So 2007, if it has to be patched, it will be patched. 2007 will be alright by God's grace.

Original_Search_Title: Conference Hall : Tenure Elongation --the Heart of the Matter
Headline: Nigeria: Conference Hall : Tenure Elongation --the Heart of the Matter
Body_Text:
IN Uganda, President Yoweri Museveni is in his 21st year as President. people derisively called his most recent tenure 'sad term'. The African record for tenure extension is in the sight of Albert Omar Bongo who is 38 years old on the seat. If he finishes his new mandate, he would have done 45 years, one year clear of the 44-year record that death stopped His Imperial Majesty, Haile Selassie from extending. Our panelists looked at all the reasons that could suggest tenure extension, and why there should be none. As the debates continue on constitutional amendments, our panelists are concerned about the lack of transparency and the poor attention that matters that would benefit Nigerians are getting.
NB: Some of those invited who could have added to the broad views did not attend. We note the apology from Chief Florence Ita-Giwa, Presidential National Assembly liaison aide, who said she was busy arranging the visit of the Chinese Premier to the National Assembly.
Is tenure elongation in any way desirable?
Uwazurike: I do not think it is necessary. Ours is a young democracy and what democracy means is knowing what the rules are, maintaining and respecting the rules and carrying everybody along. We always make reference to the United States because when it comes to the presidential system of government, its practice there has been longer than in any country.
With the presidential system, there is a definite tenure. Remember our constitution is supposed to be rigid. In other words, it is not easy to amend. But when PDP won their land slide election, we knew that it would become possible. But then, to carry people along was something Nigerians had been waiting for. Why do you want to amend the Constitution?
If you ever have any man in mind when amending a constitution, you have failed. The reason is that the man you have in mind may drop dead tomorrow. If he does so, like any of us can drop dead, will you stop the amendment? That is why you look for ideals whenever you are amending a constitution.
What are the ideals we should be looking for in amending tenure for elected public officers, if it is desireable?
Kua: While I agree with ideals, the people we have in government today came into government based on an existing document.
The Constitution is supposed to be a directive document that writes about every daily activity in a country. That is why we have the Assemblies to enact laws that will guide activities as directed or enshrined in the Constitution. In a voting polity, there are principles about rotation, power sharing, merit and it is expected that at a particular time, people who are voted into particular positions of government will deliver service to the people. The biggest ideal will be that we want to serve our country, we want to defend the instrument that unites Nigerians, how we want to live, how we want to be governed, how we want to thrive, how we want business to be done.
If we want fundamental principles, we will not say 'because I am in position today, this is what I want.' We are looking at a situation where the power belongs to the people who will be able to select the people who would rule over them and deliver service, such that if they fail, the people will be able to check them during the course of election. By process of recall or a situation where he will have the honour to say " I thought that I could do this but I cannot, so I want to give a chance to someone else." The issues here are that no one will become the country, no one person will be an office. The office will exist in itself as a matter to serve and offer service to the people and it will be occupied at a time as directed by the principle which would have been listed or as enacted in law. In the first place, if we want to change the rules of a game, should it be changed by the players? And should it be even changed while the game is going on? So, we have questions about the way the whole thing is going on and we find that it is not in good faith.
We believe that if we sit quietly and say nothing, in the future, we shall regret that we kept quiet because the handwriting is on the wall. People had been clamouring for changes because the military had given us this constitution but we purportedly called it different names. Now we are very close to 2007 and what we have been unable to do within seven years is getting to being done in two months and Nigerians are worried at the speed, at the capability to succeed. Are we following the principle that has been laid down in the Constitution in the exercise? So until we are ready to obey our own rules, we would not be putting ourselves in the right position to put up rules for other people to obey either today or in the future. I think we are to get back to first agreeing that we shall uphold the Constitution that is in existence today and then, if everybody had agitated that more than 100 positions need to be changed, we follow the right process to do it, not looking at who ma y gain out of it, but looking at the benefits for our country.
Under what conditions can tenure elongation be beneficial to the people?
Shenge: I do not intend to answer that question directly. The question of tenure extension should not really define the whole of our existence. When we say the rules ought to be strictly followed and obeyed, if it is for reasons of continuation that it does not need regime continuity. All you need is for the reforms to be institutionalised. Let us assume that a particular occupant of a position of power says "look, I have seen a great idea or dream," I have always insisted that when you have a great dream, in order not to fall foul of the accusation of self-interest, you cannot keep this to yourself, you must also mobilise people and forces around it. For example, if there is an intention on the part of the people in the federal seats of power and they say they have been wrong-headed in the first place, in opposing the need for national conference, it is genuine, people need national conference and that all they have been doing is really wrong-headed, now we want to put thing s right.
In some situations where you have great emergencies or realisations for some great reforms, to be done, not reforms that we have been seeing since 1999 and there is a great outcry about them. The fact is that there is a need for fundamental re-structuring of the society. The President may not even have to be there, this thing can be set rolling without the President being there. Suppose the President dies tomorrow, are we going to reverse ourselves? If the President or certain forces want tenure extension, they should tell us why and not the thing about wanting to continue with reforms. I shall not be surprised if tomorrow people start talking about fundamental structuring of Nigeria and then, we need to start asking questions like, how is it going to be done? Is it going to be done the way we have been posturing, just calling people at the top, who do not even have roots among the people? My own interest has to do with a fundamental restructuring of Nigeria.
I do not believe that democracy in our kind of setting will simply mean one man, one vote. It may mean one man one vote; one man one vote as a principle is necessary but it is not a sufficient condition for it. In other words, we need to ask questions and so far, the questions are not being answered. What we have as answers now do not satisfy my idea of such a thing. We shall really need to ask the necessary questions. What do we want to do to the society which is in a very serious crisis? The Niger-Delta is very restive, in fact, it is boiling. President Obasanjo has been in power for about seven years and the problem of the Niger-Delta has not been resolved. The question of whether it is possible for something to actually happen in Holland today without bloody repercussions really coming up, imagine in some corner of Nigeria, that kind of possibility is there. How do we actually ensure that we have a safe society where people can sit together? Essentially, this would need r e-structuring. I think it is this kind of issue that we need to really address.
Can reforms be the basis for a person to say "I need to stay a little longer"?
Ugwunebo: I shall like to say that tenure elongation for the purpose of completion of any reform or project is unacceptable because government is supposed to be a continuous thing. If you are in power today, it does not mean if you start a project today, you have to complete it. Otherwise, it will become impossible for people to leave government at the end of the term, they will always start something and then give the reason as being that what they started is not completed hence they cannot go. I think it is not a reason for holding unto power. I believe that the only reason why there should be an elongation is, if, for instance the nation is in a state of war and that is what happened in the American situation that they have been referring to. If, for instance, towards the end of a particular term, there is a particular war in which Nigeria is engaged, that makes it necessary to extend the term as the country may not be able to afford the resources needed to conduct the ele ction because election also costs money.
The extension will last for the period of war. As soon as it is over, nobody will say that the tenure should continue. The Constitution makes provisions for elongation in the situation of war and not in a situation where there is no war. Seeking tenure elongation when there is no war is just an excuse for those who want to remain in power. I do not believe that power elongation is based on reforms completion and in any case, the reforms we are talking about are not 100 per cent good. A new government can amend some of the reforms because there has to be continuity. The new government can bring its own reforms and carry along some of the reforms it inherited from the former government. One man cannot say because he is there, things are working fine and when he is not there, the reverse will be the case .It is not possible. Once the situations are on ground, it is possible that once you leave at the end of your term, the new government will complete whatever good reforms you ha ve started. I do not think that reform is good basis for tenure elongation.
Is it possible that public officers think that the reforms will not be continued because they have not served the people well and they are not institutionalised considering the fact that if the above conditions have been fulfilled, it will be very difficult for any new government to throw the reforms away?
Kua: When you talk about extension of tenure and you are looking at a particular office holder, then it is not acceptable. Our constitution allows for elongation of tenure in terms of organisations, political parties, because if a political party has won an election twice and its programmes are good, the people will show support for its manifesto by voting for it. The programmes that the president and governors are executing are supposed to be programmes of the party upon which they won elections. Nobody in Nigeria is elected as an independent candidate. To that extent, the machinery and organs of the political parties can canvass and seek offices in further elections. The programmes can continue not necessarily with the person who already is in power but they can perpetuate their good programmes based on their manifestos and what they have done if people vote for this political party again.
If we move away from the dominant factor that makes a particular person an idol in a seat, we shall see that it is possible for good programmes to continue, good institutionalised reforms to continue. It does not mean that if you are in power today and tomorrow someone else is elected, you cannot contribute to governance. We have meetings that former Heads of State attend, where they play advisory role to people elected after them. It is possible to continue to contribute to good governance whether you occupied a seat at the federal level or not, if you really have something good to offer. We had a situation where one man was supposed to be the sole presidential candidate, the man died. The elections did not hold. Nigeria is still Nigeria today. Is that what we want to recreate? Except we look at elongation of tenure in terms of a personality, if we look at it in terms of political parties that win seats during elections and govern and we look at political parties that are th ere in perpetuity, that can marshall programmes, that can put people for elections who can be voted for such that their programmes can go on, I think the continuation has perpetuated itself.
Johnson: It is important that we distinguish between the amendment of this Constitution for any purpose whatsoever and the issue of tenure elongation relating to an individual. The Constitution itself, as we all know, allows for amendment, and as long as the process for amendment of the Constitution is properly followed, then I see no problem with it, whether or not, certain people have been included.
Democracy is such that the right you have to hoist the flag of your nation is almost someone else's right to burn it. We should not forget that the right of the country to do this and the right of some individuals to disagree and say this is what we want to do. Do not lose sight of the political undertone and undercurrents surrounding tenure elongation. I will want you to think back to 2002. The whole problem at that time was that some people wanted constitutional amendments to make it a single term of five years. A lot of noise occurred at that time and I do not profess to be clairvoyant but I am sure that this will end up in a lot of noise as well and I pray that this thing will go smoothly. But if the Constitution is being amended either to increase the time of replacement of Governors or what have you, if it is amended, so be it, but let them come back to come and meet the people. Nobody is saying if you amend, you would not go through elections.
However, if you are saying that there is an individual and you want to rail-road everyone into amending the Constitution and not facing an election to suit your purposes, everyone will say no to that, because if you have served for the first term and you have projects or reforms coming through that are fantastic, it is my right to determine after four years if I want you to continue with those reforms or not. Unfortunately, I feel that the political undercurrent surrounding that issue will not even make it possible to discuss it properly because if you discuss it properly, some people will say these ones are pro-government, these ones are anti-government or whatever. It is the right of any Nigerian to say (not just Obasanjo's right) that " I believe this man should stay there or not." After all, the Constitution is a written paper and that written paper must be followed. It has its guidelines about how you cannot change a game halfway and you cannot change the rules halfway t hrough the game. It is the right of people and activists to challenge such happenings.
So, we all have conflicting rights but we all have the responsibilities as to how we channel these things. We are not proactive, we keep chasing. The press has a duty to ensure that these debates are constant, even before these problems arise. So we know what we are discussing now should make us think and rethink this issue of federalism, whether we are practising it properly or not and whether with the African culture and nature, we all have Yoruba, Igbo, Hausa, South-South or whatever or being dictatorial even in our own home, whether that should make us reconsider the parliamentary system of government, if the presidential system of government concentrates too much power in one person's hands. So these things are things we must look at all times in a constant manner but as of the moment, it is for the politician that everything has to move in the direction of that goal to get them there. But, as I said earlier, if it is an institutional change of the Constitution, then, th ey have the right to do that. The procedure must be followed. They should make sure that any Governor or President who wants to recontest should come back to the populace during elections.
Are we following the proper procedure?
Uwazurike: With all due respect to the members of the National Assembly, I do not think they are following the right procedure. The procedure for making a bill is very clear. First, you bring a bill, private or government, and you pass it through the first reading. After the first reading, it will come back, which is what they did last week. The records will show what transpired. Second reading follows and after this, it gets to the committee stage where the committee is expected to work on it and come back to present the report of what they have done to the Assembly. Then it goes through the third reading, if it passes through the third reading, the bill has been passed. In the National Assembly, it should be by two-third of the majority, just clear two-third majority. Now, you move it to all the States of the Federation, the various State Houses of Assembly where you need a resolution by two-third of the State Houses of Assembly. In this case, it can be simple majority. The n they come back and the duty of the Senate President who is also the Chairman of the National Assembly, is to say this amendment has been effected and it becomes law the moment he signs. It does not go to the President. The procedure being followed today with all respect is very funny. I know that when Anyim Pius Anyim, Chuba Okadigbo and Evan Enwerem were Senate Presidents, they set up a committee led by the then Deputy Senate President and also included those in the House of Representatives led by the Deputy Speaker. They could not finish their job in any case, they did not even go anywhere at all.
Without any bill being presented, a committee was set up for the amendment of the Constitution. I am aware that when President Obasanjo came into power, he set up this Clement Eberi Committee to which I also made my own submission. It travelled all over Nigeria and also went abroad. Before the first reading of this bill, the committee had gone round. It went round pursuant to what? Without a bill, whose vote or money did they spend? Was it money from the vote of the National Assembly? Because when a committee is set up by the National Assembly, it makes money available for it. These are the things that make one feel funny. The report of the Mantu Committee has been presented to the Senate. Since it was presented to the Senate, do we call it a bill? There is even an attempt to rush it because I know it can pass through the three stages in one day. They were to start looking at the various aspect of the amendments. The Constitution is very clear, whatever aspect of the Constitu tion you want to amend, you amend that section only. Each member has a right to say something concerning the bill. It is not proper to simply say we are amending the constitution for those it favours. You do not do that in the amendment of the Constitution, you must follow the rules strictly.
The Constitution is the source of all laws in Nigeria and there are so many items to be amended which are all included but I think it is a case of where some intellectuals conceived of this third term. Instead, of allowing them to do it, the job was given to job men who I will call 'hatchet men', who have been told what to do and whatever they like. In Nigeria, we have legality and legitimacy. You may be able to do this but how do people look at you? For whatever you are doing to have legitimacy, you must carry the people along. With all respect to my colleagues here, I know that the moment the Constitution is amended, if President Obasanjo wants to recontest election, he can do that for three terms because the Constitution takes effect from the date of the amendment.
Anything in the past is gone. The National Assembly must carry Nigerians along and now look at the problem they are having. You were talking of including the Governors, during the first reading of the bill, there was no mention of the Governors before then. The record of what transpired shows that the Governors were not included. Of course, the Governors rejected it. They are now causing divisions. Many of the Senators want to be governors and you are now telling them to allow the governors have an easy ride. It makes no sense. There are many Nigerians who wanted to contest against President Obasanjo in 2003, but they decided to relax thinking that the man would leave in 2007 and now, they have to wait and this trend will continue. I think we have bungled the issues and for this, I blame the leadership of the National Assembly. It went to the answers before going to the questions. You are supposed to be a chairman of the panel to collate the views of Nigerians and you are tel ling us they are already attacking you because of this.
If we have had a procedure that is flawed so far and we make laws based on such procedure, what type of laws are we making?
Shenge: Fundamentally, there is a difference between statute and the Constitution. The Constitution is the grund norm, that, of course, calls all the more for strict adherence to the process of law-making, alteration or amendment. Whereas it may not even be necessary to do a bill since it is not a statute you want to amend. A statute begins its life in infancy, as a foetus, by way of a bill. It is when it has undergone the whole process that it then becomes a law. Even then, when you have clauses or sections of the Constitution, all you do is dress it properly and purposely lay it on the table and then it must undergo all these stages, first and second reading, committee stages, public hearing for unhindered participation.
But of course, I cannot say that what we had by the way of public hearing qualifies to be called so, we have a feeling that if we can do something, we go ahead and do it. It is a different thing from saying "If this thing is to be done, then what is the best way of doing it"? I do not believe we can say that the procedure so far is proper and all that calls for vigilance on the part of members of the public and participants in the process. Part of the reasons why elections can be rigged is simply because even in many parts of the country, the noble values are collapsing. I think civil society groups and those who have given themselves the duty of organising and sensitising the society, need to look at what our problem is now, whether we can continue to act the way we have been acting before, whether we do not have to really look at the vacuum we have, some of the disappearance of values and see what can be done.
The two ways I know of challenging the certainty of due process include, having a recourse to the institution of the state, the courts for example.
Are people in the National Assembly concerned and vocal enough about these changes and how they ought to be made? How can the civil society help in directing what is going on?
Ugwunebo: I believe in democracy, no matter how strong your views are, you should appreciate that the other person is entitled to his views. The process is not being followed. This is an amendment that is supposed to be done by the legislators, no matter the party they belong to. There is nothing that says because you belong to party A, whatever majority members of your party support, you must support. Some parties have started issuing public threats of expulsion of members who do not support their views on third term. So many people are interested in continuing with their offices. Some people feel that when they are expelled by their party, that is the end of their political career. So there is no way they can now be free to debate the matter freely and fairly.
Ordinarily, I do not see anything wrong with anybody having the other view, if majority of the people say this is what we want, then it can be done, but not in our system where some people are afraid of getting out of government. There are people whose business is government, in fact, even if the present President leaves, the next thing they will be struggling to do is to see how they will get close to the new man coming in so that they can remain around government. Some of them are professionals who cannot find something else to do, their business is government. If they are not special advisers, ministers or party leaders, they seem to think they cannot exist because they know the kind of resources they are getting from there. So, I am not against anybody getting interested in amending any section of the Constitution provided it is done through due process. If it is done by due process and majority of the people say they want even a life President, so be it.
But in our case we are insisting that due process must be followed and that if the government wants to blackmail the society to force its opinion on the society, the civil society groups can educate the people. Some of the newspapers are doing a very good work and when the pressure continues and they find that people are resisting the move, then they will either allow due process or drop the idea. Students can get involved as well as ASUU and the trade unions. In other societies, the civil society groups and trade unions including students will continue to put pressure on them. People can even go to court to challenge it and if you have a judiciary that is free, the judicial opinion can assist in changing this kind of situation. I do not believe that the best way is to keep quiet for the tenure elongation to be forced on Nigerians, but if anything has to be done, why the rush? If this amendment is not done between now and the end of this tenure, what will happen? What is wron g with a new government coming in and continuing with the constitutional amendment? Is it not possible? Must it be done by this government?
Why did it wait till now to consider constitutional amendments? Does this tell us why there may not be due process?
Kua: I agree that this problem is actually foundational because the heat that this issue is generating simply says that as Nigerians we have no faith or complete trust in our electoral and legislative processes. The people that are there today are to a large extent not the real representatives of the people. Therefore, they are not accountable to their constituencies as it were. The idea here is that if you belong to the winning party, whatever method they use is as good as made. Therefore if a political party says I will suspend you, the man keeps quiet. If that man knew that people had voted him because they believe he can deliver service and wherever he goes, the people who voted for him will go with him. So once the fever for change starts to come towards election, there is a tendency to try to strategise to perpetuate business (if you like) as usual, so that the people who have advantages in government will not be thrown out.
The people who were agitating for a five year term in 2003 were looking for another one year to go on because they were coming to the end of four years and some of them were thinking that they may not have another go at elections. Now, when those issues were resolved and some of them were re-elected and were coming close to another time, there was a strategy to change the rules of the game again and to move on. I know that in the State and National Assemblies, people have constituency votes. They are supposed to actually have offices where they interact with the electorate, address issues and educate them. We were even talking of whether the people know whether they have a right to amend the Constitution. This is the work that if people that were voted for will be done amongst the people in their constituencies and they will have a feel of the people they represent and that will become their own voice and position during voting and the views that they propagate during debates . We have a country where everybody knows the answer and practises what he believes is an answer.
And when we are unable to collate views and get them to function, we have disordered tunes. The people agree with themselves have a distinctive vision, another group there has another one and because of how big we are, it becomes very difficult to co-ordinate and manage. People crossing into government do not want to get out and all of these are strategies, underground moves from whatever level to try and perpetuate interests that are personal. We need to increase vigilance, we need to let people know the power of their vote, we need to let the people hold the so called representatives accountable for the actions they take as individuals, how they affect the people they purport to represent. If these roles are not clearly marked out and there is no check point, I do not see how we will survive, whether we are amending the Constitution or whether we are creating a new grundnom or not. Look at the scenario of how a Constitution affects you if it was in place while it was in pla ce while you were in office. So, you are in an office today and we modify the Constitution, we amend it.
Post amendments, when promulgated, will it become the best Constitution? If you put a clause that we as the people have amended and today promulgated as the new Constitution, then nobody has ever won an election, we are starting the country on a clean state. If we say we are amending a certain sections, we are trying to have a better ruling document, therefore it is the same old Constitution with these amendments. All these are not clear. There are bogeys everywhere because people know that at the end of it all, no matter what we say, somebody is trying to put a trap up and he is going to bend it. At that stage, we will be unable to do anything. The best time to talk is now, those of us who will not be inside the room for debate, those of us who were not called to present papers in this zonal places. I know of people who wanted to present papers at the zonal hearings whose papers were not received. Somebody walked out in Maiduguri, somebody actually walked out because a Gover nor shouted him down. I think we have to re-establish faith in the system. Let the people know that when these is an election, they will be allowed to vote, so that when something goes wrong with the people who are supposed to represent them, they can vote them out of office. Let the people who are going to make the laws be accountable to their constituencies.
They do not have a voice of their own but they have the voice of the people who they represent. If these things are put in place, most of the fears will not be there because if the people know that things are going to change, they will at least contribute to it. It is the fear that these things can be done overnight, back dated and corrections made after the decisions have been taken. If you look at the reports of the Committee, a man comes out to say something and there are no data or memoranda and the Committee is able to make something out of it. So, whose judgement or opinion is it and were it taking? We need to educate our people about foundational issues. If we have a law that is illegally made, how can we support illegality for tomorrow? If the due process is not followed and the people are not talked to, if they do not even know you are doing it for them, how do they follow you? Are we going to create leaders who necessarily do not have followers? I think we should go back and establish in the mind of people what is right. I do not think it is a question about reforms or what government is doing. The question is about the people not knowing anything, the ones you spoke about. There are some people who control the President.
Johnson: Nigerians are very lazy people, intellectually. If you go to bankers, for example, and you raise this issue of third term, you will see that most of them will not be able to discuss with you based on any fact or figures, not even the section of the Constitution. The problem arises that those who are termed activists are seen as trouble makers. I am not even talking about the government. I am talking about the people who at the end of the day have to lead. I am not a journalist but an editor in England once said he had to take a stand. Unfortunately we seem to be fighting the battle because the people themselves ask no questions, require nothing from their representatives or government, they do not personally. I think the centre is too powerful. If President Obasanjo loves this country, I believe he loves Nigeria really, and wants this country to develop properly, I believe this issue of derivation should be increased to 50 percent or more. It should also include all natural resources. Everyone will start to work .
Some States will fold up and go back to where they are coming from and say "look we have to be part of you, we cannot fend for ourselves and we are not contributing to the commonwealth of nations" Nigeria is a commonwealth of nations. So let us know, if we cannot break up like that, let there be more independence and more determination everywhere, that way, the centre begins to become unattractive. Even the Governors have too much power. We need to look at our culture. Does your son or niece have an idea of what you are saying at home? If no, then a person who is controlling a battalion will not listen to you. Once it is in our culture, we should look for a system of government that does not place too much power in the hands of one individual.
If by May 29, there is no third term, there is no election,. There is a long civil strike all over the country and the Constitution does not provide for a caretaker committee. How do we run our country, from May 29, in that kind of situation?
Uwazurike: Under Section 135, the tenure of the President is clearly specified by death, impeachment or resignation. Then it gives an exception under Section 135(3) when the nation is at war, the National Assembly can extend the tenure of the President by six months at any given time. It did not give a limit to the number of extensions . In other words, Mr. President can be there for the next 10 years - with six months extensions. But there is a lacuna. It says for six months as may be extend by the National Assembly. It only means if it is in the opinion of the President it will not be stable to hold an election because of the war. Now the tenure for members of the National Assembly is four years.
After four years, they are out. If the National Assembly decides to extend the tenure of the President in case there is a war, there is no constitutional provision for the extension of the tenure of the National Assembly, at the end of the four years, they will stop being legislators. The confusion starts from that point. The Electoral Act is supposed to guide us in the coming elections. It has not yet been amended. If on the long run, the third term agenda fails, the Electoral Act may not see the light of day, then the procedure for election will not be there and that means another stalemate. The President cannot just suddenly leave . We will then have a dictator ruling directly. Just like Section 135(2). We have various things to do and one of them is what happens next. If you have an all-powerful President, who can control this situation then what he will do is to stay there endlessly. So as it is, Nigerians are in a quandary.
The only prayer is, please Mr. President, stablise the system of succession. Other countries have done that. You do not need to put this country in any serious crisis before you say, "yes, I believe in two terms, I am going". In Benin Republic, Mr. President was special guest of honour when the young banker took over. In Ghana the opposition candidate defeated the government candidate. Nobody created any crisis in that country and they handed over. I do not see that happening in Nigeria. If that happens, we will use the instruments of coercion in the state beginning with EFCC and ICPC to go at everybody just to create a crisis. So, I think we should show example and if we cannot show example let us emulate other countries. Mr. President today can leave a legacy that nobody can overtake which is continuity of the constitution, continuity of the political system, which is certainly of election time. If you want to amend the Constitution there are other important provisions that can be amended and Nigerians will doff their hats for you.
I will give you some examples. The Niger Delta he has done some cosmetic interventions like what he is doing now trying to exclude Abia and Imo States which are also oil producing from the menace of the oil producing companies. Those people are there because those in the Izombe, Asa and Owaza are not militants, so they are not considered for employment and job opportunities and amenities put in the areas that their neighbors will enjoy. If he wants to help this country, he can amend the power of the President to appoint INEC chairman, transfer it to the National Council of States. As it is, the National Council of States can only advise when an issue is presented, it is not compulsory that the President must abide by the advice he gets. The Chairman of the Police Service Commission, the Attorney General of the Federation, the Accountant General of the Federation, the Auditor General of the Federation, among others - remove the powers of the President from appointing these peo ple, transfer them to the Council of States, subject to a confirmation by the Senate, then we will have a true separation of powers. The same goes for the appointment of the Inspector General of Police. He is there at the whims and caprices of the President. Normally, it is the Police Council that says "we appoint this person, now go to the Senate". The retirement or resignation of Alhaji Tafa Balogun was received by the President. Nobody knew what happened. Whatever he has done was immaterial. The nomination of the present IG was done by the President. That is not what the Constitution says. The same thing goes for the military chiefs.
There is a way you devolve powers in this country so that the local governments will get their allocations straight from the Federation Account without going through the States. The local governments will now be truly independent and no State Governor will sack an LG chairman and appoint a caretaker chairman in his place. These are things that have been observed in the Constitution. If we want to straighten this country, direct revenue allocation will help INEC and the Judiciary, they should not be looking up to the President to release funds for their operations. It is very wrong. If he wants to emasculate them, he will do that. The same goes for the National Assembly. They have had some proposals for the past two years, there was no money to execute them. Now that their votes are needed, the money is released. No reasonable man will simply think the President is acting fairly.
We have ICPC and it says no contract will be awarded except there is a cash backing for it. If you award contracts without cash backing for it, you are guilty of a criminal offence. I am sure ICPC has not persecuted anybody on this issue. Yet contracts are being awarded at federal and state levels without cash backing and nobody is being prosecuted.
If the President wants to help Nigeria, EFCC should be removed from politics. Today, EFCC cuts the image of being used to threaten all those who are not seen to be the President's friends. If EFCC is seen to be going only after the opponents of the President, it will be seen as political persecution.
The President can help this country and stop it from being a "Banana Republic". The President is concerned what history will think of him. He will not want go down in history as an enemy of Nigerians, this can happen if he bows out now. If he wants to soil his image, let him continue to listen to those hawks around him, who are manipulating the system.
Shenge: It is quite scary. I have been genuinely worried about this problem for quite some time. It is not enough to just go to the roof tops and abuse the President on tenure elongation. What manner of people are we? What manner of people are trying to humanise this system? Nobody is really attending to these questions. Why is it that people want to hold on to power? Nobody is asking the question. Why should a presidential commission take charge of the Niger Delta when they have the resources? Some people are not even thinking of the need for a transparent and orderly succession system in Nigeria and all it will take, for example, the kind of structure that Nigeria needs that will actually make it unnecessary in fact unattractive for anybody to want to continue to be in power at the centre. If you have greatly reduced the power at the center and then you give greater autonomy, greater space for the people in zones and states to really govern themselves, they will develop. I suspect strongly that the reason we are where we are is that we are not ready to attend to the fundamental issues. We have corruption in Nigeria because we have a system and structure that really breed it. Nobody is loyal to Nigeria. The pretences that run through the pages of newspapers aside, deep down, most of us, because of the way the peculiar issue of nationalities has been handled, and also because of the injustices upon which Nigeria is built. Most of us, if we get to power, will nearly run Nigeria dry.
Instead of the President thinking he can really fight corruption, no matter how genuinely he wants to do it, he should have known that the structure of Nigeria is not really allowing it. He should give the people of Niger Delta control over their resources. Those of us who do not have oil can see how we can start developing ourselves. There are many countries that do not have these natural resources. Our people are indolent. There are people who want Nigeria to continue the way it is. Perhaps, the only hope they have is that the President will come from their part of the country. And then it is so easy for people to continue talking about very abstract concept of democracy, succession and others. The fundamental problem about Nigeria is that the structure of Nigeria is so tilt and lopsided. It does not encourage honesty and hard work. I am from the Yoruba part of the country. In the 70s when I was about finishing my secondary school education, if somebody suddenly came from L agos and started to exhibit affluence, parents will call on their children and say "My son be careful about this Lagos boy. He just left secondary school two years ago".
It is not a matter of this devolution of powers being arranged from top or centre. It has to be a popular thing. It has to do with people at the regional level. Why must we run a uniform system in Nigeria? Why must we run a uniform legal system in Nigeria? It is not compulsory. Since the majority of the transactions that affect most of our people are things that have to with Customs. Now judges at the State level are being queried and disciplined by the National Judicial Council. You have policemen who feel free to do whatever they want in their own way simply because they know that anything goes. Nobody can really do anything about you. I think we have to go back, even if the third term fails, 2007 and beyond, you know Nigerians have short term memory, we suffer from collective amnesia, do not be surprised that by 2008 they will now say, "oh we probably made a mistake". That is the way people are already complaining that perhaps the Abacha's regime was better, when compared with Obasanjo's.
Why is it that we are repeating this trend as it is in the imagination of the people that the subsequent regime is better than the succeeding one? That simply means there is something defective in our structure and I think it needs fundamental restructuring - a popular national conference which must be actively supported by the government is imperative. We are not going anywhere. Otherwise, we will continue to have corruption everywhere
Ugwunebo: I will say God forbid, that we will not have anywhere to go by May next year. I want to recommend to the government to put the machinery in motion for election for next year because as at now, there is nothing that shows that the government is interested in transition. INEC should be funded so it should start taking steps. The electoral law should be put in place.
The way it is, I think that the third term supporters are thinking that once the campaign succeeds, the President will be the only candidate for the elections. I am urging the government to put the machinery in place so that it will be real that we are going to have a transition next year. Nigerians should be exemplary, we are the biggest in Africa. Many African countries are settled about their succession, Nigeria should not be the country where questions about succession should continue to be controversial. I believe that if we are giant of Africa, which we are, we should not only stabilise our succession system but show example to others. I also want to say that we should have sense of history. Why is it that the same Nigerians who had the experience of Abacha yesterday want to repeat history? I will appeal to the President to make his position clear. He has good policies, no doubt, but I believe that if a good structure is in place for a good successor to take over, the s uccessor may even improve on the policies, so that we will get somewhere.
Kua: The Constitution we have is supposed to be based on separation of powers between the legislative, executive and the judiciary. Now, if we are almost halfway into the year and we are going to be looking at transition to a new government post mandatory two terms as enshrined in our current Constitution by May 29, 2007 and a lot on the ground needs to be done which is left undone because the National Assembly is supposed to handle the amendment of the Constitution. We will end up with a situation where people are planning and preparing to play a game whose rules we do not know.
Many people have printed posters about being President or Governor in 2007. They do not know whether the offices will be vacant. We have no faith in the electoral system because many people still believe that our elections are never really free and fair. Are we not worsening the situation by creating this environment that is already sending jitters into the spines of people? The setting now is that if you are not on one side, you ought not to be a Nigerian. People are still forming new political parties. When will we know presidential candidates? Are we preparing for Utopia? I doubt so much.
When we talk about power and we say the President should do this and allow that, most of these things we express simply means that we know that the people whose duty is to do this job are not free. Otherwise, beyond appointing the chairman of INEC, the President is not supposed to organise elections. Beyond approving a budget, the funds will not be released based on a condition. The President is not expected to teach the Assembly how to apply the votes that he sends to it. I think as Nigerians, we are getting to the point where in various areas of life and particularly with who governs us, we should be telling anyone and we should be standing for ourselves to say that "look the power to appoint and to elect is in our hands and we will not be sitting when selection, appointment and what manner of methods are going on". It is very difficult to say this at this stage. But it is only in this country that a man who never contested an election can win.
You can win an election in this country if the election did not hold. I do not think all these are done by the President. So, while we decry the fact that there is too much power in the hands of these people, the problem here is that we are not asking them to be accountable to anyone. The checks and balance that should exist has been crashed, systematically. And if I understand our country very well, it is no longer a question of availability of procedures, it is a question of our ability to insist that what we have agreed, what has been arranged and stated is followed. Yes, the hands of the people who should ensure these things are followed are tied.
The judiciary gets its allocation from the President. The same people are the ones we have called to come and give account of when they were in office and they refuse. Many groups are speaking to the people other than speaking to the government. Most of these fundamental issues need to be addressed and we need to rise to the occasion. The time is very short. Let us not concentrate only on fighting what is happening in the Assembly or the intention of one man trying to perpetuate himself in office. We have to understand that even when these laws are made, the people should have the opportunity agree or disagree through the ballot box.
Johnson - In every country, there is a law to provide and to protect the generality of the public from an individual or from a group. Come May 29, 2007. I sincerely believe things will be right. I do not think that there will be fighting and all the anticipated troubles. There is too much money for Nigerians to fight. There is too much money in this country for it to cause any problem. Rather, we should come to a round table and settle these issues. So 2007, if it has to be patched, it will be patched. 2007 will be alright by God's grace.

Original_Search_Title: Communication We Have; It is Technology We Need Most
Headline: Rwanda: Communication We Have; It is Technology We Need Most
Body_Text:
Kigali — There are very few countries in the world where one can wake up in the morning, have breakfast, say good morning to your house-help as you are leaving, go to your place of work and attend a meeting,
go to the bank, to the market later, report a crime at the local police station, pass by the municipal authority, if you are a business person pass by the Tender Board and Revenue Authority and go to all the necessary places for everyday life speaking the same language.
Every Rwandan can communicate with the other very effectively. Language helps people share experiences, formulate values, exchange ideas, transmit knowledge and sustain culture. The ability to communicate as a nation is one of the reasons the Rwandan culture has gone against all odds to survive.
Rwanda is endowed with the natural resource of having a population of eight million who can speak the same language. This Rwandan gem even crosses borders and the eight million geographical Rwandans are able to communicate in their language to huge populations of all the countries neighbouring Rwanda. Any Rwandan is able to carry on their day to day chores speaking only their mother tongue.
No Rwandan needs to go to school to learn Kinyarwanda and therefore every Munyarwanda, in his or her mother tongue, has the same chance (other factors remaining constant) of accessing a bank loan or at least inquiring about it; bargaining at the market, following prayers in every church in Rwanda, explaining the medical history of his or her illness to any Rwandan doctor in any hospital countrywide, bidding or arguing out his or her bid at the National Tender Board, verifying and hopefully paying his or her tax liabilities with the Rwanda Revenue Authority, solicit for a plot or present 'ikirego' (complaint) at the Mayor's office, and once in a while line up in a long queue to tell his or her 'kibazo' (problem) to the President of the Republic.
For the whole nation to be able to communicate is a very valuable asset. Many countries struggle to establish a common language for their citizens so as to ease the management of that citizenry. Rwanda, inherently, has that common language. A Rwandan can also communicate in his or her mother tongue to populations outside the Rwandan borders of all the countries bordering Rwanda. Most have relatives across all these borders.
In the eastern DRC, a Rwandan can move about carrying on his everyday chores in Kinyarwanda. A Rwandan would also easily cross into Tanzania and transact business deep into the heart of the country in Kinyarwanda. A Rwandan need not know any other language to survive in Burundi, much as he or she would travel up to and beyond Masaka in Uganda speaking only Kinyarwanda. Soon there will be a Windows Kinyarwanda version and the communication mast on Mt Kalisimbi will open up the skies in communication.
Engine
Communication is the engine of development. The human race was developed by communication. Developed nations used communication to prosper. The more a society can communicate within itself, the more the potential for development. On the other hand, the more a society can communicate within itself, the higher the ability for it to self-destruct.
