A society which is highly communicative within itself can easily develop or destroy itself. First of all, it may develop a tendency to be very oral and therefore may become less scientific. It may also tend to be highly informal in its approach to issues and this may inhibit development. The commonality of language or the ability to communicate for a nation can be double edged: much as it can be used to develop a society, it can also be used to destroy it.
Had all the Rwandans not been able to speak Kinyarwanda, the infamous RTLM, Kambanda, Bagosora and all the genocidal leaders would not have managed to convince the population to start hacking their neighbours, raping their neighbours' wives and killing children who had been playing innocently with their own children. The power of communication was again used to convince virtually the whole nation (the educated inclusive) to flee from their country into the DRC and languish as refugees by using a mixture of lies and coercive propaganda.
On the contrary, an advertisement by the Ministry of Health telling all Rwandans where polio vaccines countrywide will be administered virtually eradicated polio in Rwanda as nearly all children were vaccinated. A radio announcement on the use of condoms and safe sex reaches over eight million people in a second. This can make a difference.
So how do we preserve and exploit the golden advantage of speaking the same language and being able to communicate, while at the same time guarding against the likely event that this advantage can easily be turned into a killer sword?
The victims of the 1994 had been communicating with their would-be killer neighbours for decades, living side by side in the same plains and valleys, rearing the same animals, drinking the same banana wine and generally communicating socially as they had, and still have the same culture and language. This led them to trust and gave them an unsuspecting disposition. Had there not been any communication, at the first tell-tale signs of genocidal tendencies, which had been abundant, they would have long fled. Communication became their downfall.
A rumour spread in a nation whose population all speak the same language can become a national rumour; whereas in a country whose population speaks different languages a rumour cannot be national. For example, if an Acholi of Uganda starts a rumour in his or her mother tongue, you can be sure that more than three quarters of the Ugandan population may die without ever hearing of it. A rumour started by a Kikuyu of Kenya may never land in the ears of a Luo or a Kisii, not to mention the whole of the northern and eastern Kenya.
However, a rumour started by a Rwandan -- wherever he may be in the country -- will most likely reach all the countrymen and women in less than an a hour (with the mobile phone network, this is an exaggeration of time). If we spread developmental rumours with this kind of ICT, we should be able to attain Vision 2020 in 2010.
Communication is one of the things that helped the developed countries develop. Apart from communication, these countries have developed systems which hold people accountable for their actions -- systems built by numerous checks and balances which enforce accountability and transparency at all levels and which cannot be bypassed at any one moment by anyone. Of course there are no perfect systems, but the more institutionalised the checks and balances, the more the potentiality of development. Checks and balances which require people to be accountable and transparent are usually transformed into culture over a period of time.
In the course of evolution, man (and woman) has tried to organise himself (and herself) using different systems. These systems are borne out of putting together checks and balances and organising them in such a way that they govern the relations of all the players in that system. One philosopher once said that even angels need something to regulate them in their angelic relations up in heaven.
Regulation
Well, human beings are not angels. This is why it is even more necessary for us to have that "something" to regulate their relations -- especially so when angels live in abundance while human beings have to share very limited resources. The manner in which a society organises to regulate relations determines whether that society will develop or not. The more a society consolidates and strengthens the checks which make up its system, the more it develops. A culture of development must be developed.
Apart from communication and setting up systems, the process of development has another very crucial aspect: Justice. Even with effective communication and proper systems, individuals misuse office, thereby hindering development. This is where the law comes in.
The law comes in to punish and to set examples to others, to deter them from committing similar offences. When facts are known and the law does not punish, it will set wrong examples and create a culture of impunity. When facts are laid down, proven and no action is taken because of other considerations, then effective communication and establishing systems are a waste of time.
Inaction also encourages rumours because salt and pepper will be added to the facts and may lead to a general belief in laxity. A situation of being jointly and severally inefficient, those guilty will bask in this situation; in fact, they will propagate and generalise it to make it commonplace, thereby distracting attention. They will dwell on petty things and divert all attention to the petty; avoiding in-depth investigations or inquiries but propagating generalities.
Those who do not respect the law are forced to respect it using the very law. Administrative action is taken first, then the law applied. What is hearsay should be separated from the plain facts. What is rumour and malice should also be separated from fact, but what is fact should be immediately acted upon both administratively and legally.
Information and communication we have; all we need is technology and the culture of development to achieve Vision 2020 in 2010.
The writer is the Managing Director / Editor-in-Chief of The New Times.

Original_Search_Title: A Defining Duel, This Mini Election
Headline: Kenya: A Defining Duel, This Mini Election
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When you want something badly enough, the gods will let you have it! This gem from ancient Greek mythology best illustrates the Kenyan political scene in the build-up to the eagerly anticipated "mini General Election" forced upon us by the Marsabit tragedy.
Ever since the ferociously fought referendum, our political scene has been in perpetual fluxion, with politicians spoiling for a duel. Though comprehensively wasted last November, Kibaki loyalists, in a desperate bid to wipe the grimy egg off their faces, were quick to dismiss the plebiscite outcome as politically irrelevant, derisively baptising the victorious ODM an "amorphous" pack of cards.
The Orange Armada, emboldened by that epochal triumph against a well-heeled incumbent, proclaimed the future orange and dared team Emilio to call a snap election and be shown that indeed "wembe ni ule ule".
Meanwhile, things have unfolded frenetically in the Narc remnants. Charity Ngilu and Musikari Kombo found common ground in blackmailing Kibaki to drop some crumbs onto their laps. Stung into realisation that the President was vulnerable, his men pulled a fast one on the pair by founding Narc-Kenya in record time.
Cynical, even cruel, but politically smart, the Narc-Kenya move was deft, killing two birds at one throw: It cleverly danced Ngilu and Kombo off the floor, while clothing DP in a new gown. Forget the posturing by the other DP small fries. It is a clever decoy to give Narc-Kenya time and space to fly.
With youthful exuberance, the new kid on the block is eager to flex its muscle, sending a chill down the nebulous Ngilu-Kombo axis. The pair has responded with boring predictability by replaying the old tantrum of threats to quit government, whose razor-thin majority in the House is their manipulation chip. With due respect, I do not see the two executing this lame threat. Both are much weaker today than last December, when they arm-twisted Kibaki to acquiesce to their cabinet demands. The president and his handlers know this.
The stage is thus set for an intriguing duel likely to shape the battle lines for 2007. Narc-Kenya has already played its hand by declaring it will field candidates in all five constituencies. Rescinding this decision will hurt the budding party, painting it as indecisive and vulnerable. A re-invented party eager to retain power wouldn't want to be hobbled by such bad PR only 18 months to the polls.
If Narc-Kenya's participation in the upcoming by-elections leads to the departure of Kombo and Ngilu, it is a price the party can afford. It may compromise the government majority in the House, but it would not be politically fatal. A win for Narc-Kenya even in only one of these constituencies would be a welcome gush of wind in its sails.
You do not issue threats you cannot enforce. Last December I warned Ngilu and Kombo in this column that Kibaki was reducing them to political scavengers. I cautioned the pair that playing ball with Kibaki in his own court by his own rules would cost them dearly. It has.
The fast fracturing Ford-Kenya and Ngilu's Narc shell are courting further irrelevance by spewing empty threats. Last December they lost a grand opportunity to reshape the fulcrum of this government. Instead they became content with momentary ego-caressing handouts. In hard political terms, the two are firmly out of the circuit of power, and their respective parties will be irrelevant in the by-elections. Their best option is to walk out of government now, then use the next 18 months to build a third political plank as a serious alternative to Narc-Kenya and ODM. Narc is dead. Let it rest in pieces!
ODM too has its headaches. Retired President Daniel Moi's posture this past week, which rhymes with Uhuru Kenyatta's, was indicative of a seeming Kanu aloofness. If the orange brigade ends up reading from different scripts in this duel, the flamboyant orange balloon will lose lots of the referendum air.
But while this may bring relief to the scattering banana squadrons, and gravely disappoint the millions of orange enthusiasts countrywide, it would not mortally hurt the respective prospects of LDP and Kanu in the main battle next year. Each has fairly distinct niches. A united front would, however, be a definite clincher.
Whatever the case, the five by-elections are a perfectly timed opportunity to unravel the political scene as sleeves start to roll up for 2007. Step aside all fickle hearts. This is a duel for gladiators.
* The writer, currently a Fellow at the American University in Washington DC, is an Advocate of the High Court and Founder of The Chambers of Justice.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni's Road to Victory
Headline: Uganda: Museveni's Road to Victory
Body_Text:
Kampala — GROWING up in the rural countryside of now Ntungamo district in the 1940s, Yoweri Museveni did not know he would one day be president of Uganda.
At that tender age he battled to have the basics of life like going to school, which his peasantry family may not have appreciated at the time. The young Museveni did not mind trudging several miles to and fro school without shoes.
In his later years in school, he came across a Pan African group of students that mentored his political career.
Today, Museveni is taking oath as the President of Uganda for the third consecutive term in office, after winning a five-year term.
The road to victory began on November 11, last year when the NRM's National Executive Committee (NEC) endorsed Museveni as the party chairman and 2006 presidential candidate.
The approval came a few hours after the Central Executive Committee (CEC), the vetting organ of his party approved Museveni as the party's flag-bearer.
State Minister for Child Affairs Okot Ogong and Dr Elizabeth Nabatanzi, an NRM supporter vied for the same seat. Nabatanzi, is Museveni's Special Assistant for special duties now. But as expected, the two pulled out in favour of Museveni.
But the lighter side of the race was when Mr Willy Tiberyeba and comedian Bush Ayinebyona, alias Vicks Kingo, joined the race at Namboole, the venue for the NRM's delegate's conference.
Ainebyoona's though was miraculously nominated; NEC threw out his candidature.
The CEC meeting, which took over eight hours, also recommended Al-Haji Moses Kigongo to be the NRM's vice-chairperson. Kigongo was unopposed.
In an address on December 5, after he was nominated the NRM presidential candidate, Museveni said in line with causing social transformation of the country, the main focus will be introduction of Universal Secondary Education (USE) starting next financial year.
He said the introduction of USE is geared at total eradication of illiteracy in the country.
He also said he would stop the levying of fees and taxes on people operating small businesses such as food vendors in towns and trading centres.
Museveni also said the NRM had a programme to extend micro-credit facility to sub-county level all over the country so that all people, especially the rural poor and other marginalised groups like women, that do not have collateral security, to access credit.
Museveni's supporters adopted dry banana leaves as their candidates symbol.
But the road ahead was tough and a gruelling one as he had to juggle between the campaign trail and State duties in a short time.
His main opponent and the President of Forum for Democratic Change, Col. Kizza Besigye, was arrested on November 14, 2005 on allegations of treason, concealment of treason, and rape.
The treason case includes his alleged links to the rebel, Lord's Resistance Army and People's Redemption Army, and the rape charge refers to an incident in November 1997 allegedly involving the daughter of a friend.
The arrest led to demonstrations and riots in Kampala and towns around the country. Pro-Besigye protesters believed the charges were fabricated to stop Besigye from challenging Museveni in the elections.
While Museveni cut short his campaign trek in the countryside to return to State House, Nakasero to attend to urgent State duties, Besigye equally disengaged from upcountry to Kampala to answer rape and treason cases.
For Besigye, his time was divided between the campaign and appearances in court.
The other candidates in the race were Dr Abed Bwanika, independent, Mr John Ssebaana Kizito, DP, Ms Miria Obote, UPC, Independent candidate Nasser Sebaggala registered, but later quit the race, and asked his supporters to vote Ssebaana.
The elections took place on February 23, this year. This was the first multiparty election since Museveni, took over power in 1986.
Results released by the Electoral Commission on February 25, showed Museveni had won 59% of the vote, giving him a third term as President, while main rival Besigye took 37%. Ssebaana got 1.58%, Bwanika 0.95%, Miria 0.82.
Besigye petitioned the Supreme Court and on April 6, the judges dismissed the petition by a four to three majority paving way for today's swearing in.

Original_Search_Title: The Psychology of Tazarce
Headline: Nigeria: The Psychology of Tazarce
Body_Text:
There is something about the character of the Nigerian psyche that beats imagination and defies all manner of understanding. Nigerians have been adjudged the happiest people on earth, yet they have all the reasons to be the saddest country in the world. Indeed, the very fact that the people are this happy is indicative of a lack of seriousness with themselves, with life and with the still ill-defined national purpose. It is not for nothing that it is seen as big-for-nothing by the rest of Africa.
Despite its large population, which is the biggest in Africa; despite its being endowed with a great diversity of natural resource, an endowment that is, no doubt, the most extensive on the continent; and despite the great diversity of its people which also, without doubt, is greater than that of any African country, the best that one can say about Nigeria is that it is just there.
It is there as the continental leader that is perpetually behind in the continental march forward; and to all practical purposes, it is the role model that plays no role. Whenever it tries to play some role, it always becomes a case of the physician unable to heal himself. It is the leader that, instead of being looked up to, is looked down upon by the continent.
In a move designed more to please the West than help give effective leadership at home, Nigeria tried enforcing the peace in Sierra Leone and Liberia. This was something its economy could ill-afford, and, in any case, not an act that could be accomplished without glaring contradictions at home. Nigeria couldn't keep the peace in its own Niger Delta, or stifle the virtual urban warfare unleashed in almost all of its cities by robbers and assassins. Yet it is there trying to keep peace in foreign lands.
It flexed muscle in Sao Tome and Principe to check a military putsch and in Togo stop a civilian coup. It insisted in each case that the constitution be adhered to and respected to the letter. This was at a time when President Olusegun Obasanjo was putting finishing touches to his own assault on the constitution to clear the way for him to beat the constitutional term limit.
When talk about the elongation of tenure began, everybody's response was that it was impossible here. Nigeria is not a banana republic, was the usual retort. Obasanjo dares not begin to do that type of adventure here, because, they say, he has seen what happened to General Ibrahim Babangida and General Sani Abacha. He also will fail.
But what exactly happened to the two generals was never elaborated. It never got to be said, for instance, that Babangida would have succeeded had it not been for General Shehu Yar'Adua and some people in Kaduna, even though June 12 strugglers had always taken the credit for making him step aside.
And of course Abacha would have successfully transformed had there been no divine intervention in the matter. Agitation there was and we had the National Democratic Coalition fugitives putting up pressure on the international scene; but Abacha was not one to worry about that. When Yar'Adua took on Abacha at the National Constitutional Conference, and contemplated repeating the Kaduna strategy, Abacha, who was not unaware of what he had done earlier, promptly picked him.
Obasanjo was around in the country when all this was happening. A forlorn retired general, soon to be a forlorn prisoner; he realised that the critical element necessary to stop a determined leader in Nigeria from becoming a life-president is missing. He didn't miss noticing this apparent lack of reason or capacity in Nigerians to oppose the government in power where it matters most - in the streets.
Out of prison, into politics and now in power, he began spinning the web of his terms of office. How he got his second term was even more unconstitutional than the manner he is trying to get the third term in office. Initially, for many, the third term bid was an unfounded rumour. Then it became founded a rumour. But it was always officially denied. Even when half of it came out as a slip of the tongue during Obasanjo's visit to Germany, it was still in the realm of speculative surmise.
After listening to the political grapevine and deciphering high society bush telegraph, speculative surmise soon became informed conclusion. And even for the sceptics, after Senator Ibrahim Mantu's performance in Port Harcourt, belief in the inevitability of third term trouble became the accepted wisdom. And the third term train rolled on. Meanwhile, the people of Nigeria were left engaged in an endless he-will-succeed - he-will-fail debate. As the debate became a national pastime, everybody was a participant and a spectator.
There was no political backlash for Obasanjo. The political class is composed of individuals most of whom repeat democratic platitude without actually believing in democracy; they talk of national unity without believing in it; and they condemn corruption while they look forward to it. There is no price to pay by the political leadership because the political sophistication of the populace is demonstrably lower than, for instance, that of the people of Niger Republic.
As a political force, the populace is unaware of its power or is unwilling to use it to influence events or change situations and circumstances in the country. Its level of tolerance and docility is legendary. Compared to Egypt, where Cairo residents could go on a riot if the price of bread is increased by 20%, the calm acceptance by Nigerians of a three-fold increase in petroleum product prices eight times within a three-year period by Nigerians makes the country the most politically-peaceful in the world.
It is a country effectively without civic defences. Despite many promising starts, there is still a glaring absence of sincere and outspoken non-governmental organisations dedicated to holding the political leadership to account.
That is why, for Obasanjo the debates and the voting in the National Assembly are just matter-of-fact goings-on that can as surely stop him from doing what he intends to do as the rounds of golf played on the nearby IBB Golf Course can.
But even if the National Assembly in the end does deny Obasanjo the majority required, for a number of reasons, the details of the voting will not be a good indicator of the real mood of the nation. Many of the legislators will vote not necessarily according to the will of their constituents or their conscience, but according to the circumstances they find themselves.
The third term bid is a phenomenon that is universally-opposed throughout the country. The fact that it does gather some measure of support in the National Assembly is one more indication of the great disparity between the wishes of the people of this country and their so-called representatives.
Currently, there are many influences that rob legislators of their resolve to act according to the dictates of their conscience. For some who are close to government or in the debt of the ruling party, they may harbour inordinate hopes for more favours and be living in fear of losing privileges.
There is also this uncanny regularity of coincidence between the expression of opposition to the third term bid by certain public officers - governors, the Senate President, the Speaker, heads of parastatals, etc. - and the arrival of the officials of the Independent Corrupt Practices and Other Related Offences Commission, ICPC and the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission, EFCC at their gates.
The legislature may deny Obasanjo the two-thirds majority he needs, but bent as he is on third term, Obasanjo will attempt to see it through with the blessings of the National Assembly or without.
Unless people are ready, willing and able to stop the rot - physically if necessary, Nigeria's Constitution will almost certainly be raped by Obasanjo as he has been doing since 1999.
The Constitution provides for separation of powers as a safeguard against dictatorship; but, going by his actions, Obasanjo seems to think that what it confers on him is powers of separation which he uses against the people as a safeguard against democracy. On a number of occasions, he has indicated that he believes in political pluralism, but by action, he seems to think that it refers to the plurality of votes that his executive bills must always get in the National Assembly.
He has often spoken in glowing terms about the rule of law and its necessity in a democracy. From all indications, he has not seen the necessity for it in Nigeria's democratic experiment: because, going by his attitude to court orders, it will be more accurate to say that the president believes in the "rule of lawlessness." And he says he believes in opposition: the only problem is that he doesn't brook it.
Clearly, the nation has no refuge from his style, neither in the court of law nor in the chamber where law is made. You can't possibly defeat a president who is not a democrat at heart, whose known antecedent is that he has no patience with the niceties of democratic culture.
The psychology of the consciousness of the promoters and supporters of the third term bid is not imbued with or grounded in the culture of democracy; and it can only be understood in terms of its blind pursuit after power and its irresponsible use to lay hands on people's resources. From the governors, who will never get enough; to the legislators, who are in the process of being bought; to the party that wishes to rule forever, and to the businessmen, who are bankrolling the whole enterprise and milking the nation dry, that is all there is to them; money and more money; power and more power - but to no purpose.
There are only a few options left now. Members of the National Assembly have always had evidence of the president's gross misconduct. Now is the time, in the interest of the legislators and in the national interest, to impeach the man. From the foregoing, it is reasonable to assume that such a determined non-democratic president will probably ignore even an impeachment. In which case, people will have to decide what to do. Amen.

Original_Search_Title: CPDM Youths Protest Against Buea Rural MP
Headline: Cameroon: CPDM Youths Protest Against Buea Rural MP
Body_Text:
A group of disgruntled youths of the ruling CPDM party in Bova II, Buea, Wednesday, May 10, staged a protest against the Member of Parliament, MP, for Buea Rural, Hon. Emilia Monjoa Lifaka.
The placard-carrying youths surfaced as the MP breezed into Bova to hand over chairs, uniforms, sawing machines, a refrigerator, gas cooker, portable sandpaper machine and other equipment, worth about FCFA 2.5 million to the nursery, primary and secondary schools in the village.
The youths, going by the messages on their placards, accused the MP of returning to the village to campaign five years after she was elected. One of the placards reminded Hon. Lifaka that, "we are not monkeys that you can deceive with bananas." "You said we did not vote for you, what have you come to do?" one of the placards read. "We need jobs not beer," yet another placard stated.
The youths carried the placards all through the occasion that last three hours.
Reacting to the protest, Hon. Lifaka said it epitomised democracy. She, however, said her enemies have not started work yet because it is clear that if they were to challenge her she would beat them.
She feared the number of opposition Social Democratic Front, SDF, militants in Bova is increasing because, according to her, "the CPDM is not used to placards."
Hon. Lifaka said she works hard for the CPDM just as she works in the National Assembly.
That is why, she said, she was appointed one of the five members of the good governance committee in Parliament. She later told the press some CPDM bigwigs in Buea sponsored the protest against her, promising to name names later.
The MP said she recognised the needs of the school and felt she could help. All the items she bought are very useful for the three schools, she noted. "I went to fetch water, my turn has come, so I have come," she declared.
The Chief of Bova II, Esahe Esume, said CPDM subsection presidents have all boycotted the occasion, even though they were invited. Chief Esume warned fellow CPDM members to be careful for what is happening might be misinterpreted.
The Chief said when Hon. Lifaka was gunning for the post of MP, some people said they would not vote for her. "But you saw the result. She won. That is how it is going to be," Chief Esume enthused.
Motombi Mbome, YCPDM Fako III Section President, urged his comrades not to follow anybody who wants to misdirect them, because they will soon expire," apparently referring to the protesters. Pointing at a house in which he lived 24 years ago, hard by, Motombi said, "We should believe in reforms. We are in 2006 and it is better to be late than never," he said.
Ruth Ndando, WCPDM Fako III Section President, debunked allegations that Hon. Lifaka had come to campaign because elections are around the corner. "Hon. Lifaka realised we were sleeping and decided to wake us out of our slumber. Those who say she came because elections are next year are telling a lie," she declared.
Francis Ngundu, Southwest Provincial Delegate for Secondary Education, tongue lashed the youths for choosing such an occasion to stage their protest. "This is not a day that anyone should discourage somebody who has come to help our children," he stated.
"Some people try to dabble with politics whereas they do not know how to go about it. If somebody has anything against a politician they should wait some other time. We should not fool ourselves by writing nonsense," he thundered.
CPDM Fako III Section President, Arthur Lysinge, said those who attack individuals harm the CPDM party. Lysinge said it is through gestures like Hon. Lifaka's that the party is made stronger.
Stating that devils always temp righteous people, Lysinge urged detractors to join them if they cannot beat them. "If you think you can perform better than our MP, then gather your strength and wait for elections in 2007, do not start to run when the whistle has not been blown," he said.
Oscar Linonge, Vice Subsection President of Bova II, who led the protest, told the press after the occasion that, no political bigwig prodded them. "No politician came here to manipulate us. We took our initiative because we are big enough to know that we are being cheated," Linonge defended.
He said since Hon. Lifaka was elected in 2002, this was the first she was visiting Bova II. "We chose today to make our grievances known, even though we have not been given the chance to express ourselves."
He faulted Hon. Lifaka for planning the occasion in Buea Town, without their knowledge. Linonge said they stand accused of not being members of the CPDM whereas they support the party. He warned that if their problems are not solved, Bova II will not vote the CPDM come 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Stigmatization Worsens Effects of HIV/Aids
Headline: Rwanda: Stigmatization Worsens Effects of HIV/Aids
Body_Text:
Kigali — I thought I would write a piece about the situation in Darfur which, thank goodness, might not end up half as bad as I had thought previously because of the peace agreement. Kudos to the African Union for the initiative made to end the genocide over there. But I haven't made the Darfur my subject this time.
The happenings in the Sudan deserve more than a fleeting mention but, this time another occurrence got me hot and bothered under the collar. As I often do before I get out of bed each morning, I switched on my small FM/AM radio and tuned into the BBC's Focus on Africa programme. I still remember the exact date. It was Thursday, April 20. The lead story was a protest in the Kenyan capital, Nairobi. Not exactly a big deal especially after the Orange and Banana fracas (the constitutional referendum that pitted President Mwai Kibaki against members of his own cabinet and political party).
The reason that I got riled up was the reason behind the march. Hundreds of people were marching through the Kenyan capital to protest at the brutal killing of an HIV-positive boy! According to reports, the young boy called Isaiah Gakuyo was allegedly stabbed to death with a pitch-fork because of his HIV status. Police are still hunting his uncle, who was supposedly looking after him. Gakuyo's mother and grandmother had both reportedly died from Aids-related illnesses.
AIDS activists say the killing highlighted the stigma faced by those living with HIV/AIDS in Kenya. At one point, he was looked after by members of Nobel laureate Wangari Maathai's Green Belt Movement after he had reportedly been mistreated by his uncle.
The Kenya Times newspaper reports that the boy had to use separate eating utensils from other members of the family. "The boy was facing violence on a daily basis," Inviolata Mwali Mmbwavi, one of the march organizers, who runs a group working with Kenyan AIDS victims, told Reuters news agency. "We don't want this to happen again".
I wish that her wish comes true sooner rather than later because the fact of the matter remains that AIDS stigma is still alive and well. Sadly.
That one news broadcast made me decide to research whether the mere fact that one had HIV/AIDS was in itself a pre-death sentence. Potential death, not only from the virus itself but also from violence directed from the very community that is supposed to care for and protect the people in their community living with the virus.
It seems that the message out there is that as long as you keep your status to yourself you are safe. Lord help you if you decide to make your status public! The fear surrounding AIDS is so deep that public revelation of HIV infection often leaves sufferers socially ostracized, and sometimes subject to violent attack.
One of the first South Africans to publicly say she had AIDS, Gugu Dlamini, was stoned to death by her neighbours in 1998 while in 2003 a victim of gang-rape was murdered after she informed her attackers she was infected with the disease.
Thanduxolo Doro, a spokesman for the National Association of People with AIDS, said social stigma was strong in rural areas where the bulk of South Africa's HIV infections occur. "People live in small communities, and the fear of attack, or even of just being mocked by their neighbours, can keep them quiet," he said. "AIDS has been associated with bad behaviour, with sleeping around. Nobody wants to be associated with that." That is possible in South Africa but not in our very own Rwanda right? Wrong. Just because you and I haven't read about a victims stabbing with a pitchfork doesn't mean that all is roses and sunshine.
The society we live in is, surprisingly, very conservative. So, while the CNLS (the national Aids commission), the media and even the church take a pleasingly anti-stigma platform the 'muturage' in the village isn't as 'modern' as they are. With the HIV/AIDS incidence skyrocketing in the villages, according to CNLS, stigma and the fear of stigma will only increase.
Stigma is of utmost concern because it is both the cause and effect of secrecy and denial, which are both catalysts for HIV transmission. Fear of stigma limits the efficacy of HIV-testing programmes across sub-Saharan Africa, because in most villages everyone knows--sooner or later--who visits test sites.
While in some places the advent of free and accessible antiretroviral therapy has offered hope and encouraged people to go for testing, stigma remains a barrier to testing even where treatment is available. Without HIV testing, an essential first step to treatment, years may go by while people who are infected transmit the virus to others. When individuals finally become ill and seek care, treatment as a prevention strategy has lost much of its potential effectiveness.
Fear of stigma can cause pregnant women to avoid HIV testing, the first step in reducing mother-to-child transmission. It may force mothers to expose babies to HIV infection through breast-feeding because the mothers do not want to arouse suspicion of their HIV status by using alternative feeding methods.
Fear of stigma, and the resulting denial, may even inhibit condom use in HIV-affected couples. Further evidence of how stigma leads to denial is the way in which newspaper obituaries avoid mentioning HIV/AIDS as a cause of death.
HIV-related stigma directly hurts people, who lose community support due to their real or supposed HIV infection. Individuals may be isolated within their family, hidden away from visitors, or made to eat alone. These repercussions may or may not be simple acts of heartlessness. They may be a well-intentioned but ignorant attempt to preserve the family. In the community, the entire family may be sanctioned because one member is ill.
And guess what happens next? I'm sure that you know. HIV rates going through the roofâ-oe.is that what we really want to assail us? I think not.

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Accept Aid Selectively
Headline: Uganda: Govt to Accept Aid Selectively
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has said Uganda will accept foreign aid selectively and the role of development partners should be limited to supporting the country's economic vision.
The President said he had suggested that Global Fund be used for research on malaria, because he had successfully campaigned against AIDS without Global Fund, but his suggestions were discarded.
"This Global Fund came to lead my people into temptation," he said. "So this aid must be selectively accepted."
Opening the Public Expenditure Review Workshop at Speke Resort Munyonyo on Tuesday, Museveni said Uganda's performance would have been even more impressive if the principle of vision-led aid had been upheld.
He reminded Uganda's development partners that they had the duty to support the country's vision. He cautioned that surrendering sovereignty in favour of money was a big mistake.
"Their role is to support the vision of Uganda - nothing more, nothing less," he said. "Any aid-led vision rather than vision-led strategy is bound to fail or, at best, produce mediocre results."
Museveni, however, said Uganda had scored impressively since 1986 on several fronts. He cited the significant reduction in the number of people living below the poverty line, a stable macro-economic framework and rapid expansion in primary school enrolment through UPE (universal primary education).
He said in order to realise Uganda's economic vision of achieving medium income status, focus would be on macroeconomic stability maintenance, universal secondary education, improved governance, increased power generation, investments in science and technology and industrial research.
Museveni said Uganda had registered success in research fields. A scientist made a breakthrough in banana research and is in the process of setting up a banana processing factory to manufacture flour and starch, he said.

Original_Search_Title: May 12 is Pride Day Ugandans Will Live to Remember for Years
Headline: Uganda: May 12 is Pride Day Ugandans Will Live to Remember for Years
Body_Text:
Friday, May 12, 2006 is one of those memorable days one will live to remember. This is the day President Museveni will be sworn in as President of Uganda. It will be a joyous day for those who campaigned for his success and the NRM supporters in general. Kololo will be yellow.
Looking back it has been a long journey. In 2001 President Museveni was sworn in. A few months later, Mr Bidandi Ssali started his campaign calling for a successor to Mr Museveni and a return to multiparty politics.
In December of that year, the National Executive Committee sat at Kyankwanzi to address the issue of multiparty which had generated debate within the Movement. A committee, chaired by Dr Crispus Kiyonga, was set up to study this subject and report to NEC.
In its report, the committee recommended strengthening the Movement but Bidandi, who was a committee member, wrote a minority report that recommended a return to multiparty.
When the President read the committee's report, he convinced the NEC members to accept a return to multiparty.
Consequently, NEC was summoned in March 2003 to receive the Kiyonga report. In that meeting, it took the President two days to convince the delegates to accept a return to a multiparty system.
It was in the same meeting and the National Conference that took place early April 2003 that the issue of removal of term limits was discussed. Recommendations were made by the conference to the Sempebwa Constitutional Review Commission embracing a multiparty system and removal of term limits from the Constitution.
The above is a brief on how the celebration of May 12 started.
Kategaya hated kisanja
When the issue of removal of term limits came up at Kyankwanzi and the national debate that followed in the media, people like Mzee Bidandi, Mzee Kategaya, Miria Matembe, Mugisha Muntu and others opposed it.
The Movement Secretariat had recommended in writing the retention of term limits, but a majority of the delegates at Kyankwanzi argued for the removal of the restrictions. It was only Kalangala and Masindi districts whose memoranda opposed the removal of term limits.
It was later learnt that the people who read the memoranda from those two districts expressed their individual opinions and not the district position. I commend the superior organisation of those who supported removal of term limits.
They made their case and had consulted the district Movement leaders before coming to Kwankwanzi. Those who attempted to convince them to abandon their position failed.
The fruits of that organisation can be seen as President Museveni swears in May 12th.
Of course there were other players especially the MPs who finally passed the constitutional amendment bill that removed the term limits.
To them I say congratulations. One cannot forget the Movement mobilisers who went to the population to sensitise them to support the removal of term limits.
Without this sensitisation some of the MPs who later supported the removal of term limits wouldn't have done so. When many went back to their constituencies, they found a population demanding removal of term limits.
I remember the Masaka Young Movementists led by Mr Byakatonda and their Kampala counterparts, Muwanga Lutaaya, Sebina Sekitoleko, Asiimwe and honourable Nyombi Thembo (at that time he was not yet a minister) organised a rally to which I was invited to give a talk on the resolutions of Kyankwanzi.
Dissenters later joined
A certain MP from Masaka, whom I will not mention, was given banana leaves and he threw them away. At that time in 2003, the balance of forces was not certain and some of the people feared to be identified on either side. Because of the pressure from the population, by the end of 2004 the MP I have referred to above was donning banana leaves.
MPs also took to the public and the first rally by MPs in support of kisanja took place in Ntungamo in January 2004 in Mwesigwa Rukutana's constituency. Ministers Sam Kutesa, Hope Mwesigye, MPs Anifa Kawooya, Ida Mehangye, Rwakimari and others, addressed it.
The members of Parliament who supported removal of term limits and those of us who were not in Parliament but were in support of removal of term limits (without forgeting MP elect Frank Tumwebaze, Ofwono Opondo, Fred Bamwine, Aisha Kabanda, Tamale Mirundi and many others) through seminars, radios and writing in the newspapers were called sycophants, creepers who were taking Uganda to doom.
They used to say that if Ugandans agree to amend the constitution and later elect President Museveni there would be chaos.
Opponents predicted chaos
I remember Mzee Kategaya being quoted in The Weekly Observer advising the people of Ankole against supporting removal of term limits, saying that other tribes would hate them.
All these threats and intimidation by the anti-kisanja forces have come to naught. There were also threats and lectures from some embassies with their carrot of aid cuts but Ugandans simply ignored them.
To them (majority Ugandans) it was no Museveni no aid.
Those who took the right path have finally been victorious and the benefits are the swearing in. It sounds sweet doesn't it?
To all those who supported the kisanja movement please rejoice as your efforts have come to fruition.
Now that you have put food on the table there are guests who will come to eat what you have prepared including those who despised you or simply looked on when you were in the kitchen cooking.
Your role as victors is to consolidate the achievements in the NRM manifesto and score on the promises.
Strategy for 2011
The NRM should listen to the concerns of the population and address them through government programmes and policies. This way the 2011 victory will be guaranteed. No one will win in 2011 because of religion or tribal bigotry but performance.
After all the Joint Christian Council opposed the kisanja openly, not to mention one top religious leader who vehemently opposed the kisanja, but the flock didn't listen to them. In 2001 some Mengo ministers openly campaigned for Besigye.
Mengo backed Besigye
In the last elections the Katikkiro, while receiving Besigye at Bulange, criticied those who "claim to have a vision" but the voters in Buganda voted for President Museveni.
The voters know when to obey the religious and cultural leaders and when to choose political leaders. So the NRM should put emphasis on deliverance. This is what will bring popularity to the party.
The author is the Presidential Assistant for Political Affairs

Original_Search_Title: Constitution Amendment Can't Ruin Judiciary, FG Replies Uwais
Headline: Nigeria: Constitution Amendment Can't Ruin Judiciary, FG Replies Uwais
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Government yesterday dismissed as baseless and unnecessary, an alarm sounded by the retiring Chief Justice of Nigeria (CJN), Justice Muhammadu Lawal Uwais over the capacity of the constitution amendment bill to ruin the nation's judiciary and its fledgling democracy if passed as it is.
Attorney-General of the Federation and Minister of Justice, Chief Adebayo Ojo (SAN) who replied the CJN yesterday over the issue said the proposal cited by the jurist in the bill seeking to empower the executive to appoint judicial officers in the country would rather assist in strengthening the judiciary instead of destroying it.
Chief Ojo wondered if there was anything unusual about the proposal, given the provisions of the 1963 and 1979 constitutions on the appointment of judicial officers in the country.
"The CJN was appointed as justice of the Supreme Court and Chief Justice of Nigeria under a provision similar to the one contained in the proposal before the National Assembly."
Ojo (SAN) said that since independence, the constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria had always empowered the President to appoint the Chief Justice of Nigeria and the justices of the Supreme Court.
He cited page 79 of the 1963 constitution, section 112 which empowered the President to appoint the Chief Justice of Nigeria on the advice of the prime minister subject to the confirmation of the Senate.
He said justices of the Supreme Court were also appointed by the President.
He added that even under the 1979 constitution under which Justice Uwais was appointed by General Olusegun Obasanjo as a justice of the Supreme Court and Chief Justice of Nigeria by late General Sani Abacha, he said section 211 (1) of the constitution says "the president shall, in his discretion, appoint the Chief Justice of Nigeria subject to the confirmation by the National Assembly.
According to him, "Section 211 (2) of the same constitution says the President shall appoint other justices of the Supreme Court subject to the confirmation by the National Assembly.
"It was under this same power that the incumbent president appointed Justice Uwais as Supreme Court Justice in 1979 without any involvement of the NJC.
"In 1995, Justice Uwais was made the CJN on the sole discretion of Abacha (late Gen. Sani Abacha) without any recourse to NJC and nobody said anything about politicising it.
"What is being proposed now is section 211 of the 1979 constitution. It will strengthen the judiciary.
"What it means is that erudite lawyers can be appointed straight to the apex court without serving at lower courts.
"After all, it is history today that Justice Olawale Elias was never a judge before his appointment as a justice of the Supreme Court and as Chief Justice of the Federation, same for Justice Nnamani.
"Even at a time Elias was Attorney-General of the Federation but he later moved to the apex court and he did well as a justice.
"All the appointments were merited, they would not be politicised if that section of the bill sails through.
"I have a high regard for the Chief Justice of Nigeria but I humbly disagree with him on this. Remember that whatever appointment is made by the President will still be subject to the confirmation of the Senate," he added.
Uwais, had, last week, warned against the wholesale passage of the controversial bill seeking to amend the 1999 constitution, saying there were some dangerous provisions therein with capabilities of destroying the nation's judiciary and its fledgling democracy.
The CJN cited a section in the bill which transferred the powers of appointment and dismissal of judges from the National Judicial Council (NJC) to the President in the case of Federal judicial officers and to the governors in the case of state judicial officers.
He had warned that should the bill sail through, Nigerians should say bye-bye to democracy because Nigeria would be reduced to a banana republic.
He said some dangerous sections in the bill targeted to paralyse the judiciary should be killed otherwise the nation would be worse for it.
The said dangerous section of the bill seeks to amend section 231 (1) and (2); 238 (1) and (2); 250 (1) and (2); 256 (1) and (2); 261 (1) and (2), 266 (1) (2); 271 (1) and (2) and 282 (1) and (2) of the 1999 constitution.
Each of the said sections sought to be amended provides for the procedure of appointing judicial officers from the Supreme Court to the customary courts both at the state and the Federal judicature.
Uwais had said: "Our concern at the moment in the judiciary is that what we have at the moment in the bill will erode the powers of the National Judicial Council (NJC).
Under the present 1999 Constitution, it is the function of the National Judicial Council to recommend to the president and governors for the appointment of heads of court.

Original_Search_Title: PRSP Becomes One-Year Old
Headline: Sierra Leone: PRSP Becomes One-Year Old
Body_Text:
Freetown — Africell mobile phone company, popularly dubbed "Poverty Reduction Strategy Phone Company" (PRSP), celebrated its first year of existence in the country Saturday.
March 14th was the actual first anniversary of the company.
In his speech at the Country Lodge, Managing Director of Africell-Lintel (SL) Ltd., Mohamad Ghaddar said the night was a "momentous occasion for Africell in particular and for the people of Sierra Leone in general." He continued that when President Kabbah first launched the company on 14th March 2005, its operations were only limited to Freetown and its environs only. The Managing Director said work on the modalities for expansion up country then began in earnest. He said the company was faced with enormous challenges, but thanks to the concerted efforts and expertise of their dedicated staff and the tremendous support of the government.
On the issue of expansion, Ghaddar said 14 months after the initial launching, they are gathered at the Country Lodge to celebrate the giant strides Africell has made. The company, he said, has transcended the narrow confines of Freetown to now cover Rotifunk, Mano, Moyamba, and Bo districts in the South;Kenema, Shegbwema, Koidu and Sefadu in the east; Port Loko, Lungi, Lunsar, Kambia, Makeni, Magburaka, ,Makali, Bagbema and Mile 91 in the north. He assured the peninsular villages of York, Borbor, Hamilton, Tokeh and No. 2 River and Banana Island have been connected to the Africell network, making it one of the most widespread networks in the country.
The Managing Director emphasized that this feat could never have been achieved without the support of the government through the ministry of Transport and Communications and the Ministry of Finance.
On the issue of products of Africell and their tariffs, The Managing Director said they have tailored everything in line with their corporate objective to provide quality communication services to the people of Sierra Leone at the most affordable prices. He said these qualities have earned Africell the nickname "PRSP" and "Wan Tem Scratch" for their connection speed and voice quality.
He maintained Africell currently has the cheapest tariff overall. "Our sim cards are sold for Le. 9000 loaded with 200 units of talk time," the Managing Director bluffed adding that they currently operate a tri-band tariff system that spans from 30 per unit (i.e.1 unit per minute) to totally free on net calls overnight.
He said they operate a per second billing system wherein customers are charged for cumulative seconds.
"We provide value added services like conference calls, calls divert, caller line identification/restriction, call waiting and call hold, SMS info providing horoscope, sports, business and current affairs updates," he said.
The managing director continued that they provide flexible solution for their customers that suit their requirements and meet their needs for efficient and effective communication across the whole country.
On the issue of corporate social responsibility, Ghaddar said Africell has a personal commitment to promote those activities that touch the heart of Sierra Leoneans. He said in the last 14 months, the company has invested in various areas including organizing inter secondary school quiz competitions; provide funding support for the Sierra Leone Association of Journalists in their 2005 conference; provide funding support for the Under 17 National team in their last world Cup qualifying match against The Gambia and partnering with the SLBS TV to sponsor for the first time, pre and post match analysis during the last African Nations Cup among others.
He continued that on the country's Independence Day celebration, on 27th April 2006, Africell in collaboration with the Sierra Leone Football Association organized a friendly international match between Leone Stars and Guinea, which the former won.
He said Africell conducted a raffle draw after the match and Alpha Barrie, a student of Milton Margai College of Education and technology won the Star Prize, which is a trip to Germany to watch the 2006 World Cup finals. He said several other consolation prizes were given out.
The Managing Director said they have signed a memorandum of understanding with SLBS TV for screening of the June 2006 World Cup to be hosted in Germany so that Sierra Leoneans can watch the entire tournament from the comfort of their homes.
On the issue of future expansion, the Africell boss maintained in the next two weeks, Kabala, Tongo Fields, Daru, Pendembu, Masiaka and Tombo would start talking on Africell which will cover phase two of the company's expansion plans.
He said they intend starting the phase three expansion programme in June. Africell, he said, aims to cover all the remaining major towns and cities in all districts of the country.
Ghaddar said the company aims to be the most preferred mobile telecommunications company in Sierra Leone, their unique combination of quality and affordability coupled with their involvement in social life of Sierra Leoneans will continue to form the foundation for achieving this.
Ghaddar concluded in thanking all Sierra Leoneans for their support and confidence in the company.
Minister of Transport and Communications, Dr. Prince Alex Harding who deputized President Kabbah in his keynote address said Africell was celebrating a success story. He said the success story was based on the performance of the company, as they were quick to take advantage of enabling environment in the country.
Dr. Harding recalled there was a lull in the operations of Africell, but the company restrategized and hit the market with a bang, which is good for the country and the company. "Within one year Africell has been able to do what others have taken long years to do. They have brought into the country state-of-the-art technology," the Transport Minister said.
On his part, Chairman of the occasion, Minister of Development and economic Planning, Mohamed Daramy said Africell has made giant strides during its short period of operations. "Africell will soon be present in many parts of the country," he assured.

Original_Search_Title: The Role of Adults in the Youth Culture
Headline: Nigeria: The Role of Adults in the Youth Culture
Body_Text:
AMBIVALENCE is a luxury one can little afford where youthful exuberance is a not too distant memory. When I was much younger, I always liked to be in what I used to call a de-militarized zone; I was not a soldier in any camp, either in God's army or the devil's. I could ignore most things if I was not personally affected. I was a Moslem when I was with Moslems and a Christian when with Christians; life was easier if I reduced everything to science and logic. I vaguely believed in God but was not keen to get too close or put in any hard work to substantiate any real faith; whatever could not be proved physically in my book was religious mumbo jumbo!
Growing up in a Christian home meant I had inherited my parents' belief system without making any commitment of my own. I came to the lazy conclusion that God was different thing to different people, religion was determined by factors of culture and environment, out of curiosity I did a bit of research and found that the major religions had evil and good in common; just different ways of getting followers to fall in line. Well, life doesn't always stay so simple and very soon the very real existence of evil begins to create a need for a safe haven, an omnipotent and all powerful presence to offer comfort in times of need. One of the things I miss about youth is that feeling of forever; when you are young, adults are old and boring, and life is a rollercoaster that never stops!
You get hurt, you cry and move on; you fall and you get up, dust yourself off and continue life. Life moves at such a fast pace that it becomes difficult to factor in consequences when taking decisions. I recall what I believe was one of the most stupid things I ever did in my youth, a friend had bought a convertible car and we were going to rock it. I suddenly decided to sit on the boot, holding on to nothing for support at break neck speed. I remember the wind tearing at my hair and my eyes dripping with water as my eyelashes splayed into the eye balls. It was for less than five minutes but the stupidity of my actions was driven home by a ghastly motor accident that claimed the life of a friend just days later. Like me she had decided to stand up through the sun roof of another sports car but had lost her life when the car somersaulted! Youth is often the excuse for some needless tragedies!
Maturity and God become a reality when mortality becomes a certainty. My second encounter with mortality was a rather rude awakening. It was a day before my 25th birthday and I had gone to the video club to return some over due tapes. While there I came across two friends and they were stating their intention of eating and drinking themselves to oblivion at the party that had become an annual event in my house and we chatted for a bit after which I made my way home. I'll never forget that birthday, I was looking forward to the day and all of my presents, I slept late and came down to find people huddled in groups in the living room. My friends were all looking forward to the party but wasn't 9:30 am a bit early for a party that was for early evening? On moving closer I realised most of them were crying!! I knew something was wrong but never in my wildest dreams could I have guessed what they were about to tell me!!
Experiencing grief
I am not even sure who told me but I remember hearing the news and it seemed to echo in my head again and again! KT is dead! How? When? I was with him and Banana (the nickname for Biodun; the other friend) at the video club till 10 pm, he was going to bed, how could he have died! Was he shot? My questions caused more tears as the answer I got was as ridiculous as it was insane. He simply did not wake up! This was utter madness! How on earth does an otherwise healthy twenty something year old simply die quietly in his sleep? My spirits crashed faster than a ton of bricks and something in me snapped to attention; we were not invincible!
Youth was not a shield against death or disease. Quite a number of my friends all say that most of us grew up that day. It was not as if any of us had never experienced grief or loss but we had got used to a belief system and order of life that things happened in a rational and sequential manner! You died from illness, old age, in a motor accident or worse still armed robbers or policemen just shot you by design or mistake. Our logical deductions did not make an allowance for such tragedies that become acts of God when there are no reasonable explanations. Nobody is exempted, not me or anyone; the realisation of imminent mortality created a lot of fear and I felt a strong yearning for more meaning in life. I was sure God had not put me on earth to just eat, chop and party.
These days, I am often accused of being very impatient and even high handed with some young people and I must confess to being guilty 6 times out of 10, though not often for the reason most believe. I am not indifferent or uncaring but I hate to see people make needless mistakes that could have consequences that cut across board unevenly. Unfortunately patience is not a virtue I lay claim to and I tend to give up on people when they seem bent on self destruction. Mentoring is a lot better than shoving advice down on people's throats without explanations or any consideration; unfortunately that takes a lot of time. There are so may wrong turns I could have avoided if I had a stronger role model to guide me when I was younger, rather I had adults who never listened to anything I had to say, yet felt competent to judge and condemn at the slightest excuse.
It is such an irony that I am beginning to sound like some of the adults I used to resent when I was younger but I give thanks that I am still in touch with my youth enough to pull back and befriend instead of condemn. Life is a cycle that keeps rolling, we all play the same role, it's the exact same script just different actors. Young people often never think of the consequences of their actions, that job is for the adults. My grouse is often not with the very young, it is mostly with the adults that encourage bad behaviour when they know better. A couple of years ago, a young girl was looking to buy a dress in one of my stores and a little scene unfolded that leaves me firmly convinced that this generation could be in some trouble. She came with her mother and could not have been older than 14; her breasts were still at the budding stage and was reminiscent of young womanhood.
She needed some assistance and we were trying to see how the dress could be adjusted to fit her petite frame when an aging sun burnt and I believe Italian man walked right into the changing room! I tried to block his entrance when the mother informed me that it was okay. "That's her husband," she said. I've been in the service industry long enough to know it's not my place to show disgust or judgement but it took all of the self control I had not to strangle the mother!
He began to grope the poor child in full view of everyone and I would have made my escape if the girl had let go of my hand. I managed to get him out of the changing room and let the poor child change in peace.
The tragedy of the situation was that the girl and I were the only ones who felt ashame; her mother and the ageing paedophile carried on as though it were normal! Turning a blind eye to a child's misdemeanour because it's easier or in this case for obvious personal gain is inexcusable. The economic situation of the country has taken authority from the parents and when children offer some financial relief, parents don't have the luxury of getting on a moral high ground. I guessed that the girl was probably sacrificing herself for the sake of her family but the explanation did not make the situation any less disgusting! I have seen some things of late that suggest to me that adults play the most damning part in the terrible youth culture we have now.
If adults won't clearly define family values, how can the young ones be expected to interpret them correctly? Many magazines featured semi nude pictures of young girls on their covers this past week and I think it indicts the adults a lot more than even the girls in question. Youthful exuberance is a legitimate excuse but surely the adults must know that the photographs will come back to haunt them at some time? It is not my place to judge anyone but we all had better sit up and begin to play a more active part in the youth culture before we totally lose control. Last week a doctor said that 6 out of every 10 people who tested for HIV were positive and worse still they were as young as 13! That is an indictment on every single one of us, young and old.

Original_Search_Title: Battle Lines Drawn for Northern Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Battle Lines Drawn for Northern Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — As Kenyans eagerly await Speaker of the National Assembly Francis Ole Kaparo to declare five seats vacant, political parties have gone overdrive in wooing potential aspirants perceived to be strong. Northern Kenya is already in election mood and as Ali Abdi reports, it will take more than strong candidates to win here.
The battle lines for the 'mini-elections' in Saku, Laisamis, North Horr and Moyale constituencies have been drawn.
The seats were left vacant following the death of MPs Abdi Tari Sasura (Saku), Titus Ngoyoni (Laisamis), Dr Bonaya Godana (North Horr) and Dr Guracha Galgalo (Moyale) in the April 10 plane crash in Marsabit town. The MPs were among 14 dignitaries who died while on a peace mission to Marsabit. The other MPs killed in the tragedy were Mirugi Kariuki (Nakuru Town) and Lt Gen Abdullahi Aden (East African legislative Assembly).
It is either widows or brothers of the fallen MPs who will face perennial bitter rivals of the former legislators.
The major issue cutting across the four neighbouring constituencies will be banditry and cattle rustling among the dominant Borana, Gabra and Rendille communities.
In North Horr and Moyale, the aspirants will have to contend with the external aggression from both the Oromo Liberation Front (OLF) rebels and the Ethiopian security forces.
The Ethiopians often cross into Kenya to kill and steal from the residents and both local leaders and the Government have failed in the past to address the critical issue.
Clan politics and alliances have always significantly contributed to the outcome of elections in all the constituencies.
North Horr: The widow of the late Dr Godana, Sarah is so far the only aspirant in this constituency that is the largest in the country.
A series of meetings held both in Nairobi and North Horr concluded that whoever is proposed from the deceased's family will go to Parliament unopposed.
This is why Dr Godana's perennial rival in the last two general elections, Elema Isako Fila of Narc (LDP wing), stepped down for the widow.
Tradition, Fila said, entails that "a woman is allowed to finish what was left undone by her deceased husband".
He noted that anyone outside the family who stands now for the by-election would be viewed as merciless and greedy not only by the elders but the whole Gabra community. Fila would have contested had former Lamu DC Ukur Yattani, who is on further studies in the UK, contested. The administrator, who is viewed as a confidant of the late MP, left the country last weekend after attending the meetings that proposed Sarah.
The sole aspirant will have to deal with recurrent clashes that pit her community against the Borana in Saku, Moyale and southern Ethiopia and the Rendille in Laisamis.
Sarah, also a Gabra, is a retired nurse. Her last place of work before she retired was at Kenyatta National Hospital. She will most probably contest on a Kanu ticket.
Moyale: Three aspirants have so far declared their interest in this constituency that is also a district.
The late area MP's younger brother, Wario Malla Galgallo, will battle it out with Haji Adan Wachu and Mohammed Malicha Galgalo.
Malla, 27, is single and unlike his elder brother, a Muslim. He completed his studies at the University of Nairobi where he was pursuing a Bachelor of Commerce degree course last December.
In an interview, elders Huqa Guleid, Ibrahim Bakata and Haji Khalif said elders from the district settled on Malla so that he could complete the mandate the late Dr Guracha was given by the voters in 2002.
Just like in the other three constituencies, the pastoralists believe that in the case of death, widows or relatives of the deceased should 'inherit' the seat.
"The late MP was elected and his term ends in 2007. He died while on a peace mission. This is why we asked other potential candidates not to vie and instead leave it to the younger brother," said Bakata.
Dr Galgalo's closest challenger in the last General Election, Mohamud Ali and a leading NGO official, Chachu Tadicha bowed to the 'traditions' and opted out of the race.
Ali, a city based quantity surveyor and Tadicha, the coordinator of Community Initiative Facilitation and Assistance (Cifa), were considered heavyweight challengers before they announced their intention not to vie.
Malla comes from the dominant Karayu clan and he heavily relies on sympathy votes especially from his home in Sololo division. Haji Wachu is the secretary general of the influential Supreme Council of Kenya Muslims (Supkem) and is likely contest on a Narc-Kenya ticket.
The 48-year-old father of seven is also a director with the National Anti-Corruption Steering Committee and the National Council for Population and Development. He holds a higher diploma in public administration and law and was a paralegal officer with the High Court in Nairobi for 15 years.
He comes from the equally dominant Warjeeda clan and also hails from Sololo. Mohammed Galgalo was a two-term area MP between 1983-1988 and 1992-1997. He was nominated to Parliament in 1997 by Ford Kenya. He vied on a Narc ticket in 2002 and lost to Dr Galgalo.
The 54-year-old former teacher and education officer hails from Warjeeda clan like Wachu, which could complicate matters for both of them when it comes to sharing votes.
Laisamis: The late Ngoyoni's widow, Mary will face three other aspirants who include a former MP and a woman.
Mary was proposed by village elders to vie for the seat held by her husband because the deceased's children are young and in school while his brothers are illiterate. Elders Nkarung'a Bagajo from the late MP's Sale clan accompanied by his counterparts from Dubsai and Tubcha clans, Henry Debelen and Lengesen Lugi respectively, said the decision to have the widow vie was arrived at following meetings in Kargi, Korr and Merille.
The late MP's popularity was reflected during the referendum in which the Banana side got 3,681 against Orange's 1,783. Laisamis was the only constituency that voted for the 'Yes' side in northern Kenya.
The widow said at her Nairobi's South 'C' residence that her priority would be to pursue peace with the Rendille neighbours.
"I have a feeling that Marsabit is cursed. All leaders from the district need to bring the locals together. We must repent our sins and pray. Peace is top on my agenda," said Mrs Ngoyoni.
The widow said she would reveal her party after the completion of the mourning period set to be from the third week of this month. Given that her late husband was an assistant minister by the time of his death, Mary is expected to vie on the ruling party's ticket.
Mary will have to contend with challenges posed by Allyce Esintele Kureiya, the only other woman to vie for the seat in this gender-biased constituency since independence. The 41-year-old is a holder of BEd degree from Kenyatta University and is currently the programme manager of the Netherlands Development Organisation (SNV), in charge of South Rift and Nairobi.
Ms Kureiya has been in the NGO world for the last two decades working among the pastoralists of Isiolo, Samburu, Marsabit and Moyale among other places. She has been particularly involved in capacity building and community development project.
The lady, who wears many hats in the field of leadership, advocacy and management says her candidature represents real hope for the Laisamis residents who have been languishing in poverty, disease and insecurity.
Quoting Britain's wartime Prime Minister Winston Churchill, "We make a living by what we get, but we make a life by what we give," the aspirant says she is out to give her all in order to change Laisamis for better in terms of security and general development.
Robert Kochale, who lost to Ngoyoni, also hails from the Sale clan. He however said he was contesting because the prevailing security situation calls for a person with the necessary experience.
He said during his tenure as the area MP between 1992 and 2002, clan clashes with neighbouring tribes were minimal and he wants to use that experience to restore peace in Marsabit. He will vie on a Kanu ticket.
Kochale, who is also a director with the Kenya Industrial Research and Development Institute (Kirdi) will have to struggle to convince Sale elders to vote for him. The clan has also proposed the late Ngoyoni's widow.
The other candidate reportedly eyeing the seat is US based Joseph Lekuton.
Saku: The battle for Saku will pit two heavyweights - the elder Sasura, Hussein and the man whose chief campaigner he was in the1988 and 1992 general elections, Jarso Jillo Falana (popularly known as "JJ") of LDP.
In the 1997 and 2002 general elections that pitted Sasura and Falana, the campaign periods were marred by violence and scores of their supporters were injured.
Sasura had been a chief and close associate of Falana but they parted ways. Hussein was also close to JJ and was actually assisted to become a DO but they fell out when Sasura became the area MP.
Due to the existing differences between the duo and the one between the deceased and Falana, the battle for Saku is expected to provide more fireworks than ever before.
Hussein, who is yet to decide the party ticket to contest on, holds a Bachelor of Education degree from Kenyatta University and is currently an education officer with the Nairobi City Council.
The Borana followed in an ascending order by the Rendille, Burji and the Gabra predominantly occupy Saku.
The battle will be close as it has always been. In the last elections, the late Sasura polled 5,718 against Falana's 4,470. The majority of Rendille and Burji voted for Sasura while the Gabra overwhelmingly backed Falana. Sasura got more of the Borana votes.
He will, like his late younger brother, enter into an alliance with the Rendille and Burji and fight to get at least half of the Borana votes. If he succeeds in this, then things might go his way.
On the other hand, things could go Falana's way if the Borana deny Hussein the sympathy votes and he manages to get some votes from the Burji and Rendille.
Falana, who is a director of the State-owned Kenya Broadcasting Corporation, is reportedly also eyeing the Government wing of Narc.

Original_Search_Title: Red Card for Soccer Racism
Headline: Uganda: Red Card for Soccer Racism
Body_Text:
They say he accumulates goals as quickly as he picks up red and yellow cards. Others call him arrogant and hot-tempered, but love him or hate him; you can't deny that Samuel Eto'o is one of the greatest soccer stars on the international scene.
Commentators say that in Eto'o, Barcelona have made probably their most astute signing. When Real Madrid scouts signed Eto'o, he was just 16. But everything went wrong; Madrid loaned him to a second division club and later dumped him.
Everything may have gone wrong at Madrid but everything is going well for Eto'o right now. Well, almost. Despite being one of the greatest players, many European fans can't come to terms with the fact that a black man is excelling in Europe. During some league matches, whenever he touches the ball, monkey noises fill the stadium.
Racism is turning the beautiful game that is supposed to unify people into an ugly, divisive game - even Arsenal's Thierry Henry has repeatedly been called monkey. Many fans have thrown bananas on the field when black players touch the ball.
Some soccer players have now teamed up with several organisations to tackle racial insults in stadiums. Thierry started the campaign in response to on-the-field jeers he faced while playing for France and Arsenal .
Despite racial insults, black players continue to play, win and excel in Europe. God must have created in them a resolute and steadfast heart to persevere no matter what the difficulty.

Original_Search_Title: Where is Nnamani?
Headline: Nigeria: Where is Nnamani?
Body_Text:
Each time we reflect on how far we have come as a people, we are shocked by some of the absurdities we have had to live through; and still live with. Most times we are awakened by the revolting thought that, in spite of our size and endowments in human and material resources, Nigeria, our Nigeria, can, afterall, become a glorified banana republic of a sort. The incurable optimists among us may dismiss this fear as an impossibility that can never happen here. Indeed, most Nigerians declared in like manner, when military president, General Ibrahim Babangida stepped aside in August, 1993. But how far did that take us? Did we not end up being ruled by the maximum ruler, General Sani Abacha?
The truth is that (Nigeria being a theatre of the absurd) anything is possible. Therefore, it may well be, as Senator Arthur Nzeribe has revealed, that the inslting issue of third term or tenure elongation is "a done deal". One thing is clear from the emerging scenario of the "done deal" assault: the mere apprehension of the evil that is so very imminent, has already put our destiny into a situation of utmost danger.
To be sure, and as William Jennings Bryan, an American political leader and orator would say, "destiny is no matter of chance. It is matter of choice. It is not a thing to be waited for, it is a thing to be achieved". One implication of this truth, is that we can change our destiny with our thoughts. Yes; we shall become what we wish to become, but only when our habitual thoughts align with what we desire. It is a matter for regret that our thoughts have become incoherent; in fact, effeminate, as a result of the inanity called third term or tenure elongation.
Our once virile men have been castrated by circumstances they cannot control; and, therefore, have they become impotent, to thejeopardy of our tomorrow. These are trying times indeed for Nigeria and Nigerians. These are times that try men's hearts. These are therefore, not the times for ease and comfort, but, as Winston Churchill would declare, times to dare and endure. Today, some of our brightest and best have been taken captive, and forced by circumstances clearly beyond them, to say and do things, which, left alone, they may never have contemplated. The need to attack the "circumstances", and set the captives free, has become a compelling. Among other "unofficial prisoners of conscience", the case of Dr. Chimaroke Nnamani, the Governor of Enugu State, is intriguing. He has been missing in action ever since the Summit of Leaders of the South which he hosted in Enugu, under agreement, approval and support of those who now plot to do him in. But why should the turn of events which obviously dislocated the calculations and expectations of the sponsors, be now blamed on another wise obedient servant who, in truth, had no control over the events/reactions that followed?
