We seek the return of Chimaroke Nnamani to the centre stage, because he has demonstrated over time, that it is a healthy thing to, from time to time, hang question marks on the things we have long taken for granted, but which have not been helpful to our march to greatness as a nation. Before now, it was easy to place Chimaroke Nnamani as a man who has already seen tomorrow, and has invested heavily in it. Our reading is that his interest in the tomorrow is borne, not out of selfish considerations, but out of genuine concern for succeeding generations.
This contrasts sharply to the disposition of the pretenders whose concern is only with the present. Apart from being the champion of what have today become popular as "Legacy Projects" (among other democracy dividends), Chimaroke Nnamani has stood out in this political dispensation, as one who brings a touch of philosophy to bear on issues of governance, and takes them to levels of abstraction and appreciation that ennoble the heart.
In these times when some have left the substance and are pitiably pre-occupied with chasing shadow, many certainly miss Chimaroke Nnamani whose lectures used to provoke the mind to reach to the depths in the collective search for meaning and essence. Certainly, we miss the depth of knowledge which normally would drive his discourse and expose of issues that broaden the word view of those who listen to his treatise on the state of the Nigerian nation. What has happened to Chimaroke Nnamani is not altogether surprising; for, as Tobias Smollett, a Scottish novelist would say, "Who bravely dares must sometimes risk a fall". And now, if it will require a one-million-mam-march to lift him up from the "fall", and return him to the turf, we will be standing in the front rank with other patriots. Wherever he may be, Chimaroke Nnamani should carefully consider this piece of advice from W.J.Slim, a British Fieldmarshall and statesman: "When you cannot make up your mind which of two even ly-balanced courses of action you should take -choose the bolder".

Original_Search_Title: Review Team Winds Up Hearings
Headline: Kenya: Review Team Winds Up Hearings
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The team named by the President to jump-start constitutional review talks concluded its public hearings yesterday - without hearing views from the Orange Democratic Movement.
But the committee of eminent persons, led by Mr Bethuel Kiplagat, said that although its public hearings had come to a close, it was still ready to receive views from individuals and organised groups.
It is required to prepare and submit a report to the President by May 30.
ODM leaders, who declined to present their views to the committee, yesterday said they would not recognise its recommendations either.
They said the failure to consult all players in the review process before the 15-member team was formed was a sign that the Government did not want a new constitution.
MPs Raila Odinga, Mutula Kilonzo and William Ruto said the movement had appointed its own committee to find ways of restarting the review.
The MPs also said there was no administrative arm of Parliament - the people's representative - to oversee ways to jump-start the review talks. The President was doing things without consultation, they said.
But the Catholic Church, the Methodist Church in Kenya and Evangelical churches embraced the committee.
Yesterday, the committee received views from the top Catholic body - the Kenya Episcopal Conference - the Methodist church, the evangelical churches and other groups.
Kanu vice-chairman and former Cabinet minister Dalmas Otieno and a former commissioner with the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC), Prof Githu Muigai, were among individuals who also aired their views at the committee's headquarters at Cooperative Bank House in Nairobi.
The Catholic and Methodist churches added their voices to those calling for the constitution review to be delinked from next year's General Election.
Bishop Philip Sulumeti, the Kenya Episcopal Conference vice-chairman, and the presiding bishop of the Methodist church, Dr Stephen M'Impwii, said the next phase should be left in the hands of experts. But before the country embarks on a new process, there is need for reconciliation and national healing, the leaders who made separate representations, said.
Bishop Sulumeti said the committee of experts should be lean and should not include members of the defunct CKRC.
Prof Muigai recommended a minimal reforms package before the next election, similar to that reached just before the 1997 election under the Inter-Parties Parliamentary Group initiative.
Mr Kilonzo, the ODM chairman, said: "You cannot look for a way forward without consulting ODM" because the movement won the support of the people during the November referendum.
Mr Odinga accused the Government of reneging on its pledge to carry out law reforms. He wondered why offences like trespass and loitering still existed in statute books yet they were enacted to serve colonial interests. He said he would not give his opinion to Kiplagat's panel because it was a "Banana team".
Reported by Tony Kago, Mark Agutu and David Mugonyi

Original_Search_Title: Same Old May 1 Banners And Demands
Headline: Zambia: Same Old May 1 Banners And Demands
Body_Text:
Lusaka — As they have been doing over the years, workers today march carrying heavy banners with the same demands they have been making at every May 1 parade.
Instead of improving, their conditions are worsening by the day. Workers today face more difficulties and complex challenges than they did fifteen years ago. Everything seems to be going down for them. First, the membership of our trade unions is declining. The country has lost so many formal jobs and this means the membership of our trade unions has been declining. The decline in membership is accompanied by a reduction in the finances of our trade unions. These two factors have led to further weakening of the trade unions in Zambia.
The loss of membership and the decline in finances is having devastating effects on our trade unions. As a result of this weakening of the labour movement, many workers erroneously don't see the need for trade unions. It is increasingly becoming difficult for our trade unions to even strike. We know that the withdrawal of labour is the only real and potent weapon workers have in pressing for their demands. But this is increasingly becoming difficult to apply. The situation is also being compounded by the onerous legal requirements for any strike action to take place.
All these factors have also affected the development of reliable and effective trade union leadership. And the lack of quality leadership in the labour movement, at a time it is most needed, has left many workers helpless, desperate and confused.
To what can we attribute this situation? It cannot be denied that there was a systematic move by the Chiluba government to deliberately weaken the labour movement in Zambia. After Frederick Chiluba came to power in 1991 using the labour movement as a bridge, he attempted to blow up this bridge probably in the hope that no one should use it to unseat him. Political measures were taken to cause confusion and disunity in the labour movement ranks. And with the opportunism that Chiluba had unleashed in the trade union ranks, it was not difficult for him to use that and disunite the leaders of the labour movement. Since 1991, so many pieces of legislation have been enacted and whose effect has been to erode the workers' rights. Things which our workers won for themselves through their participation in the independence struggle and the other concessions they won through their struggles in the First and Second republics were quickly lost soon after Chiluba came to power.
The neo-liberal anti-worker economic policies and programmes of the International Monetary Fund and World Bank implemented by the Chiluba regime worsened matters for the workers. These policies and programmes resulted in the closure of so many companies all over the country, drastically reducing the number of workers and trade union membership. These policies and programmes were also accompanied by new labour laws that further weakened our trade unions.
Today, Zambia has no labour movement worth talking about. And even some labour leaders who are genuinely and seriously committed to the cause of our workers seem to be overwhelmed and confused by the magnitude of the challenges workers are today facing. And some of them have given up in the light of what seems to be insurmountable problems.
And these problems are compounded by the fragmentation of the labour movement. The proliferation of trade unions is one thing that doesn't seem to be helping matters. Of course, those who lead these unions have got their own arguments and justifications. As Frederick Angels once observed, there will always be an argument and justification for anything or everything. But what matters here is to look at what has been achieved or has not been achieved through this proliferation of trade unions. And to make matters worse, there is also a proliferation at the umbrella level - with two confederations, Zambia Congress of Trade Unions and the Federation of Free Trade Unions of Zambia. This doesn't make sense, especially in a situation where the general membership of trade unions is on the decline.
It is clear to us that labour movement in this country is no longer the powerful political force it used to be. This being the case, its general approach, strategies and tactics must take into account this factor. They say when you are few and weak, you fight in the bananas to make the maximum noise and confuse the enemy about your size, capacity or strength.
What does this mean? What it mean is that our labour movement first and foremost, needs to achieve the maximum levels of unity within its own ranks. All artificial differences, no matter how strongly workers and their leaders feel about them, must be pushed aside to pave way for a new form of unity among our workers within and across industries. Our labour movement in its current state cannot achieve much without the support and solidarity of other social and political forces within the nation. The labour movement needs the support of all - the Church and all other civil society organisations and institutions. And it is the duty of leaders of the labour movement to work for the mobilisation of this support and solidarity. The conditions have changed and the new environment under which they are operating or struggling call for a change in methods, strategies and tactics.
Pure and simple traditional trade unionism cannot deliver anything in the current conditions. This is because the neo-liberal policies and programmes that have been used to push them into this weak position are not simply a matter of economic doctrine. It is also a political strategy and doctrine of imperialism in its current stage.
And moreover, if we look back at the history of our labour movement, more has been achieved when it has taken a much broader and far-sighted outlook than when it has narrowed itself simply to questions of bread and butter.
We know the labour movement is trying, in its own limited way, to broaden its horizon but more needs to be done in this domain if we have to see a reversal of fortunes.
Again, as we have said every May 1, what the workers have achieved for themselves nothing was given to them graciously. They have had to fight for everything they have today. What they cannot win for themselves through struggle, no one will give it to them. We therefore urge our workers and their leaders to struggle without respite for their rights and a dignified life.
And as we have always done, we wish our workers all the best as they today ponder and meditate over their future and the struggles that lie ahead.

Original_Search_Title: NAFDAC Seizes 3,000 Tins of Fake Custard Powder
Headline: Nigeria: NAFDAC Seizes 3,000 Tins of Fake Custard Powder
Body_Text:
Enugu — Agents of the National Agency for Food, Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) in Enugu last Thursday impounded three thousand buckets of fake and substandard "Yoke" custard powder worth about N1.5 million brought for sale at the Ogbete main market, Enugu.
The custards which were in Egg and Banana brands and parked in 750 unmarked cartons were concealed in a lorry conveying building materials from Onitsha to the Coal City. The fake consumable, according to the agency, were suspected to have been produced at yet- to-be-identified factory in the commercial city of Onitsha.
Showing reporters the impounded fake product and the lorry that conveyed it Friday, Head, NAFDAC Establishment Inspectorate Directorate in the state, Pharmacist Udoekpo Ekpo, said that he personally led a team of NAFDAC officials to the market following a tip-off on the influx of the fake custards into the market.
The team, he said, went to the market about 6 am Thursday and waited till 8.15 am when a lorry brought the product down to Old Park near the Ogbete main market where goods meant for the market are normally offloaded by drivers.
He said, "Following a tip-off that the fake custards come into Ogbete main market every week, yesterday (Thursday), as early as 6 am I was out with my team to carry out surveillance on the market. We expected the lorry from Onitsha and we stationed at Old Park which is their usual offloading point. At exactly 8 am the trucks came and we carried out surveillance but we saw nothing.
"But at about 8.15 am, this particular lorry conveying the fake product came in and we discovered that the custards were concealed in other items including building materials. With the cooperation of Mobile policemen from "Mopol 3" and the Market Union Chairman, Chief Ossy Peters, we were able to bring down the lorry and the 750 cartons of fake custards to our office."
Ekpo said that the driver of the lorry took to his heels on sighting the NAFDAC team, adding that it was not possible to immediately determine the source of the fake product since the driver fled with the waybill.
"We need the waybill to enable us identify the owner as well as the factory where they were produced in Onitsha. We hope that the owner of the truck will come forward to claim it and then help us to get to the illegal manufacturer. The factory must be shutdown and the owner prosecuted accordingly" he said.
The custard packaged in a two kilogram plastic container had no NAFDAC registration number but had fake batch number on the container without manufacturing date. The containers also had fake factory address while the powder was poured directly into the container without the cellophane bag normally used by the original manufacturer of the product, New Generation Industries Limited, Lagos to package it.
Also speaking on the development, the South East Zonal head of the agency, Mr. Pascal Njoku, noted with satisfaction the cooperation of the Nigerian masses in the battle against fake and adulterated products. He advised the public to always expose those behind such fake products, assuring that the agency would not relent in its campaign to eradicate fake and substandard products in the country. Njoku said that the custards would be destroyed after a thorough investigation which would be targeted at getting to the source of the custards with a view to forestalling further production.

Original_Search_Title: Key Kibaki Allies Not in Frontline of Narc-Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Key Kibaki Allies Not in Frontline of Narc-Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — One thing that was quite interesting to note at the unveiling of Narc-Kenya on Friday, was that familiar faces that have previously been close to President Kibaki were not on the frontline.
Men like Mr Kiraitu Murungi or Dr Chris Murungaru are clearly not in the party's driving seat, though they fully support its creation. Equally apparent is that their successors in power such as Mr John Michuki seem to be very much in the background.
The one holding brief after the Narc-Kenya's Friday "consultative" meeting was Immigration minister Gideon Konchellah. From the outset, the party wanted to cast itself in a cosmopolitan light and as one propelled by fresh faces. In fact, the pointmen Narc-Kenya up to now has been deliberately putting on display are youthful types such as MPs Danson Mungatana, Maina Kiunjuri, Ananiah Mwaboza, and so on. It has also lined up a battery of regionally spaced Cabinet ministers such as Mr Konchellah himself, Mr Raphael Tuju, Mr Chirau Ali Mwakwere, Mr Amos Kimunya, and others.
Mathira MP Nderitu Gachagua, one of Narc-Kenya's fervent supporters, predicted "a lot of activity" after the party formally opens its offices in Nairobi on Thursday. Afterwards, the party is planning a countrywide membership registration blitz.
Opposition sources, indeed, confirm that the new party seems to be extremely well-funded as well as apparently enjoying patronage from very high levels in government. Among those who have kept their distance is Ford-People leader Simeon Nyachae, though his party belongs to the so-called Government of National Unity (GNU).
One person who is known to be quite unhappy with Narc-Kenya is Mrs Charity Ngilu, the original Narc's chairperson. She is upset about the manner the new party has embraced her former allies such as Prof Kivutha Kibwana and Mr Mutua Katuku, who she believes are being used to try and displace her influence in Ukambani.
Banana camp
Mrs Ngilu is also still smarting from her referendum experiences; she had complained bitterly when the Yes bigshots opted to use Prof Kibwana, Mr Katuku and MPs Kalembe Ndile and Adelina Mwau to campaign for the Banana camp in Ukambani.
What was most interesting was that, prior to Mrs Ngilu being coaxed by State House to rejoin the Yes fold, the Kibwana-Katuku-Ndile axis seemed to be working quite closely with Narc "activist" Mary Wambui.
More and more Mrs Ngilu's remaining clout rests with her "ownership" of the Narc registration certificate and also her personal relationship with President Kibaki. Though the manner he has dealt with the likes of Dr Murungaru and Mr Murungi has strengthened a view that when it is expedient the President can sideline allies without a scruple, one politician who has been close to Mrs Ngilu for a long time believes that Mr Kibaki would find it difficult to treat her that way owing to the particular circumstances that brought them together politically.
It is not quite clear where President Kibaki's confidantes such as Ministers Michuki or Njenga Karume lie in all this. The two have never spoken out or be seen to be pushing the Narc-Kenya agenda (Mr Karume is still officially a member of Kanu).
"People like Michuki will go where the President goes," says an MP, who is now in the Narc-Kenya bandwagon. The problem is that nobody is even quite sure about President Kibaki's true stand. The closest the public got to it was when Vice-President Moody Awori hinted last month that the President "supported" the new party.

Original_Search_Title: Third Term: It's Resistance All the Way
Headline: Nigeria: Third Term: It's Resistance All the Way
Body_Text:
Kaduna/Abuja — THE battle front shifted momentarily from the Federal Capital Territory to the bustling city of Kaduna, Thursday in the fight against tenure elongation. The meeting of the Northern Governors Forum (NGF) provided the setting for the politicking and protestations that marked the parley.
The signs of what to come emerged before the governors went in for their meeting when thousands of placard-carrying youths besieged the General Usman Katsina House at Kawo, Kaduna State to protest against the third term agenda. Donning PDP caps, the youths had blocked the road leading to the venue and holding aloft their placards with inscriptions like "no third term," "no to elongation of tenure," "Ba mu so (we don't want it)."
While the meeting lasted, tempers rose at different stages with two governors at a time shouting at each other and getting close to fisticuffs until the Sokoto State governor, Alhaji Attahiru Bafarawa intervened.
In a communiqué issued at the end of the NGF meeting which lasted for more than four hours, the governors stated that "after an extensive deliberation on the on-going constitution amendment in the country, the Forum resolved that members should go ahead and consult with their respective constituencies."
But evidences of cracks in the Forum were on display when four of the governors, Engr. Abdullahi Kure (Niger), Boni Haruna (Adamawa), George Akume (Benue) and Alhaji Ahmad Sani of Zamfara addressed the media to dissociate themselves from the seeming support for the third term project. The quartet condemned "in strongest possible terms, the idea of the on-going constitutional amendment which primary objective is to achieve the third term agenda."
'We'll prove our strength when the time comes'
Alhaji Ahmad Sani, the Zamfara governor who had since declared that he would contest the 2007 presidential election, with or without the tenure elongation, reiterated this position to his fellow Northern governors during Thursday's deliberations. Beyond that, he vowed that at the appropriate time, they would prove to the proponents that they have what it takes to shoot down the project. "We (the four governors) are speaking for ourselves and others (Northern governors) not here. We're not the only ones who do not support the third term agenda. When the times comes, we'll prove our strength. We urge all democratic elements to rise against the plot."
Another of the protesting governors said: "It's all rubbish. We'll shoot this thing (extension) down. Of course, we know there are some of our colleagues who are for it. They may have their reasons. But at the right time, we'll know those who have the people at heart and whom our legislators would listen to."
The Zamfara governor spoke further: "We note with concern the growing brazen manner the constitutional amendment process is so far being conducted. From the so-called zonal public hearings to the shameless attempt in the Senate recently to smuggle an amendment to the original bill that would provide for tenure elongation or third term for the governors, (who were inadvertently omitted in the original bill even after it went through the first reading), it is evident that the whole idea of the entire constitutional amendment was not meant to bring about national stability, unity and sustenance of ourdemocratic rebirth. Rather, it was a road map to national instability, confusion and total distortion of our constitutional order.
"We equally condemn other dangerous aspects of the amendment which seek to bring about unitarism as against true federalism. It is our candid opinion that all provisions in the constitutional Amendment Bill that seek to erode the country's federal structure in terms of powers and responsibilities of the constituent units should not be considered".
The governors commended members of the National Assembly who in the face of persecution, intimidation, harassment, coercion and blackmail have remained resolute, courageous and committed to the course of fighting the third term agenda and its attendant consequences. We urge them to remain steadfast and focused until this monstrous third term project and other dangerous aspects of the amendment are democratically defeated."
No retreat, no surrender at National Assembly
As the crucial vote on the proposed constitutional amendments draws nearer, signs of desperation were all evident in the camps of the two major divides in the raging constitutional controversy that has now enveloped the nation. For the two contending forces, the National Assembly is the venue for the battle. The chips had since settled with the submission of the document by the Senator Ibrahim Mantu-led committee to the federal legislature.
Events have been happening in such a frenetic speed. Bills are altered, leading to widespread altercations. Letters are written to the Senate, read on the floor and subsequently deleted from the records. Nocturnal meetings are scheduled and cancelled, and new dates fixed with no certainty that they would hold. In both the Senate and the House of Representatives, yesterday's friends have become arch rivals and sworn foes because they do not share the same belief in the third term project.
Now, the desperation is more pronounced in the camp of the pro-third term lobby, the coalition of legislators, presidential aides and businessmen who want an elongation of the present tenure of President Olusegun Obasanjo. And even with the pro-third term lobby finding it difficult to break through the constitutional barrier of a two-third majority in the two Houses of the National Assembly, it is still 'no retreat, no surrender.'
At the heart of the latest constitutional crisis is the proposal to amend the constitution to allow the president and state governors to serve up to three terms of four years each. The proposal since it became public has drawn the fury of vested and otherwise remote political interests in the Nigerian federation. The proposal which emanated from the Senator Ibrahim Mantu-led Joint National Assembly Committee on the Review of the Constitution (JCRC) has proceeded apace, receiving the imprimatur of the committee over and above other proposals on the tenure of the president and governors. The stride of the Mantu committee to enact its proposal has, however, slowed down, apparently bogged by the dithering procrastination of the third term activists on their capacity to overwhelm the constitutional requirements needed to alter provisions of the 1999 Constitution.
The major stipulation in the constitution on altering the constitution as provided in Section 9 (2) states that: "An act of the National Assembly for the alteration of this Constitution, not being an Act to which Section 8 of this Constitution applies, shall not be passed in either House of the National Assembly unless the proposal is supported by the votes of not less than two-thirds majority of all the members of that House and approved by resolution of the Houses of Assembly of not less than two-thirds of all the States..."
Stiff war of numbers
For the pro and anti-third term lawmakers, it has been a stiff war of numbers. What the two sides have been strategically doing is updating their records to be sure of where they stand and when to strike or beat a retreat.
The pro-third term lobby, earlier this week, measured its strength and pulled away from allowing deliberations on the constitutional amendments because of the fear that defeat was imminent. Sources in the camp disclosed that by their assessment, there were just a little over sixty senators who would vote in favour of the amendment if it came to the floor of the National Assembly. They need a total of 72 votes to pull through. According to supporters of tenure extension, "we don't understand what the noise is all about. It's all in the spirit of democracy that while some of us (lawmakers) want it (third term), there are those who don't want it. What each side has to do is to lobby and see that it wins.
"So, it hasn't come to name-calling and such rowdy sessions. On the floors of the two Houses, the matter will be decided. Let them (opponents of tenure elongation) wait till that time and see who wins. It's as simple as that. People should show maturity by the way they conduct themselves, their preferred positions notwithstanding."
Another presidency source insists that "it's amazing that people are dancing themselves lame before the main thing. Why can't we all wait till the National Assembly takes its decision? Those running up and down to embassies and newspaper offices are the ones showing panic and desperation."
But the anti-third term campaigners fault the claim of 60 senators ready to cast their votes for power elongation. Though it expressed satisfaction on its success so far in frustrating the third term amendment, the camp was, however, not resting on its oars. In fact, it was reinforcing the pressure on the President to quit in 2007 with high profile campaigns to foreign diplomatic missions in Abuja and recent visits to media houses.
Only last Monday, the publicity committee of the 2007 Movement, which is the major vehicle of the anti-third term coalition made a circuit of the diplomatic missions of some permanent members of the United Nations Security Council in Abuja. The legislators comprising Senators Saidu Dansadau (ANPP, Zamfara), Ben Obi (PDP, Anambra), Olurunnimbe Mamora (AD, Lagos) and Alhaji Aminu Tambuwal, the ANPP leader in the House of Representatives briefed the diplomats of the national security implications of allowing tenure extension.
Why we visited foreign embassies
"We spoke to them about what may become the attitude of the proponents of third term should they (third term lobbyists) be defeated. We need the support of their home governments to advise Obasanjo to accept defeat,"' Senator Dansadau told newsmen at the end of the diplomatic shuttles which were rounded up with separate visits to Senate President, Ken Nnamani and House of Reps Speaker, Aminu Bello Masari.
"We told them of the irregularities that characterised the amendment of the constitution right from the public hearing to the time that the report was laid in both chambers. We told them that we're only opposed to sections 137 and 182 which intend to elongate the tenure of the President and the state governors."
The lawmakers also addressed the diplomats on the sustenance of the on-going reforms of the Obasanjo administration, a key point the pro-third term lobby has used to buttress its argument for tenure elongation.
"We told them that policies of government are built around institutions and not around a person. Democratic institutions are not built around a person as is the case now in Nigeria," he said.
Besides the diplomatic pressure, the anti-third term campaigners are also exploiting the loophole or what some others consider as the deliberate mistake in excluding governors from the third term deal in the bill submitted to the Senate. Whereas the bill laid by Mantu before the Senate penultimate Tuesday specifically made provision that the President could be entitled to three terms of four years each, the same bill was ambiguous in the provision for governors.
Today, pro-third term agitators say that the ambiguity arose from an error in the compilation of the report of the JCRC. However, others saw it as a deliberate mistake instigated by some senators with gubernatorial ambitions. By inserting the two-term exclusion clause for governors, the way would be open for several of them with gubernatorial ambitions to actualise their dreams without the encumbrance of encountering the sitting governors. Yet, others opine that the latest move to insert a third term for the governors which caused an uproar in the Senate, Tuesday, was a desperate move by the third- termers having realised that the state rulers would not try to influence some of their stooges at the National Assembly and would not play ball if the matter should go to the state assemblies.
Which way for governors?
The report of the exclusion of the governors was sufficient to stir the Governors Forum to a hurried meeting in Abuja last week where they also lobbied to re-insert the provision for three terms for themselves. However, few of them opposed to the third term agenda either walked out or avoided the meeting entirely.
Governors Abdulkadir Kure (Niger) and Orji Uzor Kalu (Abia) even though they were around in Abuja stayed away from the meeting while Adamawa's Boni Haruna walked out after seeing the frenzy of his colleagues to accommodate themselves in the third term agenda. The governors did have their way, not because they were loved by the President or those masterminding the third term agenda, but essentially for the political support they could give in navigating the amendment proposals through the two chambers of the National Assembly and the State Houses of Assembly.
But then, the attempt to reinsert the third term for governors and to circulate the altered copy of the new bill was enough to stir a near riot last Tuesday as the Senate resumed after the Easter holidays. The insertion of the third term for governors was seen by some members of the anti-third term coalition as an abuse of legislative procedures since it was done after the first reading of the amendment bill.
"It's not procedurally correct, I know that. But if they bring it to the floor again, we will prove to them that it is not procedurally correct and even if it were to be, the motives are wrong and unacceptable," Senator Uche Chukwumerije, one of the principal opponents of the third term amendment proposal told Saturday Vanguard in an interview.
Senator Timothy Adudu, himself a member of the technical committee of the JCRC which compiled the report agreed as much, saying that any alteration after the submission of the bill was wrong. In his assessment he, nevertheless, said such alteration could not be said to be unconstitutional. "If it was amended after the first reading was taken, then that's an alteration of the bill which is not supposed to be done. So, it was wrong, but again while it could be procedurally wrong, it may not be unconstitutional."
Mistake or not, the gaffe was sufficient to stir the Senate into a very rowdy session last Tuesday. Ahead of the session, the legislators had gathered in twos and threes within and outside the chamber, pondering over what they said was an alteration of the bill already progressing through the Senate chambers.
As Senator Nnamani and his entourage of principal officers entered the chambers, the protesting senators started chanting "thief, thief" in the direction of some elements. It took nearly twenty minutes before the Senate President was able to bring down the chaos in the chamber.
In his ruling on the issue, he said: "I observed a journal of the National Assembly in circulation and it seems to have created a confusion. I recommend that the journal be set aside and the old one should be used. I do not want to be seen as if we are being manipulated from outside or we are manipulating something.
"If we are distinguished, we should be seen as acting as one. If one is talking in anger, we cannot say something reasonable and the amendment, if any, can be done later." The decision was immediately hailed by Senators Uche Chukwumerije (PDP, Abia North), Saidu Dansadau and Yari Gandi, activists in the anti-third term coalition. Senator Chukwumerije said that the presentation of the altered version was an indication that proponents of the tenure elongation project were prepared to break every law to achieve their goal.
2007 Movement takes battle to Northern govs
The 2007 Movement, a coalition of federal lawmakers opposed to tenure elongation took its battle to the Northern Governors Forum, Thursday when the Senator Uche Chukwumerije-led body met with the governors. The Movement handed over the above statement to the Forum.
"Your Excellencies, we are members of 2007 Movement. This is a bi-partisan platform of federal legislators committed to the sustenance of the cannons of constitutional democracy, rule of law and federalism. For now, these cannons face a deadly threat from the plot to extend the tenure of the incumbent President. "Your Excellencies, we come to you today as partners in a historic struggle.
Your Excellencies and we, the legislators are partners because we all shall be victims of a calamity and culprits of history if thetension precipitated by the third term agenda deteriorates to an unmanageable scale and our dearcountry comes to grief. By the same token, we consider you partners in a historic struggle because you and we are major players who are in a position to pull our country back from this precipice."You know better than many of us that Nigeria is once again at the cross roads: Do we turn to the left or to the right? A turn to the left will lead us to political instability and economic retardation. A turn to the right will lead us to the consolidation of our democracy and pace of economic development. The 2007 Movement believes that the only option that we have now for the consolidation of our democracy and continued economic growth is retention of the two-term executive tenure in the Constitution and rejection of temptation to executive tenure elongation." Of the numerous reasons which support this position, we briefly state three here.
The first is that the overwhelming majority of Nigerians do not want any change in existing constitutional provision for a two-term executive tenure. You as grassroot leaders, you listen to the heartbeats of your people. You know in your hearts that our people do not want executive tenure extension. As true statesmen whose focus is on the next generation and not just next election, you will, we are sure, respect and articulate the wishes of the people."The second is the need to promote sense of participation and accelerate pace of rotation of top executive posts among the zones and peoples of Nigeria. Executive tenure extension will freeze this process, slow down the pace, bottle up alienations, and precipitate sooner or later a major political andsocial explosion. Why should we be retreating backwards while other African nations aredemocratizing and moving forward? "The third is the need to consolidate rule of law. If we allow the rule to be changed to anchor an incumbent Pr esident's achievements, we will have created a dangerous precedent which successor regimes will exploit.
This will mean that in no time Nigeria will slide into the abyss of a banana republic."Your Excellencies, we appeal to you to rise to the full potential of your greatness as leaders of thisnation and keep Nigeria on the track of untrammelled growth by rejecting the satanic temptation ofexecutive tenure extension.Remember, Your Excellencies, the wise counsel of President Obasanjo in a similar period of darkness in our nation not long ago. Permit me to quote his wise words:
He declared at that time thus: "I beg you in the name of Allah not to mistake the silence of our people for acquiescence or weakness and the sycophancy of the greedy and opportunistic people who parade the corridors of power as representatives of the true feelings of our people. Nigeria needs peace and stability. It is too fragile for another commotion."
"Your Excellencies, we reiterate our conviction that you are statesmen and grassroot leaders. We reiterate our conviction that Nigeria stands today on a precipice of disaster and Allah has placed you in a key position to save the nation. Nigeria's greatness lies in institution-building, not in hero worship and personalization of policies. The choice before us today is between a great rise to true greatness or a great relapse to stagnation and disintegration. Your choice, Your Excellencies, will be a decisive factor. History and Allah beckon to you to rise to true greatness.

Original_Search_Title: Law Panel Extends Its Sitting
Headline: Kenya: Law Panel Extends Its Sitting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans have one more day to say what went wrong with the constitutional review.
The committee of eminent persons, chaired by diplomat Bethuel Kiplagat, extended their sitting to Tuesday to allow more people and groups to present views. Monday will be a public holiday - Labour Day.
Yesterday Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua, attorney-general Amos Wako and Keiyo South MP Nicholas Biwott presented their views before the 15-member committee at Kenyatta International Conference Centre in Nairobi.
And key government officials yesterday extended a reconciliatory hand to the Orange Democratic Movement leaders in a departure from the stance by certain individuals that they could not recognise an unregistered organisation.
Announced extension
As Ms Karua and Mr Wako asked the ODM leaders to embrace the law review panel, its chairman announced the extension of public hearings to Tuesday, to give more people time to make their presentations.
The ODM leaders, Ms Karua and Mr Wako said, should not to waste the chance to appear before the committee and state how they wished the constitutional review to proceed.
They said the ODM leaders, who have in the past declared they did not recognise the panel, were free to criticise or offer suggestions on the way forward.
The ODM team includes mostly Kanu and Liberal Democratic Party MPs, who under the Orange symbol successfully campaigned for the rejection of the proposed constitution in last year's referendum, defeating the pro-government Banana group.
Chief ODM leaders - Raila Odinga, Najib Balala, Anyang' Nyong'o, Kalonzo Musyoka, Linah Chebii, Ochillo Ayacko and Willian ole Ntimama - were left out when the President named a new Cabinet in December.
Yesterday, Ms Karua and Mr Wako stated that they were at the KICC in their personal capacities, and were therefore not giving the Government position.
Ms Karua, who was the first to speak, said the review process lost direction when it was hijacked by politicians.
She asked the committee not to allow political interests to distract them, and instead focus on the long term interests of the country. "The referendum experience left us divided, polarised and wounded. We need to level the ground," she said.
The committee, she said, would advise on whether the review should be done before the 2007 General Election. She said the team had the option to recommend the formation of a constituent assembly, inviting of experts or the holding of a referendum.
Ms Karua described the committee as a presidential basket where Kenyans could drop their views.
On his part, Mr Wako, who started off by saying he had just escorted Ms Karua to the panellists, asked those who had not yet presented their views to embrace the committee. He said there were a lot of discrete consultations before the panel was named to avoid political bickering.
Derail process
Mr Biwott asked the committee not to entertain politicians who wanted to derail the process.
Once the review process picks momentum, he said, politicians would find a way of fitting in. "You should move on, and the politicians will find a place to fit in the unfolding dynamics," he said.
Former review commission secretary Patrick Lumumba asked President Kibaki to lead the review process from the front.
He said the President should consult widely with leading politicians who were opposed to the Wako Draft, then assemble a small, lean, mean and honest team drawn from Kenyans of goodwill to spearhead the process.
"If I were asked why we do not have a constitution now, I would say it's because of politics. We lost the momentum when we agreed to go to the 2002 General Election without a new constitution. Political contests for power were allowed to take centre stage, with Bomas being turned into a form of political match," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Jolly Pit Sets a Two-Way Trap
Headline: Namibia: Jolly Pit Sets a Two-Way Trap
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The presence on Namibian soil of a couple of Hollywood celebrities has created somewhat of a conundrum for both our government and the local media.
The couple is Hollywood ace, Brad Pitt, and his consort, Angelina Jolie. As, I believe, the entire world knows by now, they are holed up at Langstrand for a relaxing holiday with their children, only in the end it did not turn out to be so relaxing as they had hoped for.
Already celebrity scream magazines in South Africa spotted the couple several weeks ago as our local media announced their arrival. Jolie, in particular, is no stranger to Namibia as she was in Swakopmund a few years ago to shoot some or other movie about helping refugees. As far as I am aware, this is Pitt's first visit.
Jolie's first visit proved that we have a splendid natural film set that we can offer to directors in Hollywood, and that the logistics needed to run a film set, are available at a price which I assume must seem jokingly low to Hollywood entertainment companies.
As it turned out, Ms Jolie must have enjoyed her previous stay enough to the extent that she decided to make us the guests of her next vacation, and to bring her man along. That is where the problems started. Celebrities build their careers on the fact that millions of people with nothing better to do, want to know more about them. This general craving for tracking the lives of celebrities has spawned an entire industry in publishing and broadcasting, and from that arose another profession: the paparazzi.
Now, short of saying I hate the paparazzi, I'll only go as far as to say that I think they are a despicable crowd riding on the back of other professional (and ethical) journalists. In my view, they defile the work of serious reporters, but unfortunately, there is huge demand for the sort of trash they produce. So, there is no shortage in the number of paparazzi that are prepared to follow celebrities everywhere just so that they can steal a sneak picture to sell to some trashy magazine in Europe or the States, who in turn write for a trailer trash audience.
But, in our case, or rather the Pitt Jolie case, there is another partner in crime, and that is our own government. Not wanting the celebrities to have their holiday ruined by paparazzi, the Namibian police officially took it upon themselves to rid Langstrand of any individual who appears to be there without a good reason. And the Ministry of Home Affairs came down hard on two photographers who seemingly wandered around Langstrand not for the purpose of taking pictures of the odd penguin that turns up on the beach. The whole saga turned into a farce this week when our own cabinet issued an official statement saying that no work permits will be extended to photographers who want to visit Namibia for the sole purpose of spoiling Mr Pitt and Ms Jolie's holiday. And that is the point at which the government squarely put its foot in it.
Sure, the paparazzi is a pest, and yes, we all want the visitors to enjoy Namibia, but, they still are celebrities, and it is not against the law to go to Langstand with the hope of catching a glimpse of the couple and their children. And it is not the cabinet's job or place to protect them. I think the biggest farce lies in the fact that our cabinet had to busy itself with the mundane issue of discussing what to do, or not to do, to ensure the safe stay of two foreigners, other than what we normally to do ensure the safety of all visitors. By issuing that statement, cabinet is helping to reinforce the popular notion of banana republics in Africa, and a poopy government that jumps at the complaints of people with a little bit of influence. I mean, we and our government, have many more serious issues to apply our energy to, than to worry whether two celebrities like us. The only thing the government should have done was to ensure that the law is being enforced, not play nation al bodyguards to the couple, and definitely not shoving the paparazzi around. That they could leave to the couple's bodyguards.
I am not what you may call a movie buff. I have seen some memorable performances by Mr Pitt, notably Legends of the Fall and Seven Years in Tibet. Personally I think his portrayal of a nutcase in 13 Monkeys was his best. But this has nothing to do with his stay in Namibia and as long as he stays within the law, and the paparazzi stay within the law, how they upset his vacation should not merit the cabinet's time and energy.
As for Ms Jolie, I am really scratching my head to try and remember one film, any film, in which she has played.

Original_Search_Title: Four Govs Restate Opposition
Headline: Nigeria: Four Govs Restate Opposition
Body_Text:
Northern Governors are sharply divided over supporting or opposing the third term bid of President Obasanjo. The division came to the open yesterday during the Northern Governors Forum meeting held at the Hassan Usman Katsina House, Kawo, Kaduna.
While the chairman of the forum, Kwara state governor, Dr. Bukola Saraki, read the communiqué of the meeting, his Zamfara state count erpart, Alhaji Ahmed Sani Yar-ima read out another statement to waiting journalists in the company of three other governors.
In the communiqué read by Saraki, the governors said, "after an extensive deliberation on the ongoing constitutional amendment in the country, the forum resolved that members should go ahead to consult with their respective constituencies."
However, in the statement read by the Zamfara state gov-ernor in company of Abdulkadir Kure (Niger), Boni Haruna (Adamawa) and the Deputy Governor of Benue state, Chief Ogiri Ajene, who represented Gove-rnor George Akume, the three anti-third term governor totally rejected the constitutional amendments.
The statement titled: "our position on the ongoing 1999 constitution amendment," said "it is evident that the whole idea of the entire constitutional amendment was not meant to bring about national stability, unity and sustenance of our democratic rebirth.
"Rather, it was a road map to national instability, confusion and total distortion of our constitutional order.
"While we concede that the country's 1999 constitution is not a perfect documents, its defect must not be used as an excuse to tinker with it or accommodate a personal agenda.
"We equally condemn other dangerous aspects of the amendment which seeks to bring about unitarism as against federalism that is anchored on devolution of powers to the constituent units of the federation.
"We wish to use this opportunity to commend all well meaning Nigerians who share our concern on the ongoing consti-tutional amendment and have demonstrated in particular, their objection to the third term agenda.
"In a special way, we commend members of the National Assembly who in the face of persecution, intimidation, harassment, coercion and blackmail, have remained resolute, cour-ageous and committed to the course of fighting the third term agenda and its attendant consequences. We urged them to remain steadfast and focused until monstrous third term project and other dangerous aspects of the amendment are democratically defeated.
"We equally in a special way, commend the Nigerian press for their patriotic zeal in promoting and defending our nascent democracy and for exposing the antics of the anti-democratic forces in our country.
"We appeal to Nigerians to rise up to the occasion and resist the unpardonable attempt to foist on the people of this great country, the third term project which is not only selfish but the path to dictatorship, anarchy and total reversal of our democratic rebirth.
"We call on our colleagues to be on the side of history and the people in our resolve to bequeath to the people a sustainable democracy by seeing through this deception of third term."
At the meeting which Daily Trust gathered was stormy, a group of anti-third term Senators including Seidu Dansadau, Uche Chukwumerije, Idris Kuta, Tokunbo Afikuyomi and Ben Obi and some members of the House of Representatives were in attendance to sway the gove-rnors' against the third term agenda.
In a speech read by Senator Chukwumerije under the auspius of the 2007 Movement, a bi-partisan association of anti-third term legislators, they said the country is on the precipice and gave three reasons to support their position.
"The first is that the over-whelming majority of Nigerians do not want any change in existing constitutional provision for a two term executive tenure.
"You as grassroots leaders, you listened to the heartbeats of your people. You know in your hearts that our people do not want executive elongation of tenure. As true statesmen whose focus is on the next generation and not just the next election, you will, we are sure, respect and articulate the wishes of the people."
Secondly, the anti-third term lawmakers said the tenure elon-gation will freeze the process of rotation of top executive posts among the zones. It will also, according to them, "bottle up alienations and precipitate sooner or later, a major political and social explosion.
"Why should we be retreating while other African nations are democratising and moving forward,?" the lawmakers asked the northern governors.
The third reason, they said, is the need to consolidate the rule of law adding, "if we allow the rule to be changed to anchor an incumbent president's achievements, we will have created a dangerous precedent which successor regimes will exploit. This will mean that in no time, Nigeria will slide into the abyss of a banana republic."
"Nigeria's greatness," the lawmakers said, "lies in institution-building not in hero worship and personalisation of policies.
"The choice before you today is between a great rise to true greatness or a great relapse to stagnation and disintegration. History and Allah beckon to you to rise up to true greatness," Senator Chukwumerije said.
The Governors of Kaduna, Sokoto, Kwara, Kogi, Borno, Zamfara, Bauchi, Adamawa, Niger, Gombe and Kano were in attendance while those of Yobe, Taraba, Benue, Katsina and Nasarawa were presented by their deputies.
The Governors of Jigawa and Kebbi were represented by the secretary to the state government (SSG).
Meanwhile, the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), national chairman, Senator Ahmadu Ali, declared yesterday that there is no going back on the third term bid for President Olusegun Obasanjo.
Apparently irked by the growing resentment against the tenure elongation beyond 2007, Ali described the opposition from the National Assembly in Abuja as "evil and disgruntled."
Playing host to a delegation of the Plateau state chapter of the PDP in Abuja on Thursday, Ali described the opposition lawmakers as deviants who have derailed from the path laid out by the party.
He also described other Nigerians opposed to the plot as selfish, egocentric and power seeking.
Reacting to the ordeal of the Deputy Senate President and chairman of the National Assembly Joint Committee on Constitutional Review, Alhaji Ibrahim Mantu, who is being investigated by the Senate for alleged financial mismanagement and embezzlement, Ali said the party would do everything possible to ensure that Mantu survives the bid to remove him.
He insisted that the deputy senate president is being persecuted for his role in the ongoing constitutional review exercise. Ali said, "Mantu's problem has to do with the constitution review process. But Mantu is carrying out his assignment to the best of his ability and with the support of the PDP nationwide. It is unfortunate that some of our members are opposed to the party position on the matter.
"They have allowed them-selves to be led by the nose. We have majority in the National Assembly yet our members have constituted themselves into an opposition.
"But that is what happens when you have too many child-ren. Some of them will definitely deviate from the path laid out for them by their parents and behave out of tune.
"Mantu is doing his job properly. He is carrying out a delicate assignment and he is doing it very well so we shall support him. Whatever tribulations he is going through, he will come out unscathed. We are not worried because some of the allegations are spurious. If a former Deputy Inspector General of Police does not know his onions then we are in trouble.
"This is the first time that an elected government is trying to produce a constitution. The subterfuge that the opposition and the media have driven Nigeria into is to give the amendment a skewed perspective.
"The selfish, egocentric and power seeking individuals opposed to us decided to label the entire exercise third term agenda. All we can say to the evil people against constitutional amendment is Allah ya isa (God will punish them)."
Ali said those against the third term plot "were talking through their ass," adding that his party is canvassing an elongation of tenure for Obasanjo because the country needs somebody "who will be there long enough to understand the ropes of governance".
Meanwhile, there were demonstrations by thousands of youths at the venue of this meeting.
"The idea of three terms of four years is more appealing so that we can get a policy of irreversibility of policies right."
Earlier, chairman of the Plateau state chapter of the PDP and leader of the delegation, Chief Jethro Akun, urged the party to rally round Mantu and save him from incessant attacks.
He said, "our advice is that we should stand up as a majority party that has the mandate of Nigerians to protect any of our members that is given any assignment to do without allowing the opposition to personalise and attempt to runt the person down like in the case of Senator Mantu.

Original_Search_Title: Third Term Divides Northern Governors
Headline: Nigeria: Third Term Divides Northern Governors
Body_Text:
Kaduna — A REGULAR meeting of the 19 Northern states' governors became, yesterday, the latest victim of the controversial third term plot, when it could not take a common position on whether to support President Olusegun Obasanjo or not.
Hundreds of youths besieged the venue of the meeting, protesting against the third term plan.
Elsewhere, Vice President Atiku Abubakar, a fierce opponent of the third term bid, declared that the struggle for democracy and freedom "is a long and tortuous one" and "those committed to the struggle must be prepared for the long haul."
Although the Governors' Forum issued a communique after the four-hour meeting, saying after "an extensive deliberation" on the on-going constitution amendment in the country it "resolved that members should go ahead and consult with their respective constituencies," four of them disassociated themselves from the communique.
Governors Boni Haruna (Adamawa), George Akume (Benue), Ahmed Sani (Zamfara) and Abdulkadir Kure (Niger) asked Nigerians to resist any tenure elongation.
Their spokesman, Governor Sani of Zamfara State condemned "in strongest possible terms, the idea of the on-going constitution amendment which primary objective is to advocate the third term agenda."
He said: "We note with concern the growing brazen manner the constitution amendment process is so far being conducted. From the so-called zonal public hearings to the shameless attempt in the Senate recently to smuggle an amendment to the original bill that would provide for tenure elongation or third termfor the governors (who were inadvertently omitted in the original bill even after it went through the firstreading), it is evident that the whole idea of the entire constitution amendment was not meant to bringabout national stability, unity and sustenance of our democratic rebirth. Rather, it was a road map tonational instability, confusion and total distortion of our constitutional order.
"We equally condemn other dangerous aspects of the amendment which seek to bring about Unitarianism as against true federalism. It is our candid opinion that all provisions in the Constitution Amendment Bill that seek to erode the country's federal structure in terms of powers and responsibilities of the constituent units should not be considered."
The governors commended members of the National Assembly "who in the face of persecution, intimidation,harassment, coercion and blackmail have remained resolute, courageous and committed to the course offighting the third term agenda and its attendant consequences. We urge them to remain steadfast andfocused until this monstrous third term project and other dangerous aspects of the amendment aredemocratically defeated."
They then appealed to all Nigerians "to rise up to the occasion to resist the unpardonable attempt to foiston the people of this country the third term project which is not only selfish, but the path to dictatorship, anarchy and total reversal of our democratic rebirth." The governors said although they conceded that the 1999 constitution was not a perfect document, itsfallibility must not be used as an excuse for tinkering with it to accommodate a personal agenda.
Before the meeting got underway, the 2007 Movement, a group of National Assembly members opposed to the third term, had met with the governors with a view to getting their support to scuttle the agenda.
Anti-3rd term Senators woo govs
Senator Chukwumerije who led the team told the governors that the plot to extend the tenure of President Obasanjo would precipitate crisis in the country and urged the governors to join the struggle in order not to be "victims of a calamity and culprit of history if the tension precipitated by the third term agendadeteriorates to an unmanageable scale and our dear country comes to grief.
"Your Excellencies, you know better than many of us that Nigeria is once again at the cross roads: Do weturn to the left or to the right? A turn to the left will lead us to political instability and economic retardation. A turn to the right will lead us to the consolidation of our democracy and pace of economicdevelopment.
"Executive tenure extension will freeze this process, slow down the pace, bottle up alienation, andprecipitate sooner or later a major political and social explosion."
Chukwumerije who was accompanied by Senators Saidu Dansadau, Ben Obi, Nuhu Aliyu, Tokunbo Afikuyomi and Idris Kuta warned the governors that if they allowed the rule of law to be changed to anchor an incumbent president's achievement, "we will have created a dangerous precedent which successor regimes will exploit," thus causing the country to slide into the abyss of a banana republic.
He told newsmen later that the third term plot was evil and satanic and urged Nigerians to resist it.Thousands of youths wearing PDP caps had blocked the road to the venue of the meeting, carrying anti-third term placards which read: "No third term", "No to elongation of tenure", "Tenure elongation will breedtyranny and 3rd term, Bamuso, meaning we don't want.
I'll resist 3rd term--ATIKU
Meanwhile, Vice President Atiku Abubakar yesterday described as unacceptable and evil the on-going move to elongate the tenure of office of President Obasanjo.
Atiku who spoke in Abuja at the public presentation of a book entitled Judicial Integrity, Independence and Reforms in honour of the retiring Chief Justice of Nigeria, Justice Muhammad Lawal Uwais, also asked all Nigerians, lovers of democracy and the rule of law to join him in rising against the tenure extension plot.
He called on the nation's judiciary not to disappoint Nigerians in playing its role as the last hope of the common man even as he urged everyone not to sit on the fence on the matter.
He said "as everyone here knows, our country is, to say the least, going through very difficult political times. This is largely because of the inability of some of us to appreciate that every modern society, especially complex ones such as ours, has to be rule-driven if they are to survive and thrive.
"Our recent history has been one characterised by brutal military dictatorship, one-man rule and attempts to subvert the will of the people in order to perpetuate dictatorship. Our people collectively fought those attempts successfully although many paid very dearly for it.
"As our current political situation shows, the struggle for democracy and freedom is a long and tortuous one. Those committed to that struggle must prepare for the long haul. And they will continue to require a courageous, independent and impartial judiciary in that struggle
"The law remains the last hope of the common person in a civilised community. Where the law fails or those who wield power disregard the law, people tend to take laws into their hands and anarchy tends to reign. We must collectively struggle to prevent that fate from befalling our country. We are too important a people and too important a country to Africa and the world to let that happen. Posterity will never forgive us if we do.
"This is not a time for neutrality or sitting on the fence. You either believe that democracy and the rule of law are good and must be secured or you do not. I remember the immortal words of Frantz Fanon that every on-looker is either a coward or a traitor. The price for standing up for the truth and justice may be stiff. But we cannot really call ourselves leaders and civilised citizens if we do not have the courage to uphold the truth and struggle for what is right.
"If we allow fear to paralyse us, then, we are running away from democracy and freedom. That is not a good option," he said.
Turning to the Chief Justice of Nigeria who was being honoured, Atiku also said: "I am pleased and highly honoured to be here with you at this public presentation of a book in honour of Chief Justice Uwais.
"I am happy to be here because I like ideas which is what this book is about. I am also happy because we are giving honour to a worthy son of Nigeria, a distinguished jurist, a meticulous researcher and scholar, an efficient administrator, a good family man and above all, a decent human being.
"I am also here because the book in question deals with an issue that is dear to me and I believe to most Nigerians in this critical and troubling period in our history.
"In this a period during which our judiciary has been seriously challenged by the pervasive influence of partisan politics, it is the presence of the likes of Chief Justice Uwais that sustains the hope of Nigerians in the judiciary.
"Thanks to those jurists of integrity and honour, Nigerians still have the courage and optimism to struggle for justice, fairness, due process, respect for the rule of law and constitutionalism. In short, Nigerians are, as a result, able to sustain their struggle for democracy and justice.
"I have known Honourable Justice Uwais for many years ad among his many remarkable attributes are his humility, civility, sincerity and forthrightness. He has been an upright judge of impeccable character. He has demonstrated immense wisdom, great intellect, personal and professional integrity and a deep sense of responsibility. These attributes have enabled him to preside over the nation?s supreme court and act as the chief judicial officer of the country as if these are easy tasks."

Original_Search_Title: Job Adverts Linked to Creation of Districts
Headline: Kenya: Job Adverts Linked to Creation of Districts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government has advertised for district commissioners in what is seen as a first step towards the creation of 34 new districts as announced last year.
In a newspaper advertisement, the Government wants to recruit seven senior district commissioners (Grade I) to serve as deputy provincial commissioners.
Applicants are also being sought to fill several posts in the categories of Senior DC Grade II and DC Grade II .
According to the advert, the successful applicants will serve "large districts with complex development activities."
The DCs will perform general administration of the districts, co-ordinate development programmes, oversee security matters including conflict resolution, and supervise staff.
The Government has also advertised for 500 other candidates to fill vacant positions in several key ministries.
Although President Kibaki announced the creation of the 34 districts last October, nothing had hitherto been done to implement the directive.
In addition, leaders in some regions are still bickering over boundaries and the location of the new headquarters.
The President's announcement, that came just before Kenyans voted on the proposed new constitution in the referendum, was seen by critics as an attempt to woo the people to pass the document.
But the Banana camp, that was in favour of the draft, was defeated, dealing a blow to the Kibaki administration.
Internal Security Minister John Michuki, in whose docket the Provincial Administration falls, warned in January that the Government would rescind the decision to create new districts in areas where leaders failed to agree on boundaries and headquarters.
He gave the squabbling teams up to January 31 to reach an agreement to enable the Government implement the move.
But almost three months later, nothing has been done towards creating the new administrative zones.
The Government is also yet to seek parliamentary approval for 25 existing districts that were created by former President Moi.
Squabbles over boundaries have been reported in almost every part of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Let's Go Bananas With the Dutch
Headline: Uganda: Let's Go Bananas With the Dutch
Body_Text:
The Dutch will this Saturday mark the Queen's Day holiday which also coincides with the jubilee celebrations of the reign of the country's popular Queen, Beatrix.
The local Netherlands Embassy in honour of the holiday will hold a special Dutch soul with the beat-classic band, Lets Go Bananas.
Bava Studio's Pamela Adongo, a coordinator of the show said the four-member band group is jetting into the country tonight at 8p.m.
The quartet will perform at Sabrina's Pub on Friday after which it will stage another show at Club Obligatto on Sunday.
Let's Go Bananas will crown its performance on Monday at a place to be communicated later.
All shows will start at 8 p.m. at an entrance fee of Shs10,000.
Irene Namubiru and Jeckaki Band will curtain-raise for the band.
The group will provide variety with performances ranging from soul, jazz, reggae, rock and pop music.
Queen's Day is traditionally celebrated on the late queen Juliana's birthday, which falls on April 30. 67-year-old Queen Beatrix too was crowned on this day.
Back in Netherlands, spirited crowds will this week take to the streets to enjoy the festive mood, ahead of the Royal Holiday. This has now become an annual traditional. According to the Dutch News poll published last week, 92 percent of the Dutch are satisfied with the role Quuen Beatrix has played above party politics.

Original_Search_Title: Race to Replace Mirugi Kicks Off As Son Declares Interest
Headline: Kenya: Race to Replace Mirugi Kicks Off As Son Declares Interest
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Election fever has gripped Nakuru Town just a fortnight after the death of Internal Security assistant minister Mirugi Kariuki in the Marsabit plane crash.
And to add fuel to the political fire, Mirugi's eldest son William Kariuki has jumped into the arena and wants to fit into his late father's shoes.
In what appears to promise a titanic battle, 27-year-old Kariuki, a student in the United States, is expected to face it off with seasoned politicians and former MPs who have openly shown an interest in the seat.
Various political parties have been holding meetings behind closed doors to strategise on the expected by-election.
Although the Speaker of the National Assembly, Francis Ole Kaparo, is yet to declare the parliamentary seat vacant, the race for Mirugi's replacement began in earnest after his burial last weekend.
Political temperatures have been rising by the day in a town that will soon be a battleground between the Government and the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM).
The outcome of the by-election in Nakuru Town constituency will determine the political future for both President Mwai Kibaki and the ODM, which plans to test its popularity ahead of the next General Election.
Already the political grapevine has it that Kanu and the Liberal Democratic Party of Kenya (LDP), key parties in the movement, are holding discussions about fielding a single candidate to face Narc-Kenya in the by-election.
A Rift Valley Kanu MP who did not wish to be named, said they were discussing the option. He was optimistic that the plan would work.
During the November referendum in which the Government side (Banana) was handed a humiliating defeat by the ODM (Orange), Nakuru Town constituency gave Banana a narrow win with 24,118 votes against 21,018 votes for Orange.
Kanu has not been active in the area since former MP David Njuki Manyara, who was elected the party's local branch chairman, left for the US for further studies.
Indeed, their house is not in order following a recent coup staged by a group allied to Kerio South MP Nicholas Biwott.
The wrangles in the party have allowed LDP gain strength in the area going by the recent political activities like the referendum and the demonstration organised to condemn the raid on the Standard Group's offices by State agents.
Being a multi-ethnic constituency with more than 100,000 registered voters and with the General Election just around the corner, the race will not be an easy ride for either camp or the aspiring candidates.
It should be taken into account that voters in Nakuru have never been consistent with their choice of MPs.
The late Mirugi was a lawyer by profession, a democrat who fought for justice and a leader with a vision.
His predecessor, Manyara, was a sharp contrast to Mirugi. He then had a weak academic background and is a down-to-earth man who rose from being a truck driver to become a Member of Parliament and was always at ease in the company of the common mwanachi.
It is not clear whether Manyara, who went to America a year ago for further studies, will be available to join the race.
Another issue of concern is the low voter turnout that normally characterises by-elections in Kenya.
Nakuru town constituency has 105,000 registered voters but only 45 per cent turned up to vote in the last General Election that saw Mirugi enter Parliament for the first time.
In the referendum, only 45,000 voters cast their ballots.
The candidate from a party that manages to mobilise people to vote in the by-election will definitely carry the day.
A number of individuals including professionals in various fields have shown an interest in the seat.
Among those who have been holding meetings to strategise on the seat is Dr Omondi Ogada, a local physician with no known political background.
Philip Ndeta, the Director of Learning Development-Kenya has also shown an interest.
Bishop Mike Brawan of Metro Church-Kenya, who played a key role in the Orange campaigns in the November referendum, will also be in the race.
Former MPs Mark Mwithaga, Dr Lwali Oyondi and Amos Kabiru Kimemia are also likely to make a try.
Mayor Benson Mwangi, businessman Gibson Mahinda and lawyers Elizabeth Omwenyo are also said to be interested.
Other touted to be in the race are insurance executive Zachary Njeru, one time Nakuru mayor Isaac Kirubi and long serving Kanu politician Barrack Okul.
The campaign issues will revolve around who among those seeking to replace Mirugi will carry on with the development projects the former MP initiated in the constituency.
The business community under the Nakuru Business Association is another force that cannot be ignored.
With vested interests in the town's development, the association is likely field a candidate.
The association chairman, Peter Kinya, has made it clear that they too are stakeholders and members would like to have an MP who will give them an ear and help in improving infrastructure in the town.
Churches will also have a big role to play in the by-election. All along, the late Mirugi has been working closely with religious leaders. Religious leaders would want Mirugi's successor to follow in his footsteps. Youth in the constituency also have what they are looking for in the next MP.
They feel that the time has come for one of them to represent Nakuru Town in Parliament as that is the only way they can benefit from the funds allocated to constituencies by the Government through Members of Parliament.
They have made it clear that their support will definitely go to a youthful, mature, morally and socially upright individual, a true democrat and a person who commands respect in the society.
Supporters of the Government through the local branch of the National Alliance Rainbow Coalition (Narc), under the chairman Joshua Mureithi, who was a close alley to Mirugi, say they are waiting for the seat to be declared vacant.
Mureithi called a Press conference at the Nakuru Town constituency office where he said they were yet to identify a suitable candidate.
He called on the National Elections Board not to interfere with the forthcoming nominations to fill the seat.
The branch officials feel that any attempt to impose a candidate on the Nakuru Town constituents will cost the Government side the seat.
Mureithi said the local branch would not take directives from Nairobi and would conduct the nominations single-handedly.
"We urge Narc headquarters in Nairobi to liaise with the Nakuru branch office before making decisions concerning the filling of the seat," said Mureithi.
Interested candidates, he said, should desist from engaging in premature campaigns since writs declaring the seat vacant were yet to be issued. He claimed that some local politicians were rushing to Nairobi to get clearance.
"Some opportunists have already started rushing to Nairobi with a list of preferred candidates, let them keep off," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Hope the Next Independence Day Will Bring Real Joy, Freedom
Headline: Zimbabwe: Hope the Next Independence Day Will Bring Real Joy, Freedom
Body_Text:
IT was sad to wake up in a foreign country to realise that Tuesday was Zimbabwe's Independence Day, marked in commemoration of freedom from the rule of Ian Smith some 26 years ago.
My mind started to wonder about and threw me back to the much-awaited Independence Day in 1980, reflecting on how my parents and everyone else celebrated. I still recall it very vividly although I was just a little boy.
I was also happy that our prime minister would be a black man called Robert Mugabe and the president a black man too called Canaan Banana.
I also started to evaluate the progress that this Independence brought to my well-being and the freedom that we gained for ourselves as a black nation.
It is unfortunate to note that this Independence hasn't meant anything encouraging at all, only suffering, corruption, misery and hunger.
If the truth be told, I am not ashamed to say that the Smith regime, despite the inhuman treatment of the black people, had a very sound economy and corruption-free governance.
Bob Marley was invited for our Independence bash where he sang about the African liberation
in celebration of a new, free Zimbabwe.
He preached about tolerance and respect for each other and congratulatory messages poured in. Little did we know that this was the beginning of an end for our beloved country.
It's high time we reclaimed our Zimbabwe and start rebuilding with a new leader at the helm. Those who have failed us should refrain from dreaming they will rule forever, or die in power.
We have had enough and need change to be able to enjoy our motherland without fear of reprisals if we open up our mouths to express our feelings. Hopefully, the next Independence celebrations will bring joy and real freedom.

Original_Search_Title: Sh487m Spent Printing Copies of Proposed Law
Headline: Kenya: Sh487m Spent Printing Copies of Proposed Law
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government spent Sh487 million last year to print four million copies of the Draft Constitution.
Internal Security assistant minister Joseph Kingi disclosed that the Government Press, which is responsible for printing all official government documents, could not do the job as it had not installed multi-million shilling machines bought in 1996, for unspecified reasons.
Draft rejected
The draft was rejected at the November referendum after a gruelling three-month campaign which saw the Cabinet, leaders and the public split between the Yes and the No campaign teams with the Banana and Orange symbols respectively.
