President Kibaki led the Banana camp, while former ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang' Nyong'o, Linah Jebii Kilimo, Najib Balala, Ochillo Ayacko and William ole Ntimama teamed up with Kanu's Uhuru Kenyatta and William Ruto, among other leaders, to champion the Orange camp. The Orange team's No vote won at the referendum.
Following the humiliating defeat, the President sacked all the pro-Orange ministers.
Yesterday, Mr Kingi said the multi-million shilling machines would be installed "soon."
Earlier, the assistant minister told Mr Reuben Ndolo (Makadara, Narc) that the printing contracts were awarded to 11 companies as per the Kenya Gazette Supplement No 63 production list.
In a supplementary question, the Makadara MP asked why such a huge sum was used, while copies of the Bomas Draft were already available.
The assistant minister declined to answer, saying the question was irrelevant.
But Mr Charles Keter (Belgut, Kanu) asked why the printing contracts were given to private firms while the Government Press was created to do such jobs.
Mr Kingi said the department was unable to cope with the order for four million copies which were required at short notice.
Mr Mutinda Mutiso (Kilome, Narc) reminded the assistant minister that the Government had spent millions of shillings buying machines from abroad, and demanded to be told why they were not used in the historic national exercise.
Said the assistant minister: "The machines were purchased, but they are yet to be connected. We will do it soon."
Supported colleagues
Rising on a point of order, Mr Jimmy Angwenyi (Kitutu Chache, Ford People) also supported his colleagues and demanded to be told why the multi-million shilling printing machines bought in 1996 had not been installed.
Mr Kingi responded: "The machines were bought, but have not been installed. We will do that soon."

Original_Search_Title: Thousands Seek Solace At Yaounde Spiritual Hospital
Headline: Cameroon: Thousands Seek Solace At Yaounde Spiritual Hospital
Body_Text:
Over 6000 Christian faithfuls from various religious denominations thronged the Yaounde spiritual hospital at the Mbankolo neigbourhood at weekend to seek solutions to their problems.Prophet Père Soffo who died last year founded the spiritual hospital known in French as Bureau Lumière (Source of Light).
According to testimonies from Christians, from 1975 to 2005 Père Soffo healed the physically sick and spiritually bankrupt through prayers and messages of redemption.
It was in this spirit that the Bureau Lumière organised a special prayer session on April 15-16 2006, to mark the Easter Feast.
The celebrations were dubbed "Banana Blessings."
According to one faithful, Bertrand Erere of CRTV Centre, the Banana Blessings analogy has a deep spiritual meaning.
To him, it is a symbolic act that calls on Christians to throw away the bad and the ugly and go only for the good in their behaviours.
"When you want to eat a banana, you first peel it, throw away the peelings and eat only what is good.
In the same light, our Prophet wants us to throw away our sins and continue only with our virtuous Christian acts," Erere told The Post.
Faithfuls were seen with bananas that were later blessed as a symbolic way of urging them to do only the good. By 1: 30 pm on Saturday when the successor of Père Soffo, Marie Lumière, arrived at the Mbankolo Ceremonial Ground, thousands of Christians rumbled off in song and dance.
Dressed in immaculate white, the Christians sang praises to the Lord God as they struggled to outdo each other in choreographic display.
In her massage, Prophetess Marie Lumiere said the period of Lent marks an important period during which Christians are supposed to plunge themselves in self-denial and self-abnegation.
She said the Christians symbolically died with Christ because of their sins and are expected to live a new spiritual life when they rise from death with Christ on Easter Sunday.
Marie Lumiere said Bureau Lumiere is not a denomination but a spiritual hospital that not only heals the sick, but also helps people who want to live and die in the Lord. Bureau Lumière, she intimated, has the cure for spiritual emptiness and atheism.
The Prophetess said Christians could effectively heal themselves through prayers, by loving each other and by living according to the will of God.
She attributed the upsurge of the embezzlement of public funds in the country as acts of people who stay away from the Lord. She said if Jesus Christ gave in to corruption, materialism, greed and vanity, the whole world would have been wallowing in the abyss of doom.
Patients visit Bureau Lumière on Mondays, Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Thursdays and Saturdays. While Thursdays are reserved for women who are seeking for pregnancy, Saturdays are for men only.
The outfit has chapters in Douala, Gabon and Benin. Its motto is love, patience and forgiveness.

Original_Search_Title: Number of Voters in Next Elections at Stake
Headline: Mauritius: Number of Voters in Next Elections at Stake
Body_Text:
Port Louis — Due to the constant changes - "crossing the floor", allegations of bribery and personal feuds - that are taking place on the political scene these days, our democracy is being made a mockery.
This, of course, has been discouraging for voters, as politics smells bad in this country. Intelligent voters are wondering whether or not it's worth voting in future since they believe that their sacred civic duties as voters have been baffled.
Gone are the days when ideologies and self-respect used to be the real convictions of our MPs. Today, politics has taken a different dimension as long as status and financial means are concerned. Investitures are given to candidates according to the size of their purses and not because of their political convictions and ideology. For instance, let us discuss about the political situations between our main parties.
So far the MSM wants to play it solo in its campaign to get the maximum supporters. Whether or not its leader has in mind that the only way to achieve power is something that only time can tell! Besides, that party has the same electorate as the Labour party. The MSM is still a challenging force but could survive only if its leader gets rid of two or three of its active members that the electorate won't even touch with a barge pole, leave alone the general election.
Majority members of the MSM politburo are praying for a new "Bleu Blanc Rouge" alliance. They strongly believe such an alliance will materialise again. And this has led to complacency. If ever it comes to that, the MSM would find itself gradually fading away on the political scene. Knowing Navin Ramgoolam, he would kill it softly and many of its stalwarts won't be ticket-able as candidates.
In the eventuality that the MSM joins the Labour party, it is almost certain that it would have to renounce its major policies such as its opposition to the Gokhool reform. On the other hand, the immediate consequence of the MSM joining the Labour party would result in a vacuum in the majority community. It is a well-known fact that nature abhors vacuum; so it is a certainty that another political party will emerge to fill it up. It could be a new political party created by Ashock Jugnauth with other MSM dissidents.
Recruitment among the young
With this in mind, the MSM is advised to keep away from such an alliance. It needs to consolidate its strength on the field with a distinct identity. It should start making a massive recruitment among the young generation and even the older ones who may have more experience and wisdom. Otherwise, people would think of the MSM with nostalgia if it disappears from the political scene.
The MMM is more or less a practical political party that has perhaps contributed a lot in making other parties achieve power. What has gone wrong is entrenched in the mind of the majority community - that only a "vaish" is entitled to become prime minister. BÃ©renger also could never have become prime minister were it not for the deal between Sir Anerood Jugnauth and himself to pave the way for Pravind Jugnauth to become prime minister. Today the party has lost the true militancy of its heydays because the class distinction between workers is fading away. As it stands today, the MMM is only a marginal party. It is the five-cent party to make the rupee for other main parties.
The Social Alliance is in theory the strongest party but not in practice. Already there are signs of its impending explosion. A section of both Hindus and Muslims have taken their distance as the party strategy is not meeting their expectations socially, economically and structurally. Tension is gradually mounting among its members from one or two small parties, who have already contacted Ashock Jugnauth. The latter is organizing lots of meetings in his constituency in an effort to get as many supporters as possible. He wants to fortify the party he intends to create sooner or later.
What is happening to our main political parties today make us wonder if our country is gradually moving towards a banana republic where nepotism, dynasty, corruption, turn coats, and undesirable potential candidates are affecting our system.

Original_Search_Title: Country Gets Into Election Gear
Headline: Kenya Gets Into Election Gear
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The forthcoming by-elections will be tight popularity contests between the leading political blocks.
Already christened a mini-general election, the last time Kenya witnessed such was in 1966 when the late doyen of opposition politics in Kenya, Jaramogi Oginga Odinga, walked out of Kanu with more than a dozen MPs to form the Kenya People's Union (KPU).
In the subsequent electioneering, the country was engulfed in campaigns that could be mistaken for a general election.
At stake in this election will be five seats most of which were previously held by Kanu, headed by party chairman Uhuru Kenyatta, also Leader of Official Opposition in parliament.
Kibaki will want to win all of them as would Raila Odinga of the Liberal Democratic Party or the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM).
The politics of ODM gravitating around Raila, Uhuru, William Ruto and Kalonzo Musyoka will play a key role in the coming by-elections.
But within ODM, there are also camps. Uhuru is getting less enthusiastic about the Movement and is said to be comfortable with the party going it alone in the next polls.
That puts him at cross-purposes with Ruto, who favours the idea of Kanu working with LDP in future elections.
Kanu feels it needs to retain the vacant seats. An official has already declared that the party would do all it can to retain the seats.
"We are bracing ourselves for by-elections as a party. These are seats we will do anything to retain," the Kanu official said.
"That part of Eastern Province is a very strong Kanu area cannot be ignored. We are unwilling to cede any seat. We are determined to make sure we retain that area," the official added.
It would be interesting to see if Kibaki will want the seats through Narc-Kenya, his new party according to reports earlier attributed to Vice President Moody Awori, or through Narc, the governing coalition that is now in shambles.
It will also be a test case for the much-touted unity within the ODM: Will they rally behind one party, say Kanu, or will they go it separately, with LDP wanting to increase its representation in parliament where it has only one MP, Paddy Ahenda of Kasipul Kabondo.
Ford-People, now a partner in Kibaki's Government of National Unity (GNU), did fairly well in the last election in Northern Kenya. Will the party want to solidify that performance or will it go for Kibaki's chosen candidates?
Ford-Kenya, both a coalition partner and a GNU member has been "re-energising itself in readiness for 2007".
The party too is likely to weigh its popularity in the region by fronting its own candidates.
For Kibaki's government, it will be a test case on his popularity in the affected regions. Unlike in the referendum campaign which was a test on his regime - rather than on the then proposed constitution - the by-elections will be indicative of whether he could win the hearts of the voters in the five constituencies.
In the referendum, the Banana camp only won in Nakuru and Laisamis - both of them narrowly - and it will be interesting to see whether the voting patterns will change. As in the referendum the main issues will vary but will be informed by other peripheral issues that range from clanism to security.
Moyale Constituency: In the last election Kanu's Guracha Galgalo received 10,507 votes against Ford-People's Mohamud Ali's 5,063 votes. Narc's Malicha Galgalo got only 677.
How the voting patterns change this time will be determined by the clan selection of candidates and how they will position themselves in different parties.
Here matters of insecurity and pasture and the Boran interests will be critical for any party to win. The man to watch at the moment is the former Starehe Boys' Centre alumnus Ali, an influential figure in the Darara Welfare Association.
One of the other issues that will determine politics here is the relationship between the locals and the politics in southern Ethiopia which - although odd - influences local politics more than politics in far away Nairobi.
Kanu has held this seat since 1969 when the constituency was created.
The recent referendum results gave the combined force of Orange Democratic Movement some 5,535 votes against Banana's 4,981 votes.
Saku Constituency: The battle for Saku is actually the battle for Marsabit Town. The death of Abdi Taru Sasura opens the battle again as Narc tries to get a seat it almost clinched in 2002 after Jilo Falana polled 4,470 against Sasura's 5,718. Like in nearby Moyale the battle lines are drawn, not on party politics but on individual candidates. Sasura, a former chief of Dirib Gombo location earned his support after he acted as the voice of reason between the warring communities at a time when the Borans wanted to drive out the Burji out of Marsabit.
Also, Sasura played politics by aligning himself closer to the late Bonaya Godana which earned him the Gabra votes in the constituency but they later fell out after Sasura claimed that Godana was meddling in the local politics. Saku is also inhabited by the Rendille and Burji communities and these will offer the swing votes if they do not have their own candidates.
In the past Saku's elections have been violent thanks to the clan rivalry that informs the local duel. Here the Borana resent the fact that the Gabra and other communities have settled and invested there.
Saku was created in 1988 when the Marsabit North constituency was divided into two constituencies, the Saku and the North Horr.
In the referendum vote Saku was won by the ODM with 4,583 votes against Banana's 3,418.
North Horr: For the first time North Horr will have a different MP for they have only had Dr Bonaya Godana since it was curved from the Marsabit North in 1988. Previously the nomadic Gabra had no interest in voting and it was only after the creation of North Horr that they started registering as voters. For any party to get a say in North Horr they must search for a powerful Gabra candidate who would fit in Godana's shoes. The Gabra are the majority here.
A possible candidate to watch is Elema Fila who also contested in the Kanu nominations of 1997. In the Orange-Banana campaigns the Banana camp was eclipsed and polled a paltry 247 votes against Orange's 7,023.
Laisamis: Kanu has dominated politics in Laisamis such that in 1997 its candidate, Robert Kochalle, went in unopposed having won the seat in 1992 with 6,273 against DP's Sokotey Seye who garnered 734.
The area suffers from lack of water, grazing fields and infrastructure. Like other constituencies in Marsabit District, the fight for the parliamentary seat is normally based on clanism and personalities.
In the last election Titus Ngoyoni won on a Kanu ticket and his recent appointment as an assistant minister means that Narc might be interested in the seat and would go for it on the promise that any victor might have a place in the government.
Whether Narc will field Abdi Ngurusi, a member of Public Complaints Committee of National Environmental Management Authority will be known soon. There might be ex-Ford People candidate P. A. Mbirikany.
Laisamis is the only other constituency where Banana won in the referendum after the 'Yes' got 3,681 against 'No's' 1,783.
Nakuru Town: At the heart of Rift Valley politics the battle for Nakuru Town will be a mini-election by itself. Basically a cosmopolitan town this will be where the real test will be for the Narc government as it tries to win back the seat. Any loss of the Nakuru seat will be a pointer that Kibaki's popularity is on the wane having wrestled the seat from Ford Asili in the 1997 General Election when its candidate David Manyara won with 22,000 votes ahead of Mrs Alicen Chelaite of Kanu with nearly 13,000 votes.
Dominated by Kikuyus, Kalenjins, Kambas, Luos, Luhyas and Kisiis the voting pattern is mainly a reflection of how various tribes align themselves in the party line up. With more than 100,000 voters Nakuru town is of major interest and was at the heart of the Orange and Banana campaigns where the Banana won the day with 25,704 against ODM's 23,157. With such a margin it will be the place where eyes will turn.

Original_Search_Title: Classic Cup Tie Warning Signals for Santos
Headline: South Africa: Classic Cup Tie Warning Signals for Santos
Body_Text:
It's a cup competition that has always been kind to Cape Town football clubs. This weekend is no different when Santos and Hanover Park fly the flag for the Mother City in the quarter-finals of the lucrative Absa Cup.
On Saturday, Premier League side Santos head for Maritzburg for what looks the proverbial banana skin against an unpredictable Maritzburg Classic team, while Second Division Hanover Park face off against the mighty Orlando Pirates at Green Point on Sunday evening, in what is certainly the biggest and most important match in the history of the unfashionable Cape Flats side.
Santos and the other Cape Premier League club, Ajax Cape Town, have a great record in the Absa Cup, a competition based on the popular English FA Cup where the minnows have an opportunity to battle it out with top-flight opponents.
And Cape Town's love-affair with the event culminated in a thrilling Absa Cup final between Santos and Ajax at Athlone in 2003 - won 2-0 by Santos.
This time round, heading into the weekend's quarter-final matches, Santos are in the throes of a magnificent run of form - home and away.
But they'll have to be wary of a Maritzburg side who have shown that on one week they can beat Kaizer Chiefs and then the next week slump to a 4-1 loss to Bloemfontein Celtic.
Also Classic were responsible for dumping Santos' neighbours, Ajax Cape Town, out of the Absa Cup.
So, despite Roger de Sa's men having found the form they were struggling for in the early part of the season, Maritzburg will certainly prove no walkover. And if Santos want to seal a spot in the semi-finals, they'll have to be at their best - and, most importantly, guard against complacency.
Hanover Park, on the other hand, as club boss Riedewaan Anthony says, "have nothing to lose and everything to play for".
"We are ready to give our all. Just having got so far, already makes us winners," said Anthony.
Absa Cup quarter-finals
Saturday
Maritzburg, 3.15pm: Maritzburg Classic v Santos
FNB: Kaizer Chiefs v Black Leopards
Sunday
Bloemfontein: Bloem Young Tigers v Sundowns
Green Point, 8pm: Hanover Park v Orlando Pirates

Original_Search_Title: Hit Sitcom 'Allo 'Allo Comes to Life in Zim
Headline: Zimbabwe: Hit Sitcom 'Allo 'Allo Comes to Life in Zim
Body_Text:
Harare — If you enjoyed the hugely popular sitcom 'Allo 'Allo! you'll want to visit 'Alo 'Alo', the latest version of CafÃ© Rene, situated next to the Alliance Francaise in Harare.
This is where Adrian and Lesley Orford, re-incarnations of Rene and Edith Artois, ably assisted by Fifi Lafanne (Angie in real life), recently opened their doors to an eager public.
The Alliance Francaise has always been a lively meeting place for lovers of the arts. Besides providing a venue for literary readings, drama, song, dance and the visual arts, the Alliance also offers language classes and facilitates cultural exchanges. In addition there's a great library with the latest fiction and magazines, and an up-to-date selection of dvds. I've never been able to pick up a copy of Paris Match without developing a thirst for a cappuccino coffee, so the opening of 'Alo Alo' is more then welcome.
On a perfect, sunny autumn afternoon last week, I managed to prise George away from his desk, with the offer of lunch at 'Alo Alo'. The cafÃ© was packed to the gunnels with an enthusiastic clientele, intent on making an early start to the weekend. Luckily, some friends were just leaving, and offered us their table.
While the previous dÃ©cor, using wooden tables and chairs, may have been more suited to a cafÃ© operating at the time of the French resistance, the current wrought iron furniture, with bright tiled table tops, is comfortably stylish.
Neither leggy waitresses nor beautiful spies were to be seen, but we ordered a bottle of iced mineral water ($100,000.00) from a waiter, smartly dressed in black trousers and a crisp white shirt, and contemplated the menu.
At this point, friends Emmanuel and Michael, bearing a bottle of Ruby Goudini Merlot, arrived and joined our table. What had started out as a quick lunch break developed into a delightful leisurely repast, owing much to ambience and bonhomie, rather than to outstanding cuisine.
I chose a well-made ham omelette from the snack menu, priced at $350,000.00. Garnished with fancy lettuce and a cherry tomato, this tasted great with a slice of home made olive bread and a glass of Goudini. George's Pork Chop Cordon Bleu, topped with ham and cheese, ($750,000.00), was served with patty pan squash and tomato concasse. Emmanuel said that his cheeseburger ($450,000.00) was good, while Michael's Thai Chicken ($650,000.00) looked more like well-stuffed pitta bread than something to write home about. We all ordered extra french-fries, hot and crispy, at $150,000.00 a plate.
The menu is retro English rather than French, offering Chicken Maryland with sweetcorn fritters and fried banana ($750.000.00), a popular feature of 1950s dinner parties given by my aged parents, then in their hey-day, and Lamb Cutlets with mint sauce ($850.000). Starters include Chicken Livers wrapped in bacon ($450,000.00), and Crumbed Mushrooms. There are also pasta and vegetarian dishes, similarly priced.
Perhaps the chef will try his hand at Choucroute Garnie, or a tempting Coq au Vin. A quick glance at the homemade gateaux revealed carrot cake and muffins, though no doubt croissants were also available. We never got around to checking out the dessert trolley, but will save that for another visit.
Many of the dishes borne aloft to other tables looked attractive and tasty, and there was a general atmosphere of satisfaction and enjoyment among the patrons. There is currently no liquor licence at 'Alo Alo", so "leesen vairy carefully, for I shall zay zees only once" you must bring your own plonk.
It was well after half past three when we settled our bill with cashier Fifi Lafanne. After a brief stop at the library to pick up a magazine and dvd, we went home to commence the weekend in earnest.

Original_Search_Title: MPs: Enemies in Life, Comrades in Death
Headline: Kenya: MPs: Enemies in Life, Comrades in Death
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In life, they were at loggerheads over insecurity in their
constituencies. On Monday, while on a peace mission to achieve that
elusive peace, they died together.
Since December 2004, MPs Titus Ngoyoni (Laisamis), Abdi Sasura
(Saku) and Dr Guracha Galgalo (Moyale) had not been on talking terms
with their North Horr counterpart, Dr Bonaya Godana. In fact, the
picture that they depicted was one of hatred- it was not possible to
bring them together even when the issue at hand was beneficial to all
of them.
The clashes pitting the Borana against Gabra and Rendille eventually
led to the Turbi massacre of last July 14, where an estimated 70 people
were killed. In the blame game, Sasura, Ngoyoni and Guracha were in the
same camp, leaving Godana a lone ranger.
The trio blamed their North Horr counterpart for their woes and
general insecurity in Marsabit and the neighbouring Moyale. But Godana
squarely blamed his colleagues, saying they were behind the clashes in
the region.
During one of the trips to Marsabit, the rivals found each other on
the same plane along with Assistant minister Mirugi Kariuki. The trio
did not speak to Godana throughout the round trip.
Apart from taking some people to court over the Turbi massacre, the
Government had done nothing to bring law and order to that region. The
Government's failure to contain the deteriorating security situation
perhaps made the MPs to resort to other tactics.
Sasura, Guracha and Ngoyoni moved to 'isolate' Godana when they
trooped into the Government-backed Banana camp during the November 21
referendum on the proposed constitution. Out of the six MPs from the
area, only Godana and Isiolo South's Abdul Bahari remained in the
Orange team.
In the upper part of Eastern Province, the major issues in the
proposed constitution were the Kadhi's courts and land. The Laisamis,
Saku and Moyale MPs were all staunch Kanu members and featured in the
Opposition party's Shadow Cabinet, but they joined the Government side.
After the referendum, Ngoyoni was appointed an Assistant minister
for Regional Development while Sasura became the deputy Chief Whip.
Ngoyoni had even threatened to resign from the Government if Godana and
senior police officers, led by police boss Amos Cheboi, were not
investigated.
He repeated the threat a fortnight ago when he wrote to both the
Police Commissioner Hussein Ali and National Security minister John
Michuki over the clashes in Marsabit.
In his letter, Ngoyoni accused the police of working in cahoots with his North Horr counterpart to frustrate his constituents.

Original_Search_Title: Arts Academy Goes South
Headline: Namibia: Arts Academy Goes South
Body_Text:
THE College of the Arts will present its annual Arts Academy in Keetmanshoop from Monday to Wednesday at the Multi Purpose Youth Centre.
Courses offered include Keyboard, Brass instruments, e.g. Trumpet, Trombone and Tuba, Guitar, Bass Guitar, Percussion, Choral Singing, Saxophone and Visual Art.
The College of the Arts has recognised that most churches and schools in Namibia desperately need people who can read music, play an instrument, lead services with music or train and lead choirs.
In an effort to provide tuition programmes for choir leaders and instrumentalists, we have developed and successfully presented an Arts Academy every year since 1994. It is an ongoing effort to promote and advance arts education at the regional level.
The programme has since become an important highlight on the college calendar.
Previous course-goers have attended from all over Namibia and have come from diverse cultures and religious backgrounds. Others have simply attended for their love of music as well as achieving personal goals.
The three-day course will be presented by 12 lecturers of the College of the Arts. Registration is at 09h00 on Monday. The official opening by mayor Thibolt of Keetmanshoop as well as Mr Sebastien Lanoye takes place on Tuesday at 09h00.
Course-goers receive a certificate of attendance. The cost is only N$20. Course participants are requested to make their own arrangements concerning accommodation. Participants studying a music instrument are requested to bring the instrument if they have one.
For further information please contact Banana Shekupe at tel. 081 2209817 or Hermien Vries at (061) 374102.
Through this academy the College of the Arts hopes to successfully promote the different art forms, to develop and upgrade skills in the arts, to provide the necessary materials for art study, as well as to identify new talents that can further be trained at the College of the Arts through its regular training programmes.
We aspire to train leaders who will in turn pass this knowledge on to the community and to train teachers who will in turn train their students.

Original_Search_Title: Those Who Have Ears...
Headline: Nigeria: Those Who Have Ears...
Body_Text:
Lagos — The observed trends of happenings in the country lately shows that our leaders are seriously plunging the dreams of many youths and well-intentioned Nigerians into an irredeemable abyss.
What really is at stake here is the imminent collapse of a democracy which all Nigerians have craved for; fought for and for which some died for. The same government through their different machineries brought many to book because they went against the law. Nonetheless what is presently observed is that: " the Baboon that accused the Monkey of stealing banana in public; made sure the monkey was duly punished and took the banana from him. Now the Baboon is quietly holding meetings on how to feast on the same bananas"
What system of government are we gradually being dragged into? Is it Autocracy? Or Oligarchy? Or Babatocracy! Nigerians are too intelligent to be pushed around as if they are babies. We heard Mr. President say recently in America that he is not a Manoeuvrer; not a manipulator! Well, good trees do not produce bad fruits and vice versa. Can we go to the records? Can we know what has happened so far to all those who have made their intentions known with regards to not being in support of the third term and what his Excellency and his men have done to them? What has been the role of the EFCC with regards to the third term up till now?
History, amazingly is a great treasure for those who are wise and are able to learn from it? Wisdom they often say comes with age. Interestingly enough not many take the wealth of advice contained therein. History will soon laugh and laugh and laugh because even though he was consulted her advice was thrown into the trash can. Who are the advisers of the number one man of this great nation in whose hands lie the future of many people? Let them advise him well. We do not and cannot afford to go back again 30 years in our history. Those who have ears to hear let them hear. The developed nations would not listen to us when the time comes. Infact, they would laugh and laugh and laugh because we had been foolish!

Original_Search_Title: One Year of Nnamani: Has the Senate Rediscovered Its Integrity?
Headline: Nigeria: One Year of Nnamani: Has the Senate Rediscovered Its Integrity?
Body_Text:
Abuja — With a stocky framethat cuts the image of a rugged American footballer, Senate President Ken Nnamani could give the impression of just a man of brawn. His ornamental adornments that tend to mollify the visage of a hard man, and deep Igbo accent, undiluted by years of American life could give the impression of a successful Onitsha merchant!
But one year after his elevation to the nation's number three citizenship, Senator Nnamani has set a management record that rubbishes the accomplish-ments of even the most philosophically gifted presiding officers of the Fourth Republic Senate.
Elected just a year ago on April 5, 2005, it could be shocking to chroniclers of the Nigeria Senate that Nnamani is yet to grapple with an open contention for his seat. Is the Senate losing its tradition for spreading banana peels in the path of its presiding officers?
Evan(s) Enwerem the first President of the Fourth Republic Senate did not spend as much as one year in office before he was removed. His successor, the intellectually inclined and grandiloquent entertainer, Chuba Okadigbo, even with a doctorate in political science was only weeks on the seat before the impeachment drums started beating.
Anyim who succeeded Okadigbo did not fare better staying only weeks before a combination of the bruised loyalists of Okadigbo and presidency supporters in the Senate would move against him. Although it remains to Anyim's eternal credit that his massive frame which presents him like a Sumo wrestler was huge enough to wad off his opponents.
Anyim managed to survive, but always treading softly on the banana peels all around him, fighting off one threat after another. Senator Adolphus Wabara who emerged as the President of the Senate at the beginning of the present National Assembly did not enjoy a more stable tenure. Allegations of disproportionate financial allocations that began to haunt Wabara in the first months of his tenure followed him all the way inspiring Wabara must go chants from time to time until he was finally shoved away last year.
Senator Nnamani who succeeded him came on the wave of popular agitation by Senators to have their own man in the saddle. Tellingly, Senator Nnamani's emergence as President of the Senate was itself significant. It was the second time in the history of the Fourth Republic Senate after the emergence of Okadigbo that the presidency would not have its way in installing its man as the President of the Senate.
Remarkably, the presidency had nothing less than a month to prod a successor to replace the doomed Wabara after the revelation of the N54 million bribe for budget scandal.
It was either that there was no fighting spirit left among presidency officers or that the field of available candidates to pick from was not agreeable enough.
Nnamani, who as an ordinary senator had shown an independent streak, was considered by several to be out of the cards for the villa.
Senator Ike Ekweremadu, the only other acceptable choice from Enugu State, (the only State in the Southeast that was as at then yet to benefit from the rotating Senate Presidency in the zone), was not regarded by his colleagues to be sufficiently independent of the villa for the office.
It was suggested by some elements in the Senate then that Ekweremadu was the choice of the presidency and the PDP.
The suggestion that Ekweremadu was the choice of the presidency was itself to cost Ekweremadu support among senators who were determined to enthrone an independent minded presiding officer following the subjugation of the Senate to the whims of the villa under Wabara.
So it was not surprising that PDP Senators in the mock election conducted on the eve of the Senate election chose Nnamani over Ekweremadu as the next presiding officer.
The zonal spread of the votes at the mock election was to later show that nearly all Senators from the Southwest voted in support of Ekweremadu and against Nnamani, the eventual winner.
That action was to later manifest in the only little trouble to have hounded Senator Nnamani when the Southwest Senators let it out that their block votes for Ekweremadu was upon the directive of the presidency and the PDP.
With the background of the alleged indignities perpetuated by his predecessor, it was instructive that Nnamani's immediate goal on assumption of office was to restore the dignity of the Senate, which ordinarily should be regarded as the nation' most exclusive club.
He said as much in a May 15, 2005 commendation letter to Mr. Dele Olojede, the only Nigerian winner of the American Pulitzer. Senator Nnamani was quoted in the letter as promising "to wipe clean the slate of sludge that has blurred the vision of our democracy and tarnished the hallowed chambers, which ought to have been used as a platform for enacting laws that should help alleviate the socio-economic transformation of our country and the lives of its people."
In word and action, Senator Nnamani has gone about his pledge restoring some measure of dignity to the reputation of the Senate battered by combined allegations of listlessness and financial scandals under Wabara.
Senator Nnamani in his administration of the Senate has to some reasonable degree showed an independent streak aligning the Senate on a course in between legislative assertiveness and collaboration.
One area of such independence is the Senate's screening of ministerial nominees.
Before now, allegations of bribery by nominees combined with patronising questions by Senators to undermine the whole ministerial screening process. But under the Nnamani leadership, nominees have been rigorously screened and a couple falling short of expectation have even been refused confirmation. One of them, a former ambassador screened by the previous Senate showed a gross ineptitude underlined by his inability to enunciate the simple meaning of NEEDS, the government's economic blueprint.
Nnamani's independent course has been well reflected in the ongoing quest to enact a third term for the President and governors. Despite the unpretentious alignment of his immediate subordinates in the leadership to the third term campaign lobby, Nnamani has commendably detached himself from the soiled campaign.
Where he has intervened, Nnamani has had to assert his determination to lead the Senate to do what is right even at seemingly rebuking the third term campaigners.
Welcoming Senators back at the beginning of the year after the Christmas and Sallah holidays, Nnamani said:
"Our economic prosperity and social stability require policy continuity, not necessarily regime continuity. Indeed, regime change and periodic renewals of the peoples' mandate are critical ingredients of participatory democracy," the Senate President said.
Urging his colleagues not to shy away from taking a position on sensitive national issues, he promised Nigerians that the Senate will work towards keeping faith and protecting their democratic rights.
He said: "The National Assembly will continue to enact laws that make it possible for some of these good policies to continue after the end of the tenure of this government.
"During the break the agitation about the alleged Third Term Agenda became feverish. Expectedly, the pendulum of suspicion swung towards the direction of the National Assembly. We appreciate the right of Nigerians to contribute to the debate about the form governance should take before and after 2007.
"We assure Nigerian citizens that we will keep the faith and protect democratic rights. I appeal to our colleagues to focus on the challenges of ensuring that we bequeath to Nigerians after 2007, institutions and culture of politics that can guarantee continuity of such reform programmes that can transform the economic and social conditions of ordinary Nigerian citizens.
"We are settled in our commitment to uphold the constitution. We remain unshaken in our devotion to the common good and will continue to press for the deepening of democratic politics and the respect for the rule of law by all public officers."
His liver has, however, failed him sometimes like early last month when he crudely detached himself from a Senate sponsored workshop organised by Senator Uche Chukwumerije which he supposedly feared was about to be hijacked by opponents of the Obasanjo administration.
One hiccup that confronted the Nnamani administration was the face off he had with the Southwest delegation in the early weeks of his charge after the reconstitution of Senate Committees which saw Southwest Senators losing their overall number of Committee chairs. He passed over the attack against him with brusque determination on the wings of the support of the North-central Senators who gained Committee chairs at the expense of the Southwest.
Senator Nnamani has also had to contend now and again with distractions from his native Enugu State with altercations with officials of the state government.
His ascendancy as Senate President has confounded the power equation in the State which until then saw Governor Chimaroke Nnamani (no relation of the Senate President) as the ultimate political godfather in the State.
Since he would need the apparatus of the federal might to engage Governor Nnamani it is understandable that Senator Nnamani would have to moderate his politics in order to be so properly equipped. There is some suggestion that some of his seeming utterances of collaboration may be influenced by the exigencies of local politics in Enugu State.
Summarising the Nnamani administration, Senator Joy Emodi said:
"He has done very well and brought remarkable changes into the affairs of the Senate and that is why we have justice. He is a very democratic leader who doesn't impose his views on colleagues and that has earned him a high degree of respect from fellow Senators. He has equally brought finesse into the administration of the Senate and you can imagine we even have tea corners around the Senate chambers."
Posterity will yet pass its own judgment!

Original_Search_Title: Ngilu in New Setback Over Narc-Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Ngilu in New Setback Over Narc-Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The emergence of the Narc-Kenya party has left Health minister Charity Kaluki Ngilu in yet another major political limbo. Like a jilted lover, Mrs Ngilu is in outright denial - that her closest political ally, President Kibaki, is, indeed, in a new arrangement.
Vice-President Moody Awori's announcement that the President identifies himself with the party must have hit the Kitui Central MP like a thunder bolt. Casting doubts on Mr Awori's remark, Mrs Ngilu maintained that Narc-Kenya was started by politicians who are not willing to share leadership positions with others.
"I don't feel betrayed. However, what I think is that this is a situation of a leader taking it all," she said last Sunday, as if she had just digested and conceded the VP's announcement.
As the President's principal deputy, it is unlikely that the Funyula MP would have gambled with such a weighty affair. In any case, neither State House nor the President's closest allies have denied this to date.
But perhaps the most painful thing for Mrs Ngilu must be the fact that hers has all along been a case of back-stabbing by politicians from her own backyard.
Earlier, when there was an apparent scheme to wrest Narc from her, the man charged with the duty was none other than her ally-turned-foe, Mr Katua Katuku.
As chairman of the so-called national recruitment executive committee, the Mwala MP and Water minister spearheaded a spirited effort against Mrs Ngilu that nearly shoved her aside.
Now, Environment minister Kivutha Kibwana, whom she reportedly bargained for to get a Cabinet post last December, is among the key players behind Narc-Kenya.
Add to this to the beating she is receiving from her perennial political nemesis, Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, and you understand that things may not be looking bright for the nation's one-time most influential female politician.
The arrival of Narc-Kenya has thrown Mrs Ngilu's career into total disarray. Having distanced herself from the Orange Democratic Movement, this latest development is a major setback.
Mrs Ngilu's dilemma is best exemplified by her political antics during and after last year's referendum campaigns. Making an eleventh hour entry into the Banana campaign, she dismissed claims that she had been destined for the Orange side.
Indeed, it is not a secret that Mrs Ngilu has received enough hiccups from the Government side, where she belongs, and that she has been giving mixed signals to the Orange team.
The one plausible reason why she cannot move on is that the opposition side is already a full house. Mr Musyoka, in particular, appears to have inherited her political bloc in Ukambani.
Her biggest puzzle, therefore, is that she has to confine herself to one side of the fence, unless, of course, she does not mind playing second fiddle to Mr Musyoka or getting a peripheral position in the already crowded ODM.
As well as the regional arithmetic, the one on gender also appears to have been concluded on the other side of the fence. Already there are several credible candidates for the "Mama Orange" title, including former Cabinet minister Linah Jebii Kilimo and MPs Julia Ojiambo and Nyiva Mwendwa.
Nonetheless, "Mama Rainbow" - as Mrs Ngilu was fondly referred to during the 2002 election campaigns - still has a soft spot for the opposition. During last week's debate on composition of the House Business Committee, for instance, she opted to "donate" her slot to the opposition.
Combative in approach and aggressive in operation, Mrs Ngilu is a very focused and ambitious politician. The minister, who entered politics in 1992 following the repeal of Section 2A of the Constitution that re-introduced multi-party politics, established herself as one of the most powerful female politicians within five years in Parliament.
Little wonder in 1997, having crossed over from the Democratic Party, she caused a stir countrywide when she staged an electrifying presidential campaign on an Social Democratic Party ticket dubbed "masaa".
It is not clear whether the magic had anything to do with her policies or good looks - especially amidst a group of male candidates - but she excited many voters countrywide, only for her campaign to die down at the last minute. She finished fifth overall behind, retired President President Moi, DP's Kibaki, NDP's Raila Odinga and Ford-K's Mr Michael Wamalwa.
Things have never been the same ever since. This is not the first time, though, Mrs Ngilu is being subjected to political desertion. After aiding her 1997 campaigns, Kisumu Rural MP Peter Anyang' Nyong'o almost certainly engineered her exit from the party ahead of the 2002 poll.
In what was seen as a calculated move to lock her out of the presidential race on an SDP ticket, Prof Nyong'o and other party chiefs quickly crafted a new rule requiring that the party's flag bearer must hold a university degree.
Mrs Ngilu had to abandon the party and form a new one - National Party of Kenya. Ironically, it is NPK that later acted as the political vehicle for all opposition chiefs, including Mr Kibaki and even Prof Nyong'o, after it transformed into Narc.
True to the adage, "once bitten twice shy", Mrs Ngilu is afraid this could be happening to her again.
Although she is the Government's unofficial political agent in Ukambani, Mrs Ngilu is driving an almost empty bus. ODM's Mr Musyoka enjoys the support of most of the MPs. Among the others, Prof Kibwana has repeatedly mentioned that he is not a member of her party.
Other big names within the Government, Mr Katuku and Mr Kalembe Ndile are independent-minded. In fact, the Banana campaign in Ukambani initially hit a snag because of the differences between these politicians and Mrs Ngilu.
The Health minister, however, enjoys the support of female MPs from the wider Eastern Province, especially the nominated MPs Cecil Mbarire and Adelina Mwau.
But Mrs Ngilu's problems are being compounded by the steady rise of Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua.
Regarded at one point as the top-most female politician, Mrs Ngilu of the famous "Big Three", alongside Mr Kibaki and Mr Wamalwa, has been eclipsed by the Gichugu MP.
With the death of Mr Wamalwa and the recent resignation of Prof George Saitoti from the Cabinet, many would have expectedthat she would have been appointed to the post of VP or at least deputy Leader of Government Business - a post currently held by Ms Karua.
Working formula
It is too early, however, to write Mrs Ngilu's political obituary.She has yet to exhaust her cards and might just strike a working formula with either Ford-Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo, or any other political grouping.
Born in 1952, in Mbooni, Makueni District, Mrs Ngilu attended Mbooni Intermediate School after which she went to the Prestigious Alliance Girls High School.
She enrolled for a secretarial course at the Government Secretarial College in 1972. Her course soon paid off as she got a job at the Central Bank of Kenya a year after.
She left her job in 1975 to train as a Certified Public Secretary in Business Management at the Kenya Institute of Administration. In 1978, she got a job at Chase Manhattan Overseas Corporation as an Administrative Manager, which she held until 1981. An enterprising Mrs Ngilu started her own business, Ani-Plastics Limited.

Original_Search_Title: Saitoti's Political Future Hangs in Balance
Headline: Kenya: Saitoti's Political Future Hangs in Balance
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Education minister Prof George Saitoti is on a mission among the Maasai to salvage his political career.
After the train of events that ended with the mathematics professor
losing his Cabinet position early this year, he has returned to his
Kajiado North constituency with messages that project both uncertainty
and confidence about his political future.
"Mimi siwezi kuwatumbukiza katika shimo la nyoka wala siwezi kutema
mate yangu kisha nikalamba tena." (I cannot lead you into a pit full of
snakes and will never lick my own saliva).
These are the phrases the former minister commonly uses whenever he addresses his Kajiado North constituents lately.
At these rallies, Saitoti never tires of telling his constituents not to write him off from active politics.
Saitoti certainly is one politician who has undergone a series of
political frustrations despite having been the longest serving
vice-president.
In Kajiado he puts on a brave face and insists that he is still a political force.
What is clear though is that Saitoti is now a lonely politician in
Maasailand after years of being the most prominent person from the
region. During a church service at Kiserian Catholic
Parish on March 26, Saitoti nearly shed tears as he recounted his political frustrations.
He had, he confessed, experienced a lot of political tribulations in
his 23-year political career. Saitoti told the congregation of his
unexpected sacking by Moi only to be re-appointed to the Cabinet
through a roadside announcement.
"You were even told that the vice-president's seat could not add any food on your tables."
Saitoti has also been reminding his constituents of the historic March 18, 2002, Kanu delegates' conference at Kasarani.
"You also know how I was mistreated during the conference and
subsequently sacked in the run-up to the last General Election," he
says.
One thing Saitoti always emphasises is that he would never betray
President Kibaki because he had done him no wrong since Narc came to
power.
The Kajiado North MP's political rivals however see his current
predicament as a blessing for their plans to take him on in the next
General Election.
The former VP's allies however believe that he could soon be making
a comeback and that is just a matter of time before he bounces back to
the Cabinet.
Following Saitoti's exit from the Cabinet, at least seven candidates
have declared their interest in opposing him during next year's polls.
They include Peter ole Mositet, Saitoti's long-time political
nemesis Daniel ole Muyaa, Captain (Rtd) Edward Keen, a son of veteran
politician John Keen, Saruni ole Sane, Moses Sakuda, Oliver Seki, Alex
Sialala and Councillor George Nyoike. The number could rise.
Mositet is the local Liberal Democratic Party's branch chairman and
has vowed to ensure that Saitoti does not get the party's nomination.
Political analysts say that despite Saitoti being reportedly endowed
with considerable financial resources, the referendum results last year
left him vulnerable.
Olkejuado County Council chairman Julius ole Ntayia says, "Saitoti
is well endowed financially and the best thing for him to do is form
his own political party."
Ntayia believes that there is a political plot against the Kajiado North MP by Kibaki's confidants.
"Some people around the President have never been comfortable with
Saitoti owing to his political experience and would not let him get
anywhere in politics," he says.
According to Ntayia, Saitoti is his own worst enemy due to the "professor's unquestioning loyalty".
After the referendum in November Saitoti has resembled a wounded
lion. Although he justified his support for the Banana side by telling
his supporters that he had neither gambled with his political career
nor supported a losing side, the results dealt him a staggering blow.
The Orange Democratic Movement bagged 23,000 votes against Banana side's 19,000 in a constituency with 78,000 registered voters.
The results are believed to have undermined the former minister's national standing.
Having been one of the Banana's most ardent campaigners, the defeat
cast doubt on Saitoti's ability to marshal political support at any
level if he were to vie for the presidency.
A source close to the former minister blamed the loss on "Saitoti's poor campaign strategies".
"You cannot leave the entire campaign machinery in the hands of your family members and a few political rejects," he said.
To cap it all, the Goldenberg scandal successfully hounded Kajiado
Central MP Joseph Nkaisserry says: "Once you lose touch with the entire
community's grassroots leadership you miss the reality on the ground
and are politically doomed."
But a former civic leader who is a Saitoti confidant says: "It's a
matter of time before you see Saitoti back in the Cabinet. He will soon
get over the whole Goldenberg Scandal."
He and other supporters of the former minister must have felt
emboldened in their conviction when Saitoti's name did not appear on
the list of the to be prosecuted over the Goldenberg scam.
Among the issues that led to the community's rejection of the Wako
draft was the contentious land Chapter which was seen as being aimed at
repossessing communal lands.
"Both Nkaisserry and Ntimama are gifted political orators who are
able to gauge the community's political mood as opposed to Saitoti, who
cannot speak the native language." says former Olkejuado County Council
Chairman Daniel ole Muyaa.
This, Muyaa says, enables the two to correctly read the mood on the
ground as opposed to Saitoti, who has to rely on informers on matters
affecting the community.
Whether or not Saitoti will surprise his detractors and make a political comeback remains unclear.
Ntimama however cautions that there is no leadership vacuum in the
community despite Saitoti's exit from the Cabinet, adding that the
community has resolved to fully back ODM.

Original_Search_Title: Cocaine Haul Goes Up in Flames, But Owners Still Elusive
Headline: Kenya: Cocaine Haul Goes Up in Flames, But Owners Still Elusive
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A worker at Kemri shows the furnace used to burn the cocaine haul seized in Nairobi and Malindi.
The burning of the one-tonne cocaine haul in Nairobi yesterday settled the public anxiety that the drug had generated in recent months.
But the network that brought the hard drug to Kenya still remains unknown - and at large.
The shipment intercepted in Malindi and Nairobi entered the country disguised as bananas from Colombia. It is believed to have been destined for Amsterdam, where the solid blocks of cocaine in one-kilogramme sachets were to be repackaged and sold in Europe.
Another shipment of 295 kilogrammes was recovered in Amsterdam, and Dutch authorities believe the consignment originated from the same cartel.
Nairobi's centrality as a regional communication hub and its inadequate surveillance systems are the major attractions for drug traffickers, who consider it as a low risk transit point.
Drug traffickers are dangerous men and women who will kill to protect their turf.
The emergence of narco-States like Colombia, which supplies 80 per cent of the world's cocaine, and Afghanistan, where insurgents grew opium which they sold in exchange for arms, reveals the drugs' potent threat to world security.
Kenya has been sucked into the global narcotics trade - mainly as a transit point - as attested to by the growing number of "mules" who have been intercepted carrying heroin pellets in their bellies for onward transmission to Europe.
Health experts say cocaine and other hard drugs have a limited market due to their exorbitant prices, but the use of other forms of drugs, such has cannabis sativa (bhang) continues to grow.
Pure cocaine was first used in the 1880s as a local anaesthetic in eye, nose and throat surgeries because of its ability to provide anaesthesia as well as to constrict blood vessels and limit bleeding. However, many of its therapeutic uses are now obsolete after safer drugs were developed.
The effects of cocaine are wide and varied. It is powerfully addictive and compulsive. Some of the physical effects of cocaine use include constricted blood vessels and increased temperature, heart rate and blood pressure. Users may also experience feelings of restlessness, irritability and anxiety.
The UN Office on Drugs and Crime estimates that 200 million people aged between 15 and 64 years have consumed illicit drugs at least once in the last 12 months.
And the drug trade is equally large, with a retail value estimated at Sh23.1 trillion ($321 billion).
Cocaine use in Western countries, where it goes by the street names of coke, nose candy, blow and snow among others is deeply entrenched, with powder being the preferred form.
In the US, the street trade in cocaine was estimated at Sh2.5 trillion ($35 billion) in 2003 alone, exceeding incomes of large corporations such as the telecommunication firm, AT&T and Starbucks, the cafes chain.
American security agents were exposed for supplying hard drugs to African-American slums.
But the world superpower still has a mortal fear of drug traffickers, who it has tried to unsuccessfully keep out of its soil. And none demonstrates this better than the story of Pablo Escobar, the Colombian drug baron who is considered history's most ruthless and powerful drug dealer.
Colombia is the world's leading producer of cocaine, accounting for more than three-quarters of the world's annual yield. It also imported the drug grown in Peru and Bolivia.
Pablo Escobar started off as a car thief before gaining fame as a "respected" drug lord who Forbes magazine named the seventh-richest man, boasting fleets of airplanes, among other luxuries and a daily wage of $1 million when his cartel was at its peak.
Escobar's business motto was fairly simple: Plata o plomo (silver or lead) which meant bribe or face assassination.
Although he infiltrated the Colombian economic and political systems (some accounts allege he was responsible for the assassination of three presidential candidates), he remained a darling of the people in his hometown, Medellin.
Using Robin Hood tactics of giving money to the poor, and promoting sports, which was one of his favourite indulgences, he galvanised support from the people, many of who served as informers against the authorities.
His trafficking network had spread from the Americas to Asia, as did his financial base that hit $25 billion by the time of his death in 1993.
The US, terrified at the impact of the drugs on its society, signed an extradition treaty with Colombia that made it legal to try drug barons in the US for drug trafficking.
Escobar, tired of the hide and seek games that he had played with Colombian authorities all his life, turned himself in on condition that he would be handed over to the US.
He was even allowed to use a portion of his vast wealth to build his own "prison" where he would be confined for five years. The "prison" was essentially a private club where the drug baron enjoyed a luxurious life, while dashing out to party with friends or enjoy sports.
This came to an end when Press photos displayed the opulence in the "prison" and other pictures of him partying outside it.
The authorities, embarrassed by the expose, decided to turn him to a regular jail, but Escobar fled till he was stopped by a hail of bullets on December 2, 1993, just a day after marking his 44th birthday.
Escobar's drug ring, El Cartel de Medellin, crumbled after his death, and most of its business was taken over by the Cali Cartel, which is based in the southern part of Colombia, in the city of Cali.
The Cali Cartel was founded in the 1970s by Gilberto Rodriguez Orejuela and his brother, Miguel Rodriguez Orejuela, alongside Jose Santacruz Londono and Helmer "Pacho" Herrera.
At the outfit's high point, they were estimated to control 80 per cent of the cocaine exports from Colombia to the US and are suspected to have fragmented in recent years.
Other recent well known cartels in Mexico, the second most active country in drug trafficking to the US are the Juarez, Tijuana and Tamaulipas cartels.
Death of Escobar
The Colombian drug crisis, however, did not subside with the death of Escobar.
The surviving drug cartels are ruthless and judges are routinely threatened with death. Although Escobar is rumoured to have been behind the 1985 storming of the Colombian Supreme Court by left-wing guerillas, which resulted in the murder of half the judges present, the reign of fear still remains.
More often than not, judges deliver judgments through anonymous voice recordings to avoid victimisation, demonstrating the severe erosion of law and order that drug dealers have caused.
Other avenues of control include politics, where barons often sponsor or participate in elections, placing their stooges in strategic places to ensure adequate lobbying or protection whenever they need it.
And of course there is corruption, or what Escobar called "silver."

Original_Search_Title: Exposure Will Bring More Political Accountability - Former Corruption Tsar
Headline: Kenya: Exposure Will Bring More Political Accountability - Githongo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Public reaction to the recent exposure of scandals, the slow pace
with which the Government was dealing with corruption and attacks on
the media suggest that a culture of political accountability may be
developing in Kenya, former Ethics Permanent Secretary John Githongo
said in Washington, DC.
Githongo, who gave a talk titled "Corruption in Kenya: A
Whistleblower's Account," at the Cato Institute on Wednesday, sharply
criticised the Government for its complacency in dealing with
corruption, and added that corrupt deals were still alive.
He said only continuous pressure from the media, civil society,
Parliament and voices from within the executive could bring change. The
audience of more than 200 included officials from the European
Commission, World Bank, International Monetary Fund and the US
government, as well as Kenya Government officials and diplomats from
several countries, including Lesotho, Eritrea and Germany.
Githongo said the Government's defeat had at the referendum on the
proposed new constitution showed that it misunderstood the reality that
vote-buying no longer works.
"That a sitting government could lose a referendum despite its
access to resources was not, to me, entirely surprising. What was
surprising was that the Banana leaders were stunned by the defeat and
its scale," Githongo said.
"This signalled what was perhaps a more fundamental disconnect, one
so vast that it is not unlikely that Kenya was entering a phase of
unprecedented events, driven by executive actions based on limited
information and indecisiveness."
Festering issues played out at referendum
Githongo said the most significant reforms by the Kibaki
administration were made within the first nine months of taking power,
before disagreements between coalition parties and a standoff over the
position of Prime Minister rendered Narc dysfunctional. The referendum
on the Constitution became the arena on which the festering issues were
played out, he said.
He said the "bizarre" police attack on the Standard Group offices,
television station and printing press on March 2, which came at a time
when the Government had been struggling to keep up with an increasingly
informed electorate that had high expectations and the confidence to
express them, was a puzzling contradiction.
Githongo said the setbacks have been well-absorbed by "an
increasingly mature and sophisticated body politic, whose unspoken
response is "see you at the next elections".
"Kenyans seem to pity the Government as much as they are outraged by it," he said.
Marian Tupy, assistant director of the Project on Global Economic
Liberty at the Cato Institute, who introduced Githongo, cited several
experts' views and argued that while the rest of the world had grown
richer, Africa had grown poorer and the Gross Domestic Product, the
value of all goods and services produced per capita, had shrunk by 11
per cent.
He said the proposed solution of providing massive infusion of
foreign aid might be a disincentive for economic growth and actually
promote or increase the level of corruption.
The Cato Institute, a non-profit public foundation, was founded in 1977.

Original_Search_Title: Include ODM in Review, Kibaki Told
Headline: Kenya: Include ODM in Review, Kibaki Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An assistant minister asked the Government to include the Orange Democratic Movement in the constitutional review.
Mr Asman Kamama of Lands said the changing the country's laws was a crucial task that should not be left to one side of the political divide.
The ODM team includes mostly Kanu and Liberal Democratic Party MPs, who, under the Orange symbol successfully campaigned for rejection of a proposed constitution at last year's referendum, defeating the pro-Government Banana camp.
Following the referendum results, President Kibaki dissolved his Cabinet and prorogued Parliament. The leading lights in the Orange camps were left when the Head of State appointed new ministers and their assistants.
They were former ministers Raila Odinga, Najib Balala, Anyang' Nyong'o, Kalonzo Musyoka, Linah Chebii, Ochillo Ayacko, and William Ole Ntimama.
And last month, the President appointed a 15-member committee of eminent persons to find the way forward in the review.
The Orange camp has already dismissed the team led by Ambassador Bethuel Kiplagat, saying it was a waste of time and money.
Break stalemate
Yesterday, Mr Kamama (Baringo East, Ford) called for unity among leaders to break the review stalemate.
"Let us conclude the work by having the Orange team on board. The Banana team cannot finalise constitutional review alone," he said, amid foot-thumping by Opposition MPs.
Earlier, two Mr Joseph Nkaissery (Kajiado Central, Kanu) and Mr George Khaniri (Hamisi, Narc) criticised the President for not consulting the ODM before appointing the Kiplagat team.
Said Mr Khaniri: "Who are those eminent persons? With its intelligence network, doesn't the Government know why it was defeated at the referendum? They (Government side) may be many here in the House, but very few outside there."
The MP said the speech delivered by the President during State opening of the opening the present session of Parliament last week was full of rhetoric.
Mr Khaniri and Mr Nkaissery said the Government had failed to provide security to the public. They asked the Government to give the "real identify" of two controversial foreigners accused of being mercenaries.
"Kenya is under God's care. How can a foreigner threaten a Cabinet minister yet no one in the Government seems to know where he came from? ?" asked Mr Nkaissery.
He said the the President should have touched on the threat posed by bird flu and the country's preparedness.

Original_Search_Title: Coast Leaders Differ Over New Party Plan
Headline: Kenya: Coast Leaders Differ Over New Party Plan
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Coast leaders have differed over proposals to form a new political party for the region.
Former Mombasa mayor Taib Ali Taib said time was not ripe to form a political party, adding that residents should be sensitised on voter registration first.
"We must ensure that we have millions of voters in the region before we think of forming a political party. With masses we shall have bargaining power," he said.
But Bahari MP Joe Khamisi said his constituents wanted a new political party.
"My Bahari people want a political party to be formed to fight for the agenda of the Coast. I rally behind them," he said.
Khamisi, who is the founder of the Coast People's Forum (CPF), was the only MP who attended the meeting at Mombasa Beach Hotel.
Tourism minister Morris Dzoro said he could not attend as he was travelling to Europe. Changamwe MP Ramadhan Kajembe said he was at a wedding, while Galole MP Tola Kofa said he was travelling to his home in Tana River.
Khamisi wondered why the MPs failed to show up at the meeting even after he sent them invitation letters early this month.
"The MPs used to complain that we do not invite them officially to CLF meetings.
"We did send them letters but still they did not show up, save for apologies from Dzoro, Kajembe and Kofa," he said.
The Bahari MP told the MPs that their seats were not personal property, adding that residents would reject them if they failed to deliver.
Khamisi said it was baseless for MPs to fail to attend CPF meetings just because some of its members were eyeing their parliamentary seats.
"We cannot bar parliamentary aspirants in the Coast from attending CPF meetings for the sake of pleasing sitting MPs The parliamentary seats do not belong to individuals," he said.
Taib described the Coast MPs as "cowards" hiding under the shadow of prominent national leaders.
Taib said that during the last national referendum, the leaders supported the Banana camp against the wishes of locals.
"Coast MPs tend to dance to the tune of those in authority rather than those who elected them to parliament. Their stomachs are more important to them than fighting for their people's interests," said Taib.
On February 12, most Coast MPs snubbed the launch of the leaders' forum.

Original_Search_Title: Zim Yearns for the Days Before Aippa
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zim Yearns for the Days Before Aippa
Body_Text:
Harare — IF recent press reports are anything to go by, the government is
actively working to bring about sweeping changes to the Access of
Information and Protection of Privacy Act (AIPPA), Zimbabwe's media
regulation instrument.
Recent reports suggest that the government has agreed to remove
offending clauses and sections in AIPPA, which resulted in the closure
of three newspapers since the Act became law in 2002.
The reports quote freely from "informed sources" who attended an
African Union human rights organ in Banjul, Gambia, last December.
According to sources quoted in the reports, Zimbabwe, under pressure
from the African Charter on Human and People's Rights, is now willing
to institute changes to the Act to remove repressive sections or
clauses in AIPPA, and Information and Publicity Minister Tichaona
Jokonya is said to be in the process of effecting changes to the
draconian piece of legislation, sections of which merely complement the
dreaded Public Order and Security Act (POSA).
Against these expectations, Jokonya has been reported as saying the
media was a weapon of self-destruction. This statement, if the minister
was reported accurately, has been taken to refer to only a section of
the industry, which continues to bear the brunt of an Act that has been
applied selectively to cow and destroy the private media industry in
the country.
If the minister is indeed working to bring about sweeping changes to
AIPPA, that would be a great day indeed for the beleaguered private
sector industry.
But watchers of government policy trends are sceptical. The
government has never shown itself to be willing to be influenced by the
weight of public opinion even in a matter of such great public interest
as the application of AIPPA.
To the government, inflexibility is a virtue rather a vice, while
moving with the weight of public opinion is considered a weakness. But
this unyielding attitude is fraught with some problems for the
government. It is the making of the economic chaos now prevailing in
the country, with devastating poverty and other social evils for the
majority of the population.
The problem facing the country is that its government is unwilling,
if one may borrow Justice Makarau's now celebrated pronouncement, "to
take prisoners" in its war against its perceived enemies. To the
government it must be a fight to the bitter end. If you do not take
prisoners in a theatre of war then you must kill them off or be killed.
This appears to be the guiding philosophy of a government that has
run out of initiatives to find a lasting solution to the current
problems gripping the country.
In its confrontation with the independent media, the government
maintains an argument of silence, which, everyone knows will not lead
to any conclusion or resolution of matters of mutual concern.
There is an urgent need, therefore, for the government to take the
initiative to end the impasse by removing the causes of this impasse.
The government needs to work with all players in the industry to find a
lasting solution to the problems affecting the industry. Most of these
problems have been caused by AIPPA to the great detriment of the
country.
Solution to these problems does not lie in working only with
interests in the public media. The answer lies in an all-embracing
initiative to resolve outstanding issues. AIPPA has affected the
private sector more than any other, and government should recognise
this fact and consult the sector's representatives.
These remarks assume that government is willing to resolve the
problems spawned by AIPPA, which has been used as an instrument of
victimisation. Glaring injustices have been meted out against the
private media without any one lifting a finger to redress the
situation. The argument of silence prevails.
The Minister of Information and Publicity inherited an extremely
untenable legacy left by his predecessor, but is he willing to exorcise
Jonathan Moyo's ghost from the scene of his new responsibility to
tackle the problems facing the industry, or does he seek to maintain
the status quo and become a strange bed-fellow with a man most
Zimbabweans yearn to forget?
The idea of an independent media council is laudable, but let first
things be done first. No amount of wishful thinking will, however, make
the proposed council independent as long as AIPPA remains in its
present form.
The instruments of the Act that negate the ideal of an independent
media commission or council must be removed before there can be any
hope of an independent body overseeing the industry.
Those who have been in the industry for any length of time must know
that self-regulation has been the norm in this country since the
establishment of the first newspaper in the country at the turn of the
last century.
But the industry now exists in an environment in which other people
prefer regulation by law, and in pursuance of this policy, AIPPA was
promulgated. We now know the results of the past three years of its
existence and what this means, not only to the industry, but for the
country's future well-being as well.
It is imperative that all sectors of the industry should work
together towards removal of those sections and clauses of the Act that
inhibit the growth and prosperity of in the industry as a whole. The
industry should stand united against those who want to drive a wedge
between the public and private sectors to confuse by remote control.
The Act as it is, is riddled with pitfalls, ambiguities and
inconsistencies all of which have been used to frustrate the
dispensation of justice to all. Citizens have been victimised, robbed
and workers left destitute, all in the name of AIPPA.
It is a fact of life that the Act has been applied selectively to
ride roughshod over the rights of the private media sector. It is also
true that sins of omission have been committed to frustrate citizens
seeking to enter the industry.
A classic case is that of the Africa Tribune Newspapers whose only
default was its balance sheet. The Act says a successful applicant for
registration is entitled to l2 months grace to, it is implied, find
money to operate.
In the ATN case the "only" reason registration was denied was that
the balance sheet did not meet the "perceptions" of the Act to qualify.
So the promoter of a media service provider that had been purchased as
going concern was not given time to find the money.
There are others, but let us for now spare the reader the details.
The Act has been manipulated to ensure citizens failed to qualify for
registration. Where in the world is an applicant required to produce a
balance sheet as a pre-condition to registration?
The tragic sadness of it all is that for over 100 years without AIPPA, this country has done exceptionally well.
But for the first time in the history of the country, the population
has known real deprivation, pinching poverty affects the majority of
the people, and health services have collapsed for most of them and
also for the first time in the country's experience unknown diseases
like cholera now ravage the people in all centres of population
This has come as a great shock for the people of Zimbabwe who have
always associated cholera with what is disparagingly called banana
republics, and not their own country.

Original_Search_Title: Kaparo Sets Standards for MPs And Tells Them to End Wrangles
Headline: Kenya: Kaparo Sets Standards for MPs And Tells Them to End Wrangles
Body_Text:
Nairobi — MPs were yesterday given a reading on the decorum expected from them during the fifth session of Parliament.
But National Assembly Speaker Francis ole Kaparo questioned their ability to rise to the challenges facing millions of voters who were living "in times of hopelessness".
"This is the penultimate session of the ninth Parliament. We must remind ourselves of the stark realities facing our nation where millions of people are going through very painful pangs of hunger," he said before President Kibaki officially opened the session.
Mr Kaparo also spoke of the pre and post referendum politics and the widening divisions among the country's leaders. He reminded MPs that the country was bigger than individuals.
He said: "This House must rise to the occasion and provide leadership. It must be an example of unity and not division; love, not hate; and purposefulness instead of hopelessness."
The new session, he said, must deal with hunger, unemployment, poverty and other challenges facing the country.
Falsehoods and propaganda
He also challenged the House to reverse the falsehoods, propaganda and hatred that had become a feature in political pronouncements.
Mr Kaparo, the man on whose shoulders rest the difficult task of guiding the House, said it was the duty of MPs to stop the country from racing towards the abyss.
"This House must urgently get out this morass. We must rescue the nation from the dangerous cascade to division and hopelessness," he said.
Looking at the MPs seated before him, Mr Kaparo asked them to place the nation before sectarian interests in debates and when voting on motions and Bills.
He said: "This country is bigger than any positions we hold. I pray that you all hear me. I beseech you to approach your duties bearing that in mind."
Earlier, men of the cloth had set the tone for the Speaker when they urged MPs to exorcise the ghosts of bananas and oranges - which informed their referendum campaigns - and work for their people.
Mr Kaparo picked the cue and said it was the duty of MPs to end the difficult times Kenyans were going through.
"In very difficult times, people look up to God and their leaders for solutions. The Kenyan leadership is gathered in this chamber. I would like to pose this question: Are we equal to the monumental task?" he asked.
"I am afraid I am hesitant to give an answer. I am afraid because speaking the truth has been criminalised in our times. We thrive on falsehoods, propaganda, hate and, yes, inertia."
Mr Kaparo said sobriety and a sense of purpose should guide the MPs to stop them from "converting this country into a quarrelling nation".
