Then, he turned to political parties and demanded that they reign in their members and instill discipline in their conduct on the floor of the House and in parliamentary committees.
He warned the parties against repeating last session's attempts to scuttle the business of House committees, saying that this would be "perilous".
"Political parties should exercise disciplinary control over MPs and exercise democratic ideals in House committees," he said.
Last year, majority of LDP and Kanu MPs boycotted sittings of the Parliamentary Select Committee on the Constitution, a move which nearly delayed the conclusion on the review process.

Original_Search_Title: Call Snap Election This Year, Now ODM Leaders Tell the President
Headline: Kenya: Call Snap Election This Year, Now ODM Leaders Tell the President
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The President should call a snap election this year, MPs allied to the Orange Democratic Movement have said.
According to them, the Government had lost the moral authority govern.
MPs Kalonzo Musyoka, Raila Odinga, William Ruto and others allied to the movement said the Government had failed to learn from its past mistakes and had no business remaining in power.
Hamisi MP George Khaniri set the ball the rolling when he asked Kenyans to demand a snap election this year, saying the Government had let them down.
State of confusion
His views were supported by Migori MP Owino Likowa who said the Government had left Kenyans in a state of confusion over its handling of issues like the mercenaries allegations and corruption.
Addressing mourners at Gusii Stadium during a funeral service for former Knut Kisii branch executive secretary Christopher Matoke, the leaders asked their colleagues who were still loyal to the Government to quit.
"The Narc Government is on fire and is inhabited with snakes which are ready to bite those still loyal. This is why we are urging MPs loyal to the Government and whose names have not been tainted by any scandals to quit before they are axed by the same blade that shaved the banana team during the last referendum," said Mr Odinga.
He urged MPs form Kisii District - some of whom were present - to disassociate themselves from the Government if they wanted to remain in politics.
Earlier, Ford People MPs Hezron Manduku (Nyaribari Masaba) and Mwancha Okioma (Kitutu Masaba) were heckled and made to cut short their speeches after the crowd demanded that ODM leaders address them.
"I ask Hon Obwocha to lead Kisii Ford People MPs from the Government," Mr Odinga said.
On his part, Mr Musyoka said the Government has lost the moral authority to govern.
He asked Kenyans not to lose hope saying the future of the country was in the hands of the ODM. He said the movement's leaders were still united and would field a single presidential candidate.
Harassing leaders
Mr Ruto accused the Government of harassing ODM leaders by ordering Mr Odinga and Musyoka to record statements over mercenary claims while giving the Armenians brothers preferential treatment.
South Mugirango MP Omingo Magara and his Rongo counterpart Ochillo Ayacko asked ODM to remain united and pick a presidential candidate as demanded by those who voted 'no' during the November referendum

Original_Search_Title: Gani Berates Ojo Over Comments
Headline: Nigeria: Gani Berates Ojo Over Comments
Body_Text:
Owerri/Onitsha/Benin/Lagos — OUTSPOKEN lawyer, Chief Gani Fawehinmi (SAN) yesterday described as "nonsense" remarks credited to the Attorney General of the Federation and Minister of Justice, Chief Bayo Ojo (SAN) over the boycott of courts by lawyers, saying that the latter has demonstrated "deep ignorance of the legal process".
Similarly, National President of Nigerian Bar Association (NBA), Prince Lanke Odogiyan, in separate reaction, said the minister's reaction was unfortunate, shocking and quite unexpected even as he expressed satisfaction over the response of lawyers to the two-day protest which ended yesterday.
Like what obtained Monday, lawyers again shunned court proceedings nationwide in obedience to the NBA directive that they do so to protest government's disregard of court orders.
Daily Champion recalls that Chief Ojo had told the Federal High Court, Abuja, that the leadership of NBA lied when it claimed that it was not aware of the suit filed by the Federal Government challenging the power of the National Executive Cou8ncil (NEC) of the NBA to call out lawyers on strike.
He told the presiding Justice Anwuli Chikere that "I personally served the court's papers at NBA's office in Lagos."
Ojo who was reacting to claims by the NBA that it was not served the court's papers and therefore had to embark on the boycott, said that the association's NEC lacked the powers to call lawyers out on strike.
He subsequently withdrew a motion for an interlocutory injunction to stop the strike pending the determination of the case.
But taking a swipe on the minister, Gani said he (Ojo) has no right to complain on the issue, stressing that the boycott was against the mismanagement of the society by the government.
Said he: "The minister has shown a deep ignorance of legal process. Is service of court papers synonymous with a court order?" he asked.
Also reacting, Odogiyan said the minister couldn't have served the courts papers himself as that was the duty of the court bailiff.
"It is the court bailiff that serves court papers and shouldn't be him. May be he sent people from his office who came to drop it in our secretariat but that is not to our knowledge," he told Daily Champion in an interview.
Meanwhile, for the second day, lawyers from all parts of Edo State yesterday stayed away from various courts of law as part of the nationwide boycott of all courts.
Unlike the situation on Monday when the protest started, all activities at the various Courts of law in Edo State were totally paralyzed in Edo State yesterday with even some of the judiciary staff staying away from office.
There were strong indication that the nation may have lost huge amount of money in form of revenues from the boycott as revenue department of the High Court in Benin were conspicuously absent from their duty posts. The situation also affected some of the business owners within the various courts as everywhere was totally deserted.
Some of the High Court Judges who turned up for duties last Monday were equally absent yesterday while scores of villagers who were unaware of the development were seen stranded within the Court premises.
Speaking to newsmen on the development, Edo State Chairman of the NBA, Mr. Ogbeide Ihama, said the association has demonstrated that the rule of law in the country must be obeyed.
"We want the entire nation and in fact the people at the helm of affairs in Nigeria to note that we can never support any violation of Court order because it is a prelude to break down of law and order. The judiciary should be respected because it is the last hope of common man in the Country. "NBA is an association that is committed to uphold the integrity of the judiciary in Nigeria and that is why we have demonstrated our protest in this form" he said.
On his part, the chairman of NBA, Aguata branch, Chief Cosmas Ezeanowai-Obiezu has commended the national leadership of the association for the protest.
Ezeanowai-Obiezu who spoke to newsmen yesterday said that the current position of NBA should be enough signal both to government functionaries and politicians who flagrantly disobey court orders, that they cannot do that and get scot-free.
He said that the boycott symbolises lawyers' social and judicial responsibility to ensure that court orders are not disobeyed with impunity by government and its officials.
The Aguata NBA boss commended the courage of NBA president and the members of the NEC, saying that for the first time in many years NBA is taking leadership in fighting against injustice in the country.
Similarly, Daily Champion correspondent, who monitored the boycott in Makurdi, the Benue State capital, observed that all courts in Makurdi did not sit, as judges too stayed away as a result of the lawyers' boycott
At the state Ministry of Justice, which houses about seven courts, litigants were seen roaming about, with judicial staff discussing the boycott in groups.
The Makurdi Branch Chairman of NBA, Mr. Tony Ijohor told newsmen that members of the Bar have been fully informed and mobilised to ensure the effectiveness of the boycott, disclosing that "so far the response of members to the boycott order has been very impressive." He warned however, that should any member of the Bar be tempted to violate the order from its apex body, he would be appropriately sanctioned.
The NBA State Publicity Secretary, Mr. Pascal Adinya, told Daily Champion that the Federal governmnet's disobedience of court orders of late have been informed by desperation for an unconstitutional third term. He advised government to have a rethink as according to him, "Nigeria is a constitutional democracy and not a Banana Republic."
Another legal practitioner, Mr. Aker Ator, said besides the NBA boycott telling the federal government that it should be prepared for a legal challenge from the NBA, "the boycott will send a signal to the Western Powers that a constitutional crisis is brewing in Nigeria."

Original_Search_Title: Muyonjo Remains True to His Clients
Headline: Uganda: Muyonjo Remains True to His Clients
Body_Text:
Some of us who have been following Kampala's eating scene over the
last two decades remember a time when there were only a couple of
worthwhile eating joints in the Central Business District that served
decent local food. I am speaking of the now defunct Kyosimba Onnanya
that used to be adjacent to the former Coop Bank on William Street
along with Mr Muyonjo's further up the street on Wilson Road.
Both were down to earth establishments that came to represent
inexpensive food that consistently excelled in terms of being tasty,
wholesome and in general the quality was a cinch. Sadly, the former
went out of business in the twilight of the last century while the
latter very nearly went belly under when one of those nouvea riche
property moguls turned it into a tasteless modern edifice.
As is all too often the case, upon constructing the new block, the
landlord demanded rent that was tantamount to a king's ransom and out
of the reach of Muyonjo, the restaurateur, thus he was forced to move
off the beaten track but still within the confines of the city. By some
good fortune, he was able to stay on the same street albeit where
Wilson Road intersects with Ben Kiwanuka Street. It is a perilous
gamble when one is forced to move joints , and all the more so in a
market that is not only fickle but totally saturated with the jua
kali-like who are notorious for peddling cheap food at the proverbial
Shs 1,000 bob mark.
Ultimately he managed to find a property that is not far from where
he used to be though admittedly it is dilapidated and the building
would have been condemned in any modern metropolis abroad.
We recently had lunch there and as ever the irrepressible and
indefatigable Muyonjo was seated in the corner in front of the food
counter personally writing down orders while overseeing the running of
the place. By the way, he still does the shopping.
One of the better-appreciated features of this place is the variety
of say the semutundu. Such a shame that few establishments offer this
wonderful and delicate fish in the smoked form, where it is steamed
with just a hint of the aromatic smoked banana leaf, the other form
being smoked and then stewed in groundnut sauce or else smoked and
served in the form of a stew.
The other big points about Muyonjo's are their stews and luwombos.
The former come in boiled form and are great favourites for many a
diner. The matooke is never a let down.
A visit here would not be complete without mention of the fruit
platter. Though not artistically presented, generous portions of
watermelon, pineapple, jackfruit among others are just the thing to
compliment a hearty meal. The half portion at Shs1,500 is enough to
serve a couple.
RATING:
XXXX UNRIVALLED
XXX EXCELLENT
XX VERY GOOD
X GOOD
NONE POOR TO SATISFACTORY
These views are purely the reviewer's personal reaction to food,
ambience and service with price being factored into consideration. The
menu listings are subject to change without notice.
Place: Muyonjo's Restaurant and CafÃ©
Rating: X
Food: Good Buganda fare
Location: Wilson Road, near Arua Park across from the Total petrol station
Smoke Free zone: Proscribed indoors
Price range: A tenner will buy a meal for two plus a couple of soft drinks
Recommended dishes: Smoked semutundu, matooke, boiled beef or goat stew
Service: Swift and efficient
Ambience: Dismal
Sound level: Noisy and privacy is a luxury unheard of here
Hours: Breakfast and lunch served till late everyday save for Sunday
Reservations: No need

Original_Search_Title: A Scramble to Plot the Course for Constitutional Review
Headline: Kenya: Politics-Kenya: A Scramble to Plot the Course for Constitutional Review
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Groups in Kenya which include politicians and activists say they will present plans to complete a review process that is aimed at providing a new constitution for the East African country.
This follows their dismissal of the Feb. 24 appointment of a presidential committee to jumpstart the constitutional review, which stalled after citizens voted against a revamped constitution that was subjected to a referendum last November.
While government urged a "yes" vote in the poll, opposition politicians and certain former ministers from the ruling coalition campaigned for a rejection of the draft.
Instead, opponents of the document threw their support behind an earlier version known as the 'Bomas draft', which emerged from discussions that took place at the 'Bomas of Kenya' cultural centre near the capital, Nairobi.
These discussions were held over 2003 and 2004 under the auspices of the National Constitutional Conference (NCC), which brought together 600 delegates from across the country. They based their talks on a draft prepared in 2002 by the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission, which had previously travelled throughout the country to collect the views of citizens as to what form the new constitution should take.
The main innovations of the Bomas draft included the downsizing of presidential authority: a post was created for a prime minister who would share power with the head of state.
This provision came after Kenyans interviewed by the review commission said they were opposed to an executive head of state, fearing a repeat of presidential abuses of power that had marred their country's post-independence history.
However, the version of the constitution that was ultimately put before citizens during the referendum was amended to restore presidential powers -- even though parliament was initially supposed to accept or reject the Bomas draft in its entirety.
The National Alliance Party of Kenya (NAK), an arm of the ruling coalition that includes President Mwai Kibaki and those close to him, was able to push through the Consensus Act -- enabling NAK legislators to alter the Bomas draft in favour of a strong presidency, and a non-executive prime minister.
The groups which are seeking a way forward for the constitutional debate include civil society organisations and the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) -- a group comprising opposition politicians and former ministers from the Liberal Democratic Party, a member of the ruling National Rainbow Coaltion. (During last year's referendum, those opposed to the constitution adopted the orange as their symbol, while those in favour of the draft used the banana.)
The Bomas draft, say the groups, will be at the heart of their strategy.
"I can assure you that a substantive part of our proposal is going to be founded on the Bomas draft," Mutula Kilonzo, a leading ODM official, told IPS.
"We shall also be taking advantage of the Bomas conference which adjourned sina die (until further notice). This is a wonderful window that remains available for the country. This means that the Bomas conference can be recalled."
According to Kilonzo, an ODM advisory committee established Thursday will begin meeting next week to draw up proposals on how to proceed with the constitutional review.
Similarly, the Multi-Sectoral Forum (MSF), which comprises civil society organisations working to further democracy, human rights and good governance, is planning a national conference between May and June.
This meeting will discuss loopholes that allowed government to tamper with the draft constitution -- despite claims that the version put forward by the NCC more truly reflected the aspirations of Kenyans.
"There will be a need to amend the Consensus Act that made it possible for the government to amend the Bomas draft, which was the popular draft," said Paddy Onyango, executive director of the Citizens Coalition for Constitutional Change -- a leading member of the MSF.
"It is this draft that will form the basis of our negotiations. I do not see any other document that can pass in terms of content."
Still others are calling for government to convene a meeting with the ODM, civil society organisations and religious groups. Their hope is that the Bomas draft could be used as a basis for resolving disputes between the various parties about the constitution, and that a final draft could then be produced for presentation to parliament -- or to be put forward in a second referendum.
Government maintains that it is committed to providing a new constitution.
Martha Karua, the new minister for justice and constitutional affairs, said upon her appointment earlier this year that putting a new constitution in place was at the head of government's list of priorities. This would achieved before the next general election in 2007, added Karua.
Kibaki's administration, which came to power in December 2002, initially promised the country a new constitution within its first 100 days in office.

Original_Search_Title: Citizens Should Drive Review Process
Headline: Kenya: Citizens Should Drive Review Process
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The clamour for constitutional change can be traced to mass protests and activities which characterised the demand for the repeal of Section 2A of the Constitution. After the 1992 multiparty elections and with the realisation that the polls did not expand democratic space, attention was focused on the attainment of more comprehensive reforms.
In 1997, the Constitution of Kenya Review Bill was published after intense negotiations between the Government and the Opposition. This Act was part of the minimal constitutional and legislative reforms package prior to the 1997 General Election under the aegis of the Inter-party Parliamentary Group.
In October 1999, the parliamentary select committee on constitutional review was formed with the mandate to collect and collate views from Kenyans and to recommend how the Constitution could be reviewed. Parliament adopted the committee's report on April 26, 2000.
Collect views from the public
The report recommended that Parliament nominates 21 persons from whom the President would appoint 15 to become commissioners of the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC), who would collect views from the public.
On May 18, 2001, CKRC Act was amended, raising the number of commissioners to 27, leading to the merger of CKRC and the Ufungamano group.
Then Narc came to the scene late in 2002 bringing together different parties, including the Democratic Party, Liberal Democratic Party, National Party Of Kenya and the Social Democratic Party.
Narc was registered as a political party, but without any formal dissolution of the member parties. Members of the various parties, thus stood for the election as members of one political party.
In pre-election coalitions, the various parties would field candidates through their respective parties while consenting to a single presidential candidate based on a clearly understood proposed structure of government and power-sharing arrangement.
Narc prioritised delivery of a new constitutional dispensation. True to the promise, on taking over government, it reconvened the National Constitutional Conference (NCC) by amending the CKRC Act 2001 accordingly. However, deep rooted mistrust between the so called government friendly delegates and the rest of the delegates soon engulfed the arena.
As a result, a group of aggrieved Kenyans led by the Rev Timothy Njoya moved to court to challenge various aspects of the conference, including the legality of the district delegates; the provisions of section 47 of the Constitution on review; and the issue of the referendum
The court ruled that the NCC, popularly known as Bomas group, was not a constituent assembly. In light of the fact that Parliament is a creature of the Constitution, it did not have the power to enact the Constitution. The Bomas group, as constituted, was not an elected body.
In the reasoning of the court therefore, the primordial power of the people is not donated by law but is presumed and assumed. It cannot be faulted simply because Section 47 of the current constitution has not been amended.
Thus, the challenge is to keep the constitutional review debate alive. Since politicians cannot be relied upon to do that, the civil society should champion the crusade for a new constitution. It should be recalled that the Ufungamano Initiative, despite not having a legal mandate, was able to push the Government to the table to pursue a people-driven constitutional change.
Seven years down, we are now more advantaged. Two court rulings - the Njoya group and Yellow Movement cases - support the propositions that:
Constituent power resides in the people and can be exercised by them without undue worry as to whether it has been expressly donated by the written law.
That the process of initiating constitution-making is not the preserve of government, but can be commenced by any person , entity or group provided the final document receives the stamp of authority of the people.
Consultative meeting
In view of these, the appropriate way to proceed should be for the Government to convene a consultative meeting with the Orange Democratic Movement, the Banana team, religious leaders and the civil society Organisations.
The forum would then nominate seven eminent persons to examine the available documents, and using the Bomas Draft as a working paper, make the necessary amendments, bearing in mind the original report of the CKRC and the court rulings.
The final document would then be presented to Parliament, or be put to the people either in a constituent assembly or through a referendum.
In the event that the Government is unwilling to do this, then it is open for any person to initiate the process. The Ufungamano group could have done this, but its membership has since dissipated.
If, however, the proposal as above does not work, there always remains the possibility of pursuing the remaining court cases, including the ones filed by Martin Shikuku and others on the one hand, and the LDP/Kanu case on the other.
All around us, countries have reviewed and revised their Lancaster House constitutions, some even twice. It would be our collective shame as a country to admit to the world that we cannot sit and reason together, and come up with a document to govern ourselves.
Mr Otiende Amollo, an advocate, is a former chairman of the International Commission of Jurists.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Meet in City for Talks On Drawing Up the Budget
Headline: Kenya: MPs Meet in City for Talks On Drawing Up the Budget
Body_Text:
Nairobi — MPs meet in Nairobi this morning for the first time since the House went on recess last year to discuss budget making.
The 222 law-makers will also tackle a plan to establish a parliamentary fiscal analysis office and how it will affect Parliament's operations and its relations with the other arms of government.
The two-day workshop will be opened at Safari Park Hotel by National Assembly Speaker Francis ole Kaparo.
In November, Parliament adjourned early to allow members to campaign for or against the proposed new constitution in the referendum.
The MPs were divided into the Banana (Yes) and the Orange (No) teams, and the latter carried the day. President Kibaki and the Constitutional Affairs minister at the time, Mr Kiraitu Murungi, led the Banana camp, while his Cabinet colleagues Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka as well as Kanu national chairman Uhuru Kenyatta led the Orange campaign.
Other No leaders included Mrs Linah Kilimo, Prof Anyang' Nyongo', Mr Najib Balala and Mr Ochillo Ayacko.
The President sacked the Orange Cabinet and assistant ministers in the reshuffle that followed Banana's debacle in the public poll.
Today's workshop, which is sponsored by the Parliamentary Service Commission, the World Bank and the Canadian International Development Agency, will tackle the democratisation of budgeting and the Constituency Development Fund and offices.
Deputy Speaker David Musila will chair the session on democratising budgeting, while the introduction will be done by Finance minister Amos Kimunya and the chairman of the Parliamentary Finance Committee, Dr Oburu Oginga.

Original_Search_Title: 'Standard' Computers At KICC
Headline: Kenya: 'Standard' Computers At KICC
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Some of the computers impounded from The Standard offices by hooded
policemen last week are in Government offices at the Kenyatta
International Conference Centre, Nairobi.
A senior security official was spotted carrying two of the computer
processing units to the offices in the company of a white man. Heavily
built security officers accompanied the two.
Soon after they took the CPUs to KICC, National Security minister
John Michuki announced that the Government had retrieved "serious"
information from the gadgets. KICC is a Government institution that
houses several offices among them that of Government spokesman, Alfred
Mutua.
Close to 60 computer parts and transmitters were on Wednesday night
driven out of the Nairobi provincial headquarters to Banana on the
outskirts of the city. It is believed that the gadgets are being
manipulated at the KICC offices before police announce the results as
promised by Michuki. The whereabouts of mobile phones stolen from
employees were yet to be known.
Senior police officers refused to comment on the issue. "The
incident is beyond our reach and comment and you better ask Michuki
himself," said one officer. Several 'Standard' employees, who were on
duty when the policemen raided the company premises, were robbed of
mobile phones and other valuables.
Unlike the computers, no inventory was made of the phones. The
computers were loaded onto a waiting car and taken to Nairobi area
police headquarters.
On Friday, a senior security officer who had earlier attended a
daylong meeting at Harambee House took them to KICC. Sources said
computer experts were examining the CPUs in a bid to "get subversive
information".
The Standard Group Board has denied the minister's claims that the computers had subversive information.
Hooded policemen took away the computers from The Standard
headquarters at the I&M Bank Tower and Likoni printing press in a
raid that has attracted condemnation.

Original_Search_Title: Will House Tackle the Crises That Bedevil the Country?
Headline: Kenya: Will House Tackle the Crises That Bedevil the Country?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When President Kibaki announced the date for the re-opening of
parliament yesterday, a day after the raid on the Fourth Estate, his
aides wanted him to show one thing: that he is in total control.
Although "polishing of the mace" was given as the excuse to delay
the opening of Parliament, Kibaki did not want to be seen to be taking
orders from the Orange Democratic Movement that had been demanding the
re-opening of the House to deal with emerging national issues.
But neither the loss of the Referendum vote nor the Anglo Leasing
scandal moved the president to action. Actually, when the Orange
Democratic Movement (ODM) members threatened to storm parliament by
February 14 Kibaki just laughed it off.
The current constitution gives Kibaki powers to "summon, prorogue and dissolve Parliament".
According to the Constitution "a period of 12 months shall not
intervene between the last sitting in one session and the first sitting
thereof". It is a clause that Kibaki has used at the moment to delay
the calling back of Parliament.
But with the date now fixed, all eyes will be on the business of the
House which will include the budget speech by the new minister of
Finance, Amos Kimunya.
Parliament also returns with more backbenchers from both NAK and the
Liberal Democratic Party - part of the fallout over the Orange-Banana
campaign and the Anglo-Leasing saga.
Delayed twice, Parliament went on recess as the country prepared for
the crucial Referendum and reopens when everyone wants to see how
farther war on corruption will run now that investigations on Anglo
Leasing, Goldenberg and Ndung'u Report are the main focus.
With some of the Kibaki supporters - Dr Chris Murungaru, David
Mwiraria and Kiraitu Murungi - pushed to the backbench it will be
interesting to see them join Raila Odinga, Anyang Nyong'o and Kalonzo
Musyoka in the back rows. But focus will be on the Leader of the
Official, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta, to see how much he will be able to keep
the Kibaki government on its toes.
Opening at a time there is an outrage over Government excesses in
various sectors, this will no doubt be one of the most lively sessions
of the August House and there might be attempts to pass a vote of no
confidence on the Government from the floor of the House.
Actually, that has been one of the main fears inside State House
where Kibaki's inner circle wanted him to reopen Parliament when
post-referendum tempers cooled. But as the tempers subsided, the
country faced the Githongo Revelations that forced Finance minister,
Mwiraria, George Saitoti (Education) and Kiraitu Murungi (Energy).
Again focus will be on the President's speech on March 21 and the
debate that will follow. Will Parliament be able to handle the crisis
that has bedecked the nation? Only time will tell but this will be the
most interesting session since Narc came to power.

Original_Search_Title: Open Letter to Parliament, Kufuor & Rawlings
Headline: Ghana: Open Letter to Parliament, Kufuor & Rawlings
Body_Text:
Accra — 12 practical steps for job creation & development of Ghana
In theory Ghana has NO EXCUSES for not developing. Similar rhetoric
was sounded recently to the govt. by the Chairman of the World Bank.
The desire among Ghanaians at home and Diaspora to lift this nation
from developing to full develop state is now a PASSION. A passion that
is similar or greater than during her independence nearly 50 years ago.
This nation MUST & SHALL rise again and lead Africa.
"We are the BEST and nothing less is good enough for Ghana". Use these words as Mantra and remember it from dusk to dawn.
Here are some practical steps used for nation development since the industrial revolution. Start building now.
1. Hyper modern hospitals in at least 6 regional capitals.
The present hospitals in Ghana dates back to colonial days and early
years of independence, and would not meet the nations need for
healthcare and the challenges of the next 25years. Building new
hospitals would help reduce so called "Brain Drain" of Ghanaian doctors
and medical personnel.
These professionals love their country just like everybody else, but
lack of challenging tasks and facilities compels them to look for
greener pastures. Job creation during building phase of new hospitals
estimated at 2000 new jobs. After completion 5000-7000 new jobs.
2. New harbours are urgently needed in Tema, Takoradi, Cape Coast, and Keta.
The new harbours in Tema and Takoradi shall be the hubs and
locomotive for national industrial development, but also serving partly
as new naval base for defence & security of the nation. The new
harbours in Cape Coast and Keta shall be Ghana's future Oil & Gas
terminals and offshore activities. Segment of these harbours shall be
cruise / tourist ship harbours. Job creation during building phase 5000
new jobs. Estimated 10,000 new jobs when completed.
3. New dry-docks for shipbuilding & ship maintenance.
Ghana must start building ships. Every coastal nation with respect
for herself must be able to build ships both for transportation,
defence, protection of national resources and territorial waters.
Governments must endeavour to find foreign partners for Shipbuilding
under license or as Joint Ventures. Ghana need VLCC (Very Large Crude
Carrier) size dry-docks. South Africa and Egypt are the only countries
building ships on the continent. The same countries and the Canary
Islands are the only ones with ship maintenance yards with decent
dry-docks. Job creation during building phase 2000 new jobs. Estimated
7000 new jobs when completed.
4. New airports, aeronautics & space technology infrastructures.
New international world class airports in Accra, Kumasi, Takoradi,
Ho, Cape Coast, Sunyani, Tamale, and Bolgatanga are urgently needed for
balanced industrial development and national defence (Air force base).
The Air force segment MUST immediately enter into Aircraft and Space
Technology building program. Ghanaians co-developed the Mars spacecraft
in 10-15 years they can build the first aircraft. Ghana Air force would
love this challenge. Set high goal and it would be achieve. In joint
venture with General Electric, Boing, Airbus, Rolls Royce, etc. Job
creation during building phase 5000 new jobs. Estimated 20,000 new jobs
when completed.
5. Electronics, telecommunication & information technology.
Ghana MUST enter the Electronics Industry with full strength, as
Joint Ventures or License Manufacturer. It is time to make Ghana's own
Brand Televisions, Radio, Computers & Electronics components.
Telecommunications systems & coverage in Ghana are still at
mediocre level as compared with countries Ghana wish to be compared
with.
Communication brings understanding and Telecommunication is the life
line of every developed nation. NO nation can develop today without
Telecommunication & Information Technology. In fact, every primary
school pupil MUST receive FREE computer machine & printer from the
government. This MUST be chiselled in the constitution of Ghana.
Furthermore, there shall be ZERO tax on computer in Ghana. Government
& Private institutions should be able to grant soft loans to
employees to acquire personal computers (PCs). The end result of this
program would bring immeasurable reward to Ghana's socio-economical
development.
Estimated 1,500,000 new jobs in 5 years.
6. Oil & gas, future energy source.
It's a FACT that, Ghana has hydrocarbons Oil & Gas deposit, and
sooner or later it would be exploited. Keta basin and Saltpond / Half
Assini offshore are the most promising. If infrastructure mentioned
earlier are not in place in the next 3-5 years, then Ghana would be a
poor oil-producing nation. Ghana MUST participate as equal partners at
all levels. Both in funding and technology. In order to develop, Ghana
should no longer give priority to selling Kente, Art & Craft and
pineapple. That is for banana republics. Ghana must join the REAL
actors in Oil & Gas, BIO-ENERGY (garbage convert to energy), WIND
ENERGY, SOLAR ENERGY, HOT WATER from simple ground pumps development.
Estimated 2,000 new jobs when completed. On long term the figure could
reach 200,000 new jobs.
7. Education & research centres.
Thanks to the vision of Dr. Nkrumah, Former President Jerry
Rawlings, President John Kufuor and individual efforts by educators,
Ghanaians have excelled in education. This vision must be kept alive
and focused toward the development of Ghana. I call for a New Education
System Of Ghana (NESOG), it MUST start now and begin in the
kindergarten (Free school buses is definitely a good investment, also,
drivers should be professionals to prevent accidents). It must be put
together by some of the finest educators available in and outside the
country. And it should be equivalent or better than the best in the
world. We all are aware that Knowledge is a highly priced commodity in
the free economic market. Smart brains no longer constrain to national
boundaries and certainly not patriotic until a nation is prepared to
pay the price to keep it. Ghana must be a KNOWLEDGE BASED SOCIETY WITH
LATERAL EDUCATION SYSTEM. Ghana's resources such as bauxite, diamonds,
gold, timber would NOT last forever, but Ghana's knowledge SHALL
prevail. Furthermore, to reduce the migration of scholars, Ghana must,
as a matter of urgency, set aside 2%-4% of her Gross National Product
(GNP) for Research & Development (R&D) in Nation building,
Defence & Security, Industry, Health & Social welfare,
Environmental protection, National & International affairs.
Most research institutions could be located near universities and in regions where job creation is low.
These would create easy recruitment and balanced Development and
create 1500 new jobs. On long term the figure could reach 150,000 new
jobs in 5 years.
8. Tourism is the new breadbasket of Ghana.
The potentials of this dormant industry are enormous. Revenue from
tourism alone can finance 40% of all projects mentioned in item 1-7
& 9. But, one would ask do we have tourism in Ghana? Not really.
Tourism in Ghana is still in the cradle stage and is not well
organised. Much more have to be done and urgently to tap the potential
available for Ghana.
Project "JOSEPH" is a great idea, but Ghana does not have the
capacity and Infrastructure at this time to welcome the Diaspora.
Remember, the Diaspora is approx. half a billion strong, sophisticated,
with plenty of resources. Fifty percent live in developed countries,
are well informed and would not accept any lesser standard of living.
Imagine if one percent of this group (5 millions) visit Ghana in a year
accommodations and infrastructure would implode.
However, by encouraging more private enterprises and foreign Joint
Ventures through tax reduction and facilitating Land acquisition and
re-allocation for tourism, this challenge can be managed and create an
estimated 500.000 new jobs in 5 years.
9. Personal identification number.
Fourteen years ago I proposed the Personal Number or Personal
Identification Number concept to journalists in Ghana, this concept is
yet to be implemented. A nation with no proper tools for Accountability
& Statistics is doomed to economic stagnation. Auditors, Bankers,
Statistians, Economits, Legislators, etc. are struggling due to lacks
of correct tools & figures to calculate their projections.
Example, ask Electoral commission or government, how many voters do
we have by 2008 elections? And you are sure nobody has a clue!! Is it
strange that parties voters complain of fraud? There is simply no data
to confirm the number. With PIN, election that takes days to call would
only take hours since the system is computerised. If you ask, how many
babies are born in Ghana a day? How many people die a month, how many
houses are in the country, how many beds we have in our hospitals, how
many cars traffic our roads, how many persons evade tax per month etc,
then you realise the importance of Personal Identification Number.
Ghana can earn at least two Billion US Dollars per year in revenue by
implementing this concept. Every bank account and transaction would be
tracked and checked by auditors. All Taxations would be registered and
monitored, but also excess tax would be returned to the rightful
owners. You do not need to import 500 cars / vans and pay thousands of
voter's reg istration personnel to get your voters registered. You
simply send voters card to their mailbox or nearest post office for
pick up on showing their Personal Identification Number. Every citizen
gets Personal Identification Number at birth at the hospital and
confirmed by National Register at City Hall or Town Council or Police
station. Get your passport in ONE HOUR, Register a Company in five
days, Clear your car in 45 minutes, and receive your pension at home
and many more. ALL this by showing or saying your Personal
Identification Number (guided by your PIN-code of 11 Digits, consisting
of your date of Birth, Gender and PIN). Estimated new jobs 50,000.
10. Forty five percent (45%) women in government & managerial positions.
Despite the fact that women outnumber men in Ghana, all political
parties including NPP, NDC, and CPP are still dominated by men. Women
make only 20% in parliament, 10% in government and 5% in top managerial
jobs in the country. This MUST change!!! Any political party which does
not clearly chisel this in their agenda and or have at least 45% women
representation in their candidate lists should be considered women
discriminatory and should face the consequences thereafter. Women are
core values of any developed nation and their role should not be under
estimated. Estimated new jobs are UNLIMITED. Ghana MUST be the first
country in Africa to present a government consisting of 45% women. And
soon the second woman president in Africa.
11. Tiles on all regional capitals pavements.
Yes you've heard me right, tiles on ALL regional capitals pavements.
"Eliminate the dust, smog & bring clear & cleaner air to the
cities" This would NOT only save Ghana $10 billion in 5 years in
Health, Safety & Environment, but also bring about thousands of
jobs. Former Night soil carriers can be re-trained to lay tiles.
Thousands of several unemployed can get jobs. The dividend are
enormous, you get Cleaner cities easy to maintain, reduces air
pollution in inner cities, reduced flooding during raining seasons,
cleaner air, good for tourists and city dwellers. Project duration
& costs USD$500Million in 5-15 years. Created jobs 50,000.
12. Ghana finance & world trade centre.
Ghana's Finance & Business centres need be in line with the
developed world. Ghana Wall Street would boost the Ghana Stock Exchange
(GSE).
Offshore Banking is a step in right direction. If Monaco, a state
smaller in size than Aflao or Nungua is Financially richer than Ghana,
then Offshore Banking and Free trade Zone are some issues to address.
How do we finance these 12 practical steps?
Tourism - 40%
Private Investments - 40%
Loans (IMF, WORLD BANK, ADB, ARAB BANK, OTHERS) - 20%
Keep hope alive!!!!
Jordan Eric Mathia,VP. Marketing & Sales Marine Ventures Inc. Norway

Original_Search_Title: 'It's Policy Continuity Vs Regime Change'
Headline: Nigeria: 'It's Policy Continuity Vs Regime Change'
Body_Text:
Lagos — If democracy is the lifeblood of the nation, then the National Assembly, which makes laws for its sustenance, is its red corpuscles. Although there has been an attempt by the House of Representatives to stand to be counted on the side of the people, there's been much pessimism about the upper legislative chamber in recent times: too much, perhaps, judging by the recent evidence that it has quietly started to do better than most people expected.
This cynicism is now being threatened by the Senate's unassuming posture over the third term plot. Without confirmation yet from President Olusegun Obasanjo, the plot is already doing a great deal of damage, and its main casualty may be the Senate. Interestingly, Senate President Ken Nnamani, is at the middle of the imminent storm. Hard times make for bad judgment, Ntai Bagshaw writes, and Nnamani and Speaker Aminu Bello Masari must carefully weigh the options to keep their heads above water.
Fear seems to be the dominant mood of the moment. Attacks by Niger Delta militants, massacres by religious bigots, extra judicial killings by policemen, and the bird flu scare have brought with them appalling scenes of devastation, death and suffering. Now even bickerings over the alleged third term plot is a treat. Many fear that the just-ended constitutional review debates across the nation could be carrying a 'virus,' which might soon kill hundreds of Nigerians.
Since Nigerians began coming down hard on the plot to extend the tenure of elected public office holders beyond 2007, it is hardly surprising that the Senate is scrambling to redeem its weary image. After the rowdy session witnessed in the Upper House Wednesday this week, not a few senators have resolved to 'fly at half mast,' except, of course, Senator Idris Kuta. Kuta, it was, who chastised his colleagues for failing to halt the constitution review sittings while the nation was being engulfed by the cartoon protests. Describing the exercise was "a waste of time," he had lashed out at its protagonists, terming them as people, who were desperate to achieve their goal at all cost. Already, there are strong allegations that monies have changed hands in the Senate to push the third term agenda through.
ThisDay gathers that since the rowdy session at the Senate, pockets of meetings have been holding to deliberate over the best way to treat the third term issue. When this reporter called Senate President Ken Nnamani this week, he declined to speak on the matter, pleading that he was "in a meeting." But a source reveals that Nnamani is no longer disposed to commenting on the third term issue "because he has been misquoted by the press and misunderstood by Nigerians."
When contacted, Reginald Okochi, his Special Adviser on Media, agrees. Okochi told ThisDay that Nnamani has nothing new to say on the matter. "The Senate President has already made his position clear," he says. "When the Senate reconvened after recess early this year, he decried the fever over the third term issue, asserting that continuity of regime is not the same as continuity of policies and programmes of government. He just presented a case. That does not mean he is for third term." Nnamani's statement on the third term issue is neither here nor there. Though describing regime change as ingredients of participatory democracy, he has failed to put down his foot on the constitutional provisions that concern the President's tenure, passing the buck instead to Nigerians.
Undoubtedly, Obasanjo has done appreciably well in moving the economy towards sustained growth. His policies have won the hearts of even the nation's most daring creditors. Yet, like Nnamani would likely (but dare not) ask, "Is there none amongst 140 million Nigerians to continue the 'good works' of the President?" The Senate President has every reason to be 'careful'. The office he occupies seems to be perpetually plagued by the 'banana peel' bug. Since 1999, the country has witnessed five Senate Presidents come and go. Little wonder Nnamani's statements on the third term issue have been rather watery lately. "I alone as Senate President cannot amend or review the constitution," he is wont to say. "It would take a constitutional process with input from all Nigerians to do that."
It is this process that Deputy Senate President Ibrahim Mantu is now spearheading. Is setting up the Mantu-led committee justified? Or are politicians simply helping to feed public panic, and then covering themselves by promising to stand against a threat which may never materialise? To ask these questions is not to counsel complacency, but to apply the kind of test, which is required in any kind of disaster planning. With the media full of warnings of impending third term plot, an overreaction is fast gathering storm.
It is clear that the coming weeks will be frustrating. A sceptic might point out that measures which sound plausible in the midst of general public alarm can turn out to be counterproductive. So far, there is less known of the scale or likelihood of the threat. In what smacks of cheap power play, Obasanjo is yet to confirm or deny the third term allegation. A few weeks back, when South Africa's ruling party, ANC, tipped President Thabo Mbeki to run for another term in office, Mbeki was quick to discard the statement, saying he would leave office after the expiration of his term in 2009. That action has saved South Africans from the brouhaha that is currently enveloping Nigeria.
Many, therefore, hold Obasanjo responsible for the ongoing frenzy. So far, his friends and close associates seem to be doing much of the talking. From Bode George to Rev Father Mathew Kukah, statements confirming and/or refuting the third term plot have not helped matters. The President hasn't even picked a likely successor. And the polls only a whiff away.
All the same, some sceptics say the threat may fizzle out in the coming weeks. Others do not think so. They say it could be much wiser to wait. Like IBB would put it, "Nobody knows who will be alive by May 2007, so let's subject our fears to God." These are all legitimate points, and there is undeniably a lot of uncertainty about the matter at stake. It is true that governments, companies and individuals regularly have no choice but to plan situations of uncertainty. The trick is to do it in a way that is rational and proportionate. This is what is needed now. Why? Because the unpalatable truth is that if findings of the constitutional review sittings (due out next week) favour the third term bid, there will probably not be enough time for Nigerians to stop its agenda. Panic about the third term plot will do no good, but could do a lot of harm.
Forget the uncanny greed of kleptomaniac governors supporting an extension of the President's tenure in office. The best test of a democracy is the freedom of its people to choose their leaders. And no matter what, nobody can that that right away from the people. The good news is that many Nigerians realise that things cannot go on as they are, even though some are loth to do anything about it.
Nnamani's next steps
These are dire times for Nnamani. Mindful of his office, Nigerians are nervously watching his every move just to catch a better glimpse of this stand on the matter. Fascinatingly, an uneasy calm has settled over the Senate, with the 'distinguished' senators opting to keep mum over the third term issue. Nnamani shouldn't be jittery about this. The main lesson of the continuing frenzy is that for the first time Nigerians are arguing more about the future than the past. The mere fact that Nigerians are decisive is something that should give the Senate President something to cheer about. Yet, the task before him is arduous - one that goes against his ambivalent posture. He should carefully hoard his reputation, especially now that he is trying to establish it. He must protect it strictly, anticipating all attacks on it, for as the Chinese puts it, "When the tree falls, the monkeys scatter."
There is nothing in the world more powerful than an idea. No weapon can destroy it; no power can conquer it, except the power of another idea. History beckons. Nnamani must champion the anti-third term idea if he is to quash that of its spin doctors.

Original_Search_Title: Bean Cake Seller, Six Others Die in Petrol Tanker Fire
Headline: Nigeria: Bean Cake Seller, Six Others Die in Petrol Tanker Fire
Body_Text:
It was all tears in Elele, Ikwere local government area of Rivers state when a speeding petrol tanker skidded off the road, rammed into structures along the road and burst into flames roasting a middle aged woman who sells Bean cakes, locally known as akara by the road side and six others including motorcycle (Okada) operators and hawkers.
Daily Trust gathered that the petrol tanker which was coming from Port Harcourt, was on its way to Owerri when the accident occurred. A witness said the tanker was on top speed and as it tried to negotiate a bend, it skidded off the road and immediately, there was an explosion and the vehicle burst into flames. The woman, who was very popular for her tasty akara among the local people was on her daily routine of frying in the morning when the fuel from the tanker caught up with her own akara fire and roasted her alive. Some motorcycle operators who were patroni-sing the bean cake woman and others who were scouting for passengers were also roasted alive while some hawkers who were standing by the road-side with trays of oranges and banana also suffered the same fate.
The fire service said three of its men who arrived the sc-ene were wounded and were rushed to the Madonna hos-pital owned by the Catholic Church in the area. Our rep-orter also gathered from the police that about seven other people who sustained various degrees of injuries were receiving treatment at the hospital.
The police public relations officer in the state, DSP Ireju Barasua, who confirmed the incident, said it occurred at about 9:30 am. She advised hawkers to learn from the incident and make sure that they do their business away from the road. She also called on roadside sellers to relocate their shops to a safer place away from the road as that could be very dangerous in the event of any incident.
The local government chairman, Hon. Kerian Wo-bodo, who visited the scene, described the accident as the worst to hit the area in recent times and called on the auth-orities to intervene to reduce carnage on the Elele highway. He said the rising level of accidents on that axis and the attendant loss of lives and properties worries the comm-unity.
He offered to foot part of the bill to be incurred by those injured and commise-rated with the bereaved families.
In a related development, a disaster hit the marine tran-sport system in Bayelsa state yesterday as a passenger ferryboat was hit by thunder and its contents emptied into the river. Daily Trust gathered that the boat which was loa-ded with an unspecified num-ber of passengers, was going to Ekowe from Yenogao, the state capital.
The driver had berthed at Otuokpoli waterfront in Ogbia local government area to discharge some passe-ngers. It was while it was leaving the bay to continue the journey that a lightening followed by thunder struck the boat and scattered its contents. Five people were injured in the incident while other occupants swarm ashore safely. As at the time of filing this report, our reporter gathered that the boat driver was still missing and is feared dead. Men of the Bayelsa state taskforce on Marine, we gathered, were on hand to rescue the passengers but have not been able to locate the boat driver.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Makes Triumphant Return to Kampala City
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Makes Triumphant Return to Kampala City
Body_Text:
Kampala — Thousands of Kampala residents yesterday treated President Yoweri
Museveni and wife, Janet to a triumphant welcome back to the city from
western Uganda where they went to vote last week.
Dancing and chanting, people bedecked in the NRM party colours and
dry banana leaves escorted Museveni from Nsangi Trading Centre on the
Kampala-Masaka highway to the State House gate where they urged him to
have a fourth term in office.
Yesterday's hero's welcome accorded to Museveni in the city
contrasted with his unimpressive performance in Kampala in the February
23 presidential elections where his main rival, Dr Kizza Besigye polled
highest.
With sweat flowing freely from his face and that of his wife, who
has just won the Ruhaama parliamentary seat, Museveni took nine hours
to travel from his upcountry home in Rwakitura, Kiruhura district to
State House, Nakasero after a record 38 stopovers, a journey that
usually takes him three hours.
"I greet you all for supporting the Movement. We went through the
elections and won" Museveni said when he reached Kyengera Trading
Centre near Kampala at 5.09p.m.
"The other day when I passed here those who did not know the Movement was strong thought it had run out of batteries," he said.
"Therefore those of you in Kampala who spoilt your votes and gave
them to FDC should return them to the Movement because no one in Uganda
can defeat us."
Museveni went on: "I would like to thank the 170,000 who voted the
Movement in Kampala and the 125,000 in Wakiso, thanks for joining the
winning team," drawing defeaning cheers from the crowd.
The President said he would woo all those who abandoned him and voted for the FDC to return to the Movement.
At the last stopover at the State House gate, Museveni described his
victory as the sixth major battle he had been engaged in since the
1981-1986 war that ushered him into power.
He promised Kampala residents that his next government will ensure that the city is transformed and made work-friendly.
The Uganda Taxi Operators and Drivers Association (Utoda)
supplemented the police in controlling both human and motor traffic
along the route that had been jammed for much of the early afternoon.
The President said he will hold a celebratory rally at Kololo
Independence grounds this Saturday where he intends to go into details
of what his next government will do for the country.

Original_Search_Title: New Review Panel Must Be Disbanded
Headline: Kenya: New Review Panel Must Be Disbanded
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When reacting to the chorus of outrage that greeted last weekend's unilateral appointment by President Kibaki of a non-inclusive panel of experts to chart the way forward for a new constitution, Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua asserted that the Government "cannot be consulting on everything it wants to undertake".
Lumping one of the nation's most solemn responsibilities along with the thousands of matters the Government attends to shows yet again that the President's team has still not understood, despite the humiliating defeat it suffered in the referendum, the importance Kenyans attach to the need for a constitution they can support. Nor has the President's team learned that it is not a government project.
A new constitution, which will be acceptable to the people, requires the fullest participation of all the key stakeholders and most importantly the parliamentary political parties. This participatory approach, as enshrined in the Bomas exercise, was abandoned when the Government decided to try to push through a constitution with narrow partisan goals last year.
Consensus among key players
When Mr Raila Odinga met with President Kibaki in December and proposed a meeting to discuss the way forward on the Constitution, he agreed that we would meet with our teams so as to reach consensus among all the key players. At that planned meeting, we were to discuss, among other things, the fundamental principles, the process and the road map to the new constitution. But in a clear indication of how little interest there is in moving forward through consensus rather than through unilateral fiat, the planned meeting with the President was twice postponed by State House and then cancelled altogether.
The kind of consultation that the President had seemingly agreed on is an absolute prerequisite for a smooth and healing process required to ensure the passage of a new constitution, which will unite Kenyans and lay the foundation for meeting the nation's and the people's most pressing needs. This new round of constitution-making must be a transparent and inclusive national exercise, which from the outset will enjoy bipartisan support. Why is it being begun so secretly?
The formation of a new team of experts without consultations and in secrecy is unacceptable and must be rescinded. Who chose the group? Who vetted these experts? What are their credentials for this exercise? Are they indeed all experts? Are they going to try to revive the rejected constitution through some cosmetic changes? Most important, is such a panel necessary?
Appointing partisan allies to shepherd the process will guarantee yet another major political disaster for a nation that has been subjected to too many in the last three years. And in any event, why should taxpayers bear the expensive burden for a group that will support Banana interests? Unless it is formally accepted that this is a Banana advisory team, the team unilaterally appointed by the President must be disbanded and replaced by one based on consultation with the stakeholders.
Before any new steps are taken on the Constitution, we must all sit down and decide how we should proceed. Do we need a constituent assembly for the exercise? Do we need legal and technical experts to guide the process? Is another referendum required for formalising final approval?
You cannot begin this exercise with a bolt from the blue announcing the appointment of a panel when the way forward has not even been discussed, leave alone agreed on.
The Government seems to have embarked on yet another costly exercise without consulting the key stakeholders of the constitution process. Surely it cannot believe that the vanquished (the Government) can dictate to the victors (the people and their representatives, the ODM) how the new attempt to craft a constitution should proceed.
We must begin the process for crafting the new constitution by ensuring that there is goodwill among all stakeholders so that the new exercise is not seen as an attempt by one side to secure partisan interests. We indicated in our very first statement after our referendum victory in November that we were willing to co-operate with the Government on the crafting of a new constitution. ODM still stands ready to do the same.
Let us also categorically state, for the benefit of doubting Thomases, that the ODM is as united, cohesive and determined to spearhead the cause of a people-driven Constitution to a successful conclusion as it was during the constitution referendum.
Government's lack of action
We would like to take this opportunity to announce that the ODM is in the process of appointing its own team of technical experts to advise us on how to achieve a constitution, which will fulfil our people's long-suppressed aspirations for a more productive, more inclusive and more harmonious life.
In that regard, we must condemn the Government's lack of action to proceed forcefully in prosecuting those who are known to have been behind the Anglo Leasing and other mega scandals that those in power have perpetrated against the people of Kenya. Indeed, we wonder if this new constitution panel is one of the many tactics designed to divert attention from these scandals and buy time for the Government until the next election.
We are in any event convinced that the existing investigative, prosecutorial and judicial mechanisms are comprehensively compromised by those who currently wield power. They will never deliver justice because most of those who lead these institutions were appointed by those who are under investigation and are in fact their close cronies.
Parliament is in fact the only institution which has the authority and credibility to investigate a case in which major figures of the Kibaki administration are named.
Mr Lone is a media consultant.

Original_Search_Title: First the Floods, Now No Fuel!
Headline: Namibia: First the Floods, Now No Fuel!
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Motorists using the main south transport corridor that links Namibia to South Africa were not only affected by the inaccessible roads during the weekend, but were yesterday faced with another predicament - lack of fuel.
Operations Manager for BP Namibia in Mariental Baans Tsumib confirmed to New Era that the two main Engen service stations at the town were closed.
He said his office might only open the service stations tomorrow when the level of floodwater is expected to go down. "We are ready to supply as soon as the water dries up, hopefully by Wednesday," he said.
Faced with the same situation, Regional Manager for Shell Company Etienne Delport stated that though the service stations in Mariental have fuel, it is impossible to pump because the hand-pumps are submerged in water.
The opening of the service stations would not only depend on the water levels going down but also on tests that will decide whether or not the fuel is contaminated.
The tarred road passing Mariental has been under water since the town was flooded and is now accessible. An engineer with the Roads Authority said although the road was still under water, it was now open to traffic.
Yesterday, the alternative road to Mariental, which passes through Gibeon, Gochas and Stampriet, was also temporarily closed due to a truck that had overturned and blocked the road.
The truck has since been towed away and there is no hold up anymore.
Yesterday morning, King Quentin, Managing Director of Taeuber and Corssen (pty) Ltd, a company that supplies chicken and cheese to most supermarkets in the country including Pick 'n' Pay, indicated that four of their delivery trucks were stranded between Kalkrand and Rehoboth due to lack of fuel.
"We hope to get to Rehoboth because in Kalkrand, there is no diesel." His fears were that the four are refrigerated trucks and if they could not make it to Windhoek, the perishable goods, 60 tonnes of chicken and 20 tonnes of cheese, would go bad. Should that happen, the loss would run into thousands of dollars, he told New Era. The trucks were expected in Windhoek on Sunday morning for offloading. However, the delay did not have a negative impact on the local supply, as there was sufficient stock, King stated.
The manager of South African chain shops such as Fruit & Veg City that operates in the capital and the town of Swakopmund, Leon Nel told New Era that nine trucks loaded with fresh vegetables and fruits were delayed by one day because of the Mariental floods.
Though not to a large extent, this inconvenienced some customers who might have wanted to purchase fresh vegetables and fruits such as grapes, pears, peaches, and bananas, which cannot be obtained locally.
"Our customers would have had fresh veggies and fruits in the morning today but we will have a day without certain stock," he said.
While this is the case, there is an alternative route, the Trans Kalahari Corridor (TKC), which is not only faster but saves costs, says the Walvis Bay Corridor Group (WBCG).
For goods emanating from the Gauteng area especially, WBCG Business Development Officer Stella Auala says, the TKC makes more business sense.
"When we are talking about time and costs, it's an alternative route," she added.
Although the corridor group has been conducting awareness campaigns to make people utilise the route, it has been a challenge to change people's mindsets.
"We have marketed it as an alternative but people stick to the same route," she said.

Original_Search_Title: New Review Panel Must Be Disbanded
Headline: Kenya: New Review Panel Must Be Disbanded
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When reacting to the chorus of outrage that greeted last weekend's unilateral appointment by President Kibaki of a non-inclusive panel of experts to chart the way forward for a new constitution, Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua asserted that the Government "cannot be consulting on everything it wants to undertake".
Lumping one of the nation's most solemn responsibilities along with the thousands of matters the Government attends to shows yet again that the President's team has still not understood, despite the humiliating defeat it suffered in the referendum, the importance Kenyans attach to the need for a constitution they can support. Nor has the President's team learned that it is not a "government project".
A new constitution which will be acceptable to the people requires the fullest participation of all the key stakeholders and most importantly the parliamentary political parties. This participatory approach, as enshrined in the Bomas exercise, was abandoned when the Government decided to try to push through a constitution with narrow partisan goals last year.
Consensus among key players
When Mr Raila Odinga met with President Kibaki in December and proposed a meeting to discuss the way forward on the Constitution, he agreed that we would meet with our teams so as to reach consensus among all the key players. At that planned meeting, we were to discuss, among other things, the fundamental principles, the process and the road map to the new constitution. But in a clear indication of how little interest there is in moving forward through consensus rather than through unilateral fiat, the planned meeting with the President was twice postponed by State House and then cancelled altogether.
The kind of consultation that the President had seemingly agreed on is an absolute prerequisite for a smooth and healing process required to ensure the passage of a new constitution which will unite Kenyans and lay the foundation for meeting the nation's and the people's most pressing needs. This new round of constitution-making must be a transparent and inclusive national exercise which from the outset will enjoy bipartisan support. Why is it being begun in such a secretive?
The formation of a new team of experts without consultations and in secrecy is unacceptable and must be rescinded. Who chose the group? Who vetted these experts? What are their credentials for this exercise? Are they indeed all experts? Are they going to try to revive the rejected constitution through some cosmetic changes? Most important, is such a panel necessary?
Appointing partisan allies to shepherd the process will guarantee yet another major political disaster for a nation that has been subjected to too many in the last three years. And in any event, why should taxpayers bear the expensive burden for a group that will support Banana interests? Unless it is formally accepted that this is a Banana advisory team, the team unilaterally appointed by the President must be disbanded and replaced by one based on consultation with the stakeholders.
Before any new steps are taken on the Constitution, we must all sit down and decide how we should proceed. Do we need a constituent assembly for the exercise? Do we need legal and technical experts to guide the process? Is another referendum required for formalising final approval?
You cannot begin this exercise with a bolt from the blue announcing the appointment of a panel when the way forward has not even been discussed, leave alone agreed on.
The Government seems to have embarked on yet another costly exercise without consulting the key stakeholders of the constitution process. Surely it cannot believe that the vanquished (the Government) can dictate to the victors (the people and their representatives, the ODM) how the new attempt to craft a constitution should proceed.
We must begin the process for crafting the new constitution by ensuring that there is goodwill among all stakeholders so that the new exercise is not seen as an attempt by one side to secure partisan interests. We indicated in our very first statement after our referendum victory in November that we were willing to co-operate with the Government on the crafting of a new constitution. ODM still stands ready to do the same.
Let us also categorically state, for the benefit of doubting Thomases, that the ODM is as united, cohesive and determined to spearhead the cause of a people-driven Constitution to a successful conclusion as it was during the constitution referendum.
Government's lack of action
We would like to take this opportunity to announce that the ODM is in the process of appointing its own team of technical experts to advise us on how to achieve a constitution which will fulfil our people's long-suppressed aspirations for a more productive, more inclusive and more harmonious life.
In that regard, we must condemn the Government's lack of action to proceed forcefully in prosecuting those who are known to have been behind the Anglo Leasing and other mega scandals that those in power have perpetrated against the people of Kenya. Indeed, we wonder if this new constitution panel is one of the many tactics designed to divert attention from these scandals and buy time for the Government until the next election.
We are in any event convinced that the existing investigative, prosecutorial and judicial mechanisms are comprehensively compromised by those who currently wield power. They will never deliver justice because most of those who lead these institutions were appointed by those who are under investigation and are in fact their close cronies.
Parliament is in fact the only institution which has the authority and credibility to investigate a case which implicates all the major figures of the Kibaki administration.
Mr Lone is a media consultant.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Issues Threat On New Review Committee
Headline: Kenya: Orange Issues Threat On New Review Committee
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement has rejected a panel of experts
appointed by President Kibaki to chart the way forward in the quest for
a new constitution - and promptly set itself on a collision course with
the Government.
Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua wasted no
time telling off politicians antagonising the Eminent Persons
Committee, even as reactions to the President's Thursday move continued
to pour in.
Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua (second right) at the
ACK Mugumo Church in her Gichugu Constituency on Sunday. Pic by Moses
Omusula
In a warning shot to the Government, ODM asserted that the panel's
decision would be rejected the same way the Wako Draft was defeated at
last year's November 21 referendum on the proposed constitution.
And ODM announced that it would come up with a parallel panel to
counter the one picked by the Head of State, with the possibility that
the review could again be transacted in an atmosphere of suspicion,
conflict and hostility.
On Sunday, Kanu Secretary-General and ODM spokesman, William Ruto,
demanded that Kibaki disbands the team led by Ambassador Bethwel
Kiplagat and then seek dialogue with stakeholders on the way forward.
He said the Wako Draft was rejected because the window to dialogue between those in power and other stakeholders had been shut.
Divergent views on the way forward
But Karua told those already fighting the Kiplagat-led outfit to give it a chance to carry out the duties it was mandated to do.
She said the team was mandated to only look into thorny issues and
suggest the way forward for the review, adding they would not make the
ultimate decisions.
"We have not yet reached a stage of identifying representatives and
negotiators and ask them to give their views. The committee is only a
basket where Kenyans can give views on the way forward," she said.
Karua said the 15-member committee would help resolve the
contentious issues through suggestions from Kenyans and then help give
the way forward towards conclusion of constitutional review.
Karua said Kenyans had already given divergent views on the way
forward, with some suggesting the formation of a constituent assembly
and others calling for a panel of experts to handle the stalled process.
Speaking at St Mary's Church, Mugumo, in her Gichugu Constituency on
Sunday, Karua told off the committee's critics, saying the Government
could not be consulting on all issues.
"The Government would have decided that views be channelled to my
office or that of the Attorney-General. It cannot be consulting on
everything it wants to undertake," she said.
She defended the committee, saying it was only acting as a facilitator to the collection of views on the way forward.
Constitution-making a national exercise
However, Ruto - who was speaking while campaigning for a Kanu civic
candidate David Kikwai in the forthcoming civic by-election at Kedowa
in Kipkelion, Kericho District - warned that the new panel was headed
for serious problems because it appeared to be yet another "Government
project" and was not inclusive of Kenyans.
"We in ODM are ready for dialogue with the Government over the
review process, but if it insists to have its own way, we will have no
alternative but to come with a parallel panel," Ruto said.
Ruto was accompanied by MPs Dr Sammy Rutto (Kipkelion), Charles
Keter (Belgut), Philip Rotino (Sigor) and Kipsigis County Council
chairman Richard Samoei and his Londiani counterpart, Jackson Kikwai.
Ruto said constitution-making was a national exercise where all
Kenyans regardless of party, tribe, colour, race or religion must have
their views accommodated.
The Wako Draft, Ruto said, was rejected as it did not contain the
views that Kenyans presented to the over 600 Bomas of Kenya delegates.
"Let the Kibaki panel go ahead with the revival of the stalled
constitution and we will beat them the same way we did with the Wako
Draft after the Government failed to dialogue with us," he said.
In Nairobi, the Anglican Church of Kenya (ACK) welcomed the naming of a team of experts to jumpstart the review.
All Saints Cathedral Provost, the Rev Peter Karanja, said it was
only fair to give the President and the new team of experts a chance to
discharge their mandate.
"The President has a right to appoint the review committee, and let
us first wait and see the outcome then we can react on it," Karanja
said after presiding over a church service.
The provost was optimistic the 14-member team comprising mostly
university lecturers would iron out sticky issues and recommend the way
forward.
'Commission has no credibility'
Other members of the committee are Prof Ole Moi Yoi, Dr Kaendi
Munguti, Dr Karuti Kanyinga, Juma Mwachihi, Mwambi Mwasura and Dr
Jacinta Muteshi.
Also in the team are Dr Richard Barasa, Peter Wambura, Dr Nemwel
Nyamwaka Bosire, Prof Ngethe Njuguna, Dr Patricia Kameni Mbote, former
judge Abdul Majid Cockar, Wanza Kioko and Prof Kassim Farah.
Former Cabinet minister William ole Ntimama poured cold water on the
committee, saying it would not succeed because it did not have the
support of majority of Kenyans. Ntimama dismissed the committee as a
ploy by the Banana team to bring back the constitution that gives the
President dictatorial and autocratic powers.
The Narok North MP said the composition of the committee was meant to legitimise the referendum the Banana team lost.
"To me, Kiraitu Murungi (former Justice minister) and Martha Karua
(Justice minister) have been preaching to Kenyans from the pulpit of
arrogance to force Kenyans to accept what I call the Kilifi/Kiraitu
draft," Ntimama said.
He claimed the commission had no credibility and was not supported
by a majority of Kenyans. Ntimama said it was clear to everybody the
commission would just squander taxpayers' money the same way the
Constitution of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC) did.
Public Service minister Moses Akaranga said the team was competent
to deliver a new constitution. He took issue with leaders hell-bent on
criticising Kibaki's leadership, and pointed a finger specifically at
ODM members.
"Based on what we have already experienced in the search for a new
constitution, it is clear that only a group of experts can be able to
write a new constitution," he said.
The minister was addressing worshippers at Kondele Pentecostal Assemblies of God Church in Kisumu.
'Government courting trouble'
The Kiplagat team also got the backing of former Cabinet minister Marsden Madoka.
"We should not dismiss the already appointed team whose
responsibility is to find out why the Government suffered a heavy
defeat at the referendum. Let Kenyans be assured Parliament will decide
the way forward," said Madoka, who is also the Mwatate MP.
In Nakuru, a youth political forum said the new effort to jumpstart the constitution review was an insult to Kenyans.
Members of group said the new initiative was a clear indication the Government was not keen on an all-inclusive process.
Addressing a Press conference at the end of a three-day workshop on
leadership at Merica Hotel, the youth said the committee was not likely
to bring a new constitution for the country.
The workshop drew participants from across the country and was sponsored by German foundation, Friedrich Ebert Stiftung.
