Their spokesmen, Stephen Kihanya and Kepta Ombati, said the
Government had lost the moral authority to spearhead the review after
it was heavily defeated in the last November referendum.
"We are appalled at the way the Government is purporting to handle
the review. It is not long ago when Kenyans resoundingly rejected the
daft constitution," Kihanya said.
Kanu National Youth Leader Badi Ali said the Government was courting
trouble by failing to consult widely before putting the review process
back on track.

Original_Search_Title: Trouble in Orange Democratic Movement
Headline: Kenya: Trouble in Orange Democratic Movement
Body_Text:
Nairobi — As Mwingi North Member of Parliament Kalonzo Musyoka denied
reports that he secretly met President Mwai Kibaki on Thursday last
week, fresh details point to an intense struggle for the Orange
Democratic Movement's leadership.
Blaming the reports directly on his political detractors in ODM,
whom he described as agents working for other people, Kalonzo fell
short of admitting that the group whose unity saw it win the crucial
referendum vote last year was falling apart.
Knowledgeable sources confided in The Sunday Standard that recent
political events in which the leading lights of ODM have been declaring
their candidacy for the 2007 General Election "have everything to do
with alliance-building, with a particular eye on the three million
votes in Rift Valley Province".
On one side of the ODM divide is said to be a "working agreement"
between Raila Odinga and William Ruto, while on the other is an attempt
to build an alliance between Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru
Kenyatta and Kalonzo.
"Really, to me whether Kalonzo went to State House or not is not the
issue. The issue is that the undercurrents of the struggle for
supremacy within ODM are now bursting into the open," the source said.
It is instructive that yesterday Eldoret North MP William Ruto, who
is also the ODM spokesman, reiterated that the movement's nominations
for presidential candidates would have to be competitive.
"ODM will lose some people who have no stand and those who think it
was designed to help their ambitions. But that will make it better. We
will retain people who are willing to compete," Ruto said.
"The era of so and so tosha (enough) is gone both in Kanu and ODM.
Anybody who wants Kanu or ODM nominations must be prepared to compete,"
he added.
ODM is an informal unity between Kanu and the Liberal Democratic
Party formed in the lead-up to the watershed referendum last year to
oppose the then Draft Constitution.
The leading lights of the movement are Raila, Musalia Mudavadi,
Uhuru, Kalonzo, Ruto, Gideon Moi and Najib Balala. All but Mudavadi and
Gideon have declared their candidacy for the 2007 election.
Gideon is said to be backing the alliance between Uhuru and Kalonzo
while Musalia and Balala - close allies to Raila - are said to be
willing to back whoever wins in a fair nomination exercise within ODM.
The two expect to be supported if they are so picked.
The entry of many LDP leaders into the race for the party's
presidential ticket has been drawing a quiet tension between Kalonzo
and his colleagues and have forced him to start looking elsewhere for a
political future.
Kanu and LDP politicians who have held discussions with the Mwingi
North MP say he might not participate in either ODM or LDP nominations,
but has not indicated what his options are.
Even before the birth of ODM, silent tension had been growing between Kalonzo and his colleagues in LDP since early 2004.
The entry of former VP Musalia Mudavadi into LDP is said to have
sent Kalonzo plotting for his survival - outside LDP. By then, Raila
was still silent on his plans, and nobody knew Balala would join the
presidential race.
A meeting Kalonzo held with Kalenjin professionals in April 2004 set
off a chain of events that threw a wedge between the him and his
colleagues in LDP.
But it is the acrimonious debate in the House over the Bomas Draft that pushed suspicions to new levels.
The Government won the crucial vote to alter the Bomas Draft, an
effort that gave birth to the Orange and Banana camps in the November
2005 referendum.
After an eight-hour acrimonious debate in Parliament, the Government
side carried the day with 102 votes against the opposition's 61 with
two abstentions.
Kalonzo's failure to cast the ballot in the House on that day is
said to have cost him some of his most ardent supporters in the party.
Earlier, on the Easter Monday of 2004, Kalonzo had held a
controversial meeting (according to LDP insiders) with 70 Kalenjin
opinion leaders.
Kalonzo met some of the point men from the Kalenjin community,
including Ruben Chesire at the Serena Hotel, before hitting the road
for the Rift Valley.
Moments after leaving with his team, other LDP officials arrived at
the hotel, but staff who had assumed they were coming for the Kalonzo
meeting told them he had just left for Rift Valley.
"That issue nearly broke the party into two," an official told The Sunday Standard.
Kalonzo is said to have been on a hunt for allies since then, and
has pitched tent in Rift Valley where he hopes to win the support of
the moneyed allies of retired President Moi and the populous Rift
Valley population.
That is how Ruto comes into the picture of those suspected to be complicating Kalonzo's fortunes.
In the 2004 meeting, the Kalenjin leaders asked Kalonzo about the
possibility of being Uhuru's running mate, although no conclusion was
reached.
But the group also wanted to know whether Kalonzo was his own man or an emissary of Raila.
With Ruto's entry, the Rift Valley vote is at best split. Those
allied to Moi are said to be sympathetic to Kalonzo, whom they believe
will work with Uhuru in the next election.
Those allied to Ruto are said to favour an arrangement where the Kanu Secretary General would team with Raila next year.
In the Rift Valley, including the last meeting the former minister
held at Soi Lodge last week, his pitch is that his community has a long
history with the Kalenjin.
He cites the fact that many of his tribesmen served together with a
number of soldiers from the Rift Valley in the Second World War.
The former minister also cites the tenure of General Jackson
Mulinge, who thwarted the coup attempt against the Moi regime as a
further indication that the Kamba elite protected the former President
when he was in power.
The Mwingi North MP has also centred his campaigns in the Rift
Valley on the presence of the Africa Inland Church in both Ukambani and
among the Kalenjin, citing it as one of the ties that bind.
In one of his recent tours of the area, Kalonzo is said to have said
his rivals in ODM - Raila and Ruto - declared their candidatures from
some small corners of the country. He predicted that Balala and
Mudavadi would also do so from such small corners.
Kalonzo promised that when his time to declare comes, he would do so from Rift Valley.
But even within the Moi circles, there is no clarity on where
Kalonzo fits, especially because they believe the retired President -
the one most influential political voice in the populous Rift Valley -
is still with Uhuru. There are also those in the Moi camp who are
thought to be silently sympathetic to Ruto.
Authoritative sources told The Sunday Standard yesterday that the
declarations for the Presidency under individual parties - LDP and Kanu
- are a precursor to the consolidation of community blocks for power
trading under ODM in 2007.
The sources also say that "tired of being kingmaker, Raila has now decided to go for the top job."
Whereas Raila can rely on the solid support of the Luo in case he
contests, Ruto has been receiving support from a large number of Rift
Valley MPs - on one occasion in defiance of retired President Moi's
advice to the contrary.
Uhuru, who came a good second to Kibaki in the 2002 election, could
have a resurgence in Central Province, now said to be looking for an
alternative to Kibaki whose regime has been dogged by misfortune,
scandal and a lacklustre performance.
Kalonzo, who opinion polls have been depicting as the most favourite
contender for the presidency, has no distinct national constituency
except within a larger organisation such as ODM. Pundits aver that in
Kenya's highly tribalised politics, Kalonzo could be over-estimating
his level of influence, especially given that he has a formidable
political foe for the Ukambani vote in Health Minister Charity Ngilu.
Raila and Uhuru have contested the presidency before and learnt
valuable lessons. But Kalonzo, Musalia, Ruto, and Balala would be doing
it for the first time.
As political events unravel with dizzying speed, it will be
interesting to see if the ODM competitors maintain a united front -
despite the outcome of their own nominations - beyond the 2007 election.
Notably, neither Kanu nor LDP can realistically go it alone in 2007.
As that date approaches, Kenyans can expect many trade-offs and
surprises as politicians engage in self-destructing acts and others
picking up the spoils with glee.
But as far the Rift Valley vote goes, they are likely to throw their weight behind former President Moi's favourite.
Meanwhile, Narc (without the LDP) will be banking on a fallout in ODM for a lifeline.

Original_Search_Title: Political Unity At Coast Elusive As New Battle Lines Drawn
Headline: Kenya: Political Unity At Coast Elusive As New Battle Lines Drawn
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The much talked-about unity in Coast province is, going by the
latest political developments, likely to remain as elusive as ever,
especially with the revocation last week of Mr Taib Ali Taib's
nomination as a councillor, leading to his losing the mayor's post.
The move by Local Government minister Musikari Kombo is seen by Mr
Taib's supporters as punishment for his support for the Orange camp
during the constitution referendum in November.
Sources say ministers from the region who are uncomfortable with the
recently launched Coast Leaders Forum, of which Mr Taib is treasurer,
went to State House on February 17 and sought the mayor's sacking.
They told President Kibaki that the grouping, which is chaired by
Bahari MP Joe Khamisi, was aimed at undermining pro-government MPs.
And with Mr Taib now of Town Hall, political battle lines have are
drawn between pro-government Narc MPs and those allied to the Orange
Democratic Movement.
Mr Khamisi says that acting mayor Shariff Ali Shekue is a mere
stop-gap. "Very soon Shekue will be hounded out," he says. "He will
then be replaced by someone who will assure the business sharks that
they will benefit from the lucrative contracts that are expected to
come with the world cross-country championship to be staged in Mombasa
next year."
There is the equally lucrative waste management tender which had
been awarded to Italian company Jacorossi Impresse, but whose fate now
hangs in the balance with Mr Taib's ouster.
Town Hall sources say jostling has started in earnest for Mr Taib's
replacement. National Heritage minister Suleiman Shakombo, Transport
minister Ali Mwakwere and assistant minister Anania Mwaboza are said to
favour Freetown councillor John Mcharo, while Tourism minister Morris
Dzoro and assistant minister Joseph Kingi are rooting for Jomvu's Mr
Karisa Nzai - the acting deputy mayor.
Politics is also being played out around the Coast Leaders Forum.
Although he says its main aim is to give the region a strong bargaining
chip in national politics, Mr Khamisi has not been able to convince his
colleagues, particularly those in government, about the intentions.
"We have always complained of being marginalised economically and of
being used by outsiders for their political ambitions, yet when we come
up with a forum which can help us to address these concerns, some of us
oppose it," he gripes.
Selling the forum has become difficult because of some MPs'
opposition to it. It will, therefore, not be surprising if the name is
changed to reflect the participation of the ordinary people so that it
becomes the Coast People's - and not Leaders' - Forum.
This could come as early as this weekend Coast people living in
Nairobi, including professionals and the academia, meet to discuss the
issue, according to Mr Khamisi.
The Coast Parliamentary Group was initially expected to bring about
the unity, but it remains disjointed because of political rivalry and
the great divide between pro- and anti-government MPs.
When he launched the forum, Mr Khamisi was promptly kicked out as
chairman of the parliamentary grouping and replaced with the moderate
Mwatate Kanu MP, retired major Marsden Madoka.
Mr Taib's ouster as mayor has also brought into sharp focus the
simmering rivalry between him and former National Heritage minister
Najib Balala. The two temporarily buried the hatchet when Mr Taib
joined ODM last year, but the former mayor is determined to challenge
Mr Balala for the Mvita parliamentary seat in next year's General
Election.
Mr Balala upped the ante by announcing that he was joining the
increasingly crowded race for the ODM presidential nomination. But
although the announcement has been endorsed by several influential
Muslim organisations, some MPs dismissed it a ploy to further divide
the region.
The fact that Mr Taib and Mr Balala, who were once great buddies,
are now poles apart underscores the fact that the small but influential
and wealthy Arab community is actually divided.
"The Arab community is contributing to the confusion," says Mr
Khamisi. "They have not seen the need to reconcile Taib and Balala, and
they are, in fact, fuelling the acrimony."
But Mr Taib differs: "In competitive politics that we are
practising, it is useless to try and reconcile leaders - it is the
people who choose their leader once they trust and have faith in him."
Mr Taib says that what the Coast lacks is a leader that will unite the
people. The region has yet to get one with the charisma to unite the
people, he says, and warns that unless such a leader is found, the
region will always be kept in the periphery of national politics.
He thinks that the only one in recent times who almost made the mark was Cabinet minister Karisa Maitha, who has since died.
He gives the example of Luo Nyanza where, he says, there have never
been divisions because the people have faith in and trust Lang'ata MP
Raila Odinga and, before him, his father Oginga Odinga.
Mr Taib points at the outcome of the referendum at the Coast.
"Whereas the majority of MPs were for the Banana, the people voted
overwhelmingly for Orange because they have no faith in the current
leadership in the region, he says.
But by declaring his presidential ambition, Mr Balala might as well be trying to project himself as such a leader.
Mr Khamisi says, however, that the Mvita MP should have been
proposed by the people instead of MP Reuben Ndolo of Makadara, Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: A Wedding Like No Other
Headline: Kenya: A Wedding Like No Other
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Right from the moment one walked into the Samantha's Bridal Wedding Fair at the Sarit Centre, it was clear that the exhibition was all about putting together breathtaking weddings. Bright colours and exquisite designs adorned the more than 40 stands showcasing the best that Kenya has to offer the discerning bride. Just taking one round through the fair took more than two hours (a joy for some and a headache for others), especially if one was really out to get ideas on how to plan their wedding.
There were bright flowers, designer cakes ranging from the traditional to the unorthodox, and right at the entrance was a chocolate fountain. The chocolate flowed endlessly, just as water fountains in homes or hotels do, right into a base adorned with ripe fruits. To demonstrate how it worked, Sunaina Choda, the designer, chopped a pineapple into small pieces, pieced one with a toothpick and then rolled it into the flowing chocolate. Not surprisingly, a number of kids waited anxiously for their turn.
This was the place to be for any bride-to-be. Understandably, there were more women guests at the fair eagerly checking out the exquisite bridal gowns, bridesmaids garments, outfits for the groom and his men, and clothes for the pageboys and flower girls. Bodices to keep the lady's figure in check and sheer negligees for the honeymoon drew plenty of 'ahs' and 'ohs'.
Soft music played in the background. The catwalk on which the exhibiting fashion designers paraded their creative wedding gowns and tuxedos was enough to convince even the hardest cynic that having a wedding was worth giving consideration to, if not to proclaim their love but just to wear the awesome gowns!
Samantha's Bridal Weddings Fair was a testament to the progress and growth that the wedding industry has achieved in Kenya. Gone are the days when the wedding couple called together a committee of family and friends to raise money and to sign up for various duties. And planning is not the only bit about weddings that has changed. From the traditional must-be-white gown and must-happen-in-a-church weddings, we now have more ethnic African or Indian gowns. Weddings are increasingly being performed away from churches. Instead, marriages are being solemnised by the poolside, in a park or by the beach.
Invitation cards too, have gone beyond what many could have imagined only a few years back. No more white envelopes in the same old format written in the same old words. Today, most cards are hand-made with a more personal touch and taste. Some are in banana fibre, others in the shape of the African continent and not many put into envelopes. The cards are used to give a preview of the theme or the colour scheme of the wedding.
Wedding planners have revolutionised the way weddings are done making it much easier to have the wedding of your dreams with as little hassle as possible.
The bridal fair, which brought together most of the city's players in the wedding industry, was the brainchild of Catherine Masitsa-Rosza, publisher of Samantha's Bridal Weddings magazine.
"Most people now see weddings as an expression of themselves," says Masitsa. "For this reason, wedding planners, fabric designers, caterers, jewellers and the hospitality industry have come up with exciting packages to fill the need to make weddings personal and memorable. And this was my inspiration to organise this bridal fair: to be the facilitator and the one to show how the industry has grown and to bring the industry together and the same time, bring them to their prospective customers."
Weddings have clearly become so much more than gowns and flowers and cakes. According to Masitsa, various factors have influenced the way weddings are done today.
"While there are no formal statistics, it is safe to say that the industry has grown by leaps and bounds, owing to various factors. Seventy five per cent of Kenya's population are young, the majority of them under 25," she says, before adding on a light note, "For as long as people keep falling in love, weddings will continue to take place." Only 10 years ago, the immediate family catered for most weddings but now couples are saving up and funding their own wedding. This development has been good for the industry, because they are much more demanding in terms of quality of services.
The marrying age is another factor that has influenced how weddings are done. "Marrying at 26 is now seen as marrying young. What women were to marriage at 20, now they are even at 30," says Masitsa. "This has translated into women marrying after they are already established in their careers. It means that they now have less time to plan for their weddings, which in turn has created the immense need for wedding planners."
Masitsa is quick to add that most brides are still diehard romantics and will not have anything but a white gown. "Anything can change but they won't do without the 'Here comes the bride all dressed in white' part."
But then there are those who want the best of two worlds - having something traditional and something African about their weddings. "There was a time when what was African was seen as bad or non-progressive. Now that has changed and there's been a blend of what's African but stylish, with conventional fashion," said Masitsa. "The result is something so good that it can be on the catwalks in Milan or the streets of Nairobi.
To show the great importance of a wedding day, Masitsa puts it in contrast with the other two most important days in life. "There are three most important days in life: the day one is born, the day one is married and the day one dies. No one has control over when they are born or when they die. But with weddings, we have been granted all control. We'd be wise to do the best we can with the only important day we can control! And the great news is that there is all the help one needs to make this happen."
The fair that ran for four days at the prestigious Sarit Centre was a statement that appeared designed to prove wrong the saying that all weddings are similar but every marriage different. With all the creativity and ever-growing opportunities, the possibilities to make your wedding unique are infinite.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Names New Review Committee
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Names New Review Committee
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday named a 15-member committee to complete the constitutional review.
The wreckage of the chartered plane that crashed in Busia, killing
then Labour minister Mohamed Khalif (Labour). The accident was part of
an assortment of bad luck that has dogged the Kibaki
administration.Photo File
The panel will be chaired by career diplomat and peace-broker Bethuel Kiplagat, a Kenya Gazette notice said yesterday.
The members, who were appointed "in their personal capacities" and
"shall serve independently," are expected to elect a vice-chairperson
from among them.
Almost all the members named in the Gazette have not been involved
in the review before and, except former chief justice Abdul Majid
Cockar, former permanent secretary Peter Wambura and long-serving
Kenyatta University council chairman Ole Moi Yoi, they are fairly
little known.
They include former directors of University of Nairobi's Institute
of Development Studies, Prof Ng'ethe Njuguna and Dr Karuti Kanyinga.
A report of findings
Others are Dr Kaendi Munguti, Mr Juma Mwachihi, Mr Mwambi Mwasaru,
Dr Jacinta Muteshi, Dr Richard Barasa, Dr Nemwel Nyamwaka Bosire, Dr
Patricia Kameri Mbote, Ms Wanza Kioko and Prof Kassim Farah.
The committee has up to May 30 to prepare a report on its findings and submit it to the President.
Among its terms of reference is to find out weaknesses, strengths,
successes or failures in the review and give recommendations on the way
forward.
They are also to recommend a process for national healing to allow reconciliation and fruitful dialogue.
This will help to resolve the contentious issues and make a piece of
"legislation that will underpin the review process and lead to a new
Constitution of Kenya," the President said.
The committee will hold sessions in Nairobi, and it will be up to
them to hold them "in private in order to instil confidence in the
people appearing before them and allay their fears of adversity or
reprisals" or in public.
They are to rely on the past official drafts drawn under the Constitution of Kenya Review Act.
The secretariat will be at the the ministry of Justice and
Constitutional Affairs headquarters at Cooperative Bank House, Nairobi.
President Kibaki mandated them to identify all the obstacles the review has faced or is likely to face.
The appointment of the team, he said, was in response to Kenyans' desire of a new constitution.
The President pointed out that during the debate in the run-up to
the referendum, there were some issues which appeared not to have been
adequately debated and resolved.
But, he said: "Substantial progress towards the development of a new constitution has been made.
"There is need to heal the nation from the divisions emerging during
the referendum campaigns in order to create an enabling environment for
participatory process."
But while some people praised the move, others said it was made without consultations.
Liberal Democratic Party leader Raila Odinga dismissed the
appointments as a ploy by the President to divert public attention from
Anglo Leasing-like scandals.
The move, he said, was a scheme by the wing defeated at the
referendum. "This is a Banana affair; we (LDP) don't recognise it," he
told the Nation in Kisumu.
Mr Odinga said it was wrong for the President to "on his own"
appoint a team to chart the way forward in the review "without telling
us where salaries for the panel members will come from."
Such funds, he said, had not been budgeted for and endorsed by Parliament.
Kanu vice-chairman Chris Okemo criticised the President for making what he described as a unilateral decision.
"The President ought to have done what we demanded immediately after
the referendum - invite the Orange Democratic Movement so that we may
sit down together and prepare the panel's terms of reference," he said.
The general secretary of the National Council of Churches, the Rev
Mutava Musyimi, said: "I am glad something is happening on the
constitution review process front, but I cannot comment now on whether
I support the President's decision or not."
But he said all the interested parties should have been consulted.
Said the chairman of the Law Society of Kenya, Mr Tom Ojienda: "There
was no input on our part, yet we have always maintained that the whole
process should be all-inclusive.
"It is like trying to reinvest the wheel. As a starting point, the whole process should be consultative."
Mr Ojienda's views were echoed by council member Evans Monari, who
demanded that the President unite Kenyans first before the review
restarts.
The secretary-general of the Central Organisation of Trade Unions
(Cotu), Mr Francis Atwoli, welcomed the move, saying that Kenya was
"waiting with bated breath to see how this group rolls out a process so
turbulent that it has often threatened to tear the country apart."
Said a member of the defunct Constitution of Kenya Review
Commission, Prof Githu Muigai: "Any move or process that will help to
move forward the constitution review agenda is a step in the right
direction."
Kanu secretary-general William Ruto said the Government was restarting the review on a wrong note, and that the move would fail.
Not a government priority
Catholic Archbishop Zacchaeus Okoth of Kisumu said the review was not a priority for the Government at the moment.
"Right now, Kenyans are more eager to know how the Government will unravel the Anglo Leasing riddle," he said.
Nairobi archbishop Ndingi mwana a'Nzeki and Archbishop John Njue of
Nyeri said they would react only after reading and understanding the
implications of the appointments.
The terms of reference are to:
-Facilitate the airing of views by Kenyan on the constitutional
review process so far in terms of what Kenyans consider the weaknesses,
strengths, successes or failures of the process, and make proposals on
the way forward;
- Identify any legal, political, social, economic, religious,
governance or other issues or obstacles, whether past or present, which
stood in the way and/or may stand in the way of achieving a successful
conclusion of the constitutional review process;
- Receive written memoranda and/or oral presentations by organised
groups and individuals on all foregoing matters and matters incidental
thereto;
- Undertake consultations and receive advice from local, regional
and international constitutional experts on all the foregoing issues
and in particular, on how to establish an effective legal framework for
the completion of the review process;
It is expected that when the team holds its sessions, it will be
particularly interested in views on the contentious issues in the
rejected Wako Draft, including the position of a prime minister,
whether the presidency should be executive or mainly ceremonial, the
levels of devolution of power and whether or not to enshrine religious
courts in the Constitution.
RELATED NEWS
We are equal to the task says new law review team

Original_Search_Title: What Art Says About Today's Politics
Headline: Uganda: What Art Says About Today's Politics
Body_Text:
Kampala — Situated on a sunny square off Parliament Avenue, spacious and well-lit art and crafts gallery DesignAgenda is a key location for viewing contemporary Ugandan fine art.
The gallery curator is 24-year-old Elisabeth Mbabazi, who studied fashion and graphics and also works as a designer when she is not organising exhibitions and purchasing hand-made crafts for the venue.
Recording History-The Current Scene is an opened expression on the controversy and tensions around elections, for which Mbabazi commissioned various artists to communicate their opinions with regards to the issues around the current political situation.
Her request resulted in a collection of varied and rich paintings from nine fine-artists. The exhibition showcases well known names such as Yusuf Ssali and Xenson Senkaaba, but also new faces that have the potential to become remarkable figures of the Ugandan art scene.
Whereas some pieces state the interrogations and feelings of the author in a straight-forward manner, others chose a more subtle style of work, blending in symbols from Ugandan heritage and abstract representation.
Amongst them is Ahimbisibwe Ronex who, with Kisanja: Burn it or Embrace it, depicts the sense of compulsion in the present political system. The piece is mainly characterised by two central figures; one burning and the other cuddling dried banana leaves, trademark and symbol of the National Resistance Movement.
A more positive perspective on the addressed issues is the mixed media piece by newcomer Barieye Navio (Massive Support), who believes the elections will unify people of all backgrounds, age and geographic location by going to vote for a common cause. His bright painting in orange and red shows five separated routes lined with people walking towards the heart of the canvas; a geometrical figure of ballot boxes.
Finally, personal opinions and interpretations put aside, the quality and diversity of artwork exhibited certainly makes DesignAgenda a stop over for all art appreciators.

Original_Search_Title: 'Anything Can Happen to Us, Anytime'
Headline: South Africa: Rights-South Africa: 'Anything Can Happen to Us, Anytime'
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — As a community relations officer for the Johannesburg-based Forum
for the Empowerment of Women, a non-governmental organisation, Zanele
Muholi has become all too familiar with the prejudice against lesbians
that exists in South Africa.
Over recent years, says Muholi, she has spoken "to more than 50
victims of rape and hate speech", and recorded five major cases of
violence against lesbians.
The latest incident occurred in Cape Town earlier this month. It
involved a 19-year-old woman, Zoliswa Nkonyana, who was stabbed and
stoned to death by a mob of young people in a predominantly black
residential area of the coastal city, Khayelitsha.
This was not the first time that a gay woman had been attacked in
the area. In 2003, another woman was seriously injured in the same
place, notes Muholi.
In Soweto, the largest black residential to be established in
Johannesburg under apartheid, attitudes towards lesbians are similarly
hostile.
"It's scary. Whenever people see lesbians holding hands or kissing
in the street, they react by unleashing a torrent of verbal abuse which
amounts to hate speech. Some of them would want to hit you physically,"
a woman who lives in Soweto told IPS in a telephone interview from the
neighbourhood.
She declined to reveal her identity for fear of being attacked. "We
live in fear. Anything can happen to us, anytime," the woman said.
Muholi believes "ignorance, arrogance and disrespect" lie at the
heart of prejudice against gay women. Matters are worsened, she adds,
by a culture of impunity: "They (attackers) know that they will get
away with it (violence against lesbians)."
Rape is seen as an act that can alter the sexual orientation of gay
women. Apart from scarring them psychologically, however, sexual
assault can also serve as a death sentence: "Some end up catching
sexually-transmitted diseases like AIDS or becoming pregnant," says
Muholi. According to the Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS,
about one in five adults in South Africa has contracted HIV.
But while lesbians face prejudice in the streets of Khayelitsha,
Soweto and elsewhere, their rights are being entrenched in South
Africa's legal system.
Last year, the constitutional court ruled in favour of same-sex
marriage in the country. Members of parliament have been given until
the end of this year to amend the law accordingly.
In 2002, the court also ruled that gay couples had the right to
adopt children, reportedly making South Africa the first African state
to legalise such adoptions. South Africa's constitution outlaws
discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation.
The constitutional court's 2005 decision was roundly condemned by religious groups.
"The legalising of same-sex marriages is doomed to have a morally
deleterious effect on the institution of the family, traditionally
defined as the permanent union between husband and wife," the Catholic
Church said in a statement issued Dec. 7, 2005.
Njongonkulu Ndungane, the Anglican archbishop of Cape Town, voiced
similar sentiments. "We do not regard partnership between two persons
of the same sex as a marriage in the eyes of God," he said.
However, Ndungane acknowledged that many might disagree with the Anglican Church's standpoint on this matter.
"We recognise that we live in a country which is home to many
beliefs, cultures and practices," he noted. "It would be arrogant and
presumptuous of us to attempt to force our values and viewpoints on
people who think differently from us."
Elsewhere in the Southern African region, gays and lesbians are also
confronted with prejudice -- as evidenced by Zimbabwean President
Robert Mugabe's statement, made several years ago ago, that they were
"worse than dogs and pigs".
Canaan Banana, Zimbabwe's first post-independence head of state, was
convicted for sodomy in 1998, and jailed for a year. The court
proceedings revealed that he had abused other men while in office.
Former Namibian president Sam Nujoma has also spoken out against homosexuality.
Back at the Forum for the Empowerment of Women, Muholi expresses the
hope that -- this time -- Nkonyana's killers will be brought to book.
"I hope those guys will be arrested and tried. I feel sorry for
Nkonyana's friend who ran away during the assault. She needs trauma
counseling," she says.

Original_Search_Title: Containing the Plastic Bags Menace
Headline: Kenya: Containing the Plastic Bags Menace
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It has been termed South Africa's national flower and Ireland's national flag, but the plastic bag has made a name for itself in Kenya, too.
Anywhere you go in the country, there's no avoiding it.
At the supermarket, fruits and vegetables are bagged once and then again just to make sure they don't come in contact with the bagged milk carton or the bagged bar of soap.
At a street kiosk, a banana is handed over in a flimsy black plastic bag, only to be eaten immediately after. The buyer, ready to chomp down on his snack, leaves the bag on the ground.
Outside, plastic bags decorate Kenya's landscape like fields of flowers. Thin and flimsy, they get caught in trees and pile up in water bodies.
In Kenya, 4,000 tons of plastic bags are produced each month, with only about half going through waste management. The other half is left piling up in our environment and, in some cases, our homes.
And the way things are now, the unavoidable mess of plastic bags is here to stay - they each take up to 1,000 years to degrade.
But before the scourge becomes unstoppable and irreversible, a pilot project in Nairobi is looking to change the environmental degradation caused by plastic bags by encouraging three simple principles - reuse plastic bags, recycle the ones you have used and avoid plastic bags entirely.
Partnership between Unep and Nema
The project is a partnership between UN environment agency Unep and the National Environment Management Authority (Nema) - a parastatal. Since it was launched in July, the pilot project has involved various interested parties in trying to break away from a plastic dependence.
It seeks to make sure Nairobians, and eventually all Kenyans, become responsible plastic users.
Undoubtedly, the introduction of plastic changed our world. It is lightweight, cheap to produce and a functional way to carry goods. Plastic largely replaced paper bags, helping to reduce deforestation.
It is for these reasons that plastic bags became so popular and their production increased.
But here and in other developing countries, a problem emerged. According to Mr Desta Mebratu, the Unep officer working on the pilot project, consumers have become dependent on plastic without the proper infrastructure to support the dependence.
"The infrastructure is not developing itself in such a way that it can take care of all the waste coming from society," he said.
According to Practical Action, an advocacy group, plastic makes up 20 per cent of the urban waste generated in Nairobi everyday. Only about 25 per cent of it is collected, mostly from higher-income estates.
What's more, said Nema officer Mwai Wanjohi, plastic waste is more costly to recycle than others because it must be separated from other solid waste before being processed. There is a reluctance from industries to take on that extra cost, he said.
The result: Plastic doesn't get collected or recycled and is often left in the environment. Not only do the bags become a blight on our landscape, but they also cause environmental problems.
For one, the sprawling plastic waste we leave behind is picked up by animals, who can choke on them. "When you carry your mandazi in a plastic bag and then leave it in the environment, I want to tell you that you have killed an animal," said Mr Joseph Ngondi, an officer with the Green Belt Movement, the environmental NGO founded by Tetu MP Wangari Maathai.
Furthermore, according to a Unep report released last year, plastic bags clog rivers and waterways and make soil infertile.
And since garbage collection is insufficient in Nairobi, especially in its slum areas, there is a tendency to burn piles of it, including plastic bags, which release small amounts of chemicals such as lead and cadmium into the atmosphere, Mr Ngondi said.
Technology has tried to keep up. Last year, Nakumatt supermarkets introduced environmentally friendlier bags, which degrade in 10 to 12 months. But Mr Ngondi said the technology is questionable. Unless the plastic bag is made out of completely natural products, it will degrade and become invisible to the naked eye, but any synthetic elements will still pollute the environment, he said.
The plastic bag problem isn't limited to Kenya, of course. Countries around the world have had to deal with the ever-increasing use and misuse of plastics, especially the bags. Some, like Rwanda, have resorted to drastic measures such as the outright banning of all plastic imports, while others have limited the bag thickness to encourage re-use - thinner, flimsier bags are less likely to be re-used, Mr Wanjohi said.
There are tried and tested strategies for dealing with plastic bags, but Mr Mebratu explained that each country must find which ones fit within its economic and social possibilities.
The Nairobi pilot project intends to find and implement appropriate policies that promote the re-using, recycling or complete avoidance of plastic bags.
While a total plastics ban may have worked in Rwanda, Mr Mebratu said, the tactic is not likely to happen in Kenya, which has a larger plastics manufacturing sector. "If you are to stop producing plastics, we will lose jobs," Mr Ngondi said.
Mr Mebratu agreed: "Plastics are part of modern society and they have significantly contributed to the well-being of people."
He said the team working on the pilot project had not been pushing for a total ban, but rather a phasing out of the flimsy, thinner bags that are less likely to be re-used and therefore more likely to end up as litter.
"It's not about banning a product as such," he said. "It's about making a rational utilisation of that product."
Mr Ngondi said Kenyans were not aware of the gravity of the problem, and they won't use plastics rationally unless they were enticed to do so.
One way to encourage them is to introduce a levy fee, which would in some way charge shoppers for every bag they use. The pilot project participants support the levy, but Mr Mebratu said it "isn't being suggested as an immediate action."
Mr Clive Mutunga, an officer at the Institute for Public Policy Research and Analysis (Kippra), which is working on the project, said the design of this levy remains uncertain - would consumers be forced to pay for bags at the till, or would supermarkets, for instance, be charged for the quantity they distribute?
Nonetheless, he said a levy would discourage people from being wasteful of plastic bags. "We're trying to make it expensive for people to shop using plastic bags," said Mr Benjamin Langwen, a Nema deputy director.
But what alternatives are there for people who want to avoid bags? "Long ago, we used to go to the market with baskets," said Mr Ngondi, who supports re-usable and durable fabric bags.
Mr Phiagarajan Ramamurthi, the Nakumatt director of operations, said the company planned to introduce woven bags in the next two months. "Initially we'll probably give them out for free and after that we may charge something and see how people react," he added.
Option of returning bags
Nakumatt distributes 50 million plastic bags a year. Mr Ramamurthi said that while it was company policy to separate products by wrapping them in different plastic bags, the company has reduced its plastics use by 15 per cent over the past six months.
Nakumatt also offers customers the option of returning their plastic bags to the store, which will then ensure they are properly sorted and recycled, Mr Ramamurthi said.
The pilot project wraps up in July, when people involved hope to begin implementing policies, both in government and the private sector.
And while the exact policies that might solve Kenya's plastic bags problem are not entirely clear just yet, there's hope that eventually, change will come.
"This is a manageable problem. Almost all countries have passed through this stage and they have dealt with it," Mr Mebratu said. "Here in Kenya it can also be managed."

Original_Search_Title: Local Dishes Give Diamond Motel an Edge Above Others
Headline: Tanzania: Local Dishes Give Diamond Motel an Edge Above Others
Body_Text:
Arusha — It's hard to find a restaurant that caters to all generations these days, and Diamond Motel, located ten minutes drive away from the Central Business District [CBD], is a welcome change. Most restaurants in Arusha are all about the food, not so much about the presentation. So it must be a tick for Diamond Motel that the ideas are there.
Rising to the challenge of making local dishes which taste and look good is something often reserved for three to four - star restaurants. Diamond Motel's seasonal menu screams fusion, and prepared with a local cuisine. Many patrons swam the restaurant enjoying meals prepared from expansive menus which reflect a highly trained chef.
Main courses are heavy on beef, chicken or fish, and it is hard to find fault with the Bukoba food speciality. Thin boiled or roasted bananas come over a warm salad of potatoes, mussels and olives balanced one another.
As you would expect, boiled chicken or beef with varied vegetables are on the menu, and although they are perfectly good, skip them for the boiled soup if your stomach is half full. An equally fine choice is the pork dry-fry served with warm bananas.
The food is good and so is the restaurant with the open dining room near a sizeable large patio. Seating is comfortable and seats are not attached to the backing, making them tip forward as diners lean toward the table. Tables are spaced widely apart, but just about every diner has a panoramic view of the seven rooms available for accommodation. A phone call on 0744 021 555 or 2504931 to reserve a table is recommended.
A part of the entrance is given to a welcoming garden with a secure parking area partly hidden. The hosts are true to their promise as diners entertained by present-day music in the background as tension seems to flow out with the tide. "All customers entering our door mean business and in return we offer impeccable services" Said Mr. Mbarara Narcis, the Managing Director.

Original_Search_Title: Besigye Protests As His Guards Are Changed
Headline: Uganda: Besigye Protests As His Guards Are Changed
Body_Text:
Kampala — Forum for Democratic Change leader Kizza Besigye has declined state
security following a reshuffle of bodyguards he has had since he was
released from prison on January 2. Dr Besigye fears that the change of
the bodyguards provided by the State was meant to put his personal
safety in jeopardy instead of strengthening it.
Daily Monitor witnessed the change of the policemen at Mulago,
Kampala yesterday soon after Besigye had held a campaign rally there.
Five burly policemen immediately jumped onto his car to steer as he
continued with his tour of Kampala. "The other guards were withdrawn by
government claiming that they were partisan," said Mr Sam Akaki, FDC's
acting spokesperson.
Asked whether Besigye had actually rejected the new guards, believed
to be security operatives rather than professional police, Akaki said,
"He is giving it serious consideration because the circumstances under
which the other guards were withdrawn is still unclear."
Sources within FDC told Daily Monitor yesterday that the change of
the guards was prompted by a complaint from the Chief of Military
Intelligence, Col. Leo Kyanda, to the Inspector General of Police, Maj.
Gen. Kale Kayihura.
Kayihura was reported to have, in turn, given directives to his
subordinates in the Special Branch and Mobile Police units to make
immediate changes in deployment.
The bodyguards, four of them plain clothed and 11 uniformed police,
were withdrawn yesterday and ordered to report back to their Special
Branch and Mobile Police Patrol units.
They had on a number of occasions arrested State operatives trailing
Besigye on the campaign trail across the country. On one occasion, Lt.
Frank Banana, a senior officer at the Internal Security Organisation,
was arrested trailing Besigye in Kiriri, Mpigi. On another occasion,
two Presidential Guard Brigade soldiers attached to First Lady Janet
Museveni's detail were arrested in Rwashamaire, Ntungamo as Besigye
campaigned there.
One of the soldiers had a pistol while another had a recorder and a telephone he was using to make "suspicious" calls.
Police spokesman Assuman Mugenyi declined to comment on the change
in Besigye's security detail, referring Daily Monitor to Benjamin
Namanya, the commandant of the Mobile Police Patrol Unit. "I am not
aware of any change," Namanya said. "Why don't you refer to the police
spokesman?"
The guards were reportedly recalled on their personal mobiles and
told to record statements over their "partisanship". A similar change
in the security detail of Democratic Party presidential candidate
Ssebaana Kizito was confirmed by his media coordinator John Bosco
Mayiga.
"The operations commander of the MPPU rang them recalling them," Mr
Mayiga said. "But the candidate has protested. He says the guards were
given to him by the Electoral Commission and they cannot be withdrawn
without the EC informing him. He is demanding for reasons why they are
being withdrawn."
Ssebaana went to the EC yesterday to protest.
An aide to UPC presidential candidate Miria Kalule Obote said,
"There was an apparent threat that they (guards) were going to be
changed. We lodged a protest and somehow it did not happen." The change
of guards came a day to the end of the campaigns, which have witnessed
increasing violence on the last stretch.
On Wednesday a reservist attached to the office of the Rubaga
Division Assistant Resident District Commissioner opened fire on a
crowd that had accompanied Besigye to Bulange, Mengo to pay homage to
the Katikkiro of Buganda, Mr Dan Muliika. The incident that left three
people dead followed what eyewitnesses called a provocative attempt by
the reservist, Lt. Ramathan Magara, to drive through the crowd.
President Museveni on Sunday blamed the incident on FDC supporters
whom he accused of provoking Magara by blocking the road. On Sunday
military trucks drove through Besigye's crowd as he addressed a rally
at Mukono, leaving three injured and admitted.

Original_Search_Title: How Narc Activist Wambui Became Powerbroker And Kingmaker
Headline: Kenya: How Narc Activist Wambui Became Powerbroker And Kingmaker
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Of late a residence on Mageta Road in Nairobi's Lavington area has become one of the most sought political addresses in town.
It is the residence of Othaya Narc activist Mary Wambui.
Located near the Lavington Methodist Church, the palatial house -
which once served as residence of South African High Commissioner
Kingsley Makhubela - is a must-know for budding politicians.
With Wambui emerging as one of the most powerful women in Kenyan
politics, political groups have been identifying themselves with her.
As the Anglo Leasing saga continued to claim more victims, focus is
turning on how two politically powerful women in Kenya will act. One of
them is the Narc activist and politician, the other is First Lady Lucy
Kibaki.
Although the two have not, in any way, been implicated in the Anglo
Leasing scandal, they have emerged as leaders of powerful camps
jostling for space around the Kibaki presidency. The First Lady has
always denied that such camps exist.
But as the Hurlingham Group goes up in smoke, the question pundits
are asking is: What role is Wambui playing in the new power games?
Addressing a public gathering in Kajiado last week, Wambui said
those behind Anglo Leasing must be arrested and charged in court for
stealing public money. The very next week, Murungaru, who has been
mentioned in connection with the scandal, was arraigned in court for
failing to provide a statement of his property to the Kenya
Anti-Corruption Commission.
It has been noted that with the ongoing fallout over Anglo Leasing,
the Wambui camp is having its final laugh. She is associated with the
Muthaiga Group.
With the Anglo Leasing scandal claiming members of the Hurlingham Group, the winner in the corridors of power for now is Wambui.
Her close associates say she might run for the Othaya seat (in case
Kibaki abandons the presidential bid next year) or the Nyeri Town seat.
Insiders say the Anglo Leasing fall is Wambui's perfect opportunity
to make a comeback into real politics like the days when she was known
in Othaya as "Wambui wa Kanu".
Wambui, who is a board member of several schools in Othaya, is a powerful voice around the Kibaki presidency.
Kibaki's old buddies are said to have been aligning themselves around Wambui, who enjoys State facilities, including security.
While members of the Kitchen Cabinet have not directly asked the
President to sack "corrupt" ministers, Wambui has since March 2005 be
prodding them to quit.
Although she holds no position in government, Wambui, in her many
rallies and philanthropic work, talks of "we" - as in government.
Wambui is no ordinary Narc activist for while others hunt
desperately for press coverage, she gets media attention even on
mundane issues.
It was not lost to observers that during the Banana-Orange campaign
Wambui hit the trail with lots of give-outs and promises. While not
receiving delegations too, at Silver Springs Hotel, Nairobi, she would
be in the countryside campaigning solo or accompanied by a handful of
politicians. Interestingly, all media attention seems to be on her and
today she gets more media coverage than even Lady Lucy.
Wambui displays both power and wealth. Fancy this: On November 4,
2005, she received defectors in Machakos to the 'Yes' campaign although
then assistant minister Prof Kivutha Kibwana was present.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Should Clean Up Nairobi City Council
Headline: Kenya: Minister Should Clean Up Nairobi City Council
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Mvita MP Najib Balala wants Local Government minister, Musikari Kombo, to rid Nairobi City Council (NCC) of corruption.
He said Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) welcomed Kombo's cracking
of the whip in Mombasa by sacking the Mayor, Taib Ali Taib, and
suspending the Town Clerk, Franklin Magaju, to allow for investigation.
But Balala said ODM was concerned about piecemeal and selective action against corruption.
"ODM believes Nairobi City Council is the mother of all corruption
and the minister should have had it weeded of the vice long time ago,"
Balala said.
At a news conference at the Coast ODM secretariat in Mombasa, Balala
said ODM would not condone corruption or support selective war on it.
He said there was danger in taking selective action on corruption,
as this could be used to silence political opponents and protect
friends.
"The Government should not use its machinery to silence its
political opponents in the guise of fighting corruption. Corruption
must be tackled from the roots," he said. Taib, who is Balala's rival
for the Mvita parliamentary seat, jumped from the Banana to the Orange
camp during the November 2005 referendum.
Yesterday, Balala asked Kombo to order for free and fair investigation and prosecution of corrupt council officials.
He told Kombo to make all those implicated in corrupt deals carry their crosses.
He opposed the transfer of Magaju from Mombasa to Nakuru to serve as
town clerk, saying ODM wanted him investigated and put the Mombasa
Municipal Council in order. "We are still waiting to see free and fair
investigation, prosecution and imprisonment of people implicated in
corruption throughout Kenya, and not Mombasa Municipal Council alone,"
Balala said.
Balala told the Government that ODM was still to see ministers mentioned in the Anglo Leasing scandal resign or be sacked.
He said his group would not rest until action was taken against
prominent politicians linked to corruption some of who were hiding
behind political affiliations.

Original_Search_Title: Battle for Mvita Begins in Earnest As Taib's Fall Redefines Coast Politics
Headline: Kenya: Battle for Mvita Begins in Earnest As Taib's Fall Redefines Coast Politics
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When Local Government minister Musikari Kombo sent Mombasa mayor Taib Ali Taib packing, he helped redefine Mombasa politics.
Not that Taib was in a particularly powerful position, but his
departure will determine the way Coast politicians approach the 2007
General Election.
The departure of Taib was a victory for former Cabinet minister
Najib Balala, whose Mvita seat is also eyed by Taib. It is no wonder
that during the Banana-Orange campaigns, the two often clashed as
Balala accused his rival of personalising the referendum by putting his
picture on the Orange campaign posters.
Of late two political camps have emerged within the larger Orange
Democratic Movement with Taib and Bahari MP Joe Khamis forming a camp
against Balala, who dubbed himself "Mr Clean" on Friday.
While Balala leads the Coast Parliamentary Group, Khamis has formed the Coast Leaders Forum.
The fall of Taib has focused attention on Coast politics and
observers found it significant that it was soon followed by an
announcement by Balala that he was eyeing the presidency.
"I cannot steal anything from public coffers while in office," the
former National Heritage minister said in Mvita, his political home
ground. That announcement, he hopes, will propel him from local
politics onto the national arena and give him more mileage, now that
Taib is not even a councillor.
Taib alleges that his removal - ostensibly to pave the way for
investigation into claims of corruption at the council - was
politically instigated.
He claimed that Kombo removed him to gain access to the Sh1b for the
Mombasa Waste Management project and the Sh500m budgeted for the
Mombasa World Cross Country Championships.
Considering that his removal has come at a time when there is a
national crackdown on the masterminds of the Anglo Leasing and the
Goldenberg scams, Taib will have to look for political marksmen to
tackle his political enemies who now have a good excuse to block him
from Town Hall.
At the council, the removal of Taib was met with jubilation and
cancellation of tenders. But the greatest damage was to his political
standing.
Chances of going to the LDP for survival are slim because of
Balala's presence and chances of playing ball with the system are near
nil.
"A lot of money has been embezzled at the council. For instance,
Latf funds running into millions cannot be accounted for," said Balala,
as he turned the knife on his enemy.
While some ODM leaders interpret the removal as punishment for
Taib's refusal to join the Banana campaign, it will be interesting to
see how Balala and his supporters help to drown their political nemesis.
The chances are that Balala will happily let Taib sink leaving him as the Mvita supremo now that Shariff Nassir is dead.
Taib was previously allied to the National Alliance Party of Kenya
(Nak) and owed his nomination after the 2002 General Election to then
Local Government minister, Mr Karisa Maitha. But the death of Maitha
left him to fight on his own and it was surprising when he jumped onto
the Orange bandwagon after the Kenya Anti-Corruption Commission
released a report on graft at the council.
Apart from Khamis, Taib is not enjoying any support from Coast MPs
and it will be a big blow for him if the Sh2.5b cleaning tender row is
used against him.
Taib came to prominence as lawyer for the unregistered Islamic Party
of Kenya. He once survived the mayoral battle by having Maitha
degazette the nomination of five Mombasa LDP councillors who were
opposed to him.
The formation of the Coast Forum means that Balala and Khamis will
be pulling in opposite directions and creating cracks in the LDP at the
Coast.
"Khamisi is using the forum to nurture his own political ambitions
and is bitter after missing a Cabinet position that he had been
promised," Balala claims.
One of the most striking aspects of the Balala-Taib fight is the
tendency to attack each other in public. But Balala has the upper hand
since he enjoys the support of LDP leader Raila Odinga.
This has been a busy season for formation of political camps in
Coast Province, and this month Balala joined seven Coast MPs at the
Nyali Beach Hotel and voted out Mr Khamisi as the chairman of the Coast
Parliamentary Group. Mwatate MP Marsden Madoka replaced him.

Original_Search_Title: Myths And History of Siamese Twins
Headline: Zambia: Myths And History of Siamese Twins
Body_Text:
Although the few pairs who have survived unseparated into adulthood
can describe their experience , it's impossible to imagine what it is
like to have such an intimately shared existence joined to another
human being 24 hours a day.
As surgical techniques improve, more of these rare children can be
separated although the operation always carries risks and sometimes
means the death of one or even both babies
Deciding to separate means making ethical or moral judgments that
question many of society's basic assumptions about individuality. Such
as need for privacy and whether people have the right to whatever their
situation, conjoined twins are extra-ordinary and memorable , and can
only be marveled at the co-orperation and compromise that underpins
their daily lives.
In joined they are seen not as freaks but as real unforgettable
people caught up in a rare and remarkable physical phenomenon that the
rest of the people barely can imagine.
Throughout history, conjoined twins or Siamese twins as they are commonly known have appeared in myths and legends.
The Greek and Roman god Janus had two faces, one young, one old.
Centaurs, a combination of horse and man, may have been inspired by
parapagus twins who often have four legs.
A common heraldic symbol, the Double-Headed Eagle", is common throughout Central Europe.
Some myths surrounding the birth of Siamese twins are: eating twin Bananas may lead to Siamese twins.
Fact :Siamese twins are born with part of their bodies joined
together . It happens purely by chance and takes place during the
development of a single embryo.
In the majority of twin development the embryo should separate
within two weeks after conception for Siamese twins ,this occurs later
and the embryo is not completely separated.
This condition is serious as the mortality rate for babies born with
this condition is high. Fortunately Siamese twins may be detected by
ultrasound examination during pregnancy. When detected at the early
stage of pregnancy the couple may decide whether to continue with the
pregnancy or not.
Treatment options vary with each case depending on which portion of
the baby's body are joined together and whether there are associated
deformities . In most cases surgery is performed.
One of the earliest documented cases of conjoined twins or Siamese
twins are Mary and Eliza Chulkhurst, also known as the Biddenden Maids.
Mary and Eliza were born in 1100, the sisters lived for 34 years in Biddenden, County of Kent, England.
Mary and Eliza, though often depicted as joined at the hip and
shoulders, were likely pygopagus twins who were joined at the buttocks
and lower backs.
After the death of one sister, doctors hoped to save the life of the other by separating them surgically.
The surviving twin refused, declaring, "As we came together, we will go together." She died several hours later.
Upon their deaths, a local church received 20 acres of land. In
remembrance of their generosity, small cakes and biscuits imprinted
with the image of the sisters were given to the poor every Easter
Sunday.
Nearly 900 years after their deaths, the Biddenden Maids are still honoured by this unique service.
In Zambia today medical personal say that the University Teaching
Hospital (UTH) has recorded 17 cases of Siamese twins but of these the
famous one was that of Joseph and Luka .
Born in 1997 Luka and Joseph's case received wide publicity due to
its complex mix of tissue and nerves in the brain. Joseph and Luka
Banda are brothers born in 1997 in Matero residential area.
Luka and Joseph with a combined birth weight of 5.9 kg were joined
at the head. The mother, Mrs Banda had no slightest idea that she would
have twins in that manner until when they were born.
The wonder twins underwent a 30 hour long surgery to separate them
and it was conducted at GaRankuwa hospital in Pretoria South Africa.
The medical team consisted of both local and foreign doctors and
after the 30 hours of agony and waiting the two boys were finally
separated from being joined at their heads to individuals.
The twins' brains were lying adjacent thereby posing the increased risk of blood loss with all the implications entailed.
The parents of the two boys described the entire operation as a
miracle and could not hide their gratitude but praised God for its
success.
They also highly commended those who supported them throughout the operation as it was not an easy task.
The Bandas who already had five children after Luka and Joseph's
experience vowed never to bear any more siblings due to the anguish
they went through over the twins' condition instead devoted everything
to them and the rest of the family.
The two boys are now staying in Chilenje in a house that was
purchased for their parents through the good will of Government though
one of them is required to attend a special school at UTH while the
other one goes to school together with other peers.
The most recent case of Siamese twins is that of Astridah Nakamba born on October 20 , 2005 in Mbala district hospital.
The UTH combined team of medical doctors scheduled to conduct the
operation the first of its kind will be led by Professor Lupando
Munkonge .
They will conduct the complicated operation on baby Astridah locally unlike the one which was done on Luka and Joseph abroad.
Others on the operating team are G Desai, Dr James Munthali, Dr
Chikoya Dr Goran, Dr E . Chomba , Dr Chabala, Dr Sultanov , Dr Libib ,
Dr Kalinchenko and Dr Abdulkarim.
The peculiar features on Astridah's body suggesting that she is a
Siamese twin comprise of functional female organs but with an extended
parasite body joining from the waist.
The baby also has a small male organ, two semi grown hands but
without a head. The two bodies however share the same buttocks and legs.
Professor Munkonge said the operation on baby Astridah would wait until she turns nine months old.
Briefing the press recently, Professor Munkonge said the medical
team was determined to conduct a successful operation at the home
ground without any help from expert personnel.
The doctors have already established that the parasite growth
attached to Astridah's body and the fully developed body were not
sharing any bones apart from the flesh at the waist.
UTH managing director Dr Tackson Lambert alluded that Astridah's
case was the 17th one to be attended to at the institution since 1970.
Dr Lambert however said the current case was strange and
distinguishable from other cases because normally Siamese twins were of
the same sex.
He however said medical investigations would still be conducted to
verify if the features appearing on the other part of the body were
really male organs or not.
The most important element in the success of the operation was to
constitute a team of competent doctors to carry out various roles.
Though the operation was expensive, UTH would use the Government
grant to successfully separate the attached body organs on Astridah.
The 4.8 kilogramme Siamese twin is so far growing well despite the parasite growth on it .
The doctors stated in their explanation that the baby's mother did
not experience any complications in pregnancy and only realised that
she delivered a child with complications after birth of Astridah.
When spoken to, Mrs Nakamba said she has had seven other pregnancies before and did not experience any complications .
She was married but her husband could not afford to accompany her to
Lusaka's UTH where Astridah is being seen by the doctors because the
expenses involved were way beyond their reach.
She was however hopeful that the operation intended on Astridah would be successful as she loved her baby as a gift from God.
To help it carry out the operation successfully without any
impediments considering the costs involved, UTH management has appealed
to all Zambians and well wishers with any financial and material
support of some kind towards the intended operation of the Siamese twin
to come forward and present them to the UTH management.

Original_Search_Title: The Gambia At 41
Headline: Gambia: The Gambia At 41
Body_Text:
The Gambia never ceases to bedazzle skeptics and pessimists. The staying power of this country remains formidable and it shall remain so for a very long time to come. This weekend, we will be 41; yes, four decades plus one year of nationhood.
There is a lot to celebrate. In 1965, the founding fathers, such as the then premier, Sir Dawda Jawara, were told by many pessimists that the country they were so eagerly leading into independence within the Commonwealth will be a 'banana republic', a state that had no chance of surviving because it was so ill- endowed with resources, both human and financial, that it will collapse or fall into Communist hands. Then in 1965, there was indeed rumour of the South Americanisation of The Gambia.
The pessimist had reason for doubt. The country then had less than a dozen graduates to man its machinery, there was no fixed income except spin offs from peanuts, and the grant in aid which the colonial master, Britain, grudgingly gave to help us pay recurrent expenditure, was becoming untenable. Our leaders were inexperienced youth, some of them former garden boys, others fresh from being clerks or unqualified teachers. These were the type of people who made up the first post-independence cabinet. This was enough to make anyone doubt our survival.
Now here we are. In June we are hosting Africa, the biggest diplomatic achievement in our history. Now we have a university. There is a national TV, schools have increased hundred fold since 1965, girls are staying longer in school because of scholarships so generously given, killer diseases have been eradicated, there is a working multi party democracy, and a shouting press. Of course, we have AIDs, unemployment, malaria and other challenges like all other African countries. But we have remained stable and peaceful and that is what has made us different from the rest.
By being peaceful, we have got good friends who come to our aid.
Forty one years is yet too young in the life of a country, yet it is a good start for us. Of course, things could have
ferences. It is an identity cultivated within our shared religious traditions and communal experience of everyday life.
Africa has had enough of catastrophic ethnic confrontations: Rwanda, Ivory Coast, and Liberia always come to mind. Political fault-lines based on tribal identities are the last things any well meaning Gambian wants to see happen in The Gambia. The Gambian Constitution has empowered President Jammeh to use any means necessary in order to protect the lives of Gambians and secure the peace. We hope that President Jammeh shall execute with due diligence the powers vested upon him in order to maintain a safe and prosperous Gambia.
Within the APRC, we praise the Lord that we can find wisdom in an elderly statesman such as the gentleman from Baddibou, Honorable S M Dibba; love from a distinguished mother and intelligent lady in the Vice President Isatou Njie-Saidy; and a fraternal camaraderie based on trust, diligence and plain speaking candor with President Jammeh. The APRC is blessed with a cooperative spirit towards all Gambians, unity of purpose and discipline in politics. These are principles that the opposition can never possess for themselves.
President Jammeh is a man named after a Fula spiritual teacher whom he adores all his life. He once told me in a private conversation that nothing uplifts his spirit more than the complex rhythms flowing from Jaliba Kuyateh . A man like President Jammeh, who nourishes his soul from the example of a Fula spiritual leader, and rocks with the Mandinka Kora music can never be in disharmony with any tribal group in the Gambia. President Jammeh loves all Gambians and he shall always champion their rights and welfare.

Original_Search_Title: Besigye Finds Love in Rukungiri Town
Headline: Uganda: Besigye Finds Love in Rukungiri Town
Body_Text:
Kampala/Rukungiri — DR Kizza Besigye yesterday took his campaign to his home town,
Rukungiri, and asked the people to vote him because of what he promises
to do once elected, but not because he is from the area.
"Don't vote me because I am your son, but because you think I will
make your lives better," said the FDC presidential candidate, drawing
cheers from the crowd at Rukungiri Stadium .
Rukungiri was the last place Besigye visited in November, soon after
his return from exile before he was arrested and charged with rape,
treason and terrorism.
During the 2001 campaigns, many of Besigye's supporters in the area
were beaten and harassed and one person, Baronda, was killed.
Many FDC supporters and admirers had waited all morning for the
party leader's motorcade with many lining up along Rukungiri's High
Street to catch a glimpse of the candidate.
Groups of women singers struggled to have their chants in praise of
Besigye heard above the tut-tut-tut din of boda-boda cyclists and the
shrill notes from hundreds of whistles and excited shouts and screams
from the crowds.
There was a carnival atmosphere complete with banana shoots
decorating the roadsides, while the rising fumes from the roaring
motorcycles added to the intoxicating mix of pride, passion and
politics.
It might have been Valentine's Day with love in the air, but Besigye
spoke with a broken heart, reminding his supporters that he had been
wronged by President Yoweri Museveni's government.
Besigye said Museveni and the NRM had desperately tried to eliminate
his presidential bid,but said he derived more inspiration from that.
"On realising that he [Museveni] had failed, he said that Kizza
Besigye had never studied up to Primary Seven," Besigye said in his
native Luhororo.
While campaigning in the area, during the time when Besigye was
still detained at Luzira Prison for rape and treason, Museveni told the
people in Rukungiri that the State had incriminating evidence in the
treason case against Besigye.
Museveni said Besigye had misled many youths shortly after the 2001
presidential election into joining armed rebellion and the law would
take its course against him.
The President also sought to present himself as a better candidate than Besigye, with his "Wealth for All" proposal.
But Besigye said the charges were trumped up and meant to kill his presidential bid.
The FDC candidate urged the electorate to vote with their minds and
not with their hearts. Like a lover who turns up for a date with a work
proposal, Besigye made no secret of his contempt for the fanfare,
reminding the crowd that it was not yet time to celebrate.
He urged party supporters not to sing praises for him, but to take
interest in the policy proposals he had been articulating throughout
his campaign.
The support in Rukungiri, Besigye said, had been matched elsewhere
from Teso to Tooro. "The whole of Uganda has seen that Museveni and the
Movement should go," he said.
"The whole of Uganda has decided to come together; that FDC and
Besigye should take over." Although a son-of-the-soil, Besigye knows
better than to take his support in Rukungiri for granted; in the 2001
presidential election, he scored 28 per cent in what was a nasty and
violence-ridden election.
If yesterday's mood in Rukungiri town is anything to go by, Besigye
should improve his numbers in the district -- and the peaceful nature
of the campaign in Rukungiri so far indicates that the love in the air
was not just about Valentine's Day.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Men Running Scared Over Protests
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Men Running Scared Over Protests
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement and the civil society were set on a
collision course with the Government over their planned mass action.
The President's inner circle is also deeply concerned about a
meeting of MPs scheduled for Parliament today to force the Head of
State to reopen the House and act decisively on members of his Cabinet
implicated in the Anglo Leasing scandal.
On Monday, as daggers were drawn for another round of battle, the
country appeared to relapse into the Banana-Orange groupings that
characterised the referendum period late last year as the threat of
street demonstrations mounted and President Kibaki stared at another
siege on his hold on Government.
Ford-Kenya said it would stay away from the meeting as it was
heading for a party retreat even though it expressed its disappointment
that those mentioned in the Anglo Leasing scandal were still in
Government in spite of the party's demand that they step aside.
Kanu, with its top officials out of the country, remained undecided
with vice chairman Chris Okemo saying the party had no invitation.
The National Alliance Party of Kenya threw its weight behind Kibaki
and termed those calling for mass action as cheap publicity seekers,
playing out scenes similar to those that the country witnessed during
the referendum on the Proposed Constitution.
MPs to hold meeting
But former Roads minister Raila Odinga on Monday confirmed that MPs
from across the political divide would hold a meeting at Parliament
Buildings on Tuesday.
He said the meeting, scheduled to start at 10am, would bring
together MPs from LDP, Kanu, Ford Kenya, DP and other parties
represented in the House.
"We have notified the Speaker and all the MPs have also been informed about the meeting,' said Raila.
"Anyone calling for mass action is irresponsible and is looking for cheap political mileage," Lands minister Amos Kimunya said.
Meanwhile the police are yet to decide on whether to allow the planned mass action.
Police spokesman Jaspher Ombati said the decision would be reached
once the street demonstration organisers submit their notification.
"A decision will be reached after they submit the notification letter," he said.
The Shame and Name Corruption Network (Nascon) is the umbrella organisation of the 76 groups.
Nascon coordinator, Mr Geoffrey Birundu and his team are expected at
police headquarters on Tuesday, to deliver their notification to Police
Commissioner, Maj -Gen Hussein Ali.
Birundu said the organisation would release a comprehensive plan of action on Tuesday.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo: Don't Blame the Devil
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo: Don't Blame the Devil
Body_Text:
'NEITHER blindness nor ignorance corrupts people and governments.
They soon realize where the path they have taken is leading them, but
there is an impulse within them, which they do not resist, favoured by
their nature and reinforced by their habits. It continues to propel
them forward as long as they have a remnant of strength. Most see their
ruins before their eyes, but they proceed into it.' - 'Von Ranke.
I direct this opinion to you, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo. On December
22, 2005, you were on The President Speaks, an NTA programme, where you
usually 'address' issues on the state of the nation. Indeed, Cyril
Stober, the anchor, prefaced the programme in that line. A Nigerian
citizen put a simple and clear-cut question to you on the 'third term
agenda.' You prevaricated. You said 'we are talking economy' and
thereafter, went ahead to answer a question you were not asked. You had
earlier responded to a political question on Bayelsa State and later
answered one on the PDP put to you by Cyril Stober, who did all he
could to ask you questions you love to hear because of fear for his job.
Indeed, you want to extend your tenure; you want to go beyond 2007.
That is why you could not answer the question. Unknown to you, your
silence on this burning issue is an answer. It is a familiar pattern in
Nigerian politics. Babangida did the same and so did the late Gen. Sani
Abacha. Across African nations, it's the same story. We had Samuel Doe
of Liberia, Mobutu Sese Seko of Zaire (now DRC), Gnassingbe Eyadema of
Togo. We still have Omar Bongo in Gabon, Blaise Campaore in Burkina
Faso, Paul Biya in Cameroun and Robert Mugabe in Zimbabwe. President
Obasanjo, Nigeria is different from all these countries. Of course, not
in terms of crime, corruption, illiteracy, militarism, dictatorship,
ethnicity, human rights abuses, electoral fraud, judicial recklessness
and abject poverty, but on success of regime extension. In Nigeria,
Yakubu Gowon tried it; he was swept out of power. Interestingly, you
were part of that coup d'etat. Ibrahim Babangida was chased out of
State House in 1993 while Gen. Abacha died in office in 1998. None was
therefore able to enforce regime extension. You cannot get away with a
third term or life presidency in Nigeria. It's like walking to the
political gibbet. You see a destruction, you walk into it. Mind you Mr.
