President, when that time comes, do not blame the devil. This piece
will stand against you.
I put it to you that if after eight years of foisting lawlessness on
the nation you succeed in amending the constitution by subterfuge, as
the electoral fraud that kept you in power in 2003, you are bound to
share the lot of one of those your friends mentioned above. You do not
need any soothsayer or star gazer to tell you this. Nigerian history
says so. Hear Gen. Gowon: "We have to avoid the tendency of leadership
prolonging itself or even overstaying its acceptability- my experience,
that of Generals Babangida and Abacha should be a good lesson for all
leadership in Nigeria today and tomorrow" (Thisday 30/12/05).
But why must you choose the path of political suicide, Chief
Obasanjo? You are an old man. You were a military head of state. Now,
you are the head of government. By 2007, you would have led the nation
for 12 years. What again do you have to offer? Why turn Nigeria into a
banana republic, where anything goes? I appeal to your sense of reason.
Must you forget the God that kept you from the claws of the now
deceased scorpion, Gen. Abacha? The God that saw you through the valley
of the shadow of death? You could have been part of our sad history if
not for God. Those who fought against your incarceration are today your
enemies.
You made them so. One of them was Chima Ubani of blessed memory. He
died as a result of your anti-social policies and politics. Without you
in government, Chima Ubani would have been alive today. What an irony
you made life to be! With you, Nigeria will exist. Without you, the
nation matches on. So, you cannot be the Nigerian messiah. In fact, all
things put together, the nation is not better off than 1999 when you
came on board. We are back to the Abacha era; the darkest period of
Nigerian history. We have countless reasons to believe so. Your love
for arbitrariness and willful scorn for judicial pronouncements have
reduced our humanity and placed us at par with a community of beasts. A
worse time we could not imagine!
Why not leave the Government House honourably in 2007? Let it be on
record that you came and you did your best within your ability. Let the
future in Nigeria write your name in gold. In your time, there was GSM
revolution although 9 out of every 10 Nigerians usually don't have
credit. Teachers and lecturers never had it so good for many years
before you came to Aso Rock in 1999 although inflation has wiped off
those gains. You dealt a devastating blow on the cult system in the
military, where generals from the same section of the country succeeded
themselves in coups after coups and ran the equivalent of a dynasty,
although they possessed the billions of naira stolen from government
houses across the federation. You would not be accused of playing the
ethnic card although your people (Yoruba) are the first and chief
victims of your preference for unconstitutional unitarism and rule of
might as against the rule of law.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Disputes Figures
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Disputes Figures
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni has dismissed minister Nsaba Buturo's
prediction that the NRM will win with 65% of the vote in the February
23 elections.
Museveni, the NRM presidential candidate, said he will win with over
75% because the party is getting support day and night countrywide.
Buturo, the NRM national media team coordinator, told the press on
January 31 that private opinion polls gave the party a 60% lead.
He said the polls were conducted weekly. On February 7, Buturo said NRM's lead "is surging towards 65% of the national vote."
In a press conference broadcast live on the four Bunyoro-based FM
stations, Museveni said, "I don't agree with Hon. Nsaba Buturo's
predictions of 65% for NRM. The percentage has to be more than that, at
least more than 75% because we have massive support."
Addressing journalists at the presidential lodge at Kijungu in Hoima
town, Museveni said, "our support is overwhelming because the Acholi
and Langi did not vote for us in 2001 but now they are going to vote
for us because we have weakened Kony and they are happy."
Museveni, who spoke for more than one-and-a-half hours, said,
"Ugandans cannot vote for Besigye who is a liar and has no programme."
He said Besigye, the FDC presidential candidate, fled to South
Africa to start rebellion against the Movement Government but failed
and came back.
"Nobody can use the gun to fight our government because our army is
strong enough to defend the country and that's why Besigye failed," he
said.
He said the NRM would get a lot of support because West Nile had been worked on as promised in the 2001 manifesto.
Museveni defended himself on allegations that he was a dictator
saying, "a dictator cannot rule Uganda for a third term. that is
propaganda by Besigye and his group."
He said Besigye was greedy for political power.
Museveni said his government would construct three fish processing
factories near Lake Albert to thank Banyoro for their support over the
years.
He denied allegations that he planned to refine petroleum oil at Kaiso Toonya in Mbarara.
Meanwhile, Henry Mukasa, Fred Kayizziand Amlan Tumusiime report that
Museveni said Besigye, as National Political Commissar, failed to
recruit and train cadres for the Movement.
Museveni also said Besigye failed in his first appointment as state minister for internal affairs.
He said he, who concentrated on the army as Chief of Defence Forces, built its capacity.
"I have not seen any big job he (Besigye) did as chief priest of the
Movement. He was supposed to recruit priests (cadres) to spread the
gospel of the Movement. He failed as NPC. Did he recruit Reagan Okumu a
cadre of Kony?" Museveni asked.
He was addressing a huge rally at Hoima Boma ground on Wednesday
evening. Earlier, he addressed rallies at Bweyale trading centre in
Masindi and at Masindi Stadium.
Museveni walked on a yellow carpet on arrival in Hoima and was adorned with a sash of dry banana leaves by a supporter.
He said given Besigye's performance curve while in the Movement, he
and his FDC could not contribute anything to the country. He dissuaded
the residents of Hoima from voting for Besigye or his party candidates.
"Only the Movement can maintain peace and bring development," he said.
He said FDC was in alliance with the LRA rebel group.
He said FDC supporters wanted to create lawlessness by looting when Besigye was arrested but government was in control.

Original_Search_Title: Mayoral Candidates Do Not Address Kampala's Problems
Headline: Uganda: Mayoral Candidates Do Not Address Kampala's Problems
Body_Text:
Kampala — The mayoral candidates for Kampala have been talking in their
campaigns about everything, save for the core issues that affect our
capital city. When the candidates promise to fix the potholes in our
roads, clean up the city or light it up at night, they are dealing with
the effects rather than the causes of our maladies.
We know that banana peels, used polythene bags and other forms of
filth are more likely to be found in slum areas rather than Kololo or
Mutundwe. This means that the primary cause of the city filth are the
slums and slum dwellers, but none of the candidates is telling us how
he proposes to deal with this problem.
Likewise, nobody has talked about the fact that under the 1995
Constitution Kampala was made a district and as such fell to be
governed under the provisions of the Local Government Act like all the
other districts. This means that the capital city was equated in its
running with rural districts like Kapchorwa and Kalangala, although the
demands of an urban area are quite different from those of a rural
set-up.
In practical terms, this means that the administrative structure of
a rural district is replicated for the city with the result that with
Kampala's five divisions, you have to contend with five executive
committees of eight members each in addition to City Hall's own
executive, which all perform purely administrative roles. In other
words, a total of 48 executives help to run the city compared to
Johannesburg's four executives under an executive mayor and seven
directors. When you consider that the same structure is repeated at
parish and village levels, you begin to see the problem of
over-administration we are in.
Like big corporations in the modern world, cities are managed by
results guided by the principles of service delivery, transparency and
accountability. In Johannesburg, for example, all senior management
team members are employed under performance agreements and their
performance is measured against set targets. This is unlike our case
where people in top management at City Hall are like civil servants.
The Act sets up the local government in the city to be based on the
City Council and the city division councils and goes on to provide that
a division shall be equivalent to a sub-county and exercise sub-county
functions.
This is incomprehensible because sub-counties do not have to deal
with such mundane things like sewerage, pavements or street lighting,
which are the sine qua non of a city government. The Act also makes
every local government a body corporate in its own right and in effect
in the case of Kampala, insubordinates the division to City Hall,
thereby laying the ground for friction, which have landed central
division and KCC in court before.
Our local governments generate their revenue from graduated and
property taxes, fees, fines, advertisements and bicycle licenses, and,
according to the Act, very little else apart from grants from the
Central Government. KCC cannot generate independent income outside this
framework, which curtails its ability to deliver. Other cities benefit
a lot from public borrowing through bond issue. KCC should be allowed
to benefit from this method to finance its programmes of a capital
nature.
The problem of planning and enforcement should also be addressed.
According to the Local Government Act, the District Council is the
district planning authority of a district. This means KCC can only plan
for areas within its boundaries, which leaves the areas within the
urban sprawl on their own. The result has been the appearance of
unplanned and uncontrolled developments all around Kampala. There is
need for a metropolitan planning authority whose brief will cover this
sprawl up to Entebbe and Mukono, for example.
The issue of land in the city is also problematic. Originally
Kampala was built on 20sq miles which were slightly increased in 1933
through the compulsory acquisition of mailo land so as to include
Mulago and Makerere within its boundaries. In the mistaken belief that
a capital city must be extensive in area, the boundaries were wildly
extended in modern times to include rural areas from which no revenue
can be raised, but which at the same time demand services from the city
council with the result that the meagre resources are overstretched
even more.
The case of Mengo municipality, which has been created out of Rubaga
division, is of historical interest. In his book The Lake Regions of
Central Africa, Burton referred to "Kibuga, the capital of Uganda" and
quoted Arabs he met at Tabora in 1858, 50 years before the Uganda
protectorate was established as saying "Kibuga is the residence of the
Great Mkama of Uganda". Early visitors to the kibuga were impressed by
the wide and well maintained roads which entered it from all sides.
Stanley wrote: " broad avenues imperial enough in width we came up
to one of these avenues, the ground of which was reddish clay, strongly
mixed with the detritus of hematite. It gave a clear breadth of 100
feet of prepared ground and led by a gradual ascent to the circular
road which made the circuit of the hill outside the palace enclosure".
The circular road described by Stanley is still there surrounding
the Lubiri at Mengo, but the broad avenues or Kabakanjagala, which were
preserved up to 1967, have now been built on.
The kibuga, whose boundaries approximated the present day Rubaga
Division with a population of around 70,000 people by 1900, was
preserved as a "Gombolola y'Omukulu w'Ekibuga" from 1900 up to 1962
when it became Mengo Municipality of Buganda government which lasted up
to 1967.
Instead of returning this historical area to Mengo under the new
regional tier, it was made a municipality of Wakiso district, which
will deprive it of many advantages of being part of the capital.
The whole concept of our city management should be reexamined and in
particular a law specifically to cater for Kampala is long overdue.
Under this law attention should be given to the appointment, not
election, of an executive mayor and four executives all appointed under
performance contracts. A management team should similarly be appointed
and the role of the city council down-sized. The divisions should
become departments of the council while the hub of service delivery
should be by town managers under performance contracts.

Original_Search_Title: I Am the Only One Who Understands East African Federation - Ssebaana
Headline: East Africa: I Am the Only One Who Understands East African Federation - Ssebaana
Body_Text:
Kayunga, Malaba — Democratic Party presidential candidate John Ssebaana Kizito has
said President Yoweri Museveni should not cling on to power using the
excuse of the East African Federation because he is ignorant about it.
Addressing voters at a rally in Kayunga district on Sunday, Ssebaana
said he is the only candidate who understands the East African
Federation, because he participated in its formation in the 1960s.
"When I hear Museveni saying he wants another term because of the
East African Federation, I just laugh. What does he know about this
federation? I am the only candidate who understands it," he said.
Ssebaana was a member of the East African Legislative Assembly between 1967 and 1977.
In December last year, Museveni said he would retire after realising his dream of the East African Federation in 2013.
He has also repeated the same message on several rallies he has held around the country.
Ssebaana scoffed at Museveni's recent announcement to scrap the
local taxes levied by city authorities, saying it would not help him
get votes.
He said if Museveni thinks he would use the removal of taxes to get
votes, he should also wave other taxes like Value Added Tax from which
the central government generates bigger revenue.
On his campaign trail in Kampala last month, Museveni promised to scrap taxes levied on small businesses like market vendors.
Ssebaana pledged to remove the rural -urban divide in education standards by facilitating rural schools.
Ssebaana attacked NRM supporters for spreading the banana wilt
through transferring the dry banana leaves from one area to another.
Kayunga residents gave Ssebaana a bunch of bananas.
Addressing residents of Malaba yesterday, Ssebaana said Museveni had
used tax collecting institutions like the Special Revenue Protection
Services to harass poor people especially in border areas.
He cautioned residents not to follow Museveni's allegations that he
want every Ugandan to become rich in the next five years yet he failed
to achieve it in the past 20 years.

Original_Search_Title: The Journey to Interaction Leadership Training Programme 2005/06
Headline: Sierra Leone: The Journey to Interaction Leadership Training Programme 2005/06
Body_Text:
Freetown — It was in the early part of August 2005 that I came in contact
with the InterAction Leadership Programme 2005/2006 through the local
tabloids, which is organized and sponsored by the British Council and
other helping partners like QOD (Question of Difference), that is based
in U.K. and LEAD (Leadership for Environment and Development), based in
the Republic of South Africa. Our batch is the second to take part in
this development paradigm that is organized for Africa.
What is this InterAction thing all about? At first I thought it was
any talkshop or workshop-like something for a day or two on matters of
sensitization. When I later contacted a colleague of mine whom we were
all at the Njala University College together who briefly told me that
it was geared towards bringing Africans together and trying to change
their perception and myths of their continent and helping them change
their communities to a better state. I was energized and decided to
collect my application form at The British Council, Tower Hill to be
filled and brought back within a specified number of days. This indeed
I did on the last day of submission. On the 31st of August,
shortlisting of Candidates for interviews was done and it was by the
British Council Co-ordinator (BCC) and In-Country Facilitator (ICF). It
was done on merit basis, because I never knew any of them until during
the interview when I had to prove to them that I was fit for the
training. There were about 240 applicants from all spheres of life
(University students, Executives, Chief Executives, Civil servants,
teachers, journalist, etc.), only 28 candidates were shortlisted after
proper scrutiny of our scripts. I was then called to an interview and
came out to be amongst the 14 people needed for the programme. I could
tell you for free that the setup of the panelists was made up of people
of higher calibre and exemplary character that need not be doubted.
They were five in number all from different organizations to make the
process fair. When I was then emailed/ telephoned that I was part of
the fourteen people for this year's InterAction Leadership Programme
(ILP) 2005/06, I was elated.
All 14 candidates were summon one Saturday in October where we were
given the training manual and schedule for the whole programme: - The
In-Country launch which took place at Cape Sierra Hotel (12-13 October
2005) - The Pan African Event (PAE) which took place at South Africa,
Ghana and Kenya occurred at different dates, and participants were
given the privilege to attend only a country ie. 5 for Kenya, 5 for
South Africa and 4 for Ghana in the same year.
- The Modules, which started in January and will end in March 2006
and that, will be climaxed with the awarding of certificates as has
been done with our predecessors.
I want you to know that all of these activities are residential and the British Council settles all the hotel bills.
During our In-Country launch, we discussed a lot of issues
concerning, Qualities of a Good Leader, the six passions and the seven
principles of leadership, the Majesty of Africa etc. which will be
brought to the public in more publications to come. This event was
climaxed on the last day (13th October 2005) with the formal launching
of the programme in the presence of the British Council Director for
Sierra Leone, Mr. Tom Walsh and a host of other important dignitaries
and former participants. That was when I started changing my perception
towards Africa and my country Sierra Leone. Earlier on I thought Sierra
Leone in particular and Africa in general was no good a place to dwell
in until after I started going through this powerful programme and its
manual that I thought I needed Africa more than before and should be
part of it to develop it.
The Pan African Event (PAE) for South Africa which I was part of
took place on the 8-10 November 2005. It did host about140 participants
from 18 African countries and a European country- U.K., plus the
facilitators led by Charlie Irvine a South African base in the U.K. The
Sierra Leonean participants were Emil Rogers, Ayesha Sankoh, Marie
Benjamin, Violet Kawa and me. It was a real good team to represent
Sierra Leone, trust me.
Five of us left for Ghana on the 1st November 2005 to visa our
passports for Johannesburg, South Africa on board the SLOK airline that
morning and arrived at the Kotoka International airport early. The five
of us boarded a taxi to the British Council office where they later
took us in one of their vehicles to the Coconut Groove Regency Hotel at
the back of the Ghana Immigration Office. Within the few days that we
were waiting for our visas, we made visits to our High Commissioner to
Ghana, Commissioner Alie Bangura, we visited their famous business
centres (Makola, Art Centre, Melcom, etc.). We ate a lot of their local
dishes especially 'Banku and Palava source' which was cherished by one
of my best friends, Emil Rogers, Commercial Manager at the Lungi
Airport.
We got our passports back on Saturday and left for Johannesburg at
around 2:45am on Sunday on board the South African Airways. We took
about 6 hours to our destination and the flight was indeed comfortable.
Upon arrival we were received at the airport to our hotel, The
Kopanong Hotel and Conference Centre at Benoni, Jo'burg about some
20minutes drive from the Airport. Indeed I was impressed with the
country as it was like an European country in Africa in terms of
infrastructure and all. Damn! Their lanes are wider, they have a lot of
flyovers, well-structured chalets, cold weather, you name it.
Upon arrival at the hotel we each signed for our rooms which were
all chalets and well furnished with heaters, freezers, air
conditioners, TVs, chocolates, tea kettles with all its assessories,
shampoo and after shower creams. It was like paradise on earth.
The food was another side of the story. We had food of all sorts
starting from a healthy breakfast, buffet lunch and dinner, ice creams,
yorghurts, caramels, apples, guavas, bananas, pears and a series of tea
breaks; all types of drinks of ones choice (Shandy, Lemonade, Coke,
Fanta, Windhoek beer, Heineken, Castle lager, etc.) throughout the day
and some part of the night. We had a well balanced diet. The day we
arrived (6th November) we straightaway started interacting with other
participants from the other countries like theNigerians, senegalese,
Ugandans, Ghanians, etc., as if we had met them for ages. It was just
too natural.
At around 3:00pm on the 7th of November 2005, we were supplied our
packages containing our bags, note pads, a pen, evaluation
form,biography form, next of kin form, a copy of "Looking Back Going
Forward" book, a South African DVD and a name tag. The first night (7th
November 2005) was a welcoming night where we had all the participants,
the facilitators, the British Council team of organizers headed Hugh
Moffatt and David Green who is the British Council Director General. It
was such an informal and wonderful occasion. There was real interaction
with no barrier.
Foods and drinks were things not to mention cause they were in abundance. We were like family at Kopanong.
For the next few days of the training proper (8-10 November), we
were all put into groups of about 8 or 9 participants of different
countries. On the night of day two, the Cultural Night, there was a
real display of real African Cultural, as we had the Ethiopians brewing
their local coffee, Kenyans clad in their Masai regalia, the Sierra
Leoneans in their Cotton cloth, Sudanese, not to talk of our host,
South Africans (especially madam Beauty Sekety) who ceased the show
with her power costume. The occasion was like Africa has come together.
Can you imagine Eritrea and Ethiopia all interacting. Everybody danced
with each other and guess what, we were dancing to pure African songs
brought by participants.
Topics discussed during those three powerful days were: The Majesty
of Africa (wall of greatness), where each country had to paste on the
wall their strengths; Appreciative inquiry (AI) and its assumptions,
the chaos of communication, the 6 passions, 7 principles of ILP were
again discussed, the TICing model and preparations for modules 1,2,3
which as I said earlier it will be explained in subsequent
publications. On the last night of the programme, we were taken to a
Restaurant called BOMA in which we spent a very healthy send - off
evening with a South African female music artist, Tu Nokwe, who
entertained us. On a whole we had sometime to visit their shopping
malls like East Gate and East Rand. These places were well structured
with their banks in them and security structures in place. Indeed
friends, South Africa are a bigger bro.
We left for Ghana on the 11th November at around 10:00pm local time
where we spent about two days since we were to leave on Sunday
afternoon. In Accra due to some flight problems, we had some delays and
we could only leave at around 10:00pm on board the SLOK Air line. On a
whole, the PAE was a challenging and interesting event that is
difficult to erase from our minds.
Then We started the Module 1 which was on the 16th January 2006 and
ended on the 19th January 2006 in which we had our In- Country
Facilitator (ICF) Dr.
Roland Buck and another facilitator from Ghana, Amelia Arthur
steering the boat. These two wonderful children of Africa introduced
powerful topics to us like Conflict mnemonics and their resolutions,
Development Paradigms, TICing model, Affirmative Disruptive Questions
and Listening chairs. These were all interesting topics that will help
transform Sierra Leoneans in their different Environments or
Communities. These topics were so powerful that they made us all to
start changing our old and negative perception of Sierra Leone and
Africa to a more positive one.
Friends, Interaction is one of the best programmes ever brought to
us Africans to help reclaim the glory Africa once had during the
pre-colonial era. A programme like this cannot be swept under the
carpet without me saying it. Those who were unfortunate should be
opportune to have a taste of it from the participants so that we all
can be positive change agents in our little communities, be it a
church, a mosque, governmental and non-governmental organizations,
football clubs, the SLAG community, etc. As the Hasdic saying goes,
"The man who thinks he can live without others is mistaken; the one who
thinks others can't live without him is even deluded".
And indeed that is manifested by the collaboration between both
Africa and the British Council, hence our first principle - Africa for
Africa through collaboration.
British Council, once more I say thanks to you for your gumption.
Kudos to you and all your partners, may the collaboration continue.

Original_Search_Title: Balala And Taib Renew Rivalry
Headline: Kenya: Balala And Taib Renew Rivalry
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Political rivals Najib Balala and Taib Ali Taib have renewed their
war of words with each taking a different view on the recently formed
Coast Leaders Forum.
While Mombasa Mayor Taib, the forum's interim treasurer, supports
the organisation chaired by Bahari MP Joe Khamisi, Mr Balala, the MP
for Mvita, said the forum was aimed at undermining other elected
leaders.
The two were speaking in different meetings at the Coast over the weekend.
"Mr Khamisi is using the forum to nurture his own political
ambitions and is bitter after missing a Cabinet position that he had
been promised," Mr Balala said after commissioning a Sh225,000 borehole
at Guraya in his constituency.
Mr Balala said that during the referendum campaigns, Mr Khamisi was
working with the Banana team and even went to receive President Kibaki
at the Moi Airport when his colleagues from the Coast were in Lamu
drumming up support for the Orange team.
Mvita seat
Although Mr Taib announced last year that he would challenge Mr
Balala for the Mvita seat, the two appeared to have narrowed their
differences as both campaigned against the proposed constitution.
But last Monday, Mr Balala joined seven Coast MPs at the Nyali Beach
Hotel and voted out Mr Khamisi as the chairman of the Coast
Parliamentary Group. They replaced him with Mwatate MP Marsden Madoka
in an acting capacity.
"We decided to replace Mr Khamisi not because we don't want a Coast
political party but because it was him who refused the idea a few
months ago saying he was the Liberal Democratic Party's national
vice-chairman. What has changed since?" Mr Balala asked.
But Mr Taib supported Mr Khamisi's bid to launch the leaders forum saying that leadership was not the preserve of sitting MPs.
Speaking at Mikindani primary school in Changamwe, when he toured
projects funded under the Local Authority Transfer Fund, the mayor said
Coast Province lacked strong leadership and that the only way to change
the situation was to have the Coast Leaders Forum.
He vowed to face head-on any leader who wanted to divide the Coastal people along tribal or ethnic lines.
Cabinet post
When contacted, Mr Khamisi denied claims that he had been promised a
Cabinet post by President Kibaki. He said he formed the forum to seek
views on whether to form a Coast political party before the 2007
General Election.
According to him, the forum enjoyed backing from Coast professionals
abroad "and with or without the MPs' participation, it will be
officially launched this Saturday in Kilifi.

Original_Search_Title: A Simple Guide to Becoming Part of the MTV Generation
Headline: Rwanda: A Simple Guide to Becoming Part of the MTV Generation
Body_Text:
Kigali — With the advent of satellite TV (DSTV and MNET) and East African TV
in East Africa, many young city folks have become fully-fledged members
of the MTV generation.
Being a member of this generation means that, unlike most folks, you fully appreciate the fact that we live in a global village.
You are aware that there are a lot of important things happening on
the world stage and you want to be a part of this. No longer shall you
confine yourself to the banal events unfolding in tiny banana republics
like Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, Kenya or Tanzania.
You are a big player, so you think. Big and only concerning yourself
with happenings on the world stage. That's why MTV is your number one
channel of choice. With MTV you cannot go wrong. You will be the first
one to know whether it is actually true that Paris Hilton recently
adopted an orphaned puppy (oh, how sweet!).
If you are lucky MTV might even inform you whether this was a
one-off or whether she intends to make it a habit and adopt orphaned
kittens as well, which would mean she is on her way to becoming the
patron saint of orphaned animals. Thanks to MTV, you will be one of the
few East Africans who can confidently state, without blinking, the
number of bullets that have pierced through 50 Cent's massive frame.
If you are the cerebral kind, then MTV is the right place to be. You
will be able to learn from Beyonce herself what the term 'bootylicious'
means. It does not get any better than this, does it?
To those miserables who are yet to join this generation, now that
you have been made aware of some of the benefits obtained from becoming
a member of this 'exclusive' generation, you are likely to develop an
itchy feeling. This should be no cause for alarm.
According to a leading psychiatrist who prefers to remain anonymous,
this itchy feeling is one of the symptoms manifested by someone who is
very anxious to join this generation. Now that we have established your
intention to join this generation, let's get started by sprucing up
your credentials so that you too can, one day, walk with a spring in
your step.
There are just a few things that you have to adjust. For starters is
your name. Aisha just won't do. You may ask why? All I can say is that
no bona fide member of the MTV generation ever called herself that, not
even in her sleep. That's why you will have to "pimp up" Aisha a bit,
more like Alicia. Neither will long monikers like Kushemererwa cut it.
If you are the bearer of such a name seriously consider going for the
uber-chic version; Kush (pronounced in the same way as cash).
Henceforth, any attempts to call you by your old discarded name will
routinely fall on deaf ears.
Having taken care of the name bit, you are half-way to being the
envy of your peers. Next, there is the small but important aspect of
phraseology. In the past people with a wide vocabulary may have been
greeted with a slight bow of the head. Not any more, suggests the MTV
lot.
These days the fewer words you use in putting across your ideas the
'cooler' you are and therefore the higher the chances of becoming a
member of this generation. You guessed right: using curse words is very
welcome thank you.
Also, lace your sentences with words like beef, tripping, blazed,
game, crib, diss, disrespect, rep and the like. More importantly, make
it a point to end your every line with the rhetorical question "You
know what I mean?" Now, it's likely that at most times very few people
are likely to 'know what you mean'.
Well all you can say to them is, tough on you. Hardly your fault
that the poor gits can't cope with living in a global village, is it?

Original_Search_Title: Tribalism Kills Humour
Headline: Kenya: Tribalism Kills Humour
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Our national life, fired up by a fierce political monomania, has become unbearably humourless.
Any lover of literature can see what great damage this trend occasions on satire.
Let us apply the word satire to any work in prose or verse that uses
wit to ridicule or expose vice or folly, and mocking lesser faults,
such as vanity, infatuation, or even tribal idiosyncrasies.
As human beings, we often allow into our innermost sanctums only
those friends with whom we enjoy complete and uncensored camaraderie -
the kind of pals who'll give us their ear, enjoy our hilarity, and take
no offence at our satire. Before our tribal instincts became
over-activated, anecdotes flourished freely. No Kikuyu, for instance,
fumed much if lightly reminded his affinity to the shilling; no Coastal
would bother to go physical just because someone recycled the old joke
about waiting lazily under a coconut tree for the fruit to drop on its
own.
With unique lakeside mirth, most Luos would laugh away the one about
depleting a mountain of "kuon" by sniffing the aroma only, in absence
of enough fried fish to go around.
You could even have afforded to freely enjoy the Somali with his
camel and the Maasai Moran over his carefree lifestyle without
incurring bodily harm. But now, in contemporary Kenya, even innocuous
stereotyping can no longer be seen in any other context but ethnic
supremacist. Come to think of it: who would be tickled by Shakespeare's
'Merchant of Venice' in the absence of the pound-of-flesh activities
attributed so hilariously to the Jew Shylock?
Clearly, our ever-growing tribal barriers are exacting a price not
only on the larger national development, but also on our intellectual
infrastructure. Essayists have extolled the virtues of well-intentioned
humour.
It was William Irving who so aptly stated that "honest good humour
is the oil and wine of a merry meeting and there is no jovial
companionship equal to that where jokes are small and the laughter
abundant."
For instance, just prior to, and immediately after the fruity
constitutional referendum, politicians, ever the heroes of the
destitute masses, crisscrossed the landscape spewing unsavoury epithets
across their respective tribes' fences, and recruiting vote-wielding
disciples.
Such insults, not unlike commercial advertisements, were craftily
packaged to play upon our ethnic susceptibilities. How we gulp down the
politicians' ominous tutorials hook, line and sinker, may be itself a
good measure of our collective national IQ. So easily are our tribal
firewalls insulted that when the late doyen of satire, Wahome Mutahi,
constantly referred to the 'Mogotio' goat as the embodiment of
feeble-mindedness, some residents were so greatly irked that they wrote
letters to the editor demanding that he uses a metaphor from Central
province instead. Come on, o ye Kenyans! Oughtn't we to loosen up a
bit? Oscar Wilde said, "There is only one thing in the world worse than
being talked about, and that is not being talked about."
Such antipathy has been the unfortunate trend in this writer's
lifetime and its spectacular success has had tragic consequences on the
mutuality of different communities. As things stand, we hardly reserve
any admiration for any other community outside our own, and literature,
satire in particular, gains nothing from the rich ethnic mosaic that is
Kenya. In this same context, we do not sufficiently regard our genuine
national heroes such as Paul Tergat (athletics) Wangari Maathai (Nobel
Peace Prize), Koitalel or the Mau Mau (freedom fighters) among many
others because their veneration may seem as exaltation of certain
section of society to much discomfort of the others. More so when these
heroes are not unequivocal on their banana or orange allegiance!
Many think Mutahi the humourist left a vacant throne that cannot be
filled. I beg to differ. Potential satirists abound, but have no one to
target for fear of offending! The overbearing pseudo-political
atmosphere is forcing into inactivity- nay, mortifying-the creative
faculties of our minds, instead of inspiring their development. Even
the innocent term 'tribe' is falling out of favour because it has
suddenly acquired connotations of backwardness.
It is during the tranquil Solomonic era that ancient Israel attained
her highest level of intellectual discourse. Perhaps we could still
redeem the time, and take advantage of Kenya's relative peace to
inspire scholarly pursuits. And forgetting our hard-faced politicians,
and their hate and ravenousness to match, let us do it with a bit of
humour!
* The writer teaches at Moi University

Original_Search_Title: Col. Besigye is a Deserter, Says Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Col. Besigye is a Deserter, Says Museveni
Body_Text:
Lira — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has described Forum for Democratic Change
(FDC) presidential candidate Col. Kizza Besigye as a deserter who does
not deserve to lead Uganda.
Launching his third-term campaign in Apac district, Museveni urged
residents to deny Besigye votes for deserting Ugandans and fleeing to
South Africa.
"Besigye went to South Africa after he was defeated and now he is
trading lies. He ran and left us here fighting LRA rebels," Museveni
said. "Now he is back, enjoys the smooth tarmac and new hotels built by
us. These are the type of people who want to reap where they have not
sown. Besigye sowed desertion and should reap desertion at the ballot
box," he said.
Museveni arrived in Apac with pomp. Women carried baskets of flowers
which they poured on the ground for Museveni's convoy to drive on.
Tourism minister Jovino Akaki dressed Museveni with a yellow coat decorated in banana fibres.
Museveni said Besigye was an accomplice in the gruesome Barlonyo massacre.
"In fact Besigye and his group were on the side of those who caused
the Barlonyo massacre. He should tell us the circumstances under which
Opoka died," Museveni said.

Original_Search_Title: Plateau Accounts: Lar Appeals to FG
Headline: Nigeria: Plateau Accounts: Lar Appeals to FG
Body_Text:
Jos — PIONEER PDP National Chairman and first Civilian Governor of
Plateau State, Chief Solomon Lar has appealed to the Federal Government
to wade into the freezing of the accounts of the state government by
the Economic and Financial Crime Commission (EFCC) saying that the
commission is over-stepping its mandate.
Chief Lar who broke his silence on the issue while receiving wife of
the state Governor, Mrs Valentina Dariye yesterday, said it was
regrettable that the EFCC would go ahead to freeze the accounts of a
state government even after a Federal High Court had ruled that it had
no power to investigate the accounts of a state government.
He said going to Lagos to obtain the order of the Federal High Court
in Lagos to freeze the accounts of the Plateau State Government
amounted to an abuse of the court process which was at variance with
the rule of law.
Lar who addressed members of the National Association of Polytechnic
Students (North-Central zone) who held a peaceful demonstration to
protest the freezing of the accounts which had made their parents
unable to send them back to school, said it was clear that the accounts
were frozen to cause disaffection for the state government.
"The aim is to destroy Plateau State. They think that by freezing
the accounts the civil servants would rise against the governor so as
to bring the government down. Nuhu Ribadu has said that before the end
of January, Dariye will no longer be on the seat. This is February and
Dariye is still on the seat.
"But Plateau people are reasonable. They cannot be intimidated or
instigated to impeach the governor. This is our government and we put
him (Dariye) there. This is not a banana republic so everything has to
be done in accordance with due process", Chief Lar said.
He added that it was clear that a repeat of what happened in Bayelsa
and Oyo states was planned for the state but insisted that due process
should be respected no matter how strongly one feels about a matter, in
the interest of democracy.
He said the intervention of the Federal Government in the matter is
vital so that the state workers are not made to suffer unduly as a
result of delay in the payment of their salaries which may lead to
chaos and confusion.
The students led by the National Vice President (North Central) of
the National Association of Polytechnic Students Prince Paul Yakubu
condemned the freezing of the state accounts vowing to march to Abuja
in protest if the EFCC fails to defreeze the accounts as it is bringing
hardship on the entire people of the state.

Original_Search_Title: Liberian Refugee Killed in Cote d'Ivoire
Headline: Liberian Refugee Killed in Cote d'Ivoire
Body_Text:
Dupu-Yoboke, Ivory Coast — As report of fighting intensified between an Ivorian kru man and
the Moussin people from Burkina Faso residing in the Ivory Coast, a
Liberian identified as Kpanwin Wreh, has been killed in the Ivorian
town of Dupu-Yoboke.
During a visit of our correspondent to the Tabou Region, some of the
Liberian refugees informed him that Kpanwin Wreh, was arrested by one
of the Ivorian Kru man while trekking from Tabou to another nearby
village when he was accused of being a rebel.
After a lengthy argument with the Ivorian Kru man, the late Kpanwin Wreh, insisted that he was never a rebel.
The people of Dupu-Yoboke took him in their town forcely where he
was reportedly shot with a single barrel gun twice in his head.
The body of the Liberian refugee was then thrown among the banana
bushes, where after a day he was found lying dead with the bullets
wounds in his head.
Meanwhile, the incident was then reported to authorities of the
United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) Tabou Region
sub-office, who then contacted the police, one Liberian refugee added.
Those involved have already been arrested and taken to Tabou for investigation by Ivorian police officers.

Original_Search_Title: The Shining Sonrise to Soar Higher
Headline: Rwanda: The Shining Sonrise to Soar Higher
Body_Text:
Kigali — When, in 2001, the school was started, little did they know that
one day, it would be at the helm of the academic giants in of the
country. From humble beginnings, Sonrise Boarding Primary School is a
force to reckon with. A visit to Ruhengeri gives you a chance to have
the feel and touch of uniqueness in this remote district that produced
the best school in the 2005 primary leaving examinations in whole
country.
Sonrise Boarding Primary School is only a thirty minutes' ride north
east of Ruhengeri town. Surrounded by hills, the school is full of life
and serene. The rambles of children as they excitedly roam and wander
during break time, smartly attired in their white shirts and khakis,
around a school compound so green and tidy, with the shrieks and
whistles of the tree branches, teachers exuding that mission-like look
and focus, so purposeful and dedicated. The children's mantras and
chants blend so blissfully with the surrounding environment to give an
aura and air of how the little angels will cron and resonate in their
special haven in heaven which the Lord has prepared for them. This is a
model school of excellence.
Discipline in this school is so paramount, reigns and rules supreme in all that pupils and teachers do.
Innocent Zawadi, the Head teacher reveals that Sonrise Boarding
Primary School was born in the mind and vision of Bishop John Rucyahana
in 2001. Bishop Rucyahana's vision was to help orphans who lost their
parents during the 1994 Genocide and the children who were destabilised
during the insurgency in Ruhengeri Northern province.
Three quarters of the school children are sponsored by the school
while the rest are self-sponsored. "Being a Christian-based school,
cases of indiscipline are almost unheard of," says the director of the
school. The strict management of time is an austerity that I also
witness on the day of my visit. As soon as the bell ending the break
goes, the pupils swiftly and nobly stream into their respective
classrooms silently
"Keeping time is an instrumental aspect here. Sonrise teachers make
sure that they don't misuse time as far as teaching is concerned. The
syllabi are completed in time which helps pupils to have ample time for
intensive revision. With such an approach, good results are assured,"
reveals the Headteacher.
Beyond the ordinary academic work and curriculum, the school has a
variety of extra curricula activities which keep the school light
burning and rising. Weekly debates, quiz, music, drama, dance and
sports competitions are some of the areas that are emphasized.
The teaching staff are drawn from all over East Africa and the
students too cross borders from Congo, Uganda, Kenya and a few whose
parents work in the country from India.
The School manages to keep staff members happy by giving them a
handsome package. They do this using the collections from the few
pupils who pay school fees and the rest of the collection is used to
buy scholastic materials and meet other school needs. Besides, the
school is sponsored by Sonrise Foundation from the U.S.A.
The orphaned children, some of who are victims of the Genocide, are
traumatised which in most cases affects their performance. To assuage
on this poignant and distressing situation, a fully fledged guidance
and counselling department was set up to rehabilitate and rebuild the
children's confidence and esteem. The sessions usually take place on
Mondays and Fridays.
"One aspect that has made Sonrise Boarding Primary school to become
a Rwandan academic inselberg is the dedication of the school
administrators to mentor and fully guide their children, right from
primary one up to the last year of the primary section-primary six.
This puts the learners at par with both the psychological and
environmental learning requirements. Through debates, the languages are
improved, confidence and esteem strengthened and the general expression
abilities of pupils put on the high." confides Innocent Zawadi.
But all is not done and gone. More is still in the pipeline.
Expansion, growth and development of Sonrise is taking a new dimension.
The sky seems to be the limit. Having built a strong foundation for the
primary section, now the management is focusing its attention and
directing its resources towards building a very well equipped secondary
section. And the plan is that even a tertiary institution will, with
time, be established to make a strong academic hub, an intellectual
complex of a kind.
Caring for the young pupils, whose parents are far away, has always
been a big challenge to many school administrators. Not so with
Sonrise. For every fifty pupils, there is a well trained matron who
gives parental care, moral guidance and general supervision. But even
those pupils in the upper primary are given some young children to take
care of like they were their own junior brothers or sisters. In the
evenings and over the weekends, they inspect their beds, clothes,
bathing and health.
Presently, the school has a total enrolment of 440 pupils among whom
80 were admitted in primary one this year. "Sonrise's brilliant
performance this year has encouraged many parents to bring their
children here. And we promise to keep the standards." says the
headmaster with an aura of pride and satisfaction, adding that, "We
have already hit our ceiling level of 450 pupils. We cannot recruit
anymore."
Nevertheless, this should not worry parents who wish to see their
children go through the hands of this dream school. "For us to be able
to increase on our total threshold, we have a plan of putting up
another storeyed building which will help us expand on the room
available both for classes and accommodation", says the headmaster.
Because a healthy mind dwells in a healthy body, the school hires
the services of two permanent nurses. The sick children are treated
from the school clinic but serious cases are referred to Ruhengeri
Hospital or King Faisal Hospital in Kigali.
Good health and good diet are real bedfellows which is what is
happening at the school. In the morning, pupils take porridge with
milk. At ten in the morning, pineapples and red bananas are served
while the main menu is mainly composed of Rice, beans, beef and water.
This, no wonder, explains the good looking pupils, astute and sharp at
what they do.
When all the future prospects of the school are accomplished,
Sonrise is bound to abound as an academic giant. And given the fact
that in only five years they have managed to make it to the top, given
the inventiveness and dedication of both the staff and management, one
can only be optimistic that their dream of remaining the cream is not
far from reality.

Original_Search_Title: Vice-President Quizzed On Anglo Leasing Deals
Headline: Kenya: Vice-President Quizzed On Anglo Leasing Deals
Body_Text:
Nairobi — For the first time in the country's history, a sitting Vice-President has been questioned over corruption.
Vice-President Moody Awori was this week expected to record a
statement on what he knows about the multi-billion Anglo Leasing
scandal, following his interview last week by investigators from the
Kenya Anti-Corruption Commission.
His questioning came as a human rights organisation lodged
complaints against Mr Awori, two senior Cabinet ministers, a former
minister and the Head of the Civil Service, Mr Francis Muthaura, for
allegedly violating the Public Officers Ethics Act.
The Kenya National Commission on Human Rights claims the five
beached the act by "knowingly giving false or misleading information
about the Anglo Leasing deals.
It handed its complaint to the Parliamentary Service Commission,
chaired by Speaker Francis ole Kaparo on January 26, while a complaint
about Mr Muthaura, a civil servant, was sent to the Public Service
Commission on the same date.
The two ministers are Mr David Mwiraria (Finance) Mr Kiraitu Murungi
(now Energy, previously Justice) and the former minister is Mr Chris
Murungaru (Internal Security and later, Transport.)
The complaints were laid even as the KACC detectives interviewed
President Kibaki's deputy in his Jogoo House office, on January 24.
The investigators recorded details of their discussion with Mr
Awori, and told him they would return to his office this week to record
his formal statement.
Officials close to Mr Awori and KACC director Aaron Ringera confirmed the meeting took place.
It follows a series of exclusive disclosures in the Sunday Nation
and Daily Nation about the Anglo Leasing scandal and a number of
similar deals.
The detectives wanted Mr Awori to explain what he knew about the
Sh2.7 billion contract to supply a terrorist-proof passport system to
the Immigration Department and the people who negotiated and sealed the
deal.
They questioned him because Immigration was under his control as
Home Affairs minister when the deal was sealed in the first months of
the Kibaki presidency.
The passport scandal was first exposed in Parliament on April 2, 2004.
It involved a Sh800 million project to supply a system of
tamper-proof passports, initially under the Moi administration. The
project was revised when Narc took over and it was then expanded and
costed at Sh2.7 billion.
According to the Public Accounts Committee and the Auditor General,
payments were approved and made without Parliamentary approval.
The V-P becomes the first high-ranking member of President Kibaki's
Government to face KACC investigators in the renewed hunt for the
faceless individuals behind the scandals, involving contracts worth
billions of shillings.
In addition to Mr Murungi, Mr Mwiraria and Mr Murungaru, other key
figures lined up for questioning local businessmen Jimmy Wanjigi and
Deepak Kamani, as well as President Kibaki's personal assistant, Mr
Alfred Getonga.
The two businessmen were named in Parliament in April 2004 as being
linked to the scandal. They have previously been interviewed by KACC
but were not taken to court.
Mr Mwiraria, Mr Murungi and Mr Murungaru have publicly stated they
would present themselves to KACC investigators once they were summoned.
But Mr Awori brushed off the question when confronted by journalists
at the Kenya School of Monetary Studies who asked him about the
possibility of being interviewed.
Mr Awori was unavailable for comment last night. His Press officer,
Mr Brown Kutswa, said he would issue a statement later but had not done
so by the time we went to press.
The Nation has confirmed that the KACC head, Mr Ringera, has written
to former ethics permanent secretary John Githongo, asking him return
to Kenya to help unravel the scandals which have haunted the
three-year-old Kibaki administration.
Officials close to Mr Ringera said Mr Githongo had confirmed
receiving the invitation, dated January 24, and was willing to return.
But he did not state when he would return and on what terms, the
officials added.
Mr Githongo resigned on February 7 last year, while on an official
trip to Europe with Mr Ringera. He has been staying in Britain.
KACC began its latest investigation into the Anglo Leasing deals
following revelations by Mr Githongo that in November he sent a dossier
to President Kibaki and Mr Ringera alleging all the contracts the Narc
administration entered with the shadowy firm were corrupt.
The dossier named key members of President Kibaki's Government
linked to the suspect deals and detailed the roles they played in
signing and paying out vast "commitment fees" to unknown businessmen,
and subsequent attempts to cover-up the affair.
The contracts in focus were Anglo Leasing's Sh2.7 billion passports
projects, a Sh4.1 billion deal to build forensic laboratories for the
CID and the purchase of a Navy ship for Sh4.1 billion, among others.
The Anglo Leasing scandals hit the headlines after Mr Githongo
accused key ministers of complicity in the multi million-shilling
schemes involving contracts for a company that did not exist.
In a series of exposes by the Sunday Nation and Daily Nation last
week it was revealed that Mr Githongo believed the controversial
contracts were being pushed by President Kibaki's inner circle to raise
money for political purposes; to help pay for the Banana Yes-vote
campaign in the referendum on the proposed Constitution and for
campaigning for Narc in the run up to the next General Election.
The revelations have precipitated one of the worst crises for the Government since Mr Kibaki became President.
Officials close to Mr Ringera said that in his letter to Mr
Githongo, the KACC chief said he would personally arrange for his
security if he agreed to return home to shed more light on the contents
of his report.
Should Mr Githongo refuse to return to Nairobi because he feared for
his safety, the KACC chief was willing to send detectives to the UK to
record his statement, the officials said.
On Friday Mr Deepak Kamani, his father Mr Chamanlal Kamani and Mr
Wanjigi were ordered to surrender their passports to KACC, following
fears they were planning to leave Kenya before they could be
interviewed about Anglo Leasing and a number of similar deals.
If they left, it could prejudice investigations, KACC told the Kibera magistrates' court in Nairobi.
The commission said it was investigating various projects, and had
found that the Kamanis and Mr Wanjigi were involved in several
contracts.
They told the magistrate that the wanted businessmen had gone
underground and detectives had failed to trace them in their offices
and known homes.
And in yet another twist to the complex dealings, Mr Murungaru on
Sunday announced that the Sh4.1 billion Navy ship deal was expressly
authorised by President Kibaki in June 2003.
No procurement of such a magnitude could have gone ahead without the President's signature, he said.
Mr Ringera has already ordered Mr Murungaru to account for his
wealth and that of his immediate family members and his business
associates.
Following a public outcry after the scandals were exposed, President
Kibaki sacked the then Treasury PS Mr David Magari, former Home Affairs
PS Mr Sylvester Mwaliko and several senior officers in the Treasury and
the Attorney-General's chambers.
All the officials were later taken to court but their cases are yet to be concluded.
However, names of the principal architects of the scandal have remained a mystery.

Original_Search_Title: OP Had Its Own Budget Like State House - Diangamo
Headline: Zambia: OP Had Its Own Budget Like State House - Diangamo
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Continued from Saturday
Diangamo: Early 1995 during the time Honourable Penza, the late was
Minister of Finance, these issues of inadequacy funds for the security
came to our concern. So early 1995, the then director general Mr Xavier
Chungu had a discussion with Ministry of Finance on these projects and
proposed how he could go around meeting these obligations.
Court: You attended all these meetings?
Diangamo: I did once.
Court: Talk about the meeting you attended?
Diangamo: The Ministry of Finance told him (Chungu) to put in
written form and he did this on 13 April 1995 outlining those issues.
Then it is at this point that the minister consulted officials in the
ministry. I was there in the meeting and the Minister of Finance wrote
back to the director general approving his proposal. He wrote a letter
on 21, May 1995. At that point, the director general requested some of
our colleagues specifically Mrs Stella Chibanda who was deputy director
loans and investment to work out the modalities with Mr James Mtonga.
In that consultative process, Zanaco MD was Mr Kaitano Chungu.
Thereafter the next meeting I recall attending was in 1996 where we
were now being briefed on this facility. The cardinal point was that
this facility should be kept away from the eyes of the IMF. The reason
was that the facility will enable government meet payments to strategic
security programmes that were not included in the IMF programme.
Kapumpa: Were you told how the facility was utilised?
Diangamo: Before, we were briefed how it would operate. It was that
the Ministry of Finance was going to be responsible for the facility
and that the director general of OP was going to borrow money from
Zanaco on behalf of government and that the Zanaco MD should be
providing funds upon requested by the Ministry of Finance.
Kapumpa: Were you told how the repayment would be made?
Diangamo: Yes, Your Honour. The repayments were going to be made
through the same fashion as the borrowing instructions. When say the
Ministry of Home Affairs requested for some funds, they would approach
the Secretary to the Treasury or the permanent secretary Ministry of
Finance and articulate the problem. The Ministry of Finance had two
options. The first one was to look at the general revenues and if the
budget line in Ministry of Home Affairs allowed that additional
expenditure. The second option is where the budget line is exhausted
meaning that we can't pay out. At that point, we have no option but to
give instructions to Zanaco to release the money. We will ask the
Ministry of Finance permanent secretary or secretary to the treasury to
get in touch with the OP director general requesting him to arrange
borrowing of the required amount from Zanaco. The Secretary to the
Treasury or the permanent secretary will inform Zanaco MD that these
funds must be made available. Th e director general will make
arrangements how this money will be delivered to institutions needing
the money.
Kapumpa: Were you involved in what you have discussed?
Diangamo: Yes.
Kapumpa: Once, twice or many occasions?
Diangamo: I will say on many occasions.
Kapumpa: Were you aware of the State House arrangement concerning what you have discussed?
Diangamo: I was aware and I was involved in some of the transactions.
Kapumpa: Cite once instance in which you were involved in relation to State House?
Diangamo: I recall that in 2001, I was acting permanent secretary
budget and the Secretary to the Treasury called me and informed me that
State House needed some funding. I told him that I will check the
balances. Afterwards, I went back to inform him that there were no
funds. There was a reason that revenue had not reached our account.
However, I informed him State House had not exhausted its provision and
it was on paper. Then we decided to go to Zanaco and gave State House
some money. I contacted the director general after that then the MD
Zanaco so that we could facilitate and that happened. State House
received the money.
Kapumpa: Do you know how State House received the money?
Diangamo: I became aware.
Kapumpa: How?
Diangamo: I asked the DG and MD that have you delivered the money, and they said yes the money had been taken.
Kapumpa: Did you find out who delivered the money?
Diangamo: Yes, I was aware, it was the MD Zanaco Mr Musonda.
Kapumpa: Could you tell the court how money was paid back?
Diangamo: Your Lord, it's important to note that the OP had its own
budget like State House and Ministry of Finance had its own budget. The
budget for the special division was for them to meet their operational
requirements. The Ministry of Finance budget was basically into
components funding appropriate ministries. These funds were controlled
by the permanent secretary financial management. The second component
was for activities of national character such as pensions, debt
service, capital to parastatals and counterpart funds. So what would
happen was that the DG would borrow from Zanaco to undertake his
operation pending funding from Ministry of Finance. In other words,
when we instructed Zanaco to give DG the money, he promised that he
will pay back. The third one was when national requirement came into
place. This money will come through either the ministries or
institutions came with special requests for financial assistance and
the Ministry of Finance has to provide th at funding. For example, when
we need to deploy soldiers to the border post and we need to pay ZNBC
employees who are on go slow. If Ministry of Finance cannot afford to
pay salaries, we will then tell the Zanaco MD to make funds available.
Kapumpa: You said Mr Musonda delivered money to State House, is that normal?
Diangamo: Quite normal.
Kapumpa: Explain?
Diangamo: First of all, Mr Musonda was a public officer, a member of
the board appointed by Ministry of Finance and he was employed by
government. Therefore, I would not expect Mr Musonda to delegate a
junior officer to take money to State House. Protocol in the civil
service doesn't allow that.
Kapumpa: Is it normal for bank officials to deliver money to clients?
Diangamo: It is very normal. I for one used to receive money from
Zanaco officials especially when travelling on official duties. It's
not only Zanaco but even other commercial banks do that.
Kapumpa: Did you know the board chairman of Zanaco and the members?
Diangamo: The chairman was Mr Shimukowa and among the board members I knew Mr James Mtonga.
Kapumpa: Would you know whether any other official was aware of this arrangement?
Diangamo: The deputy MD, Mr Mwambazi would know. Beyond that going lower, there was no need to do so.
Kapumpa: What about going higher?
Diangamo: It only applied to the MD and the chairman.
Kapumpa: How do you classify these lending?
Diangamo: These are not personal loans but government debts accrued
for the purpose of strategic discreet on behalf of the state.
Kapumpa: Do you know any other official who could have been involved in this programme?
Diangamo: One I know was a deputy director Mr Yotam Zulu.
Kapumpa: There is a letter to Zanaco MD Mr Musonda with regard to the K4 billion..
Nchito: Counsel is trying to introduce issues through the back door.
Kapumpa: When you were Secretary to the Treasury at Ministry of
Finance, were you involved in any programme regarding ZCCM (Zambia
Consolidated Copper Mines)?
Diangamo: Before I answer that question, I want to tell the court that payments were done in the same way they were borrowed.
Nchito: The witness should answer the questions but he is introducing new issues.
Court: There are several monies here and generalising information will not help us.
Kapumpa: Are you able to recall any payments made in November 1998?
Diangamo: I know particular transactions that I was party to.
Kapumpa: What can you recall?
Diangamo: For instance, in 2001 when ZNBC went on strike, they
required money to pay their salaries. I have to instruct the DG and MD
Zanaco to prepare those funds.
Kapumpa: How much?
Diangamo: About K300 million.
Kapumpa: Any other?
Diangamo: The presidential trips during the Congo peace talks.
Kapumpa: Which president?
Diangamo: president Chiluba.
Kapumpa: Explain what happened to the presidential trips?
Diangamo: I had to ask the DG and the MD for Zanaco to make available the funds.
Kapumpa: Any other incident that you can recall?
Diangamo: Between 1999 and 2000, the former president Dr Chiluba
awarded salary increment to members of the defence force. That
additional increase could not be met in the GDP. We tried to reason
with our colleagues but they were adamant.
Kapumpa: Who is we?
Diangamo: Me and the Secretary to the Treasury and we could not
manage. The defence chiefs were adamant and they needed the money. We
knew that if we were going to pay, we will go beyond the budget
expenditure. So there was a way out. After consulting the Secretary to
the Treasury, I directed the DG and the MD for Zanaco to provide these
resources.
Kapumpa: Any other you can recall?
Diangamo: The other one concerns the challenger that was owned by
the Zambian Airways. Government had to pay the bills after the defunct
of Roan Airways. I therefore, directed the DG to avail funds to pay the
bill.
Kapumpa: Any other incident you can recall?
Diangamo: Government officials travelled too much during the IMF
programmes so we had to ask the DG to borrow money so that these
delegations could travel abroad. Another incident was in 1999 when
Rwandan refugees became unruly on account of feeding them only on
nshima. They demanded to be given things like bananas and there was no
provision in the budget to pay specialised food for refugees. However,
in order to maintain peace in the country, I gave instructions to the
DG and MD Zanaco to provide funds.
Kapumpa: How much?
Diangamo: About K500 million.
Kapumpa: Were you aware as to which account the money was coming from?
Diangamo: I was not aware suffice to say the money was coming from Zanaco.
Kapumpa: How was that money paid back?
Diangamo: If the money was directed for the OP operations, they had
to repay. Upon receiving the funds from the Ministry of Finance, they
had to pay back. If the borrowing was on behalf of State House, upon
receiving funding from Ministry of Finance, we made arrangements to pay
back we were asking the controlling officer at State House to pay back.
Kapumpa: Who was the controlling officer?
Diangamo: At one time there was Mr Ndhlovu who was also permanent
secretary. But not all permanent secretaries are controlling officers.
Kapumpa: Can you recall another one?
Diangamo: Mr Kopulande. And when deploying soldiers to Angola, I
would instruct the loans and investment department to debit Ministry of
Finance Control 63 at Bank of Zambia and credit the same to Zanaco.
Control 63 is an account of Ministry of Finance.
Kapumpa: Were you aware at the time of such repayments from OP to Zanaco?
Diangamo: For the ones I authorised to borrow.
Kapumpa: At Ministry of Finance, were you involved in ZCCM programmes and if so tell the court?
Diangamo: Your Lord, ZCCM was fully owned by government which was
set up to promote public policy in the area of mining and related
activities. Ministry of Finance was the beneficial owner of the
company. When the MMD came into power, they embarked on various
privatisation programmes. One of those companies to be privatised was
ZCCM. The programme was an integral part of government. Those were the
structural benchmarks. As such we decided that ZCCM be liquidated.
However, the process would be properly done by a new company called
ZCCM-Investment Holdings that was to be an agency for the
implementation. The privatisation policy decisions were made by
government. So having realised that the privatisation process was
expensive, government solicited for donor assistance to raise funds to
undertake various aspects of the privatisation of ZCCM. I was involved
in leading some of the delegations that went for negotiations. In 1998,
I visited Russia, the same year I went to the Europe an Union, in 1999
I went to ADB (African Development Bank), the same year I went to DFID.
In all this I was trying to put up some money. Finally funds were
sourced to implement the programme.
Kapumpa: How much funds?
Diangamo: We used to have it in bunches of US$70 million, US$100
million just like that. Under that programme was the retrenchment of
ZCCM employees. When we floated ZCCM for privatisation in 1994, the
preferred bidders especially RAMCOZ was interested. However, RAMCOZ was
not interested in taking up all the workers as such under the reform
programme. Under the reform programme the component of retrenchment was
created with some funds sourced.
Kapumpa: Where were these funds?
Diangamo: These funds were deposited in Zanaco. It was for retrenchment and associated activities.
Kapumpa: What are these associated activities?
Diangamo: It was decided that before retrenchees were paid, there
was need to assess who deserved to be paid. Some cases involved paying
widows or widowers.
Kapumpa: How much was a retrenchment component?
Diangamo: The balance kept on changing but the fact is that the payments were not so frequent.
Kapumpa: Could you be aware of signatories to the ZCCM account?
Diangamo: I wouldn't know.
Kapumpa: You talked about two transactions, what were they?
Diangamo: When we received money, we paid allowances to auditors. I
authorised ZCCM-IH to effect the payments because no one could tamper
with it without the authority of Ministry of Finance.
Kapumpa: How much was involved?
Diangamo: About K4 billion.
Kapumpa: Could you tell the court about that K4 billion?
Diangamo: In May 2000, I was acting permanent secretary budget. At
that time I was also coordinating the IMF technical committee which was
responsible for preparing all documents and any other information. The
technical committee had to ensure that all the benchmarks were adhered
to.
Kapumpa: How did you sign the letter to transfer the money from ZCCM account?
Diangamo: Mr Mtonga advised that we use funds in Zanaco meant for ZCCM retrenchment to clean the overdraft for OP in the bank?
Kapumpa: Look at P43, is that the letter you signed?
Diangamo: Yes.
Kapumpa: Date?
Diangamo: 29, May 2001
Kapumpa: Were you a signatory to the ZCCM account?
Diangamo: No.
Kapumpa: So how did you write the letter?
Diangamo: As earlier said, now we are talking about ZCCM Investment
Holdings fully owned by government whose assets and mandate fall under
the jurisdiction of the Secretary to the Treasury just like Zanaco. The
Secretary to the Treasury is a chief executive responsible for all
assets and monies that accrue property. The Secretary to the Treasury
is chief executive officer for all public monies as mandated by the
general warrant that the President signed. I was controlling officer
for all donor funds and assets that accrue to government wherever they
are. The implication is that the Secretary to the Treasury directing or
through the permanent secretary economic affairs has an overriding
power on all matters pertaining to the entire public sector. Public
sector I mean, civil service, parastatal and others. So the Secretary
to the Treasury can directly or indirectly intervene in any of these
for the purpose of fostering national interest. So the Secretary to the
Treasury on 29th May 2000 having sanctioned that these funds be used to
firstly regularise our position as government. Secondly to clear the OP
debts in Zanaco and third avoid the PRGF from being suspended by the
IMF. It was very much in order for the Secretary to the Treasury to
give such instructions.
Kapumpa: You talked about national interest and public interest, what interest were you serving in this transaction?
Diangamo: There were some conditions which were mandatory in order
to progress but some were not mandatory. For example the issue of cash
was mandatory. I know that revenue flows are not exponential but
discreet. What I mean is like when you open a tap of water. Water will
continue coming out at the same speed that you first opened the tap. So
the funding was not consistent. Sometimes there was no money but we
were told you guys you can't be so incompetent to fail to that money.
Kapumpa: What was going to happen if you didn't transfer the money from the ZCCM account?
Diangamo: The IMF would have found the bank not in order since
Zanaco was a benchmark and the programme was going to be suspended
immediately. The implication is that donors were going to stop giving
aid, the kwacha would depreciate further, prices will go up, we will
fail to have savings and the level of reserves at Bank of Zambia would
go down.
Kapumpa: Did you gain anything or did Mr Musonda gain as a result of these transactions?
Diangamo: Not at all.
Kapumpa: Did the IMF come for monitoring?
Diangamo: Yes.
Kapumpa: What happened?
Diangamo: They found that the books of Zanaco were in order and
afterwards they gave us a certificate of clean performance in the
programme.
Kapumpa: What did you do later?
Diangamo: A few days later we decided that the ZCCM money should be reimbursed and the OP overdraft resurfaced.
Kapumpa: On the issue of borrowing, how was the issue of interest dealt with for lending by OP from Zanaco?
Diangamo: We got pressure from various people but they had indicated that they will pay interest later.
Kapumpa: So government didn't pay interest?
Diangamo: No.
Kapumpa: But were you aware?
Diangamo: Yes, on several occasions they came to my office and we
met the managing director and the Secretary to the Treasury and we
asked the bank to give us time.
Kapumpa: What about the issue of government guidelines when opening
an account, could you say about your understanding about their
implications?
Diangamo: From time to time guidelines are given to banks and
ministries to give perimeters in which to operate. Depending on the
situation through strategic management, that may be modified to suit
certain situations. That is why they are guidelines. They are basically
for open civil service because closed civil service which include
defence and security had a code of conduct. A good example is the
Office of the President which may not necessarily follow these
guidelines.
Kapumpa: There is a witness who came here and said about the letter
you wrote. The witness said both you and Mr Musonda acted fraudulently
for writing letter when you were not signatories to the ZCCM account,
what do you say?
Diangamo: That statement is very irresponsible. The witness was a
junior officer who has no clear knowledge on how Mr Musonda and myself
interacted in national issues. We were operating at a very high level.
How you say that for the sake of protecting your interest. I can come
back as finance minister what is she going to do? We were doing that to
stabilise the economic environment. Is that implying that Zambian
people stole? It is very unfortunate that a manager of the bank can
call the Secretary to the Treasury fraudulent. That to me is
insubordination.
Nchito: The last words he said are disparaging the character of the
witness. This has been done before and it is not for the first time.
Court: We will not proceed in this manner. I may allow or disallow.
I only took down very limited comments. When we get there, we shall see
how much you can go to ask questions.
Kapumpa: You don't expect the witness to say a bold statement that the MD was corrupt without being rebutted.
Trial continues on February 21.

Original_Search_Title: 'Ex-Kigali Ngali Prefect Supervised Ntarama Killings'
Headline: Rwanda: 'Ex-Kigali Ngali Prefect Supervised Ntarama Killings'
Body_Text:
Arusha — Former prefect of former Kigali Ngali province, Francois Karera,
supervised the slaughter of thousands of Tutsi refugees at Ntarama
Church in Bugesera in the present-day Southern Province, the UN court
trying the 1994 Genocide suspects heard on Friday.
A protected witness code-named BML, told the court that Karera
ordered the church killings on the 15th of April, 1994, a day after he
had visited the refugees and promised them security.
"I was hiding in a nearby ditch when a four-bus convoy full of
Interahamwe and soldiers arrived along with Karera. He immediately
instructed the killers that he did not want to see any Tutsi at the
place alive, and then fired the first shot towards the church," the
witness told the court session, presided over by the tribunal
president, Erik Mose, a Norwegian.
He said the attackers used rifles, clubs, machetes and other
traditional weapons to annihilate the refugees. "I noticed him very
well as he gave the directive to kill in a loud voice. He feared
nobody."
The witness, whose identity was concealed for security reasons, also
said that after the attackers had hacked many people to death in the
church courtyard, they sent the temple ablaze to kill those inside.
"Many people had fled inside the church. They hurled grenades at the
church and wiped out all that were inside. I heard a lot of screaming;
the killers were on the loose after Karera's instructions."
He estimated the number of assailants to between 300 and 400, adding
that they were ferried to the church in four buses belonging to
ONATRACOM, a state-owned transport company.
"Karera disembarked from the second bus along with the Interahamwe
and soldiers. Many them also came out of the other buses, with the
former wearing banana leaves around their heads," said the witness.
He said that after the killers had gone, he and other people in the
hiding came to the scene of the deadly attack to their disbelief. "It
was unbelievable; bodies and blood were all over the place, outside and
inside the church. Until today, there are bones in that church."
The post-genocide government made the church one of the several
genocide museums in the country. Eleven years after the tragedies, the
place is still grappling with the far-reaching consequences including a
backlog of genocide cases.
Asked whether he could describe the physical appearance of the
accused, the witness said, "He is a dark slightly grey-haired man with
a medium height." He was also asked to identify Karera among the people
that were in the courtroom and did it.
"He is putting on a black jacket, a blue shirt and a neck-tie that
is almost red," he said. "The witness has rightly identified Karera
your honour," a lady trial attorney, who was putting questions to
witness BML told the presiding judge.
Asked how he survived the marauding militia, the witness said, "only
God made it happen. It was not by myself. Well, after I saw what
happened I went to another hiding place near River Akagera in a swamp;
that is where the Rwandese Patriotic Front/Army RPF/A) Inkotanyi
