soldiers found and rescued me on the 14th of May 1994." He said most of
his relatives had perished in the Ntarama Church attack.
Judge Mose adjourned the case to this Monday, for the defence to cross-examine the witness.
Karera's trial started on 9th January, 2006, and the prosecution is
still lining up witnesses to testify against him. He is also accused of
committing several genocide crimes in Nyamirambo, Nyarugenge district,
Kigali city, where he was living during that period.

Original_Search_Title: Register for 2007 Elections, MPs Urge Kalenjins
Headline: Kenya: Register for 2007 Elections, MPs Urge Kalenjins
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three Rift Valley legislators have urged the Kalenjin community
to remain united and register as voters for next year's General
Election.
MPs William Ruto, Jebii Kilimo and Charles Keter urged the community to register in large numbers.
"Let us remain united and register in large numbers so as to vote as a block during the next General Election," said Ruto.
The leaders were speaking on Friday, at a Nairobi hotel, during a Kalenjin theme night dubbed Kitwek Nite.
The nightlong celebrations featured song and dance. Ruto, who has
declared that he will vie for the presidency on a Kanu ticket in 2007,
was received with wild cheers by thousands of revellers.
Amid cheers of "Go for it Ruto", he praised the community for voting
as a block and rejecting the Wako Draft during last November's
referendum.
"I thank you for your unity during the referendum, but remember the
fight is not yet over. Let's continue to remain united," he said.
The crowd was electrified with songs by popular Kalenjin bands and Kass FM radio presenters.
At hand also was the Kass comedy team, which imitated former
president Moi and former MPs Mark Too and Ezekiel Barngetuny, to the
delight of the crowd.
Ruto said the community had a role in shaping the political landscape of the country, only if they acted collectively.
Kilimo urged leaders to educate the masses on their rights. "As
leaders, we should not abandon the masses because of our selfish
interests. We should instead fight for them and remain true to our
vision," she said amid applause.
Kilimo was dropped from the Cabinet after she ditched the Banana camp in the run up to the referendum.
Keter said the Kalenjin community's time to take the reins of power
had come, after learning bitter lessons following Kanu's defeat in the
2002 General Election.
The community, he added, should in future vote as a block.
Several leaders urged Ruto to go for the presidency, saying the community supported him.
"We will support you in your bid for the big seat. Let those who
refuse to recognise your candidature support candidates of their
choice," said councillor Magut.

Original_Search_Title: I Am Back - Kasulu
Headline: Uganda: I Am Back - Kasulu
Body_Text:
Kampala — PATRICK Kasulu, the real estate firm, Property Masters' boss, was recently granted bail after 17 months in jail over
complaints related to his business. He told Esther Namugoji about life on remand and his comeback.
Do you know what happened to bring on all the cases?
All the cases were between April and August 2004 and yet I had been
in operation since 2001. That is when the Kampala mafia invaded me. I
was conned by a racket of fraudsters, three of whom were inside the
company and are reportedly on the run. They were in connivance with
some four external economic mafiosi. What I intend to do with them? You
know of recent I decided to become a man of God so I forgave them and
left them in God's hands.
How did it feel to go to prison?
The prison community was very eager and excited to see me. They
listened as the radios gave a step-by-step account of my movements,
from Central Police, to CID Headquarters, to Court, right up to the
prison gates. When I arrived they applauded.
I was in Murchison Bay prison but was transferred to Luzira Upper
Prison for better management. When that happens people wonder whether
you are in trouble but it was because they thought I was living in
luxury at Murchison Bay. I had my mobile phone, access to alcoholic
drinks, newspapers, an executive size mattress and a VIP ward to myself.
How were living conditions in Luzira Upper Prison?
I was even more comfortable there. I had a single room, whereas
other prisoners stay in a kraal - wards of 200 people and corridors. I
had access to television, radio and a special diet.
Some fellow prisoners also used to help me with domestic work,
voluntarily. I had a chance of exercising by playing football. I was
allowed to dress they way I wanted.
How is this possible in prison?
Prisoners are categorised according to level of education, income
and social status and I used to call myself a special voluntary remand
prisoner. There is a provision for those who can afford to maintain
themselves to do so.
My daily menu was good. I never lost any weight. I used to have
breakfast of eggs and brown bread, coffee with ginger and a banana.
At court I would have tea with milk, katogo and fried eggs at about
10:30am and then a full buffet lunch. Back at the cell I drank a litre
of mineral water, had tea with milk and bread at 5:30pm and at 6:30pm
my last meal would be two mangoes and an apple.
You came to town every day. Why was that so?
Yes. After I was arrested, the Police announced in the media that
whoever had a complaint against me should report to them. In the
process, 3,119 clients showed up. They were regarded as complainants,
yet most of them were only inquiring about the status of their
transactions. In those days CID headquarters was like Namugongo on
Martyrs' Day. Each day I dealt with 50 clients and what surprised
Police is that I was in the know about each case, its status and the
solution.
Everyone that met me went back satisfied. Because of that, the
Police continued requisitioning for me because I had the solution to
those cases.
How did you relate with the prisoners?
I am very good at socialising, so I had a very good relationship
with everyone there. The first time I was denied bail there was
celebration in prison when I returned because I was their spokesman
against mistreatment.
A letter I wrote about prisoners' plight was published in the press
and since then there were great improvements on the issues I raised.
That is why some prisoners wished I wouldn't go. They said they would
suffer if I left.
I was also very generous, helping them with paper, sugar, fighting
lice and was an outspoken critic against sodomy. I intend to continue
advocating prisoners' human rights.
Which way now for Property Masters?
When I was on remand, I concentrated on sorting out clients' issues
and all of them went well. If anyone ever dealt with Property Masters
and there is anything pending for whatever reason, they can contact us
on 078/075 403340.
Since my release I have been overwhelmed by calls from satisfied
clients encouraging me not to give up the business. Most of them say
something must have happened in 2004 to cause the problem because they
were not complaining.
I put almost everyone who had been in this business out of business
and that was also part of the problem. But we are back. I will also say
like President Yoweri Museveni, "The people are the ones asking me to
stay."

Original_Search_Title: Charming the Ladies
Headline: Uganda: Charming the Ladies
Body_Text:
Kampala — For the first time in Uganda's 43 years of independence, a woman -
Miria Kalule Obote, the Uganda People's Congress (UPC) flag bearer -
could become the country's next president.
Miria's candidature is a significant milestone, as it's the first
time a woman is competing for a position only men have held up to now.
Not only is it symbolic of the progressive journey of women from the
kitchen to the centre of national politics, but it may have raised the
stakes for women's participation in the elections.
Women in Uganda account for 12.5 million of Uganda's 24.4 million
people, according to the 2002 Uganda Population and Housing Census. The
Electoral Commission does not yet have figures to show how many women
have registered to vote in the upcoming elections, but it is believed
that they outnumber the males.
Women, especially the rural and uneducated, were the driving force
of the previous two elections - 1996 and 2001- overwhelmingly voting
for President Yoweri Museveni.
During his reign, women's lives have been transformed in several
ways. There has been emphasis on the education of girls. At tertiary
level girls are given a bonus 1.5 points to compete favourably with
boys. Women have had representation in Parliament and politics and at
one time one of them rose to the position of vice-president.
Micro-finance has been mostly directed at women, not for any
political consideration, but because their propensity to pay back
exceeds that of men. These and several other factors have cultivated
the women support for Museveni and the Movement.
Changed scene
However, a lot has changed. The Movement is history and in its place
is a new fledgling multiparty system that has not only widened the
political spectrum, but is also likely to increase women's
participation in politics.
Still, the parties and the candidates are not leaving anything to
chance. Each of them has a well-defined programme for women in their
manifesto.
For instance, NRM has placed emphasis on better education through
the introduction of Universal Secondary Education; low-interest
micro-finance credit, better health, improved security, peace and
prosperity for all.
The Forum for Democratic Change (FDC), on the other hand, wants to
empower women by lowering taxes and increasing production, which they
hope will further increase household incomes.
They also plan to increase employment by tackling insecurity, which
they argue limits women's participation in production. Likewise, UPC
and the Democratic Party (DP) have promised to pursue gender sensitive
policies to safeguard the dignity of women and improve the condition of
rural women.
Stuck on looks
However, according to veteran politician Jaberi Bidandi Ssali these
issues are not likely to have a great impact on the way women vote. He
says the typical average woman voter - the majority of who are peasants
- does not vote basing on what they are likely to benefit from a
candidate once elected, but the personalities.
"Most of them do not look beyond the appearance, charm and popularity of a candidate.
And yet if they concentrated on the issues, that would give them an
opportunity to make more informed choices and to hold their leaders
accountable," says Bidandi, who is now acting chairman of the People's
Progressive Party.
Sarah Mukasa, a programme Officer for Akina Mama Wa Afrika, agrees,
but argues that the hurried change of the political systems may have
caught women on the wrong foot.
"I don't think women, or anybody for that matter, knows anything
about multiparty democracy, how it works or how they can use it to
advocate for women's issues," she says. "As women, it is important not
to allow ourselves to be used in opportunistic ways and then discarded
after the elections. We should demand for better policies, such as
legislation to protect vulnerable women."
Who's the leading suitor?
Of all the five candidates, Museveni appears to be the leading
suitor for the elusive women's vote. With two elections under his belt,
Museveni is a veteran when it comes to wooing women.
In the last two elections, he turned on the charm with a slew of
seductive promises, that had the women turn out in record numbers to
vote for him. His wife Janet also chipped in with her task force of
mainly born-again women leaders to mobilise support.
However, two of his critical women are missing this time round -
Janet is fighting her own battle in Ruhama, while former Vice-President
Dr. Speciosa Wandira Kazibwe is pursuing a PhD at Harvard University in
the US.
Kazibwe was the Movement's biggest symbol of women emancipation and empowerment.
Her departure has certainly left a void yet to be filled. The
election of deputy speaker Rebecca Kadaga as a NRM's woman national
vice-president was aimed at addressing this gender imbalance, but it
remains to be seen whether she will be able to cast the same spell on
women as Kazibwe did. Still, there is a lot going for the NRM, as far
as the women's vote is concerned. It is said women are usually the
first group of people Museveni meets during his campaigns mainly to
find out their problems and to personally request them to mobilise
support for his candidature.
NRM is also the only party that has fielded women candidates for in all elective posts at the local and national level.
"We have the women's vote, there is no doubt about that. They know
what this government has done for them and they know what we are going
to do for them. That is why they have been turning out in the thousands
for our rallies," Kadaga says.
FDC Secretary General Alice Alaso, however, says women are now more enlightened and aware of their rights.
"Ugandan women have for the last 20 years been led to believe that
their rights as enshrined in the Constitution were gifted by President
Museveni. We have an opportunity now to enlighten these women and to
show that we have the vision and the capacity to address issues that
affect them," she says.
Like Kadaga, Alaso says women constitute the biggest number of
people at Col. (rtd.) Dr. Kizza Besigye's rallies. Whereas she admits
that Besigye's rape trial may have been potentially damaging, the
continual show of support from women at the rallies indicates that it
will not affect his overall support. "I believe women this time are
going to vote not for those who make them put on banana leaves, but for
real issues," Alaso says.
DP's John Ssebaana and independent candidate Dr. Abed Bwanika have
both have tried to court the women's vote, but they don't seem to have
got the same response as NRM and FDC.
How the women will vote, according to a poll recently commissioned by the Daily Monitor:
Museveni: 52%
Besigye: 28%
Ssebaana: 4%.
Miria: 4%
Bwanika: 0
Undecided: 9%

Original_Search_Title: Either We're Hypocrites Or Our Conscious is Seared
Headline: Kenya: Either We're Hypocrites Or Our Conscious is Seared
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Last week was a tragic one for this country. Not only were we
stunned by "new revelations" into the Anglo Leasing scandal, but also a
building erected on corruption came tumbling down killing at least 14
people.
Anglo Leasing first. The "new revelations" on the scam early last
week painted this society in a hypocritical mode. Politicians pranced
around demanding "action" on the scam. A visitor to Kenya would have
been forgiven for thinking that Anglo Leasing was a whole new novelty.
And yet this is a scandal that has been with us for the better part
of three years. Names have been named and action has been called for
before.
This newspaper has dedicated acres of space to covering the scam.
Notably, during the November 2005, referendum, it kept faith with the
real truth and - in the din of pervading manipulation - broadcast
loudly that Anglo Leasing was a scandal of monstrous proportions.
In the newspaper's October 23, 2005, edition, the page-one headline
told it all: "Ministers put on the spot over Anglo Leasing". It named
these ministers as David Mwiraria, Kiraitu Murungi and Chris Murungaru.
In that story, most of the "new revelations" were covered. Death
threats on the former anti-corruption tsar John Githongo were
mentioned, miscellaneous accounts were revealed, cover-up attempts were
unravelled, and the issue of what other monies had been wired into the
miscellaneous accounts was raised.
That expose so stung the Kenya Anti-Corruption Commission (Kacc)
that they bought space to "remind" the newspaper and Prof Anyang'
Nyong'o - the principle source of the story - of the sub judice clause.
Therefore, the truth is that most of this so-called "new
revelations" are not new at all - not if you have been reading this
newspaper.
Another issue it reported on truthfully during the referendum as the
gears of manipulation shifted to high-speed mode was how the money that
the Banana Team used to fund the campaigns was raised.
Entitled, "Billions for 'Yes' as 'No' rocks the Coast", the expose
on September 11, 2005 detailed how over Sh3 billion (raised through the
Anglo Leasing scandal) was being spent.
It, therefore, surprises me that even MPs and other institutions who
were then manipulating Kenyans away from the real issues, including the
festering sore that was Anglo Leasing, are now talking about integrity.
How interesting! Let's then talk about integrity. Integrity is about
possessing and steadfastly adhering to high moral principles and
professional standards. Integrity is too important to be obfuscated by
short-term business or sectarian interests.
Believing in integrity means suffering for it - like this newspaper
has been denied advertising support by the Government, its departments
and government-friendly businesses (the subject of our editorial on
January 8, 2006) but it has refused to back down on truth. That is
integrity. Integrity is about consistency not convenience.
At what point did those talking about integrity today change heart?
When their interests started hurting? When their demands to Kibaki were
not met? When the public started losing trust in them? Will this
hypocrisy go unnoticed?
Kenyans, constant vigilance is the price of freedom. Until you hate
corruption enough to do something about it, until you hate corruption
enough not to forget the past, until you hate corruption enough to be
permanently on the lookout for manipulation dressed as good intentions;
you will forever live suspended in the entrancement of hypocrisy and
subservience to a mighty establishment determined to control your very
thinking.
It is exactly this kind of hypocrisy that leads a nation to
self-destruction. When everything important is viewed through lenses of
convenience only then society lacks the requisite trustworthy watchdog.
Then no one really cares about fundamentals of human existence -
respect for life and property, honesty, liberty, justice, individuality
and the rule of law. Nothing is sacred, nothing is new, nothing is true
and not one objective standard of integrity holds.The questions to ask
now are: Do Kenyans really care enough about corruption to engage in a
sustained campaign against it? Can Kenyans really keep fidelity with
the truth long enough to notice the difference? And are Kenyans
discerning enough to tell real truth from orchestrated whitewashes?
Indeed, do Kenyans ask themselves such simple questions as "why now"?
Can Kibaki sack his friends if only to pave way for investigations
into scandals rocking his Cabinet? Can Local Authorities Minister
Musikari Kombo take full responsibility for last week's building
disaster in Nairobi and resign? Or can Justice Aaron Ringera stop
talking and do something really great? (Mr Ringera, may I add, giving
people against whom you have evidence 30 days to explain themselves is
tantamount to giving them time to interfere with "the" evidence, isn't
it?)
Can Kombo, on whose desk two reports on graft in local authorities
sit and have been sitting since August 2004, really claim that the
Nyamakima tragedy was an "accident"? One of those reports on his desk
details how corruption in City Hall has resulted in the construction of
unsafe buildings both for human habitation and business.
The building that collapsed was only six months old and the report
on which the Minister was supposed to have acted to forestall such
accidents was one year and five months old in the minister's office.
This is the minister who would now have us believe that the problem
was with the court system. This is the minister who would have us
believe that he cares for human life. This is the minister who would
have us believe that he will take action on criminals he has known have
existed for a year-and-a-half!
The Sunday Standard carried a story on the very first day of this
year pointing out ministries to watch for their proclivity towards
corruption. That of Local Authorities was top on the list. Lest you say
you were not told, that ministry remains on top. Watch it.

Original_Search_Title: Ssebaana Rallies Ssembabule As UPC's Miria Asks for Third Term
Headline: Uganda: Ssebaana Rallies Ssembabule As UPC's Miria Asks for Third Term
Body_Text:
Masaka/Kisoro — Democratic Party leader and presidential candidate John Ssebaana Kizito has said that this year's National Resistance Movement celebrations, held on Thursday, are the last that President Yoweri Museveni will preside over.
Ssebaana told a campaign rally at Mateete Trading Centre in Sembabule district on Thursday that, by this time next year, Museveni will be grazing cattle at his country home in Rwakitura, having lost the February 23 election.
Mr Museveni marked 20 years in power on Thursday with a large military parade at Kololo airstrip in Kampala and a speech highlighting his government's achievements. Ssebaana, however, accused the President of getting his priorities wrong.
He said: "Today, President Museveni is at Kololo demonstrating to Ugandans tanks and other weapons on which he has squandered taxpayer's money as the only achievement he has made in all the 20 years that he has been in power."
Ssebaana said he hoped to revive DP's structures across the country to give it the power it is widely believed to have won in the disputed 1980 elections. The Uganda Peoples Congress was declared winner of the discredited poll prompting Museveni and his Uganda People's Movement to take up arms in 1981, eventually taking power in 1986.
"President Museveni behaved like a man who comes to help you to get your shirt back from a thief but then decides to wear it himself instead," said Ssebaana.
"He decided to fight Obote because he had stolen victory from us but he has never handed it back to us ever since he got it from Obote."
He said by displaying military weapons at Kololo Airstrip on Thursday, Museveni intended to intimidate Ugandans to vote for him.
"The days of intimidation have ended. If intimidation were still a tactic to reckon with, we wouldn't be here. All his talk that he passed through a furnace is intended to intimidate you," he said.
Meanwhile, UPC leader and presidential candidate, Miria Kalule Obote took her campaign to southwest Uganda on Thursday, asking residents in Kisoro to give her party, which ruled the country between 1962-1971 and 1981-1985, a third term in office.
"We have been in government twice and want to come back for the third time because we are a party that serves the people," she told a crowd of supporters at the Mayor's Gardens in Kisoro.
She accused the NRM of not delivering on campaign promises.
"We do not deceive like the NRM," Obote said. "They put pipes on the roadside and then you think they are bringing piped water but 20 years down the road, there is no piped water. They bring road construction workers and they start grading the road but that's as far as it goes."
During the rally, Obote challenged Museveni's ability to industrialise the country. She said: "This government has promised to create industries for development but the first thing it has done is to sell off all the old industries."
"Jinja is in a pathetic state. People who used to work in these industries are now selling bananas and riding boda bodas."
She also criticised the quality of the Universal Primary Education programme and promised to rebuild teacher-training colleges in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Femalespeak-Competing With the Football
Headline: Kenya: Femalespeak-Competing With the Football
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Chinese astrologers should have declared 2006 to be the year of
the ball. Everywhere you go, there is one football tournament or the
other. If it isn't the Premier League it is the La liga, if it isn't
the Cup of Nations, then it is the World Cup.
When the wave of balls sweeps this land, it takes our men with it
and turns them into strangely behaving fanatics. During football
tournaments, most men behave like politicians - they make strange
allegations; they keep defecting and re-defecting from one team to
another and suddenly start wearing funny coloured costumes.
You will hear men declaring that beneath their massive girths lie
dazzling dribbling and scoring skills. You will hear men who are
literally 'sat' on by their wives and bosses barking orders and cursing
while lounging in their sofas. Matatu and morning conversations are no
longer about oranges and bananas, they are almost always about ball,
ball and more ball. If thought that women's monthly mood swings and
tantrums are crazy, wait until you see grown men sulking, being
petulant and losing their libido just because some ball didn't get into
what women consider a sieve but which men insist on calling a goal.
These men's skins break into spots, their heads swell and shrink
depending on which way the game goes. And wait until you see how they
fling their arms and legs up in the air when their teams win! You will
understand that men are strange creatures indeed! It is common
knowledge that these strange behaviours could result in huge emotional
stress for women. Today we offer you a few tips on how to survive the
messy balls of 2006.
To start off, every woman needs to increase her level of tolerance
for huge groups of men in her house or around her man. During this
time, men become generous hosts and invite troops of their buddies into
their living rooms. Food budgets and menus must be quickly readjusted
to cater for those men performing the important national duty of
watching football in a group. These bunches of football lovers will
show up invariably dressed in the same hideous manner.
All colour codes will be violated in the T-shirts and track suits
and shorts that they will be wearing. All bodily hair will be out on
display as beards compete with nostril and armpit hair. Toenails that
rarely see the sun or feel the pinch of a nail cutter will be laid bare
in a broad range of "akalas" and open sandals.
The various facial and bodily reactions of men at the sight of lost
opportunities and goals scored are enough to scar you and your children
for life. From grimaces to winces to guffaws to strange facial
contortions, you will come to understand why Darwin came up with the
theory of evolution. Women are also advised to remove their precious
china and cutlery from their living rooms during this season.
There are men who have been known to break plates, smash window and
tear down curtains when their teams lose. Women of this land are
therefore called upon to develop thicker hides, double layer their
eardrums and protect their eyesight from the sights and sounds of our
resident football fanatics. There is some truism that the X chromosome
in women causes them to nag and question ceaselessly. However, they are
advised to silence these habits during the football season.
Nothing is more infuriating to a man than having his woman bombard
him with questions during a game. Or worse still to have a woman claim
that Ronaldinho is from Croatia and his game is not all that! For men,
football is genius, art and entertainment all in one; it isn't a forum
for women's education. A penalty shoot moment is most inappropriate for
trying to find out who decided that the team wears yellow or how come
all referees are bald headed.
The finals are not the ideal spot to try to unravel the mysteries
and origins of the game. You are likely to get strange reactions that
could endanger your life and of those who are close to you. At this
time, women are advised to take some time off and learn the basics of
football. Before the mother of all football, the World Cup checks in,
women need to consult with their brothers and male buddies to get some
decent understanding of the workings of the game.
Whatever you do, do not seek a cure for your ignorance during the
matches. Do not ask how many players make a team and whatever you do,
do not wonder out loud what is so great about grown men chasing some
piece of hide. Keep in your mind at all times the fact that there are
women who have lost limbs and lives in the hands of football fans.
Lastly, ensure that you acquire the political ability to shift
loyalties quickly. Make sure that you know what your mate's team is at
whatever point in time. If he is a Bulgaria fan on day one, Senegal on
day two and China day three make sure you are will him all the way.
Take a lesson from our politicians and watch the way they move
seamlessly from one camp to another, from one fruit to another. Even
when you think your mate suffers from poor judgement in selecting
teams, do not let it be known. Do not for a minute think that you can
shift your allegiances to those of your mate's enemies. That is enough
to kill your romance forever. They say that many romances have been
made and killed on the football pitch.

Original_Search_Title: Masaka Youth Create Besigye Interest Corner
Headline: Uganda: Masaka Youth Create Besigye Interest Corner
Body_Text:
Masaka — YOUTHS in Masaka have created an 'interest corner' in town for the
Forum for Democratic Change presidential candidate, Dr Kizza Besigye.
The corner is along Elgin Road near the junction between Hajji Mpuuga's Building and that of the late Male in Masaka town.
Every morning, the youths sing the national anthem before laying the
mats on which they place Besigye's portraits and bottles of mineral
water, soda, and beer.
Most of them are boda-boda operators.
Throughout the day, they youths take turns to watch over the corner lest some thugs make off with the mats and the drinks.
When Besigye visited Masaka on January 13, the youths presented a
cake to the retired colonel, which he cut after disembarking from his
car despite the huge crowd that filled the streets. One of the
caretakers of the corner, Mr Godfrey Okwir, told Daily Monitor on
Monday that: "This is a very effective way of spreading our
presidential candidate's campaign. So many people stop by everyday to
look at this very beautiful spot which speaks by itself."
Meanwhile, Besigye yesterday addressed seven rallies in the Busoga
region after attending court in Kampala where his wife Winnie Byanyima
and the party Treasurer General Jack Sabiiti were charged with libel
and giving false information.
Besigye addressed rallies in Mayuge, Iganga, Magamaga, Busudde,
Busaale, Bugade and Kaliba. While in Mayuge, Besigye told the people
that if they vote President Yoweri Museveni, they would be voting
mugogwazi (fish bones) and not democracy.
"How can people living by the lakeside eat bones instead of fish?
Besigye asked the lakeside residents at Pakasabira in Mayuge district.
Before he arrived, people burnt essanja (dry banana leaves), an NRM
symbol for a third term for Museveni. Besigye said that accross from
Arua, Kasese to Malaba, the people have decided to have change of
president because Museveni has failed.
"He is not listening to people's needs. Isn't that a sign of
failure?" Besigye asked. He advised the people to be vigilant on the
election day, Febuary 23.
"It's no doubt that Museveni is going. Be firm and vigilant on the
polling day, keep around after casting your ballot," the FDC leader
said.

Original_Search_Title: Of Dreams Na Di Sorriest Part And 2007
Headline: Sierra Leone: Of Dreams Na Di Sorriest Part And 2007
Body_Text:
Freetown — For those of you who have stayed a little bit far away from home,
bobor belleh is (was) the new buzzword . It is fighting (fought) for
recognition in everyday parlance back home. Be it in mende, limba or
fullah, you now hardly hear a young man (apart from the politicians)
speak without mentioning bobor belleh.
It is even said to be a phenomenon whose theory was prescribed by one
of the country's brand new musical sensations, Emerson Borbor Belleh
Bockarie . But what is this bobor belleh that has now taken up
permanent position in our local lexicon? Has it got anything to do
with kwashiorkor or it is merely referring to a kid with a protruded
stomach, or big man ? Hey! Hold your peace, not from my mouth you
will get the answer.
Well, forget about bobor belleh for a while, what comes next in
Sierra Leone? Did I hear you say 2007?
Yes, you are right but not quite too right. During my barely a week
dash to town recently, I was baptized with another buzz word- na di
sooriest part. It came from one of the latest hits in town. When I
first heard my young nephew uttering the words, I was dumb-founded, I
thought he was speaking in tongues. I asked him to translate what he
had just said and his reply was , " Uncle Benk, na di sorriest part
." Yes, this was the sorriest part, a highly charged, inflammatory
and explosive political song done by our own Dadi Saj and this time
again, just like any other time, it targets the politicians and the
day of reckoning - 2007.
2007 is now like a Gucci, a Tommy Hillfigher , Armani or Calvin Klein
fashion designers trademark . In Sierra Leone, it has to be 2007 or
nothing else. Put your minds into reverse gear- We have been promised
that in 2007 no Sierra Leonean will go to bed hungry; they say
Bumbuna will be completed in 2007; the roads from Freetown to
Kamabumba will be paved with gold in 2007; micro -credit scheme will
flow like manna from heaven in 2007; everybody will go to school
without paying a kobo in 2007; the Mayor will no longer worry about
the ever present filth in and around the city in 2007; and may be,
just may be, the bridge from Kissy to Tagrin will be completed in
2007 even before it is started. Yes, 2007 is the year of promises for
Sierra Leoneans- a year Solo B has been having sleepless nights over.
He can't wait for it to come and just as he is putting all his hopes
on hopes, the musicians who have now taken the role of the opposition
and indeed, that of the fourth estate have decided not to rest on
their oars but to join the fray. All their attention just like Berewa
and co has been drawn to 2007. Wait a minute, go to Paddy's, Binta's
or Old School. Listen to the Dee Jays doing their thing. What will
you hear? Strictly Sierra Leonean hits with the rappers churning out
with one breath lyrics plastered with vibes like , " Ay bo duya mek
dem lef we , lek dem go sidom saful mek dem nor torment we o, right
now man dem nor gladi so mek dem push na corner wit dis dem boku
campaign," , " wetin we go do for take di next election ? na wetin we
go do for pull we country na gron ?" and you name the rest but it was
the lyrics of " na di sorriest part" that caught my mind . Dadi Saj
says he has an alibi for it; dreaming and he was parachuted to 2007.
Dadi Saj is not a Joseph the dreamer type. His dreams have never come
to the public but this time round, "na di sorriest part" did and it
came in the form of a song. His dream could not have been revealed at
any other opportune time but when all the J. Cs ( Just cams ) are (
were ) in town and trust the lyrical bomber to give them a take away
package.
Coming particularly from the United States, most of them must have
been thrilled with either the lyrics or video clips of the Kayne
Wests , the R Kellys , the Rehannas and indeed the 50 cents of this
world.
In the quest to be relevant, Emerson recently took his Borbor Belleh
and Tutu party up town and they soon became instant hits in
Washington, Maryland and indeed in every other Sierra Leonean
community in the U.S.
Well, what Emerson gave to them there and what the K- Mans and the
Manzul Avec C- Bolts and other have been giving to them either in the
United Kingdom or any other part of Europe were just the peels from a
very ripe and juicy banana .. Back in town, it was Dadi Saj who ruled
their world with of course, "na di sorriest part." I did not have the
opportunity of meeting face to face with Saj to ask him for an
interpretation of his dream but trust Freetown and its dancehalls and
rum bars, but his presence was felt everywhere.
In his dream, Saj focused on the heart to heart issues that have
continued to make the man at Kroo Bay or Songo the laughing stock of
even colleagues in Tema ( Ghana) Senegambia ( The Gambia ), Arjengule
( Nigeria ), Lome ( Togo ) and even Kakata in Liberia .
2007, according to him, Sierra Leone would be like heaven - work in
abundance, light? - No wahala, food?- No problem, money? Hakuna mata
everything will be wutetheteh but alas! Sweet dreams. But was it in
the dream that he asked who is going to be the next president in
2007? He says that is his own 'tsunami,' the sorriest part.
Clearly, his headache is the caliber of person that will take over
from Kabbah be it Berewa, Ernest Koroma or Charles Margai.
Truly, Saj was indeed in a dreamland, a fairy tale full of all the
niceties that can only aptly decorate paradise - I heard him ask if
Foday Sankoh was seen within the corridors of Heaven but do you think
Sankoh has any business being there ?
Oh my God! Everybody wants to be president in 2007 but for what? I
heard one of the aspirants saying it is because of continuity but do
you believe this? Is it to continue selfish interest or to mine our
gold, diamond, silver and bauxite with complete disregard for
national interest?
Saj has every reason to dream of good roads paved with diamonds and
gold, of Sierra Leoneans going to bed after eating breakfast , lunch
and dinner, of angels coming down to keep company with mortals at
Hill Station Lodge and even of Christians going to Mosques to worship
and Moslems reciprocating. However my brother and sister, with the
clique of leaders and the mentality of our very selves, this can only
come true in a dream and this is the sorriest part. Go to nearby
Ghana, Senegal and even tiny Gambia, you will be surprised at the
level of development. Liberia is waking up and about to start flying.
But in Sierra Leone, we can only dream that by 2007, Sierra Leone go
tan lek heaven." And this is the sorriest part.
The writer, onetime editor of Concord Times now works with the United
Nations Mission in Liberia (UNMIL)

Original_Search_Title: Kanu Risks Becoming an Ethnic Party, Warns MP
Headline: Kenya: Kanu Risks Becoming an Ethnic Party, Warns MP
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kanu risks being reduced to a regional party, Tinderet Member of
Parliament Henry Kosgey has said
Kosgey, who is the party's national vice-chairman, said the recent
declaration by Eldoret North MP William Ruto that he would vie for the
presidency did not reflect the party's national aspiration.
Speaking at various trading centres in his constituency, Kosgey told
Rift Valley Kanu MPs to reject regional politics.
"There is need for national consultation in the party when it comes to a
sensitive issue like the presidency," he said.
The MP said the party's national governing council would meet in Nairobi
to discuss the issue because it threatened to divide the party on ethnic
lines. He said political aspirations should be guided by a sense of
nationalism party especially when dealing with issues such as the
presidency.
Kosgey, who is also the chairman of the party's Rift Valley
parliamentary group, said Kanu, unlike other parties, had a national
outlook.
"I would like you to be focused, especially on the presidency. Soon the
national governing council will meet to discuss the matter," he said.
He said there was need for the party to exhibit collective
responsibility instead of issuing personal statements that portray the party
as a regional entity.
"Those pushing for the presidency should first take reflection of what
happened during the referendum when the Orange team handed the Banana camp a
devastating defeat," he said.
He said only teamwork could lead to victory in the 2007 general
election.
Political supremacy
Meanwhile, the battle for political supremacy in the Rift Valley between
Ruto and Gideon Moi is likely to come out in the open at a rally planned for
the weekend.
Gideon, who is Baringo Central MP, will be the chief guest at Silibwet,
Bomet District, on Saturday. Local politicians, including those opposed to
Ruto's bid for the presidency, are expected to attend.
It will be a follow-up to a weekend meeting between Gideon and Kanu
delegates from Baringo and Koibatek districts that was ostensibly called to
drum support for the Baringo Central MP in the wake of hostile schemes by
pro-Ruto leaders.
Some 10 MPs met last week to replace Gideon as the Rift Valley Kanu
representative, but the "elections" flopped after Kosgey intervened.
On Sunday, Gideon confirmed he would attend the Saturday meeting to
"chart the way forward." He also accused a Kanu politician he did not name
of fighting him through proxies. He said he believed his attempted
replacement was linked to Ruto's quest for presidency.
"You don't need to be a rocket scientist to see the issues are
related. How come such a thing has not happened before? It is a case of an
individual illegally scheming behind the curtains to further his ambition,"
he said.
But he said he would face his opponents and dared them to hold fresh
"proper" elections involving Kanu delegates.
"They will find in me a worthy opponent. What the MPs attempted to do
last week was illegal. It is the delegates who gave me the job not a cabal
of MPs," he said.
Although Gideon will be in Silibwet to officially flag off the
provincial cross country event and later open the Bomet constituency office,
politics will likely dominate the event.

Original_Search_Title: 'Compile Obasanjo's Impeachable Offences' - Waba Tells Nass
Headline: Nigeria: 'Compile Obasanjo's Impeachable Offences' - Waba Tells Nass
Body_Text:
Lagos — Members of both the Houses of Senate and Representatives were over the weekend urged to compile all the impeachable offences committed by President Olusegun Obasanjo, since his administration came into being in 1999.
The call was made by President of the West African Bar Association, Barrister Femi Falana, while speaking with newsmen in Lagos. Falana also insisted that the current 'regime' has lost all legal and moral legitimacy to remain in power, and that President Obasanjo should seriously heed a recent call by Nobel Laureate, Professor Wole Soyinka, to relinquish power or face mass action.
"Nobody has said Obasanjo should be impeached. Soyinka only advised him to leave. I fully and unequivocally adopt the statement credited to Prof. Wole Soyinka, which was made in the interest of Nigerian generations yet unborn, so we can have political stability in Nigeria".
According to him, "I would like to call on the National Assembly that has been so concerned with the Oyo State crisis, with the 3rd term agenda, disobedience to court orders and willful disregard to the laws of Nigeria, including all the Appropriation Acts of 1999.
I submit that no Appropriation Act out of seven signed by Obasanjo into law, has been observed. Infact, they have been observed in breach by a government that swore to observe the laws and Constitution of the land".
"Obasanjo's regime has persistently encouraged and supported provocative disobedience of court orders. The regime has also habitually endorsed thuggery in Anambra, Ekiti, Oyo, and Lagos States, contrary to constitutional provisions. Apart from outlawing thuggery, the Constitution guarantees safety of lives and properties of every citizen. This regime has lost all legal and moral legitimacy to remain in power".
"One is therefore compelled to call on the National Assembly to proceed in its determination by embarking without any further delay in compilation of all the impeachable offences committed by Obasanjo since 1999. We want full participation of all National Assembly members. Civil Society Organsiations (CSOs) are fully prepared to supply all recognised evidence that would facilitate the exit of a regime that has become a monstrous impediment in the consolidation of democracy".
"To avert such an embarrassing situation, Mr. President should take Soyinka's advice very seriously by stepping down. Gbenga (President Obasanjo's son) was right when he said his father was old and tired. He (Obasanjo) is advised to embark on voluntary retirement with his outdated ideas".
Speaking on the resumption of work by the Alhaji Ibrahim Mantu-led committee on constitutional review, Falana said, "one should commend both Houses for standing on the side of constitutionalism and rule of law. Both Nnamani (Ken, Senate President) and Masari (Bello, Reps Speaker) have assured Nigerians that National Assembly would not pass any resolution that would legitimise the 3rd term agenda. Once we have that assurance, we are confident that those selfish governors who are supporting him (Obasanjo) would soon be shamed".
"The report of the Mantu committee would soon be dumped in the dustbin of history, where it rightly belongs. On mass action, we organised one in September last year, successful rallies through the six geo-political zones in Nigeria. Those rallies were unprecedented in the annals of popular struggle in Nigeria. They (rallies) sounded a note of warning. Now that we are back in the trenches, the forces of dictatorship should be ready to be pushed out of power".
On the Oyo State crisis, the WABA leader said, "the struggle for democracy must transcend personal or selfish interest of the individuals concerned otherwise there will be no end to it. This point was never lost to all the counsel involved in this case. We are learning great lessons from this case. Section 188(10) the ouster clause is going to face a monumental challenge".
"This case is going to be used to redefine the position on impeachment. Also, by this case, a point will be proved that Nigeria will not be allowed to be reduced to a banana republic. A number of patriots paid the supreme sacrifice to put this rickety democracy in place. Nobody will be allowed to take Nigeria back to the dark age of dictatorship. By the time Ladoja (impeached Oyo State governor) is re-instated, I am sure he will be a genuine defender of democracy", Falana said.

Original_Search_Title: North Africa Deadly With the Dead Ball
Headline: North Africa Deadly With the Dead Ball
Body_Text:
It is no surprise that hosts and defending champions dread opening matches; they are potential banana skins.
However, it is possible that the reason I had a sense of de ja vu as I watched Egypt ease past Libya was because I had predicted that the hosts of this year's MTN African Nations Cup would win it.
That prediction was the least of my worries though, as I was more concerned with paying attention to the small details that give North Africans the technical and tactical edge over their sub-Saharan rivals.
While it was inevitable that the cameras should be tuned to golden boy Ahmed Hossam Mido's every touch and the mazy runs of Egypt's creative inspiration Mohammed Barakat, I was eager to see who would take charge of the hosts' dead balls.
Up stepped Mohammed Abu Terika, not a household name on the continent, to strike a freekick with delightful bend on it for Egypt's second goal.
Sub Saharan Africa has produced very few dead ball masters since Kalusha Bwalya, with Okocha, Geremi and Didier Drogba the only recognisable players with respectable stats at CAN 2006.
Yet I get the feeling that Egypt has more than just Terika with the ability to execute, and that Tunisia and Morocco will unveil yet more experts.

Original_Search_Title: More Controversy in Algak Vote Outcome
Headline: Kenya: More Controversy in Algak Vote Outcome
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The controversy surrounding the recent Association of Local Government
Authorities of Kenya (Algak) elections in Mombasa deepened yesterday when
one of the voters alleged his name was forged.
Vihiga Mayor Henry Ramuka claimed yesterday that his name was used to
indicate that his council had participated in the election despite his being
absent from the meeting during the voting.
Eldoret Mayor Josiah Magut unseated councillor Joe Aketch of
Nairobi.
Cllr Magut was declared winner by the presiding officer, Mrs Angeline
Hongo, after receiving 58 votes against Mr Aketch's 57.
But the losing camp disputed the outcome on the grounds that only 114
councils were present during the election.
Mr Ramuka added another turn to the saga yesterday when he told the
Sunday Nation on the phone: "Mr Magut is in office illegally because the
election was tampered with in his favour and I am prepared to give evidence
in court to prove that my council never participated in the election."
Mr Aketch disowned the results and said he would not hand over the
office to Mr Magut until fresh elections were called.
Cllr Ramuka said he boycotted the elections when the two contenders
brought in the Banana and Orange campaigns into the exercise, a move he
noted was bound to split the organisation.
In another development, the delegate who was elected treasurer during
the election, Mr Pius Mutemi from Masaku county council, declined to take
his post, saying he was never consulted.
The Aketch group, led by Nairobi Mayor Dick Wathika, said all the
councils that voted for him would stop paying their subscriptions to the
Magut team until fresh elections were held.

Original_Search_Title: 2,000 Chiefs Face the Sack
Headline: Kenya: 2,000 Chiefs Face the Sack
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government plans to sack over 2,000 chiefs by March this year, The Saturday Standard has reliably learnt.
The chiefs' fate was sealed yesterday during the two-day induction seminar for Cabinet Ministers, Assistant Ministers and Permanent Secretaries at the Kenya School of Monetary Studies, Nairobi.
President Mwai Kibaki officially opened the induction, which began on Thursday.
Sources at the closed-door meeting revealed that Internal Security Minister John Michuki promised the gathering that at least 2,000 chiefs would be shown the door by March.
There are about 6,000 chiefs in the country.
Michuki's remarks followed complaints by several Cabinet ministers and assistant ministers that the Provincial Administration, especially the chiefs, are frustrating Government programmes at the grassroots.
Already, several chiefs have been sacked following the humiliating defeat the Government was handed at the November 21 referendum.
They were sacked after a Cabinet Minister from Nyanza blamed them for having contributed to the loss of the Banana team.
Sources said the Cabinet ministers were so incensed by the chiefs who failed to campaign for the Banana team during the referendum that they demanded action against them.
But the Cabinet minister from Nyanza told the gathering that he identified those chiefs who had frustrated government programmes and forwarded their names to the Minister in charge of the Provincial Administration.
The Chiefs who were named by the Minister have since being sacked.
According to the source, Michuki asked the ministers and their assistants to forward to him names of the chiefs who did not support them during the referendum.
Assistant Minister Beth Mugo told her colleagues that if they do not do anything now, it would be difficult to make an impact at the 2007 General Election.
Mugo, according to sources, said the Government could no longer rely on civil servants.
She suggested that the Government employs professionals to steer its campaign in the next General Election.
Government Spokesman Dr Alfred Mutua told the Cabinet ministers and assistant ministers that if nothing is done in the next six months, they should forget about winning the forthcoming polls.
The sources said that there was general consensus that the entire media was hostile to the Government.
They singled out the Kenya Broadcasting Corporation for turning against the Government and yet it was a State Corporation whose duty is to support Government all the time.
The matter is said to have been raised by Mrs Mugo.
But Dr Mutua asked the ministers to work with the media and try to convince them to support their views.
Dr Mutua added that he would continue to bombard the media with information on what the Government was doing.
On Thursday, the meeting was unanimous that the national TV station has not lived up to expectations that it should serve as a Government mouthpiece.
The Government officials complained to the management of KBC that the station did not support the Government fully during the just concluded referendum.
Sources at the induction seminar said the station was put on the spot and its management asked to change its policy.
The corporation's Managing Director, Wachira Waruru, who presented a paper at the meeting, told the gathering that KBC ceased to be a propaganda tool long ago.
Waruru told the dignitaries that previous governments almost destroyed KBC by using it as a propaganda tool, which makes no sense in a liberalised environment.
"This government should not make the same mistake," warned Waruru in his presentation.
"KBC should serve all Kenyans not just those who happen to be in power," said Waruru.
A firm Waruru reminded the audience that KBC has a duty to support government initiatives and policies by covering Cabinet ministers and their assistants in their official duties. "But KBC has no obligation to promote individual political opinions of ministers," said Waruru.
He added: "A minister will call me to complain because his political rival in the constituency has appeared on TV, but will never comment about the numerous radio programmes addressing the development needs of his constituents."
Waruru charged that he is under constant pressure from ministers to tilt reports in favour of their political views.
He insisted that what KBC needs, "which is what we are trying to do, is to give it credibility - like the BBC - so that it is respected and trusted by everybody".
"The Government has failed to fund KBC operations as a public service broadcaster to the extent that KBC is the only public broadcaster in the world that is not government-funded, yet all I get from ministers is complaints about coverage," said Waruru.
KBC, he said, has survived because of the improvement of services, resulting in higher advertisement revenues and credibility.

Original_Search_Title: Verbatim
Headline: Ghana: Verbatim
Body_Text:
Accra — "If Obasanjo attempts to use the faceless bootlickers in the legislature to fiddle with the constitution in order to have a third 4-year mandate, Nigeria will burn. Mind you, Nigeria isn't Uganda; Nigeria isn't one of the banana republics where presidents wake up with a whim that becomes law by lunch time."
-Nigerian civil society groups warning President Obasanjo not to disrespect the Nigerian constitution or else there will be 'fire' in the country.
"Ghanaians have compared the regimes of both the NDC and the New Patriotic Party and realized that the NDC had better prospects for them. This goodwill will make Ghanaians vote for the NDC in 2008. Having corrected some of our past mistakes, we are coming back this time not as starters in governance, but an experienced side that has all it takes to dish out good governance and general development."
-Mr. Alban Bagbin, Minority Leader and Member of Parliament for Nadowli, stating that the recent spate of resignations that have hit the party after its Koforidua's violent national delegates congress will not affect the party's fortunes come 2008. Quote cited in the Daily Graphic of Tuesday, January 17, 2006, page 15.
"Ours is not just a share of the national cake, but we want to be appreciated and consulted. We want more natural trust and respect. If you were a priest posted to a nominally idol worshipping area and you went and met a five member congregation and left after five years keeping only that number then you have failed. They should not suggest by their actions that it is for selfish reasons that they seek positions in the party."
-Togbe Afede XIV, the Agbogbomefia of Ho Asogli state, accusing NPP officers of the region of non-performance and failing to promote the interest of the region.
"The beach is dead all day and even if you find yourself among a bunch of local disorganized noisy groups on a drinking and picnicking spree on the weekend, you might end up drowning without ambulance to take you away in good time or good divers to spew you out. Go to the Zoo and you will see animals in prison, restricted habitats and under budgeted service on display at a site meant to educate, inform and inculcate the sense of love for your unique and diverse wildlife heritage."
-Mr. Okyeame Ampadu-Agyei of conservation International, Ghana, in a feature in The Chronicle of Tuesday January 2006, illustrating a vivid picture of the state of tourists' sites in Ghana.
"We know that your vote was a vote for change; a vote for peace, security and we have heard you loudly. I vow to wage war on Liberia's major public enemy, corruption. Everyone appointed to high positions of public trust will be required to declare their assets. I will lead by example. I will be the first to comply by declaring my assets for publication within a week from today."
-Mrs. Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf, Liberia President elect, promising to fight corruption. She spoke while taking her oath of office.
"After a careful consideration of the evidence before the committee, the committee found that Dr. Josiah Aryeh had an illicit relationship with some members of the NPP, namely; Nii Owoo and Paa Nii Crabbe. Together with Dr. Josiah Aryeh, they held secret meetings with some key members of the NPP such as Mr. Steve Ntim, the First National Vice Chairman of the party and Mr. Yaw Boahen."
-The Bagbin Committee of Enquiry' report, page 14, 2005, on the alleged flirtation of Dr. Josiah Aryeh, then General secretary of the NDC with members of the NPP during the 2004 general election.
"As far as I am concerned, there is no party on the ground. The sentiments are very positive for the CPP. You go to many villages and some people say, 'we gave the NDC eight years. We have given the NPP eight years. We want to give another party eight years. But which party? There is no CPP there to take advantage of that situation. That is the fact on the ground. There is no party and there is no organization because there have been terrible traces of bad leadership. So when people talk about me forming a political party, what I say is that they are putting the cart before the horse."
-Dr. Paa Kwesi Nduom, CPP Member of Parliament and Minister for Public Sector Reform, diagnosing the problems of his part. Quote cited in the Crusading Guide of Tuesday, January 17, 2006.

Original_Search_Title: FDC Chief Besigye Fears Fresh Charges
Headline: Uganda: FDC Chief Besigye Fears Fresh Charges
Body_Text:
Kampala — FORUM for Democratic Change (FDC) presidential candidate Dr. Kizza Besigye has said the government is planning fresh charges against him to put him out of the presidential race.
"I have been informed of the new evil plans by some sections of the security organs to further charge me with the intent to put me out of the political race. It is to create a basis to make me fail in my political race and contest," Besigye said.
He said the security organs, after realising that he was going to win the rape and terrorism cases against him, are coaching people to make false allegations against him.
He did not name names yesterday while addressing journalists at Eneku village in Soroti town.
Besigye said whereas this would not have any worthy legal implications, it would affect him negatively and divert him from campaigning.
"This will not only waste time for me, but also waste resources in legal battles, hiring lawyers and going to court, wasting time I would have used for campaigning," Besigye said. He said he was going to make a formal statement on the matter at an appropriate time.
"I want security organs to restrain from this evil plan and level the ground for all candidates to campaign," he said.
Besigye also said there was abuse of power in presidential campaigns, which made the contest unfair and not free. He said the Government and its functionaries like the DISO and GISO should stop from intimidating his supporters.
Besigye also attacked the Inspector General of Police, Maj. Gen. Kale Kayihura, saying he was partisan and was turning the police into a para-military group like Kalangala action plan.
"I want to express my dissatisfaction with the IGP, Kale Kayihura. He is a fundamentalist of the movement like Kakooza Mutale," he said.
Besigye said he was compiling all his complaints to present to the IGP and other government organs.
He also attacked the New Vision, Uganda Broadcasting Corporation (Radio Uganda and TV), saying they are biased in the coverage of the presidential campaigns.
He said the New Vision is concentrating on covering President Museveni and Dr. Abed Bwanika, an independent presidential candidate.
Besigye said the New Vision is like an NRM campaign pamphlet.
He said UBC Radio and TV had ignored other candidates except Museveni and Bwanika.
Besigye also said, "The level of political harassment of our supporters has escalated. Many of our supporters are subjected to arbitrary arrest by the police. They are detained at the police cells and are not charged, or are charged with being idle and disorderly," he said. Besigye spent yesterday campaigning in Teso.
On Wednesday, Besigye told a rally at Olio primary school in Serere that he was already the winner of the election to be held on February 23, reports Nathan Etengu.
"There is no doubt that we have already won the election. What remains is for us to make sure that our votes are not stolen," he told a huge crowd.
He told a crowd at Soroti Independence garden, "I have no doubt that we are going to give you the next government. We must make sure that we sweep away all the dry banana leaves and burn them."

Original_Search_Title: Inauguration of Anarchy
Headline: Nigeria: Inauguration of Anarchy
Body_Text:
Lagos — If there is one issue that confirms that corruption has become the
defining currency for socio-political interaction in Nigeria today, it is
the current drama in Oyo State where the government is now, for all
practical purposes, hijacked by an unelected 'strongman' who installed a
proxy in a patron-client relationship that has now exposed our country as no
better than a banana republic.
While this is not the first time we would be having this kind of sordid
affair in our polity in which a political 'investor' would hold the people
hostage in the bid to recoup his 'investment', in the present circumstance,
all the institutions of State have conspired to create, or more
appropriately, reinforce, the impression that political legitimacy in
Nigeria is founded on the expectation that office holders must 'share the
spoils' under an arrangement dictated by godfathers.
From the presidency to the police to the court, Oyo State has exposed
not only the hypocrisy of this government about the war on corruption but
has indeed shown that in our country today, power is held in trust not for
the people but for a few mandarins while office holders will survive or fail
not by their stewardship but their ability to distribute state resources to
satisfy some corrupt godfathers in the name of 'loyalty', which has become
the ideology of the moment.
What I find inexplicable is that President Olusegun Obasanjo, who in
1999 gave indication that he was opposed to this clientele politics, when he
pointedly told his sponsors to forget the idea of material reward, has
himself become the leading promoter of this primitive culture of political
engagement as we have seen in Anambra and Oyo States. The danger, as my
colleague, Kayode Komolafe, pointed out most poignantly yesterday, is that
those who revel in the lawlessness, if not official decadence, that has been
foisted on the people of Oyo State today obviously have learnt no lesson
from history and may, at the fullness of time, bear the ultimate
consequences for what he (Komolafe) appropriately described as the
inauguration of anarchy, a phrase I find rather arresting for my headline
today.
Instructively, I am writing this piece from Herfordshire, a serene
countryside village in the United Kingdon, where I arrived Tuesday morning
to participate in the Aspen Institute Nigeria Leadership Seminar for which I
am invited as one of 20 Nigerians from across the world that include Mr Bayo
Ogunlesi, Prof. Ibitayo Akinwande, Jimmy Gboyega Delano, Dele Olojede et al
while from home are Father Mathew Kukah, Bunmi Oni, Ndidi Okonkwo Nwuneli,
Nuhu Ribadu, Jim Ovia, Tony Elumelu, Oye Hassan Odukale, Nasir el-Rufai,
Bola Adesola, Frank Nweke and Major General Sarki Mouktah (rtd).
In the last couple of weeks, I have had to read a lot of books as
demanded by the programme but now I wonder whether the organisers have not
invited the wrong people because those who influence events in my country
hardly bother about Jean Jacques Rousseau's 'Social Contract' or the
writings of Milton Friedman, Thomas Hobbes, Thomas Jefferson, Arthur Okun,
Chinua Achebe and all such literatures; all they do, as in the case of Oyo
State, is to spend quality hours that could otherwise be spent in productive
enterprise, eating 'amala' at Molete and massaging the ego of one old man
who can catapult them from obscurity to public offices and instant, but
ill-gotten, wealth. But for how long do we continue on that road to
collective self-destruction?
Perhaps for political expediency or purely for persoanl reasons like
vengeance, or both, President Obasanjo has unwittingly allowed himself to be
associated with a process where some gangsters trampled on the constitution,
subverted the rule of law, enthroned and glorified violence as a weapon of
political interrogation and ended up using brute force to get an elected
public official out of office in exactly the same way motor park touts
(Agberos) usually conduct their affairs in Mushin or Oshodi in Lagos, all at
the behest of a godfather.
What makes it more pathetic is that all the people that should
ordinarily speak up, especially political leaders in Oyo State, are either
compromised or they cannot see beyond the person of Ladoja who, it must be
admitted, may be as unworthy a character as his estranged godfather. But for
us to properly situate the gravity of what is happening and the implication
for our democracy, we should examine how we came to this sorry pass in which
power has now been successfully hijacked in the state just for one man to
have unhindered access to public wealth.
For all one may say about him, however, and it is actually the tragedy
of the whole episode, the only honest person in the whole debacle is Alhaji
Lamidi Ariyibi Adedibu because, unlike other actors, he is not pretending
that this whole farce is about public good or ideals. On the contrary, he
has told the world that he is in this fight for money and that he has merely
been promoting his personal interest when he said publicly: 'Ladoja is too
greedy. He was collecting N65 million as security vote every month. You know
the governors don't account for security votes. He was to give me N15
million of that every month, he reneged. Later, it was reduced to N10
million, yet he did not give me.'
What is presented before the world in that Adedibu thesis of politics,
and he must have done his 'research' very well on what governors do with
'security votes', is that the crisis in Oyo State is not because Ladoja is
corrupt (as he may well be) or that he has scant regard for the rule of law
(which is true). It is not even that the embattled governor is as docile in
office as his personal facade but rather that he (Ladoja) has been
unfaithful to an under-the-table deal in which he (Adedibu) would be given
N15 million on a monthly basis as a first line charge in the Oyo State
allocation perhaps under the subhead: godfather!
From this, we can infer that if there were no disagreement between
Ladoja and Adedibu in the sharing of loot, there would have been no crisis
in Oyo State regardless of how badly the governor performs in office. What
therefore bothers me here is the role our President has played in his
previous attempts to mediate the crisis. Given Adedibu's public posture that
his problem with Ladoja has to do with public money, it is inconceivable
that he would express any altruistic misgiving in secret meetings so we can
safely assume that in all the attempts at intervention, the bottomline has
always been what percentage of Oyo State money should be handed to Adedibu
as godfather. That may explain why we have often been told by Peoples
Democratic Party (PDP) leaders at varying levels that the problem is
lingering because 'Ladoja does not keep to agreements' and you ask,
agreements to share public funds?
While politics, by its nature, goes with a measure of coruption, and
every society has patrons like Adedibu who must 'eat the labour of their
work', I have never heard of a society that would openly promote, with the
connivance of people in authortity, that an individual be given any amount
of public wealth without offering any direct service as we see in the
Adedibu/Ladoja wager and that is why this democracy is imperriled because we
are building its foundation on quicksand. What's more, in a system where
there are several Adedibus and and many more potential Adedibus who would
expect to be patronised, how can we have development and how would anyone
claim to be fighting corruption and be taken seriously by civilised people?
How can we encourage a system where public money would be given to a
private person under the guise of 'security vote' and still argue that
Transparency International is not rating our country fairly in their
corruption perception index in which we now annually excel in the ladder of
shame? It is indeed Adedibu's public affirmation of the reason for falling
out with Ladoja that makes many people to see the work of the kangaroo
impeachment panel, a member of which I met in his residence the day I
visited him, as no more than hollow rituals and the allegations against the
governor as mere afterthought even if they are true.
But what was shocking is the way the acting Oyo State Chief Judge,
Justice Afolabi Adeniran, allowed himself to be so shamefully used to
perpetuate lawlessness and it must be clear to him now that his career as a
judge may soon be over. Because I cannot imagine any decent person allowing
himself to be tried by such a judge who has no respect for the law. I have
said it before that anybody who wants to rule Oyo State must be a student of
Niccolo Machaivelli, but Ladoja, notwithstanding the lessons he ought to
have learnt from Enugu and Anambra States where godfathers must now have
unpleasant tales to tell, displayed an appaling level of incompetence and
political naivety that eventually made it easy for Adedibu to push him out
so cheaply. Rather instructively too, beyond the media and civil society, he
has had no support, even from politicians in his state who ordinarily find
Adedibu's amala politics reprehensible. What sort of man is that?
I have in recent weeks received mails suggesting that I seem to have no
sympathy for Ladoja and it is true though it is misrepresented by some
people to mean I support Adedibu. While I never imagined that the 18 Oyo
State law breakers would 'impeach' a governor using Molete arithmetics, I
particularly did not, and still do not, think Ladoja is a responsible
governor given his own contempt for the rule of law.
It is indeed so unfortunate that one would be using Ladoja's predicament
as a symbol in the current struggle to enthrone democracy anchored on the
rule of law, as we do today, essentially because the issue transcends his
person. The truth is that Ladoja himself is seeking equity with unclean
hands given the way he handled the Ibarapa Council episode in which he has
for three years denied the elected Chairman his office, and allowed his PDP
crony to serve a mandate he did not win, notwithsanding the avalanche of
court orders he flagrantly refused to obey. But Ladoja is not the issue in
the Oyo political crisis, the point is that we must take our democracy back
from anti-democratic elements like Adedibu and his sponsors.
Incidentally, in explaining his relationship with Adedibu, Ladoja who is
gradually finding his voice, recounted an episode in 2002 when he was
seeking the office, but what he left out should be as interesting as what he
said: 'After we had our quarrel and we made up in 2002, he (Adedibu) came to
me and said, 'let us work together'. I said without him we had been winning
elections, so what is it that he could do that I cannot do?, and he said
'three things'. He asked me, 'do you know how to abuse people?', and I said
No. Then he asked, 'can you take away your clothes in public and fight?', I
said No. Thirdly, he asked, 'can you tell lies against somebody and swear by
the Quran and again call witnesses?'. I said 'impossible!'. Then, he said,
'those are the things we always do in politics.'
The question to ask after this confession is: How come Ladoja still
surendered himself to such an unscrupulous character who used these same
tricks to get him 'elected' as Oyo State Governor in 2003? This question is
important because it helps to explain how public funds end up in private
hands under a political arrangement that is now more rent-dependent than the
economic system that is equally anchored and sustained by corrupt
patronage. Because if Adedibu laid his cards on the table about his
intentions with public money as he is wont to do, Ladoja must also have
agreed and probably swore to an oath of 'loyalty' before Adedibu would make
his machinery available for the political enterprise; afterall, Ladoja has
been claiming; for almost three years now, that Adedibu is his father so we
can conveniently say like father like son!
But even with all these contradictions, for our democracy to survive,
this 'impeachment' must not be allowed to become a fait accompli. The law is
very clear as to the procedures for removing a governor which were clearly
violated by the Adedibu lawmakers. But what is more worrisome is that they
could get institutional support for their illegal act, first by the police
and then by a corrupt arm of the Oyo State judiciary.
When the Chief Justice of Nigeria, Justice Muhammed Uwais, said recently
that our governments, at all levels, choose what judgement to obey and what
to ignore, we never imagined we would get to a level of this dangerous
impasse but it is now incumbent on President Obasanjo to help resolve the
logjam because only he can, at this point: Simply by withdrawing from
Adedibu and his 'governor' the legitimacy they do not have but which is now
being enforced through the intrumentality of state sponsored violence which
the police protection represents.
Impeachment, as we have been told, is a political tool and we agree. But
even at that, there are clear rules of engagement which must be followed,
the reason why most Nigerians looked the other way when DSP Alamieyeseigha
was being served his just dessert by the Bayelsa State House of Assembly
even without a sctrict adherence to legislative procedures though they had
the number. But to cynically ignore the rule of law and the constitution in
the bid to get an elected governor out of office just to satisfy the corrupt
appetite of one 'strongman' is to expose Nigeria to the world as a nation
that not only tolerates corruption but indeed promotes and glorifies it as a
state policy.

Original_Search_Title: Besigye is a False Prophet, Says Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Besigye is a False Prophet, Says Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has described FDC presidential candidate Col. Kizza Besigye as a false prophet.
Museveni, campaigning in Jinja as part of his eastern tour, accused
Besigye of peddling lies about the NRM achievements, and promised to
'detoxicate the toxins' he has sowed in the region.
"I hear there are false prophets here. I am going to unveil their
lies on radio this evening. They tell you that only Indians work in the
factories, but we revived them (factories)," Museveni said, while
challenging the excited supporters at Kakira.
He asked them how many of them work on the sugar estates, and those
in the factory, and the hands of the latter exceeded the former.
He said Indians re-opened the factories and had the skills. He said
the Movement achievements were very many, citing Universal Primary
Education (UPE).
"These night-dancers are telling you that education for all (UPE)
means bonna basiruwale (universal ignorance). I am told that is what
Besigye has been telling you here. As I am going to correct all those
lies, you will know why Besigye ran away from the army," he said.
Museveni, who is seeking a third term, said any one saying UPE is
academic stuntedness, is a detractor and an enemy of the State. He said
statistics show that 2.9 million UPE pupils sat Primary Leaving
Examinations to join post-primary institutions.
"Why shouldn't Ugandans be happy with such a development? It means
those people in the opposition who cannot see what we have done cannot
lead this country.
"Support us and we get a landslide victory and continue leading the country," Museveni said.
Museveni, who left Kampala for Jinja at 1:55pm, made stop-overs on
the Kampala-Jinja highway, as enthusiastic supporters carrying posters
and dry banana leaves stopped him to address them.
At different stages, supporters were seen sweeping the road ahead of
the convoy, especially in Mbiko, Njeru, Nabusanya and Bugembe.
Traffic along the route was interrupted as a fleet of trucks,
omni-buses, salon cars and motorcycles jammed the dual carriageway
while honking. Supporters blew whistles and chanted: "No change".
It took the President about two-and-half hours to move from Mbiko
(Njeru) to Nabisenya, a 3km-stretch, as hundreds of supporters swarmed
the convoy, and mounted roadblocks so he could address them.
Museveni said Universal Secondary Education would be introduced to absorb UPE products.
He said the Movement returned peace in the country and turned the economy around.
Museveni did not hold the main rally, but addressed a series of
small rallies on the highway through Bugembe, to Kakira. After the
Kakira estate rally, Museveni drove through Buyengo, Butagaya, Kagoma,
a route that Besigye used when he campaigned in Jinja.
At those stop-overs there were kadodi, larakaraka and kisoga dances.
He is slated to speak on Nile Broadcasting Services owned by state
minister for trade, Igeme Nabeta.
At the Mukono-Jinja border, Museveni was received by deputy Speaker
Rebecca Kadaga and ministers Daudi Migereko, Isaac Musumba,
Zirabamuzaale Magoola, Nsaba Buturo, Igeme Nabeta, and droves of
supporters.
Museveni has already been to the west and Buganda.

Original_Search_Title: From Rags to Riches: Bahati Beat the Odds to Help Fellow Orphans
Headline: Uganda: From Rags to Riches: Bahati Beat the Odds to Help Fellow Orphans
Body_Text:
Kampala — ON his third birthday, he had an encounter with a trail of
misfortunes. His mother left home to give birth and died during labour.
His father was poisoned under unclear circumstances. The little boy
sought refuge in the hands of his grandmother - but she also died when
he had just joined Primary Six.
After the death of the trio, David Bahati became a village wonder
boy. He gambled with life and took to selling banana leaves and doing
casual jobs to raise school fees.
However, today, he is an accomplished academician, leader and humanitarian activist.
Recently, he beat Steven Bamwanga, MP Ndorwa West in the NRM-O
primaries in Kabale district to become the sole Movement parliamentary
seat contender in this constituency. He now has to beat two opponents
to achieve his dream. This is what locals in Ndorwa never envisioned
about a boy who, at one time, was feared could not survive infancy.
"Our prayer was that he grows into an adult and finds some odd work
