to eke a living. That is the best we hoped he would be in life," says
Mzee Musa Kijagi, who witnessed the small boy's woes.
At school, Bahati was nicknamed a problem student.
Former schoolmates remember him as never having missed a school
meal. He fed on posho and beans throughout the term, while his
colleagues had the opportunity to sample other dishes brought by their
parents. During school holidays, he stayed at school to do manual work
to raise fees.
Thirty years later, his resolve and determination have paid off.
Once a hopeless orphan, he has defied all odds to become a role model
in his village, where the problems of orphans and limited resources
reign.
"I was always hopeful and counted on God. Despite circumstances that
surround your life, you can make it if you are determined," says Bahati.
When he takes a stroll around the village paths of Rubona in Kabale,
locals besiege him with countless problems. Memories of a small boy who
frequented town to sell banana leaves are no more.
"He initiated a scholarship fund for orphans in this area and many
of the children have benefited," says Mande Coporo of the same village.
Julius Mucunguzi, a senior communications officer with World Vision,
recalls that there was a time Bahati missed school meals, not because
he wanted but he had been sent home for school fees.
"If they sent fees defaulters home, the only time to get those who
didn't leave was during meals. He would attend class and forego the
meals since he saw no reason of leaving school when he was not going to
get the fees anyway.
Many of the other students envied him despite his despicable situation," says Mucunguzi.
At a glance, Bahati looks reserved and cuts the posture of an
introvert. He speaks in a low tone, punctuated with illustrative
gestures in a reserved mood. The relatively macho-sized youth with a
round face occasionally smiles. But behind the jolly personality is a
history of tribulations.
Elders in his home area remember him as a small boy who used to trek
miles to Kabale town carrying loads of banana leaves twice his weight
to sell. This was his source of income for school fees.
"Vending banana leaves was the only way I could raise school fees," says Bahati.
Thirty years after the demise of his parents, the only graduate in a
family of five has proved that success and determination are closely
linked.
"Then, his only hope was surviving to see the next day, but now, he
is a successful man. He has set up programmes to reach out to people
with problems," Rev. Canon Aryeija says.
Bahati is the pioneer of Rubona Scholarship Fund, catering for
orphans and bright students from Kabale who cannot afford fees. The
fund mainly targets UPE leavers who can't afford O'Level education.
We plan to take the initiative to A'Level since O'Level will be addressed under the Universal Secondary Education," he says.
Donning a grey suit, when he begins to speak, the politician in him
is not convincing - at least going by standards of a typical
politician. He speaks slowly and humbles himself even when he is
addressing an excited crowd of supporters.
"He is a politician whose action and deeds are not based on verbosity," remarks one of his campaign agents.
In his New Year message, after his landslide victory to become the
sole NRM-O parliamentary candidate for Ndorwa West, Bahati said, "The
role of a Member of Parliament is legislation. I will push for laws
that can help address the problems of my people."
Bahati was only 13 when the Movement government came to power and has come to appreciate the system.
He says it is time for the first-born generation of the Movement to ensure its continuity.
"We want to give the system continuity. I will use this opportunity
to mobilise for the Movement and for Museveni because of his untainted
track record in transforming the country," he says.
Bahati's inspiration in life is his late grandmother, Esther
Mabuguma. She instilled in him the values of being respectful,
trustworthy, patient and generous.
"Even when you have nothing to offer, a generous heart can be
everything," Bahati recalls words his grandmother told him as a child.
Born in 1973 to the late David Kasisiri and Joy Kantobo, he
completed primary education in 1986. Later, he joined Kigezi High
School for O and A'Level.
He almost failed to put pen to his last paper in O'Level because he
had an outstanding debt with the school. However, the headmaster then,
Henry Turyagenda, stood surety for him.
While in school, he took up several responsibilities. He was a
prefect in O'Level and held several leadership positions at university.
During his A' Level, he first offered Physics, Chemistry and Maths
but his frequent absenteeism made the course difficult for him. He
ended up changing to Maths, Economics and Geography.
During his A'Level, he won a scholarship from Strommey Foundation, a
Norwegian Christian charity, through Kigezi Diocese. The scholarship
was offered to poor but bright students in Kabale.
After completing A'Level in 1992, Bahati joined Makerere University
and graduated with a Bachelor of Commerce Degree. He joined Diamond
Trust Bank as a clerk before heading to the Population Secretariat as
an accountant.
At the secretariat, he rose through the ranks to become head of finance and administration.
A colleague who worked with him at the secretariat described him as
a reserved but very focused workaholic with a magnetic personality. In
2001, Bahati secured a scholarship from the Government to pursue a
Masters in Business Administration at Cardiff University.
At Cardiff, he was the chairman of the Business School and Treasurer
Afro-Caribbean Society. Bahati says none of his siblings went far in
education.
In his endeavour to give back to the community, Bahati plans to
address several issues including improving household incomes and
health, especially for pregnant mothers who lack medical facilities in
Kabale.
"My mother died during labour. If facilities were available, she
would have lived to raise me and the baby who died with her,"he says.
Bahati, who also doubles as a chartered accountant, walked down the
aisle with Juliet Kyomugisha in 2001. The couple is blessed with two
sons.

Original_Search_Title: Parents Debunk Film Star's 'Resurrection'
Headline: Nigeria: Parents Debunk Film Star's 'Resurrection'
Body_Text:
Parents of Balaraba Mohammed, a film star who lost her life on her wedding day in a ghastly motor accident on Kaduna-Zaria expressway, have debunked rumours that their daughter had risen from death.
Rumours that late Balaraba had resurrected spread like wild-fire in almost all major cities of the North recently."
The news is all lies started by some mischievous crooks," lamented Mallam Mohammed Kaura, Balaraba's 60-year-old father who said he first heard the news while on business at Panbegua town in Kaduna State.
Responding to questions from Weekly Trust Magazine, Mallam Mohammed who for more than half an hour could only utter "Inna Lillahi Wa Inna Ilaihir Raji'un" meaning "From Allah we all came and to Him will we return," later managed to find the right words to describe the rumours."
It was only the devil's clarion call and echoed by the devil's disciples among us," he said.He said he never believed that the news was aired on the Federal Radio of Nigeria Kaduna which he described as a reputable media outlet. The rumours had it that Jakar Magori a Hausa magazine programme had broadcasted that a certain Nupe man had declared that Balaraba had risen from death or that she never died in the first place.
Reportedly, the anonymous Nupe man had confessed to the use of metaphysical means to cause the 'artificial accident' and made it appear real. The rumour also had it that the death was equally faked and made to seem true to mortal sensitivities.
His motive, according to the myth was that he loved Balaraba so much that he could not bear the humiliation of watching her getting married to another man.
The Nupe man, the rumour said, was reported by Jakar Magori as having said that Balaraba is presently safe in his custody and that if the parents agreed to give her in marriage to him, he would produce her.
The unsubstantiated story also had it that the grave in which late Balaraba was buried had been dug open but only banana peels were found in place of her body. "What abomination, what lies, malice. How could anyone believe that rubbish?" Balaraba's father asked, saying that as a muslim he had faith that one only dies once and will never rise until the day of reckoning.
Said he, "Balaraba has died. Her body was brought to us right here. I felt the pulse and confirmed her dead. The body went through all the rituals for funeral. This story cannot be true. All well-meaning persons should disregard it.
"He said he had to cut short his business and return home to his family for fear that they may be in some danger. "I suspected that this was the machinations of some con men and that they might be up to some mischief. I feared for the safety of my family.
"Mallam Mohammed then called on all those who have any affection for his late daughter to join him in prayers for the repose of her soul.
Our reporters found that people have been trooping to Balaraba's family house since the news began to go round.Family sources also said that Shu'aibu Lawal a.k.a. 'Kumurchi,' Balaraba's star-crossed groom was shocked by the rumours and had to travel to Kaduna from his hometown, Kano to cross-check the news.
The source told Weekly Trust Magazine that Kumurchi's first point of call was the cemetery where he confirmed that Balaraba's tomb had not been tampered with. From the graveyard, he went to Baralaba's family house where the mother confirmed his suspicion and consoled him. Malama Binta Mohammed, late Balaraba's mother, told our reporters that she too never believed the story.
However, Malama Binta could not help getting confused as crowds kept trooping to their house and gossip flew around the quarters. "I find it very unreasonable. How could people start this sort of wicked rumour?" she asked Malama Binta who apparently had not yet overcome the shock of the sudden death of her children was not in the mood of talking to newsmen.
In tears, she appealed to everybody to leave her alone to her calamities. "Only I alone know the agony I went through when they died. And just when I was beginning to cheer up, wicked souls are tormenting me again," she said. Asked whether she viewed the rumours as an attempt by Balaraba's admires to immortalise her, Malama Binta said "there is no admiration in this. It is the devil trying to hurt us.
I know they have died. No power on earth can bring them back to life. Let these evil souls allow the poor girl to lie peacefully in her grave.
"Perhaps no other natural phenomenon has so haunted mankind as death. It has figured in the legends and myths of nearly every culture so that it has become second nature to build certain myths round the death of loved ones or those who in their life time were highly admired. This may explain how the rumour of Balaraba's 'resurrection' got started.

Original_Search_Title: Besigye Eats in Bush
Headline: Uganda: Besigye Eats in Bush
Body_Text:
Kampala — FDC presidential candidate Col. Dr. Kizza Besigye, campaigning in
Rakai on Monday, broke off for some minutes and went to a roadside bush
to have his lunch.
He broke off for lunch a few kilometres to Rakai town after a hectic trek of his tour of the district.
Besigye had began his rally at Lyantonde Town Council from where his
convoy went through a slippery and rough road to lwamaggwa and Rakai
town council, where he arrived at 2:00pm, hours behind his schedule.
He was forced to suspend stopovers at several points where fans had waited for him.
Besigye's aides and the Police refused the journalists to go near
where his two vehicles had parked in a bush at Kasozi, a few metres
away from the main road.
Other vehicles in the convoy parked at the roadside and waited for Besigye.
Rakai district Special Branch officer Denis Ssebugwawo, who guided
the convoy with Police escorts, said Besigye, who moved with his food,
decided not to eat from the town because his supporters would not have
allowed him time to eat.
"He just wanted to avoid being disturbed if he went to Rakai town.
He moved with his food and when he saw a safe place at Kasozi, he
decided to have his lunch there," Ssebugwawo said.
At Kyotera, Besigye refused to burn essanja (dry banana leaves), the
symbol for President Yoweri Museveni's third term, which had been
presented to him with a matchbox by supporters. He said he would get
rid of Museveni peacefully.
Besigyeasked opposition party supporters to stop discriminating
against each other but join forces to get rid of Museveni and the NRM.
"You should stop referring to yourselves as DP or FDC, CP or UPC but
let's support each to remove a big 'log' in our way first," Besigye
said as large crowds cheered him at Kalisizo town in Rakai.

Original_Search_Title: Nyachae: We Lost Right to Restart Review
Headline: Kenya: Nyachae: We Lost Right to Restart Review
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana team has no moral authority to spearhead the constitutional review, having lost at the referendum, said a Cabinet minister yesterday.
Roads and Public Works minister Simeon Nyachae said as one of the leaders who championed the rejected Wako draft but lost, he had no right to spearhead talks on jump-starting the process.
In a democratic set-up, those whose proposal has been rejected by the people should give the others room, said the Ford People leader.
"What moral authority do you have when your proposals were rejected by the people?" he asked at a news conference in his office.
The Parliamentary Select Committee on the Constitution, which he headed, prepared a report on contentious issues in the Bomas Draft and also came up with a draft constitution.
But when Mr Nyachae presented the report, House Speaker Francis ole Kaparo struck off part of it, saying the team had overstepped its mandate.
It was the PSC's report, together with the Bomas Draft, that attorney-general Amos Wako was asked by Parliament to use in drawing up the draft that was thrown out at the November 21 referendum.
Mr Nyachae, however, announced that his party had scheduled a National Executive Council meeting for Monday to discuss the constitution review.
The MP denied being part of the group that visited State House last week to prevail upon the President not to hold a meeting with leaders of the Orange Democratic Movement.
"How do I offer advice when am not even a Narc member. The inner politics of Narc is not my business."

Original_Search_Title: Cheaper Bid for New Laws Needed
Headline: Kenya: Cheaper Bid for New Laws Needed
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Constitution Review Commission wound up its work yesterday, leaving the country with the unfinished agenda for a new constitution.
Kenyans gave their verdict when 3.5 million people voted against the proposed constitution and 2.5 million for it.
However, this was not the death knell on the search for a new constitution. A number of proposals have been floated since the November 21 referendum.
The Government wants a committee of experts. The Law Society of Kenya has named a group to lead the process. The Orange and religious leaders are for one that brings together all groups regardless of their opinions.
Yet the starting point for the new effort remains as controversial as the acrimony which characterised the review.
There are those who think the current Constitution can be the starting point and that politicians need only agree on which parts should be amended.
Their argument is that the Constitution has, after all, taken the country this far - 42 years - guiding Kenya into one of the strongest economies in the region.
Then there is the group that feels the Bomas Draft should be the pedestal because it was "people-driven".
Another group argues that the Government's draft rejected at the referendum should merge with the Bomas Draft to provide the starting point.
In all these, however, there is consolation that all sides realise Kenyans agitated for and still want a new constitution.
There is also the realisation that 16 years of searching have been lost, with the taxpayer forking out Sh7 billion.
Kenyans would be loth to pay such a price yet again.
A less expensive method involving a team of few wise people and a less cumbersome route are the key to success in the second attempt.
Activated to do a good job
It is, for instance, argued in some quarters that the country has institutions that have not been given a chance in the review and which could be activated to do a good job.
One is the Law Reform Commission, a seven-member team existing under an Act of Parliament and whose mandate is to review laws. The commission may spend much less than the top-heavy CKRC.
More importantly, it would be foolhardy to pretend that even experts or the commission could come up with an acceptable document, without the input of leaders - mainly from the Orange and Banana camps. At least the outcome of the referendum showed that no side can win independently and talks akin to the historic 1997 Inter-Parties Parliamentary Group initiative could bring about consensus.
This is because Kenyans gave their views on what type of a Constitution they want, and those views are known.
The next phase of the review must avoid every mistake made in the previous process.
Some of the mistakes included lack of dialogue and consensus building within the spirit of give and take.
For instance, if all sides were to meet and agree on the contents touching on the Executive, the Legislature and devolution, among others, it would only require the attorney-general, who is an employee of the Kenyan taxpayer, to draft a law dealing with those sections.
Better still the Law Reform Commission, also a body of legal experts, may have a contribution to make before they get feedback.
Only then can they draft the law. The Bill would then be taken to Parliament and passed with much ease and a costly referendum would also have been avoided.
Kenyans want a new Constitution, but they want to be spared the pain of paying for it through the nose.

Original_Search_Title: 3rd Term and Our Kitchen Constitution
Headline: Nigeria: 3rd Term and Our Kitchen Constitution
Body_Text:
There is a difference between being adept at double speak, and an inept liar.
Double speak is an art, an ancient one which must be carefully cultivated and cultured, and which has been associated with institutions such as the diplomatic corps and traditionally diplomatic people such as the Yorubas. The bite is taken off the prevarication by the humour in its presentation and the respect in the connotation. The effect on the listener is in the main more pleasant than that of a lie.
An inept liar, on the other hand, is not the most pleasant of fellows. He fails to convince, and worsens this by making it obvious that he is lying, and he does not care who knows it.
While president Obasanjo has been criticized for the size of his cabinet and retinue of advisers, little has been said about the individual quality of their constituents. It is the more reason that the president's adviser on Public Affairs stands out, not for his pedigree but for his exclusive ability to turn public affairs into private affairs.
As we enter into the New Year 2006, the 3rd term question also heats up, 2007 being no longer some time in the distant future, but just next year.
There is reason to think that Nigeria is set to enter into the most turbulent election year it has ever seen. There have been impeachments and rumours of impeachments. There have been convictions and accusations and intrigues and violence, and, as well, lies. Only in the last four years in our national history has a man confessed to giving away his mandate before he'd got it (he didn't actually) and remained governor of a state. Only in this same space of time has a governor been arrested abroad, jumped bail and come back home. And why not, federal chief executives are ignoring court orders, and Senators are being fingered in budget scandals.
Truth is, Nigeria has never had it so messy, and if there is sense in the philosophy that says it is a sign of things getting better. But if it is true and he can pull it off just like Chris Matthews says about George W. Bush and his Iraq experiment, OBJ deserves a Mount Rushmore created from Zuma Rock and his face on it. He deserves it just for trying and correctly detecting that these guys and their money is the problem. Unfortunately he is not exactly exonerated or immune himself.
Corruption has become a big issue in our polity and economy, and an artlessly lying adviser on public affairs does little good either to the public image of his boss or the credibility of his country. Chief Femi Fani Kayode has become, inadvertently, a thorn in Obasanjo's political flesh rather than oil in its wheel.
The constitutional manipulation which Chief Fani Kayode publicly suggests will help his 'constituency' in the achievement of extended power might yet happen, but only because this is Nigeria, where normally natural laws of morality can be swamped into the murk of immense funds, and the funds can still be readily appropriated as long as the misappropriator is in the same camp.
To varying extents, this can be said to be a global phenomenon. Yet the Nigerian case is particularly peculiar. In a way which might now be said to have become traditional words with feminine association such as 'banana' and 'kitchen' have been used in phrases such as 'banana Republic' and 'Kitchen Cabinet' in describing inferior or less than standard phenomena in business, government and politics.
Despite the sexist connotations, our constitution has often been associated with the word 'kitchen' and this association has seen much public approval.
Critics of the 1999 constitution would give the impression of a bored army general sitting, mainly out of boredom, at his stainless steel kitchen counter one evening when his retinue of domestic staff was off. Yet it is the only one we have to work with_ the only document which holds Nigeria together as an entity, and which retains the only semblance of law and order in our national life. It is the document on which Gani Fawehinmi bases all his court challenges but it is also, unfortunately, the same document on which manipulators hang on to.
It is also unfortunate that attempts by the people to create the direction in which their nation will develop have all been thwarted, but for PRONACO's obstinacy.
The constitution can only be as incorruptible as the people who wrote it.
Indeed, there is every indication that they wrote it with their own political future in mind. At the end of the day, it does not matter if Obasanjo and the rest of them do a 3rd or 10th term, as long as it is the wish of the majority of the people. But is not, as they probably know, and the fact that they can buy it will not change that fact.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team to Meet Over Polls
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team to Meet Over Polls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) leaders will soon meet to discuss modalities of fielding a single Presidential candidate in the 2007 General Election.
Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) secretary-general Joseph Kamotho said that Kanu, LDP and other parties within the ODM would be involved in the discussion.
He explained that the registration of ODM by some individuals would be contested in court to ensure the party reverts to the movement's team, which includes Raila Odinga and Uhuru Kenyatta.
The LDP boss told The Standard said that Central MPs who are dissatisfied with President Kibaki's style of leadership were to blame themselves. The race for the Presidency in the LDP has attracted Mwingi North MP Kalonzo Musyoka and Raila.
The other possible candidates are former Vice President Musalia Mudavadi, Uhuru and Chris Okemo. Raila has already indicated that LDP would support a popular candidate to win the elections.
On Monday, Kamotho tore into the 17 MPs from the Mount Kenya region who demanded for the sacking of some of President Kibaki's advisers. He termed their efforts by the MPs as fruitless and of no consequence.
"They were party to what took place in Bomas and even campaigned for the Banana side. They have no principles, they cannot be trusted, that is hogwash," declared Kamotho. He said that the MPs, who include Assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri and Kabete MP Paul Muite, should focus on national issues, including constitutional review, fight against poverty, unemployment, and the fight against corruption and hunger.
Kamotho said the MPs were bitter because they were not appointed to the Cabinet.
"They should go and get the slots the way ministers Musikari Kombo and Charity Ngilu did, instead of complaining," he added.
Kamotho said that it was a waste of time for the MPs to complain because they had failed to join the ODM, which was fighting for the people's rights.

Original_Search_Title: Debates On Draft Divided Kenyans
Headline: Kenya: Debates On Draft Divided Kenyans
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The rejected Constitution will go down in the annals of the country's history as one the most read and debated document by Kenyans.
More than four million copies were published and distributed countrywide by the Government through the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC).
The 197-page document will be remembered for causing the biggest rift in the Cabinet and the country in general, with professionals, political and religious leaders taking different positions on its merits and demerits. They used referendum campaigns to hurl insults at each other in their attempts to woo voters, but often failed to dwell on the 21 chapters of the draft document.
The opponents who included the Law Society of Kenya and the International Commission of Jurists, said the draft had glaring anomalies. They cited the following controversial provision in Chapter One - Supremacy of the Constitution.
"This Constitution is the supreme law of the Republic and binds all State organs at all levels of government and all persons throughout Kenya.
"(2) The validity or legality of this Constitution is not subject to challenge by or before any court or State organ."
As the divisions threatened to tear the country apart, President Kibaki suspended all Cabinet meetings during the campaigns.
This was after eight of his ministers teamed up with the Official Opposition party, Kanu, to oppose the document at the November 21 referendum.
The Wako draft was translated into 16 languages which were used in radio broadcasts.
The draft was referred to as "a mongrel" by its critics in the Orange campaign team, but praised as the "best constitution in the world" by its supporters in the Banana team.
Attorney-general Amos Wako had cobbled together the proposed Constitution from several official documents which were presented to the CKRC by religious leaders, politicians, professionals and the civil society.
The most famous of them was the Bomas Draft, adapted by some of the 629 delegates at the 2003-2004 National Delegates Conference at Bomas of Kenya, Nairobi.
Other documents were the Naivasha and the Kilifi consensus reports prepared by a group of parliamentarians, the faiths-led Ufungamano Initiative led by the secretary general of the National Council of Churches in Kenya, the Rev Mutava Musyimi; and another by the Law Society of Kenya.
The biggest stumbling block was the sharing of executive powers; devolution; enacting and/or amending the constitution; and the legality of the Wako draft.
The No camp had argued that the Bomas Draft had distributed executive powers to the President, the deputy president, the prime minister and the Cabinet. They said the current Constitution distributed executive power to the President and the Cabinet.
But, they argued, under the Wako draft, if the President committed the most serious abuses, it would be virtually impossible to remove him or her from office since a 75 per cent parliamentary majority would be required for that to succeed.
The role of political parties would also be undermined by the Wako Draft, they claimed, saying that it negated the Bomas requirement that the premier be appointed from the largest party or coalition in the National Assembly.
Another troubling weakness, they argued, was that the draft was self-executing and in order to become law, over 30 new Bills would have to be passed in the first year of its enactment - a most difficult target when one considers that Parliament has passed 10 Bills every year, and that the 30 would be difficult to achieve.
Despite the criticism of the Wako draft, the President and the Yes team campaigned for it to the last day.
But the voters rejected it overwhelmingly.

Original_Search_Title: Suspects Arrested At Besigye Campaign
Headline: Uganda: Suspects Arrested At Besigye Campaign
Body_Text:
Kampala — TWO men suspected to be trailing FDC candidate Kizza Besigye as he campaigned in Mpigi district yesterday were arrested.
The incident almost turned violent as angry Besigye supporters tried
to grab the men from the Police hands and lynch them. Guns were raised
in firing position to keep the rowdy mob at bay.
The men who identified themselves as James Frank Banura, a former
soldier and Frank Nyakairu, a businessman, drove a black Toyota Corona
UAF 262H.
However, sources identified James Frank Banura as Banana, a one time security operative.
Besigye's Police escorts arrested the men at Kiriri town where Besigye stopped to greet a roadside crowd.
The Police move was prompted by a complaint from FDC deputy
spokesperson Sarah Eteru who said the car had trailed Besigye's convoy
for 20 kilometres from Buwama town. Eteru said the men might be state
agents.
"You have been following us, do you want to kill Besigye?" enraged Eteru asked the men.
A police search found only one-metre-long herders' cane besides the
driver's seat. The men were detained at Kiriri Police Post and their
vehicle impounded. Besigye proceeded to his main rally at Mpenja
trading centre.
Banura said they were FDC supporters and were following their
candidate like other supporters. "Our only crime is following my
candidate. I came with them from Kampala," he said.
But he failed to produce an FDC membership card. Banura pleaded with
Besigye's security head Mbabasa not to leave them in Kiriri for fear of
the irate crowd.
Besigye told supporters in Mpenja not to be intimidated by the
incident. He addressed six rallies in Mpigi, getting big crowds at
Kibibi, Kanoni and Bulo.
Meanwhile, Besigye has said his party is not to blame for the cutting of foreign aid by donors.
He said the aid cut was caused by financial irregularities and failure to uphold democracy by the Government.
"While it is unfortunate for the country to suffer the brunt of
losing aid, the Government is entirely responsible for the current
situation," Besigye said.
Besigye was speaking on Saturday at rallies in Budondo, Butagaya, Kagoma, Busedde, Kakira, Bugembe and Busoga Square in Jinja.
He said the Government had secured numerous grants but the money had
not benefited communities. He cited the mismanagement of the Global
Fund for AIDS.
Recently, the UK, Norway, Ireland, the Netherlands and Sweden cut
budget support, citing the way the political transition is progressing.
In Bugembe and Jinja, traffic came to a halt as excited crowds,
chanting party slogans, poured on the streets, flashing Besigye's
V-sign. They travelled on motorcycles and trucks, braving the dusty
feeder roads. They chanted: "Omusajja agenda" (President Museveni will
suffer defeat).
In Buwenge, Kakira, Bugembe and Jinja, supporters swept the roads as his convoy snaked in.
Motorists on the Jinja-Iganga highway between Kakira and Jinja
parked by the road-side to give way to the huge crowd that occupied the
double-carriage way.
Besigye took about two hours to move from Bugembe to Busoga Square, 4km away as supporters kept pouring into the road.
Shops on Jinja's Main Street closed.
At Busoga Square, the crowd surged forward as Besigye's official car
drove in. Children were carried high up to save them from suffocation.
The Police fire brigade and military Police were on standby.
Besigye said the Government had failed to promote accountability in
public offices and that big wigs in government implicated in corruption
had not been punished.
He said despite the glaring evidence, the Government had not
punished the culprits who continue to hold big positions in government.
"President Museveni claiming that FDC is responsible for cutting of
aid is blatant hypocrisy. Has he shown cause why these culprits have
become stingingly rich overnight at the expense Ugandans?" he asked.
In Kakira, Besigye decried excessive taxes whose burden he said weighed heavily on the ordinary Ugandans.
"The FDC government shall scrap these unnecessary taxes," he said.
He said he would phase out Resident District Commissioners, whom he
described as useless, to save money to pay medical workers and teachers.
He said the two professions had survived on crumbs for a long time.

Original_Search_Title: Support My Bid for Presidency, Says Raila
Headline: Kenya: Support My Bid for Presidency, Says Raila
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Roads minister Raila Odinga yesterday said he would not give up his ambitions to vie for the presidency in next year's General Election.
The Lang'ata MP said he was ready to face his rivals in the presidential race. Speaking in Karachuonyo at two separate funerals, Raila however clarified that he would back the person nominated by the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM).
"I have held the leopard by its head. It is now upon you to spear it to death," Raila said.
He urged members of his Luo community to arm themselves with voters cards, arguing it was the only political weapon at their disposal to use against political enemies.
Addressing mourners at the funeral of the late Amos Midamba at Kakwajuok village in East Karachuonyo, Raila said in Dholuo "Nyocha awacho ni aol gi wacho ni ng'ane tosha omiyo nyaka ung'e ni ok an ngoro," (I said recently I am tired of saying other candidates are fit for the presidency, You must note I am not a coward!)
Raila told mourners: "I want you to go and register as voters and teach yourselves how to turn up enmass at the poll, to enable you support my candidature."
"I can only win this battle if you stand firm with me and arm yourselves with voters and identity cards to support my cause, not by standing by the roadside and cheering me on," he added.
Later at the funeral service of Joewart Kagumbas, the slain Mumias Sugar Factory manager, Raila said ODM was fighting for political change, but in a peaceful and legal manner.
Kagumbas' funeral was attended by hundreds of mourners and Mumias Sugar Company staff led by Managing Director Evans Kidero.
Raila asked police to investigate threats on his life fully, and bring the men who have been trailing his movements to book. He said his Ndhiwa colleague, Ojode had recorded a police statement and he had said the police had the ability to find the alleged stalkers.
Raila said the political battle ahead would be more fierce and cautioned his ODM colleagues not to relax their efforts but instead brace for a bruising challenge next year.
"We tactfully outwitted Banana at the referendum, but this is in no way the same battle as General Election. Do not think it will be easy. They know our ammunition and they are preparing their arsenals to jolt us," Raila said.
Speaking on the aborted State House appointment with President Kibaki, Raila said ODM was ready for dialogue with the Government and would grant it an audience to discuss a new constitution, but ODM will not abdicate its role in discussing the matter.

Original_Search_Title: ODM Set for Nationwide Rallies Despite Setback
Headline: Kenya: ODM Set for Nationwide Rallies Despite Setback
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Even after losing its name to another group, the Orange Democratic Movement plans a series of rallies across the country, to thank voters for rejecting the Proposed New Constitution in the November referendum.
The ODM's team leader, Mutula Kilonzo, told 'The Sunday Standard' that apart from the 'thank you' rallies, the movement would embark on civic education on its principles and the search for a new constitution.
"The organs of ODM will be unveiled this year. The various parties will tell us who their leaders are and how they fit in the movement. But above all, we will keep the Government on its toes," Kilonzo said.
But the movement is not dismissing the fact that some individuals have claimed the name. Yesterday, Kilonzo said ODM leaders have a 'Plan B' should the name go, but would fight to retain it.
"Registration of ODM by Banana supporters was one of the key things I had detailed my team to discuss with Kibaki had the Thursday meeting taken place. But we have a fallback," he said.
Kilonzo spoke about internal threats, and the fear that the ODM may not hold when nomination of presidential candidates begins.
The Kanu Nominated MP, who said he is working on ODM structure, said his organisation would hold "beyond 2007."
"That people think we will fall apart indicates that they know we are united," Kilonzo said.
Kilonzo said some people started talking of ODM falling apart as soon as Raila Odinga declared that he would run for the presidency.
"There are people known for 'Railamania' and there are those with 'Railaphobia'. Those who called to say ODM is dying are those with the 'Railaphobia'. As the referendum showed, they are not our supporters. The people saying ODM will collapse have never been its supporters and would never be," he said.
The ODM leader said the picture in the media of Orange leaders together as they waited to meet President Kibaki last Thursday was "telling".
"In 1992 or 1997, could presidential contenders stand together after each declared his or her ambition? Maybe the leaders might have, but their supporters would have killed each other. The fact that Orange leaders are still together cannot be taken for granted. Kenyans need to accept that things are changing," Kilonzo said.
The Nominated MP added: "In that picture the media carried this week stood the next President. And he will be an ODM candidate."
Kilonzo is taking solace in the fact that ODM leaders rejected Cabinet positions as they had agreed ahead of the referendum. This, he said, is a sign of determination to succeed.
"These guys gave us an undertaking that they would not take positions in the Cabinet if Kibaki did not approach us collectively. Haven't you seen them rejecting offers? We know State House wanted to meet Raila alone. Didn't we see him calling his colleagues and sharing that with them? Those who think ODM is going to fall apart should continue to think so. They are not our supporters," Kilonzo said.
But he acknowledged that the rumbling at the Coast, where LDP vice-chairman Joe Khamisi has been talking of the region being sidelined, is an important issue.
"I am aware of the complaint. But it is normal. We will make Khamisi happy. I hear him and I appreciate his concern. We all do. But I want to assure him that there has not been a deliberate effort to sideline coastal leadership and there will never be," Kilonzo said.
The Catholic Church has urged President Kibaki to meet ODM leaders and other Narc parties.
Through the head of the Episcopal Conference, Bishop Cornelius Korir, the Church is asking the President to initiate dialogue with the referendum winners.
In a telephone interview with The Sunday Standard, Korir said it was wrong for the President to ignore ODM and LDP and that he should unite Kenyans.
"They are part and parcel of the ruling party Narc and everybody knows that, so the best thing the President can do is to meet them and chart the way forward for this country," he said.
Korir, who is also the Eldoret diocese bishop and chairman of the conference that brings together all Catholic bishops, said Kenya needs such dialogue, especially now, with talk of plans to revive the constitutional review.
At the same time, Archbishop John Njue of the Nyeri Archdiocese yesterday called on the Government to invite experts to jump-start the constitution review.
"It is important for consultations to be held before the process is revived but the review should be left to experts," said Njue.
Elsewhere, two central Kenya MPs have asked Raila not to give up on his initiative to seek reconciliation with the President.
Tourism assistant minister Raphael Muriungi and Maragwa MP Alias Mbao, said Raila was right in seeking dialogue and hailed the gesture as 'good for peace and unity.
"It is only through dialogue that the President can solve differences with MPs aligned to ODM to guarantee sustained economic growth," Muriungi said.
The two leaders were speaking at the Meru North Disability Centre yesterday where they presented five wheelchairs.
The wheelchairs were donated by the American Butheist Society.
Muriungi and Mbao urged Raila to ignore the hiccups in his endeavour to meet the President, but advised him to keep his word.
Mbao said dialogue was vital for post-referendum healing and jumpstarting the review.
"It is good for politicians to discuss issues of national importance instead of dwelling on the past," Mbao said.

Original_Search_Title: Power Games Behind Abortive State House Meeting
Headline: Kenya: Power Games Behind Abortive State House Meeting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Unknown to many, indications that talks between President Kibaki and
leaders of the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) were headed for a dead end
became evident only a couple of minutes after the President shook hands with
Langata MP Raila Odinga.
Although the two leaders agreed to confer over the Constitution review
during their brief encounter last Wednesday, the Sunday Nation has reliably
established that a number of thorns were strewn their way eventually
culminating in cancellation of the scheduled meeting.
There is no denying that the meeting between the President and his
former Roads and Public Works minister on the sidelines of the Commonwealth
Speakers' conference at Safari Park Hotel, Nairobi was electrifying. Even
more confusing are the circumstances under which the Langata MP was in
attendance.
The function provided a perfect opportunity for the influential
legislator to pull a surprise move on his short-lived political partner now
turned foe. In fact Mr Odinga staged a major strategic media coup by
stealing the thunder from the international conference.
It was a major plus for the Liberal Democratic Party leader, who unlike
other party leaders who have had to deal with the Head of State through
third party emissaries - official and unofficial - has always insisted on
directly squaring his deal with the "real man".
And while he got a "real" deal from the "real" man, who immediately
summoned the Head of Civil Service, Mr Francis Muthaura, to fix a date,
State House and other Government officials succeeded in overturning this
fulfilment, portraying Mr Odinga instead as a crooked operator.
The Wednesday encounter has since been turned into a circus of
sorts. While ODM leaders claim they fixed an appointment with the President
for the following day (Thursday), State House maintains that President
Kibaki and Mr Odinga loosely agreed to meet "soon".
Mr Musila
If soon is the word, as both Mr Muthaura and Mr Odinga concede, then one
wonders how "soon" came so soon - on Thursday. The State House spin, in this
case, is easier to buy than the ODM's stated position.
Chances are that the President, who was apparently ambushed by Mr
Odinga, willingly and in good faith gave the green light for a meeting,
whose details he did not adequately digest. It is unlikely that details of
the date, time and composition of his would-be guests - LDP or ODM - would
have been arrived at within the two or three minutes encounter with the
Langata MP.
Says Information assistant minister, Mr Koigi wa Wamwere: "I doubt that
all this information was made available to the President within that short
time and that he made a firm decision of the Thursday meeting based on these
facts."
While appreciating the positive gesture exhibited by the President and
Mr Odinga, the Subukia MP opines that the ODM team must have had a hidden
agenda as demonstrated by the persistent push for a meeting on their
conditions.
"The initial encounter between the two leaders (Mr Kibaki and Mr Odinga)
was motivated by the scramble for political advantage. And that explains why
other players immediately stepped in and destroyed the harmony and goodwill
they had set," the minister told the Sunday Nation.
Only few are privy to details of the aborted meeting. Yet still, knowing
that they could be locked out of the meeting, Mr Odinga, in his
characteristic way, chose to go public about the meeting, thereby giving
State House little room for manipulation.
Mr Muthaura
However, the fact that the Government denied the existence of the
meeting too late - a day after it had been made public and only hours after
the President consulted with some of his ministers - is quite telling.
While the President may have mistakenly conceded to an appointment with
a wrong group, the bigger picture of the cancellation of his meeting with
ODM leaders is worrying.
The President has lost yet another opportunity to steal the thunder from
the ODM by demonstrating that he is genuinely interested in dialogue and
political healing after the bruising referendum.
By overturning his perceived wish, captured on camera in broad daylight
and relayed across the country, the President's handlers have only but
succeeded in painting him as a weak leader.
Liberal Democratic Party chairman, Mr David Musila, a career
administrator who served as provincial commissioner for over a decade,
observes that a leader must strive to be his own man, lest everybody takes
advantage of him.
"He told us one thing and moments later his advisers prevailed upon him
to communicate to us a totally different thing. This is quite unfortunate
because they have subjected him to a big embarrassment by portraying him as
an indecisive leader whose decisions are subject to approval by his
juniors," says the Mwingi South MP.
When confronted with an almost similar situation last year, the
Vice-President, Mr Moody Awori, went ahead with a parliamentary group
meeting called by the Narc Parliamentary Secretary, Mr Otieno Kajwang',
despite open hostility from MPs allied to NAK.
Mr Awori
Mr Kajwang' who is allied to the LDP circulated letters to Narc MPs
inviting them to the meeting. Although the Narc Parliamentary Whip, Mr
Norman Nyagah, disowned the letter and even questioned Mr Awori's
willingness to preside over it, the VP proceeded to chair the session in
what was largely seen as a principled act of independent mindedness.
In the latest State Hose meeting saga, it emerged that the cancellation
of the meeting, set for Thursday at 4pm, was relayed to Mr Odinga only hours
to the scheduled meeting.
"As we gathered at County Hall to strategise on the meeting, we received
calls from our sources that some ministers were driving to State House to
demand audience with the President. Of course we knew they were not up to
any good," a Liberal Democratic Party official confided to the Sunday
Nation.
A day earlier, during consultations with ODM leaders, Mr Muthaura at one
point attempted to dissuade them from meeting the President, suggesting that
they instead meet Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Martha Karua
since their concerns were "Constitution-related".
The all-important consultations, that scheduled the State House meeting,
were quietly executed by three individuals - Mr Muthaura, Mr Odinga and Mr
Musila. The latter was alerted of the "breakthrough" by the Langata MP
moments after meeting the President. Mr Musila was at the conference venue
in his capacity as deputy Speaker of the National Assembly.
"I am surprised that the President agreed to ignore us even after the
appointment and instead chose to listen to the same fellows who lied to him
that Banana would carry the day (at the referendum). It is a pity," he
says.
Although the proposed meeting was hailed by many, including the Central
Organisation of Trade Unions Secretary General, Mr Francis Atwoli, as a rare
but positive move that would create political stability, the ODM element was
a source of discomfort to some.
It is unlikely that President Kibaki's advisers, including his closest
allies in the Cabinet, most of who have publicly expressed their disapproval
of ODM, would have encouraged him to meet the group. In fact, it has been
murmured that some ministers prevailed upon the President not to proceed
with the meeting arguing that the ODM was not a legal entity.
Little wonder then that Mr Muthaura, who had reportedly informed Mr
Odinga about Thursday's meeting on Wednesday evening, was the one who called
the former Cabinet minister at 2.30pm to cancel it and advise that the Head
of State wanted to meet him alone or with LDP leaders (not ODM).
However, the LDP leader declined. He told journalists he was not ready
to meet the President alone, in line with a decision taken by ODM leaders
that none of them should individually consult with the President on
constitutional matters.
Said Mr Odinga: "I wanted to go with them as a team. I cannot go alone
because this is a movement. We agreed that no one should go to meet the
President, or the Government side, on the issue of the Constitution
alone. We must move as a team."
He maintained that ODM wanted structured talks with the President. But
there are a host of other reasons why the meeting may have been
cancelled. After the fireworks of the referendum that saw Mr Odinga rebel
against his boss and accordingly hand him a humiliating defeat, the picture
showing him and the President smiling and exchanging niceties may not have
gone down well with some in the President's camp.
It must have reminded many in the Government of last year's instance
where, during a trip to Luo Nyanza, the President showered praise on Mr
Odinga reiterating that the former minister was still his key political
ally.
The action generated a lot of protest among the Government-friendly MPs
who sulked that the President was embracing "the enemy".
In a hurriedly organised meeting, the MPs wondered why President Kibaki
would hail a man whose party, LDP, was making life "impossible" for them in
Parliament.
The enraged MPs went ahead to nominate ministers Simeon Nyachae and
Njenga Karume to seek an appointment with the President at State House to
express their displeasure and ask him to forthwith cease his political
romance with the Langata MP, failure to which they would rebel against him
and his Government.
Indeed, these are some of the machinations that worry Mr Wamwere:
"Except for the fact that the State House meeting was overpoliticised, it
was a positive gesture. However, at this rate I doubt that we shall even
realise a new Constitution by the end of the year unless MPs stop
politicising and meddling in every crucial issue."

Original_Search_Title: Moi: Be Wary of Kanu Enemies
Headline: Kenya: Moi: Be Wary of Kanu Enemies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The registration of a splinter Kanu is a diversionary tactic by enemies
of the party, former president Daniel arap Moi has said.
He told Kanu members to defeat such tactics that had seen a new party -
New Kanu Alliance Party - registered.
"The detractors trying to confuse you must be defeated. They are trying
to test your intelligence. Show them that you can see through their cheap
tricks," he told Kanu members yesterday.
He called on all genuine members of Kanu to remain steadfast and fight
for its existence.
A statement Mr Moi issued from his Kabarak home in Nakuru said: "I call
on Kanu members to ignore the diversionary tactics being used by the enemies
of Kanu in registering the so-called new Kanu."
The registration of New Kanu Alliance Party came just days after the
Orange Democratic Movement was registered.
ODM leaders immediately disowned the new officials of the party, arguing
that the registration was a Government ploy.
Mr Moi also said that Kenya's politics was "being dangerously"
tribalised and warned that it was not good for the future of the
country.
Mr Biwott
The retired president was for a long time the chairman of Kanu. He
handed its leadership to the leader of the Official Opposition, Mr Uhuru
Kenyatta, in January last year.
Mr Kenyatta defeated Keiyo South MP Nicholas Biwott in elections the
latter refused to accept, arguing that they were rigged.
However, the registrar of societies registered the officials allied to
Mr Kenyatta's camp. But she later wrote to them asking that they sort out
their differences or she would take action.
New Kanu secretary-general Mugambi Imanyara last week said they had
registered the new outfit.
He said there was nothing sinister in the registration, adding that the
new officials had applied to take the symbols - the cockerel, party colours
and one the finger salute.
A key member of the Kanu faction allied to Keiyo South MP Nicholas
Biwott, Dr Amukowa Anangwe, supported the move to register the new splinter
group, on Tuesday.
Mr Kenyatta
Dr Anangwe accused the Kanu leadership of taking for granted the
ownership of their defining symbols.
He admitted to being a supporter of the newly registered party.
Kanu secretary-general William Ruto said his party had instructed
lawyers to seek the annulment of the new party whose registration, he
asserted, had been done in bad faith.
He said the decision by the registrar of societies to write New Kanu
into law was meant to confuse his party and distract it from carrying out
its duties as the Official Opposition.
Yesterday, Mr Moi said it was only Kanu that had a clear and definable
hierarchical order countrywide.
Mr Moi said the attempt to divide the party was a great disservice to
the people of Kenya in particular the promotion of democracy.
Mr Ruto
He asked members to continue strengthening and consolidating their party
with the same spirit and enthusiasm exhibited during the Orange
campaigns.
The Orange team - like Mr Moi - opposed the proposed new constitution
saying it was not good for the country. The team defeated the Banana
group.
Mr Moi said although he had not in the past reacted sufficiently to
important issues, he felt he had to comment since Kenya's politics was being
dangerously tribalised. Elsewhere, 29 Kanu branches from Central Province
yesterday termed the registration of a parallel party as Government
harassment of Mr Kenyatta.
Chairmen of the branches said the Government knew too well who the
officials of Kanu were and should therefore not have registered the New Kanu
party.

Original_Search_Title: Third Term and Our Kitchen Constitution
Headline: Nigeria: Third Term and Our Kitchen Constitution
Body_Text:
There is a difference between being adept at double speak, and being an
inept liar. Double speak is an art, an ancient one which must be carefully
cultivated and cultured, and which has been associated with institutions
such as the diplomatic corps, and traditionally diplomatic peoples such as
the Yorubas.
The bite is taken off the prevarication by the humour in its
presentation and the respect in the connotation. The effect on the listener
is, in the main, more pleasant than that of a lie. An inept liar, on the
other hand, is not the most pleasant of fellows. He fails to convince, and
worsens this by making it obvious that he is lying, and he does not care who
knows it. While president Obasanjo has been criticized for the size of his
cabinet and retinue of advisers, little has been said about the individual
quality of their constituents.
It is the more reason that the president's adviser on Public Affairs
stands out, not for his pedigree, but for his exclusive ability to turn
public affairs into private affairs. As we enter into the New Year 2006, the
3rd term question also heats up, 2007 being no longer some time in the
distant future, but just next year. There is reason to think that Nigeria is
set to enter into the most turbulent election year it has ever seen. There
have been impeachments and rumours of impeachments. There have been
convictions and accusations and intrigues and violence, and, well,
lies. Only in the last four years in our national history has a man
confessed to giving away his mandate before he'd got it (he didn't,
actually), and remained governor of a state. Only in this same space of time
has a governor been arrested abroad, jumped bail and come back home. And why
not, federal chief executives are ignoring court orders, and Senators are
being fingered in budget scandals.
Truth is, Nigeria has never had it so messy, and if there is sense in
the philosophy that says it is a sign of things getting better. But if it is
true, and he can pull it off, just like Chris Matthews says about George
W. Bush and his Iraq experiment, OBJ deserves a Mount Rushmore created from
Zuma Rock and his face on it. He deserves it just for trying and correctly
detecting that these guys and their money is the problem. Unfortunately he
is not exactly exonerated or immune himself!
Corruption has become a big issue in our polity and economy, and an
artlessly lying adviser on public affairs does little good either to the
public image of his boss or the credibility of his country. Chief Femi
Fani-Kayode has become, inadvertently, a thorn in Obasanjo's political flesh
rather than oil in its wheel.
The constitutional manipulation which Chief Fani Kayode publicly
suggests will help his "constituency" in the achievement of extended power
might yet happen, but only because this is Nigeria, where normally natural
laws of morality can be swamped into the murk of immense funds, and the
funds can still be readily appropriated as long as the misappropriator is in
the same camp. To varying extents, this can be said to be a global
phenomenon. Yet the Nigerian case is particularly peculiar. In a way which
might now be said to have become traditional, words with feminine
association such as "banana", and "kitchen" have been used in phrases such
as "banana republic" and "Kitchen Cabinet" in describing inferior or less
than standard phenomena in business, government and politics. Despite the
sexist connotations, our constitution has often been associated with the
word "kitchen", and this association has seen much public approval.
Critics of the 1999 constitution would give the impression of a bored
army general sitting, mainly out of boredom, at his stainless steel kitchen
counter one evening when his retinue of domestic staff was off. Yet it is
the only one we have to work with -- the only document which holds Nigeria
together as an entity, and which retains the only semblance of law and order
in our national life. It is the document on which Gani Fawehinmi bases all
his court challenges but it is also, unfortunately, the same document on
which manipulators hang on to. It is also unfortunate that attempts of the
people to create the direction in which their nation will develop have all
been thwarted, but for PRONACO's obstinacy. The constitution can only be as
incorruptible as the people who wrote it. Indeed, there is every indication
that they wrote it with their on political future in mind. At the end of the
day, it does not matter if Obasanjo and the rest of them do a 3rd or 10th
term, as long as it is the wish of the majority of the people. But is not,
as they probably know, and the fact that they can buy it will not change
that fact.

Original_Search_Title: Reddykyulass the Band
Headline: Kenya: Reddykyulass the Band
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Reddykyulass trio of Walter Mong'are (Nyambane), Tony Njuguna and John Kiarie has formed a band simply but cleverly called The Band to spruce up its act.
Mong'are will handle the piano, KJ, vocals and Njuguna saxophone.
Other members of The Band are vocalists Linda Muthama, Edna Ratemo and Gloria Nakitare; guitarists Paul Moody (lead) and Muli Mbuta (bass); Mohamed Musa (drums); George Nderitu (percussion) and Robert "Onyi" Onyango keyboards with Nyambane.
Word has that the idea took root a few months ago when Njuguna impersonated President Kibaki playing a saxophone.
Nyambane says The Band, which has already performed in Multichoice, Marketing Society of Kenya, Kenya Airways, East Africa Portland Cement, Kenya Red Cross Society, East Africa Cables and Coca Cola functions, will play live wherever they will be performing.
"There will be no singing over pre-recorded stuff". And The Band will perform selectively. Only "the finest" will sample our talent and finesse".
Elitist? "No," according to Nyambane. "We perform for reputable clients, which is why we consider ourselves scarce but available."
The Band, added Nyambane, will not kill itself for money and will set time aside to relax, review its performances, plan ahead and above all, rehearse. Muthama and Ratemo, were part of the musical group that released Survivor while Nderitu has travelled a lot in his career as a percussionist.
Musa was in Fresh Ley Mwamburi's Everest Kings that did Stella Wangu. Mike Twafiqe the group's manager, had the same role in the defunct Hot Rod Band before it broke up. According to Nyambane, piecing up the group was easy as the auditioning offered the best in Kenya.
What about Nyambane's Angels with whom he released with Sweety Banana? "They are still around and I can still count on their services when I need them," he said.
For now, the comedian is concentrating on making the band the country's number one

Original_Search_Title: What the People Say On Third Term
Headline: Nigeria: ...and What the People Say On Third Term
Body_Text:
Lagos — Controversies trailing the alleged third term bid of President Olusegun
Obasanjo has continued unabated, thus heating up the entire polity. Sheriff
Balogun in this report, reviews views of some prominent persons on the issue
Going by stipulations in Section 135 (1) of the 1999 Constitution, the
President, irrespective of what his ambition may be, "shall vacate his
office at the expiration of a period of four years commencing from the date,
when (a) in the case of a person first elected as President under this
Constitution, he took the oath of allegiance and the oath of office; and (b)
in any other case, the person last elected to that office under this
Constitution took the oath of allegiance and oath of office or would, but
for his death, have taken such oaths."
Section 180 (2) also states that "the Governor shall vacate his office
at the expiration of a period of four years commencing from the date when -
(a) in the case of a person first elected as Governor under this
Constitution, he took the oath of allegiance and oath of office; and (b) the
person last elected to that office took the oath of allegiance and oath of
office or would, but for his death, have taken such oaths". But the
constitution also provides the President or a governor with the opportunity
to seek a renewal of his office through a re-election process.
The recurrent clamour for a third term by certain advocates since the
inception of the second term of President Olusegun Obasanjo has been viewed
by many commentators as unhealthy for the polity.
Irked by the determination of the perceived enemies of democracy to
ensure the elongation of the present government, various individuals and
groups have embarked on a renewed plan to fight the battle head long.
The Ethnic Nationality Forum (ENF), the umbrella association of all
ethnic groups in the country has registered its bitterness against the
agenda, vehemently kicking against the recent call of Southern Political
leaders for the amendment of the constitution, saying the call would rather
boost the chance of the President and governors to realise their alleged
third term agenda. The ENF's criticism of the call is in view of the
increasing number of advocates of the agenda. Notable among them is the
Speaker of Imo State House of Assembly, Kelechi Nwagwu, who vowed that his
assembly would support any constitutional amendment to that effect.
The Pan Ndi-Igbo Foundation (PNF USA), is another critic of the third
bid. Its president, Dr. Acho Orabuchi, did not mince words in persuading
Nigerians to resist the 'evil act.' "We encourage well meaning Nigerians and
organisations to stand firm against third term plot. The third term idea or
plan should be shelved immediately for the progress of democracy in
Nigeria," Orabuchi said.
Also, the Deputy National Chairman, National Democratic Party (NDP)
South-west zone, Hon. Dipo Olaitan, said Obasanjo should follow the rule of
law and abide by the constitution with the decision of the people. According
to him, "it would not in the best interest of the people to allow Obasanjo
to go for third term, moreso, the nation is blessed with talented and
experienced politicians that could lead the nation to the promised land
after 2007."
Meanwhile, the Catholic Archbishop of Abuja, Bishop John Onaiyekan,
during a Christmas carol service that was jointly organised by NTA and FRCN
in Abuja, and attended by Obasanjo, admonished the President not to follow
the route of other sit-tight African leaders who turn their countries to
banana republics.
In the same vain, Nya-Etot, guber aspirant for the 2007 general
elections in Akwa Ibom, posited that "the theory of life presidency for
Obasanjo is not just balderdash, but a display of abysmal ignorance of his
sycophants." According to him, "Obasanjo has seen all he needs to see in
Nigeria. When the fish gets too big for the stream, he moves to the bigger
river," adding that the reforms, the anti-corruption war, the transparency
and accountability drives are nothing but a dress rehearsal for the ultimate
destination.
Presidential candidate of All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) in the 2003
general elections, Maj-Gen Muhammadu Buhari (rtd) in an interactive meeting
with senators and members of the House of Representatives elected on the
platform of the party urged its members to stand firm against the third plot
of President Obasanjo.
He said "every school boy knows there are moves by this government to
continue in office beyond 2007".
Giving support to Buhari's position, Nobel Laureate, Prof. Wole Soyinka
in Lagos, reviewed the political situation of the country and accused the
President of driving the country to anarchy through executive lawlessness
and dictatorship.
But Third Republic Senator, Chief Aniete Okon, in his view said the
speculation that Obasajo is scheming for the third term are the handiwork of
mischief makers who want to frustrate on going efforts to amend the
constitution and rid it of its many contradictions that have distracted the
nation's federal structure.
Former Special Assistant (Media and Publicity) to President Obasanjo,
Dr. Doyin Okupe, described third term as a white elephant project, which
Nigerians should not reckon with. According to him, "would you take good and
not take the bad. Luckily for us, we do not have to worry about
Obasanjo. Let us look beyond 2007. We have to ensure that whoever emerged
fits into the picture of a modern leader."
Also, former governor of old Anambra State, Chief C.C. Onoh, posited
that there was no need dissuading Obasanjo from actualising the third term
agenda if "chooses to commit suicide." He added that "self-serving
politicians are beating the death drum for Obasanjo by asking him to extend
his tenure."
National Chairman of Alliance for Democracy (AD), Chief Bisi Akande, in
his opposition to the third term bid, maintained that the bid portends grave
signals to the health of the nation's democracy. According to him, "the
nation's democratic project could be truncated unless progressive minded
Nigerians check the move."
While General Yakubu Gowon (rtd), former military Head of State, who
spoke from his past experience as former leader said "we have to avoid the
tendency of leadership prolonging itself or even overstaying its
acceptability. My experience, that of General Ibrahim Babangida and (late)
General Sani Abacha should be a good lesson for all leadership in Nigeria
today and tomorrow."
President John Kuffor of Ghana during his visit to the Country last
year, adviced President Obasanjo to respect the provisions of the
constitution. According to him, "I am a constitutionalist and if the
national constitution has prescribed a term in office, then all the people
must work by the spirit and letter of the constitution," adding that
"constitutions are made by nations to give guidelines or set the framework
with which the nation must be governed. If in the wisdom of the nation, the
people should decide to alter their constitution. That is their right."
The British High Commission to Nigeria, Mr. Richard Gozney, said "the
idea of a third term is a hypothetical one. The man high up there (President
Obasanjo) to us is a man who respects the constitution," adding that "he has
not changed the constitution and he has not told us anything regarding
extension people talk about."
However, the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) National Chairman,
Dr. Ahmadu Ali, claimed innocence of the campaign, saying "I am not aware of
any third term ambition of Mr. President. Quite honestly, it is the hype by
the media that has perpetuated it. They created it and they are perpetuating
it and the opposition elements are happy to find something to latch on to
and ride on the back of that if only to smear the name of PDP."
According to him "the PDP is proud of President Obasanjo and if the
country decides on their own to amend their constitution, who are we to
prevent anybody from enjoying it?...What we are saying is that for God's
sake, if this is most suitable at this time, let us use it to move forward,
not stop-go. We have had enough of stop-go development."
Former Minister of Mines and Power during Alhaji Shehu Shagari's regime,
Alhaji Ibrahim Hassan, noted that all the governors reportedly supporting
Obasanjo for third term are those who are guilty of corruption. According to
him, "the third term issue is satanic and all the governors supporting it
are selfish and greedy," adding that "Nigerians would stand against it and
that the President would not realise his ambition, no matter whatever it
would take."

Original_Search_Title: Issues That 'Ll Make Or Mar Obasanjo's Legacy
Headline: Nigeria: Issues That 'Ll Make Or Mar Obasanjo's Legacy
Body_Text:
Lagos — A deep reflection on the year that just ended may show that it did not
have a happy ending. Beyond the serial pain from the rot in sectors like
aviation, there was an avalanche of issues that laid bare the deepening
complexities of the unresolved national question. Despite the high
expectation that heralded the year, it nonetheless ended with deep anxieties
about the future of the polity.
Last year, the government somewhat belatedly and at the same time
reluctantly, agreed to fast-track the much desired review of the
constitution. On the surface, it looked like a somewhat positive effort but
even those who were prepared to forget the structural distortions of the
political reform conference, were left to the peril of too much trust. It
emerged that the subliminal intentions loomed larger than the need for a new
constitution. Rather than yield a new constitution, the conference yielded
the third term monster.
In the same year, the gnome of regional competition for power was
injected with a stereoid. Contest for the presidency left the domain of
political parties to the domains of regional groupings. The North and South
ended the year with a slanging match over where the next president will come
from and not what to do with it. The issues loomed larger than the yawning
need for additional efforts to execute the reform on which the future
sustenance of the polity and the country would clearly depend. As we
shuffled, thrashed and dissipated energy on nonsense, the new year, 2006,
stole in.
In my thinking, 2006 is very important to the democracy. It should even
be more critical to the Obasanjo administration. The facts and the
imperatives lend themselves. Of the time element, its impact is obvious. The
administration has just six months of serious work left. If, as it is
anticipated that the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), begin its primaries in
June, the value of that six months will in reality, be lessened, for many of
the people at the vortex of policy executions will be bogged down by
politicking. Even those who are not contesting will be involved in helping
their party or their godsons to be elected. To be candid, there is no time
left. If one says it is over, it may not be a cruel truth, for its import in
terms of how much time is available to the Obasanjo administration, is real
as its consequences. And the way nature orders its elements, this is not
what anybody can hurry. All the time is gone, spent, used, wasted or lost,
which ever applies.
This year therefore, ought to have been received with a lot of
introspection, and with the ernestness of a marathon runner, who had done
the rounds and has come face-to-face with the tape. However, the sort of
vibrations that are seeping out of the seat of power in Abuja do not
indicate that sobriety was applied in receiving the year. It may be early
days, but every good citizen ought to wish that the sort of convulsions that
dominated 2005 are not multiplied in 2006.
In point of fact, the one person who should be more concerned about
every minute of this year is President Olusegun Obasanjo himself. If he is
truly a man of history, as his aides always say, he ought to become very
selfish about the way things are done this year. This is the time to pay
very serious attention to his legacy. A way to begin to sit down and reflect
what he would be remembered for. A lot of the time, Obasanjo's aides talk
about how much he has done for the country. But they fail to articulate the
essence of the Obasanjo legacy. At the end of all this, what will Obasanjo
be remembered for? A lot of time has lapsed, and a lot has been done and a
lot has been spoken of. This administration has just six months to wrap it
up. This is the only time left for the President to have that legacy. This
year presents an opportunity to fill whatever gap there may be in that
regard.
It is also a tricky year, full of many banana peels that would test the
strength of Obasanjo's leadership. Most of the unresolved issues of 2005
must be resolved this year. How they are resolved will determine the value
of the Obasanjo legacy.
Some of the issues include the seemingly contentious population census
slated for the first half of the year. Previous headcounts ended in very
serious controversies about the real demographics of Nigeria. This one is
building up to that climax in some areas that see a hidden agenda in the
removal of ethnicity and religion in the demographics. If the President
achieves a rancour-free head count, he obviously has added to that legacy. A
contrary outcome will seriously devalue that.
Another issue is the review of the constitution. As desirable as it is,
the effort has already been compromised by perception of manipulation of
several issues. It would be scandalous to allow this matter to drag beyond
the eight years of the Obasanjo Presidency. As controversial as it is, the
government must find a way to conclude that review. Why it must do so is
that it was one of the issues on which the PDP government rode to
power. Nigerians expect the President to deliver on this. A new and less
controversial constitution will add value to whatever Obasanjo would, or
should, or could claim as his legacy.
There are others but the last and most critical is a credible succession
or transfer of power. Incorporated in this are matters that relate to the
power struggle in the PDP, the alleged third term project, and the struggle
between the North and South for the next presidency.
This issue or cocktail of issues will make or mar the Obasanjo
legacy. The reason is that orderly transfer of power from civilian to
civilian has been a complex problem for Nigeria. Disputations over allegedly
rigged elections have always yielded governments with drastically reduced
legitimacies which the military find easy to overthrow. But that problem may
undergo some mutation in 2007 if the skirmishes inside the PDP is not
extinguished on time. And the way the polity is arranged, how these will
help to make or mar a smooth transfer of power in 2007 will depend on
President Obasanjo.
However, in all, how all of these will end will decide Obasanjo's place
in history. In his shoes, better legacy will be easier to achieve by
marrying a desire for history's kind strokes to a selfish attitude, which
ensures that more work is done in the apparently short time left so as to
have these legacies. If he fails to control these avalanche of issues which
largely result from power struggles, he would have put his legacy in serious
jeopardy. For the little time left will race past, without notice.

Original_Search_Title: Flirting With Life Presidency
Headline: Nigeria: Flirting With Life Presidency
Body_Text:
NIGERIANS always like to pretend that certain things in other less endowed African countries can never happen here. And this smug feeling has given rise to the clichÃ©: 'it can't happen here'. Anybody living in such illusion had better wake up to the reality of today's Nigeria; because the country is on an inexorable journey to a proper banana republic. That it had not happened before is purely a matter of luck, nothing more. And in case any one is wondering what a banana republic is, it is a country with life presidents, and weak democratic institutions. There are many countries in Africa with such scenarios: Zimbabwe, Uganda, Gabon, Egypt until recently Kenya and Zambia.
It is correct that Nigeria had in the past gone close to fascism, but somehow managed to escape it. These were during the times of Generals Yakubu Gowon, Ibrahim Babanigda and Sani Abacha. Thoughout this period the country was saved the trauma of fascistic affliction; the people never definitively made an example of such rulers who attempted to prolong their stay in power.
Gowon's adventurism was truncated by his colleagues, who organized the putsch against his regime. Those who moved against Gowon merely aggregated public misgivings his junta had raised to get rid of him. Babangida, would have succeeded if he had been a little cleverer. His problem was just that he committed himself to the high moral ground to handover, but somewhere along the line made the mistake of rethinking his aims, without convincing Nigerians, why he should remain in office. By the time his circuitous transition programme ended with the annulment of June 12, 1993 election, the people had, had enough of him. Public disenchantment was very high, and when it became obvious he could no long guarantee the survival of his government and the loyalty of his top commanders, he stepped aside. If Babangida, had, had the time to reconsolidate his hold of the army sufficiently, he would have held on to power.
Abacha's misadventure ended with his death. But, before he passed on, the movement for him to remain in office was proceeding in accordance with the direction the framers of his game plan had shown. Interestingly, the Abacha experiment is now being re-tested, because those who designed it have found a willing accomplice in Obasanjo. So far, he has kept to his own side of the game-plan. His strategy has been to keep quiet, not committing himself, but allowing associates and aides to either deny such a project is on the ground, or argue for it.
