It does not matter that the country is split on this issue, and it is certain for the rest of his tenure that the issue of whether he would run or not, will continue to overshadow every other political development.
For the moment, the plan to amend the Constitution and allow for an additional term is proceeding in line with the agenda, but the x factor which many of those involved in this project are concerned with is the reaction of the people. Will the people go for it, or will they kick against it? Because there is no guarantee of where the pendulum will swing, what they are doing is to work at this extension one step at a time, with the hope to use the National Assembly to achieve a fait accompli. With the number of PDP members in the National Assembly and 28 state legislatures, it will be a stroll to amend the Constitution, and include whatever provisions they want.
They can do this provided the people acquiesce to their schemes. Indeed, the aforementioned examples of close shaves with life presidency failed not because the people made an example of those who tried it, but because of opportunistic power struggle instigated by public outcry. The current case it seems will not be different. And this is not good. The time has come for the people to make an example of leaders who attempt to coerce or manipulate the system; otherwise, whoever succeeds Obasanjo will continue with this tendency. This is because it is not the individual that is primarily the cause, but the system.
Nothing curbs impunity more than when a people rise to punish and contain it. It is quite possible that a revolutionary leadership can do such if it is ideologically driven. In Ghana for instance, President Jerry Rawlings, following his executions of past leaders in 1978, steered that country on the path of recovery and development. The Obasanjo gambit is being promoted by those who cite his records of performance and that only he can do it. At nearly 70, or even older, Obasanjo's behaviour is very perplexing. That he never thought of a capable successor is one of the biggest tragedies of his leadership. If the parliament grants him tenure extension, can they also give him a life extension law? Because with each passing day he remains in office there would emerge the rule of a cabal with Obasanjo as mere figure head. Whether he accepts it or not his faculties will soon begin to fail him. The biological clock cannot be turned back no matter what he does. The truth is, there is
nothing Obasanjo has achieved in office that cannot be undone in 24 hours! The greatest edifice he could have built for himself would have been to pursue far reaching political reforms of restructuring and devolution of powers. Nothing else he can do would be more enduring.
Mr Odili is a staff of Vanguard Newspapers

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki And Raila Plan Surprise Review Talks
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki And Raila Plan Surprise Review Talks
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Talks that could restart the Constitution review are to be held today at
State House, Nairobi, following a surprise meeting between President Kibaki
and Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga.
The meeting between the President and Mr Odinga took place on the
sidelines of an international conference in Nairobi.
It was their first encounter since Mr Odinga was sacked along with six
other former ministers in the Orange Democratic Movement who led the
campaign against a new Constitution.
The ODM humiliated the Government in the November 21 referendum, in
which Mr Odinga and the Orange leaders gained the support of 3.5 million
voters, against the Banana group's 2.5 million.
A top Government official yesterday confirmed the State House meeting
was scheduled for this afternoon and Mr Odinga also confirmed that his brief
meeting with the President and subsequent consultations with the head of
Public Service, Mr Francis Muthaura, had led to the decision to fix the
talks.
The two sides were expected to thrash out ways of restarting the
review. They were also expected to agree on the way forward, how long the
review should take, an acceptable structure for the review, and how those
who will lead it should be chosen.
Mr Odinga said he had briefed the Orange leaders before and after
meeting Mr Kibaki and that they had agreed the only way to avoid the present
impasse was to meet and arrive at a consensus.
The President and Mr Odinga came face to face at Safari Park Hotel,
Nairobi, during the 18th Commonwealth Speaker's Conference.
It took those present by surprise and their discussion lasted less than
five minutes with Mr Odinga doing more of the talking while the Head of
State nodded and smiled as he listened.
The President had just delivered a keynote address and was being
photographed with delegates by the swimming pool when Mr Odinga joined
him.
The former minister, who was accompanied by Migori MP Owino Likowa, then
moved closer and shook the President's hand and started to engage him in
conversation in the full view of the Press and other delegates who kept a
respectful distance.
None of the other Orange leaders who led the campaign against a new
Constitution attended the function.
They were Kanu national chairman Uhuru Kenyatta, his secretary general
William Ruto, and six other former ministers who were not included in
President Kibaki's Cabinet when he announced it on December 7: Mr Kalonzo
Musyoka (Environment); Mr Najib Balala (National Heritage); Prof Anyang'
Nyong'o (National Planning); Mrs Jebii Kilimo (Immigration), Mr William ole
Ntimama (Public Service) and Mr Ochillo Ayacko (Sports).
After the brief talk, the President then beckoned Mr Muthaura and gave
him some instructions, before the Public Service head moved away and Mr
Odinga concluded the discussion.
After the meeting, President Kibaki was joined by Cabinet ministers
Joseph Munyao (Livestock Development), Mr Musikari Kombo (Local Government)
and Mr Maina Kamanda (Sports), who escorted him to his limousine before he
was driven back to State House.
Stayed behind
However, Mr Muthaura stayed behind at Safari Park to continue the
consultations which led to setting up today's meeting.
Later, Mr Odinga told the Press he had discussed restarting the
Constitution review with the President and agreed to hold a formal meeting
"soon" to find a way forward.
Asked whether the meeting would be attended by his Orange colleagues and
those in the Liberal Democratic Party, he said only it was to be for
"leaders." He refused to be drawn further, saying it would pre-empt the
purpose of the meeting.
Asked to comment on his relations with President Kibaki, whom he had
asked Kenyans to vote for in his famous Uhuru Park declaration of "Kibaki
tosha"(Kibaki is suitable) ahead of the 2002 General Election, he maintained
that they were still friends.
"My relation with the President is very good. We had agreed to disagree
on the Constitution by backing different sides and there should be no fuss
about it. We have no personal differences. He had respected my views as I
had also respected his views; we have no personal differences," he
said.
Mr Odinga went on: "I raised the issue of the Constitution with the
President and he agreed that we hold a meeting soon to discuss the matter
further ... I don't want to pre-empt the matter further so I cannot divulge
any more information."
The former minister repeated his New Year's message to Kenyans, saying
2006 should be a year of reconciliation, tolerance and dialogue.
Mr Odinga also thanked the President for giving him an audience and for
agreeing to hold a meeting on the Constitution.
Resounding defeat
His comments appeared to be an attempt to end the recriminations that
followed the Government's resounding defeat by the Orange No-vote campaign
in the referendum.
The blow prompted the President to dismiss his entire Cabinet - except
Vice-President Moody Awori and attorney general Amos Wako - only to
reconstitute it two weeks later minus the seven ministers who led the Orange
campaign.
The seven, plus a number of assistant ministers who also campaigned
against the Constitution, were replaced by pro-Government MPs in the
affiliate parties within the National Rainbow Coalition (Narc), mainly Mr
Kombo's Ford Kenya and Health minister Charity Ngilu's National Alliance
(Party) of Kenya, plus some members of the official Opposition Kanu, and
Ford People.
The three-month referendum campaigns were acrimonious and saw the
previous Cabinet divided with the President and 22 other ministers
supporting the Yes team, while Mr Odinga and his group led the No vote,
under the umbrella of ODM.

Original_Search_Title: Review Storm Over President's Team of 10
Headline: Kenya: Review Storm Over President's Team of 10
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki's plan to jump-start the constitution review could run into trouble even before he names members of a special committee to oversee the process.
The Liberal Democratic Party went into a meeting yesterday with a warning that it was widely consulting and could reject the list of 10 experts to be announced by the President.
Party leaders Raila Odinga and Joseph Kamotho claimed they were aware the President was planning to appoint some leaders who had participated in drafting the Proposed New Constitution (Wako Draft), which was resoundingly rejected by voters at the November 21 referendum.
"We have not been consulted and the Constitution is not a Government project. It has to be an all-inclusive project," said Raila.
The Lang'ata MP is among the politicians from LDP and Kanu who led the Orange campaign against the Wako draft.
Yesterday, he said the Orange team was aware that people sympathetic to the Banana camp that lost at the referendum would be appointed to lead the review.
A section of the civil society, through the National Convention Executive Council (NCEC), opened up another battlefront when it charged that the team President Kibaki was assembling in his latest efforts to jumpstart the review process had no mandate to write a new constitution.
Programme manager Ndung'u Wainaina said NCEC had expected the President to "consult widely" if he hoped to avoid another defeat.
"The committee can only be there to facilitate consensus, (otherwise) it would be taking us on the same road that was rejected by Kenyans at the referendum," Wainaina said.
He said Kenyans must sit down, negotiate and form a committee acceptable to all.
In his New Year message, President Kibaki pledged to announce a process that would see Kenyans get a constitution acceptable to them.
But yesterday, Kanu nominated MP and ODM legal advisor, Mutula Kilonzo, faulted Kibaki's plan to appoint the team, terming it a "presidential decree".
"We in the Orange Democratic Movement would prefer an all-inclusive and consultative method," Mutula said.
He asked Kibaki to reconvene Parliament so as to put in place the necessary legislative mechanisms to legitimise the review.
"Appointing a new team to jump-start the constitution review without putting in place a legislative framework has no chance. The modalities of doing so must be governed by the law," said Mutula.
Even as the new storm over the review started to gather, it emerged that a taskforce chaired by Attorney-General Amos Wako to shortlist the technical team that will compile the draft constitution had originally come up with 40 names before settling on the 10.
Reports from the Ministry of Justice say President Kibaki had given the taskforce the mandate to come up with a team that would be acceptable to diverse groups involved in the process.
"They first came up with 40 names, which were later reduced to 27, the same as the just disbanded Constitution of Kenya Review Commission," said a senior officer at the A-G's chambers.
Wako and the Minister for Justice and Constitutional Affairs, Ms Martha Karua, are said to have put together a list of people with proven "ability and integrity".
The list was, however, reduced to 17 after it was decided that a team of 27 would be too large and could easily end up with the tribal and political intrigues that had been witnessed at CKRC over the last five years.
Wako reportedly held further meetings with the Parliamentary Counsel, Bernadette Nzioka, and the Permanent Secretary in the Ministry of Justice and Constitutional Affairs, Ms Dorothy Angote, before finally settling on 13 names.
So far only five names on the list are known, including the proposed chairman, Mr Bethwel Kiplagat, a renowned peace broker and a career diplomat.
Also short-listed are Nairobi-based lawyer Kathurima M'Inoti, former Permanent Secretary Peter Wambura, Mr Justice Majid Saeed Cockar and lawyer Lee Muthoga.
Kathurima is the chairman of the Law Reform Commission and a member of the International Commission of Jurists.
Justice Cockar is a Judge at the Industrial Court, where he has served for over 40 years since its inception.
Muthoga has participated in several high profile cases, including the Njonjo Commission of Inquiry.
The Orange Democratic Movement has already voiced its opposition to the list, saying it was not consulted.
Yesterday, Raila and Kamotho said they would make a comprehensive statement on the list today.
Kamotho claimed legal partners of Cabinet minister Kiraitu Murungi were already holding secret meetings as experts to handle constitution matters.
Legal experts are of the view the President's best legal option is to recall Parliament to enact a new law to provide for a legitimate process.
Former International Commission of Jurists Kenya Chapter chairman, Otiende Amollo, said with the expiry of the Constitution of Kenya Review Act, a new law or legal framework must be in place to reactivate the review.
Amollo, however, said the President could also allow Kenyans to exercise their constituent powers.
This would be in line with judgments in the constitutional cases that were filed by the Yellow Movement and the Rev Timothy Njoya.
In the Yellow Movement case that sought to stop the referendum, the judges ruled that the people could make a constitution by exercising their constituent powers and still produce a legitimate document.
Amollo gave the example of the Ufungamano Initiative as one of the ways people could exercise their constituent power and write a constitution.
The Supreme Council of Kenya Muslims (Supkem) also warned that failure to consult stakeholders over the review would lead to rejection of yet another draft constitution.
Supkem's secretary-general Adan Wachu said President Kibaki should consult religious leaders, political parties and professionals.
And the National Council of Churches of Kenya (NCCK) said it had furnished the President with a comprehensive proposal to jump-start the constitution review after the referendum.
The NCCK General Secretary, the Reverend Mutava Musyimi and Anglican Archbishop Benjamin Nzimbi said the proposal, if implemented, would solve the constitution review crisis.
"We gave the proposal to the Head of State during a meeting with him last year. We hope the President will implement it," Nzimbi said.
And Mutava said the NCCK had suggested that people from different sectors be appointed to look at the review of the constitution.
"We suggested that people be appointed from the political, religious, youth, persons with disabilities, among other groups, to look at the review," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Unity Without Freedom is a Sham
Headline: Zimbabwe: Unity Without Freedom is a Sham
Body_Text:
Following is a New Year reflection by Jesuit Communications
regarding the political situation in the southern African nation.
The prisoners in a police cell are certainly united. They all use the
same bucket, eat the same 'chikafu' (food), suffer the same insults, feel
the same hardships and yet share the same hope of regaining their
freedom. But that is not the unity we mean.
In recent days, Zimbabwe has been fed with propaganda about Unity,
meaning the Unity Accord signed on 22 December 1987 between the ruling party
and its rival, ad nauseam. Even the dead had to be brought back to
(electronic) life on the TV screen to sing the same 'Unity' tune: Joshua
Nkomo especially, Simon Muzenda and Canaan Banana.
(Do we no longer respect the dead and their eternal peace? They, of
course, are defenceless and cannot protest about how their recorded words
and images are being taken out of context and manipulated.)
Unity without freedom is a sham. True unity is based on the free,
unforced agreement of the united. The fusion of two political parties may or
may not be appropriate. It is not national unity.
The unity of a people is expressed and realized in a constitutional
state to which all can subscribe, in a constitution which has the support in
its essential parts of all citizens, in the acceptance of a Bill of Rights
guaranteeing equality before the law for all, based on a firm belief in the
inalienable dignity and worth of every person, of every woman, man and
child.
Christmas which we celebrated just now gives us the ultimate reason why
every person must be respected as of infinite worth and dignity: if God, as
we Christians believe, became Man and accepted our human condition, the same
in all things as us humans except for our sinfulness, then every human being
has been given infinite value as a son or daughter of God the Father and a
brother or sister of Christ, the Son.
The constant manipulation of our (admittedly inadequate) constitution
for short-term political gains and the unwillingness of government to allow
the people to write their own constitution show complete lack of respect for
the people.
What the 'Unity' propagandists have not been telling us is that it was
also in 1987 that the executive presidency, then introduced, was given
uncontrollable powers. This has alienated the majority from this state.
There is division in fundamentals, not unity.
We did not find unity in 1987. We are still to find it in a
constitutional agreement sometime in the future. May the year 2006 bring us
nearer to that day.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Calls for Dialogue Over New Constitution
Headline: Kenya: Orange Calls for Dialogue Over New Constitution
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement has warned the Government that it would
be engaging in a futile exercise if it does not consult before the next
phase of the constitution review process.
ODM leader, William Ruto, said: "Any step taken unilaterally is doomed
to fail because that is exactly what made them lose the referendum."
He welcomed President Kibaki's New Year assurance that the process would
be all-inclusive and participatory.
"All we want now is the President to accompany those words with action,"
he said.
His comments followed information from the Ministry of Justice and the
Attorney General's office that Ambassador Bethwel Kiplagat would lead a new
team of 10 technocrats in the constitution review process.
Sources said those short-listed include city lawyer Kathurima M'Inoti,
Eng Peter Wambura, Justice Majid Saeed Cockar and lawyer Lee Muthoga.
Ruto dismissed the list, saying they were Banana (Government)
sympathisers.
"We reject the handpicking of Banana sympathisers and calling them
experts because that will not take us anywhere," he said.
He said Orange team leaders were waiting for the Government to consult
them and wondered why the Government was unwilling to consult other
stakeholders in the process. He accused those in power of not recognising
them as leaders.
All-inclusive process
Asked how he rated Kiplagat's ability to steer the process, Ruto said:
"I do not have any problem with him, but what we need is to consult and come
up with a team with all shades of opinion."
He said the Government was in the process of creating acrimony where
there should be none.
Elsewhere, the Supreme Council of Kenya Muslims has urged the President
to consult widely before embarking on the constitution making process
afresh.
Supkem also warned the Government that failure to consult religious
leaders; political parties and professionals could lead to the rejection of
yet another proposed constitution.
"The President should consult in order to jumpstart the constitutional
review process by involving all religious leaders, political parties and
professionals," council secretary- general Adan Wachu said.
Adan was reacting to Kibaki's New Year message where he promised an
all-inclusive process in the new initiative.
He said Kenyans should put aside personal ambitions and concentrate on
nation building, adding that Supkem had earlier warned that the constitution
review process would degenerate into anger, name-calling and mistrust unless
handled diligently.
Wachu urged the President to listen to others and consult widely,
particularly with leaders who brought him to power.

Original_Search_Title: Raila: I'll No Longer Fight the President
Headline: Kenya: Raila: I'll No Longer Fight the President
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Roads minister Raila Odinga yesterday said he and his Orange
Democratic Movement colleagues were not bitter for being left out of the
Government.
At the same time, he said he would no longer waste his time fighting
President Kibaki and the Narc government, but would concentrate on shaping
the country's political future.
The Langata MP said he was not interested in serving a government which,
he claimed, had lost the people's mandate.
He said he would today make a major political announcement as part of
his New Year message to Kenyans.
"I have an important message for Kenyans tomorrow (today)," Mr Odinga
told a political rally in Cabinet minister Raphael Tuju's Rarieda
constituency.
He said he had no personal problem with Mr Kibaki, and that there was no
longer need to fight him after his Banana camp lost in the referendum.
"We defeated the Government, and we shall now concentrate on other
important issues," he said.
But fellow LDP MPs Jakoyo Midiwo, Oburu Odinga, Gor Sungu, Ayiecho
Olweny and Zadock Syong'oh said the Government ought to have resigned after
losing in the public vote.
And Mr Midiwo urged Mr Odinga to contest the presidency in the 2007
General Election, saying that he (Raila) had the mandate of all Kenyans. And
Mr Odinga said he was not afraid of anything, and urged his supporters in
Nyanza to register as voters in large numbers if they really wanted him to
go for the top seat.
"I will go for that seat only if you assure me that you will turn out in
large numbers to vote," he said.
Mr Tuju did not attend the meeting as he was said to be on holiday in
Mombasa.

Original_Search_Title: ODM Spells Out Review Terms
Headline: Kenya: ODM Spells Out Review Terms
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) wants a constituent assembly formed
to write a new constitution.
Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga, one of the movement's leading lights, said the
review process should not be left in the hands of a hand-picked presidential
commission but should be all inclusive and people driven.
Each district should elect two people - a man and a woman - to join the
210 MPs in forming the assembly.
"We should go back to the Bomas draft, carry out minor amendments and
give Kenyans what they have always wanted, a people-driven constitution,"
said Mr Odinga on the telephone.
If the assembly is set up it would tackle the contentious issues that
dogged the Bomas talks leading to the birth of the Naivasha and Kilifi
accords and finally the rejected Wako Draft.
Some of the contentious issues include whether executive power should be
vested in the prime minister or the President, levels of devolution of
power, and whether or not to enshrine religious courts in the
Constitution.
The former Roads minister said ODM would spearhead a campaign for the
formation of the assembly.
He was optimistic Kenyans would have a new Constitution before the end
of 2006, he said, adding that ODM leaders were "more than willing" to enter
into dialogue with "anybody who is willing to talk to us."
The sacking of Cabinet ministers who campaigned against the Wako Draft
was wrong because it was a setback to the spirit of reconciliation.
"If you look at the way the President has conducted himself after the
referendum, he does not show the spirit of reconciliation. For example, when
you look at the Cabinet, only those who were supporting the other side were
appointed."
ODM spokesman William Ruto said Parliament should be reconvened quickly
so a law could be passed to jumpstart the review.
He said a constituent assembly was the only way out but said all players
should meet to agree on the composition of the team.
Mr Odinga thanked Kenyans for their exemplary conduct during the year
and hoped 2006 would be a year of tolerance, dialogue and helping those in
need.
"I hope the new year brings prosperity for the people of Kenya."
President Kibaki has on different occasions pledged to jumpstart the
review process.
On Christmas eve, he asked Kenyans to put their political differences
aside and help in delivering a new constitution.
"Whether you were a Banana or Orange supporter, we are Kenyans and we
should work together," he said and added that the review process would be
inclusive.
Mr Odinga's proposal was made against the backdrop of reports that
President Kibaki is considering appointing a panel of 10 eminent Kenyans to
jump-start the review.

Original_Search_Title: Take Part in Writing New Law, Says Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: Take Part in Writing New Law, Says Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans should put their political differences aside and help in
delivering a new constitution, President Kibaki said.
Speaking in Mombasa on the eve of Christmas, the President urged those
who supported and those who opposed the proposed Constitution during the
November 21 referendum to put aside their differences and work as a
team.
"Whether you are a banana or orange supporter, we are Kenyans and we
should work together," he said.
The President said the process will be an inclusive one and everyone's
views will be considered.
The announcement came only days after an opinion poll conducted by
Steadman showed that 74 per cent of Kenyans wanted the review process
concluded before the 2007 General Election. Those polled called for an
inclusive process.
According to the poll, 77 per cent of Kenyans were optimistic that their
desire to have a new constitution would be fulfilled while 31 per cent said
the new law should be reviewed by experts. Twenty-two per cent felt the
current Constitution should remain.
While celebrating the Christmas Mass, Catholic Archbishop Ndingi Mwana
a'Nzeki called for an all-inclusive approach saying no section of the
society should be excluded.
He called upon all leaders - politicians, civil servants, the church and
all Kenyans - to be fully involved in the efforts to get the new law to
avoid a possible rejection as happened in November.
Sources close to President Kibaki have intimated that he is considering
appointing a panel of 10 eminent Kenyans to jump-start the review
process.

Original_Search_Title: Coast MPs to Give President Their Demands
Headline: Kenya: Coast MPs to Give President Their Demands
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Coast MPs allied to President Kibaki will this morning present a list of
demands they want implemented before the Head of State leaves for
Nairobi.
Among the burning issues drafted by the MPs are land, job opportunities,
senior positions in the Civil Service, and a follow-up of the pre-referendum
promises he made, a few days before Kenyans voted against the proposed
constitution.
But a showdown is looming between MPs allied to the Banana camp and
those in the Orange Democratic Movement. A meeting called on Thursday by
Liberal Democratic Party vice-chairman Suleiman Shakombo, who also doubles
as the chairman of the Coast Parliamentary Group (CPG), was snubbed by those
allied to the Orange Democratic Movement. They termed the meeting
"inconsequential and a waste of time".
The MPs, led by Cabinet ministers Chirau Ali Mwakwere, Moris Dzoro and
Suleiman Shakombo, will present their list of demands at 10am. The
legislators are also demanding the sacking of an unnamed senior civil
servant at the Coast. The meeting between the MPs and Kibaki is expected to
discuss senior positions in the Civil Service and presidential directives on
absentee landlords, a pressing issue at the Coast.
Sources at State House intimated to The Standard that Kibaki was happy
with the team he retained in the Cabinet, saying "their loyalty could be a
factor in having their demands implemented".
Coast MPs allied to Kibaki include Mwakwere, Dzoro, Shakombo, Gonzi Rai,
Abdallah Ngozi, Abu Chiaba, Ananiah Mwaboza and Danson Mungatana.
On Thursday, the group snubbed a Coast Parliamentary and Leaders Group
meeting convened by Bahari MP Joe Khamisi, citing "lack of proper
communication".
"There was no agenda and the message had no letterhead. He wrote the
note on pieces of paper. Khamisi should know that we are not members of ODM
to be summoned any time without any agenda," said Mwaboza.
